## Amsler grid

¯ÇØe Amsler grid evaluates the 20┬░ of the visual ¯Ç×eld centred on
¯Ç×xation (Fig. 1.8). It is principally useful in screening for and monitoring macular disease, but will also demonstrate central visual
¯Ç×eld defects originating elsewhere. Patients with a substantial risk
of macular neovascularization (MNV) should be provided with an
Amsler grid for regular use at home.

TIP An Amsler grid is a simple and easy method of monitoring
central visual ¯Ç×eld and is commonly abnormal in patients with
macular disease.

## Charts

¯ÇØere are seven charts, each consisting of a 10-cm outer square
(Figs 1.9 and 1.10).
¯Çü Chart 1 consists of a white grid on a black background, the
outer grid enclosing 400 smaller 5-mm squares. When viewed
at about one-third of a metre, each small square subtends an
angle of 1┬░.
¯Çü Chart 2 is similar to chart 1 but has diagonal lines that aid ¯Ç×xation for patients with a central scotoma.
¯Çü Chart 3 is identical to chart 1 but has red squares. ¯ÇØe redon-black design aims to stimulate long wavelength foveal
cones. It is used to detect subtle colour scotomas and desaturation in toxic maculopathy, optic neuropathy and chiasmal
lesions.
¯Çü Chart 4 consists only of random dots and is used mainly to distinguish scotomas from metamorphopsia, as there is no form
to be distorted.
¯Çü Chart 5 consists of horizontal lines and is designed to detect
metamorphopsia along speci¯Ç×c meridians. It is of particular
use in the evaluation of patients describing di¯ÇÜculty reading.
¯Çü Chart 6 is similar to chart 5 but has a white background and
the central lines are closer together, enabling more detailed
evaluation.
¯Çü Chart 7 includes a ¯Ç×ne central grid, each square subtending an
angle of a half degree, and is more sensitive.

## Technique

¯ÇØe pupils should not be dilated, and in order to avoid a photostress e¯Çøect, the eyes should not yet have been examined on the slit
lamp. A presbyopic refractive correction should be worn if appropriate. ¯ÇØe chart should be well illuminated and held at a comfortable reading distance, optimally around 33 cm.
¯Çü One eye is covered.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe patient is asked to look directly at the central dot with the
uncovered eye, to keep looking at this, and to report any distortion or waviness of the lines on the grid.
¯Çü Reminding the patient to maintain ¯Ç×xation on the central dot,
he or she is asked if there are blurred areas or blank spots anywhere on the grid. Patients with macular disease o¯Ç£en report that
the lines are wavy whereas those with optic neuropathy tend to
remark that some of the lines are missing or faint but not distorted.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe patient is asked if he or she can see all four corners and all
four sides of the square ÔÇô a missing corner or border should
raise the possibility of causes other than macular disease, such
as glaucomatous ¯Ç×eld defects or retinitis pigmentosa.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe patient may be provided with a recording sheet and pen
and asked to draw any anomalies (Fig. 1.11).

6 Psychophysical Tests

## Light brightness comparison test

¯ÇØis is a test of optic nerve function, which is usually normal in
early and moderate retinal disease. It is performed as follows:
¯Çü A light from an indirect ophthalmoscope is shone ¯Ç×rst into the
normal eye and then the eye with suspected disease.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe patient is asked whether the light is symmetrically bright
in both eyes.
¯Çü In optic neuropathy the patient will report that the light is less
bright in the a¯Çøected eye.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe patient is asked to assign a relevant value from 1 to 5 to
the brightness of the light in the diseased eye, in comparison
to the normal eye.

## Photostress test

indicating metamorphopsia, and a dense scotoma.

¯Çü Principles. Photostress testing is a gross test of dark adaptation in which the visual pigments are bleached by light.
¯ÇØis causes a temporary state of retinal insensitivity perceived
by the patient as a scotoma. ¯ÇØe recovery of vision is dependent on the ability of the photoreceptors to re-synthesize visual
pigment. ¯ÇØe test may be useful in detecting maculopathy when

A B

for about 10 seconds; (B) the photostress recovery time is the time taken to read any three
letters of the pre-test acuity line and is normally between 15 and 30 seconds.

ophthalmoscopy is equivocal, as in mild cystoid macular oedema
or central serous retinopathy. It may also di¯Çøerentiate visual loss
caused by macular disease from that caused by an optic nerve lesion.
¯Çü Techniques

{ ¯ÇØe best-corrected distance VA is determined.

{ ¯ÇØe patient ¯Ç×xates on the light of a pen torch or an indirect
ophthalmoscope held about 3 cm away for about 10 seconds (Fig. 1.12A).

{ ¯ÇØe photostress recovery time (PSRT) is the time taken to
read any three letters of the pre-test acuity line and is normally between 15 and 30 seconds (Fig. 1.12B).

{ ¯ÇØe test is performed on the other, presumably normal, eye
and the results are compared.

{ ¯ÇØe PSRT is prolonged relative to the normal eye in macular disease (sometimes 50 seconds or more) but not in an
optic neuropathy.

retinal dystrophies prior to the impairment of other visual parameters. Colour vision depends on three populations of retinal cones,
each with a speci¯Ç×c peak sensitivity: blue (tritan) at 414ÔÇô424 nm,
green (deuteran) at 522ÔÇô539 nm and red (protan) at 549ÔÇô570 nm.

## Normal colour perception requires all these primary colours to

match those within the spectrum. Any given cone pigment may
be de¯Ç×cient (e.g. protanomaly ÔÇô red weakness) or entirely absent
(e.g. protanopia ÔÇô red blindness). Trichromats possess all three
types of cones (although not necessarily functioning perfectly),
while absence of one or two types of cones renders an individual
a dichromat or monochromat, respectively. Most individuals with
congenital colour defects are anomalous trichromats and use
abnormal proportions of the three primary colours to match those
in the light spectrum. ¯ÇØose with redÔÇôgreen de¯Ç×ciency caused
by abnormality of red-sensitive cones are protanomalous (2%
of men), those with abnormality of green-sensitive cones (6% of
men) are deuteranomalous and those with blueÔÇôgreen de¯Ç×ciency
caused by abnormality of blue-sensitive cones are tritanomalous.
Acquired macular disease tends to produce blueÔÇôyellow defects,
and optic nerve lesions, redÔÇôgreen defects.

TIP Children who are found to have a congenital colour vision
de¯Ç×ciency will ¯Ç×nd it di¯ÇÜcult or impossible to engage in certain
occupations and may bene¯Ç×t from early career counselling.

## Colour vision tests

¯Çü ¯Ç×e Ishihara test is designed to screen for congenital protan
and deuteran defects. It is simple, widely available and frequently used to screen for a redÔÇôgreen colour vision de¯Ç×cit.

## Colour vision testing

## Introduction

## Assessment of colour vision is useful in the evaluation of optic
nerve disease and in determining the presence of a congenitally

anomalous colour defect. Congenital colour vision de¯Ç×ciency
(CVD) is a common X-linked recessive condition that a¯Çøects
about 8% of Northern European men and 0.5% of women. ¯ÇØe
prevalence in Black Africans is about 4% for men and 1.5% for
women. CVD is the decreased ability to see colour or di¯Çøerences in
colour along a redÔÇôgreen axis. It can impair tasks such as choosing
clothing and seeing tra¯ÇÜc lights. Dyschromatopsia may develop in

8 Psychophysical Tests

A B

C

D

FarnsworthÔÇôMunsell 100-hue test.

¯ÇØe test can be used to assess optic nerve function. It consists
of a test plate followed by 16 plates, each with a matrix of dots
arranged to show a central shape or number that the subject is
asked to identify (Fig. 1.13A). A colour-de¯Ç×cient person will
be able to identify only some of the ¯Ç×gures. Inability to identify
the test plate (provided VA is su¯ÇÜcient) indicates non-organic
visual loss.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e City University test consists of 10 plates, each containing
a central colour and four peripheral colours (Fig. 1.13B) from
which the subject is asked to choose the closest match.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e HardyÔÇôRandÔÇôRittler test is similar to the Ishihara, but
can detect all three congenital colour defects (Fig. 1.13C).
¯Çü ¯Ç×e FarnsworthÔÇôMunsell 100-hue test is a sensitive but
longer test for both congenital and acquired colour defects.
Despite the name, it consists of 85 caps of di¯Çøerent hues in
four racks (Fig. 1.13D). ¯ÇØe subject is asked to rearrange randomized caps in order of colour progression, and the ¯Ç×ndings
are recorded on a circular chart. Each of the three forms of
dichromatism is characterized by failure in a speci¯Ç×c meridian
of the chart (Fig. 1.14).

## Plus lens test

A temporary hypermetropic shi¯Ç£ may occur in some conditions
due to an elevation of the sensory retina ÔÇô the classic example is
central serous chorioretinopathy (CSR). A +1.00-dioptre lens will
demonstrate the phenomenon.

PERIMETRY

De¯Çƒnitions

¯Çü ¯Ç×e visual ¯Çƒeld can be represented as a three-dimensional
structure akin to a hill of increasing sensitivity (Fig. 1.15A).
¯ÇØe outer aspect extends approximately 50┬░ superiorly, 60┬░
nasally, 70┬░ inferiorly and 90┬░ temporally. VA is sharpest
at the very top of the hill (i.e. the fovea) and then declines
progressively towards the periphery, the nasal slope being
steeper than the temporal. ¯ÇØe ÔÇÿbottomless pitÔÇÖ of the blind
spot is located temporally between 10┬░ and 20┬░, slightly below
the horizontal.
¯Çü An isopter is a line connecting points of the same sensitivity,
and on a two-dimensional isopter plot encloses an area within
which a stimulus of a given strength is visible. When the ¯Ç×eld
is represented as a hill, isopters resemble the contour lines on
a map (Fig. 1.15B).
¯Çü A scotoma is an area of reduced (ÔÇÿrelativeÔÇÖ) or total (ÔÇÿabsoluteÔÇÖ)
loss of vision surrounded by a seeing area.
¯Çü Luminance is the intensity or ÔÇÿbrightnessÔÇÖ of a light stimulus,
measured in apostilbs (asb). A higher intensity stimulus has a
higher asb value; this is related inversely to sensitivity.
¯Çü A logarithmic rather than a linear scale is used for stimulus
intensity and sensitivity, so that for each log unit intensity
changes by a factor of 10. With a log scale, greater signi¯Ç×cance
is given to the lower end of the intensity range. ¯ÇØe normal eye
has a very large sensitivity range, and assessment of the lower
end of the scale is of critical signi¯Ç×cance so that early damage

B

## Fixation

90

## Degrees from fixation

A B

can be detected. With a linear scale, the lower end would be
reduced to a very small portion of a graphical chart axis. ¯ÇØe
visual system itself operates on close to a logarithmic scale,
so using this method more closely matches the physiological
situation.
¯Çü Decibels. Simple log units are not used in clinical perimetry,
but rather ÔÇÿdecibelsÔÇÖ (dB), where 10 dB = 1 log unit. Decibels
are not true units of luminance but a representation, and vary
between visual ¯Ç×eld machines. Perimetry usually concentrates on the eyeÔÇÖs sensitivity rather than the stimulus intensity. ¯ÇØerefore, the decibel reading goes up as retinal sensitivity
increases, which obviously corresponds to reducing intensity
of the perceived stimulus. ¯ÇØis makes the assessment of visual
¯Ç×elds more intuitive, as a higher number corresponds with
higher retinal sensitivity. If the sensitivity of a test location is
20 dB (= 2 log units), a point with a sensitivity of 30 dB would
be the more sensitive. ¯ÇØe blind spot has a sensitivity of 0 dB.
If, on a given machine, seeing a stimulus of 1000asb gives a
value of 10 dB, a stimulus of 100asb will give 20 dB.
¯Çü Di¯ÇØerential light sensitivity represents the degree by which
the luminance of a target must exceed background luminance
in order to be perceived. ¯ÇØe visual ¯Ç×eld is therefore a threedimensional representation of di¯Çøerential light sensitivity at
di¯Çøerent points.
¯Çü ¯Ç×reshold at a given location in the visual field is the
brightness of a stimulus at which it can be detected by the
subject. It is defined as ÔÇÿthe luminance of a given fixedlocation stimulus at which it is seen on 50% of the occasions it is presentedÔÇÖ. In practice we usually talk about an
eyeÔÇÖs sensitivity at a given point in the field rather than the
stimulus intensity. The threshold sensitivity is highest at
the fovea and decreases progressively towards the periphery. After the age of 20 years the sensitivity decreases by
about 1 dB per 10 years.
¯Çü Background luminance. ¯ÇØe retinal sensitivity at any location
varies depending on background luminance. Rod photoreceptors are more sensitive in dim light than cones, and so owing to
their preponderance in the peripheral retina, at lower (scotopic)

light levels the peripheral retina becomes more sensitive in proportion to the central retina. ¯ÇØe hill of vision ¯Çƒattens, with a
central crater rather than a peak at the fovea due to the high
concentration of cones, which have low sensitivity in scotopic
conditions. Some diseases give markedly di¯Çøerent ¯Ç×eld results
at di¯Çøerent background luminance levels, e.g. in retinitis pigmentosa the ¯Ç×eld is usually much worse with low background
luminance. It should be noted that it takes about 5 minutes
to adapt from darkness to bright sunlight and 20ÔÇô30 minutes
from bright sunlight to darkness. ¯ÇØe HFA (see below) uses a
photopic (preferentially cone) level of background luminance
at 31.5asb.
¯Çü Static perimetry. A method of assessing ¯Ç×elds, usually automated, in which the location of a stimulus remains ¯Ç×xed, with
intensity increased until it is seen by the subject (threshold
is reached ÔÇô Fig. 1.16A) or decreased until it is no longer
detected.
¯Çü Kinetic (dynamic) perimetry is now much less commonly
performed than static perimetry. A stimulus of constant intensity is moved from a non-seeing area to a seeing area (Fig.
1.16B) at a standardized speed until it is perceived, and the
point of perception is recorded on a chart. Points from di¯Çøerent meridians are joined to plot an isopter for that stimulus
intensity. Stimuli of di¯Çøerent intensities are used to produce a
contour map of the visual ¯Ç×eld. Kinetic perimetry can be performed by means of a manual (Goldmann) or an automated
perimeter if the latter is equipped with an appropriate so¯Ç£ware
program.
¯Çü Manual perimetry involves presentation of a stimulus by the
perimetrist, with manual recording of the response. It was formerly the standard method of ¯Ç×eld testing but has now largely
been superseded by automated methods. It is still used occasionally, particularly in cognitively limited patients unable
to interact adequately with an automated system, and for
dynamic testing of peripheral ¯Ç×elds.
¯Çü Standard automated perimetry (SAP) is the method used
routinely in most clinical situations. Automated perimeters
in common use include the Humphrey Field Analyser (HFA),

A B

increased until perceived ÔÇô areas of lower sensitivity perceive only stimuli of greater intensity
(longer red arrows); (B) kinetic ÔÇô stimulus of constant intensity is moved from a non-seeing
area until perceived.

the Octopus, Medmont, Henson and Dicon (Figs 1.17 and
1.18). ¯ÇØese predominantly utilize static testing, though
so¯Ç£ware is available on some machines to perform dynamic
assessment.

TIP Visual ¯Ç×eld results should always be used in conjunction
with the clinical ¯Ç×ndings.

## Testing algorithms

## Threshold

¯ÇØreshold perimetry is used for detailed assessment of the hill
of vision by plotting the threshold luminance value at various locations in the visual ¯Ç×eld and comparing the results with
age-matched ÔÇÿnormalÔÇÖ values. A typical automated strategy is
to present a stimulus of higher than expected intensity. If seen,
the intensity is decreased in steps (e.g. 4 dB) until it is no longer
seen (ÔÇÿstaircasingÔÇÖ). ¯ÇØe stimulus is then increased again (e.g. 2
dB steps) until seen once more (Fig. 1.19). If the stimulus is not
seen initially, its intensity is increased in steps until seen. Essentially, the threshold is crossed in one direction with large increments, then crossed again to ÔÇÿ¯Ç×ne-tuneÔÇÖ the result with smaller
increments. ¯ÇØreshold testing is commonly used for monitoring
glaucoma.

## Suprathreshold

Suprathreshold perimetry involves testing with stimuli of luminance above the expected normal threshold levels for an agematched population to assess whether these are detected. In other
words, testing to check that a subject can see stimuli that would be
seen by a normal person of the same age. It enables testing to be
carried out rapidly to indicate whether function is grossly normal
or not and is usually reserved for screening.

## Fast algorithms

In recent years, strategies have been introduced with shorter testing times, providing e¯ÇÜciency bene¯Ç×ts with little or no detriment
to testing accuracy. ¯ÇØe HFA o¯Çøers the SITA (Swedish Interactive
¯ÇØresholding Algorithm), which uses a database of normal and
glaucomatous ¯Ç×elds to estimate threshold values and takes responses
during the test into account to arrive at adjusted estimates throughout the test. Full-threshold values are obtained at the start of the test
for four points. SITA-Standard and SITA-Fast (Fig. 1.20) versions
are available. ¯ÇØe Octopus Perimeter uses G-TOP (Glaucoma Tendency Oriented Perimetry), which estimates thresholds based on
information gathered from more detailed assessment of adjacent
points. TOP presents each stimulus once at each location, instead of
4ÔÇô6 times per location with a standard technique.

## Testing patterns

¯Çü Glaucoma

{ Most important defects in glaucoma occur centrally ÔÇô
within a 30┬░ radius from the ¯Ç×xation point ÔÇô so this is the
area most commonly tested.

{ 24-2 is a routinely used glaucoma-orientated pattern. ÔÇÿ24ÔÇÖ
denotes the extent in degrees to which the ¯Ç×eld is tested
on the temporal side (to 30┬░ on the nasal side). ¯ÇØe number a¯Ç£er the hyphen (2) describes the pattern of the points
tested. 30-2 is an alternative.

{ 10-2 is used to assess a central area of radius 10┬░. Glaucomatous defects here may threaten central vision. ¯ÇØe 10-2
pattern facilitates more detailed monitoring of the extent
of damage, especially in advanced glaucoma where there
is ÔÇÿsplitÔÇÖ ¯Ç×xation.
¯Çü Peripheral ¯Çƒeld. Patterns that include central and peripheral
points (e.g. FF-120) are typically limited to the assessment of
neurological defects.

¯Çü Binocular ¯Çƒeld testing (e.g. Esterman strategy) is used to
assess statutory driving entitlement in many jurisdictions.

## Analysis

SAP provides the clinician with an array of clinically relevant information via monitor display or printout. ¯ÇØe patientÔÇÖs name and age
are con¯Ç×rmed and a check made that any appropriate refractive
error compensation was used. General information should be
reviewed, such as the type of algorithm performed, the time taken
for the test and the order in which the eyes were tested.

## Reliability indices

Reliability indices (see Fig. 1.20A) re¯Çƒect the extent to which the
patientÔÇÖs results are reliable. If signi¯Ç×cantly unreliable, further
evaluation of the visual ¯Ç×eld printout is pointless. With SITA
strategies, false negatives or false positives over about 15% should
probably be regarded as signi¯Ç×cant and with full-threshold strategies, ¯Ç×xation losses over 20% and false positives or negatives over
33%. In patients who consistently fail to achieve good reliability
it may be useful to switch to a suprathreshold strategy or kinetic
perimetry.
¯Çü Fixation losses indicate steadiness of gaze during the test.

## Methods of assessment include presentation of stimuli to the

blind spot to ensure no response is recorded, and the use of a
ÔÇÿgaze monitorÔÇÖ.
¯Çü False positives are usually assessed by decoupling a stimulus
from its accompanying sound. If the sound alone is presented
and the patient still responds, a false positive is recorded. With
a high false-positive score the grey scale printout appears
abnormally pale (Fig. 1.21). In SITA testing, false positives are
estimated based on the response time.
¯Çü False negatives are registered by presenting a stimulus much brighter than threshold at a location where the

threshold has already been determined. If the patient fails to
respond, a false negative is recorded. A high false-negative
score indicates inattention, tiredness or malingering, but is
occasionally an indication of disease severity rather than
unreliability. The grey scale printout in individuals with
high false-negative responses tends to have a clover leaf
shape (Fig. 1.22).

TIP If the reliability indices are poor, be careful when
interpreting the results of a visual ¯Ç×eld test.

## Sensitivity values

¯Çü A numerical display (see Fig. 1.20B) gives the measured or estimated (depending on strategy) threshold in dB at each point. In
a full-threshold strategy, where the threshold is rechecked either
as routine or because of an unexpected (>5 dB) result, the second
result is shown in brackets next to the ¯Ç×rst.
¯Çü A grey scale represents the numerical display in graphical form
(see Fig. 1.20C) and is the simplest display modality to interpret: decreasing sensitivity is represented by darker tones ÔÇô
the physiological blind spot is a darker area in the temporal
¯Ç×eld typically just below the horizontal axis. Each change in
grey scale tone is equivalent to a 5 dB change in sensitivity at
that location.
¯Çü Total deviation (see Fig. 1.20D) shows the di¯Çøerence between
a test-derived threshold at a given point and the normal sensitivity at that point for the general population, correcting for
age. Negative values indicate lower than normal sensitivity,
positive values higher than normal.
¯Çü Pattern deviation (see Fig. 1.20E) is derived from total deviation values adjusted for any generalized decrease in sensitivity
in the overall ¯Ç×eld (e.g. lens opacity), and demonstrates localized defects.
¯Çü Probability value plots of the total and pattern deviation (see

to <0.5%) of the normal population in whom the measured
defect at each point would be expected. Darker symbols represent a greater likelihood that a defect is signi¯Ç×cant.

## Summary values

Summary values (ÔÇÿglobal indicesÔÇÖ on the HFA ÔÇô see Fig. 1.20F)
represent distilled statistical information, which considers agematched normal data. ¯ÇØese values are principally used to monitor progression of glaucomatous damage rather than for initial
diagnosis.
¯Çü Visual ¯Çƒeld index (VFI) in the HFA is a measure of the
patientÔÇÖs overall visual ¯Ç×eld function expressed as a percentage,
the normal age-adjusted value being 100%.
¯Çü Mean deviation (MD) on the HFA (mean defect on the Octopus) gives an indication of the overall sensitivity of the ¯Ç×eld. It
is derived from averaging the total deviation values.

¯Çü Pattern standard deviation (PSD) is a measure of focal loss
or variability within the ¯Ç×eld and considers any generalized
depression in the hill of vision. An increased PSD is therefore a more speci¯Ç×c indicator of glaucomatous damage than
MD.
¯Çü Loss variance (LV) is a summary measure on the Octopus
Perimeter similar to PSD.
¯Çü Probability values. Abnormal summary values are followed
by a probability value, representing the percentage likelihood
that an abnormal value of this level will occur in a normal
subject. ¯ÇØe lower the P value, the more likely the result is
abnormal.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe glaucoma hemi¯Çƒeld test (GHT) compares corresponding
areas in the superior and inferior hemi¯Ç×elds and relates only
to glaucoma.

Computed analysis of serial ¯Çƒelds

Computed analysis of serial visual ¯Ç×elds for progression is in widespread use. A disadvantage is the requirement for several reliable
¯Ç×elds to be carried out before analysis is e¯Çøective. ¯ÇØe quality of
available so¯Ç£ware has been improving steadily, with integrated
programs such as GPA (Guided Progression Analysis) on the HFA
and several trend analysis options on the Octopus.

High-sensitivity ¯Çƒeld modalities

SAP tends to detect ¯Ç×eld damage only a¯Ç£er substantial ganglion
cell loss is established. Attempts at detecting change at an earlier
stage include the adoption of stimuli intended to target speci¯Ç×c
ganglion cell types.
¯Çü Short-wave automated perimetry (SWAP) uses a blue stimulus on a yellow background. Sensitivity to blue light (mediated
by blue cone photoreceptors) is adversely a¯Çøected relatively
early in glaucoma. SWAP is more sensitive to early glaucomatous defects but has not been widely adopted because cataract
decreases sensitivity to blue light (the brunescent lens acts as a
yellow ¯Ç×lter) and patients frequently dislike the lengthy test. It
is available on newer HFA models.
¯Çü Frequency-doubling test (FDT). Large diameter axon (magnocellular) ganglion cells appear to be preferentially lost in
early glaucoma. ¯ÇØe frequency-doubling illusion is produced
when a low spatial frequency sinusoidal grating undergoes
high temporal frequency counter phase ¯Çƒicker (>15Hz). ¯ÇØe
rapid alternation in which the light bars become dark and vice
versa produces the illusion of the grating having doubled its
frequency; magnocellular ganglion cells are believed to mediate the pathways used. Screening (Fig. 1.23) and extended testing (Humphrey Matrix) perimeter versions are available, the
latter being suitable for detailed assessment and monitoring of
glaucoma. Fig. 1.23 Screening frequency-doubling perimeter with display.

18 Perimetry

## Sources of error

¯Çü Inexperienced or unskilled technician. ¯ÇØough less important with SAP than manual perimetry, correctly setting up the
test, explaining the procedure to and reassuring the patient
and monitoring performance are fundamental to obtaining an
accurate ¯Ç×eld.
¯Çü Incorrect patient details. ¯ÇØe patientÔÇÖs date of birth must be
entered correctly to facilitate appropriate normative database
matching.
¯Çü Poor patient performance.
¯Çü Uncorrected refractive error can cause a signi¯Ç×cant decrease
in central sensitivity. If a patient with hypermetropia who usually wears contact lenses is tested wearing spectacles, this will
have the e¯Çøect of magnifying and enlarging any scotomas as
compared with contact lenses. Most perimetry is performed
with a stimulus at approximately reading distance, so a near
correction should be used for individuals who are presbyopic.
¯Çü Spectacle rim artefact. Spectacles can cause rim scotomas if
small aperture lenses are used or if incorrectly dispensed (Fig.
1.24A). Narrow-aperture trial frame lenses are unsuitable for
perimetry.
¯Çü Miosis decreases sensitivity in the peripheral ¯Ç×eld and
increases variability in the central ¯Ç×eld in both normal and
glaucomatous eyes. Pupils less than 3mm in diameter should
therefore be dilated prior to perimetry; a consistent mydriatic
should be used for serial tests.
¯Çü Media opacities (usually cataract) can have a profound e¯Çøect,
exaggerated by miosis.
¯Çü Ptosis, even if mild, can suppress the superior visual ¯Ç×eld (Fig.
1.24B). Similar e¯Çøects result from dermatochalasis, prominent
eyelashes and deeply set eyes.

¯Çü Inadequate retinal adaptation may lead to error if perimetry
is performed soon a¯Ç£er ophthalmoscopy.

TIP Perimetry is a subjective examination and is not always
reliable.

## Microperimetry

¯Çü Microperimetry is a visual ¯Ç×eld test that measures retinal sensitivity and ¯Ç×xation behaviour in patients with macular disease and
focal glaucoma involving the central visual ¯Ç×eld. It allows an exact
correlation between pathology involving the macula and the corresponding functional abnormality. Microperimetry is more sensitive than SAP in detecting subtle abnormality of visual function.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe MAIA perimeter is a table-top instrument (Fig. 1.25). ¯ÇØe
test is usually undertaken a¯Ç£er 20 minutes of adaptation in
mesopic conditions. Fundus tracking occurs utilizing a linescanning laser ophthalmoscope (SLO), with super-luminescent
diode illumination using a central wavelength of 850nm.
¯Çü Goldmann size 3 stimuli are projected onto the central 9┬░ of
the fundus. A 4-2 staircase thresholding technique is used, with
each stimulus applied for 200 milliseconds against a white background. Using this instrument normal retinal sensitivity is 18dB.
¯Çü Results are printed, together with reliability indices (¯Ç×xation
loss), probabilities and retinal sensitivity values, which are
colour-coded. Microperimetry is particularly useful in assessing the e¯Çøect of existing and future therapeutic interventions
on the macula (Fig. 1.26). It can also be helpful in patients with
early glaucoma, particularly when there is subtle change close
to ¯Ç×xation.

30 30

A B

upper lid artefact.

20 Slit Lamp Biomicroscopy of the Anterior Segment

SLIT LAMP BIOMICROSCOPY OF THE
ANTERIOR SEGMENT

¯ÇØe purpose of slit lamp examination of the cornea and anterior
segment is to determine the position, depth and size of any abnormality (Fig. 1.27).

## Direct illumination

Direct illumination with a di¯Çøuse light is used to detect gross
abnormalities:
¯Çü A narrow obliquely directed slit beam is used to visualize a
cross-section of the cornea.

## Direct illumination

## Scleral scatter

## Retroillumination

segment.

¯Çü Further narrowing of the beam to a very thin optical section
moved across the cornea can determine the depth of a lesion.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe height of the coaxial beam can be adjusted to measure the horizontal and vertical size of a lesion or associated epithelial defect.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe use of a red-free ¯Ç×lter makes red objects appear black, thereby
increasing contrast when observing vascular structures. A cobalt
blue ¯Ç×lter is normally used in conjunction with ¯Çƒuorescein.

## Scleral scatter

## Scleral scatter involves decentering the slit beam laterally so that
the light is incident on the limbus with the microscope focused

centrally. Light is transmitted within the cornea by total internal re¯Çƒection. A corneal stromal lesion will become illuminated
because of forward light scatter. ¯ÇØis technique is especially useful
to detect subtle stromal haze, or cellular or lipid in¯Ç×ltration.

## Retroillumination

Retroillumination uses re¯Çƒected light from the iris or fundus a¯Ç£er
pupil dilatation to illuminate the cornea. ¯ÇØis allows the detection
of ¯Ç×ne epithelial and endothelial changes, such as epithelial cysts,
keratic precipitates and small blood vessels.

Specular re¯Ç×ection

Specular re¯Çƒection shows abnormalities of the endothelium such
as reduced cell density and guttata. Pseudoguttata probably represent reversible endothelial cell oedema and in¯Çƒammatory cells
beneath the endothelial layer.

## Portable slit lamp

A hand-held slit lamp allows high-quality examination of the anterior segment in individuals who cannot be examined at a regular
slit lamp and is particularly useful when performing eye examinations out of a hospital setting (Fig. 1.28).

FUNDUS EXAMINATION

## Direct ophthalmoscopy

¯Çü A hand-held slit lamp direct examination of the structures
of the fundus using an ophthalmoscope can reveal disease of
the eye itself or may reveal an abnormality indicative of disease elsewhere in the body (for example: diabetes, systematic
hypertension, raised intracranial pressure). ¯ÇØe major advantage of direct ophthalmoscopy is that it can be used at the bedside. ¯ÇØe image obtained is signi¯Ç×cantly magni¯Ç×ed (15 times
normal) (Fig. 1.29).
¯Çü ¯ÇØe direct ophthalmoscope can be used to examine the ÔÇÿredÔÇÖ
re¯Çƒex using retroillumination. By placing a +15D lens in position and looking at the fundus at a distance of 15ÔÇô20cm, lens
and vitreous opacities can be detected (Fig. 1.30).
¯Çü ¯ÇØe light beam can be used to (a) illuminate the cornea, allowing detection of a foreign body, (b) check the pupil for irregularity and (c) determine the light re¯Çƒexes. Some ophthalmoscopes
incorporate a cobalt blue ¯Ç×lter, which allows corneal abrasions
and ulcers to be seen a¯Ç£er the insertion of ¯Çƒuorescein.

TIP A direct ophthalmoscope can be used at the bedside
and provides a magni¯Ç×ed view of the fundus, but there is no
stereopsis and the ¯Ç×eld of view is small.

## Technique

¯Çü It is preferable to dilate the pupil if there is no contraindication. However, a good view can be obtained through an undilated pupil by darkening the examination room and by asking
the patient to look into the distance.
¯Çü When examining the right eye, the clinician should stand at
the patientÔÇÖs right side, holding the ophthalmoscope in the
right hand. When examining the le¯Ç£ eye, the clinician should
stand at the le¯Ç£ side, holding the ophthalmoscope in the le¯Ç£
hand and using the le¯Ç£ eye (Fig. 1.31).
¯Çü ¯ÇØe ÔÇÿOÔÇÖ on the illuminated lens dial of the ophthalmoscope
should be selected.
¯Çü To overcome corneal re¯Çƒection, the small aperture can be used
and the light beam directed towards the edge of the pupil, rather
than through the centre. ¯ÇØe linear polarizing ¯Ç×lter may be helpful.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe examinerÔÇÖs free hand should rest on the patientÔÇÖs forehead.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe patient is slowly approached at a slight angle, from the
temporal side.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe light beam is directed at the pupil and the optic disc should
come into view at a distance of about 3.5cm from the eye. If the
disc is not in sharp focus, the ophthalmoscope lenses should
be rotated into the aperture using the index ¯Ç×nger until the
disc becomes clearly visible.

22 Fundus Examination

A B

¯Çü In hyperopic individuals use a plus lens (green) and in myopic
individuals a minus lens (red).
¯Çü Examine the disc for clarity of outline, colour, elevation and
condition of vessels. Check for spontaneous venous pulsation.
Follow the vessels as far as possible into the retinal periphery.
¯Çü To locate the macula, focus the light on the disc then move the
light about 2 disc diameters temporally. Alternatively, ask the
patient to look at the light.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe retinal periphery can be examined by asking the patient to
look up/down and to the sides.

## Slit lamp biomicroscopy

A range of diagnostic contact and non-contact lenses are available for use with the slit lamp. Contact lenses should not be used
if a penetrating injury is suspected or in the presence of corneal
trauma, hyphaema or corneal infection.
¯Çü Non-contact lenses

{ 60D. High-magni¯Ç×cation lens optimized for viewing the
posterior pole from a working distance of 13mm. When
estimating optic disc diameter use a correction factor of
├ù0.88ÔÇô1.0 for the Volk lens and ├ù1.0 for the Nikon lens.

{ 90D. Wide-¯Ç×eld lens with lower magni¯Ç×cation and shorter
(7mm) working distance. Can be used with smaller pupils.
¯ÇØe correction factor is ├ù1.3 (Fig. 1.32).

{ 78D. Intermediate properties; ideal for general-purpose
examination. ¯ÇØe correction factor is ├ù1.1.

{ Miscellaneous. Numerous other lenses are available, o¯Çøering qualities such as a very wide ¯Ç×eld of view and extremely
small pupil capability.

## Technique

Indirect ophthalmoscopy utilizes a high-power convex lens which is
designed to obtain a wide ¯Ç×eld of view of the fundus. ¯ÇØe image is
vertically inverted and laterally reversed. ¯ÇØe technique is as follows:
¯Çü ¯ÇØe slit beam is adjusted to a width of about one-quarter of its
full round diameter.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe illumination is set at an angle coaxial with the slit lamp
viewing system.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe magni¯Ç×cation and light intensity are adjusted to the lowest
settings.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe light beam should be centred to pass directly through the
patientÔÇÖs pupil.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe lens is held directly in front of the cornea just clearing the
lashes so that the light beam passes through its centre.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe fundus is examined by moving the joystick and vertical adjustment mechanism of the slit lamp whilst keeping the lens still.
¯Çü Magni¯Ç×cation is increased to show greater detail.
¯Çü To view the peripheral retina the patient should be instructed
to direct their gaze accordingly.

## Using new technology for retinal
imaging in rural settings

In areas of the world with few specialist eye-care practitioners,
primary health-care workers can take retinal photographs with a

A

B

smartphone, using a clip-on device (Fig. 1.33). ¯ÇØis can then be
sent electronically to a specialist centre for an opinion on the further management.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe Vula Mobile App, which has been fully evaluated in rural
settings in Africa, is a successful example of this technology.

Goldmann three-mirror examination

¯Çü Goldmann three-mirror lens consists of four parts: the central lens and three mirrors set at di¯Çøerent angles. Because the
curvature of the contact surface of the lens is steeper than that
of the cornea, a viscous coupling substance with the same
refractive index as the cornea is required to bridge the gap
between the cornea and the goniolens. It is important to be
familiar with each part of the lens, as follows (Fig. 1.34A):

{ ¯ÇØe central part provides a 30┬░ upright view of the posterior pole.

{ ¯ÇØe equatorial mirror (largest and oblong-shaped) enables
visualization from 30┬░ to the equator.

{ ¯ÇØe peripheral mirror (intermediate in size and squareshaped) enables visualization between the equator and the
ora serrata.

¯Çü Mirror positioning

{ ¯ÇØe mirror should be positioned opposite the area of the

the mirror should be positioned at 6 oÔÇÖclock.

{ When viewing the vertical meridian, the image is upside
down but not laterally reversed, in contrast to indirect
ophthalmoscopy. Lesions located to the le¯Ç£ of 12 oÔÇÖclock
in the retina will therefore also appear in the mirror on the
le¯Ç£-hand side (Fig. 1.34 B and C).

{ When viewing the horizontal meridian, the image is laterally reversed.

{ Anaesthetic drops are instilled.

{ Coupling ¯Çƒuid (high viscosity methylcellulose or equivalent) is inserted into the cup of the contact lens; it should
be no more than half full.

{ ¯ÇØe patient is asked to look up. ¯ÇØe inferior rim of the lens

{ ¯ÇØe illumination column should always be tilted except

with the mirror at 6 oÔÇÖclock).

of posterior pole.

{ When viewing horizontal meridians (i.e. 3 and 9 oÔÇÖclock
positions in the fundus) the column should remain central.

{ When viewing the vertical meridians (i.e. 6 and 12 oÔÇÖclock
positions) the column can be positioned le¯Ç£ or right of centre.

{ When viewing oblique meridians (i.e. 1.30 and 7.30 oÔÇÖclock)

{ When viewing di¯Çøerent positions of the peripheral retina,
the axis of the beam is rotated so that it is always at rightangles to the mirror.

{ To visualize the entire fundus the lens is rotated for 360┬░ using

{ To obtain a more peripheral view of the retina the lens

{ ¯ÇØe vitreous cavity is examined with the central lens using

{ ¯ÇØe posterior pole is examined.

¯Çü Scleral indentation at the slit lamp can be accomplished using
a three-mirror lens with a special attachment (Eisner funnel)
or a purpose-made ora serrata contact lens that combines a
mirror angled similarly to a gonioscopy lens with an incorporated attachment to facilitate scleral depression (e.g. Goldman
One Mirror Glass 904 lens).
¯Çü Miscellaneous contact lenses are divided principally into
those conferring high magni¯Ç×cation for an optimal posterior
pole view, and those o¯Çøering a wide ¯Ç×eld of view, allowing
visualization extending to the ora serrata under optimal conditions. Small pupil capability is available, and a ¯Çƒange is o¯Çøered
on many lenses with the aim of improving stability of retention
and of lens position on the eye.

Head-mounted binocular indirect
ophthalmoscopy

¯ÇØe term binocular indirect ophthalmoscopy (BIO) is by convention used to refer to the head-mounted technique, though strictly
it also applies to slit lamp indirect ophthalmoscopy. BIO allows

26 Fundus Examination

retinal visualization through a greater degree of media opacity
than slit lamp biomicroscopy, and readily facilitates scleral indentation. Light is transmitted from the headset to the fundus through
a condensing lens held at the focal point of the eye, providing an
inverted and laterally reversed image that is observed through a
stereoscopic viewing system (Fig. 1.38A).
¯Çü Lenses of various powers and diameters are available for BIO
(Fig. 1.38B). A lens of lower power confers increased magni¯Ç×cation, but a smaller ¯Ç×eld of view. ¯ÇØe magni¯Ç×cation can be
calculated by dividing the dioptric power of the examining
lens into 60. (For example, a 20 D lens would have a magni¯Ç×cation of 3.) Yellow ¯Ç×lters may improve patient comfort.

{ 20 D (magni¯Ç×es ├ù3; ¯Ç×eld about 45┬░) is the most commonly
used for general examination of the fundus.

{ 28 D (magni¯Ç×cation with the head-mounted set of ├ù2.27;
¯Ç×eld of 53┬░) has a shorter working distance and is useful
when examining patients with small pupils.

{ ¯ÇØe pupils should be dilated. Reducing the ambient illumination is o¯Ç£en helpful as this improves contrast and allows

{ For the examination of small children (e.g. retinopathy

a squint hook employed to direct the position of the eye.
¯Çü Scleral indentation

{ ¯ÇØe main function of scleral indentation (depression) is to
improve visualization of the retina anterior to the equator;
it also permits kinetic evaluation (Fig. 1.40).

{ Indentation should be attempted only a¯Ç£er the basic technique of BIO has been mastered. It requires practised

coordination between the relative position of the indenter
and the viewing apparatus, as well as care to prevent
patient discomfort. For example, to view the ora serrata at
12 oÔÇÖclock, the patient is asked to look down and the scleral
indenter (a cotton-tipped applicator is preferred by some
practitioners) is applied to the outside of the upper eyelid
at the margin of the tarsal plate.

{ With the indenter in place, the patient is asked to look up.
At the same time the indenter is advanced into the anterior orbit parallel with the globe, and the examinerÔÇÖs eyes
are aligned with the condensing lens and indenter (see Fig.
1.39B).

{ Gentle pressure is exerted so that a mound is created. A¯Ç£er

{ ¯ÇØe indenter should be kept tangential to the globe at all

even risk perforation if the sclera is very thin.

{ For viewing the 3 and 9 oÔÇÖclock positions, indentation
directly on the sclera is sometimes necessary, facilitated by

the slit lamp using some fundus contact lenses.

## Fundus drawing

When available, wide-¯Ç×eld photographic imaging can be an excellent aid in recording the features of a retinal detachment (Fig.
1.41), but documentation generally takes the form of a manually
drawn illustration that optimally is colour-coded (Fig. 1.42). If a
retinal detachment is found, the boundaries are drawn by starting
at the optic nerve and then extending to the periphery. Detached
retina is shaded in blue and ¯Çƒat retina in red. ¯ÇØe course of retinal vessels (usually veins) is indicated with blue. Retinal breaks are
drawn in red with blue outlines; the ¯Çƒap of a retinal tear is also
drawn in blue. ¯ÇØin retina may be represented by red hatching outlined in blue and lattice degeneration by blue hatching outlined

A B

A B

with indentation.

28 Tonometry

retinal dialysis.

in blue. Retinal pigment is indicated in black, retinal exudates in
yellow and vitreous opacities in green.

TONOMETRY

## Goldmann tonometry

## Principles

Goldmann applanation tonometry is based on the ImbertÔÇôFick
principle, which states that for an ideal, dry, thin-walled sphere,
the pressure inside the sphere (P) equals the force necessary to
¯Çƒatten its surface (F) divided by the area of ¯Çƒattening (A) (i.e. P =
F/A). ¯ÇØe intraocular pressure (IOP) is proportional to the pressure applied to the globe (in practice, the cornea) and the thickness of the wall of the globe (i.e. the thickness of the cornea, which
is variable). ¯ÇØe human eye, however, is not an ideal sphere ÔÇô the
cornea is rigid and resists ¯Çƒattening. Capillary attraction of the tear
meniscus, however, tends to pull the tonometer towards the cornea.

## Corneal rigidity and capillary attraction cancel each other out

when the ¯Çƒattened area has a diameter of 3.06 mm, as in Goldmann
tonometry (Fig. 1.43A). ¯ÇØe Goldmann tonometer is an accurate
variable-force tonometer consisting of a double prism (Fig. 1.43B).
¯ÇØe tonometer prism should be disinfected between patients and
replaced regularly in accordance with the manufacturerÔÇÖs instructions. Two per cent Na-hypochlorite (dilute bleach) o¯Çøers e¯Çøective
disinfection against adenovirus and herpes simplex virus. However,
disinfectants can cause the tonometer tip to swell and crack with
time, which can lead to disinfectant entering the tonometer tip
resulting in a corneal abrasion when applied to the cornea. Seventy
per cent isopropyl alcohol wipes do not o¯Çøer protection against
viral infections. Disposable tonometer prisms and caps have been
introduced to address concerns of infection from reusable prisms.

## Technique

¯Çü Topical anaesthetic (commonly proxymetacaine 0.5%) and a
small amount of ¯Çƒuorescein are instilled into the conjunctival sac.

A B

¯Çü ¯ÇØe patient is positioned at the slit lamp with his or her forehead ¯Ç×rmly against the headrest and instructed to look straight
ahead (o¯Ç£en at the examinerÔÇÖs opposite ear) and to breathe
normally.
¯Çü With the cobalt blue ¯Ç×lter in place and illumination of maximal intensity directed obliquely (approximately 60┬░) at the
prism, the prism is centred in front of the apex of the cornea.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe dial is preset at 1 (i.e. 10 mmHg).
¯Çü ¯ÇØe prism is advanced until it just touches the apex of the cornea (Fig. 1.44A).
¯Çü Viewing is switched to the ocular of the slit lamp.
¯Çü A pattern of two green semi-circular mires will be seen, one
above and one below the horizontal midline, which represent
the ¯Çƒuorescein-stained tear ¯Ç×lm touching the upper and lower
outer halves of the prism. Mire thickness should be around
10% of the diameter of its total arc (Fig. 1.44B). Care should
be taken to horizontally and vertically centre the mires so that
two centralized semi-circles are observed.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe dial on the tonometer is rotated to vary the applied force.
¯ÇØe inner margins of the semi-circles align when a circular
area of diameter precisely 3.06 mm is ¯Çƒattened.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe reading on the dial, multiplied by 10, gives the IOP in
mmHg.

TIP Inadvertent ¯Ç×nger pressure applied to the globe when
undertaking applanation tonometry or squeezing of the eyelids
can cause an anomalously high IOP reading.

## Sources of error

¯Çü Inappropriate ¯Ç£uorescein pattern. Excessive ¯Çƒuorescein will
result in the mires being too thick, with consequent overestimation of IOP. Insu¯ÇÜcient will make the semi-circles too
thin, with consequent underestimation (see Fig. 1.44B, le¯Ç£ and
centre).

A

¯Çü Pressure on the globe from the examinerÔÇÖs ¯Ç×ngers, eyelid squeezing or restricted extraocular muscles (e.g. thyroid
myopathy) may give an anomalously high reading.
¯Çü Central corneal thickness (CCT). Calculations of IOP by
Goldmann applanation tonometry (GAT) assume that CCT
is 520 ╬╝m, with minimal normal variation. If the cornea is
thinner, an underestimation of IOP is likely to result, and if
thicker, an overestimation. Corneas tend to be thicker than
average in individuals with ocular hypertension, and thinner
in normal-tension glaucoma (NTG). Following refractive surgery procedures, the cornea is both thinner and structurally
altered, leading to an underestimation of the IOP. Some methods of IOP measurement (e.g. DCT ÔÇô see below) may reduce
the e¯Çøect of structural confounding variables. Other corneal
mechanical factors may also be important but are less well
de¯Ç×ned.
¯Çü Corneal oedema may result in arti¯Ç×cial lowering of IOP,
hypothesized to be due to a boggy so¯Ç£ening; the associated
increased CCT seems to be more than o¯Çøset.
¯Çü Astigmatism, if signi¯Ç×cant, may give distorted mires as well as
leading to mechanically induced errors. If over 3 dioptres, the
average reading of two can be taken with the prism rotated 90┬░
for the second, or optimally the prism is rotated so that the red
line on the tonometer housing is aligned with the prescription
of the minus axis.
¯Çü Incorrect calibration of the tonometer can result in a false
reading. Calibration should be checked before each clinical
session using the manufacturerÔÇÖs calibration arm (Fig. 1.44C).
¯Çü Wide pulse pressure. It is normal for there to be a small oscillation of IOP in concert with the rhythm of ocular perfusion.
If this ÔÇÿpulse pressureÔÇÖ is substantial, either the midpoint or the
highest level observed may be taken.
¯Çü Repeated readings over a short period will o¯Ç£en be associated with a slight fall in IOP due to a massaging e¯Çøect on the
eye.

B C

(B) ¯ÇØuorescein-stained semi-circular mires ÔÇô the diagram at right showing the correct endpoint using mires of appropriate thickness; (C) tonometer calibration bar in position.

30 Tonometry

¯Çü Other factors, including a tight collar and breath-holding,
both of which obstruct venous return, can raise IOP.

TIP The Goldmann tonometer loses accuracy with constant
use and should be checked for calibration error on a regular
basis.

## Other forms of tonometry

¯Çü Pneumotonometry (Fig. 1.45A) is based on the principle of
applanation, but the central part of the cornea is ¯Çƒattened by a
jet of air rather than a prism. ¯ÇØe time required to su¯ÇÜciently
¯Çƒatten the cornea relates directly to the level of IOP. Contact is
not made with the eye and topical anaesthesia is not required,
so it is particularly useful for screening in the community. ¯ÇØe
sudden jet of air can startle the patient. Accuracy is improved
if an average of at least three readings is taken.
¯Çü Portable applanation tonometry (Perkins) uses a Goldmann
prism in conjunction with a portable light source (Fig. 1.45B).

It is hand-held and can therefore be used in bed-bound or
anaesthetized patients.
¯Çü Dynamic contour tonometry (DCT) (e.g. PASCAL) uses a
solid-state sensor and a corneal contour-matching surface,
with the aim of measuring IOP relatively independently of
corneal mechanical factors such as rigidity. It is mounted on
a slit lamp in similar fashion to the Goldmann tonometer and
IOP is shown on a digital display. Studies comparing DCT and
GAT IOP readings with manometric intracameral IOP seem to
con¯Ç×rm DCT as providing a more physiological measurement.
¯Çü Electronic indentation/applanation tonometry (e.g. TonoPen AVIA ÔÇô Fig. 1.45C) is a hand-held electronic contact
tonometer (a modi¯Ç×ed version of the older MackayÔÇôMarg
tonometer). ¯ÇØe probe tip contains a transducer that measures
applied force. Besides portability, its main advantage is the
facility to measure IOP reasonably accurately in eyes with distorted or oedematous corneas and through a so¯Ç£ contact lens.
¯Çü Rebound tonometry (e.g. iCare ÔÇô Fig. 1.45D) involves a
1.8 mm plastic ball attached to a wire. Deceleration of the
probe upon contact with the cornea is proportional to IOP.

A B

C D

tonometer; (C) Medtronic Tono-Pen; (D) iCare IC100.

Anaesthesia is not required. ¯ÇØe instrument can be used for
self-monitoring and for screening in the community.
¯Çü Home tonometry (e.g. iCare HOME2) is available for patients
who require regular monitoring of their IOP, especially when
information is needed on diurnal IOP ¯Çƒuctuation. It is fairly
easy to use and provides accurate measurements. It has a positioning system for correct alignment of the instrument and a
magnet within the applicator which prevents the probe from
falling out.
¯Çü Indentation (impression) tonometry (e.g. Schiotz) is a
seldom-used portable device that measures the extent of corneal indentation by a plunger of known weight.
¯Çü Implantable tonometers are under development and if a clinically workable device is realized should facilitate accurate lifelong 24-hour IOP measurement.

## Ocular response analyser and corneal
hysteresis

¯ÇØe ocular response analyser (e.g. Reichert ORA) utilizes air-pu¯Çø
technology to record two applanation measurements: one while
the cornea is moving inward and one when the cornea returns to
its normal position. ¯ÇØe average of these two IOP measurements
provides a Goldmann-correlated IOP measurement. ¯ÇØe di¯Çøerence between the IOP measurements is called corneal hysteresis
(Fig. 1.46). Corneal hysteresis is not a measure of the sti¯Çøness of
the cornea, but rather a measure of how corneal tissue absorbs and
dissipates energy during deformation and return. Corneal hysteresis appears to provide a measurement of IOP that is less a¯Çøected
by factors such as previous laser refractive surgery. It also o¯Çøers
information on which patients with glaucoma are more likely to
progress (patients with a low hysteresis value are at a greater risk of
glaucoma progression) and it may serve as a biomarker to aid glaucoma case detection. ¯ÇØere is also a strong relationship between
corneal hysteresis and the magnitude of IOP reduction with topical prostaglandin therapy.

GONIOSCOPY

## Introduction

## Overview

¯Çü Gonioscopy is a method of evaluating the anterior chamber
(AC) angle, and can be used therapeutically for procedures
such as laser trabeculoplasty and goniotomy.
¯Çü Other means of angle assessment, such as anterior segment
optical coherence tomography (OCT) and high-frequency
ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM), o¯Çøer advantages in some
aspects of angle analysis, but current clinical opinion suggests
they should supplement rather than replace gonioscopic evaluation (see Ch. 11).

TIP Gonioscopy is an indispensable technique that allows
the ¯Ç×ltration angle to be examined in patients with ocular
hypertension or glaucoma.

Applanation Signal Pressure (air pulse)

ÔÇ£InÔÇØ Signal Peak dslope1 ÔÇ£OutÔÇØ Signal Peak

Response Analyzer (Reichert). It is de¯Ç×ned as the di¯Ç£erence
between the pressure at which the cornea bends inward
during air-jet applanation and the pressure at which it bends
out again.

## Optical principles

¯ÇØe angle of the AC cannot be visualized directly through the
intact cornea because light from angle structures undergoes ÔÇÿtotal
internal re¯ÇƒectionÔÇÖ at the anterior surface of the precorneal tear
¯Ç×lm (Fig. 1.47, top). When light travels from a medium of higher
to one of lower refractive index (such as cornea to air) it will
be re¯Çƒected at the interface between the two unless the angle of
incidence is less than a certain ÔÇÿcritical angleÔÇÖ dependent on their
refractive index di¯Çøerence (46┬░ for the tear ¯Ç×lmÔÇôair interface). ¯ÇØe
phenomenon is utilized in optical ¯Ç×bre signal transmission, where
it ensures that light is retained within the core of a cable. Because
the refractive index of a goniolens is similar to that of the cornea,
it eliminates total internal re¯Çƒection by replacing the tear ¯Ç×lmÔÇôair
interface with a tear ¯Ç×lmÔÇôgoniolens interface (Fig. 1.47, bottom).
Light rays can then be viewed as they exit the contact lens, directly
or indirectly (see below).

## Indirect gonioscopy

Indirect goniolenses use a mirror to re¯Çƒect rays from the angle
such that they exit the goniolens at much less than the critical
angle. ¯ÇØey provide a mirror image of the opposite angle and can
be used only in conjunction with a slit lamp.

Non-indentation gonioscopy

substance of refractive index similar to the cornea is
required to bridge the gap between cornea and lens.

TIP Gonioscopy should be undertaken in a room where the
ambient illumination is low, as pupil size in¯ÇØuences the grade of
angle closure.

¯Çü Technique

{ It is essential that the examination takes place in a room in
which the ambient illumination is low.

{ ¯ÇØe size and intensity of the slit beam should be reduced to
the absolute minimum compatible with an adequate view.
Do not direct the beam through the pupil.

{ ¯ÇØe patient is seated at the slit lamp and advised that the
lens will touch the eye but will not usually cause discomfort. ¯ÇØe forehead must be kept against the headband and
both eyes should remain open.

{ A drop of local anaesthetic is instilled.

¯Çü Goniolenses

{ ¯ÇØe classic Goldmann lens consists of three mirrors, one
of which is speci¯Ç×cally for gonioscopy. Some goniolenses
have one (Fig. 1.48), two or four mirrors.

{ Lenses of similar basic structure but with modi¯Ç×cations
include the Magna View, Ritch trabeculoplasty and the
Khaw direct view.

{ Because the curvature of the contact surface of the lens
is steeper than that of the cornea, a viscous coupling

{ A drop or two of coupling ¯Çƒuid (e.g. hypromellose 0.3%) is
placed on the contact surface of the lens.

{ ¯ÇØe patient is asked to look upwards and the lens is inserted
rapidly so as to avoid loss of the coupling ¯Çƒuid. ¯ÇØe patient
then looks straight ahead.

{ Indirect gonioscopy gives an inverted view of the portion
of the angle opposite the mirror.

{ Once the initial examination has been performed and the
¯Ç×ndings noted, increasing the level of illumination may
help in de¯Ç×ning the angle structures.

{ When the view of the angle is obscured by a convex iris,
it is possible to see ÔÇÿover the hillÔÇÖ by asking the patient to
look in the direction of the mirror. Only slight movement
is permissible, otherwise the structures will be distorted
and a closed angle may appear open.

{ Excessive pressure with a non-indentation lens narrows
the angle appearance (in contrast to the e¯Çøect of pressure
during indentation gonioscopy ÔÇô see below). Excessive

A B

pressure also causes folds in the cornea that compromise
the clarity of the view.

{ In some eyes, suction on the cornea from the lens may
arti¯Ç×cially open the angle. Awareness of the need to avoid
retrograde, as well as anterograde, pressure on the lens will
tend to prevent inadvertent distortion.

TIP Indentation gonioscopy is a useful technique to
determine the degree of peripheral anterior synechiae in a
patient with appositional angle closure.

Indentation (dynamic, compression)
gonioscopy

¯Çü Goniolenses include the Zeiss (Fig. 1.49), Posner and Sussman
(no handle), all of which are four-mirror gonioprisms.

## Direct gonioscopy

{ ¯ÇØe contact surface of the lenses has a curvature ¯Çƒatter
than that of the cornea, negating the need for a coupling
substance.

{ ¯ÇØe lenses do not stabilize the globe and are relatively
unsuitable for laser trabeculoplasty.

{ ¯ÇØe lenses are useful for indentation gonioscopy.
¯Çü Technique

{ ¯ÇØe ¯Ç×rst stages are as set out above for non-indentation
gonioscopy.

{ Indentation is performed by gently pressing the lens posteriorly against the cornea. ¯ÇØis forces aqueous into the
angle, pushing the peripheral iris posteriorly.

{ If the angle is closed only by apposition between the iris
and cornea it will be forced open, allowing visualization of
the angle recess (Fig. 1.50).

{ If the angle is closed by adhesions between the peripheral
iris and cornea ÔÇô peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS) ÔÇô it
will remain closed.

{ Dynamic gonioscopy can be invaluable in helping to de¯Ç×ne
the structures in angles that are di¯ÇÜcult to assess, such as
in distinguishing an extensive or double highly pigmented
Schwalbe line from the pigmented meshwork.

## Direct goniolenses work by constructing the viewing surface of the

lens in a domed or slanted con¯Ç×guration such that exiting light
rays strike the contact lens/air interface at a steeper than critical
angle so that they will pass through to the observer. ¯ÇØis approach
is called ÔÇÿdirectÔÇÖ because light rays from the angle are viewed
directly, without re¯Çƒection inside the lens. ¯ÇØey do not require
a slit lamp and are used with the patient in the supine position,
typically under general anaesthesia in the evaluation and surgical
treatment of infantile glaucoma.
¯Çü Direct goniolenses include the Koeppe (Fig. 1.51A and B),
Medical Workshop, Barkan and SwanÔÇôJacob.
¯Çü Technique

{ Gonioscopy is performed with the patient in the supine
position (note that this may deepen the angle) in conjunction with an operating or hand-held microscope or magnifying loupes.

{ ¯ÇØe technique cannot be used with a desktop slit lamp so
clarity, illumination and variable magni¯Ç×cation are not
comparable with indirect lenses.

## Surgical gonioscopy

¯Çü Technique

{ ¯ÇØe key to successful microinvasive glaucoma surgery is
adequate visualization of the ¯Ç×ltration angle.

{ ¯ÇØis is best achieved by rotating the operating microscope
downwards (arrow) (Fig. 1.51C) and by tilting the patientÔÇÖs
head away from the surgeon by 30ÔÇô35┬░ (arrow), until the
coaxial light is aligned along the iris plane. Surgical instruments can then approach the angle parallel to the iris plane.

{ A useful surgical landmark is the scleral spur, which separates canal-based surgery (via the trabecular meshwork,
anteriorly), from suprachoroidal-based surgery (via the
ciliary body band, posteriorly).

{ Pigment within the trabecular meshwork can serve as a
visual guide to the Schlemm canal. ¯ÇØe canal can also be

located by applying gentle pressure with the edge of the

Identi¯Çƒcation of angle structures

Accurate identi¯Ç×cation of angle structures (Fig. 1.52AÔÇôC) is not
always straightforward, even for the experienced clinician.
¯Çü Schwalbe line. ¯ÇØis is the most anterior structure, appearing
whitish to variably pigmented. Anatomically it demarcates the
peripheral termination of Descemet membrane and the anterior limit of the trabeculum. It may be barely discernible in
younger patients. In contrast, there may be pigment deposits on or anterior to the Schwalbe line ÔÇô a Sampaolesi line ÔÇô
especially in heavily pigmented angles (e.g. pseudoexfoliation

syndrome). It may have a double-line con¯Ç×guration, when
the posterior component may be mistaken for the pigmented
meshwork (see Ch. 11).
¯Çü ¯Ç×e corneal wedge is useful in locating an inconspicuous
Schwalbe line. Using a narrow slit beam, two distinct linear
corneal re¯Çƒections can be identi¯Ç×ed, one on the inner and one
on the outer corneal surface. ¯ÇØe outer re¯Çƒection will arc round
across the corneoscleral interface ÔÇô due to the sclera being
opaque ÔÇô to meet the inner re¯Çƒection at the apex of the corneal
wedge that coincides with the Schwalbe line (Fig. 1.52D).
¯Çü ¯Ç×e trabeculum extends from the Schwalbe line to the scleral
spur, with an average width of 600 ╬╝m. In younger people it
has a ground-glass translucent appearance. ¯ÇØe anterior nonfunctional part lies adjacent to the Schwalbe line and has a
whitish colour. ¯ÇØe posterior pigmented functional part lies
adjacent to the scleral spur and has a greyish-blue translucent
appearance in the young. Trabecular pigmentation is rare prior

36 Central Corneal Thickness

to puberty, but in older eyes involves the posterior trabeculum
to a variable extent, most marked inferiorly. Patchy trabecular
pigmentation in a suspiciously narrow angle raises the possibility of intermittent iris contact.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e Schlemm canal may be identi¯Ç×ed in the angle, especially
if non-pigmented, as a slightly darker line deep to the posterior trabeculum. Blood can sometimes be seen in the canal,
either physiologically (sometimes due to excessive pressure on
the episcleral veins with a goniolens), or in the presence of low
intraocular or raised episcleral venous pressure.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e scleral spur is the most anterior projection of the sclera
and the site of attachment of the longitudinal muscle of the
ciliary body. Gonioscopically it is situated immediately posterior to the trabeculum and appears as a narrow whitish band
that yellows with age.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e ciliary body stands out just behind the scleral spur as a
pink, dull brown or slate grey band. Its width depends on the
position of iris insertion and it tends to be narrower in hypermetropic eyes and wider in myopic eyes. ¯ÇØe angle recess represents the posterior dipping of the iris as it inserts into the
ciliary body. It may not be visible in some eyes due to a physiological anterior iris insertion, though ¯Ç×xed pathological angle
narrowing due to PAS ÔÇô adhesions between the iris and angle
structures ÔÇô should be excluded.
¯Çü Iris processes are small, usually tenuous extensions of the
anterior surface of the iris that insert at the level of the scleral
spur and cover the ciliary body to a varying extent (Fig. 1.53).
¯ÇØey are present in about one-third of normal eyes and are
most prominent during childhood and in brown eyes. ¯ÇØe
processes should not be confused with PAS, which typically
extend more anteriorly and are more substantial.
¯Çü Blood vessels. Radial vessels at the base of the angle recess are
o¯Ç£en seen in normal eyes. Pathological blood vessels run randomly in various directions. As a general principle, any blood
vessel that crosses the scleral spur onto the trabecular meshwork is abnormal. Larger circumferential vessels may also be
seen.

Pathological ¯Çƒndings

CENTRAL CORNEAL THICKNESS

Central corneal thickness (CCT) can be measured using pachymetry or by Orbscan (see Fig. 11.5). ¯ÇØe normal distribution is
540┬▒30 ╬╝m (mean ┬▒1 standard deviation). CCT in¯Çƒuences GAT
readings. Eyes with a thin cornea have a true IOP that is greater
than the measured IOP. Conversely, eyes with a thick cornea have a
true IOP that is lower than the measured IOP. ¯ÇØere is no validated
and useful correction algorithm for these two measurements.
Patients with NTG tend to have thin CCT measurements. It is an
important value when determining risk of conversion to glaucoma
in individuals with raised IOP (see Ch. 11). It is also an easy and
practical indicator of corneal endothelial function.

TIP CCT should be measured in individuals with ocular
hypertension, as it helps to determine the risk of conversion to
glaucoma.

38 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

## Anatomy

¯Ç×e skin (Fig. 2.1A) consists of the epidermis, dermis and related
structures (adnexa).

## Epidermis

¯Ç×e epidermis comprises four layers of keratin-producing cells
(keratinocytes). It also contains melanocytes, Langerhans cells
and Merkel cells. ¯Ç×e layers of the epidermis around the eye are
described below. Cells migrate super¯ÇØcially, undergoing maturation and di¯Ç£erentiation through successive layers.
¯Çü Keratin layer (stratum corneum or horny layer) consists of ¯Çøat
cells devoid of nuclei.
¯Çü Granular cell layer (stratum granulosum) typically consists of one
or two layers of ¯Çøattened cells containing keratohyaline granules.

¯Çü Prickle cell layer (stratum spinosum) is approximately
five cells deep. The cells are polygonal in cross-section
and have abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. Their free borders are united by spiny-appearing desmosomes (cellular
junctions).
¯Çü Basal cell layer (stratum basale) comprises a single row of
columnar-shaped proliferating cells containing melanin
derived from adjacent melanocytes.

## Dermis

¯Ç×e dermis is much thicker than the epidermis. It is composed
of connective tissue and contains blood vessels, lymphatics and
nerve ¯ÇØbres in addition to ¯ÇØbroblasts, macrophages and mast cells.
Upward dermal extensions (papillae) interdigitate with downward
epidermal projections (rete ridges). In the eyelid the dermis lies
on the orbicularis muscle. Adnexa lie deep in the dermis or within
the tarsal plates.

A B

C D

the surface; pilosebaceous elements are conspicuous in the dermis and a few blood vessels
and sweat glands are also seen; (B) dysplasia with loss of cell polarity; (C) dyskeratosis ÔÇô a
non-surface epithelial cell producing keratin; (D) parakeratosis ÔÇô retention of cell nuclei into
the surface keratin layer. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology,
Butterworth-Heinemann, 2001.)

¯Çü Sebaceous glands are located in the caruncle and within eyebrow hairs. Tiny sebaceous glands are associated with the thin
(vellus) hairs covering periocular skin.
¯Çü Meibomian glands are modi¯ÇØed sebaceous glands found in
the tarsal plates. ¯Ç×ey empty through a single row of 20ÔÇô30
ori¯ÇØces on each lid. A gland consists of a central duct with
multiple acini, the cells of which synthesize lipids (meibum)
that form the outer layer of the tear ¯ÇØlm.
¯Çü Glands of Zeis are modi¯ÇØed sebaceous glands associated with
lash follicles.
¯Çü Glands of Moll are modified apocrine sweat glands opening either into a lash follicle or directly onto the anterior
lid margin between lashes. They are more numerous in the
lower lid.
¯Çü Eccrine sweat glands are distributed throughout eyelid skin
and are not con¯ÇØned to the lid margin, in contrast to glands
of Moll.
¯Çü Pilosebaceous units comprise hair follicles and their sebaceous glands (see Fig. 2.1A).

## Terminology

## Clinical

¯Çü Macule. Localized area of colour change without in¯ÇØltration,
depression or elevation, less than 1 cm in diameter.
¯Çü Papule. A solid elevation less than 1 cm in diameter.
¯Çü Vesicle. Circumscribed lesion containing serous ¯Çøuid (less
than 0.5 cm across).
¯Çü Bulla. A large (more than 0.5 cm) serous ¯Çøuid-¯ÇØlled lesion
(plural ÔÇô bullae).
¯Çü Pustule. A pus-¯ÇØlled elevation less than 1 cm in diameter.
¯Çü Crust. Solidi¯ÇØed serous or purulent exudate.
¯Çü Nodule. A palpable solid area measuring more than 1 cm.
¯Çü Cyst. A nodule consisting of an epithelial-lined cavity ¯ÇØlled
with ¯Çøuid or semi-solid material.
¯Çü Plaque. A solid elevation of the skin, greater than 1 cm in
diameter.
¯Çü Scale. Readily detached fragments of shed keratin layer.
¯Çü Papilloma. A benign neoplastic warty or tag-like projection of
the skin or mucous membrane.
¯Çü Ulcer. A circumscribed area of epithelial loss. In skin an ulcer
extends through the epidermis into the dermis.

## Histological

¯Çü Tumour strictly refers only to a swelling, though is commonly
used to denote a neoplasm.
¯Çü Neoplasia. Abnormal tissue growth, either benign (localized,
non-invasive and non-spreading) or malignant (progressive
growth with the potential for distant spread).
¯Çü Atypia refers to an abnormal appearance of individual cells,
e.g. abnormal mitotic ¯ÇØgures.
¯Çü Dysplasia is an alteration of the size, morphology and organization of cellular components of a tissue. ¯Ç×ere is disturbance

of normally structured and recognized layers of tissue (e.g. loss
of cell polarity ÔÇô Fig. 2.1B).
¯Çü Carcinoma in situ (intraepidermal carcinoma, Bowen disease) exhibits dysplastic changes throughout the thickness of
the epidermis.
¯Çü Hyperkeratosis. An increase in thickness of the keratin layer
that appears clinically as scaling. Hyperkeratosis can be a feature of benign or malignant epithelial tumours.
¯Çü Acanthosis. ¯Ç×ickening of the prickle cell layer.
¯Çü Dyskeratosis is keratinization other than on the epithelial
surface (Fig. 2.1C).
¯Çü Parakeratosis is the retention of nuclei into the keratin layer
(Fig. 2.1D).

## General considerations

¯Çü Classi¯Çƒcation. Epidermal, adnexal or dermal.
¯Çü Diagnosis. ¯Ç×e clinical characteristics of benign lesions are
a tendency to a lack of induration and ulceration, uniform
colour, limited growth, regular outline and preservation of
normal lid margin structures. Biopsy may be required if the
appearance is suspicious.

{ Incisional biopsy involves removal of a portion of a lesion
for histopathology.

{ Excision biopsy is performed on small tumours and ful¯ÇØls
both diagnostic and treatment objectives.
¯Çü Treatment options include:

{ Excision of the entire lesion and a small surrounding portion of normal tissue.

{ Marsupialization involves the removal of the top of a
cyst allowing drainage of its contents and subsequent
epithelialization.

{ Ablation with laser or cryotherapy.

NON-NEOPLASTIC LESIONS

Chalazion (meibomian cyst)

## Pathogenesis

A chalazion is a sterile chronic granulomatous in¯Çøammatory lesion
(lipogranuloma) of the meibomian, or sometimes Zeis, glands
caused by retained sebaceous secretions. Histopathology shows a
lipogranulomatous chronic in¯Çøammatory picture with extracellular fat deposits surrounded by lipid-laden epithelioid cells, multinucleated giant cells and lymphocytes (Fig. 2.2A). Blepharitis is
commonly present; rosacea can be associated with multiple and
recurrent chalazia. Bortezomib, a proteasome inhibitor used in
the treatment of multiple myeloma, predisposes to the formation
of chalazia within 3 months of initiation of treatment. A recurrent
chalazion should be biopsied to exclude masquerading malignancy.

TIP If a chalazion recurs in the same lid position in an
older patient, consider undertaking a biopsy to exclude
malignancy.

40 Non-Neoplastic Lesions

C D

processing; (B) unin¯Ç×amed chalazion; (C) marginal chalazion with superimposed bacterial
infection; (D) conjunctival chalazion; (E) conjunctival view of chalazion clamp in place; (F)
after curettage. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology,
Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms

{ Subacute/chronic: gradually enlarging painless rounded
nodule (Fig. 2.2B).

or by incision and curettage via a horizontal incision on the
conjunctival surface or vertically through the grey line.
¯Çü Prophylaxis

{ Systemic tetracycline may be required as prophylaxis in
patients with recurrent chalazia, particularly if associated
with acne rosacea.

{ Treatment of blepharitis, e.g. daily lid hygiene regimen.

{ Acute: sterile in¯Çøammation or bacterial infection with
localized cellulitis. A secondarily infected meibomian
gland is referred to as an internal hordeolum (Fig. 2.2C).
¯Çü Signs

{ A nodule within the tarsal plate, sometimes with associated in¯Çøammation (Fig. 2.2D).

{ Bulging inspissated secretions may be visible at the ori¯ÇØce
of the involved gland.

{ ¯Ç×ere may be an associated conjunctival granuloma.

{ A lesion at the anterior lid margin may be connected to
a typical chalazion deeper in the lid or be due to isolated
involvement of a gland of Zeis.

## Treatment

¯Çü Oral antibiotics are required for signi¯ÇØcant bacterial infection, but not for sterile in¯Çøammation.
¯Çü Conservative. At least a third resolve spontaneously so observation may be appropriate, especially if the lesion is showing
signs of improvement.
¯Çü Hot compress application several times daily may aid resolution, particularly in early lesions.
¯Çü Expression. Compression between two cotton-tipped applicators is sometimes e¯Ç£ective in expressing the contents of a fresh
lesion near the lid margin.
¯Çü Steroid injection into or around the lesion has been reported
to give similar resolution rates to incision and curettage (see
below). It may be preferred for marginal lesions or lesions
close to structures such as the lacrimal punctum because of
the risk of surgical damage.

{ Reported regimens include 0.2ÔÇô2 ml of triamcinolone
acetonide aqueous suspension diluted with lidocaine to
a concentration of 5 mg/ml and 0.1ÔÇô0.2 ml of 40 mg/ml,
injected with a 27- or 30-gauge needle.

{ ¯Ç×e success rate following one injection is about 80%. A
second can be given 1ÔÇô2 weeks later.

{ Local skin depigmentation and fat atrophy are uncommon
complications, which are less likely to occur by utilizing a
conjunctival approach.

{ Retinal vascular occlusion is a rare complication.
¯Çü Surgery

{ Following local anaesthesia in¯ÇØltration, the eyelid is
everted with a specialized clamp (Fig. 2.2E), the cyst is
incised vertically through the tarsal plate and its contents
curetted (Fig. 2.2F).

{ Limited excision of solid in¯Çøammatory material (sent for
histopathology) with ¯ÇØne scissors may be helpful in some
cases, especially if there is no focus of secretions.

## Other eyelid cysts

¯Çü Cyst of Zeis is a small, non-translucent cyst on the anterior lid
margin arising from obstructed sebaceous glands associated
with the eyelash follicle (Fig. 2.3A).
¯Çü Cyst of Moll (apocrine hidrocystoma) is a small retention cyst
of the lid margin apocrine glands. It appears as a round, nontender, translucent ¯Çøuid-¯ÇØlled lesion on the anterior lid margin
(Fig. 2.3B).
¯Çü Sebaceous (pilar) cyst is caused by a blocked pilosebaceous
follicle and contains sebaceous secretions; the gland ori¯ÇØce will
o¯ÇÜen be visible (Fig. 2.3C). It is only rarely found on the eyelid
although it may occasionally occur at the inner canthus.
¯Çü Comedones are plugs of keratin and sebum within the dilated
ori¯ÇØce of hair follicles that o¯ÇÜen occur in patients with acne
vulgaris. ¯Ç×ey may be either open (blackheads), containing
a darkened plug of oxidized material (Fig. 2.3D), or closed
(whiteheads).
¯Çü Milia are caused by occlusion of pilosebaceous units resulting
in retention of keratin. ¯Ç×ey are tiny, white, round, super¯ÇØcial
papules that tend to occur in crops (Fig. 2.3E).
¯Çü Epidermal inclusion cyst is usually caused by implantation of
epidermis into the dermis following trauma or surgery. It is a
slow-growing, round, ¯ÇØrm, super¯ÇØcial or subcutaneous lesion
containing keratin (Fig. 2.3F).
¯Çü Epidermoid cyst is uncommon and usually developmental,
occurring along embryonic lines of closure. It is similar in
appearance to an epidermal inclusion cyst.
¯Çü Dermoid cyst is usually subcutaneous or deeper and is typically attached to the periosteum at the lateral end of the brow
(Fig. 2.3G). It is caused by skin sequestered during embryonic
development.
¯Çü Eccrine hidrocystoma is less common but similar in appearance to a cyst of Moll except that it is usually located along the
medial or lateral aspects of the lid and is close to, but does not
involve, the lid margin itself (Fig. 2.3H).

## Xanthelasma

## Introduction

Xanthelasma (plural ÔÇô xanthelasmata) is a common, frequently
bilateral condition typically a¯Ç£ecting middle-aged and elderly
individuals. It is a subtype of xanthoma. Hyperlipidaemia is
found in about one-third of patients, in whom corneal arcus may
also be present. In contrast to chalazion, fat in xanthelasmata is
mainly intracellular, with lipid-laden histiocytes (foam cells) in
the dermis.

{ A suture should not be used.

{ Topical antibiotic is used three times daily for 5 days following curettage.
¯Çü Marginal lesions can be managed by steroid injection, by
curettage of an associated deeper chalazion, by shave curettage

42 Non-Neoplastic Lesions
