blackheads; (E) milia (arrows); (F) epidermal inclusion cyst; (G) dermoid cyst; (H) eccrine hidrocystomas. (Courtesy of A Pearson ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs D and H.)

A B

## Diagnosis

Xanthelasmata are yellowish subcutaneous plaques, usually in the
medial aspects of the eyelids (Fig. 2.4A), commonly bilateral and
are multiple (Fig. 2.4B).

## Treatment

¯Ç×is is principally for cosmesis. Recurrence occurs in up to 50%
and is most common in patients with hypercholesterolaemia.
¯Çü Simple excision is commonly performed where adequate
excess skin is present.
¯Çü Microdissection. Larger lesions can be raised in a ¯Çøap, the
fatty deposits dissected from overlying skin under a surgical
microscope using micro scissors and the skin replaced.
¯Çü Other methods. Good results can be obtained using chemical peeling with bi- or trichloroacetic acid. Laser ablation and
cryotherapy have advantages but may be more prone to scarring, including pigmentary changes.

BENIGN EPIDERMAL TUMOURS

## Squamous cell papilloma

Histopathology in all clinical types is similar, showing ¯ÇØnger-like
projections of ¯ÇØbrovascular connective tissue covered by irregular
acanthotic and hyperkeratotic squamous epithelium (Fig. 2.5A).

## Squamous cell papilloma is a very common benign epithelial

tumour with a range of clinical appearances, including narrowbased (pedunculated or ÔÇÿskin tagÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 2.5B), pink broad-based
(sessile ÔÇô Fig. 2.5C) and whitish thread-like (¯ÇØliform) hyperkeratotic lesions (Fig. 2.5D). ¯Ç×e incidence increases with age. Some
cases result from human papilloma virus infection. Treatment
usually involves simple excision, but other options include cryotherapy and laser or chemical ablation.

## Seborrhoeic keratosis

## Histopathology shows expansion of the squamous epithelium

of the epidermis by proliferating basal cells, sometimes with

keratin-¯ÇØlled horns or cystic inclusions (Fig. 2.6A). Seborrhoeic
keratosis (basal cell papilloma) is an extremely common slowly
growing lesion found on the face, trunk and extremities of elderly
individuals as a discrete light- to dark-brown plaque with a friable,
greasy, verrucous surface and a ÔÇÿstuck-onÔÇÖ appearance (Fig. 2.6B).
¯Ç×ey are frequently numerous. ¯Ç×e di¯Ç£erential diagnosis includes
pigmented basal cell carcinoma, naevus and melanoma. Treatment
involves shave biopsy (occasionally simple excision), electrodesiccation with curettage, laser ablation, cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen and chemical peeling.

## Actinic keratosis

Histopathology (Fig. 2.7A) shows irregular dysplastic epidermis
with hyperkeratosis, parakeratosis and occasionally cutaneous
horn formation. Actinic (solar, senile) keratosis is a common
slowly growing lesion that rarely develops on the eyelids. It typically a¯Ç£ects elderly, fair-skinned individuals on areas of sundamaged skin, such as the forehead and backs of the hands, and
appears as a hyperkeratotic plaque with distinct borders and a
scaly surface that may become ¯ÇØssured (Fig. 2.7B). Occasionally
the lesion is nodular or wart-like and may give rise to a cutaneous
horn. It has potential, though low, for transformation into squamous cell carcinoma. Treatment involves biopsy followed by excision or cryotherapy.

BENIGN PIGMENTED LESIONS

## Freckle

A freckle (ephelis, plural ephelides) is a small (generally 1ÔÇô5 mm)
brown macule due to increased melanin in keratinocytes, typically
in sun-exposed skin. ¯Ç×is is particularly common in fair-skinned
individuals and children with red hair, where the MC1R gene is
involved (Fig. 2.8A). ¯Ç×e number of freckles varies with the level of
sun exposure and can sometimes regress completely. Histopathology shows hyperpigmentation of the basal layer of the epidermis,
with a normal melanocyte population.

## Lentigo

Lentigo (plural: lentigines) are harmless ¯Çøat, brownish lesions with
a clearly de¯ÇØned scalloped edge that are larger than freckles. ¯Ç×ey
may have a dry or slightly scaly surface. Solar lentigines occur in
middle-aged individuals and result from sun damage. ¯Ç×ey are
persistent and do not disappear in the winter (Fig. 2.8B). Histopathology shows a proliferation of keratinocytes and an increase in
the number of melanocytes.

## Congenital melanocytic naevus

## Congenital naevi are uncommon and histologically resemble

their acquired counterparts (see below). ¯Ç×ey are usually small
and of uniform colour. Rare variants include a ÔÇÿkissingÔÇÖ or split
naevus that involves the upper and lower eyelid (Fig. 2.9A) and
may occasionally contain numerous hairs (Fig. 2.9B), and a large

lesion covering the face (Fig. 2.9C) or an extensive area of the body
(ÔÇÿgiant hairy naevusÔÇÖ). Large lesions have the potential for malignant transformation (up to 15%). Treatment, if necessary, involves
complete surgical excision.

## Acquired melanocytic naevus

## Diagnosis

¯Ç×e clinical appearance and potential for malignant transformation
of a naevus is determined by the histological location within the skin.
¯Çü Junctional naevus occurs in young individuals as a uniformly
brown macule or plaque (Fig. 2.10A). ¯Ç×e naevus cells are
located at the junction of the epidermis and dermis and have a
low potential for malignant transformation (Fig. 2.10B).
¯Çü Compound naevus occurs in middle age as a raised papular
lesion. ¯Ç×e shade of pigment varies from light tan to dark

46 Benign Adnexal Tumours

A

B C

large facial naevus. (Courtesy of A Pearson ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B; RSR Naik ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

brown but tends to be relatively uniform throughout (Fig.
2.10C). ¯Ç×e naevus cells extend from the epidermis into the
dermis (Fig. 2.10D). It has a low malignant potential related to
the junctional component.
¯Çü Intradermal naevus, the most common, typically occurs in
older patients. It is a papillomatous lesion, with little or no
pigmentation (Fig. 2.10E). Histologically, naevus cells are
con¯ÇØned to the dermis and have no malignant potential (Fig.
2.10F).
¯Çü Variants of naevus include balloon cell naevus, halo naevus,
Spitz naevus (juvenile melanomas) and dysplastic naevus
(atypical moles). Multiple dysplastic naevi constitute the dysplastic naevus syndrome (atypical mole syndrome ÔÇô AMS).
Individuals with AMS are at increased risk of developing
conjunctival and uveal naevi and cutaneous, conjunctival and
uveal melanomas.

## Treatment

## Treatment is indicated for cosmesis or if the naevus has unusual

features that raise concern about malignancy. Excision should be

complete in most cases, with at least a 3 mm margin if melanoma
is suspected.

BENIGN ADNEXAL TUMOURS

## Syringoma

## Syringomas are benign proliferations arising from eccrine sweat

glands. ¯Ç×ey are characterized by small papules that are o¯ÇÜen multiple and bilateral (Fig. 2.11A).

## Pilomatricoma

Pilomatricoma (pilomatrixoma, calcifying epithelioma of Malherbe) is derived from the germinal matrix cells of the hair bulb
and is the commonest hair follicle proliferation seen by ophthalmologists. It a¯Ç£ects children and young adults and is more common in females. Histopathology shows irregular epithelial islands
exhibiting viable basophilic cells at the periphery and degenerate
ÔÇÿshadowÔÇÖ cells centrally. Clinically it appears as a mobile purplish

ptosis.

dermal nodule that may have a hard consistency due to calci¯ÇØcation (Fig. 2.11B). Calci¯ÇØcation is frequently present and there is

¯Ç×e lesion is usually removed surgically. Other less common disorders of hair follicle proliferation include trichofolliculoma, trichoepithelioma and trichilemmoma.

MISCELLANEOUS BENIGN TUMOURS

## Capillary haemangioma

Capillary haemangioma (strawberry naevus) is one of the most
common tumours of infancy. It is three times as common in girls as
boys. Histopathology shows proliferation of varying-sized vascular
channels in the dermis and subcutaneous tissue. It presents shortly
a¯ÇÜer birth as a unilateral, raised bright red lesion (Fig. 2.12A), usually in the upper lid. A deeper lesion appears purplish (Fig. 2.12B).
¯Ç×e lesion blanches on pressure and may swell on crying. Ptosis is

frequent and there may be orbital extension (see Ch. 4). Occasionally the lesion may involve the skin of the face and some patients
have strawberry naevi on other parts of the body. It is important
to be aware of an association between multiple cutaneous lesions
and visceral haemangiomas and to consider systemic assessment
in appropriate cases. Treatment is described in Chapter 4

TIP A capillary haemangioma can be easily and successfully
treated by regular application of a topical beta-blocker to the
a¯ÇØected lesion.

Port¯Çƒwine stain

## Introduction

Port-wine stain (naevus ¯Çøammeus) is a congenital malformation of
vessels within the super¯ÇØcial dermis, consisting histopathologically

A B

D E

C

F

separated by ¯Çƒbrous septa; (B) and (C) clinical appearance; (DÔÇôF) progression of port-wine
stain over time, with associated underlying soft tissue hypertrophy.

of vascular spaces of varying calibre separated by thin ¯ÇØbrous septa
(Fig. 2.13A). About 10% have associated ocular or CNS involvement, including SturgeÔÇôWeber (see below) and other de¯ÇØned
syndromes.

## Diagnosis

Port-wine stain manifests clinically as a sharply demarcated so¯ÇÜ
pink patch that does not blanch with pressure, most frequently
located on the face. It is usually unilateral and tends to be aligned
with the skin area supplied by one or more divisions of the trigeminal nerve (Fig. 2.13B and C). Darkening to red or purple
takes place with age and there is commonly associated so¯ÇÜ tissue hypertrophy (Fig. 2.13DÔÇôF). Bleeding may occur from focal
overlying lobulations (pyogenic granulomas ÔÇô see below).

## Treatment

Treatment with laser (e.g. pulsed-dye) is e¯Ç£ective in decreasing skin discoloration, particularly if undertaken early. Topical

preparations such as imiquimod and rapamycin, alone or with
adjuvant laser, show promise. So¯ÇÜ tissue debulking is used in a
small number of cases. Screening for glaucoma should begin in
infancy. Systemic investigation is considered in some patients, particularly those with a lesion of the lumbar area.

SturgeÔÇôWeber syndrome

SturgeÔÇôWeber syndrome (encephalotrigeminal angiomatosis) is a
congenital, sporadic phacomatosis.
¯Çü Port-wine stain, extending over the area corresponding to
the distribution of one or more branches of the trigeminal
nerve.
¯Çü Leptomeningeal haemangioma involving the ipsilateral parietal or occipital region may cause contralateral focal or generalized seizures, hemiparesis or hemianopia.
¯Çü Ocular features may include ipsilateral glaucoma, episcleral
haemangioma, iris heterochromia and di¯Ç£use choroidal haemangioma (see Ch. 20).

50 Malignant Tumours

A B

(B) plexiform neuro¯Çƒbroma ÔÇô characteristic S-shaped upper lid.

## Pyogenic granuloma

Pyogenic granuloma is a rapidly growing vascularized proliferation of granulation tissue that is usually antedated by surgery,
trauma or infection, although some cases are idiopathic. Clinically
there is a painful, rapidly growing, vascular granulating polypoidal lesion (Fig. 2.14A) that may bleed following relatively trivial
trauma. Cutaneous lesions should be excised. Conjunctival pyogenic granuloma is discussed in Chapter 6

Neuro¯Ç×broma

Cutaneous neuro¯ÇØbromas are benign nerve tumours, usually
nodular or pedunculated, that can be found anywhere on the skin.
An isolated neuro¯ÇØbroma is common in normal individuals, but
if multiple lesions are present, neuro¯ÇØbromatosis (see Ch. 19)
should be excluded. Plexiform neuro¯ÇØbromas typically present in
childhood as a manifestation of neuro¯ÇØbromatosis type 1 with a
characteristic S-shaped deformity of the upper eyelid (Fig. 2.14B).
Treatment of solitary lesions involves simple excision, but removal
of the more di¯Ç£use plexiform lesions may be di¯ÇÖcult.

MALIGNANT TUMOURS

¯Ç×e treatment of malignant eyelid tumours in general is discussed
at the end of this section.

## Rare predisposing conditions

Young patients who su¯Ç£er from one of the following conditions
may develop eyelid malignancies.
¯Çü Xeroderma pigmentosum is characterized by skin damage
on exposure to sunlight, leading to progressive cutaneous
abnormalities (Fig. 2.15A). More than 90% have ocular or
periocular involvement and 65% experience photophobia. It
is inherited in an autosomal recessive (AR) fashion. A¯Ç£ected

patients have a bird-like facial appearance and a signi¯ÇØcant
propensity to the development of basal cell carcinoma (BCC),
squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and melanoma, which are
commonly multiple. Conjunctival malignancies have also
been reported.
¯Çü GorlinÔÇôGoltz syndrome (naevoid BCC syndrome) is a rare
autosomal dominant (AD) disorder characterized by extensive congenital deformities of the eye, face, bone and central
nervous system. Many patients develop multiple small BCC
during the second decade of life (Fig. 2.15B) and are also predisposed to medulloblastoma, breast carcinoma and Hodgkin
lymphoma.
¯Çü MuirÔÇôTorre syndrome is a rare AD condition that predisposes
to cutaneous and internal malignancies. Cutaneous tumours
include BCC, sebaceous gland carcinoma and keratoacanthoma. Colorectal and genitourinary carcinomas are the most
common systemic tumours.
¯Çü Bazex syndrome can be used to describe two distinct conditions: BazexÔÇôDupr├®ÔÇôChristol syndrome, an X-linked dominant condition characterized by multiple BCCs, commonly
facial including the eyelids, associated with skin changes
including follicular indentations without hairs on extensor
surfaces (follicular atrophoderma), hypohidrosis and hypotrichosis and acrokeratosis paraneoplastica of Bazex, in which
eczema-like and psoriasiform lesions are associated with an
underlying malignancy of the upper respiratory or digestive
tract.
¯Çü Other predispositions include immunosuppression, prior
retinoblastoma and albinism.

## Basal cell carcinoma

## Introduction

BCC is the most common human malignancy and typically
affects older people. The most important risk factors are fair

A B

Goltz syndrome. (Courtesy of J Krachmer, M Mannis and E Holland, from Cornea, Mosby
2005 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

skin, inability to tan and chronic exposure to sunlight. Ninety
per cent of cases occur in the head and neck and about 10%
of these involve the eyelid. BCC is by far the most common
malignant eyelid tumour, accounting for 90% of all cases. It
most frequently arises from the lower eyelid, followed in relative frequency by the medial canthus, upper eyelid and lateral
canthus. Madarosis is commonly seen in association with the
tumour, which is slow-growing and locally invasive, but nonmetastasizing. Tumours located near the medial canthus are
more prone to invade the orbit and sinuses, are more difficult to
manage than those arising elsewhere and carry the greatest risk
of recurrence. Tumours that recur following incomplete treatment tend to be more aggressive.

## Histopathology

¯Ç×e tumour arises from the cells that form the basal layer of
the epidermis. ¯Ç×e cells proliferate downwards (Fig. 2.16A) and
characteristically exhibit palisading at the periphery of a tumour
lobule of cells (Fig. 2.16B). Squamous di¯Ç£erentiation with the
production of keratin results in a hyperkeratotic type of BCC.
¯Ç×ere can also be sebaceous and adenoid di¯Ç£erentiation while
the growth of elongated strands and islands of cells embedded
in a dense ¯ÇØbrous stroma result in a sclerosing (morphoeic) type
of tumour.

## Clinical features

Eyelid BCC conforms to one of the morphological patterns below.
¯Çü Nodular BCC is a shiny, ¯ÇØrm, pearly nodule with small overlying dilated blood vessels. Initially, growth is slow and it may
take the tumour 1ÔÇô2 years to reach a diameter of 0.5 cm (Fig.
2.16C and D).
¯Çü Nodulo-ulcerative BCC (rodent ulcer) is centrally ulcerated with pearly raised rolled edges and dilated and irregular blood vessels (telangiectasis) over its lateral margins (Fig.
2.16E). With time it may erode a large portion of the eyelid
(Fig. 2.16F).

¯Çü Sclerosing (morphoeic) BCC is less common and may be
difficult to diagnose because it infiltrates laterally beneath
the epidermis as an indurated plaque (Fig. 2.16G and H).

## The margins of the tumour may be impossible to delineate
clinically and the lesion tends to be much more extensive

on palpation than inspection. On cursory examination a
sclerosing BCC may simulate a localized area of chronic
blepharitis.
¯Çü Other types not usually found on the lid are cystic, adenoid,
pigmented and multiple super¯ÇØcial.

TIP Sclerosing BCC can mimic a localized area of unilateral
chronic blepharitis.

## Squamous cell carcinoma

## Introduction

SCC is a much less common, but typically more aggressive tumour
than BCC, with metastasis to regional lymph nodes in about 20%
of cases. Histopathology shows dysplastic changes throughout
the thickness of the epidermis (Fig. 2.17A). Careful surveillance
of regional lymph nodes is therefore an important aspect of initial management. ¯Ç×e tumour may also exhibit perineural spread
to the intracranial cavity via the orbit. SCC accounts for 5ÔÇô10% of
eyelid malignancies and may arise de novo or from pre-existing
actinic keratosis or carcinoma in situ (Bowen disease, intraepidermal carcinoma ÔÇô Fig. 2.17B). Immunocompromised individuals, such as those with acquired immunode¯ÇØciency syndrome
(AIDS) or following renal transplantation are at increased risk,
as are those with a predisposing syndrome such as xeroderma
pigmentosum. ¯Ç×e tumour has a predilection for the lower eyelid
and the lid margin. It occurs most commonly in older individuals with a fair complexion and a history of chronic sun exposure. ¯Ç×e diagnosis of SCC may sometimes be di¯ÇÖcult because

(pink) islands of dysplastic squamous epithelium within the dermis. Note the large nucleus

54 Malignant Tumours

certain ostensibly benign lesions such as keratoacanthoma and
cutaneous horn may reveal histological evidence of invasive SCC
at deeper levels of sectioning.

## Histopathology

¯Ç×e tumour arises from the squamous cell layer of the epidermis.

## It is composed of variably sized groups of atypical epithelial cells
with prominent nuclei and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm

within the dermis (Fig. 2.17C). Well-di¯Ç£erentiated tumours
may show characteristic keratin ÔÇÿpearlsÔÇÖ and intercellular bridges
(desmosomes).

## Clinical features

¯Ç×e clinical types are variable and there are no pathognomonic
characteristics. ¯Ç×e tumour may be indistinguishable clinically
from a BCC but surface vascularization is usually absent, growth is
more rapid and hyperkeratosis is more common.
¯Çü Nodular SCC is characterized by a hyperkeratotic nodule that
may develop crusting, erosions and ¯ÇØssures (Fig. 2.17D).
¯Çü Ulcerating SCC has a red base and sharply de¯ÇØned, indurated
and everted borders, but pearly margins and telangiectasia are
not usually present (Fig. 2.17E).
¯Çü Cutaneous horn with underlying invasive SCC (Fig. 2.17F).

TIP Ostensibly benign lesions such as keratoacanthoma and
cutaneous horn may reveal histological evidence of SCC in
deeper levels of sectioning.

## Keratoacanthoma

## Introduction

Keratoacanthoma is a rare, rapidly growing but subsequently
regressing tumour that usually occurs in fair-skinned individuals
with a history of chronic sun exposure. Immunosuppressive therapy
is also a predisposing factor. Invasion and metastasis are rare. Histopathological examination reveals irregular thickened epidermis
surrounded by acanthotic squamous epithelium. A sharp transition
from the thickened involved area to normal adjacent epidermis is
referred to as shoulder formation (Fig. 2.18A) and a keratin-¯ÇØlled
crater may be seen.

## Diagnosis

A pink dome-shaped hyperkeratotic lesion develops, o¯ÇÜen on the
lower lid (Fig. 2.18B) and may double or treble in size within weeks
(Fig. 2.18C). Growth then ceases for 2ÔÇô3 months, a¯ÇÜer which
spontaneous involution occurs, when a keratin-¯ÇØlled crater may
develop (Fig. 2.18D). Complete involution may take up to a year
and usually leaves an unsightly scar.

## Treatment

## Treatment involves complete surgical excision with a margin of at

least 3 mm or utilizing Mohs surgery. Radiotherapy, cryotherapy
or local chemotherapy are sometimes used.

## Sebaceous gland carcinoma

## Introduction

Sebaceous gland carcinoma (SGC) is a very rare, slowly growing
tumour that most frequently a¯Ç£ects the elderly, with a predisposition for females. It usually arises from the meibomian glands,
although on occasion it may arise from the glands of Zeis or elsewhere. Histopathology demonstrates lobules of cells with pale
foamy vacuolated lipid-containing cytoplasm and large hyperchromatic nuclei. Pagetoid spread refers to extension of a tumour
within the epithelium and is not uncommon. Overall mortality
is 5ÔÇô10%. Adverse prognostic features include upper lid involvement, tumour size of 10 mm or more and duration of symptoms of
more than 6 months.

## Clinical features

In contrast to BCC and SCC, SGC occurs more commonly on the
upper eyelid where meibomian glands are more numerous. ¯Ç×ere
may be simultaneous involvement of the lower and upper lid on
one side (5%).
¯Çü Yellowish material within the tumour is highly suggestive of
SGC.
¯Çü Nodular SGC presents as a discrete, hard nodule, most commonly within the upper tarsal plate (Fig. 2.19A) and may
exhibit yellow discoloration due to the presence of lipid; it can
be mistaken for a chalazion.
¯Çü Spreading SGC in¯ÇØltrates into the dermis and causes a di¯Ç£use
thickening of the lid margin (Fig. 2.19B) o¯ÇÜen with eyelash
distortion and loss and can be mistaken for blepharitis.

TIP SGC can be confused with chronic or recurrent localized
meibomian gland in¯Ç×ammation.

## Lentigo maligna and melanoma

## Introduction

Melanoma rarely develops on the eyelids but is potentially lethal.
Although pigmentation is a hallmark of skin melanomas, half of
lid melanomas are non-pigmented and this may give rise to diagnostic di¯ÇÖculty. Features suggestive of melanoma include recent
onset of a pigmented lesion, change in an existing pigmented
lesion, irregular margins, asymmetrical shape, colour change or
presence of multiple colours and diameter greater than 6 mm.

## Lentigo maligna

Lentigo maligna (melanoma in situ, intraepidermal melanoma,
Hutchinson freckle) is an uncommon condition that develops
in sun-damaged skin in elderly individuals (Fig. 2.20A). Malignant melanoma arises within lentigo maligna in 3ÔÇô10% of cases.
Histopathology shows intraepidermal proliferation of spindleshaped atypical melanocytes replacing the basal layer of the epidermis. Clinically lentigo maligna presents as a slowly expanding

A B

C D

epidermis with a keratin-containing cup; (B) small nodule on lid margin; (C) hyperkeratotic
nodule; (D) keratin-¯Çƒlled crater during involution.

A B

56 Malignant Tumours

A B

C D

arising from lentigo maligna; (C) spreading melanoma onto tarsal conjunctiva; (D) histopathology showing melanoma cells (arrows) within the dermis. Note the large nucleus with irregular
nuclear border and large, purple nucleoli. (Courtesy of ADN Murray ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

pigmented macule with an irregular border and variable pigmentation. Nodular thickening and areas of irregular pigmentation are
highly suggestive of malignant transformation (Fig. 2.20B).Treatment is usually by complete excision with large margins. Imiquimod 5% topical cream is a reasonable alternative in those with
extensive involvement or in those who decline surgery.

## Melanoma

Super¯ÇØcial spreading melanoma is characterized by a plaque
with an irregular outline and variable pigmentation (Fig. 2.20C).
Nodular melanoma is typically a blueÔÇôblack nodule surrounded
by normal skin. Histopathology shows large atypical epithelioid
melanocytes scattered throughout the epidermis and invading the
dermis (Fig. 2.20D). Treatment is usually by wide excision and
may include local lymph node removal. Early detection carries a
good prognosis and surgical excision is o¯ÇÜen curative, but longterm survival for patients with metastatic disease is poor. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy are of limited e¯ÇÖcacy. However, new

approaches using immunotherapy (ipilimumab, nivolumab, pembrolizumab, talimogene laherparepvec) are showing considerable
promise. Targeted treatment for advanced melanoma exhibiting
the BRAF gene is also available (verumurafenib, dadrafenib, trametinib). Research is under way to produce a vaccine for cutaneous melanoma.

## Merkel cell carcinoma

## Merkel cells are a form of sensory receptor concerned with light

touch. Merkel cell carcinoma is a rapidly growing, highly malignant tumour that typically a¯Ç£ects older adults. Its rarity may lead
to di¯ÇÖculty in diagnosis and delay in treatment and 50% of patients
have metastatic spread by presentation. Factors involved in the
development include exposure to the Merkel cell polyomavirus,
a weakened immune system and sun exposure. Histopathology
shows a sheet of Merkel cells. A violaceous, well-demarcated nodule with intact overlying skin is seen, most frequently involving

A B

the upper eyelid (Fig. 2.21A). Treatment is by excision, o¯ÇÜen with
adjuvant therapy.

## Kaposi sarcoma

Kaposi sarcoma is a vascular tumour that typically a¯Ç£ects individuals with AIDS. Many patients have advanced systemic disease
although in some instances the tumour may be the only clinical
manifestation of human immunode¯ÇØciency virus (HIV) infection.
Histopathology shows proliferating spindle cells, vascular channels and in¯Çøammatory cells within the dermis. Clinically a pink,
red-violet to brown lesion (Fig. 2.21B) develops, which may be
mistaken for a haematoma or naevus. Treatment is by radiotherapy
or excision and by optimal control of AIDS.

## Treatment of malignant tumours

## Biopsy

Biopsy can be either incisional, using a blade or a biopsy punch, in
which only part of the lesion is removed for histological diagnosis,
or excisional, in which the entire lesion is removed. ¯Ç×e latter may
consist of shave excision using a blade to remove shallow epithelial
tumours, such as a papilloma and seborrhoeic keratosis, or fullthickness skin excision for tumours that are not con¯ÇØned to the
epidermis.

## Surgical excision

Surgical excision aims to remove the entire tumour with preservation of as much normal tissue as possible. Smaller tumours can
be removed via an excision biopsy and the defect closed directly,
whilst awaiting histological con¯ÇØrmation of complete clearance. Most small BCCs can be cured by excision of the tumour
together with a 2ÔÇô4 mm margin of clinically normal tissue. More
radical surgical excision is required for a large BCC and aggressive tumours such as SCC, SGC and melanoma. It may not be
possible to close all defects at the time of initial removal, but
it is necessary to ensure complete clearance of tumour prior to

undertaking any reconstruction. ¯Ç×ere are several options for the
coordination of histopathological diagnosis and tumour clearance with excision.
¯Çü Conventional para¯Ç×n-embedded specimen. Rapid processing can reduce the interval to con¯ÇØrmation of histological
clearance but still requires that reconstruction be performed
as a separate procedure. Faster con¯ÇØrmation can be achieved
using either frozen-section control or micrographic surgery
(see next) and reconstruction can then take place on the same
day.
¯Çü Standard frozen section involves histological examination
of the margins of the excised specimen at the time of surgery
to ensure that they are tumour-free. If no tumour cells are
detected, the eyelid is reconstructed on the same day. However,
if residual tumour is present, further excision is performed at
the appropriate edge of the surgical site until no tumour is
detected.
¯Çü Mohs micrographic surgery involves layered excision of the
tumour; specimens are usually examined frozen. Processing
of each layer enables a map of the edges of the tumour to be
developed. Further tissue is taken in any area where tumour
is still present until clearance is achieved. Although timeconsuming, this technique maximizes the chances of total
tumour excision whilst minimizing sacri¯ÇØce of normal tissue.
¯Ç×is is a particularly useful technique for tumours that grow
di¯Ç£usely and have inde¯ÇØnite margins with ¯ÇØnger-like extensions, such as sclerosing BCC, SCC, recurrent tumours and
those involving the medial or lateral canthi. ¯Ç×e irregular contours around the eyelids and extension of tumours into orbital
fat can make interpretation di¯ÇÖcult.

## Reconstruction

¯Ç×e technique of reconstruction depends on the extent of tissue
removed. It is important to reconstruct both anterior and posterior
lamellae, each of which must be reconstructed with similar tissue.
Anterior lamellar defects may be closed directly or with a local ¯Çøap
or skin gra¯ÇÜ. Options for the repair of full-thickness defects are set
out below.

58 Malignant Tumours

¯Çü Small defects involving less than one-third of the eyelid can
usually be closed directly, provided the surrounding tissue is
su¯ÇÖciently elastic to allow approximation of the cut edges (Fig.
2.22A D). If necessary, a lateral cantholysis can be performed
for increased mobilization.
¯Çü Moderate size defects involving up to half of the eyelid may
require a ¯Çøap (e.g. Tenzel semi-circular) for closure (Fig.
2.23A D).
¯Çü Large defects involving over half of the eyelid may be closed
by one of the following techniques:

{ Posterior lamellar reconstruction may involve an upper lid
free tarsal gra¯ÇÜ, buccal mucous membrane or hard palate
gra¯ÇÜ, or a Hughes tarsoconjunctival ¯Çøap from the upper
lid, which is le¯ÇÜ attached for 4ÔÇô6 weeks before transection
(Fig. 2.24A F).

{ Anterior lamellar reconstruction may involve skin
advancement, a local skin ¯Çøap or a free skin gra¯ÇÜ (Fig.
2.24D). ¯Ç×e patient must be made aware that gra¯ÇÜed skin
is unlikely to be a perfect match. At least one reconstructed

lamella requires its own blood supply to maximize the viability of a free gra¯ÇÜ component.

Laissez-faire

## Full reconstruction of the defect created by tumour removal may

not always be required. In the laissez-faire approach the wound
edges are approximated as far as possible and the defect is allowed
to granulate and heal by secondary intention. Even large defects
can o¯ÇÜen achieve a satisfactory outcome with time.

## Radiotherapy

¯Ç×e recurrence rate following irradiation alone is higher than a¯ÇÜer
surgery, and radiotherapy does not allow histological con¯ÇØrmation
of tumour eradication. Recurrences following radiotherapy are dif¯ÇØcult to treat surgically because of the poor healing properties of
irradiated tissue.
¯Çü Indications

{ Patients who are either unsuitable for, or refuse, surgery.

{ Highly radiosensitive tumours, such as Kaposi sarcoma.

## Cryotherapy

Cryotherapy may be considered for a small super¯ÇØcial BCC and can
be a useful adjunct to surgery in some patients. Complications include
skin depigmentation, madarosis and conjunctival overgrowth.

## Immunotherapy

Vismodegib (administered orally) has been approved for use in
patients with extensively advanced BCC. Overall response rates
in up to two-thirds of patients have been reported. ¯Ç×e initial
response to treatment can predict a complete response outcome.
However, systemic side e¯Ç£ects are common and are the main reason for treatment failure.

Oral nicotinamide for non-melanoma skin
cancers

Nicotinamide in a dose of 500mg once daily appears to reduce the
incidence of skin malignancies. Because of its favourable safety

DISORDERS OF THE EYELASHES

## Misdirected lashes

## Introduction

¯Ç×e roots of the eyelashes (cilia) lie against the anterior surface
of the tarsal plate. ¯Ç×e cilia pass between the main part of the
orbicularis oculi and its more super¯ÇØcial part (Riolan muscle),
exiting the skin at the anterior lid margin and curving away from
the globe. It is particularly important to be familiar with the normal anatomical appearance of the lid margin in order to be able
to identify the cause of eyelash misdirection. From anterior to
posterior:
¯Çü Eyelashes (cilia).
¯Çü ¯ÇØe grey line, by de¯ÇØnition the border between the anterior
(lashes, skin and orbicularis) and posterior (tarsal plate and
conjunctiva) lamellae.

¯Çü ¯ÇØe meibomian gland ori¯Çƒces are located just anterior to the
mucocutaneous junction. ¯Ç×e edge of the tarsal plate is deep to
the gland ori¯ÇØces; the glands themselves run vertically within
the plate.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe mucocutaneous junction is where keratinized epithelium
of the skin merges with conjunctival mucous membrane.
¯Çü Conjunctiva lines the posterior margin of the lid.

## Clinical features

## Trauma to the corneal epithelium may cause punctate epithelial

erosions, with ocular irritation o¯ÇÜen worsened by blinking. Corneal ulceration and pannus formation may occur in severe cases.
¯Ç×e clinical appearance varies with the cause.
¯Çü Trichiasis refers to misdirection of growth from individual
follicles (Fig. 2.25A and B), rather than a more extensive
inversion of the lid or lid margin. ¯Ç×e follicles are at anatomically normal sites. It is commonly due to in¯Çøammation such
as chronic blepharitis or herpes zoster ophthalmicus, but can
also be caused by an injury or by surgery, such as incision and
curettage of a chalazion (Fig. 2.25C).

A B

C D

of R Bates ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. D.)

62 Disorders of the Eyelashes

¯Çü Marginal entropion has increasingly been recognized as
a very common cause of eyelash misdirection, secondary to subtle cicatricial posterior lamellar shortening
that rotates a segment of the lid margin towards the eye.

## The mucocutaneous junction migrates anteriorly and the

posterior lid margin becomes rounded rather than physiologically square. Typically, numerous aligned lashes are
involved.
¯Çü Congenital distichiasis is a rare condition that occurs
when a primary epithelial germ cell destined to differentiate into a meibomian gland develops instead into a
complete pilosebaceous unit. The condition is frequently
inherited in an AD manner with high penetrance but variable expressivity. The majority of patients also manifest
primary lymphoedema of the legs (lymphoedemaÔÇôdistichiasis syndrome). A partial or complete second row of
lashes is seen to emerge at or slightly behind the meibomian gland orifices. The aberrant lashes tend to be thinner and shorter than normal cilia and are often directed
posteriorly. They are usually well tolerated during infancy
and may not become symptomatic until the age of about
5 years.
¯Çü Acquired distichiasis is caused by metaplasia of the meibomian glands into hair follicles such that a variable number
of lashes grow from meibomian gland openings. ¯Ç×e most
important cause is intense conjunctival in¯Çøammation (e.g.
chemical injury, StevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome, ocular cicatricial
pemphigoid). In contrast to congenital distichiasis, the cilia
tend to be non-pigmented and stunted (Fig. 2.25D) and are
usually symptomatic.
¯Çü Epiblepharon ÔÇô see later.
¯Çü Entropion. In contrast to marginal entropion, profound inversion of a substantial width of the lid is readily identi¯ÇØed ÔÇô see
later.

## Treatment

¯Çü Epilation with forceps is simple and e¯Ç£ective but recurrence
within a few weeks is essentially invariable. It can be used as a
temporizing measure or in the occasional patient who refuses
or cannot tolerate surgery.
¯Çü Electrolysis or electrocautery (hyfrecation) are broadly similar
electrosurgical techniques in which, under local anaesthesia,
a ¯ÇØne wire is passed down the hair follicle to ablate the lash.
It is generally useful for a limited number of lashes, but scarring can follow. Frequently multiple treatments are required to
obtain a satisfactory result.
¯Çü Laser ablation is also useful for the treatment of a limited
number of aberrant eyelashes and is performed using a spot
size of 50 ╬╝m, duration of 0.1ÔÇô0.2 s and power of 800ÔÇô1000
mW. ¯Ç×e base of the lash is targeted and shots are applied
to create a crater that follows the axis of the follicle (Fig.
2.26). Success is broadly comparable to that achieved with
electrosurgery.

¯Çü Surgery

{ Tarsal fracture (transverse tarsotomy) is performed for
marginal entropion. A¯ÇÜer placing a 4-0 traction suture,
a horizontal incision is made through the tarsal plate via
the conjunctiva, at least halfway down the plate, along the
a¯Ç£ected length of the lid and extended to 2ÔÇô3 mm either
side of the involved region. Depending on the extent of lid
involvement, either two or three double-armed absorbable
sutures are passed through the upper edge of the lower section of the tarsal plate to emerge just anterior to the lashes,
leaving the lid margin very slightly everted (Fig. 2.27A
D). ¯Ç×e sutures are le¯ÇÜ in place following the surgery.
Occasionally short-term use of a bandage contact lens is
required to prevent corneal abrading.

{ A full-thickness eyelid pentagon resection can be used for
a focal group of aberrant lashes, typically a¯ÇÜer trauma, or
for localized marginal entropion.

{ Other options include lid splitting (see next) with follicle
excision and anterior lamellar rotation surgery.
¯Çü Cryotherapy applied externally to the skin just inferior to the
base of the abnormal lashes, or ÔÇô especially in distichiasis ÔÇô to
the internal aspect of the anterior lamella of the lid following splitting of the margin at the grey line (Fig. 2.28A), can
be used for numerous lashes. A¯ÇÜer separation of the anterior
and posterior lamellae under local anaesthesia (Fig. 2.28B),
the cryoprobe is applied to the posterior lamella (Fig. 2.28C),
using a double freezeÔÇôthaw cycle at ÔêÆ20 ┬░C with a plastic eye
protector in place. Full-thickness sutures hold the anterior
lamella recessed on the posterior lamella (Fig. 2.28D). Suturing of the lid margin is not usually necessary following limited splitting. ¯Ç×e method is e¯Ç£ective, but carries a high rate
of local adverse e¯Ç£ects, especially loss of lashes and entropion of the upper lid if there is inadequate recession of the
anterior lamella. It is less commonly performed now than in
previous years.

## Eyelash ptosis

## Eyelash ptosis refers to a downward sagging of the upper lid lashes

(Fig. 2.29A). ¯Ç×e condition may be idiopathic or associated with
¯Çøoppy eyelid syndrome, dermatochalasis with anterior lamellar
slip or long-standing facial palsy.

## Trichomegaly

Trichomegaly is excessive eyelash growth (Fig. 2.29B). ¯Ç×e main
causes are listed in Table 2.1

## Madarosis

ALLERGIC DISORDERS

## Acute allergic oedema

## Acute allergic oedema may occur rapidly in a child exposed to cat

or dog hairs (Fig. 2.30A). When it is caused by exposure to pollen or by insect bites it manifests with the sudden onset of bilateral boggy periocular oedema (Fig. 2.30B), o¯ÇÜen accompanied by
conjunctival swelling (chemosis ÔÇô see Ch. 6). Treatment is o¯ÇÜen
unnecessary, but systemic antihistamines are sometimes given.

## Contact dermatitis

Contact dermatitis is an in¯Çøammatory response that usually follows exposure to a medication such as eye drops, cosmetics or
metals. An irritant can also cause a non-allergic toxic dermatitis. ¯Ç×e individual is sensitized on ¯ÇØrst exposure and develops an
immune reaction on further exposure; the mediating reaction is
type IV (delayed type) hypersensitivity. Signs consist of lid skin

scaling, angular ¯ÇØssuring, oedema and tightness (Fig. 2.30C).
¯Ç×ere may be chemosis, redness and papillary conjunctivitis. Corneal involvement is usually limited to punctate epithelial erosions.
Treatment consists primarily of avoidance of allergen exposure,
provided it can be identi¯ÇØed. Cold compresses provide symptomatic relief. Topical steroids and oral antihistamines can be used but
are rarely required.

IDIOPATHIC DERMATITIS

## Atopic dermatitis

Atopic dermatitis (eczema) is a very common idiopathic condition,
typically occurring in patients who also su¯Ç£er from asthma and hay
fever. Eyelid involvement is relatively infrequent but when present
is invariably associated with generalized dermatitis. ¯Ç×ickening,
crusting and ¯ÇØssuring of the lids (Fig. 2.30D) is typical and staphylococcal blepharitis, vernal or atopic keratoconjunctivitis are also
commonly present. Herpetic blepharitis and keratoconjunctivitis

hydrate the skin and the judicious use of mild topical steroid such
as hydrocortisone 1%. Uncommon ocular associations include
keratoconus, cataract and retinal detachment (see also Ch. 6).

Periori¯Ç×cial dermatitis

## This is a common chronic inflammatory skin eruption of

unknown cause that can affect the periocular region. It
resembles rosacea and allergic dermatitis, but is considered
a disease of the hair follicle, with biopsy samples showing a
lymphohistiocytic infiltrate. Clusters of red papules, vesicles
and pustules with an erythematous base and a sharp border
are seen, with dry surrounding skin. It usually affects young

to middle-aged women. Exposure to sunlight, heat and wind
can exacerbate the inflammation. The condition is benign and
self-limiting. If topical steroids have been used, they should be
slowly discontinued and replaced with a bland emollient without preservatives. Specific treatment includes topical metronidazole 0.75%, topical erythromycin 1% and oral tetracycline
for 4ÔÇô8 weeks.

IMMUNE-RELATED INFLAMMATION

## Dermatomyositis

## Dermatomyositis is a chronic inflammatory disorder of muscles
which can present in an acute or chronic fashion with muscle

weakness and a skin rash. In children, about half develop eyelid swelling and a heliotrope rash (Fig. 2.31). In adults it may
be associated with ovarian, breast or lung cancer, as an autoimmune response. The condition is treated with systemic
steroids or immunosuppressive medication (methotrexate,
azathioprine).

a child.

## Systemic monoclonal antibody therapy

BACTERIAL INFECTIONS

## External hordeolum

An external hordeolum (stye) is an acute staphylococcal abscess
of a lash follicle and its associated gland of Zeis that is common
in children and young adults. A stye presents as a tender swelling in the lid margin pointing anteriorly through the skin, usually
with a lash at its apex (Fig. 2.32A). Multiple lesions may be present and occasionally an abscess may involve the entire lid margin.
Treatment involves topical (occasionally oral) antibiotics, hot compresses and epilation of the associated lash.

## Impetigo

Impetigo is a super¯ÇØcial skin infection caused by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes, typically a¯Ç£ecting children.

## Involvement of the eyelids is usually associated with infection of

the face. Painful erythematous macules rapidly develop into thinwalled blisters, which develop golden-yellow crusts on rupturing
(Fig. 2.32B). ¯Ç×ere may be fever, malaise and local lymphadenopathy. Treatment is with topical and sometimes oral antibiotics (beta-lactamase resistant) and preventative measures to reduce
transmission as the condition is highly contagious. It is particularly
dangerous to neonates, contact with whom should be avoided.

## Erysipelas

Erysipelas (St AnthonyÔÇÖs ¯ÇØre) is an uncommon acute, potentially
severe, dermal and super¯ÇØcial lymphatic infection usually caused
by S. pyogenes. Diabetes, obesity and alcohol abuse are predisposing factors. An in¯Çøamed erythematous plaque develops (Fig.
2.32C). A well-de¯ÇØned raised border distinguishes erysipelas from
other forms of cellulitis. Complications such as metastatic infection are rare. Treatment is with oral antibiotics, but recurrence is
common.

## Necrotizing fasciitis

## Necrotizing fasciitis is a rare but commonly very severe infection

involving subcutaneous so¯ÇÜ tissue and the skin, with associated

A B

C D

(D) necrotizing fasciitis.

rapidly progressive necrosis. It is usually caused by S. pyogenes
and occasionally S. aureus. ¯Ç×e most frequent sites of involvement
are the extremities, trunk and perineum, as well as postoperative
wound sites. Unless early aggressive treatment is instituted, in the
form of surgical debridement and high-dose intravenous antibiotics, death may result. Redness and oedema are followed by the
formation of large bullae and black discoloration of the skin due to
necrosis (Fig. 2.32D).

VIRAL INFECTIONS

# Molluscum contagiosum

## Introduction

Molluscum contagiosum is a skin infection caused by a humanspeci¯ÇØc double-stranded DNA poxvirus that typically a¯Ç£ects
otherwise healthy children, with a peak incidence between 2
and 4 years of age. Transmission is by contact and subsequently
by autoinoculation. Multiple and occasionally con¯Çøuent lesions
may develop in immunocompromised patients. Histopathology
shows a central pit and lobules of hyperplastic epidermis with

intracytoplasmic (HendersonÔÇôPatterson) inclusion bodies that
displace the nuclear remnant to the edge of the cell. ¯Ç×e bodies are
small and eosinophilic near the surface and large and basophilic
deeper down (Fig. 2.33A).

## Diagnosis

Single or multiple pale, waxy, umbilicated nodules develop (Fig.
2.33B). White cheesy material consisting of infected degenerate
cells can be expressed from the lesion. Lesions on the lid margin
(Fig. 2.33C) may shed virus into the tear ¯ÇØlm and give rise to a secondary ipsilateral chronic follicular conjunctivitis. Unless the lid
margin is examined carefully the causative molluscum lesion may
be overlooked. Staphylococcal nodules of the lid may look similar,
but are whiter and not umbilicated (Fig. 2.33D).

## Treatment

Spontaneous resolution will usually occur within a few months,
so treatment may not be necessary, particularly in children, unless
complications such as a signi¯ÇØcant secondary conjunctivitis are
problematic. Options include shave excision, curettage, cauterization, chemical ablation, cryotherapy and pulsed-dye laser.

68 Viral Infections

A B

C D

E F

hyperplastic epidermis and a pit containing intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies; (B) multiple
molluscum nodules; (C) lid margin nodule; (D) should be di¯ÇØerentiated from Staphylococcus
pustules; (E) Mpox (monkey pox) lesion on lid margin with follicular conjunctivitis and lid swelling; (F) Mpox skin pustules. (Courtesy of RSR Naik ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. D; G Kontos ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs E and F.)

TIP In a patient with chronic follicular conjunctivitis that
does not respond to topical antibiotics, look for molluscum
contagiosum involving the lid margin.

Mpox (Monkey pox)

Mpox is caused by a zoonotic virus in the genus Orthopoxvirus. It
was once considered rare in humans, but the quantity and severity of outbreaks is increasing, possibly as a consequence of waning
immunity since the stopping of routine smallpox vaccination. In
July 2022 the World Health Organization declared the outbreak a
Public Health Emergency of International Concern.
¯Çü It is spread from animals by handling infected meat and from
human-to-human through exposure to contaminated body
¯Çøuids and intimate contact with the pox lesions. Sexual transmission occurs predominantly in a man who has sex with a
man who carries the virus. ¯Ç×e virus is spread from the onset
of symptoms until all the lesions have scabbed and fallen o¯Ç£.
¯Çü Early symptoms include headache, fever and fatigue. ¯Ç×is is
followed a few days later by the development of vesicles on the
face and then elsewhere on the body, particularly on the palms
of the hand and soles of the feet.
¯Çü Approximately one in ¯ÇØve cases have ocular lesions. Pustules
on the lid margin can result in follicular conjunctivitis, secondary lid swelling (Fig. 2.33E) and preauricular lymph gland
enlargement. In those with conjunctivitis there is a higher
frequency of other systemic symptoms, compared to those
without conjunctivitis. Corneal involvement in the form of
keratitis may range from mild to severe. Loss of vision has
been reported in about 5%.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e lesions on the body resemble chickenpox (Fig. 2.33F),
except that swollen glands are found. ¯Ç×ey start as small spots
which become bumps that ¯ÇØll with clear ¯Çøuid; then umbilicated pustules which subsequently burst and scab.

¯Çü ¯Ç×e illness is usually mild and most will recover within a few
weeks without treatment. ¯Ç×ere is no cure, but the antiviral
drugs cidofovir and tecovirimat and vaccinia immune globulin
may be used in severe cases. ¯Ç×e smallpox vaccine is 85% protective in preventing infection in close contacts.

## Herpes zoster ophthalmicus

Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO ÔÇô Fig. 2.34A and B) is a common, generally unilateral infection caused by varicella-zoster
virus. It is discussed in detail in Chapter 7

## Herpes simplex

## Introduction

## Herpes simplex skin rash results from either primary infection or

reactivation of herpes simplex virus previously dormant in the trigeminal ganglion. Prodromal facial and lid tingling lasting about
24 hours is followed by the development of eyelid and periocular
skin vesicles (Fig. 2.35A) that break down over 48 hours. Although
typically con¯ÇØned to a single dermatome and with individual
lesions that are o¯ÇÜen similar in appearance, the distribution of
the herpes simplex skin rash contrasts with the sharply delineated
unilateral involvement in HZO (see Fig. 2.34). ¯Ç×ere is commonly
an associated papillary conjunctivitis, discharge and lid swelling.
Dendritic corneal ulcers can develop, especially in atopic patients,
in whom skin involvement can be extensive and very severe
(eczema herpeticum ÔÇô Fig. 2.35B).

## Treatment

In many patients the in¯Çøammation will gradually settle without
treatment over about a week. If treatment is necessary, a topical
(aciclovir cream ¯ÇØve times daily for 5 days) or oral (oral aciclovir,
famciclovir or valaciclovir) antiviral agent can be used. Antibiotics

A B

crusting rash with periocular oedema; the right side shows passive swelling. (Courtesy
T Carmichael ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

70 Blepharitis

A B

(e.g. co-amoxiclav, erythromycin) may also be required in patients
with secondary bacterial infection. ¯Ç×is is particularly common in
eczema herpeticum.

BLEPHARITIS

## Chronic blepharitis

## Introduction

Chronic blepharitis (chronic marginal blepharitis) is a common
cause of ocular discomfort and irritation. ¯Ç×e poor correlation
between symptoms and signs, the uncertain aetiology and mechanisms of the disease process all combine to make management dif¯ÇØcult. Blepharitis may be subdivided into anterior and posterior
forms, although there is considerable overlap and both types are
o¯ÇÜen present (mixed blepharitis).
¯Çü Anterior blepharitis a¯Ç£ects the area surrounding the bases of
the eyelashes and may be staphylococcal or seborrhoeic. It is
sometimes regarded as related more to chronic infective elements and hence more amenable to treatment and remission
than the posterior form. An aetiological factor in staphylococcal blepharitis may be an abnormal cell-mediated response to
components of the cell wall of S. aureus, which may also be
responsible for the red eyes and peripheral corneal in¯ÇØltrates
seen in some patients. It is more common and more marked
in patients with atopic dermatitis. Seborrhoeic blepharitis is
strongly associated with generalized seborrhoeic dermatitis
that characteristically involves the scalp, nasolabial folds, skin
behind the ears and the sternum.
¯Çü Posterior blepharitis is caused by meibomian gland dysfunction and alterations in meibomian gland secretions.

## Bacterial lipases may result in the formation of free fatty

acids. This increases the melting point of the meibum,
preventing its expression from the glands, contributing to
ocular surface irritation and possibly enabling growth of S.
aureus. Loss of the tear film phospholipids that act as surfactants results in increased tear evaporation and osmolarity
and an unstable tear film. Posterior blepharitis is commonly

thought of as a more persistent and chronic inflammatory
condition than anterior blepharitis; there is an association
with acne rosacea.
¯Çü Demodex. A reaction to the extremely common hair follicle
and sebaceous gland-dwelling mite Demodex and other microorganisms may play a causative role in some patients ÔÇô Demodex folliculorum longus in anterior blepharitis and Demodex
folliculorum brevis in posterior blepharitis ÔÇô though the mite
can be found normally in a majority of older patients, most
of whom do not develop symptomatic blepharitis. Collarettes,
a combination of partially digested epithelial cells, keratin,
waste and mite eggs, are considered a pathognomonic sign of
Demodex infection. It has been proposed that circumstances
such as overpopulation or hypersensitivity (perhaps to a bacillus carried symbiotically by Demodex) may lead to symptoms.
Demodex mites are a major cause of the animal disease mange.
¯Ç×e characteristics of the di¯Ç£erent forms of blepharitis are set
out in Table 2.4

## Diagnosis

Involvement is usually bilateral and symmetrical, with no visual
disturbance.
¯Çü Symptoms are caused by disruption of normal ocular surface
function and reduction in tear stability and are similar in all
forms of blepharitis, though stinging may be more common
in posterior disease. Because of poor correlation between the
severity of symptoms and signs it can be di¯ÇÖcult to objectively
assess the bene¯ÇØt of treatment. Burning, grittiness, mild photophobia and crusting and redness of the lid margins with
remissions and exacerbations are characteristic. Symptoms are
usually worse in the mornings although in patients with associated dry eye they may increase during the day. Contact lenses
may be poorly tolerated.
¯Çü Signs ÔÇô staphylococcal blepharitis

{ Hard scales and crusting are found, mainly located around
the bases of the lashes and are associated with collarettes,
which are cylindrical collections around lash bases (Fig.
2.36A).

{ ¯Ç×e tear ¯ÇØlm is oily and foamy and o¯ÇÜen unstable, and
froth may accumulate on the lid margins (Fig. 2.37D) or
inner canthi.
¯Çü Demodex infestation may lead to cylindrical dandru¯Ç£-like
scaling (collarettes) around the base of eyelashes, though
this is not always present (see Fig. 2.36A). ¯Ç×e mites can be

72 Blepharitis

A B

C D

on the eyelid margin.

demonstrated under ├ù16 slit lamp magni¯ÇØcation by ¯ÇØrst manually clearing around the base of an eyelash and then by gently
rotating the lash or moving it from side to side for 5ÔÇô10 seconds with ¯ÇØne forceps. If one or more mites (0.2ÔÇô0.4 mm long)
do not emerge, the lash should be gently epilated. Slide microscopy can be performed on the mites or lashes if necessary.
¯Çü Secondary changes include papillary conjunctivitis, inferior
corneal punctate epithelial erosions, corneal scarring and
vascularization including Salzmann nodular degeneration
and advancing wave-like epitheliopathy-type changes, stye
formation, marginal keratitis and occasionally bacterial keratitis (especially in contact lens wearers) and phlyctenular eye
disease.

## Treatment

¯Ç×ere is limited evidence to support any particular treatment protocol for blepharitis. Patients should be advised that a permanent
cure is unlikely, but control of symptoms is usually possible. ¯Ç×e
treatment of anterior and posterior disease is similar for both

types, particularly as they commonly co-exist, but some treatments are fairly speci¯ÇØc for one or the other.
¯Çü Lid hygiene can be carried out once or twice daily initially,
although compliance and technique are highly variable.

{ A warm compress should ¯ÇØrst be applied for several minutes to so¯ÇÜen crusts at the bases of the lashes.

{ Lid cleaning is subsequently performed to mechanically
remove crusts and other debris, scrubbing the lid margins
with a wet cotton bud or clean, wet face-cloth Contrary
to popular belief, baby shampoo should not be recommended, as it destabilizes the tear ¯ÇØlm and is a common
cause of contact dermatitis.

{ Commercially produced soap/alcohol impregnated pads
for lid scrubbing are available and are o¯ÇÜen highly e¯Ç£ective, but care should be taken not to induce mechanical
irritation.

{ When substantial meibomian gland disease is present, the
regimen may include expression of accumulated meibum
by rolling the ¯ÇØnger anteriorly over the margin.

{ ¯Ç×e putative action of lid hygiene against Demodex is via
prevention of reproduction.

{ Lid hygiene can be performed less frequently as the condition is brought under control.
¯Çü Antibiotics

{ Topical sodium fusidic acid, erythromycin, bacitracin,
azithromycin or chloramphenicol is used to treat active
folliculitis in anterior disease and is occasionally used for
an extended period. Following lid hygiene, the ointment
should be rubbed onto the anterior lid margin with a cotton bud or clean ¯ÇØnger.

¯Çü Novel therapies include topical ciclosporin, pulsed light
application and purpose-designed devices to probe, heat and/
or express the meibomian glands (e.g. LipiFlow) in posterior
disease.
¯Çü Complications are treated speci¯ÇØcally.

TIP Demodex infection is characterized by itching and the
presence of collarettes, a combination of partially digested
epithelial cells, keratin, waste and mite eggs. The infestation
can be treated with accurately applied tea tree oil products.

## Phthiriasis palpebrarum

¯Ç×e crab louse Phthirus pubis is adapted to living in pubic hair, but
is also commonly found in other hair-covered body areas such as
the chest, axillae and eyelids (phthiriasis palpebrarum). Symptoms
consist of chronic irritation and itching of the lids, but the lice are
o¯ÇÜen an incidental discovery. Conjunctivitis is uncommon. ¯Ç×e
lice are readily visible anchored to lashes (Fig. 2.38A); lice have
six legs rather than the eight possessed by ticks (see next). Ova
and their empty shells appear as oval, brownish, opalescent pearls
adherent to the base of the cilia (Fig. 2.38B). Treatment consists of
mechanical removal of the lice and their attached lashes with ¯ÇØne
forceps. If necessary, topical yellow mercuric oxide 1% or petroleum jelly can be applied to the lashes and lids twice a day for 10
days. Delousing of the patient, family members, clothing and bedding is important to prevent recurrence.

## Tick infestation of the eyelid

## Ticks can attach themselves to the eyelid and should be removed at

the earliest opportunity in order to minimize the risk of contracting a tick-borne zoonosis such as Lyme disease, Rocky Mountain
fever, African tick bite fever (Fig. 2.39) or tularaemia. If the tick is
attached some distance from the eye such that spray can safely be
applied, an insect repellent containing pyrethrin or a pyrethroid
should be sprayed on the tick twice at intervals of a minute. Alternatively, a scabies cream containing permethrin can be applied.
¯Ç×ese have a toxic e¯Ç£ect that prevents the tick from injecting
saliva and a¯ÇÜer 24 hours it should drop o¯Ç£ or can be removed with
¯ÇØne-tipped forceps at the slit lamp (blunt-tipped needle-holders
are an alternative in restrained small children). It is critical that
the tick is detached as close to its skin attachment as possible in
order to remove its head and mouthparts, following which it might
be retained in sealed packaging to permit identi¯ÇØcation if necessary. In areas endemic for Lyme disease, some authorities suggest routine antibiotic prophylaxis with doxycycline following a
con¯ÇØrmed deer tick bite. Patients should be told to seek medical
advice urgently at the onset of suspicious symptoms, particularly
erythema migrans, over the subsequent few weeks. Lyme disease transmission is thought to require attachment of the tick for
at least 36 hours. African tick bite fever is a rickettsial infection,
which is fairly common in sub-Saharan Africa. ¯Ç×e onset of symptoms occurs 4ÔÇô10 days a¯ÇÜer the bite.

{ Oral antibiotic regimens include doxycycline (50ÔÇô100
mg twice daily for 1 week and then daily for 6ÔÇô24 weeks),
lymecycline (one 408 mg capsule daily for up to 3 months),
other tetracyclines, or azithromycin (500 mg daily for 3
days for three cycles at 1-week intervals). Antibiotics are
thought to reduce bacterial colonization and may also
exert other e¯Ç£ects such as a reduction in staphylococcal
lipase production with tetracyclines. Tetracyclines may be
more e¯Ç£ective in the treatment of posterior disease and
azithromycin in anterior. Tetracyclines should not be used
in children under the age of 12 years or in pregnant or
breastfeeding women because they are deposited in growing bone and teeth. Patients should also be made aware of
the possibility of increased sun sensitivity. Erythromycin
250 mg once or twice daily is an alternative.
¯Çü Plant and ¯Çƒsh oil supplements have been shown to be of substantial bene¯ÇØt in some cases.
¯Çü Topical steroid. A low potency preparation such as ¯Çøuorometholone 0.1% or loteprednol four times daily for 1 week is
useful in patients with substantial active in¯Çøammation, especially papillary conjunctivitis; occasionally a higher strength
preparation is used.
¯Çü Tear substitutes and other dry eye treatments are typically
helpful for associated tear insu¯ÇÖciency and instability.
¯Çü Tea tree oil has been suggested as a treatment based primarily
on its activity against Demodex infestation in¯Çƒvitro, although
there remains some uncertainty to the e¯Ç£ectiveness in¯Çƒvivo. ¯Ç×e
optimal vehicle and regimen have not been established, but
lid, eyebrow and periocular skin cleansing once daily with a
50% scrub and application of 5% ointment has been described.
Preservative-free, sterile, ready-to-use wipes containing 2.5%
terpinen-4-ol are available and can be applied once or twice
daily to the lid margins (depending on severity) for 4 weeks.

## People should be educated on how to accurately apply tea

tree oil products, because adherence and method of application are likely to a¯Ç£ect e¯ÇÖcacy. Topical permethrin and topical (1% cream) or oral (two doses of 200 ╬╝g/kg 1 week apart)
ivermectin have also been used by some practitioners. Early
pre-clinical results using lotilaner 0.25% ophthalmic solution
(TP-03) are encouraging, as it is well tolerated and e¯Ç£ective
in reducing collarettes and Demodex density within 4 weeks
of commencing treatment. High temperature cleaning of bedding, the use of tea tree shampoo and facial soap, and treating
the patientÔÇÖs partner may all help to reduce recurrences.

74 Blepharitis

A B

A B

## Angular blepharitis

¯Ç×e infection is usually caused by Moraxella lacunata or S. aureus
although other bacteria and, rarely, herpes simplex have also been
implicated. Red, scaly, macerated and ¯ÇØssured skin is seen at the
lateral and/or medial canthi of one or both eyes (Fig. 2.40). Skin
cha¯ÇØng secondary to tear over¯Çøow, especially at the lateral canthus, can cause a similar clinical picture and may also predispose
to infection. Associated papillary and follicular conjunctivitis may
occur. Treatment involves topical chloramphenicol, bacitracin or
erythromycin.

## Childhood blepharokeratoconjunctivitis

Childhood blepharokeratoconjunctivitis is a poorly de¯ÇØned condition that tends to be more severe in Asian and Middle Eastern
populations. Presentation is usually at about 6 years of age with
recurrent episodes of anterior or posterior blepharitis, sometimes Fig. 2.40 Angular blepharitis. (Courtesy of S Tuft.)
associated with recurrent styes or chalazia. Constant eye rubbing

and photophobia may lead to misdiagnosis as allergic eye disease.
Conjunctival changes include di¯Ç£use hyperaemia, bulbar phlyctens and follicular or papillary hyperplasia.
Corneal changes include super¯ÇØcial punctate keratopathy,
marginal keratitis, peripheral vascularization and axial subepithelial haze. Treatment is with lid hygiene and topical antibiotic ointment at bedtime. Topical low-dose steroids (prednisolone 0.1% or
¯Çøuorometholone 0.1%) and erythromycin syrup 125 mg daily for
4ÔÇô6 weeks may also be used.

PTOSIS

Classi¯Ç×cation

Ptosis is an abnormally low position of the upper lid, which may be
congenital or acquired.
¯Çü Neurogenic ptosis is caused by an innervational defect such
as third nerve paresis and Horner syndrome (see Ch. 19) (Fig.
2.41A).
¯Çü Myogenic ptosis is caused by a myopathy of the levator muscle itself, or by impairment of transmission of impulses at the
neuromuscular junction (neuromyopathic). Acquired myogenic ptosis occurs in myasthenia gravis, myotonic dystrophy
and progressive external ophthalmoplegia (see Ch. 19) (Fig.
2.41B).
¯Çü Aponeurotic or involutional ptosis is caused by a defect in the
levator aponeurosis (Fig. 2.41C).
¯Çü Mechanical ptosis is caused by the gravitational e¯Ç£ect of a
mass or by scarring (Fig. 2.41D).

## Clinical evaluation

## General

¯Ç×e age at onset of ptosis and the duration will usually distinguish
congenital from acquired cases. If the history is ambiguous, old
photographs may be helpful. It is also important to enquire about
symptoms of possible underlying systemic disease, such as associated diplopia, variability of ptosis during the day and excessive
fatigue.

## Pseudoptosis

A false impression of ptosis may be caused by the following:
¯Çü Lack of support of the lids by the globe may be due to an
orbital volume de¯ÇØcit associated with an arti¯ÇØcial eye (Fig.
2.42A), microphthalmos, phthisis bulbi, or enophthalmos.
¯Çü Contralateral lid retraction, which is detected by comparing
the levels of the upper lids, remembering that the margin of
the upper lid normally covers the superior 2 mm of the cornea
(Fig. 2.42B).
¯Çü Ipsilateral hypotropia causes pseudoptosis because the upper
lid follows the globe downwards (Fig. 2.42C). It disappears
when the hypotropic eye assumes ¯ÇØxation on covering the
normal eye.
¯Çü Brow ptosis due to excessive skin on the brow, or seventh nerve
palsy, which is diagnosed by manually elevating the eyebrow.

A

B

C

D

¯Çü Dermatochalasis. Overhanging skin on the upper lids
(Fig. 2.42D) may be mistaken for ptosis, but may also cause
mechanical ptosis.

## Measurements

¯Çü MarginÔÇôre¯Çøex distance is the distance between the upper lid
margin and the corneal re¯Çøection of a pen torch held by the
examiner on which the patient ¯ÇØxates (Fig. 2.43). ¯Ç×e normal
measurement is 4ÔÇô5 mm.
¯Çü Palpebral ¯Çƒssure height is the distance between the upper
and lower lid margins, measured in the pupillary plane (Fig.
2.44). ¯Ç×e upper lid margin normally rests about 2 mm below
the upper limbus and the lower 1 mm above the lower limbus. ¯Ç×is measurement is shorter in males (7ÔÇô10 mm) than
in females (8ÔÇô12 mm). Unilateral ptosis can be quanti¯ÇØed by
comparison with the contralateral side. Ptosis may be graded
as mild (up to 2 mm), moderate (3 mm) and severe (4 mm
or more).
¯Çü Levator function (upper lid excursion) is measured by
placing a thumb firmly against the patientÔÇÖs brow to negate

the action of the frontalis muscle, with the eyes in downgaze

and the amount of excursion is measured with a rule (Fig.
2.45B). Levator function is graded as normal (15 mm or
more), good (12ÔÇô14 mm), fair (5ÔÇô11 mm) and poor (4 mm
or less).
¯Çü Upper lid crease is taken as the vertical distance between
the lid margin and the lid crease in downgaze. In females
it measures about 10 mm and in males 8 mm. Absence of
the crease in a patient with congenital ptosis is evidence of
poor levator function, whereas a high crease suggests an
aponeurotic defect (usually involutional). The skin crease is
also used as a guide to the initial incision in some surgical
procedures.
¯Çü Pretarsal show is the distance between the lid margin and the
skin fold with the eyes in the primary position.

## Associated signs

¯Çü ¯ÇØe pupils should be examined to exclude Horner syndrome
and a subtle pupil-involving third nerve palsy ÔÇô the latter is an
unlikely acute clinical presentation (see Ch. 19).
¯Çü Increased innervation may ¯Çøow to the levator muscle of a
unilateral ptosis, particularly in upgaze. Associated increased
innervation to the contralateral normal levator will result in lid
retraction. ¯Ç×e examiner should therefore manually elevate the
lid and look for drooping of the opposite lid. If this occurs, the

particularly following large levator resections or suspension
procedures.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe tear ¯Çƒlm should be inspected ÔÇô a poor volume or unstable ¯ÇØlm may be worsened by ptosis surgery and should be
addressed preoperatively as far as possible.

## Simple congenital ptosis

## Diagnosis

A

¯Çƒrmly against the brow to reduce the e¯ÇØect of the frontalis
muscle and ask the patient to look down; (B) the patient then

patient should be warned that surgical correction may induce
a lower position in the opposite lid.
¯Çü Fatigability is tested by asking the patient to look up without
blinking for 30ÔÇô60 seconds. Progressive drooping of one or
both lids, or an inability to maintain upgaze, is suggestive of
myasthenia gravis (see Ch. 19). Myasthenic ptosis may show
an overshoot of the upper lid on saccade from downgaze to the
primary position (Cogan twitch sign) and a ÔÇÿhopÔÇÖ on side-gaze.
¯Çü Ocular motility defects, particularly of the superior rectus,
must be evaluated in patients with congenital ptosis. Correction of an ipsilateral hypotropia may improve the degree of
ptosis. De¯ÇØcits consistent with a subtle or partial third nerve
paresis should be identi¯ÇØed.
¯Çü Jaw-winking can be identi¯ÇØed by asking the patient to chew
and move the jaws from side to side (see below).
¯Çü Bell phenomenon is tested by manually holding the lids open,
asking the patient to try to shut the eyes and observing upward
and outward rotation of the globe. A weak Bell phenomenon
carries a variable risk of postoperative exposure keratopathy,

## Congenital ptosis probably results from a failure of neuronal
migration or development with muscular sequelae secondary to

this. A minority of patients have a family history.
¯Çü Signs

## Treatment

## Treatment should be carried out during the preschool years once

accurate measurements can be obtained, but may be considered
earlier in severe cases to prevent amblyopia. Levator resection (see
below) is usually required.

TIP A weak Bell phenomenon can result in exposure
keratopathy after ptosis surgery.

Marcus Gunn jaw¯Çƒwinking syndrome

## Introduction

About 5% of all cases of congenital ptosis are associated with the
Marcus Gunn jaw-winking phenomenon. Most are unilateral.
Although the exact aetiology is unclear, it has been postulated that
a branch of the mandibular division of the ¯ÇØ¯ÇÜh cranial nerve is
misdirected to the levator muscle.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Signs

{ Retraction of the ptotic lid in conjunction with stimulation of the ipsilateral pterygoid muscles by chewing,

{ Unilateral or bilateral ptosis of variable severity (Fig. 2.46A
and B).

{ Absent upper lid crease and poor levator function (Fig.
2.46C F).

{ In downgaze the ptotic lid is higher than the normal
because of poor relaxation of the levator muscle. ¯Ç×is
is in contrast to acquired ptosis, in which the a¯Ç£ected
lid is either level with or lower than the normal lid on
downgaze.

{ Following surgical correction, the lid lag in downgaze may
worsen.
¯Çü Associations

{ Superior rectus weakness may be present because of its
close embryological association with the levator.

{ Compensatory chin elevation in severe bilateral cases.

{ Refractive errors are common and more frequently responsible for amblyopia than the ptosis itself.

78 Ptosis

A B

C D

E F

A B

on opening the mouth.

sucking, opening the mouth (Fig. 2.47) or contralateral
jaw movement.

{ Less common stimuli to winking include jaw protrusion,
smiling, swallowing and clenching of teeth.

{ Jaw-winking does not improve with age, although patients
may learn to mask it.

¯Çü Mild cases with reasonable levator function of 5 mm or better
and little synkinetic movement may be treated with unilateral
levator advancement.
¯Çü Moderate cases. Unilateral levator disinsertion can be performed to address the synkinetic winking component, with
ipsilateral brow (frontalis) suspension so that lid elevation is
due solely to frontalis muscle elevation.
¯Çü Bilateral surgery. Bilateral levator disinsertion with bilateral
brow suspension may be carried out to produce a symmetrical
result.

## Treatment

Surgery should be considered if jaw-winking or ptosis represents a
signi¯ÇØcant functional or cosmetic problem.

## Third nerve misdirection syndromes

¯Ç×ird nerve misdirection syndromes may be congenital, but more
frequently follow acquired third nerve palsy. Bizarre movements of
the upper lid accompany various eye movements (Fig. 2.48). Ptosis
may also occur following aberrant facial nerve regeneration. Treatment is by levator disinsertion and brow suspension.

## Involutional ptosis

Involutional (aponeurotic) ptosis is an age-related condition
caused by dehiscence, disinsertion or stretching of the levator aponeurosis, limiting the transmission of force from a normal levator
muscle to the upper lid. Due to fatigue of the M├╝ller muscle, it
frequently worsens towards the end of the day, so that it can sometimes be confused with ptosis secondary to myasthenia. ¯Ç×ere is a
variable, usually bilateral, ptosis with a high upper lid crease and
good levator function. In severe cases the upper lid crease may be
absent, the eyelid above the tarsal plate is very thin and the upper
sulcus deep (Fig. 2.49). Treatment options include levator resection, advancement with reinsertion or anterior levator repair.

TIP Involutional ptosis may be confused with ocular
myasthenia gravis because it frequently gets worse towards the
end of the day.

## Mechanical ptosis

## Mechanical ptosis is the result of impaired mobility of the upper

lid. It may be caused by dermatochalasis, large tumours such as
a neuro¯ÇØbroma, heavy scar tissue, severe oedema and anterior
orbital lesions.

## Surgery

## Anatomy

¯Çü ¯ÇØe levator aponeurosis fuses with the orbital septum about 4
mm above the superior border of the tarsal plate (Fig. 2.50A).
Its posterior ¯ÇØbres insert into the lower third of the anterior
surface of the tarsal plate. ¯Ç×e medial and lateral horns are
expansions that act as check ligaments (Fig. 2.50B). Surgically, the aponeurosis can be approached through the skin or
conjunctiva.
¯Çü Eyelids of Asians. ¯Ç×e main di¯Ç£erence in the upper lid appearance is a low or absent skin crease in about 50% of Asians due
to the low insertion of the levator aponeurosis, close to the
lashes. ¯Ç×e septum also inserts lower on the aponeurosis. ¯Ç×e
presence of a medial canthal fold can cause lash ptosis (Fig.
2.51).
¯Çü M├╝ller muscle is inserted into the upper border of the tarsal
plate and can be approached transconjunctivally.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe inferior tarsal aponeurosis consists of the capsulopalpebral expansion of the inferior rectus muscle and is analogous to the levator aponeurosis.

A

B

(B) retraction of the right lid on right gaze. (Courtesy of A
Pearson.)

creases and deep sulci.

¯Çü ¯ÇØe inferior tarsal muscle is analogous to M├╝ller muscle.

ConjunctivaÔÇôM├╝ller resection

¯Ç×is involves excision of M├╝ller muscle and overlying conjunctiva with reattachment of the resected edges (Fig. 2.52A C). ¯Ç×e
maximal elevation achievable is 2ÔÇô3 mm, so it is used in cases of
mild ptosis with good (at least 10 mm) levator function, which
includes most cases of Horner syndrome and mild congenital
ptosis.

Levator advancement (resection)

## In this technique the levator complex is shortened through either

an anterior ÔÇô skin (Fig. 2.53A C) ÔÇô or posterior ÔÇô conjunctival ÔÇô approach. Indications include ptosis of any cause, provided
residual levator function is at least 5 mm. ¯Ç×e extent of resection
is determined by the severity of the ptosis and the amount of levator function. ¯Ç×e predictability of correcting height is the same
whether an anterior or posterior approach is used. However, the
advantage of a posterior approach is the predictability of the lid
contour.

Brow (frontalis) suspension

Brow (frontalis) suspension is used for severe ptosis (>4 mm) with
very poor levator function (<4 mm) from a variety of causes. Typical indications include ptosis associated with third nerve palsy,

ECTROPION

## Involutional ectropion

## Introduction

Involutional (age-related) ectropion a¯Ç£ects the lower lid of elderly
individuals. It causes epiphora (tear over¯Çøow) and may exacerbate
ocular surface disease. ¯Ç×e red appearance of the exposed conjunctiva is cosmetically poor. In long-standing cases the tarsal conjunctiva may become chronically in¯Çøamed, thickened and keratinized
(Fig. 2.55A). Aetiological factors include:

¯Çü Horizontal lid laxity, demonstrated by pulling the central part of
the lid 8 mm or more from the globe, with a failure to snap back
to its normal position on release without the patient ¯ÇØrst blinking.
¯Çü Lateral canthal tendon laxity, characterized by a rounded
appearance of the lateral canthus and the ability to pull the
lower lid medially more than 2 mm.
¯Çü Medial canthal tendon laxity, demonstrated by pulling the
lower lid laterally and observing the position of the inferior
punctum. If the lid is normal the punctum should not be
displaced more than 1ÔÇô2 mm. If laxity is mild the punctum
reaches the limbus and if severe it may reach the pupil.

## Treatment

¯Ç×e approach to repair depends on apparent causation and the
predominant location of the ectropion.
¯Çü Generalized ectropion is treated with repair of horizontal
lid laxity. ¯Ç×is is achieved with a lateral tarsal strip procedure,
in which the lower canthal tendon is tightened by shortening
and reattachment to the lateral orbital rim (Fig. 2.56). ¯Ç×is is
particularly helpful if the lateral canthus is rounded and lax,
with associated tear over¯Çøow. Excision of a tarsoconjunctival
pentagon is an alternative that can be placed to excise an area
of misdirected lashes or keratinized conjunctiva (Fig. 2.57).
¯Çü Medial ectropion, if mild, may be treated with a medial conjunctival diamond excision (medial spindle procedure). It is
o¯ÇÜen combined with a tarsal strip or lateral canthal sling (see
ÔÇÿTreatmentÔÇÖ), or pentagon excision as signi¯ÇØcant horizontal
laxity frequently co-exists.
