and the lacrimal glands are essential for the normal function of these tissues.
¯Çü Neural via ¯Çƒbres adjacent to the lacrimal glands and goblet
cells that stimulate aqueous and mucus secretion.

## Mechanism of disease

¯ÇØe four core inter-related mechanisms thought to be responsible for the manifestations of dry eye are tear instability, tear
hyperosmolarity, in¯Çƒammation and ocular surface damage.
In¯Ç×ammation in the conjunctiva and accessory glands as well as
the ocular surface is present in 80% of patients with KCS and
may be both a cause and consequence of dry eye, amplifying
and perpetuating disease. ¯ÇØe presence of in¯Ç×ammation is the
rationale for speci¯Çƒc anti-in¯Ç×ammatory measures such as steroid
therapy. ¯ÇØere is a strong association between dry eye syndrome
and reduced levels of systemic androsterone sulphate and epiandrosterone sulphate.

TIP Whether a patient has primary aqueous de¯Çƒciency
or evaporative dry eye, the end result is ocular surface
in¯Ç×ammation and discomfort.

E¯Ç×ect of environmental factors

As well as the basic classi¯Çƒcation, DEWS draws attention to the
e¯Ç£ect of the environment on the type of dry eye with which a
patient presents. ¯ÇØese can be both internal factors, such as age,
hormonal status and behaviour patterns, and external, such as the
exacerbation of evaporative factors in an atmosphere with low
relative humidity.

{ Topical drugs including the e¯Ç£ect of preservatives.

{ Contact lens wear.

{ Ocular surface disease, such as allergic conjunctivitis.

160 Sj├Âgren Syndrome

SJ├ûGREN SYNDROME

Sj├Âgren syndrome (SS) is an autoimmune disorder characterized by lymphocytic inflammation and destruction of lacrimal
and salivary glands (Fig. 5.2A) and other exocrine organs. The
classic clinical triad consists of dry eyes, dry mouth (Fig. 5.2B)
and parotid gland enlargement (Fig. 5.2C), but other features
are common and can affect all organ systems. The condition is
classified as primary when it exists in isolation and secondary
when associated with another disease, commonly rheumatoid
arthritis or systemic lupus erythematosus. Primary SS affects
females more frequently than males. Although in clinical practice the diagnosis may be made on less stringent grounds, the
American College of Rheumatology (ACR) criteria for diagnosis specify, in patients with a clinical picture suggestive of SS,
the following:
¯Çü Positivity for anti-SSA or anti-SSB antibodies, or positive
rheumatoid factor together with signi¯Çƒcantly positive antinuclear antibody.
¯Çü Ocular surface staining above a certain grade.
¯Çü Focal lymphocytic sialadenitis to a speci¯Çƒed extent on salivary
gland biopsy (see Fig. 5.2A).
Widely used but older American-European Consensus Group
criteria are more extensive and include more clinical ¯Çƒndings, but
give results consistent with the ACR criteria.
Treatment options for SS include a range of symptomatic
treatments for dry eye, as discussed below, dry mouth and other
manifestations, salivary stimulants (e.g. oral pilocarpine) and, in
some cases, immunosuppression and biological blockers such as
rituximab.

## Clinical features

## Symptoms

¯ÇØe most common ocular symptoms are feelings of dryness, grittiness and burning that characteristically worsen over the course of
the day. Stringy discharge, transient blurring of vision, redness and
crusting of the lids are also common. Lack of emotional or re¯Ç×ex
tearing is unusual. ¯ÇØe symptoms of KCS are frequently exacerbated
on exposure to conditions associated with increased tear evaporation (e.g. air-conditioning, wind and central heating) or prolonged
reading or video display unit use, when blink frequency is reduced.

TIP The symptoms associated with keratoconjunctivitis sicca
are exacerbated by exposure to wind, air-conditioning and
central heating.

## Signs

¯Çü Posterior (seborrhoeic) blepharitis with meibomian gland
dysfunction is o¯Çøen present (Fig. 5.3A and B).

INVESTIGATION

¯ÇØe aim of investigation is to con¯Çƒrm and quantify a clinical diagnosis of dry eye. Although the symptoms in a given patient do
not change, objective signs can ¯Ç×uctuate. ¯ÇØe correlation between
symptoms and tests is poor. However, the reliability of tests
improves as the severity of dry eye increases. ¯ÇØe tests measure the
following parameters:
¯Çü Stability of the tear film as related to its break-up time
(BUT).

{ ¯ÇØe marginal tear meniscus (strip) is a crude measure
of the volume of aqueous in the tear ¯Çƒlm. In the normal
eye the meniscus is 0.2ÔÇô0.4 mm in height, but in dry eye
becomes thin (less than 0.25 mm) or absent (Fig. 5.5B).
¯Çü Cornea
¯Çü Punctate epithelial erosions that stain well with ¯Ç×uorescein
(Fig. 5.6A).
¯Çü Filaments consist of strands of mucus and debris (such as shed
epithelial cells) and are typically attached at one end to the
corneal surface (Fig. 5.6B). ¯ÇØe ¯Çƒlaments stain well with rose
Bengal (Fig. 5.6C), but less so with ¯Ç×uorescein.
¯Çü Mucous plaques with similar constituents may occur in severe
dry eye. ¯ÇØey consist of semi-transparent, white-to-grey, o¯Çøen
slightly elevated lesions of varying size (Fig. 5.6D).

¯ÇØere is no clinical test that allows the diagnosis of evaporative
dry eye to be de¯Çƒnitively con¯Çƒrmed. It is therefore a presumptive
diagnosis based on the presence of associated clinical ¯Çƒndings. It
is suggested the tests are performed in the following order because
the Schirmer strip paper can damage the ocular surface and cause
staining.

Tear ¯Çƒlm break-up time

¯ÇØe tear ¯Çƒlm BUT is abnormal in aqueous tear de¯Çƒciency and meibomian gland disorders. It is measured as follows:
¯Çü Fluorescein 2% or an impregnated ¯Ç×uorescein strip moistened
with non-preserved saline is instilled into the lower fornix.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe patient is asked to blink several times.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe tear ¯Çƒlm is examined at the slit lamp with a broad beam
using the cobalt blue ¯Çƒlter. A¯Çøer an interval, black spots or
lines appear in the ¯Ç×uorescein-stained ¯Çƒlm (Fig. 5.8A), indicating the formation of dry areas.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe BUT is the interval between the last blink and the appearance of the ¯Çƒrst randomly distributed dry spot. A BUT of less
than 10 seconds is suspicious.
¯ÇØe development of dry spots always in the same location may
indicate a local corneal surface abnormality (e.g. epithelial basement membrane disease) rather than an intrinsic instability of the
tear ¯Çƒlm.

## Schirmer test

¯ÇØe Schirmer test is a useful assessment of aqueous tear production. ¯ÇØe test involves measuring the amount of wetting of a special (no. 41 Whatman) ¯Çƒlter paper, 5 mm wide and 35 mm long.
¯ÇØe test can be performed with or without topical anaesthesia.
In theory, when performed with an anaesthetic (Schirmer 2)
basic secretion is measured and without anaesthetic (Schirmer
1) it measures maximum basic plus re¯Ç×ex secretion. In practice,
however, topical anaesthesia cannot abolish all sensory and psychological stimuli for re¯Ç×ex secretion. ¯ÇØe test is performed as
follows:
¯Çü Excess tears are delicately dried. If topical anaesthesia is
applied the excess should be removed from the inferior fornix
with ¯Çƒlter paper.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe ¯Çƒlter paper is folded 5 mm from one end and inserted at
the junction of the middle and outer third of the lower lid, taking care not to touch the cornea or lashes (Fig. 5.8B).
¯Çü ¯ÇØe patient is asked to keep the eyes gently closed.
¯Çü A¯Çøer 5 minutes the ¯Çƒlter paper is removed and the amount of
wetting from the fold measured.
¯Çü Less than 10 mm of wetting a¯Çøer 5 minutes without anaesthesia
or less than 6 mm with anaesthesia is considered abnormal.
Results can be variable and a single Schirmer test should not
be used as the sole criterion for diagnosing dry eye, but repeatedly
abnormal tests are highly supportive.

## Ocular surface staining

¯Çü Fluorescein stains corneal and conjunctival epithelium (see

enter the tissues.
¯Çü Rose Bengal is a dye that has an a¯ÇÜnity for dead or devitalized
epithelial cells that have a lost or altered mucous layer (Fig.
5.8C). Corneal ¯Çƒlaments and plaques (see Fig. 5.6B and D) are
also shown up more clearly by the dye and the use of a red-free
¯Çƒlter may help visualization. A 1% solution of rose Bengal or a
moistened impregnated strip can be used. ¯ÇØe dye may cause
intense stinging that can last for up to a day, particularly in
patients with severe KCS. To minimize irritation a very small
drop should be used, immediately preceded by a drop of topical anaesthetic and the excess washed out with saline.
¯Çü Lissamine green stains in a similar fashion to rose Bengal but
causes less irritation and may be preferred (see Fig. 5.4B).
¯Çü ¯ÇØe pattern of staining may aid diagnosis:

{ Interpalpebral staining of the cornea and conjunctiva (see

{ Superior conjunctival stain may indicate superior limbic
keratoconjunctivitis.

{ Inferior corneal and conjunctival stain is o¯Çøen present in
patients with blepharitis or exposure (Fig. 5.8D).

TIP In dry eye syndrome lissamine green solution is
preferable to 1% rose Bengal solution as it stains in a similar
fashion, but causes less irritation.

## Other investigations

¯ÇØe following tests are rarely performed in clinical practice.
¯Çü Fluorescein clearance test and the tear function index may
be assessed by placing 5 ╬╝l of ¯Ç×uorescein on the ocular surface
and measuring the residual dye in a Schirmer strip placed on
the lower lateral lid margin at set intervals. Delayed clearance
is observed in all dry eye states.
¯Çü Tear ¯Ç×lm osmolarity measurement techniques are available
and are emerging as an accurate means of diagnosis (Fig.
5.8E and F). ¯ÇØe threshold value that distinguishes between
a healthy eye and an eye with dry eye syndrome varies from
305 mOsm/l and 316 mOsm/l, depending on the degree of tear
¯Çƒlm instability. A widely accepted threshold is 308 mOsm/l
and a value of 316 mOsm/l appears to discriminate between
mild and moderate/severe dry eye. Tear osmolarity may not
correlate with ocular symptoms, but it does correlate with
e¯Ç£ective treatment when evaluated in the long term.
¯Çü Tear constituent measurement. Tear samples can be assayed
for the presence of markers known to be elevated (e.g. matrix
metalloproteinase-9) or decreased (e.g. lactoferrin) in dry eye.
¯Çü Phenol red thread test uses a thread impregnated with a pHsensitive dye. ¯ÇØe end of the thread is placed over the lower lid
and the length wetted (the dye changes from yellow to red in
tears) is measured a¯Çøer 15 seconds. A value of 6 mm is abnormal. It is comparable to the Schirmer test but takes less time
to perform.

¯Çü Tear meniscometry is a technique to quantify the height and
thus the volume of the lower lid meniscus.
¯Çü Impression cytology can determine goblet cell numbers.

TIP Measurement of tear ¯Çƒlm osmolarity is a useful tool when
used to con¯Çƒrm the diagnosis and response to treatment in dry
eye syndrome.

TREATMENT

## Strategy

¯ÇØe underlying causative processes of dry eye are generally not
reversible, and management is therefore structured around the
control of symptoms and the prevention of surface damage.
DEWS have produced guidelines based on earlier International
Taskforce Guidelines for Dry Eye, in which suggested treatment
options depend on the level of severity of disease graded from
1 to 4. ¯ÇØe DEWS guidelines can also be applied in a graded
approach, proceeding to the next level if the preceding measures
are inadequate.

¯Çü Education and environmental/dietary modi¯Ç×cations

{ Establishment of realistic expectations and emphasis on
the importance of compliance.

{ Lifestyle review, including the importance of blinking
whilst reading, watching television or using a computer
screen (which should be orientated below eye level to
minimize palpebral aperture size) and the management of
contact lens wear.

{ Environmental review, e.g. increasing humidity may be
possible for some environments.

{ Instillation aids for eye drops (manufacturer-supplied or
makeshi¯Çø, such as nut-crackers to hold plastic bottles)
should be advocated for patients with reduced dexterity
(e.g. rheumatoid arthritis).

{ Caution the patient that laser refractive surgery can exacerbate dry eye.
¯Çü Systemic medication review to exclude contributory e¯Ç£ects
and eliminate o¯Ç£ending agents. Discontinuation of toxic/preserved topical medication if possible.
¯Çü Arti¯Ç×cial tear substitutes, including gels and ointments ÔÇô
see below. Some authorities advocate that use of preserved
drops should fall within level 1 and categorize non-preserved
drops as a level 2 measure. Mucolytic agents may be speci¯Çƒcally indicated for some patients.
¯Çü Eyelid therapy. Basic measures such as warm compresses and
lid hygiene for blepharitis. Reparative lid surgery (e.g. entropion, ectropion, excessive lid laxity or scleral show) may be
considered as an early measure. Nocturnal lagophthalmos
can be addressed by taping the lids closed at bedtime, wearing
swimming goggles during sleep, or in extreme cases by lateral
tarsorrhaphy.

166 Treatment

¯Çü Non-preserved tear substitutes are categorized as level 2
treatment by some authorities.
¯Çü Anti-inflammatory agents such as topical steroids,
oral omega fatty acids and other agents such as topical
ciclosporin.
¯Çü Tetracyclines (for meibomianitis, rosacea).
¯Çü Punctal plugs.
¯Çü Secretagogues, e.g. pilocarpine, cevimeline, rebamipide.
¯Çü Moisture chamber spectacles and spectacle side shields.

¯Çü Serum eye drops. Autologous or umbilical cord serum.
¯Çü Contact lenses.
¯Çü Permanent punctal occlusion.

¯Çü Systemic anti-in¯Çƒammatory agents.
¯Çü Surgery

{ Eyelid surgery, such as tarsorrhaphy.

{ Salivary gland auto-transplantation.

{ Mucous membrane or amniotic membrane transplantation for corneal complications.

## Tear substitutes

## Tear substitutes have a relatively simple formulation that cannot

approximate the complex components and structure of the normal tear film. Their delivery is also periodic rather than continuous. Almost all are based on replacement of the aqueous phase
of the tear film. There are no mucus substitutes and paraffin is
only an approximation to the action of tear lipids. The optimal
frequency of instillation varies with agent and with severity.
¯Çü Drops and gels. A large range of preparations is available. One
agent or category of preparation has not demonstrated superiority and particular agents are o¯Çøen preferred by individual
patients with limited rationale.

{ Cellulose derivatives (e.g. hypromellose, methylcellulose)
are appropriate for mild cases.

{ Carbomer gels adhere to the ocular surface and so are
longer-lasting, but some patients are troubled by slight
blurring.

{ Other agents include polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), which
increases the persistence of the tear ¯Çƒlm and is useful in
mucin de¯Çƒciency, sodium hyaluronate, povidone, glycerine, propylene glycol, polysorbate and others.

¯Çü Arti¯Ç×cial tear inserts emplaced once or twice daily o¯Ç£er
extended duration treatment and are preferred by some
patients.
¯Çü Mucolytic agents. Acetylcysteine 5% drops may be useful in
patients with corneal ¯Çƒlaments and mucous plaques, which
acetylcysteine dissolves; it may cause stinging on instillation.
Acetylcysteine is malodorous and has a limited shelf-life. Manual debridement of ¯Çƒlaments may also be useful.
¯Çü Preservatives can be a potent source of toxicity, especially a¯Çøer
punctal occlusion. Numerous non-preserved drops are now
available, including some multi-dose products and in general
should be used in preference to preservative-containing preparations in any more than mild disease or with instillation more
than three or four times daily. If possible, preservative-free formulations should also be used for dry eye patients when other
topical medication is required, for example in the treatment of
glaucoma. Newer preservatives such as Polyquad and Purite
seem to exhibit lower ocular surface toxicity than older agents
such as benzalkonium chloride.
¯Çü Haemoderivative treatment has been used to manage severe
ocular surface disease including gra¯Çø-versus-host-related dry
eye disease, Sj├Âgren syndrome, post-LASIK dry eye persistent
epithelial defects and recurrent erosions (see below).

TIP Excessive use of preserved lubricating eye drops can
result in corneal toxicity.

## Punctal occlusion

## Punctal occlusion reduces drainage and thereby preserves natural

tears and prolongs the e¯Ç£ect of arti¯Çƒcial tears. It is of greatest value
in patients with moderateÔÇôsevere KCS who have not responded to
frequent instillation of topical agents.
¯Çü Temporary occlusion can be achieved by inserting collagen
plugs into the canaliculi. ¯ÇØese dissolve over a number of
weeks. ¯ÇØe main aim is to ensure that epiphora does not occur
following permanent occlusion.

{ In severe KCS both the inferior and superior canaliculi can
be plugged.
¯Çü Reversible prolonged occlusion can be achieved with silicone
(Fig. 5.9) or long-acting (2ÔÇô6 months) collagen plugs.

{ Initially the inferior puncta are occluded and the patient is
reviewed a¯Çøer 1 or 2 weeks.

{ If the patient is now asymptomatic and without epiphora,
the plugs can be removed and the inferior canaliculi permanently occluded (see below).

{ Diquafosol is a newer agent that works as a topical
secretagogue.
¯Çü Ointments containing petrolatum (para¯ÇÜn) mineral oil can
be used at bedtime to supplement daytime drops or gel instillation; daytime use is precluded by marked blurring. Some practitioners do not prescribe these for long-term use.
¯Çü Eyelid sprays are applied to the closed eye and typically contain a liposome-based agent that may stabilize the tear ¯Çƒlm and
reduce evaporation.

{ Plugs that pass into the horizontal portion of the canaliculus cannot be visualized and although they can usually be
¯Ç×ushed out with saline, if they cause epiphora this is not
always possible and surgical retrieval may be needed.
¯Çü Permanent occlusion should be undertaken only in patients
with severe dry eye who have had a positive response to
temporary plugs without epiphora. It should be avoided in

{ Problems include extrusion, granuloma formation and
distal migration.

A B

patients, especially if young, who may have reversible pathology. All four puncta should not be occluded at the same time.

{ Permanent occlusion is performed following punctal
dilatation by coagulating the proximal canaliculus with
cautery. Following successful occlusion, it is important to
watch for signs of recanalization.

{ Laser cautery seems to be less consistently e¯Ç£ective than
surgical thermal coagulation.

TIP Punctal occlusion with a silicone plug can be a useful
therapeutic option in patients with moderateÔÇôsevere
keratoconjunctivitis sicca.

Anti-in¯Ç×ammatory agents

¯Çü Topical steroids, generally low-intensity preparations such
as ¯Ç×uorometholone are e¯Ç£ective supplementary treatment for
acute exacerbations. ¯ÇØe risks of longer-term treatment must
be balanced against the potential bene¯Çƒts in each case.
¯Çü Omega fatty acid supplements (e.g. omega-3 ¯Çƒsh oil, ¯Ç×ax seed
oil) can have a dramatic e¯Ç£ect on symptoms and may facilitate
the reduction of topical medication.
¯Çü Oral tetracyclines for an extended course, o¯Çøen 3 months at
a relatively low dose, may control associated blepharitis, especially meibomianitis and reduce tear levels of in¯Ç×ammatory
mediators. Doxycycline may be preferred to minocycline on
the grounds of adverse e¯Ç£ect pro¯Çƒle.
¯Çü Topical ciclosporin (usually 0.05%) reduces T-cell-mediated
in¯Ç×ammation of lacrimal tissue, resulting in an increase in the
number of goblet cells and reversal of squamous metaplasia of
the conjunctiva.

## Contact lenses

Although contact lens wear can exacerbate dry eye, particularly
due to in¯Ç×ammatory, sensory and evaporative e¯Ç£ects, the symptoms can be outweighed by the reservoir e¯Ç£ect of ¯Ç×uid trapped

behind the lens. In addition, contact lenses are e¯Ç£ective at relieving symptoms resulting from secondary corneal changes. However, patients should be cautioned that there is an increased risk
of bacterial keratitis.
Several manufacturers have developed contact lenses speci¯Çƒcally designed to reduce dry eye discomfort during lens wear. ¯ÇØe
materials used (silicone hydrogel) retain moisture for 12ÔÇô16 hours
and are single-use daily disposable. Occlusive gas-permeable
scleral contact lenses provide a reservoir of saline over the cornea
and are occasionally used in a patient with an extremely dry eye
and exposure. Silicone rubber lenses, which were used in the past,
are no longer available.

## Optimization of environmental humidity

¯Çü Reduction of room temperature to minimize evaporation of
tears.
¯Çü Room humidi¯Ç×ers may be tried but are frequently disappointing because much apparatus is incapable of signi¯Çƒcantly
increasing the relative humidity of an average-sized room. A
temporary local increase in humidity can be achieved with
moist chamber goggles or side shields to glasses but may be
cosmetically unacceptable.

## Miscellaneous options

¯Çü Botulinum toxin injection to the orbicularis muscle may help
control the blepharospasm that o¯Çøen occurs in severe dry eye.
Injected at the medial canthus it can also reduce tear drainage,
presumably by limiting lid movement.
¯Çü Oral cholinergic agonists such as pilocarpine (5 mg four times
daily) and cevimeline may reduce the symptoms of dry eye and
dry mouth in patients with Sj├Âgren syndrome. Adverse e¯Ç£ects
including blurred vision and sweating may be less marked with
cevimeline.
¯Çü Submandibular gland transplantation for extreme dry eye
requires extensive surgery and may produce excessive levels of
mucus in the tear ¯Çƒlm.

168 Treatment

¯Çü Serum/autologous blood eye drops. Autologous or umbilical
cord serum (20ÔÇô100%), the blood component remaining a¯Çøer
clotting, has produced subjective and objective improvements
in studies in patients with dry eye and may aid the healing of
persistent epithelial defects. However, their production and
storage are associated with practical challenges. Finger prick
autologous blood is a low cost, readily accessible and practical
treatment that appears to be safe and e¯Ç£ective.

¯Çü Cenegermin 0.002%, a topical recombinant human nerve
growth factor (registered for use in neurotrophic keratopathy)
may have a role to play in severe Sj├Âgren disease. ¯ÇØe safety
and e¯ÇÜcacy are being investigated.
¯Çü Photobiomodulation therapy applied to the eyelids. Low
level light, red light and intense pulsed light are relatively new
interventional options that may be helpful in the treatment of
dry eye secondary to severe meibomian gland dysfunction.

170 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

## Anatomy

¯Çƒe conjunctiva is a transparent mucous membrane that lines the
inner surface of the eyelids and the anterior surface of the globe,
terminating at the corneoscleral limbus. It is richly vascular, supplied by the anterior ciliary and palpebral arteries. ¯Çƒere is a dense
lymphatic network, with drainage to the preauricular and submandibular nodes corresponding to that of the eyelids. It has a key
protective role, mediating both passive and active immunity. Anatomically, it is divided into the following:
¯Çü ¯Çƒe palpebral conjunctiva starts at the mucocutaneous junction of the lid margins and is ¯Ç×rmly attached to the posterior
tarsal plates. ¯Çƒe tarsal blood vessels are vertically orientated.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe forniceal conjunctiva is loose and redundant.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe bulbar conjunctiva covers the anterior sclera and is continuous with the corneal epithelium at the limbus. Radial
ridges at the limbus form the palisades of Vogt, the likely reservoir of corneal stem cells. ¯Çƒe stroma is loosely attached to the
underlying Tenon capsule, except at the limbus, where the two
layers fuse. ¯Çƒe plica semilunaris (semilunar fold) is present
nasally, medial to which lies a ¯ÇØeshy nodule (caruncle) consisting of modi¯Ç×ed cutaneous tissue.

## Histology

¯Çü ¯Çƒe epithelium is non-keratinizing and around ¯Ç×ve cell layers
deep (Fig. 6.1). Basal cuboidal cells evolve into ¯ÇØattened polyhedral cells, subsequently being shed from the surface. Mucussecreting goblet cells are located within the epithelium, being
most dense inferonasally and in the fornices.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe stroma (substantia propria) consists of richly vascularized loose connective tissue. ¯Çƒe accessory lacrimal glands
of Krause and Wolfring are located deep within the stroma.

## Secretions from the accessory lacrimal glands are essential

components of the tear ¯Ç×lm.

¯Çü Conjunctiva-associated lymphoid tissue (CALT) is critical in the initiation and regulation of ocular surface immune
responses. It consists of lymphocytes within the epithelial layers, lymphatics and associated blood vessels, with a stromal
component of lymphocytes and plasma cells, including follicular aggregates.

## Clinical features of conjunctival

in¯Çƒammation

## Symptoms

Non-speci¯Ç×c symptoms include lacrimation, grittiness, stinging
and burning. Itching is the hallmark of allergic disease, although
it may also occur to a lesser extent in blepharitis and in patients
with dry eye syndrome. ¯Çƒe visual acuity is not usually a¯Ç£ected.
Signi¯Ç×cant pain, photophobia or a marked foreign body sensation
suggest corneal involvement.

## Discharge

¯Çü Watery discharge is composed of a serous exudate and tears
and occurs in acute viral or acute allergic conjunctivitis.
¯Çü Mucoid discharge is typical of chronic allergic conjunctivitis
and dry eye.
¯Çü Mucopurulent discharge typically occurs in chlamydial or
acute bacterial infection.
¯Çü Moderately purulent discharge occurs in acute bacterial
conjunctivitis.
¯Çü Severe purulent discharge is suggestive of gonococcal
infection.

## Conjunctival reaction

¯Çü Hyperaemia that is di¯Ç£use, beefy-red and more intense away
from the limbus is usual in bacterial infection (Fig. 6.2A). ¯Çƒis
ÔÇÿconjunctival injectionÔÇÖ should be distinguished from the ciliary injection of iridocyclitis (see Ch. 12).
¯Çü Haemorrhages may occur in viral conjunctivitis, when they are
o¯Çøen multiple, small and discrete (ÔÇÿpetechialÔÇÖ) and severe bacterial conjunctivitis, when they are larger and di¯Ç£use (Fig. 6.2B).
¯Çü Chemosis (conjunctival oedema) is seen as a translucent
swelling (Fig. 6.2C), which may protrude through the eyelids.

## Acute chemosis usually indicates a hypersensitivity response

(e.g. pollen), but can also occur in severe infective conjunctivitis. Subacute or chronic chemosis has numerous causes:

{ Local, e.g. thyroid eye disease, chronic allergic conjunctivitis, ocular or eyelid surgery, trauma.

{ Increased systemic vascular permeability, e.g. allergic conditions, infections including meningitis, vasculitis.

{ Increased venous pressure, e.g. superior vena cava syndrome, right-sided heart failure.

{ Decreased plasma oncotic pressure, e.g. nephrotic
syndrome.
¯Çü Membranes

{ Pseudomembranes (Fig. 6.2D) consist of coagulated exudate adherent to the in¯ÇØamed conjunctival epithelium. ¯Çƒey

BACTERIAL CONJUNCTIVITIS

## Acute bacterial conjunctivitis

Acute bacterial conjunctivitis is a common and usually selflimiting condition caused by direct contact with infected secretions. The most common isolates are Streptococcus pneumoniae,
Staphylococcus aureus, Haemophilus influenzae and Moraxella
catarrhalis. A minority of severe cases are caused by the sexually
transmitted organism Neisseria gonorrhoeae, which can readily
invade the intact corneal epithelium. Meningococcal (Neisseria
meningitidis) conjunctivitis is rare and usually affects children.

## Diagnosis

## Lymphadenopathy

¯Çƒe most common cause of lymphadenopathy associated with
conjunctivitis is viral infection. It may also occur in chlamydial
and severe bacterial conjunctivitis (especially gonococcal) and
Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome. ¯Çƒe preauricular site is typically a¯Ç£ected.

TIP Viral conjunctivitis tends to occur in epidemics and
commonly causes preauricular lymphadenopathy.

¯Çü Symptoms

{ Acute onset of redness, grittiness, burning and discharge.

{ Involvement is usually bilateral although one eye may
become a¯Ç£ected 1ÔÇô2 days before the other (Fig. 6.4A).

{ On waking, the eyelids are frequently stuck together and
may be di¯ÇÜcult to open.

A B

C

D

discharge; (D) profuse purulent discharge secondary to gonococcus.

174 Bacterial Conjunctivitis

{ Systemic symptoms may occur in patients with severe conjunctivitis associated with gonococcus, meningococcus,
Chlamydia and H. in¯Çƒuenzae. In children, the possibility
of progression to systemic involvement should always be
borne in mind.
¯Çü Signs are variable and depend on the severity of infection.

{ Some practitioners, particularly in the USA, believe that
chloramphenicol should not be used for routine treatment
because of a possible link with aplastic anaemia.

{ ¯Çƒe vision is usually normal.

{ Eyelid oedema and erythema may occur in severe infection, particularly gonococcal.

{ Conjunctival injection as previously described (Fig. 6.4B
and see Fig. 6.2A).

{ ¯Çƒe discharge can initially be watery, mimicking viral conjunctivitis, but rapidly becomes mucopurulent (Fig. 6.4C).

{ Hyperacute purulent discharge (Fig. 6.4D) may signify
gonococcal or meningococcal conjunctivitis.

{ Super¯Ç×cial corneal punctate epithelial erosions are

{ Peripheral corneal ulceration may occur in gonococcal

{ Lymphadenopathy is usually absent except in severe gonococcal and meningococcal infection.

must be sought, but if in doubt treatment with intramuscular benzylpenicillin, ce¯Çøriaxone or cefotaxime, or oral
cipro¯ÇØoxacin should not be delayed.

TIP A hyperacute purulent ocular discharge may be a sign
of gonococcal or meningococcal conjunctivitis. A specimen
should be taken for microscopy, culture and sensitivity (MCS) in
order to prescribe an appropriate systemic antibiotic.

## Treatment

About 60% resolve within 5 days without treatment.
¯Çü Topical antibiotics, usually four times daily for up to a week
but sometimes more intensively, are frequently administered
to speed recovery and prevent re-infection and transmission.
¯Çƒere is no evidence that any particular antibiotic is more
e¯Ç£ective. Ointments and gels provide a higher concentration
for longer periods than drops, but should be avoided during
the day because blurred vision may follow. ¯Çƒe following antibiotics are available:

{ Chloramphenicol, aminoglycosides (gentamicin, neomycin, tobramycin), quinolones (cipro¯ÇØoxacin, o¯ÇØoxacin,
levo¯ÇØoxacin, lome¯ÇØoxacin, gati¯ÇØoxacin, moxi¯ÇØoxacin,
besi¯ÇØoxacin), macrolides (erythromycin, azithromycin)
polymyxin B, fusidic acid and bacitracin.

## Giant fornix syndrome

## Giant fornix syndrome is an uncommon entity causing chronic

relapsing pseudomembranous purulent conjunctivitis. It is
believed to be due to retained debris in a voluminous upper fornix
acting as a focus for persistent bacterial colonization (usually S.
aureus) in an elderly patient with levator disinsertion. Large protein aggregations may be visualized in the upper fornix, though
double eversion with a retractor may be necessary to identify
these. Secondary corneal vascularization and lacrimal obstruction are common. It is frequently unilateral. Treatment involves
repeated sweeping of the fornix with a cotton-tipped applicator
and topical and systemic antibiotics; intensive topical steroid may
be helpful. Surgical reconstruction of the fornix may be necessary
in recalcitrant cases.

## Adult chlamydial conjunctivitis

## Pathogenesis

Chlamydia trachomatis (Fig. 6.5) is a species of Chlamydiae, a
phylum of bacteria that cannot replicate extracellularly and hence
depends on host cells. ¯Çƒey exist in two principal forms: a robust
infective extracellular ÔÇÿelementary bodyÔÇÖ and a fragile intracellular replicating ÔÇÿreticular bodyÔÇÖ. Adult chlamydial (inclusion) conjunctivitis is an oculogenital infection usually caused by serovars
(serological variants) DÔÇôK of C. trachomatis and a¯Ç£ects 5ÔÇô20% of
sexually active young adults in Western countries. Transmission
is by autoinoculation from genital secretions, although eye-to-eye
spread probably accounts for about 10%. ¯Çƒe incubation period is
approximately a week.

## Urogenital infection

¯Çü In males, chlamydial infection is the most common cause of
non-gonococcal urethritis (NGU), also termed non-speci¯Ç×c
urethritis (NSU). It should be noted that the latter term is also
sometimes used to mean urethritis in which both gonococcal and chlamydial infection have been ruled out. Chlamydial
urethritis is frequently asymptomatic in men. C. trachomatis
may also cause epididymitis and can act as a trigger for Reiter
syndrome.
¯Çü In females, chlamydial urethritis typically causes dysuria and
discharge. It may progress to pelvic in¯ÇØammatory disease
(PID), carrying a risk of infertility. Approximately 5ÔÇô10% of
women with PID develop perihepatitis (Fitz-HughÔÇôCurtis
syndrome).

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms consist of the subacute onset of unilateral or bilateral redness, watering and discharge. Untreated, the conjunctivitis becomes chronic and though self-limiting may persist for
several months. It is important to enquire about sexual exposure if chlamydial conjunctivitis is suspected.

conjunctivitis) agents venereum) agents

Empirical treatment may be given if the clinical picture is convincing pending investigation results.
¯Çü Referral to a genitourinary specialist is mandatory in con¯Ç×rmed cases, particularly for the exclusion of other sexually transmitted infections, contact tracing and pregnancy
testing.

required in up to 30% of cases. Some guidelines advocate

## Trachoma

## Pathogenesis

Trachoma is the worldÔÇÖs leading cause of preventable irreversible
blindness. It is related to poverty, overcrowding and poor hygiene,
the morbidity being a consequence of the establishment of reinfection cycles within communities. Whereas an isolated episode
of trachomatous conjunctivitis may be relatively innocuous, recurrent infection elicits a chronic immune response consisting of a
cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity (Type IV) reaction to the
intermittent presence of chlamydial antigen and can lead to loss of
sight. Prior contact with the organism confers short-term partial
immunity but also leads to a heightened in¯ÇØammatory reaction
upon re-infection. Vaccination has an e¯Ç£ect similar to primary

infection in sensitizing the individual and as a consequence is
not helpful. ¯Çƒe family childcare group is the most important reinfection reservoir and young children are particularly vulnerable.
¯Çƒe ¯ÇØy is an important vector, but there may be direct transmission
from eye or nasal discharge. Trachoma is associated principally
with infection by serovars A, B, Ba and C of Chlamydia trachomatis, but the serovars DÔÇôK conventionally associated with adult
inclusion conjunctivitis and other species of the Chlamydiaceae
family such as Chlamydophila psittaci and Chlamydophila pneumoniae have also been implicated.

## Diagnosis

Features of trachoma are divided into an ÔÇÿactiveÔÇÖ in¯ÇØammatory
stage and a ÔÇÿcicatricialÔÇÖ chronic stage, with considerable overlap.
A World Health Organization (WHO) grading system is in use
(Table 6.1).
¯Çü Active trachoma is most common in pre-school children and
is characterized by the following:

{ Mixed follicular/papillary conjunctivitis (Fig. 6.7A) associated with a mucopurulent discharge. In children under the
age of 2 years the papillary component may predominate.

¯Çü Surgery is aimed at relieving entropion and trichiasis and
maintaining complete lid closure, principally with bilamellar
tarsal rotation.

TIP Systemic tetracyclines should not be used in pregnancy
and in children under 12 years of age because of the risk of
tooth staining.

## Neonatal conjunctivitis

Neonatal conjunctivitis (ophthalmia neonatorum) is de¯Ç×ned as conjunctival in¯ÇØammation developing within the ¯Ç×rst month of life. It
is the most common infection of any kind in neonates, occurring in
up to 10%. It is identi¯Ç×ed as a speci¯Ç×c entity distinct from conjunctivitis in older infants because of its potentially serious nature (both
ocular and systemic complications) and because it is o¯Çøen the result
of infection transmitted from mother to infant during delivery.

## Causes

¯Çü Organisms acquired during vaginal delivery: C. trachomatis,
N. gonorrhoeae (now rare in wealthier countries, but
previously responsible for 25% of childhood blindness) and
herpes simplex virus (typically HSV-2). With all of these, conjunctivitis is not uncommonly associated with severe ocular
or systemic complications. C. trachomatis is the most -common cause in cases involving moderateÔÇôsevere conjunctival
in¯ÇØammation.
¯Çü Staphylococci are usually responsible for mild conjunctivitis. Other bacterial causes include streptococci, H. in¯Çƒuenzae
and various Gram-negative organisms.
¯Çü Topical preparations used as prophylaxis against infection
(see below) may themselves cause conjunctival irritation
(chemical conjunctivitis).
¯Çü Congenital nasolacrimal obstruction. Despite poor neonatal tear production, a persistently mildly watery eye with
recurrent mild bacterial conjunctivitis may be secondary to a
blocked tear duct.

## Diagnosis

{ Severe corneal opaci¯Ç×cation.

{ Dry eye caused by destruction of goblet cells and the ductules of the lacrimal gland.
¯Çü Investigations are rarely used in the a¯Ç£ected areas as in most
cases the diagnosis can be made based on the clinical features.
Various ¯Ç×eld techniques (e.g. dipstick enzyme immunoassay)
are available and other investigations are similar to those for
adult inclusion conjunctivitis.

## Management

¯Çƒe SAFE strategy for trachoma management supported by the
WHO and other agencies encompasses Surgery for trichiasis,
Antibiotics for active disease, Facial hygiene and Environmental
improvement.
¯Çü Antibiotics should be administered to those a¯Ç£ected and to all
family members. A single antibiotic course is not always e¯Ç£ective in eliminating infection in an individual and communities
may need to receive annual treatment to suppress infection.

{ Gonococcal: ¯Ç×rst week.

{ Staphylococci and other bacteria: end of the ¯Ç×rst week.

treatment of choice.

{ Erythromycin 500 mg twice daily for 14 days or doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10 days (tetracyclines are relatively contraindicated in pregnancy/breastfeeding and in
children under 12).

{ Chlamydia: 1ÔÇô3 weeks.

180 Viral Conjunctivitis

¯Çü Herpes simplex infection should always be regarded as a
systemic condition and is treated with high-dose intravenous
aciclovir under paediatric specialist care. Early diagnosis and
treatment of encephalitis (PCR of cerebrospinal ¯ÇØuid (CSF) is
positive in 95%) may be life-saving or prevent serious neurological disability. Topical aciclovir may be considered in addition.
¯Çü Microbiological advice should be sought in severe cases, especially regarding local antibiotic sensitivities.
¯Çü Paediatric specialist involvement is mandatory when systemic
disease may be present.
¯Çü Genitourinary referral for the mother and her sexual contacts
is important when an STI is diagnosed. ¯Çƒe neonate should be
screened for other STIs.
¯Çü Noti¯Ç×cation of a case of neonatal conjunctivitis to the local public health authority is a statutory requirement in many countries.

VIRAL CONJUNCTIVITIS

## Introduction

Viral conjunctivitis is a common external ocular infection, adenovirus (a non-enveloped double-stranded DNA virus) being
the most frequent (90%) causative agent. It may be sporadic, or
occur in epidemics in environments such as workplaces (including
hospitals), schools and swimming pools. ¯Çƒe spread of this highly
contagious infection is facilitated by the ability of viral particles to
survive on dry surfaces for weeks and by the fact that viral shedding may occur for many days before clinical features are apparent. Transmission is generally by contact with respiratory or ocular
secretions, including via fomites such as contaminated towels.

TIP Viral conjunctivitis is highly contagious and great care
should be taken to prevent transmission of the disease.

## Presentation

¯Çƒe spectrum of viral conjunctivitis varies from mild subclinical
disease to severe in¯ÇØammation with signi¯Ç×cant morbidity. ¯Çƒere
will o¯Çøen be a history of a close contact with acute conjunctivitis.
¯Çü Non-speci¯Ç×c acute follicular conjunctivitis is the most common clinical form of viral conjunctivitis and is typically due to
adenoviral infection by a range of serological variants. Unilateral watering, redness, irritation and/or itching and mild photophobia occur, the contralateral eye generally being a¯Ç£ected
1ÔÇô2 days later, o¯Çøen less severely. ¯Çƒe condition is usually
milder than the other clinical forms of adenoviral conjunctivitis. Patients may have accompanying (usually mild) systemic
symptoms, such as a sore throat or common cold.
¯Çü Pharyngoconjunctival fever (PCF) is caused mainly by
adenovirus serovars 3, 4 and 7. It is spread by droplets within
families with upper respiratory tract infection. Keratitis develops in about 30% of cases but is seldom severe. Symptoms are
essentially as above, though sore throat is typically prominent.
¯Çü Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (EKC) is caused mainly by
adenovirus serovars 8, 19 and 37 and is the most severe ocular
adenoviral infection. Keratitis, which may be marked, develops
in about 80%. Photophobia may be correspondingly prominent.

¯Çü Acute haemorrhagic conjunctivitis usually occurs in tropical
areas. It is typically caused by enterovirus and coxsackievirus,
though other microorganisms may present similarly. It has
a rapid onset and resolves within 1ÔÇô2 weeks. Conjunctival
haemorrhage is generally marked.
¯Çü Chronic/relapsing adenoviral conjunctivitis giving
a chronic non-specific follicular/papillary clinical picture can persist over years, but is rare and eventually
self-limiting.
¯Çü Herpes simplex virus (HSV) can cause a follicular conjunctivitis, particularly in primary infection. ¯Çƒis is usually unilateral and there are o¯Çøen associated skin vesicles.
¯Çü Systemic viral infections such as those common in childhood, e.g. varicella, measles and mumps, can feature an associated follicular conjunctivitis. Varicella-zoster virus secondary
infection commonly causes conjunctivitis as part of ophthalmic shingles. Conjunctivitis secondary to HIV infection is
recognized.
¯Çü Molluscum contagiosum is a skin infection caused by a
human speci¯Ç×c double-stranded DNA poxvirus that typically a¯Ç£ects otherwise healthy children, with a peak incidence
between the ages of 2 and 4 years. Transmission is by contact,
with subsequent autoinoculation. A chronic follicular conjunctivitis can be associated and is due to skin lesion shedding
of viral particles. Chronic unilateral ocular irritation and mild
discharge is typical. ¯Çƒe eyelash line should be examined carefully in patients with chronic conjunctivitis so as not to overlook a molluscum lesion.
¯Çü SARS-CoV-2 virus, responsible for the COVID-19 pandemic, can lead to multi-organ involvement, including the
eye. Viral RNA can be isolated in the tears of up to 25%
of patients with laboratory-proven moderate or severe
COVID-19 infection. Conjunctivitis is the most commonly reported ocular manifestation and results in hyperaemia, chemosis and epiphora. ¯Çƒe infectious potential and
risk of transmission from tears or ocular discharge is not
known. Other ocular consequences of the virus include retinal microangiopathy (common) and optic neuritis (rare).
COVID-19 vaccination generally does not increase the risk
of ocular adverse events, except in some patients with preexisting uveitis where reactivation of ocular in¯ÇØammation
may follow.

## Signs

¯Çü Eyelid oedema ranges from negligible to severe.
¯Çü Lymphadenopathy is common: tender preauricular.
¯Çü Conjunctival hyperaemia and follicles are typically prominent. Papillae may also be seen, particularly in the superior
tarsal conjunctiva.
¯Çü Severe in¯ÇØammation may be associated with conjunctival
haemorrhages (Fig. 6.9A) (usually petechial in adenoviral
infection), chemosis, membranes (rare) and pseudomembranes
(Fig. 6.9B), sometimes with conjunctival scarring a¯Çøer resolution (Fig. 6.9C).
¯Çü Keratitis (adenoviral):

{ Epithelial microcysts (non-staining) are common at an
early stage.

A B

C

D

haemorrhage; (B) pseudomembrane; (C) residual scarring; (D) subepithelial in¯Ç×ltrates.
(Courtesy of S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs B and C.)

{ Punctate epithelial keratitis (staining) may occur, usually within 7ÔÇô10 days of the onset of symptoms, typically
resolving within 2 weeks.

{ Focal white subepithelial/anterior stromal in¯Ç×ltrates (Fig.
6.9D) o¯Çøen develop beneath the fading epithelial lesions,
probably as an immune response to the virus. ¯Çƒey may
persist or recur over months or years.

{ Small pseudodendritic epithelial formations sometimes
occur.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis is sometimes present, but is mild.
¯Çü Molluscum contagiosum

{ A pale, waxy, umbilicated nodule on the lid margin (Fig.
6.10A) associated with follicular conjunctivitis (Fig. 6.10B)
and mild watery and mucoid discharge.

{ Bulbar nodules and con¯ÇØuent cutaneous lesions may occur
in immunocompromised patients.

¯Çü Giemsa stain shows predominantly mononuclear cells in
adenoviral conjunctivitis and multinucleated giant cells in
herpetic infection.
¯Çü Nucleic acid ampli¯Ç×cation techniques such as PCR are sensitive and speci¯Ç×c for viral DNA.
¯Çü Viral culture with isolation is the reference standard but is
expensive and fairly slow (days to weeks) and requires speci¯Ç×c
transport media. Sensitivity is variable but speci¯Ç×city is around
100%.
¯Çü A ÔÇÿpoint-of-careÔÇÖ immunochromatography test takes 10 minutes to detect adenoviral antigen in tears; sensitivity and speci¯Ç×city are excellent.
¯Çü Serology for IgM or rising IgG antibody titres to adenovirus
has limitations and is rarely used.
¯Çü Investigation for other causes such as chlamydial infection
may be indicated in non-resolving cases.

## Treatment

¯Çƒe treatment of herpetic ocular surface disease is addressed in

## Investigation

Investigation is generally unnecessary, but should be considered if
the diagnosis is in doubt or there is failure of resolution.

182 Allergic Conjunctivitis

A B

lesion (arrow).

¯Çü Spontaneous resolution of adenoviral infection usually
occurs within 2ÔÇô3 weeks, so speci¯Ç×c treatment is typically
unnecessary. No antiviral agent with clinically useful activity
against adenovirus has yet been produced.
¯Çü Reduction of transmission risk by meticulous hand hygiene,
avoiding eye rubbing and towel sharing. ¯Çƒere should be scrupulous disinfection of instruments and clinical surfaces a¯Çøer
examination of an infected patient (e.g. sodium hypochlorite,
povidone-iodine).
¯Çü Molluscum contagiosum. Although lesions are self-limiting
in immunocompetent patients, removal is o¯Çøen necessary
to address secondary conjunctivitis or for cosmetic reasons.

## Expression is facilitated by making a small nick in the skin at

the margin of the lesion with the tip of a needle.
¯Çü Topical steroids such as prednisolone 0.5%four times daily may
be required for severe membranous or pseudomembranous
adenoviral conjunctivitis. Symptomatic keratitis may require
weak topical steroids but these should be used with caution as
they do not speed resolution but only suppress in¯ÇØammation
and lesions commonly recur a¯Çøer premature discontinuation.

## Steroids may enhance viral replication and extend the period

during which the patient remains infectious. Intraocular pressure should be monitored if treatment is prolonged.
¯Çü Other measures

{ Discontinuation of contact lens wear until resolution of
symptoms.

{ Arti¯Ç×cial tears four times daily may be useful for symptomatic relief. Preservative-free preparations may give
superior comfort and if supplied in single-dose units may
reduce transmission risk.

{ Cold (or warm) compresses for symptomatic relief.

{ Topical antihistamines and vasoconstrictors may improve
symptoms, particularly itching.

{ ¯Çƒe place of non-steroidal anti-in¯ÇØammatory drops is not
well established, but may be e¯Ç£ective in some circumstances such as steroid weaning. ¯Çƒey are not thought to
promote viral replication.

{ Removal of symptomatic pseudomembranes or
membranes.

{ Topical antibiotics if secondary bacterial infection is
suspected.

{ Povidone-iodine is very e¯Ç£ective against free (although
less so against intracellular) adenovirus and has been proposed as a means of decreasing infectivity.

ALLERGIC CONJUNCTIVITIS

Atopy is a genetically determined predisposition to hypersensitivity reactions upon exposure to speci¯Ç×c environmental antigens.
Clinical manifestations include the various forms of allergic conjunctivitis, as well as hay fever (seasonal allergic rhinitis), asthma
and eczema. Allergic conjunctivitis is a type I (immediate) hypersensitivity reaction, mediated by degranulation of mast cells in
response to the action of IgE. ¯Çƒere is evidence of an element of
type IV hypersensitivity in at least some forms.

## Acute allergic conjunctivitis

## Acute allergic conjunctivitis is a common condition caused by an

acute conjunctival reaction to an environmental allergen, usually
pollen. It is typically seen in younger children a¯Çøer playing outside in
spring or summer. Acute itching and watering are common, but the
hallmark is chemosis (Fig. 6.11), which is frequently dramatic and
worrying to the child and parents. Treatment is not usually required
and the conjunctival swelling settles within hours as the acute increase
in vascular permeability resolves. Cool compresses can be used and a
single drop of adrenaline 0.1% may reduce extreme chemosis.

## Seasonal and perennial allergic
conjunctivitis

¯Çƒese common subacute conditions are distinguished from each
other by the timing of exacerbations, thought to relate principally
to di¯Ç£ering stimulating allergens in each.

A B

¯Çü Seasonal allergic conjunctivitis (ÔÇÿhay fever eyesÔÇÖ), worse during the spring and summer, is the more common. ¯Çƒe most
frequent allergens are tree and grass pollens, although the speci¯Ç×c allergen varies with geographic location.
¯Çü Perennial allergic conjunctivitis causes symptoms throughout
the year, generally worse in the autumn when exposure to house
dust mites, animal dander and fungal allergens is greatest. It is
less common and tends to be milder than the seasonal form.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Transient acute or subacute attacks of redness,
watering and itching, associated with sneezing and nasal
discharge.
¯Çü Signs. Normal vision. Conjunctival hyperaemia with a relatively mild papillary reaction, variable chemosis and lid
oedema.
¯Çü Investigations are generally not performed although conjunctival scraping in more active cases may demonstrate the
presence of eosinophils. Skin testing for particular allergens is
rarely required.

## Treatment

¯Çü Arti¯Ç×cial tears for mild symptoms.
¯Çü Mast cell stabilizers (e.g. sodium cromoglicate, nedocromil
sodium, lodoxamide) must be used for a few days before exerting maximal e¯Ç£ect, but are suitable (except lodoxamide) for
long-term use if required.
¯Çü Antihistamines (e.g. emedastine, epinastine, levocabastine,
bepotastine) can be used for symptomatic exacerbations and
are as e¯Ç£ective as mast cell stabilizers.
¯Çü Dual action antihistamine and mast cell stabilizers (e.g.
azelastine, ketotifen, olopatadine) act rapidly and are o¯Çøen
very e¯Ç£ective for exacerbations.
¯Çü Combined preparation of an antihistamine and a vasoconstrictor (e.g. antazoline with xylometazoline).
¯Çü Non-steroidal anti-in¯ÇØammatory preparations (e.g. diclofenac) can provide symptomatic relief but are rarely used.

¯Çü Topical steroids are e¯Ç£ective but rarely necessary.
¯Çü Oral antihistamines may be indicated for severe symptoms.
Some, such as diphenhydramine, cause signi¯Ç×cant drowsiness
and may be useful in aiding sleep. Others, such as loratadine,
have a far less marked sedative action.

## Vernal keratoconjunctivitis

## Pathogenesis

Vernal keratoconjunctivitis (VKC) is a recurrent bilateral disorder
in which both IgE- and cell-mediated immune mechanisms play
important roles. It primarily a¯Ç£ects boys and onset is generally from
about the age of 5 years onwards. ¯Çƒere is remission by the late teens
in 95% of cases, although many of the remainder develop atopic
keratoconjunctivitis. VKC is rare in temperate regions, but relatively
common in warm dry climates such as the Mediterranean, subSaharan Africa and the Middle East. In temperate regions over 90%
of patients have other atopic conditions such as asthma and eczema
and two-thirds have a family history of atopy. VKC o¯Çøen occurs on
a seasonal basis, with a peak incidence over late spring and summer,
although there may be mild perennial symptoms.

Classi¯Çƒcation

¯Çü Palpebral VKC primarily involves the upper tarsal conjunctiva. It may be associated with signi¯Ç×cant corneal disease as a
result of the close apposition between the in¯ÇØamed conjunctiva and the corneal epithelium.
¯Çü Limbal disease typically a¯Ç£ects Black and Asian patients.
¯Çü Mixed VKC has features of both palpebral and limbal disease.

## Diagnosis

¯Çƒe diagnosis is clinical and investigations are usually not required.
Eosinophils may be abundant in conjunctival scrapings.
¯Çü Symptoms consist of intense itching, which may be associated
with lacrimation, photophobia, a foreign body sensation, burning and thick mucoid discharge. Increased blinking is common.

184 Allergic Conjunctivitis

¯Çü Palpebral disease

{ Early-mild disease is characterized by conjunctival hyperaemia and di¯Ç£use velvety papillary hypertrophy on the
superior tarsal plate.

{ Macropapillae (<1 mm) have a ¯ÇØat-topped polygonal
appearance reminiscent of cobblestones. Focal (Fig. 6.12A)
or di¯Ç£use (Fig. 6.12B) whitish in¯ÇØammatory in¯Ç×ltrates
may be seen in intense disease.

{ Progression to giant papillae (>1 mm) can occur, as adjacent smaller lesions amalgamate when dividing septa rupture (Fig. 6.12C).

{ Mucus deposition between giant papillae (Fig. 6.12D).

{ Decreased disease activity is characterized by milder conjunctival injection and decreased mucus production.
¯Çü Limbal disease

{ Gelatinous limbal conjunctival papillae that may be associated with transient apically located white cellular collections (HornerÔÇôTrantas dots ÔÇô Fig. 6.12E and F).

following a long history of atopic dermatitis (eczema). Asthma is
also extremely common in these patients. About 5% have su¯Ç£ered
from childhood VKC. ¯Çƒere is little or no gender preponderance.
AKC tends to be chronic and unremitting, with a relatively low
expectation of eventual resolution and is associated with signi¯Ç×cant visual morbidity. Whereas VKC is more frequently seasonal
and generally worse in the spring, AKC tends to be perennial and
is o¯Çøen worse in the winter. Patients are sensitive to a wide range
of airborne environmental allergens.

## Diagnosis

¯Çƒe distinction between AKC and VKC can be made clinically. Eosinophils tend to be less common in conjunctival scrapings than with VKC.
¯Çü Symptoms are similar to those of VKC, but are frequently
more severe and unremitting.
¯Çü Eyelids

{ In tropical regions, limbal disease may be severe (Fig. 6.12
G and H).
¯Çü Keratopathy is more frequent in palpebral disease and may
take the following forms:

{ Superior punctate epithelial erosions associated with layers
of mucus on the superior cornea (Fig. 6.13A).

{ Epithelial macroerosions caused by a combination of epithelial toxicity from in¯ÇØammatory mediators and a direct
mechanical e¯Ç£ect from papillae.

{ Plaques and ÔÇÿshieldÔÇÖ ulcers (Fig. 6.13B and C) may develop
in palpebral or mixed disease when the exposed Bowman membrane becomes coated with mucus and calcium
phosphate, leading to inadequate wetting and delayed reepithelialization. ¯Çƒis development is serious and warrants
urgent attention to prevent secondary bacterial infection.

{ Subepithelial scars that are typically grey and oval and may
a¯Ç£ect vision.

{ Pseudogerontoxon can develop in recurrent limbal disease. It is characterized by a paralimbal band of super¯Ç×cial
scarring resembling arcus senilis (Fig. 6.13D), adjacent to
a previously in¯ÇØamed segment of the limbus.

{ Vascularization does not tend to be prominent, though
some peripheral super¯Ç×cial vessel ingrowth is common,
especially superiorly.

{ Keratoconus and other forms of corneal ectasia are more
common in VKC and are thought to be at least partly due
to persistent eye rubbing.

{ Limbal involvement similar to that of limbal VKC can be
seen, including HornerÔÇôTrantas dots.
¯Çü Keratopathy

{ Skin changes (Fig. 6.14A) are more prominent than in
VKC and are typically eczematoid: erythema, dryness,
scaling and thickening, sometimes with disruption to epidermal integrity such as ¯Ç×ssuring and scratches (excoriation), the latter due to intense itching.

{ Associated chronic staphylococcal blepharitis and madarosis are common.

{ ¯Çƒere may be keratinization of the lid margin.

{ Hertoghe sign: absence of the lateral portion of the
eyebrows.

{ DennieÔÇôMorgan folds: lid skin folds caused by persistent
rubbing.

{ Tightening of the facial skin may cause lower lid ectropion
and epiphora.

{ Ptosis is not uncommon.
¯Çü Conjunctival involvement is preferentially inferior palpebral,
whereas in VKC it is worse superiorly.

{ Discharge is generally more watery than the stringy
mucoid discharge in VKC.

{ Hyperaemia; chemosis is not uncommon during active
in¯ÇØammation.

{ Papillae are initially smaller than in VKC although larger
lesions may develop later.

{ Di¯Ç£use conjunctival in¯Ç×ltration and scarring may give a
whitish, featureless appearance (Fig. 6.14B).

{ Cicatricial changes can lead to moderate symblepharon
formation, forniceal shortening (Fig. 6.14C) and keratinization of the caruncle (Fig. 6.14D).

{ Herpes simplex keratitis is more common than average,
though less so than in atopic keratoconjunctivitis. It can be
aggressive and is occasionally bilateral.
¯Çü Eyelid disease is usually mild, in contrast to atopic
keratoconjunctivitis.

## Atopic keratoconjunctivitis

## Pathogenesis

Atopic keratoconjunctivitis (AKC) is a rare bilateral disease that
typically develops in adulthood (peak incidence 30ÔÇô50 years)

## General measures

¯Çü Allergen avoidance, if possible. Allergen (e.g. patch) testing is
sometimes useful, but o¯Çøen gives non-speci¯Ç×c results.
¯Çü Cool compresses may be helpful.
¯Çü Lid hygiene should be used for associated staphylococcal
blepharitis. Moisturizing cream such as E45 can be applied to
dry, ¯Ç×ssured skin.
¯Çü Bandage contact lens wear to aid healing of persistent epithelial defects.

## Local treatment

¯Çü Mast cell stabilizers (e.g. sodium cromoglicate, nedocromil
sodium, lodoxamide) reduce the frequency of acute exacerbations and the need for steroids and so form the basis of many
regimens, but are seldom e¯Ç£ective in isolation. Several days to
weeks of treatment are needed for a reasonable response and
long-term therapy may be needed (lodoxamide is not licensed
for long-term use).

Treatment of VKC and AKC

¯Çƒe management of VKC does not di¯Ç£er substantially from that of
AKC, although the latter is generally less responsive and requires
more intensive and prolonged treatment.

188 Allergic Conjunctivitis

¯Çü Topical antihistamines (e.g. emedastine, epinastine, levocabastine, bepotastine) when used in isolation are about as
e¯Ç£ective as mast cell stabilizers. ¯Çƒey are suitable for acute
exacerbations but generally not for continuous long-term use
and courses of several preparations are licensed for use only in
courses of limited duration. A trial of several di¯Ç£erent agents
may be worthwhile.
¯Çü Combined antihistamine and vasoconstrictor (e.g. antazoline with xylometazoline) may o¯Ç£er relief in some cases.
¯Çü Combined action antihistamine/mast cell stabilizers (e.g.
azelastine, ketotifen, olopatadine) are helpful in many patients
and have a relatively rapid onset of action.
¯Çü Non-steroidal anti-in¯ÇØammatory preparations (e.g.
ketorolac, diclofenac) may improve comfort by blocking nonhistamine mediators. Combining one of these with a mast cell
stabilizer is an e¯Ç£ective regimen in some patients.
¯Çü Topical steroids (e.g. ¯ÇØuorometholone 0.1%, rimexolone 1%,
prednisolone 0.5%, loteprednol etabonate 0.2% or 0.5%) are
used for severe exacerbations of conjunctivitis and signi¯Ç×cant
keratopathy. Reducing conjunctival activity generally leads to
corneal improvement. ¯Çƒey are usually prescribed in short but
intensive (e.g. 2-hourly initially) courses, aiming for prompt
tapering. Although the risk of elevation of intraocular pressure is low, monitoring is advisable if long-term treatment is
necessary. Stronger preparations such as prednisolone 1% can
be used, but carry a higher risk of steroid-induced glaucoma.
Shield ulcers are o¯Çøen refractory to topical steroid treatment.
¯Çü Steroid ointment (e.g. hydrocortisone 0.5%) may be used to
treat the eyelids in AKC, though as with eye drops, the duration of treatment should be minimized and the intraocular
pressure (IOP) monitored.
¯Çü Antibiotics may be used in conjunction with steroids in severe
keratopathy to prevent or treat bacterial infection.
¯Çü Acetylcysteine is a mucolytic agent that is useful in VKC for
dissolving mucus ¯Ç×laments and deposits and addressing early
plaque formation.
¯Çü Immunomodulators

{ Ciclosporin (0.05ÔÇô2% between two and six times daily)
may be indicated if steroids are ine¯Ç£ective, inadequate or
poorly tolerated, or as a steroid-sparing agent in patients
with severe disease. ¯Çƒe e¯Ç£ects typically take some weeks to
be exerted and relapses may occur if treatment is stopped
suddenly. Irritation and blurred vision are common.

{ Calcineurin inhibitors show increasing promise as an
alternative to steroids in the treatment of allergic eye disease. Tacrolimus 0.03% ointment can be e¯Ç£ective in AKC
for severe eyelid disease. Instillation into the fornix has
been e¯Ç£ective in modulating conjunctival in¯ÇØammation in
refractory cases. Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment is particularly
useful when applied topically in patients with shield ulcers
and corneal epitheliopathy and can be used without topical
steroids. Factors associated with a poor response to tacrolimus include the presence of a giant papillae and palpebral
abnormality.

{ Supratarsal steroid injection may be considered in
severe palpebral disease or for non-compliant patients.

¯Çƒe injection is given into the conjunctival surface of the
anaesthetized everted upper eyelid; 0.1 ml of betamethasone sodium phosphate 4 mg/ml, dexamethasone 4 mg/ml
or triamcinolone 40 mg/ml is given.

TIP Topical steroids should not be used long term in a patient
with allergic conjunctivitis without monitoring the IOP.

## Systemic treatment

¯Çü Oral antihistamines help itching, promote sleep and reduce
nocturnal eye rubbing. Because other in¯ÇØammatory mediators
are involved besides histamines, e¯Ç£ectiveness is not assured.
Some antihistamines (e.g. loratadine) cause relatively little
drowsiness.
¯Çü Antibiotics (e.g. doxycycline 50ÔÇô100 mg daily for 6 weeks,
azithromycin 500 mg once daily for 3 days) may be given to
reduce blepharitis-aggravated in¯ÇØammation, usually in AKC.
¯Çü Immunosuppressive agents (e.g. steroids, ciclosporin, tacrolimus, azathioprine) may be e¯Ç£ective at relatively low doses
in AKC unresponsive to other measures. Short courses of
high-dose steroids may be necessary to achieve rapid control
in severe disease. Monoclonal antibodies against T cells have
shown some promise in refractory cases.
¯Çü Other treatments that may be e¯Ç£ective in some patients
include aspirin in VKC (avoided in children and adolescents
due to Reye syndrome risk), allergen desensitization and plasmapheresis in patients with high serum IgE levels.

## Surgery

¯Çü Super¯Ç×cial keratectomy may be required to remove plaques or
debride shield ulcers and allow epithelialization. Medical treatment must be maintained until the cornea has re-epithelialized
in order to prevent recurrences. Excimer laser phototherapeutic keratectomy is an alternative.
¯Çü Surface maintenance/restoration surgery such as amniotic
membrane overlay gra¯Çøing or lamellar keratoplasty, or eyelid
procedures such as botulinum toxin-induced ptosis or lateral
tarsorrhaphy, may be required for severe persistent epithelial
defects or ulceration. Gluing may be appropriate for focal
(ÔÇÿpunched-outÔÇÖ) corneal perforations.

Non-allergic eosinophilic conjunctivitis

Non-allergic eosinophilic conjunctivitis (NAEC) is a recently
proposed chronic non-atopic condition said to occur predominantly in middle-aged women in whom dry eye is also commonly present. It has been suggested that it is relatively common
but under-diagnosed. It is thought to be of similar pathogenesis
to non-allergic eosinophilic rhinitis. Conjunctival eosinophilia is
present without signi¯Ç×cant IgE levels in the serum or tear ¯Ç×lm.
Symptoms are similar to those of allergic conjunctivitis ÔÇô itching,
redness, foreign body sensation and mild watery discharge. Treatment is with a 1ÔÇô2 week course of topical steroid for exacerbations
followed by maintenance with topical mast cell stabilizers, nonsteroidal anti-in¯ÇØammatory agents or antihistamines.

conjunctivitis.

## Contact allergic blepharoconjunctivitis

Analogous to contact dermatitis, this refers to the acute or subacute T-cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity reaction seen most
commonly by ophthalmologists as a reaction to eye drop constituents and by optometrists as a reaction to contact lens solutions.
Mascara is a less common cause. ¯Çƒere may be a conjunctival reaction, but signs predominantly involve the eyelid skin: erythema,
thickening, induration and sometimes ¯Ç×ssuring occur (Fig. 6.15).
Treatment is by discontinuation of the precipitant. A mild topical
steroid ointment can be helpful.

Giant (mechanically induced) papillary
conjunctivitis

## Pathogenesis

Mechanically induced papillary conjunctivitis, the severe form of
which is known as giant papillary conjunctivitis (GPC), can occur
secondary to a variety of mechanical stimuli of the tarsal conjunctiva. It is most frequently encountered with contact lens (CL) wear,
when it is termed contact lens-associated papillary conjunctivitis
(CLPC). ¯Çƒe risk is increased by the build-up of proteinaceous
deposits and cellular debris on the CL surface. Ocular prostheses
(Fig. 6.16), exposed sutures and scleral buckles, corneal surface
irregularity and ¯Ç×ltering blebs can all be responsible. A related
phenomenon is the so-called ÔÇÿmucus ¯Ç×shing syndromeÔÇÖ, when, in a
variety of underlying anterior segment disorders, patients develop
or exacerbate a chronic papillary reaction due to repetitive manual
removal of mucus. Giant papillae can also be seen in other conditions, such as VKC and AKC.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms consist of a foreign body sensation, redness, itching, increased mucus production, blurring and loss of CL tolerance. Symptoms may be worse a¯Çøer lens removal. Patients
should be questioned about CL cleaning and maintenance.

¯Çü Signs

{ Changing the type of CL solution, particularly discontinuation of preservative-containing preparations.

{ Switching to monthly then daily disposable CL if the condition persists a¯Çøer renewing non-disposable lenses.

{ Variable mucous discharge.

{ Substantial CL protein deposits may be present.

{ Excessive CL mobility due to upper lid capture.

{ Superior tarsal hyperaemia and papillae. By de¯Ç×nition,
ÔÇÿgiantÔÇÖ papillae are >1.0 mm in diameter, but the clinical
syndrome of mechanically induced papillary conjunctivitis commonly features only ¯Ç×ne/medium papillae, particularly in early or mild disease.

{ Focal apical ulceration and whitish scarring may develop
on larger papillae.

{ Keratopathy is rare because of the relatively subdued secretion of in¯ÇØammatory cytokines.

{ Ptosis may occur, mainly as a result of irritative spasm and
tissue laxity secondary to chronic in¯ÇØammation.

## Treatment

Other causes of conjunctival papillae should be excluded, as well
as CL intolerance due to other causes, such as a reaction to lens
cleaning solutions and dry eyes.
¯Çü Removal of the stimulus

{ CL wear should be discontinued for several weeks and the
current lenses replaced. For mildÔÇômoderate disease, this
may be adequate for resolution, sometimes in conjunction
with reduced wearing time. In severe CLPC a longer interval without lens wear may be needed.

{ Removal of other underlying causes, such as exposed
sutures or a scleral buckle.

{ Assessment of the status and ¯Ç×t of an ocular prosthesis.

{ Filtering bleb: partial excision, revision with nonpenetrating drainage surgery or glaucoma drainage device
implantation.
¯Çü Ensure e¯Ç£ective cleaning of CL or prosthesis

190 Conjunctivitis in Blistering Mucocutaneous Disease

{ Rigid lenses carry a reduced risk of CLPC (5%), probably
because they are easier to clean e¯Ç£ectively.

{ Cessation of CL wear, substituting spectacles or refractive
surgery, may be necessary for severe or refractory disease.

{ Regular (at least weekly) use of CL protein removal tablets.

## Progression of ocular disease has been divided into stages as

follows:
¯Çü FosterÔÇÖs classi¯Ç×cation system

{ Stage II: forniceal shortening, particularly inferiorly.

{ Prosthesis: polishing, cleaning with detergent, coating.
¯Çü Topical

{ Stage I: early stage associated with chronic conjunctivitis,
tear dysfunction and subepithelial ¯Ç×brosis.

{ Mast cell stabilizers should be non-preserved in patients

hour a¯Çøer drop instillation prior to lens insertion. Most
can be continued long-term if necessary.

{ Antihistamines, non-steroidal anti-in¯ÇØammatory agents
and combined antihistamines/mast cell stabilizers may
each be of bene¯Ç×t.

{ Topical steroids can be used for the acute phase of resistant cases, particularly those where e¯Ç£ective removal of the
stimulus is di¯ÇÜcult, as in bleb-related disease.

{ Stage IV: surface keratinization and cicatricial

¯Çü MondinoÔÇÖs classi¯Ç×cation system (based on inferior forniceal

{ Stage III: symblepharon formation.

{ Papillary conjunctivitis, di¯Ç£use hyperaemia, oedema and
subtle ¯Ç×brosis (Fig. 6.17A).

{ Fine lines of subconjunctival ¯Ç×brosis and shortening of
the inferior fornices. Symblepharon (plural: symblephara)
formation refers to adhesion between the bulbar and palpebral conjunctiva (Fig. 6.17B and C).

{ Stage I: up to 25%.

{ Stage II: 25ÔÇô50%.

{ Stage III: 50ÔÇô75%.

{ Stage IV: greater than 75%.
¯Çü Symptoms. Insidious or relapsingÔÇôremitting non-speci¯Ç×c
bilateral conjunctivitis; misdiagnosis (e.g. dry eye) is common.

CONJUNCTIVITIS IN BLISTERING
MUCOCUTANEOUS DISEASE

## Mucous membrane pemphigoid

## Introduction

Mucous membrane pemphigoid (MMP), also known as ocular
cicatricial pemphigoid (OCP), comprises a group of chronic autoimmune mucocutaneous blistering diseases. An unknown trigger
leads to a type II (cytotoxic) hypersensitivity response resulting in
antibodies binding at the basement membrane zone (BMZ), the
activation of complement and the recruitment of in¯ÇØammatory cells,
with localized separation of the epidermis from the dermis at the
BMZ and subsequent progression to scarring. Studies of HLA typing have found an increased susceptibility to the disease in patients
with HLA-DR4. ¯Çƒe HLA-DQB1 allele in particular shows a strong
association with OCP and other forms of pemphigoid disease.
A wide range of epithelial tissues can be involved, including
the skin and various mucous membranes. Particular clinical forms
of MMP tend to involve speci¯Ç×c target tissues: bullous pemphigoid
(BP) shows a predilection for skin and ocular mucous membrane
pemphigoid (OMMP, also known as ocular cicatricial pemphigoid ÔÇô OCP) involves the conjunctiva in the majority of cases and
causes progressive scarring (cicatrization). ¯Çƒe disease typically
presents in old age and a¯Ç£ects females more commonly than males
by a 2:1 ratio. Other causes of cicatrizing conjunctivitis include
StevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome, trachoma, drug-induced, trauma and
severe or chronic conjunctivitis of many types. MMP should not
be confused with pemphigus, a distinct group of disorders.

## Ocular features

¯Çü Diagnosis is principally clinical, but biopsy of involved
mucous membrane o¯Çøen shows supportive changes (linear
antibody and complement BMZ deposition).
¯Çü MichelÔÇÖs transport medium should be used when taking a
specimen for direct immuno¯ÇØuorescence and immunoelectron microscopy.

## Systemic features

¯Çü Mucosal involvement is very common and is characterized by
subepidermal blisters, most frequently oral (Fig. 6.19A). Severe
manifestations include oesophageal and laryngeal strictures.
¯Çü Skin lesions are less common (25%) and present as tense blisters and erosions of the head and neck, groin and extremities
(Fig. 6.19B).

## Systemic treatment

Systemic treatment is the mainstay of management. Any detectable
in¯ÇØammatory activity should be suppressed.

{ Necrosis in severe cases.

{ Flattening of the plica and keratinization of the caruncle.

{ Dry eye due to destruction of goblet cells and accessory lacrimal glands and occlusion of the main lacrimal
ductules.

{ Monitoring should include the measurement of forniceal
depth and noting the position of adhesions.
¯Çü Eyelids

{ Aberrant (trichiatic) lashes, chronic blepharitis and
keratinization of the lid margin.

{ Ankyloblepharon is an adhesion at the outer canthus
between the upper and lower lids (Fig. 6.17D).
¯Çü Cornea

{ Epithelial defects (Fig. 6.18A) associated with drying and
exposure.

{ In¯Ç×ltration and peripheral vascularization (Fig. 6.18B).

{ Keratinization and conjunctivalization of the corneal surface (Fig. 6.18C) due to epithelial stem cell failure.

{ End-stage disease is characterized by total symblepharon
and corneal opaci¯Ç×cation (Fig. 6.18D).

conjunctival ¯Ç×brosis (arrow); (B) symblepharon formation; (C) severe ¯Ç×brosis with forniceal

¯Çü Dapsone (diaminodiphenylsulfone) is a useful ¯Ç×rst-line treatment in patients with mildÔÇômoderate disease. Approximately
70% of patients respond favourably. It is contraindicated in
glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase de¯Ç×ciency. Sulfasalazine
is sometimes better tolerated.
¯Çü Antimetabolites (e.g. azathioprine, methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil) are alternatives for mildÔÇômoderate disease if
dapsone is contraindicated, ine¯Ç£ective or poorly tolerated and
are suitable for long-term therapy. Dapsone can be used in
conjunction if necessary. Cyclophosphamide may be reserved
for severe or refractory disease.
¯Çü Steroids (prednisolone 1ÔÇô1.5 mg/kg) are e¯Ç£ective for rapid
disease control, but adverse e¯Ç£ects limit long-term use. IOP
should be monitored.
¯Çü Other measures include intravenous immunoglobulin therapy and rituximab. Remission has been reported with a combination regimen.

## Local treatment

¯Çü Topical

{ Lid hygiene and low-dose oral tetracycline for blepharitis.
¯Çü Subconjunctival mitomycin C and/or steroid injection may be
used as a temporizing aid or if systemic immunosuppression
is not possible.
¯Çü Contact lenses may be used with caution to protect the cornea
from aberrant lashes and from dehydration.

## Reconstructive surgery

Reconstructive surgery, preferably under systemic steroid cover,
should be considered when active disease is controlled. Seemingly trivial surgical intervention can lead to exacerbation of
disease.
¯Çü Aberrant eyelashes (see Ch. 2).
¯Çü Punctal occlusion to aid tear retention.

{ Arti¯Ç×cial tears (preferably preservative-free) are an integral part of most regimens.

{ Topical steroids, ciclosporin or tacrolimus may be used as
an adjunct to systemic immunosuppressive treatment.

{ Retinoic acid may reduce keratinization.

{ Antibiotics when indicated.
