StevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome/toxic
epidermal necrolysis (Lyell syndrome)

## Introduction

¯Çƒe terms ÔÇÿStevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome (SJS)ÔÇÖ and ÔÇÿerythema multiforme majorÔÇÖ have historically been used synonymously. However,
it is now believed that erythema multiforme (without the ÔÇÿmajorÔÇÖ)
is a distinct disease, milder and recurrent, with somewhat dissimilar clinical features. Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN ÔÇô Lyell
syndrome) is a severe variant of SJS. SJS/TEN patients tend to be
young adults, though other groups may be a¯Ç£ected. ¯Çƒe condition
involves a cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity reaction, usually
related to drug exposure. A wide range of medications have been
incriminated, including antibiotics (especially sulfonamides and
trimethoprim), analgesics including paracetamol (acetaminophen),
nevirapine (widely prescribed as a part of combination therapy for
HIV infection), cold remedies and anticonvulsants. Infections due
to microorganisms such as Mycoplasma pneumoniae and HSV
and some cancers have also been implicated. Because symptoms
o¯Çøen take weeks to develop, in many cases the precipitant cannot
be identi¯Ç×ed. Mortality overall is around 5% in SJS (death is commonly due to infection), but is considerably higher in TEN.

## Ocular features

In the acute stage there are o¯Çøen practical obstacles to standard slit
lamp examination, as the patient may be bedridden and undergoing barrier nursing. A portable slit lamp may be helpful.
¯Çü Symptoms. Acute ocular symptoms may include redness,
mildÔÇôsevere grittiness, photophobia, watering and blurring.
¯Çü Acute signs

{ Haemorrhagic crusting of the lid margins (Fig. 6.20A) is
characteristic. Skin lesions may be con¯ÇØuent and it is o¯Çøen
di¯ÇÜcult for an examiner to open the eyes without causing
marked discomfort.

{ Papillary conjunctivitis, which can range from mild, transient and self-limiting to severe (Fig. 6.20B).

{ Conjunctival membranes and pseudomembranes (Fig.
6.20C), severe hyperaemia, haemorrhages, blisters and
patchy infarction.

{ Keratopathy: a spectrum of lesions from punctate erosions
to large epithelial defects, secondary bacterial keratitis and
occasionally perforation.

{ Iritis is not infrequent and panophthalmitis has been
reported.
¯Çü Late signs

{ Conjunctival cicatrization (Fig. 6.20D) with forniceal
shortening and symblepharon formation.

{ Keratinization of the conjunctiva and lid margin (Fig.
6.20E), sometimes with abrasive plaque formation.

{ Eyelid complications include cicatricial entropion
and ectropion, trichiasis, metaplastic lashes and
ankyloblepharon.

{ Keratopathy including scarring, vascularization and keratinization (Fig. 6.20F) as a result of the primary in¯ÇØammation and/or infection, as well as cicatricial entropion and
aberrant lashes.

{ Watery eyes due to ¯Ç×brosis of the lacrimal puncta. Dry eyes
may also occur as a result of ¯Ç×brosis of lacrimal gland ductules and conjunctival metaplasia with loss of goblet cells.

## Systemic features

## Systemic treatment

¯Çü Removal of the precipitant if possible, such as discontinuation of drugs and treatment of suspected infection.
¯Çü General supportive measures such as maintenance of adequate hydration, electrolyte balance and nutrition (especially
protein replacement) are critical. Management in a specialist
burns unit should reduce the chance of infection when the
extent of skin involvement is substantial.
¯Çü Systemic steroids remain controversial. ¯Çƒere are reports
of increased mortality in older papers, but later research
has raised the possibility that early short-term high-dose
intravenous treatment may improve outcomes.
¯Çü Other immunosuppressants including ciclosporin, azathioprine, cyclophosphamide and intravenous immunoglobulin

## Skin biopsy may help to establish the diagnosis but is rarely

necessary.
¯Çü Symptoms. Flu-like symptoms, which can be severe, may last
up to 14 days before the appearance of lesions. In many cases
the patient is very ill and hospitalization is required. Symptoms of systemic mucosal involvement include nasal pain and
discharge, pain on micturition, diarrhoea, cough, shortness of
breath and pain on eating and drinking.
¯Çü Signs

{ Mucosal involvement is characterized by blistering and
haemorrhagic crusting of the lips (Fig. 6.21A). ¯Çƒe blisters
may also involve the tongue, oropharynx, nasal mucosa
and occasionally the genitalia.

{ Small purpuric, vesicular, haemorrhagic or necrotic skin
lesions involving the extremities, face and trunk (Fig.
6.21B). ¯Çƒese are usually transient but may be widespread.
Healing usually occurs within 1ÔÇô4 weeks, leaving a pigmented scar.

{ Widespread sloughing of the epidermis is uncommon.

{ ÔÇÿTargetÔÇÖ lesions showing the classic three zones are now
viewed as characteristic of erythema multiforme rather
than SJS/TEN.

## Ocular treatment

¯Çü Acute disease. Daily review is advisable initially in most patients
to check the corneas and exclude symblepharon formation.

{ Topical lubricants are used as frequently as necessary, e.g.
hypromellose 0.3% preservative-free up to hourly, highviscosity ointment during sleep.

{ Prevention of corneal exposure, e.g. moisture chambers,
gel pads if mechanically ventilated.

{ Topical steroids may be used for iritis and for conjunctival
in¯ÇØammation, though a bene¯Ç×t for the latter has not been

{ Topical cycloplegia (e.g. atropine 1% once or twice daily)
may improve comfort.

{ Lysis of developing symblephara with a sterile glass rod or

{ Pseudomembrane/membrane peeling can be considered,
although the bene¯Ç×t is unproven.

keratitis.

culture.

{ IOP monitoring may be prudent, using portable tonometry if necessary.
¯Çü Chronic disease

{ Adequate lubrication, including punctal occlusion if
required.

{ Topical transretinoic acid 0.01% or 0.025% may reverse
keratinization.

{ Bandage contact lenses (typically gas permeable scleral
lenses) to maintain surface moisture, protect the cornea
from aberrant lashes and address irregular astigmatism.

{ Mucous membrane gra¯Çøing (e.g. buccal mucosa autogra¯Çø)
for forniceal reconstruction.

{ Corneal rehabilitation may involve super¯Ç×cial keratectomy for keratinization, lamellar corneal gra¯Çøing for
super¯Ç×cial scarring (preferred to penetrating keratoplasty), amniotic membrane gra¯Çøing, limbal stem cell
transplantation and keratoprosthesis implantation in
end-stage disease.

Graft-versus-host disease

## Introduction

Ocular gra¯Çø-versus-host disease (GVHD) occurs in patients who
have undergone allogenic haematological stem cell transplantation,

{ Treatment of aberrant lashes (see Ch. 2).

196 Miscellaneous Disorders of the Conjunctiva

an operation that is undertaken on an estimated 25,000 people
annually worldwide. It can occur in patients who have acute or
chronic GVHD, although it is more frequently seen in the chronic
form. Clinically it manifests in a similar fashion to StevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome. Ocular manifestations develop in 40ÔÇô60% of
patients a¯Çøer stem cell transplantation and can lead to signi¯Ç×cant
ocular surface consequences.
¯Çü Ocular features

¯Çü Symptoms include a foreign body sensation, burning, mild
photophobia, mucoid discharge and frequent blinking, and are
o¯Çøen intermittent.
¯Çü Conjunctiva

{ Dry eye syndrome (keratoconjunctivitis sicca).

{ Conjunctival hyperaemia with pseudomembrane and
membrane formation.

{ Chronic in¯ÇØammation leading to cicatricial changes in the
conjunctiva and occasionally entropion formation.

{ Hyperaemia of a radial band of the superior bulbar conjunctiva (Fig. 6.23A) that stains with rose Bengal and may
be best seen macroscopically.

{ Limbal papillary hypertrophy (Fig. 6.23B). Limbal palisades may be lost superiorly.

{ Papillary hypertrophy of the superior tarsal plate, o¯Çøen
having a di¯Ç£use velvety appearance (Fig. 6.23C).

{ Light downward pressure on the upper lid results in a fold
of redundant conjunctiva crossing the upper limbus.

{ Superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis.

{ Corneal manifestations including punctate epithelial
keratopathy, marginal keratitis and ¯Ç×lamentary keratitis.
Severe disease can lead to corneal erosion, thinning, ulceration and possible perforation.

{ Keratinization can be demonstrated on biopsy or impression cytology.
¯Çü Cornea

{ Petechial haemorrhages may be present.

{ Superior punctate corneal epithelial erosions are common
and are o¯Çøen separated from the limbus by a zone of normal epithelium.

{ Superior ¯Ç×lamentary keratitis develops in about one-third
of cases.

{ Mild superior pannus resembling arcus senilis may be seen
in long-standing disease.

## Treatment

¯Çü Arti¯Ç×cial tears can be helpful (see above).
¯Çü Topical cyclosporine 0.5% is the mainstay of treatment.
¯Çü Systemic treatment does not necessarily improve the ocular
symptoms and signs. Oral steroids are o¯Çøen used.

MISCELLANEOUS DISORDERS OF THE
CONJUNCTIVA

## Superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis

## Introduction

Superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis (SLK) is a relatively uncommon
chronic disease of the superior limbus and the superior bulbar and
tarsal conjunctiva. It a¯Ç£ects one or both eyes of middle-aged women,
approximately 50% of whom have abnormal thyroid function (usually hyperthyroidism). Approximately 3% of patients with thyroid
eye disease have SLK. It can be associated with gra¯Çø-versus-host disease. ¯Çƒe condition is probably under-diagnosed because symptoms
are typically more severe than signs. ¯Çƒe course can be prolonged
over years although remission eventually occurs spontaneously.
¯Çƒere are similarities to mechanically induced papillary conjunctivitis and a comparable clinical picture has been described with CL
wear and following upper lid surgery or trauma. It is unlikely to be
caused by tight upper eyelids. ¯Çƒe condition is believed to be the
result of blink-related trauma between the upper lid and the superior bulbar conjunctiva, precipitated in many cases by tear ¯Ç×lm
insu¯ÇÜciency and an excess of lax conjunctival tissue. With increased
conjunctival movement there is mechanical damage to the tarsal and
bulbar conjunctival surfaces, the resultant in¯ÇØammatory response
leading to increasing conjunctival oedema and redundancy, with
the creation of a self-perpetuating cycle. It may be analogous to conjunctivochalasis a¯Ç£ecting the lower bulbar conjunctiva (see Ch. 3).

## Diagnosis

Enquiry should be made about contact lens wear and previous eyelid surgery or trauma.

{ Keratoconjunctivitis sicca is present in only about 50%.
¯Çü Investigation

{ ¯Çƒyroid function testing should be performed if the patient
is not known to have thyroid disease.

{ Biopsy or impression cytology may reveal keratinization of
the superior bulbar conjunctiva.

TIP Check thyroid function in patients with superior limbic
keratoconjunctivitis.

## Treatment

¯Çü Topical

{ Lubricants to reduce friction between the tarsal and bulbar
conjunctiva should be used regularly and frequently.

{ Acetylcysteine 5% or 10% four times daily to break down
¯Ç×laments and provide lubrication.

{ Mast cell stabilizers and steroids to address any in¯ÇØammatory component. Steroids may be best used in short intensive courses with rapid tapering and should be reserved for
severe cases.

{ Promising results have been reported with topical
rebamipide.

{ Ciclosporin 0.05% twice daily as primary or adjunctive
therapy, particularly in the presence of coexisting keratoconjunctivitis sicca.

{ Retinoic acid to retard keratinization.

{ Autologous serum 20% drops can be bene¯Ç×cial but may
require instillation up to 10 times a day.
¯Çü So¯Ç£ contact lenses, which intervene between the lid and the
superior conjunctiva, are e¯Ç£ective in some cases. Interestingly,
a unilateral lens may provide bilateral relief.

di¯ÇØuse velvety papillary hypertrophy.

## Ligneous conjunctivitis

## Introduction

Ligneous conjunctivitis is a very rare potentially sight- and even
life-threatening disorder characterized by recurrent, o¯Çøen bilateral

¯Ç×brin-rich pseudomembranous lesions of wood-like consistency
that develop mainly on the tarsal conjunctiva. It is generally a systemic condition and may involve the periodontal tissue, the upper
and lower respiratory tract, kidneys, middle ear and female genitalia. Death can occasionally occur from pulmonary involvement. In
susceptible patients abnormal patterns of damage repair are found,
particularly a failure of normal clearance of products of the acute
stages of the healing process. ¯Çƒis is manifested predominantly in
mucosal tissue. A de¯Ç×ciency in plasmin-mediated ¯Ç×brinolysis may
be a key common factor in many patients. Episodes may be triggered by relatively minor trauma, or by systemic events such as
fever and anti¯Ç×brinolytic therapy.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Histopathology shows amorphous subepithelial deposits of
eosinophilic material consisting predominantly of ¯Ç×brin (Fig.
6.24A).
¯Çü Presentation is with non-speci¯Ç×c conjunctivitis, usually in
childhood (median age 5 years), although onset may be at any
age. A conjunctival lesion is commonly noted by parents.

is rare. It is important to discontinue any anti¯Ç×brinolytic drugs.

so¯Çøen pseudomembranes and facilitate removal.

{ Following membrane removal, hourly heparin and steroids are commenced immediately and continued until

disappeared.

## Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome

Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome is a rare condition consisting of chronic low-grade fever, unilateral granulomatous conjunctivitis (Fig. 6.25) with surrounding follicles and ipsilateral
regional (preauricular) lymphadenopathy. It is synonymous
with cat-scratch disease (caused by Bartonella henselae ÔÇô see Ch.
12), although several other causes have been implicated, including tularaemia, insect hairs (ophthalmia nodosum), Treponema

Bengal in factitious conjunctivitis. (Courtesy of S Tuft.)

pallidum, sporotrichosis, tuberculosis and acute C. trachomatis
infection.

## Factitious conjunctivitis

## Introduction

Self-injury (factitious keratoconjunctivitis) is most o¯Çøen intentional, but can also occur inadvertently, as in mucus ¯Ç×shing
syndrome and removal of contact lenses. Damage may be the
result of either mechanical trauma or of the instillation of irritant but readily accessible household substances, such as soap.
Occasionally over-instillation of prescribed ocular medication is
responsible.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Reported symptoms may seem disproportionate
to signs. ¯Çƒe patient may have sought multiple medical opinions over an extended period, o¯Çøen from a range of specialists
for di¯Ç£erent complaints.
¯Çü Signs

{ Inferior conjunctival injection and staining with rose Bengal (Fig. 6.26), with quiet superior bulbar conjunctiva.

{ Linear corneal abrasions, persistent epithelial defects and
occasionally focal corneal perforation.

DEGENERATIONS

## Pinguecula

## Introduction

A pinguecula (plural pingueculae) is an innocuous but extremely
common asymptomatic elastotic degeneration of the conjunctival stroma. A yellowÔÇôwhite mound or aggregation of smaller
mounds is seen on the bulbar conjunctiva adjacent to the limbus
(Fig. 6.27A). It is more frequently located at the nasal than the
temporal limbus, but is frequently present at both. Calci¯Ç×cation
(Fig. 6.27B) is occasionally present. ¯Çƒe cause is believed to be
actinic damage, similar to the aetiology of pterygium (see below),
which pinguecula resembles histologically. ¯Çƒe distinction is
that the limbal barrier to extension has remained intact with
a pinguecula. Occasionally a pinguecula may become acutely
in¯ÇØamed (pingueculitis ÔÇô Fig. 6.27C), particularly if the lesion
is prominent or overlying calci¯Ç×cation leads to epithelial breakdown. ¯Çƒere is a small subgroup of patients with an in¯ÇØamed
nasal pinguecula and impaired ocular surface lubrication, who
can progress to pterygium (Fig. 6.27D).

## Treatment

## Treatment is usually unnecessary because growth is absent or very

slow.
¯Çü Irritation may be treated with topical lubrication.
¯Çü Pingueculitis can be treated with lubrication if mild or with a
short course of topical steroid.
¯Çü Excision may be indicated for cosmetic reasons or for signi¯Ç×cant irritation. In contrast to pterygium (see next), the recurrence rate is low and simple excision is usually adequate.
¯Çü ¯Çƒermal laser ablation can be e¯Ç£ective. Gentian violet marking may be necessary to ensure adequate absorption in lighterskinned individuals.

{ Secondary infection with Candida spp.

{ Sterile ring in¯Ç×ltrate and hypopyon.

{ Corneal scarring.

## Management

¯Çü Exclude other diagnoses.
¯Çü Close observation may be required.
¯Çü Confrontation o¯Çøen leads to failure to return for review.
¯Çü A psychiatric opinion may be appropriate.

## Pterygium

## Introduction

D

to pterygium.

## Clinical features

A pterygium (plural pterygia) is a triangular fibrovascular subepithelial ingrowth of degenerative bulbar conjunctival tissue
over the limbus onto the cornea. It typically develops in patients
who have been living in sunny countries and, as with pinguecula, may represent a response to ultraviolet exposure and to
other factors such as chronic surface dryness. The condition
tends to run in families. A pterygium is histologically similar to
a pinguecula and shows elastotic degenerative changes in vascularized subepithelial stromal collagen (Fig. 6.28A). In contrast
to pingueculae, pterygia encroach onto the cornea, invading the
Bowman layer. Pseudopterygium appears similar clinically but
is caused by a band of conjunctiva adhering to an area of compromised cornea at its apex. It forms as a response to an acute
inflammatory episode such as a chemical burn, corneal ulcer
(especially if marginal), trauma and cicatrizing conjunctivitis.

¯Çü Symptoms. Patients who present with a history of recent
enlargement are more likely to require early excision for subsequent aggressive growth. Aggressive growth or an atypical
appearance should prompt excision biopsy.

{ Most small lesions are asymptomatic.

{ Irritation and grittiness are caused by a dellen ÔÇô localized
drying ÔÇô e¯Ç£ect at the advancing edge due to interference
with the precorneal tear ¯Ç×lm (more likely if the head of the
pterygium is especially elevated).

{ Patients who wear contact lenses may develop symptoms
of irritation at an earlier stage due to edge li¯Çø.

{ Lesions may interfere with vision by obscuring the visual
axis or inducing astigmatism.

{ ¯Çƒere may be intermittent in¯ÇØammation similar to
pingueculitis.

{ Cosmesis may be a signi¯Ç×cant problem.

(Fig. 6.28B and C).

{ Linear epithelial iron deposition (Stocker line) may be
seen in the corneal epithelium anterior to the head of the
pterygium (Fig. 6.28B).

{ Fuchs islets are small discrete whitish ¯ÇØecks consisting
of clusters of pterygial epithelial cells o¯Çøen present at the
advancing edge.

{ A pseudopterygium (Fig. 6.28D) is classically distinguished by the fact that the location is away from the
horizontal (though this may occasionally be seen with true
pterygia) and that there is a ¯Ç×rm attachment to the cornea
only at its apex (head).

¯Çü Medical treatment of symptomatic patients is as for pinguecula. ¯Çƒe patient should be advised to wear sunglasses to reduce
ultraviolet exposure.
¯Çü Surgery. Simple excision (ÔÇÿbare scleraÔÇÖ technique) is associated
with a high rate of recurrence (around 80%), o¯Çøen with more
aggressive behaviour than the original lesion.

{ ¯Çƒe pterygium is excised from the cornea and conjunctiva
leaving bare sclera (Fig. 6.29A).

{ Conjunctival autogra¯Çøing. ¯Çƒe donor conjunctival patch is
usually harvested from the superior or upper-temporal paralimbal region (Fig. 6.29B). ¯Çƒe gra¯Çø is sutured into position
using 10-0 nylon sutures (Fig. 6.29C). ¯Çƒe site heals rapidly.
Conjunctival gra¯Çøs can be secured with tissue glue (for example: TISSEEL ¯Ç×brin glue) rather than sutures, thus shortening
operating time and reducing postoperative irritation.

{ Adjunctive treatment with mitomycin C or beta-irradiation

{ Peripheral lamellar keratoplasty may be required for deep

TIP In a patient undergoing surgery for pterygium, the risk
of recurrence can be reduced by using either a conjunctival

## Concretions

## Concretions are extremely common and are usually associated

with ageing, although they can also form in patients with chronic
conjunctival in¯ÇØammation such as trachoma. ¯Çƒey appear as multiple tiny cysts containing yellowishÔÇôwhite deposits of epithelial
debris including keratin, commonly located subepithelially in the
inferior tarsal and forniceal conjunctiva (Fig. 6.30A). ¯Çƒey can
become calci¯Ç×ed and, particularly if large, may erode through the
overlying epithelium (Fig. 6.30B) and cause marked irritation. If
symptomatic, treatment involves removal at the slit lamp with a
needle under topical anaesthesia.

## Conjunctivochalasis

Conjunctivochalasis usually appears as a fold of redundant conjunctiva interposed between the globe and lower eyelid, protruding over the lid margin (Fig. 6.31). It is probably a normal ageing
change that may be exacerbated by in¯ÇØammation and mechanical
stress related to dry eye and lid margin disease. Symptoms include
watering of the eye due to obstruction of the inferior punctum
and interference with the marginal tear meniscus. Treatment consists of topical lubricants and treatment of any blepharitis. A short
course of topical steroids or another anti-in¯ÇØammatory agent may
be helpful. Conjunctival resection can be performed in severe
cases (see also Ch. 3).

Retention (primary epithelial inclusion)
cyst

Conjunctival retention cysts are thin-walled lesions on the bulbar conjunctiva containing clear (Fig. 6.32A) or occasionally
turbid ¯ÇØuid or blood (Fig. 6.32B). ¯Çƒey do not usually cause
discomfort, but may be a mild cosmetic blemish. Histology
shows a ¯ÇØuid-¯Ç×lled internal cavity lined by a double epithelial
layer. Treatment, if required, is initially by simple puncture with
a needle under topical anaesthesia, but recurrence is common.

## Bleeding should be encouraged within the ruptured cyst as it
may promote adhesion of the walls and reduce the chance of

recurrence. Cyst wall excision under topical anaesthesia can be
carried out for recurrent cyst formation. ¯Çƒe di¯Ç£erential diagnosis includes secondary inclusion cysts following conjunctival
surgery and lymphangiectasia. ¯Çƒe latter is characterized by
strings of cystic or sausage-shaped clear-walled channels, which
may become ¯Ç×lled with blood (haemorrhagic lymphangiectasia

- Fig. 6.33).

SUBCONJUNCTIVAL HAEMORRHAGE

## Subconjunctival haemorrhage is a very common phenomenon

that is o¯Çøen idiopathic and apparently spontaneous, occurring
particularly in older people (Fig. 6.34A. It may result from surgery, conjunctivitis and trauma (Fig. 6.34B). ¯Çƒe haemorrhage is
usually asymptomatic but a momentary sharp pain or a snapping
or popping sensation is sometimes felt. Coughing, sneezing and
vomiting are common precipitants. In younger people contact

B

lens wear is a common association and in older individuals systemic vascular disease (hypertension) is prevalent. A local ocular cause should be ruled out by slit lamp examination. Patients
are o¯Çøen taking aspirin or similar products that have an e¯Ç£ect
on platelet function. Bleeding diatheses are a very rare association, but vitamin C de¯Ç×ciency and abusive trauma should always

be considered in infants. ¯Çƒe vision is usually una¯Ç£ected unless
a substantially elevated haemorrhage leads to a large localized
corneal wetting de¯Ç×cit (dellen), which is o¯Çøen uncomfortable.
A large bleed can track into the eyelids. Spontaneous resolution
over a week or two is typical, but two or three narrowly spaced
episodes are not uncommon.

206 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

## Anatomy and physiology

## General

¯Çƒe cornea is a complex structure which, as well as having a protective role, is responsible for about three-quarters of the optical
power of the eye. ¯Çƒe normal hydration level is 78%. Nutrients are
supplied and metabolic products removed via the aqueous humour
posteriorly and the tears anteriorly. ¯Çƒe cornea is the most densely
innervated tissue in the body and conditions such as abrasion and
bullous keratopathy are associated with marked pain, photophobia
and re¯Ç×ex lacrimation. A subepithelial and a deeper stromal nerve
plexus are supplied by the ¯ÇØrst division of the trigeminal nerve.

## Dimensions

¯Çƒe average corneal diameter is 11.5 mm vertically and 12 mm
horizontally. It is 540 ╬╝m thick centrally on average and thicker
towards the periphery. ¯Çƒe normal hydration level is 78%. Central corneal thickness does not di¯Ç£er between males and females,
but varies between individuals and races. It is a key determinant
of the intraocular pressure (IOP) measured with conventional
techniques.

## Structure

¯Çƒe cornea consists of the following layers (Fig. 7.1):
¯Çü ¯Çƒe epithelium is strati¯ÇØed squamous and non-keratinized,
and is composed of:

{ A single layer of columnar basal cells attached by hemidesmosomes to an underlying basement membrane.

{ Two to three strata of ÔÇÿwingÔÇÖ cells.

{ Two layers of squamous surface cells.

{ ¯Çƒe surface area of the outermost cells is increased by
microplicae and microvilli that facilitate the attachment
of the tear ¯ÇØlm and mucin. A¯Çøer a lifespan of a few days
super¯ÇØcial cells are shed into the tear ¯ÇØlm.

## Endothelium

of the stroma. ¯Çƒe membrane consists of an anterior banded
zone that is deposited in utero and a posterior non-banded
zone laid down throughout life by the endothelium, for which
it serves as a modi¯ÇØed basement membrane. It has regenerative
potential.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe endothelium consists of a monolayer of polygonal cells.
Endothelial cells maintain corneal deturgescence throughout life by pumping excess ¯Ç×uid out of the stroma. ¯Çƒe young
adult cell density is about 3000 cells/mm [2] . ¯Çƒe number of
cells decreases at about 0.6% per year and neighbouring cells
enlarge to ¯ÇØll the space. ¯Çƒe cells cannot regenerate. At a density of about 500 cells/mm [2] corneal oedema develops and
transparency is impaired.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe existence of a sixth corneal layer between the stroma and
Descemet membrane (Dua layer) has been proposed, though
some authorities believe this to be a previously described continuation of the posterior stroma.

TIP The cornea is densely innervated and a corneal abrasion
is usually associated with intense pain, photophobia and re¯Ç×ex
lacrimation.

{ Corneal stem cells are located at the corneoscleral limbus,
possibly in the palisades of Vogt. De¯ÇØciency may result in
chronic epithelial defects and ÔÇÿconjunctivalizationÔÇÖ (epithelial instability, vascularization and the appearance of goblet
cells). ¯Çƒey are thought to be critical in the maintenance of
a physiological barrier, preventing conjunctival tissue from
growing onto the cornea (e.g. pterygium). De¯ÇØciency may
be addressed by stem cell auto- or allotransplantation.
¯Çü Bowman layer is the acellular super¯ÇØcial layer of the stroma
and is formed from collagen ¯ÇØbres.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe stroma makes up 90% of corneal thickness. It is arranged
in regularly orientated layers of collagen ¯ÇØbrils whose spacing
is maintained by proteoglycan ground substance (chondroitin sulphate and keratan sulphate) with interspersed modi¯ÇØed
¯ÇØbroblasts (keratocytes). Maintenance of the regular arrangement and spacing of the collagen is critical to optical clarity.
¯Çƒe stroma can scar, but cannot regenerate following damage.
¯Çü Descemet membrane is a discrete sheet composed of a ¯ÇØne latticework of collagen ¯ÇØbrils that are distinct from the collagen

## Signs of corneal disease

Super¯Çƒcial

¯Çü Punctate epithelial erosions (PEE), tiny epithelial defects that
stain with ¯Ç×uorescein (Fig. 7.2A) and rose Bengal (Fig. 7.2B),
are generally an early sign of epithelial compromise. Causes
include a variety of stimuli. ¯Çƒe location of the lesions may
give an indication of aetiology:

{ Superior ÔÇô vernal disease, chlamydial conjunctivitis, superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis, ¯Ç×oppy eyelid syndrome
and mechanically induced keratoconjunctivitis.

{ Interpalpebral ÔÇô dry eye (can also be inferior), reduced
corneal sensation and ultraviolet keratopathy.

{ Inferior ÔÇô chronic blepharitis, lagophthalmos, eye drop
toxicity, self-induced, aberrant eyelashes and entropion.

{ Di¯Ç£use ÔÇô some cases of viral conjunctivitis and toxicity to
drops.

{ Central ÔÇô prolonged contact lens wear.
¯Çü Punctate epithelial keratitis (PEK) appears as granular,
opalescent, swollen epithelial cells, with focal intraepithelial infiltrates. They are visible unstained but stain well
with rose Bengal and variably with fluorescein. Causes
include:

{ Infections: adenoviral, chlamydial, molluscum contagiosum, early herpes simplex and herpes zoster, microsporidial
and systemic viral infections (e.g. measles, varicella, rubella).

## Deep

¯Çü In¯Ç×ltrates are yellowÔÇô or greyÔÇôwhite opacities located initially
within the anterior stroma (Fig. 7.3A), usually associated with
limbal or conjunctival hyperaemia. ¯Çƒey are stromal foci of
acute in¯Ç×ammation composed of in¯Ç×ammatory cells, cellular
and extracellular debris including necrosis. ¯Çƒe key distinction
is between sterile and infective lesions (Table 7.1); ÔÇÿPEDALÔÇÖ:
Pain, Epithelial defects, Discharge, Anterior chamber reaction,

## Location is a useful mnemonic to distinguish these two types

of lesions. Suppurative keratitis is caused by active infection
with bacteria, fungi, protozoa and occasionally viruses. Noninfectious ÔÇÿsterile keratitisÔÇÖ is due to an immune hypersensitivity response to antigen as in marginal keratitis and with
contact lens wear.
¯Çü Ulceration refers to tissue excavation associated with an epithelial defect (Fig. 7.3B), usually with in¯ÇØltration and necrosis.
¯Çü ÔÇÿMeltingÔÇÖ describes tissue disintegration in response to enzymatic activity, o¯Çøen with mild or no in¯ÇØltrate, e.g. peripheral
ulcerative keratitis.
¯Çü Vascularization occurs in response to a wide variety of stimuli. Venous channels are easily seen, whereas arterial feeding
vessels are smaller and require higher magni¯ÇØcation. Nonperfused deep vessels appear as ÔÇÿghost vesselsÔÇÖ, best detected by
retroillumination.
¯Çü Lipid deposition (Fig. 7.3C) may follow chronic in¯Ç×ammation
with leakage from corneal new vessels.
¯Çü Folds in Descemet membrane, also known as striate keratopathy (Fig. 7.3D), may result from corneal oedema. Causes
include in¯Ç×ammation, trauma (including surgery) and ocular
hypotony.
¯Çü Descemetocoele (US spelling ÔÇô descemetocele) is a bubblelike herniation of Descemet membrane into the cornea
(Fig. 7.3E), plugging a defect that would otherwise be
full-thickness.
¯Çü Breaks in Descemet membrane (Fig. 7.3F) may be due to corneal enlargement (Haab striae in infantile glaucoma) or deformation such as keratoconus and birth trauma. Acute in¯Ç×ux of
aqueous into the corneal stroma (acute hydrops) can follow.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe Seidel test demonstrates aqueous leakage. A drop of 1% or
2% ¯Ç×uorescein is applied to the surface of the eye. Using a slit
lamp, the cobalt blue ¯ÇØlter is used to detect a change in colour
from dark orange to bright yellowÔÇôgreen secondary to localized dilution at a site of leakage.

## Documentation of clinical signs

Clinical signs should be illustrated with a colour-coded labelled
diagram. Measuring lesion dimensions is particularly useful to
facilitate monitoring (Fig. 7.4). Slit lamp photography is an increasingly used supplement or alternative, but must be of high quality.
¯Çü Opacities such as scars and degenerations are drawn in black.
¯Çü Epithelial oedema is represented by ¯ÇØne blue circles, stromal
oedema as blue shading and folds in Descemet membrane as
wavy blue lines.

{ Miscellaneous: ¯Çƒygeson super¯ÇØcial punctate keratitis and
eye drop toxicity.
¯Çü Subepithelial infiltrates. Tiny subsurface foci of nonstaining inflammatory infiltrates (Fig. 7.2C). Causes
include severe or prolonged adenoviral keratoconjunctivitis, herpes zoster keratitis, adult inclusion conjunctivitis,
marginal keratitis, rosacea and Thygeson superficial punctate keratitis.
¯Çü Super¯Ç×cial punctate keratitis is a non-speci¯ÇØc term
describing any corneal epithelial disturbance of dot-like
morphology.
¯Çü Filaments. Strands of mucus admixed with epithelium,
attached at one end to the corneal surface, that stain well with
rose Bengal (see Fig. 7.2B). ¯Çƒe unattached end moves with
each blink. Grey subepithelial opacities may be seen at the
site of attachment. Dry eye is by far the most common cause.
Other causes include superior limbic keratoconjunctivitis,
neurotrophic keratopathy, long-term ocular patching and
essential blepharospasm.
¯Çü Epithelial oedema. Subtle oedema may manifest with loss of
normal corneal lustre, but more commonly, abundant tiny epithelial vesicles are seen. Bullae form in moderateÔÇôsevere cases
(Fig. 7.2D). ¯Çƒe cause is usually endothelial decompensation,
but it can follow acute elevation of IOP.
¯Çü Super¯Ç×cial neovascularization (Fig. 7.2E) is a feature of
chronic ocular surface irritation or hypoxia, as in contact lens
wear.
¯Çü Pannus describes super¯ÇØcial neovascularization accompanied
by degenerative subepithelial change (Fig. 7.2F).

## Specular microscopy

## Specular microscopy is the study of corneal layers under very high

magni¯ÇØcation (100 times greater than slit lamp biomicroscopy).
It is mainly used to assess the endothelium, which can be analysed for cellular size, shape, density and distribution. ¯Çƒe healthy

A

B

mosaic and greater size of the remaining cells. (Courtesy
of J Sharp.)

endothelial cell is a regular hexagon (Fig. 7.5A) and the normal cell
density in a young adult is about 3000 cells/mm [2]

¯Çü Physics. When a light beam of the specular photomicroscope
passes through the cornea it encounters a series of interfaces
between optically distinct regions. Some light is re¯Ç×ected specularly (i.e. like a mirror) back towards the photomicroscope
and forms an image that can be photographed and analysed.
¯Çü Indications

{ Evaluation of the functional reserve of the corneal endothelium prior to intraocular surgery is the most common indication. A clear cornea with normal thickness
on pachymetry is not necessarily associated with normal
endothelial morphology or cell density. Corneal oedema is
considerably more likely to occur with a cell density below
700 cells/mm [2] but unlikely above 1000 cells/mm [2]

{ Donor cornea evaluation.

{ To demonstrate pathology, particularly cornea guttata
(Fig. 7.5B), Descemet membrane irregularities and posterior polymorphous dystrophy.

## Corneal topography

## Corneal topography is used to image the cornea by projecting a

series of concentric rings of light on the anterior surface, constituting a Placido image. ¯Çƒe re¯Ç×ected light is analysed using
computer so¯Çøware to produce a detailed surface map. A major
application is the detection and management of corneal ectasia,
principally keratoconus. Screening for corneal ectasia is especially important prior to refractive surgery. It is used occasionally for contact lens ¯ÇØtting and to measure corneal thickness.
Scheimp¯Ç×ug imaging is a new technology that may o¯Ç£er advantages in topographic imaging. Anterior segment optical coherence tomography (OCT) and ultrasound biomicroscopy can also
be used to image the cornea.

## Principles of treatment

Control of infection and in¯Ç×ammation

¯Çü Antimicrobial agents should be started as soon as preliminary investigations have been performed. ¯Çƒe choice of agent
is determined by the likely aetiology according to clinical ¯ÇØndings. Broad-spectrum treatment is generally used initially,
with more selective agents introduced if necessary, when the
results of investigation are available.
¯Çü Topical steroids should be used with caution as they may
promote replication of some microorganisms, notably herpes simplex virus and fungi, and retard reparative processes
such as re-epithelialization. Nevertheless, they are vital in a
range of conditions for the suppression of destructive visioncompromising in¯Ç×ammation.
¯Çü Systemic immunosuppressive agents are useful in some conditions, particularly autoimmune disease.

## Promotion of epithelial healing

Re-epithelialization is of great importance in any corneal disease,
as thinning seldom progresses if the epithelium is intact.
¯Çü Reduction of exposure to toxic medications and preservatives
wherever possible.
¯Çü Lubrication with arti¯ÇØcial tears (unpreserved if possible) and
ointment. Taping the lids closed as a temporary measure is
o¯Çøen used as a nocturnal adjunct.
¯Çü Antibiotic ointment prophylaxis should be considered.
¯Çü Bandage so¯ÇØ contact lenses should be carefully supervised
to exclude superinfection and duration kept to a minimum.
Indications include:

{ Promotion of healing by mechanically protecting regenerating corneal epithelium from the constant rubbing of the
eyelids.

{ To improve comfort, particularly in the presence of a large
corneal abrasion.

{ Botulinum toxin injection into the levator muscle to
induce a temporary (2ÔÇô3 months) ptosis.

{ Temporary or permanent lateral tarsorrhaphy or medial
canthoplasty and occasionally central tarsorrhaphy (Fig.
7.6B).
¯Çü Conjunctival (Gundersen) ¯Ç£ap will protect and allow healing of a corneal epithelial defect. It is particularly suitable for
chronic unilateral disease in which the prognosis for restoration of useful vision is poor. Buccal mucous membrane is an
alternative.
¯Çü Amniotic membrane patch gra¯ÇØing (Fig. 7.6C) for persistent
unresponsive epithelial defects.
¯Çü Tissue adhesive (cyanoacrylate glue) to seal small perforations. ¯Çƒe glue can be applied to one side of a bespoke trimmed
patch of sterile plastic drape, which is pressed over the defect
a¯Çøer the edges are dried with a cellulose sponge. ¯Çƒe patch
remains in place to seal the defect and a bandage contact lens
is inserted for comfort and to aid retention of the patch (Fig.
7.6D).
¯Çü Corneal cross-linking using ultraviolet light-A and ribo¯Ç×avin
(photoactivated chromophore for infectious keratitis-corneal
collagen cross-linking: PACK-CXL) can be used to treat resistant corneal infections, particularly if there is evidence of corneal melting. ¯Çƒis treatment is usually used in combination
with standard antimicrobial therapy, but has also been shown
to have a microbicidal e¯Ç£ect when used as ¯ÇØrst-line treatment
in patients with early bacterial keratitis.
¯Çü Limbal stem cell transplantation may be used if there is stem
cell de¯ÇØciency as in chemical burns and cicatrizing conjunctivitis. ¯Çƒe source of the donor tissue may be the fellow eye
(autogra¯Çø) in unilateral disease or a living or cadaver donor
(allogra¯Çø) when both eyes are a¯Ç£ected. A newer technique
involves the in¯Çƒvitro replication of the patientÔÇÖs own stem
cells with subsequent re-implantation of the enhanced cell
population.
¯Çü Smoking retards epithelialization and should be discontinued.

BACTERIAL KERATITIS

## Pathogenesis

## Pathogens

## Bacterial keratitis usually develops only when ocular defences have

been compromised (see below). However, some bacteria, including Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Neisseria meningitidis, Corynebacterium diphtheriae and Haemophilus in¯Ç×uenzae are able to penetrate
a healthy corneal epithelium, usually in association with severe
conjunctivitis. It is important to remember that infections may be
polymicrobial, including bacterial and fungal co-infection. Common pathogens include:
¯Çü Pseudomonas aeruginosa is a ubiquitous Gram-negative
bacillus (rod) commensal of the gastrointestinal tract. ¯Çƒe
infection is typically aggressive and is responsible for over 60%
of contact lens-related keratitis.
¯Çü Staphylococcus aureus is a common Gram-positive and
coagulase-positive commensal of the nares, skin and

{ To seal a small perforation (Fig. 7.6A).
¯Çü Surgical eyelid closure is particularly useful in exposure and
neurotrophic keratopathy, as well as in persistent epithelial
defects. Lid closure may be used as a conservative method to
heal an infective ulcer in selected cases, such as an eye with no
visual potential in a patient with severe dementia.

212 Bacterial Keratitis

A B

C D

with a small perforation; (B) central tarsorrhaphy; (C) amniotic membrane graft over a persistent epithelial defect; (D) tissue glue under a bandage contact lens (arrow) in an eye with
severe thinning. (Courtesy of S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs A and C; S Chen ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

conjunctiva. Keratitis tends to present with a focal and fairly
well-de¯ÇØned white or yellowÔÇôwhite in¯ÇØltrate.
¯Çü Streptococci. S. pyogenes is a common Gram-positive commensal of the throat and vagina. S. pneumoniae (pneumococcus) is a Gram-positive commensal of the upper respiratory
tract. Infections with streptococci are o¯Çøen aggressive.

## Risk factors

¯Çü Contact lens wear, particularly if extended, is the most important risk factor. Corneal epithelial compromise secondary to
hypoxia and minor trauma is thought to be important, as is
bacterial adherence to the lens surface. Wearers of so¯Çø lenses
are at higher risk than those wearing rigid gas permeable
and other types. Infection is more likely if there is poor lens
hygiene, but it can also occur even with apparently meticulous
lens care and with daily disposable lenses.
¯Çü Trauma, including refractive surgery (particularly LASIK

  - laser-assisted in situ keratomileusis), has been linked to

bacterial infection, including with atypical mycobacteria. In
developing countries agricultural injury is the major risk factor, when fungal infection should be considered.
¯Çü Ocular surface disease such as herpetic keratitis, bullous
keratopathy, dry eye, chronic blepharitis, trichiasis and
entropion, exposure, severe allergic eye disease and corneal
anaesthesia.
¯Çü Other factors include local or systemic immunosuppression,
diabetes and vitamin A de¯ÇØciency.

TIP Bacterial corneal ulceration should be excluded in a
patient who wears contact lenses and presents with a painful
red eye and blurred vision.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Presentation is with unilateral pain, photophobia, blurred
vision and mucopurulent or purulent discharge.

A B

C D

infection. (Courtesy of T Carmichael ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B; C Barry ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. D.)

¯Çü Signs

{ An epithelial defect with in¯ÇØltrate involving a larger area
and signi¯ÇØcant circumcorneal injection (Fig. 7.7A and B).

{ Stromal oedema, folds in Descemet membrane and anterior uveitis, commonly with a hypopyon (Fig. 7.7C) and

{ Chemosis and eyelid swelling in moderateÔÇôsevere cases.

{ Scleritis can develop, particularly with severe perilimbal

{ Endophthalmitis is rare in the absence of perforation.

{ Subsequent scarring may be severe, including vascularization. In addition to opaci¯ÇØcation, irregular astigmatism

corneal in¯ÇØltrates associated with contact lens wear, peripheral

¯Çü Corneal scraping. ¯Çƒis may not be required for a small in¯ÇØltrate, particularly one without an epithelial defect and away

## Treatment

## General considerations

¯Çü Hospital admission should be considered for patients who are
not likely to comply or are unable to self-administer treatment.
It should also be considered for aggressive disease, particularly
if involving an only eye.
¯Çü Discontinuation of contact lens wear is mandatory.
¯Çü A clear plastic eye shield should be worn between eye drop
instillation if signi¯ÇØcant thinning (or perforation) is present.
¯Çü Decision to treat

{ Intensive treatment may not be required for small in¯ÇØltrates that are clinically sterile and may be treated by

{ Other stains, generally not requested at initial investigation, are listed in Table 7.3
¯Çü Culture and sensitivity reports should be obtained as soon as
possible. ¯Çƒe type of bacteria will provide an indication of the
antibiotic category to be used. An indication of resistance on
standard sensitivity testing does not necessarily extrapolate to
topical antibiotic instillation, where very high tissue levels can
be achieved.

216 Bacterial Keratitis

lower-frequency topical antibiotic and/or steroid and by
temporary cessation of contact lens wear.

{ It is important to note that the causative organism cannot
be de¯ÇØned reliably from the morphological appearance of
the ulcer.

{ Empirical broad-spectrum treatment is usually initiated
before microscopy results are available.

{ Daily follow-up is needed until a response to antibiotic
treatment is noted.

{ Empirical duotherapy usually involves a combination of
two forti¯ÇØed antibiotics, typically a cephalosporin and an
aminoglycoside, in order to cover common Gram-positive
and Gram-negative pathogens.

{ The antibiotics are not commercially available and must
be specially prepared (Table 7.5). A standard parenteral
or lyophilized antibiotic preparation is combined with
a compatible vehicle such that the antibiotic does not
precipitate. Optimally, constitution should take place
in the sterile preparation area of a pharmaceutical
dispensary.

Novel drug preparations, with higher concentrations or
modi¯ÇØed vehicles, have been introduced to enhance antibacterial activity.

{ Cipro¯Ç×oxacin instillation is associated with white corneal
precipitates (Fig. 7.9) that may delay epithelial healing.
¯Çü Antibiotic duotherapy may be preferred as ¯ÇØrst-line empirical treatment in aggressive disease or if microscopy suggests
streptococci or a speci¯ÇØc microorganism that may be more
e¯Ç£ectively treated by a tailored regimen (see Table 7.4).

## Local therapy

Topical therapy (Table 7.4) can achieve high tissue concentration
and initially should consist of broad-spectrum antibiotics that
cover most common pathogens. Initially instillation is at hourly
intervals day and night for 24ÔÇô48 hours and then is tapered according to clinical progress.
¯Çü Antibiotic monotherapy has the major advantage over duotherapy of lower surface toxicity, as well as greater convenience.

{ A commercially available ¯Ç×uoroquinolone is the usual
choice for empirical monotherapy and appears to be about
as e¯Ç£ective as duotherapy.

{ Cipro¯Ç×oxacin or o¯Ç×oxacin are used in countries where widespread resistance to earlier-generation ¯Ç×uoroquinolones has
not been identi¯ÇØed. Activity against some Gram-positive
organisms, particularly some streptococci, may be limited.

{ Resistance to ¯Ç×uoroquinolones has been reported in some
areas (e.g. Staphylococcus spp. in the USA and Pseudomonas in India). Moxi¯Ç×oxacin, gati¯Ç×oxacin and besi¯Ç×oxacin
are new generation ¯Ç×uoroquinolones that largely address
this and also have better activity against Gram-positive
pathogens. Moxi¯Ç×oxacin has superior ocular penetration.

## Management of apparent treatment failure

It is important not to confuse ongoing failure of re-epithelialization
with continued infection. Drug toxicity, particularly following frequent instillation of forti¯ÇØed aminoglycosides, may give increasing
discomfort, redness and dischargedespite the eradication of infection.
¯Çü If no improvement is evident following 24ÔÇô48 hours of intensive treatment, the antibiotic regimen should be reviewed,
including contact with the microbiology laboratory to obtain
the latest report.
¯Çü ¯Çƒere is no need to change the initial therapy if this has
induced a favourable response, even if cultures show a resistant organism.
¯Çü If there is still no improvement a¯Çøer a further 48 hours, suspension of treatment should be considered for 24 hours then
re-scraping performed with inoculation on a broader range
of media (see Table 7.2) and additional staining techniques
requested (see Table 7.3). Consideration should be given to the
possibility of a non-bacterial causative microorganism.
¯Çü If cultures remain negative, it may be necessary to perform a
corneal biopsy for histology and culture.
¯Çü Consider collagen cross-linking treatment (PACK-CXL) if
there is a suspicion of corneal melting or bacterial resistance.
¯Çü Excisional keratoplasty, penetrating or deep lamellar, may be
considered in cases resistant to medical therapy, or for incipient or actual perforation (see below).

TIP Topical drug and preservative toxicity may cause a failure
of corneal re-epithelialization, which can be confused with
persistent infection.

{ H. in¯Ç×uenzae infection should be treated with oral amoxicillin with clavulanic acid.

{ N. gonorrhoeae requires a third-generation cephalosporin
such as ce¯Çøriaxone.
¯Çü Severe corneal thinning with threatened or actual perforation
requires:

{ Cipro¯Ç×oxacin for its antibacterial activity.

TIP Fluoroquinolone eyedrops have limited e¯ÇØectivity against
Gram-positive organisms (particularly Streptococcus).

## Systemic antibiotics

Systemic antibiotics are not usually given, but may be appropriate
in the following circumstances:
¯Çü Potential for systemic involvement, when microbiological/
infectious disease specialist advice should optimally be sought
but should not delay treatment:

{ N. meningitidis, in which early systemic prophylaxis may
be life-saving. Treatment is usually with intramuscular benzylpenicillin, ce¯Çøriaxone or cefotaxime, or oral
cipro¯Ç×oxacin.

218 Fungal Keratitis

## Perforation

A small perforation in which infection is controlled may be manageable with a bandage contact lens. Tissue glue is o¯Çøen adequate
for a slightly larger dehiscence. A penetrating keratoplasty or
corneal patch gra¯Çø may be necessary for larger perforations, or
in those where infection is extensive or inadequately controlled.

## Occlusive surface repair techniques may be appropriate in some

circumstances, such as an eye with no useful visual potential.

## Endophthalmitis

No clear protocol exists for the management of this rare complication, but a similar approach to postoperative endophthalmitis
should be considered, whilst continuing speci¯ÇØc management of
the corneal infection (see Ch. 10). Secondary sterile intraocular
in¯Ç×ammation should not be mistaken for intraocular infection.

## Visual rehabilitation

¯Çü Keratoplasty (lamellar may be adequate) may be required for
residual dense corneal scarring.
¯Çü Rigid contact lenses may be required for irregular astigmatism but are generally only introduced at least 3 months a¯Çøer
re-epithelialization.
¯Çü Cataract surgery may be required because secondary lens
opacities are common following severe in¯Ç×ammation. Even
in the absence of severe corneal opaci¯ÇØcation, surgery may be
hampered by corneal haze, posterior synechiae and zonular
fragility.
¯Çü Prognosis depends on the size, location, depth and aetiology
of the corneal ulcer.

FUNGAL KERATITIS

## Introduction

## Pathogenesis

## Fungi are a group of microorganisms that have rigid walls and
a distinct nucleus with multiple chromosomes containing both

DNA and RNA. Fungal keratitis is rare in temperate countries but
is a major cause of visual loss in tropical and low-income countries. ¯Çƒough o¯Çøen evolving insidiously, fungal keratitis can elicit
a severe in¯Ç×ammatory response ÔÇô corneal perforation is common
and the outlook for vision is frequently poor. Two main types of
fungi cause keratitis:
¯Çü Yeasts (e.g. genus Candida), ovoid unicellular organisms that
reproduce by budding, are responsible for most cases of fungal
keratitis in temperate climates.
¯Çü Filamentous fungi (e.g. genera Fusarium and Aspergillus),
multicellular organisms that produce tubular projections
known as hyphae. ¯Çƒey are the most common pathogens in
tropical climates, but are not uncommon in cooler regions. ¯Çƒe
keratitis frequently follows an aggressive course.

## Predisposing factors

Common predisposing factors include chronic ocular surface disease, the long-term use of topical steroids (o¯Çøen in conjunction

{ Other stains include periodic acidÔÇôSchi¯Ç£, calco¯Ç×uor white
(Fig. 7.10D) and methenamine silver.
¯Çü Culture. Corneal scrapes should be plated on Sabouraud
dextrose agar, although most fungi will also grow on blood
agar or in enrichment media. It is important to obtain an
e¯Ç£ective scrape from the ulcer base. Sensitivity testing for
antifungal agents can be performed in reference laboratories
but the relevance of these results to clinical e¯Ç£ectiveness is
uncertain. If applicable, contact lenses and cases should be
sent for culture.

with prior corneal transplantation), contact lens wear, systemic
immunosuppression and diabetes. Filamentary keratitis may be
associated with trauma, o¯Çøen relatively minor, involving plant
matter or gardening/agricultural tools.

Candida and ¯Ç×lamentous keratitis

## Clinical features

¯Çƒe diagnosis is frequently delayed unless there is a high
index of suspicion and o¯Çøen bacterial infection will initially
have been presumed. Clinical signs are not a de¯ÇØnitive means
of distinguishing bacterial and fungal corneal infection. Signs
such as satellite in¯ÇØltrates (see below) can be caused by other
microorganisms.
¯Çü Symptoms. Gradual onset of pain, grittiness, photophobia,
blurred vision and watery or mucopurulent discharge.
¯Çü Candida keratitis

{ YellowÔÇôwhite densely suppurative in¯ÇØltrate is typical.
¯Çü Filamentous keratitis

{ Grey or yellowÔÇôwhite stromal in¯ÇØltrate with indistinct
¯Ç×u¯Ç£y margins (Fig. 7.10A).

{ Progressive in¯ÇØltration, o¯Çøen with satellite lesions (Fig.
7.10B and C) or a ring-shaped in¯ÇØltrate.

{ Feathery branch-like extensions may develop.

{ Rapid progression with necrosis and thinning can occur.

{ Penetration of an intact Descemet membrane may occur
and lead to endophthalmitis without evident perforation.
¯Çü An epithelial defect is not invariable and is sometimes small
when present.
¯Çü Other features include anterior uveitis, hypopyon, endothelial plaque, raised IOP, scleritis and sterile or infective
endophthalmitis.
¯Çü Di¯Çøerential diagnosis includes bacterial, herpetic and acanthamoebal keratitis. Bacterial infection may sometimes present in a subacute fashion, particularly when atypical organisms
are responsible. It is important to beware of co-infection,
including with an additional fungal species.

## Investigations

## Samples for laboratory investigation should be acquired before

commencing antifungal therapy.
¯Çü Staining

{ Potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation with direct
microscopic evaluation is a rapid diagnostic tool that can
be highly sensitive.

{ Gram and Giemsa staining are both about 50% sensitive.

A B

C D

epithelial defect and folds in Descemet membrane; (B) ring in¯Çƒltrate and satellite lesions
(arrow); (C) large ulcer with satellite lesions and hypopyon (arrow); (D) Candida mycology
stained with calco¯Ç×uor white. (Courtesy of S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

¯Çü Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) analysis of specimens is
rapid and highly sensitive (up to 90%) and may be the current
investigation of choice. Calcium-containing swabs can inhibit
polymerase activity and local collection protocols should be
ascertained prior to specimen collection.
¯Çü Corneal biopsy is indicated in suspected fungal keratitis in the
absence of clinical improvement a¯Çøer 3ÔÇô4 days and if no growth
develops from scrapings a¯Çøer a week. A 2ÔÇô3 mm block should be
taken, similar to scleral block excision during trabeculectomy.
Filamentous fungi tend to proliferate just anterior to Descemet
membrane and a deep stromal specimen may be required. ¯Çƒe
excised block is sent for culture and histopathological analysis.
¯Çü Anterior chamber tap has been advocated in resistant cases
with endothelial exudate, because organisms may penetrate
the endothelium.
¯Çü Confocal microscopy frequently permits identi¯ÇØcation of organisms in¯Çƒvivo, but is not widely available outside tertiary centres.

## Treatment

Improvement may be slow in comparison to bacterial infection.
¯Çü General measures are as for bacterial keratitis although hospital admission is usually required.
¯Çü Removal of the epithelium over the lesion may enhance penetration of antifungal agents. It may also be helpful to regularly
remove mucus and necrotic tissue with a spatula.
¯Çü Topical antifungals should initially be given hourly for 48
hours and then reduced as signs permit.

{ Candida infection is treated with amphotericin B 0.15% or
econazole 1% (alternatives include natamycin 5%, ¯Ç×uconazole 2%, clotrimazole 1% and voriconazole 1 or 2%).

{ Filamentous infection is treated with natamycin 5% or
econazole 1% (alternatives are amphotericin B 0.15%,
miconazole 1% and voriconazole 1 or 2%).

{ Natamycin is the only topical drug approved by the FDA
for use in mycotic keratitis.

220 Herpes Simplex Keratitis

¯Çü A broad-spectrum antibiotic might also be considered to
address or prevent bacterial co-infection.
¯Çü Cycloplegia as for bacterial keratitis.
¯Çü Subconjunctival ¯Ç×uconazole may be used in severe cases.
¯Çü Systemic antifungals may be given in severe cases, when
lesions are near the limbus and for suspected endophthalmitis. Options include voriconazole 400 mg twice daily for one
day then 200 mg twice daily, itraconazole 200 mg once daily,
reduced to 100 mg once daily, or ¯Ç×uconazole 200 mg twice
daily.
¯Çü Tetracycline (e.g. doxycycline 100 mg twice daily) may be
given for its anticollagenase e¯Ç£ect when there is signi¯ÇØcant
thinning.
¯Çü Perforation ÔÇô actual or impending ÔÇô is managed as for bacterial keratitis.
¯Çü Super¯Ç×cial keratectomy can be e¯Ç£ective to debulk a lesion.
¯Çü ¯Çƒerapeutic keratoplasty (penetrating or deep anterior lamellar) is considered when medical therapy is ine¯Ç£ective or following perforation.
¯Çü Anterior chamber washout with intracameral antifungal
injection may be considered for unresponsive cases in which
there is a stable corneal in¯ÇØltrate but enlarging endothelial
exudation.

## Microsporidial keratitis

## Introduction

Microsporidia (phylum Microspora) are obligate intracellular
single-celled parasites previously thought to be protozoa but now
reclassi¯ÇØed as fungi. ¯Çƒey rarely cause disease in the immunocompetent and until the advent of acquired immunode¯ÇØciency
syndrome (AIDS) were rarely pathogenic for humans. ¯Çƒe most
common general infection is enteritis and the most common ocular manifestation is keratoconjunctivitis.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Signs

{ Bilateral chronic di¯Ç£use PEK (Fig. 7.11A).

{ Unilateral slowly progressive deep stromal keratitis (Fig.
7.11B) may rarely a¯Ç£ect immunocompetent patients.

{ Sclerokeratitis and endophthalmitis are rare.
¯Çü Biopsy shows characteristic spores and intracellular parasites.
¯Çü PCR of scrapings may have relatively low sensitivity.

## Treatment

¯Çü Medical therapy of epithelial disease is with topical fumagillin.
Highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) for associated
AIDS may also help resolution. Stromal disease is treated with
a combination of topical fumagillin and oral albendazole 400
mg once daily for 2 weeks, repeated 2 weeks later with a second
course. Patients should be closely monitored for hepatic toxicity.
Long-term fumagillin treatment may be required and it is di¯ÇÜcult to eradicate the parasites in immunocompromised patients.
¯Çü Keratoplasty may be indicated although recurrence of disease
can occur in the gra¯Çø periphery. Cryotherapy to the residual
tissue may reduce this risk.

HERPES SIMPLEX KERATITIS

## Introduction

Herpetic eye disease is the most common infectious cause of corneal blindness in developed countries. As many as 60% of corneal
ulcers in developing countries may be the result of herpes simplex
virus and 10 million people worldwide may have herpetic eye disease. Approximately 10% of those who have any history of herpes
simplex keratitis (HSK) will eventually have a ¯ÇØnal visual acuity of
less than 6/60. Even in the patients who retain good vision, corneal opacities can result in aberrations, astigmatism and reduced

A B

in¯Çƒltrates. (Courtesy of S Tuft.)

quality of vision. In addition to visual loss, HSK also reduces the
quality of life of those a¯Ç£ected, even when the eye is not in¯Ç×amed.

Herpes simplex virus (HSV)

HSV is enveloped with a cuboidal capsule and has a linear doublestranded DNA genome. ¯Çƒe two subtypes are HSV-1 and HSV-2
and these reside in almost all neuronal ganglia. HSV-1 causes
infection above the waist (principally the face, lips and eyes),
whereas HSV-2 causes venereally acquired infection (genital herpes). Rarely HSV-2 may be transmitted to the eye through infected
secretions, either venereally or at birth (neonatal conjunctivitis).
HSV transmission is facilitated in conditions of crowding and poor
hygiene.

## Primary infection

Primary infection, without previous viral exposure, usually
occurs in childhood and is spread by droplet transmission, or
less frequently by direct inoculation. Due to protection by maternal antibodies, it is uncommon during the ¯ÇØrst 6 months of life.

## Occasionally severe neonatal systemic disease may occur in which
early diagnosis and intravenous antiviral treatment are critical to

reduce mortality and disability. ¯Çƒe presence of maternal antibodies means that dendritic corneal ulcers may be seen. Most primary infections with HSV are subclinical or cause only mild fever,
malaise and upper respiratory tract symptoms. Blepharitis and
follicular conjunctivitis may develop but are usually mild and selflimited. Treatment, if necessary, involves topical aciclovir ointment
for the eye and/or cream for skin lesions and occasionally oral antivirals. ¯Çƒere is unfortunately no evidence that antiviral treatment
at this stage reduces the likelihood of recurrent disease.

## Recurrent infection

Recurrent disease (reactivation in the presence of cellular and
humoral immunity) occurs as follows:
¯Çü A¯ÇØer primary infection the virus is carried to the sensory
ganglion for that dermatome (e.g. trigeminal ganglion) where
latent infection is established. Latent virus is incorporated in
host DNA and cannot be eradicated with presently available
treatment.
¯Çü Subclinical reactivation can periodically occur, during which
HSV is shed and patients are contagious.
¯Çü Clinical reactivation. A variety of stress factors such as fever,
hormonal change, ultraviolet radiation, trauma, or trigeminal
injury may cause clinical reactivation, when the virus replicates and is transported in the sensory axons to the periphery.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe pattern of disease depends on the site of reactivation,
which may be remote from the site of primary disease. Hundreds of reactivations can occur during a lifetime.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe rate of ocular recurrence a¯Çøer one episode is about 10%
at 1 year and 50% at 10 years. ¯Çƒe higher the number of previous attacks the greater the risk of recurrence.
¯Çü Risk factors for severe disease, which may be frequently
recurrent, include atopic eye disease, childhood, immunode¯ÇØciency or suppression, malnutrition, measles and malaria.
Inappropriate use of topical steroids may enhance the development of geographic ulceration (see below).

TIP Herpes simplex is the commonest infectious cause of
corneal disease in developed countries.

## Epithelial keratitis

## Clinical features

Epithelial (dendritic or geographic) keratitis is associated with
active virus replication.
¯Çü Symptoms. MildÔÇômoderate discomfort, redness, photophobia, watering and blurred vision.
¯Çü Signs in approximately chronological order:

{ Reduced visual acuity.

{ Swollen opaque epithelial cells arranged in a coarse punctate or stellate (Fig. 7.12A) pattern.

{ Central desquamation results in a linear-branching (dendritic) ulcer (Fig. 7.12B), most frequently located centrally.
¯Çƒe branches of the ulcer have characteristic terminal buds
and its bed stains well with ¯Ç×uorescein.

{ ¯Çƒe virus-laden cells at the margin of the ulcer stain with
rose Bengal (Fig. 7.12C). ¯Çƒis may help distinction of herpetic ulceration from alternative conditions, particularly
an atypical recurrent corneal abrasion.

{ Corneal sensation is reduced.

{ Inadvertent topical steroid treatment may promote progressive enlargement of the ulcer to a geographical or
ÔÇÿamoeboidÔÇÖ con¯ÇØguration (Fig. 7.12D).

{ Mild associated subepithelial haze is typical.

{ Anterior chamber activity may be present, but is usually
mild.

{ Follicular conjunctivitis may be associated; topical antivirals can also cause this.

{ Vesicular eyelid lesions may coincide with epithelial
ulceration.

{ Elevated IOP is not uncommon (tonometry should be performed on the una¯Ç£ected eye ¯ÇØrst and a disposable prism
used).

{ Following healing, there may be persistent PEE and
irregular epithelium that settle spontaneously and
should not be mistaken for persistent active infection.
A whorled epithelial appearance commonly results
from assiduous, especially prolonged, topical antiviral
instillation.

{ Mild subepithelial haze (Fig. 7.12E) may persist for
weeks a¯Çøer the epithelium heals. In some cases, mild
scarring may develop, which tends to become more evident a¯Çøer each recurrence and may eventually threaten
vision.

{ Recurrence may occur a¯Çøer a corneal gra¯Çø, undertaken for
stromal scarring (Fig. 7.12F).
¯Çü Investigation is usually unnecessary as the diagnosis can
be made clinically. Pre-treatment scrapings can be sent in
viral transport medium for culture. PCR and immunocytochemistry are also available. Giemsa staining shows
multinucleated giant cells. HSV serological titres rise only on
primary infection, but can be used to con¯ÇØrm previous viral

222 Herpes Simplex Keratitis

A B

C D

E F

with ¯Ç×uorescein; (C) margins of a dendritic ulcer stained with rose Bengal; (D) geographic ulcer;
(E) residual subepithelial haze; (F) recurrent ulceration after a corneal graft. (Courtesy of S Tuft

            - ¯Çƒg. C; T Carmichael ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. F.)

exposure, usually in cases of stromal disease when the diagnosis is in doubt.
¯Çü Di¯Çøerential diagnosis of dendritic ulceration includes herpes
zoster keratitis, healing corneal abrasion (pseudodendrite),
acanthamoeba keratitis, epithelial rejection in a corneal gra¯Çø,
tyrosinaemia type 2, the epithelial e¯Ç£ects of so¯Çø contact lenses
and toxic keratopathy secondary to topical medication. It has
recently been reported that dendritiform keratopathy can be
caused by polyquadernium-1, a common preservative in contact lens solutions and tear replacement products.

## Treatment

Treatment of HSV disease is predominantly with nucleoside
(purine or pyrimidine) analogues that disrupt viral DNA. ¯Çƒe
majority of dendritic ulcers will eventually heal spontaneously
without treatment, though scarring and vascularization may be
more signi¯ÇØcant.
¯Çü Topical. ¯Çƒe most frequently used drugs are aciclovir 3% ointment or ganciclovir 0.15% gel, each administered ¯ÇØve times
daily. Tri¯Ç×uridine is an alternative, but requires instillation up
to nine times daily until the epithelium closes and then ¯ÇØve
times daily. ¯Çƒe drugs are relatively non-toxic, even when
given for up to 60 days. ¯Çƒey have approximately equivalent
e¯Ç£ect, acting preferentially on virus-laden epithelial cells and
penetrating e¯Ç£ectively into the stroma; 99% of ulcers heal
within 2 weeks. Idoxuridine and vidarabine are older drugs
that are less e¯Ç£ective and are toxic to the corneal epithelium.
¯Çü Debridement may be used for resistant cases. ¯Çƒe corneal surface is wiped with a sterile cellulose sponge or cotton-tipped
applicator (cotton bud). Epithelium should be removed 2 mm
beyond the edge of the ulcer, since involvement extends beyond
the visible dendrite. ¯Çƒe removal of the virus-containing cells
protects adjacent healthy epithelium from infection and eliminates the antigenic stimulus to stromal in¯Ç×ammation. A topical antiviral agent should be used in conjunction.
¯Çü Signs of treatment toxicity include super¯ÇØcial punctate erosions, waves of whorled epithelium, follicular conjunctivitis
and, rarely, punctal occlusion. Absence of epithelial whorling
with a persistent epithelial lesion raises the possibility of poor
or non-compliance.
¯Çü Oral antiviral therapy (e.g. aciclovir 200ÔÇô400 mg ¯ÇØve times a
day for 5ÔÇô10 days, famciclovir or valaciclovir) is indicated in
most immunode¯ÇØcient patients, in children and patients with
marked ocular surface disease. It is an e¯Ç£ective alternative
to topical treatment when the latter is poorly tolerated, or in
resistant cases. ¯Çƒe newer oral agents may be better tolerated
than aciclovir and require less frequent dosing, but optimal
regimens are not yet de¯ÇØned.
¯Çü Interferon monotherapy does not seem to be more e¯Ç£ective
than antivirals, but the combination of a nucleoside antiviral
with either interferon or debridement seems to speed healing.
¯Çü Skin lesions (see Ch. 2) may be treated with aciclovir cream
¯ÇØve times daily, as for cold sores, and if extensive an oral antiviral may be given.
¯Çü Cycloplegia, e.g. homatropine 1% once or twice daily can be
given to improve comfort if necessary.

¯Çü Topical antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended by some
practitioners.
¯Çü Prostaglandin derivatives should be avoided if treatment is
required to control the IOP, as these drops promote herpes
virus activity.
¯Çü Topical steroids are not used unless signi¯ÇØcant disciform keratitis is also present (see below).
¯Çü Slow healing or frequent recurrence may indicate the presence of a resistant viral strain and an alternative topical agent
or debridement may be tried. In especially refractory cases,
a combination of two topical agents with oral valaciclovir or
famciclovir may be e¯Ç£ective. A signi¯ÇØcant minority of resistant
cases are due to varicella-zoster virus.

TIP Topical steroids should not be used in epithelial herpes
keratitis as this can result in worsening of the ulceration and
may lead to corneal perforation.

## Disciform keratitis

¯Çƒe aetiology of disciform keratitis (endotheliitis) is unclear. It may
be the result of active HSV infection of keratocytes or endothelium, or a hypersensitivity reaction to viral antigen in the cornea.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Blurred vision of gradual onset, which may be
associated with haloes around lights. Discomfort and redness
are common, but tend to be milder than in purely epithelial disease. A clear past history of epithelial ulceration is not always
present and the possibility of a mimicking infection such as
acanthamoeba or fungal keratitis should be borne in mind.
¯Çü Signs

{ A central zone of stromal oedema, o¯Çøen with overlying
epithelial oedema (Fig. 7.13A and B). Occasionally the
lesion is eccentric.

{ Large (granulomatous) keratic precipitates underlying the
oedema.

{ Folds in Descemet membrane in severe cases.

{ A surrounding (Wessely) immune ring of deep stromal
haze (Fig. 7.13C) signi¯ÇØes deposition of viral antigen and
host antibody complexes.

{ ¯Çƒe IOP may be elevated.

{ Reduced corneal sensation. ¯Çƒis may aid in distinguishing
other forms of infection.

{ Healed lesions o¯Çøen have a faint ring of stromal or subepithelial opaci¯ÇØcation and thinning.

{ Consecutive episodes may be associated with gradually worsening subepithelial and/or stromal scarring and
super¯ÇØcial or deep vascularization (Fig. 7.13D).

{ Mid-stromal scarring from disciform keratitis is a cause of
interstitial keratitis.

## Treatment

A broad approach to management is set out below, but regimens
should be tailored individually. Careful monitoring and adequate

224 Herpes Simplex Keratitis

A B

C D

membrane folds; (B) stromal oedema; (C) Wessely ring; (D) scarring with vascularization from
recurrent disease

treatment, dependent on severity of in¯Ç×ammation, is critical to
minimize progression of scarring. Patients should be cautioned to
seek treatment at the ¯ÇØrst suggestion of recurrence, though some
authorities feel that minimal in¯Ç×ammation may not warrant treatment or can be addressed with cycloplegia alone.
¯Çü Initial treatment is with topical steroids (prednisolone 1% or
dexamethasone 0.1%) with antiviral cover, both four times
daily. As improvement occurs, the frequency of administration of both is reduced in parallel over not less than 4 weeks.

## It is prudent to keep steroid intensity and duration to the

minimum required for e¯Ç£ective control of in¯Ç×ammation. IOP
should be monitored. Cycloplegia can be used to improve
comfort if necessary.
¯Çü Subsequently prednisolone 0.5% once daily is usually a safe
dose at which to stop topical antiviral cover. Some patients
require a weaker steroid such as ¯Ç×uorometholone 0.1% or
loteprednol 0.2% on alternate days for many months. Periodic
attempts should be made to stop the steroid altogether.
¯Çü With active epithelial ulceration it is reasonable to try to keep
the steroid intensity as low as possible for adequate e¯Ç£ect, with

a more frequent antiviral regimen, e.g. initially topical antiviral
¯ÇØve times daily, with steroid two or three times daily, titrated
according to the signs of activity of both. Oral antiviral treatment may be helpful but its e¯ÇÜcacy in this situation has not
been established.
¯Çü Oral steroids are sometimes used in severe stromal in¯Ç×ammation as an adjunct.
¯Çü Topical ciclosporin 0.05% may be useful, particularly in the
presence of epithelial ulceration and to facilitate tapering of
topical steroids such as in steroid-related IOP elevation.
¯Çü Fine needle diathermy and laser techniques have been reported
as successfully addressing established corneal neovascularization and improving vision.

## Necrotizing stromal keratitis

¯Çƒis rare condition is thought to result from active viral replication within the stroma, though immune-mediated in¯Ç×ammation is
likely to play a signi¯ÇØcant role. It may be di¯ÇÜcult to distinguish from
severe disciform keratitis and there may be a spectrum of disease,

including overlap with neurotrophic keratopathy. As with disciform
keratitis, a similar clinical picture may be caused by other infections.
¯Çü Signs

{ Stromal necrosis and melting, o¯Çøen with profound interstitial opaci¯ÇØcation (Fig. 7.14A).

{ Anterior uveitis with keratic precipitates underlying the
area of active stromal in¯ÇØltration.

{ An epithelial defect may be present.

{ Progression to scarring, vascularization (Fig. 7.14B) and
lipid deposition is common (see Fig. 7.3C)
¯Çü Treatment is broadly similar to that of aggressive disciform
keratitis, but oral antiviral supplementation, initially at the
upper end of the dose range, is commonly used. ¯Çƒe restoration of epithelial integrity is critical.

## Neurotrophic keratopathy

Neurotrophic keratopathy (see also separate topic) is caused by
failure of re-epithelialization resulting from corneal anaesthesia,
o¯Çøen exacerbated by other factors such as drug toxicity.
¯Çü Signs

{ A non-healing epithelial defect is an early sign, sometimes
a¯Çøer prolonged topical treatment (see Fig. 7.31A).

{ ¯Çƒe stroma beneath the defect is grey and opaque and may
become thin.

elevation is common and is o¯Çøen presumed to be due to trabeculitis. However, steroid-induced IOP elevation may also be relatively
common in herpetic iritis. ¯Çƒe aetiology may be missed unless
there is a history of previous herpes simplex keratitis. Patchy iris
atrophy (Fig. 7.15A) may provide a clue and transillumination
(Fig. 7.15B) may demonstrate subtle lesions. Aqueous sampling
for PCR is diagnostic. Treatment is primarily with topical steroids,
but adjunctive oral aciclovir may be given.

## Other considerations

## Prophylaxis

¯Çü Long-term oral aciclovir reduces the rate of recurrence of
epithelial and stromal keratitis by about 50% and is usually
tolerated well. Prophylaxis should be considered in patients
with frequent debilitating recurrences, particularly if bilateral or involving an only eye. ¯Çƒe standard daily dose of aciclovir is 400 mg twice daily for 12 months, but if necessary,
a higher dose can be tried, based on practice in the management of systemic herpes simplex infection. Continual use for
many years has been documented for systemic indications,
but it can result in aciclovir-resistant HSV-1 viruses. ¯Çƒe
prophylactic e¯Ç£ect decreases or disappears when the drug is
stopped. Excretion is via the kidney, so renal function should
be checked periodically during long-term treatment.
¯Çü Oral valaciclovir (500 mg once daily) or famciclovir are alternatives that are probably as e¯Ç£ective as aciclovir, require less
frequent dosing and may be better tolerated.
¯Çü Topical. Oral prophylaxis tends to be preferred to long-term
topical administration as epithelial toxicity may occur, leading
to mild blurring and persistent discomfort. Allergy and punctal stenosis are also potential problems.
¯Çü Vaccination. ¯Çƒerapeutic vaccination strategies are under
investigation.

{ Secondary bacterial or fungal infection may occur.
¯Çü Treatment is that of persistent epithelial defects. Topical steroids to control any in¯Ç×ammatory component should be kept
to a minimum.

## Iridocyclitis

## Herpetic iridocyclitis can occur without signs of active corneal

in¯Ç×ammation and may be associated with direct viral activity. IOP
