TIP Long-term use of oral aciclovir reduces the recurrence
rate of epithelial and stromal herpes keratitis.

## Complications

¯Çü Secondary infection. Herpetic eye disease is a major predisposing factor for microbial keratitis.
¯Çü Glaucoma secondary to inflammation or chronic steroid
use may progress undetected, particularly if there is a poor
view of the optic disc. Corneal thinning and distortion
may give rise to an inaccurate reading on applanation and
alternative forms of tonometry may be superior in these
cases.
¯Çü Stromal vascularization and lipid keratopathy. Topical
aganirsen results in a signi¯ÇØcant reduction in corneal vessels
and an improvement in the quality of life, but no signi¯ÇØcant
improvement in visual acuity is likely to follow.
¯Çü Cataract secondary to in¯Ç×ammation or prolonged steroid use.
¯Çü Iris atrophy secondary to kerato-uveitis (see Fig. 7.15).

## Keratoplasty

A trial of a rigid contact lens is o¯Çøen worthwhile prior to committing to surgery. Recurrence of herpetic eye disease and rejection
are common and threaten the survival of corneal gra¯Çøs.
¯Çü Topical antivirals given during a rejection episode may
reduce epithelial viral reactivation but toxicity may delay
re-epithelialization.
¯Çü Prophylactic oral aciclovir (400 mg twice daily) improves
gra¯Çø survival and should be given to patients undergoing penetrating keratoplasty for herpetic eye disease. It should also be
considered in patients with severe atopic eye disease but no
history of ocular HSV involvement. ¯Çƒe duration of treatment
and the optimum dose has not been established. Immunohistochemistry should be performed on the excised tissue to con¯ÇØrm the presence of herpes antigen.

HERPES ZOSTER OPHTHALMICUS

## Introduction

Herpes zoster is common, and it has been estimated that one in
three people will develop the condition in their lifetime. Most
patients are not immunocompromised.

## Pathogenesis

Herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO) is the term used for shingles
involving the dermatome supplied by the ophthalmic division of the
¯ÇØ¯Çøh cranial (trigeminal) nerve. Ocular involvement can also occur
(though is rarely clinically signi¯ÇØcant) when the disease a¯Ç£ects the
maxillary division alone. Varicella-zoster virus (VZV) causes both
chickenpox (varicella) and shingles (herpes zoster). VZV belongs to
the same subfamily of the herpes virus group as HSV ÔÇô the viruses
are morphologically identical, but antigenically distinct. A¯Çøer an
episode of chickenpox, the virus travels in a retrograde manner
to the dorsal root and cranial nerve sensory ganglia, where it may
remain dormant for decades, with reactivation thought to occur
a¯Çøer VZV-speci¯ÇØc cell-mediated immunity has faded. Re-exposure
to VZV via contact with chickenpox, or by vaccination, may reinforce immunity and protect against the development of shingles.

## Mechanisms of ocular involvement

¯Çü Direct viral invasion may lead to conjunctivitis and epithelial
keratitis.
¯Çü Secondary in¯Ç£ammation and occlusive vasculitis may cause
episcleritis, scleritis, keratitis, uveitis (including segmental iris
infarction), optic neuritis and cranial nerve palsies. In¯Ç×ammation and destruction of the peripheral nerves or central ganglia, or altered signal processing in the central nervous system
(CNS) may be responsible for post-herpetic neuralgia. Cicatrizing complications may arise following severe eyelid, periocular skin and conjunctival involvement.

¯Çü Reactivation causes necrosis and in¯Ç×ammation in the a¯Ç£ected
sensory ganglia, causing corneal anaesthesia that may result in
neurotrophic keratopathy.

## Risk of ocular involvement

¯Çü Hutchinson sign describes vesicles in the skin supplied by the
external nasal nerve, a branch of the nasociliary nerve supplying the tip, side and root of the nose and ophthalmic herpes
zoster (Fig. 7.16A). Contrary to previous teaching, HutchinsonÔÇÖs sign may not be predictive of ocular involvement, as
both false-positive and false-negative ¯ÇØndings are common.
However, a rash in the distribution of the supratrochlear nerve
(lower part of the forehead) is predictive. ¯Çƒere is no apparent
correlation between the severity of the rash and that of ocular
complications.
¯Çü Age. HZO occurs most frequently in the sixth and seventh
decades. In the elderly, signs and symptoms tend to be more
severe than in the young and of longer duration.
¯Çü AIDS patients tend to have severe disease, and shingles can be
an early indicator of human immunode¯ÇØciency virus (HIV)
infection. A lower threshold for HIV testing should be adopted
in populations at particular risk. ¯Çƒe development of shingles
in children or young adults classically has prompted a search
for immunode¯ÇØciency or malignancy, though the requirement
for this has been questioned as an abnormality will be found in
only a small minority.

TIP All patients with herpes zoster involving the frontal nerve
(V1) should be fully evaluated for ocular involvement.

## Acute shingles

## General features

¯Çü A prodromal phase precedes the appearance of the rash. It
lasts 3ÔÇô5 days and is characterized by tiredness, fever, malaise
and headache. Symptoms involving the a¯Ç£ected dermatome
vary from a super¯ÇØcial itching, tingling or burning sensation
to a severe boring or lancing pain that is either constant or
intermittent. Older patients with early severe pain and a larger
area of involvement are at particular risk of post-herpetic
neuralgia.
¯Çü Skin lesions

{ Painful erythematous areas with a maculopapular rash
develop (Fig. 7.16B) and may be confused with cellulitis or
contact dermatitis.

{ ¯Çƒe rash respects the midline, which may aid in distinguishing shingles from HSV skin infection. Pain is also
markedly worse in shingles. Bilateral disease is very rare.

{ Within 24 hours, groups of vesicles (Fig. 7.16C) appear
and these become con¯Ç×uent over 2ÔÇô4 days.

{ Although the rash itself does not a¯Ç£ect the lower eyelid in
HZO, boggy oedema of the upper and lower lids is common (see Fig. 7.16B) and o¯Çøen spreads to the contralateral
side of the face (particularly if the patient sleeps on the side
opposite to the infection).

{ ¯Çƒe vesicles o¯Çøen pass through a pustular phase before
they crust (Fig. 7.16D) and dry a¯Çøer 2ÔÇô3 weeks.

{ Large, deep haemorrhagic lesions are more common in
immunode¯ÇØcient patients (Fig. 7.16E).

{ ¯Çƒe lesions heal to leave residual skin destruction and
depigmented scars (Fig. 7.16F).

{ Zoster sine herpete is shingles without a rash and may be
more common than previously realized.
¯Çü Disseminated zoster involving multiple dermatomes and
organ systems may develop in immunode¯ÇØciency or malignancy and with the advent of PCR testing, complications such
as meningoencephalitis have increasingly been identi¯ÇØed in
immunocompetent individuals.
¯Çü Investigation. In the event that clinical diagnosis is uncertain,
typically in immunode¯ÇØciency, vesicular ¯Ç×uid can be sent for
PCR, or immunomicroscopy. A viraemia lasting a few days
occurs in acute shingles. PCR of plasma for VZV DNA may be
positive (40%), especially in immunosuppressed patients and
can be particularly useful if zoster sine herpete is suspected.
IgM antibodies to VZV are found in only a minority of early
stage and convalescent patients.

## Treatment

¯Çü Oral antiviral treatment, optimally given within 72 hours of
rash onset, reduces the severity and duration of the acute episode and the risk of post-herpetic neuralgia. ¯Çƒe incidence of
late ophthalmic complications is also reduced by about 50%.
Patients presenting later than 72 hours but still at the vesicular
stage also derive bene¯ÇØt from treatment. Aciclovir (800 mg ¯ÇØve
times daily for 7ÔÇô10 days) has been the mainstay of treatment,
but newer agents such as valaciclovir 1 g three times daily or
famciclovir 250ÔÇô500 mg three times daily have more convenient regimens, are better tolerated and are at least as e¯Ç£ective as aciclovir. Brivudine is available in some countries. Fatal
interactions with 5-¯Ç×uoropyrimidines have been reported
and it should not be used in combination with even regional
5-¯Ç×uorouracil.
¯Çü Intravenous aciclovir 5ÔÇô10 mg/kg three times daily is generally indicated only for severe disease, particularly encephalitis
and for moderateÔÇôsevere immunocompromise.
¯Çü Systemic steroids (e.g. prednisolone 60 mg daily for 4 days,
then 40 mg for 4 days, then 20 mg for 4 days) remain somewhat controversial but are commonly used in moderateÔÇôsevere
disease, particularly for neurological complications. ¯Çƒey
should be given only in conjunction with a systemic antiviral and should probably be avoided in immunode¯ÇØciency. A
moderate reduction in acute pain and accelerated skin healing
is conferred, but steroids have no e¯Ç£ect on the incidence or
severity of post-herpetic neuralgia.
¯Çü Immunocompromised patients require the input of an infectious disease specialist. Antiviral treatment should be extended
and intravenous treatment may be optimal. Systemic steroids
should probably be avoided.
¯Çü Symptomatic treatment of skin lesions is by drying, antisepsis
and cold compresses. ¯Çƒe bene¯ÇØt of topical antibiotic-steroid
combinations is uncertain.

avoided at least until crusting is complete.
¯Çü VZV uveitis is considered in depth in Chapter 12

## Prevention

Vaccination against herpes zoster reduces the incidence of HZO.

## Two vaccines are available and both are approved for individuals

over the age of 50 years.
¯Çü Zoster vaccine live is an attenuated live virus with an important limitation that e¯ÇÜcacy is lost within 10 years.
¯Çü Recombinant zoster vaccine requires two injections and has a
10% incidence of local or acute systemic reaction.

## Eye disease

## Acute eye disease

¯Çü Acute epithelial keratitis develops in over 50% of patients
within 2 days of the onset of the rash and usually resolves

spontaneously within a few days. It is characterized by dendritic lesions that are smaller and ¯ÇØner than herpes simplex
dendrites and have tapered ends without terminal bulbs (Fig.
7.17A). ¯Çƒe lesions stain better with rose Bengal than with
¯Ç×uorescein (Fig. 7.17B). Treatment, if required, is with a topical antiviral.
¯Çü Conjunctivitis (follicular and/or papillary) is common and
o¯Çøen occurs in conjunction with lid margin vesicles. Treatment is not required in the absence of corneal disease, though
some practitioners give topical antibiotic and/or antiviral
prophylaxis.
¯Çü Episcleritis occurs at the onset of the rash and usually resolves
spontaneously. A mild non-steroidal anti-in¯Ç×ammatory may
be used if necessary.
¯Çü Scleritis and sclerokeratitis are uncommon but may develop at
the end of the ¯ÇØrst week. Treatment of indolent lesions is with
oral ¯Ç×urbiprofen 100 mg three times daily. Oral steroids with
antiviral cover may be required for severe involvement.
¯Çü Nummular keratitis usually develops at the site of epithelial lesions about 10 days a¯Çøer the onset of the rash. It is

iris atrophy on transillumination; (D) scleral atrophy; (E) staphyloma with severe corneal scarring; (F) mucous plaque keratitis

characterized by ¯ÇØne granular subepithelial deposits surrounded by a halo of stromal haze (Fig. 7.17C). ¯Çƒe lesions
fade in response to topical steroids but recur if treatment is

¯Çü Stromal (interstitial) keratitis (Fig. 7.17D) develops in about
5% 3 weeks a¯Çøer the onset of the rash and signi¯ÇØcant scarring
can occur (Fig. 7.18A). It usually responds to topical steroids
but can become chronic and require slow tapering.
¯Çü Disciform keratitis (immune-mediated endotheliitis) is less
common than with herpes simplex infection but may lead to

¯Çü Anterior uveitis a¯Ç£ects at least a third of patients and can
be associated with sectoral iris ischaemia and atrophy (Fig.
7.18B and C).
¯Çü Posterior uveitis (see Ch. 12). Progressive retinal necrosis is

## Posterior segment examination should always be performed

in patients with HZO as retinal vasculitis may occasionally
occur.
¯Çü IOP should be monitored as elevation is common, including
steroid-induced. Prostaglandin derivatives should be avoided
if treatment is necessary.
¯Çü Neurological complications may require intravenous antivirals and systemic steroids.

be the only diagnostic clue. Reactivation of keratitis, episcleritis,
scleritis or iritis can occur.

Post¯Çƒherpetic neuralgia

Post-herpetic neuralgia is de¯ÇØned as pain that persists for more
than one month a¯Çøer the rash has healed. It develops in up to 75%
of patients over 70 years of age. Pain may be constant or intermittent, worse at night and aggravated by minor stimuli (allodynia),
touch and heat. It generally improves slowly over time, with only
2% of patients a¯Ç£ected a¯Çøer 5 years. Neuralgia can impair the quality of life and may lead to depression of su¯ÇÜcient severity to present
a danger of suicide. Patients severely a¯Ç£ected should be referred to
a specialist pain clinic. Treatment may involve the following:
¯Çü Local

{ Cold compresses.

{ Topical capsaicin 0.075% or lidocaine 5% patches.
¯Çü Systemic treatment may be used in a staged fashion:

{ Simple analgesics such as paracetamol.

{ Stronger analgesics such as codeine.

{ Tricyclic antidepressants, e.g. nortriptyline, amitriptyline,
initially 25 mg nightly adjusted up to 75 mg for several
weeks if necessary.

{ Carbamazepine 400 mg daily for lancinating pain.

{ Cranial nerve palsy a¯Ç£ecting the third (most common),
fourth and sixth nerves usually recovers within 6 months.

{ Gabapentin (300ÔÇô600 mg up to three times daily),
sustained-release oxycodone (10ÔÇô30 mg twice daily), or
both.

{ Optic neuritis is rare.

{ CNS manifestations are rare but include encephalitis, cranial arteritis and GuillainÔÇôBarr├® syndrome.

INTERSTITIAL KERATITIS

## Introduction

Interstitial keratitis (IK) is an in¯Ç×ammation of the corneal stroma
without primary involvement of the epithelium or endothelium.
In most cases, the in¯Ç×ammation is thought to be an immunemediated process triggered by an appropriate antigen. ¯Çƒe term
is most commonly used to refer to the late appearance of feathery mid-stromal scarring with ghost vessels rather than the acute
presentation. ¯Çƒe former is typically an incidental ¯ÇØnding. Syphilitic IK is the archetype, but the relative frequency of causes varies
markedly by geographic region. ¯Çƒere is a wide range of causes,
including herpes simplex, varicella zoster and other viral infections, tuberculosis, Lyme disease and other infections (parasitic
diseases are an important cause in areas where these are endemic),
sarcoidosis, Cogan syndrome and non-infectious in¯Ç×ammatory
conditions.

Syphilitic IK

IK of syphilitic origin is usually the result of congenital infection,
though acquired syphilis can also be responsible. All patients with
acute IK, or with the incidental discovery of its chronic appearance, should be investigated to exclude congenital (and acquired)
syphilis, irrespective of the presence of systemic clinical signs.
¯Çƒere are numerous reported cases of congenital syphilis being
identi¯ÇØed for the ¯ÇØrst time in later life on this basis.

## Chronic eye disease

¯Çü Neurotrophic keratopathy similar to that seen in HSV infection develops in up to 50%, but is usually relatively mild and
settles over several months. Prolonged severe disease occurs in
a minority (see also separate topic).
¯Çü Scleritis may become chronic and lead to patchy scleral atrophy (Fig. 7.18D). Staphyloma formation is rare (Fig. 7.18E).
¯Çü Mucous plaque keratitis develops in about 5%, most commonly between the third and sixth months. It is characterized by elevated mucous plaques staining with rose
Bengal (Fig. 7.18F). Treatment involves a combination of topical
steroid and acetylcysteine. Untreated, plaques resolve a¯Çøer a
few months, leaving a faint di¯Ç£use corneal haze.
¯Çü Lipid degeneration may develop in eyes with persistent severe
nummular or disciform keratitis (see Fig. 7.18A).
¯Çü Lipid-¯Ç×lled granulomas similar to those resulting from
chronic irritation may develop in the tarsal conjunctiva and
may progress to erosive calci¯ÇØed concretions.
¯Çü Subconjunctival scarring may occur.
¯Çü Eyelid scarring may result in ptosis, cicatricial entropion and
occasionally ectropion, trichiasis, lid notching and madarosis.

## Relapsing eye disease

In the relapsing phase lesions may reappear years a¯Çøer an acute
episode, which may have been forgotten and eyelid scarring may

232 Interstitial Keratitis

## Congenital syphilis

Infection of the fetus can occur across the placenta, leading to stillbirth, subclinical infection, or a range of clinical features.
¯Çü Early systemic features include failure to thrive, a maculopapular rash, mucosal ulcers, characteristic ¯ÇØssures around the
lips (rhagades) and a range of organ involvement.
¯Çü Late systemic signs include sensorineural deafness, saddleshaped nasal deformity (Fig. 7.19A), sabre tibiae (Fig. 7.19B),
bulldog jaw (mandibular prominence due to maxillary underdevelopment), Hutchinson teeth (notched, small, widely
spaced teeth ÔÇô Fig. 7.19C) and Clutton joints (painless e¯Ç£usions in large joints, especially the knees).
¯Çü Ocular features include anterior uveitis, IK (see below), dislocated/subluxated lens, cataract, optic atrophy, salt and pepper pigmentary retinopathy (Fig. 7.20) and Argyll Robertson
pupils.

Presentation of syphilitic IK

¯Çü Symptoms. ¯Çƒe presentation of IK following congenital
syphilitic infection is usually between the ages of 5 and 25
years. ¯Çƒe initial symptoms are those of acute anterior uveitis with severe blurring. Involvement is bilateral in 80%,
although usually not simultaneous. In acquired disease IK
is less common and usually unilateral, typically occurring
years a¯Çøer the age at which the disease was contracted,
although it can occur as part of the syndrome of primary
infection.
¯Çü Signs

{ Profoundly decreased visual acuity is typical in the active
stage.

{ Limbitis associated with deep stromal vascularization,
with cellular in¯ÇØltration and clouding that may obscure
the still-perfused vessels to give the characteristic pinkish
ÔÇÿsalmon patchÔÇÖ appearance (Fig. 7.21A).

{ Granulomatous anterior uveitis.

{ A¯Çøer several months the cornea begins to clear and the
vessels become non-perfused (ÔÇÿghost vesselsÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 7.21B).

{ If the cornea later becomes in¯Ç×amed, the vessels may re¯ÇØll with blood and may rarely bleed into the stroma (Fig.
7.21C).

A

B

C

sexes a¯Ç£ected equally; children can also be a¯Ç£ected. Systemic features occur in 30% and may include multisystem vasculitis that can
be life-threatening; multispecialty management is vital.

{ ¯Çƒe healed stage is characterized by ghost vessels, feathery
deep stromal scarring (Fig. 7.21D) and sometimes thinning, astigmatism and band keratopathy.
¯Çü Treatment of active syphilitic IK is with topical steroids and
cycloplegics, as well as immediate systemic therapy under the
care of a genitourinary or infectious diseases specialist.

## Cogan syndrome

## Introduction

Cogan syndrome is a rare systemic autoimmune vasculitis characterized by intraocular in¯Ç×ammation and vestibuloauditory dysfunction (particularly neurosensory) developing within months of
each other. ¯Çƒe disease primarily occurs in young adults, with both

## Diagnosis

Ocular and inner ear symptoms are o¯Çøen separated by a substantial period; the acute phase may last from months to years. Susac
syndrome (retinocochleocerebral vasculopathy) should be considered in the di¯Ç£erential diagnosis.
¯Çü Vestibuloauditory symptoms. Deafness, tinnitus and vertigo.
¯Çü Ocular symptoms. Redness, pain, photophobia and blurred
vision.
¯Çü Ocular signs. Corneal involvement commences with faint bilateral peripheral anterior stromal opacities. Deeper opacities and
corneal neovascularization (mid-stromal vascular loops) then
ensue (Fig. 7.22A), o¯Çøen with central progression (Fig. 7.22B).
Uveitis, scleritis and retinal vasculitis may develop.
¯Çü Investigations

syphilitic infection.

{ Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) may be elevated and the white cell count raised.

{ MRI may show inner ear and other abnormalities.

{ Antibodies to inner ear antigens may be detectable.

C D

re-perfused vessels; (D) typical feathery scarring, showing the tracks of ghost vessels (arrow).

234 Protozoan Keratitis

A B

scarring.

## Treatment

¯Çü Topical steroids for keratitis, with additional measures as
appropriate.
¯Çü Systemic steroids. Vestibuloauditory symptoms require
immediate treatment with 1ÔÇô2 g/kg prednisolone to prevent hearing loss. Immunosuppressive therapy may also be
required. Systemic steroids may also be required for scleritis
or retinal vasculitis.

PROTOZOAN KERATITIS

## Acanthamoeba

## Introduction

Acanthamoeba spp. are ubiquitous free-living protozoa commonly
found in soil, fresh or brackish water and the upper respiratory
tract. ¯Çƒe cystic form (Fig. 7.23A) is highly resilient. Under appropriate environmental conditions, the cysts turn into trophozoites,
with tissue penetration and destruction. In developed countries
acanthamoeba keratitis is most frequently associated with contact
lens wear, especially if tap water is used for rinsing.

## Diagnosis

¯Çƒe condition is o¯Çøen diagnosed initially as herpes simplex keratitis. With more advanced signs the condition should be distinguished from fungal keratitis.
¯Çü Symptoms. Blurred vision and discomfort. Pain is o¯Çøen severe
and characteristically disproportionate to the clinical signs.
¯Çü Signs

{ Gradual enlargement and coalescence of in¯ÇØltrates to form
a ring abscess (Fig. 7.23D and E) is typical.

{ Scleritis may develop and is generally reactive rather than
an extension of infection.

{ Slowly progressive stromal opaci¯ÇØcation and
vascularization.

{ Corneal melting may occur at any stage when there is stromal disease. ¯Çƒe melt o¯Çøen develops at the periphery of the
area of in¯ÇØltrate (Fig. 7.23F).
¯Çü Investigations

{ Staining of corneal scrapings using periodic acidÔÇôSchi¯Ç£
or calco¯Ç×uor white (a ¯Ç×uorescent dye with an a¯ÇÜnity for
amoebic cysts and fungi). Gram and Giemsa stains may
also demonstrate cysts.

{ Culture. Non-nutrient agar seeded with dead E. coli, which
trophozoites consume.

{ Other investigations include immunohistochemistry, PCR
and in¯Çƒvivo confocal microscopy. Corneal biopsy may be
necessary for diagnosis.

{ In early disease the epithelial surface is irregular and greyish (Fig. 7.23B).

{ Epithelial pseudodendrites resembling herpetic lesions
may form.

{ Limbitis with di¯Ç£use or focal anterior stromal in¯ÇØltrates.

{ Characteristic perineural in¯ÇØltrates (radial keratoneuritis) are seen during the ¯ÇØrst few weeks and are virtually
pathognomonic (Fig. 7.23C).

TIP In a patient with acanthamoeba keratitis a good visual
outcome is achieved by making a speedy diagnosis, avoiding
a misdiagnosis of herpes keratitis and avoiding the use of
corticosteroids before commencing speci¯Çƒc treatment.

## Prevention

Teach contact lens wearers how to clean their contact lenses.

## Reduce their risk of contracting this disease by warning them that

they should not use tap water to clean their lenses.

## Treatment

¯Çü Early treatment. It is important to maintain a high index
of suspicion for Acanthamoeba in any patient with a limited
response to antibacterial therapy. ¯Çƒe outcome is very much
better if treatment is started early.
¯Çü Debridement of involved epithelium is believed to be helpful,
as it may facilitate eye drop penetration.

(Courtesy of S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. F.)

{ Simultaneous antibacterial treatment for co-infection may

{ Relapses are common as treatment is tapered and it may be

the blood vessels) with a secondary lymphocytic in¯ÇØltration. ¯Çƒe
lesions are culture-negative but S. aureus can frequently be isolated
from the lid margins.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Mild discomfort, redness and lacrimation. ¯Çƒe
in¯Ç×ammation may involve both eyes.
¯Çü Signs

{ Inferior punctate epitheliopathy is an early manifestation.

{ Subepithelial marginal in¯ÇØltrates separated from the limbus by a clear zone, o¯Çøen associated with an adjacent area
of conjunctival hyperaemia (Fig. 7.24A).

{ Characteristically, any epithelial defect will be considerably smaller than the area of in¯ÇØltrate.

{ Coalescence and circumferential spread (Fig. 7.24B and C).

{ Usually little or no anterior chamber reaction, even with
large in¯ÇØltrates.

{ Without treatment, resolution generally occurs in 1ÔÇô4
weeks, depending on severity. Occasionally there is residual super¯ÇØcial scarring and slight thinning with mild
pannus (Fig. 7.24D). Iris new vessels may develop in the

in¯Ç×ammation settles.

{ Chronic blepharitis is typical.

{ Oral miltefosine can be considered in patients where there has

## Amoebicidal treatment should be continued in concert with

and for several weeks a¯Çøer steroids.
¯Çü Pain control is with an oral non-steroidal anti-in¯Ç×ammatory
agent.
¯Çü Corneal cross-linking treatment (PACK-CXL) can be considered
in severe cases, but two sessions of treatment are o¯Çøen needed.

including perforation. Late scarring may also require penetrating keratoplasty.

TIP Acanthamoeba infection of the cornea results in pain that
is often severe and disproportionate to the clinical signs.

HELMINTHIC KERATITIS

## Onchocerciasis

Onchocerciasis (ÔÇÿriver blindnessÔÇÖ) is discussed in Chapter 12. Keratitis is a common feature.

BACTERIAL HYPERSENSITIVITYMEDIATED CORNEAL DISEASE

## Marginal keratitis

## Introduction

## Marginal keratitis is believed to be caused by a hypersensitivity
reaction against staphylococcal exotoxins and cell wall proteins

## Treatment

A weak topical steroid such as ¯Ç×uorometholone or prednisolone
0.5% is instilled four times daily for 1ÔÇô2 weeks, sometimes combined with a topical antibiotic. An oral tetracycline course (erythromycin in children, breastfeeding mothers and in pregnancy) may
be required for recurrent disease. Blepharitis is treated as necessary.

## Phlyctenulosis

## Introduction

Phlyctenulosis is usually a self-limiting disease but may rarely be
severe. Most cases in high-income countries are the result of a presumed delayed hypersensitivity reaction to staphylococcal antigen,
sometimes associated with rosacea. In low-income countries, the
majority are associated with tuberculosis or helminthic infestation,
but causation may be uncertain and a range of other agents has
been implicated.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Photophobia, lacrimation and blepharospasm,
o¯Çøen in a child or young adult.
¯Çü Signs

{ A small white limbal (Fig. 7.25A) or conjunctival nodule
(Fig. 7.25B) associated with intense local hyperaemia.

{ A limbal phlycten may extend onto the cornea (Fig. 7.25C
and D).

{ Spontaneous resolution usually occurs within 2ÔÇô3 weeks.
A healed lesion o¯Çøen leaves a triangular limbal-based scar
associated with super¯ÇØcial vascularization and thinning

A B

C D

but occasionally severe thinning and perforation can
ensue.

{ Very large necrotizing or multiple (miliary) lesions may
occur.
¯Çü Investigation for tuberculosis is generally indicated only in
endemic areas or in the presence of speci¯ÇØc risk factors.

## Treatment

A short course of topical steroid accelerates healing and is o¯Çøen
given with a topical antibiotic. Recurrent troublesome disease may
require an oral tetracycline and it is important to treat associated
blepharitis.

ROSACEA

## Introduction

Rosacea (acne rosacea) is a common chronic idiopathic dermatosis
involving the sun-exposed skin of the face and upper neck. Ocular
complications develop in 6ÔÇô18% of patients. Facial telangiectasia,
papule and pustule formation, rhinophyma and facial ¯Ç×ushing may

{ Focal or di¯Ç£use corneal thinning in severe cases, usually in
the inferior cornea.

occur (Fig. 7.26A). In contrast to acne vulgaris, comedones (blackheads or whiteheads) are absent.
¯Çƒe aetiology is probably multifactorial and may involve vascular factors, together with an abnormal response to commensal
skin bacteria and Demodex follicular mites. Exacerbation by H.
pylori infection has been suspected.

## Ocular rosacea

¯Çü Symptoms include non-speci¯ÇØc irritation and lacrimation.
¯Çü Lid signs include margin telangiectasia (Fig. 7.26B) and posterior blepharitis, o¯Çøen associated with recurrent meibomian
cyst formation.
¯Çü Conjunctival hyperaemia, especially bulbar. Rarely, cicatricial
conjunctivitis, conjunctival granulomas and phlyctenulosis
may occur.
¯Çü Cornea

{ Inferior punctate epithelial erosions.

{ Peripheral vascularization in an in¯Ç×amed eye (Fig. 7.26C).

PERIPHERAL CORNEAL ULCERATION/
THINNING

## Introduction

Peripheral corneal ulceration/thinning, known as ÔÇÿperipheral
ulcerative keratitisÔÇÖ (PUK) when in¯Ç×ammatory, refers to a presentation characterized by thinning and/or ulceration preferentially
a¯Ç£ecting the peripheral rather than central cornea and spreading

A B

C D

around the margin. It should be noted that any cause of corneal
ulceration can a¯Ç£ect the periphery.
¯Çü Marginal keratitis. ¯Çƒis is discussed above.
¯Çü Mooren ulcer.
¯Çü Terrien marginal degeneration.
¯Çü Dellen. Localized corneal disturbance associated with drying
of a focal area, usually associated with an adjacent elevated
lesion (e.g. pinguecula or a large subconjunctival haemorrhage

  - Fig. 7.27A) that impairs physiological lubrication. Generally
mild though can occasionally be severe, including descemetocoele formation/corneal perforation.
¯Çü Associated with systemic autoimmune disease.
¯Çü Others. Ocular rosacea, furrow degeneration (mild peripheral
thinning in the elderly, usually benign ÔÇô Fig. 7.27B), pellucid
marginal degeneration.

## Mooren ulcer

## Introduction

## Mooren ulcer is a rare autoimmune disease characterized by progressive circumferential peripheral stromal ulceration with later

central spread. It may represent a host reaction to calgranulin C,
a normal hidden antigen in corneal keratocytes. ¯Çƒere are two
forms: the ¯ÇØrst a¯Ç£ects mainly older patients, o¯Çøen in only one eye
and usually responds well to medical therapy. ¯Çƒe second is more
aggressive and likely to need systemic immunosuppression, carries a poorer prognosis, may be bilateral and associated with severe
pain and tends to occur in younger patients, including widespread
reports in South Asian men. In at least some (usually milder) cases,
there is a precipitating corneal insult such as surgery or infection.

## Associated systemic autoimmune disease and corneal infection

should always be ruled out.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Pain is prominent and may be severe. ¯Çƒere is
photophobia and blurred vision.
¯Çü Signs

{ Peripheral ulceration involving the super¯ÇØcial one-third
of the stroma, with variable epithelial loss. Several distinct
foci may be present and subsequently coalesce.

{ An undermined and in¯ÇØltrated leading edge is characteristic (Fig. 7.28A).

240 Peripheral Corneal Ulceration/Thinning

A B

surgery; (B) extensive furrow degeneration. (Courtesy of S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

A B

edge in advanced disease.

{ Limbitis may be present, but not scleritis, which aids in
distinguishing from systemic disease-associated PUK.

{ Progressive circumferential and central stromal thinning
(Fig. 7.28B).

{ Vascularization involving the bed of the ulcer up to its
leading edge but not beyond.

{ ¯Çƒe healing stage is characterized by thinning, vascularization and scarring.

{ Iritis is not uncommon.
¯Çü Complications include severe astigmatism, perforation following minor trauma (spontaneous perforation is rare), secondary bacterial infection, cataract and glaucoma.

TIP Peripheral ulcerative keratitis is caused by an infection
until proven otherwise. In the absence of an infection
investigate for systemic autoimmune disease.

## Treatment

¯Çü Topical steroids as frequently as hourly are combined with a
low-frequency prophylactic topical antibiotic. If an e¯Ç£ective
response is seen, treatment is tapered over several months.
¯Çü Topical ciclosporin (up to 2%) may be e¯Ç£ective, but can take
weeks to exert a signi¯ÇØcant e¯Ç£ect.
¯Çü Tacrolimus 0.1% ointment is e¯Ç£ective in controlling refractory cases.
¯Çü Adjunctive topical therapy includes arti¯ÇØcial tears and collagenase inhibitors such as acetylcysteine 10ÔÇô20%.
¯Çü Conjunctival resection, which may be combined with excision of necrotic tissue, is performed if there is no response to
topical steroids. ¯Çƒe excised area should extend 4 mm back
from the limbus and 2 mm beyond the circumferential margins. Keratoepithelioplasty (suturing of a donor corneal lenticule onto the scleral bed) may be combined to produce a

physical barrier against conjunctival regrowth and further
melting. Steroids are continued postoperatively.
¯Çü Systemic immunosuppression may be needed, including steroids for rapid e¯Ç£ect, and should be instituted earlier for bilateral disease, or if involvement is advanced at ¯ÇØrst examination.
Biological blockers show some promise, e.g. rituximab
¯Çü Systemic collagenase inhibitors such as doxycycline may be
bene¯ÇØcial.
¯Çü Lamellar keratectomy involving dissection of the residual
central island in advanced disease may remove the stimulus
for further in¯Ç×ammation.
¯Çü Perforations. Management is as discussed earlier in this chapter.
¯Çü Visual rehabilitation. Keratoplasty (with immunosuppressive
cover) may be considered once in¯Ç×ammation has settled.

## Peripheral ulcerative keratitis
associated with systemic autoimmune
disease

## Introduction

PUK may precede or follow the onset of systemic features. Severe
peripheral corneal in¯ÇØltration (Fig.7.29A), ulceration (Fig. 7.29B)
or thinning unexplained by evident ocular disease should prompt
investigation for a (potentially life-threatening) systemic collagen vascular disorder. ¯Çƒe mechanism includes immune complex
deposition in peripheral cornea, episcleral and conjunctival capillary occlusion with secondary cytokine release and in¯Ç×ammatory
cell recruitment, the upregulation of collagenases and reduced
activity of their inhibitors. Systemic associations include:
¯Çü Rheumatoid arthritis (RA ÔÇô the most common). PUK is bilateral in 30% and tends to occur in advanced RA (Fig.7.29BÔÇôD).
¯Çü Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener granulomatosis)
is the second most common systemic association of PUK (Fig.
7.29 E and F). In contrast to RA, ocular complications are the
initial presentation in 50%.
¯Çü Other conditions include polyarteritis nodosa, relapsing polychondritis, systemic lupus erythematosus and Crohn disease
(see Fig. 7.29A).

TIP Peripheral ulcerative keratitis in the absence of other
ocular disease should prompt investigation for a systemic
autoimmune disease.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Crescentic ulceration with an epithelial defect, thinning and
stromal in¯ÇØltration at the limbus. Spread is circumferential
and occasionally central. In contrast to Mooren ulcer, extension into the sclera may occur.
¯Çü Limbitis, episcleritis or scleritis are usually present. As with
a Mooren ulcer, there is no separation between the ulcerative
process and the limbus.
¯Çü Advanced disease may result in a ÔÇÿcontact lensÔÇÖ cornea (see Fig.
7.29C) or perforation.

¯Çü Rheumatoid paracentral ulcerative keratitis (PCUK) is
thought to be a distinct entity, with a punched-out more centrally located lesion with little in¯ÇØltrate in a quiet eye (see Fig.
7.29D). Perforation can occur rapidly and there is usually a
good response to topical ciclosporin, with bandage contact
lens and tissue glue application, if necessary, rather than systemic treatment.

## Treatment

Treatment is principally with systemic immunosuppression in collaboration with a rheumatologist.
¯Çü Systemic steroids, sometimes via pulsed intravenous administration, are used to control acute disease, with immunosuppressive therapy and biological blockers for longer-term
management.
¯Çü Topical lubricants (preservative-free).
¯Çü Topical antibiotics as prophylaxis if an epithelial defect is
present.
¯Çü Oral tetracycline (e.g. doxycycline 100 mg once or twice
daily) for its anticollagenase e¯Ç£ect.
¯Çü Topical steroids may worsen thinning so are generally
avoided; relapsing polychondritis may be an exception.
¯Çü Surgical management is generally as for Mooren ulcer,
including conjunctival excision if medical treatment is
ine¯Ç£ective.

## Terrien marginal degeneration

Terrien disease is an uncommon idiopathic thinning of the peripheral cornea occurring in young adult to elderly patients. Although
usually categorized as a degeneration, some cases are associated with episodic episcleritis or scleritis. About 75% of a¯Ç£ected
patients are male and the condition is usually bilateral but may be
asymmetrical.

## Diagnosis

{ Pseudopterygia sometimes develop (Fig. 7.30C).

¯Çü Symptoms. ¯Çƒe condition is commonly asymptomatic, but
gradual visual deterioration can occur due to astigmatism. A
few patients experience episodic pain and in¯Ç×ammation.
¯Çü Signs

{ Fine yellowÔÇôwhite refractile stromal opacities, frequently
associated with mild super¯ÇØcial vascularization, usually
start superiorly, spread circumferentially and are separated from the limbus by a clear zone. ¯Çƒere is no epithelial defect and on cursory examination the condition may
resemble arcus senilis.

{ Slowly progressive circumferential thinning results in a
peripheral gutter, the outer slope of which shelves gradually, while the central part rises sharply (Fig. 7.30A). A
band of lipid is commonly present at the central edge (Fig.
7.30B).

{ Perforation is rare but may be spontaneous or follow blunt
trauma.

## Treatment

¯Çü Safety spectacles (e.g. polycarbonate) if thinning is signi¯ÇØcant.
¯Çü Contact lenses for astigmatism. Scleral or so¯Çø lenses with
rigid gas permeable ÔÇÿpiggybackingÔÇÖ.
¯Çü Surgery ÔÇô crescentic or annular excision of the gutter with
lamellar (Fig. 7.30D) or full-thickness transplantation ÔÇô gives
reasonable results and may arrest progression.

NEUROTROPHIC KERATOPATHY

## Introduction

## Neurotrophic keratopathy occurs when there is loss of trigeminal

innervation to the cornea resulting in partial or complete anaesthesia. In addition to loss of the protective sensory stimulus, reduced
innervation results in intracellular oedema, exfoliation, loss of
goblet cells and epithelial breakdown with persistent ulceration.
Causes include trigeminal ganglion surgical ablation for neuralgia,

stroke, tumour, peripheral neuropathy (e.g. diabetes) and ocular
disease such as herpes simplex and herpes zoster keratitis (when
sensation loss may be sectoral).

## Diagnosis

A full cranial nerve examination is mandatory.
¯Çü Signs

{ Corneal sensation is reduced.

{ Stage 1: interpalpebral epithelial irregularity and staining,
with mild opaci¯ÇØcation, oedema and tiny focal defects.

{ Stage 2: larger persistent epithelial defect with rolled and
thickened edges (Fig. 7.31A), subsequently assuming
a punched-out con¯ÇØguration with underlying stromal
oedema.

{ Stage 3: stromal melting, o¯Çøen with minimal discomfort.
Secondary infection may occur.

{ Perforation is uncommon but may occur rapidly, especially with secondary infection (Fig. 7.31B).

244 Exposure Keratopathy

A B

prolapse. (Courtesy of S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

## Treatment

¯Çü Discontinuation, if possible, of potentially toxic medications.
¯Çü Topical lubricants (non-preserved) for associated dry eye or
corneal exposure. Topical insulin-like growth factor-1, substance P and neurogenic growth factor have been evaluated
but are not commercially available.
¯Çü Anticollagenase agents: topical (e.g. acetylcysteine, tetracycline ointment) or systemic (e.g. tetracyclines).
¯Çü Cenegermin 0.002%, a topical recombinant human nerve
growth factor, appears to be bene¯ÇØcial in moderate (persistent epithelial defect) or severe (corneal ulcer) neurotrophic
ulcer in adults. Instilled six times daily for 2 months, this
medication increases the corneal nerve density and facilitates healing. Di¯Ç£use lamellar keratitis has been reported as
a late side e¯Ç£ect in some patients. ¯Çƒe long-term e¯Ç£ectiveness is not known.
¯Çü Protection of the ocular surface

branches (Fig. 7.32F). Sensation returns 6ÔÇô8 months a¯Çøer the
surgery.

EXPOSURE KERATOPATHY

## Introduction

Exposure keratopathy is the result of incomplete lid closure (lagophthalmos), with drying of the cornea despite normal tear production. Lagophthalmos may only be present on blinking or gentle
lid closure, with full forced lid closure. Causes include neuroparalytic, especially facial nerve palsy, reduced muscle tone as
in parkinsonism, mechanical such as lid scarring, eczematous
skin tightening and post-blepharoplasty and proptosis.

## Diagnosis

{ Simple taping of the lids, particularly at night, may provide
modest protection.

¯Çü Symptoms are those of dry eye.
¯Çü Signs

{ Botulinum toxin-induced ptosis.

{ Tarsorrhaphy: temporary or permanent, lateral or central,
according to the underlying pathology and visual potential.

{ ¯Çƒerapeutic silicone contact lenses may be ¯ÇØtted, provided
the eye is carefully monitored for infection.

{ Mild punctate epithelial changes involving the inferior third of the cornea, particularly with nocturnal
lagophthalmos.

{ Stromal melting (Fig. 7.33B), occasionally leading to
perforation.

{ Inferior ¯ÇØbrovascular change with Salzmann degeneration
may develop over time.

{ Epithelial breakdown (Fig. 7.33A).

{ Amniotic membrane patching with temporary central
tarsorrhaphy.
¯Çü Perforation is dealt with as discussed earlier in the chapter.
¯Çü Corneal neurotization from the supratrochlear nerve using
a sural nerve gra¯ÇØ. Corneal sensation can be restored using a
multidisciplinary surgical approach. A segment of the medial
cutaneous branches of the sural nerve is connected to the
contralateral supratrochlear nerve (Fig. 7.32A and B). ¯Çƒe
gra¯Çøed nerve is brought under the skin and into the opposite
orbit (Fig. 7.32C and D). ¯Çƒe nerve is divided and positioned
under the conjunctiva and attached to the peripheral corneoscleral junction (Fig. 7.32E). ¯Çƒe conjunctiva is closed over the

{ Secondary infection.

Treatment depends on the severity of exposure and whether recovery is anticipated.

{ Arti¯ÇØcial tears (unpreserved) during the day and ointment
at night.

ointment.

MISCELLANEOUS KERATOPATHIES

## Infectious crystalline keratopathy

Infectious crystalline keratopathy is a rare indolent infection usually occurring in a patient on long-term topical steroid therapy with
an associated epithelial defect, most frequently following penetrating keratoplasty. Streptococcus viridans is most commonly isolated,
although numerous other bacteria and fungi have been implicated.
Slowly progressive, greyÔÇôwhite, branching stromal opacities are
seen, associated with minimal in¯Ç×ammation and usually intact overlying epithelium (Fig. 7.34). A bio¯ÇØlm is present that allows survival
of the microorganism, reduces antibiotic bioavailability and makes
diagnostic detection di¯ÇÜcult. Culture or biopsy is performed to determine the organism and topical antibiotics instilled for several weeks.
Adjunctive therapies to enhance the e¯ÇÜcacy of treatment include disruption of the microorganism bio¯ÇØlm by laser, intrastromal antibiotic
and keratectomy. In cases that do not respond to treatment or where
corneal scarring is present, corneal transplantation is required.

Thygeson super¯Ç×cial punctate keratitis

¯Çƒygeson super¯ÇØcial punctate keratitis is an uncommon idiopathic, usually bilateral, condition characterized by exacerbations
and remissions. It is commonly of onset in young adulthood but
can a¯Ç£ect patients of any age and may recur over decades.

## Treatment

¯Çü Topical

{ Lubricants may su¯ÇÜce in mild cases.

{ Steroids. A low-potency preparation is used twice daily
initially, with gradual tapering to as little as once-weekly
instillation. High-intensity treatment may sometimes be
needed initially.

{ Ciclosporin 0.05% is generally used if the response to
steroids is inadequate, or as an alternative in longer-term
therapy. However, some authorities recommend ciclosporin for initial treatment. Tacrolimus may also be e¯Ç£ective.

{ Antivirals have not been found to be consistently helpful.
¯Çü Contact lenses (extended wear or daily disposable so¯Çø) may
be considered if steroids are ine¯Ç£ective or contraindicated, as
an alternative to ciclosporin.
¯Çü Phototherapeutic keratectomy brings short-term relief but
recurrence is likely.

## Filamentary keratopathy is a common condition that can

cause considerable discomfort. It is thought that a loose area of

{ Filaments stain well with rose Bengal (Fig. 7.36B) and to a

## Recurrent corneal epithelial erosion

## Introduction

## Recurrent corneal epithelial erosion is caused by an abnormally weak attachment between the basal cells of the corneal

epithelium and their basement membrane. Minor trauma, such
as eyelidÔÇôcornea interaction during sleep, can be sufficient to
precipitate detachment. Erosions may be associated with previous trauma or rarely corneal surgery and with some corneal
dystrophies. Intervals between episodes can be very variable,
even in the same patient, but may occur in spates over a short
period.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Severe pain, photophobia, redness, blepharospasm
and watering typically waken the patient during the night or

are present on awaking in the morning. ¯Çƒere is usually (but
not invariably) a prior history of corneal abrasion, sometimes
years previously, which may have been minor compared with
the recurrent symptoms.
¯Çü Signs

{ An epithelial defect (Fig. 7.37) may not be present by the
time the patient is examined, as healing can o¯Çøen be very
rapid (hours), but the extent of loose epithelium may be
highlighted by areas of pooling of ¯Ç×uorescein and rapid
tear ¯ÇØlm breakup.

{ In¯ÇØltrate should not be present, though greyish sloughed
and rolled epithelium may sometimes be reminiscent of
this.

{ ¯Çƒere may be no sign of abnormality once a defect has
healed, but signs of epithelial basement membrane disturbance, such as microcysts, punctate or linear/¯ÇØngerprint opacities, are o¯Çøen present. ¯Çƒese will typically be
bilateral in a stromal dystrophy and unilateral if injury is
the cause.

## Treatment

¯Çü Acute symptoms

{ Antibiotic ointment four times daily and cyclopentolate
1% twice daily.

{ Pressure patching should not be used as it may impair
healing and does not improve comfort.

{ In severe cases a bandage contact lens alleviates pain but
may not improve healing. Antibiotic drops rather than
ointment should be used.

{ Debridement of heaped/scrolled areas of epithelium with
a sterile cellulose sponge or cotton-tipped applicator (cotton bud) may improve comfort and allow healing from the
edges of the defect.

{ Topical diclofenac 0.1% reduces pain.

{ Topical anaesthetic dramatically relieves pain but should
not be dispensed for patient use.

{ Hypertonic sodium chloride 5% drops four times daily and
ointment at bedtime may improve epithelial adhesion.

A World Health Organization (WHO) grading system is set out
in Table 7.7
¯Çü Symptoms are night blindness (nyctalopia), discomfort and
loss of vision.
¯Çü Conjunctiva

## Diagnosis

{ Following resolution, some authorities advise using a prophylactic topical lubricant such as carbomer gel three or
four times daily for several months.
¯Çü Recurrent symptoms

{ Topical lubricant gel or ointment, or hypertonic saline
ointment, instilled at bedtime used long term may be
su¯ÇÜcient.

{ A combination of oral doxycycline and topical steroid may be helpful. Both have been shown to inhibit
metalloproteinase inhibitors important to disease
pathogenesis.

{ Xerosis is characterized by dryness of the conjunctiva in
the interpalpebral zone with loss of goblet cells, squamous
metaplasia and keratinization.

{ Bitot spots are triangular patches of foamy keratinized epithelium (Fig. 7.38A) in the interpalpebral zone thought to
be caused by Corynebacterium xerosis.
¯Çü Cornea

{ Lustreless appearance due to secondary xerosis.

{ Long-term extended-wear bandage contact lens.

{ Simple debridement of the epithelium in involved areas,
which may be followed by smoothing of Bowman layer
with a diamond burr or excimer laser. Most patients
treated with excimer keratectomy do not get recurrences
and the side e¯Ç£ects are minimal.

{ Anterior stromal puncture for localized areas o¯Ç£ the visual
axis. It may not be necessary to remove the epithelium to
facilitate this.

{ Sterile corneal melting by liquefactive necrosis (keratomalacia), which may lead to perforation (Fig. 7.38B).
¯Çü Retinopathy, characterized by yellowish peripheral dots, may
occur in advanced cases and is associated with decreased electroretinogram amplitude.

## Treatment

Keratomalacia is an indicator of very severe vitamin A de¯ÇØciency
and should be treated as a medical emergency due to the risk of
death, particularly in infants.

## Xerophthalmia

## Introduction

Vitamin A is essential for the maintenance of the bodyÔÇÖs epithelial surfaces, for immune function and for the synthesis of retinal
photoreceptor proteins. Xerophthalmia refers to the spectrum of
ocular disease caused by inadequate vitamin A intake and is a late
manifestation of severe de¯ÇØciency. Lack of vitamin A in the diet
may be caused by malnutrition, malabsorption, chronic alcoholism or by highly selective dieting. ¯Çƒe risk in infants is increased
if their mothers are malnourished and by coexisting diarrhoea or
measles.

A B

250 Corneal Ectasia

¯Çü Systemic treatment involves oral (oil-based 200000 IU) or
intramuscular (aqueous-based 100000 IU) vitamin A for keratomalacia. Multivitamin supplements and dietary sources of
vitamin A are also administered.
¯Çü Local treatment consists of intense lubrication, topical retinoic
acid and management of perforation.

CORNEAL ECTASIA

## Keratoconus

## Introduction

Keratoconus (KC) is a progressive disorder in which central or
paracentral corneal stromal thinning occurs, accompanied by
apical protrusion and irregular astigmatism. ¯Çƒe prevalence is
0.1% to 0.2% and men are slightly more likely to be a¯Ç£ected than
women. Presentation is commonly during the teens or twenties, with features initially in only one eye. Approximately 50%
of normal fellow eyes will progress to KC within 16 years. Both
eyes are a¯Ç£ected eventually, at least on topographical imaging, in

{ Retinoscopy shows an irregular ÔÇÿscissoringÔÇÖ re¯Ç×ex.

almost all cases. It can be graded by the highest axis of corneal
power on keratometry as mild (<48 D), moderate (48ÔÇô54 D) or
severe (>54 D). Most patients do not have a family history, with
only about 10% of o¯Ç£spring developing KC; autosomal dominant
transmission with incomplete penetrance has been proposed.
Risk factors include allergy, asthma, eczema and persistent eye
rubbing from any cause. Systemic associations include Down,
EhlersÔÇôDanlos and Marfan syndromes and osteogenesis imperfecta. Ocular associations include vernal keratoconjunctivitis, blue sclera, aniridia, Leber congenital amaurosis, retinitis
pigmentosa.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Unilateral impairment of vision due to progressive
myopia and astigmatism. Occasionally, initial presentation is
with acute hydrops (see below).
¯Çü Signs

{ Direct ophthalmoscopy from a distance of half a metre
shows a fairly well delineated ÔÇÿoil dropletÔÇÖ re¯Ç×ex (Fig. 7.39A).

A B

C D

(C) Fleischer ring demonstrated by cobalt blue light as a blue circle (arrow); (D) typical cone.
(Courtesy of R Fogla ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

{ Slit lamp biomicroscopy shows very ¯ÇØne, vertical, deep
stromal stress lines (Vogt striae ÔÇô Fig. 7.39B), which disappear with pressure on the globe.

{ Epithelial iron deposits, best seen with a cobalt blue ¯ÇØlter, may
surround the base of the cone (Fleischer ring ÔÇô Fig. 7.39C).

{ Progressive corneal protrusion in a cone con¯ÇØguration
(Fig. 7.39D), with thinning maximal at the apex.

{ Bulging of the lower lid in downgaze (Munson sign) (Fig.
7.40A).

{ Acute hydrops is caused by a rupture in the stretched Descemet membrane that allows a sudden in¯Ç×ux of aqueous
into the cornea (Fig. 7.40B and C), with accompanying pain,
photophobia and decreased vision. Although the break usually heals within 6ÔÇô10 weeks and the oedema clears, a variable amount of stromal scarring (Fig. 7.40D) may develop.
¯Çƒis sometimes gives improved vision by ¯Ç×attening the
cornea. Acute episodes are initially treated with cycloplegia,
hypertonic (5%) saline ointment and patching or a so¯Çø bandage contact lens. Accelerated resolution has been reported
with intracameral gas injection in the acute stage.
¯Çü Keratometry readings are steep.

¯Çü Corneal topography (video keratography) and various novel
corneal pro¯ÇØling techniques are highly sensitive for detection
and essential in monitoring. Characteristically, astigmatism
progresses from a symmetrical bow-tie pattern, through an
asymmetrical appearance to an inferotemporally displaced steepsided cone (Fig. 7.41). Sometimes a central (ÔÇÿnippleÔÇÖ) cone may
develop. Contact lens warpage can sometimes appear similar to
a cone on topography, but is generally more arcuate-shaped.

## Treatment

¯Çü Eye rubbing should be avoided.
¯Çü Spectacles or so¯Çø contact lenses are generally su¯ÇÜcient in
early cases. If thinning is marked, it may be prudent to consider wearing safety spectacles over contact lenses.
¯Çü Rigid contact lenses, sometimes scleral, are required for
higher degrees of astigmatism to provide a regular refracting
surface.
¯Çü Corneal collagen cross-linking (CXL), using ribo¯Ç×avin drops
to photosensitize the eye followed by exposure to ultraviolet-A
light, can stabilize the cornea if progression has been shown.
CXL is successful in more than 90% of cases and in some cases

## Abs

can even reverse the ectasia. ¯Çƒe complication rate is low, but
because the epithelium is removed at the time of surgery there
is a small risk of infection or corneal scarring. A bandage
contact lens should be used for 3 days a¯Çøer the procedure to
reduce postoperative pain. It usually takes 1ÔÇô2 weeks for the
eye to settle.
¯Çü Intracorneal ring segment implantation (Fig. 7.42) using
laser or mechanical channel creation is relatively safe and typically provides at least a moderate visual improvement, facilitating contact lens tolerance in advanced cases. It can be used
at the same time as CXL.
¯Çü Keratoplasty, either penetrating or deep anterior lamellar
(DALK), may be necessary in patients with severe disease. A
history of hydrops is a contraindication to DALK due to the
presence of a Descemet membrane discontinuity. Outcomes
may be compromised by residual astigmatism and by anisometropia, necessitating contact lens correction for optimal acuity.
¯Çü LASIK is contraindicated. Patients should be screened for KC
prior to corneal refractive surgery.

## Pellucid marginal degeneration

## Pellucid marginal degeneration is a rare progressive peripheral

corneal thinning disorder, typically involving the inferior cornea
in both eyes. Presentation is usually in adulthood.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Slowly progressive blurring due to astigmatism.
¯Çü Signs

{ Bilateral, slowly progressive, crescentic 1ÔÇô2 mm band of
inferior corneal thinning extending from 4 to 8 oÔÇÖclock,
1 mm from the limbus (Fig. 7.43A).

{ ¯Çƒe epithelium is intact and the cornea above the thinned
area shows ectasia and appears ¯Ç×attened.

{ In contrast to keratoconus, Fleischer rings and Vogt striae
do not occur and acute hydrops is rare.
¯Çü Corneal topography shows a ÔÇÿbutter¯Ç×yÔÇÖ pattern, with severe
astigmatism and di¯Ç£use steepening of the inferior cornea (Fig.
7.43B).

A

B C
200¯Çƒm 200¯Çƒm

A B

di¯ÇØuse steepening of the inferior cornea.

## Treatment

Early cases are managed with spectacles and contact lenses. Surgical options, none of which is ideal, in patients intolerant to
contact lenses include large eccentric penetrating keratoplasty,
thermocauterization, crescentic lamellar keratoplasty, wedge
resection of diseased tissue, epikeratoplasty and intracorneal
ring segment implantation. Results of collagen cross-linking are
encouraging.

## Keratoglobus

## Keratoglobus is an extremely rare condition that can be present at

birth when di¯Ç£erential diagnosis is from congenital glaucoma and
megalocornea and associations may be present, or acquired, with
onset in adulthood. In contrast to keratoconus, the cornea develops globular rather than conical ectasia and is associated with generalized corneal thinning (Fig. 7.44). Acute hydrops is rare, but the
cornea is more prone to rupture on relatively mild trauma. Corneal

254 Corneal Dystrophy

A B

topography shows generalized steepening. Surgery is di¯ÇÜcult and
contact lens wear is o¯Çøen unsatisfactory. Intrastromal ring segments and cross-linking may have utility. Special care should be
taken to protect the eyes from trauma.

CORNEAL DYSTROPHY

¯Çƒe corneal dystrophies are a group of progressive, usually bilateral, variable corneal opacifying disorders, many of which are
associated with decreased vision and discomfort. Based on biomicroscopical and histopathological features they are classi¯ÇØed
into epithelial, Bowman layer, stromal and Descemet membrane
and endothelial. One or more underlying genetic abnormalities
have been identi¯ÇØed for most.

TIP As a general rule, most types of corneal dystrophy are
bilateral, primarily a¯ÇØect one layer of the cornea and are slowly
progressive.

## Epithelial dystrophies

Cogan (epithelial basement membrane)
dystrophy

Isolated familial cases of epithelial basement membrane (mapdot-fingerprint) corneal dystrophy have been reported. Since
most patients seen in clinical practice have no documented
family history of the condition, these corneal changes are now
considered degenerative or secondary to trauma. The disease
is often misdiagnosed, principally because of the variable
appearance.
¯Çü Inheritance. ¯Çƒe condition is usually sporadic and these cases
may be degenerations rather than true dystrophies, in contrast
to the rare familial (autosomal dominant ÔÇô AD) cases.
¯Çü Histology shows thickening of the basement membrane with
deposition of ¯ÇØbrillary protein between the basement membrane and the Bowman layer. Basal epithelial cell hemidesmosomes are de¯ÇØcient.

¯Çü Onset is in the second decade. About 10% of patients develop
recurrent corneal erosions in the third decade and the remainder are asymptomatic throughout life. ¯Çƒe occurrence of bilateral recurrent erosions with no history of trauma suggests
basement membrane dystrophy.
¯Çü Signs. Lesions are o¯Çøen best visualized by retroillumination or
scleral scatter. Over time pattern and distribution varies; they
may be absent or subtle in a fellow eye. Similar features can be
seen with recurrent erosions from any cause.

{ Dot-like and microcystic epithelial lesions (Fig. 7.45A).

{ Subepithelial map-like patterns surrounded by a faint haze
(Fig. 7.45B).

{ Whorled ¯ÇØngerprint-like lines.

{ Bleb-like subepithelial pebbled glass pattern.
¯Çü Treatment is similar to that of recurrent corneal erosions.

## Meesmann epithelial dystrophy

Meesmann dystrophy is a rare non-progressive abnormality of
corneal epithelial metabolism, underlying which mutations in the
genes encoding corneal epithelial keratins have been reported.
¯Çü Inheritance is AD.
¯Çü Histology shows irregular thickening of the epithelial basement membrane and intraepithelial cysts.
¯Çü Symptoms. Patients may be asymptomatic, or there may be
recurrent erosions and blurring (usually mild).
¯Çü Signs

{ Myriad tiny intraepithelial cysts of uniform size but variable density are maximal centrally and extend towards but
do not reach the limbus (Fig. 7.45C).

{ ¯Çƒe cornea may be slightly thinned and sensation
reduced.
¯Çü Treatment other than lubrication is not normally required.

## Others

Other epithelial and subepithelial dystrophies are: gelatinous
drop-like corneal dystrophy (Fig. 7.45D), Lisch epithelial corneal
dystrophy (Figs 7.45E and F), subepithelial mucinous corneal
dystrophy.

dystrophies
ReisÔÇôB├╝cklers corneal dystrophy

## Stromal dystrophies

Lattice corneal dystrophy, TGFB1 type

¯Çƒis may be categorized as an anterior variant of granular stromal
dystrophy (GCD type 3 ÔÇô see below) and is also known as corneal
basement dystrophy type I (CBD1).
¯Çü Inheritance is AD; the a¯Ç£ected gene is TGFB1.
¯Çü Histology. Replacement of the Bowman layer by connective
tissue bands.
¯Çü Symptoms. Severe recurrent corneal erosions in childhood.
Visual impairment may occur.
¯Çü Signs

{ GreyÔÇôwhite geographic subepithelial opacities, most dense
centrally (Fig. 7.46A), increasing in density with age to
form a reticular pattern. Histopathology, including electron microscopy, may be required for de¯ÇØnitive distinction
from ¯ÇƒielÔÇôBehnke dystrophy in some cases.

¯Çƒis is usually regarded as the classic form of lattice dystrophy.
Clinical variants (e.g. IIIA ÔÇô Fig. 7.47A and B) associated with
more than 25 heterozygous mutations in TGFB1 have been
described.
¯Çü Inheritance. AD; gene TGFB1.
¯Çü Histology. Amyloid, staining with Congo red and exhibiting green birefringence with a polarizing filter (Fig.
7.47A).
¯Çü Symptoms. Recurrent erosions occur at the end of the ¯ÇØrst
decade in the classic form, when typical stromal signs may not
yet be present. Blurring may occur later.
¯Çü Signs

{ Refractile anterior stromal dots (Fig. 7.47C), coalescing
into a relatively ¯ÇØne ¯ÇØlamentous lattice that spreads gradually but spares the periphery (Fig. 7.47D).

{ A generalized stromal haze may progressively impair
vision.

{ Corneal sensation is reduced.
¯Çü Treatment is directed at the recurrent erosions. Excimer keratectomy achieves satisfactory control in some patients.

ThielÔÇôBehnke corneal dystrophy

Also termed honeycomb-shaped corneal dystrophy and corneal
basement dystrophy type II (CBD2); features are generally less
severe than ReisÔÇôB├╝cklers.
¯Çü Inheritance. AD; gene TGFB1 and at least one other.
¯Çü Histology. Bowman layer ÔÇÿcurly ¯ÇØbresÔÇÖ on electron microscopy.
¯Çü Symptoms. Recurrent erosions in childhood.
¯Çü Signs. Subepithelial opacities are less individually de¯ÇØned than
the granular dystrophy-type lesions (see below) seen in ReisÔÇô
B├╝cklers dystrophy. ¯Çƒey develop in a network of tiny rings
or honeycomb-like morphology, predominantly involving the
central cornea (Fig. 7.46B).
¯Çü Treatment is not always necessary.

{ Corneal sensation is reduced.
¯Çü Treatment by penetrating or deep lamellar keratoplasty is frequently required. Recurrence is not uncommon.

Lattice corneal dystrophy, gelsolin type

Also known as LCD2 and Meretoja syndrome, this is a systemic
condition rather than a true corneal dystrophy.
¯Çü Inheritance. AD; gene GSN.
¯Çü Histology shows amyloid deposits in the corneal stroma.
¯Çü Ocular symptoms. Ocular irritation and late impairment of
vision; erosions are rare.
¯Çü Ocular signs

{ Sparse stromal lattice lines spread centrally from the
periphery.

{ Corneal sensation is impaired.

Granular corneal dystrophy, type 1 (classic)

¯Çü Inheritance. AD; gene TGFB1. Homozygous disease gives
more severe features.
¯Çü Histology. Amorphous hyaline deposits staining bright red
with Masson trichrome (Fig. 7.48A).
¯Çü Symptoms. Glare and photophobia, with blurring as progression occurs. Recurrent erosions are uncommon.
¯Çü Signs

{ Discrete white central anterior stromal deposits resembling sugar granules, breadcrumbs or glass splinters separated by clear stroma (Fig. 7.48B).

{ Gradual increase in number and size of the deposits with
deeper and outward spread, sparing the limbus.

{ Gradual con¯Ç×uence and di¯Ç£use haze lead to visual impairment (Fig. 7.48C).

{ Corneal sensation is impaired.
¯Çü Treatment by penetrating or deep lamellar keratoplasty is usually required by the ¯ÇØ¯Çøh decade. Super¯ÇØcial recurrences may
require repeated excimer laser keratectomy.

Also known as Avellino and combined granular-lattice dystrophy.
¯Çü Inheritance. AD; gene TGFB1.
¯Çü Histology shows both hyaline and amyloid.
¯Çü Symptoms. Recurrent erosions tend to be mild. Visual impairment is a later feature.
¯Çü Signs are usually present by the end of the ¯ÇØrst decade in heterozygotes. Fine super¯ÇØcial opacities progress to form stellate
or annular lesions (Fig. 7.48D), sometimes associated with
deeper linear opacities.
¯Çü Treatment is usually not required. Corneal trauma accelerates
progression; refractive surgery is contraindicated.

## Macular corneal dystrophy

¯Çü Inheritance. Autosomal recessive (AR); gene CHST6; the condition is relatively common in Iceland.
¯Çü Histology. Aggregations of glycosaminoglycans intra- and
extracellularly; stain with Alcian blue and colloidal iron (Fig.
7.49A).
¯Çü Symptoms. Early (end of ¯ÇØrst decade) visual deterioration;
recurrent erosions are very common.
¯Çü Signs

{ Dense but poorly delineated greyish-white spots centrally
in the anterior stroma and peripherally in the posterior
stroma (Fig. 7.49B and C). ¯Çƒere is no clear delineation
between opacities, which may be elevated.

{ Progression of the lesions occurs in conjunction with anterior stromal haze, initially involving the central cornea
(Fig. 7.49D).

{ ¯Çƒere is eventual involvement of full-thickness stroma,
extending to the limbus with no clear zone.

{ ¯Çƒinning is a fairly early feature, with late thickening from
oedema due to endothelial dysfunction.

{ Sensation is reduced.
¯Çü Treatment. Penetrating keratoplasty. Recurrence is
common.

Schnyder (crystalline) corneal dystrophy

¯Çƒis is a disorder of corneal lipid metabolism, associated in some
patients with systemic dyslipidaemia. ¯Çƒe use of crystalline in the
name is no longer recommended as corneal crystals are not a ubiquitous feature.
¯Çü Inheritance. AD; gene UBIAD1.
¯Çü Histology. Phospholipid and cholesterol deposits.
¯Çü Symptoms. Visual impairment and glare.
¯Çü Signs

{ Central haze is an early feature (Fig. 7.50A), progressing
to more widespread full-thickness involvement over time
(Fig. 7.50B).

Fran├ºois central cloudy dystrophy

It is not certain that this entity is a dystrophy; it may be clinically indistinguishable from the degeneration posterior crocodile shagreen.
¯Çü Inheritance. AD has been reported, but not clearly established.
¯Çü Symptoms. Almost always none.
¯Çü Signs. Cloudy greyish polygonal or rounded posterior stromal
opacities, most prominent centrally (Fig. 7.51).
¯Çü Treatment is not required.

## Others

Congenital stromal corneal dystrophy, ¯Ç×eck corneal dystrophy,
posterior amorphous corneal dystrophy and pre-Descemet corneal dystrophy.

## Descemet membrane and endothelial
dystrophies

## Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy

¯Çƒis disorder is characterized by bilateral accelerated endothelial
cell loss. It is more common in women and is associated with a
slightly increased prevalence of glaucoma.
¯Çü Inheritance. Most are sporadic, with occasional AD inheritance. Mutation in COL8A2 has been identi¯ÇØed in an earlyonset variant and in the TCF4 gene in most other cases.
¯Çü Symptoms. Gradually worsening blurring, particularly in the
morning, due to corneal oedema. Onset is usually in middle
age or later.
¯Çü Signs

(Courtesy of W Lisch.)

{ Cornea guttata: the presence of irregular warts or ÔÇÿexcrescencesÔÇÖ on Descemet membrane secreted by abnormal
endothelial cells (Fig. 7.52A).

{ Subtle corneal endothelial radial lines which correlate with
the peripheral cell arrangement that is seen microscopically.

{ Specular re¯Ç×ection shows tiny dark spots caused by disruption of the regular endothelial mosaic (Fig. 7.52B). Progression occurs to a ÔÇÿbeaten metalÔÇÖ appearance (Fig. 7.52C).

{ Endothelial decompensation gradually leads to central
stromal oedema and blurred vision, worse in the morning.

{ Epithelial oedema develops in more advanced cases, with
the formation of microcysts and bullae (bullous keratopathy

    - Fig. 7.52D) accompanied by discomfort. Rupture of bullae
is associated with marked acute pain secondary to the exposure of nerve ¯ÇØbres. Subepithelial scarring and peripheral
vascularization may be seen in longstanding cases.
¯Çü Treatment

{ Conservative options include topical sodium chloride
5% drops or ointment, reduction of IOP and use of a hair
dryer for corneal dehydration.

{ Ruptured bullae can be made more comfortable by the
use of bandage contact lenses, cycloplegia, antibiotic ointment and lubricants. Anterior stromal puncture may be
helpful.

{ Posterior lamellar keratoplasty (e.g. Descemet membranestripping automated endothelial keratoplasty ÔÇô DSAEK ÔÇô or
Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty ÔÇô DMEK)
and penetrating keratoplasty (see Ch. 7) have a high success rate.

{ Options in eyes with poor visual potential include conjunctival ¯Ç×aps and amniotic membrane transplantation.

{ A promising new treatment, topical Rho-kinase inhibitor with prior transcorneal endothelial cryotherapy, seems
to stimulate endothelial cell proliferation and improve
function.
¯Çü Cataract surgery may worsen the corneal status because of
endothelial cell loss and protective steps should be taken to
reduce this. Modern machines using torsional phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation use less ultrasound time than the older machines, which
used longitudinal phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation and are therefore less
likely to cause endothelial loss. A ÔÇÿtriple procedureÔÇÖ (combined
cataract surgery, lens implantation and keratoplasty) may be
considered in eyes with corneal oedema. Corneal oedema is
less likely to occur a¯Çøer cataract surgery if the central corneal
thickness is less than 630ÔÇô640 ╬╝m.

TIP Persistent corneal oedema may follow cataract surgery
in a patient with Fuchs endothelial dystrophy, particularly if
the preoperative central corneal thickness is more than
630ÔÇô640 ╬╝m.

## Posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy

¯Çƒere are three forms of posterior polymorphous dystrophy,
PPCD1ÔÇô3. Associations include iris abnormalities, glaucoma and
Alport syndrome. ¯Çƒe pathological basis involves metaplasia of
endothelial cells.

¯Çü Inheritance is usually AD. ¯Çƒe gene VSX1 has been implicated
in PPCD1, PPCD2 is caused by mutations in COL8A2 and

¯Çü Symptoms. Usually absent, with incidental diagnosis.
¯Çü Signs. Subtle vesicular, band-like or di¯Ç£use endothelial lesions
(Fig. 7.53A and B).
¯Çü Treatment is not required.

## Congenital hereditary endothelial dystrophy

Congenital hereditary endothelial dystrophy (CHED) is a rare dystrophy in which there is focal or di¯Ç£use thickening of Descemet
membrane and endothelial degeneration. CHED2 is a more common and more severe form than CHED1 and is occasionally associated with late-onset deafness (Harboyan syndrome).
¯Çü Inheritance

¯Çü Symptoms. Photophobia and watering are common in
CHED1, but not in CHED2.
¯Çü Signs

{ Corneal clouding and thickening (Fig. 7.54) are neonatal in CHED2 and develop during the ¯ÇØrst year or two in
CHED1.

{ Visual impairment is variable and visual acuity may surpass that expected from the corneal appearance.

{ Nystagmus is more common in CHED2.
¯Çü Treatment. Lamellar or penetrating keratoplasty.

{ CHED1 is AD with the gene locus on chromosome 20.
CHED1 may not be distinct from PPCD.

## Arcus senilis

Arcus senilis (gerontoxon, arcus lipoides) is the most common
peripheral corneal opacity. It frequently occurs without any

## Vogt limbal girdle

## Vogt limbal girdle is an innocuous condition that is present in

up to 60% of individuals over 40 years of age, more commonly
in women. It consists of whitish crescentic limbal bands composed of chalk-like ¯Ç×ecks centred at 9 and/or 3 oÔÇÖclock, more o¯Çøen
nasally. ¯Çƒere may be irregular central extension. Type I may be

a variant of band keratopathy, featuring a ÔÇÿSwiss cheeseÔÇÖ hole pattern and a clear area separating the lesion from the scleral margin
(Fig. 7.55B). Type II is more prevalent and is distinguished by the
absence of holes and typically also of a juxtalimbal clear zone (Fig.
7.55C). Histologically the changes in both are similar to pinguecula and pterygium.

## Cornea farinata

Cornea farinata is a visually insigni¯ÇØcant condition characterized
by bilateral, minute, ¯Ç×our-like deposits in the deep stroma, most
prominent centrally (Fig. 7.55D).

## Crocodile shagreen

Crocodile shagreen is characterized by asymptomatic, greyishwhite, polygonal stromal opacities separated by relatively clear
spaces (Fig. 7.55E). ¯Çƒe opacities most frequently involve the
anterior two-thirds of the stroma (anterior crocodile shagreen),
but on occasion may be found more posteriorly (posterior

crocodile shagreen). It may be indistinguishable from Fran├ºois

West Indian punctate keratopathy

¯Çƒis is an unusual condition of unknown cause that is seen in
elderly asymptomatic individuals, generally of West Indian origin.
Males are more a¯Ç£ected than females. It normally a¯Ç£ects only one
eye, but is occasionally bilateral, with one to four lesions found in
the interpalpebral ¯ÇØssure outside the visual axis. ¯Çƒe lesions are
round, measuring 0.5 mm or less, and are located at the level of
Bowman membrane, extending just into the super¯ÇØcial stroma.
¯Çƒey usually have a central dot which is intensely white in colour,
surrounded by a paler halo which merges with the adjacent cornea.
¯Çƒe corneal epithelium appears normal and does not stain with
¯Ç×uorescein. Treatment is not required (Fig. 7.55F).

## Lipid keratopathy

{ Gradual central spread to form a band-like chalky plaque
containing transparent small holes (Fig. 7.57B) and occasionally cle¯Çøs.

{ Advanced lesions may become nodular and elevated with
considerable discomfort due to epithelial breakdown.
¯Çü Treatment is indicated if vision is threatened or if the eye is
uncomfortable. It is important to recognize and treat any
underlying condition.

¯Çü Primary lipid keratopathy is rare and occurs apparently spontaneously. It is characterized by white or yellowish, o¯Çøen with a
crystalline element, stromal deposits consisting of cholesterol,
fats and phospholipids and is not associated with vascularization (Fig. 7.56A).
¯Çü Secondary lipid keratopathy is much more common and
is associated with previous ocular injury or disease that has
resulted in corneal vascularization. ¯Çƒe most common causes
are herpes simplex and herpes zoster keratitis (Fig. 7.56B).
¯Çü Treatment is primarily aimed at medical control of the underlying in¯Ç×ammatory disease. Other options include:

{ Photocoagulation or needle cautery (suture needle grasped
with cautery forceps) of feeder vessels.

{ Penetrating keratoplasty may be required in advanced but
quiescent disease, though vascularization, thinning and
reduced sensation may a¯Ç£ect the outcome.

## Band keratopathy

{ Chelation is simple and e¯Ç£ective for relatively mild cases
and is performed using a microscope. ¯Çƒe corneal epithelium overlying the opacity and a solid layer of calci¯ÇØcation are ¯ÇØrst scraped o¯Ç£ with forceps and a scalpel
blade (e.g. No. 15). ¯Çƒe cornea is then rubbed with a
cotton-tipped applicator dipped in a solution of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) 1.5ÔÇô3.0% until all
calcium has been removed. Adequate time (15ÔÇô20 minutes) must be allowed for chelation to occur and more
than one session may be necessary. Re-epithelialization
can take many days.

{ Other modalities: diamond burr, excimer laser keratectomy and lamellar keratoplasty.

## Spheroidal degeneration

Spheroidal degeneration (Labrador keratopathy, climatic droplet keratopathy) typically occurs in men whose working lives are
spent outdoors. Ultraviolet exposure is likely to be an aetiological
factor. ¯Çƒe condition is relatively innocuous but visual impairment
may occur. A secondary form can follow in¯Ç×ammation or injury.
¯Çü Histology. Irregular proteinaceous deposits in the anterior
stroma that replace the Bowman layer.
¯Çü Signs

{ Amber-coloured granules in the super¯ÇØcial stroma of the
peripheral interpalpebral cornea (Fig. 7.58A).

{ Increasing opaci¯ÇØcation, coalescence and central spread.

Band keratopathy consists of the age-related deposition of calcium
salts in the Bowman layer, epithelial basement membrane and
anterior stroma.
¯Çü Causes

{ Advanced lesions commonly protrude above the corneal
surface (Fig. 7.58B) and the surrounding stroma is o¯Çøen
hazy. ¯Çƒe conjunctiva can be involved.
¯Çü Treatment. Protection against ultraviolet damage with sunglasses and super¯ÇØcial keratectomy or lamellar keratoplasty in
a minority.

{ Ocular. Chronic anterior uveitis (particularly in children
with juvenile idiopathic arthritis), glaucoma, phthisis
bulbi, silicone oil in the anterior chamber, chronic corneal
oedema and severe chronic keratitis.

## Salzmann nodular degeneration

{ Age-related a¯Ç£ecting otherwise healthy individuals.

{ Metabolic (metastatic calci¯ÇØcation) secondary to hypercalcaemia is uncommon and includes causes like hyperparathyroidism, vitamin D toxicity, milk-alkali syndrome,
sarcoidosis, end-stage renal disease, Paget disease and
multiple myeloma. Hyperuricaemia is a rare cause.

## Salzmann nodular degeneration consists of nodules of hyaline

tissue, usually located anterior to the Bowman layer. It can occur
in any form of chronic corneal irritation or in¯Ç×ammation such
as trachoma, dry eye, chronic blepharitis and chronic allergic
keratoconjunctivitis.
¯Çü Signs

{ Hereditary causes include familial cases and ichthyosis.
¯Çü Signs

{ Super¯ÇØcial stromal opacities progressing to elevated whitish or blueÔÇôgrey nodular lesions that may be round or
elongated (Fig. 7.59).

{ Peripheral interpalpebral calci¯ÇØcation with clear cornea
separating the sharp peripheral margins of the band from
the limbus (Fig. 7.57A).

{ ¯Çƒe base of a nodule may be associated with pannus and
epithelial iron deposition.
¯Çü Treatment consists mainly of lubrication together with control
of the cause. Removal is via manual super¯ÇØcial keratectomy ÔÇô
the lesions can o¯Çøen be ÔÇÿpeeledÔÇÖ away and the surface ¯Ç×attened
