Advancing wave-like epitheliopathy

Advancing wave-like epitheliopathy (AWE) is characterized by an
irregular advancing epithelial plaque encroaching gradually on the
cornea, typically originating at the superior limbus (Fig. 7.60) and
sometimes extending circumferentially from a pterygium. Topical ¯Ç×uorescein generally demonstrates the lesion well. Irritation
and redness are common. ¯Çƒe vision may be a¯Ç£ected with central involvement. Reported risk factors include contact lens wear,
certain contact lens solutions, topical glaucoma medication, prior
ocular surgery and some skin conditions such as rosacea. Treatment of the cause may be curative, but otherwise is with 1% silver
nitrate solution to the adjacent limbus or cryotherapy (1ÔÇô2 seconds twice) to the limbus and abnormal tissue. Distinction from
neoplasia may occasionally warrant impression cytology or excision biopsy.

METABOLIC KERATOPATHY

## Cystinosis

Cystinosis is a rare AR (gene: CTNS) lysosomal storage disorder
characterized by widespread tissue deposition of cystine crystals,
leading to paediatric renal failure and a range of other severe systemic problems. Non-nephropathic (ocular), nephropathic and
intermediate forms can occur. Keratopathy may develop in the
¯ÇØrst year, with progressive deposition of crystals in the cornea
(Fig. 7.61A) and conjunctiva associated with photophobia, epithelial erosions and visual impairment. Systemic treatment is with
cysteamine, which can be given in eye drop form to reverse corneal
crystal formation.

Inferior extent illuminated in the slit beam (arrow).

## Mucopolysaccharidoses

¯Çƒe mucopolysaccharidoses (MPS) are a group of lysosomal storage disorders involving enzyme dysfunction along the pathways for
breakdown of glycosaminoglycans, long chain carbohydrates formerly
known as mucopolysaccharides. Altered metabolites accumulate
intracellularly in various tissues. Inheritance is mainly AR. Systemic
features vary with the type of MPS, but can include facial coarseness,
skeletal anomalies, heart disease and learning di¯ÇÜculties. Keratopathy
comprises punctate corneal opaci¯ÇØcation and di¯Ç£use stromal haze
(Fig. 7.61B) and occurs in all MPS except Hunter and San¯ÇØlippo. Other
ocular features may include pigmentary retinopathy and optic atrophy.

## Wilson disease

Wilson disease (hepatolenticular degeneration) is a rare condition involving the widespread abnormal deposition of copper in
tissues. It is caused by a de¯ÇØciency of caeruloplasmin, the major

A B

C D

W Lisch ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. D.)

copper-carrying blood protein. Presentation is with liver disease,
basal ganglia dysfunction or psychiatric disturbances. A KayserÔÇô
Fleischer ring is present in 95% of patients with neurological signs
and consists of a brownish-yellow zone of ¯ÇØne copper dusting in
peripheral Descemet membrane (Fig. 7.61C). ¯Çƒis is best detected
on gonioscopy when subtle. ¯Çƒe deposits are preferentially distributed in the vertical meridian and may disappear with penicillamine therapy. Anterior capsular ÔÇÿsun¯Ç×owerÔÇÖ cataract is seen in some
patients.

Lecithin¯Çƒcholesterol¯Çƒacyltransferase
de¯Ç×ciency

Lecithin-cholesterol-acyltransferase (LCAT) de¯ÇØciency is a disorder of lipoprotein metabolism that has complete (Norum disease,
with systemic manifestations including renal failure) and partial

(¯ÇØsh eye disease, causing only corneal opaci¯ÇØcation) forms that are
both AR (gene: LCAT). Keratopathy is characterized by numerous
minute greyish dots throughout the stroma, o¯Çøen concentrated in
the periphery in an arcus-like con¯ÇØguration (Fig. 7.61D).

## Immunoprotein deposition

Di¯Ç£use or focal immunoprotein deposition is a relatively uncommon manifestation of several systemic diseases, including multiple
myeloma, Waldenstr├Âm macroglobulinaemia, monoclonal gammopathy of unknown cause, some lymphoproliferative disorders
and leukaemia. Corneal involvement may be the earliest manifestation. Bands of punctate ¯Ç×ake-like opacities are seen, mostly at
the level of the posterior stroma. Treatment is that of the underlying disease. Severe corneal involvement may require corneal
transplantation.

268 Contact Lenses

Tyrosinaemia type 2 (oculocutaneous tyrosinaemia, RichnerÔÇô
Hanhart syndrome) is a very rare AR disease (gene: TAT) in which
an enzyme de¯ÇØciency leads to elevated plasma tyrosine levels.
Ocular involvement may occasionally be the presenting feature.

## Painful palmar and plantar hyperkeratotic lesions and variable

CNS involvement are seen. A bilateral pseudodendritic keratitis
with crystalline edges o¯Çøen begins in childhood and causes photophobia, watering and redness.

## Fabry disease

Fabry disease is an X-linked lysosomal storage disorder caused
by a de¯ÇØciency of the enzyme alpha-galactosidase A that leads to
abnormal tissue accumulation of a glycolipid. All males with the
gene develop the disease and some heterozygous females. Systemic
features include periodic burning pain in the extremities (acroparaesthesia) and GI tract, angiokeratomas (Fig. 7.62A), cardiomyopathy and renal disease. Ocular manifestations include white
to golden-brown corneal opacities in a vortex pattern (75%) that
may be the ¯ÇØrst feature of the disease (Fig. 7.62B) facilitating early
intervention, wedge- or spoke-shaped posterior cataract (Fabry
cataract), conjunctival vascular tortuosity (corkscrew vessels) and
aneurysm formation (Fig. 7.62C) and retinal vascular tortuosity.

CONTACT LENSES

## Therapeutic uses

¯Çƒe risks of ¯ÇØtting a contact lens to an already compromised eye
are greater than with lens wear for cosmetic reasons. ¯Çƒe bene¯ÇØtÔÇô
risk balance should be considered in each case individually, with
education and regular review to ensure early diagnosis and treatment of complications. ¯Çƒe choice of lens type is dictated by the
nature of the ocular pathology.

## Optical

## Optical indications are aimed at improving visual acuity when this

cannot be achieved by spectacles in the following situations:
¯Çü Irregular astigmatism associated with keratoconus can be
corrected with rigid contact lenses a¯Çøer spectacles have failed
and before corneal gra¯Çøing becomes necessary. Patients with
astigmatism following corneal gra¯Çøing may also bene¯ÇØt.
¯Çü Super¯Ç×cial corneal irregularities can be neutralized by
rigid contact lenses, which provide a smoother and optically
more regular surface. Visual acuity can only be substantially
improved if irregularities are not too severe.
¯Çü Anisometropia in which binocular vision cannot be achieved
by spectacles due to aniseikonia, such as may occur following monocular cataract surgery with high refractive error
correction.

## Promotion of epithelial healing

¯Çü Persistent epithelial defects o¯Çøen heal if the regenerating
corneal epithelium is protected from the constant rubbing of

A

B

C

keratopathy (arrow); (C) conjunctival vessel tortuosity and
aneurysms.

the lids, allowing the development of hemidesmosomal attachments to the basement membrane.
¯Çü Recurrent corneal erosions associated with basement membrane dystrophy may require long-term contact lens wear to

reduce the recurrence rate. In post-traumatic cases, lens wear
can usually be discontinued a¯Çøer a few weeks. Lens wear also
improves comfort.

## Pain relief

¯Çü Bullous keratopathy can be managed with so¯Çø bandage lenses
that relieve pain by protecting the exposed corneal nerve endings from the shearing forces of the lids during blinking. ¯Çƒe
lens may also ¯Ç×atten bullae into di¯Ç£use ¯ÇØne epithelial cysts.
¯Çü Filamentary keratopathy resistant to topical treatment will
usually achieve some relief from so¯Çø contact lens wear.
¯Çü Other indications include ¯Çƒygeson super¯ÇØcial punctate keratitis and protection of the corneal epithelium from aberrant
lashes in trichiasis. ¯Çƒey can also be used as a temporizing
measure in entropion prior to de¯ÇØnitive surgery.

## Preservation of corneal integrity

¯Çü A descemetocoele can be temporarily capped with a tight¯ÇØtting, large-diameter so¯Çø or scleral lens to prevent perforation and encourage healing.
¯Çü Splinting and apposition of the edges of a small corneal
wound can be achieved by means of a contact lens. Slightly
larger perforations may be sealed with glue followed by
insertion of a bandage contact lens to both protect the glue
and prevent irritation of the lids from the glueÔÇÖs irregular
surface.

## Miscellaneous indications

¯Çü Ptosis props to support the upper lids in patients with ocular
myopathies.
¯Çü Maintenance of the fornices to prevent symblepharon formation in cicatrizing conjunctivitis.
¯Çü Drug delivery enhancement by a hydrogel lens imbued with
topical medication is an occasional indication.

## Complications

## Mechanical and hypoxic keratitis

¯Çü Pathogenesis. Insu¯ÇÜcient oxygen transmission through the
lens. A tightly ¯ÇØtting contact lens that does not move with
blinking will impair tear circulation under the lens. ¯Çƒis is
exacerbated by lid closure if the lens is worn during sleep.

## Hypoxia leads to anaerobic metabolism and lactic acidosis
that inhibits the normal barrier and pump mechanisms of the

cornea.
¯Çü Super¯Ç×cial punctate keratitis is the most common complication. ¯Çƒe pattern may give a clue as to the aetiology. For example, staining at 3 and 9 oÔÇÖclock is associated with incomplete
blinking and drying in rigid lens wearers.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe tight lens syndrome is characterized by indentation and
staining of the conjunctival epithelium in a ring around the
cornea.

¯Çü Acute hypoxia is characterized by epithelial microcysts (Fig.
7.63A) and necrosis and endothelial blebs. Very painful macroerosions may develop several hours a¯Çøer lenses are removed
following a period of overwear.
¯Çü Chronic hypoxia may result in vascularization (Fig. 7.63B)
and lipid deposition. Super¯ÇØcial peripheral neovascularization
of <1.5 mm is common in myopic contact lens wearers and can
be monitored.
¯Çü Treatment depends on the cause but may involve:

{ Increasing oxygen permeability by re¯ÇØtting with a thinner
lens, a gas permeable rigid lens or a silicone hydrogel so¯Çø
lens.

{ Modifying lens ¯ÇØt to increase movement.

{ Reducing lens wearing time.

Immune response (hypersensitivity) keratitis

A hypersensitivity response to bacterial antigen or the chemicals
used in lens care can lead to the development of sterile marginal
corneal in¯ÇØltrates. ¯Çƒe mechanism is thought to be similar to that
of marginal keratitis.
¯Çü Signs. Mildly red eye associated with in¯ÇØltrates, o¯Çøen marginally located, with no or minimal epithelial defects.
¯Çü Treatment involves cessation of lens wear until resolution
occurs. Topical antibiotics and steroids may be used in some
cases, but if the diagnosis is uncertain, treatment should be
that of bacterial keratitis.

## Toxic keratitis

¯Çü Pathogenesis. Acute chemical injury may be caused by inadvertently placing a contact lens on the eye without ¯ÇØrst neutralizing toxic cleaning agents such as hydrogen peroxide. Chronic
toxicity can result from long-term exposure to disinfecting
preservatives such as thiomersal or benzalkonium chloride.
¯Çü Signs

{ Acute pain, redness and chemosis on lens insertion, which
may take 48 hours to resolve completely.

{ Vascularization and scarring of the cornea (Fig. 7.63C)
and limbal conjunctiva in chronic cases.
¯Çü Treatment may involve switching to daily disposable lenses or
using a non-preserved disinfectant such as hydrogen peroxide.

## Suppurative keratitis

## Contact lens wear is the greatest risk factor for the development of

bacterial keratitis (Fig. 7.63D). ¯Çƒe risk is probably least for rigid
contact lenses. Bacteria in the tear ¯ÇØlm are normally unable to bind
to the corneal epithelium, but following an abrasion and in association with hypoxia, they can attach and invade the epithelium.

## Microorganisms may also be introduced onto the corneal surface

by poor lens hygiene or the use of tap water for rinsing.

Contact lens-associated giant papillary
conjunctivitis

See Chapter 6.

CONGENITAL ANOMALIES OF THE
CORNEA AND GLOBE

## Microcornea

¯Çƒe normal neonatal corneal diameter is 10 mm and the adult diameter of 12 mm is usually reached by the age of 2 years. Microcornea is a
rare autosomal dominant (sometimes sporadic) unilateral or bilateral
condition in which the horizontal corneal diameter is 10 mm or less
over 2 years of age (Fig. 7.64A), or less than 9 mm in the newborn.
¯Çƒe cornea is steep on keratometry. ¯Çƒere may be hypermetropia and
a shallow anterior chamber but other dimensions are normal. Ocular
associations include glaucoma (angle-closure and open angle), congenital cataract, leukoma (Fig. 7.64B), persistent fetal vasculature,
coloboma, optic nerve hypoplasia, aniridia and nanophthalmos. Systemic associations have been reported. Refractive error and amblyopia should be managed appropriately.

## Microphthalmos

Microphthalmos (microphthalmia ÔÇô Fig. 7.65A) is a condition
in which the entire eye is small, with an axial length at least
two standard deviations below the mean for age. Simple or pure
microphthalmos (nanophthalmos ÔÇô see below) refers to an eye
that is structurally normal apart from a short length and complex microphthalmos to eyes with other features of dysgenesis,
for example a coloboma (Fig. 7.65B) or orbital cyst and a range
of other ocular abnormalities. It may be unilateral or bilateral;
when unilateral, abnormalities may be present in the fellow eye.
Vision is variably a¯Ç£ected, in conjunction with severity. It is
typically sporadic but mutations in numerous genes have been
implicated. Around 50% of cases may be associated with systemic abnormalities, including of the CNS. Potential environmental causes include fetal alcohol syndrome and intrauterine
infections.

A B

opacity with dense cataract.

A B

C D

and bilateral iris colobomas; (C) bilateral simple anophthalmos; (D) monocular anophthalmos.

272 Congenital Anomalies of the Cornea and Globe

## Nanophthalmos

## In nanophthalmos the entire eye is small with an axial length of

less than 20 mm. Both eyes are usually a¯Ç£ected. Ocular associations include glaucoma (especially angle-closure as the lens is
large relative to the size of the eye), hypermetropia, ametropia,
amblyopia and strabismus. In childhood, management of refractive error and amblyopia are critical. Surgery for glaucoma is
particularly hazardous and can result in aqueous misdirection
and choroidal e¯Ç£usion (ÔÇÿsmall eyeÔÇôbig troubleÔÇÖ). ¯Çƒe best option
is lens extraction with IOL implantation. ¯Çƒe surgery is technically challenging, but has the advantage of deepening the anterior
chamber and reducing the refractive error if a suitable highpower lens implant is inserted.

## Anophthalmos

Anophthalmos (anophthalmia) refers to the complete absence
of any visible globe structure (Fig. 7.65C and D), though a
microphthalmic remnant or cyst may be present. It is associated with other abnormalities such as absence of extraocular muscles, a short conjunctival sac and microblepharon.

## Causative factors are probably broadly similar to those of

microphthalmos.

## Megalocornea

Megalocornea is a rare, bilateral, non-progressive condition that is usually X-linked recessive. Approximately 90% of
affected individuals are male. The adult horizontal corneal
diameter is 13 mm or more, with a very deep anterior chamber
(Fig. 7.66A). There is usually high myopia and astigmatism but

normal corrected visual acuity. Lens subluxation may occur due
to zonular stretching and pigment dispersion syndrome may
occur (see Ch. 11). Numerous systemic associations have been
reported.

## Cornea plana

¯Çƒis is an extremely rare bilateral condition in which the cornea is
¯Ç×atter than normal (Fig. 7.66B) ÔÇô the radius of curvature is larger.
¯Çƒere is a corresponding reduction in refractive power resulting
in high hypermetropia. Two forms are described, cornea plana 1,
which is dominantly inherited (CNA1), being milder than cornea
plana 2, which is recessively inherited (CNA2). Associated ocular
abnormalities are common.

## Sclerocornea

Sclerocornea is a very rare, usually bilateral, condition that may
be associated with cornea plana (see below). Sporadic cases are
common, but a milder form can be inherited as AD and a more
severe form as AR. Peripheral corneal opaci¯ÇØcation, with no visible border between the sclera and cornea, confers the appearance
of apparently reduced corneal diameter in mildÔÇômoderate disease
(Fig. 7.67A). Occasionally the entire cornea is involved (Fig. 7.67B).

## Keratectasia

Keratectasia is a very rare, usually unilateral, condition thought to
be the result of intrauterine keratitis and perforation. It is characterized by protuberance between the eyelids of a severely opaci¯ÇØed
and sometimes vascularized cornea (Fig. 7.68). It is o¯Çøen associated with raised IOP.

A B

¯Çƒg. B.)

A B

## Posterior keratoconus

## Posterior keratoconus is a sporadic condition in which there

is unilateral non-progressive increase in curvature of the posterior corneal surface. The anterior surface is normal and

visual acuity relatively unimpaired because of the similar
refractive indices of the cornea and aqueous humour. Generalized (involvement of the entire posterior corneal surface) and
localized types are described (paracentral or central posterior
indentation ÔÇô Fig. 7.69).

KERATOPLASTY

## Introduction

Corneal transplantation (gra¯Ç×ing) refers to the replacement of
diseased host corneal tissue by healthy donor cornea. A corneal
gra¯Ç× (keratoplasty) may be partial-thickness (anterior or posterior
lamellar) or full-thickness (penetrating).

## General indications

¯Çü Optical keratoplasty is performed to improve vision. Important indications include keratoconus, scarring, corneal dystrophies (Fig. 8.1A), pseudophakic bullous keratopathy and
corneal degenerations.
¯Çü Tectonic gra¯Ç×ing may be carried out to restore or preserve
corneal integrity in eyes with severe structural changes such as
thinning with descemetocoele (US spelling ÔÇô descemetocele)
(Fig. 8.1B) and perforated corneal ulcer (Fig. 8.1C).

A

C

¯Çü ¯Çƒerapeutic corneal transplantation facilitates removal of
infected corneal tissue in eyes unresponsive to antimicrobial
therapy (Fig. 8.1D).
¯Çü Cosmetic gra¯Ç×ing may be performed to improve the appearance of the eye, but is a rare indication.

## Donor tissue

Donor tissue should be removed within 12ÔÇô24 hours of death.
¯ÇØere is an attempt to age-match donors and recipients. Corneas
from infants (3 years and under) are used only very occasionally,
even for paediatric transplants, as they are associated with surgical,
refractive and rejection problems. Most corneas are stored in coordinating ÔÇÿeye banksÔÇÖ prior to transplantation, where pre-release
evaluation includes medical history review and donor blood
screening to exclude contraindications and microscopic examination of the cornea including endothelial cell count determination.
Corneas are preserved in hypothermic storage (up to 7ÔÇô10 days)
or organ culture medium (4 weeks) until needed. Culture allows

B

D

(D) penetrating keratoplasty for pseudomonas keratitis ÔÇô a dense cataract and posterior
synechiae are visible. (Courtesy of S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

extended testing for infective contamination. Contraindications to
ocular tissue donation are set out below, though there is international variation and the list is not exhaustive:
¯Çü Death from unknown cause.
¯Çü Certain systemic infections such as human immunode¯Ç£ciency
virus (HIV), viral hepatitis, syphilis, congenital rubella, tuberculosis, septicaemia and active malaria.
¯Çü Prior high-risk behaviour for HIV and hepatitis such as sex
with someone HIV-positive, men who have sex with men,
intravenous drug abuse and prostitution.
¯Çü Within the last 12 months: sex with someone who has engaged
in high-risk behaviour, who has received blood clotting factor concentrates or has undergone tattooing, acupuncture, ear/
body piercing.
¯Çü Infectious and possibly infectious diseases of the central nervous system, such as CreutzfeldtÔÇôJakob disease, systemic
sclerosing panencephalitis, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, encephalitis, Alzheimer disease and other
dementias, Parkinson disease, multiple sclerosis and motor
neurone disease.
¯Çü Receipt of a transplanted organ.
¯Çü Receipt of human pituitary-derived growth hormone.
¯Çü Brain or spinal surgery before 1992.
¯Çü Most haematological malignancies.
¯Çü Ocular disease such as in¯Çøammation and disease likely to compromise gra¯Ç× outcome, some malignant ocular tumours (e.g.
retinoblastoma) and corneal refractive surgery.

## Recipient prognostic factors

¯ÇØe following host factors may adversely a¯ÇÜect the prognosis of a
corneal gra¯Ç× and, if possible, should be optimized prior to surgery.
In general, the most favourable cases are keratoconus, localized
scars and dystrophies.
¯Çü Severe stromal vascularization (Fig. 8.2), absence of corneal
sensation, extreme thinning at the proposed hostÔÇôgra¯Ç× junction and active corneal in¯Çøammation.

resulting from recurrent herpes simplex keratitis. (Courtesy
of C Barry.)

¯Çü Abnormalities of the eyelids, such as blepharitis, ectropion,
entropion and trichiasis. ¯ÇØese conditions should be addressed
before surgery.
¯Çü Recurrent or progressive forms of conjunctival in¯Çøammation,
such as atopic conjunctivitis and ocular cicatricial pemphigoid.
¯Çü Tear ¯Ç£lm dysfunction.
¯Çü Anterior synechiae.
¯Çü Uncontrolled glaucoma.
¯Çü Uveitis.

## Penetrating keratoplasty

Component layer gra¯Ç×ing of the cornea is increasingly utilized,
but full-thickness keratoplasty remains commonly performed and
is the appropriate procedure for disease involving all layers of the
cornea (Fig. 8.3). Key surgical points include:
¯Çü A common gra¯Ç× size is 7.5 mm. Small gra¯Ç×s may lead to
high astigmatism and larger diameters are associated with an
increasing tendency to peripheral anterior synechiae formation and raised intraocular pressure (IOP).
¯Çü ¯Çƒe donor button is usually about 0.25 mm larger in diameter
than the host site.
¯Çü Preparation of donor cornea should always precede excision
of host tissue, in case a problem with the former means that the
surgery cannot be completed.
¯Çü Trephination. Either mechanically guided manual or automated (including laser) trephination is commonly used.
¯Çü Sutures. ¯ÇØe gra¯Ç× may be secured with either continuous (Fig.
8.4A) or interrupted (Fig. 8.4B) suture techniques, or a combination of both.

## Postoperative management

¯Çü Topical steroids (e.g. prednisolone acetate 1%, dexamethasone
phosphate 0.1%) are used to decrease the risk of immunological gra¯Ç× rejection. Initial administration is typically every 2
hours with gradual tapering depending on the likelihood of
rejection and clinical progress. Long-term instillation at low
intensity, such as once daily for a year or more, is usual.
¯Çü Other immunosuppressants such as oral azathioprine and
topical and systemic ciclosporin are usually reserved for highrisk patients.
¯Çü Cycloplegia (e.g. homatropine 2% twice daily) is typically used
for 1ÔÇô2 weeks.
¯Çü Oral aciclovir may be used in the context of pre-existing
herpes simplex keratitis to minimize the risk of recurrence.
¯Çü Monitoring of IOP. Applanation tonometry is relatively unreliable so measurement is commonly performed during the
early postoperative period with a non-applanation method.
¯Çü Monitoring endothelial function. ¯ÇØis can be done indirectly
and quickly by measuring the central corneal thickness with a
pachymeter.
¯Çü Removal of sutures is performed when the gra¯Ç×ÔÇôhost junction has healed. ¯ÇØis is o¯Ç×en a¯Ç×er 12ÔÇô18 months, although in
elderly patients it may take much longer. Removal of broken or
loose individual sutures should be performed as soon as identi¯Ç£ed, as this reduces the risk of gra¯Ç× rejection.

TIP In a patient with a corneal graft, broken or loose sutures
should be removed as this reduces the risk of localized
vascularization and graft rejection.

## Postoperative complications

¯Çü Early complications include persistent epithelial defects, loose
or protruding sutures (Fig. 8.5A and B) (risk of infection ÔÇô
marked sterile reaction, papillary hypertrophy), wound leak
(sometimes with ¯Çøat anterior chamber or iris prolapse), uveitis, elevation of intraocular pressure, traumatic gra¯Ç× rupture
(Fig. 8.6A), cystoid macular oedema, microbial keratitis (Fig.
8.6B), endophthalmitis (Fig. 8.6C) and rejection (see below).
A rare complication is a ¯Ç£xed dilated pupil (Urrets-Zavalia
syndrome).

¯Çü Late complications include astigmatism, recurrence of underlying disease, late wound dehiscence, retro-corneal membrane
formation, glaucoma, rejection (see below) and failure without
rejection.

## Corneal graft rejection

Immunological rejection of any layer of the cornea can occur.
Rejection of separate layers (endothelial, stromal and epithelial)
can occur in isolation, but typically a combination is present.
Simple gra¯Ç× failure can occur in the absence of rejection, although
rejection is a common contributory factor.

TIP The risk of corneal graft rejection is increased
signi¯Çƒcantly by the presence of host stromal vascularization.

280 Keratoplasty

A

B

C

keratitis; (C) endophthalmitis. (R Bates ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A; S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

¯Çü Pathogenesis. ¯ÇØe corneal gra¯Ç× is immunologically privileged, with an absence of blood vessels and lymphatics and the
presence of relatively few antigen-presenting cells. In¯Çøammation and neovascularization contribute to loss of this privilege.

## Important predisposing factors for rejection include eccentric

or larger gra¯Ç×s (over 8 mm in diameter), infection (particularly herpetic), glaucoma and previous keratoplasty. If the host
becomes sensitized to histocompatibility antigens present
in the donor cornea, rejection may result. Human leukocyte
antigen (HLA) matching has a small bene¯Ç£cial e¯ÇÜect on gra¯Ç×
survival. Gender incompatibility has recently emerged as an
important risk factor for gra¯Ç× rejection and failure. Whereas
the cornea of a female donor can be used in either male or
female recipients, the cornea of a male donor should not be
allocated to a female recipient.
¯Çü Symptoms. Blurred vision, redness, photophobia and pain
are typical, but many cases are asymptomatic until rejection
is established. ¯ÇØe timing of onset is very variable, occurring
from days to years a¯Ç×er keratoplasty.
¯Çü Signs vary depending on the type of gra¯Ç×.

{ Ciliary injection associated with anterior uveitis is an early
manifestation (Fig. 8.7A).

{ Epithelial rejection may be accompanied by an elevated
line of abnormal epithelium (Fig. 8.7B) in a quiet or mildly
in¯Çøamed eye, occurring at an average of 3 months.

{ Subepithelial rejection is characterized by subepithelial
in¯Ç£ltrates, reminiscent of adenoviral infection (Krachmer spots ÔÇô Fig. 8.7C) on the donor cornea, with deeper
oedema and in¯Ç£ltrative opaci¯Ç£cation.

{ Stromal rejection features deeper haze. It can be chronic
or hyperacute, the latter in association with endothelial
rejection.

{ Endothelial rejection is characterized by a linear pattern of
keratic precipitates (Khodadoust line ÔÇô Fig. 8.7D) associated with an area of in¯Çøammation at the gra¯Ç× margin.

{ Stromal oedema is indicative of endothelial failure.
¯Çü Management. Early intensive treatment greatly improves the
likelihood of reversing the rejection. ¯ÇØe most aggressive regimen is generally required for endothelial rejection, followed in
order of severity by stromal, subepithelial and epithelial. Intraocular pressure monitoring is critical.

{ Preservative-free topical steroids hourly for 24 hours are
the mainstay of therapy. ¯ÇØe frequency is reduced gradually over several weeks. Steroid ointment can be used at
bedtime as the regimen is tapered. High-risk patients can
be maintained on the highest tolerated topical dose (e.g.
prednisolone acetate 1% four times daily) for an extended
period.

{ Topical cycloplegia (e.g. homatropine 2% or atropine 1%
once or twice daily).

{ Topical ciclosporin 0.05% to 2% may be of bene¯Ç£t, but the
onset of action is delayed.

{ Systemic steroids. Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 1ÔÇô2
weeks with subsequent tapering. If given within 8 days of
onset, intravenous methylprednisolone 500 mg daily for
up to 3 days may be particularly e¯ÇÜective, suppressing
rejection and reducing the risk of further episodes.

{ Subconjunctival steroid injection (e.g. 0.5 ml of 4 mg/ml
dexamethasone) is sometimes used.

{ Other systemic immunosuppressants such as ciclosporin,
tacrolimus or azathioprine can be considered.

A

B

C D

intense peripheral vascularization. (Courtesy of S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs A, B and C.)

¯Çü Di¯ÇØerential diagnosis includes gra¯Ç× failure (no in¯Çøammation), infective keratitis including fungal and herpetic, uveitis,
sterile suture reaction, raised IOP and epithelial ingrowth.

TIP In a patient with acute corneal graft rejection, early
intensive treatment greatly improves the likelihood of reversing
the episode of rejection.

Super¯Çƒcial lamellar keratoplasty

¯ÇØis involves partial-thickness excision of the corneal epithelium
and stroma so that the endothelium and part of the deep stroma
are le¯Ç× behind as a bed for appropriately partial-thickness donor
cornea. ¯ÇØe area gra¯Ç×ed depends on the extent of the disease process to be addressed.
¯Çü Indications

{ Opaci¯Ç£cation of the super¯Ç£cial one-third of the corneal
stroma not caused by potentially recurrent disease.

## Deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty

Deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) is a technique in
which corneal tissue is removed almost to the level of Descemet
membrane. A theoretical advantage is the decreased risk of rejection because the endothelium, a major target for rejection, is not
transplanted. ¯ÇØe major technical di¯ÇÖculty lies in judging the
depth of the corneal dissection as close as possible to Descemet
membrane without perforation and if this is not achieved the
visual outcome may be compromised.
¯Çü Indications

{ Disease involving the anterior 95% of corneal thickness
with normal endothelium and the absence of breaks or

{ Marginal corneal thinning or in¯Ç£ltration as in recurrent
pterygium, Terrien marginal degeneration and limbal dermoid or other tumours.

{ Localized thinning or descemetocoele formation (see Fig.
8.1B).

{ Chronic in¯Çøammatory disease such as atopic keratoconjunctivitis that carries an increased risk of gra¯Ç× rejection.
¯Çü Advantages

{ No risk of endothelial rejection, although epithelial/subepithelial/stromal rejection may occur.

{ Less astigmatism and a structurally stronger globe compared with penetrating keratoplasty.

{ Increased availability of gra¯Ç× material since endothelial

{ Di¯ÇÖcult and time-consuming with a high risk of

Stem cells are undi¯ÇÜerentiated cells that give rise to the di¯ÇÜerentiated cells that form tissues and organs. Tissue-speci¯Ç£c stem cells
are important for maintaining homeostasis and for tissue repair
a¯Ç×er injury. Corneal epithelium is constantly self-renewing and
does so throughout life. Progeny of corneal stem cells divide and
di¯ÇÜerentiate to form basal corneal epithelial cells (transient amplifying cells) that di¯ÇÜerentiate into wing cells (post-mitotic cells)
and, ¯Ç£nally, super¯Ç£cial squamous cells (terminally di¯ÇÜerentiated
cells).

## Various techniques have been described to replenish the

limbal stem cell population in patients with severe ocular surface disease and associated stem cell deficiency. These include
transplantation of a limbal area of limited size from a healthy
fellow eye, complete limbal transplantation of a donor annulus
and ex¯Çƒvivo expansion by culture of either host or donor stem
cells with subsequent transplantation. In patients with unilateral ocular involvement in StevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome and
ocular cicatricial pemphigoid, donor tissue should not be taken
from the unaffected eye (autologous).
¯Çü Signs of limbal stem cell de¯Ç£ciency (LSCD) include:

## Endothelial keratoplasty

Endothelial keratoplasty involves removal of diseased endothelium along with Descemet membrane (DM) through a corneoscleral or corneal incision. Folded donor tissue is introduced
through the same small (2.8ÔÇô5.0 mm) incision. Descemet stripping (automated) endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK) uses an automated microkeratome to prepare donor tissue and is currently the
most commonly performed technique. A small amount of posterior stromal thickness is transplanted along with DM and endothelium (Fig. 8.9). In Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty
(DMEK) only the DM and endothelium are transplanted.
Because the gra¯Ç× is only 5ÔÇô10 ╬╝m thick it is di¯ÇÖcult to handle.
However, the gra¯Ç×s are able to attach to the host tissue with less
unevenness, which leads to more rapid visual recovery. Visual

{ Conjunctivalization of cornea with goblet cells (con¯Ç£rmed
with confocal microscopy, impression cytology with acid
Schi¯ÇÜ stain or monoclonal antibody against cytokeratin 19).

{ Super¯Ç£cial and deep corneal vascularization.

{ Fibrovascular pannus.

{ Persistent epithelial defects.

{ Scarring.
¯Çü Algorithm for surgical interventions for LSCD

{ All associated abnormalities (lids, conjunctiva, intraocular
pressure) should be treated ¯Ç£rst.

{ In total bilateral stem cell de¯Ç£ciency, use of allogra¯Ç×s
from a living related donor or a cadaver donor is the only

{ Congenital: for example, aniridia.

{ Traumatic: chemical and thermal burns.

{ Chronic in¯Çøammatory disease: StevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome, ocular cicatricial pemphigoid.

{ Ocular surface malignancy.

{ Contact lens-related pathology.

{ Regeneration of corneal surface epithelium.

{ Visual improvement and improved comfort.

284 Refractive Procedures

KERATOPROSTHESES

Keratoprostheses (Fig. 8.11) are arti¯Ç£cial corneal implants used in
patients unsuitable for keratoplasty. ¯ÇØe modern osteoodontokeratoprosthesis consists of the patientÔÇÖs own tooth root and alveolar bone
supporting a central optical cylinder and is usually covered with a buccal mucous membrane gra¯Ç×. Surgery is di¯ÇÖcult and time-consuming
and is performed in two stages, 2ÔÇô4 months apart. In experienced
hands the retention rate is about 85% at 18 months. Approximately
80% of patients achieve visual acuity between counting ¯Ç£ngers and
6/12 and occasionally even better. A poor outcome is usually associated with pre-existing optic nerve or retinal dysfunction. High patient
motivation is a key factor to consider before proceeding to surgery.
¯Çü Indications

{ Bilateral blindness from severe, but inactive anterior segment
disease with no realistic chance of success from conventional keratoplasty, e.g. StevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome, ocular
cicatricial pemphigoid, chemical burns and trachoma.

{ Vision of counting ¯Ç£ngers or less in the better eye.

{ Intact optic nerve and retinal function, without marked
glaucomatous optic neuropathy.
¯Çü Complications

{ Glaucoma in up to 40%. Management is inevitably extremely
challenging and requires aggressive IOP-lowering medication and o¯Ç×en the insertion of a drainage shunt.

{ Membrane formation behind the prosthesis (25%); usually
managed with YAG-laser membranectomy.

{ Tilting or extrusion.

{ Retinal detachment and endophthalmitis (about 3% per
patient year).

or lens, the principal refracting components. Myopia, hypermetropia (hyperopia) and astigmatism can all be addressed,
though correction of presbyopia is yet to be achieved on a consistently satisfactory basis.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe patient population consists of people with stable refractive error who desire an improvement in visual acuity or function a¯Ç×er surgical correction.
¯Çü A thorough preoperative assessment is mandatory. Not
everyone who requests this treatment is suitable. People who
require a high level of quality vision, e.g, certain professions
(pilot, air-tra¯ÇÖc controller, truck-driver) and speci¯Ç£c hobbies
(contact sports, marksman, amateur astronomer) must understand the limitations of the surgery before proceeding.
¯Çü Informed consent is vitally important.
¯Çü Contraindications include: people with unacceptably high
expectations, dry eye syndrome, non-stable refractive error,
some systemic diseases (SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, immunocompromise) and those with ocular disease (chronic
blepharitis, previous herpes simplex, glaucoma, macular
degeneration).
¯Çü ¯Çƒe advantages and potential risks should be discussed and
the common adverse side e¯ÇÜects such as under- and overcorrection, dry eye symptoms and eventual presbyopia should
be emphasized. Care should be taken to discuss which adverse
events are likely to be transient and which may be permanent.
¯Çü Alternatives, including new and emerging technology for
treatment, should be discussed.
¯Çü Patient satisfaction depends on patient expectations and the
surgical outcome. Most are satis¯Ç£ed a¯Ç×er the surgery, but some
may be unhappy because of induced ocular side e¯ÇÜects and
visual aberrations, even though the intended correction has
been achieved.

TIP People who require a high level of quality vision because
of their occupation or hobbies need to understand the
limitations of refractive surgery and make an informed decision
before proceeding to surgical correction.

## Correction of myopia

¯Çü Surface ablation procedures (see below) can correct lowÔÇô
moderate degrees of myopia.
¯Çü Laser in situ keratomileusis (LASIK ÔÇô see below) can correct
moderate to high myopia depending on initial corneal thickness, but for very high refractive errors one of the intraocular
procedures below is necessary.
¯Çü Refractive lenticule extraction (SMILE ÔÇô see below) is a small
incisional laser technique for the correction of myopia and
myopic astigmatism.
¯Çü Clear lens exchange gives very good visual results but carries a
small risk of the complications of cataract surgery (see Ch. 10),
particularly retinal detachment in individuals with high
myopia.
¯Çü Iris clip (ÔÇÿlobster clawÔÇÖ) implant is attached to the iris (Fig.
8.12A). Complications include subluxation or dislocation due
to dislodgement of one or both attachments, an oval pupil,

REFRACTIVE PROCEDURES

## Introduction

¯Çü Refractive surgery encompasses a range of procedures aimed
at changing the refraction of the eye by altering the cornea

A B

implant with anterior iris attachment at 3 and 9 oÔÇÖclock; (B) emplacement of a posterior chamber phakic implant between the iris and anterior lens surface. (Courtesy of J Krachmer, M

¯Çü Phakic posterior chamber implant (implantable contact lens,
ICL) is inserted behind the iris and in front of the lens (Fig.
8.12B) and supported in the ciliary sulcus. ¯ÇØe lens is composed of material derived from collagen (Collamer) with a
power of ÔêÆ3 D to ÔêÆ20.50 D. Visual results are usually good, but
complications include uveitis, pupillary block, endothelial cell
loss, cataract formation and retinal detachment.
¯Çü Radial keratotomy (Fig. 8.13) is now predominantly of historical interest.

Correction of hypermetropia (hyperopia)

¯Çü Surface ablation procedures can correct low degrees of
hypermetropia.
¯Çü LASIK can correct up to 4 D.
¯Çü Conductive keratoplasty (CK) involves the application of
radiofrequency energy to the corneal stroma in order to correct lowÔÇômoderate hypermetropia and hypermetropic astigmatism. Burns are placed in one or two rings in the corneal
periphery using a micro-needle high-frequency probe. ¯ÇØe
resultant thermally induced stromal shrinkage is accompanied
by an increase in central corneal curvature. Complications
are infrequent, but the disadvantages include early overcorrection, signi¯Ç£cant regression and induced astigmatism.
CK may also be helpful for presbyopia (see below). Laser thermal keratoplasty with a holmium laser is no longer used.
¯Çü Other modalities include clear lens extraction and phakic lens
implants as described above for myopia. Intraocular surgical
procedures are the only options for high degrees of refractive
error.

## Correction of astigmatism

¯Çü Limbal relaxing incisions/arcuate keratotomy involves making paired arcuate incisions on opposite sides of the cornea

(Fig. 8.14A) in the axis of the correcting ÔÇÿplusÔÇÖ cylinder (the
steep meridian). ¯ÇØe resultant ¯Çøattening of the steep meridian
coupled with a smaller steepening of the ¯Çøat meridian at 90┬░ to
the incisions reduces astigmatism. ¯ÇØe desired result can be controlled by varying the length and depth of the incisions and their
distance from the optical centre of the cornea. Arcuate keratotomy may be combined with compression sutures placed in the
perpendicular meridian, when treating large degrees of astigmatism such as can occur following penetrating keratoplasty.
¯Çü PRK and LASEK can correct up to 3 D.
¯Çü LASIK can correct up to 5 D.
¯Çü Lens surgery involves using a ÔÇÿtoricÔÇÖ intraocular implant incorporating an astigmatic correction (Fig. 8.14B). Postoperative rotation of the
implant away from the desired axis occurs in a minority of cases.
¯Çü Conductive keratoplasty (see ÔÇÿCorrection of hypermetropiaÔÇÖ
above).

## Correction of presbyopia

¯Çü Lens extraction, either to treat cataract or for purely refractive
purposes. Acronyms used include clear lens exchange (CLE),
refractive lens exchange (RLE) and presbyopic lens exchange
(PreLEx). Much research e¯ÇÜort is being applied to the development of e¯ÇÜective accommodating and multifocal prosthetic
lenses.

{ Implantation of a bifocal, trifocal di¯ÇÜractive, multifocal
refractive, extended depth of focus or ÔÇÿaccommodatingÔÇÖ/
pseudo-accommodative intraocular lens implant can optically restore reading vision, but glasses may still have to be
used for some tasks. If appropriately screened, most recipients of multifocal IOLs are happy with the visual outcome.
However, dissatisfaction may occur as a consequence of
nocturnal glare, halo and reduced contrast sensitivity. In
patients with intractable visual symptoms IOL exchange
surgery should be undertaken. In some jurisdictions,
implantation of a multifocal IOL is a contraindication to
the holding of a private or commercial pilotÔÇÖs licence, or to
military service.

¯Çü Corneal multifocality: presbyLASIK. Several di¯ÇÜerent
approaches are under development utilizing a laser procedure
to alter the shape of the cornea such that a bifocal or transitional e¯ÇÜect is induced. Laser manufacturers have developed
speci¯Ç£c treatment algorithms based on the near-addition target
(or planned according to the subjectÔÇÖs age) to create a multifocal corneal pro¯Ç£le. However, the e¯ÇÜective change in refraction
comes from the controlled change in the corneal asphericity
from the centre to the periphery. ¯ÇØe bene¯Ç£t of presbyLASIK
over monofocal ablation is not yet clear.
¯Çü Scleral expansion surgery. Results have been inconsistent and
unpredictable and this technique has not achieved sustained
popularity.
¯Çü Intracorneal inlays provide substantial bene¯Ç£t in presbyopia,
though in the past the biocompatibility of some materials has
been relatively poor and complications such as corneal haze
and extrusion can necessitate removal.

{ Types: (a) refractive corneal inlays (Fig. 8.15A and B), (b)
corneal reshaping inlays and (c) small aperture inlays (Fig.
8.15C and D).

{ ÔÇÿMonovisionÔÇÖ consists of the targeting of IOL-induced

extractionÔÇÖ).

## Laser refractive procedures

To settle any contact lens-induced corneal distortion prior to
de¯Ç£nitive keratometry, so¯Ç× contact lenses should probably be discontinued for 2 weeks and hard/rigid gas permeable lenses for at
least 3 weeks (some surgeons suggest 1 week for each year of wear
to date).

## Laser in situ keratomileusis

Laser (or laser-assisted) in situ keratomileusis (LASIK) is a very
common refractive procedure. Refraction should be stable for at
least one year before LASIK surgery is undertaken. ¯ÇØe excimer
laser, which can ablate tissue to a precise depth with negligible disruption of surrounding areas, is used to reshape corneal stroma
exposed by the creation of a super¯Ç£cial ¯Çøap. ¯ÇØe ¯Çøap remains
attached by a hinge to facilitate accurate and secure repositioning. Myopia is corrected by central ablative ¯Çøattening and hypermetropia by ablation of the mid-periphery so that the centre
becomes steeper. LASIK can generally be used to treat higher
refractive errors than surface ablation techniques (see below):

hypermetropia up to 3-4 D, astigmatism up to 5 D and myopia
up to 6ÔÇô8 D depending on initial corneal thickness. For refractive
errors outside these parameters an alternative technique should be
considered. To decrease the risk of subsequent ectasia, a residual
corneal base at least 250 ╬╝m thickness must remain a¯Ç×er ablation.
¯ÇØe amount of tissue removed, and therefore the amount of refractive error correctable, is limited by the original corneal thickness.

## It follows that high refractive errors can be addressed only by an

intraocular procedure. In addition to treatment of a wider range of
refractive errors, advantages over surface ablation include greater
postoperative comfort, faster visual rehabilitation, more rapid stabilization of refraction and milder stromal haze. ¯ÇØe major disadvantage is the potential for serious ¯Çøap-related complications.
¯Çü Preoperative evaluation includes:

{ Corneal pachymetry to determine residual stromal bed
thickness and to exclude mild keratoconus.

{ Corneal topography to exclude irregular astigmatism, keratoconus, and forme fruste keratoconus.

{ Complete ocular examination.

{ Tear ¯Ç£lm osmolarity to exclude dry eye syndrome.

288 Refractive Procedures

{ Corneal OCT to produce an epithelial map of the cornea.

bed and undersurface of the ¯Çøap. Ethanol or mitomycin C
can be applied, but this can result in adverse e¯ÇÜects.

{ Corneal and whole eye wavefront aberrometry.
¯Çü Technique

{ A suction ring centred on the cornea is applied to the globe
which raises the intraocular pressure substantially.

{ ¯ÇØe ring stabilizes the eye and provides the guide track for
a mechanical microkeratome, which is advanced across
the cornea to create a thin ¯Çøap. In recent years, the creation
of the ¯Çøap is undertaken using a femtosecond laser, which
allows a precise and uniform ¯Çøap thickness of 100ÔÇô120 ╬╝m
to be made.

{ ¯ÇØe ¯Çøap is re¯Çøected and the bed reshaped by the laser, followed by ¯Çøap repositioning (Fig. 8.16A).

{ Wavefront-guided LASIK is a variation of LASIK, where
the excimer laser ablates a precise pattern based on measurements from a wavefront aberrometer.

{ ÔÇÿOptomisedÔÇÖ LASIK treatment refers to an ablation algorithm, patented by some lasers in standard mode to produce larger optical zones and better corneal asphericity, in
order to reduce unwanted visual aberrations and postoperative glare.

{ Di¯ÇÜuse lamellar keratitis (ÔÇÿsands of the SaharaÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 8.16E)
may develop 1ÔÇô7 days following LASIK. It is characterized
by granular deposits at the ¯Çøap interface. Treatment is with
intensive topical antibiotic and steroid.

{ Bacterial keratitis (Fig. 8.16F) is rare. ¯ÇØe organism must
be cultured and the sensitivity determined so that an
appropriate topical antibiotic can be prescribed.

{ Epithelial hyperplasia can result in regression and is diagnosed using an epithelial OCT map.

{ Pressure-induced stromal keratitis (PISK) is a late-onset
visible ¯Çøuid cle¯Ç× which occurs in the interface as a result
of elevated IOP, usually secondary to prolonged topical
steroid use. ¯ÇØis may present as an under-correction or
regression.

{ Corneal ectasia (see Ch. 7). ¯ÇØis is a devastating complication which is fortunately uncommon (0.2ÔÇô0.6%). Ectasia is
more likely to occur when there is: (a) abnormal preoperative topography (e.g. forme fruste keratoconus or pellucid
marginal degeneration), (b) low preoperative central corneal thickness, (c) low post-ablation residual stromal bed
thickness, (d) greater percentage corneal tissue altered, (e)
high myopia, (f) young age. Careful pre-procedure screening should be performed to detect any predisposition.

{ Persistent epithelial defects.

{ LASIK Plus is a procedure where cross-linking is undertaken a¯Ç×er the laser treatment. It might be considered
in patients at risk of developing postoperative ectasia or
refractive regression (refractive error greater than ÔêÆ7D).
A ribo¯Çøavin solution is applied to the stromal bed while
the ¯Çøap is open, followed by irrigation of the stromal bed,
replacement of the ¯Çøap and application of ultraviolet light.
¯Çü Intraoperative complications include ÔÇÿbuttonholingÔÇÖ (penetration) or amputation of the ¯Çøap, incomplete or irregular ¯Çøap
creation and, rarely, penetration into the anterior chamber.
¯ÇØis is signi¯Ç£cantly less likely to occur when the ¯Çøap is created
with a femtosecond laser.
¯Çü Early postoperative complications

TIP LASIK may cause transient symptoms of dry eye and

{ Flap decentration.

{ Wrinkling (Fig. 8.16B), distortion or dislocation of the ¯Çøap.

{ Dry eye and tear ¯Ç£lm instability are the commonest side

{ Transient light sensitivity syndrome. ¯ÇØis usually occurs

{ Subepithelial haze (Fig. 8.16C) leading to symptoms of

{ Epithelial ingrowth under the ¯Çøap (1ÔÇô2%) (Fig. 8.16D).

Like LASIK, photorefractive keratectomy (PRK) employs excimer
laser ablation to reshape the cornea. PRK is able to correct myopia
up to 6 D, astigmatism up to around 3 D and lowÔÇômoderate hypermetropia. ¯ÇØe main disadvantages compared with LASIK are the
lower degrees of refractive error correctable and slower epithelial
healing with unpredictable postoperative discomfort. However, as
a ¯Çøap is not created there is a lower risk of serious complications
than with LASIK, including corneal ectasia and late ¯Çøap dislocation and it may be the procedure of choice for patients at higher
than average occupational or leisure-related risk of eye injury. It is
also suitable for patients rendered ineligible for LASIK due to low
corneal thickness. Other indications for surface ablation rather
than LASIK include borderline dry eye syndrome, epithelial basement membrane disease, prior corneal transplantation or radial
keratotomy and large pupil size.
¯Çü Technique

{ ¯ÇØe corneal epithelium is removed prior to ablation.
Methods used may include a sponge, an automated brush
(Amoils epithelial scrubber) and alcohol.

{ Ablation of the Bowman layer and anterior stroma (Fig.
8.17) is performed, generally taking 30ÔÇô60 seconds. In
modern systems, sophisticated tracking mechanisms
adjust laser targeting with eye movement and will pause
the procedure if the eye is signi¯Ç£cantly decentred.

and the ¯Ç×ap is repositioned; (B) retroillumination showing wrinkling of the ¯Ç×ap; (C) subepithelial haze; (D) epithelial ingrowth; (E) di¯ÇØuse lamellar keratitis (ÔÇÿsands of the SaharaÔÇÖ);
(F) bacterial keratitis, with an arrow showing the edge of the ¯Ç×ap. (Courtesy of M Leyland ÔÇô
¯Çƒg. D; S Tuft ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. E; R Bates ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. F.)

A B

stroma after removal of the epithelium. This results in repro¯Çƒling of the anterior curvature of
the cornea and therefore changes its refractive power; (B) active laser ablation of the anterior

{ ¯ÇØe epithelium usually heals within 48ÔÇô72 hours. A bandage contact lens is generally used to minimize discomfort.
Subepithelial haze invariably develops within 2 weeks and
commonly persists for several weeks to months. Diminished ¯Ç£nal visual acuity is rare but there may be decreased
contrast and nocturnal glare. Intraoperative application
of mitomycin C (mitomycin-LASEK or M-LASEK) may
reduce haze.
¯Çü Complications include slowly healing epithelial defects, corneal haze with blurring and haloes, poor night vision and
regression of refractive correction. Uncommon problems
include decentred ablation, scarring, abnormal epithelial healing, irregular astigmatism, reduced corneal sensation, sterile
in¯Ç£ltrates, infection and acute corneal necrosis.
¯Çü Variations of PRK. A range of procedural variations with
correspondingly varying terminology have been described.
LASEK (laser epithelial keratomileusis or laser-assisted subepithelial keratectomy), Epi-LASIK (epipolis or epithelial
LASIK; epipolis is a Greek word meaning super¯Ç£cial), modi¯Ç£ed PRK, advanced surface (laser) ablation (ASA or ASLA)
and Trans-PRK (trans-epithelial PRK) are variations of PRK
that utilize a variety of techniques to try to reduce discomfort
and post-laser haze and to speed visual recovery. ASA and
modi¯Ç£ed PRK are sometimes used generally to refer to all surface ablative procedures. In LASEK the epithelium is detached
and peeled back a¯Ç×er pre-treatment with dilute alcohol. Laser

is then applied and the epithelium repositioned. Epi-LASIK
employs a mechanical device, an epikeratome, to elevate a
hinged sheet of epithelium with an oscillating blunt plastic
blade and alcohol application is not usually required. Some
recent reports suggest that healing occurs more rapidly if the
epithelium is simply detached entirely without replacement
(¯Çøap-o¯ÇÜ Epi-LASIK). In Trans-PRK, epithelial ablation is performed with the laser prior to the refractive ablation, reducing
operative time and possibly conferring other bene¯Ç£ts.

Small incision lenticule extraction (SMILE)

¯Çü Small incision lenticule extraction ÔÇô SMILE is a technique
that uses a femtosecond laser (Carl Zeiss VisuMax) to cut a
lens-shaped piece of corneal tissue (a lenticule) within the
intact cornea. ¯ÇØis is then removed using a minimally invasive
4 mm incision. When the lenticle is removed via a LASIK-style
¯Çøap the procedure is termed refractive lenticular extraction
(ReLex).
¯Çü Results are similar to those obtained with LASIK.
¯Çü Advantages

{ Surface disturbance is minimal in comparison to surface
ablation procedures.

{ Low risk of ¯Çøap-related complications.

{ Biomechanical advantage.

{ Faster recovery of dry eye symptoms.

{ Quicker corneal reinnervation.

292 Episcleritis

ANATOMY

¯Çƒe scleral stroma is composed of collagen bundles of varying size
and shape that are not uniformly orientated as in the cornea and
so are not transparent. ¯Çƒe inner layer of the sclera blends with the
uveal tract. Anteriorly the episclera consists of a connective tissue layer between the super¯Ç×cial scleral stroma and Tenon capsule.
¯Çƒere are three pre-equatorial vascular layers:
¯Çü Conjunctival vessels are the most super¯Ç×cial. Arteries are tortuous and veins straight.
¯Çü Super¯Çƒcial episcleral plexus vessels are straight with a radial
con¯Ç×guration. In episcleritis, maximal congestion occurs at
this level (Fig. 9.1A). Topical phenylephrine 2.5% will also
constrict the conjunctival and 10% also the super¯Ç×cial episcleral vessels.
¯Çü Deep vascular plexus lies in the super¯Ç×cial part of the sclera
and shows maximal congestion in scleritis (Fig. 9.1B). A purplish hue, best seen in daylight, is characteristic.

EPISCLERITIS

Episcleritis is a common, usually idiopathic and benign, recurrent and frequently bilateral condition. Females may be a¯ÇØected
more commonly than males, except possibly in children, in whom
episcleritis is rare. ¯Çƒe average patient is middle-aged. It is typically self-limiting and tends to last from a few days up to 3 weeks,
but rarely longer. Associated disease, either ocular (e.g. dry eye,
rosacea, contact lens wear) or systemic (e.g. collagen vascular disorders such as rheumatoid arthritis, herpes zoster ophthalmicus,
gout, Crohn disease and others), has been identi¯Ç×ed in tertiary
referral centres. Infectious causes are very rare but a wide range
has been reported. Investigation of recurrent cases is as for scleritis
(see later).

TIP Acute episcleritis is common, usually idiopathic and has a
good visual prognosis.

## Simple episcleritis

Simple episcleritis accounts for 75% of cases. It tends to recur
(60%), decreasing in frequency with time. Features o¯Ç£en peak
within 24 hours, gradually fading over the next few days.
¯Çü Symptoms. Discomfort ranges from absent (up to 50%) to
moderate. Grittiness is common and photophobia may occur.
¯Çü Signs. More than half of cases are simultaneously bilateral.

{ Visual acuity is normal.

{ Redness may be sectoral (Fig. 9.2A) or di¯ÇØuse (Fig. 9.2B).
O¯Ç£en it has an interpalpebral distribution, in a triangular
con¯Ç×guration with the base at the limbus.

{ Chemosis, ocular hypertension, anterior uveitis and keratitis are all rare.
¯Çü Treatment

{ If mild, no treatment is required. Cool compresses or
refrigerated arti¯Ç×cial tears may be helpful.

A

B

the super¯Ç×cial episcleral plexus; (B) scleritis with scleral thickening and maximal vascular congestion of the deep vascular
plexus.

{ A weak topical steroid four times daily for 1ÔÇô2 weeks is
usually su¯Çøcient, though occasionally more intensive
instillation is needed initially or a more potent preparation
can be used with rapid tapering. A topical non-steroidal
anti-in¯ÇÜammatory (NSAID) is an alternative, though may
be less e¯ÇØective.

{ An oral NSAID is occasionally required (e.g. ibuprofen
200 mg three times daily, or occasionally a more potent
agent such as indomethacin).

A B

A B

## Nodular episcleritis

Nodular episcleritis also tends to a¯ÇØect females more than males
but has a less acute onset and a more prolonged course than the
simple variant.
¯Çü Symptoms. A red eye is typically ¯Ç×rst noted on waking. Over
the next 2ÔÇô3 days the area of redness enlarges and becomes
more uncomfortable.
¯Çü Signs. Attacks usually clear without treatment, but tend to last
longer than simple episcleritis.

{ A tender red vascular nodule, almost always within the
interpalpebral ¯Ç×ssure (Fig. 9.3A). Occasionally more than
one focus is present.

{ A slit lamp section shows an underlying ¯ÇÜat anterior
scleral surface, indicating the absence of scleritis (Fig.
9.3B).

{ Intraocular pressure (IOP) is very occasionally elevated.

{ An anterior chamber reaction may be present but is
uncommon (10%).

{ A¯Ç£er several episodes, in¯ÇÜamed vessels may become permanently dilated.

{ It is important to exclude other causes of a nodule such
as phlyctenulosis (a phlycten is within rather than beneath
the conjunctiva) or a conjunctival granuloma.
¯Çü Treatment is similar to that of simple episcleritis.

IMMUNE-MEDIATED SCLERITIS

## Scleritis is an uncommon condition characterized by oedema and

cellular in¯Ç×ltration of the entire thickness of the sclera. Immunemediated (non-infectious) scleritis is the most common type and
is frequently associated with an underlying systemic in¯ÇÜammatory condition, of which it may be the ¯Ç×rst manifestation. Scleritis
is much less common than episcleritis and comprises a spectrum
from trivial and self-limiting disease to a necrotizing process that
can involve adjacent tissues and threaten vision. A classi¯Ç×cation of
non-infectious scleritis is shown in Table 9.1. Recurrences tend to be
of the same type, though 10% progress to more aggressive disease.

## Posterior

Anterior non¯Çƒnecrotizing scleritis
Di¯Çƒuse

{ In contrast to episcleritis, a slit lamp beam shows an elevated anterior scleral surface.

{ Multiple nodules may expand and coalesce if treatment is
delayed.

{ Instillation of 10% phenylephrine drops will constrict the
conjunctival and super¯Ç×cial episcleral vasculature but not
the deep plexus overlying the nodule.

{ As the in¯ÇÜammation in the nodule subsides, increased
translucency of the sclera becomes apparent.

{ ¯Çƒe duration of the disease is similar to di¯ÇØuse scleritis.

{ More than 10% of patients with nodular scleritis
develop necrotizing disease, but if treatment is instituted early superficial necrosis does not occur and the
nodule heals from the centre leaving a small atrophic
scar.

Di¯ÇØuse disease is slightly more common in females and usually
presents in the ¯Ç×¯Ç£h decade.
¯Çü Symptoms. Ocular redness progressing a few days later to
pain that may radiate to the face and temple. ¯Çƒe discomfort
typically wakes the patient in the early hours of the morning,
improves later in the day and responds poorly to common
analgesics. ¯Çƒe vision may be blurred.
¯Çü Signs

{ Vascular congestion and dilatation associated with
oedema. If treatment is started early the disease can be
completely inhibited.

{ ¯Çƒe redness may be generalized (Fig. 9.4A) or localized to
one quadrant. If con¯Ç×ned to the area under the upper eyelid the diagnosis may be missed.

{ Secondary features can include chemosis, eyelid swelling,
anterior uveitis and raised IOP.

{ As the oedema resolves, the a¯ÇØected area o¯Ç£en takes on
a slight grey/blue appearance because of increased scleral
translucency (Fig. 9.4B). ¯Çƒis is due to rearrangement of
scleral ¯Ç×bres rather than a decrease in scleral thickness.

## Anterior necrotizing scleritis with

in¯Ç×ammation

Necrotizing disease is the aggressive form of scleritis. ¯Çƒe age
at onset is later than that of non-necrotizing scleritis, averaging
60 years. ¯Çƒe condition is bilateral in 60% of patients and unless
appropriately treated, especially in its early stages, may result in
severe visual morbidity and even loss of the eye.

TIP Necrotizing scleritis is a serious and aggressive form
of scleritis that requires systemic immunotherapy to control
in¯Çƒammation and to preserve vision.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Gradual onset of pain that becomes severe and
persistent and radiates to the temple, brow or jaw. It frequently
interferes with sleep and responds poorly to analgesia.
¯Çü Signs vary according to the following three types of necrotizing disease.

{ Vaso-occlusive is commonly associated with rheumatoid
arthritis. Isolated patches of scleral oedema with overlying non-perfused episclera and conjunctiva are seen (Fig.
9.6A). ¯Çƒe patches coalesce and if unchecked rapidly proceed to scleral necrosis (Fig. 9.6B).

{ Granuloma formation may occur in conjunction with
conditions such as granulomatosis with polyangiitis or
polyarteritis nodosa. ¯Çƒe disease typically starts with
injection adjacent to the limbus and then extends posteriorly. Within 24 hours, the sclera, episclera, conjunctiva and
adjacent cornea become irregularly raised and oedematous
(Fig. 9.7).

{ Surgically induced scleritis typically starts within 3 weeks
of a procedure, though much longer intervals have been
reported. It may be induced by any type of surgery including strabismus repair, trabeculectomy with excessive exposure to mitomycin C (Fig. 9.8A), excision of pterygium
(Fig. 9.8B) and scleral buckling. ¯Çƒe necrotizing process
starts at the site of surgery and extends outwards, but tends
to remain localized to one sector.

{ Recurrences at the same location are common unless an
underlying cause is treated.
¯Çü Prognosis. ¯Çƒe average duration of disease is around 6 years,
with the frequency of recurrences decreasing a¯Ç£er the ¯Ç×rst 18
months. ¯Çƒe long-term visual prognosis is good.

## Nodular

¯Çƒe incidence of nodular and di¯ÇØuse anterior scleritis is the same
but a disproportionately large number of those with nodular disease have had a previous attack of herpes zoster ophthalmicus. ¯Çƒe
age of onset is similar to that of di¯ÇØuse scleritis.
¯Çü Symptoms. ¯Çƒe insidious onset of pain followed by increasing
redness, tenderness of the globe and the appearance of a scleral
nodule. ¯Çƒe vision is o¯Ç£en reduced.
¯Çü Signs

{ Scleral nodules may be single or multiple and most frequently develop in the interpalpebral region close to the
limbus (Fig. 9.5A and B). ¯Çƒey have a deeper blueÔÇôred
colour than episcleral nodules and are immobile.

## Investigation

¯Çü Laboratory. Tests should be employed as adjuncts to clinical
assessment and evaluation by a general physician or rheumatologist should be considered. Speci¯Ç×c tests may include erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP),
full blood count (e.g. anaemia related to in¯ÇÜammatory connective tissue disease, eosinophilia for polyarteritis nodosa, atopy
or ChurgÔÇôStrauss syndrome), rheumatoid factor, antinuclear
antibodies (ANA), antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies
(ANCA) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (CCP) antibodies, serum uric acid, syphilis serology, Lyme serology, hepatitis
B surface antigen (polyarteritis nodosa) and antiphospholipid
antibodies. Investigation for tuberculosis, sarcoidosis or ankylosing spondylitis may be appropriate (see Ch. 12).
¯Çü Radiological imaging. Chest, sinus, joint and other imaging may
be indicated in the investigation of a range of conditions such as
tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, ChurgÔÇôStrauss syndrome, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, ankylosing spondylitis and other conditions.
¯Çü Angiography. Fluorescein angiography of the anterior segment helps to distinguish necrotizing disease by the presence
of non-perfusion and can be used for monitoring. Occlusion
is predominantly venular in in¯ÇÜammatory disease and mainly
arteriolar in scleromalacia perforans (see below). Indocyanine
green is a more accurate indicator of disease activity.
¯Çü Ultrasonography can help to detect associated posterior scleritis (see below).
¯Çü Biopsy. ¯Çƒis may be considered in resistant cases, especially if
infection is suspected.

## Complications of anterior scleritis

¯Çü Acute in¯Çƒltrative stromal keratitis may be localized or di¯ÇØuse
(Fig. 9.9).
¯Çü Sclerosing keratitis, characterized by chronic thinning and
opaci¯Ç×cation in which the peripheral cornea adjacent to the
site of scleritis resembles sclera.

A

B

excision.

¯Çü Peripheral ulcerative keratitis is characterized by progressive
melting and ulceration and may constitute a severe risk to the
integrity of the eye. In granulomatous scleritis the destruction
extends directly from the sclera into the limbus and cornea.
¯Çƒis characteristic pattern is seen in granulomatosis with
polyangiitis, polyarteritis nodosa and relapsing polychondritis. Peripheral corneal ulceration can occur at any stage of a
necrotizing scleritis and, in rare cases, precede its onset. (See
also Ch. 7.)
¯Çü Uveitis, if severe, may denote aggressive scleritis.
¯Çü Glaucoma is the most common cause of eventual loss of
vision. ¯Çƒe intraocular pressure can be very di¯Çøcult to control
in the presence of active scleritis.
¯Çü Hypotony (rarely phthisis) may be the result of ciliary body
detachment, in¯ÇÜammatory damage or ischaemia.
¯Çü Perforation of the sclera as a result of the in¯ÇÜammatory process alone is extremely rare.

TIP Glaucoma is the commonest cause of visual loss in
anterior scleritis, so control of the IOP is critically important.

## Scleromalacia perforans

¯Çü Scleromalacia perforans (5% of scleritis) is a specific type
of progressive scleral thinning without inflammation that
typically affects elderly women with longstanding rheumatoid arthritis, but has also been described in association
with other systemic disorders. Despite the nomenclature,
perforation of the globe is rare as integrity is maintained by
a thin layer of fibrous tissue. Differential diagnosis is from
the innocuous scleral hyaline plaque and senile scleromalacia (see below).
¯Çü Symptoms. Mild non-speci¯Ç×c irritation. Pain is absent and
vision una¯ÇØected and keratoconjunctivitis sicca may be
suspected.
¯Çü Signs

{ Necrotic scleral plaques near the limbus without vascular
congestion (Fig. 9.10A).

{ Coalescence and enlargement of necrotic areas.

{ Slow progression of scleral thinning, with exposure of
underlying uvea (Fig. 9.10B and C).
¯Çü Treatment may be e¯ÇØective in patients with early scleromalacia, but is less useful in advanced disease.

{ No consistent bene¯Ç×t from any speci¯Ç×c agent has been
demonstrated. Frequent lubricant instillation, local or
systemic anticollagenase agents, immunosuppressives
(including topical and oral, but not periocular injection of,
steroids and topical ciclosporin) and biological blockers
have been used. (See below.)

A

B

C

and exposure of underlying uvea. (Courtesy of R Bates ÔÇô ¯Çƒg.
A; C Barry ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

{ Underlying systemic disease should be treated.

{ Protection from trauma is important.

{ Surgical repair of scleral perforation (e.g. patch gra¯Ç£ing)
may be required to prevent phthisis bulbi.

## Posterior scleritis

## Posterior scleritis is a potentially blinding condition in which

diagnosis is commonly delayed, with an adverse prognostic
e¯ÇØect. ¯Çƒe in¯ÇÜammatory changes in posterior and anterior scleral
disease are identical and can arise in both segments simultaneously or separately. ¯Çƒe age at onset is o¯Ç£en less than 40 years.
Young patients are usually otherwise healthy, but about a third
over the age of 55 have associated systemic disease.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Pain does not correlate well with the severity
of in¯ÇÜammation but tends to be more severe in those with
accompanying orbital myositis. Photophobia is not a dominant feature.
¯Çü Signs. ¯Çƒe disease is bilateral in 35%.

{ Choroidal folds (see Ch. 14) are usually con¯Ç×ned to the
posterior pole (Fig. 9.11A).

{ Exudative retinal detachment occurs in around 25%. ¯Çƒe
yellowish-brown subretinal exudative material can be mistaken for a choroidal tumour.

{ Uveal e¯ÇØusion with choroidal detachment may be present
(Fig. 9.11B).

{ Disc oedema with accompanying reduction of vision is common and is caused by spread of in¯ÇÜammation into the orbital
tissue and optic nerve. Treatment must not be delayed in
these patients as permanent visual loss can rapidly ensue.

{ Myositis is common and gives rise to diplopia, pain on
eye movement, tenderness to touch and redness around a
muscle insertion.

{ Proptosis is usually mild and is frequently associated with
ptosis.

(arrow), with the characteristic ÔÇÿTÔÇÖ sign (see text).

oedema, choroidal folds and retinal detachment. Fluid in the
Tenon space may give a characteristic ÔÇÿTÔÇÖ sign, the stem of the
T being formed by the optic nerve and the cross bar by the
¯ÇÜuid-containing gap (Fig. 9.12).
¯Çü MR and CT may show scleral thickening and proptosis.

Di¯Çƒerential diagnosis

¯Çü Subretinal mass. Alternative lesions include miscellaneous
granulomatous conditions and choroidal neoplasia.
¯Çü Choroidal folds, retinal striae and disc oedema may also occur
in orbital tumours, orbital in¯ÇÜammatory disease, thyroid eye
disease, papilloedema and hypotony.
¯Çü Exudative retinal detachment. VogtÔÇôKoyanagiÔÇôHarada
(VKH) syndrome and central serous retinopathy.
¯Çü Orbital cellulitis may cause proptosis and periocular oedema
but is associated with marked pyrexia.

{ Occasional features include raised IOP, periorbital oedema
and chemosis. Associated anterior scleritis is a useful diagnostic aid but occurs only in a minority.
¯Çü Ultrasonography may show increased scleral thickness,
scleral nodules, separation of Tenon capsule from sclera, disc

## Important systemic associations of
scleritis

## Rheumatoid arthritis

¯Çü ¯Çƒe autoimmune disease rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is the
most common systemic association of scleritis and is characterized by a symmetrical deforming in¯ÇÜammatory polyarthropathy, with a spectrum of possible extra-articular
manifestations.
¯Çü Presentation is commonly in the third decade with joint swelling, usually of the hands (Fig. 9.13A and B). It is much more
common in females than males.
¯Çü Rheumatoid factor autoantibodies are present in 80ÔÇô90%.
¯Çü All forms of immune-mediated scleritis have been described
in RA and the clinical course is o¯Ç£en more aggressive than
when there is no systemic association. Other ocular manifestations of RA include keratoconjunctivitis sicca (secondary
Sj├Âgren syndrome), ulcerative keratitis (see Fig. 7.29BÔÇôD) and
acquired superior oblique tendon sheath syndrome (very rare).

TIP Rheumatoid arthritis is the most common systemic
association of scleritis and can manifest with any form of
immune-mediated scleral in¯Çƒammation.

## Granulomatosis with polyangiitis

¯Çü Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener granulomatosis)
is an idiopathic multisystem granulomatous disorder characterized by small vessel vasculitis typically a¯ÇØecting primarily
the paranasal sinuses (Fig. 9.13C), lower respiratory tract, the
kidneys and skin (Fig. 9.13D).
¯Çü ¯Çƒere is a male predominance. Presentation is in the ¯Ç×¯Ç£h
decade on average, o¯Ç£en with pulmonary symptoms.
¯Çü Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (cANCA) are found in
over 90% of patients with active disease.
¯Çü Scleritis is o¯Ç£en rapidly progressive, necrotizing and granulomatous. Other ocular manifestations include peripheral
ulcerative keratitis (see Fig.7.29E), occlusive retinal vasculitis,
orbital in¯ÇÜammatory disease, nasolacrimal obstruction, dacrocystitis and, rarely, tarsalÔÇôconjunctival disease.

## Relapsing polychondritis

¯Çü Relapsing polychondritis is a rare idiopathic condition characterized by small vessel vasculitis involving cartilage resulting in
recurrent, o¯Ç£en progressive, in¯ÇÜammatory episodes involving
multiple organ systems such as the ears (Fig. 9.13E), nasal cartilage (Fig. 9.13F), respiratory system, heart and joints.
¯Çü Presentation is frequently in middle age.
¯Çü Scleritis is o¯Ç£en intractable and may be necrotizing or nonnecrotizing. Isolated anterior uveitis may also occur.

## Polyarteritis nodosa

¯Çü Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN) is an idiopathic aneurysmal vasculitis a¯ÇØecting medium-sized and small arteries, with a
wide range of manifestations across multiple organ systems.

Without treatment the 5-year survival is about 10%. ¯Çƒe
causes of death are kidney failure, myocardial infarction or
stroke. Modern immuno-suppressive treatment improves the
prognosis considerably.
¯Çü Presentation is in the third to sixth decades, o¯Ç£en with constitutional symptoms. ¯Çƒe male:female ratio is about 3:1.
¯Çü Ocular involvement may precede the systemic manifestations
by several years. About one-third of patients have hepatitis B
infection. Scleritis is o¯Ç£en aggressive and necrotizing. Peripheral ulcerative keratitis, orbital pseudotumour and occlusive
retinal periarteritis are other reported ocular features.

## Systemic lupus erythematosus

¯Çü Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic multisystem
autoimmune in¯ÇÜammatory disease. ¯Çƒere is o¯Ç£en a family history, but no single causal gene has been identi¯Ç×ed.
¯Çü Females are a¯ÇØected far more o¯Ç£en than males (ratio of 9:1).
¯Çü Antinuclear antibody testing is the key serological test in this
condition. If the test is negative, SLE can be excluded (sensitivity: 99%; speci¯Ç×city: 49%).
¯Çü Up to 70% of patients with SLE have skin involvement, including the characteristic ÔÇÿbutter¯ÇÜyÔÇÖ rash on the cheeks, which
is o¯Ç£en induced by exposure to sunlight (Fig. 9.13G). Red
scaly discoid patches can be seen elsewhere on the skin and
hair loss may occur (Fig. 9.13H). Joint and muscle pain has
been reported in up to 90%, but does not usually result in
joint destruction. ¯Çƒe heart, lungs, kidneys and brain may be
a¯ÇØected.
¯Çü Ocular involvement is found in up to one-third of patients.
¯Çƒe most common manifestation is dry eye syndrome and secondary Sj├Âgren syndrome in 25%. Retinopathy occurs in up
to 10% and is linked to the severity of the condition, indicating inadequate control of the systemic disease. Ischaemic optic
neuropathy and bilateral optic neuritis are uncommon features
of SLE. ¯Çƒe incidence of episcleritis and scleritis is 1% and may
be the presenting feature of the disease, usually manifesting
with anterior di¯ÇØuse or anterior nodular scleritis.
¯Çü Because of the long-term use of hydroxychloroquine treatment, maculopathy may follow in some patients (see Ch. 21).

## Crohn disease

¯Çü Crohn disease is a chronic, relapsing in¯ÇÜammatory bowel disease of unknown cause.
¯Çü Extraintestinal complications occur frequently during the
course of the disease. ¯Çƒe commonest of these are peripheral
arthritis and erythema nodosum.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe eye is involved in 4% to 10% of patients and a wide spectrum of in¯ÇÜammatory conditions may be found. Acute episcleritis, acute anterior uveitis and marginal keratitis (see Fig. 9.9)
are the commonest manifestation, although conjunctivitis,
optic neuritis, orbital in¯ÇÜammatory disease and retinal vasculitis have been reported. Acute anterior scleritis is rare (see

activity show a close correlation.
