Treatment of immune¯Çƒmediated scleritis

¯Çü Topical steroids do not a¯ÇØect the natural history of the scleral
in¯ÇÜammation, but may relieve symptoms and oedema in nonnecrotizing disease.
¯Çü Systemic NSAIDs should be used on their own only in nonnecrotizing disease. It is o¯Ç£en necessary to try a number of different drugs before ¯Ç×nding one that provides adequate relief of
symptoms. A cyclooxygenase (COX)-2 inhibitor may be preferred in elderly patients or if there is a history of peptic ulceration, but can result in cardiovascular side e¯ÇØects. Patients should
be warned of the risk of gastrointestinal tract haemorrhage.
¯Çü Periocular steroid injections may be used in non-necrotizing
disease but their e¯ÇØects are usually transient and should not be
used in necrotizing scleritis.
¯Çü Systemic steroids (e.g. prednisolone is 1ÔÇô1.5 mg/kg daily) are
used when NSAIDs are inappropriate or inadequate (necrotizing disease). Intravenous methylprednisolone (0.5ÔÇô1 g daily
for 3 days) may be used initially for severe cases.
¯Çü Immunosuppressives and/or immunomodulatory agents
should be considered if control is (a) incomplete with steroids
alone, (b) as a steroid-sparing measure in long-term treatment,
or (c) to treat underlying systemic disease. Patients with scleritis secondary to the following conditions: granulomatosis with
polyangiitis, polyarteritis nodosa, RA (with necrotizing scleritis) and relapsing polychondritis, should be o¯ÇØered immediate
treatment with immunomodulatory agents (see Ch. 12). A wide
range of drugs are available, including cytostatics (e.g. cyclophosphamide, azathioprine, methotrexate), drugs acting on
immunophilins (e.g. ciclosporin, tacrolimus) and biologicals. In
necrotizing disease, rituximab is particularly e¯ÇØective.

TIP Rituximab is particularly e¯ÇØective in the treatment of
necrotizing scleritis.

PORPHYRIA

Scleritis is a rare complication of congenital erythropoietic porphyria and two of the adult forms of porphyria: porphyria

variegata and porphyria cutanea tarda. Overproduction and
excessive storage of porphyrins occur in the liver. ¯Çƒese highly
photoactive chromophores are also deposited in the dermis and
sclera, which are subsequently stimulated by exposure to the sun.
¯Çƒe sclera becomes thin or excavated over a sharply demarcated
area localized to the sun-exposed interpalpebral region adjacent to
the cornea (Fig. 9.14). Acute di¯ÇØuse anterior scleritis and posterior
scleritis may occur. Patients should avoid sunlight and undergo
phlebotomy.

INFECTIOUS SCLERITIS

Infectious scleritis is rare, but may present diagnostic di¯Çøculty as the
initial clinical features are similar to those of immune-mediated disease. In some cases, infection may follow surgical or accidental trauma,
endophthalmitis, or may occur as an extension of corneal infection.

## Causes

¯Çü Herpes zoster is the most common infective cause. Necrotizing scleritis is extremely resistant to treatment and may result
in a thinned or punched-out area (Fig. 9.15A and B).
¯Çü Tuberculous scleritis is rare and di¯Çøcult to diagnose. ¯Çƒe sclera
may be infected by direct spread from a local conjunctival or
choroidal lesion, or more commonly by haematogenous spread.
Involvement may be nodular (Fig. 9.15C) or necrotizing.
¯Çü Leprosy. Recurrent necrotizing scleritis can occur, even a¯Ç£er
apparent systemic cure. Nodular disease may be seen in lepromatous leprosy.
¯Çü Syphilis. Di¯ÇØuse anterior scleritis may occur in secondary
syphilis and occasionally scleral nodules may be a feature of
tertiary syphilis.
¯Çü Lyme disease. Scleritis (Fig. 9.15D) is common but typically
occurs long a¯Ç£er initial infection.
¯Çü Other causes include fungi, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and
Nocardia.

## Treatment

Once the infective agent has been identi¯Ç×ed, speci¯Ç×c antimicrobial therapy should be initiated. Topical and systemic steroids may
also be used to reduce the in¯ÇÜammatory reaction. If appropriate,

surgical debridement can be used to debulk a focus of infection
and facilitates the penetration of antibiotics.

SCLERAL DISCOLORATION

## Alkaptonuria

In this autosomal recessive condition, a defect in homogentisic
acid oxidase results in the accumulation of homogentisic acid
in collagenous tissues such as cartilage and tendon (ochronosis). Ocular manifestations include bluish-grey or black generalized pigmentation of the sclera and the tendons of horizontal
recti associated with discrete pigmented globules (Fig. 9.16).
Systemic features include dark urine and arthropathy.

## Haemochromatosis

¯Çƒe systemic features of haemochromatosis are caused by
increased iron deposition in various tissues. Features may be more

sclera and horizontal rectus tendons.

A

C

B

D

osteogenesis imperfecta type IIA; (C) cutaneous hyperelasticity and (D) joint hypermobility in
EhlersÔÇôDanlos syndrome type VI.

subtle than the classic triad of a bronze complexion, hepatomegaly and diabetes. Inheritance is autosomal recessive. Dry eye and
rusty-brown perilimbal conjunctival and scleral discoloration may
develop.

BLUE SCLERA

## Blue scleral discoloration is caused by thinning or transparency

with resultant visualization of the underlying uvea (Fig. 9.17A).
Major associations are discussed below. Rare associations
include MarshallÔÇôSmith syndrome (accelerated prenatal skeletal maturation and growth), RussellÔÇôSilver syndrome (short
stature and other features) and HallermannÔÇôStreiffÔÇôFran├ºois
syndrome.

## Osteogenesis imperfecta

Osteogenesis imperfecta is an inherited disease of connective tissue, usually caused by defects in the synthesis and structure of type

1 collagen. ¯Çƒere are multiple types, at least two of which have ocular features.
¯Çü Type I is autosomal dominant. Patients su¯ÇØer few fractures
with little or no deformity, hyperextensible joints, dental hypoplasia, deafness and easy bruising. Possible ocular features
include blue sclera, megalocornea and corneal arcus.
¯Çü Type IIA is either sporadic or inherited in an autosomal
dominant manner. Systemic features include deafness, dental
anomalies, multiple fractures (Fig. 9.17B) and short limbs,
with death in infancy from respiratory infection. Ocular manifestations include blue sclera and shallow orbits.

EhlersÔÇôDanlos syndrome type VI

EhlersÔÇôDanlos syndrome VI (ocular sclerotic) is an inherited disorder of collagen formation. Patients have thin and hyper-elastic
skin (Fig. 9.17C) that bruises easily and heals slowly. ¯Çƒe joints
are hypermobile (Fig. 9.17D), which may lead to recurrent dislocation and falls. Cardiovascular disease can be severe, including a

304 Miscellaneous Conditions

bleeding diathesis, dissecting aneurysms, spontaneous rupture of
large blood vessels and mitral valve prolapse. ¯Çƒere are six major
types but type VI and, rarely, type IV, are associated with ocular
features. As well as blue sclera, other manifestations include scleral
fragility (globe rupture may be caused by mild trauma), epicanthic folds, microcornea, keratoconus, keratoglobus, ectopia lentis,
myopia and retinal detachment.

MISCELLANEOUS CONDITIONS

## Congenital ocular melanocytosis

## Congenital ocular melanocytosis is an uncommon condition

characterized by an increase in number, size and pigmentation
of melanocytes in the sclera and uvea. ¯Çƒe periocular skin, orbit,
meninges and so¯Ç£ palate may also be involved.
¯Çü Ocular melanocytosis, the least common, involves only the
eye. Multifocal slate-grey pigmentation is seen within the
sclera and episcleraÔÇô the process does not involve the overlying conjunctival layers, unless there is incidental conjunctival
pigmentation. ¯Çƒe overlying conjunctiva is mobile over the
episcleral pigmentation but the pigmentation itself is intrinsic
and cannot be moved over the globe. ¯Çƒe peripheral cornea is
occasionally involved.
¯Çü Dermal melanocytosis (one-third) involves only the skin.
¯Çü Oculodermal melanocytosis (naevus of Ota) is the most common type and involves both skin and eye. Naevus of Ota is
bilateral in 5%. It occurs frequently in darker-complexioned
races but is rare in White people. ¯Çƒere is deep bluish hyperpigmentation of the facial skin, most frequently in the distribution of the ¯Ç×rst and second trigeminal divisions (Fig.
9.18AÔÇôC). It may be subtle in pale-skinned individuals, when
it is best detected under good lighting.
¯Çü Ipsilateral associations

{ Iris hyperchromia is common (see Fig. 9.18A).

{ Iris mammillations are uncommon, tiny, regularly spaced
villiform lesions (Fig. 9.18D). ¯Çƒey may also be found in
neuro¯Ç×bromatosis type 1, AxenfeldÔÇôRieger anomaly and
Peters anomaly.

{ Fundus hyperpigmentation (Fig. 9.18E).

{ Trabecular hyperpigmentation (Fig. 9.18F). ¯Çƒis is associated with glaucoma in about 10% of cases.

{ Uveal melanoma develops in a small minority of patients
and long-term anterior and posterior segment review is
required.

TIP Patients with oculodermal melanosis are at risk of
developing glaucoma and occasionally uveal melanoma.

Idiopathic sclerochoroidal calci¯ÇØcation

Idiopathic sclerochoroidal calci¯Ç×cation is an innocuous, agerelated condition that usually involves both eyes of an a¯ÇØected
older adult.
¯Çü Signs. Geographical yellowÔÇôwhite fundus lesions with illde¯Ç×ned margins (Fig. 9.19), o¯Ç£en multiple and located in the
superotemporal or inferotemporal mid-periphery associated
with the vascular arcades.
¯Çü Ultrasonography shows highly re¯ÇÜective choroidal plaquelike lesions with orbital shadowing.
¯Çü Differential diagnosis is mainly from osseous metaplasia associated with a choroidal haemangioma and from
choroidal osteoma, which is usually a single ÔÇô though
often large ÔÇô lesion that usually (80ÔÇô90%) involves only
one eye.

## Scleral hyaline plaque and senile
scleromalacia

Scleral hyaline plaques are oval dark-greyish, generally sharply
demarcated, areas located close to the insertion of the horizontal
rectus muscles (Fig. 9.20). Senile scleromalacia refers to a spontaneously occurring irregular, oval or kidney-shaped partialthickness scleral defect found at the same location, typically with
one or more scleral hyaline plaques at the opposite location in the
same eye or in the fellow eye. Separation of a scleral hyaline plaque
to leave an area of scleromalacia has been described. Both these
entities typically a¯ÇØect elderly patients, are innocuous and should
not be confused with scleromalacia perforans (see above), which
occurs in somewhat younger patients, may be located anywhere
in the anterior sclera, is not associated with hyaline plaques elsewhere and can progress to a full-thickness scleral defect with uveal
exposure.

308 Acquired Cataract

INTRODUCTION

¯Ç×e primary function of the lens is to focus light on the retina.
¯Çü Presbyopia is the gradual loss of the ability of the lens to focus
on near objects. It is an inevitable ageing phenomenon which
occurs as a consequence of loss of accommodation secondary
to reduced lens elasticity. Spectacles will usually correct the
refractive error, but surgical treatment has become increasingly relevant in recent years.
¯Çü Cataract (lens opaci¯Çƒcation) is the commonest cause of
reversible visual disability in the world. ¯Ç×ere is no medical treatment to prevent the development or progression of
cataract. However, phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation with intraocular lens
implantation is extremely successful in restoring vision and is
the commonest surgical procedure undertaken in the world.

## Anatomy

¯Çü Zonules hold the lens in position (Fig. 10.1A).
¯Çü ¯Ç×e capsule is an acellular membrane that encloses the lens
material.
¯Çü Cuboid epithelial cells are located beneath the anterior capsule and extend to the lens equator; cells in the germinative
zone divide continuously and di¯Ç£erentiate to form lens ¯ÇØbers
(Fig. 10.1B).
¯Çü ¯Ç×e lens substance is made up of a central nucleus surrounded
by corticular material.

E¯Çƒect on vision

Cataracts o¯Çøen develop slowly and in the early stages the vision
alters subtly without the person being aware of the change. ¯Ç×e e¯Ç£ect
on vision depends on: (a) the type of cataract, (b) the extent of opaci¯ÇØcation and (c) the position of the opaci¯ÇØcation. Symptoms include:
¯Çü Reduced visual acuity, which initially can be corrected with
spectacles.
¯Çü Loss of contrast sensitivity.
¯Çü Change in colour perception, particularly an inability to
accurately perceive blue colours.
¯Çü Glare secondary to light scattering, causing di¯ÇÜculty with
night driving.
¯Çü Monocular diplopia, especially when looking at a point
source of light.
¯Çü Di¯ÇØculty seeing in low-light situations, including poor night
vision.

ACQUIRED CATARACT

Age-related cataract

## Subcapsular cataract

## Anterior subcapsular cataract lies directly under the lens capsule

and is associated with ¯ÇØbrous metaplasia of the lens epithelium. Posterior subcapsular opacity lies just in front of the posterior capsule
and has a granular or plaque-like appearance on oblique slit lamp

biomicroscopy (Fig. 10.2A), but typically appears black and vacuolated on retroillumination (Fig. 10.2B). ¯Ç×e vacuoles are swollen
migratory lens epithelial cells (bladder or Wedl), similar to those
commonly seen postoperatively in posterior capsular opaci¯ÇØcation.
Due to its location at the nodal point of the eye, a posterior subcapsular opacity o¯Çøen has a particularly profound e¯Ç£ect on vision.
Patients are characteristically troubled by glare, for instance from the
headlights of oncoming cars, and symptoms are increased by miosis,
such as occurs during near visual activity and in bright sunlight.

## Nuclear sclerotic cataract

Nuclear cataract is an exaggeration of normal ageing change (Fig.
10.2C). It is o¯Çøen associated with myopia due to an increase in the
refractive index of the nucleus, resulting in some elderly patients
being able to read without spectacles again (ÔÇÿsecond sight of the
agedÔÇÖ). In contrast, in the healthy ageing eye (and in occasional
cases of cortical and subcapsular cataract) there is a mild hypermetropic shi¯Çø. Nuclear sclerotic cataract is characterized by a yellowish hue due to the deposition of urochrome pigment and is best
assessed with an oblique slit lamp beam. Retroillumination will
show a good red re¯ÇÖex, but careful observation will reveal a subtle
distinction between the nucleus and cortex (Fig. 10.2D). When
advanced, the nucleus appears brown and, in rare cases, black.

## Cortical cataract

Cortical cataract may involve the anterior, posterior or equatorial
cortex. ¯Ç×e opacities start as cle¯Çøs and vacuoles between lens ¯ÇØbres
due to cortical hydration. Subsequent opaci¯ÇØcation results in typical cuneiform (wedge-shaped) or radial spoke-like opacities (Fig.
10.2E and F), o¯Çøen found initially in the inferonasal quadrant. As
with posterior subcapsular opacity, glare is a common symptom.

## Christmas tree cataract

Christmas tree cataract, which is uncommon, is characterized
by polychromatic needle-like formations in the deep cortex and
nucleus (Fig. 10.2G and H).

310 Acquired Cataract

## Cataract maturity

¯Çü Immature cataract is one in which the lens is partially opaque.
¯Çü Mature cataract is one in which the lens is completely opaque
(Fig. 10.3A and B).
¯Çü Hypermature cataract has a shrunken and wrinkled anterior
capsule (Fig. 10.3C) due to leakage of water out of the lens.
¯Çü Morgagnian cataract is a hypermature cataract in which liquefaction of the cortex has allowed the nucleus to sink inferiorly
(Fig. 10.3D).

## Cataract in systemic disease

## Diabetes mellitus

Hyperglycaemia is re¯ÇÖected in a high level of glucose in the aqueous humour, which di¯Ç£uses into the lens. Here glucose is metabolized into sorbitol, which accumulates within the lens, resulting in
secondary osmotic overhydration. In mild degree, this may a¯Ç£ect
the refractive index of the lens with consequent ¯ÇÖuctuation of

refraction in line with the plasma glucose level, hyperglycaemia
resulting in myopia and vice versa. Cortical ¯ÇÖuid vacuoles develop
and later evolve into frank opacities. Classic diabetic cataract,
which is rare, consists of snow¯ÇÖake cortical opacities (Fig. 10.4A)
occurring in the young. It may mature within a few days or resolve
spontaneously. Age-related cataract occurs sooner in people with
diabetes mellitus. Nuclear sclerosis is common and tends to progress rapidly. Around one in ¯ÇØve people who undergo cataract surgery have diabetes. ¯Ç×e presence of diabetic macular oedema prior
to cataract surgery is associated with worsening of the oedema,
resulting in postoperative visual loss. ¯Ç×e retina needs to be monitored before and a¯Çøer cataract surgery, even in patients with minimal oedema.

## Myotonic dystrophy

About 90% of patients with myotonic dystrophy (see Ch. 19)
develop ¯ÇØne iridescent cortical opacities in the third decade, sometimes resembling Christmas tree cataract. ¯Ç×ese evolve into visually disabling wedge-shaped cortical and subcapsular opacities,
o¯Çøen star-like in conformation (Fig. 10.4B), by the ¯ÇØ¯Çøh decade.

Later, the opacities may become indistinguishable from typical
cortical cataract.

## Atopic dermatitis

About 10% of patients with severe atopic dermatitis develop cataracts in the second to fourth decades. ¯Ç×ese are o¯Çøen bilateral and
may mature quickly. Shield-like dense anterior subcapsular plaque
that wrinkles the anterior capsule (Fig. 10.4C) is characteristic.
Posterior subcapsular opacities may also occur.

Neuro¯ÇØbromatosis type 2 (see Ch. 19) is associated with early
cataract in more than 60% of patients. Opacities are posterior subcapsular (Fig. 10.4D) or capsular, cortical or mixed and tend to
develop in early adulthood.

## Secondary cataract

A secondary (complicated) cataract develops as a result of other
primary ocular disease.

## Chronic anterior uveitis

## Chronic anterior uveitis is the most common cause of secondary

cataract, the incidence being related to the duration and intensity of
in¯ÇÖammation. Topical and systemic steroids used in treatment are
also causative. ¯Ç×e earliest ¯ÇØnding is o¯Çøen a polychromatic lustre at
the posterior pole of the lens. If in¯ÇÖammation persists, posterior and
anterior opacities (Fig. 10.5A) develop. Cataract appears to progress
more rapidly in the presence of posterior synechiae (Fig. 10.5B).

## Acute congestive angle closure

Acute congestive angle closure may cause small anterior grey-white
subcapsular or capsular opacities, glaukom¯ÇÖecken (Fig. 10.5C), to
form within the pupillary area. ¯Ç×ese represent focal infarcts of the
lens epithelium and are pathognomonic of previous acute congestive angle closure.

## High myopia

High (pathological) myopia can be associated with posterior subcapsular lens opacity and early-onset nuclear sclerosis, which ironically may increase the myopic refractive error.

312 Acquired Cataract

retinitis pigmentosa. (Courtesy of S Chen ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. D.)

## Hereditary fundus dystrophies

Hereditary fundus dystrophies (see Ch. 15) such as retinitis pigmentosa, Leber congenital amaurosis, gyrate atrophy and Stickler syndrome are usually associated with posterior subcapsular lens opacity.
Anterior subcapsular lens opacity is rare (Fig. 10.5D) because surgery
is usually undertaken at an early stage, when the opaci¯ÇØcation is limited to the posterior subcapsular region. Cataract surgery tends to
improve visual function even in the presence of severe retinal changes.

## Secondary to medication

Systemic and topical steroids can lead to cataract formation. ¯Ç×ese
are usually posterior subcapsular in appearance and can be visually debilitating. Despite a good visual acuity, symptoms of glare
and reduced near vision are pronounced (see Ch. 21). Long-term
use of chlorpromazine leads to an anterior star-shaped lens opacity
(see Fig. 21.2B).

## Traumatic cataract

## Trauma is the most common cause of unilateral cataract in young

people.
¯Çü Penetrating trauma (Fig. 10.6A and B).
¯Çü Blunt trauma may cause a characteristic ¯ÇÖower-shaped opacity (Fig. 10.6C).
¯Çü Electric shock is a rare cause of cataract, patterns including
di¯Ç£use milky-white opaci¯ÇØcation and multiple snow¯ÇÖake-like
opacities, sometimes in a stellate subcapsular distribution (Fig.
10.6D).
¯Çü Infrared radiation, if intense as in glassblowers, may rarely
cause true exfoliation of the anterior lens capsule (Fig. 10.6E).
¯Çü Ionizing radiation exposure such as for ocular tumour treatment may cause posterior subcapsular opacities (Fig. 10.6F).
¯Ç×ese may not manifest for months or years.

314 Management of Acquired Cataract

MANAGEMENT OF ACQUIRED
CATARACT

## Preoperative considerations

## Indications for surgery

¯Çü Visual improvement is by far the most common indication
for cataract surgery. An operation is indicated when the opacity develops to a degree su¯ÇÜcient to cause di¯ÇÜculty in performing daily activities. Clear lens exchange (replacement of
the healthy lens with an arti¯ÇØcial implant) is an option for the
management of refractive error.
¯Çü Medical indications are those in which a cataract is adversely
a¯Ç£ecting the health of the eye. Examples include phacolytic
or phacomorphic glaucoma. Cataract surgery to improve the
clarity of the ocular media may also be required in the context
of monitoring or treatment of fundus pathology.

## Systemic preoperative assessment

For elective surgery, a general medical history is taken and any
problems managed accordingly. Table 10.1 sets out suggested further enquiry and action in relation to a range of systemic diseases.
Routine preoperative general medical examination, blood tests and
electrocardiogram (ECG) are not usually required for local anaesthesia. If general anaesthesia is planned, assessment is according
to local protocol, e.g. general examination, urea and electrolytes,
random blood glucose, full blood count and ECG. An anaesthetic
opinion may be considered for chronically unwell patients or those
with complex medical problems.
¯Çü Current medication should be recorded. ¯Ç×is will o¯Çøen guide
general medical assessment. Medications relevant to eye surgery include:

{ Systemic alpha-blockers (e.g. tamsulosin) are commonly
associated with intraoperative ¯ÇÖoppy iris syndrome
(IFIS).

{ Management of anticoagulant therapy or an antiplatelet
agent should follow local protocol. Most surgeons do not
stop antiplatelet drugs for cataract surgery, though this
may be preferred for oculoplastic procedures and glaucoma surgery. Anticoagulation status, usually expressed as
the international normalized ratio (INR) level, should be
within the therapeutic range appropriate for the individual
indication (e.g. usually higher for heart valve thrombosis
prophylaxis than following deep vein thrombosis). A common approach is to check the INR within the 24 hours
prior to surgery in stable patients.
¯Çü Allergy. True allergy rather than intolerance should be
con¯ÇØrmed.

{ Medication, including sulfonamides and antibiotics commonly used following cataract surgery.

{ Iodine or shell¯ÇØsh ÔÇô the latter may indicate an iodine allergy.
If allergy to iodine is present an alternative skin and conjunctival antiseptic such as chlorhexidine should be used.

{ Others: allergy to latex (latex-free gloves may be necessary), sticking plaster, local anaesthetics, insect bites

(cross-reaction with hyaluronidase that is o¯Çøen used with
local anaesthesia).
¯Çü Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) carriage. Relevant national and local protocols for the identi¯ÇØcation and management of patients at high risk for MRSA
carriage should be followed.
¯Çü Transport (to hospital and to the operating theatre within
hospital): special arrangements may be needed for patients
with poor mobility or exceptionally high body mass.

TIP Systemic alpha-blockers (e.g. tamsulosin) are the main
cause of intraocular ¯ÇØoppy iris syndrome when undertaking
phacoemulsi¯Ç×cation.

## Ophthalmic preoperative assessment

A detailed and pertinent ophthalmic evaluation is required. ¯Ç×e
following should be considered:
¯Çü Visual acuity is usually tested using a Snellen chart.
¯Çü Cover test. A heterotropia may indicate amblyopia, which carries a guarded visual prognosis, or the possibility of diplopia
if the vision is improved. A squint, usually a divergence, may
develop in an eye with poor vision due to cataract and lens
surgery alone may straighten the eye.
¯Çü Pupillary responses. Because cataract never produces an
a¯Ç£erent pupillary defect, its presence implies substantial posterior pole pathology.
¯Çü Ocular adnexa. Dacryocystitis, blepharitis, chronic conjunctivitis, lagophthalmos, ectropion, entropion and tear ¯ÇØlm abnormalities may predispose to endophthalmitis. ¯Ç×ese conditions
should be dealt with before intraocular surgery.
¯Çü Cornea. Eyes with decreased endothelial cell counts (e.g.
substantial cornea guttata) have increased vulnerability to
postoperative decompensation. Specular microscopy and
pachymetry may be helpful in assessing risk and precautions
should be taken to protect the endothelium (see below). A
prominent arcus senilis is o¯Çøen associated with a surgical view
of decreased clarity, as are stromal opacities.
¯Çü Anterior chamber. A shallow anterior chamber can render
cataract surgery di¯ÇÜcult. Recognition of a poorly dilating
pupil allows intensive preoperative mydriatic drops, planned
mechanical dilatation prior to capsulorhexis and/or intracameral injection of mydriatic. A poor red re¯ÇÖex compromises the
creation of a capsulorhexis, but can be largely overcome by
staining the capsule with trypan blue.
¯Çü Lens. Nuclear cataracts tend to be hard and may require more
power for phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation, while cortical and subcapsular opacities tend to be so¯Çøer. Black nuclear opacities are
extremely dense and extracapsular cataract extraction rather
than phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation may be the preferred option. Pseudoexfoliation indicates a likelihood of weak zonules (phakodonesis ÔÇô lens wobble ÔÇô may be present), a fragile capsule and
poor mydriasis.
¯Çü Fundus examination. Pathology such as age-related macular
degeneration may a¯Ç£ect the visual outcome. Ultrasonography
may be required, principally to exclude retinal detachment

TIP Careful preoperative examination of the posterior pole
is needed in a patient requiring cataract surgery to exclude

## General principles of informed consent

¯Çü Consent can be defined as ÔÇÿthe process of providing information that enables the patient to make an informed decision to undergo a specific treatmentÔÇÖ. The process takes
time, patience and clarity of explanation. It is important to
establish whether the patient has the capacity to give consent and has the ability to understand, retain, weigh-up
information and make a decision without duress. A ÔÇÿcompetentÔÇÖ person has the right to refuse treatment, even if by
refusing the proposed treatment it could result in visual disability, blindness or death.
¯Çü Consent for cataract surgery. It is essential that the patient
arrives at a fully informed decision before proceeding to cataract surgery. It is useful to provide written information and a
link to an online video of the surgery. As well as discussing
the bene¯ÇØts, risks should be conveyed at a level appropriate to
each patientÔÇÖs level of understanding, with an explanation of
the more common and severe potential problems.
¯Çü Risk. Two legal principles are relevant in the United Kingdom:

eye drops, intraocular pressure (IOP) spike, iridocyclitis,
posterior capsular opaci¯ÇØcation.

{ ModerateÔÇôsevere but less common complications: posterior capsular rupture/vitreous loss (1% or less for
experienced surgeons, higher for trainees dependent on
experience), zonular dehiscence, dropped nucleus (about
0.2%), leaking wound, cystoid macular oedema (CMO),
corneal decompensation su¯ÇÜcient to need corneal gra¯Çø,
unexpected refractive outcome (may need contact lens
wear, lens implant exchange or corneal surgery), retinal
detachment (<1%), IOL dislocation, persistent ptosis and
diplopia.

{ Rare but invariably very serious complications: endophthalmitis (0.1%) and suprachoroidal haemorrhage (0.04%).

{ ¯Ç×e risks of anaesthesia should be conveyed by the person administering it. Local anaesthesia carries a low risk
of problems. Some rare complications have the potential
to be very serious including loss of the eye and even death:
allergy to the anaesthetic agent, retrobulbar haemorrhage
(see Ch. 22), perforation of the globe and inadvertent infusion of anaesthetic agent into the cerebrospinal ¯ÇÖuid via
the optic nerve sheath, causing brainstem anaesthesia.

{ Risk should be disclosed if a reasonable body of medical
opinion would consider it important to disclose the risk.

{ If a reasonable person in the patientÔÇÖs position would
attach signi¯ÇØcance to the risk, they should be told about it.
¯Çü Points for discussion with the patient may include:

{ Immediate sequential bilateral cataract surgery (ISBCS)
versus delayed sequential bilateral cataract surgery. ¯Ç×e
option of ISBCS is subject to debate because of the potential risk of bilateral sight-threatening complications, especially endophthalmitis. However, large trials have shown
no di¯Ç£erence between the two alternatives in respect
of visual acuity or complications at 1 month and 1 year
postoperatively. Meticulous selection and preparation of
appropriate cases is essential.

{ ¯Ç×e merits and disadvantages of the various refractive
options and in particular, the use of a multifocal or toric IOL.

{ Reassurance that most cataract operations are straightforward with good visual results.

{ Complications can be dealt with e¯Ç£ectively and cause no
long-term di¯ÇÜculties, but that some rare problems can be
very serious and may mean that a second operation will
be necessary.

{ A warning that in about 1 in 1000 cataract operations the
eye will be le¯Çø with little or no sight; in about 1 in 10,000
the patient will lose the eye.

{ Relatively mild and usually easily treatable but common
complications include periocular ecchymosis, allergy to

## Biometry

## Biometry facilitates calculation of the lens power likely to result in

the desired postoperative refractive outcome. In its basic form this
involves the measurement of two ocular parameters, keratometry
and axial (anteroposterior) length.
¯Çü Keratometry involves determination of the curvature of the
anterior corneal surface (steepest and flattest meridians),
expressed in dioptres or in millimetres of radius of curvature. This is commonly carried out with the interferometry
apparatus used to determine axial length (see below), but if
this is unavailable or unsuitable, manual keratometry (e.g.
JavalÔÇôSchi├©tz keratometer) or corneal topography can be
performed.
¯Çü Optical coherence biometry (Fig. 10.7A) is a non-contact
method of axial length measurement that utilizes two coaxial
partially coherent low-energy laser beams to produce an interference pattern (partial coherence interferometry). Modern
biometry devices also perform keratometry, anterior chamber
depth and corneal white-to-white measurement and are able
to calculate IOL power using a range of formulae. Measurements have high reproducibility and generally require less skill
than ultrasonic biometry (see below).
¯Çü A-scan ultrasonography is a slightly less accurate method of
determining the axial dimension and can be acquired either
by direct contact (Fig. 10.7B) or more accurately (but with
greater di¯ÇÜculty) by using a water bath over the eye (immersion ultrasonography). ¯Ç×e sound beam must be aligned with
the visual axis for maximal precision. Each re¯ÇÖecting surface
is represented by a spike on an oscilloscope display monitor
(Fig. 10.7C).
¯Çü IOL power calculation formulae. Numerous formulae have
been developed that utilize keratometry and axial length to
calculate the IOL power required to achieve a given refractive

A

B

C

(C) ultrasonographic monitor display (A/C, anterior chamber depth; L, lens thickness).

outcome. Some formulae incorporate additional parameters
such as anterior chamber depth and lens thickness to try to
optimize accuracy. ¯Ç×e SRK-T, Haigis, Ho¯Ç£er Q and Holladay
1 and 2 are commonly used. Speci¯ÇØc formulae may be superior
for very short or long eyes, but opinions vary and it is always
wise to plan individually for an unusual eye, consulting the latest research and recommendations. Short eyes in particular are
prone to unexpected mean spherical error following surgery.
Based on current literature, for shorter eyes (AL< 22mm) Hoffer Q, Haigis, Hill-RBF and Kane formulae are recommended,
whereas for long eyes (AL>26mm) BU II, Holladay, Haigis
(with optimized constant) and Kane formulae produce better
outcomes. Factors that confound axial length measurements
include:

situation. Most involve the calculation of the post-refractive
procedure ÔÇÿtrueÔÇÖ corneal power using a special process (refractive history method, contact lens method) and insertion of this
into a standard (e.g. Ho¯Ç£er Q) or speci¯ÇØc (e.g. Masket) formula, but the Haigis-L regression formula uses statistical data
to facilitate calculation on post-refractive surgery eyes using
only standard inputs. It may be prudent to utilize more than
one method of IOL calculation. ¯Ç×e patient needs to be warned
that an under- or over-correction may follow the surgery.
¯Çü Contact lenses. If the patient wears so¯Çø contact lenses, these
should not be worn for up to a week prior to biometry to allow
corneal stabilization. ¯Ç×ose patients who have a long history
of wearing rigid gas permeable contact lenses require up to
6 weeks to achieve refractive stability before undertaking the
measurements.
¯Çü Personalized A-constant. If a consistent postoperative refractive deviation is found in most of an individual surgeonÔÇÖs cases,
it is assumed that some aspects of personal surgical (or possibly biometric) technique consistently and similarly in¯ÇÖuence
outcome. A personalized A-constant can be programmed into
biometry apparatus to take this into account.

{ Poor patient ¯ÇØxation.

{ Irregularities in globe contour.

{ Silicone oil in the vitreous.
¯Çü Previous refractive surgery. Any form of corneal refractive
surgery is likely to make a signi¯ÇØcant di¯Ç£erence to the IOL
power required and standard IOL calculations are unsuitable.
Several di¯Ç£erent methods have been described to address this

318 Management of Acquired Cataract

TIP To achieve accurate preoperative biometry, soft
contact lenses should not be worn for one week before the
measurements are taken. Refractive stability takes up to 6
weeks for those wearing rigid gas-permeable contact lenses.

## Postoperative refraction

¯Çü Margins of error. Postoperative unaided refraction is within
1.0D of predicted outcome in almost 90% of patients and
within 0.5D in two-thirds.
¯Çü Emmetropia is typically the desired postoperative refraction,
though spectacles will be needed for near vision since a conventional IOL cannot accommodate. Many surgeons aim for
a small degree of myopia (about ÔêÆ0.25 D) to o¯Ç£set possible
errors in biometry. Postoperative hypermetropia, which necessitates correction for clear vision at all distances, is less well
tolerated than myopia.
¯Çü Contralateral eye. Postoperative refractive planning must consider the contralateral eye. If this has a signi¯ÇØcant refractive error
but is unlikely to require cataract surgery within a few years, the
postoperative target for the operated eye might be set for within
less than 2.0 D of its fellow, to avoid problems with image size
discrepancy and di¯ÇÜculty with binocular fusion. In some cases,
such as when there is an early lens opacity in the fellow eye or
when ametropia is extreme, the patient can be o¯Ç£ered lens surgery to the other eye to facilitate targeting both at emmetropia.
¯Çü ÔÇÿMonovisionÔÇÖ is a concept in which the (usually) nondominant eye is le¯Çø with between 1 and 2 dioptres of myopia
to allow unaided vision for reading, whilst emmetropia is targeted in the dominant eye. ¯Ç×is is attractive to some patients,
generally those who have previously been using contact lenses
or spectacles to achieve monovision.
¯Çü Multifocal lens options use a variety of optical means to
achieve satisfactory near, distance and intermediate vision (see
Ch. 8). Most patients are satis¯ÇØed with the results, but a signi¯ÇØcant number are unhappy, complaining of phenomena such as
glare and loss of contrast sensitivity. Highly accurate refractive
outcomes, including limited astigmatism, are necessary for
optimal function and a greater likelihood of tolerance.
¯Çü Younger patients. With a conventional monofocal IOL, patients
younger than about 55 years need to be aware that they will
experience a sudden loss of active focusing and that it will o¯Çøen
take some time to adjust to the loss of unaided near vision.

TIP Patients younger than 55 years need to be warned
that active focusing will be lost after the implantation of a
conventional monofocal IOL.

## Intraocular lenses

## Positioning

An IOL (Fig. 10.8A) consists of an optic and haptics. ¯Ç×e optic is
the central refracting element and the haptics the arms or loops
that sit in contact with peripheral ocular structures to centralize
the optic. Modern cataract surgery, with preservation of the lens

{ Collamer is composed of collagen, a poly-HEMA-based
copolymer and an ultraviolet-absorbing chromophore. It is
marketed principally on the basis of high biocompatibility
and a favourable track record.
¯Çü Rigid IOLs are made entirely from polymethylmethacrylate
(PMMA). ¯Ç×ey cannot be injected or folded so require an
incision larger than the diameter of the optic, typically 5 or 6
mm, for insertion. For economic reasons, they continue to be
widely used in developing countries. PCO rates are higher with
PMMA lenses than silicone and acrylic. Some surgeons favour

capsule, a¯Ç£ords positioning of the IOL in the ideal location ÔÇô ÔÇÿin
the bagÔÇÖ (Fig. 10.8B). Complicated surgery, with rupture of the posterior capsule, may necessitate alternative positioning in the posterior chamber with the haptics in the ciliary sulcus (a three-piece
IOL only, not one-piece including those with plate haptics, as these
may not be stable), or in the anterior chamber (AC) with the haptics supported in the angle ÔÇô AC positioning requires a speci¯ÇØc lens
type. In some circumstances a supplementary IOL may be placed
in the sulcus in addition to an IOL in the capsular bag, for instance
to address a residual refractive error following primary surgery
(secondary pseudopolyphakia) and thin-pro¯ÇØle IOLs are available
for this purpose. It is preferable to avoid a secondary sulcus IOL
(primary pseudopolyphakia) in very short (e.g. nanophthalmic)
eyes due to the risk of angle closure. O¯Ç£-the-shelf IOLs of power
up to 40 D are available and custom IOLs can be produced in even
higher powers.

## Design

¯Çü Flexible IOLs introduced into the eye via an injector and
subsequently unrolled inside the eye are now in general use.
Injector-based delivery utilizes a very small incision and also
allows avoidance of implant contact with the ocular surface, so
reducing the risk of bacterial contamination. Simple folding of
the IOL is an alternative, but requires a slightly larger incision.
Flexible materials are discussed below. ¯Ç×ere seems to be no
distinct superiority of one material over another.

{ Acrylic IOLs. Hydrophobic (water content <1%) acrylic
materials have a greater refractive index than hydrophilic
lenses and are consequently thinner, though this can result
in dysphotopsia (troublesome glare and re¯ÇÖections). ¯Ç×ey
have been reported to produce a greater reaction in eyes
with uveitis, but outcomes do not seem to be materially
a¯Ç£ected. Hydrophilic acrylic (hydrogel) in theory o¯Ç£ers
superior biocompatibility, but the image of hydrogel IOLs
has been marred by the occurrence of calci¯ÇØcation requiring IOL removal in some types and in¯ÇÖammation in others
(these problems have been resolved by lens manufacturers). Posterior capsular opaci¯ÇØcation (PCO) rates may be
higher with hydrogel IOLs than with other materials.

{ Silicone IOLs are available in both loop haptic (one- or
three-piece) and plate haptic (one-piece) conformations,
the latter consisting of a roughly rectangular leaf with the
optic sited centrally. Silicone IOLs exhibit greater biocompatibility than hydrophobic acrylic IOLs, but may be prone
to signi¯ÇØcant silicone deposition in silicone oil-¯ÇØlled eyes.

A B

C

D

optic; (B) IOL in situ in the capsular bag; (C) implanted toric IOL showing diametrically
opposite sets of three dots marking the lens axis (arrow); (D) one-piece, ¯ÇØexible, multifocal
IOL (optic includes a blue light ¯Ç×lter).

heparin-coated IOLs (see below) in eyes with previous uveitis,
particularly in children.
¯Çü Sharp/square-edged optics (see Fig. 10.8A) are associated
with a signi¯ÇØcantly lower rate of PCO compared with roundedged optics and the former is now the predominant design.
However, square edges may be associated with a higher rate of
dysphotopsia (see below).
¯Çü Blue light ¯Çƒlters. Although all IOLs contain ultraviolet light
¯ÇØlters, a number also include ¯ÇØlters for blue wavelengths, in
order to reduce the possibility of damage to the retina by this
higher-energy visible light (see Fig. 10.8D). ¯Ç×e blue ¯ÇØlters
impart a slight yellow tint to the IOL, though similar only to
that of the physiological young adult lens. Some evidence suggests slightly poorer visual function in scotopic conditions of
illumination.
¯Çü Aspheric optics to counteract corneal spherical aberration
are widely available and have been shown to improve contrast,
particularly in mesopic conditions. A potential drawback is
that the aspheric element of the manufactured IOLs is set at a

single standardized level (this di¯Ç£ers between manufacturers),
but the extent of spherical aberration varies between individuals; some will be over- and some under-compensated.
¯Çü Heparin coating reduces the attraction and adhesion of
in¯ÇÖammatory cells and this may have particular application in
eyes with uveitis. However, there is no clear evidence of the
clinical bene¯ÇØt of heparin surface modi¯ÇØcation in these eyes.
¯Çü Toric IOLs (Fig. 10.8C) have an integral cylindrical refractive component to compensate for pre-existing corneal astigmatism. ¯Ç×e main potential problem is rotation within the
capsular bag, which occurs in a small percentage and may be
corrected by early surgical repositioning.
¯Çü Bifocal IOLs (Fig. 10.8D) (see also Ch. 8) utilizing refractive
or di¯Ç£ractive mechanisms aim to provide clear vision over
a range of focal distances. So-called ÔÇÿaccommodativeÔÇÖ IOLs
attempt to ¯ÇÖex and thereby alter focal length but in practice
the amplitude of accommodation is slight.
¯Çü Adjustable IOLs allow the alteration of refractive power following implantation. One version uses low-level ultraviolet

320 Management of Acquired Cataract

irradiation at the slit lamp about a week a¯Çøer surgery to induce
polymerization of its constituent molecules in speci¯ÇØc patterns with precise spherical and cylindrical (astigmatism)
correction.

## Anaesthesia

Most cataract surgery is performed under local anaesthesia (LA),
sometimes in conjunction with intravenous or oral sedation. General anaesthesia is required in some circumstances, such as children and many young adults, very anxious patients, some patients
with learning di¯ÇÜculties, epilepsy, dementia and those with a head
tremor.
¯Çü Sub-Tenon block involves insertion of a blunt-tipped cannula
through an incision in the conjunctiva and Tenon capsule
5 mm from the limbus inferonasally (Fig. 10.9A) and passing
it around the curve of the globe through the sub-Tenon space.
¯Ç×e anaesthetic is injected beyond the equator of the globe
(Fig. 10.9B). Although anaesthesia is good and complications
minimal, akinesia is variable. Chemosis and subconjunctival

haemorrhage are common but penetration of the globe is
extremely rare.
¯Çü Peribulbar block is given through the skin (Fig. 10.9C and
D) or conjunctiva with a 1-inch (25-mm) needle. It generally
provides e¯Ç£ective anaesthesia and akinesia. Penetration of the
globe is a rare but severe complication. For this reason, peribulbar should be avoided in long eyes (which also tend to have
a larger equatorial diameter).
¯Çü Topical anaesthesia involves drops or gel (proxymetacaine
0.5%, tetracaine 1% drops, lidocaine 2% gel), which can be
augmented with intracameral preservative-free lidocaine 0.2ÔÇô
1%, o¯Çøen infused during hydrodissection. Patients receiving
supplementary intracameral lidocaine have signi¯ÇØcantly less
pain than those receiving topical anaesthesia alone. Intracameral Mydrane, a preservative-free combination of tropicamide
0.02%, phenylephrine 0.31% and lidocaine 1%, is commonly
used for achieving mydriasis and anaesthesia during highvolume cataract surgery. Combined viscoelastic/lidocaine
preparations are also commercially available. Despite the
absence of akinesia, most patients can cooperate adequately.

A B

D

anaesthesia; (B) sub-Tenon in¯Ç×ltration with a blunt cannula; (C) insertion of needle for peribulbar anaesthesia. (D) peribulbar in¯Ç×ltration of anaesthetic agent.

However, approximately 5% of patients experience pain
when the surgery is undertaken with topical or intracameral
anaesthesia.

## Manual cataract surgery

When posterior chamber IOLs began to be widely used in the
1980s most surgeons adopted extracapsular cataract extraction
(ECCE), abandoning the older intracapsular technique (ICCE). In
ICCE, a cryoprobe is used to remove the lens complete with its
capsule (Fig. 10.10A). In ECCE, a¯Çøer a large anterior capsulotomy
is created, an extensive limbal incision (8ÔÇô10 mm) is completed
and the lens nucleus is expressed following hydrodissection to free
its cortical attachments (Fig. 10.10B). Cortical matter is then aspirated, leaving behind a su¯ÇÜciently intact capsular bag to support
an IOL. Suturing of the incision is required, sometimes inducing
considerable corneal astigmatism. Manual small-incision cataract
surgery (MSICS) is a variant of ECCE used to address the requirement for high-volume surgical treatment of patients with dense
cataracts in less a¯Çÿuent geographical regions. It involves the creation of a small self-sealing sclero-corneal tunnel (Fig. 10.11A),
staining of the anterior capsule to facilitate capsulorhexis (Fig.
10.11B), manual one-piece expression of the nucleus (Fig. 10.11C),
manual aspiration of the cortex (Fig. 10.11D) and IOL implantation. Visual rehabilitation is comparable to phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation
but MSICS is faster and avoids the need for expensive technology.

Phacoemulsi¯Ç×cation

## Introduction

Phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation (ÔÇÿphacoÔÇÖ) is the standard method of cataract
extraction in high-income countries and in regional centres in
most lower-income countries.

## Phacodynamics

Choosing appropriate settings makes surgery safer and easier.

¯Çü Level of irrigating bottle above the patientÔÇÖs eye is set to maintain AC stability with a reasonable IOP. Infusion ¯ÇÖow is proportional to the height of the bottle.
¯Çü Aspiration ¯Ç£ow rate (AFR) refers to the volume of ¯ÇÖuid
removed from the eye in millilitres per minute. For a higher
AFR the irrigating bottle must be elevated to compensate for
increased ¯ÇÖuid loss. High AFR results in attraction of lens
material towards the phaco tip, with faster vacuum build-up
and swi¯Çøer removal of lens matter but with less e¯Ç£ective power.
A high AFR should usually be avoided by trainee surgeons.
¯Çü Vacuum, measured in mmHg, is generated during occlusion
when the pump is attempting to aspirate ¯ÇÖuid. Vacuum level
determines how tightly material is held by the phaco tip when
occluded, providing the ability to manipulate lens fragments.

## High vacuum can decrease the total power required to remove the

lens. As with AFR, a lower-vacuum setting slows down the speed
of intraocular events, reduces the intensity of surge (see below)
and makes inadvertent aspiration of iris or lens capsule less likely.
¯Çü Post-occlusion surge. When occlusion of the phaco tip by lens
material is broken, pent-up energy results in a sudden temporary increase in out¯ÇÖow ÔÇô ÔÇÿsurgeÔÇÖ. ¯Ç×is may result in complications such as capsular rupture and as far as possible is
suppressed by modern phaco machines.

## Pump type

¯Ç×e main implication of the type of pump employed by a particular phaco machine is the e¯Ç£ect on vacuum behaviour.
¯Çü Peristaltic (¯Ç£ow) pumps pull ¯ÇÖuid and lens material into the
phaco tip by compressing tubing over variable speed rollers.
Vacuum is generated only when occlusion of the tip occurs,
following which the pump slows and stops when a set maximum is achieved.
¯Çü Venturi (vacuum) pumps create a negative pressure in a vessel by passing compressed gas across its entrance. ¯Ç×is has the
practical e¯Ç£ect of synchronizing vacuum and AFR so there is
generally no independent means of adjusting AFR. Depression

of the foot pedal increases vacuum towards the pre-set maximum independent of occlusion and tip vacuum is therefore
always available.
¯Çü Hybrid pumps are o¯Ç£ered by some modern machines.

## Handpiece

¯Ç×e phaco handpiece features a tip consisting of a hollow titanium
needle with an enclosing ¯ÇÖuid-cooling sleeve (Fig. 10.12) to protect the cornea from thermal and mechanical damage. Its emulsifying action is mediated by very high-frequency (ultrasonic)
vibration leading to jackhammer, cavitation and other e¯Ç£ects.
Some machines o¯Ç£er variants such as a torsional phaco action or
water jet-mediated phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation. Phaco tips of di¯Ç£ering
shapes and sizes are available, each having particular cutting and
holding characteristics.

## Ophthalmic viscosurgical devices

Ophthalmic viscosurgical devices (OVDs or viscoelastics) are biopolymers that play a critical role in modern cataract surgery.

¯Çü Cohesive OVDs are used to create and maintain intraocular
spaces, for example to maintain the AC during capsulorhexis
and in¯ÇÖation of the capsular bag to facilitate introduction of an
IOL. Higher molecular weight variants maintain intraocular
space more e¯Ç£ectively, but tend to promote iris prolapse in shallow ACs and confer a more sustained postoperative IOP rise.
¯Çü Dispersive OVDs are more adherent to surfaces than cohesive
OVDs and are typically used to protect the endothelium. ¯Ç×ey
are more di¯ÇÜcult to remove from the eye than cohesive viscoelastics, but are less likely to cause an IOP spike. ¯Ç×e major
practical disadvantage of dispersive OVDs is their tendency to
retain air bubbles and lens fragments, compromising the surgical view.
¯Çü Adaptive OVDs display mixed characteristics.
¯Çü ÔÇÿSo¯Çø shellÔÇÖ technique involves the injection prior to the capsulotomy stage of an outer dispersive layer followed by an inner
cohesive nucleus. Some surgeons use this routinely, others
only for eyes at higher risk of corneal decompensation (e.g.
cornea guttata).

chamber and maintain plugging of the capsular defect, facilitating completion of lens removal.

## Technique

¯Çü Preparation

{ Topical anaesthetic is followed by povidone-iodine 5%
(Fig. 10.13A) or chlorhexidine instillation into the conjunctival sac and cleaning of the eyelids (Fig. 10.13B),
ensuring thorough eyelash application. ¯Ç×e antiseptic
should be le¯Çø to work for a minimum of 3 minutes.

A

C

(B) in position during surgery.

¯Çü Pupillary manipulation. In an eye with a small pupil, a high
molecular weight cohesive viscoelastic (e.g. Healon GV) will push
the iris away from the lens and help to induce mydriasis. Adaptive
OVDsÔÇÖ cohesive e¯Ç£ect can be used to dilate a pupil intraoperatively and their dispersive e¯Ç£ect to maintain the dilatation. OVDs
can be used to break posterior synechiae with minimal trauma.

## Femtosecond lasers in cataract surgery

Femtosecond lasers, used in refractive surgery for several years,
have recently been adopted by some surgeons, replacing several
of the manual steps of phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation with an automated
process. ¯Ç×e corneal incisions, the capsulorhexis and initial
fragmentation of the crystalline lens, as well as astigmatismrelieving incisions (Fig. 10.16), can be performed with laser.

## Potential advantages include greater precision and integrity

of incisions, reduced phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation energy and possibly improved refractive outcomes due to more precise capsulorhexis placement. Disadvantages include substantially higher
cost, longer total operating time and di¯ÇÜculties with technically
challenging cases (e.g. small pupils). ¯Ç×ere is a substantial learning curve.

## Operative complications

## Rupture of the posterior lens capsule

Capsular rupture may be accompanied by vitreous loss, posterior
migration of lens material and, rarely, expulsive haemorrhage.
Sequelae to vitreous loss, particularly if inappropriately managed,
include CMO, retinal detachment, endophthalmitis, updrawn
pupil, uveitis, vitreous touch, vitreous wick syndrome, glaucoma
and posterior dislocation of the IOL.
¯Çü Signs

{ Sudden deepening or shallowing of the AC and momentary pupillary dilatation.

{ ¯Ç×e nucleus falls away and cannot be approached by the
phaco tip.

{ Vitreous aspirated into the phaco tip o¯Çøen manifests with
a marked slowing of aspiration.

{ ¯Ç×e torn capsule or vitreous gel may be directly visible.

TIP Rupture of the posterior capsule manifests with a sudden
change in the anterior chamber depth and momentary pupillary
dilatation.

## Posterior loss of lens fragments

## Dislocation of fragments of lens material into the vitreous cavity

(Fig. 10.18A) a¯Çøer zonular dehiscence or posterior capsule rupture
is rare but potentially serious as it may result in glaucoma, chronic
uveitis, retinal detachment or chronic CMO. Initially, any uveitis
or raised IOP must be treated. It may be reasonable to adopt a conservative approach for small fragments, but pars plana vitrectomy
will usually be required for larger pieces.

visible; (B) dislocated IOL.

Posterior dislocation of IOL

Dislocation of an IOL into the vitreous cavity (Fig. 10.18B) is rare.
Loss can occur via a posterior capsular dehiscence, or in an eye
with fragile zonular attachments (e.g. pseudoexfoliation) the entire
capsular bag may dislocate. Complications include vitreous haemorrhage, retinal detachment, uveitis and chronic CMO. Treatment
involves pars plana vitrectomy with IOL removal, repositioning or
exchange depending on the extent of capsular support.

## Suprachoroidal haemorrhage

A suprachoroidal haemorrhage involves a bleed into the suprachoroidal space from a ruptured posterior ciliary artery. If su¯ÇÜciently
severe it may result in extrusion of intraocular contents (expulsive
haemorrhage). It is a dreaded complication, but extremely rare
(0.04%) with phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation. Contributing factors include
advanced age, glaucoma, increased axial length, systemic cardiovascular disease, vitreous loss and conversion from phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation to ECCE. A high intraoperative index of suspicion is
critical and if there is any suggestion of a suprachoroidal haemorrhage the operation should be terminated and the incision sutured
immediately.
¯Çü Signs

{ Progressive shallowing of the AC, increased IOP and prolapse of the iris.

{ Vitreous extrusion, loss or partial obscuration of the red
re¯ÇÖex and the appearance of a dark mound behind the
pupil.

follows. Postoperatively, intensive topical and systemic steroids
should be used to reduce intraocular in¯ÇÖammation, with standard postoperative antibiotic treatment and IOP management.
Non-steroidal anti-in¯ÇÖammatory drugs (NSAIDs) should be
avoided for analgesia and any antiplatelet or anticoagulant discontinued short-term, provided this is safe.
¯Çü Subsequent treatment, if spontaneous absorption fails to
occur, consists of drainage of a large haemorrhage. ¯Ç×is can be
performed 7ÔÇô14 days later, by which time liquefaction of blood
clot has taken place. ¯Ç×e visual prognosis for a large haemorrhage is highly variable. Prolonged chorioretinal apposition
(>14 days) reduces the prognosis. Pars plana vitrectomy may
be considered when the retina appears adherent or detached,
though even apposed ÔÇÿkissingÔÇÖ haemorrhages may resolve
spontaneously without apparent retinal problems. If appropriate, completion of cataract surgery may be considered a¯Çøer a
further 1ÔÇô2 weeks.

## Acute postoperative endophthalmitis

## Pathogenesis

¯Ç×e contemporary reported incidence of acute endophthalmitis
following cataract surgery varies substantially between studies, but
is probably at least 0.1%. Acute intraocular infection is invariably
a severe event. Toxins produced by infecting bacteria and the host
in¯ÇÖammatory responses cause rapid and irreversible photoreceptor damage and ongoing e¯Ç£ects can continue long a¯Çøer the ocular
contents have been rendered sterile.
¯Çü Risk factors are di¯ÇÜcult to establish but may include operative
complications such as posterior capsule rupture, prolonged
procedure time, combined procedure (e.g. with vitrectomy),
clear corneal sutureless incision, temporal incision, wound
leak on the ¯ÇØrst day, delaying postoperative topical antibiotics
until the day a¯Çøer surgery, topical anaesthesia, adnexal disease
and diabetes.

{ In severe cases, posterior segment contents may be
extruded into the AC and through the incision.
¯Çü Immediate treatment involves closure of the incision with a
suture. Speed of closure is the key and residual viscoelastic can
be le¯Çø in the eye. Intraoperative posterior sclerostomy should
not be undertaken. ¯Ç×e diagnosis should be con¯ÇØrmed at the
slit lamp as soon as possible. IOP-lowering medication such as
oral acetazolamide is given to treat the pressure spike that o¯Çøen

TIP In a one-eyed patient, make sure that the adjacent socket
is free of infection before undertaking cataract surgery.

¯Çü Pathogens. About 90% of isolates are Gram-positive and 10%
Gram-negative. Staphylococcus epidermidis is the most common and with early treatment carries a reasonable prognosis.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e source of infection usually cannot be identi¯ÇØed with certainty. It is thought that the ¯ÇÖora of the eyelids and conjunctiva are the most frequent source, including contamination
via incisions in the early postoperative stages. Other potential sources include contaminated solutions and instruments,
environmental air and the surgeon, and other operating room
personnel.

## Prophylaxis

Because of the low rate of endophthalmitis it is very di¯ÇÜcult to
establish the e¯Ç£ectiveness of any preventative measure.
¯Çü Instillation of 5% povidone-iodine into the conjunctival fornices and leaving this undisturbed for at least 3 minutes prior
to surgery.
¯Çü Scrupulous preparation of the surgical site, with re-draping if
eyelash coverage is inadequate.
¯Çü Treatment of pre-existing infections such as blepharitis, conjunctivitis, chronic dacryocystitis and infection in the contralateral eye or socket.
¯Çü Antibiotic prophylaxis

{ Intracameral cefuroxime (1 mg in 0.1 ml) injected into the
AC at the end of surgery.

{ Alternatively, moxi¯ÇÖoxacin (0.5 mg in 0.1 ml) injected into
the AC at the end of surgery could be used if cefuroxime
is not available.

{ Intracameral vancomycin has been reported to cause
haemorrhagic occlusive retinal vasculitis in some patients
and should be avoided as routine prophylaxis.

{ Postoperative subconjunctival injection can achieve bactericidal levels in the AC for at least 1ÔÇô2 hours.

{ Preoperative topical ¯ÇÖuoroquinolone antibiotics are frequently given in regimens from 1 hour to 3 days before
surgery, but evidence for their e¯ÇÜcacy is lacking.
¯Çü Early resuturing of leaking wounds rather than observation is
likely to be prudent.
¯Çü Reviewing personal surgical practice to eliminate potentially
risk-prone elements, particularly if a signi¯ÇØcant rate of endophthalmitis is encountered.

TIP Preoperative instillation of 5% povidone-iodine to the
surface of the eye reduces the risk of endophthalmitis.

¯Çü Symptoms. Pain, redness and visual loss.
¯Çü Signs vary according to severity.

{ Vitritis with an impaired view of the fundus.

Di¯Ç×erential diagnosis

measured with a caliper. 0.2ÔÇô0.4 ml is aspirated from the midvitreous cavity. If using a disposable vitrector, the tubing is

¯Çü Microbiology. Specimens should be sent to the microbiology
laboratory immediately. Most laboratories prefer to receive a
sample in the acquiring apparatus and will divide the specimen
for microscopy and culture. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR)
can be helpful in identifying unusual organisms and if culture
results have been negative. However, its high sensitivity means
that contamination can lead to false-positive results.

## Treatment

¯Çü Intravitreal antibiotics are the key to management because

should be administered immediately a¯Çøer culture specimens
have been obtained. Antibiotics commonly used in combination are ce¯Çøazidime, which will kill most Gram-negative
organisms (including Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and vancomycin to address Gram-positive cocci (including MRSA).

{ ¯Ç×e concentrations are ce¯Çøazidime 2 mg in 0.1 ml and

provides details of preparation.

{ ¯Ç×e antibiotics are injected slowly into the mid-vitreous
cavity using a 25-gauge needle.

can be taken in a minor procedure room to avoid delay.

12ÔÇô24 hours provided fungal infection has been excluded from

and gastric protection (e.g. lansoprazole 30 mg once daily)

## Subsequent management

## Subsequent management should proceed according to culture

results and clinical response.
¯Çü Signs of improvement include contraction of ¯ÇØbrinous exudate and reduction of AC cellular activity and hypopyon. In
this situation treatment is not modi¯ÇØed irrespective of culture
results. Ultrasonography may be useful in vitreous assessment.
¯Çü If the clinical signs are worsening a¯Çøer 48 hours antibiotic
sensitivities should be reviewed and therapy modi¯ÇØed accordingly. Pars plana vitrectomy should be considered if not previously performed. Intravitreal antibiotics can be repeated
a¯Çøer 2 days. If amikacin has previously been used, repeated
administration should probably be avoided to reduce the risk
of retinal toxicity.
¯Çü Outcome is related to the duration of the infection prior to
treatment and the virulence of organisms.

{ If VA at presentation is perception of light, 30% of eyes
achieve 6/12 following treatment. If VA is better than light
perception, this ¯ÇØgure increases to 60%.

{ Infection with Bacillus cereus or streptococci o¯Çøen has a
poor visual outcome despite aggressive and appropriate
therapy, with 70% and 55% respectively achieving a ¯ÇØnal
VA of 6/60 or less. ¯Ç×is poor visual outcome may be related
to early retinopathy from exotoxins.
¯Çü Late problems

{ Persistent vitreous opaci¯ÇØcation. Aggressive and extended
topical, periocular and if necessary oral steroid treatment
will o¯Çøen lead to resolution. Vitrectomy can be considered
if the opaci¯ÇØcation is persistent.

{ Maculopathy in the form of epiretinal membrane, cystoid
oedema and ischaemia.

{ Hypotony. Wound leak should be excluded and persistent
in¯ÇÖammation addressed. Choroidal e¯Ç£usion should be
identi¯ÇØed and drained if necessary. Retinal detachment
and anterior vitreous membranes may require vitrectomy.

{ Other problems include chronic uveitis, secondary glaucoma, retinal detachment and phthisis.

Delayed-onset postoperative
endophthalmitis

## Pathogenesis

Delayed-onset endophthalmitis following cataract surgery develops when an organism of low virulence, such as P. acnes, becomes
trapped within the capsular bag (saccular endophthalmitis).
Organisms can become sequestered within macrophages, protected
from eradication but with continued expression of bacterial antigen. Onset ranges from 4 weeks to years (mean 9 months) postoperatively and typically follows uneventful cataract surgery. It may
rarely be precipitated by laser capsulotomy release of the organism.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Painless mild progressive visual deterioration is
typical and ¯ÇÖoaters may be present.

332 Management of Acquired Cataract

¯Çü Signs

{ Low-grade anterior uveitis, sometimes with medium-large
keratic precipitates (Fig. 10.21A). A degree of vitritis is
common.

{ ¯Ç×e in¯ÇÖammation initially responds well to topical steroids, but recurs when treatment is stopped and may eventually become steroid-resistant.

{ An enlarging capsular plaque composed of organisms
sequestrated in residual cortex within the peripheral capsular bag is common (Fig. 10.21B).
¯Çü Di¯ÇÜerential diagnosis is principally from other causes of anterior uveitis, particularly idiopathic, sterile post-surgical and
chronic/recurrent viral infection (see Ch. 12).
¯Çü Initial management. Later-generation ¯ÇÖuoroquinolones, such
as moxi¯ÇÖoxacin, penetrate the eye well and are concentrated
within macrophages. An empirical 10ÔÇô14-day course of moxi¯ÇÖoxacin (alternatives include clarithromycin) may be worthwhile prior to more invasive options.
¯Çü Investigation. Sampling of aqueous and vitreous should be
considered if oral antibiotics are ine¯Ç£ective. Anaerobic culture should be requested if P. acnes infection is suspected and
isolates may take 10ÔÇô14 days to grow. ¯Ç×e detection rate can
be greatly improved with the use of PCR, which should also
screen for the common causes of viral anterior uveitis.
¯Çü Treatment if persistent

{ Intravitreal antibiotics alone are usually unsuccessful in
resolving the infection.

{ Removal of the capsular bag, residual cortex and IOL,
requiring pars plana vitrectomy. Secondary IOL implantation may be considered at a later date. Intravitreal antibiotics are combined: vancomycin (1ÔÇô2 mg in 0.1 ml) is
the antibiotic of choice and can also be irrigated into any
capsular remnant. P. acnes is also sensitive to methicillin,
cefazolin and clindamycin.

Posterior capsular opaci¯Ç×cation

Visually signi¯ÇØcant PCO, also known as ÔÇÿa¯Çøer cataractÔÇÖ, is the
most common late complication of uncomplicated cataract surgery, occurring in 20ÔÇô25% of patients. It is caused by the proliferation of lens epithelial cells that have remained within the
capsular bag following cataract extraction. ¯Ç×e incidence of PCO
is reduced when the capsulorhexis opening is in complete contact with the anterior surface of the IOL. PMMA (and probably
to a lesser extent hydrogel) IOLs are particularly prone to PCO.
Implant design is more important than material. A square optic
edge appears to inhibit PCO (though may have a higher rate of
dysphotopsia ÔÇô see below).

TIP Posterior capsular thickening is the commonest cause of
late visual deterioration after small incisional cataract surgery
and is easily treated with a Nd:YAG laser.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms include persistent slowly worsening blurring, glare
and sometimes monocular diplopia.
¯Çü VA is variably reduced, though dysfunction may be more
marked on contrast sensitivity testing.
¯Çü Signs typically include more than one pattern of opaci¯ÇØcation.

{ Vacuolated (pearl-type) PCO (Fig. 10.22A) consists of proliferating swollen lens epithelial cells, similar to the bladder (Wedl) cells seen in posterior subcapsular cataract (see

particularly when grouped into clusters at the edge of a
capsulotomy (Fig. 10.22B), though strictly HirschbergÔÇô
Elschnig pearls refers to globular or grape-like collections
of swollen cells seen following traumatic or surgical anterior capsular rupture.

A B

C

E

(B) Elschnig pearl formation (arrow) on edge of capsulotomy; (C) slit-lamp appearance of
