334 Management of Acquired Cataract

{ Fibrosis-type PCO (Fig. 10.22C) is thought to be due to
¯ÇØbroblastic metaplasia of epithelial cells, which develop
contractile qualities.

{ A Soemmering ring (Fig. 10.22D) is a whitish annular or
doughnut-shaped proliferation of residual cells that classically
formed almost in the periphery of the capsular bag following
older methods of cataract surgery, but is uncommon now. It
may form at the edge of a capsulorhexis or capsulotomy.

## Treatment

## Treatment involves the creation of an opening in the posterior capsule

using a Nd:YAG laser (termed a posterior capsulotomy) (Fig. 10.22E).
¯Çü Indications. ¯Ç×e presence of visual symptoms is the main indication (reduced VA and glare). Less commonly, capsulotomy is
performed to improve an inadequate fundus view impairing
assessment and treatment of posterior segment pathology.
¯Çü Technique. Safe and successful laser capsulotomy involves
accurate focusing and use of the minimum energy required.
Laser power is initially set at 1mJ/pulse and may be increased
if necessary. A series of punctures is applied in a cruciate or
circumferential pattern using single-pulse shots. ¯Ç×e opening
should equate approximately to the size of the physiologically
dilated pupil under scotopic conditions ÔÇô this should average
around 4ÔÇô5 mm in the pseudophakic eye. A larger capsulotomy may be necessary if glare persists, or for retinal examination or treatment, but the capsulotomy should not extend
beyond the edge of the optic in case vitreous prolapses around
its edge. It may be prudent to adopt a higher threshold for
treatment, and minimizing its extent, in eyes at risk of retinal
detachment (e.g. high myopia), CMO (e.g. history of uveitis)
or lens displacement (e.g. pseudoexfoliation). Some research
suggests that the total energy applied should be less than 80mJ
in order to reduce the risk of a signi¯ÇØcant IOP spike.
¯Çü Complications include pitting of the IOL (Fig. 10.22F) that
is usually visually inconsequential. ¯Ç×e IOP may rise, particularly in patients with glaucoma, but is typically mild and
transient. A retinal tear or detachment may follow the treatment and myopic individuals should be warned to return if

they develop symptoms compatible with a posterior vitreous
detachment (PVD). CMO may occur, but is less common
when the capsulotomy is delayed for 6 months or more a¯Çøer
cataract surgery. IOL subluxation or dislocation is rare.

Anterior capsular ¯Ç×brosis and
contraction

Since the advent of continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis, signi¯ÇØcant
contraction of the anterior capsular opening is sometimes seen
(capsulophimosis ÔÇô Fig. 10.23). It typically progresses over months
and if severe, Nd:YAG laser anterior capsulotomy may be required.
Risk factors include a small capsulorhexis, pseudoexfoliation
syndrome (where the capsulorhexis contracts postoperatively by up
to 25%), retinitis pigmentosa and plate-haptic silicone IOL.

## Capsular bag distension syndrome

In this rare complication of phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation and IOL implantation ¯ÇÖuid accumulates between the IOL and the posterior capsule leading to distension of the posterior capsule, slight anterior
displacement of the IOL and a myopic shi¯Çø (Fig. 10.24). ¯Ç×e
trapped ¯ÇÖuid becomes turbid in time, which results in a reduction of vision. Most cases have an axial length that is greater than
25mm and a small capsulorhexis, with adhesion between the
lens optic and the anterior capsular rim. Anterior segment OCT
can be helpful to con¯ÇØrm the diagnosis. A rapid improvement in
vision follows the application of Nd:YAG laser to the posterior
capsule.

## Miscellaneous postoperative
complications

## Cystoid macular oedema

Symptomatic CMO (see Ch. 14) is relatively uncommon following
uncomplicated phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation and in most cases is mild and
transient. It occurs more o¯Çøen a¯Çøer complicated surgery and has a

A B

peak incidence at 6ÔÇô10 weeks, although the interval to onset may
be much longer.
¯Çü Risk factors include epiretinal membrane, a history of CMO
in the other eye, operative complications such as posterior
capsular rupture with vitreous loss, particularly with vitreous incarceration into the incision site (Fig. 10.25A), anterior
chamber IOL (Fig. 10.25B), secondary IOL implantation, diabetes (approximately ten times greater risk), uveitis and topical
prostaglandin treatment.
¯Çü Symptoms. Blurring, especially for near tasks, and sometimes
distortion. Subtle CMO may not be readily visible clinically,
but is demonstrated well on OCT.

TIP In the event of vitreous loss and raised IOP, avoid
prostaglandin drops as this increases the risk of cystoid
macular oedema.

turbid ¯ÇØuid between the posterior surface of the lens (arrow
heads) and the posterior capsule (arrow). (Courtesy of
J Wesierski.)

¯Çü Treatment. One or a combination of the following modalities
may be used.

{ Anterior vitrectomy or YAG laser applied to a vitreous
wick if present.

{ Topical NSAIDs (e.g. ketorolac four times daily, bromfenac
twice daily, nepafenac three times daily) reduce the risk of
developing CMO in non-diabetic and diabetic patients. In
addition, these drops may be bene¯ÇØcial even in long-standing
cases. Treatment may be necessary for several months.

{ Steroids. Topically, by periocular or intravitreal (triamcinolone acetate 0.05ÔÇô0.1 ml of 40 mg/ml) injection.

{ Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors given systemically or
topically.

{ Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents.

{ Pars plana vitrectomy may be useful for CMO refractory
to medical therapy, even in eyes without apparent vitreous
disturbance.

## Dysphotopsia

Up to 1 in 10 patients complain of annoying visual phenomena
following uncomplicated cataract surgery with monofocal IOL
implantation. Multifocal IOLs have particular issues that are discussed separately above. Improvement in and adaptation to the
symptoms typically occurs over several months and the number of
monofocal IOL patients actually requiring further surgery for the
symptoms is very small. Rounded-edged IOLs may be less prone to
negative photopsia and silicone IOLs may be less liable to result in
dysphotopsia than acrylic IOLs. Optic and capsulorhexis size may
also be important.
¯Çü Symptoms. A dark shadow in the temporal periphery (negative dysphotopsia ÔÇô o¯Çøen the most troublesome), scintillations,
haloes, peripheral or central ¯ÇÖaring or ¯ÇÖashes (positive dysphotopsia) and possibly monocular diplopia.
¯Çü Treatment

{ Encouraging the patient that the symptoms usually
improve over time, both because of anatomical changes

A B

incision; (B) secondary anterior chamber IOL with pupil block after previous vitreous loss.

336 Management of Acquired Cataract

(e.g. capsulorhexis edge thickening) and because the brain
is able to ignore unwanted images.

{ Positive nocturnal symptoms can be helped with gentle
pupillary constriction (e.g. brimonidine), but dilatation
may help negative dysphotopsia.

{ Successful alleviation of symptoms has been reported with
a variety of techniques, including reverse optic capture
(vaulting the optic forward out of the capsular bag, leaving the haptics in place), ciliary sulcus IOL re-implantation
and piggyback IOL implantation in the sulcus.

{ IOL exchange (round-edged) may be considered.

{ Laser capsulotomy is best avoided as it markedly complicates IOL exchange. However, in some eyes, Nd:YAG laser
removal of a sector of capsulorhexis edge has resulted in
reduced symptoms.

A

B

C

with one haptic in the angle (arrows) and the other in the bag;
(C) red re¯ÇØex showing slight inferior decentration of an IOL.

TIP Inaccurate biometry is the commonest cause of a
refractive surprise. Make sure the biometry is in keeping with
the previous refraction.

## Corneal decompensation

Corneal oedema (see Ch. 7) may occur postoperatively, but is
usually mild and transient. Eyes with pre-existing corneal endothelial pathology, particularly low cell counts, are at increased
risk. Causes of signi¯ÇØcant oedema include a dense nucleus,
which requires high phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation energy, complicated or
prolonged surgery, pseudoexfoliation, intraoperative endothelial trauma and elevated postoperative IOP. Use of a dispersive
viscoelastic may help to protect the corneal endothelium during
surgery in high-risk eyes.

## Ptosis

Mild ptosis secondary to a variety of mechanisms (especially the use
of postoperative topical steroids), is not uncommon a¯Çøer cataract surgery, but usually improves without intervention. Corrective lid surgery should not be undertaken for at least a year postoperatively.

Malposition of the IOL

Although uncommon, malposition (Fig. 10.26AÔÇôC) may be associated with both optical and structural problems. Signi¯ÇØcant malposition may require repositioning or replacement, occasionally
with an iris or scleral-¯ÇØxated lens.

## Retinal detachment

Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RRD) is uncommon a¯Çøer
uncomplicated phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation with IOL implantation. Preoperative risk factors include lattice degeneration, retinal breaks
and high myopia. ¯Ç×e key intraoperative risk is vitreous loss. Pars
plana vitrectomy is usually the surgical modality employed for
pseudophakic RRD.

Refractive ÔÇÿsurpriseÔÇÖ

Small under- or over-corrections are not uncommon a¯Çøer this surgery. ¯Ç×e risk of this happening can be reduced by preoperatively
checking that the di¯Ç£erence in axial length between eyes is less than
0.7mm and that the di¯Ç£erence in average keratometry between
eyes is less than 0.9D. If there is a signi¯ÇØcant residual refractive
error as a consequence of a failure to achieve the intended postoperative refractive target, patient dissatisfaction may ensue.

¯Çü Management

{ Identify the cause.

{ Obtain a subjective refraction.

{ Examine the eye to exclude conditions like a distended
capsular bag (myopic shi¯Çø) and CMO (hyperopic shi¯Çø).

{ Check the biometry and the strength of the implanted IOL.

{ Repeat the biometry, keratometry (undiagnosed keratoconus) and measure corneal thickness (undiagnosed previous laser refractive surgery).
¯Çü Treatment

{ If no obvious cause can be identi¯ÇØed and the patient is
happy to use spectacles or wear a contact lens to correct
the refractive error, no further treatment is needed.

{ If the patient remains dissatis¯ÇØed and once the refraction is
stable, undertake further surgery.

{ IOL exchange is a good early option, but should be undertaken before capsular ¯ÇØbrosis occurs.

{ Corneal refractive surgery could be considered, as this can
deal with a range of refractive errors.

{ ÔÇÿPiggy backÔÇÖ sulcus IOL implantation is a reasonable option
for high degrees of uncorrected refractive error, but is less
accurate than laser refractive surgery.

transferase (GALT). Unless galactose (milk and milk products) is withheld from the diet, severe systemic complications
culminate in early death. ÔÇÿOil dropletÔÇÖ lens opacity (see Fig.
10.27E) develops within the first few days or weeks of life
in a large percentage of patients. Exclusion of galactose may
reverse early lens changes.

## Lowe syndrome

Lowe (oculo-cerebro-renal) syndrome is an X-linked recessive
(gene: OCRL1) inborn error of amino acid metabolism with neuromuscular, renal and other manifestations. Cataract is universal
and microphakia may also be present. Congenital glaucoma is
present in about half of patients. Female carriers may have visually
insigni¯ÇØcant cortical lens opacities.

## Mannosidosis

Mannosidosis is an AR disorder with de¯ÇØciency of ╬▒-mannosidase.
Infantile and juvenile-adult forms are seen, both of which feature progressive mental deterioration, musculoskeletal and other
abnormalities. Punctate lens opacities arranged in a spoke-like
pattern in the posterior lens cortex are frequent. Corneal clouding
can also occur but is less common.

## Other metabolic disorders

Potential causes include hypo- and pseudohypoparathyroidism
and hypo- and hyperglycaemia.

## Associated intrauterine infections

## Rubella

## Congenital rubella results from transplacental transmission of

virus from an infected mother and may lead to severe fetal malformations. Pearly nuclear or more di¯Ç£use unilateral or bilateral
cataract occurs in around 15%. (See also Ch. 12.)

## Toxoplasmosis

Ophthalmic features of congenital toxoplasmosis include cataract,
chorioretinitis, microphthalmos and optic atrophy. (See also Ch. 12.)

## Cytomegalovirus infection

Systemic features of congenital cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection
include jaundice, hepatosplenomegaly, microcephaly and intracranial calci¯ÇØcation. Ocular features apart from cataract include
chorioretinitis, microphthalmos, keratitis and optic atrophy.

## Varicella

Systemic features include learning disability, cortical cerebral atrophy, cutaneous scarring and limb deformities. Death in early infancy
is common. Ocular features may include cataract, microphthalmos,
chorioretinitis, optic disc hypoplasia and optic atrophy.

## Others

Measles, syphilis, herpes simplex and human immunode¯ÇØciency
virus (HIV). (See also Ch. 12.)

## Postoperative dry eye syndrome

## Cataract surgery is a risk factor for the induction or exacerbation

of dry eye symptoms in up to one-third of patients. Switching
to preservative-free postoperative medication and prescribing
preservative-free arti¯ÇØcial tears is key to the management. Additional treatment options are detailed in Chapter 5

CONGENITAL CATARACT

## Aetiology

Congenital cataract occurs in about 3 in 10,000 live births. In
general, one-third are associated with systemic disease, onethird are inherited and one-third are of unknown cause. Twothirds are bilateral and a cause can be identified in about half
of these. Autosomal dominant (AD) inheritance is the most
common aetiological factor. Others include chromosomal
abnormalities, metabolic disorders and intrauterine infections. Isolated inherited congenital cataracts carry a better
visual prognosis than those with coexisting ocular and systemic
abnormality. Unilateral cataracts are usually sporadic, without a
family history or systemic disease and affected infants are usually otherwise healthy.

## Associated metabolic disorders

## Galactosaemia

Galactosaemia is an autosomal recessive (AR) condition
characterized by impairment of galactose utilization caused
by absence of the enzyme galactose-1-phosphate uridyl

338 Congenital Cataract

## Other systemic associations

Down syndrome (trisomy 21)

¯Çü Systemic features include learning di¯ÇÜculties, stunted growth,
distinctive facial and peripheral features, thyroid dysfunction,
cardiorespiratory disease and reduced life span.
¯Çü Ocular features. Cataract of varied morphology (75%). ¯Ç×e
opacities are usually symmetrical and o¯Çøen develop in late
childhood. Other features include iris Brush¯ÇØeld spots (see

Edwards syndrome (trisomy 18)

¯Çü Systemic features. Characteristic facial and peripheral features,
deafness, cardiac anomalies, learning disability and early death.
¯Çü Ocular features apart from cataract include ptosis, microphthalmos, corneal opacity, uveal and disc coloboma and vitreoretinal dysplasia.

## Miscellaneous

HallermannÔÇôStrei¯Ç£ syndrome features impaired growth and other
features, with cataract in 90%. NanceÔÇôHoran syndrome is an
X-linked condition comprising distinctive dental and facial anomalies together with congenital cataract and microcornea. Female
carriers may show Y suture opacities (see Fig. 10.27F).

## Management

## Ocular assessment

In the neonate, determining the visual signi¯ÇØcance of lens opacity
is based principally on the appearance of the red re¯ÇÖex and the
quality of the fundus view. ¯Ç×ere are three possibilities:
¯Çü A very dense cataract with no red re¯ÇÖex.
¯Çü A less dense but still visually signi¯ÇØcant cataract (e.g. central
or posterior opacities over 3 mm in diameter) will permit visualization of the retinal vasculature with the indirect but not
with the direct ophthalmoscope.
¯Çü A visually insigni¯Çƒcant opacity will allow clear visualization
of the retinal vasculature with both the indirect and direct
ophthalmoscope.
¯Çü Other indicators of severe visual impairment include
absence of central ¯ÇØxation, nystagmus and strabismus.
¯Çü Morphology

{ Blue dot opacities (Fig. 10.27A) are common and innocuous.

{ Nuclear opacities (Fig. 10.27B) are con¯ÇØned to the embryonic or fetal nucleus. ¯Ç×e cataract may be dense or composed of ¯ÇØne dust-like (pulverulent) opacities.

{ Coronary (supranuclear) cataract lies in the deep cortex,
surrounding the nucleus like a crown. It is usually sporadic
but occasionally hereditary (Fig. 10.27C).

{ Lamellar opacities a¯Ç£ect a particular lamella of the lens
both anteriorly and posteriorly and may be associated with
radial extensions (ÔÇÿridersÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 10.27D). Lamellar opacities may be AD or occur in isolation as well as in association with metabolic disorders and intrauterine infections.

{ Central ÔÇÿoil dropletÔÇÖ opacities (Fig. 10.27E) are characteristic of galactosaemia.

{ Sutural, in which the opacity follows the anterior or posterior Y suture (Fig. 10.27F). ¯Ç×is may be seen in female
NanceÔÇôHoran carriers.

{ Anterior polar cataract may be ¯ÇÖat or project into the AC
(Fig. 10.27G). Occasional associations include persistent
pupillary membrane, aniridia, Peters anomaly and anterior
lenticonus.

{ Posterior polar cataract (Fig. 10.27H) may be associated with posterior lenticonus or fetal vascular remnants
including a Mittendorf dot. ¯Ç×is form of opacity is o¯Çøen
closely integrated with the lens capsule and/or a preexisting defect, with a very high risk of dehiscence during
surgery.
¯Çü Associated ocular pathology may involve the anterior (e.g.
corneal clouding, microphthalmos, glaucoma, persistent fetal
vasculature) or posterior segments (e.g. chorioretinitis, Leber
amaurosis, rubella retinopathy, foveal or optic nerve hypoplasia). Its presence may give an additional indication of visual
prognosis. ¯Ç×e di¯Ç£erential diagnosis of leukocoria may apply
(see Ch. 20).
¯Çü Assessment of family members for subclinical familial cataract is prudent.
¯Çü Ultrasonography should be performed if the fundus is not
visible and may reveal a de¯ÇØnitive cause such as persistent fetal
vasculature.
¯Çü Special tests such as forced-choice preferential looking and
visual evoked potentials may provide useful supporting
information.

## Systemic investigations

Investigation of familial cataract is unnecessary, but otherwise
the following should be considered. Assessment beyond a search
for infection and possibly urinary reducing substance is probably
unnecessary in unilateral cases.
¯Çü Screening for intrauterine infections should usually be performed in all unilateral and bilateral cases.
¯Çü Urine. Urinalysis for reducing substance a¯Çøer drinking milk
(galactosaemia) and chromatography for amino acids (Lowe
syndrome).
¯Çü Other investigations may include fasting blood glucose, serum
calcium and phosphorus, red blood cell GALT and galactokinase
levels. Children who have calcium and phosphorus anomalies
severe enough to cause cataract are likely to be unwell.
¯Çü Referral to a paediatrician may be warranted for dysmorphic
features or suspicion of other systemic diseases. Chromosome
analysis may be useful in this context.

## Treatment

¯Ç×e requirement for urgent surgery is balanced by the fact that the
earlier this takes place, particularly before 4 weeks of age, the higher
the chance of glaucoma developing during the juvenile years.
¯Çü Bilateral dense cataracts require surgery between 4 and
10 weeks of age to prevent the development of stimulus

A B

C D

E F

G H

with riders (arrow); (E) ÔÇÿoil dropletÔÇÖ; (F) sutural with blue dots; (G) anterior polar; (H) posterior
polar. (Courtesy of Ken K Nischal ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. E.)

340 Ectopia Lentis

deprivation amblyopia. If severity is asymmetrical, the eye
with the more marked opacity should be addressed ¯ÇØrst.
¯Çü Bilateral partial cataracts may not require surgery until later,
or indeed at any stage. In case of doubt, it may be prudent to
defer surgery in favour of careful monitoring.
¯Çü Unilateral dense cataract merits more urgent surgery. However, there is no consensus regarding timing, except that 6
weeks is the latest point at which elective surgery should be
performed. Many authorities would advocate surgery between
4 and 6 weeks, followed by aggressive anti-amblyopia therapy.
Despite this, the VA results are o¯Çøen disappointing. If the cataract is detected a¯Çøer 16 weeks of age, then the visual prognosis
is particularly poor.
¯Çü Partial unilateral cataract can usually be observed or treated
non-surgically with pupillary dilatation and possibly part-time
contralateral occlusion.
¯Çü Surgery involves anterior capsulorhexis, aspiration of lens
matter, capsulorhexis of the posterior capsule, limited anterior
vitrectomy and IOL implantation, if appropriate. It is important to correct associated refractive errors.

## Postoperative complications

Surgery carries a higher incidence of complications than in adults.
¯Çü Posterior capsular opaci¯Çƒcation is nearly universal if the
posterior capsule is retained and can have a substantial
amblyogenic e¯Ç£ect. Posterior capsulorhexis with vitrectomy is
generally performed during the primary lens extraction.
¯Çü Secondary membranes may form across the pupil, particularly if postoperative uveitis is not treated aggressively.
¯Çü Proliferation of lens epithelium is universal, o¯Çøen forming
a Soemmering ring (see Fig. 10.22D). It is usually visually
inconsequential.
¯Çü Glaucoma

{ ¯Ç×e younger the age at which the surgery is undertaken the
greater the risk of developing glaucoma.

{ Secondary open-angle glaucoma may develop in up to
20% of eyes by 5 years a¯Çøer surgery.

TIP After cataract surgery in a child, measures need to be
taken to prevent amblyopia.

ECTOPIA LENTIS

## Introduction

## Ectopia lentis refers to a hereditary or acquired displacement of the

lens from its normal position. ¯Ç×e lens may be completely dislocated, rendering the eye functionally aphakic (luxated), or partially
displaced, remaining partly within the pupillary area (subluxated).
¯Ç×e early stages of subluxation may manifest with a tremulous lens
(phacodonesis), demonstrated on the slit lamp by lens wobble on
rapid return of the eye to the primary position.

## Acquired causes

¯Çü Trauma.
¯Çü Pseudoexfoliation.
¯Çü In¯ÇÖammation, e.g. chronic cyclitis, syphilis.
¯Çü Hypermature cataract.
¯Çü Large eye, e.g. high myopia, buphthalmos.

## Without systemic associations

¯Çü Familial ectopia lentis. ¯Ç×is is an AD condition characterized
by bilateral symmetrical superotemporal displacement. It may
manifest congenitally or later in life.
¯Çü Ectopia lentis et pupillae is a rare congenital bilateral disorder with AR inheritance characterized by displacement of
the pupil and the lens in opposite directions (Fig. 10.28A).
¯Ç×e pupils are small and dilate poorly. Microspherophakia
may be present.
¯Çü Aniridia is occasionally associated with ectopia lentis (Fig.
10.28B).

## With systemic associations

{ AD inheritance (gene: FBN1) with variable expressivity.

{ Angle closure may occur in the immediate postoperative period secondary to pupillary block, especially in
microphthalmic eyes.
¯Çü Retinal detachment is an uncommon and usually late
complication.

## Visual rehabilitation

¯Ç×e visual results of cataract surgery in infants are hampered by
amblyopia, which should be treated aggressively (see Ch. 18).
¯Çü Spectacles are useful for older children with bilateral aphakia.
¯Çü Contact lenses provide a superior optical solution for
unilateral or bilateral aphakia. After the age of about 2
years compliance may worsen as the child becomes more
independent.
¯Çü IOL implantation is increasingly being performed in younger
children and appears to be e¯Ç£ective and safe in selected cases.
Hypermetropia (correctable with spectacles) is initially targeted and as the child ages slow change towards emmetropia
and then myopia is usually found.

¯Çü Marfan syndrome

{ Musculoskeletal features include a tall, thin stature with
disproportionately long limbs (arm span > height), long
¯ÇØngers (Fig. 10.29A) and toes (arachnodactyly), a narrow
high-arched (ÔÇÿgothicÔÇÖ) palate (Fig. 10.29B).

{ Kyphoscoliosis, sternal abnormalities, mild joint laxity, muscular underdevelopment and predisposition to
hernias.

{ Cardiovascular lesions include dilatation of the aortic root,
mitral valve prolapse and aortic aneurysm formation.

{ Bilateral ectopia lentis (80%); subluxation is most frequently superotemporal (Fig. 10.29C). ¯Ç×e zonules are
frequently intact so that accommodation is retained,
although rarely the lens may dislocate into the AC or vitreous (Fig. 10.29D).

{ Other ocular features: angle anomaly may lead to glaucoma and lattice retinal degeneration to retinal detachment. ¯Ç×ere may be hypoplasia of the dilator pupillae,
microspherophakia and strabismus.

¯Çü WeillÔÇôMarchesani syndrome is a rare systemic connective
tissue disease, conceptually the converse of Marfan syndrome.

{ Inheritance is AR or AD, the latter resulting from polymorphisms in FBN1, the same gene as Marfan syndrome.

{ Systemic features include short stature, short ¯ÇØngers and
toes (brachydactyly) and learning di¯ÇÜculties (Fig. 10.30A).

{ Ectopia lentis (50%). Subluxation is in an inferior direction
and occurs in late childhood or early adulthood. Microspherophakia (Fig. 10.30B) is common, so that pupillary
block with angle closure may ensue.
¯Çü Homocystinuria is an AR disorder in which decreased enzymatic metabolism of the amino acid methionine results in systemic accumulation of methionine and homocysteine.

{ Systemic features include coarse blond hair, blue irises,
malar ¯ÇÖush, Marfanoid habitus, neurodevelopmental
delay, marked thrombotic predisposition and early atherosclerosis (Fig. 10.30C).

{ Treatment involves oral pyridoxine, folic acid and vitamin B12 to reduce plasma homocysteine and methionine
levels.

{ Ectopia lentis, typically inferonasal, is almost universal by
the age of 25 years in untreated cases (Fig. 10.30D). ¯Ç×e
zonules, which normally contain high levels of cysteine

{ Other ocular features include iris atrophy, optic atrophy,

include sul¯ÇØte oxidase de¯ÇØciency (ectopia lentis is universal)
and occasionally Stickler syndrome (retinal detachment is the
most common ocular manifestation ÔÇô see Ch. 16), EhlersÔÇô
Danlos syndrome and hyperlysinaemia.

## Management

¯Ç×e main complications of ectopia lentis are refractive error of any
type depending on lens position, optical distortion due to astigmatism and/or lens edge e¯Ç£ect, glaucoma (see Ch. 11) and, rarely,
lens-induced uveitis.
¯Çü Spectacle correction may correct astigmatism induced by
lens tilt or edge e¯Ç£ect in eyes with mild subluxation. Aphakic

correction may also a¯Ç£ord good visual results if a signi¯ÇØcant
portion of the visual axis is clear in the undilated state.
¯Çü Surgical removal of the lens is indicated for intractable ametropia, meridional amblyopia, cataract, lens-induced glaucoma
or uveitis, or endothelial touch.

ABNORMALITIES OF LENS SHAPE

## Anterior lenticonus

Anterior lenticonus consists of a bilateral axial projection of the anterior surface of the lens into the AC (Fig. 10.31A and B). ¯Ç×e ÔÇÿoil dropletÔÇÖ
sign can be seen on retroillumination (Fig. 10.31C). Almost all patients
have Alport syndrome, a hereditary condition characterized by progressive sensorineural deafness and renal disease associated with
abnormal glomerular basement membrane (see Fig. 15.22). Retinal
¯ÇÖecks and posterior polymorphous corneal dystrophy may also occur.

## Posterior lenticonus

Bulging of the posterior axial lens (Fig. 10.31D) is associated with
local thinning or absence of the capsule in posterior lenticonus.
Most cases are unilateral, sporadic and not associated with systemic disease. With age, bulging progressively increases and the
lens cortex may opacify. Posterior subcapsular opaci¯ÇØcation is
common (Fig. 10.31E). Progression of cataract is variable, but an
acutely opaci¯ÇØed lens is sometimes seen in early childhood.

A

C

## Lentiglobus

Lentiglobus is a very rare, usually unilateral, generalized hemispherical deformity of the lens. It may be associated with posterior
polar opacity.

## Microspherophakia and microphakia

¯Ç×e lens is small and spherical in microspherophakia (Fig. 10.31F),
which may be seen as an isolated familial (dominant) abnormality,
or in association with a number of systemic conditions including
Marfan and WeillÔÇôMarchesani syndromes, hyperlysinaemia and
congenital rubella. Ocular associations include Peters anomaly
and familial ectopia lentis et pupillae. Complications can include
lenticular myopia, subluxation and dislocation. Microphakia (Fig.
10.31G) is the term used for a lens with a smaller than normal
diameter. It may be found in isolation, but may also occur in
patients with Lowe syndrome.

## Coloboma

¯Ç×is is characterized by congenital indentation of the lens periphery (Fig. 10.31H) and occurs as a result of localized zonular de¯ÇØciency. It is not a true coloboma, as there is no focal absence of a
tissue layer due to failure of closure of the optic ¯ÇØssure. Occasionally a lens coloboma is associated with a coloboma of the iris or
fundus.

346 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

## Aqueous production

Aqueous humour is produced from plasma by the ciliary epithelium of the ciliary body pars plicata, using a combination of
active and passive secretion. A high-protein ¯Ç×ltrate passes out of
fenestrated capillaries (ultra¯Ç×ltration) into the stroma of the ciliary processes, from which active transport of solutes occurs across
the dual-layered ciliary epithelium. ¯ÇØe osmotic gradient thereby
established facilitates the passive ¯Ç£ow of water into the posterior
chamber. Secretion is subject to the in¯Ç£uence of the sympathetic
nervous system, with opposing actions mediated by beta-2 receptors (increased secretion) and alpha-2 receptors (decreased secretion). Enzymatic action is also critical ÔÇô carbonic anhydrase is
among those playing a key role.

Aqueous out¯Ç£ow

## Anatomy

¯Çü ¯Çƒe trabecular meshwork (trabeculum) is a sieve-like structure (Fig. 11.1) at the angle of the anterior chamber (AC)
through which 90% of aqueous humour leaves the eye. It has
three components (Fig. 11.2).

{ ¯ÇØe uveal meshwork is the innermost portion, consisting
of cord-like endothelial cell-covered strands arising from
the iris and ciliary body stroma. ¯ÇØe intertrabecular spaces
are relatively large and o¯Çøer little resistance to the passage
of aqueous.

{ ¯ÇØe corneoscleral meshwork lies external to the uveal
meshwork to form the thickest portion of the trabeculum.

## It is composed of layers of connective tissue strands with

overlying endothelial-like cells. ¯ÇØe intertrabecular spaces
are smaller than those of the uveal meshwork, conferring
greater resistance to ¯Ç£ow.

meshwork.

¯ÇØB¯Ç× corneoscleral meshwork; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× Schwalbe line; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× Schlemm
canal; ¯ÇØE¯Ç× connector channels; ¯ÇØF¯Ç× longitudinal muscle of the
ciliary body; ¯ÇØG¯Ç× scleral spur.

¯Çü Trabecular out¯Ç£ow (90%): aqueous ¯Ç£ows through the trabeculum into the Schlemm canal and then the episcleral veins.
¯ÇØis is a bulk ¯Ç£ow pressure-sensitive route so that increasing
intraocular pressure will increase out¯Ç£ow.
¯Çü Uveoscleral drainage (10%): aqueous passes across the face of
the ciliary body into the suprachoroidal space and is drained by
the venous circulation in the ciliary body, choroid and sclera.
¯Çü Iris: some aqueous also drains via the iris.

## Intraocular pressure

Intraocular pressure (IOP) is determined by the balance between
the rate of aqueous production and the rate of aqueous out¯Ç£ow. ¯ÇØe latter is related to factors that include the resistance
encountered in the trabeculum and the level of episcleral venous
pressure.

{ ¯ÇØe juxtacanalicular (cribriform) meshwork is the outer
part of the trabeculum and links the corneoscleral meshwork with the endothelium of the inner wall of the Schlemm canal. It consists of cells embedded in a dense
extracellular matrix with narrow intercellular spaces and
o¯Çøers the major proportion of normal resistance to aqueous out¯Ç£ow.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe Schlemm canal is a circumferential channel within the
perilimbal sclera. ¯ÇØe inner wall is lined by irregular spindleshaped endothelial cells containing infoldings (giant vacuoles)
that are thought to convey aqueous via the formation of transcellular pores. ¯ÇØe outer wall is lined by smooth ¯Ç£at cells and
contains the openings of collector channels, which leave the
canal at oblique angles and connect directly or indirectly with
episcleral veins. Septa commonly divide the lumen into 2ÔÇô4
channels.

## Physiology

Aqueous ¯Ç£ows from the posterior chamber via the pupil into the
AC, from where it exits the eye via three routes (Fig. 11.3).

C

A
B

## Concept of normal intraocular pressure

¯ÇØe upper range of normal for adults is 21 mmHg on applanation
tonometry (see below). Some individuals develop glaucomatous
damage with IOP less than 21 mmHg, but others remain unscathed
with IOP well above this level. Whilst reduction of IOP is a key
modi¯Ç×able element in all types of glaucoma, additional incompletely understood factors are critical in determining whether a
particular individual or eye develops glaucomatous damage. ¯ÇØese
include factors a¯Çøecting the susceptibility of the optic nerve to
damage, such as the integrity of its blood supply and structural
vulnerability to mechanical stress at the optic nerve head.

## Fluctuation

Normal IOP varies with time of day (diurnal variation), heartbeat,
blood pressure and respiration. ¯ÇØe diurnal pattern varies, with
a tendency to be higher in the morning and lower in the a¯ÇÜernoon and evening. ¯ÇØis is at least partially due to a diurnal pattern in aqueous production, which is lower at night. Glaucomatous
eyes exhibit greater than normal ¯Ç£uctuation, the extent of which
is directly proportional to the likelihood of progressive visual
¯Ç×eld damage and a single reading may therefore be misleading.

## It is good practice to note the time of day in conjunction with a

recorded IOP.

Measurement of IOP

OCULAR HYPERTENSION

De¯Çƒnition

In the general population the mean IOP is 16 mmHg. Two standard deviations on either side of this provides a ÔÇÿnormalÔÇÖ IOP
range for individuals over the age of 40 years of 11ÔÇô21 mmHg. ¯ÇØe
distribution is Gaussian with the curve skewed to the right (Fig.
11.4). ¯ÇØe upper range of normal in individuals over 70 years is 23
mmHg. It is estimated that 4ÔÇô7% of the population over the age of

detectable glaucomatous damage. ¯ÇØese individuals are referred to
as having ÔÇÿocular hypertensionÔÇÖ (OHT).
Approximately 1 in 10 will develop glaucoma over a 10year period and the majority will not develop glaucoma in their
lifetime.

Risk factors for developing glaucoma in OHT

¯ÇØe Ocular Hypertension Treatment Study (OHTS) was a multicentre longitudinal trial. In addition to looking at the e¯Çøect of
treatment of individuals with ocular hypertension (IOP in one eye
between 24 mmHg and 32 mmHg), invaluable information was
gained about the e¯Çøect of a range of putative risks for conversion
from OHT to glaucoma. ¯ÇØe percentage of OHT patients likely to
develop glaucoma at 6 years taking key factors into account is set
out in Tables 11.1 and 11.2
¯Çü Limitations include the following: the study goal was an IOP
reduction of 20%, which may not be su¯ÇÖcient in some individuals, compliance with medication was not measured and there
is a possibility that early glaucomatous damage was already
present in some patients.

variate analysis

increases with increasing IOP.

{ Age. Older age is associated with greater risk.

## Genetics of ocular hypertension

A single nucleotide polymorphism in TMC01 appears to be signi¯Ç×cantly associated with conversion to glaucoma in White people
with ocular hypertension (three-fold risk in individuals with two
risk alleles compared with those with no risk alleles). A recent
meta-analysis has identi¯Ç×ed 112 genetic loci associated with IOP
and suggests a major role for angiopoietin-receptor tyrosine kinase
signalling, lipid metabolism, mitochondrial function and developmental processes as risk factors for elevated IOP.

## Clinical evaluation

## History and examination should be carried out as for glaucoma

(see below). Consideration should be given to whether any systemic medication is being taken that might be in¯Ç£uencing IOP,
either upwards (e.g. steroids) or downwards (e.g. beta-blockers).

Pre-perimetric glaucoma

¯ÇØis concept refers to glaucomatous damage, usually manifested
by a suspicious optic disc and/or the presence of retinal nerve ¯Ç×bre
layer defects, in which no visual ¯Ç×eld abnormality has developed.
¯ÇØe ¯Ç×eld-testing modality for this purpose is usually taken as standard achromatic automated perimetry.

## Management

Raised IOP is an important risk factor in the development of
glaucoma and can be modi¯Ç×ed with treatment. However, it is
di¯ÇÖcult to justify the treatment of all individuals with raised
IOP because of the high prevalence of ocular hypertension, the
low conversion rate to glaucoma and the cost and side e¯Çøects of
treatment. In the OHTS, untreated patients with ocular hypertension had a 9.5% cumulative risk of developing primary openangle glaucoma (POAG) a¯ÇÜer 5 years. Treatment (which aimed
to reduce IOP by 20% or more and to reach 24 mmHg or less)
reduced this to 4.4%.
¯Çü Validated prediction models using the ocular parameters of
a given individual with ocular hypertension allow the 5-year
risk of developing glaucoma to be estimated and expressed as a
percentage. ¯ÇØis helps in deciding on the frequency of testing
and whether to start treatment.
¯Çü In general, only those at high risk should be treated, although
patient preference may be a decisive factor.
¯Çü Age, and therefore life expectancy, is a key point to consider.
¯Çü Most practitioners would treat every patient with an IOP of 30
mmHg or more (>40% 5-year risk of glaucoma). ¯ÇØe decision
to treat in patients with varying risk pro¯Ç×les is not straightforward and has to be made on an individual basis.
¯Çü OHT increases the risk of retinal venous occlusion, an additional point to consider when deciding whether to start
treatment.
¯Çü Treatment options are the same as for POAG, although a less
aggressive pressure-lowering approach is frequently taken.
¯Çü Careful monitoring is a reasonable alternative in many circumstances: baseline visual ¯Ç×elds and retinal nerve ¯Ç×bre layer
(RNFL)/disc imaging should be performed.

OVERVIEW OF GLAUCOMA

De¯Çƒnition

## Glaucoma is the term that is used to describe a group of conditions that have in common a chronic progressive optic neuropathy that results in characteristic morphological changes at the

optic nerve head and in the retinal nerve fibre layer. Progressive
retinal ganglion cell death and visual field loss are associated
with these changes. Intraocular pressure is a key modifiable
factor.

## Goal of treatment

¯ÇØe goal of glaucoma treatment is to slow the rate of progression
throughout a patientÔÇÖs lifetime in order to maintain visual function
and related quality of life, at a sustainable cost.

Classi¯Çƒcation

Glaucoma may be congenital (developmental) or acquired. Openangle and angle-closure types are distinguished based on the
mechanism by which aqueous out¯Ç£ow is impaired with respect to
the AC angle con¯Ç×guration. Distinction is also made between primary and secondary glaucoma. In the latter a recognizable ocular
or non-ocular disorder contributes to elevation of IOP.

## Epidemiology

Glaucoma a¯Çøects 2ÔÇô3% of people over the age of 40 years, but up to
50% may be undiagnosed. It is the second leading cause of blindness in the world. POAG is the most common form in White, Hispanic/Latino and Black individuals (with the prevalence especially
high in the latter). On a worldwide basis, primary angle closure
(PAC) constitutes up to half of cases and has a particularly high
prevalence in people of Asian descent. With improved assessment
such as the routine performance of gonioscopy in a darkened
rather than a bright environment, PAC is more prevalent in White
people than previously realized.

Natural history of open-angle glaucoma

¯ÇØe Early Manifest Glaucoma Trial (EMGT) provides prospective
natural history data on progression of glaucoma in three common
glaucoma types: high tension glaucoma (HTG), normal-tension
glaucoma (NTG) and pseudoexfoliation glaucoma (PXEG), using
change in the visual ¯Ç×eld and optic disc as end-points. Without
treatment to reduce the IOP the mean rate of change in each group
is as follows: HTG ÔêÆ1.31 dB/year, NTG ÔêÆ0.36 dB/year, PXEG
ÔêÆ3.13 dB/year. Large di¯Çøerences exist among individuals and different glaucoma types, but based on the mean rate of change in
those untreated, visual function in an average glaucoma patient
appears to deteriorate from normal to blindness over a period of
approximately 25 years. ¯ÇØis is in¯Ç£uenced signi¯Ç×cantly by using
treatment to reduce IOP. In patients with glaucoma under routine
care, approximately 1 in 8 eyes exhibit a change of mean deviation
(MD) of more than 1dB/year and the same proportion also show
this in the central 10 [o] region. When seeking to prevent functional
disability it is important to also assess progressive central visual
¯Ç×eld loss in patients with glaucoma.

PRIMARY OPEN-ANGLE GLAUCOMA

## Introduction

De¯Çƒnition

Primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) is a chronic, progressive
optic neuropathy of adult onset. It is characterized by:
¯Çü Retinal nerve ¯Ç×bre layer thinning.
¯Çü Glaucomatous optic nerve damage.
¯Çü Characteristic visual ¯Ç×eld loss as damage progresses.
¯Çü An open anterior chamber angle.
¯Çü Absence of signs of secondary glaucoma or a non-glaucomatous
cause for the optic neuropathy.
¯Çü IOP is a key modi¯Ç×able risk factor.
POAG is the most prevalent type of glaucoma in people of
European and African ethnic origin. In those older than 70 years
of age the prevalence of POAG has been reported to be approximately 6% in White populations, 16% in Black populations and
around 3% in Asian populations. It a¯Çøects both genders equally.

## Risk factors

¯Çü IOP. ¯ÇØe higher the IOP, the greater the likelihood of glaucoma.
Asymmetry of IOP of 4 mmHg or more is also signi¯Ç×cant.
¯Çü Age. POAG is more common in older individuals.

350 Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma

¯Çü Race. It is signi¯Ç×cantly (perhaps four times) more common,
develops at an earlier age and may be more di¯ÇÖcult to control
in Black people than in White people.
¯Çü Family history of POAG. First-degree relatives of patients
with POAG are at increased risk. An approximate risk to siblings is four times and to o¯Çøspring twice the normal population risk, though surveyed ¯Ç×gures vary.
¯Çü Diabetes mellitus. Longitudinal studies show no increased
risk of glaucoma. Selection bias probably explains why clinicbased studies report a higher prevalence of glaucoma in people
with diabetes.
¯Çü Myopia is associated with an increased incidence of POAG
and myopic eyes may be more susceptible to glaucomatous
damage.
¯Çü Anti¯Çá VEGF (vascular endothelial growth factor) therapy.
Patients undergoing anti-VEGF therapy for age-related
macular degeneration or diabetic macular oedema are
at risk of sustained IOP elevation. This is more likely to
occur after recurrent injections with bevacizumab than
with ranibizumab. The risk is significantly greater for
patients with glaucoma than for normal individuals. The
risk that glaucoma surgery will be needed increases after
six injections.
¯Çü Systemic calcium¯Çá channel blockers. Meta-analysis of large
population-based studies shows a higher prevalence of glaucoma in people using this medication as monotherapy.
¯Çü Vascular disease. A range of systemic conditions linked to
vascular compromise may be associated, though clear-cut
relationships have proved di¯ÇÖcult to demonstrate consistently.
Systemic hypertension, cardiovascular disease, diabetes and
vasospastic conditions such as migraine have all been implicated. Poor ocular perfusion may be a risk factor for glaucoma
progression.
¯Çü Translaminar pressure gradient. Studies suggest that a di¯Çøerence in the levels of IOP and orbital CSF pressure may increase
the likelihood of the development and progression of glaucomatous damage, perhaps due to associated deformation of the
lamina cribrosa.
¯Çü Optic disc area. Large discs are more vulnerable to damage.
¯Çü Ocular perfusion pressure is the di¯Çøerence between the arterial BP and the IOP and has been shown in population studies
to be linked to increased risk for the development and progression of glaucoma. A low ocular perfusion pressure increases
the risk.

## Genetics

¯Çü Loci. POAG has been associated with at least 127 loci in the
human genome (Nature Communications 2021). Most loci
have broadly consistent e¯Çøects in all races.
¯Çü Mutations in only the MYOC gene, coding for the protein
myocilin that is found in the trabecular meshwork and
the OPTN gene, which codes for optineurin, are broadly
accepted as causing glaucoma. A number of different mutations have been described in the MYOC gene, though the
normal function of myocilin and its role in glaucoma is as
yet undetermined.

¯Çü Family history. If a single family member develops glaucoma
prior to age 35 years, the chances of a mutation in the myocilin
gene may be as high as 33%.Genetic investigation of a patient
and family may be considered if three or more ¯Ç×rst-degree
relatives from two generations are a¯Çøected, or for research
purposes.
¯Çü A polygenetic risk score (PRS) has been developed that can
predict glaucoma susceptibility and the risk of developing
advanced disease. It is possible that in time the PRS will result
in a personalized approach for earlier treatment of high-risk
individuals, with less intensive monitoring and treatment
being possible for those at low risk.

## Pathogenesis of glaucomatous optic
neuropathy

## Retinal ganglion cell death in glaucoma occurs predominantly

through apoptosis (programmed cell death) rather than necrosis.
¯ÇØe preterminal event is calcium ion in¯Ç£ux into the cell body and
an increase in intracellular nitric oxide. Glutamine metabolism
is intrinsically involved. A¯ÇÜer initial injury, a cascade of events
results in astrocyte and glial cell proliferation and alterations in
the extracellular matrix of the lamina cribrosa, with subsequent
optic nerve head remodelling. ¯ÇØe process of glaucomatous damage and the relationship with IOP and other potential in¯Ç£uences is
still poorly understood. One or both of the following mechanisms
may be involved:
¯Çü Direct mechanical damage to retinal nerve ¯Ç×bres at the
optic nerve head, perhaps as they pass through the lamina
cribrosa. Accumulating evidence of the in¯Ç£uence of mechanical deformability in the region of the lamina cribrosa supports this.
¯Çü Ischaemic damage, possibly due to compression of blood
vessels supplying the optic nerve head. This may relate
to ocular perfusion pressure as a possible risk factor for
glaucoma.
¯Çü Common pathways of damage. Both mechanisms might lead
to a reduction in axoplasmic ¯Ç£ow, interference with the delivery of nutrients or removal of metabolic products, deprivation
of neuronal growth factors, oxidative injury and the initiation
of immune-mediated damage.

## Screening

Universal population screening for glaucoma has not been demonstrated to be cost-e¯Çøective and current practice restricts case¯Ç×nding to high-risk groups, such as: (a) older individuals, (b) those
over the age of 40 with a history of POAG in a close family member and (c) people of African racial background. In these groups,
screening tends to be performed sporadically via routes such as
commercial optometric eye examinations, which may lead to the
relative exclusion of underprivileged economic groups. Population screening with tonometry alone is unsatisfactory, since it will
label as normal a signi¯Ç×cant number of cases with other features of
POAG such as cupping and visual ¯Ç×eld loss, and routine screening
eye examinations should include visual ¯Ç×eld assessment as well as
tonometry and ophthalmoscopy.

## Diagnosis

## History

## Evaluation of the optic nerve head

## Normal optic nerve head

¯Çü Visual symptoms will usually be absent, unless damage is
advanced. Sometimes symptomatic central ¯Ç×eld defects may
occur at an early stage, in the presence of a relatively normal
peripheral ¯Ç×eld.
¯Çü Previous ophthalmic history. Speci¯Ç×c enquiry should be
made about:

{ Refractive status, as myopia carries an increased risk of
POAG and hypermetropia of primary angle-closure glaucoma (PACG).

{ Causes of secondary glaucoma such as ocular trauma or
in¯Ç£ammation. Previous eye surgery, including refractive
surgery which may a¯Çøect IOP readings.
¯Çü Family history

{ POAG or related conditions such as OHT.

{ Other ocular disease in family members.
¯Çü Past medical history

{ Asthma, heart failure or block, peripheral vascular disease,
which are contraindications to the use of beta-blockers.

{ Head injury, intracranial pathology including stroke, as
these conditions may cause optic atrophy or visual ¯Ç×eld
defects.

{ Vasospasm: migraine and Raynaud phenomenon.

{ Diabetes, systemic hypertension and cardiovascular disease may increase the risk of POAG.

{ Oral contraceptive pill for several years may be associated
with an increased risk of glaucoma.
¯Çü Current medication

{ Steroids, including skin cream and inhalants.

{ Oral beta-blockers may lower IOP.

{ Social history, including smoking and alcohol intake,
especially if toxic/nutritional optic neuropathy is
suspected.

{ Allergies, particularly to any drugs likely to be used in
glaucoma treatment, e.g. acetazolamide is contraindicated
if there is a history of sulfonamide allergy.

¯Çü Neuroretinal rim. ¯ÇØe neuroretinal rim (NRR) is the orangepink tissue between the outer edge of the cup and the optic
disc margin. ¯ÇØe inferior rim is the broadest followed by the
superior, nasal and temporal (the ÔÇÿISNTÔÇÖ rule). ¯ÇØis has high
sensitivity (81%) for glaucoma but is not speci¯Ç×c (32%), i.e.
eyes without glaucoma o¯ÇÜen do not respect the rule.
¯Çü Cup/disc (C/D) ratio. ¯ÇØe C/D ratio indicates the diameter of the cup expressed as a fraction of the diameter of the
disc. ¯ÇØe vertical rather than the horizontal ratio is generally taken (Fig. 11.6A). It is di¯ÇÖcult to estimate the C/D
ratio accurately without imaging. As a basic principle, small
diameter optic discs have small cups (Fig. 11.6B) and large
diameter discs have large cups (Fig. 11.6C). Only 2% of the
population have a C/D ratio greater than 0.7. In any individual, asymmetry of 0.2 or more between the eyes should also
be regarded with suspicion, though it is critical to exclude a
corresponding di¯Çøerence in overall disc diameter (Fig. 11.7A
and B).
¯Çü Optic disc size. Optic disc size is important in deciding if a
C/D ratio is normal and is also a prognostic indicator. Large
discs are believed to be more likely to sustain damage, particularly in NTG. ¯ÇØis may be the result of the larger diameter
conferring relative mechanical weakness and hence greater
vulnerability to IOP-induced displacement of the lamina
cribrosa, which has been found to be thinner in eyes with
NTG. Disc size varies on average between racial groups and
is largest in those of African descent. Imaging can objectively measure disc area, but vertical diameter is the parameter most frequently used clinically. Normal median vertical
diameter (for non-glaucomatous discs) is 1.5ÔÇô1.7 mm in a
white population.

{ A narrow slit beam is focused on the disc using a fundus
lens.

{ ¯ÇØe height of the beam is adjusted until it matches the
distance between the superior and inferior limits of the
NRR (not the scleral rim surrounding the neural tissue)
and the diameter in millimetres is read from the slit lamp
graticule.

## Examination

¯Çü Visual acuity (VA) is likely to be normal except in advanced
glaucoma.
¯Çü Pupils. Exclude a relative a¯Çøerent pupillary defect (RAPD). If
initially absent but develops later, this constitutes an indicator
of substantial progression.
¯Çü Colour vision assessment such as Ishihara chart testing
if there is any suggestion of an optic neuropathy other than
glaucoma.
¯Çü Slit lamp examination. Exclude features of secondary glaucoma such as pigmentary and pseudoexfoliation.
¯Çü Tonometry prior to pachymetry, noting the time of day.
¯Çü Gonioscopy.
¯Çü Optic disc examination for glaucomatous changes (see below)
should be performed with the pupils dilated, provided gonioscopy does not show critically narrow angles. Red-free light can
be used to detect RNFL defects.

{ A correction factor may be necessary, depending on the
lens used (Table 11.3). Refractive error a¯Çøects measurement only minimally, although myopia above ÔêÆ8 dioptres
may distort the result.
¯Çü Inter¯Çá eye disc asymmetry. A vertical cup/disc ratio asymmetry of 0.2 or more is found in about 5% of normal individuals
and in about 25% of those with glaucoma. However, asymmetry of the vertical cup/disc ratio alone is not useful in identifying patients with glaucoma. When comparing the two discs, a
di¯Çøerence in the appearance of the neuroretinal rim is a good
sign of early glaucoma (Fig. 11.7 CÔÇôF).

TIP A large disc has a large cup and may be entirely healthy,
while any cupping in a small disc may be abnormal.

352 Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma

A

and arrow heads show edge of optic disc); (B¯Ç× small disc with a
low cup/disc ratio; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× large disc with a proportionally larger cup.

## Changes in glaucoma

In some cases, it is not possible to be certain whether an individual optic disc is glaucomatous. ¯ÇØe clinical ¯Ç×ndings and results of
investigation should be considered together to guide management.

Glaucomatous damage results in characteristic signs involving (a)
the optic nerve head, (b) the peripapillary area and (c) the retinal
nerve ¯Ç×bre layer.
¯Çü Optic nerve head. Pathological cupping is caused by an irreversible decrease in the number of nerve ¯Ç×bres, glial cells and
blood vessels. A documented increase in cup size is always signi¯Ç×cant (Fig. 11.8). If an eye with a small optic disc and correspondingly small cup develops glaucoma, the cup will increase
in size, but even in the presence of substantial damage may still
be smaller than that of a large physiological cup.
¯Çü Subtypes of glaucomatous damage. Four morphological glaucomatous disc appearances have been described and although
the majority of discs are unclassi¯Ç×able the descriptions encompass a useful overview of patterns of glaucomatous damage
and may provide clues to underlying pathological processes.

{ Focal ischaemic discs (Fig. 11.9A) are characterized by
localized superior and/or inferior notching and are associated with localized ¯Ç×eld defects with early threat to ¯Ç×xation.

{ Myopic disc with glaucoma (Fig. 11.9B) refers to a tilted
(obliquely inserted), shallow disc with a temporal crescent of parapapillary atrophy, together with features of
glaucomatous damage. Dense superior or inferior scotomas threatening ¯Ç×xation are common. ¯ÇØis morphology is
most common in younger male patients.

{ Sclerotic discs (Fig. 11.9C) are characterized by a shallow,
saucerized cup and a gently sloping NRR, variable peripapillary atrophy and peripheral visual ¯Ç×eld loss. ¯ÇØe peripapillary choroid is thinner than in other disc types. Patients
are older, of either gender and there is an association with
systemic vascular disease.

{ Concentrically enlarging discs (veri¯Ç×ed by serial monitoring) are characterized by fairly uniform NRR thinning
(Fig. 11.9D) and are frequently associated with di¯Çøuse
visual ¯Ç×eld loss. IOP is usually signi¯Ç×cantly elevated at
presentation.
¯Çü Non¯Çá speci¯Ç×c signs of glaucomatous damage. Other disc
signs of glaucomatous damage include:

{ Disc haemorrhages (Fig. 11.10A and B) o¯ÇÜen extend from
the NRR onto the retina, most commonly inferotemporally. ¯ÇØeir presence is a risk factor for the development
and progression of glaucoma. ¯ÇØey are more common in
NTG but can also occur in healthy individuals as well as
patients with systemic vascular disease. When screening
for glaucoma, approximately three out of four people with
a disc haemorrhage will not have glaucoma.

¯ÇØC¯Ç× baring of inferior circumlinear blood vessel (arrow); ¯ÇØD¯Ç× bayoneting of superior and inferior

damage.

356 Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma

{ Baring of circumlinear blood vessels is a sign of early thinning of the NRR. It is characterized by a space between
the neuroretinal rim and a super¯Ç×cial blood vessel (Fig.
11.10C).

{ Bayoneting is characterized by double angulation of a
blood vessel. With NRR loss, a vessel entering the disc
from the retina may angle sharply backwards into the disc
and then turn towards its original direction to run across
the lamina cribrosa (Fig. 11.10D).

{ Collaterals between two veins at the disc (Fig. 11.10E),
similar to those following central retinal vein occlusion
(CRVO), are relatively uncommon. ¯ÇØey are probably
caused by chronic low-grade circulatory obstruction. Retinal vascular tortuosity may also occur.

{ Loss of nasal NRR (Fig. 11.10F) is a sign of moderately
advanced damage. A space may develop between the NRR
and the central retinal vasculature.

{ ¯ÇØe laminar dot sign occurs in advancing glaucoma. Grey
dot-like fenestrations in the lamina cribrosa (see Fig.
11.10F) become exposed as the NRR recedes. ¯ÇØe fenestrations sometimes appear linear and this itself may be a sign
of advanced damage, indicating distortion of the lamina.
¯ÇØe dots may be seen in normal eyes.

{ Sharpened edgeÔÇÖ or ÔÇÿsharpened rimÔÇÖ is a sign of advancing
damage. As NRR is lost adjacent to the edge of the disc,
the disc margin contour assumes a sharper angle backwards. Bayoneting of vessels is o¯ÇÜen seen at a sharpened
edge (see Fig. 11.10 D). ¯ÇØis should not be confused with
a ÔÇÿsharpened nasal polar edgeÔÇÖ, which refers to the sharp
angulation of the NRR at the nasal margin of a focal vertical polar notch.

TIP An optic disc haemorrhage is a risk factor for the
development and progression of glaucoma and can be missed
unless magni¯Çøcation is used to examine the disc.

beta zone (black arrow).

¯Çü Peripapillary changes. Peripapillary atrophy (PPA) surrounding the optic nerve head may be of signi¯Ç×cance in glaucoma
(Fig. 11.11) and may be a sign of early damage in patients with
ocular hypertension.

{ Alpha (outer) zone is characterized by super¯Ç×cial retinal
pigment epithelial changes. It tends to be larger and possibly more common in glaucomatous eyes.

{ Fibres arising from the macula follow a straight course to
the optic nerve head, forming a spindle-shaped area (papillomacular bundle).

{ Fibres arising from the nasal retina also follow a relatively
straight course to the optic nerve.

{ Fibres arising temporal to the macula follow an arcuate path around the papillomacular bundle to reach
the optic nerve head. They do not cross the horizontal raphe that extends from the foveola to the temporal
retinal periphery, demarcating the superior and inferior
halves of the retina.

{ ¯ÇØe arcuate ¯Ç×bres reaching the superotemporal and
inferotemporal aspect of the optic nerve head are most
vulnerable to glaucomatous damage. ¯ÇØe ¯Ç×bres in the
papillomacular bundle are the most resistant.
Within the optic nerve head, the retinal ¯Ç×bres are arranged as
follows (Fig. 11.12B):
¯Çü Fibres from the peripheral fundus lie deep within the retinal
nerve ¯Ç×bre layer (i.e. nearer the pigment epithelium) but occupy
the most peripheral (super¯Ç×cial) portion of the optic nerve.
¯Çü Fibres arising near to the optic nerve lie super¯Ç×cially within
the nerve ¯Ç×bre layer (i.e. near the vitreous) but they occupy the
central (deep) portion of the optic nerve.
¯Çü In glaucoma subtle RNFL defects precede the development of
detectable optic disc and visual ¯Ç×eld changes. ¯ÇØeir onset o¯ÇÜen
follows disc haemorrhages. Two patterns occur: (a) localized
wedge-shaped defects and (b) di¯Çøuse defects that are larger
and have indistinct borders. Defects are sometimes evident
following disc haemorrhages. Red-free (green) light increases
the contrast between normal retina and defects on slit lamp
biomicroscopy or fundus photography and typically makes
identi¯Ç×cation easier (Fig.11.13). Optical coherence tomography (OCT) and scanning laser polarimetry are highly e¯Çøective
means of quantifying the RNFL (see Fig. 11.16 and 11.17). It
should be noted that RNFL defects are not speci¯Ç×c to glaucoma and can be seen in a range of neurological disease, as well
as in apparently normal individuals.

{ Beta (inner) zone is characterized by chorioretinal atrophy. It is distinct from the scleral rim, the white band of
exposed sclera central to the beta zone. ¯ÇØe beta zone is
larger and more common in glaucoma and is a risk factor
for progression. ¯ÇØe location of beta-zone PPA seems to
indicate the orientation of likely visual ¯Ç×eld loss.
¯Çü Retinal nerve ¯Ç×bre layer. An understanding of the distribution of the 1.2 million ganglion cell axons as they pass across
the retina to enter the scleral canal to form the optic nerve
head (optic disc) is key to the interpretation of visual ¯Ç×eld loss
in relation to optic nerve cupping in glaucoma. Within the
retina the arrangement is as follows (Fig. 11.12A):

## Imaging in glaucoma

## Imaging has become an indispensable tool in the management

of patients with glaucoma. However, it is important to remember
that in order to determine a diagnosis or to assess change, imaging should always be used to supplement clinical examination and
visual ¯Ç×eld results, rather than as the sole investigation.
¯Çü Pachymetry, the measurement of corneal thickness, is an
essential part of the assessment of glaucoma patients (see

¯Çü Stereo disc photography. Stereo photography has historically
been regarded as the reference standard in optic disc imaging and remains a valuable option. ¯ÇØe images are taken by

repositioning slightly between shots, either manually or using
a stereo separator built into the camera.
¯Çü Ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM). ¯ÇØis is a type of ultrasound that creates a more detailed image than regular ultrasound and is used for imaging the anterior segment of the
eye. ¯ÇØis results in resolutions up to 25 ╬╝m axially and 50 ╬╝m
laterally, but the depth of tissue penetration is less than this.

## It is particularly useful to show the anatomy in patients with

primary angle-closure glaucoma (Fig. 11.14). In comparison
to anterior segment OCT, the main strength of UBM is its ability to demonstrate structures behind the iris, particularly the
ciliary body and lens.
¯Çü Optical coherence tomography of the anterior segment.

## Anterior segment optical coherence tomography has an

expanding range of clinical applications primarily in the evaluation of angle-closure glaucoma. It is useful when demonstrating the relationship of the peripheral iris to the ¯Ç×ltration angle
(Fig. 11.15).

¯Çü Anterior chamber depth measurement. Objective measurement of the depth of the AC is often clinically useful
in glaucoma management. Indications include assessment
of PAC risk and monitoring of progression in conditions
where the AC is shallow, such as post-trabeculectomy
hypotony and ciliolenticular block. Older methods used a
slit lamp with or without a special attachment, but an accurate and repeatable measurement can be obtained using
OCT, ultrasonographic or optical interferometric methods
(e.g. Zeiss IOLMaster).
¯Çü Optical coherence tomography of the posterior pole. OCT
is most useful when the patient is either a glaucoma suspect
or has earlyÔÇômoderate disease (Fig. 11.16). It can be used
to detect damage and progression (Fig. 11.17). In advanced
glaucoma it is less helpful because of the ÔÇÿfloorÔÇÖ effect (i.e.
when the retinal nerve fibre layer reaches 45ÔÇô50 ╬╝m it does
not decrease any further even though progressive damage
may be occurring). Macular thickness measurements may
be more helpful in these circumstances. Sensitivity and
specificity utilizing comparison with a normative database
is as high as 90%.

{ Peripapillary RNFL. ¯ÇØis involves the acquisition of a
circular scan of the retina around the optic nerve head.
Because di¯Çøerent OCT devices use di¯Çøerent scanning
protocols, caution should be taken when comparing
RNFL thickness between machines. By using the Bruch
membrane opening rather than the optic disc margin,
centration of the image can be enhanced. ¯ÇØe results are
compared to a colour-coded normative database. When
assessing structural changes over time, it is o¯ÇÜen di¯ÇÖcult to distinguish between glaucomatous change and
measurement variability or age-related structural loss.
Up to 4 ╬╝m inter-test ¯Ç£uctuation has been reported. A
new defect, widening of an established defect or deepening of a defect are important features of glaucomatous
change.

{ Optic nerve head. Radial cross-sectional scans permit an
objective and repeatable assessment of disc morphology,
with reasonable discriminatory value. ¯ÇØis function is less
commonly used than RNFL.

{ Ganglion cell complex (GCC) analysis involves measurement of the thickness of the RNFL, the ganglion cell layer
and the inner plexiform layer at the macula in order to
detect early glaucomatous damage and avoids the confounding e¯Çøect of optic disc anatomical variation. ¯ÇØis is
as e¯Çøective for diagnosing glaucoma and assessing progression as analysis of the RNFL. OCT signal quality is
good, particularly in the elderly and diseased eye. It should
be considered supplementary to RNFL assessment (Figs
11.18 and 11.19).

{ Progression analysis so¯ÇÜware has been introduced on several
machines and provides a computed assessment of the extent
of damage over time. Trend-based analysis using a number of
scans, measures the shape of change and is particularly useful
when con¯Ç×rming that progressive change has occurred.

TIP OCT imaging of the optic disc should not be used in
isolation but rather to supplement the ¯Çøndings of clinical
examination.

¯Çü Confocal scanning laser ophthalmoscopy. ¯ÇØis employs
a scanning laser ophthalmoscope (SLO) to build a threedimensional image of the optic nerve head and retina.

{ ¯ÇØe Heidelberg Retinal Tomograph (HRT) is still in widespread clinical practice, but has been superseded by optical
coherence tomography. As with the OCT, it is used to distinguish normal from glaucomatous eyes by comparison
against a normative database (Moor¯Ç×elds regression analysis) and to monitor disease progression.

{ Keratometry values must be entered and signi¯Ç×cant (>1.0
dioptre) astigmatism corrected by means of a cylindrical lens. High-quality images can usually be acquired
without pupillary dilatation and through mildÔÇômoderate
lens opacity. A¯ÇÜer image capture, for greatest accuracy
the operator should manually mark the contour line that
de¯Ç×nes the edge of the neuroretinal rim.

{ Images, data and analysis can be examined on a computer
screen or print-out (Fig. 11.20).

{ Detailed stereometric data are presented, with abnormal
readings identi¯Ç×ed.
¯Çü Scanning laser polarimetry. ¯ÇØe GDx (Glaucoma Diagnosis)
VCC (variable corneal compensation) RNFL analyser assesses
the nerve ¯Ç×bre layer thickness by using its ÔÇÿbirefringentÔÇÖ
(resolving or splitting a light wave into two unequally re¯Ç£ected
or transmitted waves) nature to change the polarization of
incident polarized diode laser light. ¯ÇØe amount of alteration
is directly related to the thickness of the layer.

{ A display provides colour images of the optic nerve head,
together with RNFL maps in four quadrants. Deviation
maps show the location and magnitude of RNFL defects as
tiny colour-coded squares and parameters for each eye are
displayed in a table (Fig. 11.21).

{ A global value based on the entire thickness map is the optimal parameter for discriminating normal from glaucoma.

{ ¯ÇØis technology has been superseded in recent years by
OCT.

TIP Imaging has become an indispensable tool in the
management of patients with glaucoma.

366 Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma

Visual ¯Çøeld defects ¯ÇØsee Ch. 1, perimetry¯Ç×

Nerve damage in glaucoma is believed to be in¯Ç£icted at the optic
nerve head and the resultant visual ¯Ç×eld defect corresponds to the
pattern of ¯Ç×bres in the retinal area served. Standard automated
perimetry (SAP) is relatively insensitive in the early stages of the
disease.
¯Çü Early changes include increased variability of responses in
areas that subsequently develop defects and slight asymmetry
between the two eyes. Special modalities such as frequency
doubling technology (FDT) and short wavelength automated

perimetry (SWAP) may demonstrate defects at an earlier
stage.
¯Çü Small paracentral depressions (Fig. 11.22A) can form at a
relatively early stage, o¯ÇÜen superonasally. ¯ÇØey are more commonly seen in NTG.
¯Çü Nasal step represents a di¯Çøerence in sensitivity above and
below the horizontal midline in the nasal ¯Ç×eld. ¯ÇØe defect is
bounded by the horizontal midline, corresponding to the retinal nerve ¯Ç×bre layer horizontal raphe. Inferior optic disc and
OCT changes with a corresponding superior nasal step are
shown in Fig. 11.22B

A B

E F

patients (see text).

¯Çü Temporal wedge is less common than a nasal step but has
similar implications.
¯Çü Arcuate defects (Fig. 11.22C) develop as a result of coalescence of paracentral scotomas. ¯ÇØey typically develop between
10┬░ and 20┬░ of ¯Ç×xation as downward or upward extensions
from the blind spot around ¯Ç×xation. With time, they tend to
elongate circumferentially along the distribution of arcuate
nerve ¯Ç×bres (Fig. 11.22D and E).
¯Çü A ring scotoma develops when superior and inferior arcuate
defects become continuous, usually in advanced glaucoma (Fig.
11.22F).
¯Çü End¯Çá stage changes are characterized by a small island of central vision, typically accompanied by a temporal island. ¯ÇØe
10-2 perimetry pattern facilitates monitoring of the residual
central ¯Ç×eld.
¯Çü Minimal criteria for glaucomatous damage on SAP. One
of the following defects on Humphrey visual ¯Ç×eld testing
(Hodapp, Parrish and AndersonÔÇÖs criteria)

{ Glaucoma hemi¯Ç×eld test outside normal limits on at least

of the visual ¯Ç×eld by evaluating threshold at mirror-image

with age-adjusted normal values.

in glaucoma.
¯Çü Clinical uses. ¯ÇØis technology detects subtle optic neuropathy
and may have a role to play in the follow-up of some patients
with glaucoma. It is not a substitute for clinical examination,
visual ¯Ç×eld testing and OCT examination of the RNFL. Steady
state pattern ERG may demonstrate: (a) decline of ganglion
cell function, (b) treatment response, (c) recovery of ganglion
cell function (Fig. 11.28).

## Management

¯ÇØe primary aim of treatment is to prevent functional impairment
of vision within the patientÔÇÖs lifetime and to maintain a good quality of life by slowing the rate of ganglion cell loss. Currently the
only proven method of achieving this is to reduce IOP. Both higher
mean IOP and substantial variation in IOP are predictive of progressive visual ¯Ç×eld loss in patients with glaucoma, whether newly
diagnosed or advanced.

## Patient instruction

An explanation should be o¯Çøered concerning the nature of the disease and relevant literature provided. ¯ÇØe timing of medication use
should be speci¯Ç×ed, and the patient educated in the technique of
eye drop instillation. At follow-up visits the patientÔÇÖs pro¯Ç×ciency
at instilling drops should be checked. In order to maximize drug
contact time with the anterior segment and to minimize systemic
absorption, the patient should be instructed either to perform lacrimal sac occlusion by applying ¯Ç×ngertip pressure at the medial
canthus or to close the eyes for about 3 minutes a¯ÇÜer instillation.
Common or severe potential adverse e¯Çøects should be explained
at the commencement of treatment and their occurrence enquired
about at review visits.

{ Corrected PSD that occurs in less than 5% of normal individuals on two consecutive ¯Ç×elds.
¯Çü Summary measures should always be considered. On average,
an annual deterioration in mean total deviation of just over
1.0dB can be expected in untreated patients, but there is signi¯Ç×cant individual variation (see ÔÇÿNatural history of openangle glaucomaÔÇÖ above) (Fig. 11.24).
¯Çü Staging. A discrete-levels staging system can be used to determine
the stage of damage. Early glaucomatous loss is a MD of < ÔêÆ6 dB
(Fig. 11.25), moderate glaucomatous loss ÔêÆ6 to ÔêÆ12 dB (Fig. 11.26)
and severe glaucomatous loss > ÔêÆ12 dB (Fig. 11.27). Any absolute
defect (0 dB) in the central 5 degrees is considered ÔÇÿsevereÔÇÖ.

Pattern ERG (pERG)

¯ÇØe electroretinogram (ERG) measures the electrical activity of
the retina in response to a light stimulus (see Ch.15). Pattern
ERG (pERG) uses a similar stimulus to that used when measuring visual evoked potential (pattern reversal) and targets macular
ganglion cell function. It is an objective measure of retinal function that can be recorded non-invasively under physiological
conditions.
¯Çü Accurate refraction and a clear ocular media are important
for pERG measurements.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe pERG waveform consists of a small negative de¯Ç£ection
near 35ms (N35 component), a positive de¯Ç£ection near 50ms

## Treatment goals

¯Çü Target pressure. It is assumed that the pre-treatment level
of IOP has damaged the optic nerve and will continue to do
so. An IOP level is identified below which further damage
is considered unlikely: the target pressure. This is identified
considering the severity of existing damage (particularly a
greater vertical C/D ratio and a higher mean deviation on
visual fields), the level of IOP, CCT, the rapidity with which
damage occurred if known and the age and general health
of the patient. Greater age is associated with a higher likelihood of rapid progression, but a shorter life expectancy may
also influence management. Therapy should maintain the
IOP at or below the target level. If not achievable by conservative measures, a decision is made on whether to proceed
to surgery or to continue monitoring with an above-target
IOP.
¯Çü A reasonable initial goal is to reduce the IOP to less than
18 mmHg. This is based on the results of the Advanced
Glaucoma Intervention Study (AGIS), which found that
significant visual field progression is unlikely to occur
in most patients in the medium term if IOP can be kept
below 18 mmHg at all times.
¯Çü Progression risk, as found by the EMGT, is influenced by:
(a) magnitude of initial IOP reduction, (b) height of the
baseline IOP, (c) presence of pseudoexfoliation syndrome,
(d) presence of bilateral disease, (e) worse mean deviation,
(f) older age.
¯Çü Response to progression. If damage progresses despite a target pressure having been reached consistently, the target IOP
is set to a lower level. As damage progresses, the loss of each
remaining ganglion cell has a greater proportional impact on
visual function and there is less reserve capacity. If further
visual loss occurs despite apparently good IOP control, surgery
should be considered.

## Medical therapy

See later in this chapter.
¯Çü Commencing medical therapy

{ Any drug chosen should be prescribed in the lowest concentration consistent with the desired therapeutic e¯Çøect
and administered as infrequently as possible.

{ Ideally the drug with the fewest potential side e¯Çøects
should be used.

{ Initial treatment is usually with one type of medication,
typically a prostaglandin analogue or beta-blocker.
¯Çü Review

{ ¯ÇØe interval to review a¯ÇÜer starting medication is set
according to the individual patient, but is usually 4ÔÇô8
weeks.

{ Response to the drug is assessed against the target IOP.

{ If the response is satisfactory, subsequent assessment is
generally set for a further 3ÔÇô6 months.

{ If there has been little or no response the initial drug is
withdrawn and another substituted.

¯Çü Perimetry. If IOP control is good and glaucomatous damage

¯Çü Gonioscopy should be performed annually in most patients

¯Çü Optic disc examination should be performed at each visit,
