readily detectable clinically such as impaired optic nerve
perfusion. ¯ÇØe possibility of an alternative pathology, particularly a compressive lesion, should always be considered
in these circumstances.
¯Çü Laser trabeculoplasty (see later in this chapter). Selective laser
trabeculoplasty (SLT) is as e¯Çøective as medical monotherapy
in reducing the IOP. Based on the 6-year results of the LiGHT
trial (which shows that SLT results in reduced progression
rates, cost-saving and improved quality of life compared to
topical medication) SLT has recently been recommended as
¯Ç×rst-line treatment in patients with newly diagnosed ocular
hypertension and chronic open-angle glaucoma.
¯Çü Surgery (see later in this chapter). Trabeculectomy is the
surgical procedure most commonly performed for POAG.

## In recent years the threshold for glaucoma drainage device

implantation has been lowered by many surgeons. Nonpenetrating surgery (e.g. deep sclerectomy, viscocanalostomy) is utilized extensively in POAG by some specialists.
¯ÇØere is a role for micro-invasive glaucoma surgery (MIGS)
in selected cases of glaucoma. Phacoemulsi¯Ç×cation alone is
frequently associated with a 15% fall in IOP, but is generally
only o¯Çøered to patients in whom signi¯Ç×cant lens opacity is
present. It can be combined with a ¯Ç×ltration or a MIGS procedure. Progressive damage is more likely to be slowed a¯ÇÜer
surgery, probably because the resultant IOP is o¯ÇÜen lower
than the preoperative level, is less likely to ¯Ç£uctuate and
because adherence is no longer a factor.
¯Çü Prognosis. Most patients with POAG will not go blind in
their lifetime, but the incidence of blindness varies considerably depending on multiple factors such as the presence of
advanced damage at diagnosis and non-compliance with treatment. In a White population with POAG the lifetime chance
of blindness in both eyes has historically been 5ÔÇô10%. Given
the long-term nature of progression in glaucoma, the prognosis appears to have signi¯Ç×cantly improved with new treatment

strategies. ¯ÇØe average period from diagnosis to death has
been estimated at around 16 years and only about a third will
still be alive 20 years a¯ÇÜer diagnosis.

NORMAL-TENSION GLAUCOMA

## Introduction

NTG, also referred to as low-tension or normal-pressure glaucoma,
is usually regarded as a variant of POAG. ¯ÇØe distinction between
NTG and POAG is based on an epidemiologically derived range of
normal IOP. It is an arbitrary division that may not have signi¯Ç×cant
clinical value, though it is possible that a spectrum exists in which,
towards the NTG end, IOP-independent factors are of increasing
relative importance. Depending on ethnic background, 30ÔÇô65% of
patients with open-angle glaucoma may have an IOP that is within
the normal range on initial assessment.
NTG is characterized by:
¯Çü IOP consistently equal to or less than 21 mmHg on diurnal
testing.
¯Çü Signs of optic nerve damage in a characteristic glaucomatous
pattern.
¯Çü Visual ¯Ç×eld loss as damage progresses, consistent in pattern
with the nerve appearance.
¯Çü An open anterior chamber angle.
¯Çü No features of secondary glaucoma or a non-glaucomatous
cause for the neuropathy.

## Pathogenesis

Any aetiological factors distinct from those in POAG have not
been conclusively determined, although various mechanisms
have been postulated including anomalies of local and systemic
vascular function, structural optic nerve anomalies and autoimmune disease. With the introduction of widespread CCT assessment, NTG in some patients can be explained by very low CCT.

372 Normal-Tension Glaucoma

Overall CCT in patients with NTG is lower than in POAG. A
small proportion of NTG patients have been found to have
marked nocturnal IOP spikes, sometimes only detected on testing in the supine position.

## Risk factors

¯Çü Age. Patients tend to be older than those with POAG, though
this may be due to delayed diagnosis.
¯Çü Gender. Some studies have found a higher prevalence in
females.
¯Çü Race. NTG occurs more frequently in Japanese people.
¯Çü Family history. ¯ÇØe prevalence of POAG is greater in families
of patients with NTG than in the normal population. Mutations in the OPTN gene coding for optineurin have been identi¯Ç×ed in some patients with NTG, though also in patients with
POAG.
¯Çü CCT is lower in patients with NTG than POAG.
¯Çü Abnormal vasoregulation, particularly migraine and Raynaud phenomenon, has been found more commonly in NTG
than POAG by some investigators. However, others have found
abnormalities just as commonly in POAG. Systemic diseases
associated with vascular risk, such as diabetes, carotid insuf¯Ç×ciency, hypertension and hypercoagulability may also be
important.
¯Çü Systemic hypotension including nocturnal blood pressure
dips of >20%, particularly in those on oral hypotensive medication (Fig. 11.29). ¯ÇØe EMGT con¯Ç×rms that low blood pressure is a risk factor in patients with NTG. ¯ÇØe duration and
magnitude of decrease in nocturnal blood pressure below the
daytime mean blood pressure predicts progression in NTG.
¯Çü Obstructive sleep apnoea syndrome may be associated, perhaps via an e¯Çøect on ocular perfusion.
¯Çü Autoantibody levels have been found to be higher in some
groups of NTG patients by some investigators.
¯Çü Low serum retinol levels are more likely to be present in NTG
patients.
¯Çü Translaminar pressure gradient. ¯ÇØis may on average be
larger than in POAG.
¯Çü Ocular perfusion pressure may be relatively lower than in
POAG.
¯Çü Myopia is associated with a greater likelihood of glaucoma and
of its progression.
¯Çü ¯Çƒyroid disease may be more common.

Di¯Ç×erential diagnosis

¯Çü Angle closure should always be ruled out by meticulous darkroom gonioscopy.
¯Çü Low CCT leading to underestimation of IOP. It is possible that
a thin posterior ocular wall may increase mechanical stress in
the region of the lamina cribrosa. Prior refractive surgery and
corneal ectasia also lead to falsely low IOP readings.
¯Çü POAG presenting with apparently normal IOP because of
wide diurnal ¯Ç£uctuation. Plotting a diurnal IOP curve over an
8-hour period (phasing) during o¯ÇÖce hours may detect daytime elevation, but detection of nocturnal IOP spikes requires
substantial resource commitment.

{ Splinter haemorrhages on the disc margin are more frequent than in POAG and are associated with a greater likelihood of progression. ¯ÇØese are o¯ÇÜen missed if the disc is

¯Çü Previous episodes of raised IOP may have occurred as a
result of ocular trauma, uveitis or local or systemic steroid
therapy.
¯Çü Masking by systemic treatment such as an oral beta-blocker,
commenced a¯ÇÜer glaucomatous damage has already been
sustained.
¯Çü Spontaneously resolved pigmentary glaucoma. ¯ÇØe typical
examination features of pigmentary glaucoma tend to become
less evident with increasing age. ¯ÇØe IOP in some cases of
POAG may also spontaneously normalize over time.
¯Çü Progressive retinal nerve ¯Ç×bre defects not due to glaucoma
such as may occur in myopic degeneration and optic disc
drusen.
¯Çü Congenital disc anomalies simulating glaucomatous cupping,
such as a disc pit or coloboma (see Fig. 19.24).
¯Çü Neurological lesions causing optic nerve or chiasmal compression can produce visual ¯Ç×eld defects that may be misinterpreted as glaucomatous and neuroimaging should be
performed if there is any suspicion, particularly in young
patients.
¯Çü Previous anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AION),
particularly secondary to temporal arteritis may give rise
to a disc appearance and visual ¯Ç×eld defect consistent with
glaucoma (see Fig. 19.14D). Non-arteritic AION o¯ÇÜen occurs
in a ÔÇÿcrowdedÔÇÖ disc and the fellow eye should be examined
for this. Previous retinal vascular occlusion should also be
considered.
¯Çü Previous acute optic nerve insult such as hypovolaemic or
septicaemic shock, or head injury.
¯Çü Miscellaneous optic neuropathies including in¯Ç£ammatory,
in¯Ç×ltrative and drug-induced pathology will o¯ÇÜen be clinically
obvious, but can occasionally masquerade as NTG.

## Clinical features

History and examination are essentially the same as for POAG but
speci¯Ç×c points warrant attention.
¯Çü History

{ Migraine and Raynaud phenomenon.

{ Episodes of shock.

{ Head or eye injury.

{ Headache and other neurological symptoms (intracranial
lesion).

{ Medication, e.g. systemic steroids, beta-blockers.
¯Çü IOP is usually in the high teens, but may rarely be in the low
teens. In asymmetrical disease the more damaged disc typically corresponds to the eye with the higher IOP.
¯Çü Optic nerve head

{ ¯ÇØe optic nerve head tends to be larger on average in NTG
than in POAG (Fig. 11.30).

{ The pattern of cupping is similar, but acquired optic
disc pits and focal nerve fibre layer defects are more
common.

{ Peripapillary atrophic changes may be more prevalent.

{ Indications for neuroimaging in a patient with NTG:

¯Çü Loss of VA out of proportion to cupping

¯Çü Loss of colour vision on Ishihara testing

{ Pallor disproportionate to cupping should prompt a suspicion of an alternative diagnosis.
¯Çü Visual ¯Ç×eld defects are the same as in POAG although
there is some evidence that they tend to be closer to ¯Ç×xation, deeper, steeper and more localized. In more than half of
patients, ¯Ç×eld changes are non-progressive over a period of 5
years or more without treatment. However, perhaps because
of delayed diagnosis, patients tend to present with more
advanced damage than in POAG. A high level of suspicion
for a de¯Ç×cit pattern suggesting a lesion posterior to the optic
nerve is important.
¯Çü Other investigations are as for POAG although in selected
patients the following can be considered:

¯Çü Visual ¯Ç×eld loss not consistent with retinal nerve ¯Ç×bre
layer drop-out

¯Çü Pallor of the neuroretinal rim of the disc

¯Çü Rapid progression despite normal pressure

{ Carotid duplex imaging.

{ Ocular blood ¯Ç£ow assessment (e.g. laser ¯Ç£owmetry) may
have useful clinical potential.

{ Assessment of systemic vascular risk factors.

{ Blood pressure measurement can be used to calculate ocular perfusion pressure. 24-hour ambulatory monitoring
will exclude nocturnal systemic hypotension in selected
patients.

{ Blood tests for other causes of non-glaucomatous optic
neuropathy such as vitamin B12, red cell folate, full blood
count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate/C-reactive protein, treponemal serology including Lyme disease, serum
angiotensin-converting enzyme level, plasma protein electrophoresis and autoantibody screen.

TIP In a patient with glaucoma undertake neuroimaging if
there is deterioration of colour vision or if the visual ¯Çøeld defect
is not consistent with retinal nerve ¯Çøbre layer loss.

## Treatment

## As a large proportion of untreated patients will not deteriorate

(approximately 50% at 5ÔÇô7 years), progression should be demonstrated before commencing treatment in most patients. Some
cases of NTG progress more rapidly than others and because further lowering of IOP is e¯Çøective in reducing progression, treatment
should be considered in patients with advanced glaucomatous
damage, particularly if central vision is threatened and in those
with a long life expectancy. Regular assessment including perimetry should be performed at 6-monthly intervals initially.

374 Primary Angle-Closure Glaucoma

A B

eye; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× left eye.

¯Çü Non¯Çá speci¯Ç×c advice. Patients should be encouraged to take
regular exercise. Yoga exercises that involve head-stands
should be avoided.
¯Çü Medical treatment. Prostaglandins are usually prescribed as
initial treatment. Brimonidine may have a neuroprotective
e¯Çøect in addition to an IOP-lowering e¯Çøect. Topical betablockers should be used with caution, particularly at bedtime,
as they can be systemically absorbed and cause a signi¯Ç×cant
dip in nocturnal blood pressure. Betaxolol is the beta-blocker
of choice in these circumstances.
¯Çü Selective laser trabeculoplasty is a reasonable initial option to
achieve IOP targets.
¯Çü Surgery should be considered if progression occurs despite
IOP in the low teens. Antimetabolite enhancement of trabeculectomy is likely to be indicated in order to achieve a satisfactorily low pressure and to prevent IOP spikes.
¯Çü Control of systemic vascular disease such as diabetes, hypertension and hyperlipidaemia may be important, in order to
optimize optic nerve perfusion.
¯Çü Systemic calcium¯Çá channel blockers to address vasospasm
have been advocated by some authorities.
¯Çü Antihypotensive measures. If signi¯Ç×cant nocturnal dips in BP
are detected, it may be necessary to reduce antihypertensive
medication, especially if taken at bedtime. Selected patients
might be encouraged to increase their salted food intake, in
consultation with the patientÔÇÖs cardiovascular physician.
¯Çü E¯ÇØects of sleeping in a head¯Çá up position. ¯ÇØe IOP is higher
in a ¯Ç£at position than in a 30┬░ head-up position. Although this
e¯Çøect di¯Çøers between individuals, patients with progressive
disease should be encouraged to sleep in a head-up position,
as the mean IOP is 20% lower in this position than in a ¯Ç£at
position in at least a third of individuals.
¯Çü Neuroprotective agents of proven bene¯Ç×t are not yet available.

## Memantine is used to retard neuronal death in some central

nervous system (CNS) disorders, but has not been shown to
be bene¯Ç×cial in glaucoma. Ginkgo biloba (40 mg three times
daily) may confer some bene¯Ç×t in selected cases.

PRIMARY ANGLE-CLOSURE GLAUCOMA

## Introduction

## Overview

¯ÇØe term ÔÇÿangle closureÔÇÖ refers to occlusion of the trabecular
meshwork by the peripheral iris (iridotrabecular contact ÔÇô ITC),
obstructing aqueous out¯Ç£ow. Angle closure can be primary, when
it occurs in an anatomically predisposed eye, or secondary to
another ocular or systemic factor. PACG may be responsible for up
to half of all cases of glaucoma globally and is particularly common
in Asia. It progresses rapidly and is more likely to result in visual
loss than POAG.

## Gonioscopy

## Grading of angle width

In practice, the angle is graded by many practitioners simply according to the number of structures visible (Fig. 11.31),
together with qualifying comments relating to the width of the iris
approach. ¯ÇØe angle is usually narrowest superiorly. ¯ÇØe main aims
are to evaluate the functional status of the angle, the degree of closure and the risk of future closure.
Sha¯Çƒer system. ¯ÇØe Sha¯Çøer system records the angle in degrees
between two imaginary lines tangential to the inner surface of the
trabeculum and the anterior surface of the iris about one-third of
the distance from its periphery. ¯ÇØe system assigns a numerical
grade to each quadrant of the angle.
¯Çü Grade 4 (35ÔÇô45┬░) is the widest angle, characteristic of myopia
and pseudophakia. ¯ÇØe ciliary body can be visualized without
tilting the lens.
¯Çü Grade 3 (25ÔÇô35┬░) is an open angle in which the scleral spur
is visible.
¯Çü Grade 2 (20┬░) is an angle in which the trabeculum but not the
scleral spur can be seen.

{ Gonioscopy shows posterior meshwork ITC in three or
more quadrants but no peripheral anterior synechiae
(PAS).

{ A lower threshold for diagnosis is used if gonioscopy
reveals two quadrants of ITC and signs of intermittent closure, like pigment smudging (Fig. 11.32C).

diaphragm, a crescentic shadow forms over the nasal iris
when a light is projected across the anterior chamber from
the temporal side (the ÔÇÿeclipseÔÇÖ sign) (Fig. 11.32B).

## Schwalbe line and perhaps the top of the trabeculum can be

¯Çü Slit angle is one in which there is no obvious iridocorneal contact but no angle structures can be identi¯Ç×ed.
¯Çü Grade 0 (0┬░) is closed due to iridocorneal contact.
¯Çü Indentation will distinguish appositional from synechial angle

## Other systems

¯Çü ¯ÇØe Spaeth system is detailed but underused. It allows formal description of the position of iris insertion, the angular
approach and peripheral iris curvature.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe Scheie classi¯Ç×cation refers to the angle structures visible
and allocates a Roman numeral accordingly. In contrast to
common clinical use, in the original system a higher numeral
(e.g. IV) actually signi¯Ç×es a narrower angle.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe van Herick method (Table 11.4) uses the slit lamp alone to
estimate the AC angle width:

{ A thin but bright slit beam is set approximately perpendicularly to the corneal surface (o¯Çøset from the optics by
about 60┬░) to the patientÔÇÖs temporal side for each eye.

{ ¯ÇØe beam is used to estimate the ratio of the corneal thickness to the most peripheral part of the AC.

{ It is useful as a screening tool, but overestimates angle
width in a proportion of patients, particularly those with
plateau iris con¯Ç×guration.

TIP Evaluation of the ¯Çøltration angle using gonioscopy is
key to the diagnosis and treatment of primary angle-closure
glaucoma.

Classi¯Çƒcation

## As knowledge about the epidemiology and mechanisms of angle

closure has increased, classi¯Ç×cation has moved away from a
symptom-based approach (acute, subacute and chronic) to re¯Ç£ect
the stages in the natural history of the disease. ¯ÇØe scheme below
has been suggested by a consensus group of the Association of
International Glaucoma Societies:
¯Çü Primary angle closure suspect (PACS)

{ Axial anterior chamber depth is less than normal (Fig.
11.32A). Because of the convexity of the irisÔÇôlens

{ ¯ÇØe risk of developing angle-closure disease in individuals
classi¯Ç×ed as PAC suspects is low.
¯Çü Primary angle closure (PAC)

¯ÇØe mechanisms involved in angle closure can be categorized according to the anatomical level (anterior to posterior) at which causative
forces act. In many patients more than one level is contributory.
¯Çü Relative pupillary block

{ Failure of physiological aqueous ¯Ç£ow through the pupil
leads to a pressure di¯Çøerential between the anterior and
posterior chambers, with resultant anterior bowing of the
iris (Fig. 11.34).

{ Usually anatomically relieved by peripheral iridotomy,
which equalizes anterior and posterior chamber pressure. Reduction of IOP will follow provided the angle has
opened adequately. ¯ÇØis may not occur if there are substantial PAS or an additional mechanism of angle closure
is in e¯Çøect. Trabecular meshwork damage can prevent
normalization of IOP even with an anatomically open
angle.

{ ¯ÇØe lens vault quanti¯Ç×es the portion of the lens located
anterior to the anterior chamber angle. A common de¯Ç×nition is the distance between the anterior pole of the lens
and a horizontal line joining the scleral spur at diametrically opposite locations. A large lens vault is independently
associated with angle closure, though it is not clear whether
this is entirely via a pupillary block or non-pupillary block
mechanism, or both.

{ Gonioscopy shows three or more quadrants of ITC associated with raised IOP and/or PAS, best evaluated using
indentation gonioscopy (Fig. 11.33).

{ Normal optic disc and ¯Ç×eld.

{ Some authorities further classify PAC into non-ischaemic
and ischaemic, the latter showing anterior segment evidence of prior substantial IOP elevation such as iris
changes or glaukom¯Ç£ecken (see below).
¯Çü Primary angle¯Çá closure glaucoma (PACG)

{ ITC in three or more quadrants, associated with glaucomatous optic neuropathy.

{ Optic nerve damage from an episode of severe IOP elevation, such as acute angle closure, may not appear as typical
glaucomatous cupping.

the central hump being due to the underlying central lens
supporting the iris and the peripheral hump resulting from

{ Plateau iris syndrome describes the persistence of gonioscopic angle closure despite a patent iridotomy in a patient

be necessary to demonstrate the angle closure. It is divided
into a complete form in which occlusion of the functional
trabecular meshwork (TM) is present and the IOP is elevated and an incomplete form with occlusion to a lesser
extent and normal IOP.

{ ¯ÇØis includes those cases in which a sudden change in lens
volume and/or position leads to an acute or subacute IOP

## Risk factors

¯Çü Age. ¯ÇØe average age of relative pupillary block is about 62
years at presentation. Non-pupillary block forms of primary
angle closure tend to occur at a younger age.
¯Çü Gender. Females are more commonly a¯Çøected than males.
¯Çü Race. Particularly prevalent in Far Eastern and Indian Asians,
where non-pupillary block is relatively more signi¯Ç×cant.
¯Çü Family history. Genetic factors are important but poorly
de¯Ç×ned, with an increased prevalence of angle closure in family members.
¯Çü Refraction. Eyes with ÔÇÿpureÔÇÖ pupillary block are usually hypermetropic. Non-pupillary block mechanism can occasionally
occur in myopic eyes. Up to one in six patients with hypermetropia of one dioptre or more are primary angle closure suspects, so routine gonioscopy should be considered in all adults
who are hypermetropic.
¯Çü Axial length. Short eyes tend to have a shallow AC secondary
to a relatively anterior lens position. Eyes with nanophthalmos
(axial length less than 20 mm) have a very short eye and are at
particular risk.

## Diagnosis

## Symptoms

¯Çü Unilateral painful red eye. Most patients with acute congestive angle closure present with a unilateral painful red
eye. In about 5% the symptoms occur simultaneously in
both eyes.
¯Çü Precipitating factors include watching television in a darkened room, pharmacological mydriasis or, rarely, miosis,
adoption of a semi-prone position (e.g. reading), acute emotional stress and occasionally systemic medication: parasympathetic antagonists or sympathetic agonists including
inhalers, motion sickness patches and cold/¯Ç£u remedies
(mydriatic e¯Çøect), topiramate and other sulfa derivatives
(ciliary body e¯Çøusion).
¯Çü Presentation can be with intermittent symptoms of blurring
(ÔÇÿsmoke-¯Ç×lled roomÔÇÖ) and haloes (ÔÇÿrainbow around lightsÔÇÖ)
due to corneal epithelial oedema, or acutely with markedly
decreased vision, redness and ocular/periocular pain and
headache.
¯Çü Systemic symptoms include abdominal pain, nausea and
vomiting.

## Signs

¯Çü Acute primary angle closure (APAC), previously called ÔÇÿacute
glaucomaÔÇÖ

{ VA is usually 6/60 to HM.

{ ¯ÇØe IOP is usually very high (50ÔÇô80 mmHg).

{ Appositional obstruction by the iris.

{ Degeneration of the TM itself due to chronic or intermittent contact with the iris or damage sustained due to elevated IOP.

{ Permanent occlusion of the TM by PAS. ¯ÇØe prognosis for
IOP control correlates well with the extent of PAS.

{ Conjunctival hyperaemia with violaceous circumcorneal
injection.

{ Corneal epithelial oedema.

{ ¯ÇØe AC is shallow and aqueous ¯Ç£are is usually present.

{ A non-reactive mid-dilated vertically oval pupil is classic
(Fig. 11.36).

## Investigation

a mid-dilated vertically oval pupil.

{ ¯ÇØe fellow eye typically shows an occludable angle. If this
is not present, secondary causes of angle closure should be
considered.
¯Çü Resolved APAC

{ Early: low IOP (ciliary body shutdown and e¯Çøect of intensive treatment), folds in Descemet membrane if IOP has
reduced rapidly (Fig. 11.37A), optic nerve head congestion, choroidal folds.

{ Late: iris atrophy with a spiral-like con¯Ç×guration, glaukom¯Ç£ecken (Fig. 11.37B) (white foci of necrosis in the
super¯Ç×cial lens) and other forms of cataract and irregular
pupil due to iris sphincter/dilator damage and posterior
synechiae (Fig. 11.37C). ¯ÇØe optic nerve may be normal
or exhibit varying signs of damage, including pallor and/or
cupping (Fig. 11.37D).

{ ¯ÇØe greater the duration of an attack of APAC and the
extent of post-APAC PAS, the lower the likelihood of IOP
control with medical treatment alone.
¯Çü Subacute angle closure is used to describe the clinical scenario
of intermittent episodes of spontaneously resolving mild/moderate APAC, usually in patients with pupillary block. ¯ÇØe clinical course may be chronic, or may culminate in a more severe/
unresolving episode of APAC.
¯Çü Chronic presentation

{ ¯ÇØese patients are usually asymptomatic.

{ VA is normal unless damage is advanced.

{ ¯ÇØe AC is usually shallower in relative pupillary block than
non-pupillary block.

{ IOP elevation may be only intermittent.

{ ÔÇÿCreepingÔÇÖ angle closure is characterized by a gradual
band-like anterior advance of the apparent insertion of the
iris. It starts in the deepest part of the angle superiorly and
spreads circumferentially.

{ Intermittent ITC may be associated with the formation of
discrete PAS, individual lesions having a pyramidal (ÔÇÿsawtoothÔÇÖ) appearance (see Fig. 11.33B).

¯Çü Anterior segment OCT (AS-OCT ÔÇô see Fig. 11.15), ultrasound
biomicroscopy or Scheimp¯Ç£ug photography may be useful to
supplement gonioscopic ¯Ç×ndings and for patient education.
¯Çü Anterior chamber depth measurement is helpful in some
cases.
¯Çü Biometry if lens extraction is considered.
¯Çü Posterior segment ultrasonography in atypical cases to
exclude causes of secondary angle closure.
¯Çü Provocative testing. ¯ÇØis may aid decision-making in some
circumstances, particularly when plateau iris syndrome is
suspected.

{ Pharmacological mydriasis is a poor discriminator and
carries a small risk of precipitating APAC in susceptible
patients without a patent iridotomy.

{ Dark room/prone provocative test (DRPPT): the patient
sits in a dark room, face down for 1 hour without sleeping (sleep induces miosis). ¯ÇØe IOP is checked before
and immediately a¯ÇÜer the test, as IOP can normalize
very rapidly. An IOP rise of 8 mmHg or more is considered signi¯Ç×cant. Gonioscopy without indentation
should be used to con¯Ç×rm closure of the angle. If the
test is positive in a patient with a patent laser iridotomy,
the underlying anatomical cause is usually plateau iris,
which can be con¯Ç×rmed with ultrasound biomicroscopy
(see Fig. 11.14). A positive response is abolished a¯ÇÜer
lens extraction.

Di¯Ç×erential diagnosis of acute IOP elevation

¯Çü Lens¯Çá induced angle closure due to a swollen or subluxated
lens.
¯Çü Malignant glaucoma (aqueous misdirection), especially if the
patient has recently undergone intraocular surgery.
¯Çü Other causes of secondary angle closure, with or without
pupillary block.
¯Çü Neovascular glaucoma may occasionally cause the sudden
onset of pain and congestion.
¯Çü Hypertensive uveitis, e.g. iridocyclitis with trabeculitis (particularly herpetic including cytomegalovirus), glaucomatocyclitic crisis (PosnerÔÇôSchlossman syndrome).
¯Çü Scleritis with or without angle closure.
¯Çü Pigment dispersion.
¯Çü Pseudoexfoliation.
¯Çü Orbital/retro¯Çá orbital lesions including orbital in¯Ç£ammation,
retrobulbar haemorrhage and carotid-cavernous ¯Ç×stula.

APAC

receive urgent treatment.
¯Çü Initial treatment

{ ¯ÇØe patient should lie down in a supine position to encourage the lens to shi¯ÇÜ posteriorly under the in¯Ç£uence of
gravity.

{ Optic nerve signs depend on the severity of damage.

{ Acetazolamide 500 mg is given intravenously if IOP is >50
mmHg and orally (not slow-release) if IOP <50 mmHg.

{ Contraindications include sulfonamide allergy and angle

checked for contraindications.

damage.

{ Surgical options: peripheral iridectomy, lens extraction,

treatment.

{ Analgesia and an antiemetic may be required.
¯Çü Resistant cases

{ Central corneal indentation with a squint hook or indentation goniolens to force aqueous into the angle. Epithelial

{ Pilocarpine 2% four times daily to the a¯Çøected eye and 1%
four times daily to the fellow eye.

{ Topical steroid (prednisolone 1% or dexamethasone 0.1%)
four times daily if the eye is acutely in¯Ç£amed.

{ Any or all of the following should be continued according to response: timolol 0.5% twice daily, apraclonidine 1%
three times daily and oral acetazolamide 250 mg four times
daily.

¯Çü Bilateral laser iridotomy is performed once an attack has

¯Çü Gonioscopy needs to be repeated to ensure that the angle is
open.
¯Çü Subsequent management is as for post-iridotomy chronic
PAC/PACG. A low threshold may be adopted for cataract
surgery, particularly if a signi¯Ç×cant phacomorphic element is
suspected. Trabeculectomy is occasionally necessary for persistent IOP elevation despite a successfully opened angle.

¯Çü Laser iridotomy (Fig. 11.38). ¯ÇØe ZAP (Zhongshan Angle
Closure Prevention) trial concludes that laser peripheral iridotomy (PI) has a small prophylactic e¯Çøect over a 6-year
period and should therefore only be o¯Çøered to those with the
highest risk of developing acute congestive angle closure or

{ Symptoms suggestive of previous intermittent angle
