PAC and PACG

¯Çü Management is the same as that recommended for PACS, but
with a lower threshold for further intervention if angle widening is inadequate a¯ÇÜer iridotomy, particularly if IOP remains
elevated.
¯Çü Review. Urgency and intensity of treatment and frequency of
review is tailored to the individual patient, considering IOP,
extent of angle closure and glaucomatous damage, if present.
¯Çü Medical treatment as for POAG may be required for eyes with
substantial synechial closure or with persistently elevated IOP
despite an opened angle.
¯Çü Trabeculectomy with mitomycin C is an option, but may
result in malignant glaucoma.
¯Çü Phacoemulsi¯Ç×cation with intraocular lens implantation
is highly e¯Çøective as it corrects hypermetropia, deepens the
anterior chamber and opens the ¯Ç×ltration angle. IOP control is
achieved in almost all patients whose IOP is within the normal
range prior to surgery and in up to 80% of those whose IOP is
higher than normal preoperatively. ¯ÇØe EAGLE study (E¯Çøectiveness of early lens extraction for the treatment of primary
angle-closure glaucoma) concludes that clear lens extraction

382 Pseudoexfoliation Syndrome and Glaucoma

CLASSIFICATION OF SECONDARY
GLAUCOMA

Open¯Çƒangle

Secondary open-angle glaucoma can be subdivided on the basis of
the site of aqueous out¯Ç£ow obstruction.
¯Çü Pre¯Çátrabecular, in which aqueous out¯Ç£ow is obstructed by a
membrane covering the trabeculum (Fig. 11.40A), which may
consist of:

{ Fibrovascular tissue (neovascular glaucoma).

{ Endothelial cellular membranous proliferation (iridocorneal endothelial syndrome).

{ Epithelial cellular membranous proliferation (epithelial
ingrowth).
¯Çü Trabecular, in which the obstruction occurs as a result of
ÔÇÿclogging upÔÇÖ of the meshwork (Fig. 11.40B) and secondary
degenerative changes.

{ Pigment particles (pigmentary glaucoma).

{ Red blood cells (red cell glaucoma).

{ Degenerate red cells (ghost cell glaucoma).

{ Macrophages and lens proteins (phacolytic glaucoma).

{ Proteins (probably an element in hypertensive uveitis).

{ Pseudoexfoliative material (pseudoexfoliation glaucoma).

{ Trabecular glaucoma may also be caused by alteration of
the trabecular ¯Ç×bres themselves by oedema (e.g. trabeculitis in hypertensive uveitis) or scarring (e.g. post-traumatic
angle recession).
¯Çü Post¯Çá trabecular in which the trabeculum itself is normal but
aqueous out¯Ç£ow is impaired as a result of elevated episcleral
venous pressure.

{ Carotid-cavernous ¯Ç×stula.

{ SturgeÔÇôWeber syndrome.

{ Obstruction of the superior vena cava.

## Closed angle

¯Çü With pupillary block (Fig. 11.40C)

{ Seclusio pupillae (360┬░ posterior synechiae), usually secondary to recurrent iridocyclitis.

{ Subluxated lens.

{ Phacomorphic glaucoma.

{ Capsular block syndrome with 360┬░ irisÔÇôcapsule adhesion
in a pseudophakic eye.

{ Aphakic pupillary block.

{ Anterior chamber lens implant without a patent iridotomy.
¯Çü Without pupillary block (Fig. 11.40D)

{ Secondary causes of PAS such as advanced neovascular
glaucoma and chronic anterior uveitis.

{ Cilio-choroidal e¯Çøusion.

{ Capsular block syndrome without irisÔÇôcapsule adhesion.

{ Ciliary body/iris cyst or other ciliary body or posterior
segment tumour.

{ Contraction of retrolenticular ¯Ç×brovascular tissue such
as in proliferative vitreoretinopathy and retinopathy of
prematurity.

PSEUDOEXFOLIATION SYNDROME
AND GLAUCOMA

## Introduction

Pseudoexfoliation syndrome (PXS) is an important ocular
manifestation of a systemic disorder and is the commonest
cause of secondary open-angle glaucoma. It is easily overlooked
in the early stages, as the signs are not always obvious. It is rare
before the age of 50 years, but the prevalence increases rapidly after the sixth decade of life, with a prevalence of 5% at
age 75ÔÇô85 years. It is more common in women than men and
although the condition is found worldwide, the prevalence is
highest in Scandinavia. Not all patients with PXS will develop
glaucoma and there is no reliable method of determining which
patients with the condition will subsequently develop optic disc
damage. The incidence of glaucoma (PXG) at diagnosis of PXS
is 15ÔÇô30% and the cumulative risk of eyes with PXS requiring
glaucoma treatment may be as high as 60% at 5 years. It should
be distinguished from true capsular exfoliation, which occurs
secondary to chronic infrared exposure (ÔÇÿglassblowerÔÇÖs cataractÔÇÖ

- see Fig. 10.6E).

TIP Pseudoexfoliation glaucoma progresses more rapidly
than primary open-angle glaucoma and is more likely to result
in signi¯Çøcant visual loss.

## Pathogenesis

Pseudoexfoliative material is a grey-white fibrillary substance
deriving from abnormal extracellular matrix metabolism in
ocular and other tissues. The material is deposited on various
ocular structures, including the lens capsule (Fig. 11.41), zonular fibres, iris, trabeculum and conjunctiva. Pseudoexfoliative
material has been found in skin and visceral organs, leading
to the concept that PXS is the ocular manifestation of a systemic disorder. PXS is associated with an increased prevalence
of high-tone hearing loss and cardiovascular disorders. The
pathogenesis is multifactorial, but in some populations almost
all patients with PXS have single nucleotide polymorphisms
(SNPs) in the LOXL1 gene on chromosome 15, which codes
for an enzyme that is involved in cross-linking of tropoelastin
and collagen and is therefore important for the formation and
maintenance of elastic fibres and extracellular matrix. However,
these SNPs are common in the general population and most
individuals with the SNPs do not develop PXS. Plasma and
aqueous humour homocysteine levels tend to be higher than
controls and inadequate dietary folate intake (folate reduces
homocysteine) may be a risk factor. Open-angle glaucoma
(sometimes termed ÔÇÿcapsular glaucomaÔÇÖ) is usually due to elevated IOP secondary to trabecular obstruction by pseudoexfoliative material and liberated iris pigment leading to secondary
degenerative outflow dysfunction. Curiously, despite the systemic nature of the condition and the fact that biopsy reveals
subclinical pseudoexfoliative material in the apparently unaffected eye, glaucoma remains confined to one eye in about twothirds of patients.

{ Malignant glaucoma (ciliolenticular block).

A B

C D

obstruction; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× angle closure with pupillary block; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× angle closure without pupillary block.

## Clinical features

## Diagnosis is usually incidental but can follow vision loss from

advanced glaucoma.
¯Çü Cornea. Pseudoexfoliative material may be deposited on the
endothelium and scattered pigment deposits are common. A
vertical (Krukenberg) spindle may rarely form, similar to that
seen in pigment dispersion syndrome. Endothelial cell abnormality, such as low density, is more common than average.
¯Çü Anterior chamber. Pseudoexfoliative material particles are
sometimes seen. Mild aqueous ¯Ç£are from an impaired bloodÔÇô
aqueous barrier is common.
¯Çü Iris. Granular pseudoexfoliative material deposits, pupillary
ru¯Çø loss and patchy transillumination defects at the pupillary
margin (Fig. 11.42A).
¯Çü Lens. ¯ÇØe anterior lens capsule typically shows a central disc
and a radially indented peripheral layer of pseudoexfoliative material, separated by a clear zone maintained by pupillary abrasion (Fig. 11.42B). Peripheral capsular deposition is
o¯ÇÜen visible only with pupillary dilatation (Fig. 11.42B and C).

G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, ButterworthHeinemann 2001.)

384 Pseudoexfoliation Syndrome and Glaucoma

A B

C D

ru¯ÇÜ and pseudoexfoliative material on the pupillary margin; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× peripheral pseudoexfoliative
material on the lens more obvious with pupil dilatation; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× retroillumination; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× gonioscopy
showing patchy trabecular hyperpigmentation and a markedly irregular Sampaolesi line.

Deposits may be ¯Ç£aky, with scrolled edges. Cataract is more
common in an eye with pseudoexfoliative material, probably
because of reduced ascorbate levels in the aqueous. Phacodonesis (lens instability) due to zonular weakness may be present,
but spontaneous subluxation is rare.
¯Çü Anterior chamber angle.

{ Patchy trabecular and Schwalbe line hyperpigmentation is
common, especially inferiorly.

{ A Sampaolesi line, which is an irregular band of pigment
running on or anterior to the Schwalbe line, is commonly
seen (Fig. 11.42D). It is not pathognomonic and can be
found in pigment dispersion syndrome.

{ Dandru¯Çø-like pseudoexfoliative material deposits may be
seen.

Occasionally the IOP may rise acutely despite a wide angle,
mimicking acute angle closure.
¯Çü Prognosis. ¯ÇØis is worse than POAG as the IOP is usually
higher and may exhibit marked ¯Ç£uctuation. Severe damage
may be present at diagnosis and can develop rapidly. In the
long term, these patients are at signi¯Ç×cant risk of visual loss
and blindness. It is therefore important to monitor patients
closely and it may be prudent for review in patients with PXS
to take place at intervals of no more than 6 months.
¯Çü Systemic manifestations. ¯ÇØese include: high-tone hearing
loss, ischaemic cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease and an
increased risk of pelvic organ prolapse in older women.

## Treatment

¯Çü Medical treatment is similar to that of POAG, but failure is
more common.
¯Çü Laser trabeculoplasty is more e¯Çøective than in POAG,
with mean IOP reduction around 30% following SLT. Care
should be taken not to apply excessive energy, as trabecular

{ ¯ÇØere is an increased risk of angle closure probably because
of zonular laxity.
¯Çü IOP. In most eyes the presence of glaucomatous damage is
associated with elevated IOP. ¯ÇØe majority of patients have a
chronic open-angle glaucoma that is usually unilateral at ¯Ç×rst.

pigmentation may confer higher absorption resulting in transient IOP spikes. A signi¯Ç×cant rise in IOP may occur approximately 2 years a¯ÇÜer SLT.
¯Çü Phacoemulsi¯Ç×cation alone may signi¯Ç×cantly lower IOP,
though it may give better control combined with trabeculectomy. ¯ÇØere is a high risk of complications, due to poor
mydriasis, increased fragility of the zonules and lens capsule
and endothelial de¯Ç×ciency. ¯ÇØere is also an increased risk of
a postoperative IOP spike, postoperative corneal oedema,
in¯Ç£ammation, capsular opaci¯Ç×cation, capsulorhexis contraction (capsular phimosis) and late IOL decentration or
dislocation.
¯Çü Filtration surgery in PXG has a similar success rate to POAG.
¯Çü Trabecular aspiration alone seems to confer at least a shortterm bene¯Ç×t and can be performed at the same time as other
intraocular procedures.

PIGMENT DISPERSION SYNDROME
AND PIGMENTARY GLAUCOMA

## Introduction

Pigment dispersion syndrome (PDS) is characterized by the liberation of pigment granules from iris pigment epithelium and
their deposition throughout the anterior segment. Secondary
pigmentary glaucoma (PG) is common. PDS and PG are more
common in males, particularly young myopic White men. In
African people, where it is rare, the condition tends to a¯Çøect
older hypermetropic women. About 15% of patients with PDS
will develop elevated IOP or glaucoma a¯ÇÜer 15 years. AD inheritance with incomplete penetrance seems to be present in at least
some families and a range of genetic loci have been linked. Myopia is a risk factor for clinical manifestation, higher degrees of
myopia being associated with earlier and more severe glaucoma.

## Secondary pigment dispersion can occur as a consequence of

trauma, intraocular tumour and rubbing of a malpositioned IOL
on the iris pigment epithelium.

## Pathogenesis

In primary PDS/PG, pigment shedding is precipitated by
rubbing of the posterior pigment layer of the iris against the
zonules as a result of excessive posterior bowing of the midperipheral portion of the iris (Fig. 11.43A). It is believed that
an increase in anterior chamber pressure relative to the posterior chamber occurs due to reverse pupillary block, supported
by the observation that peripheral iridotomy flattens the iris
and decreases iridozonular contact (Fig. 11.43B). The pigment epithelium itself may be abnormally susceptible to shedding in affected individuals. Pigment shedding decreases from
middle age onwards due to physiological changes resulting in
decreased iridozonular contact. Acute IOP elevation can occur
due to direct trabecular obstruction by released melanin granules. Chronic elevation appears to be caused by pigmentary
obstruction of the intertrabecular spaces and damage to the
trabeculum secondary to denudation, collapse and sclerosis.

## Patients with pigmentary glaucoma have an increased incidence

of steroid responsiveness.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Presentation. PDS and PG are typically detected at a routine
eye examination. ¯ÇØe myopic individuals who tend to develop
the condition are likely to be under regular optometric review.

## Occasionally symptoms from glaucomatous visual loss lead to

attendance, or from corneal oedema due to an acute IOP rise
following the release of pigment granules (particularly a¯ÇÜer
physical exercise). Signs of PDS are usually bilateral but may
be subtle and go undetected.
¯Çü Cornea. Pigment is deposited on the endothelium in a vertical
spindle shape (Krukenberg spindle; Fig. 11.44A) ¯ÇØe spindle is
not always present, tends to become less obvious in longstanding cases and is not pathognomonic.
¯Çü Anterior chamber (AC). ¯ÇØe AC is deep and melanin granules may be seen in the aqueous.
¯Çü Iris. Characteristic radial spoke-like transillumination defects
(Fig. 11.44B) are seen in lighter, but o¯ÇÜen not in dark, irides.
To best demonstrate these, the room should be minimally illuminated and a short, narrow but intense slit beam directed
through the pupil. Visualization may be aided by asking the
patient to look up. Melanin granules may be present on the
surface of the iris, usually inferiorly, which become less obvious with age. ¯ÇØere may be partial loss of the pupillary ru¯Çø
(Fig. 11.44C).
¯Çü Gonioscopy. The angle is wide open and there is often a
characteristic mid-peripheral iris concavity where the iris
bows backwards. The trabecular meshwork is heavily pigmented in all four quadrants, with a dense homogeneous
circumferential posterior meshwork band (Fig. 11.44D).
Pigment may also be seen on or anterior to the Schwalbe
line. Pigmentation of the angle typically reduces with
increasing age.
¯Çü Lens. Pigment granules may be deposited on the anterior surface. ¯ÇØere may be a line (Scheie stripe) or a (Zentmayer) ring
of pigment on the peripheral/equatorial surface around the
zonular insertions.
¯Çü IOP. ¯ÇØis may be volatile, some patients exhibiting higher levels and wider ¯Ç£uctuations of IOP than in POAG. Over time
the control of IOP may become easier as pigment liberation
decreases and occasionally the IOP may revert to normal.
NTG can be erroneously diagnosed if the IOP has normalized
spontaneously and the PDS signs resolved.
¯Çü Posterior segment. Peripheral retinal pigmentation may be
seen and lattice degeneration is more common than in myopic
patients without PDS or PG. ¯ÇØe incidence of retinal detachment may also be higher. Glaucomatous optic neuropathy is
dependent on disease stage and extent and may be markedly
asymmetrical. It is common to ¯Ç×nd advanced disease in one
eye and relatively mild damage in the other.

## Treatment

Individuals with PDS should be reviewed annually to exclude
the development of raised IOP and/or glaucomatous damage. If

386 Pigment Dispersion Syndrome and Pigmentary Glaucoma

A B

chamber and posterior bowing of the peripheral iris; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× ¯Ç£attening of the peripheral iris following laser iridotomy.

A B

C D

iris transillumination defects; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× pigment granules on the iris surface and inferior loss of the
pupillary ru¯ÇÜ; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× homogeneous band of trabecular hyperpigmentation.

glaucomatous atrophy is present the patient should be monitored
at 4ÔÇô6-monthly intervals as the combination of myopia and PDS
can result in rapid deterioration of visual function.
¯Çü Lifestyle measures. Excessive exercising can be associated
with increased iris pigment dispersion with acute symptoms
in some cases. If the history con¯Ç×rms this, appropriate advice
should be given to alter exercise behaviour.
¯Çü Medical treatment is similar to that of POAG. Miotics are
theoretically of bene¯Ç×t because they decrease iridozonular
contact in addition to facilitating aqueous out¯Ç£ow but are
poorly tolerated by younger patients. ¯ÇØis medication has the
disadvantage of exacerbating myopia and also carries a risk of
precipitating retinal detachment in short-sighted eyes. Topical
thymoxamine, a selective alpha-adrenergic antagonist, induces
miosis without causing spasm of accommodation, but is also
poorly tolerated as it causes irritation.
¯Çü Laser trabeculoplasty is o¯ÇÜen e¯Çøective. It is important not to
over-treat eyes with heavily pigmented angles. To reduce the
risk of an IOP spike, use the lowest laser setting and treat only
two quadrants.
¯Çü Laser iridotomy has been proposed to retard pigment liberation by reversing iris concavity and eliminating iridozonular
contact. It can reduce the risk of intermittent IOP spikes, especially in patients under the age of 40 years, but does not reduce
the risk of developing glaucoma.
¯Çü Filtration surgery is indicated more commonly than in
POAG. ¯ÇØe use of adjunctive antimetabolites improves surgical outcome, particularly in younger patients in whom
there is a risk of failure. Postsurgical hypotony and suprachoroidal haemorrhage are more common in young patients
with myopia.

## Acute bilateral iris pigment loss with

raised IOP

Bilateral acute depigmentation of the iris (BADI) and bilateral
acute iris transillumination (BAIT) have recently been proposed as
distinct idiopathic clinical syndromes involving pigment dispersal
from the iris into the anterior chamber. ¯ÇØey are said to be more
common in young to middle-aged women, occurring spontaneously or following a ¯Ç£u-like illness and possibly a¯ÇÜer oral antibiotic
treatment, especially moxi¯Ç£oxacin. It is possible that the primary
process is in¯Ç£ammation, but there is evidence that the mechanism
is phototoxicity following sensitization in predisposed individuals. Presentation is usually with acute bilateral ocular redness and
photophobia. Both may be associated with an IOP rise, but this
has been reported to be more severe and resistant in BAIT. Findings described in the two di¯Çøer in that pigment is lost from the
iris stroma in BADI and the iris pigment epithelium in BAIT, with
marked iris transillumination defects and irregular mydriasis
in the latter. It is possible that BADI and BAIT represent di¯Çøerent points on a common disease spectrum. Di¯Çøerential diagnosis
is principally from viral anterior uveitis and pigment dispersion
syndrome.

NEOVASCULAR GLAUCOMA

## Pathogenesis

Neovascular glaucoma (NVG) occurs as a result of aggressive iris
neovascularization (rubeosis iridis), which leads to progressive
angle closure and rapid glaucomatous atrophy (Fig. 11.45A and B).
¯ÇØe common aetiological factor is severe, di¯Çøuse and chronic retinal ischaemia. It is postulated that hypoxic retinal tissue produces
angiogenic factors in an attempt to revascularize hypoxic areas.
¯ÇØe most important of these is VEGF.

## Causes

¯Çü Ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion accounts for 35ÔÇô50%
of cases. A VA of less than 6/60, a relative a¯Çøerent pupillary
defect and extensive peripheral retinal capillary non-perfusion
on ¯Ç£uorescein angiography are useful predictors of the risk of
subsequent NVG. Glaucoma typically occurs 3 months a¯ÇÜer
the occlusive event (ÔÇÿ100-day glaucomaÔÇÖ), but intervals from 4
weeks to 2 years have been documented.
¯Çü Diabetes mellitus accounts for a smaller proportion. Whereas
diabetes was the cause in 30% of cases in the past, this has
dropped to 10ÔÇô15% of cases in communities where yearly
screening of diabetic retinopathy occurs. ¯ÇØe risk of glaucoma
is decreased by panretinal photocoagulation and by antiVEGF treatment. Pars plana vitrectomy in diabetics may precipitate NVG (7% overall in a large study), especially if angle
neovascularization is present preoperatively.
¯Çü Arterial retinal vascular disease such as central retinal artery
occlusion and ocular ischaemic syndrome are less common
causes.
¯Çü Miscellaneous causes include intraocular tumours, longstanding retinal detachment (RD) and chronic intraocular
in¯Ç£ammation.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms vary from none to severe pain, decreased vision,
redness and photophobia.
¯Çü Cornea. Elevated IOP, particularly when substantial and acute,
leads to corneal oedema.
¯Çü IOP may be normal early in the disease process, but is frequently extremely high later on. ¯ÇØe anterior segment will
o¯ÇÜen be congested once progression to elevated IOP has
occurred. In advanced disease hypotony may supervene.
¯Çü Anterior chamber. Flare, cells and posterior synechiae may
be present, depending on severity and stage. Presentation is
sometimes with anterior chamber haemorrhage.
¯Çü Pupillary margin. Subtle vessels at the pupillary margin are
o¯ÇÜen an early sign (Fig. 11.45C), but may be missed unless the
iris is examined carefully under high magni¯Ç×cation. Diagnosis
at this stage is likely to substantially improve the prognosis.
¯Çü Iris surface. New vessels grow radially over the surface of the
iris towards the angle (Fig. 11.45D), sometimes joining dilated
blood vessels at the collarette. At this stage the IOP may still be
normal, but elevation can occur fairly acutely.

388 Neovascular Glaucoma

A B

C D

E F

(arrow) with hyphaema; ¯ÇØF¯Ç× blind eye showing dilated pupil, ectropion uvea and cataract in
the presence of a tube (arrow). (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic
Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

¯Çü Gonioscopy. Angle neovascularization may commonly occur
without other signs, particularly a¯ÇÜer CRVO and it is important to perform careful non-mydriatic gonioscopy in eyes at
risk. Early signs may be very subtle, even in the presence of
moderate IOP elevation. Neovascular tissue proliferates across
the face of the angle, forming an obstructing ¯Ç×brovascular
membrane that subsequently contracts to close the angle.
¯ÇØe angle closes circumferentially leading to very high IOP,
severe visual impairment, congestion of the globe and pain.
¯ÇØe visual prognosis is generally poor by this stage, but aggressive management can achieve comfort and retain useful sight
in some cases.
¯Çü Cataract is common once ischaemia is established.
¯Çü Posterior segment. Signs correspond to aetiology. Glaucomatous optic neuropathy may be present.
¯Çü Investigations. Fluorescein angiography may be helpful in
con¯Ç×rming aetiology and delineating ischaemia. B-scan ultrasonography will help to exclude potential causes such as RD or
a tumour when the posterior segment view is impaired. Anterior segment OCT has been proposed as a useful tool for angle
assessment.

## Treatment

## It is critical to address the cause of the neovascularization as well as

the elevated IOP. Appropriate management of systemic disease is
also of key importance in order to reduce the risk to the fellow eye,
to reduce the risk of stroke and to prolong life expectancy. Factors
that predict a poor visual outcome include: young age, VA less than
6/60 and IOP >35 mmHg on presentation.
¯Çü Review. Frequent review during high-risk periods is critical:
the ¯Ç×rst few months following an ischaemic CRVO and the
¯Ç×rst few weeks following diabetic vitrectomy.
¯Çü Medical treatment of elevated IOP is as for POAG but
miotics should be avoided. Topical atropine 1% twice daily
will resist synechiae formation, and increase the out¯Ç£ow of
aqueous through the uveoscleral route. Topical steroids are
bene¯Ç×cial in the acute stage. Topical apraclonidine and oral
acetazolamide may be useful temporizing measures. Acetazolamide can be associated with renal dysfunction in diabetes, especially type 1 and should be used with caution in
these patients.
¯Çü Panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) is usually e¯Çøective in
inducing regression of neovascularization and, if performed
early, preventing progression to glaucoma. It will not reverse
an established ¯Ç×brovascular membrane. ¯ÇØe timing of PRP in
CRVO is discussed in Chapter 13. If the retinal view is poor,
indirect ophthalmoscopic application may provide better
access; if necessary, performed in the operating room with iris
hooks to open a small pupil caused by posterior synechiae.
Trans-scleral cryotherapy may be used in eyes with opaque
media or as an adjunct for increasing peripheral retinal
coverage.
¯Çü Intraocular VEGF inhibitors, e.g. bevacizumab (Avastin)
at a dose of 1.25 mg in 0.05 ml, is an e¯Çøective adjunctive
measure whilst waiting for PRP to take e¯Çøect, particularly if

¯Ç×brovascular angle closure has not yet supervened and usually leads to rapid pain relief. Intracameral (AC) injection is
an alternative to the intravitreal route. ¯ÇØe duration of control
from a single injection is limited and most patients require
ongoing injections. In patients with ischaemic CRVO treated
in this manner, the onset of NVG can be delayed by up to 18
months. ¯ÇØere is a small risk of central retinal artery occlusion
in patients with ocular ischaemic syndrome.
¯Çü Retinal detachment repair should be performed once the IOP
is controlled, particularly if there is a tractional detachment
involving the macula.
¯Çü Ciliary body ablation. Cyclodiode should be considered if
medical IOP control is not possible. ¯ÇØese have conventionally been used only in eyes with poor visual potential, but can
be employed if there is reasonable vision in order to prevent
severe glaucomatous damage occurring whilst neovascularization is brought under control. Lowering a substantially raised
IOP generally improves comfort and clearing corneal oedema
may facilitate an adequate retinal view for PRP. Care should
be taken not to use excessive treatment, which can lead to
hypotony. Endoscopic cyclophotocoagulation is an alternative
to trans-scleral treatment.
¯Çü Filtration surgery may be considered if VA is HM or better. Options include an arti¯Ç×cial ¯Ç×ltering shunt (glaucoma
drainage device) (Fig. 11.45E) and trabeculectomy with
mini-shunt implantation, adjunctive mitomycin C and
postoperative subconjunctival 5-¯Ç£uorouracil. Active neovascularization and in¯Ç£ammation should be controlled preoperatively (including preoperative anti-VEGF treatment)
to improve the chances of surgical success. Even with treatment there is a relatively high risk of hypotony. Postoperative anti-in¯Ç£ammatory treatment should be aggressive and
may include systemic steroids.
¯Çü Pars plana vitrectomy with peroperative endolaser at an early
stage may improve the prognosis in eyes with vitreous haemorrhage, especially in CRVO. Preoperative intravitreal antiVEGF may be of bene¯Ç×t.
¯Çü Retrobulbar alcohol injection is useful in relieving pain
but may cause permanent ptosis and does not relieve
congestion.
¯Çü Enucleation or evisceration may be considered if all else fails.

TIP The underlying cause of neovascular glaucoma needs to
be determined and treated in order to reduce the risk of visual
loss in the fellow eye and to increase life expectancy.

## Prognosis

## Patient expectations need to be realistic and the best outcome is

o¯ÇÜen a blind but comfortable eye. ¯ÇØe prevalence of blindness in
the a¯Çøected eye varies from 25% to 50% depending on the length
of follow-up (Fig. 11.45F). Life expectancy is reduced signi¯Ç×cantly
in these patients (approximately 50% of expected lifespan). A good
presenting VA in the a¯Çøected eye is a useful predictor of longer life
expectancy.

390 In¯Çƒammatory Glaucoma

INFLAMMATORY GLAUCOMA

## Introduction

## Overview

Elevation of IOP secondary to intraocular in¯Ç£ammation frequently
presents a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge. ¯ÇØe elevation of
IOP may be transient and innocuous, or persistent and severely
damaging. ¯ÇØe prevalence of secondary glaucoma increases with
chronicity and severity of disease. Secondary glaucoma is particularly common in Fuchs uveitis syndrome and chronic anterior uveitis associated with juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Posterior uveitis is
less likely to a¯Çøect the aqueous out¯Ç£ow pathway and consequently
less likely to lead to IOP elevation.

## Diagnostic dilemmas

## Assessment of glaucomatous damage may be hampered by a small

pupil or opacities in the media. Poor VA may also compromise
accurate perimetry.
¯Çü IOP ¯Ç£uctuation may be dramatic in uveitic glaucoma and
phasing may be helpful in patients with borderline IOP.
¯Çü Ciliary body shutdown caused by acute exacerbation of
chronic anterior uveitis is frequently associated with lowering
of IOP that may mask the underlying tendency to glaucoma.
Even eyes with considerably elevated IOP (30ÔÇô35 mmHg) may
become hypotonous during acute exacerbations of uveitis.

## Return of ciliary body function with subsidence of uveitis may

be associated with a rise in IOP in the presence of permanently
compromised out¯Ç£ow facility.
¯Çü Topical steroids, when used intermittently, can cause a ¯Ç£uctuating IOP.
¯Çü Iris vessels may give rise to diagnostic confusion with neovascular glaucoma.

Angle¯Çƒclosure glaucoma with pupillary
block

## Pathogenesis

Secondary angle closure is caused by posterior synechiae extending for 360┬░ (seclusio pupillae), which obstruct aqueous ¯Ç£ow from
the posterior to the anterior chamber. ¯ÇØe resultant increased
pressure in the posterior chamber produces anterior bowing of the
peripheral iris (iris bomb├® ÔÇô Fig. 11.46A) resulting in shallowing of
the anterior chamber and apposition of the iris to the trabeculum
and peripheral cornea (Fig. 11.46B). Such an in¯Ç£amed iris easily
sticks to the trabeculum and the iridocorneal contact may become
permanent, with the development of PAS.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Slit lamp biomicroscopy shows seclusio pupillae, iris bomb├®
and a shallow anterior chamber.
¯Çü Gonioscopy shows angle closure from iridotrabecular contact.

## Indentation may be used to assess the extent of appositional as

opposed to synechial angle closure.

Angle¯Çƒclosure glaucoma without
pupillary block

¯Çü Pathogenesis. Chronic anterior uveitis causes the deposition
of in¯Ç£ammatory cells and debris in the angle (Fig. 11.47A and
B). Subsequent organization and contraction pull the peripheral iris over the trabeculum, causing gradual and progressive
synechial angle closure (Fig. 11.47C) and eventual elevation of
IOP. ¯ÇØe eye with a pre-existing narrow angle may be at higher
risk.
¯Çü Diagnosis. ¯ÇØe anterior chamber is deep but gonioscopy
shows extensive angle closure by PAS.

A B

contact.

Open¯Çƒangle glaucoma

## In acute anterior uveitis

In acute anterior uveitis the IOP is usually normal or subnormal
due to concomitant ciliary shutdown. Occasionally, however,
secondary open-angle glaucoma develops due to obstruction
of aqueous outflow, most commonly as acute inflammation is
subsiding and ciliary body function returning (Fig. 11.47D).
This effect, which is often transient and innocuous, may be
steroid-induced or caused by a combination of the following
mechanisms:
¯Çü Trabecular obstruction by in¯Ç£ammatory cells and debris,
which may be associated with increased aqueous viscosity due
to leakage of protein from in¯Ç£amed iris blood vessels.
¯Çü Acute trabeculitis involving in¯Ç£ammation and oedema of the
trabecular meshwork with secondary diminution of intertrabecular porosity may result in a reduction in out¯Ç£ow facility.

## It is thought that this is especially relevant in anterior uveitis

associated with herpes zoster, herpes simplex, other viral anterior uveitides and toxoplasma retinitis.

## In chronic anterior uveitis

In chronic anterior uveitis the main mechanism for reduced out¯Ç£ow facility is thought to be trabecular scarring and/or sclerosis
secondary to chronic trabeculitis. ¯ÇØe importance of this mechanism is, however, di¯ÇÖcult to determine as most eyes also have
some degree of synechial angle closure. Because of the variable
appearance of the angle on gonioscopy, de¯Ç×nitive diagnosis of trabecular damage is di¯ÇÖcult. In some eyes, a gelatinous exudate is
seen on the trabeculum.

## Treatment

## Medical

¯Çü Medical control of IOP is more likely to be achieved if the
angle is completely open.

A B

C D

cells in the angle; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× gonioscopy showing in¯Ç£ammatory debris; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× synechial angle closure
(saw-toothed PAS¯Ç×; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× acute anterior uveitis with secondary raised IOP. (Courtesy of J Harry
and G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

392 In¯Çƒammatory Glaucoma

¯Çü If there are a number of possible mechanisms causing the rise
in IOP and the patient is using a topical steroid, stop the topical steroid for 2 days and remeasure the IOP.
¯Çü Long-acting depot steroid preparations should not be used in
known or suspected steroid-responders.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe e¯Çøect of ocular hypotensive drugs is less predictable in
uveitis, e.g. some cases may be unexpectedly sensitive to topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (CAI).
¯Çü A beta-blocker is usually the drug of ¯Ç×rst choice.
¯Çü Prostaglandin derivatives should be avoided if possible as they
may promote in¯Ç£ammation.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe choice of additional agents o¯ÇÜen depends on the IOP
level. If this is very high, oral acetazolamide may be required.
For moderate elevation (e.g. less than 35 mmHg on a betablocker) in the absence of signi¯Ç×cant glaucomatous damage, an alpha-adrenergic agonist or topical CAI might be
adequate.
¯Çü Miotics are contraindicated as they increase vascular permeability and as miosis promotes the formation of posterior
synechiae.

## Laser iridotomy

¯Çü Laser iridotomy is performed to re-establish communication
between the posterior and anterior chambers in eyes with
pupillary-block angle-closure glaucoma.
¯Çü An iridotomy is likely to become occluded in the presence of
active uveitis and intensive topical steroid should be used following the laser.
¯Çü Correction of pupillary block alone may not control the IOP if
there is insu¯ÇÖcient exposed functional angle, though a patent
iridotomy may retard progressive PAS formation.
¯Çü Surgical iridectomy is the de¯Ç×nitive method of preventing further pupil block and may be required if laser fails to maintain
a viable iridotomy.

## Surgery

¯Çü Preoperative preparation

{ Control of chronic uveitis for a minimum of 3 months
before surgery is ideal.

{ Preoperative topical steroids should be used, not only as
prophylaxis against recurrent in¯Ç£ammation but also to
reduce the conjunctival in¯Ç£ammatory cell population.

{ In patients with particularly labile in¯Ç£ammatory disease
oral prednisolone should be considered (0.5 mg/kg daily).
Immunosuppressive treatment has improved the prognosis in recent years.
¯Çü Trabeculectomy with mitomycin C or the implantation of a
long-tube implant can be used in these circumstances.

{ Combined cataract and glaucoma surgery is relatively
contraindicated, but may sometimes be performed in
conjunction with goniosynechialysis; occasionally cataract surgery alone can be appropriate. In most cases cataract surgery should be deferred for at least 6 months a¯ÇÜer
trabeculectomy.

{ Postoperative hypotony is a particular risk as a delicate
balance may exist between reduced aqueous production

and restricted out¯Ç£ow. ¯ÇØis is particularly likely to occur
if there has been a pronounced reduction in the IOP when
acetazolamide is introduced.

{ Following surgery, steroids are tapered more slowly than in
non-in¯Ç£ammatory glaucoma.
¯Çü Cyclodestructive procedures should be used with caution as
they may exacerbate in¯Ç£ammation. Possible underlying ciliary
body insu¯ÇÖciency also carries the risk of profound hypotony
that may progress to phthisis bulbi.

TIP Inappropriate use of topical steroids is a signi¯Çøcant risk
factor for the development of raised IOP and glaucoma.

PosnerÔÇôSchlossman syndrome ¯ÇØPSS¯Ç×

## Introduction

PSS (glaucomatocyclitic crisis) is a rare condition characterized
by recurrent attacks of unilateral acute raised IOP associated
with mild anterior uveitis. The mechanism is speculated to be
acute trabeculitis and there is evidence that infection, possibly
cytomegalovirus (CMV) or Helicobacter pylori, may play a role.
Prostaglandins are involved in the pathogenesis, particularly
PGE2, where the aqueous levels positively correlate with IOP.
The presence of HLA-Bw54 haplotype has been implicated in
some cases. PSS typically affects young to middle-aged adults.
Males are affected more frequently than females. Episodes are
unilateral, although 50% of patients have involvement of the
other eye at different times. The intervals between attacks vary,
but usually become longer with time. Patients should be followed even after the attacks have completely subsided, because
a significant proportion will develop chronic IOP elevation,
with the fellow eye also at risk.

## Diagnosis

An acute IOP rise in PDS and demonstrable CMV or other viral
anterior uveitis can present in an almost identical manner. ¯ÇØe
signs of the former may be atypical in older patients and those with
dark irides. Simple IOP volatility in POAG, especially the juvenile
variant, must also be distinguished.
¯Çü Presentation is with mild discomfort, haloes around lights
and slight blurring of vision in one eye and sometimes
redness.
¯Çü Slit lamp biomicroscopy typically shows a few anterior chamber cells and one to several ¯Ç×ne white central keratic precipitates. Injection is likely to be absent or minimal. Mild corneal
epithelial oedema is frequent.
¯Çü Mydriasis is common. Posterior synechiae are not a
feature.
¯Çü IOP is typically raised to over 40 mmHg, out of proportion to
iritis severity and untreated persists for hours to weeks. Elevation precedes the in¯Ç£ammatory signs.
¯Çü Gonioscopy shows an open angle without PAS.
¯Çü Glaucomatous optic neuropathy is relatively uncommon in
most cases. Reversible cupping has been described.

## Treatment

Topical steroids are used to control in¯Ç£ammation, with aqueous
suppressants to lower the IOP. Topical steroids should only be used
while there is active in¯Ç£ammation and should not be used prophylactically. Topical or oral non-steroidal anti-in¯Ç£ammatory agents
may also be bene¯Ç×cial. ¯ÇØe bene¯Ç×t of antiviral treatment is unclear.
Trabeculectomy with mitomycin C may occasionally be needed,
especially when there are signs of glaucomatous nerve damage or
progressive visual ¯Ç×eld loss.

Steroid¯Çƒinduced glaucoma

## Introduction

Around one in three individuals develop some degree of elevation of IOP in response to a course of potent topical steroid,
dividing the population into steroid ÔÇÿrespondersÔÇÖ and ÔÇÿnonrespondersÔÇÖ. Responders are more likely than non-responders
to develop POAG and a majority of patients with POAG are
responders. Steroid-induced glaucoma is a form of secondary
glaucoma, which is typically associated with the use of topical,
periocular, intraocular or steroid therapy, although it can occur
with any type of corticosteroid administration. ¯ÇØe rise in IOP
is a consequence of increased resistance to the out¯Ç£ow of aqueous secondary to altered function of the extracellular matrix or
endothelial cells of the trabecular meshwork. ¯ÇØe IOP elevation is dependent on the steroid type, potency, dose, duration
and route of administration and usually occurs 2ÔÇô4 weeks a¯ÇÜer
commencement of treatment. ¯ÇØe rise in IOP a¯ÇÜer intravitreal
triamcinolone typically lasts 2ÔÇô4 months and a¯ÇÜer dexamethasone intravitreal implant (Ozurdex) up to 6 months. ¯ÇØe clinical picture resembles that of chronic open-angle glaucoma with
a normal-appearing anterior chamber angle and an absence of
symptoms. Much less o¯ÇÜen the condition may result in an acute
presentation with pain and a signi¯Ç×cant IOP rise.

## Risk factors

¯Çü Established glaucoma/ocular hypertension.
¯Çü Family history of glaucoma.
¯Çü High myopia.
¯Çü Young age, especially childhood.
¯Çü Connective tissue disease, especially rheumatoid arthritis.

## Treatment

¯Çü Discontinuation of the topical steroid is usually all that is
required to reduce the IOP back to normal. In the acute form
this occurs within days and in the chronic form within 1ÔÇô4
weeks.
¯Çü A less potent steroid drop can be prescribed, for example
fluorometholone 0.1%, rimexolone 1% or loteprednol
etabonate.
¯Çü In 3% of patients, elevated IOP may persist despite stopping all
steroids. ¯ÇØe duration of steroid therapy in¯Ç£uences the reversibility of IOP elevation. ¯ÇØis is usually seen in patients with a
family history of glaucoma.

¯Çü Persistent IOP elevation should be managed with medication
or glaucoma surgery. Steroid-induced raised IOP is unlikely to
occur in the presence of an established bleb.

TIP In any patient with raised IOP who is using topical
steroids, simply discontinuing the steroids will often result in a
rapid reduction of IOP (thus identifying the patient as a ÔÇÿsteroid
responderÔÇÖ).

LENS-RELATED GLAUCOMA

## Phacolytic glaucoma

## Introduction

Phacolytic glaucoma is a secondary open-angle glaucoma
occurring in association with a hypermature cataract. Trabecular obstruction is caused by high molecular-weight lens
proteins that leak through the intact capsule into the aqueous humour leading to trabecular obstruction. Macrophages
containing lens proteins may also contribute (Fig. 11.48A).

## Phacolytic glaucoma should not be confused with phacogenic

(previously phacoanaphylactic) uveitis, an autoimmune granulomatous reaction to exposed lens proteins occurring with a
compromised lens capsule (see Ch. 12).

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Presentation is with pain. Vision is poor due to cataract.
¯Çü Slit lamp biomicroscopy shows corneal oedema, a hypermature cataract and a deep anterior chamber. ¯ÇØere may be large
¯Ç£oating white particles in the AC, consisting of lens protein
and protein-containing macrophages (Fig. 11.48B), which
may impart a milky appearance to the aqueous if very dense
(Fig. 11.48C) and can form a pseudohypopyon (Fig. 11.48C
and D).
¯Çü Gonioscopy, if a reasonable view can be obtained, shows an
open angle with lens-derived material and in¯Ç£ammatory cells
that are most substantial inferiorly.

## Treatment

A¯ÇÜer IOP is controlled medically, proteinaceous material is washed
out from the anterior chamber and the cataract is removed. Care
should be taken not to rupture the zonule, which is likely to be
more fragile than usual.

## Phacomorphic glaucoma

## Pathogenesis

Phacomorphic glaucoma is an acute secondary angle-closure glaucoma precipitated by an intumescent cataractous lens. Equatorial
age-related growth of the lens slackens the suspensory ligament
and allows the lens to move anteriorly. Associated anteroposterior
growth leads to increased iridolenticular contact and potentiates
pupillary block and iris bomb├®.

394 Lens-Related Glaucoma

A B

C D

¯ÇØB¯Ç× hypermature cataract, lens protein-containing macrophages ¯Ç£oating in the aqueous; ¯ÇØC¯Ç×
dense milky aqueous with pseudohypopyon; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× residual particles in the anterior chamber
following incomplete irrigation after complicated surgery. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson,
from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Presentation is similar to acute PACG with a shallow AC and
mid-dilated pupil. A dense white cataract is evident (Fig. 11.49).
¯Çü ¯Çƒe fellow eye. Phacomorphic glaucoma is more likely in eyes
with a shorter axial length and shallower AC, but the fellow eye
may demonstrate a deep AC and an open angle.
¯Çü Anterior segment OCT or US biomicroscopy may be useful.

## Treatment

¯Çü Medical treatment is initially similar to that of acute PACG.
¯Çü Miotics are omitted as they tend to increase irisÔÇôlens apposition and shi¯ÇÜ the lens anteriorly. Dilatation is sometimes helpful but should be implemented with caution.
¯Çü Systemic hyperosmotic agents may be required more commonly than in PACG.
¯Çü Laser iridotomy may be worthwhile but is o¯ÇÜen not possible (due to corneal oedema or lensÔÇôcornea proximity) or

ine¯Çøective. A similar procedure should be considered for the
fellow eye.
¯Çü Laser iridoplasty may be a useful temporizing measure.
¯Çü Cataract extraction constitutes the de¯Ç×nitive treatment, ideally once the IOP has been normalized and the eye is quiet.
¯ÇØe surgery can be di¯ÇÖcult and carries a higher risk of
complications.

## Pupillary block from disruption of lens
position

## Causes

¯Çü Blunt ocular trauma, even if relatively trivial, may result in
lens dislocation in eyes with a weak zonule as in pseudoexfoliation and homocystinuria.
¯Çü Congenitally small lens (microspherophakia), e.g. WeillÔÇô
Marchesani syndrome.

lated pupil and corneal oedema in phacomorphic glaucoma. a normal-size lens.

Dislocation may be into the AC, the zonules may be
stretched or only part of the attachments may be disrupted so
that the intact part acts as a hinge and the lens may remain fully
or partially in the posterior chamber. Vitreous herniation may
be contributory.

## Diagnosis

A lens fully or partially dislocated into the anterior chamber will
usually be evident (Fig. 11.50). Acute pupillary block will cause a
sudden severe elevation of IOP with associated visual impairment.
Imaging such as ultrasound biomicroscopy may be diagnostic.

## Treatment

¯ÇØe IOP is initially reduced with osmotic agents, which reduce vitreous volume. Treatment should be urgent as prolonged lenticulocorneal contact, particularly in the presence of high IOP, may
cause permanent endothelial damage.
¯Çü Initial treatment. The patient should adopt a supine posture with the pupil dilated, to attempt to reposition the lens
in the posterior chamber, following which a miotic can be
used with caution. Bilateral laser iridotomy may provide
extended control in some cases, but lens extraction may be
necessary.
¯Çü De¯Ç×nitive treatment consists of surgical lens extraction.
An anterior chamber IOL, iris- or sclera-¯Ç×xated IOL will be
necessary.

TRAUMATIC GLAUCOMA

## Hyphaema

## Introduction

In a patient with a small traumatic hyphaema, which is usually innocuous and transient, IOP elevation may still result
from trabecular obstruction by red blood cells. The size of a
hyphaema is a useful indicator of visual prognosis and risk

of complications. A hyphaema involving less than half the
anterior chamber is associated with a 20% incidence of complications and a final VA of better than 6/18 in 80% of eyes
(Fig. 11.51A). However, a hyphaema involving more than half
the anterior chamber is associated with an 80% incidence
of complications and a final VA of better than 6/18 in only
one-third (Fig. 11.51B). The visual prognosis is determined
mainly by the extent of injury to the retina sustained at the
time of the original injury. Severe and prolonged elevation
of IOP may cause corneal blood staining and damage to the
optic nerve. The optic nerve is endangered by IOP of greater
than 50 mmHg. Secondary haemorrhage, often more severe
than the primary bleed, may develop within 3ÔÇô5 days of the
initial injury and is associated with a reduced visual outcome.
Patients with sickle-cell haemoglobinopathy are at increased
risk of complications, especially IOP elevation due to trabecular meshwork obstruction by deformed red cells and vascular occlusion.

## Treatment

{ A protective eye shield should be worn.

¯Çü General

{ A coagulation abnormality, particularly a haemoglobinopathy, should be excluded.

{ Any current anticoagulant medication should be discontinued after liaison with a general physician to assess
the risk. NSAIDs should not be used for analgesia. Likewise, specialist advice should be sought regarding the
management of a patient with a haemoglobinopathy,
particularly before administering high-risk medication
(see below).

{ Hospital admission may be required for a large hyphaema.

{ Strict bed rest is probably unnecessary, but substantially limiting activity is prudent and the patient should
remain in a sitting or semi-upright posture, including
during sleep.

396 Traumatic Glaucoma

A B

raised IOP and increased risk of glaucoma and corneal blood staining.

¯Çü Medical

{ Topical prostaglandins, beta-blockers and/or a topical or
systemic CAI is administered, depending on the IOP. CAI
should not be used in sickle haemoglobinopathies. Miotics should also be avoided as they may increase pupillary
block and disrupt the bloodÔÇôaqueous barrier.

{ Occasionally a hyperosmotic agent is needed, though as
with CAI and alpha-agonists a high threshold is adopted
in sickle patients.

{ Topical steroids should be used since they reduce the risk
of secondary haemorrhage and suppress in¯Ç£ammation.

{ Atropine is o¯ÇÜen recommended in patients with a large
hyphaema to achieve constant mydriasis and to reduce the
chance of secondary haemorrhage.

## Angle recession glaucoma

## Introduction

Angle recession involves rupture of the face of the ciliary body,
the portion between the iris root and the scleral spur, due to
blunt trauma. Although a large percentage of eyes with traumatic
hyphaema exhibit some degree of angle recession, glaucoma develops in less than 10% a¯ÇÜer 10 years. ¯ÇØe rise in IOP is secondary
to associated trabecular damage rather than from angle recession
itself. However, the risk of glaucoma is directly related to the extent
of angle recession and is unlikely to follow if less than three quadrants are recessed.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Presentation is with unilateral chronic glaucoma.
¯Çü Slit lamp examination may show signs of previous blunt
trauma. These may be mild, such as a small sphincter
rupture.
¯Çü Gonioscopy may initially reveal irregular widening of the ciliary body band (Fig. 11.52). In longstanding cases, the recession may become obscured by ¯Ç×brosis and the angle may show
hyperpigmentation.

## Treatment

¯Çü Follow¯Çá up: Individuals with more than two quadrants of angle
recession should have their IOP checked on a yearly basis.
¯ÇØey should be reassured that their risk of developing secondary glaucoma is low.
¯Çü Medical treatment is as for other types of secondary openangle glaucoma but is frequently unsatisfactory. Laser trabeculoplasty is of no bene¯Ç×t.
¯Çü Trabeculectomy with adjunctive antimetabolite is generally
e¯Çøective.
¯Çü An arti¯Ç×cial ¯Ç×ltering shunt or cyclodiode should be considered if trabeculectomy fails.

{ Anti¯Ç×brinolysis with systemic tranexamic acid or aminocaproic acid (ACA) may be considered under higher-risk
circumstances such as recurrent bleeding.
¯Çü Laser photocoagulation of angle bleeding points via a gonioprism has been described, though gonioscopy should probably
be deferred for 5ÔÇô6 days post-injury.
¯Çü Anterior chamber paracentesis or surgical evacuation of
blood is required in around 5%. Indications for surgery include
a total hyphaema, an IOP of >50 mmHg for 2 days or an IOP
>35 mmHg for 5 days. Surgical intervention reduces the risk
of permanent corneal staining and optic atrophy and prevents
the occult development of peripheral anterior synechiae and
chronic secondary glaucoma. Particular vigilance is required
in patients with sickle-cell anaemia (even moderate pressure
elevation can lead to optic atrophy), patients with prior glaucomatous optic neuropathy and in young children with a risk of
amblyopia. A glaucoma ¯Ç×ltration procedure may be necessary
in some cases.
¯Çü On discharge the patient should be advised to avoid any activity with a risk of even minor eye trauma for several weeks.
Symptoms of a rebleed should prompt immediate review.

widening of the ciliary body band.

GHOST CELL ÔÇÿGLAUCOMAÔÇÖ

## Pathogenesis

Ghost cell ÔÇÿglaucomaÔÇÖ is due to trabecular obstruction by degenerate erythrocytes, which causes raised IOP and, rarely, glaucoma.
Approximately 2 weeks a¯ÇÜer a vitreous haemorrhage, haemoglobin leaks from the erythrocytes, which evolve into ghost cells.
¯ÇØese then pass through a defect in the anterior hyaloid face and
pass into the anterior chamber. Because their pliability is lost, the
cells become entrapped within the pores of the trabecular meshwork and obstruct aqueous out¯Ç£ow. ¯ÇØe condition may occur in
the following settings:
¯Çü Cataract surgery in the context of pre-existing vitreous
haemorrhage.
¯Çü Vitreous haemorrhage in an already aphakic or pseudopha¯Çá
kic eye.
¯Çü When cataract surgery is complicated by a vitreous
haemorrhage and hyphaema. The hyphaema clears but
the red cells in the vitreous persist and evolve into ghost
cells.

## Clinical features

¯Çü ¯ÇØe cornea may be oedematous due to elevated IOP.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe anterior chamber exhibits ghost cells, which may be recognized as reddish-brown or khaki particles in the aqueous. ¯ÇØey
should not be confused with leucocytes, since this may result
in unwarranted treatment for uveitis.

## Treatment

¯Çü Medical treatment with aqueous suppressants.
¯Çü Irrigation of the anterior chamber with washing out of the
ghost cells if medical therapy fails.
¯Çü Pars plana vitrectomy may occasionally be required for persistent vitreous haemorrhage.

IRIDOCORNEAL ENDOTHELIAL
SYNDROME

## Introduction

Iridocorneal endothelial (ICE) syndrome typically a¯Çøects one eye
of a middle-aged woman. It consists of the following three clinical
presentations: Chandler syndrome, progressive (also termed essential) iris atrophy and iris naevus (CoganÔÇôReese) syndrome. ¯ÇØe
pathological basis in all three is an abnormal corneal endothelial
cell layer with a predilection for proliferation and migration across
the anterior chamber angle and onto the surface of the iris, with
subsequent progression to glaucoma (50%) and corneal decompensation in a substantial proportion of involved eyes. Glaucoma
is due to trabecular obstruction by proliferating tissue followed by
angle closure secondary to contraction. Polymerase chain reaction
(PCR) testing shows the presence of herpes simplex virus DNA in
a substantial percentage of ICE syndrome corneal and trabecular
meshwork specimens, suggesting a possible viral aetiology.

## Diagnosis

Clear di¯Çøerentiation between the three presentations may be
di¯ÇÖcult. Apparent transition from one to another has been
reported. Di¯Çøerentiation depends primarily on iris appearance,
but there is o¯ÇÜen substantial overlap. Gonioscopically visible
changes in some patients may be subtle despite elevated IOP, particularly in early disease. ¯ÇØe specular microscopic appearance is
characteristic.
¯Çü Chandler syndrome is the most common clinical presentation and is characterized by an abnormal corneal endothelial
appearance said to resemble hammered silver (Fig. 11.53A).

## It frequently presents with blurred vision and haloes due to

corneal oedema. Iris atrophy is absent in about 60% and in
the remainder is variable in severity. Corectopia is mildÔÇô
moderate when present. When glaucoma occurs, it is usually
less severe than in the other two presentations.
¯Çü Progressive (essential) iris atrophy is characterized by iris
changes including corectopia (pupil malposition ÔÇô Fig. 11.53B),
iris atrophy in the early stage, pseudopolycoria (supernumerary false pupil) (Fig. 11.53C), and broad-based PAS that o¯ÇÜen
extend anterior to the Schwalbe line (Fig. 11.53D). Severe iris
atrophy occurs in the late stage and is associated with ectropion uveae and severe glaucoma (Fig. 11.53E).
¯Çü Iris naevus (CoganÔÇôReese) syndrome is characterized by
either a di¯Çøuse naevus that covers the anterior iris, or by iris
nodules (Fig. 11.53F). Iris atrophy is absent in 50% of cases
and in the remainder, it is usually mildÔÇômoderate, although
corectopia may be severe. ¯ÇØe appearance may be mimicked
by a di¯Çøuse iris melanoma.

## Treatment

¯Çü Medical treatment of glaucoma is usually ine¯Çøective.
¯Çü Trabeculectomy with mitomycin C can be tried, but the longterm results are poor.

GLAUCOMA ASSOCIATED WITH
INTRAOCULAR TUMOURS

Approximately 5% of eyes with intraocular tumours develop a secondary elevation of IOP. Potential mechanisms are set out below:
¯Çü Trabecular block. Distinction between di¯Çøerent mechanisms
may not be possible clinically.

{ Angle invasion by a solid iris melanoma (see Fig. 20.16F)

{ Trabecular in¯Ç×ltration by neoplastic cells originating
from an iris melanoma (Fig. 11.54A). Rarely, tumour
seeding from a retinoblastoma may also invade the
trabeculum.

{ Melanomalytic glaucoma may occur in some eyes with
iris melanoma. It is due to trabecular blockage by macrophages that have ingested pigment and tumour cells, similar to phacolytic glaucoma.
¯Çü Secondary angle closure

{ Neovascular glaucoma is the most common mechanism in
eyes with choroidal melanoma or retinoblastoma.

{ Anterior displacement of irisÔÇôlens diaphragm may occur
in an eye with a ciliary body melanoma or a large tumour
of the posterior segment (Fig. 11.54B).

GLAUCOMA SECONDARY TO
EPITHELIAL INGROWTH

## Introduction

Epithelial ingrowth (downgrowth) is a rare but potentially blinding complication of anterior segment surgery or trauma, occurring when conjunctival or corneal epithelial cells migrate through
a wound and proliferate in the anterior segment (Fig. 11.55A).
Elevation of IOP is due to trabecular obstruction by one or more
of an epithelial membrane, secondary synechial angle closure and
desquamated epithelial and in¯Ç£ammatory cells. ¯ÇØe associated
glaucoma can be particularly intractable and the prognosis poor.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Persistent postoperative anterior uveitis.
¯Çü Di¯Çøuse epithelialization characterized by a greyish translucent
membrane with a scalloped border that involves the posterior
corneal surface in the area of a surgical or traumatic wound.
¯Çü Cystic and ¯Ç×brous proliferative patterns sometimes occur and
tend to have a better prognosis (Fig. 11.55B).
¯Çü Pupillary distortion.

## Treatment

¯ÇØe aim of treatment is the eradication of all invading epithelium
to avoid recurrence.
¯Çü Block excision involves the simultaneous excision of adjacent
iris and pars plicata of the ciliary body, together with all layers
of the sclera and cornea in contact with the lesion. ¯ÇØe resultant defect is covered with a tectonic corneoscleral gra¯ÇÜ. ¯ÇØe

A B

trabeculum; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× anterior displacement of the iris by a ciliary body melanoma.

400 Iridoschisis

A B

surface and ¯Çøltration angle; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× cystic epithelial ingrowth after penetrating keratoplasty. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann
2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

A B

area of iris involvement may be delineated by applying argon
laser burns, which will cause whitening of the a¯Çøected area.
¯Çü Cryotherapy may be applied to devitalize the epithelium
remaining on the posterior surface of the cornea, in the angle
and on the ciliary body. Intraocular air is used to insulate other
tissues from the e¯Çøects of the cryotherapy.
¯Çü Intracameral 5¯Çá ¯Ç£uorouracil has been tried with variable
results.
¯Çü Intravitreal injection of methotrexate (400 mg/0.1 ml every
2 weeks for six cycles) has been reported to lead to successful
resolution of the ingrowth.
¯Çü Glaucoma drainage devices are of value for medically uncontrolled glaucoma associated with extensive epithelial ingrowth
unsuitable for surgical excision.

IRIDOSCHISIS

Iridoschisis is a rare condition typically a¯Çøecting both eyes of an
older patient. Primary angle closure and PACG is associated in
up to 90%. ¯ÇØis appearance may rarely occur a¯ÇÜer trauma. ¯ÇØe
mechanism is incompletely understood, but it has been proposed
that intermittent substantial elevation of IOP results in iris atrophy, with severity ranging from stromal atrophy to ¯Ç×brillar disintegration of the anterior layer. Changes are more marked inferiorly
(Fig. 11.56A), but can occasionally involve the superior iris (Fig.
11.56B). ¯ÇØe pupil is usually normal and the anterior chamber
is typically shallow. Gonioscopy commonly shows an occludable
angle, o¯ÇÜen with PAS. Angle closure should be treated with a laser
iridotomy.

PRIMARY CONGENITAL GLAUCOMA

## Introduction

Primary congenital glaucoma (PCG) is rare, with an incidence of
1:10000 live births, in many populations. Boys are more commonly
a¯Çøected than girls in most surveys. ¯ÇØe rate is 5ÔÇô10 times higher in
children from consanguineous relationships. Involvement is more
o¯ÇÜen bilateral, but frequently asymmetrical. ¯ÇØe condition can be
classi¯Ç×ed as follows:
¯Çü True congenital glaucoma (40%), in which IOP is elevated
during intrauterine life.
¯Çü Infantile glaucoma (55%), which manifests prior to age 3.
¯Çü Juvenile glaucoma, the least common, in which IOP rises
between 3 and 16 years of age.
Primary congenital glaucoma is, by de¯Ç×nition, not associated
with other major ocular abnormalities. It is thought to be caused
by impaired aqueous out¯Ç£ow due to maldevelopment of the anterior chamber angle (trabeculodysgenesis). It is usually sporadic, but
approximately 10% are inherited in an autosomal recessive fashion
with variable penetrance. Angiopoietin receptor TEK mutations
play a role in the aetiology of some cases of primary congenital glaucoma and are transmitted as an autosomal dominant pattern with
variable expressivity. Other gene mutations have been implicated
in autosomal recessive congenital glaucoma, prominently CYP1B1coding for cytochrome P450 and LTBP2. Currently, the chance of
identifying a genetic cause is 40% when genetic testing is done. ¯ÇØe
prognosis is dependent on severity and age at onset/diagnosis. Secondary infantile glaucoma can be caused by a range of conditions
including tumours such as retinoblastoma, persistent fetal vasculature (persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous) and uveitis.

TIP Primary congenital glaucoma is always treated surgically
as soon as the diagnosis has been con¯Çørmed.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Presentation usually occurs when watering, photophobia or
blepharospasm is noticed by parents or a health-care professional, or when an abnormality is found, such as corneal haze,
large (Fig. 11.57A) or asymmetrical eye size (Fig. 11.57B).
¯Çü Corneal haze is due to di¯Çøuse oedema secondary to raised
IOP, or localized oedema due to breaks in Descemet membrane (Fig. 11.57C).
¯Çü Buphthalmos is a large eye (see Fig. 11.57AÔÇôC) as a result
of stretching due to elevated IOP prior to the age of 3 years.
A corneal diameter of more than 12mm before the age of 1
should be treated with suspicion, especially if there is asymmetry. ¯ÇØe thinned sclera o¯ÇÜen appears blue due to increased
visualization of the underlying uvea. Complications include
myopia and lens subluxation.
¯Çü Haab striae (Fig. 11.57D) are curvilinear healed breaks in
Descemet membrane.
¯Çü Corneal scarring and vascularization.
¯Çü Optic disc cupping in infants may regress once IOP is normalized. Most normal infants exhibit no apparent cup.
¯Çü Evaluation under general anaesthesia is generally required.
Intravenous ketamine lowers IOP less than other agents.

{ IOP measurement should be performed ¯Ç×rst, optimally
with more than one method (e.g. Perkins, Tono-Pen, iCare).

## It is preferable where possible for this to be measured in a

conscious or sedated child; 10ÔÇô12 mmHg is normal.

{ Anterior chamber examination with an operating microscope and/or portable slit lamp.

{ Optic disc examination; asymmetry or a cup/disc ratio of
>0.3 is suspicious.

{ Corneal diameter measurement; >12 mm prior to the age
of 1 year is highly suspicious.

{ Increased axial length measurement using ultrasound: a
measurement of more than 20.25mm at 1 month is usually abnormal, particularly if there is inter-eye asymmetry.
One of the most reliable signs of IOP control a¯ÇÜer surgery
is stabilization of the axial length measurements.

{ Gonioscopy using a direct goniolens may be normal or
reveal trabeculodysgenesis (Fig. 11.58), vaguely characterized by an anteriorly located iris insertion and a hypoplasticappearing peripheral iris. ¯ÇØe old concept of a discrete
(Barkan) membrane has not been de¯Ç×nitively con¯Ç×rmed.

{ Refraction.

## Treatment

Surgery is always required and is successful in 80ÔÇô90%. Medication may be used as temporary or supplementary therapy. Caution
is required with selection of medication in young children, as most
are relatively contraindicated.
¯Çü Goniotomy. Under direct gonioscopic visualization, an incision is made at the midpoint of the trabecular meshwork (Fig.
11.59A).
¯Çü Trabeculotomy may be necessary if corneal clouding prevents an adequate view of the angle and is also an option when
repeated goniotomy has failed. A partial-thickness scleral
¯Ç£ap is created (Fig. 11.59B) and a Harms trabeculotome (Fig.
11.59C) is inserted into the Schlemm canal and rotated into
the anterior chamber.
¯Çü A modification of trabeculotomy is used in some centres.

## An illuminated canaloplasty device is threaded into the

Schlemm canal followed by a 6-0 Prolene suture, which is
then pulled into the anterior chamber thus opening 360% of
the trabecular meshwork (Fig. 11.59D). The postoperative
IOP using this technique is approximately 5 mmHg lower
at 2 years than that obtained with the traditional approach.
¯Çü Other procedures when angle surgery fails include trabeculectomy, tube shunt implantation and ciliary body ablative
procedures.
¯Çü Monitoring of IOP, corneal diameter, axial length and other
parameters is required long term.
¯Çü Amblyopia and refractive error should be managed
aggressively.

Di¯ÇÜerential diagnosis

¯Çü Cloudy cornea

{ Birth trauma.

{ Rubella keratitis (congenital rubella is also associated with
congenital glaucoma).

{ High myopia.
¯Çü Epiphora

{ Delayed/failed canalization of the nasolacrimal duct.

{ Lacrimation secondary to ocular irritation, e.g. conjunctivitis, aberrant eyelashes, entropion.

IRIDOCORNEAL DYSGENESIS

## Posterior embryotoxon

## Posterior embryotoxon refers to a prominent and anteriorly

displaced Schwalbe line, seen as a thin grey-white arcuate ridge
adjacent to the limbus on the inner surface of the cornea (Fig.
11.60A). It is an innocuous isolated finding in up to 15% of
the general population, but is one of the features of AxenfeldÔÇô
Rieger anomaly. It is also seen in the multisystem genetic disorder Alagille syndrome, in which optic disc drusen are also
common.

AxenfeldÔÇôRieger syndrome

## Introduction

AxenfeldÔÇôRieger syndrome is the umbrella term for a spectrum
of disorders featuring bilateral developmental ocular anomalies:

Axenfeld anomaly, Rieger anomaly and Rieger syndrome. It is
caused by defective neural crest cell-related processes during fetal
development. ¯ÇØis can lead to incomplete development of the trabecular meshwork or Schlemm canal. An abnormal endothelial cell
membrane has been identi¯Ç×ed on anterior segment structures in
some patients. ¯ÇØe key implication of the syndrome is a 50% risk
of glaucoma. Associated variants in several di¯Çøerent genes have
been found, including PITX2, PAX6, FOXC1 and RIEG2. Di¯Çøerent
genetic abnormalities can give a similar clinical picture (genetic heterogeneity). Cases may be sporadic, but a family history is common,
when inheritance is autosomal dominant with variable expressivity
but very high penetrance. ¯ÇØere is no gender predilection.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Axenfeld anomaly is characterized by posterior embryotoxon
(Fig. 11.60A) with attached strands of peripheral iris, the latter
best viewed with gonioscopy.

404 Iridocorneal Dysgenesis

A B

C D

E F

hypoplasia; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× full-thickness iris hole with corectopia (in the right eye); ¯ÇØD¯Ç× severe corectopia (in the left eye of the same patient shown in ¯ÇØC¯Ç×); ¯ÇØE¯Ç× gonioscopy showing peripheral
anterior synechiae; ¯ÇØF¯Ç× dental anomalies.

¯Çü Rieger anomaly o¯ÇÜen manifests with an anterior segment
appearance similar to that of iridocorneal endothelial (ICE)
syndrome.

{ Posterior embryotoxon.

{ Iris stromal hypoplasia (Fig. 11.60B).

{ Ectropion uveae.

{ Corectopia and full-thickness iris defects (Fig. 11.60C and
D).
¯Çü Gonioscopy in mild cases shows the Axenfeld anomaly. In
severe cases, broad leaves of iris adhere to the cornea anterior
to the Schwalbe line (Fig. 11.60E).
¯Çü Glaucoma develops in about 50%, usually during childhood or early adulthood. Surgical management is often
necessary.
¯Çü Rieger syndrome is characterized by the Rieger anomaly
together with extraocular malformations that, as with the ocular features, are caused by defective neural crest cell-related tissue development:

{ Dental anomalies (Fig. 11.60F), including hypodontia (few
teeth) and microdontia (small teeth).

{ Facial anomalies: maxillary hypoplasia, broad nasal bridge,
telecanthus and hypertelorism.

{ Other anomalies include redundant paraumbilical skin
and hypospadias. Hearing loss, hydrocephalus, cardiac and
renal anomalies and congenital hip dislocation are rare.

## Aniridia

## Genetics

Aniridia is a rare bilateral condition that may have life-threatening
associations. It occurs as a result of abnormal neuroectodermal
development secondary to a mutation in the PAX6 gene. PAX6 is
adjacent to gene WT1, mutation of which predisposes to Wilms
tumour.
¯Çü Autosomal dominant aniridia accounts for about two-thirds
of cases and has no systemic implications. Penetrance is complete (all patients with the genotype will have the phenotype)
but expressivity (severity) is variable.
¯Çü Sporadic, including WARG, previously known as Miller syndrome (Wilms tumour, Aniridia, mental Retardation, Genitourinary abnormalities), includes about a third of patients.
Children with sporadic aniridia have about a 30% chance of
developing Wilms tumour.
¯Çü Gillespie syndrome accounts for only about 1% of cases.
Inheritance is AR but is not caused by PAX6 mutations. Cerebellar ataxia and learning disabilities are features.

## Diagnosis

All patients with sporadic aniridia should have abdominal ultrasonography every 3 months until 5 years of age, every 6 months
until 10 years of age and annually until 16 years of age to detect the
development of Wilms tumour or until molecular genetic analysis
con¯Ç×rms the absence of a WT1 mutation.
¯Çü Presentation is typically at birth with nystagmus and photophobia. ¯ÇØe parents may have noticed the absence of irides or
apparently large pupils.
¯Çü Aniridia is variable in severity, ranging from minimal, detectable only by retroillumination, to total absence (Fig. 11.62A
and B).
¯Çü Lids o¯ÇÜen show meibomian gland dysfunction.
¯Çü Cornea

## Peters anomaly

## Introduction

Peters anomaly is a rare but o¯ÇÜen severe condition that is bilateral
in more than half of cases. It is the result of defective neural crest
cell migration during fetal development. Manifestations range
from mild to severe. Most cases are sporadic, although autosomal
recessive inheritance has been described.

## Clinical features

Peters type I a¯Çøects the cornea alone, type II shows both corneal
and lens abnormalities.
¯Çü Central corneal opacity of variable density (Fig. 11.61A and B).
¯Çü Posterior corneal defect involving the posterior stroma, Descemet membrane and endothelium with or without iridocorneal or lenticulocorneal (Fig. 11.61C) adhesions.
¯Çü Glaucoma occurs in about 50% due to associated angle anomaly. Onset is usually in infancy but occasionally in childhood
or later. ¯ÇØe prognosis tends to be worse than in primary congenital glaucoma.
¯Çü Systemic associations including craniofacial and central
nervous system anomalies have been reported. ÔÇÿPeters plusÔÇÖ
syndrome includes a particular constellation of systemic
abnormalities.

{ Total corneal central stromal scarring and vascularization
may occur in end-stage disease.
¯Çü Fundus. Possible abnormalities include foveal and/or optic
nerve hypoplasia and choroidal coloboma.
¯Çü Gonioscopy even in eyes with apparently total aniridia usually shows a hypoplastic or rudimentary frill of iris tissue (Fig.
11.62C).
¯Çü Lens changes can include cataract and subluxation.
¯Çü Glaucoma (75%) usually presents in late childhood or adolescence. It is caused by synechial angle closure secondary to the
contraction of rudimentary iris tissue. Treatment is di¯ÇÖcult
and the prognosis guarded.

{ Tear ¯Ç×lm instability, dry eye and epithelial defects are
common.

{ Limbal stem cell de¯Ç×ciency may result in ÔÇÿconjunctivalizationÔÇÖ of the peripheral cornea.

## Treatment

¯Çü Glaucoma

{ Medical treatment is usually inadequate.

{ Trabeculectomy with mitomycin C or combined trabeculectomyÔÇôtrabeculotomy have been tried in the past but
usually fail to control the IOP.

{ Glaucoma drainage devices o¯Çøer the best chance of longterm successful control of IOP.

{ Diode laser cycloablation may be necessary if other
modalities fail.
¯Çü Painted contact lenses may be used to create an arti¯Ç×cial pupil
and improve both vision and cosmesis. Simple tinted lenses are
an alternative. Both may improve nystagmus.
¯Çü Lubricants are frequently required for associated
keratopathy.
¯Çü Cataract surgery is o¯ÇÜen required. (Fig. 11.62D). A tinted
arti¯Ç×cial lens implant may be used to try to reduce photophobia. Trauma to the corneal limbus should be minimized in
order to preserve stem cell function.
¯Çü Prosthetic iris implantation has been described in pseudophakic aniridic eyes, but may be complicated by, or worsen,
glaucoma.
¯Çü Limbal stem cell transplantation with or without keratoplasty may be required.
¯Çü Refractive errors, amblyopia and squint should be managed
aggressively.

GLAUCOMA IN PHACOMATOSES

SturgeÔÇôWeber syndrome

## Introduction

SturgeÔÇôWeber syndrome (encephalotrigeminal angiomatosis)
is a congenital, sporadic phacomatosis (see Ch. 2). Glaucoma
ipsilateral to the facial haemangioma develops in about 30%
and in 60% of these IOP elevation occurs before the age of
2 years and may result in buphthalmos (Fig. 11.63A). In the
remainder, glaucoma may develop at any time from infancy
to adulthood. The pathogenesis is uncertain but putative
mechanisms include trabeculodysgenesis in infants and raised
episcleral venous pressure associated with arteriovenous communication in an episcleral haemangioma (Fig. 11.63B) in
older patients.

## Treatment

¯Çü Medical treatment alone may be adequate.
¯Çü Goniotomy may be successful in eyes with angle anomalies.
¯Çü Combined trabeculotomyÔÇôtrabeculectomy gives good
results in early-onset cases, but carries a relatively high risk
of choroidal e¯Çøusion and suprachoroidal haemorrhage. Other
surgical options may be utilized.

Neuro¯Ç×bromatosis is a disorder that primarily a¯Çøects cell growth
of neural tissues. Inheritance is AD with irregular penetrance
and variable expressivity (see Ch. 19). Glaucoma is relatively rare
and, when present, usually unilateral and congenital. About 50%
of patients with glaucoma have an ipsilateral plexiform neuro¯Ç×broma of the upper eyelid, or facial hemiatrophy (Fig. 11.64A).
Various mechanisms have been identi¯Ç×ed, including congenital
angle anomaly, which may be associated with ectropion uveae (Fig.
11.64B and see Fig. 19.103).

remnants of the iris root; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× transillumination of pseudophakic eye showing silhouetted
ciliary processes.

MEDICAL TREATMENT OF GLAUCOMA

## Introduction

## It is important to attempt to maximize adherence by providing an

explanation of the disease and the rationale for treatment. A discussion of the medication being prescribed, including technique
and timing of administration and its potential adverse e¯Çøects, is
also essential. ¯ÇØe provision of written information may be helpful.
Most glaucoma medications are administered topically, but signi¯Ç×cant systemic absorption can still occur, with resultant systemic
adverse e¯Çøects. Systemic absorption may be minimized by lacrimal
occlusion following instillation: simply closing the eyes for 3 minutes will reduce systemic absorption by about 50% and this can be
enhanced by applying digital pressure over the lacrimal sac ÔÇô these
measures also prolong eyeÔÇôdrug contact. E¯Çøects on the periocular
skin may be reduced by blotting over¯Ç£ow from the eyelids with a
clean dry tissue immediately a¯ÇÜer instillation. Glaucoma medications should be avoided in pregnancy, if possible, with systemic
carbonic anhydrase inhibitors perhaps carrying the greatest risk
due to teratogenicity concerns.

## Prostaglandin derivatives

## Introduction

¯ÇØe major mode of prostaglandin (PG) action is the enhancement
of uveoscleral aqueous out¯Ç£ow, although increased trabecular
out¯Ç£ow facility and other mechanisms have been identi¯Ç×ed. ¯ÇØeir
IOP-lowering e¯Çøect is typically greater than alternatives and a
reduction of 27ÔÇô35% from baseline can be expected. A prostaglandin derivative is typically preferred to a beta-blocker as ¯Ç×rst-line
treatment for glaucoma because of the latterÔÇÖs potential for systemic side e¯Çøects. Duration of action may extend for several days,
though administration once every day (at bedtime) is generally
recommended. Systemic side e¯Çøects are few. ¯ÇØe most commonly
troublesome ocular side e¯Çøect is conjunctival hyperaemia. If one
prostaglandin fails to show adequate e¯ÇÖcacy, inter-individual

receptor variation means that an alternative preparation may be
superior in a given patient. Paradoxically, the concomitant use of
more than one PG can cause a rise in IOP.

## Agents

¯Çü Latanoprost may cause fewer ocular adverse events than
other PG agents and so is o¯ÇÜen used ¯Ç×rst line. Approximately
5ÔÇô10% of patients show no response (IOP reduction of less
than 10%) to latanoprost, but may respond to one of the other
prostaglandins.
¯Çü Travoprost is similar to latanoprost but fewer patients tend to
be non-responders. Polyquad is a novel proprietary preservative introduced by a major pharmaceutical manufacturer in its
travoprost formulation that may reduce ocular surface-related
adverse e¯Çøects.
¯Çü Bimatoprost 0.03% has a slightly greater IOP-lowering
e¯Çøect than latanoprost, but is more likely to cause conjunctival hyperaemia. A new 0.01% preparation has a comparable
IOP-lowering e¯Çøect to latanoprost with less hyperaemia.
Preservative-free bimatoprost is available. Sustained-release
intra-cameral bimatoprost is undergoing clinical evaluation and preliminary results indicate that IOP control can be
achieved in up to 70% of subjects for 6 months with a single
injection.
¯Çü Ta¯Ç£uprost is a preservative-free drop. Its IOP-lowering e¯ÇÖcacy may be slightly less than that of other PG agents, but it is
well tolerated and seems to cause less disruption of the ocular
surface.
¯Çü Omidenepag 0.002% solution is a new selective E-prostanoid
sub-type 2 agonist that is used once daily. It has a similar e¯Çøect
on the IOP as other prostaglandin agents. It is e¯Çøective in
patients who do not respond to latanoprost and does not cause
periorbitopathy. Hyperaemia occurs in about 5%.

Side e¯Ç×ects

¯Çü Ocular

{ Conjunctival hyperaemia is very common.
