        - darkening of left iris and eyelid skin; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× periocular atrophy on the left; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× blepharoconjunctivitis due to topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitors; ¯ÇØE¯Ç× allergic conjunctivitis due to brimonidine; ¯ÇØF¯Ç× iritis secondary to brimonidine, showing keratic precipitates.

{ Eyelash lengthening, thickening, hyperpigmentation (Fig.
11.65A) and occasionally an increase in number.

{ Irreversible iris hyperpigmentation (Fig. 11.65B) occurs
in up to a quarter of patients a¯ÇÜer 6 months. ¯ÇØe highest
incidence is in greenÔÇôbrown irides, less in yellow-brown
irides and least in blue-grey/brown irides. It is caused by
an increase in the number of pigmented granules within
the super¯Ç×cial stroma rather than an increase in the
number of melanocytes. Iris naevi and freckles are not
a¯Çøected.

{ Hyperpigmentation of periocular skin is common but
reversible.

{ Periocular atrophy (Fig. 11.65C).

{ Preoperative use of PG agents may increase the likelihood
of cystoid macular oedema following cataract surgery. ¯ÇØis
is more likely to occur if PG agents are used in the postoperative period, particularly if there has been vitreous loss at
the time of the surgery.

{ Anterior uveitis is rare, but prostaglandins should be used
with caution in in¯Ç£amed eyes.

Beta¯Çƒblockers

## Introduction

Beta-blockers reduce IOP by decreasing aqueous production, mediated by an e¯Çøect on the ciliary epithelium. A reduction of 21ÔÇô27% from baseline can be expected in most patients.
In approximately 10% of cases the response decreases with time
(tachyphylaxis), sometimes within only a few days. ¯ÇØere may be
limited supplementary e¯Çøect if a topical beta-blocker is prescribed
for a patient who is already on a systemic beta-blocker. ¯ÇØe combination may also increase the risk of systemic side e¯Çøects. Betablockers should not be instilled at bedtime as this medication may
cause a signi¯Ç×cant drop in blood pressure while the individual is
asleep, thus reducing optic disc perfusion and potentially causing
visual ¯Ç×eld deterioration. ¯ÇØe IOP-lowering e¯Çøect is also believed
to be less marked during sleep, as nocturnal aqueous production is
normally less than half the daytime rate. However, a beta-blocker
may be preferred under some circumstances, such as monocular
treatment to avoid the cosmetic disadvantage of the asymmetrical
periocular skin darkening and/or conjunctival hyperaemia with
prostaglandins. Beta-blockers are also preferred in conditions such
as ocular in¯Ç£ammation and cystoid macular oedema, or where
there is a history of herpes simplex keratitis.

Side e¯Ç×ects

¯Çü Ocular. Ocular side e¯Çøects are few but include allergy and
punctate keratitis. Granulomatous uveitis has been reported
with metipranolol.

{ Promotion of herpetic keratitis can occur, so prostaglandins should be used with caution in patients with a history
of the condition.
¯Çü Systemic side effects include occasional headache, precipitation of migraine in susceptible individuals, malaise, myalgia, skin rash and mild upper respiratory tract
symptoms.

410 Medical Treatment of Glaucoma

¯Çü Systemic. ¯ÇØough severe problems are rare, deaths have been
associated with topical beta-blocker use.

{ Bronchospasm. ¯ÇØis may be fatal in asthma or other
reversible airways disease and it is critical to exclude a history of asthma before prescribing a beta-blocker. About
1 in 50 patients without asthma will develop reversible
airways disease requiring treatment within 12 months of
commencing a topical beta-blocker.

{ Cardiovascular. ¯ÇØere is a strong suggestion that cardiovascular mortality is higher in patients taking a topical beta-blocker. E¯Çøects include heart block, bradycardia,
worsening of heart failure and hypotension, induction of
the latter by topical beta-blocker having been reported as a
common cause of falls in elderly patients. ¯ÇØe pulse should
be assessed before prescription. A peripheral vasoconstrictive e¯Çøect means that they should be avoided or used
with caution in patients with peripheral vascular disease,
including Raynaud phenomenon.

{ Unpleasant but less severe side e¯ÇØects include sleep disorders, reduced exercise tolerance, hallucinations, confusion, depression, fatigue, headache, nausea, dizziness,
decreased libido and dyslipidaemia.

{ Alopecia is uncommon but is reversible on stopping the
beta-blocker medication.

## Agents

¯Çü Timolol is available in various forms, including 0.25% and
0.5% solutions used twice daily. ¯ÇØere is no evidence of a clinically signi¯Ç×cant di¯Çøerence in e¯ÇÖcacy between the two solution
concentrations. Gel-forming preparations of 0.1%, 0.25% and
0.5% are used once daily in the morning.
¯Çü Betaxolol 0.25% and 0.5% twice daily has a lower hypotensive
e¯Çøect than timolol. However, optic nerve blood ¯Ç£ow may be
increased due to a calcium-channel blocking e¯Çøect. Betaxolol
is relatively cardioselective (beta-1 receptors) and is therefore
less likely to cause bronchoconstriction.
¯Çü Levobunolol is a long-acting beta-blocker and is used once or
twice daily with a similar pro¯Ç×le to timolol.
¯Çü Carteolol twice daily is similar to timolol and also exhibits
intrinsic sympathomimetic activity. It has a more selective
action on the eye than on the cardiopulmonary system and so
may have a lower systemic side e¯Çøect incidence.
¯Çü Metipranolol twice daily is similar to timolol but has been
linked with granulomatous anterior uveitis.

## Topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitors

## Introduction

¯ÇØe carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (CAI) are chemically related
to sulfonamide antibiotics. ¯ÇØey lower IOP by inhibiting aqueous secretion and via the topical route are used three times daily
as monotherapy or twice daily as adjunctive treatment. In general, they are slightly less e¯Çøective than beta-blockers but may
have a supplementary neuroprotective e¯Çøect. ¯ÇØey precipitate
corneal decompensation in patients with corneal endothelial

dysfunction. Idiosyncratic bone marrow suppression can occur.
¯ÇØough cross-reaction is uncommon, topical (and systemic)
CAI are relatively contraindicated in patients allergic to sulfonamide antibiotics. Research suggests that concomitant treatment
with a topical and systemic CAI does not usually give an additive
e¯Çøect.

## Agents

¯Çü Dorzolamide. ¯ÇØe main adverse e¯Çøects are stinging and a
transient bitter taste following administration. Allergic blepharoconjunctivitis may occur, but is uncommon (Fig. 11.65D).
¯Çü Brinzolamide is similar to dorzolamide, but is less likely to
cause stinging and local allergy. It is a suspension and a white
residue may be le¯ÇÜ on the eyelids a¯ÇÜer instillation if excess is
not wiped away.

Alpha¯Çƒ2 agonists

## Introduction

Ocular alpha-2 receptor stimulation decreases aqueous synthesis
via an e¯Çøect on the ciliary epithelium and increases uveoscleral
out¯Ç£ow. ¯ÇØere is probably a neuroprotective e¯Çøect. ¯ÇØis medication crosses the bloodÔÇôbrain barrier and should be used with great
caution in young children, in whom severe CNS depression and
hypotension have been reported (contraindicated under the age of
2 years). ¯ÇØey may potentiate vascular insu¯ÇÖciency. ¯ÇØey should
not be given with oral monoamine oxidase inhibitor antidepressants due to the risk of hypertensive crisis.

## Agents

¯Çü Brimonidine 0.2% twice daily as a single agent generally has a
slightly less marked IOP-lowering e¯Çøect than timolol. Allergic
conjunctivitis (Fig. 11.65E) is common, but the onset may be
delayed for up to 18 months a¯ÇÜer commencement of therapy.
Granulomatous anterior uveitis can occur, but is rare (Fig.
11.65F). Systemic side e¯Çøects include xerostomia and fatigue,
the latter sometimes being severe. A brimonidine preparation, Alphagan P, containing a proprietary preservative, Purite,
has been introduced as an alternative to the more common
benzalkonium-containing forms and may have greater ocular
surface tolerability.
¯Çü Apraclonidine 1% (or 0.5%) is used principally to prevent
or treat an acute rise in IOP following laser surgery on the
anterior segment. ¯ÇØe 0.5% concentration is typically used as
a temporizing measure over the course of several weeks, such
as whilst a patient is awaiting glaucoma surgery. It is not suitable for long-term use because of a loss of therapeutic e¯Çøect
over weeks to months and a high incidence of topical side
e¯Çøects.

## Miotics

## Introduction

## Miotics are cholinergic agonists that are predominantly used

in the treatment of angle closure, though they were formerly a

mainstay of the treatment of open-angle glaucoma. ¯ÇØey can
be useful in patients with pseudophakic or aphakic glaucoma.
In angle-closure glaucoma, miotic-induced contraction of the
sphincter pupillae pulls the peripheral iris away from the trabecular meshwork, opening the angle. Miotics also reduce IOP
by contraction of the ciliary muscle, which increases the facility
of aqueous out¯Ç£ow through the trabecular meshwork. Local side
e¯Çøects include miosis, brow ache, myopic shi¯ÇÜ and exacerbation
of the symptoms of cataract. Visual ¯Ç×eld defects appear denser
and larger. Systemic side e¯Çøects are rare, but include confusion,
bradycardia, bronchospasm, gastrointestinal symptoms and urinary frequency.

## Agents

¯Çü Pilocarpine 0.5%, 1%, 2%, or 4% solution as four times daily
monotherapy is equal in e¯ÇÖcacy to beta-blockers. Pilocarpine gel (Pilogel) 4% (discontinued in the UK in 2011) is
instilled once daily at bedtime so that induced myopia and
miosis are predominantly con¯Ç×ned to sleep. Gel, or drops
twice daily, may be used to prevent angle closure following
laser iridotomy in the presence of a substantial non-pupillary
block element.
¯Çü Carbachol is an alternative to pilocarpine.

## Combined preparations

Combined preparations with similar ocular hypotensive e¯Çøects to
the sum of the individual components improve convenience and
patient adherence. ¯ÇØey are also more cost e¯Çøective. Proprietary
examples include:
¯Çü Cosopt: timolol and dorzolamide, administered twice daily.
¯Çü Xalacom: timolol and latanoprost, once daily.
¯Çü Timpilo: timolol and pilocarpine, twice daily.
¯Çü Combigan: timolol and brimonidine, twice daily.
¯Çü DuoTrav: timolol and travoprost, once daily.
¯Çü Ganfort: timolol and bimatoprost, once daily.
¯Çü Taptiqom: timolol and ta¯Ç£uprost, once daily.
¯Çü Azarga: timolol and brinzolamide, twice daily.
¯Çü Simbrinza: brimonidine and brinzolamide: the only combination that does not contain the beta-blocker timolol, administered twice daily.

## New topical medications

¯Çü Latanoprostene bunod 0.024% (Vyzulta) is a new FDAapproved topical medication that reduces IOP significantly. It has a dual mechanism of action: the latanoprost
component increases outflow through the uveoscleral
route and the butanediol mononitrate component undergoes further metabolism in the anterior chamber to produce nitric oxide, which has an effect on the trabecular
meshwork leading to enhanced aqueous outflow. It is
used once daily and reduces the IOP more effectively
than latanoprost alone. It has similar side effects to other
prostaglandins.

¯Çü Rho¯Çá kinase (ROCK) inhibitors are a novel class of topical
medication that increase out¯Ç£ow of aqueous through the trabecular meshwork. ¯ÇØe drops are instilled once daily and are
e¯Çøective in combination with latanoprost (Rocklatan). Rhokinase inhibitors are slightly more e¯Çøective than latanoprost
in reducing the IOP.

## Systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitors

## Introduction

Systemically administered CAI are generally used for short-term
treatment, particularly in patients with acute glaucoma. Because
of their systemic side e¯Çøects, long-term use is reserved for patients
at high risk of visual loss. Sulfonamide (ÔÇÿsulfaÔÇÖ) allergy is a relative
contraindication.

## Agents

¯Çü Acetazolamide is available as 250 mg tablets (250ÔÇô1000
mg daily in divided doses), sustained-release 250 mg capsules (250ÔÇô500 mg daily) and 500 mg powder vials for
injection (single dose, typically used in acute angle-closure
glaucoma).
¯Çü Dichlorphenamide 50 mg tablets (50ÔÇô100 mg two or three
times daily).
¯Çü Methazolamide 50 mg tablets (50ÔÇô100 mg two or three times
daily). ¯ÇØis has a longer duration of action than acetazolamide
but is less widely available.

Side e¯Ç×ects

¯Çü Ocular. Choroidal e¯Çøusion, particularly a¯ÇÜer cataract surgery.
Angle closure may result.
¯Çü Systemic. Paraesthesia (ÔÇÿpins and needlesÔÇÖ sensation in
the extremities), hypokalaemia (reduced blood potassium
level ÔÇô common), malaise and lowered mood, gastrointestinal symptoms, renal stones, StevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome
(very rare), dose-related bone marrow suppression, idiosyncratic aplastic anaemia (exceptionally rare but with 50%
mortality).

## Osmotic agents

## Introduction

Osmotic agents lower IOP by creating an osmotic gradient
so that water is ÔÇÿdrawn outÔÇÖ from the vitreous into the blood.
They are employed when a short-term reduction in IOP is
required that cannot be achieved by other means, such as in
resistant acute angle-closure glaucoma or when the IOP is very
high prior to intraocular surgery. They are of limited value in
inflammatory glaucoma, in which the integrity of the bloodÔÇô
aqueous barrier is compromised. Side effects include cardiovascular overload as a result of increased extracellular volume
(caution in patients with cardiac or renal disease), urinary
retention (especially elderly men), headache, backache, nausea
and confusion.

412 Laser Treatment of Glaucoma

## Agents

¯Çü Mannitol is given intravenously (1 g/kg body weight or 5 ml/
kg body weight of a 20% solution in water) over 30ÔÇô60 minutes, with a peak action within 30 minutes.
¯Çü Glycerol is an oral agent (1 g/kg body weight or 2 ml/kg body
weight of a 50% solution) with a sweet and sickly taste and can
be given with lemon (not orange) juice to avoid nausea. Peak
action occurs within 1 hour. Glycerol is metabolized to glucose
and careful monitoring with insulin cover may be required if
administered to a (well-controlled only) diabetic patient.
¯Çü Isosorbide is a metabolically inert oral agent with a minty
taste, using the same dose as glycerol. It may be safer for diabetic patients.

## Other systemic therapeutic options

¯Çü Trials have commenced to determine whether regular supplementary nicotinamide (also known as niacinamide, the watersoluble active form of vitamin B3) is helpful in patients with
glaucoma.
¯Çü Losartan, an angiotensin-receptor blocker, may have a neuroprotective role.

LASER TREATMENT OF GLAUCOMA

## Laser trabeculoplasty

## Introduction

Laser trabeculoplasty (LTP) involves the delivery of laser to the
trabecular meshwork with the aim of enhancing aqueous out¯Ç£ow
and thereby lowering IOP.
¯Çü Selective laser trabeculoplasty (SLT) has increased in
popularity over recent years and is now widely performed.
Based on the results of the LiGHT trial, SLT has been recommended as ¯Ç×rst-line treatment in patients with ocular
hypertension and primary open angle glaucoma. Individuals
in this trial who underwent SLT ¯Ç×rst (rather than drops ¯Ç×rst)
were less likely to show disease progression and less likely
to require trabeculectomy or phacoemulsi¯Ç×cation with IOL
implantation 6 years a¯ÇÜer diagnosis. A 532 nm frequencydoubled, Q-switched Nd:YAG laser is used to selectively
target melanin pigment in TM cells, leaving non-pigmented
structures unscathed. It is probably similar in e¯ÇÖcacy to
medical monotherapy and argon laser trabeculoplasty (see
below). ¯ÇØe mechanism is incompletely understood, but possibly includes stimulation of TM cell division, macrophage
and extracellular matrix recruitment. Laser application is
made easier by a broad targeted and treated area (Fig. 11.66,
le¯ÇÜ), which may lead to more consistent results. Reported
protocols (e.g. 180┬░ or 360┬░ TM treatment) and results vary
markedly, but IOP reductions of 10ÔÇô40% can be expected
a¯ÇÜer 6 months in responsive patients, with 25% being common. Probably around two-thirds of patients will achieve a
reasonable IOP fall within 6 months of 180┬░ TM treatment.

## In patients with ocular hypertension and early glaucoma

argon (right) and subthreshold (micropulse) laser trabeculoplasty.

approximately 80% will be drop free and at target pressure
at 3 years. ¯ÇØe e¯Çøects generally wane over time, but because
there is no thermal tissue damage, treatment can be repeated
with a successful outcome, even if initial treatment has been
unsuccessful. ¯ÇØe prior use of topical glaucoma medication
does not seem to a¯Çøect results. Energy delivered to the TM
is much lower than with argon laser and complications are
relatively mild but include transient mild in¯Ç£ammation with
mild discomfort, PAS formation and IOP elevation. ¯ÇØe latter is usually mild but substantial rises have been reported,
especially in heavily pigmented angles, for which overtreatment should be avoided. ¯ÇØe concern has been raised that the
extensive treated area causes damage to corneal endothelial
cells, with rare reports of endothelial decompensation. Herpes simplex keratitis reactivation has been reported, as has
macular oedema.
¯Çü Argon laser trabeculoplasty (ALT) is a long-established
procedure that uses laser burns to achieve IOP reduction
comparable to SLT. ¯ÇØere is an extensive body of published
research reporting good outcomes. Mechanisms are likely to
overlap with those of SLT and there may also be a mechanical opening of the trabecular spaces. As the TM sustains
thermal damage, repeat treatment is of limited bene¯Ç×t and
is infrequently performed. Complications include peripheral
anterior synechiae, acute elevation of IOP (should be monitored carefully over subsequent weeks in patients with severe
glaucomatous damage), cystoid macular oedema and anterior uveitis (usually mild). ¯ÇØere is concern that there may
be an adverse e¯Çøect on the outcome of subsequent ¯Ç×ltration
surgery.
¯Çü Micropulse laser trabeculoplasty (MLT) is a relatively new
modality that uses extremely short duration pulses of laser to
deliver thermal energy to the TM to stimulate cells without
damage. Unlike SLT and ALT, there is no visible tissue reaction. A smaller area is targeted than in SLT (Fig. 11.66, right),
limiting potential collateral e¯Çøects on adjacent tissue. Initial
results suggest a benign safety pro¯Ç×le with results comparable
to other LTP forms.

## Indications

¯Çü Type of glaucoma. LTP can be used in primary and secondary
open-angle glaucoma (including primary, pseudoexfoliative
and pigmentary) and can also be used in ocular hypertension.
¯Çü Primary therapy. As SLT has increasingly demonstrated a
favourable safety pro¯Ç×le, its use as a primary alternative to
topical medication has increasingly been recommended.
¯Çü Failure of adherence to the medical regimen.
¯Çü Adjunctive treatment to avoid polypharmacy.
¯Çü Intolerance of topical medication including allergy.
¯Çü Failure of medical therapy, as a less aggressive treatment
measure than surgery.

## Technique

¯Çü LTP is performed under topical anaesthesia.
¯Çü A drop of apraclonidine or brimonidine is instilled 30ÔÇô60
minutes pre-procedure with the aim of preventing or minimizing an early post-laser IOP rise. A similar drop is instilled
post-procedure.
¯Çü Some practitioners instil a drop of pilocarpine prior to the procedure, particularly if the angle is not wide. ¯ÇØere is likely to
be greater potential for PAS formation in narrow angles, particularly with ALT, and this should be borne in mind when
considering a particular patientÔÇÖs suitability for LTP.
¯Çü A goniolens is inserted with the mirror at the 12 oÔÇÖclock position and the inferior angle is visualized.
¯Çü ALT: initial settings are commonly 50 ╬╝m spot size, 0.1 s duration and 700 mW power (range of 400ÔÇô1200 mW, largely
dependent on angle pigmentation). ¯ÇØe aiming beam is
focused at the junction of the pigmented and non-pigmented
TM ensuring that the spot is round and has a clear edge. ¯ÇØe
optimal reaction is a very light blanching or the appearance of
a minute gas bubble. If the reaction is inadequate, the power is
increased by 50ÔÇô200 mW. Fi¯ÇÜy burns are applied at regularly
spaced intervals over 180┬░ of the angle. Many practitioners
apply initial treatment of 180┬░ of the angle, treating the other
180┬░ if the initial response is unsatisfactory. Primary treatment
of the entire circumference is associated with a higher risk of
IOP spikes. Topical ¯Ç£uorometholone or prednisolone 0.5%
four times daily for 1 week is prescribed post-laser.
¯Çü SLT: a common initial power setting is 0.8 mJ. As with ALT,
this should be varied depending on angle pigmentation
(range 0.3ÔÇô1.0 mJ). ¯ÇØe spot size and duration are ¯Ç×xed at
400 ╬╝m and 3ns respectively. ¯ÇØe TM is brought into focus
rather than the aiming beam. ¯ÇØe beam is centred on the pigmented TM and then ¯Ç×red, an optimal reaction consisting
of a few tiny (ÔÇÿchampagneÔÇÖ) bubbles with adjustment of the
power higher or lower as required to achieve this. ¯ÇØe number of burns applied is as for ALT. ¯ÇØe total energy used for
SLT is considerably less than for ALT and it is common not
to prescribe any post-laser anti-in¯Ç£ammatory drops, though
non-steroidal or weak steroid drops can be used if signi¯Ç×cant
in¯Ç£ammation occurs.
¯Çü With practice it is possible to perform LTP by continually
rotating the goniolens and applying each burn through the
centre of the mirror. Using this technique, treatment of the

entire inferior half of the angle is accomplished by ¯Ç×rst rotating the lens to one side (e.g. anticlockwise) by 90┬░ whilst applying 25 shots, then returning to the 12 oÔÇÖclock position before
applying an additional 25 shots whilst rotating the lens to the
opposite side (clockwise in this example).
¯Çü An IOP check should be performed 30ÔÇô60 minutes a¯ÇÜer the
laser to exclude a substantial early spike, with further IOP
measurement, treatment and review as appropriate if this
occurs, depending on each patientÔÇÖs risk pro¯Ç×le.
¯Çü Medical glaucoma therapy is generally continued.
¯Çü Follow-up is dependent on the perceived level of risk (usually
1ÔÇô2 weeks).

## Laser iridotomy

## Introduction

## Laser iridotomy is used principally in the treatment of primary

angle closure, but may also be indicated in secondary angle closure
with pupillary block. It is also sometimes performed in pigment
dispersion syndrome, though its e¯Çøectiveness in this scenario
remains under investigation.

## Technique

¯Çü A topical anaesthetic agent is instilled.
¯Çü Apraclonidine or brimonidine is given prophylactically as for
LTP.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe pupil is constricted with topical pilocarpine (e.g. one drop
of 2%).
¯Çü A special iridotomy contact lens (e.g. Abraham [Fig. 11.67A],
Volk MagPlus) is inserted.
¯Çü Many practitioners target a site under the upper eyelid between
11 and 1 oÔÇÖclock (Fig. 11.67B), though some prefer 3 or 9 oÔÇÖclock
(Fig. 11.67C). ¯ÇØe highest risk of monocular dysphotopsia or
glare (see below) occurs when an iridotomy is half-covered
by the lid margin. Radially, the iridotomy should be located
within the outer third in order to reduce the risk of damage to
the crystalline lens. Targeting an iris crypt, if present, is usually associated with much easier achievement of an adequate
opening.
¯Çü It is important to remember that e¯Çøective power settings vary
somewhat between machines. ¯ÇØe spot size and duration
are ¯Ç×xed. Most iridotomies are made with power settings of
4ÔÇô5mJ. For a thin blue iris, the typical required energy level is
2ÔÇô4mJ. ¯ÇØe risk of crystalline lens damage increases with settings of more than 5mJ. Some practitioners prefer single pulse
shots, others shots of up to three pulses.
¯Çü Pre-treatment with thermal (argon or diode) laser is often
required in thick dark irides. Suitable parameters include
power of 600ÔÇô900 mW using a small spot size of 50 ╬╝m
and relatively short duration of 0.03ÔÇô0.05 s, though larger,
lower power, longer duration settings can be equally
effective.
¯Çü The beam is focused precisely, and the laser fired. Successful penetration is characterized by a gush of pigment debris.
The number of shots required to produce an adequate iridotomy is very variable. The optimal size is 150ÔÇô200 ╬╝m.

414 Laser Treatment of Glaucoma

A B

C D

¯Çü Overtreatment should be avoided due to the risk of substantial postoperative in¯Ç£ammation and a pressure spike. Further
treatment can be applied a¯ÇÜer a few days if the iridotomy is not
patent. In urgent circumstances moving to a di¯Çøerent site is a
reasonable alternative.
¯Çü A second drop of apraclonidine is instilled following the procedure. Oral acetazolamide may also be given in patients at
high risk, such as those with advanced glaucomatous damage
or high IOP pre-treatment.
¯Çü A potent topical steroid (e.g. dexamethasone 0.1%) is prescribed post-procedure. Varying regimens have been described,
with a limited evidence base for the optimal approach. Instillation every 10 minutes for a half hour followed by hourly for
6ÔÇô8 hours immediately post-laser is commonly used and signi¯Ç×cantly reduces the risk of in¯Ç£ammation and posterior synechiae. Four times daily for 1 week is a reasonable subsequent
regimen, particularly in patients with a lightly pigmented iris.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe IOP should be checked 1ÔÇô2 hours a¯ÇÜer the procedure
to exclude an early spike. Routine review is usually at 1 or 2
weeks, with subsequent monitoring according to individual
circumstances. Patients with marked glaucomatous damage

may require extended ocular hypotensive cover and earlier
review.

## Complications

¯Çü Bleeding occurs in around 50% but is usually mild and stops
a¯ÇÜer only a few seconds. Persistent bleeding can be terminated
by increasing contact lens pressure.
¯Çü IOP elevation. Usually early and transient but occasionally
persistent.
¯Çü Iritis. Especially if excessive laser is applied or post-laser steroid therapy is inadequate, or in darker irides (including those
due to prostaglandin derivative treatment) (Fig. 11.67D).
¯Çü Corneal burns may occur if a contact lens is not used or
if the AC is shallow, but these usually heal rapidly without
sequelae.
¯Çü Cataract. Localized lens opacities occasionally develop at the
treatment site. ¯ÇØere is some evidence that age-related cataract
formation may be accelerated by iridotomy.
¯Çü Linear dysphotopsia (unwanted optical images) may occur
regardless of the location, size of the iridotomy or the amount
of laser energy used, but usually settles without intervention.

## Diode laser cycloablation

Diode laser ablation (cyclodiode) lowers IOP by destroying part of
the secretory ciliary epithelium, thereby reducing aqueous secretion. More than one treatment session is commonly required for
adequate IOP control because ciliary epithelium can regenerate.
Diode laser (810 nm wavelength) is applied to the ciliary processes
using either a trans-scleral technique (trans-scleral cyclophotocoagulation; TS-CPC) or using an endoscope (ECP). In the past it
was used mainly in uncontrolled end-stage secondary glaucoma
with minimal visual potential, mainly to control pain. However,
its use in eyes with good vision, especially those with a poor prognosis for penetrating drainage surgery, has been well described
over recent years. More than one treatment session is commonly
required for adequate pressure control. Moderate post-procedure
pain and anterior segment in¯Ç£ammation are common. A temporary IOP rise is not uncommon during the ¯Ç×rst few weeks. Serious complications are rare but include chronic hypotony, phthisis
bulbi, suprachoroidal haemorrhage, corneal decompensation and
retinal detachment.

## Indications

¯Çü Raised IOP in patients with poor vision.
¯Çü Pain relief in a blind eye with raised IOP.
¯Çü In patients with uncontrolled secondary glaucoma (e.g. neovascular glaucoma or malignant glaucoma).
¯Çü In patients where glaucoma ¯Ç×ltration surgery or drainage
implantation is unlikely to be successful or technically di¯ÇÖcult
(e.g. in the presence of conjunctival scarring).

Technique of trans-scleral
cyclophotocoagulation

¯Çü A sub-Tenon or peribulbar anaesthetic is administered.
¯Çü Laser settings are 1.5ÔÇô2 s and 1500ÔÇô2000mW; the spot size is
¯Ç×xed.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe power is adjusted over sequential shots until a ÔÇÿpoppingÔÇÖ
sound is heard and then reduced to just below that level.

¯Çü Approximately 12ÔÇô24 burns are placed posteriorly to the
limbus over 360┬░, avoiding the neurovascular bundles at
3 and 9 oÔÇÖclock (Fig. 11.68). Fewer shots (e.g. treatment of
only one or two quadrants) can be used for eyes with good
vision, in order to reduce the risk of complications. However, more treatment sessions are likely to be required using
this approach.
¯Çü A strong topical steroid is prescribed hourly on the day of
treatment and then 2-hourly for 2 days and four times daily
for at least 2 weeks. A topical antibiotic and a cycloplegic (e.g.
cyclopentolate 1% twice daily) are used for 3 days.
¯Çü Pre-laser glaucoma treatment may be continued, or reduced
slightly.
¯Çü Oral non-steroidal anti-in¯Ç£ammatory agents may be prescribed for 2 days.
¯Çü Review is generally a¯ÇÜer 1ÔÇô4 days, depending on risk, to
exclude signi¯Ç×cant reactive in¯Ç£ammation and/or an IOP spike.

Technique of endo-cyclophotocoagulation

¯Çü A sub-Tenon or peribulbar anaesthetic is administered.
¯Çü Laser settings: 0.2W, continuous wave.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe probe can be inserted either at the limbus or through
the pars plana. In the limbal approach a cohesive viscoelastic is placed behind the iris and anterior to the lens to deepen
the ciliary sulcus space. In a pseudophakic eye a pars plana
approach is preferable.
¯Çü ¯ÇØree to four quadrants are treated.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe power is titrated to achieve whitening and shrinkage of
the ciliary processes by positioning the probe either closer or
further from the processes (Fig. 11.69).
¯Çü Post-procedural management as above.

## Complications

¯Çü Moderate post-procedural pain and anterior segment in¯Ç£ammation is common.
¯Çü A temporary IOP rise may occur during the ¯Ç×rst few weeks.
¯Çü Visual reduction in 6ÔÇô13% of patients.

A B

application.

416 Trabeculectomy

¯Çü Conjunctival burn may rarely be a consequence of TS-CPC if
the eye becomes dry during the procedure or in the presence
of subconjunctival blood.
¯Çü Fibrinous uveitis, hyphaema and cystoid macular oedema may
follow ECP in approximately 5% of cases.
¯Çü Serious complications are rare and include chronic hypotony,
phthisis bulbi, suprachoroidal haemorrhage and serous retinal
detachment.
¯Çü ¯ÇØere are single reports of scleritis and sympathetic ophthalmitis following this treatment.

## Laser iridoplasty

## Laser iridoplasty is performed to widen the anterior chamber angle
by contraction of the peripheral iris away from the angle recess

(Fig. 11.70A). It can be used to attempt to break an episode of acute
angle closure, but is more commonly applied on an elective basis,
for example in plateau iris syndrome (Fig. 11.70B and C). Complications tend to be mild, but heavy treatment can be associated with
a substantial and persistent IOP spike that may be potentiated by
heavy iris pigmentation. Altered accommodation is fairly common
but almost always transient.

## Technique

¯Çü A topical anaesthetic is instilled.
¯Çü One drop each of 1% pilocarpine and 1% apraclonidine is
instilled.
¯Çü Via an iridotomy lens, 1ÔÇô2 burns per clock hour are applied to
the periphery, 500 ╬╝m size, 100ÔÇô400mW, 0.2ÔÇô0.5 s duration,
aiming for slight visible iris contraction.
¯Çü Post-procedure 1% apraclonidine is given (oral prophylaxis ÔÇô
e.g. acetazolamide ÔÇô may be given if signi¯Ç×cant glaucomatous
optic neuropathy is present).
¯Çü Topical ketorolac, prednisolone 1% or dexamethasone 0.1%
four times daily for a week is a common regimen.
¯Çü Review is typically 1ÔÇô2 hours post-laser, then a¯ÇÜer 1 week and
subsequently depending on progress and glaucomatous damage ÔÇô patients with signi¯Ç×cant glaucomatous neuropathy may
need frequent review for the ¯Ç×rst few weeks to exclude an IOP
spike.

B

C

TRABECULECTOMY

Trabeculectomy is glaucoma ¯Ç×ltration surgery that lowers IOP by
creating a ¯Ç×stula, protected by a super¯Ç×cial scleral ¯Ç£ap, to allow
aqueous out¯Ç£ow from the anterior chamber to the sub-Tenon
space (Fig. 11.71).

## Indications

¯Çü Failure of conservative therapy to achieve adequate IOP
control.
¯Çü Progressive deterioration despite seemingly adequate IOP
control (including poor adherence to the recommended medical regimen).
¯Çü Primary therapy. Advanced disease requiring a very low target pressure may achieve a superior long-term outcome from
early surgery, particularly in younger patients.
¯Çü Patient preference. Occasionally patients express a strong desire
to be free of the commitment to chronic medical treatment.

## Technique

Numerous modi¯Ç×cations are in use. ¯ÇØe following description
relates predominantly to the classical procedure. By placing the
incisions at 12 oÔÇÖclock there is less likelihood of bleb dysaesthesia.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe pupil is constricted preoperatively (e.g. pilocarpine 2%).
¯Çü A bridle suture is inserted (commonly superior cornea or
superior rectus muscle).
¯Çü A limbal or fornix-based ¯Ç£ap of conjunctiva and Tenon capsule is fashioned superiorly.
¯Çü Episcleral tissue is cleared and major vessels cauterized.
¯Çü Incisions are made through about 50% of scleral thickness, to
create a ÔÇÿtrapdoorÔÇÖ lamellar scleral ¯Ç£ap (Fig. 11.72A). ¯ÇØis ¯Ç£ap
may be rectangular (3 ├ù 3ÔÇô4 mm), trapezoidal or triangular,
according to preference.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe super¯Ç×cial ¯Ç£ap is dissected forwards until clear cornea is
reached (Fig. 11.72B).
¯Çü A paracentesis is made in temporal peripheral clear cornea.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe AC is entered along most of the width of the trapdoor base.
¯Çü A block of deep sclera is excised, usually using a punch (e.g.
Kelly ÔÇô Fig. 11.72C).
¯Çü A peripheral iridectomy is created to prevent blockage of
the internal sclerostomy (Fig. 11.72D). Some surgeons omit

this step in pseudophakic eyes, but there remains a small
risk of iris prolapse into the sclerostomy site if that option
is adopted.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe super¯Ç×cial scleral ¯Ç£ap is sutured at its posterior corners,
either so that it is lightly opposed to the underlying bed or
tightly closed with releasable or lysable sutures to reduce the
risk of postoperative leakage. Some surgeons insert a suture
into each of the radial edges to reduce the risk of a substantial
lateral leak (Fig. 11.72E).
¯Çü Balanced salt solution is injected through the paracentesis to
deepen the anterior chamber and to test the patency of the ¯Ç×stula (see Fig. 11.72E).
¯Çü Conjunctiva/Tenon capsule ¯Ç£ap is sutured (Fig. 11.72F). Irrigation through the paracentesis is repeated to produce a bleb,
which is checked for leakage.
¯Çü A drop of atropine 1% is instilled. When no iridectomy has
been performed, pilocarpine 2% may be used instead.
¯Çü Steroid and antibiotic are injected under the inferior
conjunctiva.
¯Çü Steroid and antibiotic drops are used four times daily for 2
weeks and then changed to steroid alone for a further 8ÔÇô12
weeks.

Ex¯ÇƒPress mini¯Çƒshunt

This is a valveless titanium MRI-compatible stent inserted
under a scleral flap during a modified trabeculectomy, with a
principal aim of standardization of drainage (Fig. 11.73). Following creation of the scleral flap as for a standard trabeculectomy, a needle is used to enter the anterior chamber instead of
creating a punch sclerostomy. A peripheral iridectomy is not
performed. The rate of complications such as hypotony and
hyphaema is lower than with standard trabeculectomy, but IOP
control is equivalent. It is not viewed as suitable in primary
angle-closure glaucoma without prior or contemporaneous
cataract surgery.

E F

super¯Çøcial scleral ¯Ç£ap; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× excision of deep scleral tissue with a punch; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× peripheral iridectomy; ¯ÇØE¯Ç× injection of balanced salt solution into the anterior chamber; ¯ÇØF¯Ç× appearance following suturing of a fornix-based ¯Ç£ap.

Antimetabolites in ¯Çøltration surgery

## Indications

## Adjunctive antimetabolites inhibit the natural healing response

that may preclude successful ¯Ç×ltration surgery. ¯ÇØey should be

used with caution because of potential complications and are usually considered in the presence of risk factors for surgical failure. In
uncomplicated glaucoma the use of low-dose antimetabolites may
improve long-term control of IOP.
¯Çü Risk factors for surgical failure:

{ Previous failed trabeculectomy or MIGS.

{ Previous conjunctival or cataract surgery.

{ Secondary glaucoma (e.g. in¯Ç£ammatory, neovascular,
post-traumatic).

{ Demographic: Black race, age under 65 years.

{ Patients on topical medication (particularly sympathomimetics) for over 3 years.

¯Çü Postoperative subconjunctival injection of 0.1 ml of 25 mg/
ml or 50 mg/ml solution can be used. Placement may be
away from the ¯Ç×stula, even at the opposite limbus. Various
regimens are described, including daily injections for several
postoperative days and ad hoc use if a drainage bleb appears
to be unduly vascularized or ¯Ç×brotic. It is also o¯ÇÜen used as
an adjunct to a limited ÔÇÿneedlingÔÇÖ revision of a trabeculectomy (see below).

Mitomycin C

Mitomycin C (MMC) is an alkylating agent that inhibits proliferation of ¯Ç×broblasts and suppresses vascular ingrowth. It is more
potent than 5-FU. It is generally used intraoperatively in the manner described above for 5-FU, a typical exposure protocol being
0.2 mg/ml for 2 minutes, though a higher concentration (e.g. 0.4
mg/ml) may be used for particularly high-risk patients. Higher
concentrations and extended exposure times are associated with
an increased risk of complications. A cystic thin-walled bleb is
common following the use of mitomycin C and may predispose to
chronic hypotony, late-onset bleb leak and endophthalmitis. ¯ÇØe
bleb pro¯Ç×le can be considerably improved by placing the MMCsoaked sponges well away from the limbus.

## Bevacizumab

Bevacizumab is a monoclonal antibody against VEGF that can be
used at the time of trabeculectomy. Intracameral or subconjunctival bevacizumab is more e¯Çøective than placebo in these circumstances, but it appears to increase the risk of bleb encapsulation.
It is not more e¯Çøective than mitomycin C and combining it with
MMC does not appear to improve the success rate.

## Shallow anterior chamber

5-¯ÇØuorouracil

5-¯Ç£uorouracil (5-FU) inhibits ¯Ç×broblast proliferation by retarding DNA synthesis. It is a less aggressive antimetabolite than
mitomycin C (see below), but substantial complications can
still occur, notably persistent corneal epithelial defects and bleb
leakage.
¯Çü Intraoperative use involves the application of one or more
small cellulose sponges soaked in a 50 mg/ml solution,
placed under the dissected flap of TenonÔÇÖs capsule at the site
of filtration for 5 minutes prior to creation of the scleral
trapdoor.

A shallow anterior chamber (Fig. 11.74A) following trabeculectomy may be due to pupillary block, over¯Ç×ltration or malignant
glaucoma. Severe and sustained shallowing is uncommon, the

A B

¯ÇØB¯Ç× cataract following a ¯Ç£at anterior chamber.

420 Trabeculectomy

that do not may develop severe complications such as peripheral
anterior synechiae, corneal endothelial damage and cataract (Fig.
11.74B).

## Pupillary block

Pupillary block may occur with a non-patent peripheral iridectomy.
¯Çü Signs

{ High IOP and ¯Ç£at bleb.

{ Negative Seidel test.

{ Iris bomb├® with a non-patent iridectomy.
¯Çü Treatment involves Nd:YAG laser to the pigment epithelium
at the iridectomy site if the anterior iris stroma appears to have
been largely removed (common), or the creation of a new laser
iridotomy.

Over¯Çƒltration

Over¯Ç×ltration may be caused by insu¯ÇÖcient resistance to out¯Ç£ow
at the lamellar scleral ¯Ç£ap, but bleb leakage through an inadvertent
buttonhole or due to inadequate closure of the conjunctiva and
Tenon capsule is more common.
¯Çü Signs

{ Low IOP with a well-formed bleb in a scleral ¯Ç£ap leak and
¯Ç£at in a bleb leak.

{ ¯ÇØe Seidel test is negative in a scleral ¯Ç£ap leak but positive
(Fig. 11.75A) in a bleb leak.

{ ¯ÇØe cornea may show signs of hypotony such as folds in
Descemet membrane.

{ Choroidal detachment (Fig. 11.75B and C) may be present.
¯Çü Treatment depends on the cause and degree of shallowing.

{ Initial management in eyes with mild over¯Ç×ltration such as
may be caused by a small bleb leak, may consist simply of
observation, with atropine to prevent PAS formation.

{ Subsequent treatment if the above measures are ine¯Çøective involves temporary tamponade of the conjunctiva to
enhance spontaneous healing by simple pressure patching, a large diameter so¯ÇÜ bandage contact lens, a collagen
shield or a Simmons shell designed for the purpose.

{ De¯Ç×nitive treatment o¯ÇÜen consists of the insertion of additional conjunctival sutures and if necessary, placement of
a transconjunctival scleral ¯Ç£ap suture. If potentially serious shallowing is present, the anterior chamber can be
reformed with a viscoelastic. A choroidal detachment
rarely requires drainage.

## Malignant glaucoma

Malignant glaucoma is a rare but serious complication of trabeculectomy in patients with primary angle closure and primary angleclosure glaucoma. It is caused by anterior rotation of the ciliary
processes and iris root (ciliolenticular block) leading to a posterior
misdirection of aqueous.
¯Çü Signs

{ Shallow anterior chamber with myopic shi¯ÇÜ. Patients
will usually notice an improvement in their unaided near
vision.

¯Çü Treatment

{ Initial treatment is with mydriatics (atropine 1% and phenylephrine 10%) to dilate the ciliary ring and to increase the
distance between the ciliary processes and the equator of

A

B

C

wide-¯Çøeld image of choroidal detachment; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× ultrasound of
choroidal detachment in a hypotonous eye.

{ High IOP and absent bleb.

{ Negative Seidel test.

the lens, thereby tightening the zonules and pulling the
lens posteriorly into its normal position. Intravenous mannitol may be used if mydriatics are ine¯Çøective, allowing
shrinkage of the vitreous gel and causing the lens to move
posteriorly.

{ Subsequent treatment if medical therapy fails is with
Nd:YAG laser ¯Ç×red through the iridectomy in order to disrupt the anterior hyaloid face. In pseudophakic eyes, laser
posterior capsulotomy and disruption of the anterior hyaloid face should be performed. Cyclodiode laser may also
be e¯Çøective.

{ Pars plana vitrectomy is performed if laser therapy fails.
¯ÇØe key is to remove su¯ÇÖcient anterior vitreous gel to
allow free ¯Ç£ow of aqueous to the anterior chamber.

distended conjunctival goblet cells. A cystic thin-walled bleb is not
uncommon a¯ÇÜer the use of mitomycin C (Fig. 11.76B). Poor ¯Ç×ltration is indicated by increasing IOP and a bleb with one of the
following appearances:
¯Çü Flat without vascularization.
¯Çü Vascularized bleb (Fig. 11.76C) due to episcleral ¯Ç×brosis.
¯Çü Encapsulated bleb (Tenon cyst) (Fig. 11.76D), characterized
by a localized, highly elevated, dome-shaped, ¯Ç£uid-¯Ç×lled cavity
of hypertrophied Tenon capsule, o¯ÇÜen with engorged surface
blood vessels.

## Causes

Causes of failure can be classi¯Ç×ed according to the site of
obstruction:
¯Çü Extrascleral causes include subconjunctival and episcleral
¯Ç×brosis, sometimes with bleb encapsulation.
¯Çü Scleral causes include over-tight suturing of the scleral ¯Ç£ap
and gradual scarring in the scleral bed.
¯Çü Intraocular causes are uncommon and include blockage of the
sclerostomy by vitreous, blood or uveal tissue or by a variety of
thin membranes derived from surrounding cornea or sclera.

Failure of ¯Çøltration

## Diagnosis

A normally functioning bleb should be slightly elevated, relatively
avascular (Fig. 11.76A) and show super¯Ç×cial microcysts, which are

A B

C D

422 Trabeculectomy

## Management

Management of ¯Ç×ltration failure depends on the cause and may
involve one or more of the following:
¯Çü Ocular ÔÇÿdigital massageÔÇÖ in an e¯Çøort to force out¯Ç£ow through
the surgical ¯Ç×stula may be performed by digital compression
through the upper lid whilst looking downwards. ¯ÇØis can be
carried out by the patient 4ÔÇô8 times a day for up to several
weeks until the bleb is deemed stable.
¯Çü Suture manipulation may be considered 7ÔÇô14 days postoperatively if the eye has high IOP, a ¯Ç£at bleb and a deep anterior
chamber. Releasable sutures can be cut or released according
to the technique of initial placement. Argon or diode laser
suture lysis is useful if releasable sutures have not been used. It
may be performed through a suture lysis lens or a Zeiss fourmirror goniolens.
¯Çü Needling of an encysted bleb may be performed at the slit
lamp or using an operating microscope under topical anaesthesia. It can be augmented with 5-¯Ç£uorouracil or mitomycin
C to enhance the success rate.
¯Çü Subconjunctival injection of 5¯Çá ¯Ç£uorouracil may be used in
the ¯Ç×rst 7ÔÇô14 days to suppress episcleral ¯Ç×brosis. An injection
of 2.5ÔÇô5 mg (0.1 ml of 25ÔÇô50 mg/ml solution) is given using a
30-gauge needle directed away from the ¯Ç×stula and repeated as
necessary. Alternatively, mitomycin C 0.1 ml of a 0.02% solution can be used.

## Late bleb leakage

¯ÇØis occurs due to disintegration of conjunctiva overlying a sclerostomy, typically following peroperative application of antimetabolites, particularly mitomycin C. Necrosis of the surface epithelium
results in transconjunctival drainage of aqueous. Complications of
untreated leaks include infection and hypotony maculopathy (see
Ch. 14).
¯Çü Signs

{ Low IOP and an avascular cystic bleb (see Fig. 11.76B).

## Bleb dysaesthesia

Postoperative ocular discomfort secondary to tear ¯Ç×lm disturbance adjacent to the bleb occurs in approximately 5% of patients
undergoing trabeculectomy. It is more likely to arise in young
patients and in those with a thin-walled nasal-positioned bleb (Fig.
11.77A). ¯ÇØe key sign is a number of small bubbles adjacent to the
bleb, which are best visualized with ¯Ç£uorescein (Fig. 11.77B). ¯ÇØe
symptoms can o¯ÇÜen be relieved with topical lubricants, but surgical revision of the bleb may be needed if symptoms persist.

Bleb¯Çƒassociated bacterial infection and
endophthalmitis

Glaucoma ¯Ç×ltration-associated infection is classi¯Ç×ed as limited
to the bleb (blebitis) or endophthalmitis, although there is some

{ Seidel testing may initially be negative with only multiple

{ A shallow anterior chamber and choroidal detachments
may be present in severe cases.
¯Çü Treatment can be di¯ÇÖcult. ¯ÇØe following are options:

{ Treatment depends on whether the leakage involves merely
ÔÇÿsweatingÔÇÖ or is due to a hole. Sweating blebs may be treated
by injection of autologous blood into the bleb, ÔÇÿcompressionÔÇÖ sutures or a transconjunctival scleral ¯Ç£ap suture, or
sometimes surgery.

{ A full-thickness hole always requires surgical revision.
¯ÇØe easiest option is to remove the existing bleb dissecting backwards from the limbus, followed by conjunctival
advancement and resuturing. A fornix relieving incision may occasionally be needed and a scleral or donor
pericardium gra¯ÇÜ can be used to limit ¯Ç£ow through the
sclerostomy.

the bleb, visible after blinking.

overlap. ¯ÇØe incidence of blebitis following trabeculectomy with
mitomycin has been estimated to be up to 5% per year, though
many studies show a far lower rate. Patients who have undergone trabeculectomy should be warned of the possibility of late
infection and strongly advised to report immediately should they
develop a red and sticky eye, or blurred vision. Preserved lubricating drops tend to get contaminated in time and should be avoided.
¯Çü Risk factors include (a) antimetabolite use, (b) an inferiorplaced bleb and (c) bleb leak. Blepharitis and conjunctivitis are
also risk factors. Late bleb leaks should be treated aggressively
to reduce the risk of infection.
¯Çü Pathogens. ¯ÇØe most frequent are Haemophilus in¯Ç×uenzae,
Streptococcus spp. and Staphylococcus spp. and Gram-negative
organisms. ¯ÇØe poor visual prognosis is related to the virulence of these organisms.

## Blebitis

Blebitis describes infection without vitreous involvement.
¯Çü Symptoms consist of a painful, red, photophobic and typically
sticky eye.

¯Çü Signs

{ A white bleb that appears to contain in¯Ç£ammatory material (Fig. 11.78A).

{ Anterior uveitis may be absent or mild, but may be moderate and a hypopyon may be present.

{ ¯ÇØe red re¯Ç£ex is normal.
¯Çü Treatment

{ Bleb-related infection is an urgent condition that needs
immediate and intensive treatment.

{ A conjunctival swab should be taken. A sample should not
be aspirated from within the bleb.

{ Broad-spectrum topical antibiotics instilled every hour,
e.g. o¯Ç£oxacin and a cephalosporin. ¯ÇØe latter may be prepared from an intravenous ampoule.

{ Oral co-amoxiclav 500/125 mg three times daily and cipro¯Ç£oxacin 750 mg twice daily for at least 5 days. Alternatively, azithromycin 500 mg daily can be used.

{ ¯ÇØe role of topical steroids is unde¯Ç×ned. ¯ÇØeir introduction
may be considered a¯ÇÜer a de¯Ç×nite response to antibiotics.

C D

424 Minimally Invasive Glaucoma Surgery (MIGS)

TIP Bleb-related infection is an urgent condition that needs
immediate and intensive treatment.

## Endophthalmitis

Fistula-related endophthalmitis, even with early treatment, can be
associated with a very poor outcome, including blindness or even
loss of the eye, mainly because the organisms that are isolated are
signi¯Ç×cantly more virulent than those found in infections that follow cataract surgery (Fig. 11.78B). An aggressive treatment regimen should be instituted as soon as possible.
¯Çü Symptoms are generally much more severe than those of
blebitis.
¯Çü Signs

{ White milky bleb, as in blebitis but of greater severity.

{ Severe injection.

{ Severe anterior uveitis; a substantial hypopyon is typical.

{ Vitritis and impairment of the red re¯Ç£ex.
¯Çü Treatment

{ Vitreous and aqueous samples should be obtained immediately on presentation and ce¯ÇÜazidime 2 mg in 0.1 ml
and vancomycin 2 mg in 0.1 ml should be injected into
the vitreous cavity. A¯ÇÜer 48 hours repeat intravitreal antibiotics and consider undertaking a posterior vitrectomy.
Start topical steroids a¯ÇÜer 48 hours. Oral ¯Ç£uoroquinolones
should be used for 10ÔÇô14 days.

{ Topical and systemic therapy as for blebitis (Fig. 11.78C
and D).

Conventional ¯Ç×ltration is therefore still the procedure of choice
when the target IOP is in the low teens though non-penetrating
surgery is probably associated with a lower risk of ÔÇÿsnu¯ÇÖng outÔÇÖ
central vision when advanced damage is present.

## Technique

¯Çü Deep sclerectomy (Fig. 11.79). A Descemet window is created to allow aqueous migration from the AC. Subsequent
egress is subconjunctival, resulting in a shallow ¯Ç×ltration bleb,
as well as along deeper suprachoroidal routes. ¯ÇØe long-term
results can be enhanced by using a collagen implant at the
time of surgery and postoperative application of Nd:YAG laser
to the meshwork at the surgical site using a gonioscopy lens
(goniopuncture).
¯Çü Viscocanalostomy involves the creation of a ¯Ç×ltering window,
with identi¯Ç×cation and dilatation of the Schlemm canal with high
density viscoelastic. ¯ÇØe super¯Ç×cial scleral ¯Ç£ap is sutured tightly so
that subconjunctival ¯Ç£uid out¯Ç£ow and bleb formation are minimized. ¯ÇØe procedure probably causes inadvertent microscopic
ruptures in the juxtacanalicular tissue and meshwork.

MINIMALLY INVASIVE GLAUCOMA
SURGERY (MIGS)

MIGS is the term applied to the various implants and techniques
that aim to lower IOP with less surgical risk than traditional glaucoma surgery. MIGS is commonly combined with cataract surgery
and is not suitable for all forms of glaucoma.
Current procedures can be classi¯Ç×ed into three groups:
¯Çü Surgery that avoids the formation of a bleb by manipulating
the Schlemm canal, either by excision of trabecular meshwork
(Trabectome, Kahook Dual Blade) or by bypassing the trabecular meshwork (iStent inject or Hydrus Microstent) (Fig.
11.80A and B), or by dilation of the canal (ab-interno canaloplasty with iTrack).
¯Çü Implants that result in drainage under Tenon capsule and
conjunctiva, leading to the formation of a bleb (Xen, Preser¯Ç£o,
InnFocus MicroShunt) (Fig. 11.80C). Mitomycin C (0.02%
0.1 ml) is usually injected under the conjunctiva adjacent to
the implant to reduce the risk of bleb ¯Ç×brosis. Bleb needling is
o¯ÇÜen required postoperatively.
¯Çü Surgery that increases aqueous out¯Ç£ow from the anterior
chamber into the supraciliary space. (MINIject). An abinterno approach is used and a 5-mm long silicone implant consisting of thousands of interconnected hollow spheres is placed
into the supraciliary space, protruding 0.5mm into the anterior
chamber. No bleb is created and an anti-metabolite is not used.

## Indications

¯Çü Mild to moderate glaucoma, where the rate of visual ¯Ç×eld
loss is slow and the target pressure goal is modest (aiming for
15ÔÇô17 mmHg).
¯Çü In selected cases combined with phacoemulsi¯Ç×cation and IOL
implantation, to reduce the need for topical medication.

TIP The organisms involved in ¯Çøstula-related endophthalmitis
tend to be more virulent than after cataract surgery. Vitreous
and aqueous samples should be obtained immediately on
presentation.

NON-PENETRATING GLAUCOMA
SURGERY

## Overview

In non-penetrating ¯Ç×ltration surgery, the anterior chamber is not
entered and the internal trabecular meshwork is preserved, thus
reducing the incidence of postoperative over¯Ç×ltration with hypotony and its potential sequelae. Two concentric lamellar scleral ¯Ç£aps
are fashioned and the deep ¯Ç£ap excised leaving behind a thin membrane consisting of trabeculum/Descemet membrane through
which aqueous di¯Çøuses from the AC to the subconjunctival space.
¯ÇØe surgery is technically challenging and requires meticulous dissection of a deep scleral ¯Ç£ap to avoid entering the anterior chamber
through the delicate Descemet membrane.

## Indications

¯ÇØe main indication for non-penetrating surgery is POAG,
although other open-angle glaucomas may also be amenable. In
general, the IOP reduction is less than that achieved by trabeculectomy, so that topical medication o¯ÇÜen needs to be recommenced.

A B

C D

¯Ç£ap; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× dissection into clear cornea exposing the Schlemm canal; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× collagen implant;
¯ÇØD¯Ç× shallow di¯ÇÜuse avascular bleb. (Courtesy of A Mermoud.)

## Complications

¯Çü In those procedures that involve manipulation of the Schlemm
canal there is a risk of implant malposition, haemorrhage,
infection and late corneal decompensation.
¯Çü In the procedures that depend on the formation of a bleb, the
complications that follow trabeculectomy may occur. Because
insertion of a micro-stent is involved, malposition and erosion
of the stent can occasionally be seen.
¯Çü In procedures involving the placement of an implant into the
supraciliary space there is a small risk of late corneal endothelial cell loss.
¯Çü Late failure of MIGS increases the risk of bleb ¯Ç×brosis should
trabeculectomy be subsequently required to control the IOP.

## Results of surgery

¯Çü ¯ÇØe results of MIGS are biased by the fact that this surgery is
o¯ÇÜen undertaken at the same time as cataract surgery, which
independently reduces the IOP by about 15%.

¯Çü Meta-analysis of ÔÇÿstand-aloneÔÇÖ iStent implantation results
reveals an overall IOP reduction of about 30% from baseline
for up to 5 years a¯ÇÜer surgery.
¯Çü Two years a¯ÇÜer ÔÇÿstand-aloneÔÇÖ MIGS (Xen implant or goniotomy/canaloplasty) one in four patients need further surgery
to control the IOP.
¯Çü ¯ÇØe Horizon study of the Hydrus Microstent concludes that
the stent is safe, less likely to result in visual ¯Ç×eld loss and is
more e¯Çøective at lowering the IOP with fewer medications
than phacoemulsi¯Ç×cation and IOL implantation alone, at 5
years.

DRAINAGE SHUNTS

## Shunts using episcleral explants

## Introduction

Glaucoma drainage devices (GDD) create a communication
between the anterior chamber and the sub-Tenon space via a

426 Drainage Shunts

A

B

C

(Courtesy of G Ratnarajan ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

tube attached to a posteriorly explanted episcleral reservoir (Fig.
11.81). Some contain pressure-sensitive valves for the regulation
of aqueous ¯Ç£ow. Reduction of IOP is due to passive, pressuredependent ¯Ç£ow of aqueous, limited by the wall of a tissue capsule
that forms around the explant over the course of several weeks

postoperatively. In recent years the use of GDD has increased, with
a large trial, the Tube Versus Trabeculectomy Study, providing
good-quality evidence of their safety and comparability to mitomycin C-enhanced trabeculectomy. Five years a¯ÇÜer tube surgery
the IOP reduction, rates of surgical failure and visual loss are similar to those found a¯ÇÜer MMC-enhanced trabeculectomy, but more
postoperative medication is needed to control the IOP in those
receiving a tube. In practice the threshold for GDD implantation
has been lowered, though the number of patients undergoing trabeculectomy remains signi¯Ç×cantly higher than those undergoing
GDD implantation. Examples of GDD implants include:
¯Çü Molteno3. ¯ÇØis consists of a silicone tube connected to one
(137 mm [2] ) or two (274 mm [2] ) polypropylene plates 13 mm in
diameter.
¯Çü Baerveldt. ¯ÇØis implant consists of a silicone tube connected
to a silicone plate of large area (250 mm [2] or 350 mm [2] ) (Fig.
11.81A). Silicone may elicit little tissue reaction in comparison
with polypropylene.
¯Çü Ahmed. ¯ÇØis is a valved implant consisting of a silicone tube
connected to a silicone sheet valve held in a polypropylene
body (184 mm [2] ) (Fig. 11.81B). ¯ÇØe valve mechanism consists
of two thin silicone elastomer membranes, with the aim of
reducing early postoperative hypotony and its complications.
¯ÇØe implant needs to be primed by injecting saline through the
valve before the surgery is undertaken.
¯Çü PAUL. ¯ÇØis is a new valveless GDD. A silicone tube (with a
signi¯Ç×cantly smaller tube diameter than other devices), is
attached to a footplate which has an area of 344mm [2] (Fig.
11.81C). ¯ÇØe footplate is longer and not as wide as the Baerveldt footplate.

## Indications

¯ÇØe circumstances under which GDD may be superior to trabeculectomy are incompletely de¯Ç×ned and many factors must
be considered, including an individual surgeonÔÇÖs experience and
expertise. GDD may be considered in the following situations:
¯Çü Severe conjunctival scarring precluding accurate dissection
of the conjunctiva.
¯Çü Uncontrolled glaucoma despite previous trabeculectomy
with adjunctive antimetabolite therapy.
¯Çü Secondary glaucoma where routine trabeculectomy, with or
without adjunctive antimetabolites, is less likely to be successful. Examples include aniridia, neovascular glaucoma, ICE
syndrome and glaucoma following traumatic anterior segment
disruption.
¯Çü Certain types of congenital glaucoma where conventional
procedures have failed.

## Complications

¯ÇØe rate of serious complications is similar to that of mitomycin
trabeculectomy.
¯Çü Excessive drainage, resulting in hypotony and a shallow anterior chamber.
¯Çü Bleb encapsulation. ¯ÇØis is characterized by raised IOP 1ÔÇô6
weeks a¯ÇÜer aqueous enters the area of the footplate. Clinically
an in¯Ç£amed thick-walled bleb can be seen over the footplate.

A B C

D E

It is more common a¯ÇÜer the insertion of an Ahmed valve than
a non-valved tube.
¯Çü Malposition (Fig. 11.82A) may result in endothelial or lenticular touch with corneal decompensation and cataract,
respectively. Ciliary sulcus or pars plana tube placement
can be used in some eyes to negate the possibility of corneal
touch.
¯Çü Tube erosion through the sclera and conjunctiva (Fig. 11.82B).
¯Çü Corneal decompensation due to endothelial cell loss.
¯Çü Early drainage failure may occur as a result of blockage of the
end of the tube by vitreous, blood or iris.
¯Çü Late drainage failure occurs in about 10% of cases per year
and is comparable to, or perhaps slightly better than, that following trabeculectomy (Fig. 11.82C).
¯Çü Double vision due to extraocular muscle interference
(Fig. 11.82D). This is more likely to occur if the footplate

is placed in the upper nasal quadrant and is less likely to
occur with the PAUL GDD because it is not placed under
the rectus muscles.

## Results

¯ÇØe results depend on the type of glaucoma. In general, an IOP
in the mid-teens is achieved, but topical medication is frequently
required in the medium and longer term. ¯ÇØe Ahmed Baerveldt
Comparison (ABC) study (where the average preoperative IOP
was 30 mmHg) showed an average postoperative IOP of 14.7
mmHg in the Ahmed group and 12.7 mmHg in the Baerveldt
group at 5 years. ¯ÇØe long-term success rate in some conditions
such as neovascular glaucoma and ICE syndrome is poor. Adjunctive mitomycin C may enhance the success rate of drainage shunt
surgery.

428 Drainage Shunts

A B

C D

encapsulation and vascularization over the footplate associated with failure of drainage;
¯ÇØD¯Ç× acquired Brown syndrome (left eye).

430 Clinical Features

CLASSIFICATION

¯Çƒe Standardization of Uveitis Nomenclature (SUN) Working
Group guidance on uveitis terminology, endorsed by the International Uveitis Study Group (IUSG), categorizes uveitis anatomically (Fig. 12.1):
¯Çü Anterior: the anterior chamber is the primary site of
in¯Ç×ammation.
¯Çü Intermediate: primarily vitreous in¯Ç×ammation including pars
planitis.
¯Çü Posterior: retina and/or choroid.
¯Çü Panuveitis: all uveal structures are involved.
An IUSG clinical classi¯ÇØcation based on aetiology is also in
use:
¯Çü Infectious: bacterial, viral, fungal, parasitic, others.
¯Çü Non-infectious: with and without a known systemic
association.
¯Çü Masquerade: neoplastic and non-neoplastic.
¯Çƒe SUN Working Group guidance includes the following
descriptions relating to the timing of in¯Ç×ammatory activity:
¯Çü Onset: sudden or insidious.
¯Çü Duration: limited (3 months or less) or persistent.
¯Çü Clinical course: acute (of sudden onset and limited duration),
recurrent (repeated episodes separated by untreated inactive
periods), or chronic (persistent duration, with relapse less
than 3 months a¯Ç£er discontinuation of treatment). Remission
is de¯ÇØned as inactivity (no visible cells) for 3 months or longer.

CLINICAL FEATURES

## Anterior uveitis

## Introduction

Anterior uveitis is in¯Ç×ammation involving the anterior uveal tract ÔÇô
the iris and the anterior part (pars plicata) of the ciliary body ÔÇô and
is the most common form of uveitis. Iritis refers to in¯Ç×ammation

## Intermediate

primarily involving the iris and iridocyclitis to involvement of

both the iris and anterior ciliary body. In practice these are interchangeable as they cannot be distinguished clinically.
Acute anterior uveitis (AAU) is the most common presentation,
of which HLA-B27-related and idiopathic forms make up the largest
proportion. Aetiology in these cases is uncertain, but may involve
cross-reactivity with particular microbial antigens in genetically
predisposed individuals. Interleukin-6, which is released by monocytes and macrophages in response to other in¯Ç×ammatory cytokines, plays a key role in the aberrant immune response that drives
uveitis. AAU can be a feature of a wide variety of ocular conditions
such as trauma (including surgery), lens-related in¯Ç×ammation and
herpes simplex infection, or can be secondary to in¯Ç×ammation
elsewhere in the eye, such as bacterial keratitis and scleritis. AAU
can also be the presenting clinical scenario, without accompanying
intermediate or posterior uveitis, in a range of systemic conditions
including chronic in¯Ç×ammatory disorders such as sarcoidosis.
Chronic anterior uveitis (CAU) is less common than AAU. It is
more commonly bilateral and associated systemic disease is more
likely. Granulomatous in¯Ç×ammatory signs (see below) are o¯Ç£en
present.

## Surveys of systemic associations of anterior uveitis vary in

their ¯ÇØndings. Table 12.1 lists important possibilities, but is not
exhaustive.
¯Çƒe prognosis is usually good in most idiopathic and HLAB27-related AAU provided management is adequate. Outcomes
are more variable in CAU and in cases where there is an underlying ocular or systemic disorder.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms in AAU consist of the rapid onset of unilateral
pain, visual loss, photophobia, redness and watery discharge,

## Anterior

## Posterior

sometimes preceded by mild ocular discomfort for a few days.
As recurrent disease is common, especially with the idiopathic
and HLA-B27-related types, there will o¯Ç£en be a history of
previous similar episodes. CAU may be of insidious or acute
onset and can be asymptomatic until the development of complications such as cataract.
¯Çü Visual acuity is variably impaired depending on the severity
of in¯Ç×ammation and the presence of complications. It is frequently only mildly reduced in AAU.
¯Çü ÔÇÿCiliary injectionÔÇÖ (perilimbal injection, ciliary ¯Ç×ush or just
ÔÇÿinjectionÔÇÖ) is circumcorneal conjunctival hyperaemia with a
violaceous (purplish) hue due to involvement of deeper blood
vessels (Fig. 12.2A) and is typically seen in anterior uveitis of
acute onset. Ciliary injection is characteristically absent in
some forms of CAU and occasionally AAU.
¯Çü Miosis due to pupillary sphincter spasm (Fig. 12.2B) predisposes to the formation of posterior synechiae (see below).
¯Çü Anterior chamber cells are a dependable indicator of in¯Ç×ammatory activity. Grading (SUN Working Group) is performed
by estimating the number of cells in a 1 mm by 1 mm slit
beam ¯ÇØeld, employing adequate light intensity and magni¯ÇØcation (Table 12.2). ¯Çƒis must be performed before pupillary
dilatation, which can lead to shedding of pigment cells into
the aqueous. Swept-source anterior segment optical coherence tomography is a sensitive method of showing individual
in¯Ç×ammatory cells (Fig. 12.2C). Cells are commonly also seen
in the anterior vitreous.
¯Çü Aqueous ¯Çƒare is haziness of the normally clear ¯Ç×uid in the
anterior chamber, re¯Ç×ecting the presence of protein due to
breakdown of the bloodÔÇôaqueous barrier. Based on work in
children with juvenile idiopathic arthritis-associated CAU, it
is now thought that in most or all patients the presence of ¯Ç×are
indicates active in¯Ç×ammation with a resultant higher risk of
complications over the longer term. Flare may be graded clinically using a slit lamp to assess the degree of interference with
visualization of iris and lens (Table 12.3). When available, laser
¯Ç×are photometry gives greater objectivity.
¯Çü Hypopyon (Fig. 12.2D) refers to a whitish purulent exudate
composed of myriad in¯Ç×ammatory cells in the inferior part of
the anterior chamber (AC), forming a horizontal level under
the in¯Ç×uence of gravity. Hypopyon is common in HLA-B27associated AAU (see below), when a high ¯ÇØbrin content makes
it immobile and slow to absorb. In patients with Beh├ºet disease
the hypopyon contains minimal ¯ÇØbrin and so characteristically
shi¯Ç£s according to the patientÔÇÖs head position.
¯Çü Keratic precipitates (KP) are deposits on the corneal endothelium composed of in¯Ç×ammatory cells such as lymphocytes,
plasma cells and macrophages (Fig. 12.3A and B). ¯Çƒey are
usually concentrated inferiorly, o¯Ç£en in a triangular pattern
with the apex pointing up (Arlt triangle) under the in¯Ç×uence
of gravity and aqueous convection currents. A notable exception is Fuchs uveitis syndrome, in which they are di¯Çøusely
distributed. ¯Çƒeir characteristics indicate the probable type
of uveitis: smaller in the non-granulomatous in¯Ç×ammation
typical of AAU and medium to large in (classically chronic)
granulomatous in¯Ç×ammation in which cell types may include

epithelioid and multinucleated cells. Large greasy-appearing
granulomatous KP are said to have a ÔÇÿmutton fatÔÇÖ appearance
(Fig. 12.3C). KP are small to medium and adopt a star-shaped
(ÔÇÿstellateÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 12.3D) or ¯ÇØlamentous morphology in Fuchs
uveitis syndrome. KP usually resolve as acute in¯Ç×ammation
subsides: longstanding non-granulomatous KP may become
pigmented. Granulomatous KP may become pigmented (Fig.
12.3E) and/or assume a ÔÇÿground glassÔÇÖ appearance. Endothelial
dusting by numerous individual cells precedes the formation
of true KP aggregates (Fig. 12.3F).
¯Çü Fibrinous exudate in the AC (Fig. 12.4A and B) is common
in severe AAU and as with hypopyon is o¯Ç£en seen with HLAB27-related in¯Ç×ammation.
¯Çü Iris nodules can occur in both granulomatous and nongranulomatous anterior uveitis (Fig. 12.5A). Busacca nodules
involve the iris stroma (Fig. 12.5B) and are a feature of granulomatous uveitis. Koeppe nodules are located on the pupillary
margin and may be the site of posterior synechiae formation
(see below) (Fig. 12.5B and C). Yellowish nodules can develop
from dilated iris vessels (roseolae) in syphilitic uveitis. Iris
ÔÇÿpearlsÔÇÖ may be seen in lepromatous CAU. Iris crystals (Russell
bodies), thought to consist of immunoglobulin deposits, are
a rare ¯ÇØnding in some cases of chronic uveitis (Fig. 12.5D),
including Fuchs uveitis syndrome.
¯Çü Posterior synechiae (PS) are in¯Ç×ammatory adhesions between
the pupil margin and the anterior lens capsule (Fig. 12.6A and
B) and may be particularly likely to form at the location of a
Koeppe nodule. ¯Çƒey can develop rapidly and to prevent their
formation initial prophylaxis with a mydriatic agent is routine
in all but very mild AAU. Once established, every attempt
must be made to break PS before they become permanent (Fig.
12.6C and D).
¯Çü Iris atrophy may o¯Çøer useful diagnostic clues. Di¯Çøuse stromal atrophy is seen in Fuchs uveitis syndrome and patchy
or sectoral atrophy can occur in herpetic uveitis (Fig. 12.7).
Both patterns may be seen in both simplex and zoster-related
in¯Ç×ammation, though the latter is said to more commonly give
a sectoral pattern.
¯Çü Heterochromia iridis refers to a di¯Çøerence in colour between
the iris of the two eyes, best seen in daylight. In the context of
uveitis, heterochromia characteristically occurs in Fuchs uveitis syndrome (see also Table 12.7).
¯Çü Iris neovascularization (rubeosis iridis) can occur, particularly in chronic in¯Ç×ammation. ¯Çƒe process tends to be
less acute than with a primary vascular cause such as central
retinal vein occlusion. Abnormal iris vessels are very common
in Fuchs uveitis syndrome, but do not cause synechial angle
closure. Iris neovascularization may also occur in posterior
uveitis, particularly when retinal perfusion is compromised.
New iris vessels may be di¯ÇÜcult to di¯Çøerentiate from dilated
normal vessels (sometimes called ÔÇÿpseudo-rubeosisÔÇÖ); normal
vessels course radially in contrast to the irregular distribution
of neovascularization. Fluorescein angiography may show
leakage from new vessels, though this can also be seen with
dilated normal vessels, particularly in the presence of active
in¯Ç×ammation.

¯Çü Posterior segment examination should always be performed
to detect a masquerading cause of anterior uveitis (e.g. retinal
detachment, tumour), primary intermediate or posterior segment in¯Ç×ammation and complications of anterior uveitis such
