## Posterior uveitis

Barry.)

Posterior uveitis encompasses the clinical entities of retinitis, choroiditis and retinal vasculitis. Some lesions may originate primarily in the retina or choroid but o¯Ç£en there is involvement of both
(retinochoroiditis and chorioretinitis). Speci¯ÇØc conditions will be
discussed later in the chapter.
¯Çü Presentation varies according to the location of the in¯Ç×ammatory focus and the presence of vitritis. For example, a patient
with a peripheral lesion may complain of ¯Ç×oaters, whereas a
patient with a lesion involving the macula will predominantly
complain of impaired central vision.
¯Çü Retinitis may be focal (solitary), multifocal, geographic or
di¯Çøuse. Active lesions are characterized by whitish retinal
opacities with indistinct borders due to surrounding oedema
(Fig. 12.8A). As the lesion resolves, the borders become better
de¯ÇØned.
¯Çü Choroiditis may also be focal, multifocal, geographic or
di¯Çøuse. It does not usually induce vitritis in the absence of

436 Investigations

A

B

C

¯Ç£C¯ÇØ vasculitis.

concomitant retinal involvement. Active choroiditis is characterized by a round, yellow nodule (Fig. 12.8B).
¯Çü Vasculitis may occur as a primary condition or as a secondary phenomenon adjacent to a focus of retinitis. Both arteries (periarteritis) and veins (periphlebitis) may be a¯Çøected,
although venous involvement is more common. Active vasculitis is characterized by yellowish or grey-white, patchy,
perivascular cu¯ÇÜng (Fig. 12.8C) sometimes associated with
haemorrhage. Quiescent vasculitis may leave perivascular
scarring, which should not be mistaken for active disease.

INVESTIGATIONS

¯Çü Tailor the investigations. Investigations are o¯Ç£en negative,
with no clear underlying cause determined in many patients.
Rather than performing a battery of screening tests, investigation is tailored to each patient, directed by clinical features.
Sometimes a likely cause may be obvious, such as severe anterior uveitis following intraocular surgery when endophthalmitis will lead the di¯Çøerential diagnosis list. In most cases, a
careful review for systemic symptoms is essential to detect any
clues to underlying disease, with referral to a specialist physician for further assessment where appropriate. Many associations of uveitis can present with a wide range of systemic
features.
¯Çü Multimodal imaging of the posterior segment provides rapid,
objective and accurate assessment of the retina and choroid
and has become important in the management and follow-up
of patients with posterior uveitis. Widespread use of this technology is improving standards of care in clinical practice, as
well as de¯ÇØning outcome measures in uveitis research.

## Investigation is generally not required in the following

circumstances:
¯Çü A single episode of unilateral mild/moderate (no hypopyon)
non-granulomatous AAU with no ocular or systemic suggestion of underlying disease.
¯Çü A systemic diagnosis compatible with the clinical features
(e.g. sarcoidosis) has already been con¯ÇØrmed.
¯Çü Typical clinical features of a speci¯ÇØc entity for which investigation is not usually indicated (e.g. Fuchs uveitis syndrome).
Situations in which investigation of anterior uveitis is generally appropriate include:
¯Çü Recurrent AAU.
¯Çü Severe AAU.
¯Çü Bilateral AAU.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis that is persistent, chronic or resistant to
treatment.
¯Çü Granulomatous in¯Ç×ammatory signs (note that granulomatous
conditions may give non-granulomatous AAU).
¯Çü Associated intermediate or posterior uveitis.
¯Çü Ocular or systemic clinical features suggesting underlying
disease.

## Repeating targeted previously negative investigations several

years later is sometimes helpful.
¯Ç×e following investigations should be considered:
¯Çü HLA tissue typing (HLA-B27). ¯Çƒe major histocompatibility complex (MHC) is a group of genes involved in white
cellÔÇôantigen interaction and other immune functions, including the encoding of cell surface glycoproteins. In humans the
MHC, found on chromosome 6, is called the human leukocyte
antigen (HLA) system. HLA typing is used to determine organ
transplantation compatibility and can also indicate predisposition to particular diseases. It has conventionally been performed by serological antigen identi¯ÇØcation, but increasingly
involves analysis of DNA. HLA-B27 is a common (e.g. 6ÔÇô8% of
the White population in the USA, 0.5% of patients of Japanese
ethnic origin) cell surface protein that presents peptides to T

cells. ¯Çƒe phenotype has a very strong association with AAU,
ankylosing spondylitis and some other in¯Ç×ammatory conditions such as reactive arthritis (Reiter syndrome), psoriatic
arthritis and arthritis in in¯Ç×ammatory bowel disease. It is present in 50% of patients with AAU who are otherwise ¯ÇØt and well
and 90% of patients with AAU who have an associated spondyloarthropathy, notably ankylosing spondylitis. Many HLA-B27
subtypes have been identi¯ÇØed and their signi¯ÇØcance is subject
to ongoing investigation. HLA types associated with ocular
in¯Ç×ammatory disease are listed in Table 12.4. HLA-B27 testing
should be performed in any adult or child with recurrent or
chronic non-granulomatous anterior uveitis.
¯Çü Syphilis serology

{ Treponemal antibody tests such as the ELISA (enzymelinked immunosorbent assay) are highly sensitive and speci¯ÇØc, but take around 3 months to become positive.

{ Non-speci¯ÇØc titratable cardiolipin antibody tests such as
the rapid plasma reagin (RPR) or venereal disease research
laboratory (VDRL) are more commonly positive in early
infection and are used to help monitor disease activity.
¯Çƒese tests become negative over time, typically in treated
disease. False-positive results can occur.

{ Both categories of test should be performed when screening for ocular syphilis.

¯Çü Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP): acute-phase reactants that are probably of limited
value, but may be elevated in a range of systemic in¯Ç×ammatory
disorders.
¯Çü Complete blood count: leukocytosis may raise suspicion
of infection and, exceptionally, haematological malignancy.
Eosinophilia may occur in parasitic infection.
¯Çü Lyme disease: serology may be considered, particularly in
endemic areas. Serology for other infectious diseases such as
brucellosis and leptospirosis can be requested if relevant risk
factors are present (e.g. endemic region).
¯Çü Antinuclear antibody (ANA): limited use, except in children.
In those with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) its presence is
associated with a higher risk of CAU. Cases of possible subclinical JIA have been reported in ANA-positive children with
CAU.
¯Çü Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA): limited
use in anterior uveitis unless associated with scleritis and/
or peripheral ulcerative keratitis, when cytoplasmic ANCA
(c-ANCA) testing should be considered as evidence of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener granulomatosis).
¯Çü Interferon-gamma release assay (e.g. QuantiFERON-TB
Gold) blood test for tuberculosis.
¯Çü HIV serology: indicated for selected patients, usually those
in whom an opportunistic infection has been diagnosed or is
suspected.
¯Çü Sacroiliac joint X-ray may show evidence of sacroiliitis in ankylosing spondylitis and other seronegative
spondyloarthropathies.
¯Çü Chest X-ray may show evidence of sarcoidosis or tuberculosis.
A high level of suspicion for both of these treatable conditions
should always be adopted, particularly if the in¯Ç×ammation is
granulomatous.
¯Çü Fundus auto¯Çƒuorescence (FAF) is a non-invasive technique
that detects lipofuscin and melanin in retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) cells. ¯Çƒis provides information on RPE function and the integrity of the chorioretinal interface and is
extremely helpful when assessing in¯Ç×ammatory conditions
of the choriocapillaris. Pronounced auto¯Ç×uorescence can be
detected in patients with multiple evanescent white dot syndrome (MEWDS) (see Fig. 12.60C), serpiginous choroidopathy (see Fig. 12.62D) and acute zonal occult outer retinopathy
(AZOOR) (see Fig. 12.68A).
¯Çü Optical coherence tomography (OCT). ¯Çƒis technique
obtains precise, reproducible, high-resolution images that correlate well with the histopathology. OCT can be used to detect
and monitor macular oedema, vitreomacular surface disease
and structural damage to the macula (see Fig. 14.49). Inner retinal in¯ÇØltrates that show hyperre¯Ç×ective focal retinal thickening are seen in Beh├ºet disease and some viral infections. Outer
retina changes are seen in punctate inner choroidopathy (PIC),
MEWDS, acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment epitheliopathy (APMPPE), serpiginous choroiditis, AZOOR and acute
macular neuroretinopathy (AMN) (see Fig. 12.67B). OCT can
be used to visualize choroidal granulomas (e.g. sarcoidosis and
tuberculosis) and primary choroidal in¯Ç×ammatory diseases

{ Clinical features suggesting a diagnosis of syphilis should
prompt urgent referral to a physician specializing in infectious or sexually transmitted diseases.
¯Çü Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE): a nonspeci¯ÇØc test that indicates the presence of a granulomatous
disease such as sarcoidosis, tuberculosis and leprosy. Elevation
occurs in up to 80% of patients with acute sarcoidosis but may
be normal during remissions. In normal children serum ACE
levels tend to be higher and diagnostically less useful. Vigorous
exercise can elevate ACE.
¯Çü Lysozyme is a group of enzymes found in polymorphonuclear
neutrophils and numerous secretions including tears. It has a
strong antibacterial action, mediating breakdown of the bacterial cell wall. Serum lysozyme assay is generally slightly less
sensitive and speci¯ÇØc than serum ACE in the diagnosis of sarcoidosis, but performing both tests may increase sensitivity
and speci¯ÇØcity.

438 Treatment

like acute VogtÔÇôKoyanagiÔÇôHarada (VKH) syndrome (see Fig.
12.18E and F) and sympathetic ophthalmia.
¯Çü Fluorescein angiography (FA; see Ch. 14). ¯Çƒe ¯Ç×uorescein molecule di¯Çøuses easily out of minimally in¯Ç×amed vessels (retinal vasculitis; see Fig. 12.32B) and can also be used
to demonstrate occlusive vasculopathy (see Fig. 12.54B). In
in¯Ç×ammatory eye disease FA is used to assess optic disc swelling and subretinal ¯Ç×uid accumulation (see Fig. 12.32D). FA is
the investigation of choice when imaging in¯Ç×ammatory retinal foci, showing early hypo¯Ç×uorescence and late hyper¯Ç×uorescence. Loss of the RPE shows up as a ÔÇÿwindow defectÔÇÖ and
pigment clumping gives a masking e¯Çøect, as seen for example
in APMPPE (see Fig. 12.61B). Chorioretinal atrophy is shown
as a hyper¯Ç×uorescent area.
¯Çü Indocyanine green angiography (ICGA; see Ch. 14). Indocyanine green angiography (ICGA) is rarely indicated in
anterior uveitis, but is the investigation of choice in patients
with predominantly choroidal in¯Ç×ammation. Because the
ICG molecule is almost completely protein-bound, retinal
leakage only occurs when there is major in¯Ç×ammation in
these vessels (e.g. in Beh├ºet disease). However, in the choroid
the protein-bound ICG leaks slowly, but unimpaired. A¯Ç£er
recirculation, more and more ICG is entrapped and gradual
impregnation of the choroid occurs. In the late phases active
absorption of ICG by the RPE results in di¯Çøuse background
hyper¯Ç×uorescence. Alteration of choroidal impregnation is
the main parameter studied by ICGA in in¯Ç×ammatory eye
disease. Two broad categories of in¯Ç×ammation are seen: (a)
choriocapillaris in¯Ç×ammation, where there is patchy hypo¯Ç×uorescent areas of di¯Çøerent sizes, especially in the late images,
e.g., APMPPE (see Fig. 12.61E) and serpiginous chorioretinopathy (see Fig. 12.62F) and (b) stromal choroiditis, where
there are foci of hypo¯Ç×uorescence, followed by late di¯Çøuse
hyper¯Ç×uorescence as a consequence of leakage from large
choroidal vessels. ¯Çƒis can be a consequence of primary choroidal in¯Ç×ammation (e.g. VKH, sympathetic ophthalmia) or
secondary choroidal in¯Ç×ammation (e.g. sarcoidosis, tuberculosis and syphilis).
¯Çü Ultrasound biomicroscopy (UBM) is particularly indicated
in cases of hypotony and may demonstrate pathology such as
subtle choroidal e¯Çøusion, cyclodialysis cle¯Ç£ and cyclitic membrane. B-scan ultrasonography can be helpful if the posterior
segment view is compromised by a very small pupil or opaque
media.
¯Çü Aqueous tap: historically rarely performed in anterior uveitis,
but viral causes are now recognized as being more frequent
than previously realized. Herpesviruses (including cytomegalovirus) and rubella should be considered in clinically suspicious cases, especially unexplained hypertensive uveitis and
cases relatively unresponsive to topical steroids. An aqueous
humour sample can be sent for: (a) polymerase chain reaction
(PCR) analysis, to check for evidence of viral genetic material
and (b) for microscopy, culture and antibody assay. PCR may
also help to exclude Propionibacterium infection in a chronically in¯Ç×amed pseudophakic eye.
¯Çü Iris biopsy is rarely performed.

¯Çü Vitreous biopsy tends to be con¯ÇØned to the investigation of
obscure posterior segment in¯Ç×ammation and suspected infectious endophthalmitis.
¯Çü Chorioretinal biopsy. To determine the cause of a persistent
and severe posterior uveitis of unknown aetiology for which
all workup is negative, consider undertaking a retinochoroidal
biopsy.

TIP Multimodal imaging using OCT and indocyanine green
angiography is indispensable in the investigation of patients
with predominantly choroidal in¯ÇØammation.

¯Çü Conjunctival biopsy: sampling of tissue such as a suspected
granuloma or in¯ÇØltrative lesion is occasionally indicated.
¯Çü Referral to a specialist physician with resultant further
investigation. ¯Çƒis is vital when systemic disease is suspected.
For instance, if respiratory symptoms are present, a chest physician may arrange additional testing such as a high-resolution
computed tomography (CT) chest scan, a whole-body gallium scan (sarcoidosis), a puri¯ÇØed protein derivative skin
test for tuberculosis (negative test is a diagnostic indicator in
sarcoidosis) and bronchoscopy with lavage/biopsy. Similarly,
neurological referral may lead to cranial magnetic resonance
imaging (MRI) and lumbar puncture, and gastroenterological
referral to endoscopy.

TREATMENT

For patients with a treatable cause of in¯Ç×ammation such as an
infection, speci¯ÇØc treatment (see individual topics) is given either
instead of or in addition to the general anti-in¯Ç×ammatory measures discussed below. Review frequency is set according to the
severity and chronicity of in¯Ç×ammation. Patients with severe
in¯Ç×ammation may need to be seen within a day or two of initiating treatment. ¯Çƒose with mild recurrent idiopathic AAU may not
need to be seen for several weeks a¯Ç£er treatment is commenced.
¯Çü Topical steroids

{ Prednisolone 1% or dexamethasone 0.1% is commonly
utilized as a ¯ÇØrst choice. Other preparations, of varying
availability geographically, include di¯Ç×uprednate 0.05%
(may be administered at a lower frequency), loteprednol
etabonate 0.2% and 0.5% (moderate to marked potency
but a lower tendency to elevate IOP), betamethasone,
prednisolone 0.5%, ¯Ç×uorometholone and rimexolone (the
latter three are of moderate to lower potency). ¯Çƒe selection of topical steroid preparation can be modi¯ÇØed according to severity and other factors such as a known tendency
to IOP elevation. Steroid ointment (e.g. betamethasone)
may be instilled at bedtime to supplement the drops. Additional anti-in¯Ç×ammatory treatment (see below) is necessary in some cases.

{ Treatment of AAU initially involves instillation at a frequency appropriate to the severity of in¯Ç×ammation, typically starting with one drop hourly in moderateÔÇôsevere
cases. Once the in¯Ç×ammation is controlled the instillation

frequency should be carefully tapered. A commonly
adopted regimen might consist of:
¯Çü one drop hourly for 3 days, then
¯Çü every 2 hours for 3 days, then
¯Çü four times a day for 1 week, then
¯Çü three times a day for 1 week, then
¯Çü twice a day for 1 week, then
¯Çü once a day for 1 week and stop. Treatment is o¯Ç£en discontinued by 5ÔÇô6 weeks.

## Periodic review is undertaken as appropriate during the

treatment course, with further assessment a week or two
a¯Ç£er cessation, following which the patient can be discharged but cautioned to re-attend urgently should symptoms recur.

comfort. Constituent quantities vary according to manufacturer but 0.3 ml containing 0.12 mg adrenaline, 1 mg atropine
and 6 mg procaine is typical. It is usually administered by subconjunctival injection. An alternative to injection is insertion
of a cotton pledget soaked in Mydricaine into the superior and
inferior fornices for 5 minutes. Serious cardiovascular events
have been reported following injection (a transient sinus
tachycardia is common) and the patient should be monitored
a¯Ç£er injection. Mydricaine No. 1 is a paediatric version that
may also be e¯Çøective in adults.
¯Çü Tissue plasminogen activator (TPA). In severe ¯ÇØbrinous
anterior uveitis 12.5ÔÇô25 ╬╝g of TPA in 0.1 ml injected into
the AC (intracamerally) with a 30-gauge needle under topical anaesthesia will dissolve dense ¯ÇØbrinous exudate and may
break down recently formed PS. Antiseptic precautions similar
to those for intravitreal injection should be taken.
¯Çü Subconjunctival steroid can be administered in severe cases
or to patients in whom poor compliance is likely. For example,
betamethasone sodium phosphate solution (4 mg in 1 ml) can
be given alone or in a combined preparation with betamethasone acetate suspension for a sustained e¯Çøect (e.g. Celestone,
6 mg in 1 ml).
¯Çü Regional steroid injection. ¯Çƒe use of an inferior approach
(ÔÇÿorbital ¯Ç×oorÔÇÖ) or posterior sub-Tenon (Fig. 12.9) injection
of depot steroid preparations (e.g. triamcinolone acetonide,
methylprednisolone acetate) is common in the treatment of
posterior segment in¯Ç×ammation, but is generally reserved
in anterior uveitis patients for the treatment of cases complicated by cystoid macular oedema (CMO) and for patients
non-compliant with topical administration. Periocular injections may also be administered at the time of surgery and may
rarely be used to supplement systemic therapy or when systemic steroids are contraindicated. ¯Çƒe peak action is at about
4 weeks, with a maximum duration of action of around 3
months. Complications include subconjunctival haemorrhage,
globe penetration, refractory elevation of IOP (up to 25%),
cataract, ptosis, eyelid haemorrhage, eyelid ischaemic necrosis,
retrobulbar haemorrhage, subdermal fat atrophy, extraocular
muscle paresis, optic nerve injury, retinal and choroidal vascular occlusion and cutaneous hypopigmentation. Systemic
adverse e¯Çøects are rare but can occur. Table 12.5 gives injection procedures; there is no clear evidence of the superiority
of one route over the other, but there may be a lower risk of
ocular perforation, of raised IOP and of ptosis with the orbital
¯Ç×oor approach. Utilizing a plastic intravenous cannula via a
superior sub-Tenon route has been described and is thought to
o¯Çøer a lower risk of perforation.
¯Çü Intraocular steroids. Intravitreal triamcinolone acetonide (4
mg in 0.1 ml, i.e. one-tenth of the orbital dose) is occasionally used in anterior uveitis for CMO unresponsive to other
forms of therapy and rarely may be considered at the time
of intraocular surgery in high-risk anterior uveitis patients.
Complications include elevation of IOP, cataract, endophthalmitis (sterile or infectious), haemorrhage, retinal detachment
and pseudohypopyon. Slow-release intravitreal implants may
occasionally be indicated.

{ Treatment of CAU is generally targeted at complete suppression of in¯Ç×ammation, with no AC cellular activity or
¯Ç×are ÔÇô the latter is now thought to be an indicator of active
in¯Ç×ammation in most or all cases. Even low-grade ongoing
activity is associated with a greater incidence of complications that outweighs the risk of complications from treatment. Exacerbations are initially treated in the same way as
AAU, though with more gradual tapering and typically a
maintenance regimen. Fuchs uveitis syndrome is an exception to this approach.

{ Common complications of topical steroids include transient elevation of IOP in susceptible individuals (ÔÇÿsteroid
respondersÔÇÖ). Long-term treatment may lead to permanent
IOP elevation with glaucomatous damage. Cataract can be
induced, but is less common than with systemic steroid
administration. ¯Çƒe risk increases with dose and duration of therapy. Corneal complications are uncommon and
include secondary infection with bacteria and fungi, recrudescence of herpes simplex keratitis and corneal melting.
Systemic side e¯Çøects are rare with topical steroids, but may
occur following prolonged administration, particularly in
children, in whom measures to reduce systemic absorption such as medial canthal pressure and blotting away of
overspill from the eyelids following instillation should be
discussed.
¯Çü Cycloplegic agents. ¯Çƒese are used in AAU and in exacerbations of CAU to prevent the formation of PS, to break down
recently formed synechiae and to promote comfort by relieving spasm of the pupillary and ciliary muscle. Commonly used
anticholinergic agents in order of increasing potency and duration of action include cyclopentolate (duration 12ÔÇô24 hours),
homatropine (3 days) and atropine (7ÔÇô10 days). In the acute
stage, phenylephrine 2.5% or 10% may be used to supplement
anticholinergics and break PS. In mild or CAU, a cycloplegic
can be instilled at bedtime to prevent di¯ÇÜculties with accommodation during the day. In children, care should be taken to
avoid systemic toxicity, as a range of systemic adverse e¯Çøects
have occurred including seizures. Prolonged uniocular cycloplegia may induce amblyopia in the susceptible age group.
¯Çü Mydricaine No. 2. ¯Çƒis is a preparation containing adrenaline
and atropine that is used to try to break fresh PS when drops
are ine¯Çøective. It also contains local anaesthetic to improve

440 Treatment

¯Çü Systemic steroids are very rarely required for anterior uveitis,
but may be needed where the response to topical treatment
is inadequate. ¯Çƒey are sometimes given as a short course
prior to intraocular surgery as prophylaxis against worsening
in¯Ç×ammation, having the advantage of rapid cessation of e¯Çøect
in comparison with depot peri- or intraocular steroid injection, but have major potential adverse e¯Çøects (Table 12.6).
¯Çü Non-steroidal anti-in¯Çƒammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such
as naproxen and tolmetin may be e¯Çøective in CAU and can
be used long-term under appropriate specialist physician
supervision.
¯Çü Antimetabolites such as methotrexate and mycophenolate
mofetil are generally not required in the treatment of anterior
uveitis, though may be necessary in exceptional patients such
as juvenile idiopathic arthritis-associated CAU when other
measures fail to control in¯Ç×ammation, or as a steroid-sparing

C Pavesio.)

Table 12.5 Procedure for Inferior Transseptal and Posterior Sub-Tenon Regional Steroid Injection

Route Technique

Inferior transseptal A topical anaesthetic such as tetracaine (amethocaine) is instilled to prevent stinging by the
(ÔÇÿorbital ¯ÇØoorÔÇÖ) injection antiseptic agents

## The skin of the lower eyelid and maxillary area is cleaned with an antiseptic agent such as an

alcohol swab or povidone-iodine 5%

## The vial containing the steroid is shaken

1 ml steroid (triamcinolone acetonide or methylprednisolone acetate 40 mg/ml) is drawn up
into a 2 ml syringe and the drawing-up needle replaced with a 25-gauge 5/8 inch (16 mm)
needle

## The patient is asked to maintain gaze straight ahead

The needle is inserted through the skin (some practitioners inject via the conjunctiva) at
approximately the junction of the outer third and inner two-thirds of the lower orbital rim,
entering close to the bony margin whilst clearing the margin itself
The needle is slowly advanced tangentially to (or, anatomy permitting, away from) the globe in
similar fashion to a peribulbar local anaesthetic block up to the needle hub
The skin may be indented to ensure the needle tip is su¯Ç£ciently posterior to deposit the
steroid away from the anterior subconjunctival area
The tip may be felt to engage the bony orbital ¯ÇØoor and/or to pierce the orbital septum; as
with the superior injection technique, the needle can be moved from side to side to ensure
the sclera has not been engaged
The plunger is slightly withdrawn and, if no blood enters the syringe, the full 1 ml is slowly
injected, and the needle carefully withdrawn
Special care is required in a patient with a large eye (e.g. myopia) to avoid penetration of the
globe
Posterior sub-Tenon A topical anaesthetic such as tetracaine (amethocaine) is instilled
approach A small cotton pledget impregnated with tetracaine, lidocaine (lignocaine) 2% gel or an
alternative is placed into the superior fornix at the site of injection for 2 minutes

## The vial containing the steroid is shaken

1 ml steroid (triamcinolone acetonide methylprednisolone acetate or 40 mg/ml) is drawn up into
a 2 ml syringe and the drawing-up needle replaced with a 25-gauge 5/8 inch (16 mm) needle

## The patient is asked to look in the direction opposite to the superotemporal injection site

The bulbar conjunctiva is penetrated with the tip of the needle, bevel towards the globe,
slightly on the bulbar side of the fornix
The needle is slowly inserted posteriorly, following the contour of the globe, keeping it as
close to the globe as possible. In order not to penetrate the globe accidentally, wide side-toside motions are made as the needle is being inserted and the limbus watched; movement of
the limbus means that the sclera has been engaged
When the needle has been advanced to the hub the plunger is slightly withdrawn and, if no
blood enters the syringe, the full 1 ml is slowly injected
A method utilizing a plastic intravenous cannula introduced via the same route following
conjunctival incision and limited blunt dissection has been described

TIP In a patient with uveitis, it is important to ensure that there
is no potentially life-threatening systemic disease present or an
infectious cause for the in¯ÇØammation.

IMMUNOMODULATORY THERAPY FOR
NON-INFECTIOUS UVEITIS

## Introduction

¯Çƒis therapy plays an important role in modifying the immune
response and should be supervised by a multidisciplinary team
that includes an ocular in¯Ç×ammatory disease specialist. Shared
decisions should be made on when to start treatment and subsequently, on whether to modify or withdraw treatment. ¯Çƒe
speci¯ÇØc medication to be used should be based on the type of
uveitis and the known e¯ÇÜcacy and safety pro¯ÇØles of the proposed agents. Before starting treatment, careful informed consent should be taken, detailing the advantages and potential side
e¯Çøects of the medication and emphasizing the need for regular
follow-up. Active infections (especially tuberculosis) should be
excluded and haematological, renal and hepatic function should
be checked. ¯Çƒe aim of treatment is to obtain steroid-free remission of the disease.

## Indications

{ ¯Çƒese agents take time to suppress in¯Ç×ammation (2 weeks
to 3 months). Topical and systemic steroids may be needed
initially to control acute in¯Ç×ammation.
¯Çü An approach to severe uveitis when systemic immunosuppression is needed (based on the underlying immunology and
aligning with clinical experience) is as follows:

{ Birdshot retinochoroidopathy.

{ Serpiginous choroidopathy.

{ Multifocal choroiditis and panuveitis.

{ Juvenile in¯Ç×ammatory arthritis.
¯Çü As an alternative to steroids

{ ¯Çƒerapeutic: inadequate response to topical and periocular
steroids or to an oral dose >0.5 mg/kg/day prednisolone;
intolerance of systemic steroids or as a steroid-sparing
measure.

{ Ocular: acute disease that is sight-threatening or chronic
and persistent, exudative retinal detachment or disease
involving the macula.

Side e¯Çƒects of medication

¯Çü ¯Çƒese need to be discussed with the patient and regular monitoring should be undertaken.
¯Çü In the doses used, the commonly prescribed immunosuppressives (steroids, azathioprine, methotrexate, mycophenolate
mofetil, cyclosporin A) do not increase cancer mortality. ¯Çƒe
use of azathioprine monotherapy or anti-tumour necrosis factor (TNF) monotherapy is associated with a small but statistically signi¯ÇØcant risk of lymphoma. ¯Çƒe risk is higher with
combination treatment.
¯Çü As a general rule most of these medications are well tolerated,
but side e¯Çøects like headache, tiredness, gastrointestinal upset,
bone marrow suppression and increased risk of opportunistic
infections have been reported.
¯Çü Individual agents have complications that are speci¯ÇØc to that
particular medication.

## Classes of medication

¯Çü Antimetabolites

{ Methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil and azathioprine
inhibit purine metabolism.
¯Çü Inhibitors of calcineurin signalling in T cells

{ Cyclosporin A, tacrolimus, voclosporin and sirolimus.
¯Çü Alkylating agents

  - Cyclophosphamide and chlorambucil.
¯Çü Biopharmacologicals

{ ¯Çƒis is a rapidly changing ¯ÇØeld and new agents are constantly evolving that are e¯Çøective in suppressing ocular
in¯Ç×ammation.

{ In¯Ç×iximab, adalimumab, etanercept, golimumab and certolizumab are anti-TNF ╬▒ monoclonal antibodies that are
registered for use in non-infectious uveitis.

{ Tocilizumab is a recombinant monoclonal antibody that
binds to interleukin-6 receptors thereby inhibiting the
activity of pro-in¯Ç×ammatory cytokines.

¯Çü Immediate treatment

{ Systemic lupus erythematosus with retinal vasculitis.

{ Beh├ºet disease with retinal vasculitis.
¯Çü Early treatment should be considered for:

{ Sympathetic ophthalmia.

{ VogtÔÇôKoyanagiÔÇôHarada syndrome.

{ Steroids and antimetabolites are expected to work for all
forms of severe uveitis.

442 Uveitis in Spondyloarthropathies

{ In T-cell critical uveitis (e.g. VKH, birdshot retinochoroidopathy) consider the use of calcineurin inhibitors.

{ In neutrophil and macrophage critical uveitis (e.g.
Beh├ºet disease, sarcoidosis) consider the use of anti-TNF
agents.

{ In B-cell critical in¯Ç×ammation (e.g. scleritis, particularly
ANCA+) treat with rituximab.

UVEITIS IN SPONDYLOARTHROPATHIES

¯Çƒe spondyloarthropathies are a group of disorders featuring
HLA-B27 positivity and enthesitis as common factors. ¯Çƒere is
o¯Ç£en a family history of one or more of the group, which comprises ankylosing spondylitis, undi¯Çøerentiated spondyloarthropathy, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis (Reiter syndrome) and
spondyloarthropathy with in¯Ç×ammatory bowel disease (ulcerative
colitis and Crohn disease). ¯Çƒey are o¯Ç£en referred to as seronegative spondyloarthropathies, in that rheumatoid factor is not present and the pathophysiological basis di¯Çøers. ¯Çƒe American Uveitis

## Society has recently endorsed the use of biological blockers such as

in¯Ç×iximab for second-line systemic immunosuppression in visionthreatening chronic uveitis.

TIP Monoclonal antibody treatment (adalimumab/in¯ÇØiximab)
has improved the prognosis in patients with vision-threatening
chronic uveitis.

## Ankylosing spondylitis

## Introduction

Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is characterized by in¯Ç×ammation,
calci¯ÇØcation and ¯ÇØnally ossi¯ÇØcation of ligaments and capsules of
joints with resultant bony ankylosis of the axial skeleton. It more
commonly a¯Çøects males, of whom 90% are HLA-B27-positive.

## Systemic features

¯Çü Presentation is commonly in the third to fourth decades with the
insidious onset of pain and sti¯Çøness in the lower back or buttocks.
¯Çü Spondyloarthritis causes progressive limitation of spinal
movements and eventually the spine may become ¯ÇØxed in ¯Ç×exion (Fig. 12.10A). Spinal stenosis and fractures may occur.
¯Çü Enthesitis is characterized clinically by in¯Ç×ammation and pain
at ligamentous attachments to bone.
¯Çü Cardiac complications are rare.
¯Çü Radiology of the sacroiliac joints shows juxta-articular osteoporosis in the early stages, followed by sclerosis and bony
obliteration of the joint (Fig. 12.10B). Calci¯ÇØcation of spinal
ligaments gives rise to a ÔÇÿbamboo spineÔÇÖ. Radiological changes
o¯Ç£en predate clinical symptoms.

## Ocular features

¯Çü AAU is by far the most common ocular association and occurs
in about 25% of patients with AS. Approximately 25% of males
with AAU will have AS. Either eye is frequently a¯Çøected at different times but bilateral simultaneous involvement is rare.
¯Çƒere is o¯Ç£en no correlation between the severity and activity of eye and joint involvement. Chronicity occurs in a few
patients. HLA-B27-positive AS patients tend to have worse
disease across a range of parameters, including earlier onset
and greater intensity of in¯Ç×ammation with an increased frequency of complications.
¯Çü Other ocular features include scleritis, episcleritis, keratitis
and mechanical ptosis.

## Reactive arthritis

## Introduction

Reactive arthritis (ReA, also known as Reitersyndrome) is characterized by a triad of non-speci¯ÇØc urethritis, conjunctivitis and arthritis.

A B

and bony obliteration of the sacroiliac joints (arrow). (Courtesy of P Beckwith ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

Around 75% of patients are positive for HLA-B27. A range of infective agents can trigger the syndrome, which develops in 1ÔÇô3% of
men a¯Ç£er non-speci¯ÇØc urethritis and around 4% of individuals a¯Ç£er
enteric infections caused by a range of organisms including Shigella,
Salmonella and Campylobacter. Chlamydia trachomatis is the most
common cause of reactive arthritis following urethritis. Chlamydia
pneumoniae respiratory infection and others may also precede ReA.

TIP Patients with uveitis secondary to the various
spondyloarthropathies are often HLA-B27-positive.

## Systemic features

¯Çü Presentation is with the acute onset of malaise, with fever and
dysuria 1ÔÇô4 weeks a¯Ç£er a linked infection in a patient aged
between 20 and 40, with arthritis that may be preceded by conjunctivitis. A variety of other features may be present, though
not always the de¯ÇØning triad.
¯Çü Peripheral oligoarthritis is acute, asymmetrical and migratory. Two to four joints tend to be involved, most commonly
the knees, ankles and toes.
¯Çü Spondyloarthritis a¯Çøects about 50% of patients, manifesting
with low back pain. ¯Çƒis sometimes becomes chronic.
¯Çü Enthesitis manifests with plantar fasciitis, Achilles tenosynovitis, bursitis and calcaneal periostitis. Reactive bone formation in the latter may result in a calcaneal spur.
¯Çü Mucocutaneous lesions include painless mouth ulceration,
circinate balanitis and keratoderma blennorrhagica ÔÇô skin
lesions resembling psoriasis ÔÇô involving the palms and soles
(Fig. 12.11A).
¯Çü Genitourinary involvement includes cervicitis, prostatitis
and epididymitis.
¯Çü Aortitis occurs in 1ÔÇô2%.

## Ocular features

¯Çƒe eye is involved in 50% of cases with a urogenital inciting infection and 75% of enteric ReA syndrome.

¯Çü Conjunctivitis is very common and classically follows urethritis, but precedes arthritis. ¯Çƒe in¯Ç×ammation is usually
mild, bilateral and mucopurulent with a papillary and/or follicular reaction (Fig. 12.11B). Spontaneous resolution occurs
within 7ÔÇô10 days and treatment is not required. Some patients
develop peripheral corneal in¯ÇØltrates.
¯Çü AAU occurs in 20%.
¯Çü Episcleritis sometimes occurs.

## Treatment

¯Çü ¯Ç×e main goal is to identify the causative organism and treat
with appropriate antibiotics if the organism is still present.
¯Çü Non-speci¯ÇØc urethritis should be treated with oral
tetracycline.
¯Çü Steroids and immunosuppressants may be needed for severe
symptoms that do not respond to simple treatment.

## Psoriatic arthritis

## Introduction

Up to 40% of patients with psoriasis develop arthritis. ¯Çƒe arthritis
is more common in White populations than other racial groups
and a¯Çøects both sexes equally. ¯Çƒere is a ¯ÇØrst-degree family history
in 40% or more and many genetic markers have been identi¯ÇØed.

## Systemic features

¯Çü Presentation of psoriatic arthritis is usually in middle age ÔÇô
later than skin features.
¯Çü Skin. ¯Çƒere are multiple types of psoriasis. Plaque-type, the
most common form, is characterized by well-demarcated
raised silvery in¯Ç×amed plaques (Fig. 12.12A) on the scalp,
trunk, arms and legs. Psoriatic erythroderma features widespread exfoliative skin changes with associated in¯Ç×ammation
and pustular psoriasis in¯Ç×amed but non-infectious pustules
limited or generalized in distribution.
¯Çü Nail changes (dystrophy) include pitting, transverse depression and onycholysis (Fig. 12.12B).

A B

444 Fuchs Uveitis Syndrome

A B

¯Çü Arthritis is typically asymmetrical and involves the distal
interphalangeal joints (sausage digits). Some patients develop
enthesitis.

## Ocular features

AAU occurs in approximately 7%. Conjunctivitis, marginal corneal in¯ÇØltrates and secondary Sj├Âgren syndrome may occur but are
uncommon.

FUCHS UVEITIS SYNDROME

## Introduction

Fuchs uveitis syndrome (FUS), also known as Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis or cyclitis (FHC), is a chronic nongranulomatous condition diagnosed at an average of 40 years of
age. ¯Çƒere is no gender or racial predilection. ¯Çƒe cause is uncertain, but there is evidence that implicates the rubella virus. Signs
in toxoplasmosis can be similar and T. gondii has also been suspected as a cause. It is possible that most of the anterior chamber
(AC) activity is due to bloodÔÇôaqueous barrier breakdown rather
than in¯Ç×ammation.

## Clinical features

Detection is o¯Ç£en incidental. Findings are usually unilateral
(90ÔÇô95%).
¯Çü Symptoms. Gradual blurring due to cataract is a common presentation, as are persistent ¯Ç×oaters. Heterochromia (see next)
may be noted.
¯Çü Heterochromia iridis (Table 12.7) is demonstrated most
e¯Çøectively in daylight and in most patients the a¯Çøected eye
is hypochromic (Fig. 12.13A). Its quality is determined by
the relative degrees of atrophy of the stroma and posterior
pigment epithelium. It may be absent or subtle, particularly
in brown eyes. In blue eyes, stromal atrophy allows the posterior pigmented layer to show through and become the
dominant pigmentation, so that the eye sometimes becomes
hyperchromic.

¯Çü Posterior synechiae (PS) are absent, except occasionally following cataract surgery.
¯Çü AC shows faint ¯Ç×are and usually only mild cellular activity,
though exacerbations can sometimes be marked. ¯Çƒe eye is
virtually always white, even during exacerbations.
¯Çü Keratic precipitates are characteristically stellate and greyÔÇô
white in colour and are located di¯Çøusely over the entire corneal
endothelium (Fig. 12.13B).
¯Çü Iris nodules (30%) on the pupillary border (Koeppe ÔÇô Fig.
12.13C). Tiny crystals (Russell bodies) may be present on the
iris surface.
¯Çü Iris atrophy is di¯Çøuse with loss of crypts. ¯Çƒe iris appears
smooth, with a prominent sphincter pupillae and sometimes
blood vessels (see Fig. 12.13C). Pigment epithelial atrophy can
be demonstrated by retroillumination (Fig. 12.13D).
¯Çü Iris vessels. Fine irregular iris surface vessels are commonly
present.
¯Çü Vitritis. Opacities in the anterior gel may be dense.
¯Çü Posterior subcapsular cataract is extremely common (Fig.
12.13E).
¯Çü Glaucoma is typically a later manifestation but is occasionally
present at diagnosis. It develops in up to 60% of involved eyes.
Several mechanisms are suspected.
¯Çü Gonioscopy may show ¯ÇØne radial angle vessels or small irregular peripheral anterior synechiae (Fig. 12.13F). ¯Çƒe vessels
are typically the source of the haemorrhage sometimes seen on
incision into the AC (Amsler sign).
¯Çü Fundus: peripheral choroiditis foci/scarring have been
reported. ¯Çƒere may be an increased incidence of retinal dialysis. Macular oedema does not occur, except following surgery.

## Investigation

Diagnosis is clinical, though investigation may be necessary to
exclude alternative conditions.

## Treatment

¯Çü Long-term monitoring is indicated to detect glaucoma and
other complications.

peripheral anterior synechiae

## Other chronic anterior uveitides

¯Çü Topical steroids may be used short-term for moderate/severe
exacerbations, but are generally not thought to be helpful in
the management of chronic low-grade in¯Ç×ammation.
¯Çü Cataract surgery carries an increased risk of complications.
Poor mydriasis and the possibility of postoperative hyphaema,
increased in¯Ç×ammation, worsening of glaucoma control and
zonular dehiscence should be considered. Preoperative topical
or systemic steroids are used by some practitioners.
¯Çü Glaucoma can be di¯ÇÜcult to control medically. Surgical
options include a glaucoma drainage device or trabeculectomy
with mitomycin-C enhancement.
¯Çü Pars plana vitrectomy may be considered for visually problematic vitreous opaci¯ÇØcation.

UVEITIS IN JUVENILE IDIOPATHIC
ARTHRITIS (JIA)

## Introduction

JIA is by far the most common systemic disease associated with
childhood anterior uveitis with a prevalence of about 1:1000. It is
de¯ÇØned as arthritis of unknown aetiology that begins before the
age of 16 years and persists for at least 6 weeks. Up to 50% of children a¯Çøected have persistently active disease a¯Ç£er 10 years. It may
result from exposure to one or more unknown antigens in genetically predisposed individuals.

TIP Immunomodulatory treatment should be considered early
in the course of JIA, which is the commonest systemic disease
associated with childhood anterior uveitis.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Arthritis. JIA is classi¯ÇØed by the International League of Associations for Rheumatology (ILAR, 2004 revision), according to
the extent of joint involvement during the ¯ÇØrst 6 months.

{ Oligoarticular is the most common form. Four or fewer
joints are involved, the knees most commonly, followed by
the ankles and wrists (Fig. 12.14A). Girls are a¯Çøected ¯ÇØve

times as o¯Ç£en as boys, with a peak age of onset around 2
years. Some patients subsequently develop polyarthritis.
About 75% of children are ANA-positive, a strong risk factor for uveitis, which is common, a¯Çøecting about 20% of
children in this group.

{ Polyarticular (rheumatoid factor-negative) a¯Çøects ¯ÇØve or
more joints, typically both small and large joints symmetrically. ¯Çƒe female:male ratio is about 3:1. ¯Çƒe disease may commence at any age throughout childhood.

## Systemic features such as fever and rash may occur but

are milder than in the systemic onset form (see below).
About 40% of children are ANA-positive. Uveitis occurs
in 5ÔÇô10% of cases.

{ Polyarticular (rheumatoid factor-positive) again a¯Çøects
¯ÇØve or more joints and may resemble adult rheumatoid
arthritis. ¯Çƒere is a very low risk of uveitis.

{ Systemic, also known as Still (StillÔÇÖs) disease. Systemic features such as fever, episodic erythematous maculopapular
rash, lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly may precede arthritis. ¯Çƒe disease occurs with equal frequency in
boys and girls and may occur at any age throughout childhood. ¯Çƒe majority are negative for ANA and uveitis is
rare.

{ Enthesitis-related (in¯Ç×ammation of the connective tissue
where tendons/ligaments attach to bone), psoriatic and
undi¯Çøerentiated are three other forms under the ILAR
classi¯ÇØcation. ¯Çƒe ¯ÇØrst two have a relatively high risk of
uveitis, but risk in the latter is usually low.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis is a key cause of morbidity in JIA. It is particularly common in oligoarticular JIA and relatively frequent in
several other types. Progression to blindness has been high in
the past, but shows a declining trend in recent years associated
with improved screening and management. Arthritis usually
antedates the diagnosis of uveitis.

{ Presentation. ¯Çƒe uveitis of JIA is particularly dangerous
because it is invariably asymptomatic and must generally
be detected by screening with slit lamp examination. Even
during acute exacerbations with +4 aqueous cells, it is rare
for patients to complain, although a few report an increase
in vitreous ¯Ç×oaters. O¯Ç£en uveitis may not be suspected
until the parents recognize complications such as strabismus, or an abnormal appearance of the eyeball due to band
keratopathy or cataract.

{ Injection is usually absent even in the presence of severe
uveitis.

{ In¯Ç×ammation is chronic and non-granulomatous. Both
eyes are a¯Çøected in 70% and there is symmetrical severity of in¯Ç×ammation. During acute exacerbations, the entire
endothelium shows ÔÇÿdustingÔÇÖ by many hundreds of cells,
but hypopyon is absent.

{ PS are common in longstanding undetected cases.

{ Band keratopathy and cataract (Fig. 12.14B) are common
in severe cases.

{ Other serious complications include glaucoma (common),
amblyopia, maculopathy (cystoid macular oedema, epiretinal membrane), cyclitic membrane and phthisis.

A B

{ Prognosis. In about 10% the uveitis is mild, with never
more than +1 aqueous cells and persists for less than 12
months. About 15% of patients have one attack lasting
less than 4 months, the severity of in¯Ç×ammation varying
from +2 to +4 aqueous cells. In 50% of cases, the uveitis is moderate to severe and persists for more than 4
months and in 25%, the uveitis is very severe, lasts for
several years and responds poorly to conventional treatment. ¯Çƒe presence of complications at initial examination appears to be an important risk factor for the
development of subsequent complications, regardless of
therapy. Good visual outcomes can be achieved by early
case recognition and rapid referral to a specialist for
immunomodulatory therapy.

## Investigation

¯Çü Systemic diagnosis and management should be performed
by a physician familiar with the management of JIA, typically a
paediatric rheumatologist.
¯Çü Antinuclear antibody (ANA). Positivity denotes an increased
risk of uveitis.
¯Çü HLA-B27 testing is useful in di¯Çøerential diagnosis (see above)
and if present may indicate an increased risk of uveitis.
¯Çü Rheumatoid factor is also useful in di¯Çøerential diagnosis.
¯Çü Screening. ¯Çƒere has been a shi¯Ç£ in recommendation towards
long-term 3- to 4-monthly review intervals in all higher-risk
categories. Review should continue in most cases until the age
of 12 years.

{ Initial examination within 6 weeks of ¯ÇØrst diagnosis of
JIA. Delayed early examination is an important cause of
morbidity.

{ Visual symptoms or a suspicion of ocular signs (synechiae,
cataract, band keratopathy) should lead to urgent ophthalmological referral and slit lamp examination within a
week.

{ Initial 2-monthly examinations for 6 months may be considered for all newly diagnosed oligoarticular, psoriatic,

{ Idiopathic juvenile chronic iridocyclitis: otherwise healthy
patients with juvenile CAU.

{ Other types of juvenile arthritis and uveitis including
juvenile reactive arthritis, juvenile in¯Ç×ammatory bowel
disease-associated arthritis.

{ Juvenile sarcoidosis is rare. Pulmonary involvement is
less common than in adults, may be granulomatous and
involve the posterior segment.

{ Lyme disease usually presents with intermediate uveitis in
conjunction with signi¯ÇØcant anterior uveitis.

polyarticular and enthesitis-related patients, regardless of
ANA status, followed by 3- to 4-monthly intervals.

{ Polyarticular: every 3ÔÇô4 months, but some guidelines
reduce the interval to 6-monthly a¯Ç£er a number of years.
Higher-risk factors that might be considered in deciding
whether to alter the interval include the presence of ANA,
onset before 7 years of age and female gender.

{ Systemic onset and polyarticular RF-positive patients:
most authorities recommend at least an initial screening examination, with some guidelines suggesting annual
review.

{ Missed appointments must be e¯Çøectively detected and
patients rebooked.

{ Information for parents should include an emphasis on the
importance of compliance with screening, as well as the
need to seek urgent advice should there be any cause for
concern such as visual symptoms, ocular redness or clouding, or abnormal pupils.

{ Self-monitoring. At eventual discharge from screening,
patients should be warned to self-monitor by checking the
monocular vision at least once a week because the risk of
uveitis has not entirely disappeared by this age. ¯Çƒey should
also attend an optometrist annually for an eye examination. Selected patients such as those with learning di¯ÇÜculties may require ongoing ophthalmological screening.
¯Çü Di¯Ç£erential diagnosis: Particular considerations in children
include:

448 Uveitis in Bowel Disease

{ Intermediate uveitis: 20% of all cases of paediatric uveitis.

{ Neonatal-onset multisystem in¯Ç×ammatory disease is a
rare, idiopathic, chronic relapsing disease that predominantly involves the skin, joints and the central nervous
system. About 50% of children develop recurrent anterior
uveitis. ¯Çƒe absence of PS and no tendency to glaucoma
and cataract formation are characteristic.

{ Masquerade syndromes such as anterior segment involvement by retinoblastoma.

{ Familial juvenile systemic granulomatosis (Blau syndrome) is a rare autosomal dominant disorder characterized by childhood onset of granulomatous disease of skin,
eyes (panuveitis and multifocal choroiditis) and joints.

## Treatment

¯Çƒe aim of treatment should be the suppression of all active in¯Ç×ammation. ¯Çƒese children should be referred as soon as possible to a
uveitis specialist.
¯Çü Topical steroids are initially e¯Çøective in 80% of cases.
¯Çü Mydriatic agents can be used to prevent synechiae formation.
A relatively short-acting preparation such as cyclopentolate
should be prescribed and discontinued as early as possible,
particularly in monocular treatment of younger children susceptible to the development of amblyopia.
¯Çü Periocular steroids.
¯Çü Oral steroids.
¯Çü NSAIDs.
¯Çü Systemic immunosuppressive agents (e.g. methotrexate, infliximab and adalimumab) should be considered at an early
stage, as this has improved the overall visual prognosis in
recent years. ¯Çƒese children are usually under the management
of a uveitis specialist, typically working in collaboration with a
paediatric rheumatologist. Etanercept should be avoided as it
can result in severe worsening of in¯Ç×ammation. Tocilizumab,
an interleukin-6 blocking agent, has recently been shown to
be e¯Çøective in the treatment of some children with refractory
uveitis secondary to JIA.

¯Çü Secondary glaucoma. If topical medication does not control the IOP, surgery may be needed. ¯Çƒe long-term results
of trabeculectomy with mitomycin C in children with JIA
are improved if they are using systemic anti-TNF medication. Baerveldt implantation also results in good long-term
IOP control, but is associated with a 5% risk of hypotony
maculopathy.
¯Çü Cataract extraction is o¯Ç£en needed. Primary IOL implantation was a controversial issue in the past, but a low complication rate with good visual results has been recently reported
with this approach in children on immunomodulatory treatment. Posterior capsular thickening requiring treatment
occurs in approximately half.

UVEITIS IN BOWEL DISEASE

## Ulcerative colitis

## Introduction

Ulcerative colitis (UC) is an idiopathic chronic relapsing in¯Ç×ammatory disease, involving the rectum and extending proximally to
involve part or all of the large intestine. ¯Çƒe disease is characterized by contiguous surface ulceration of the bowel mucosa with the
development of crypt abscesses and pseudopolyps (Fig. 12.15A).

## Longstanding disease carries an increased risk of carcinoma of

the colon. A genetic predisposition is thought to be important
as in¯Ç×ammatory bowel disease is more common in patients with
other autoimmune diseases such as ankylosing spondylitis, psoriasis and multiple sclerosis.

## Systemic features

¯Çü Presentation is in the second to third decades with bloody
diarrhoea, lower abdominal cramps, urgency and tenesmus.
Constitutional symptoms include tiredness, weight loss, malaise and fever.
¯Çü Cutaneous lesions include oral aphthous ulceration, erythema
nodosum and pyoderma gangrenosum (Fig. 12.15B).

A B

markings and straightening of the ascending colon; ¯Ç£B¯ÇØ pyoderma gangrenosum.

¯Çü Arthritis is typically asymmetrical and involves large joints
of the legs. Sacroiliitis and ankylosing spondylitis (AS) may
develop in HLA-B27-positive patients.
¯Çü Hepatic disease may be in the form of autoimmune hepatitis,
sclerosing cholangitis and cholangiocarcinoma.
¯Çü ¯Ç×rombosis may a¯Çøect both arteries and veins.

## Ocular features

¯Çü AAU occurs in about 5% and may coincide with exacerbations
of colitis. As expected, uveitis is more common in patients with
associated arthritis, AS and HLA-B27 positivity.
¯Çü Other ocular features: conjunctivitis, episcleritis and scleritis
may all be more common than in the general population.

## Crohn disease

## Introduction

Crohn disease (CD) is an idiopathic chronic relapsing disease characterized by multifocal full-thickness granulomatous in¯Ç×ammation
of the intestinal wall. It most frequently involves the terminal ileum
and colon but in contrast to UC any area of the gastrointestinal tract,
including the mouth, may be a¯Çøected. ¯Çƒere is strong evidence for
a genetic aetiological component such as mutations in the CARD15
(previously NOD2) gene. Infective agents almost certainly play a role.

## Systemic features

¯Çü Presentation is typically in the secondÔÇôthird decades with
abdominal pain and diarrhoea. Weight loss, fever, vomiting, oral aphthous ulceration and perirectal lesions such as
abscesses and ¯ÇØstulae may occur.
¯Çü Cutaneous lesions include erythema nodosum and pyoderma
gangrenosum (see Fig. 12.15B).
¯Çü Anaemia is common.
¯Çü Hepatic disease may occur.
¯Çü Skeletal features include ¯ÇØnger clubbing, acute peripheral
arthritis, sacroiliitis and AS (especially if HLA-B27-positive).

## Ocular features

Approximately 12% of patients with CD experience some form of
ocular in¯Ç×ammation. AAU occurs in 3%. Other manifestations
include episcleritis, scleritis, follicular conjunctivitis, myositis and
optic neuritis (see Ch. 9).

## Whipple disease

## Introduction

Whipple disease (intestinal lipodystrophy) is a rare chronic gastrointestinal in¯Ç×ammatory condition caused by infection with the
bacterium Tropheryma whipplei. It occurs mainly in White middleaged men and when diagnosed (duodenal biopsy; DNA detection
in blood, ocular and other ¯Ç×uids) can be cured with antibiotics.

## Systemic features

In¯Ç×ammatory bowel disease with malabsorption. Joint, cardiac
and central nervous system (CNS) involvement is common.

## Ocular features

¯Çü Uveitis. Keratitis, anterior uveitis, vitritis, retinitis with retinal haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots and potentially vascular
occlusion and multifocal choroiditis.
¯Çü Neuro-ophthalmic manifestations can be varied, e.g. gaze
palsy, nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia, papilloedema and optic
atrophy. Oculomasticatory myorhythmia is characteristic.

UVEITIS IN RENAL DISEASE

## Tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis

## Introduction

Tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis (TINU) is an uncommon
disorder of immune origin characterized by a combination of
acute tubulointerstitial nephritis and uveitis. It typically occurs in
adolescent girls. Renal disease usually precedes uveitis.

## Systemic features

Presentation is with constitutional symptoms, proteinuria, anaemia, hypertension and renal failure. ¯Çƒe response to systemic steroid therapy is good and renal function usually returns to normal
within a few months without complication.

## Ocular features

¯Çü Bilateral non-granulomatous (occasionally granulomatous)
anterior uveitis that usually responds well to topical steroids.
Disc and macular oedema may occur. Many cases are relapsing and some require systemic steroids or immunosuppressive
therapy.
¯Çü Intermediate, posterior or panuveitis may occur.

TIP In a patient with ocular and renal in¯ÇØammation, consider
the following in the di¯Çƒerential diagnosis: sarcoidosis,
tuberculosis, SLE, tubulointerstitial nephritis and IgA
nephropathy.

IgA nephropathy

IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) is a relatively common kidney
disease in which immunoglobulin A is deposited in the glomerular mesangium. Presentation is usually at age 16ÔÇô35 with recurrent
haematuria, o¯Ç£en associated with an upper respiratory tract infection, but may be asymptomatic. AAU and other ocular in¯Ç×ammatory phenomena may occur but are uncommon.

INTERMEDIATE UVEITIS

## Introduction

Intermediate uveitis (IU) is a chronic, relapsing disease of insidious
onset in which the vitreous is the primary site of in¯Ç×ammation. It
incorporates the following entities: pars planitis, posterior cyclitis
and hyalitis. ¯Çƒe diagnosis is made clinically. IU may be idiopathic
(at least half) or associated with a systemic disease. Pars planitis

450 Intermediate Uveitis

(PP) is the term used for a subset of IU in which there is snowbanking and/or snowball formation, but only if the in¯Ç×ammation
is idiopathic ÔÇô that is, with no identi¯ÇØable underlying infection or
systemic disease ÔÇô otherwise the term intermediate uveitis is used.
IU accounts for up to 15% of all uveitis cases and about 20% of paediatric uveitis. A minority of patients have a benign course, with
spontaneous resolution within several years. In other patients the
disease is more severe and prolonged with episodic exacerbations.
IU associated with systemic disease has a variable course.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Presentation is with the insidious onset of blurred
vision, o¯Ç£en accompanied by vitreous ¯Ç×oaters. ¯Çƒere is usually
no pain or redness. ¯Çƒough initial symptoms are o¯Ç£en unilateral, objective ¯ÇØndings are typically present asymmetrically in
both eyes.
¯Çü Visual acuity is variably a¯Çøected depending on in¯Ç×ammatory
activity and complications, particularly CMO. ¯Çƒe disease may
last as long as 15 years and preservation of vision will depend
largely on control of macular disease. In follow-up of up to 4
years, 75% of patients maintain a visual acuity of 6/12 or better.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis. In PP there may be a few cells and small scattered KP which occasionally have an inferior linear distribution. In other forms of IU, anterior uveitis and its associated
¯ÇØndings, such as PS, can be more prominent, especially in children and in sarcoidosis and Lyme disease.
¯Çü Vitreous. Vitreous cells with anterior predominance are universal. Vitreous condensation and haze (Table 12.8) is found in
severe cases (Fig. 12.16A). Snowballs are whitish focal collections of in¯Ç×ammatory cells and exudate, usually most numerous in the inferior vitreous (Fig. 12.16B).
¯Çü Peripheral periphlebitis (Fig. 12.16C) is common, particularly in multiple sclerosis. Careful examination of a normal
fellow eye in apparently unilateral disease may reveal mild vascular sheathing.
¯Çü Snowbanking (Fig. 12.16D) is characterized by a greyÔÇôwhite
¯ÇØbrovascular and/or exudative plaque that may occur in any or
all quadrants, but is most frequently found inferiorly.
¯Çü Neovascularization may occur, particularly in the retinal
periphery (o¯Ç£en associated with snowbanks) and on the optic
nerve head. ¯Çƒe latter usually resolves when activity is controlled. ¯Çƒiscan sometimes lead to vitreous haemorrhage, retinal
detachment and cyclitic membrane formation. Focal peripheral
retinal vasoproliferative tumours (see Ch. 20) are uncommon.
Vitreous haemorrhage is more common in children.

Table 12.8 Grading of Vitreous Haze

Haze severity Grading

(NFL)

NFL

¯Çü Optic disc swelling is common, especially in younger patients.
¯Çü CMO occurs in up to half of patients and is the major cause of
impaired visual acuity.
¯Çü Macular epiretinal membrane formation is common.
¯Çü Cataract can be caused by steroid treatment or by the in¯Ç×ammation itself.
¯Çü Glaucoma may occur in eyes with prolonged in¯Ç×ammation,
particularly if receiving long-term steroid therapy.
¯Çü Retinal detachment is generally uncommon, but as it can
progress to hypotony and phthisis in advanced cases, prevention should be a major goal of management. ¯Çƒe aetiology may
be tractional, rhegmatogenous and occasionally exudative.
Retinoschisis has also been described.

TIP An insidious onset of blurred vision accompanied by
vitreous ¯ÇØoaters without pain or redness suggests a possible
diagnosis of intermediate uveitis.

## Investigation

In¯Ç×ammatory markers such as ESR and/or CRP should be checked,
together with a complete blood count, as they may raise suspicion
of a systemic in¯Ç×ammatory process. OCT is key to excluding subtle
CMO and FA will help in assessing severity. Other investigations
are targeted at the exclusion of an underlying cause as below.
¯Çü Multiple sclerosis. Enquiry should be made in all patients
about neurological symptoms, noting that IU may precede
other symptoms of demyelination. MS should be suspected in
patients aged 20ÔÇô50 and is twice as common in women. Granulomatous AAU may occur. Cranial MRI imaging should be
performed if any suspicion is raised.
¯Çü Sarcoidosis. Sarcoid-associated IU is relatively uncommon
and as with MS may antedate the onset of systemic disease. ¯Çƒe
presence of associated granulomatous anterior uveitis should
arouse suspicion. A serum ACE level and chest X-ray should
be performed in all adult patients.
¯Çü Lyme disease-associated IU is o¯Ç£en associated with severe
anterior uveitis. Serology should be performed in individuals
from an endemic area.
¯Çü Syphilis serology ÔÇô treponemal and cardiolipin antibody tests

  - should be performed.
¯Çü Tuberculosis is an uncommon association that may give respiratory symptoms and be demonstrated on chest X-ray. Tuberculin
skin and/or blood (e.g. QuantiFERON) testing should be performed prior to steroid treatment in the presence of any suspicion.
¯Çü Other conditions that may give vitritis mimicking IU include
FUS, intraocular lymphoma (older patients), Toxocara granuloma, Whipple disease, endogenous Candida endophthalmitis
(risk factors such as intravenous drug use) and toxoplasmosis.
¯Çƒese will commonly be suspected on the basis of the history
and speci¯ÇØc clinical ¯ÇØndings.

## Treatment

An identi¯ÇØed infection or other underlying disease should be
treated speci¯ÇØcally, supplemented by anti-in¯Ç×ammatory measures.
Many authorities aim to abolish all active in¯Ç×ammation regardless

A B

C D

and snowballs; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ inferior snowbanking and snowballs. (Courtesy of CL Schepens, ME Hartnett and T Hirose, from SchepensÔÇÖ Retinal Detachment and Allied Diseases, ButterworthHeinemann 2000 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

of whether vision has been a¯Çøected. Factors such as the presence
of peripheral neovascularization may prompt earlier intervention.
¯Çü Topical steroids do not reach the posterior segment in high
concentrations and therefore have a limited role. ¯Çƒis medication is used mainly to treat any anterior segment in¯Ç×ammation. It has been proposed that in mild IU a course of frequent
topical steroid of a few weeksÔÇÖ duration may exert some bene¯ÇØt
and identify individuals at high risk of IOP elevation without
committing to the extended action of depot injection.
¯Çü Regional steroid injection. Orbital ¯Ç×oor or posterior subTenon injection as described for anterior uveitis. Depending
on severity, injection is performed 4ÔÇô6 times at intervals of 2ÔÇô4
weeks, accompanied by IOP monitoring.
¯Çü NSAID. If in¯Ç×ammation persists a¯Ç£er regional injection, an
agent such as naproxen 500 mg twice daily can be commenced.
¯Çü Cryotherapy (double freezeÔÇôthaw) to the pars plana and
retinal periphery under peribulbar anaesthesia can be highly
e¯Çøective if in¯Ç×ammation is steroid-resistant and may be

appropriate prior to systemic steroids. It can be associated
with transiently increased vitritis and other complications
including retinal detachment, cataract, vitreous and AC haemorrhage, epiretinal membrane formation and hypotony. A
repeat application may be needed a¯Ç£er several months. Some
authorities reserve cryotherapy for peripheral neovascularization with haemorrhage.
¯Çü Peripheral retinal laser adjacent to snowbanking and/or to
ischaemic areas on FA is an alternative to cryotherapy and may
be as e¯Çøective with a lower rate of complications.
¯Çü Intraocular steroid. Intravitreal triamcinolone has shown
bene¯ÇØt, but the e¯Çøect is of relatively short duration. Slowrelease implants have demonstrated promising results.
¯Çü Systemic steroids. ¯Çƒis modality is preferred over regional
steroid by some practitioners if symptomatic in¯Ç×ammation
is bilateral. Others prefer to avoid systemic steroids in most
cases, proceeding directly to immunosuppressive chemotherapeutic agents. A large dose of 1ÔÇô2 mg/kg daily is commenced,

452 VogtÔÇôKoyanagiÔÇôHarada (VKH) Syndrome

tapered slowly over months according to response. It is essential to consider the contraindications and potential adverse
e¯Çøects of steroids before prescribing.
¯Çü Immunosuppressive agents. Mycophenolate, methotrexate,
tacrolimus, cyclosporine and others are alternatives in steroidresistant in¯Ç×ammation or as steroid-sparing agents.
¯Çü Other agents demonstrating e¯ÇÜcacy in refractory disease
include interferon-beta (in MS-related IU) and the anti-TNF
in¯Ç×iximab.
¯Çü Pars plana vitrectomy substantially reduces in¯Ç×ammatory
intensity and recurrence, though the mechanism is imperfectly
understood. It is indicated in patients with tractional retinal
detachment, epiretinal membrane, refractory CMO, dense vitreous opacity, vitreous haemorrhage or substantial peripheral
neovascularization.
¯Çü Cataract and glaucoma are managed medically and surgically
if necessary.

VOGTÔÇôKOYANAGIÔÇôHARADA (VKH)
SYNDROME

## Introduction

VKH is an idiopathic multisystem autoimmune disease featuring in¯Ç×ammation of melanocyte-containing tissues such as the
uvea, ear and meninges. VKH predominantly a¯Çøects Hispanic,
Japanese and heavily pigmented individuals, usually in the 2nd
and 5th decade of life. It is associated with HLA-DR1 and HLADR4 across di¯Çøerent racial groups. VKH is sometimes subdivided into (a) VogtÔÇôKoyanagi disease, characterized mainly by
skin changes and anterior uveitis and (b) Harada disease, in
which neurological features and exudative retinal detachments
predominate.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Prodromal phase lasting a few days: neurological (meningitis and rarely encephalopathy with cranial nerve paresis and

other focal lesions) and auditory manifestations (tinnitus,
vertigo and deafness). Cranial nerve palsies and optic neuritis
may occur.
¯Çü Acute uveitic phase. Bilateral granulomatous anterior and
multifocal posterior uveitis with di¯Çøuse choroidal in¯ÇØltration, DalenÔÇôFuchs nodules (see also sympathetic ophthalmia
below), vitritis, papillitis and exudative retinal detachments
(Fig. 12.17A and Fig. 12.18A). Ciliary e¯Çøusion with irisÔÇôlens
diaphragm rotation can occur.
¯Çü Convalescent phase follows several weeks later: localized alopecia, poliosis and vitiligo, depigmented fundus appearance
(ÔÇÿsunset glowÔÇÖ fundus ÔÇô Fig. 12.17B) and depigmented limbal lesions (Sugiura sign) in pigmented, especially Japanese,
patients.
¯Çü Chronic recurrent phase is characterized by smouldering
anterior uveitis with exacerbations. Recurrent posterior uveitis
is much less common.
¯Çü Diagnostic criteria for VKH are set out in Table 12.9. In complete VKH, criteria 1ÔÇô5 must be present, in incomplete VKH,
criteria 1ÔÇô3 and either 4 or 5 must be present, and in probable
VKH (isolated ocular disease), criteria 1ÔÇô3 must be present.
¯Çü Ocular complications include macular neovascularization,
subretinal ¯ÇØbrosis, preretinal and disc new vessels and vitreous
haemorrhage, cataract and glaucoma.
¯Çü Prognosis is variable and is partly dependent on achieving
control in the early stages. Neurological and auditory manifestations tend to resolve but skin, lash and hair changes usually
persist.

## Investigation

## Systemic manifestations should be investigated and managed by an

appropriate specialist.
¯Çü Lumbar puncture if diagnosis uncertain. CSF shows a transient lymphocytic pleocytosis and melanin-containing
macrophages.
¯Çü FAF demonstrates hyperauto¯Ç×uorescence in areas of serous
detachment (Fig. 12.18B).

454 Sympathetic Ophthalmia

Table 12.9 Modi¯Çƒed Diagnostic Criteria for
VogtÔÇôKoyanagiÔÇôHarada Syndrome

1. Absence of a history of penetrating ocular trauma
2. Absence of other ocular disease entities
3. Bilateral uveitis
4. Neurological and auditory manifestations
5. Integumentary ¯Ç×ndings, not preceding onset of central

¯Çü Ultrasonography shows di¯Çøuse choroidal thickening and
excludes posterior scleritis. UBM can be used to demonstrate
a ciliary e¯Çøusion.
¯Çü FA of the acute phase shows multifocal hyper¯Ç×uorescent dots
at the level of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE ÔÇô Fig.
12.18C) followed by subretinal pooling (Fig. 12.18D). ¯Çƒe
chronic phase shows RPE window defects.
¯Çü OCT allows quanti¯ÇØcation of subretinal ¯Ç×uid. ¯Çƒe subretinal
septae are typical (Fig. 12.18E and F).
¯Çü ICGA during the acute phase of the disease shows regularly
distributed hypo¯Ç×uorescent spots, most of which remain
hypo¯Ç×uorescent during the late phase, when di¯Çøuse hyper¯Ç×uorescence over the posterior pole is also shown. ICGA is useful
for monitoring.

## Treatment

months. ¯Çƒis may be preceded by intravenous methylprednisolone
pulse therapy (500ÔÇô1000 mg/day). Topical steroids and cycloplegics are used for anterior uveitis. Steroid-resistant patients may
require immunosuppressives. Biological blockers such as in¯Ç×iximab should be used early in the course of the disease if there is no
response to steroids.

SYMPATHETIC OPHTHALMIA

## Introduction

Sympathetic ophthalmia (SO) is a bilateral granulomatous panuveitis occurring a¯Ç£er penetrating trauma where uveal prolapse may
have been a feature of the trauma. Less frequently the condition
occurs following intraocular surgery, usually multiple vitreoretinal
procedures. ¯Çƒe condition has been associated with speci¯ÇØc HLA
haplotypes, which would suggest that in some patients there is a
genetic predisposition. Presentation in trauma-induced cases is
between 2 weeks and 3 months a¯Ç£er initial injury in 65%. ¯Çƒe incidence is probably 0.2ÔÇô0.5% a¯Ç£er injury and 0.01% following intraocular surgery. A recent meta-analysis of sympathetic ophthalmia
following open globe injury found an incidence of 0.19%. Histopathology shows a di¯Çøuse lymphocytic in¯ÇØltration of the choroid.
Scattered aggregates of epithelioid cells are seen, many of which
contain ¯ÇØne granules of melanin (Fig. 12.19A). DalenÔÇôFuchs nodules, which also occur in VogtÔÇôKoyanagiÔÇôHarada syndrome (see
above), are granulomas located between Bruch membrane and the
RPE (Fig. 12.19B).

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. ¯Çƒere is a history of causative trauma. ¯Çƒe exciting eye is frequently red and irritable. ¯Çƒe sympathizing eye
develops irritation, blurred vision, photophobia and loss of
accommodation.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis develops in both eyes, which may be mild or
severe and is usually granulomatous (Fig. 12.20A). ¯Çƒe severity of in¯Ç×ammation may be markedly asymmetrical.
¯Çü Fundus: multifocal choroidal in¯ÇØltrates develop in the mid
periphery (Fig. 12.20B), with sub-RPE in¯ÇØltrates corresponding to DalenÔÇôFuchs nodules. Exudative retinal detachment,
vasculitis and optic disc swelling may all manifest. As in¯Ç×ammation settles, residual chorioretinal scarring may confer a
ÔÇÿsunset glowÔÇÖ appearance similar to VKH (see Fig. 12.17B).
Extensive chorioretinal scarring may follow (Fig. 12.20C).
¯Çü Systemic manifestations similar to those in VKH can occur
but are uncommon.
¯Çü Prognosis depends on the severity and location of disease
and the response to treatment. With aggressive therapy 75%
of sympathizing eyes retain a visual acuity of better than 6/60.
Long-term follow-up is mandatory because relapses occur in
50% of cases and may be delayed for several years.

## Investigation

¯Çü OCT is useful for quantifying and monitoring change.
¯Çü B-scan ultrasonography may demonstrate choroidal
thickening.
¯Çü FA shows multiple foci of leakage at the level of the RPE,
with subretinal pooling in the presence of exudative retinal
detachment.
¯Çü ICGA shows hypo¯Ç×uorescent spots in active disease, which
resolve with treatment.
¯Çü FAF is helpful when following these patients, as it provides a
non-invasive and objective method of monitoring disease progression and response to treatment (Fig. 12.20D).
¯Çü Ultrasound may show choroidal thickening and exudative
retinal detachment in the acute stage.

## Treatment

¯Çü Enucleation of a severely injured eye within 10ÔÇô14 days of
the injury has historically been considered e¯Çøective in preventing or reducing the severity of SO and should be considered for an injured eye with a hopeless visual prognosis.

## Evisceration is an acceptable option provided all uveal tissue

is removed.
¯Çü Steroids are the basis of initial treatment. High-dose oral prednisolone is given for several months and gradually tapered
according to response. Initiation with intravenous methylprednisolone may be used in some cases. Supplementary topical steroids and cycloplegics may be given to target anterior
uveitis and peri- and intraocular steroids, including slowrelease intravitreal implants, may facilitate reduced systemic
treatment.
¯Çü Immunomodulatory treatment should be considered early in
the course of the disease (see above).
