LENS-INDUCED UVEITIS

## Introduction

Lens-induced or phacogenic uveitis results from an immune
response to lens proteins following exposure due to incomplete
cataract extraction, trauma or rarely capsular degeneration in a
mature cataract (Fig. 12.21A). ¯Çƒe most commonly encountered
modern scenario is that of retained lens fragments following
phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation, either in the posterior segment a¯Ç£er posterior capsular rupture or zonular dehiscence, or an overlooked
piece of nucleus or so¯Ç£ lens matter settling in the AC (see also Ch.
10). ¯Çƒis must be di¯Çøerentiated from bacterial endophthalmitis,
which is usually more severe.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Variable pain, photophobia, redness and blurring,
usually with a history of recent (complicated or uncomplicated) cataract surgery and uncommonly of injury.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis may be mild, moderate or severe (Fig.
12.21B).
¯Çü Corneal oedema is common adjacent to an AC lens fragment.
¯Çü IOP is frequently elevated.
¯Çü Lens fragments may be visible in the anterior or posterior
segment.
¯Çü Vitritis of variable severity is usually present if lens fragments
lie within the vitreous cavity.
¯Çü Lens injury may be evident in cases associated with trauma.
¯Çü Complications include CMO, glaucoma, epiretinal membrane
and, rarely, more severe sequelae such as retinal detachment
and cyclitic membrane.

## Investigation

OCT, B-scan and biomicroscopic ultrasonography may be
indicated.

## Treatment

Treatment involves steroids, the route and intensity dependent on
clinical circumstances, with surgical removal of all lens material
from the AC or via pars plana vitrectomy as required. Small lens
fragments in the posterior segment can o¯Ç£en be managed conservatively and will absorb slowly over months. Cycloplegia and
IOP-lowering treatment are commonly indicated. Penetrating or
(uncommonly) blunt ocular injury should be managed concomitantly as appropriate, including removal of the damaged lens.

SARCOIDOSIS

## Introduction

Sarcoidosis is a chronic disorder of unknown cause, manifesting
with non-caseating granulomatous in¯Ç×ammatory foci. It can a¯Çøect
any organ system, but the lungs and lymph nodes are the most
commonly involved. It a¯Çøects Black people signi¯ÇØcantly more
o¯Ç£en than White people by a margin of 10:1 and is more common
in colder climates. It is one of the most common systemic associations of uveitis.

## Systemic features

¯Çü Presentation. Respiratory symptoms (cough, shortness of
breath on exertion) and constitutional symptoms (malaise,
arthralgia) each occur in about 50% of patients. L├Âfgren syndrome is an acute presentation carrying a good prognosis,
characterized by the triad of erythema nodosum, bilateral
hilar lymphadenopathy (Fig. 12.22A) on chest X-ray and polyarthralgia, usually seen in women. A minority of patients are
asymptomatic (incidentally abnormal chest X-ray). Diagnosis
may be made as the result of investigation of extrapulmonary
in¯Ç×ammation such as uveitis.
¯Çü Lung disease ranges from mild parenchymal in¯ÇØltration to
severe pulmonary ¯ÇØbrosis.

A B

Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A; ADN Murray ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

A

B

C

¯Ç£B¯ÇØ erythema nodosum; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ lupus pernio of the cheek.

¯Çü Skin lesions are seen in about 25% of patients and can include erythema nodosum (tender erythematous plaques typically involving
the shins ÔÇô Fig. 12.22B), lupus pernio (indurated violaceous lesions
involving exposed parts of the body such as the nose, cheeks, ¯ÇØngers
and ears ÔÇô Fig. 12.22C) and granulomatous papules or macules.

¯Çü Neurological disease is rare, but meningitis and cranial nerve
palsies may occur. Pituitary involvement can lead to hormonal
abnormalities.
¯Çü Cardiac involvement is relatively uncommon (5% clinically),
but is critically important as it may lead to arrhythmia and
sudden death.
¯Çü Lymphadenopathy. Enlargement of super¯ÇØcial nodes is sometimes the initial clinical manifestation.

## Ocular features

Ocular in¯Ç×ammation occurs in 25ÔÇô70% of sarcoid patients
depending on ethnicity. Granulomatous anterior uveitis is the
most common manifestation. Blindness can occur if not adequately managed. AAU typically a¯Çøects patients with acute-onset
sarcoidosis. CAU, typically granulomatous, tends to a¯Çøect older
patients with chronic pulmonary disease. ¯Çƒe International Workshop on Ocular Sarcoidosis (IWOS), reporting in 2009, identi¯ÇØed
seven key signs in the diagnosis of intraocular sarcoidosis:
¯Çü ÔÇÿMutton fatÔÇÖ KPs (Fig. 12.23A) and/or small granulomatous KPs and/or iris nodules (Koeppe and/or Busacca ÔÇô Fig.
12.23B).
¯Çü Trabecular meshwork nodules (Fig. 12.23C) and/or tentshaped PAS.
¯Çü Vitreous opacities: snowballs (Fig. 12.23D) and/or ÔÇÿstrings of
pearlsÔÇÖ.
¯Çü Multiple chorioretinal peripheral lesions (active and/or atrophic). Choroidal lesions are uncommon and vary in appearance: multiple small pale-yellow in¯ÇØltrates, sometimes with
a punched-out appearance (Fig. 12.24A and B) are the commonest and are o¯Ç£en most numerous inferiorly. Multiple large
con¯Ç×uent in¯ÇØltrates are less common (Fig. 12.24C). Multifocal
choroiditis carries a poor visual prognosis even a¯Ç£er resolution of activity, as a result of secondary macular neovascularization associated with macular or peripapillary chorioretinal
scarring. Retinal granulomas may also occur, seen as discrete
small yellowÔÇôwhite lesions (Fig. 12.24D).
¯Çü Nodular and/or segmental periphlebitis (┬▒ ÔÇÿcandle wax
drippingsÔÇÖ) and/or retinal macroaneurysm in an in¯Çƒamed
eye. Periphlebitis appears as yellowish or grey-white perivenous sheathing. Perivenous exudates referred to as ÔÇÿcandle
wax drippingsÔÇÖ (ÔÇÿen taches de bougieÔÇÖ) are typical of severe sarcoid periphlebitis (Fig. 12.25A). Occlusive periphlebitis (Fig.
12.25B) is uncommon, but peripheral retinal neovascularization may develop secondary to retinal capillary dropout. In
Black patients it may be mistaken for proliferative sickle-cell
retinopathy.
¯Çü Optic disc nodule(s)/granuloma(s) and/or solitary choroidal nodule. Solitary choroidal nodules are less common than
multiple lesions in sarcoidosis. Focal optic nerve granulomas
do not usually a¯Çøect vision (see Fig. 19.11). Persistent disc
oedema is a frequent ¯ÇØnding in patients with retinal or vitreous involvement (Fig. 12.25C and D) and papilloedema due
to CNS involvement may occur in the absence of other ocular
manifestations
¯Çü Other ocular manifestations include conjunctival nodules
resembling those of follicular conjunctivitis, lacrimal gland

458 Sarcoidosis

A B

C D

¯Ç£B¯ÇØ large iris nodules; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ nodular involvement of the trabecular meshwork; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ snowballs.

in¯ÇØltration (Fig. 12.26A), dry eye, eyelid nodules (Fig. 12.26B),
orbital and scleral lesions. Complications are those typically
seen in idiopathic uveitis, including cataract, glaucoma, posterior and peripheral anterior synechiae, band keratopathy,
vitreous haemorrhage, maculopathy (CMO, epiretinal membrane, macular neovascularization), retinal detachment and
phthisis.

## Investigation

In addition to the acquisition of histopathological evidence, IWOS
judged the following ¯ÇØve investigations to be of signi¯ÇØcant value in
the diagnosis of ocular sarcoidosis in patients having a compatible
uveitis:
¯Çü Negative tuberculin skin test in a BCG-vaccinated patient
or in a patient having had a positive tuberculin skin test previously. A tuberculin skin test is negative in most sarcoid
patients. A strongly positive reaction to one tuberculin unit
makes a diagnosis of sarcoidosis highly unlikely.
¯Çü Elevated serum ACE levels and/or elevated serum lysozyme
as described for investigation of AAU.

¯Çü Chest X-ray showing bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL).
Chest radiography is abnormal in 90%.
¯Çü Abnormal liver enzyme tests.
¯Çü Chest CT scan in patients with a negative chest X-ray result.
High-resolution CT scanning is of considerably greater value
than standard resolution imaging.
Other investigations are discussed below:
¯Çü Fibreoptic bronchoscopy with biopsy. Histopathological
con¯ÇØrmation of sarcoidosis is almost always required before
starting treatment. ¯Çƒe lung is a common site from which to
establish this in the presence of clinical or investigational evidence of pulmonary disease. However, a more easily accessible
super¯ÇØcial lesion should be chosen if available.
¯Çü ¯Ç×oracic endosonography (endobronchial or oesophageal)
with needle aspiration has been shown in a large trial to be a
more sensitive technique than bronchoscopic biopsy.
¯Çü Miscellaneous biopsy sites include super¯ÇØcial lymph nodes
or skin lesions, conjunctival nodules and lacrimal glands (up
to 75% of enlarged glands are positive). If the eye is involved,
vitreous biopsy is very useful (e.g. CD4/CD8 ratio).

A B

C D

multifocal granulomas; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ con¯ÇØuent choroidal in¯Ç×ltration; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ multiple small retinal granulomas.

¯Çü Other imaging modalities include MRI cardiac and CNS
imaging (MRI is less useful than CT for thoracic evaluation),
PET scanning and occasionally whole-body gallium scanning.
¯Çü Calcium and vitamin D levels may be abnormal depending
on disease pattern and level of activity.
¯Çü Hypercalciuria is common.
¯Çü Pulmonary function testing.
¯Çü Bronchoalveolar lavage ¯Çƒuid (BALF) shows characteristic
changes. CD4/CD8 T cell ratios are a key indicator.
¯Çü Induced sputum analysis correlates strongly with BALF and is
a non-invasive technique.
¯Çü Four diagnostic levels were de¯ÇØned by the IWOS for ocular
sarcoidosis:

{ De¯ÇØnite ocular sarcoidosis: biopsy-supported diagnosis in
the presence of a compatible uveitis.

{ Presumed ocular sarcoidosis: biopsy not done but chest
X-ray shows BHL with a compatible uveitis.

{ Probable ocular sarcoidosis: biopsy not done, no BHL on
chest X-ray but >3/7 of the intraocular signs above and
>2/5 positive laboratory tests.

{ Possible ocular sarcoidosis: lung biopsy negative but >4/7
signs and >2/5 positive laboratory tests.

## It is critical that alternative causes of uveitis are adequately

excluded by appropriate assessment and investigation.

## Treatment

## Corticosteroids have conventionally been the major treatment

modality in ocular and systemic sarcoidosis, though immunosuppressives are being used more commonly, particularly as steroidsparing agents and in refractory disease. Treatment should be
initiated aggressively to prevent sight-threatening complications.
¯Çü Treatment of anterior and intermediate uveitis is approached
in a stepwise fashion as for idiopathic in¯Ç×ammation.
¯Çü Posterior uveitis generally requires systemic steroids and
occasionally immunosuppressive agents such as methotrexate, azathioprine, ciclosporin and TNF inhibitors (e.g.
adalimumab).
¯Çü Peripheral retinal neovascularization can be treated with scatter photocoagulation to ischaemic areas demonstrated by FA.
¯Çü Cystoid macular oedema may respond to a topical NSAID.

¯Çü Cataract and glaucoma may require treatment. In¯Ç×ammation
should be suppressed prior to surgery, preferably for at least 3
months in the case of cataract surgery.

BEH├çET DISEASE

## Introduction

Beh├ºet disease (BD) is an idiopathic, multisystem syndrome characterized by recurrent aphthous oral ulcers, genital ulceration
and uveitis. Vasculitis is a key pathogenetic component and may
involve small, medium and large veins and arteries. Mortality is
around 5% at 5ÔÇô10 years, typically due to cardiovascular or CNS
complications. ¯Çƒe condition probably has an autoimmune basis
and may be precipitated by exposure to an infectious agent with
subsequent cross-reaction. It typically a¯Çøects patients from Turkey, the Middle and Far East (the ancient ÔÇÿSilk RoadÔÇÖ route), with a
lower prevalence in Europe and North America. It is strongly associated with HLA-B51; the ethnic groups with a higher prevalence
of Beh├ºet disease also have a higher rate of HLA-B51 positivity.
¯Çƒe peak age of onset is the third decade. Reported gender prevalence varies with ethnicity.

TIP A transient hypopyon in a relatively white eye is typical of
Beh├ºet disease and JIA-associated uveitis.

## Systemic features

¯Çƒe International Study Group for Beh├ºet Disease (ISGBD),
reporting in 1990, established criteria for diagnosis: (a) recurrent
oral ulceration (Fig. 12.27A) characterized by oral ulcers at least
three times in a 12-month period, plus at least two of: (b) genital
ulceration, (c) ocular in¯Ç×ammation, (d) characteristic skin lesions
(erythema nodosum ÔÇô see Fig. 12.22B, pseudofolliculitis, acneiform nodules, papulopustular lesions) and (e) a pathergy reaction:
pustule 24ÔÇô48 hours a¯Ç£er a sterile needle prick (>95% speci¯ÇØc, but
o¯Ç£en negative in European and North American patients).

Presentation does not always conform to the criteria above.
Additional features include:
¯Çü Vascular lesions. Aneurysms, including pulmonary and coronary and venous thrombosis/thrombophlebitis.
¯Çü Arthritis occurs in 30%, though arthralgia is more common.
¯Çü Dermatographia (Fig. 12.27B), similar to the pathergy reaction, indicates skin hypersensitivity and consists of the formation of erythematous lines following stroking or scratching.
¯Çü Neurological manifestations (5%) such as meningoencephalitis of the brainstem, dural sinus thrombosis and cerebral
aneurysms.
¯Çü Gastrointestinal in¯Çƒammation, especially ileocecal.
¯Çü Hepatic and renal lesions are relatively uncommon.

## Ocular features

Ocular in¯Ç×ammation occurs in about 70%, tends to be more severe
in men and is the presenting manifestation in about 10%. Signs
are virtually always bilateral eventually. Relapsing/remitting acute
onset panuveitis with retinal vasculitis and o¯Ç£en spontaneous
resolution even without treatment is the classical pattern of eye
involvement. Retinal vascular disease (vasculitis and occlusion) is
the main cause of visual impairment.
¯Çü AAU, o¯Ç£en bilateral, is typical. It is not granulomatous. A transient mobile hypopyon in a relatively white eye (Fig. 12.28A)
is characteristic.
¯Çü Vitritis may be severe and is universal in eyes with active posterior segment disease.
¯Çü Retinitis. Transient super¯ÇØcial white in¯ÇØltrates (Fig. 12.28B)
that heal without scarring may be seen during acute systemic
disease. ¯Çƒere may be deeper more di¯Çøuse retinitis similar in
appearance to viral in¯Ç×ammation. Exudative detachments can
also occur. In¯Ç×ammatory deposits analogous to KPs may be
seen on the inferior peripheral retina.
¯Çü Retinal vasculitis ÔÇô arteritis as well as phlebitis, in contrast to pure
venous involvement in sarcoidosis ÔÇô can manifest with sheathing,
perivascular haemorrhages and occlusion (Fig. 12.28C). Vascular
leakage may give rise to di¯Çøuse retinal oedema and CMO.

A B

462 Beh├ºet Disease

A B

C D

in¯Ç×ltrates; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ FA showing periphlebitis (arrow heads) and arterial attenuation (arrow); ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ endstage disease.

¯Çü Optic disc hyperaemia and oedema. Raised intracranial pressure can also cause optic disc swelling and optic atrophy in BD.
¯Çü Disc and retinal neovascularization may be seen as a response
to in¯Ç×ammation and ischaemia.
¯Çü Uncommon manifestations include conjunctivitis, conjunctival ulcers, episcleritis, scleritis and ophthalmoplegia from
neurological involvement.
¯Çü End-stage disease is characterized by optic atrophy, retinal
atrophy and gliosis and sheathing, attenuation and ghosting
of a¯Çøected vessels (Fig. 12.28D). ¯Çƒe vitreous tends to clear.
Other complications include PS, cataract, glaucoma and,
uncommonly, retinal detachment and phthisis. Severe visual
loss in males of up to two-thirds of patients at 10 years has
been reported, but is probably much lower with aggressive
management. ¯Çƒe rate in women is about half that in men.

## Investigation

¯Çü HLA-B51 (see above).
¯Çü Pathergy test (see above).

¯Çü In¯Çƒammatory markers (e.g. ESR, CRP, complement levels,
white cell count) may be elevated.
¯Çü ¯Ç×rombophilia screening is appropriate in some patients to
exclude other causes of thrombosis.
¯Çü FA delineates ischaemic areas and aids detection of posterior
segment in¯Ç×ammation and monitoring of disease activity.
¯Çü Laser ¯Çƒare photometry of the AC correlates well with FA in
determining the level of in¯Ç×ammatory activity.
¯Çü Super¯ÇØcial lesion biopsy, synovial ¯Ç×uid aspiration and lumbar
puncture may be used to help rule out alternative diagnoses.
¯Çü Systemic imaging may include brain MRI/magnetic resonance
angiography (MRA), CT/computed tomography angiography
(CTA) and conventional angiography to identify ischaemia.

## Treatment

Immunosuppressants are the mainstay of treatment, but availability and expense may limit therapeutic options in many regions.
¯Çü Topical steroids alone may be adequate if ÔÇô rarely ÔÇô there is no
trace of posterior segment involvement.

¯Çü Systemic steroids and azathioprine (2.5 mg/kg daily) in
combination are recommended for the initial management
of posterior uveitis in European League Against Rheumatism
(EULAR) 2008 BD guidelines. Steroids should be tapered
slowly. Topical and/or regional steroids may also be used. ¯Çƒere
may be a high rate of ocular hypertension with intravitreal steroid injection. Azathioprine may have a role in prophylaxis.
¯Çü Cyclosporine (2ÔÇô5 mg/kg daily) or in¯Ç×iximab, in combination
with azathioprine and systemic steroids, is recommended by
EULAR for severe eye disease (>2 lines reduction in visual acuity
and/or retinal vasculitis or macular involvement). A recent study
recommended a single in¯Ç×iximab infusion as initial treatment of
posterior uveitis. Hypertension, nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity are concerns with cyclosporine, which should be avoided in
patients with CNS involvement unless it is determined that severe
eye disease warrants the risk. In¯Ç×iximab may lead to activation of
tuberculosis and screening-positive patients should receive prophylactic treatment (e.g. isoniazid). Intravitreal administration is
a novel alternative route of administration for in¯Ç×iximab.
¯Çü In¯Çƒiximab or adalimumab should be considered early
for vision-threatening BD (American Uveitis Society
recommendation).
¯Çü Interferon-alfa (6 million IU per day subcutaneously initially, gradually tapered) with or without steroids is a EULARrecommended alternative to the ciclosporin/in¯Ç×iximab/
azathioprine/steroid regimen above for severe disease. It
should not be used in combination with azathioprine (risk of
myelosuppression).
¯Çü Anticoagulants are not recommended.

## Tissue
cysts

PARASITIC UVEITIS

## Toxoplasmosis

## Introduction

Toxoplasmosis is caused by Toxoplasma gondii, an obligate intracellular protozoan. It is estimated to infest at least 10% of adults
in northern temperate countries and more than half of adults in
Mediterranean and tropical countries. ¯Çƒe cat is the de¯ÇØnitive
host, with intermediate hosts including mice, livestock, birds and
humans. Oocysts are excreted in cat faeces and then ingested by
intermediate hosts (Fig. 12.29), including via contaminated water
supplies. Cat litter disposal with subsequent transfer to food is
a well-known potential mode of infection in humans (though
indoor cats have a low rate of toxoplasmosis infestation). ¯Çƒe
bradyzoite is an inactive stage lying dormant within cysts in tissues such as the eye, brain and skeletal muscle and consumption of undercooked meat (or eggs) from an intermediate host
can lead to infestation. Bradyzoite cysts can rupture to release
tachyzoites, the proliferating active form, stimulating an in¯Ç×ammatory reaction. Conceptually it may be helpful to think of an
acute phase and a long-term chronic phase of infection. During
the latter, new retinochoroidal scars may form asymptomatically
over the course of years or decades. A critical mode of human
infection is transplacental haematogenous spread to the fetus in
a pregnant woman with active (not inactive latent) toxoplasmosis ÔÇô this is usually primary infection in an immunocompetent
host but occasionally reactivation of latent infection, the latter predominantly in the immunocompromised. Occasionally

464 Parasitic Uveitis

infection may be transmitted via organ transplantation or blood
transfusion.

TIP Toxoplasmosis is the most common identi¯Ç×able cause for
focal retinitis, which typically causes in¯ÇØammation adjacent to a
chorioretinal scar.

## Systemic features

¯Çü Congenital toxoplasmosis. ¯Çƒe mother is o¯Ç£en symptomfree or has only mild constitutional manifestations. ¯Çƒe severity of fetal involvement is related to the duration of gestation
at the time of maternal infection, tending to be more severe
in early pregnancy, when fetal death may result (10% of all
congenital toxoplasmosis). Neurological and visceral involvement may be very severe, but many cases are subclinical,
especially in later pregnancy. Retinochoroiditis may occur in
over 75%, leaving scars that are commonly a later incidental
¯ÇØnding (Fig. 12.30).
¯Çü Postnatal childhood acquisition probably accounts for over
50% of cases of childhood toxoplasmosis. As with adults, in

immunocompetent individuals this is usually subclinical.

## Ocular lesions are probably common but may not develop for

years a¯Ç£er the initial infection.
¯Çü Acquired toxoplasmosis in immunocompetent adults is subclinical in 80ÔÇô90%. Cervical lymphadenopathy, fever, malaise
and pharyngitis are common features in symptomatic patients,
but more serious systemic manifestations are rare. Early retinitis may occur in up to 20%.
¯Çü Toxoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients may be
acquired or result from reactivation of pre-existing disease. As
well as the constitutional symptoms occurring in the immunocompetent, meningoencephalitis, pneumonitis, retinochoroiditis and a range of other features can occur.

## Ocular features

Toxoplasmosis is responsible for 20ÔÇô60% of all cases of posterior
uveitis depending on the country surveyed (85% in Brazil). Reactivation at previously inactive cyst-containing scars is the rule in
the immunocompetent, although many represent new infection.

## More than half of quiescent retinal lesions will have been acquired

from postnatal infection. Recurrent episodes of in¯Ç×ammation are

A B

C D

common, especially in the year a¯Ç£er the initial episode and occur
when the cysts rupture and release hundreds of tachyzoites into
normal retinal cells. First presentation with symptomatic ocular infection occurs at an average age of 29 years, perhaps due to
decreasing speci¯ÇØc immunity. Ocular involvement from congenital infection may only be detected later in life with the incidental
discovery of typical retinochoroidal scars, though occasionally
macular or optic nerve damage may impair vision in childhood.
¯Çü Symptoms. Unilateral acute or subacute onset of ¯Ç×oaters, blurring and photophobia.
¯Çü ÔÇÿSpill-overÔÇÖ anterior uveitis is common. It may be granulomatous or resemble FUS. Elevated IOP may follow.
¯Çü A single in¯Çƒammatory focus of ¯Ç×u¯Çøy white retinitis or retinochoroiditis associated with a pigmented scar (ÔÇÿsatellite lesionÔÇÖ)
is typical (Fig. 12.31A and B). Lesions tend to involve the posterior pole.
¯Çü De novo foci not associated with an old scar and multiple lesions
(Fig. 12.31C) are relatively uncommon in the immunocompetent but occur more frequently in the immunocompromised.
¯Çü Vitritis may be severe and impair fundus visualization. ÔÇÿHeadlight in the fogÔÇÖ is the classic description of a white retinal
in¯Ç×ammatory nidus viewed through vitritis (Fig. 12.31D).
¯Çü Vasculitis may be arterial but is more commonly venous.
¯Çü Optic disc oedema is common.
¯Çü Extensive and fulminant retinal involvement is generally
con¯ÇØned to the immunocompromised, in whom it may be
bilateral and di¯ÇÜcult to distinguish from viral retinitis.
¯Çü Retinochoroiditis may be absent in the acute phase of acquired
disease, with activity consisting of anterior uveitis, vitritis and
retinal vasculitis. Typical retinal scars may form later.
¯Çü Neuroretinitis similar to that seen in cat-scratch disease is
rare and may be a marker of acutely acquired rather than reactivated infection.
¯Çü Punctate outer retinal toxoplasmosis is an atypical manifestation featuring clusters of small (25ÔÇô75 ╬╝m diameter) greyÔÇô
white lesions and tends to occur in younger patients.
¯Çü Visual loss. Causes of permanently reduced vision (around
25% of eyes) include macular in¯Ç×ammatory lesions and
oedema, optic nerve involvement, vascular occlusion (Fig.
12.32A and B), serous, rhegmatogenous and tractional retinal
detachment (Fig. 12.32C and D) and late secondary macular
neovascularization (Fig. 12.32E and F).
¯Çü Healing in immunocompetent hosts usually occurs spontaneously within 6ÔÇô8 weeks, although vitreous opacities take longer to clear. ¯Çƒe in¯Ç×ammatory focus is replaced by a sharply
demarcated atrophic scar that develops a pigmented border
(Fig. 12.33).
¯Çü Recurrence. ¯Çƒe average number of recurrent attacks per
patient is 2.7. ¯Çƒe frequency of recurrences does not di¯Çøer in
geographic areas and is about 12ÔÇô15% in the ¯ÇØrst 2 years. Within
5 years more than half may experience a further episode.

## Investigation

Diagnosis is usually based on clinical examination ¯ÇØndings.
¯Çü Serology. Toxoplasma IgG antibodies are detectable in the
serum within 1ÔÇô2 weeks of initial infection and indicate

exposure to the organism at some point in the past, providing
circumstantial evidence to support clinical suspicion. However, community seroprevalence is high ÔÇô at least a third of
individuals in most communities. Positivity to IgM antibodies
usually means that infection has been acquired within the last
year and helps to distinguish between acute (newly acquired)
and chronic infection. It is a critical test if newly acquired
infection is suspected during pregnancy. Occasionally IgM is
positive due to persistence or reactivation following earlier
infection.
¯Çü PCR testing of intraocular ¯Ç×uid is variably sensitive (16ÔÇô67%)
but highly speci¯ÇØc and can be diagnostic in clinically uncertain
cases. Aqueous and vitreous probably give similar yields.
¯Çü Ocular ¯Çƒuid antibody assessment. Calculating the ratio
(GoldmannÔÇôWitmer coe¯ÇÜcient) of speci¯ÇØc IgG in aqueous
humour to that in serum seems to be a reasonably sensitive
(48ÔÇô90%) investigation.
¯Çü Imaging. OCT will demonstrate macular oedema. B-scan
ultrasonic imaging can be used to exclude retinal detachment
in the presence of severe vitritis and FAF may facilitate monitoring of in¯Ç×ammatory activity.

## Treatment

¯Çü Active ocular toxoplasmosis is treated with antibiotics, but
none of the current approaches has been shown to be better
than another in respect of the ¯ÇØnal visual outcome, lesion size
or recurrence rate.
¯Çü Eradication of the parasite is di¯ÇÜcult to determine, but parasite activity and multiplication may be reduced leading to a
decrease in size of the eventual retinochoroidal scar.
¯Çü Treatment is not needed in every case. ¯Çƒe agents that are
used have the potential for signi¯ÇØcant morbidity and spontaneous resolution o¯Ç£en occurs, even without treatment.
¯Çü Clear indications include:

{ A sight-threatening lesion involving the macula, papillomacular bundle, optic nerve head or a major blood vessel.

{ Severe vitritis.

{ Active ocular toxoplasmosis in the immunocompromised.
¯Çü Treatment duration: typically, 4ÔÇô6 weeks with complete healing of active lesions over 4ÔÇô6 months.
¯Çü Treatment of congenital toxoplasmosis in neonates with
antimicrobials for 1 year may reduce the frequency of subsequent development of retinochoroidal scars.
¯Çü Prednisolone (1 mg/kg) is given initially and tapered according to clinical response, but should always be used in conjunction with a speci¯ÇØc anti-Toxoplasma agent, most frequently
pyrimethamine combined with sulfadiazine (ÔÇÿclassicÔÇÖ or ÔÇÿtripleÔÇÖ
therapy, sometimes supplemented with clindamycin). Some
authorities start steroids only a¯Ç£er 24ÔÇô48 hours of antimicrobial therapy. Systemic steroids should be used with extreme
caution in immunocompromised patients and children should
undergo varicella zoster vaccination if they have not had
chickenpox.
¯Çü Pyrimethamine is a folic acid antagonist that is believed
to be highly e¯Çøective. It is administered as a loading dose
of 75ÔÇô100 mg for 1ÔÇô2 days followed by 25ÔÇô50 mg daily

466 Parasitic Uveitis

A B

C D

scar; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ two small active foci; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ vitreous haze and ÔÇÿheadlight in the fogÔÇÖ appearance of
lesion. (Courtesy of S Chen ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A; C Pavesio ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

for 4 weeks in combination with oral folinic (not folic) acid
5 mg three times a week to retard thrombocytopenia, leukopenia and folate de¯ÇØciency. Weekly blood counts should
be performed. In acquired immunode¯ÇØciency syndrome
(AIDS) pyrimethamine is avoided or used at a lower dosage
because of possible pre-existing bone marrow suppression
and the antagonistic e¯Çøect of zidovudine when the drugs are
combined.
¯Çü Sulfadiazine 1 g four times daily for 3ÔÇô4 weeks is usually given
in combination with pyrimethamine. Side e¯Çøects of sulfonamides include renal stones, allergic reactions and StevensÔÇô
Johnson syndrome.
¯Çü Intravitreal therapy with clindamycin (1 mg) and dexamethasone (400 ╬╝g) may be as e¯Çøective as triple therapy in reactivated infection. Two to three injections (at 2-weekly intervals)
may be required. It may be preferred in recurrent infection in
pregnancy, but would not generally be used in isolation in the
immunocompromised, and in newly acquired (IgM-positive)
infection systemic therapy has superior e¯ÇÜcacy. Depot steroid

intra- and periocular preparations such as triamcinolone should
be avoided as uncontrolled progression has been reported.
¯Çü Azithromycin 250ÔÇô500 mg daily shows evidence of reducing
the rate of recurrence of retinochoroiditis and its use in combination with pyrimethamine, folinic acid and prednisolone
is a promising new regimen. Clarithromycin may be a good
alternative to azithromycin.
¯Çü Co-trimoxazole (trimethoprim 160 mg/sulfamethoxazole 800
mg) twice daily in combination with prednisolone is a low-cost
and better-tolerated option, but might not be quite as e¯Çøective
as classic therapy.
¯Çü Clindamycin 300 mg four times daily may be added to triple
therapy (see above) or used instead of pyrimethamine. Pseudomembranous colitis is a potential adverse e¯Çøect.
¯Çü Atovaquone theoretically attacks encysted bradyzoites, but
does not seem to prevent recurrence in¯Çƒvivo; dosing is 750 mg
two to four times daily.
¯Çü Topical steroid and mydriatic may be given for anterior
uveitis.

A B

¯Çü Antimicrobial maintenance therapy is used in immunocompromised patients.
¯Çü Pregnancy. Treatment of recurrent ocular toxoplasmosis during pregnancy should be chosen carefully and only started if
absolutely necessary, as several of the drugs discussed above
have the potential to harm the fetus. Intravitreal therapy (see
above) for reactivated disease, or systemic treatment with
azithromycin, clindamycin and possibly prednisolone may be
appropriate. Speci¯ÇØc treatment to prevent transmission to the
fetus is not generally given except in newly acquired infection,
when urgent specialist management is appropriate. Spiramycin
alone or in combination is commonly chosen.
¯Çü Vitrectomy may be performed in selected cases.
¯Çü Prevention of recurrence. Co-trimoxazole (160mg trimethoprim combined with 800mg sulfamethoxazole) taken three
times daily for 12 months has been shown to reduce the recurrence rate from 22% to 3% for up to 3 years. A¯Ç£er that period
the likelihood of recurrence is as high as in patients who have
never received prophylaxis.

## Toxocariasis

## Introduction

## Toxocariasis is caused by infestation with a common intestinal

ascarid (roundworm) of dogs, Toxocara canis. Puppies are more
commonly infected than adult dogs and are also more likely to
spread the organism. ¯Çƒe feline variant ÔÇô Toxocara cati ÔÇô may also
be causative. Human infestation is by ingestion of soil or food
contaminated with ova shed in canine faeces. Young children are
at particular risk from the soil of parks and playgrounds. Once
ingested, ova develop into larvae, which penetrate the intestinal
wall and travel to various organs such as the liver, lungs, skin, brain
and eyes, with resultant in¯Ç×ammation.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Asymptomatic infestation is common.
¯Çü Visceral toxocariasis, also known as visceral larva migrans, is systemic infection of variable severity that usually occurs in a child
aged 2ÔÇô7 years. Fever, abdominal pain, pneumonitis, lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly and myocarditis are some of the possible
features. Spontaneous recovery is usual. Death is very rare and
usually occurs in individuals hypersensitive to parasitic antigens.
¯Çü Covert toxocariasis is associated with mild systemic symptoms.
¯Çü Ocular toxocariasis (ocular larva migrans) generally occurs
independently of visceral larva migrans and is associated with
a lower parasitic load. It is typically unilateral and in around
two-thirds causes some degree of permanent visual impairment. In contrast to visceral toxocariasis, it tends to occur in
older children and adults.

{ Chronic endophthalmitis (Fig. 12.34A) typically presents
with leukocoria, strabismus, ¯Ç×oaters or unilateral visual
loss. Features may include anterior uveitis, vitritis, chorioretinitis, papillitis and a fundus granuloma (see below).
A dense greyish-white exudate, similar to the snowbanking seen in PP, may involve the peripheral retina and pars
plana. Complications leading to a poor visual outcome
include tractional retinal or ciliary body detachment with
hypotony leading to phthisis bulbi (Fig. 12.34B).

{ Posterior pole or peripheral granuloma without in¯Ç×ammation (Fig. 12.35AÔÇôC) classically occurs in an older child or
adult with unilateral impaired vision or as an incidental
¯ÇØnding. A one to two disc diameter-sized round yellowwhite granuloma is present in the posterior fundus or
periphery. Vitreoretinal traction may lead to complications due to macular distortion and/or retinal detachment.

{ Chorioretinal scar (Fig. 12.35D).

{ Di¯Çøuse unilateral subacute neuroretinitis (DUSN). See
below.

A B

showing an in¯ÇØammatory mass and total retinal detachment. (J Harry and G Misson, from
Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

A B

C D

with a vitreous band extending to the disc; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ mid-peripheral granuloma with underlying
chorioretinal scarring; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ chorioretinal scar at the macula with granuloma.

470 Parasitic Uveitis

## Investigation

It is particularly important to distinguish a Toxocara granuloma
from retinoblastoma. Other helminthic organisms can give similar
clinical manifestations.
¯Çü Full blood count. Eosinophilia may be present, particularly in
visceral larva migrans and can become chronic.
¯Çü Hypergammaglobulinaemia especially IgE.
¯Çü Serology. Antibodies to Toxocara canis are detectable in only
about 50% of ocular cases. Positivity is common in the general
population (14% overall in the USA).
¯Çü Ultrasonography may be useful if the vitreous is hazy.
¯Çü Aqueous or vitreous sampling for eosinophilia, antibody
detection and PCR.
¯Çü Biopsy of a granuloma of the skin or elsewhere for larvae is
sometimes possible.

## Ocular treatment

¯Çü Prevention by good hygiene practices and deworming of pets.
¯Çü Steroids. Topical, regional and systemic may be considered.
¯Çü Anthelmintic agents such as mebendazole and thiabendazole
can be considered in ocular toxocariasis, remembering that
worm death may promote in¯Ç×ammation.
¯Çü Vitrectomy for sight-threatening tractional sequelae.

## Onchocerciasis

## Introduction

Onchocerciasis, which a¯Çøects the eyes and skin, is the second most
common cause of infectious blindness in the world. It is endemic

## Simulium
fly

## Microfilariae

in areas of Africa and other regions and disease is particularly
severe in savanna regions, where in some areas more than 50% of
older adults are blind from the condition. Chronic onchocerciasis shortens lifespan by reducing resistance to other diseases. ¯Çƒe
parasitic helminth Onchocerca volvulus is causative. ¯Çƒe vector is
the Simulium black¯Ç×y, which breeds in fast-¯Ç×owing water, hence
the colloquial term ÔÇÿriver blindnessÔÇÖ. Larvae are transmitted when
the ¯Ç×y bites to obtain blood. ¯Çƒey migrate to subcutaneous sites to
form onchocercomas, where micro¯ÇØlariae are produced by adult
worms (Fig. 12.36). Degenerating micro¯ÇØlariae excite an intense
in¯Ç×ammatory reaction accounting for most of the clinical manifestations of the disease. ¯Çƒe rickettsia Wolbachia lives symbiotically
in adult worms and micro¯ÇØlaria and is important for micro¯ÇØlarial production. Diagnosis is by skin-snip biopsy, serology, tear or
urine antigen detection (oncho-dipstick) and PCR of lesion ¯Ç×uid.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Systemic features are principally dermatological and include
pruritus, a maculopapular rash (onchodermatitis ÔÇô Fig. 12.37A)
involving the buttocks and extremities and areas of hypo- and
hyperpigmentation on the shins (ÔÇÿleopard skinÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 12.37B).
Scratching of itchy areas leads to licheni¯ÇØcation. Onchocercomata are non-tender subcutaneous nodules (Fig. 12.37C) that
enclose 2ÔÇô3 adult worms. Severe lymphadenopathy can occur,
with secondary lymphoedema. Eosinophilia is typical.
¯Çü Live micro¯ÇØlariae may be seen in the cornea, vitreous and suspended in the AC a¯Ç£er the patient has postured face-down for
a few minutes followed by immediate slit lamp examination.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis is an early feature. Pear-shaped pupillary dilatation may be seen and is due to PS.

## Larva

¯Ç£B¯ÇØ intraretinal pigment clumping and RPE atrophy; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ
choroidal pigment clumping and chorioretinal atrophy.

472 Viral Uveitis

¯Çü Keratitis. Punctate keratitis (snow¯Ç×ake opacities) a¯Çøects a
third of patients and consists of in¯ÇØltrates surrounding dead
micro¯ÇØlariae. Initial lesions are most commonly located at 3
and 9 oÔÇÖclock in the anterior third of the stroma. Slowly progressing sclerosing keratitis may eventually involve the entire
cornea (Fig. 12.38A).
¯Çü Chorioretinitis is usually bilateral and predominantly involves
the temporal fundus, sparing the macula until late. Widespread choroidal sclerosis and atrophy can ensue (Fig. 12.38B
and C). A perpetuating autoimmune response is hypothesized
for progressive longer-term chorioretinopathy that may persist
a¯Ç£er control of infection.
¯Çü Optic neuritis may be acute.

## Treatment

¯Çü Ivermectin (supplied in many countries by Merck at no charge)
kills micro¯ÇØlariae (but not adult worms) and is given at least annually for many years. It has been e¯Çøective in substantially reducing
transmission rates and morbidity and no recent new cases have
been reported in many communities. Ivermectin occasionally
precipitates in¯Ç×ammation, so prophylactic prednisolone may be
considered in patients with visible AC micro¯ÇØlariae. Ivermectin
may cause toxic encephalopathy in patients with Loa loa infection.
¯Çü Moxidectin is a newer drug that may be superior to
ivermectin.
¯Çü Doxycycline 100ÔÇô200 mg per day for 6 weeks targets Wolbachia, indirectly preventing micro¯ÇØlarial embryogenesis and
substantially reducing numbers for an extended period. It also
has some e¯Çøect on adult worms and may be a useful adjunct to
ivermectin or moxidectin.
¯Çü Suramin is e¯Çøective against adult worms. It is given
intravenously.
¯Çü Steroids. Anterior uveitis is responsive.

TIP Ivermectin given annually is e¯Çƒective in reducing
transmission rates and ocular morbidity in onchocerciases.

## Cysticercosis

## Introduction

Cysticercosis refers to infection by Cysticercus cellulosae, the larval
form of the pork tapeworm Taenia solium. Ingesting cysts of T.
solium in undercooked pork leads to intestinal tapeworm development (taeniasis). ¯Çƒe infested human then sheds eggs that lead
to larval infection (cysticercosis) when ingested by the same or
another individual. In¯Ç×ammation develops in response to antigens
released by dead organisms.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Systemic disease may involve the lungs, muscle and CNS
(neurocysticercosis). MRI and CT imaging are e¯Çøective at
demonstrating cysts and plain X-rays may show calci¯ÇØed cysts.
Serology and stool analysis are useful for diagnosis.
¯Çü Ocular features include cysts of the conjunctiva and rarely
the AC may show a free-¯Ç×oating cyst (Fig. 12.39A). Larvae

can enter the subretinal space (Fig. 12.39B) and may cause
exudative retinal detachment (Fig. 12.39C). ¯Çƒey can also
pass into the vitreous where released toxins can incite an
intense vision-threatening in¯Ç×ammatory reaction. A cyst
may occasionally involve the orbit, eyelids or rectus muscle
(Fig. 12.39D).

## Treatment

Systemic steroids to control in¯Ç×ammation are combined with
surgical removal of the larvae from the AC, vitreous and subretinal space. Anthelmintic agents such as albendazole may
be appropriate in systemic disease, but should be used with
caution under specialist guidance and o¯Ç£en with steroid
co-administration.

Di¯Çøuse unilateral subacute
neuroretinitis ¯Ç£DUSN¯ÇØ

## Introduction

DUSN is a clinical syndrome due to the presence of a single motile
subretinal nematode such as Toxocara canis, Baylisascaris procyonis and Ancylostoma caninum. Misdiagnosis (e.g. multifocal
choroiditis) is common as the worm is o¯Ç£en small and may be
overlooked.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Presentation is with insidious monocular visual decrease. ¯Çƒe
disease is diagnosed clinically as special investigations are not
helpful. Electroretinography (ERG) is subnormal, even in early
disease.
¯Çü Acute disease. Crops of greyÔÇôwhite outer retinal lesions (Fig.
12.40),vitritis, papillitis and retinal vasculitis.
¯Çü End-stage disease. Optic atrophy, retinal vascular attenuation
and di¯Çøuse RPE degeneration.

## Treatment

Photocoagulation (200 ╬╝m, 0.2ÔÇô0.5 s, 150ÔÇô300 mW) is the treatment of choice when a worm can be visualized (<50%). If necessary, a slit beam or very light laser burns are ¯ÇØrst used to shepherd
(sometimes lure) the photosensitive nematode away from the
fovea. Systemic albendazole (400 mg for 30 days) or vitrectomy
may be appropriate in some cases. Steroid cover may be prudent
with all treatment modalities.

VIRAL UVEITIS

Uveitis in human immunode¯ÇÜciency
virus infection

## Introduction

Human immunode¯ÇØciency virus infection/acquired immunode¯ÇØciency syndrome (HIV/AIDS) is transmitted by unprotected sexual intercourse, via contaminated blood or needles and vertically
from mother to child transplacentally, during birth or breastfeeding. HIV depletes CD4 [+] T cells, which are vital to the initiation of

A B

C D

of the larva; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ subretinal cyst with overlying exudative retinal detachment; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ axial CT
scan showing a cyst in the right medial rectus muscle. (Courtesy of Dr RSR Naik ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A;
A Pearson ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

the immune response to pathogens. Although there is currently no
cure or vaccine, the progression of disease can be slowed radically
by combination drug therapy: antiretroviral therapy (ART). ART
is now recommended for all HIV-positive individuals irrespective
of CD4 [+] T cell count. Prophylaxis against opportunistic infections
may also be instituted.

## Systemic features

spots. (Courtesy of SR Rathinam.)

¯Çü Stages of infection: (a) a ¯Ç×u-like illness may occur 2ÔÇô4 weeks
a¯Ç£er infection, sometimes including a rash; (b) clinical latency
is a predominantly asymptomatic period of several (average
8) years, sometimes with minor clinical features such as persistent generalized lymphadenopathy; (c) AIDS develops in
about half of HIV-positive individuals within 10 years and is
de¯ÇØned as HIV infection with either a CD4 [+] T count <200
cells/╬╝l or the development of one or more AIDS-de¯ÇØning
conditions.
¯Çü AIDS-de¯ÇØning clinical conditions include particular
opportunistic infections such as respiratory or oesophageal

474 Viral Uveitis

candidiasis, Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (Fig. 12.41A),
cryptosporidiosis and cytomegalovirus retinitis, speci¯ÇØc
tumours including Kaposi sarcoma (Fig. 12.41B) and certain lymphomas and other manifestations such as HIV
wasting syndrome (Fig. 12.41C) and progressive multifocal
leukoencephalopathy.

## Ocular features

¯Çü Eyelid. Blepharitis, Kaposi sarcoma, multiple molluscum
lesions and herpes zoster ophthalmicus.
¯Çü Orbit. Cellulitis (e.g. aspergillosis, contiguous sinus infection),
B-cell lymphoma.
¯Çü Conjunctiva. Kaposi sarcoma, squamous cell carcinoma and
microvasculopathy (up to 80%).
¯Çü Cornea. Keratoconjunctivitis sicca and an increased incidence
of keratitis, e.g. herpes simplex and zoster and fungal.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis associated with ocular infections, or (commonly) with drug toxicity, e.g. rifabutin, cidofovir.
¯Çü HIV-related retinal microangiopathy. Retinal microangiopathy is the most frequent retinopathy in patients with AIDS,
developing in up to 70% of patients. It is associated with a

declining CD4 [+] T cell count and higher plasma HIV-RNA
levels and is a marker for increased cytomegalovirus (CMV)
retinitis risk. Postulated causes include immune complex
deposition, HIV infection of the retinal vascular endothelium and abnormalities of ¯Ç×ow. It manifests with cotton-wool
spots and/or retinal haemorrhages (Fig. 12.41D) and sometimes capillary abnormalities such as microaneurysms. In
contrast to CMV retinitis, lesions are usually asymptomatic
and almost invariably disappear spontaneously a¯Ç£er several
weeks.
¯Çü Other viral retinitis: cytomegalovirus retinitis (most common), progressive retinal necrosis, acute retinal necrosis.
¯Çü Protozoal: Toxoplasma retinochoroiditis, o¯Ç£en atypical.
¯Çü Fungal: Pneumocystis choroiditis, Histoplasma chorioretinitis,
cryptococcal choroiditis, candidiasis.
¯Çü Bacterial: syphilis, tuberculosis.
¯Çü Neoplastic: B-cell intraocular lymphoma.
¯Çü Neuro-ophthalmological. Usually secondary to meningitis or
encephalopathy due to an opportunistic infection (e.g. toxoplasmosis, cryptococcosis, neurosyphilis) or neoplastic process (e.g. CNS lymphoma).

A B

C D

TIP Retinal microangiopathy is associated with a declining
CD4 T-cell count and is a marker for increased risk of
developing cytomegalovirus retinitis.

## Cytomegalovirus retinitis

## Introduction

Infection with cytomegalovirus (CMV), a herpesvirus, is very
common in the general population, causing no or minimal constitutional symptoms in most healthy individuals. CMV retinitis is seen in patients immunocompromised from a variety of
causes. It is a common opportunistic ocular infection in patients
with AIDS, in whom it may represent reactivation of latent
infection. Without treatment, severe visual loss is essentially
inevitable. Since the advent of ART the incidence and severity
have declined, though the prevalence remains high, partly due
to increasing survival rates in AIDS patients in whom systemic
steroid therapy may be a risk factor. ¯Çƒere is a very strong association with a low CD4 [+] count.

## Systemic features

Severe involvement of a range of organs including the lungs, CNS
and skin can occur in the immunocompromised.

## Ocular features

¯Çü Presentation is with reduced vision from macular involvement
or with ¯Ç×oaters from vitritis. One eye is usually a¯Çøected initially, progressing to both eyes in 50% of patients if untreated.
Indolent retinitis (see below) frequently starts in the periphery
without symptoms and progresses over weeks.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis can occur but is usually mild with little or no
injection. It is considered separately later in this chapter.
¯Çü Cataract is a common later-stage ¯ÇØnding.
¯Çü Vitritis is typically mild, except in immune recovery (see
below).
¯Çü Retinitis. ¯Çƒe characteristic appearance is of one or two areas
of dense white retinal in¯ÇØltration associated prominently with
¯Ç×ame-shaped retinal haemorrhages (ÔÇÿpizza pieÔÇÖ or ÔÇÿMargherita
pizzaÔÇÖ), beginning peripherally (centrally in 10%) and extending along the course of the vascular arcades (Fig. 12.42A).
Peripheral areas tend to appear granular, with fewer rounder
haemorrhages and little vasculitis. Indolent (more peripheral
and less aggressive ÔÇô Fig. 12.42B) and fulminant clinical patterns have been distinguished (Fig. 12.42C). ¯Çƒe visual acuity
can be signi¯ÇØcantly reduced if the macula is involved.
¯Çü Optic neuritis may result from direct spread or from primary
involvement.
¯Çü Retinal necrosis is evident in areas where active in¯Ç×ammation
has settled, leaving irregular pigmentation, atrophy and holes,
frequently leading to retinal detachment (Fig. 12.42D), a major
cause of visual morbidity (up to 50%).
¯Çü Frosted branch angiitis (FBA) describes marked vascular
sheathing that occurs in about 6% (see Fig. 12.42D). ¯Çƒis
appearance is seen in other conditions (see Fig. 12.71) and the

term is also used for a distinct idiopathic disorder (primary
FBA).
¯Çü Immune recovery uveitis (IRU). ¯Çƒis is a cause of limited
visual outcome in CMV retinitis, thought to be due to a rejuvenated immune response against residual viral antigen following immune reconstitution with ART. Manifestations can
be severe, progressing to phthisis in some cases.

## Treatment

Close liaison with an infectious disease physician is critical. ART is
the mainstay of management, restoring the patientÔÇÖs innate ability
to suppress CMV activity. Discontinuation of antiviral treatment is
considered when the CD4 [+] count reaches >100ÔÇô150 cells/╬╝l.
¯Çü Valganciclovir is a pro-drug of ganciclovir that is taken orally
and is as e¯Çøective for both induction (900 mg twice daily for
up to 3 weeks) and maintenance (900 mg daily). Neutropenia
is a common side e¯Çøect due to bone marrow suppression, but
can be e¯Çøectively treated with ¯ÇØlgrastim (granulocyte colonystimulating factor).
¯Çü Intravitreal injection of ganciclovir (2mg/injection), foscarnet (1.2mg), or cidofovir (20 ╬╝g) may occasionally be
indicated for lesions close to the macula or optic nerve. ¯Çƒe
marketing authorization for ganciclovir implant has been
withdrawn.
¯Çü Vitrectomy with endolaser demarcation and silicone oil tamponade is successful in around 75% of CMV-related retinal
detachments.
¯Çü Steroids may be required for IRU, though intravitreal and systemic administration should be used with caution.
¯Çü Prognosis. ¯Çƒis is poor, despite treatment, if the macula is
involved (Fig. 12.43).
¯Çü Screening of patients with low CD4 counts: 3-monthly if
< 50/╬╝l, 6-monthly 50ÔÇô100/╬╝l, yearly if >100/╬╝l.

## Progressive retinal necrosis

## Introduction

Progressive retinal necrosis (PRN, also known as progressive or
posterior outer retinal necrosis, PORN) is a rare but devastating
form of necrotizing retinitis which is usually caused by varicella
zoster virus (VZV) and possibly other herpesviruses. It occurs
predominantly in patients with AIDS, but may be associated with
other immunocompromised states, particularly drug-induced.
¯Çƒe prognosis is extremely poor, with no perception of light the
outcome in more than half of a¯Çøected eyes.

## Ocular features

¯Çü Presentation is with rapidly progressive unilateral or bilateral
visual loss.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis and vitritis are minimal, in contrast to CMV
retinitis and acute retinal necrosis (ARN) (see below).
¯Çü Retinitis. ¯Çƒree stages are recognized:

{ Early. Multifocal homogeneous yellowÔÇôwhite deep retinal
in¯ÇØltrates (Fig. 12.44A). ¯Çƒe macula may be involved at an
early stage, o¯Ç£en giving a cherry-red spot.

476 Viral Uveitis

A B

C D

appearance (arrow) and retinal vasculitis; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ advanced disease showing extensive macular
exudation; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ large posterior retinal tear with shallow localized detachment ÔÇô there is vascular sheathing reminiscent of frosted branch angiitis. (Courtesy of C Barry ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. D.)

{ Established/middle. ¯Çƒe signs typically spread rapidly
around the retina, with very extensive full-thickness
necrosis (Fig. 12.44B). Signs of vasculitis are absent or mild
and signi¯ÇØcant haemorrhage is uncommon. As in¯Ç×ammation clears, perivenular translucency is seen (Fig. 12.44C).

{ Late. Retinal atrophy may follow as the arteriolar blood
supply decreases (Fig. 12.44D). Rhegmatogenous retinal
detachment (RRD) is common, as is optic atrophy.

## Investigation

Vitreous and/or aqueous PCR assay for viral DNA; antibody assay
is less e¯Çøective.

## Treatment

Immune rescue with ART together with aggressive antiviral therapy, e.g. intravitreal and intravenous ganciclovir and foscarnet. Vitreoretinal surgery for retinal detachment o¯Ç£en yields poor results.

A B

C D

deep retinal in¯Ç×ltrates in the absence of vitritis; ¯Ç£B¯ÇØ pre-treatment showing deep yellowÔÇôwhite
in¯Ç×ltrates with well-de¯Ç×ned borders; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ and ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ post-treatment showing regression of lesions,
vascular attenuation and early retinal atrophy. (Courtesy of C Herbort.)

TIP Anterior uveitis and vitritis are minimal in a patient with
progressive retinal necrosis, which is in contrast to CMV retinitis
and acute retinal necrosis where signi¯Ç×cant in¯ÇØammation
occurs.

## Acute retinal necrosis

## Introduction

Acute retinal necrosis (ARN) is a rare but devastating necrotizing retinitis. It typically a¯Çøects otherwise healthy individuals and tends to be
caused by herpes simplex virus (HSV) in younger and VZV in older
patients (other herpesviruses are also suspected). ¯Çƒe prognosis is
relatively poor, with more than half of patients eventually achieving
only 6/60 as a result of retinal and optic nerve ischaemia or RRD.
¯Çü Systemic features. ARN has been reported following and
occurring simultaneously with HSV encephalitis and herpetic
skin infection.

¯Çü Ocular features. Presentation is initially unilateral with
blurred vision and ¯Ç×oaters. Pain is usually a feature. ¯Çƒe American Uveitis Society criteria for diagnosis are as follows:

{ Prominent anterior uveitis and vitritis (panuveitis).
Episcleritis and scleritis may occur.

{ One or more discrete foci of peripheral retinal necrosis.
Deep yellow-white in¯ÇØltrates with well-de¯ÇØned borders
are seen (Fig. 12.45A). Retinal haemorrhages can occur,
but are generally less prominent than in CMV retinitis.
¯Çƒe acute lesions resolve a¯Ç£er 6ÔÇô12 weeks, leaving behind
necrotic retina with hyperpigmented borders (Fig. 12.45B).
Secondary RRD is a major cause of visual morbidity.

{ Circumferential spread of retinal involvement. Posterior
pole involvement is late. Optic neuritis is sometimes a
feature.

{ Occlusive retinal vasculitis including arteritis. Preretinal
neovascularization can develop and may lead to vitreous
haemorrhage.

478 Viral Uveitis

A B

{ Rapid progression of disease in the absence of treatment.

## Investigation

Vitreous and/or aqueous PCR assay for viral DNA. Antibody assay
is less e¯Çøective.

## Treatment

¯Çü Aciclovir: intravenously (15 mg/kg every 8 hours) for 10ÔÇô14
days and then orally 800 mg ¯ÇØve times daily for 6ÔÇô12 weeks.
¯Çƒis may hasten resolution of the acute retinal lesions and dramatically reduces the risk of second eye involvement. Longterm therapy is occasionally required.
¯Çü Oral valaciclovir or famciclovir may be substituted for oral aciclovir, with similar outcomes but better tolerability. Valaciclovir
2 g, orally, three times daily can be used as an induction dose,
followed by a maintenance dose of 1g, orally, three times daily.
¯Çü Intravitreal ganciclovir or foscarnet may improve the
prognosis.
¯Çü Systemic steroids may be started 24 hours a¯Ç£er initiation of
antiviral therapy, especially in severe cases.
¯Çü Laser retinopexy around necrotic areas has been used in
the past, but has no e¯Çøect on the retinal detachment rate and
should be avoided.
¯Çü Vitrectomy for RRD, commonly with silicone oil tamponade.

## Herpes simplex anterior uveitis

## Introduction

Anterior uveitis may occur with or without active corneal disease (see Ch. 7). Patchy and occasionally sectoral iris atrophy that
includes the pigment epithelium (Fig. 12.46A) is common, as is
elevated IOP. ¯Çƒe pupil may be larger than its fellow. As in Fuchs
uveitis syndrome (FUS), KPs may be ¯ÇØne, stellate and di¯Çøusely
distributed across the cornea, but can also be large and grouped.
¯Çƒere is o¯Ç£en a past history of herpes simplex keratitis, cold sores
and sometimes genital herpes. Recurrent episodes of herpetic iritis

involve the same eye in almost all patients. Distinguishing herpetic
from cytomegalovirus iridocyclitis (see below) can be di¯ÇÜcult.

## Treatment

Topical steroids (e.g. prednisolone acetate 1% four times daily) and
a topical cycloplegic, in combination with an oral antiviral, e.g. aciclovir 400 mg ¯ÇØve times a day. ¯Çƒere is evidence that famciclovir or
valaciclovir is better tolerated than aciclovir. Steroids may be delayed
and used with caution if active epithelial disease is present, when a
topical antiviral may be added. Raised IOP is treated as necessary.

Varicella zoster virus ¯Ç£VZV¯ÇØ anterior
uveitis

## Introduction

Anterior uveitis of variable severity occurs in around 50% of
patients with herpes zoster ophthalmicus (HZO) and generally
starts 1ÔÇô3 weeks a¯Ç£er the acute skin rash (occasionally in patients
with HZO without dermatitis ÔÇô zoster sine herpete). Zosterassociated iridocyclitis may be recurrent, when diagnosis is usually
straightforward due to a past history of ipsilateral HZO. As with
herpes simplex, signs may be of granulomatous in¯Ç×ammation.
Sectoral iris atrophy is o¯Ç£en present (Fig. 12.46B), corneal sensation may be reduced and IOP raised. PCR analysis of aqueous is
indicated exceptionally. Anterior segment in¯Ç×ammation can occur
in primary VZV infection (chickenpox), particularly in the immunocompromised; neuroretinitis is rare. Uveitis has been reported
following VZV vaccination.

## Treatment

Topical steroids and mydriatics, in addition to standard systemic
antiviral treatment of shingles. Systemic steroids are required
rarely (e.g. optic neuritis). All patients with HZO must be monitored by an ophthalmologist dependent on severity, e.g. up to
weekly for at least 6 weeks to detect occult ocular in¯Ç×ammation
and subsequently possibly long-term to detect late complications.

A B

## Persistence or recurrence of anterior uveitis may respond to a

week-long course of aciclovir 800 mg ¯ÇØve times a day and longterm systemic antiviral prophylactic treatment may be considered
for repeated recurrence. VZV vaccination o¯Çøers protection against
shingles. See also Chapter 7

## Cytomegalovirus anterior uveitis

## Introduction

Cytomegalovirus (CMV) iridocyclitis in the immunocompetent is
more common than previously realized, albeit still less prevalent
than HSV- and VZV-related in¯Ç×ammation. It may be recurrent
or chronic and unilateral or bilateral. Elevated IOP is very common. CMV has been reported as a cause of PosnerÔÇôSchlossman
syndrome (see Ch. 11). Little or no ciliary injection, limited ¯Ç×are,
few cells, corneal endotheliitis, KP of a range of morphology and
sectoral iris atrophy have been reported. PS are rare. A key diagnostic indicator, in some cases only, may be a failure to respond to
aciclovir and/or steroids. PCR and antibody assay of an aqueous
sample should be considered if there is clinical suspicion.

## Treatment

Oral valganciclovir in proven infection, which sometimes requires
long-term continuation. IOP elevation may be persistent.

## Rubella

Rubella (German measles) is a common childhood infection and
usually follows a benign and short-lived course. However, transplacental transmission of virus to the fetus from an infected mother
can lead to congenital abnormalities of multiple organ systems,
with severity generally worse the earlier in gestation infection
occurs. Latent rubella virus may cause CAU relatively unresponsive to steroids and has been implicated in the causation of FUS.
Reported ocular features of congenital rubella include cataract,
anterior uveitis, ÔÇÿsalt and pepperÔÇÖ pigmentary retinopathy (Fig.
12.47A and B), glaucoma and microphthalmos.

## Measles and subacute sclerosing
panencephalitis

## Congenital infection with the measles virus can cause spontaneous
abortion or congenital systemic and ocular anomalies including

cataract and retinopathy. Infection acquired in childhood typically
features conjunctivitis and epithelial keratitis. Occasionally retinitis with macular and disc oedema can occur. Subacute sclerosing
panencephalitis (SSPE) is a rare progressive degenerative disease
of the CNS. ¯Çƒe condition usually a¯Çøects children and teenagers.
¯Çƒe onset of symptoms is 6ÔÇô15 years a¯Ç£er infection with measles.
¯Çƒe disease classically manifests initially with personality change
and behavioral problems, followed some months later by involuntary choreoathetoid movements and myoclonus. Further neurological deterioration slowly occurs and eventually death follows.
Papilloedema, nystagmus and optic neuritis can occur during the
course of the disease. Posterior uveitis is common and may be the
presenting feature. Ischaemic retinal vein occlusion secondary to
vasculitis has been reported but is rare (Fig. 12.48A). ¯Çƒe classical macular lesion appears white or greyÔÇôwhite, resolving to leave
areas of scarring and irregular RPE atrophy (Fig. 12.48B).

## Mumps

## Iridocyclitis and interstitial keratitis are rare complications of

mumps.

## Vaccinia

## Smallpox vaccination using the vaccinia virus has been resumed for

some groups as a result of the perceived risk of bioterrorism. ¯Çƒough
rare, a range of anterior segment manifestations has been described,
both from autoinoculation in vaccines and in close contacts.

## Zika

Zika virus is transmitted by an infected Aedes mosquito. ¯Çƒe virus
can be passed from a pregnant woman to her fetus, resulting in

480 Fungal Uveitis

A B

A B

signi¯ÇØcant birth defects, including microcephaly. In congenital
Zika, ocular e¯Çøects include chorioretinal and macular atrophy,
focal pigmentary changes in the macular region, optic nerve
abnormality and glaucoma. In adults with an acute infection, conjunctivitis is the most common ocular manifestation, followed by
uveitis. ¯Çƒe virus may persist in the lacrimal gland and in other
tissues of the eye, particularly in the retinal pigment epithelium.

## Ebola

¯Çƒe Ebola virus, which is responsible for epidemics in West Africa,
has the potential to cause clinical disease in sporadic outbreaks. In
survivors, ÔÇÿpost-Ebola virus disease syndromeÔÇÖ (PEVDS) may result
in ocular disease, arthritis, hearing loss, abdominal pain and neuropsychiatric disorders. Uveitis occurs in up to one-third of survivors
and may result in acute or chronic visual loss. ¯Çƒis can manifest as
anterior, posterior or panuveitis, with most cases occurring within

2 months of discharge. Up to 15% subsequently develop a cataract.
Optic nerve swelling has been reported to occur in 10% of survivors.
¯Çƒe virus has been detected in the aqueous in the acute situation
and can infect and persist in retinal pigment epithelial cells. Treatment with topical and oral corticosteroids is e¯Çøective but needs to
be started at the onset of symptoms to prevent long-term visual loss.

FUNGAL UVEITIS

## Presumed ocular histoplasmosis

syndrome ¯Ç£POHS¯ÇØ

## Introduction

## Histoplasma capsulatum infection occurs following inhalation of
the yeast form of this dimorphic fungus and can lead to the systemic

mycosis histoplasmosis ÔÇô pulmonary involvement is the most common feature. It is common in AIDS. POHS is relatively common

A B

C D

temporally (arrow).

in areas of endemic histoplasmosis (e.g. the Mississippi river valley
in the USA). Causality has never been de¯ÇØnitively con¯ÇØrmed, but
over 90% react positively to histoplasmin testing. It is believed that
eye disease represents an immune-mediated response to microbial
antigen, rather than immediate damage due to active infection.

## Ocular features

Sixty per cent have bilateral signs.
¯Çü Presentation. POHS is usually asymptomatic unless macular
neovascularization supervenes. Signs may be discovered at a
routine eye examination.
¯Çü Classic triad: (a) multiple white atrophic chorioretinal ÔÇÿhistoÔÇÖ
spots about 200 ╬╝m in diameter (Fig. 12.49A); (b) peripapillary atrophy (Fig. 12.49B); (c) vitritis is absent. Linear midperipheral scars (Fig. 12.49C) also occur (5%).
¯Çü Macular neovascularization (MNV) is a late manifestation occurring in less than 5% of a¯Çøected eyes. It is usually
associated with a pre-existing macular histo spot. Associated
subretinal ¯Ç×uid and haemorrhage lead to a fall in vision (Fig.
12.49D).

¯Çü Acute chorioretinitis is almost always asymptomatic and
rarely identi¯ÇØed, but discrete ovalÔÇôround whitish lesions <400
╬╝m in diameter that may develop into classic punched-out
histo spots have been described.

## Investigations

Skin testing is no longer undertaken.
¯Çü HLA testing. POHS is associated with HLA-B7 and DRw2.
¯Çü Serological testing is helpful if positive, but is usually negative
in the absence of systemic mycosis.
¯Çü FA and OCT when MNV is suspected.

## Treatment

Spontaneous regression of MNV may occasionally occur, but without treatment 60% of eyes with MNV have a ¯ÇØnal visual acuity of
less than 6/60.
¯Çü Intravitreal anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF)
injection for MNV.
¯Çü Amsler grid testing of the fellow eye at least weekly, particularly if a macular histo spot is present (25% risk of MNV).

482

Fungal Uveitis

A B

C D

chorioretinitis; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ retinal necrosis.

Endogenous Candida endophthalmitis

## Introduction

Candida (usually the commensal C. albicans) can be introduced
into the eye from the external environment by trauma or surgery
or can spread from fungal keratitis, but endogenous infection is
an important alternative route. Risk factors for metastatic spread
include intravenous drug abuse, a septic focus associated with an
indwelling catheter, chronic lung disease such as cystic ¯ÇØbrosis, general debilitation and diabetes. It is relatively uncommon in AIDS.

TIP Candida endophthalmitis is often acquired from an
endogenous source like an indwelling catheter or secondary to
intravenous drug abuse.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Presentation. Systemic candidiasis may already have been
diagnosed. Up to a third of patients with untreated candid

aemia will develop ocular involvement. Peripheral fundus
lesions may cause little or no visual disturbance while central
lesions or severe vitritis will manifest earlier. Progression is
typically much slower than bacterial endophthalmitis. Bilateral involvement is common.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis is uncommon or mild in early disease but
may become prominent later.
¯Çü Vitritis. May be marked (Fig. 12.50A), with ¯Ç×u¯Çøy ÔÇÿcotton ballÔÇÖ
(Fig. 12.50B) or ÔÇÿstring of pearlsÔÇÖ colonies, sometimes progressing to abscess formation.
¯Çü Chorioretinitis: one or more small creamy white lesions with
overlying vitritis (Fig. 12.50C). Retinal necrosis (Fig. 12.50D)
may lead to retinal detachment, with severe proliferative
vitreoretinopathy.

## Investigation

¯Çü Vitreous biopsy (preferably using a vitreous cutter rather than
a needle) to identify the organism (PCR and culture) and identify sensitivities.
¯Çü Systemic investigation, e.g. blood and urine cultures.

A B

C D

lesions; ¯Ç£B¯ÇØ large coalescent pneumocystosis lesion; ¯Ç£C¯ÇØ multifocal cryptococcal choroiditis;
¯Ç£D¯ÇØ macular disease in Aspergillus infection. (Courtesy of S Mitchell ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A; A Curi ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs C
and D.)

## Treatment

¯Çü Antifungal treatment. ¯Çƒe agent should be chosen with local
microbiological specialist guidance. Infectious Diseases Society
of America guidelines suggest intravenous amphotericin-B in
combination with oral ¯Ç×ucytosine, but resistance is a concern.

## Voriconazole orally or intravenously has a broad spectrum of
antifungal action with low reported resistance and high ocular

penetration. Adjunctive intravitreal treatment may be given
(100 ╬╝g in 0.1 ml), with serial injections probably needed.
¯Çü Pars plana vitrectomy should be considered at an early stage,
especially for severe or unresponsive disease. As well as providing a substantial culture specimen, it reduces fungal and
antigen load, facilitates therapeutic agent penetration and
clears the ocular media.

## Pneumocystis choroiditis

¯Çƒe fungus Pneumocystis jirovecii, a pulmonary commensal,
is a major cause of mortality in uncontrolled AIDS. Systemic

antimicrobial prophylaxis has replaced pulmonary-only preventative treatment with inhaled pentamidine and along with immune
reconstitution has dramatically reduced the incidence of Pneumocystis choroiditis. Multiple slowly progressing deep round yellowÔÇôorange lesions (Fig. 12.51A and B), commonly bilateral, are
characteristic. ¯Çƒere is minimal vitritis and visual loss is o¯Ç£en
negligible.

## Cryptococcus choroiditis

Cryptococcus neoformans, a dimorphic yeast, enters the body
through inhalation and can spread to the eye in the bloodstream
or from the CNS via the optic nerve. As with pneumocystosis,
cryptococcosis was formerly responsible for much morbidity, but
clinically signi¯ÇØcant infection is now much less common since the
advent of more e¯Çøective therapy for AIDS. Ocular involvement
may occur directly (e.g. multifocal choroiditis ÔÇô Fig. 12.51C) with
vasculitis and exudate or more commonly indirectly with papilloedema and ocular motility dysfunction.

484 Bacterial Uveitis

## Aspergillus endophthalmitis

Aspergillus species are common environmental fungi, but cause
disease in humans less commonly than Candida. Spores undergo
airborne spread and risk factors for infection include intravenous
drug abuse, chronic lung disease, organ transplantation and blood
disorders; neutropenia may be of particular importance. Iridocyclitis and vitritis are common. Yellowish retinal and subretinal
in¯ÇØltrates tend towards macular involvement (Fig. 12.51D) at an
earlier stage than Candida infection. ¯Çƒe disease progresses more
rapidly and the visual outcome is o¯Ç£en worse. Occlusive retinal
vasculitis is common. Systemic assessment is critical; endocarditis
presents a particular risk. Investigation and treatment are similar
to that of Candida endophthalmitis.

## Coccidioidomycosis

## Coccidioides immitis acquired by inhalation usually causes a mild

pulmonary infection, but wider systemic involvement can occur,
and reinfection can lead to chronic lung disease. Ocular features
include severe granulomatous anterior uveitis and multifocal choroiditis. Investigation and treatment are similar to that of Candida
endophthalmitis.

BACTERIAL UVEITIS

## Tuberculosis

## Introduction

Tuberculosis (TB) is a chronic granulomatous infection usually
caused in humans by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. TB is primarily a pulmonary disease but may spread by the bloodstream
to other sites. Ocular involvement commonly occurs without
clinically overt systemic disease. Immune de¯ÇØciency is a risk
factor, when atypical mycobacteria such as M. avium may cause
disease.

## Ocular features

¯Çü Anterior uveitis is common and is usually granulomatous. Iris
nodules may be present and broad PS may form.
¯Çü Vitritis is very common and may be secondary to anterior,
intermediate or posterior primary foci. Macular complications include cystoid oedema and epiretinal membrane
formation.
¯Çü Choroidal granuloma (tubercle): focal elevated dome-shaped
lesions (Fig. 12.52A) that may be unilateral or bilateral and
solitary or multiple (Fig. 12.52B). Extensive in¯ÇØltration may
occur in individuals with AIDS. A large abscess-like tubercle is
termed a tuberculoma.
¯Çü Choroiditis independent of tubercles, typically multifocal and in
a centrifugally spreading serpiginous pattern (serpiginoid), has
increasingly been recognized (Fig. 12.53A and B). Choroiditis
that tracks retinal vessels may have reasonable speci¯ÇØcity for TB.
¯Çü Retinal vasculitis is preferentially venous. Retinal haemorrhages are common. Vascular occlusion with extensive

ischaemia (Fig. 12.54A and B) and preretinal or disc neovascularization can occur. It is hypothesized that at least some
cases of Eales disease (see Ch. 13) represent a hypersensitivity
reaction to TB.
¯Çü Other manifestations include reddish-brown eyelid nodules
(lupus vulgaris), conjunctivitis, phlyctenulosis, interstitial
keratitis, scleritis, exudative retinal detachment and optic neuropathy including neuroretinitis.

## Investigation

¯Çƒe diagnosis is o¯Ç£en made clinically, considering evidence of previous TB exposure and other negative investigations. A systemic
assessment by an appropriate specialist should be arranged.
¯Çü A tuberculin skin test may show a positive result within 48
hours.
¯Çü T-SPOT.TB is a blood test where the number of antimycobacterial e¯Çøector T cells that produce interferon-gamma are
counted. Because the test does not rely on the production of an
antibody response, it can detect latent tuberculosis. ¯Çƒe prevalence of tuberculosis in a given community in¯Ç×uences the predictive value of the test: a sensitivity of 81% and a speci¯ÇØcity of
86% has been reported.
¯Çü Other investigations include sputum testing with PCR and
the interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) blood test. ¯Çƒis is
approximately as sensitive as skin testing (80% in active disease) but has the advantage of being independent of previous
BCG vaccination. HIV status must be determined. Chest X-ray,
CT, positron emission tomography (PET)/CT are among other
tests that may be considered.
¯Çü Ocular. Aqueous or vitreous sampling rarely yields
demonstrable (smear ÔÇô acid-fast bacilli on ZiehlÔÇôNeelsen
staining ÔÇô or culture ÔÇô LowensteinÔÇôJensen medium) mycobacteria. PCR is highly specific but of variable sensitivity.
OCT is useful for macular evaluation. Fluorescein angiography may be helpful in establishing whether choroiditis is
active, as well as confirming preretinal neovascularization
and demonstrating ischaemia. FAF allows activity staging,
lesions becoming progressively hypoautofluorescent with
healing.

## Treatment

¯Çü Prolonged multi-drug therapy (o¯Ç£en four initially) should be
prescribed and monitored by a specialist with experience in
systemic TB management. If ethambutol is used, monitoring
for optic neuropathy should take place. Rifabutin can cause
anterior uveitis. Non-adherence to treatment is common.
Treatment with TB medication in patients with uveitis associated with latent TB halves the risk of uveitis recurrence and
delays the onset of ¯ÇØrst recurrence.
¯Çü Topical and systemic steroids may be used concomitantly
to reduce in¯Ç×ammation-induced damage, particularly in the
early weeks of treatment, when they may retard paradoxical
worsening of the fundus appearance.
¯Çü Laser may be applied to ischaemic retina to treat preretinal
neovascularization.

superiorly; ¯Ç£B¯ÇØ FA showing corresponding areas of hyper- and hypo¯ÇØuorescence. (Courtesy
of C Pavesio.)
