486 Bacterial Uveitis

## Acquired syphilis

## Introduction

Syphilis is caused by the spirochaete bacterium Treponema pallidum. In adults the disease is usually sexually acquired when
organisms enter through a skin or mucous membrane abrasion.
Transmission by kissing, blood transfusion or percutaneous injury
is rare. Transplacental infection of the fetus can also occur, when
the mother has become infected during or shortly before pregnancy (congenital syphilis ÔÇô see Ch. 7).

## Systemic features

¯Çƒe natural history of untreated syphilis is variable.
¯Çü Primary syphilis is characterized by a painless ulcer (chancre), commonly on the genitalia or anus.
¯Çü Secondary syphilis consists of a maculopapular rash (Fig.
12.55) and other systemic features.
¯Çü Latent syphilis.
¯Çü Tertiary syphilis occurs in about 40% of untreated cases and is
characterized by cardiovascular manifestations such as aortitis,
neurosyphilis and gummatous in¯ÇØltration of bone and viscera.

## Ocular features

¯Çü Anterior uveitis occurs in about 4% of patients with secondary syphilis. It may be granulomatous or non-granulomatous
and is bilateral in 50%. Roseolae (Fig. 12.56A) are dilated iris
capillaries that may develop into yellowish nodules. IOP may
be elevated.

¯Çü Chorioretinitis can be localized (Fig. 12.56B) but is o¯Ç£en multifocal, bilateral and associated with vitritis and exudative retinal detachment can ensue.
¯Çü Acute syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinopathy
(ASPPC) is characterized by large pale-yellowish subretinal
lesions in the posterior pole (Fig. 12.56C). It is thought to be
a secondary infection of the choriocapillaris and occurs more
commonly in the immunocompromised.
¯Çü Retinitis has a ÔÇÿground glassÔÇÖ appearance. Associated vasculitis may be occlusive and involve both arteries and veins. ¯Çƒis
heals leaving di¯Çøuse chorioretinal scars (Fig. 12.56D).
¯Çü Optic neuritis and neuroretinitis.
¯Çü Other features include conjunctivitis, episcleritis and scleritis,
IU, glaucoma, cataract and miscellaneous neuro-ophthalmic
features related to CNS involvement including Argyll Robertson pupils (see Ch. 19).

## Investigation

¯Çü Serology is the mainstay and is discussed under Investigations
earlier in this chapter.
¯Çü Systemic assessment by an appropriate specialist, including
lumbar puncture to rule out neurosyphilis. HIV status should
be established.
¯Çü Aqueous and/or vitreous sampling for PCR is sometimes
indicated, such as when HIV positivity makes serology less
reliable.

## Treatment

¯Çƒis should be under the supervision of a specialist in infectious
diseases, but usually consists of an extended course of parenteral
penicillin. Alternatives may be required, for instance in penicillin allergy, but are less e¯Çøective and parallel con¯ÇØrmatory allergen testing and desensitization may be considered. Topical and
systemic steroids may be given in conjunction with antibiotics to
ameliorate in¯Ç×ammatory damage. ¯Çƒe JarischÔÇôHerxheimer reaction is a systemic response to treponemal antigens released on
commencement of therapy and may include progression of ocular
signs.

TIP Commencement of treatment for syphilis can cause an
exacerbation of ocular symptoms and signs.

## Lyme disease

## Introduction

Lyme disease (borreliosis), like syphilis, is caused by a spirochaete. ¯Çƒe disease is endemic in regions of North America,
Europe and Asia, but can be di¯ÇÜcult to diagnose. ¯Çƒe responsible
organism, Borrelia burgdorferi, is transmitted through tick bites,
with deer being an important vector (Fig. 12.57A). An adult tick
(Fig. 12.57B) can be distinguished from a head louse by the tick
having eight legs ÔÇô it is an arachnid ÔÇô and the louse six (though

acquired syphilis. lesion, erythema chronicum migrans (Fig. 12.57C), forms at the

A B

C D

posterior placoid chorioretinitis; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ old multifocal chorioretinitis.

site in 60ÔÇô80%, o¯Ç£en accompanied by constitutional symptoms
(stage 1). Neurological (e.g. cranial nerve palsies, meningitis),
cardiac (4ÔÇô8% e.g. arrhythmia) and other manifestations may
follow within a few weeks. Late (stage 3) complications include
chronic arthritis of large joints, polyneuropathy and encephalopathy. Some patients develop chronic symptoms that may not
respond to antibiotics.

## Ocular features

¯Çƒese are varied and tend to occur in established stage 2 and stage
3 (late) disease.
¯Çü Uveitis is relatively uncommon but can be anterior (granulomatous or non-granulomatous), intermediate (the most common), or posterior including multifocal choroiditis, vasculitis
and neuroretinitis.
¯Çü Other manifestations include early (stage 1) transient conjunctivitis, bilateral stromal keratitis, episcleritis (Fig. 12.57D),
scleritis, orbital myositis, optic neuritis, papilloedema and
ocular motor and facial nerve palsy (up to 25% of facial nerve
palsy in endemic areas).

## Investigations

Serology should be performed at least a month a¯Ç£er infection, but
false positives can occur. PCR of CSF or synovial ¯Ç×uid is performed
in appropriate cases. Diagnosis relies on a combination of clinical
features and positive serology. Co-infection with other tick-borne
diseases is possible.

TIP Lyme disease can be di¯Ç£cult to diagnose in the early
stages and many of the later symptoms are non-speci¯Ç×c
in nature. The diagnosis depends on a history of exposure
to ticks and a combination of clinical features and positive
serology.

## Treatment

Treatment of early acute disease is highly e¯Çøective and involves
oral doxycycline (not children or in pregnancy), amoxicillin or erythromycin. Untreated, many patients with early or

488 Bacterial Uveitis

## Rodents

## Tick bite

Infected human,
no further spread

## Ixodes
tick

A B

C D

migrans; ¯Ç£D¯ÇØ nodular episcleritis.

asymptomatic disease will experience no further problems.
Patients with established disease, including ocular, may require
extended intravenous penicillin or ce¯Ç£riaxone. Keratitis and
uveitis may need steroid treatment. Systemic steroids should
not be given without concomitant antibiotics. Appropriate personal protection (clothing, insect repellent) should be adopted
in endemic areas to reduce the risk of tick bite. Lyme disease
vaccine is available, but does not o¯Çøer complete or prolonged
protection.

## Brucellosis

Brucellosis is caused by the Gram-negative bacteria Brucella melitensis and B. abortus. It is usually transmitted from animals to man
through milk products or uncooked meat. A variety of constitutional and ocular (20%) features are reported, the latter including
chronic anterior and posterior uveitis, papilloedema and retinal
haemorrhages. Investigation should include serology and blood
culture. Treatment is with a combination of two antibiotics, e.g.
streptomycin and doxycycline, with adjunctive steroids if required.

## Patients with known brucellosis should undergo ophthalmic

examination.

## Endogenous bacterial endophthalmitis

## Introduction

¯Çƒis is an intraocular infection that originates from sources within
the body. ¯Çƒe incidence has increased in recent years. A wide
range of organisms can be responsible ÔÇô Gram-positives predominate in North America and Europe and Gram-negatives in East
Asia. Risk factors include debilitating disease of many types as
well as other factors such as intravenous drug abuse (where Bacillus species predominate). Spread can occur from any potential
focus such as an indwelling catheter, tooth abscess, endocarditis
(especially Streptococcus), urinary tract infection (especially E.
coli) or septic joint. Patients are usually systemically unwell and
mortality is relatively high ÔÇô 5ÔÇô10%. ¯Çƒe two systemic diseases
that confer an increase in mortality are pneumonia and renal/UTI
infection, mainly because these infections are caused by virulent
organisms. ¯Çƒe visual prognosis is poorer than with postoperative
endophthalmitis.

## Ocular features

Diagnosis is frequently delayed. ¯Çƒe main distinction is from
endogenous fungal endophthalmitis.

¯Çü Symptoms: blurred vision, pain and redness.
¯Çü Signs are broadly similar to those of postoperative endophthalmitis (see Ch. 10), though retinal in¯ÇØltrates may be an
early feature, re¯Ç×ecting the route of infection.

## Investigation

¯Çƒe search for a septic focus (blood and urine cultures, septic
arthritis, endocarditis, lumbar puncture etc.) should be in collaboration with an appropriate physician. Aqueous and vitreous
samples should be taken for microscopy and culture.

## Treatment

¯Çü Systemic infection is treated with intravenous antibiotics according to clinical suspicion and local microbiological advice. Broad spectrum empirical treatment may be
necessary.
¯Çü Endophthalmitis is treated with intravitreal and an oral
¯Ç×uoroquinolone. ¯Çƒe place of systemic steroids is unde¯ÇØned. Pars plana vitrectomy may improve prognosis in some
patients.

TIP Systemic antibiotics are key to the treatment of
endogenous endophthalmitis, in contrast to postoperative
endophthalmitis. The search for a septic focus should be made
in collaboration with an appropriate physician.

Cat¯Ç×scratch disease

## Introduction

Cat-scratch disease (bartonellosis) is caused by Bartonella henselae, a Gram-negative rod. Infection is usually transmitted by the
scratch (or bite) of an apparently healthy cat, though feline contact is not always described. One or more red papules at the site of
inoculation are followed by fever and regional lymphadenopathy

(Fig. 12.58A), though general symptoms are frequently absent.
Severe systemic disease occasionally occurs. ¯Çƒe visual prognosis
is usually reasonable.

## Ocular features

¯Çƒe eyes are a¯Çøected in 5ÔÇô10%. Neuroretinitis (see Ch. 19) is the
most common manifestation and consists of disc oedema with
macular exudate in a star conformation (Fig. 12.58B); focal retinochoroiditis, vasculitis and, rarely, an optic nerve head angiomatous
lesion have been reported. Conjunctivitis with a 2ÔÇô4 mm conjunctival granuloma and associated pre-auricular lymphadenopathy
(Parinaud oculoglandular syndrome) may occasionally be seen. It
is unusual for both eyes to be involved.

## Investigation

Includes serology for B. henselae.

## Treatment

Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 4ÔÇô6 weeks should be used in
immunocompetent adults. Oral erythromycin can be used in children. Other oral antibiotics, e.g. co-trimoxazole, azithromycin,
rifampicin or cipro¯Ç×oxacin are e¯Çøective. Steroids have been used
in some cases. Treatment is not usually given for mild systemic
symptoms alone.

## Leprosy

## Introduction

Leprosy (Hansen disease) is a chronic granulomatous infection caused by Mycobacterium leprae and M. lepromatosis. The
mode of spread remains uncertain, though nasal secretions
have been implicated. Infection is thought to lead to a chronic
immune response that leads to the peripheral neuropathy that
is the primary pathogenetic mechanism. Genetic factors are

A B

weeks previously, with enlargement of submandibular lymph nodes; (B¯ÇØ neuroretinitis, manifesting with a partial macular star. (Courtesy of BJ Zitelli and HW Davis, from Atlas of Pediatric
Physical Diagnosis, Mosby 2002 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

490 Bacterial Uveitis

probably important ÔÇô only 5% of the general population is
thought to be vulnerable to leprosy on substantial contact with
the organism.

## Systemic features

¯Çü Tuberculoid (ÔÇÿpaucibacillaryÔÇÖ in WHO classi¯ÇØcation).

## One or more hypopigmented macules and anaesthetic skin

patches.
¯Çü Borderline (multibacillary). ¯Çƒe most common form. Similar to tuberculoid, but more numerous and extensive lesions.
¯Çü Lepromatous (multibacillary). Widespread cutaneous thickening with leonine facial appearance (Fig. 12.59A), peripheral
plaques and nodules, upper respiratory tract involvement and
peripheral nerve lesions facilitating trauma that may result in
shortening and loss of digits.

## Ocular features

Ocular signs are principally due to direct bacterial invasion.
¯Çü Keratitis: thickened, beaded corneal nerves, punctate subepithelial lesions, pannus and vascularization (Fig. 12.59B).

¯Çü Anterior uveitis: chronic and low-grade and is classically
described as ÔÇÿplasmoidÔÇÖ (prominent ¯ÇØbrin).
¯Çü Miosis and iris atrophy (Fig. 12.59C) result from impaired
dilator pupillae innervation.
¯Çü Iris pearls (pathognomonic): usually under 0.5 mm in diameter (Fig. 12.59D).
¯Çü Other features: episcleritis and scleritis, retinal pearls, uveal
e¯Çøusion, cataract, glaucoma, decreased corneal sensation,
facial nerve palsy, eyelid deformities and phthisis bulbi.

## Investigation

Skin and occasionally ocular specimens show acid-fast bacilli. ¯Çƒe lepromin test distinguishes between tuberculoid and lepromatous leprosy.

## Treatment

¯Çü Systemic. Combination regimens of extended duration with
antibiotics such as dapsone, rifampicin and clofazimine. BCG
vaccination o¯Çøers some protection.
¯Çü Ocular. Anterior uveitis is treated with steroids. Speci¯ÇØc complications are addressed as indicated.

Rowland, from Colour Atlas of Infectious Diseases, Mosby 2003 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A; ADN Murray ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

MISCELLANEOUS IDIOPATHIC
CHORIORETINOPATHIES

## Introduction

¯Çƒe term ÔÇÿwhite dot syndromesÔÇÖ has been used in the past to
describe several di¯Çøerent idiopathic in¯Ç×ammatory conditions of
the posterior pole. ¯Çƒese conditions tend to occur between the
second to sixth decades of life, are more likely to a¯Çøect women
than men, usually have a self-limiting course and the visual
prognosis depends on the condition. However, the term is considered by many uveitis specialists to be outdated because the
white dots are a variable ¯ÇØnding and the diseases are not driven
by the same pathophysiological processes. Patients with other
entities may present with similar clinical manifestations and it
is critical to exclude alternative diagnoses, including infection
and neoplasia. Recent advances in the ¯ÇØeld of ocular imaging
have allowed better characterization of these conditions. Indocyanine green angiography enables two main disease categories
to be recognized: (a) choriocapillaritis and (b) stromal choroiditis (Table 12.10).

## Multiple evanescent white dot

syndrome ¯Ç£MEWDS¯ÇØ

## Introduction

MEWDS is an uncommon idiopathic disease of acute onset, typically occurring in young adult females, with 25ÔÇô50% describing

Table 12.10 ICG Angiographic Classi¯Çƒcation of Choroiditis

1A. Primary choriocapillaritis (trigger not known)

ÔÇó¯Çü Acute posterior multifocal placoid pigment
epitheliopathy (APMPPE)
ÔÇó¯Çü Serpiginous choroidopathy
ÔÇó¯Çü Multifocal choroiditis (MFC)
ÔÇó¯Çü Punctate inner choroidopathy (PIC)
1B. Secondary choriocapillaritis (the pathogen triggers
the immune response)
ÔÇó¯Çü Acute syphilitic posterior placoid chorioretinitis (ASPPC)
ÔÇó¯Çü TB-related serpiginous choroiditis
2A. Primary stromal choroiditis (stroma is target of
in¯Çƒammation)
ÔÇó¯Çü HLA-A29 birdshot retinochoroidopathy
ÔÇó¯Çü VogtÔÇôKoyanagiÔÇôHarada disease (VKH)
ÔÇó¯Çü Sympathetic ophthalmia
2B. Secondary stromal choroiditis (secondary to
systemic in¯Çƒammation)
ÔÇó¯Çü Sarcoidosis
ÔÇó¯Çü Tubercular chorioretinitis
ÔÇó¯Çü Syphilitic chorioretinitis

a preceding viral-like illness. In¯Ç×uenza vaccination has been
reported to occasionally trigger the disease. ¯Çƒe exact pathogenesis is not known, although immune-induced in¯Ç×ammation
of the choriocapillaris has been suspected. ¯Çƒe condition can be
di¯ÇÜcult to diagnose as the characteristic fundal white dots disappear fairly soon a¯Ç£er the symptoms start (Fig. 12.60A). Multimodal imaging is key to making the diagnosis, as changes that
are not clinically apparent are obvious on FAF, OCT and ICG
angiography.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Presentation. Common: painless monocular blurring (6/9ÔÇô
6/60), subjective scotomas and photopsia. Less common: ¯Ç×oaters, dyschromatopsia.
¯Çü Subtle posterior vitritis in 50%.
¯Çü Posterior pole lesions. Numerous small (100ÔÇô300 ╬╝m), illde¯ÇØned, deep greyÔÇôwhite patches sparing the fovea, which
has a characteristic orange granular appearance and a dulled
re¯Ç×ex.
¯Çü Optic disc oedema is occasionally present.
¯Çü Recovery occurs over weeks, o¯Ç£en leaving subtle residual
signs. Recurrence occasionally (10%) occurs. If there is recurrence, idiopathic multifocal choroiditis (see below) should be
suspected, as these two diseases can overlap.

## Investigation

¯Çü Visual ¯ÇØelds. ¯Çƒe blind spot is commonly enlarged, with a
temporal ¯ÇØeld defect.
¯Çü FA shows subtle early hyper¯Ç×uorescence in the areas corresponding to white dots, with late staining (Fig. 12.60B). Vessel
wall leakage and disc staining may be seen.
¯Çü FAF reveals hyperauto¯Ç×uorescent spots during active in¯Ç×ammation which correspond to the macular lesions (Fig. 12.60C).
FAF has been used to demonstrate subclinical lesions in
patients with only foveal granularity.
¯Çü ICGA shows hypo¯Ç×uorescent spots in the late angiographic
phase (Fig. 12.60D) that are o¯Ç£en more numerous than visible
clinically or on FA. Some authorities suggest that this could
be a sign of ¯Ç×ow void at the level of the choriocapillaris, while
others interpret this as outer retinal involvement leading to
reduced late uptake of ICG by the RPE.
¯Çü OCT shows inner-segment/outer-segment junction (ellipsoid zone) disruption (Fig. 12.60E). It is sometimes associated
with re¯Ç×ective focal lesions that cross the external limiting
membrane.

## Treatment

¯Çƒis is generally not required as the symptoms and signs start
to improve spontaneously in most cases by 8ÔÇô10 weeks (Fig.
12.60F).

## Acute idiopathic blind spot

enlargement syndrome ¯Ç£AIBSE¯ÇØ

AIBSE is a rare condition reported in young to middle-aged women
and is likely to be a variant of MEWDS. Features include photopsia
and decreased vision, with blind spot enlargement and mild disc swelling. Recovery to normal or near-normal without treatment is usual.

## Acute posterior multifocal placoid

pigment epitheliopathy ¯Ç£APMPPE¯ÇØ

## Introduction

APMPPE is an uncommon bilateral idiopathic in¯Ç×ammatory
disorder. It a¯Çøects young to middle-aged adults of both genders
equally. ¯Çƒere is a viral prodrome in one-third and it is speculated
to occur as a result of cell-mediated immunity to viral antigen. ¯Çƒe
exact pathogenesis is not known, but it has been suggested that
in¯Ç×ammation at the level of the choriocapillaris results in hypoperfusion and ischaemia of the RPE and photoreceptors. Associated cerebral vasculitis can occur but is uncommon and can cause
stroke. Erythema nodosum and other systemic manifestations of
vasculitis have been reported. ¯Çƒe clinical picture of APMPPE can
be mimicked by other entities, such as sarcoidosis and tuberculosis.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Subacute moderate visual impairment with central/paracentral scotomas and photopsia. If the macula is
involved the visual acuity can deteriorate (ranging from 6/12
to 6/60). ¯Çƒe fellow eye is a¯Çøected within a few days or weeks.

## Headache and other neurological symptoms are common and

can commence many months a¯Ç£er ocular disease onset.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis and vitritis are usually very mild.
¯Çü Fundus. Multiple large deep yellowÔÇôwhite placoid lesions, initially
at the posterior pole (Fig. 12.61A). Within weeks the majority
fade, with residual RPE disturbance of varying severity. Subretinal
macular ¯Ç×uid may be seen. Vasculitis and papillitis are rare.
¯Çü Prognosis. ¯Çƒe visual symptoms and retinal appearance typically improve within weeks and the long-term prognosis for
most patients is good. However, there appears to be a subgroup
of patients whose symptoms may recur and who have a longer
period of disease activity. In 25% visual recovery is limited to
6/15 or worse as a consequence of RPE and photoreceptor damage to the fovea. Factors that predict a poor prognosis include:
(a) age older than 60 years, (b) unilaterality, (c) an interval
before involvement of the second eye of at least 6 months, (d)
disease recurrence, (e) leakage from choroidal veins.

## Investigation

¯Çü Alternative diagnoses should be excluded.
¯Çü HLA-B7 and HLA-DR2 are associated in a substantial proportion of patients.
¯Çü FA of active lesions shows early dense hypo¯Ç×uorescence
(blockage) (Fig. 12.61B) and late hyper¯Ç×uorescent staining
(Fig. 12.61C). Late hyper¯Ç×uorescence may result from vascular
leakage, which resolves in healed lesions.

¯Çü FAF shows scattered hypoauto¯Ç×uorescence surrounded by
hyperauto¯Ç×uorescence corresponding to placoid lesions (Fig.
12.61D).
¯Çü ICGA: active placoid lesions manifest as early and late hypo¯Ç×uorescence secondary to poor choriocapillaris perfusion
(Fig. 12.61E).
¯Çü OCT of the macula shows focal disruption of the outer retina
(Fig. 12.61F).
¯Çü OCT angiography (OCTA) may show abnormal ¯Ç×ow in the
choriocapillaris.
¯Çü CNS imaging and lumbar puncture should be performed
in patients with neurological symptoms to rule out retinal
vasculitis.

## Treatment

Treatment is not usually required, but steroids should be considered for patients with macular involvement. Steroids and possibly
ciclosporin may be given for cerebral vasculitis. Patients should be
instructed to seek medical advice urgently if neurological symptoms occur.

## Serpiginous choroidopathy

## Introduction

Serpiginous choroidopathy (choroiditis) is usually bilateral, though
asymmetrical. In¯Ç×ammation occurs in the outer retina and choriocapillaris. It typically occurs in middle age, a¯Çøects men more frequently than women and is associated with HLA-B7. ¯Çƒe disease is
generally recurrent over years, with a relatively poor prognosis and
almost 25% of a¯Çøected eyes have a ¯ÇØnal visual acuity of less than
6/60. Tuberculosis (see Fig. 12.53) and syphilis (see Fig. 12.56D)
can occasionally result in a similar clinical picture (ÔÇÿserpiginoidÔÇÖ),
which can pose a diagnostic challenge, as the therapeutic approach
to these diseases is signi¯ÇØcantly di¯Çøerent.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Initially unilateral blurring of central vision, scotoma or metamorphopsia.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis and vitritis are common but usually mild.
¯Çü Fundus. Active lesions (Fig. 12.62A) are greyÔÇôwhite and
may remain active for several months before becoming
scalloped and atrophic. ¯Çƒe disease typically starts around
the optic disc and extends gradually (Fig. 12.62B), though
a variant starting at the central macula (5%) is recognized.

## Recurrence is usually contiguous with or adjacent to existing

areas, eventually resulting in extensive chorioretinal atrophy
(Fig. 12.62C).
¯Çü Complications. MNV (15ÔÇô35%), subretinal ¯ÇØbrosis, preretinal neovascularization.

## Investigation

¯Çü Tuberculosis should be excluded, especially in endemic areas.
Test for syphilis.
¯Çü FAF shows the extent of the in¯Ç×ammation. Active lesions
show a hyperauto¯Ç×uorescent halo on the edges of areas of

496 Miscellaneous Idiopathic Chorioretinopathies

hypoauto¯Ç×uorescence (Fig. 12.62D). Inactive lesions are dark
and uniformly hypoauto¯Ç×uorescent. It is a useful technique to
monitor disease progression.
¯Çü FA of active lesions shows early hypo¯Ç×uorescence and late
hyper¯Ç×uorescence (Fig. 12.62E).
¯Çü OCTA shows ¯Ç×ow void at the level of the choriocapillaris.
¯Çü ICGA of active lesions reveals marked hypo¯Ç×uorescence
throughout all phases of the angiogram (Fig. 12.62F)

## Treatment

Oral or intravenous steroids may control activity. A variety of
immunosuppressives and in¯Ç×iximab may be e¯Çøective, alone or in
combination.

Relentless placoid chorioretinitis ¯Ç£RPC¯ÇØ

¯Çƒis refers to a rare entity showing features of both APMPPE and
serpiginous choroiditis and is also referred to as ampiginous choroiditis. In contrast to APMPPE which is limited to the posterior
pole, RPC is characterized by the presence of hundreds of lesions,
located anteriorly and posterior to the equator. ¯Çƒe clinical course
is o¯Ç£en prolonged and relapsing.

Persistent placoid maculopathy ¯Ç£PPM¯ÇØ

¯Çƒis rare condition features lesions similar to those of the macular
variant of serpiginous choroidopathy, but which generally behave
in a more benign fashion unless complicated by MNV (common).

Idiopathic multifocal choroiditis ¯Ç£MFC¯ÇØ

## Introduction

MFC is an uncommon, usually bilateral but asymmetrical, chronic/
recurrent disease that typically a¯Çøects young and middle-aged adult
women. Many people present with ¯Ç×u-like symptoms before the
onset of ocular disease. ¯Çƒe severity and prognosis are very variable.

¯Çƒe aetiology is unknown and the condition is diagnosed clinically a¯Ç£er systemic and infectious disease (e.g. tuberculosis, West
Nile virus choroiditis, Candida) has been excluded.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Blurring, ¯Ç×oaters and photopsia.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis (50%).
¯Çü Vitritis.
¯Çü Fundus. Multiple discrete, ovoid, yellowish-grey lesions
50ÔÇô350 ╬╝m in diameter at the posterior pole and/or periphery, sometimes with linear clusters and/or streaks. Inactive
lesions have sharply de¯ÇØned margins and pigmented borders
resembling POHS (Fig. 12.63A). Peripapillary atrophy may be
seen. ¯Çƒe course is prolonged with the development of new
lesions and recurrent in¯Ç×ammatory episodes. MNV occurs
in 25ÔÇô35%, CMO and subretinal ¯ÇØbrosis resembling SFU (see
below) can develop.
¯Çü Optic disc oedema and blind spot enlargement may be
present.

## Investigation

¯Çü Visual ¯ÇØelds may show large defects not corresponding to the
examination ¯ÇØndings.
¯Çü FA. Early hypo¯Ç×uorescence and late hyper¯Ç×uorescence. Old
inactive lesions show window defects. If a neovascular membrane develops in the macular region, hyper¯Ç×uorescence is
seen due to leakage (Fig. 12.63B).
¯Çü ICGA shows hypo¯Ç×uorescent acute lesions which may not
be clinically apparent. Old lesions remain hypo¯Ç×uorescent
throughout.
¯Çü OCT shows elevation of the RPE with sub-RPE material of
intermediate re¯Ç×ectivity and surrounding disruption of the
outer retina.
¯Çü OCTA shows well-demarcated areas of reduced ¯Ç×ow at the
level of the choriocapillaris.
¯Çü ERG remains normal until there is advanced retinal atrophy.

A B

## Treatment

Systemic and local steroids. Steroid-resistant patients require
immunosuppressive therapy (cyclosporine or tacrolimus, together
with mycophenolate mofetil). MNV is treated with steroids and
anti-VEGF agents.

Punctate inner choroidopathy ¯Ç£PIC¯ÇØ

## Introduction

PIC typically a¯Çøects young myopic women. Both eyes are involved
in 80%, but the involvement is usually asymmetrical. It is likely
to be a form of idiopathic multifocal choroiditis, but involvement
predominantly involves the macula.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Blurring, scotoma, ¯Ç×oaters and photopsia.
¯Çü Anterior uveitis and vitritis are usually absent or very mild.
¯Çü Fundus. Several small yellowÔÇôwhite macular spots with fuzzy
borders at the level of the inner choroid and retina (Fig. 12.64A),
sometimes with an overlying serous sensory retinal detachment. ¯Çƒese evolve into sharply demarcated atrophic scars with
little pigmentation, similar to the histo spots of POHS.
¯Çü Prognosis. Central vision may be compromised by a lesion
at the fovea or, more commonly, by MNV (up to 40% ÔÇô Fig.
12.64B and C). Visual acuity improves to 6/7.5 in 50% of cases.

## Investigation

¯Çü Visual ¯ÇØeld testing shows enlargement of the blind spot in
about 40% of cases and central and paracentral scotomas.
¯Çü FAF shows hypoauto¯Ç×uorescent spots and areas of hyperauto¯Ç×uorescence (Fig. 12.64D)
¯Çü FA shows early hyper¯Ç×uorescence and late staining of lesions
and demonstrates MNV (Fig. 12.64E).
¯Çü OCTA/OCT shows submacular MNV (Fig. 12.64F) and acute
hyper-re¯Ç×ective material with disruption of the outer retina in
the acute stage.
¯Çü ICG shows multiple mid-phase hypo¯Ç×uorescent lesions in the
posterior pole. Choroidal vessels may show localized points of
hyper¯Ç×uorescence situated close to the vessel walls, indicating
vasculitis of smaller choroidal vessels and choriocapillaris.

## Treatment

¯Çü No treatment is advised for most patients unless there is evidence of MNV.
¯Çü Anti-VEGF medication should be used for choroidal neovascular formation in the macular region.
¯Çü Steroids and systemic immunosuppressive therapy can be
helpful in the management of recurrent CMV.

Progressive subretinal ¯ÇÜbrosis and
uveitis syndrome ¯Ç£SFU¯ÇØ

SFU, also known as di¯Çøuse subretinal ¯ÇØbrosis, is an extremely
rare chronic condition. It typically a¯Çøects myopic young women,
causing gradual blurring of vision in one then both eyes. Anterior

uveitis and vitritis accompanies subretinal mounds at the posterior
pole and midperiphery (Fig. 12.65A) progressing to widespread
subretinal ¯ÇØbrosis (Fig. 12.65B). Steroids may be e¯Çøective early in
the disease, but the prognosis is poor. Some experts view SFU as
part of a spectrum with MFC/MCP and PIC.

## Birdshot retinochoroidopathy

## Introduction

Birdshot retinochoroidopathy (also known as birdshot uveitis) is an uncommon idiopathic in¯Ç×ammatory disease usually
a¯Çøecting middle-aged women. It is chronic, bilateral and manifests with severe retinitis and stromal choroiditis that are independent in¯Ç×ammatory events. Nearly all patients are HLA-A29
positive.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Insidious impairment of central vision associated
with ¯Ç×oaters, nyctalopia, dyschromatopsia and photopsia.
¯Çü Vitritis is present in either or both eyes.
¯Çü Fundus. Multiple ill-de¯ÇØned ovoid cream-coloured choroidal patches, less than one disc diameter in size, in the posterior pole and midperiphery (Fig. 12.66A). ¯Çƒe lesions o¯Ç£en
appear to radiate outward from the disc but usually spare
the macula itself and are more concentrated in the inferotemporal area. Inactive lesions consist of well-delineated
atrophic spots (Fig. 12.66B). CMO, epiretinal membrane and
MNV may develop.
¯Çü Visual ¯ÇØeld testing (not visual acuity) is the appropriate functional test for monitoring disease evolution and response to
treatment.
¯Çü Prognosis. About one-third of patients have an eventual best
visual acuity of less than 6/60. However, results are starting to
improve with aggressive and sustained immunomodulatory
treatment. Choroiditis responds well to this treatment, but the
retina less so.

## Investigation

¯Çü HLA-A29: over 95% of patients are positive (versus 7% of the
general population).
¯Çü OCT will con¯ÇØrm macular oedema.
¯Çü Auto¯Çƒuorescence will generally show more numerous hypoauto¯Ç×uorescent lesions than are seen on examination.
¯Çü FA shows extensive vascular leakage (Fig. 12.66C), with disc
and vessel staining, and can show di¯Çøuse macular oedema.
Strikingly, the fovea is o¯Ç£en spared and true cystoid macular
oedema is less common (15%).
¯Çü ICGA: lesions are more numerous. ¯Çƒese are hypo¯Ç×uorescent
(Fig. 12.66D), persist into the late phases and do not correspond to the retinal lesions.
¯Çü OCTA shows areas of ¯Ç×ow void in the choroidal stroma, with
initial sparing of the choriocapillaris. With progression of the
disease the choriocapillaris can be a¯Çøected.
¯Çü ERG is normal in early disease but with time shows rod and
cone abnormalities.

500 Other In¯Çƒammatory Conditions of the Posterior Pole

TIP HLA-A29 histocompatibility antigen is present in almost
all patients with birdshot retinochoroidopathy when PCR
methodology is used.

## Treatment

¯Çü ¯Çƒe ¯ÇØrst step is to use sub-Tenon triamcinolone acetonide (40
mg), particularly in patients with monocular disease.
¯Çü Up to 90% of patients need systemic treatment. Early referral
to a uveitis specialist should be considered.
¯Çü Systemic steroids are used initially, but immunomodulatory
treatment is o¯Ç£en needed (cyclosporine or mycophenolate followed by in¯Ç×iximab).

OTHER INFLAMMATORY CONDITIONS
OF THE POSTERIOR POLE

Acute macular neuroretinopathy ¯Ç£AMN¯ÇØ

AMN is a rare self-limited condition that typically a¯Çøects healthy
young adult women. ¯Çƒe disease may a¯Çøect one or both eyes and
may be preceded by a ¯Ç×u-like illness. ¯Çƒe incidence has increased
since the start of the COVID-19 pandemic. Symptoms consist of
decreased vision and paracentral scotomas. RedÔÇôbrown wedgeshaped lesions are observed in a ¯Ç×ower petal arrangement around
the centre of the macula corresponding to the scotomas. FA is normal or shows faint hypo¯Ç×uorescence. Acute AMN is most easily
identi¯ÇØed by wedge-shaped parafoveal lesions on infra-red imaging (Fig. 12.67A). OCT shows a characteristic hyperre¯Ç×ectivity
that starts in the outer plexiform layer (Fig. 12.67B). Over time
the outer retinal layers become thinner and disruption occurs to
the outer segments and RPE. Symptoms and signs fade slowly over
months, but the scotoma o¯Ç£en remains.

## Acute zonal occult outer retinopathy

¯Ç£AZOOR¯ÇØ

## Introduction

¯Çƒe acute zonal outer retinopathies (AZOR) are a group of rare
conditions characterized by acute visual symptoms in one or both
eyes of young or middle-aged women, some of whom have an
antecedent viral-like illness. Acute zonal occult outer retinopathy
(AZOOR) is the most common of the AZOR syndromes and is
characterized by minimal fundoscopic signs early in the disease
course. ¯Çƒe other postulated members of the group have more evident ¯ÇØndings. ¯Çƒe pathogenesis is unknown.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Photopsia and mild vitritis are frequent and subtle vasculitis
is sometimes seen.
¯Çü Visual ¯ÇØeld. ¯Çƒere is an acute onset of one or more zones of visual
¯ÇØeld loss. Field loss may progress and recovery is infrequent.
¯Çü Later ¯ÇØndings include RPE clumping and vascular attenuation in the involved area and peripapillary region, although
the fundus may remain normal.

A

B

hyperre¯ÇØectivity in the outer retinal layers (arrow). (Courtesy
of P Charbel Issa.)

## Investigations

¯Çü FAF may demonstrate abnormalities (Fig. 12.68A) and helps
to de¯ÇØne the areas of retinal involvement. It is helpful to monitor for signs of progression.
¯Çü OCT and FAF show similar signs to MEWDS, but the choriocapillaris is preserved and demonstrates no evidence of
hypofluorescence on ICGA. OCT shows loss of the outer
retina in one or more zones (Fig. 12.68B). Chronically,
atrophy of the RPE and thinning of the outer retinal layer
is found, followed in time by thinning of the inner retinal
layer.
¯Çü ERG is a less important investigation these days, but characteristically shows a-wave and b-wave amplitude reduction and
delayed 30 Hz ¯Ç×icker (cones tend to be a¯Çøected more than
rods). Electro-oculography (EOG) shows absence or severe
reduction of the light rise.

## Prognosis

¯Çü Stabilization occurs within 6 months in up to 90% of patients.
Final visual acuity is 6/12 in at least one eye in most cases.
Recurrence is sometimes seen.

A

B

## Note the abrupt transition between abnormal and normal

retina (arrow heads showing hyperauto¯ÇØuorescence); ¯Ç£B¯ÇØ
OCT image of ¯Ç£A¯ÇØ showing abnormality of the ellipsoid and
interdigitation zones (arrows). (Courtesy of P Charbel Issa.)

## Treatment

¯Çü ¯Ç×ere is no proven treatment, although there are reports of
favourable outcomes from systemic steroids.

Acute idiopathic maculopathy ¯Ç£AIM¯ÇØ

AIM is a rare, self-limited condition that is most frequently unilateral and may be preceded by a ¯Ç×u-like illness. Patients are young
adults who describe sudden marked reduction in central vision.
A viral prodrome is common and associated systemic infections
have been noted. An irregularly yellow or grey exudative retinal
detachment is seen at the macula (Fig. 12.69A and B) associated
with small haemorrhages and papillitis. Within a few weeks the
exudative changes resolve (Fig. 12.69C). An irregular bullÔÇÖs eye
appearance may develop following resolution and can be associated with persistent visual loss. OCT shows hyper-re¯Ç×ectivity at
the photoreceptor outer-segment layer and RPE thickening. FAF
shows stippled hyperauto¯Ç×uorescence that becomes hypo¯Ç×uorescent. FA shows early irregular mild hyper¯Ç×uorescence at the

detachment, with subsequent intense staining (Fig. 12.69D). No
treatment is needed.

## Acute multifocal retinitis

Acute multifocal retinitis is a very rare self-limited condition that
may be preceded by a ¯Ç×u-like illness, which is possibly an atypical presentation of cat-scratch disease. It causes sudden onset mild
visual loss in young adults. Multiple areas of retinitis are seen
posterior to the equator (Fig. 12.70) along with mild vitritis, disc
oedema and sometimes a macular star. Recovery occurs over 2ÔÇô4
months. Treatment as for cat-scratch disease may be considered.

Solitary idiopathic choroiditis ¯Ç£SIC¯ÇØ

SIC is a rare entity that presents with mild visual loss or is asymptomatic. A discrete post-equatorial dull-yellow choroidal elevation
with ill-de¯ÇØned margins is seen; vitritis is present during active
disease. Contiguous subretinal ¯Ç×uid and a macular star may be
present. With healing the lesion develops a better-de¯ÇØned margin
with resolution of subretinal ¯Ç×uid and exudation. Treatment of a
vision-threatening lesion is with systemic steroids.

Frosted branch angiitis ¯Ç£FBA¯ÇØ

FBA describes a characteristic fundus picture, usually bilateral and
may represent a speci¯ÇØc entity (primary) or a common pathway
in response to multiple stimuli. Secondary FBA may be associated with infectious retinitis, notably cytomegalovirus retinitis
and other conditions such as lymphoma and leukaemia. Primary
(idiopathic) FBA is rare and typically a¯Çøects children and young
adults, in whom presentation is with bilateral visual loss (6/30 to
PL), ¯Ç×oaters and/or photopsia. ¯Çƒere may be a viral prodrome.
Florid sheathing of retinal arterioles and venules is seen (Fig.
12.71). Anterior uveitis, vitritis and retinal oedema are common.
Treatment is with systemic steroids, though some authorities
believe the prognosis, which is usually good in the primary form,
is una¯Çøected.

Idiopathic retinal vasculitis, aneurysms
and neuroretinitis syndrome ¯Ç£IRVAN¯ÇØ

Idiopathic retinal vasculitis, aneurysms and neuroretinitis syndrome is a rare entity that typically a¯Çøects one or both eyes of
healthy young women. Fundus signs consist of arteritis and multiple aneurysmal dilatations of arteriolar branches and on the optic
nerve head, together with anterior uveitis and vitritis.
Disc oedema and a macular star (neuroretinitis) are common.

## Marked macular and circumpapillary exudative retinopathy can

develop (Fig. 12.72A). ¯Çƒe vascular changes are demonstrated
very well on FA (Fig. 12.72B). Extensive peripheral capillary nonperfusion may lead to preretinal neovascularization and early
panretinal photocoagulation should be considered. Intravitreal
anti-VEGF treatment and steroids have been associated with good

results in some patients and may be given in combination.

RETINAL CIRCULATION

## Arterial system

¯Çü ¯Çƒe central retinal artery, an end artery, enters the optic
nerve approximately 1 cm behind the globe. It is composed of
three anatomical layers:

{ ¯Çƒe intima, the innermost, is composed of a single layer of
endothelium resting on a collagenous zone.

{ ¯Çƒe internal elastic lamina separates the intima from the
media.

## Venous system

Retinal venules and veins drain blood from the capillaries.
¯Çü Small venules are larger than capillaries but have a similar
structure.
¯Çü Larger venules contain smooth muscle and merge to form veins.
¯Çü Veins contain a small amount of smooth muscle and elastic tissue in their walls and are relatively distensible. ¯Çƒeir diameter
gradually enlarges as they pass posteriorly towards the central
retinal vein.

DIABETIC RETINOPATHY

## Introduction

## Diabetes is a major concern for healthcare systems throughout the

world. ¯Çƒe prevalence of diabetes is relentlessly increasing, particularly in working-age adults. Diabetic retinopathy is the commonest cause of new blindness in most industrialised countries,
mainly because of a delay in obtaining medical treatment. However, with e¯Ç×ective screening and by embracing and implementing
the conclusions of the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study
(ETDRS) and the anti-VEGF clinical trials, the risk of severe visual
loss can be reduced to less than 5%.

## Ophthalmic complications of diabetes

{ ¯Çƒe media consists mainly of smooth muscle.

{ ¯Çƒe adventitia is the outermost and is composed of loose
connective tissue.
¯Çü Retinal arterioles arise from the central retinal artery. ¯Çƒeir
walls contain smooth muscle, but in contrast to arteries the
internal elastic lamina is discontinuous.

## Capillaries

Retinal capillaries supply the inner two-thirds of the retina, with
the outer third being supplied by the choriocapillaris. ¯Çƒe inner
capillary network (plexus) is located in the ganglion cell layer, with
an outer plexus in the inner nuclear layer. Capillary-free zones are
present around arterioles (Fig. 13.1A) and at the fovea (foveal avascular zone ÔÇô FAZ). Retinal capillaries are devoid of smooth muscle
and elastic tissue. ¯Çƒeir walls consist of the following (Fig. 13.1B):
¯Çü Endothelial cells form a single layer on the basement membrane and are linked by tight junctions that form the inner
bloodÔÇôretinal barrier.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe basement membrane lies beneath the endothelial cells
with an outer basal lamina enclosing pericytes.
¯Çü Pericytes lie external to endothelial cells and have multiple
pseudopodial processes that envelop the capillaries. Pericytes
have contractile properties and are thought to participate in
autoregulation of the microvascular circulation.

{ Recurrent styes.

¯Çü Common

{ Maculopathy: diabetic macular oedema and macular
ischaemia.

{ Retinopathy: sequelae arising from retinal ischaemia (neovascularization of the optic disc and retina) and vitreous
haemorrhage.

{ Unstable refraction.
¯Çü Uncommon

A B

of Indian ink-injected retina; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× endothelial cells with elongated nuclei and pericytes with
rounded nuclei ÔÇô trypsin digest preparation. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from Clinical
Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001.)

{ Xanthelasma.

{ Accelerated age-related cataract.

{ Tractional retinal detachment.

{ Neovascularization of the iris and angle.

{ Neovascular glaucoma (NVG).

{ Ocular motor nerve palsies.

{ Reduced corneal sensitivity.
¯Çü Rare. Papillopathy, pupillary lightÔÇônear dissociation, Wolfram
syndrome (progressive optic atrophy and multiple neurological and systemic abnormalities), acute-onset cataract, rhinoorbital mucormycosis.

## Prevalence

¯Çƒe reported prevalence of diabetic retinopathy (DR) in people
with diabetes varies substantially among studies and even among
contemporary populations in the same country, but is probably
around 40%. People with diabetes are o¯ÇØen unaware that they
might have retinopathy. ¯Çƒe prevalence of DR in type 2 diabetes is
67% 10 years a¯ÇØer the diagnosis has been made and 10% will have
proliferative disease. DR is more common in type 1 diabetes than
in type 2 diabetes.

## Risk factors

¯Çü Duration of diabetes is the most important predictor of diabetic retinopathy. DR rarely develops within 5 years of the
onset of diabetes or before puberty. People with type 1 diabetes
are at particular long-term risk. Duration is a stronger predictor for proliferative disease than for maculopathy.
¯Çü Poor control of diabetes. ¯Çƒe Diabetes Control and Complication Trial (DCCT) shows that tight blood glucose control,
particularly when instituted early, can prevent or delay the
development or progression of DR (subsequently con¯Ç£rmed
by the United Kingdom Prospective Diabetes Study). However,
a sudden improvement in control may be associated with progression of retinopathy. ¯Çƒe higher the glycated haemoglobin
(HbA1c) the greater the risk of complications of diabetes such
as PDR. In people with advanced retinopathy even the most
rigorous control of blood glucose may not prevent progression.
¯Çü Hypertension is very common in those with type 2 diabetes
and should be rigorously controlled (<140/80 mmHg). Tight
control of blood pressure appears to be particularly bene¯Ç£cial
in type 2 diabetics with maculopathy.
¯Çü Nephropathy, if severe, is associated with worsening of DR.
Conversely, treatment of renal disease (e.g. renal transplantation) may be associated with improvement of retinopathy and
a better response to photocoagulation.
¯Çü Drugs. Pioglitazone is associated with a signi¯Ç£cant risk of
worsening of diabetic macular oedema.
¯Çü Pregnancy is sometimes associated with rapid progression
of DR. Predicating factors include (a) greater pre-pregnancy
severity of retinopathy, (b) poor pre-pregnancy control of diabetes, (c) control exerted too rapidly during the early stages
of pregnancy and (d) pre-eclampsia. ¯Çƒe risk of progression
is related to the severity of DR in the ¯Ç£rst trimester. Approximately 5% with mild DR and a third of those with moderate
DR will progress to PDR during the pregnancy. If substantial

DR is present, frequency of review should re¯Çøect individual
risk and can be up to 2-weekly. Diabetic macular oedema usually resolves spontaneously a¯ÇØer pregnancy and might not
need to be treated if it develops in late pregnancy.
¯Çü Other risk factors include hyperlipidaemia, cataract surgery
and anaemia.

TIP The risk of severe visual loss can be signi¯Çƒcantly
reduced by regular retinal screening and by embracing and
implementing the conclusions of the various clinical trials in
diabetes.

## Pathogenesis

DR is predominantly a microangiopathy in which small blood vessels are particularly vulnerable to damage from high glucose levels.
Direct hyperglycaemic e¯Ç×ects on retinal cells are also likely to play
a role.
Many angiogenic stimulators and inhibitors have been identi¯Ç£ed. Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) appears to be of
particular importance in the former category.

Classi¯Ç£cation

¯Çƒe classi¯Ç£cation used in the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS ÔÇô the modi¯Ç£ed Airlie House classi¯Ç£cation) is
widely used internationally. An abbreviated version is set out in
Table 13.1, in conjunction with management guidelines. ¯Çƒe following descriptive categories are also in widespread use in clinical
practice:
¯Çü Background diabetic retinopathy (BDR) is characterized by
microaneurysms, dot and blot haemorrhages and exudates.
¯Çƒese are generally the earliest signs of DR and persist as more
advanced lesions appear.
¯Çü Diabetic maculopathy strictly refers to the presence of any
retinopathy at the macula, but is commonly reserved for signi¯Ç£cant changes, particularly vision-threatening oedema and
ischaemia.
¯Çü Preproliferative diabetic retinopathy (PPDR) manifests with cotton-wool spots, venous beading, intraretinal
microvascular anomalies (IRMA) and moderate to severe
retinal haemorrhages. PPDR indicates progressive retinal
ischaemia, with a heightened risk of progression to retinal
neovascularization.
¯Çü Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) is characterized
by neovascularization on or within one disc diameter of
the disc (NVD) and/or new vessels elsewhere (NVE) in the
fundus.
¯Çü High-risk PDR is de¯Ç£ned as any one of the following: (a)
presence of vitreous haemorrhage or preretinal haemorrhage,
(b) presence of any active neovascularization, (b) location of
neovascularization on or within one disc diameter of the optic
disc, (d) NVD >1/3-disc area or NVE >1/2-disc area.
¯Çü Advanced diabetic eye disease is characterized by tractional
retinal detachment, signi¯Ç£cant persistent vitreous haemorrhage and neovascular glaucoma.

## See text for description

## Signs

## Microaneurysms

Microaneurysms are localized outpouchings, mainly saccular, of
the capillary wall that may form either by focal dilatation of the
capillary wall where pericytes are absent, or by fusion of two arms
of a capillary loop (Fig. 13.2A). Most develop in the inner capillary plexus (ganglion cell layer), frequently adjacent to areas of
capillary non-perfusion (Fig. 13.2B). Loss of pericytes (Fig. 13.2C)
may also lead to endothelial cell proliferation with the formation of
ÔÇÿcellularÔÇÖ microaneurysms (Fig. 13.2D). Microaneurysms may leak
plasma constituents into the retina as a result of breakdown in the
bloodÔÇôretinal barrier, or may thrombose. ¯Çƒey tend to be the earliest sign of DR.
¯Çü Signs. Tiny red dots, o¯ÇØen initially temporal to the fovea
(Fig. 13.3A). May be indistinguishable clinically from dot
haemorrhages.

¯Çü Fluorescein angiography (FA) allows di¯Ç×erentiation between
dot haemorrhages and non-thrombosed microaneurysms.
Early frames show tiny hyper¯Çøuorescent dots (Fig. 13.3B), typically more numerous than visible clinically. Late frames show
di¯Ç×use hyper¯Çøuorescence due to leakage.
¯Çü OCT shows microaneurysms in the middle retinal layers. Normal vessels look similar to microaneurysms, but are usually
seen in the inner retinal layers.

## Retinal haemorrhages

¯Çü Retinal nerve ¯Ç×bre layer haemorrhages arise from the larger
super¯Ç£cial precapillary arterioles (Fig. 13.4A) and assume
their characteristic shape (Fig. 13.4B) because of the architecture of the retinal nerve ¯Ç£bre layer.
¯Çü Intraretinal haemorrhages arise from the venous end of capillaries and are located in the compact middle layers of the
retina (see Fig. 13.4A) with a resultant red ÔÇÿdot/blotÔÇÖ con¯Ç£guration (Fig. 13.4C).

become a microaneurysm ÔÇô ¯Ç×at preparation of Indian ink-injected retina; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× an area of capillary

¯Çü Deeper dark round haemorrhages (Fig. 13.4D) represent
haemorrhagic retinal infarcts and are located within the
middle retinal layers (see Fig. 13.4A). ¯Çƒe extent of involvement is a signi¯Ç£cant marker of the likelihood of progression
to PDR.

## Exudates

Exudates are caused by chronic localised retinal oedema. ¯Çƒey
develop at the junction of normal and oedematous retina. ¯Çƒey
are composed of lipoprotein and lipid-¯Ç£lled macrophages located
mainly within the outer plexiform layer (Fig. 13.5A). Hyperlipidaemia may increase the likelihood of exudate formation.
¯Çü Signs

{ Waxy yellow lesions (Fig. 13.5B) with relatively distinct
margins arranged in clumps and/or rings at the posterior
pole, o¯ÇØen surrounding leaking microaneurysms.

{ With time, the number and size tend to increase (Fig.
13.5C) and the fovea may be involved.

{ When leakage ceases, exudates absorb spontaneously over
a period of months, either into healthy surrounding capillaries or by phagocytosis.

{ Chronic leakage leads to enlargement and the deposition
of crystalline cholesterol (Fig. 13.5D).
¯Çü FA will commonly show hypo¯Çøuorescence only with large
dense exudates. Although background choroidal ¯Çøuorescence
is masked, retinal capillary ¯Çøuorescence is generally preserved
overlying the lesions.

Diabetic macular oedema (DMO)

Diabetic maculopathy (macular oedema, exudates or ischaemia) is the most common cause of moderate visual impairment in diabetic patients, particularly type 2. Diffuse retinal

510 Diabetic Retinopathy

plexiform layer; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× small exudates and microaneurysms; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× more extensive exudates forming a circinate pattern; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× exudates involving the fovea, including central crystalline cholesterol deposition.

oedema is caused by extensive capillary leakage and localized
oedema by focal leakage from microaneurysms and dilated
capillary segments. The fluid is initially located between the
outer plexiform and inner nuclear layers. Later it may also
involve the inner plexiform and nerve fibre layers, until eventually the entire thickness of the retina becomes oedematous.
This is readily detectable using OCT (Fig. 13.6A) and FA (Fig.
13.6B).
¯Çü Clinically signi¯Ç×cant macular oedema (CSMO) is de¯Ç£ned in
the ETDRS as:

{ Retinal thickening within 500 ╬╝m (or 1/3-disc diameter) of
the centre of the macula (Fig. 13.7, upper le¯ÇØ).

{ Hard exudates within 500 ╬╝m of the centre of the
macula, with adjacent retinal thickening, which may
lie more than 500 ╬╝m from the foveal centre (Fig. 13.7,
upper right).

{ Retinal thickening 1-disc area or larger, any part of which
is within one disc diameter of the centre of the macula
(Fig. 13.7, lower centre).

¯Çü Focal maculopathy: well-circumscribed retinal thickening
associated with complete or incomplete rings of exudates (Fig.
13.8A). FA shows late, focal hyper¯Çøuorescence due to leakage,
usually with good macular perfusion (Fig. 13.8B).
¯Çü Di¯ÇØuse maculopathy: di¯Ç×use retinal thickening, which may be
associated with cystoid changes. ¯Çƒere are typically also scattered microaneurysms and small haemorrhages. Landmarks
may be obscured by oedema, which may render localization
of the fovea impossible (Fig. 13.9A). FA shows mid- and latephase di¯Ç×use hyper¯Çøuorescence (Fig. 13.9B).

## Ischaemic maculopathy

¯Çü Signs. ¯Çƒe retina o¯ÇØen shows dot and blot haemorrhages and
cotton wool spots, but the macula may look relatively normal
despite reduced visual acuity (Fig. 13.10A).
¯Çü FA shows capillary non-perfusion at the fovea (an enlarged
FAZ) and frequently other areas of capillary non-perfusion at
the posterior pole and periphery (Fig. 13.10B and see OCTA
appearance in Fig. 14.26D).

80% of the cotton-wool spots have hypore¯Çøective cavities

## Venous changes

## Fovea

Cotton-wool spots

Cotton-wool spots are composed of accumulations of neuronal
debris within the nerve ¯Ç£bre layer. ¯Çƒey result from ischaemic
disruption of nerve axons, the swollen ends of which are known
as cystoid bodies, seen on light microscopy as globular structures
in the nerve ¯Ç£bre layer (Fig. 13.11A). As cotton-wool spots heal,
debris is removed by autolysis and phagocytosis.
¯Çü Signs. Small ¯Çøu¯Ç×y whitish super¯Ç£cial lesions that obscure
underlying blood vessels (Fig. 13.11B and C). ¯Çƒey are clinically evident only in the post-equatorial retina, where the
nerve ¯Ç£bre layer is of su¯ÇÜcient thickness to render them
visible.
¯Çü FA shows focal hypo¯Çøuorescence due to local ischaemia and
blockage of background choroidal ¯Çøuorescence.
¯Çü OCT shows cotton-wool spots in the retinal nerve ¯Ç£bre layer,
which show a hyperre¯Çøective pattern in the acute stage. About

## Venous anomalies seen in retinal ischaemia consist of generalized

dilatation and tortuosity, looping (Fig. 13.12A), beading (focal
narrowing and dilatation) (Fig. 13.12B) and sausage-like segmentation (Fig. 13.12C). ¯Çƒe extent of the retinal area exhibiting
venous changes correlates well with the likelihood of developing
proliferative disease.

## Intraretinal microvascular abnormalities

Intraretinal microvascular abnormalities (IRMA) are arteriolarÔÇô
venular shunts that run from retinal arterioles to venules, thus
bypassing the capillary bed and are therefore o¯ÇØen seen adjacent
to areas of marked capillary hypoperfusion (Fig. 13.13A).
¯Çü Signs. Fine, irregular, red intraretinal lines that run from arterioles to venules, without crossing major blood vessels (Fig.
13.13B).
¯Çü FA shows focal hypo¯Çøuorescence associated with adjacent
areas of capillary closure (ÔÇÿdropoutÔÇÖ) but without leakage
(unlike neovascularization, where profuse leakage of ¯Çøuorescein occurs).
¯Çü OCT. Unlike new vessels, IRMA do not breach the ILM.

## Arterial changes

Subtle retinal arteriolar dilatation may be an early marker of ischaemic dysfunction. When signi¯Ç£cant ischaemia is present signs
include peripheral narrowing, ÔÇÿsilver wiringÔÇÖ and obliteration, similar to the late appearance following a branch retinal artery occlusion.

## Proliferative retinopathy

Although preretinal new vessels may arise anywhere in the retina,
they are most commonly seen at the posterior pole. Fibrous tissue,
initially ¯Ç£ne, gradually develops as vessels increase in size.

¯Çü New vessels at the disc (NVD) describes neovascularization on
or within one disc diameter of the optic nerve head (Fig. 13.14).
¯Çü NVE describes neovascularization further away from the disc
(Fig. 13.15).
¯Çü New vessels on the iris (NVI ÔÇô Fig. 13.16), also known as
rubeosis iridis, carry a high likelihood of progression to neovascular glaucoma (see Ch. 11).
¯Çü FA (see Fig. 13.14C) highlights neovascularization during the
early phases of the angiogram and shows irregular expanding
hyper¯Çøuorescence during the later stages due to intense leakage of dye from neovascular tissue. FA can be used to con¯Ç£rm
the presence of new vessels (NV) if the clinical diagnosis is in
doubt and also delineates areas of ischaemic retina that might
be selectively targeted for laser treatment.
¯Çü OCT of the retina is good at detecting new vessels (100%), but not
regression (45%) or reactivation (10%). However, OCT angiography is useful for the objective monitoring of disease a¯ÇØer treatment.

## Treatment

## General

¯Çü Patient education is critical, including the need to comply
with review and treatment schedules in order to optimize
visual outcomes. In type 2 diabetes this may involve counselling on weight reduction programs and emphasizing the
bene¯Ç£ts of regular exercise.
¯Çü Diabetic control should be optimized.
¯Çü Other risk factors, particularly systemic hypertension and
hyperlipidaemia, should be controlled in conjunction with the
patientÔÇÖs diabetologist.
¯Çü Feno¯Ç×brate 200 mg daily has been shown to reduce the progression of DR in type 2 diabetics and prescription should be
considered. ¯Çƒe decision is independent of whether the patient
already takes a statin.
¯Çü Smoking has not been de¯Ç£nitively shown to a¯Ç×ect retinopathy, but patients should be advised to stop smoking in order to
reduce the risk of macrovascular complications.

¯Çü Other modi¯Ç×able factors such as anaemia and renal failure
should be addressed as necessary.

TIP The key to the prevention of diabetic retinopathy is
patient education and long-term control of blood sugar.

## Ophthalmic treatment of diabetic macular
oedema

Until recently laser photocoagulation was the mainstay of treatment for DMO, reducing the risk of moderate visual loss by 50%
overall compared with observation. ¯Çƒe availability of intravitreal
anti-VEGF agents and strong evidence to support their e¯ÇÜcacy has
dramatically altered the approach to management in recent years.

## Many of the current guidelines for care have arisen from the conclusions of large prospective randomised controlled trials undertaken

by the Diabetic Retinopathy Clinical Research Network (DRCR.
net). It should be remembered that these studies provide average
results in groups of patients and that individual responses may vary
considerably, with some patients deriving little or no bene¯Ç£t from
these interventions. Options should always be discussed fully with
the patient. In particular, patients with good vision (6/7.5 or better) who otherwise meet criteria for treatment might prefer to be
monitored once the risks and bene¯Ç×ts of various interventions
are considered. ¯Çƒe need for meticulous follow-up and the cost of
the proposed treatment may also be an issue for some patients and
needs to be considered when discussing therapy.
¯Çü Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents

{ DRCR.net Protocol I concludes that intravitreal ranibizumab more effectively improves visual acuity than
focal/grid laser treatment for centre-involved DMO.
Laser treatment should be deferred for 6 months after
commencement of anti-VEGF therapy. Good outcomes
can be expected for at least 5 years, despite a sequential
reduction in the number of anti-VEGF injections.

in visual acuity at 5 years irrespective of whether a¯Çøibercept, bevacizumab or ranibizumab is used in patients with

¯Çü Intravitreal triamcinolone and sustained-release steroid
implants. In pseudophakic eyes intravitreal triamcinolone
steroid injection followed by prompt laser is comparable to
ranibizumab with regard to visual improvement and reduced
retinal thickening. ¯Çƒis is a good therapeutic option in pregnancy or where there is a contraindication to anti-VEGF treatment (for example recent myocardial infarct). However, there
is a signi¯Ç£cant risk of inducing an elevation of intraocular
pressure (IOP) and this must be monitored regularly. In a phakic eye there is an increased risk of cataract. Sustained-release
intravitreal steroid implants (e.g. Ozurdex and Iluvien) have
demonstrated promising results.
¯Çü Pars plana vitrectomy (PPV) may be indicated when macular
oedema is associated with tangential traction from a thickened
and taut posterior hyaloid (see Ch. 14 ÔÇô vitreomacular traction
syndrome). It has also been suggested that some eyes without a
taut posterior hyaloid may also bene¯Ç£t from vitrectomy. Clinically, a taut thickened posterior hyaloid is characterized by an
increased glistening of the premacular vitreous face. FA typically
shows di¯Ç×use leakage and prominent CMO, but OCT is usually
the de¯Ç£nitive assessment. ¯Çƒere are several other indications for
PPV in the management of diabetic eye disease (see later).
¯Çü Speci¯Ç×c recommendations

{ CSMO not involving the centre of the macula (particularly where exudates threaten the fovea) may be treated

or modi¯Ç£ed grid laser photocoagulation. If available, subthreshold macular laser treatment, intravitreal anti-VEGF
therapy or an intravitreal steroid implant may be reasonable alternatives for this indication.

## Laser treatment for proliferative retinopathy

¯Çü Scatter laser treatment (panretinal photocoagulation ÔÇô Fig.
13.20A) continues to be the mainstay of PDR treatment in most

demonstrates the bene¯Ç£t of PRP. For instance, severe NVD without haemorrhage carries a 26% risk of visual loss at 2 years that
is reduced to 9% with PRP. However, recent research (Protocol S
from DRCR.net) shows that a course of injections of intravitreal
ranibizumab is as e¯Ç×ective as photocoagulation in patients at high
risk of PDR at 5 years (see below). In addition, the CLARITY
study provides 1-year data which show that intravitreal a¯Çøibercept injections are as e¯Ç×ective as PRP in the treatment of PDR.

may worsen with successive sessions. Topical anaesthesia is

anaesthesia can be administered if necessary.

Targeted retinal photocoagulation (TRP)

Wide-¯Ç£eld FA allows accurate delineation of peripheral capillary
non-perfusion (Fig. 13.21). Selective treatment of these areas with
scatter laser has been reported as e¯Ç×ectively leading to regression
of NV whilst minimizing potential complications.

## Advanced diabetic eye disease

Advanced diabetic eye disease is a serious vision-threatening complication of DR that occurs in patients in whom treatment has
been inadequate or unsuccessful. Occasionally, advanced disease
is evident at presentation.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Haemorrhage may be preretinal (retrohyaloid), intragel or
both (Fig. 13.22A). Intragel haemorrhages usually take longer
to clear than preretinal because the former is usually more substantial. In some eyes, altered blood becomes compacted on
the posterior vitreous face to form an ÔÇÿochre membraneÔÇÖ. Ultrasonography is used in eyes with dense vitreous haemorrhage to
detect the possibility of associated retinal detachment.
¯Çü Tractional retinal detachment (Fig. 13.22B and C) is caused by
progressive contraction of ¯Ç£brovascular membranes over areas of
vitreoretinal attachment. Posterior vitreous detachment in eyes
with PDR is o¯ÇØen incomplete due to the strong adhesions between
cortical vitreous and areas of ¯Ç£brovascular proliferation. Haemorrhage o¯ÇØen occurs at these sites due to stress exerted on NV.
¯Çü Rubeosis iridis (iris neovascularization ÔÇô NVI) may occur in
eyes with PDR and if severe may lead to neovascular glaucoma
(see Ch. 11). NVI is particularly common in eyes with severe

non-perfusion ÔÇô the arrow indicates a well-de¯Çƒned example.
(Courtesy of A Ambresin.)

{ Power should be su¯ÇÜcient to produce only a light intensity
burn.

{ Spacing. Burns should be separated by 1ÔÇô1.5 burn widths.

{ Extent of treated area. ¯Çƒe initial treatment session should
consist of 1500 burns in most cases, though more may be
applied if there is a risk of imminent sight loss from vitreous haemorrhage. ¯Çƒe more extensive the treatment at a
single session, the greater the likelihood of complications.
Reported ¯Ç£gures vary, but 2500ÔÇô3500 burns are likely to
be required for regression of mild PDR, 4000 for moderate
PDR and 7000 for severe PDR. ¯Çƒe number of burns o¯Ç×ers
only approximate guidance, as the e¯Ç×ective extent of treatment is dependent on numerous variables.

{ Pattern of treatment. Treatment is generally restricted to the
area outside the temporal macular vascular arcades. It is good
practice to delineate a ÔÇÿbarrierÔÇÖ of laser burns temporal to the
macula early in the procedure to help to reduce the risk of
accidental macular damage. Many practitioners leave two disc
diameters untreated at the nasal side of the disc, to preserve
paracentral ¯Ç£eld. In very severe PDR it is advisable to treat
the inferior fundus ¯Ç£rst, since any vitreous haemorrhage will
gravitate inferiorly and obscure this area, precluding further
treatment. Areas of vitreoretinal traction should be avoided.
¯Çü Review is dependent on PDR severity and the requirement for
successive treatment applications. Initial treatment should be
fractionated over 2ÔÇô3 sessions. Once an adequate number of
burns have been applied review can be set for 4ÔÇô6 weeks.

TIP In a patient with proliferative diabetic retinopathy and
macular oedema, ¯Çƒrst treat the oedema then undertake
panretinal photocoagulation.

¯Çü Indicators of regression include blunting of vessel tips, shrinking and disappearance of NV, o¯ÇØen leaving ÔÇÿghostÔÇÖ vessels or
¯Ç£brosis (Fig. 13.20C and D), regression of IRMA, decreased
venous changes, absorption of retinal haemorrhages, disc pallor. Contraction of regressing vessels or associated induction of
vitreous separation can precipitate vitreous haemorrhage. Significant ¯Ç£brous proliferation can lead to tractional retinal detachment (see below). Patients should remain under observation, as
recurrence can occur with a requirement for additional PRP.

VEGF inhibition for proliferative retinopathy

PRCR.net Protocol S concludes that intravitreal ranibizumab treatment
is as e¯Ç×ective as PRP in patients at high risk of PDR for up to 5 years.
¯Çƒe macular oedema rate is reduced and the visual ¯Ç£eld is slightly better maintained with anti-VEGF treatment. Irrespective of the therapeutic option, approximately half will experience a vitreous haemorrhage

retinal ischaemia or persistent retinal detachment following

## Indications for pars plana vitrectomy

Vitrectomy in DR is typically combined with extensive endolaser
PRP. Visual results depend on the speci¯Ç£c indication for surgery
and the severity of pre-existing disease.
¯Çü Severe persistent vitreous haemorrhage that precludes adequate PRP is the most common indication (Fig. 13.22D). In
the absence of rubeosis iridis, vitrectomy has traditionally
been considered within 3 months of the initial vitreous haemorrhage in type 1 diabetics and in most cases of bilateral haemorrhage. However, the outcome may be better with earlier
surgery and the availability of intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy
may further modify the approach.
¯Çü DRCR.net protocol AB concludes that eyes with vitreous
haemorrhage that undergo initial vitrectomy with PRP recover
vision faster than those treated initially with a¯Çøibercept. However, the vision is similar in the two groups 2 years later, which

suggests that either option is a reasonable ¯Ç£rst-line treatment
of vitreous haemorrhage from PDR.
¯Çü Progressive tractional RD threatening or involving the macula must be treated without delay. However, extramacular tractional detachments may be observed, since they o¯ÇØen remain
stationary for prolonged periods.
¯Çü Combined tractional and rhegmatogenous RD should be
treated urgently.
¯Çü Premacular retrohyaloid haemorrhage (Fig. 13.23A), if
dense and persistent should be considered for early vitrectomy because, if untreated, the internal limiting membrane or
posterior hyaloid face may serve as a sca¯Ç×old for subsequent
¯Ç£brovascular proliferation and consequent tractional macular
detachment or macular epiretinal membrane formation. Dispersion with Nd:YAG laser (hyaloidotomy) is o¯ÇØen successful
(Fig. 13.23B and C).
¯Çü Anterior segment neovascularization and media opacities,
which prevent visualization of the posterior pole, should be
considered for vitrectomy and intraoperative laser therapy.

Before; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× and ¯ÇØC¯Ç× after Nd:YAG laser hyaloidotomy. (Courtesy
of S Chen.)

TIP Persistent vitreous haemorrhage or progressive tractional
retinal detachment threatening the macula should be treated as
soon as possible with a pars plana vitrectomy.

## Diabetic papillopathy

Diabetic papillopathy (diabetic papillitis) has been speculated to
be an uncommon variant of anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy,
though is more commonly bilateral and tends to exhibit more diffuse disc swelling.
¯Çü Pathogenesis. ¯Çƒe underlying pathogenesis is not known. ¯Çƒe
condition is more likely to occur in people with small optic
discs and a¯ÇØer a rapid decrease in the blood glucose level.
¯Çü Clinical features. It occurs mainly in younger people with diabetes and manifests with mild painless visual impairment that
is unilateral in more than half of cases. Hyperaemic disc swelling is characteristic and disc telangiectasia is o¯ÇØen present
(Fig. 13.24A and B). Crowding of the fellow disc may be found.
¯Çü Resolution occurs over several months (Fig. 13.24C), o¯ÇØen
leaving mild disc pallor. Final visual acuity (VA) is 6/12 or better in 80%, subject to the e¯Ç×ect of co-existing DR.
¯Çü Di¯ÇØerentialdiagnosis. ¯Çƒe telangiectasia should not be mistaken
for neovascularization. Distinction from retinal vein occlusion
(RVO)-type papillophlebitis (see below) rests on the presence of
more extensive retinal haemorrhages and venous congestion in
the latter, but may not be possible. Raised intracranial pressure
should be excluded if there is bilateral disc swelling.
¯Çü Treatment. ¯Çƒere is no validated treatment. Intravitreal antiVEGF agents and steroids via various routes have been tried,
with no signi¯Ç£cant bene¯Ç£t.

NON-DIABETIC RETINOPATHY

Up to 10% of individuals over the age of 40 without diabetes mellitus exhibit ÔÇô usually very mild ÔÇô retinopathic features such as
microaneurysms, dot and blot haemorrhages and cotton-wool
spots that would be consistent with a diagnosis of DR. Assuming
that an alternative ocular cause such as RVO or idiopathic macular
telangiectasia has been excluded, this ÔÇÿnon-diabeticÔÇÖ retinopathy
tends to be associated with increased cerebrovascular and cardiovascular risk and may be particularly prevalent in patients with
known or incipient hypertension. ¯Çƒere is evidence suggesting that
it may be a marker of preclinical diabetes in some patients; higher
venular calibre may also denote this. Appropriate management is
unde¯Ç£ned, though evaluation and optimal management of systemic vascular risk factors may be prudent. ¯Çƒe signs commonly
disappear spontaneously and this is more likely in those with lower
levels of cardiovascular risk.

RETINAL VENOUS OCCLUSIVE DISEASE

## Introduction

Retinal vein thrombosis is strongly associated with age-related
local and systemic factors. It is the second most common retinal
vascular disease a¯ÇØer DR. ¯Çƒe condition a¯Ç×ects 5 in 1000 people in
the industrialised world. In branch retinal vein occlusion (BRVO)
arteriosclerotic thickening of a branch retinal arteriole is associated with compression of a venule at an arteriovenous crossing
point, exacerbated by sharing an adventitial sheath. ¯Çƒis leads to

524 Retinal Venous Occlusive Disease

A

B

C

¯Ç×uorescein angiogram of optic disc shown in ¯ÇØA¯Ç× showing
telangiectasia and signs of early background retinopathy;
¯ÇØC¯Ç× disc appearance 2 months later after spontaneous
resolution.

secondary changes that include endothelial cell loss, turbulent ¯Çøow
and thrombus formation. Similarly, the central retinal vein and
artery possess a common sheath at crossing points posterior to the
lamina cribrosa so that atherosclerotic changes of the artery may
precipitate central retinal vein occlusion (CRVO). Haematological
pro-thrombotic factors are thought to be important in a minority, amplifying an atherosclerotic anatomical predisposition. Once
venous occlusion has occurred, elevation of venous and capillary
pressure with stagnation of blood ¯Çøow ensues, resulting in retinal
hypoxia, which in turn results in damage to the capillary endothelial cells, extravasation of blood constituents and liberation of
mediators such as VEGF.

## Risk factors

¯Çü Age is the most important factor. Over 50% of cases occur in
patients older than 65.
¯Çü Hypertension is present in two-thirds or more of RVO
patients over the age of 50 years and in 25% of younger
patients. It is a particularly important risk factor in patients
with BRVO.
¯Çü Hyperlipidaemia is present in one-third or more of patients,
irrespective of age.
¯Çü Diabetes mellitus is present in up to 15% of patients over 50
years of age. It is more prevalent in Asian and African patients,
but is uncommon in younger patients.
¯Çü Glaucoma and ocular hypertension are associated with a
higher risk of CRVO.
¯Çü Oral contraceptive pill. In younger females the contraceptive
pill is the most common underlying association and probably
should not be taken following RVO.
¯Çü Smoking. Current smoking may be associated with an
increased incidence of RVO.
¯Çü Uncommon. Dehydration, myeloproliferative disorders
(e.g. myeloma, polycythaemia), thrombophilia (e.g. hyperhomocysteinaemia, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome,
factor V Leiden mutation), in¯Çøammatory disease associated
with occlusive periphlebitis (e.g. Beh├ºet syndrome, sarcoidosis, Wegener granulomatosis), orbital disease and chronic
renal failure.

## Systemic assessment

¯Çƒe detection and management of associated systemic disease is
aimed principally at reducing the risk of future vascular occlusive
events, both ocular and systemic.

## All patients

¯Çü Blood pressure (BP).
¯Çü Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) or plasma viscosity
(PV).
¯Çü Full blood count (FBC).
¯Çü Random blood glucose. Further assessment for diabetes if
indicated.
¯Çü Random total and high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol. Additional lipid testing may be considered.

¯Çü Plasma protein electrophoresis. To detect dysproteinaemias
such as multiple myeloma.
¯Çü Other tests. Some authorities advocate routine investigation
for systemic end-organ damage related to the cardiovascular
risk factors commonly found in patients with RVO. ¯Çƒis is
intended to help the prevention of further non-ocular damage,
as well as facilitating systemic management to reduce the risk
of recurrent ocular venous occlusion.

{ Urea, electrolytes and creatinine to detect renal disease
associated with hypertension. Chronic renal failure is also
a rare cause of RVO.

{ ¯Çƒyroid function testing. ¯Çƒere is a higher prevalence of
thyroid disease in RVO patients.

{ Electrocardiography (ECG). Le¯ÇØ ventricular hypertrophy
is associated with hypertension.

## Selected patients according to clinical
indication

¯Çƒese tests might be considered in (a) patients under the age of
50; (b) in bilateral RVO; (c) patients with previous thromboses or
a family history of thrombosis; and (d) some patients in whom
investigation for the common associations is negative. Evidence of
a causative link for many of these is limited.
¯Çü Chest X-ray. Sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, le¯ÇØ ventricular hypertrophy in hypertension.
¯Çü C-reactive protein (CRP). Sensitive indicator of in¯Çøammation.
¯Çü Plasma homocysteine level. To exclude hyperhomocysteinaemia, for which there is reasonable evidence of an increased
RVO risk.
¯Çü ÔÇÿ¯Çƒrombophilia screenÔÇÖ. By convention this refers to heritable
thrombophilia. Tests might typically include thrombin time,
prothrombin time and activated partial thromboplastin time,
antithrombin functional assay, protein C, protein S, activated
protein C resistance, factor V Leiden mutation, prothrombin
G20210A mutation, lupus anticoagulant and in particular,
anticardiolipin antibody (IgG and IgM).
¯Çü Autoantibodies. Rheumatoid factor, antinuclear antibody
(ANA), anti-DNA antibody, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA).
¯Çü Serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE). Sarcoidosis.
¯Çü Treponemal serology. See Chapter 12
¯Çü Carotid duplex imaging to exclude ocular ischaemic
syndrome.

## Branch retinal vein occlusion

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms and VA depend on the anatomical location of the
occlusion. If the macula is involved, a sudden painless onset of
blurred vision and metamorphopsia is experienced. Peripheral
occlusion may be asymptomatic.
¯Çü Iris neovascularization (NVI) and neovascular glaucoma
(NVG) are much less common in BRVO than in CRVO (2ÔÇô3%
at 3 years).

¯Çü Fundus

{ Dilatation and tortuosity of the a¯Ç×ected venous segment,
with ¯Çøame-shaped and dot/blot haemorrhages, principally
in the retinal area drained by the thrombosed vein though
an occasional haemorrhage may be identi¯Ç£ed elsewhere.
Cotton-wool spots and retinal oedema may be present, but
this is not inevitable.

{ ¯Çƒe superotemporal quadrant is most commonly a¯Ç×ected.

{ ¯Çƒe site of occlusion may be identi¯Ç£able as an arteriovenous crossing point (Fig. 13.25).

{ ¯Çƒe acute features usually resolve within 6ÔÇô12 months
leaving venous sheathing and sclerosis and variable persistent/recurrent haemorrhage (Fig. 13.26). ¯Çƒe severity of
residual signs is highly variable.

{ Collateral vessels may form near areas of limited capillary
perfusion a¯ÇØer weeks to months. ¯Çƒey usually connect a
poorly functioning to a normally functioning segment of the
venous circulation and typically appear as tortuous or looping channels. ¯Çƒey may cross the horizontal raphe between
the inferior and superior vascular arcades (Fig. 13.27A). ¯Çƒe
appearance of collaterals is associated with a better prognosis.

{ Chronic macular oedema is the most common cause of
persistent poor VA a¯ÇØer BRVO (Fig. 13.27B).

{ Retinal neovascularization occurs in about 8% of eyes by
3 years. ¯Çƒe risk is much higher in eyes with more than
about ¯Ç£ve disc areas of non-perfusion on FA (over onethird of eyes) (Fig. 13.27C and D). NVE are more common
than NVD. NVE usually develop at the border of ischaemic retina drained by an occluded vein. ¯Çƒey typically
appear within 6ÔÇô12 months, but may develop at any time.

{ Recurrent vitreous and preretinal haemorrhage and occasionally tractional retinal detachment can occur secondary
to neovascularization.
¯Çü FA demonstrates peripheral and macular ischaemia (capillary nonperfusion, staining of vessel walls, vessel ÔÇÿpruningÔÇÖ ÔÇô small branches
failing to ¯Ç£ll), haemorrhage and oedema with collateral vessels
commonly forming in established cases (Fig. 13.27D). Venous ¯Ç£lling is delayed. In late or subtle cases, FA may be diagnostic.

TIP The commonest underlying cause of a branch retinal
vein occlusion is systemic hypertension, which needs to be
e¯Ç£ectively treated to reduce the risk of late cardiovascular and
cerebrovascular complications.

## Management

¯Çü Systemic arterial hypertension and hyperlipidaemia, if present, should be treated. Further investigations are required in
order to determine the underlying cause in patients younger
than 50 years, in those with bilateral BRVO at presentation or
in those with a history of recurrent BRVO.
¯Çü Observation without intervention is usually indicated if VA is
6/9 or better, or slightly worse but improving.
¯Çü NVE or NVD are generally regarded as an indication for
sector photocoagulation, though some authorities withhold
treatment unless vitreous haemorrhage occurs because early
