intervention does not appear to a¯Ç×ect the visual outcome.
Burns of mildÔÇômoderate intensity, 400ÔÇô500 ╬╝m actual diameter, 0.05 s duration and spaced one burn width apart are
applied to the ischaemic area (Fig. 13.28A). FA can be used
to con¯Ç£rm dubious NV if necessary and to demonstrate ischaemic areas that might be speci¯Ç£cally targeted with laser (Fig.
13.28B). Laser may be combined with 4- to 6-weekly intravitreal anti-VEGF injection.
¯Çü NVI is an indication for urgent sector PRP. Secondary glaucoma is likely to be less aggressive than in CRVO. See Chapter

¯Çü Intravitreal dexamethasone implant. ¯Çƒis confers visual
improvement in BRVO substantially superior to observation alone. ¯Çƒe treatment can be repeated a¯ÇØer 4ÔÇô6 months.
Adverse e¯Ç×ects include an increased glaucoma and cataract
risk. As with anti-VEGF therapy, it may be used alone or in
conjunction with laser.
¯Çü Laser to the macula. With the advent of intravitreal treatment,
macular laser has become less popular and should be regarded
as an adjunctive or combination modality. If VA remains
6/12 or worse a¯ÇØer 3ÔÇô6 months because of macular oedema
that is associated with good central macular perfusion on
FA, laser may be considered: 20ÔÇô100 mild burns of diameter
50ÔÇô100 ╬╝m and duration 0.01ÔÇô0.05 s, concentrating on areas
of leakage on FA. Pulses at the shorter end of the duration
range seem to in¯Çøict less damage to the retina whilst exerting

an approximately comparable therapeutic e¯Ç×ect. Treatment
should not encroach within 0.5 disc diameters of the foveal
centre. Retinal haemorrhages and blood vessels ÔÇô especially
collaterals ÔÇô should not be treated.
¯Çü Subthreshold (micropulse) laser appears to be as e¯Ç×ective as
conventional photocoagulation for macular oedema and in¯Çøicts
considerably less retinal damage. ¯Çƒe onset of action is slower.
¯Çü Intravitreal triamcinolone in a preservative-free preparation
is as e¯Ç×ective as laser in eyes with macular oedema but has a
less sustained e¯Ç×ect and a relatively high rate of cataract formation and IOP elevation.
¯Çü Review in cases that do not require early intervention
should usually take place a¯ÇØer 3 months and then at 3- to
6-monthly intervals for up to 2 years, principally to detect
neovascularization.

TIP OCT allows rapid quantitative analysis of the macula
and has become important in the assessment and subsequent
management of retinal vein occlusion.

## Prognosis

¯Çü BRVO generally has a good visual prognosis, with 50ÔÇô60% of
patients maintaining a ¯Ç£nal VA of better than 6/15, even without treatment.
¯Çü Poor prognostic factors are chronic macular oedema, macular
BRVO and new vessel formation leading to vitreous haemorrhage.
¯Çü About a quarter are le¯ÇØ with a VA of 6/60 or worse.
¯Çü Developing BRVO in one eye slightly increases the risk of a
similar event occurring in the fellow eye.

## Hemiretinal vein occlusion

Hemiretinal vein occlusion is regarded as a variant of CRVO by
some authorities and may be ischaemic or non-ischaemic. It is less
common than either BRVO or CRVO and involves occlusion of
the superior or inferior branch of the central retinal vein (CRV). A
hemispheric occlusion blocks a major branch of the CRV at or near
the optic disc. ¯Çƒe less common hemicentral occlusion involves

one trunk of a dual-trunked CRV that has persisted in the anterior
part of the optic nerve head as a congenital variant.
Prognosis depends on the severity of retinal ischaemia, but
management has been less well studied than BRVO and CRVO.

## Extensive retinal ischaemia implies a risk of neovascular glaucoma

and should be managed in the same way as ischaemic CRVO (see
below), otherwise management, particularly of macular oedema,
may be as for BRVO.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. A sudden onset altitudinal visual ¯Ç£eld defect (Fig.
13.29A).
¯Çü VA reduction varies depending on the site of the occlusion.
¯Çü NVI is more common than in BRVO but less than in CRVO.
¯Çü Fundus shows the features of BRVO, involving the superior
(Fig. 13.29B) or inferior retinal hemisphere (Fig. 13.30A).
NVD may be more common than in either CRVO or BRVO.
¯Çü FA shows masking by haemorrhages, hyper¯Çøuorescence due
to leakage and variable capillary non-perfusion (Fig. 13.30B).

A B

superior ischaemic occlusion showing cotton-wool spots and haemorrhages.

A B

(Courtesy of C Barry.)

## Impending central retinal vein
occlusion

Impending (partial) CRVO on average occurs in younger
patients than those developing more severe occlusion. ¯Çƒe
prognosis is usually good, but a proportion will deteriorate to
ischaemic CRVO. ¯Çƒe distinction between ÔÇÿimpendingÔÇÖ and
mild non-ischaemic CRVO (see below) is not clear and may be
arti¯Ç£cial. Symptoms may be absent or consist of only minor or
transient blurring that is characteristically worse on waking. On
examination, there is mild retinal venous dilatation and tortuosity (Fig. 13.31A), with relatively few small, scattered dot and
blot haemorrhages (Fig. 13.31B). ¯Çƒere may be mild macular
oedema. Fundus auto¯Çøuorescence (FAF) may reveal a fern-like
perivenular appearance (see below) and FA generally demonstrates an impaired retinal circulation. Treatment is empirical,

with lack of an established evidence base. Correcting any predisposing systemic conditions, avoiding dehydration and lowering IOP to improve perfusion have been postulated. Antiplatelet
agents, other anticoagulant measures and haemodilution are of
no proven bene¯Ç£t, but may be considered in some cases.

Non¯Çƒischaemic central retinal vein
occlusion

## Diagnosis

Non-ischaemic CRVO, sometimes called ÔÇÿvenous stasis retinopathyÔÇÖ (also sometimes used to describe ocular ischaemic syndrome

- see later) is more common than the ischaemic form. Around a
third will progress to ischaemic CRVO, o¯ÇØen within months.
¯Çü Symptoms. A sudden painless monocular fall in vision.

¯ÇØB¯Ç× scattered dot and blot haemorrhages.

¯Çü VA is impaired to a variable degree dependent on severity. Eyes
with initially good VA tend to have a good prognosis and vice

not become ischaemic, vision returns to normal or near normal in about 50%.
¯Çü Relative a¯ÇØerent pupillary defect (RAPD). Absent or mild.
¯Çü Fundus. Signs are present in all quadrants.

{ Patchy (or perivenular) ischaemic retinal whitening
(PIRW) in a perivenular pattern at the posterior pole

{ ¯Çƒe main cause of poor vision is chronic macular oedema

{ Disc collaterals are common following CRVO, appearing as a small vascular loop on the optic nerve head.

## They are also known as optociliary shunts or retinochoroidal collaterals and are thought to represent a compensatory circulation in response to impaired nerve

perfusion. Their development is believed to be associated with a markedly decreased risk of neovascularization. They can form in a range of conditions besides

¯Çü OCT is useful in the assessment of CMO, which is o¯ÇØen mild
in a non-ischaemic lesion.

TIP A VA of <6/60 and a RAPD normally indicates signi¯Çƒcant
retinal ischaemia and a poor visual prognosis in a patient with
central retinal vein occlusion.

## Ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion

Ischaemic CRVO is characterized by substantially decreased retinal perfusion with capillary closure and retinal hypoxia. Macular
ischaemia and NVG are the major causes of visual morbidity.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Sudden and severe monocular painless visual
impairment. Occasionally presentation may be with pain, redness and photophobia due to neovascular glaucoma, a prior
reduction in vision having passed unnoticed or been ignored.
¯Çü VA is usually ÔÇÿcounting ¯Ç£ngersÔÇÖ or worse. ¯Çƒe visual prognosis
is generally extremely poor due to macular ischaemia.
¯Çü RAPD is present.
¯Çü NVI. Rubeosis iridis (Fig. 13.33A) develops in about 50% of
eyes, usually between 2 and 4 months (ÔÇÿhundred-day glaucomaÔÇÖ) and there is a high risk of neovascular glaucoma. ¯Çƒe
pupillary margin should be examined at each review prior to
pharmacological mydriasis.
¯Çü Gonioscopy. Angle neovascularization (Fig. 13.33B) may
occur in the absence of neovascularization at the pupillary

{ Optic disc collaterals (opticociliary shunts ÔÇô see under

reduction in the risk of this complication (Fig. 13.34C).
¯Çü FA shows a marked delay in arteriovenous transit time, masking by retinal haemorrhages, extensive areas of capillary nonperfusion and vessel wall staining and leakage (Fig. 13.34D).
¯Çƒe presence of more than 10 disc areas of retinal capillary
non-perfusion is associated with a substantially increased risk
of neovascularization.
¯Çü OCT enables quanti¯Ç£cation of CMO.
¯Çü Electroretinogram (ERG) is depressed and the extent of this
has sometimes been used to assess neovascular risk.

Treatment of the complications of CRVO

¯Çƒe management of non-ischaemic CRVO is generally much less
aggressive than ischaemic CRVO, so it is important to distinguish
the two as far as possible.

{ Intravitreal dexamethasone implant. ¯Çƒe GENEVA trial of

and cataract. Administration within 90 days of CMO onset

study concludes that eyes treated with ranibizumab have

shows an improvement of three or more lines of vision at 1

with an average of two injections of 1 mg triamcinolone,
using a preservative-free preparation developed for intraocular use. ¯Çƒere is a slightly higher rate of IOP elevation
and cataract than with observation. Risk factors for a rise
of IOP of >10 mmHg include: higher dose (4 mg vs 1 mg),
younger age and higher baseline IOP.

{ Laser photocoagulation. Although macular oedema is anatomically improved, laser is not bene¯Ç£cial for visual outcome, except in some younger patients.

{ Patients may still develop neovascular glaucoma, but the
risk is reduced. ¯Çƒe onset of NVG may be delayed by up
to 18 months, so these patients need to be followed at

{ ¯Çƒe management of NVG is discussed in Chapter 11.

{ Ischaemic CRVO. Where possible, patients with ischaemic
CRVO should be seen monthly for 6 months. Subsequent
monitoring should usually be for 2ÔÇô3 years to detect signi¯Ç£cant ischaemia, macular oedema and NVI. Once a disc collateral has developed, the risk of neovascularization reduces.

{ Non-ischaemic CRVO. In an obviously non-ischaemic

months. Structured arrangements for review of test results
should be in place. ¯Çƒe patient should be instructed to

redness, which may indicate neovascular glaucoma and
occasionally in¯Çøammation without rubeosis, should also
be reported. Subsequent review is dependent on the clinical picture and response to treatment, with discharge from

## Prognosis

¯Çƒe main cause for poor vision a¯ÇØer a CRVO is chronic macular
oedema. ¯Çƒe prognosis for ischaemic CRVO is extremely poor due
to macular ischaemia and neovascular glaucoma.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe prognosis is usually determined by the initial visual
acuity:

{ 6/18 or better: the VA is likely to remain so.

{ 6/24ÔÇô6/60: the clinical course is variable and the ¯Ç£nal VA
is di¯ÇÜcult to predict.

{ Worse than 6/60: improvement is unlikely.

{ Investigational treatments include chorioretinal anastomosis, vitrectomy with radial optic neurotomy and local
recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (rtPA) CRV
infusion.
¯Çü Treatment of neovascularization

{ ¯Çƒere is no bene¯Ç£t in performing PRP before the development of iris and angle neovascularization.

{ Frequent follow-up is key and PRP should be performed
without delay in eyes that develop NVI or angle neovascularization. ¯Çƒis initially involves placing 1500ÔÇô2000 burns of
0.05ÔÇô0.1 s duration, spaced one burn width apart, with suf¯Ç£cient energy to produce a moderate reaction, avoiding areas
of haemorrhage (Fig. 13.35D). Treatment may be fractionated
and further photocoagulation is commonly required.

{ Adjunctive intravitreal anti-VEGF injections are commonly administered every 6 weeks until the eye stabilizes,

## Systemic management in retinal vein occlusion

¯Çü Control of systemic risk factors. Although there is no conclusive evidence that vascular mortality is higher in RVO
patients, cardiovascular risk factors are commonly identified after a retinal vein occlusion and should be dealt with.

## As well as conferring systemic benefit this may also reduce

the risk of recurrent RVO. Smoking should be actively
discouraged.
¯Çü Antiplatelet therapy. ¯Çƒe role of aspirin or alternative antiplatelet agents in reducing the risk of further retinal venous
occlusion is unclear. ¯Çƒis medication is not usually prescribed
unless there is a clear systemic indication.
¯Çü Hormone replacement therapy (HRT). ¯Çƒe risk of HRT
remains unde¯Ç£ned. Most authorities would avoid continuing
or commencing oestrogen-containing HRT following RVO.
Appropriate expert advice should be obtained. ¯Çƒe presence of

## Papillophlebitis

Papillophlebitis is an uncommon, poorly de¯Ç£ned condition that
typically a¯Ç×ects individuals under the age of 50 years who may have
a higher prevalence of hypertension and diabetes. Some or all cases
may simply be a variant of CRVO occurring in younger people,
though optic disc swelling or in¯Çøammation of the central retinal
vein have been speculated as the initiating event in at least some
instances. ¯Çƒere is likely to be some diagnostic overlap with diabetic

papillopathy. Papillophlebitis typically causes only mild to moderate
reduction of vision with no RAPD and has a generally good prognosis. Disc oedema is the dominant ¯Ç£nding and retinal haemorrhages
and other signs such as cotton-wool spots are predominantly peripapillary and con¯Ç£ned to the posterior pole (Fig. 13.36). CMO may
be present and posterior or anterior segment neovascularization have
been reported occasionally in concert with capillary non-perfusion
on FA. It may be bilateral, when raised intracranial pressure must be
ruled out. Investigation should probably be as for CRVO in a young
individual. Distinction from diabetic papillopathy may be di¯ÇÜcult.
Treatment with intravitreal anti-VEGF or steroid has been reported.

RETINAL ARTERIAL OCCLUSIVE
DISEASE

## Aetiology

¯Çƒe outer retina is supplied by the ciliary arteries via the choriocapillaris and the inner retina by the central retinal artery (CRA).

¯Çƒe ophthalmic artery gives rise to both the CRA ÔÇô its ¯Ç£rst branch

- and the ciliary arteries (see Fig. 19.5A). ¯Çƒe latter also supply the
anterior segment via the rectus muscles.
¯Çü Atherosclerosis-related embolism and thrombosis are
thought to be responsible for the majority of cases of retinal
artery occlusion, but the proportion of cases due to each of
these is unknown. ¯Çƒe origin of emboli is most commonly
an atheromatous carotid plaque and since the ophthalmic artery is the ¯Ç£rst branch of the internal carotid artery,
embolic material has an easy route to the eye. Emboli can
be refractile yellowÔÇôwhite cholesterol (Hollenhorst) plaques
(Fig. 13.37A), greyish elongated ¯Ç£brin-platelet aggregates
(Fig. 13.37B and C), non-scintillating white calci¯Ç£c particles
(Fig. 13.37D) and, rarely, vegetations from bacterial endocarditis, cardiac myxomatous material and fat. Other cardiac
causes include arrhythmia and mitral valve prolapse.
¯Çü In¯Ç£ammation in or around the vessel wall (e.g. giant cell
arteritis ÔÇô GCA, systemic lupus erythematosus, granulomatosis with polyangiitis, polyarteritis nodosa), vasospasm (e.g.
migraine) and systemic hypotension contribute in a minority.
¯Çü ¯Çƒrombophilic disorders that may be associated with retinal
artery occlusion (one-third of young patients) include hyperhomocysteinaemia, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome,
inherited defects of various natural anticoagulants and sickle
cell haemoglobinopathy.
¯Çü Susac syndrome, a rare immune-mediated vasculo-occlusive
disease of young people, consists of the triad of branch retinal
artery occlusion, encephalopathy and sensorineural hearing loss.
Women predominate and both eyes are usually a¯Ç×ected. Retinal
microaneurysms are found in 70% of a¯Ç×ected individuals. Arteriolar wall hyper¯Çøuorescence remote from the BRAO on FA is
typical of the condition. Treatment of Susac syndrome involves
immunosuppressive and immunomodulatory medication.

## Systemic assessment

## Following diagnosis and initial evaluation by an ophthalmologist
many elements of the assessment may be carried out by a specialist

stroke team. Urgent specialist vascular evaluation is essential
following a retinal arterial event, including amaurosis fugax.
¯Çƒe risk of a stroke is relatively high in the ¯Ç£rst few days a¯ÇØer a
transient ischaemic attack (TIA) and occurs in 25% at 3 years. ¯Çƒe
detection of atrial ¯Ç£brillation is of particular importance as admission for anticoagulation may be indicated.

TIP Urgent specialist cardiovascular evaluation is essential in
a patient with a retinal artery occlusion in order to reduce the
risk of a stroke or a similar event occurring in the fellow eye.

## All patients

## Many or most patients will already be aware of vascular risk factors

and/or disease.
¯Çü Smoking should be enquired about.
¯Çü Symptoms of GCA (1ÔÇô2% of central retinal artery occlusion ÔÇô
CRAO) such as headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness,
limb girdle pain, weight loss and existing polymyalgia rheumatica (see Ch. 19). GCA is extremely unlikely under 55ÔÇô60
years. It constitutes an ophthalmic emergency.
¯Çü Pulse should be palpated to detect arrhythmia, particularly
atrial ¯Ç£brillation.
¯Çü Blood pressure, checking for systemic hypertension.
¯Çü Cardiac auscultation for a murmur.
¯Çü Carotid auscultation is of limited value as the absence of a
bruit does not exclude signi¯Ç£cant stenosis.
¯Çü ECG to detect arrhythmia and other cardiac disease.
¯Çü ESR or PV and CRP to identify possible GCA.
¯Çü Other blood tests include FBC (platelets may be raised in
GCA), glucose, lipids and urea and electrolytes, the latter to
exclude derangement including dehydration.
¯Çü Carotid duplex scanning is a non-invasive screening
test involving a combination of high-resolution real-time
ultrasonography with Doppler ¯Çøow analysis. If signi¯Ç£cant
stenosis is present, surgical management may be considered.

## Selected patients

¯Çƒe following additional tests can be considered on a targeted basis
in some patients, particularly if younger and with no known cardiovascular risk factors, or if there is an atypical clinical picture
(Fig. 13.38A and B).
¯Çü Further carotid imaging (see Ch. 19).
¯Çü Cranial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) or computed
tomography (CT) may be indicated to rule out intracranial or
orbital pathology.
¯Çü Echocardiography. Usually performed in young patients or
if there is a specific indication such as a history of rheumatic fever, known cardiac valvular disease, or intravenous
drug use.
¯Çü Chest X-ray. Sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, le¯ÇØ ventricular hypertrophy in hypertension.
¯Çü 24-hour ECG to exclude intermittent arrhythmia.
¯Çü Additional blood tests

{ Fasting plasma homocysteine level to exclude
hyperhomocysteinaemia.

## Central retinal artery occlusion

¯Çü Symptoms. Sudden profound loss of vision, painless except in
GCA.
¯Çü VA is severely reduced except if a cilioretinal artery supplying a critical macular area preserves central vision (see below).
Absence of light perception usually indicates either GCA or
ophthalmic artery occlusion. ¯Çƒe prognosis is poor in all cases
unless recovery occurs in the ¯Ç£rst few hours.
¯Çü RAPD is profound, sometimes total (amaurotic pupil).
¯Çü Fundus shows similar changes to BRAO but involving all retinal quadrants.

{ Syphilis serology.

{ Blood cultures.

## Amaurosis fugax

## Amaurosis fugax is characterized by transient painless monocular

loss of vision, o¯ÇØen described as a curtain coming down over the
eye. ¯Çƒe Amaurosis Fugax Study Group divides the causes into ¯Ç£ve
categories (embolic, hemodynamic, ocular, neurologic and idiopathic), but in clinical practice it is typically used to refer to transient visual loss of embolic origin. It is common for patients to be
unaware of whether transient unilateral visual loss a¯Ç×ects one eye
or the ipsilateral hemi¯Ç£eld of both (the latter indicating cerebral
rather than more anterior ischaemia). Embolic visual loss, which
may be complete, usually lasts a few minutes. Recovery is generally in the same pattern as the loss, although usually more gradual.

## Frequency of attacks may vary from several times a day to once

every few months. ¯Çƒe attacks may sometimes be accompanied
by an ipsilateral cerebral TIA, with contralateral neurological features. Investigation and systemic management of embolic-pattern
amaurosis fugax is the same as that of retinal arterial occlusion and
should be undertaken urgently because of the high risk of stroke.

## Branch retinal artery occlusion

¯Çü Symptoms. Sudden and profound painless altitudinal or sectoral visual ¯Ç£eld loss. Branch retinal artery occlusion (BRAO)
can sometimes go unnoticed, particularly if central vision is
spared.
¯Çü VA is variable. In patients where central vision is severely compromised, the prognosis is commonly poor unless the obstruction is relieved within a few hours (see below).
¯Çü RAPD is o¯ÇØen present.
¯Çü Fundus signs may be subtle.

{ Attenuation of arteries and veins with sludging and segmentation of the blood column (ÔÇÿcattle truckingÔÇÖ/ÔÇÖbox-carÔÇÖ
appearance).

{ Cloudy white oedematous (ground glass) retina corresponding to the area of ischaemia (Fig. 13.39A and C).

{ One or more occluding emboli may be seen, especially at
bifurcation points (see Fig. 13.39A).

{ ¯Çƒe a¯Ç×ected artery is likely to remain attenuated. Occasionally, recanalization may leave absent ophthalmoscopic
signs.

{ ¯Çƒe orange re¯Çøex from the intact choroid stands out at the
thin foveola, in contrast to the surrounding pale retina, giving rise to a ÔÇÿcherry-red spotÔÇÖ appearance (Fig. 13.40A and B).

{ ¯Çƒe peripapillary retina may appear especially swollen and
opaque.

{ An occasional small haemorrhage is not unusual.

{ Emboli are visible in 20%, when Nd:YAG embolysis may
be considered (see below).

{ In eyes with a cilioretinal artery part of the macula will
remain of normal colour (Fig. 13.40C).

{ Retinal signs can sometimes be subtle. Retinal oedema
may take several hours to develop.

{ Over a few days to weeks the retinal cloudiness and ÔÇÿcherryred spotÔÇÖ gradually disappear although the arteries remain
attenuated. Later signs include optic atrophy, vessel sheathing and patchy inner retinal atrophy and RPE changes.

{ Around 2% of eyes with CRAO develop retinal or disc
neovascularization.

{ Rubeosis iridis may occur in up to about 1 in 5 eyes, typically earlier than in CRVO (4ÔÇô5 weeks compared with 3
months, though sometimes later) and along with very poor
vision may indicate ophthalmic artery occlusion.
¯Çü OCT may show a highly re¯Çøective embolic plaque within the
super¯Ç£cial optic nerve head.
¯Çü FA shows a variable delay in arterial ¯Ç£lling and masking of
background choroidal ¯Çøuorescence by retinal oedema. A
patent cilioretinal artery will ¯Ç£ll during the early phase (Fig.
13.40D).
¯Çü Electroretinography may be helpful to establish the diagnosis
if in doubt, particularly to distinguish from optic nerve disease, typically when signs are subtle. A diminished b-wave is
present.
¯Çü Ophthalmic review. The patient should be seen by an
ophthalmologist after 3ÔÇô4 weeks and a minimum of

## Cilioretinal artery occlusion

A cilioretinal artery is present in about one-third of eyes, providing the central macula with a second arterial supply derived
from the posterior ciliary circulation. Its main importance is
that when present it may facilitate preservation of central vision
following central retinal artery occlusion, provided the fovea is
supplied.
¯Çü Isolated. ¯Çƒis is rare (Fig. 13.41A and B). It may occur in
young patients with an associated systemic vasculitis.

¯Çü Combined with CRVO. ¯Çƒis (Fig. 13.41C) is not uncommon,
but occlusion is transient and the prognosis is better than in
isolated cilioretinal artery occlusion.
¯Çü Combined with anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (Fig.
13.41D), typically a¯Ç×ects patients with GCA and carries a very
poor prognosis.

## Treatment of acute retinal artery
occlusion

## Retinal artery occlusion is an emergency because it causes irreversible

visual loss unless the retinal circulation can be re-established before
the development of retinal infarction. ¯Çƒeoretically, timely dislodgement of thrombus or emboli may ameliorate subsequent visual loss. In
primate studies, partial recovery is possible if the ischaemia is reversed
within 4 hours. ¯Çƒe time when irreversible retinal damage occurs with
no visual recovery is not known in humans, but has been postulated to

of background ¯Ç×uorescence by retinal oedema but normal perfusion of a sector of retina
in the eye in ¯ÇØC¯Ç×.

be about 6 hours in studies determining the e¯Ç×ect of tissue plasminogen activator on thrombolysis. Unfortunately, treatment is usually
not e¯Ç×ective. In two-thirds the ¯Ç£nal VA is worse than 6/120 and only
one-¯Ç£¯ÇØh are le¯ÇØ with a VA of 6/12 or better. ¯Çƒe various therapeutic
options should be discussed before use, including the lack of evidence
for clear bene¯Ç£t and the associated risks. ¯Çƒe following can be tried in
patients with an occlusion of less than 6 hoursÔÇÖ duration at presentation though evidence of bene¯Ç£t is limited:
¯Çü Ocular massage using a three-mirror contact lens (allows direct
artery visualization). ¯Çƒe aim is to mechanically collapse the arterial lumen and cause prompt changes in arterial ¯Çøow, improving
perfusion and potentially dislodging an embolus or thrombus.
One described method consists of positive pressure for 10ÔÇô15 seconds followed by release, continued for 3ÔÇô5 minutes. Self-massage
through closed eyelids can be continued by the patient.

¯Çü Anterior chamber paracentesis using a 27-gauge needle to
withdraw 0.1ÔÇô0.2 ml of aqueous has been advocated by some
authorities. Povidone-iodine 5% and topical antibiotic are
instilled a few minutes prior to the procedure, with a short
course of antibiotic a¯ÇØerwards. It may be prudent for ocular
massage to be avoided following paracentesis.
¯Çü Topical apraclonidine 1%, timolol 0.5% and intravenous acetazolamide 500 mg to achieve a more sustained lowering of
intraocular pressure.
¯Çü ÔÇÿRebreathingÔÇÖ into a paper bag in order to elevate blood carbon dioxide and respiratory acidosis has been advocated, as
this may promote vasodilatation. Similarly, breathing a high
oxygen (95%) and carbon dioxide (5%) mixture, ÔÇÿcarbogenÔÇÖ,
has been advocated for a possible dual e¯Ç×ect of retarding ischaemia and vasodilatation.

540 Retinal Arterial Occlusive Disease

A B

C D

masking by retinal oedema; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× combined with central retinal vein occlusion; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× combined
with anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy. (Courtesy of C Mishra ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs A and B.)

¯Çü Transluminal Nd:YAG laser embolysis/embolectomy has
been advocated for BRAO or CRAO where an occluding
embolus is visible. Although experience with this technique
is limited, surprisingly good ¯Ç£nal visual results have been
reported. A variable number of shots of 0.5ÔÇô1mJ or higher
(to a maximum of 2.4mJ) are applied directly to the embolus
using a fundus contact lens. Embolectomy has been said to
occur if the embolus is ejected into the vitreous via a hole in
the arteriole. ¯Çƒe main complication is subretinal and vitreous
haemorrhage in approximately half, which may be curtailed
with pressure on the globe.
¯Çü Thrombolysis. Extrapolating from successful treatment of
stroke and myocardial infarction, various strategies have
been used to deliver thrombolytic agents to the ophthalmic
artery, including local arterial (internal carotid and ophthalmic) and intravenous infusion. On the basis of observational data and in the absence of other effective treatment,
patients with acute CRAO who are seen within 4.5 hours of

onset of visual loss could be considered for treatment with
intravenous tissue plasminogen activator (tPA). The commonest regimen used is intravenous (IV) alteplase (0.9mg/
kg, with 10% given over 1 minute and the remainder over
59 minutes). Patients undergoing this treatment should be
fully informed of the benefits and risks of treatment. A large
multicentre trial of local intraocular thrombolysis with
recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (rtPA) showed no
benefit over conservative treatment that included isovolaemic haemodilution. This finding may reflect the rapidity
of retinal dysfunction that occurs secondary to ischaemia
and perhaps the resistance to dissolution of embolic, versus
thrombotic obstruction.

TIP To reduce the risk of stroke and ischaemic heart disease,
the systemic factors responsible for a central retinal artery
occlusion need to be determined and treated.

## Systemic management following retinal
arterial occlusion

¯Çü The risk of stroke is relatively high in the first few days
following retinal artery occlusion or amaurosis fugax.
Urgent referral to a specialist stroke clinic is imperative.
Of those with a CRAO who are sent for urgent systemic
evaluation, 30% will have severe carotid disease, coincidental stroke or extreme hypertension and 20% will have
simultaneous myocardial infarction or critical heart disease. Almost 95% will subsequently need a change in their
systemic medication and 25% will require urgent surgical
intervention.
¯Çü General risk factors as discussed above should be addressed
and smoking should be discontinued. Urgent referral to an
appropriate physician is mandatory for signi¯Ç£cant cardiac
arrhythmia.
¯Çü Antiplatelet therapy is commenced provided there are no
contraindications. An immediate loading dose of 600 mg may
be given. Alternative/additional agents include dipyridamole
and clopidogrel. If ¯Ç£brinolysis (see above) is being considered,
this should be discussed with a physician prior to starting antiplatelet treatment.
¯Çü Oral anticoagulation (e.g. warfarin) may be prescribed for
some patients, particularly those with atrial ¯Ç£brillation.
¯Çü Carotid endarterectomy may be indicated in patients with
symptomatic stenosis greater than 70%.

## Asymptomatic retinal embolus

It is not uncommon to identify a retinal embolus on routine examination of an asymptomatic older patient. ¯Çƒis indicates a substantially increased risk of stroke and ischaemic heart disease and
management should consist of evaluation and treatment of risk
factors discussed above. A higher threshold for carotid surgery is
appropriate.

OCULAR ISCHAEMIC SYNDROME

## Introduction

Ocular ischaemic syndrome (OIS) results from chronic ocular
hypoperfusion secondary to severe (more than 90%) ipsilateral
atherosclerotic carotid stenosis. It typically a¯Ç×ects older patients
and may be associated with diabetes, hypertension, cardio- and
cerebrovascular disease. ¯Çƒe male:female ratio is about 2:1. Fiveyear mortality is around 40%, most frequently from cardiac disease. OIS, along with non-ischaemic CRVO, is sometimes termed
ÔÇÿvenous stasis retinopathyÔÇÖ but it may be prudent to avoid this
term.

## Diagnosis

OIS is unilateral in 80%. ¯Çƒe signs are variable and may be subtle
such that the condition is missed or misdiagnosed.

¯Çü Symptoms. Gradual loss of vision over weeks or months,
although occasionally loss may be sudden or intermittent
(amaurosis fugax). Ocular and periocular pain may also be
present (40%). Patients may notice unusually persistent a¯ÇØerimages, or worsening of vision with sudden exposure to bright
light (ÔÇÿbright light amaurosis fugaxÔÇÖ), with slow adaptation.
¯Çƒe prognosis for vision is o¯ÇØen very poor, though patients
with better acuity at presentation are more likely to retain this.
About 25% will deteriorate to light perception by the end of
1 year.
¯Çü Anterior segment

{ In diabetic patients the retinopathy may be more severe
ipsilateral to carotid stenosis.
¯Çü FA. Delayed choroidal ¯Ç£lling and prolonged arteriovenous
transit is the major feature. Non-perfusion, vessel wall staining
and retinal oedema may also be evident (Fig. 13.42BÔÇôD).
¯Çü Carotid imaging may involve duplex ultrasonography, digital
subtraction angiography, MR or CT angiography.

## Management

¯Çü Anterior segment in¯Ç£ammation is treated with topical steroid and a mydriatic as appropriate.
¯Çü Neovascular glaucoma is managed medically or surgically
(see Ch. 11).
¯Çü Proliferative retinopathy can be treated with PRP although
the outcome is considerably less certain than in proliferative
diabetic retinopathy. Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents may be
bene¯Ç£cial.
¯Çü Macular oedema may respond to intravitreal steroid or antiVEGF agents, or to carotid surgery.
¯Çü Carotid surgery. Endarterectomy or stenting may be performed to reduce the risk of stroke. It may be bene¯Ç£cial for
proliferative retinopathy and neovascular glaucoma and may
help to stabilize vision. Endarterectomy cannot be performed
where there is total obstruction, and in this situation extracranialÔÇôintracranial arterial bypass surgery is sometimes carried
out. It should be noted that an increase in ocular perfusion following surgery can sometimes be associated with a rise in IOP.

{ Di¯Ç×use episcleral injection and corneal oedema.

{ Aqueous ¯Çøare with few cells (ischaemic pseudo-iritis).

{ Iris atrophy with a mid-dilated, poorly reacting pupil.

{ Rubeosis iridis is common, developing in up to 90% and
o¯ÇØen progresses to neovascular glaucoma. ¯Çƒe IOP may
remain low due to poor ocular perfusion.

{ Cataract in advanced cases.
¯Çü Fundus

{ Venous dilatation, arteriolar narrowing, deep round and
¯Çøame haemorrhages and occasionally disc oedema (Fig.
13.42A) and cotton-wool spots.

{ Proliferative retinopathy with NVD and occasionally NVE.

{ Spontaneous arterial pulsation, most pronounced near
the optic disc, is present in most cases or may be easily
induced by exerting gentle pressure on the globe (digital
ophthalmodynamometry).

{ Macular oedema can occur.

542 Hypertensive Eye Disease

A B

C D

¯ÇØB¯Ç× early FA (20¯Çøs) and ¯ÇØC¯Ç× FA early phase (35¯Çøs) showing delayed choroidal ¯Çƒlling and prolonged arteriovenous transit; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× FA late phase (5¯Çømin) showing disc and perivascular hyper¯Ç×uorescence, with spotty hyper¯Ç×uorescence at the posterior pole due to leakage.

Surgery tends to be of greater bene¯Ç£t when performed before
the onset of severe ocular ischaemia.
¯Çü Investigation and management of cardiovascular risk factors in conjunction with the appropriate medical specialists is
essential. OIS is occasionally the only manifestation of marked
systemic vascular disease. Full investigation should be carried
out, broadly similar to that for retinal arterial occlusion.

HYPERTENSIVE EYE DISEASE

## Retinopathy

Systemic arterial hypertension is common. In addition to heart,
kidneys and brain, the eye is often affected by hypertension.
Ocular effects can be observed in the retina, choroid and optic
nerve. The primary response of the retinal arterioles to systemic hypertension is vasoconstriction, which is less marked
in older individuals because of involutional sclerosis conferring
increased rigidity. Arteriolosclerosis refers to hardening and

loss of elasticity of small vessel walls, manifested most obviously
by arteriovenous (AV) nipping (nicking) at crossing points. The
presence of this sign makes it probable that hypertension has
been present for many years, even if the BP is currently controlled. Mild AV changes may be seen in the absence of hypertension. In sustained hypertension the inner bloodÔÇôretinal
barrier is disrupted, increased vascular permeability leading to
flame-shaped retinal haemorrhages and oedema.
¯Çü Grade 1. Mild generalized retinal arteriolar narrowing (Fig.
13.43A).
¯Çü Grade 2. Focal arteriolar narrowing (Fig. 13.43B) and arteriovenous nipping (Fig. 13.43C). A ÔÇÿcopper wiringÔÇÖ opaci¯Ç£ed
appearance of arteriolar walls may be seen (Fig. 13.43D).
¯Çü Grade 3. Grade 2 plus retinal haemorrhages (dot, blot, ¯Çøame),
exudates (chronic retinal oedema may result in the deposition
of hard exudates around the fovea as a ÔÇÿmacular starÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig.
13.43E) and cotton-wool spots.
¯Çü Grade 4. Severe grade 3 plus optic disc swelling, which is a
marker of ÔÇÿmalignantÔÇÖ hypertension (Fig. 13.43F).

{ Reduced retinal arteriolar calibre (see Fig. 13.43A) is an

reported in some studies. Numerous other causes of

described, notably conditions associated with high and
low vascular flow.

## Choroidopathy

## Hypertensive choroidopathy is rare but may occur as the result of an

acute hypertensive crisis (accelerated hypertension) in young adults.
¯Çü Siegrist streaks are ¯Çøecks arranged linearly along choroidal
vessels and are indicative of ¯Ç£brinoid necrosis associated with
malignant hypertension (Fig. 13.45A).
¯Çü Elschnig spots are focal choroidal infarcts seen as small black
spots surrounded by yellow haloes (Fig. 13.45B).
¯Çü Exudative retinal detachment, sometimes bilateral, may
occur in acute severe hypertension such as that associated with
toxaemia of pregnancy.

PARACENTRAL ACUTE MIDDLE
MACULOPATHY (PAMM)

PAMM is an uncommon condition of the macula which occurs
mainly in individuals aged 60ÔÇô75 years, presumably as a complication of retinal microvasculopathy or systemic vascular disease. In
younger individuals no cause may be found. ¯Çƒe patient notices
a sudden positive scotoma close to ¯Ç£xation in one eye and has
no other visual or ocular symptoms. Fundus examination reveals
subtle whitish parafoveal lesions within the retina. When an OCT
scan is undertaken in the acute phase a placoid, hyperre¯Çøective
band is seen at the level of the inner nuclear layer, with a normal
outer retina (Fig. 13.46). Partial resolution is the most common
outcome, although full resolution may occasionally occur. ¯Çƒe
main di¯Ç×erential is acute macular neuroretinopathy, which can be
excluded with an OCT (see Ch. 12). ¯Çƒere is no treatment. Systemic diseases like arterial hypertension and diabetes need to be
excluded.

SICKLE-CELL RETINOPATHY

## Sickling haemoglobinopathies

## Sickling haemoglobinopathies are caused by one or more abnormal
haemoglobins that induce red blood cells to adopt an anomalous

shape (Fig. 13.47) under conditions of physiological stress such as
hypoxia and acidosis, with resultant vascular occlusion. Mutant haemoglobin variants S and C can be found in combination with the
normal adult haemoglobin A or, less commonly, with other mutant
haemoglobin variants. Retinopathy can occur in sickle-cell disease
(homozygous for mutant haemoglobin S, i.e. SS), sickle-cell C disease (SC ÔÇô the most likely to develop severe retinopathy) and sicklecell thalassaemia disease. It is rare in patients with sickle-cell trait
(SA ÔÇô found in 10% of African-Americans), unless there is other coexisting systemic disease such as diabetes or an in¯Çøammatory disorder. Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (CAI) should be avoided in these
disorders as they can precipitate sickling and vascular occlusion.

## Anterior segment

¯Çü Conjunctiva. Dark red corkscrew- or comma-shaped vessels
that are typically transient.
¯Çü Iris. Patches of ischaemic atrophy, o¯ÇØen extending from the
pupillary edge to the collarette and occasionally rubeosis.
¯Çü Hyphaema may be spontaneous or follow minor trauma.
Careful IOP control (avoiding CAI) is critical to reduce the
risk of RVO in the presence of a hyphaema.

Non¯Çƒproliferative retinopathy

¯Çü Venous changes. Tortuosity (see Fig. 13.44) is very common
and is thought to be due to peripheral arteriovenous shunting.
¯Çü Optic disc ÔÇÿsign of sicklingÔÇÖ. Dark red blots on the disc surface
due to small vessel occlusion.

in a 74-year old severely hypertensive woman. OCT in the
acute phase shows a placoid, hyperre¯Ç×ective band at the
level of the INL (arrow), sparing the outer retina. (Courtesy
of G Kontos.)

¯Çü ÔÇÿSalmon patchesÔÇÖ. OrangeÔÇôred mid-peripheral super¯Ç£cial
intraretinal haemorrhages (Fig. 13.48A) that may break
through to become preretinal or subretinal. ¯Çƒe initiating
event is thought to be a vascular occlusion. Salmon patches
resolve to leave schisis cavities containing refractile deposits,
ÔÇÿblack sunburstsÔÇÖ (see below) when the RPE is su¯ÇÜciently stimulated, or a combination lesion.
¯Çü Black sunbursts. Patches of peripheral RPE hyperplasia
and chorioretinal atrophy (see Fig. 13.48A) that evolve from
some salmon patches. The extent and morphology of pigmentation is variable, but an outer pale band is generally
present.
¯Çü Arteriolar changes. Occlusions can involve branch, central or
macular vessels (Fig. 13.48B) and if seen acutely may bene¯Ç£t
from measures such as 100% oxygen and exchange transfusion. ÔÇÿSilver wiringÔÇÖ of arterioles in the peripheral retina signi¯Ç£es previously occluded vessels. Corkscrewing of peripheral
vessels may also be seen.
¯Çü Macular (retinal) depression sign. An oval depression in the
temporal macular retina due to retinal thinning following arteriolar occlusion, with irregularity of the light re¯Çøex. Vascular
anomalies such as microaneurysms and epiretinal membranes
may occur.
¯Çü Peripheral areas of whitening or darkening.
¯Çü Angioid streaks (see Ch. 14) occur in up to 6%.

## Proliferative retinopathy

## Diagnosis

¯Çƒe development of proliferative retinopathy is usually insidious,
with no symptoms unless vitreous haemorrhage or retinal detachment occurs.

546 Thalassaemia Retinopathy

of a patient with homozygous (HbSS) sickle-cell anaemia.

¯Çü Stage 1. Peripheral arteriolar occlusion.
¯Çü Stage 2. Peripheral arteriovenous anastomosis (Fig. 13.49A)
proximal to non-perfused areas.
¯Çü Stage 3. ÔÇÿSea fanÔÇÖ neovascularization (Fig. 13.49B) develops
at the edge of perfused retina, usually with a single supplying
arteriole and a single draining venule. Disc neovascularization
may rarely occur.
¯Çü Stage 4. Vitreous haemorrhage from the NV.
¯Çü Stage 5. Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment caused by a
retinal break associated with extensive ¯Ç£brovascular proliferation (Fig. 13.49C). Tractional detachment may also occur (Fig.
13.49D).
¯Çü FA in stage 3 shows ¯Ç£lling of sea fans and peripheral capillary
non-perfusion (Fig. 13.49E) followed by leakage from the NV
(Fig. 13.49F). Wide-¯Ç£eld imaging is especially suited to evaluation of this condition.

TIP Avoid the use of carbonic anhydrase inhibitors in patients
with sickel-cell anaemia as these drugs can cause sickling and
vascular occlusion.

## Treatment

¯Çü Observation if vitreous haemorrhage has not occurred, particularly in middle-aged and older patients. Many neovascular
complexes involute spontaneously as a result of auto-infarction
or ¯Ç£brotic strangulation, subsequently appearing as greyish
¯Ç£brovascular lesions.
¯Çü Laser or cryotherapy ablation of peripheral non-perfused retina is probably the optimal approach, though ablation of neovascularization may also be used. Ablation of feeder vessels is
now rarely performed due to a high incidence of subsequent
macular neovascularization.
¯Çü Vitreoretinal surgery may be required for tractional retinal
detachment and/or persistent vitreous haemorrhage. Caution
is required as anterior segment ischaemia, potentially severe, is
very common following scleral explant application.

A

B

hyperplasia (ÔÇÿblack sunburstÔÇÖ) and preretinal haemorrhages (ÔÇÿsalmon patchÔÇÖ) (arrow); ¯ÇØB¯Ç× FA showing macular ischaemia.

THALASSAEMIA RETINOPATHY

Thalassaemia is a common genetic disorder in which a mutation gives rise to abnormal haemoglobin with a consequent failure of normal red blood cell maturation. Ocular involvement
occurs in patients with thalassaemia major and thalassaemia
intermedia. Changes are caused by the disease itself as well as
treatment with blood transfusions and iron chelating agents
such as desferrioxamine. A major mechanism is tissue deposition of iron (siderosis) from the lysing of abnormal red cells.

## The ocular features predominantly affect the posterior segment

and are non-proliferative, though vitreous haemorrhage has
been reported. Visual dysfunction is rarely severe. Manifestations include cataract, a smooth featureless iris, vascular tortuosity, angioid streaks, optic neuropathy and pigmentary retinal
mottling, including pattern dystrophy-like macular changes.

## The fundus appearance may resemble that of pseudoxanthoma

elasticum.

vessels and extensive peripheral retinal capillary non-perfusion; ¯ÇØF¯Ç× FA late phase showing
leakage from the new vessels. (Courtesy of Ken K Nischal ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

548 Retinopathy of Prematurity

RETINOPATHY OF PREMATURITY

## Introduction

Retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) is a condition that a¯Ç×ects the
developing retina of premature low birthweight infants and is one
of the commonest causes of childhood blindness worldwide. ¯Çƒe
exact cause of ROP is unclear, but early exposure to high ambient
oxygen concentrations appears to be a key risk factor. With new
methods of monitoring oxygen levels in premature babies, oxygen
use as a major risk factor has diminished in importance. Systemic
illness is an additional risk factor (for example: anaemia, sepsis
and low vitamin E levels). In the early phase of ROP development
vessel growth is retarded by hyperoxia, but subsequently retinal
hypoxia promotes anomalous vascularization. ¯Çƒe retina has no
blood vessels until the fourth month of gestation, when vascular complexes grow from optic disc hyaloid vessels towards the
periphery. ¯Çƒe nasal retina is normally fully vascularized a¯ÇØer 8
months of gestation, the temporal periphery at or by 1 month a¯ÇØer
delivery. Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is believed
to play an important role in the vascularization process. In lowincome countries there has been a rise in the incidence of ROP in
recent years, which corresponds to the establishment of intensive
neonatal treatment regimens in premature babies who would not
previously have survived.

TIP Babies born before 32 weeksÔÇÖ gestational age or
weighing less than 1500 g should be screened for retinopathy
of prematurity.

## Active disease

¯Çƒe clinical ¯Ç£ndings in ROP are described as below according to
the 2021 International Classi¯Ç£cation of Retinopathy of Prematurity (ICROP, third edition).

## Location

Concentric zones centred on the optic disc are described (Fig.
13.50). ¯Çƒe location of the most posterior retinal vascularization or
ROP lesion denotes the zone. ¯Çƒe term ÔÇÿnotchÔÇÖ is used to describe
an incursion by the ROP lesion of 1ÔÇô2 clock hours into a more
posterior zone.
¯Çü Zone I is bounded by an imaginary circle, the radius of which
is twice the distance from the disc to the centre of the macula.
With a 28-dioptre binocular indirect lens, only zone I is seen if
any part of the optic nerve head is visible.
¯Çü Zone II extends concentrically from the edge of zone I, with
a radius that extends from the centre of the disc to the nasal
ora serrata.
¯Çü Zone III consists of a residual temporal crescent anterior to
zone II.

## Staging of acute disease

¯Çƒis describes the abnormal vascular response at the junction of
immature avascular peripheral and vascularized posterior retina.

## Staging for the eye as a whole is determined by the most severe

manifestation.
¯Çü Stage 1 (demarcation line) is a thin, ¯Çøat, tortuous, grey-white
line running roughly parallel with the ora serrata. It is more
prominent in the temporal periphery. ¯Çƒere is abnormal
branching or ÔÇÿarcadingÔÇÖ of vessels leading up to the line (Fig.
13.51A).
¯Çü Stage 2 (ridge) arises in the region of the demarcation line, has
height and width and extends above the plane of the retina.

## Blood vessels enter the ridge and small isolated neovascular

tu¯ÇØs may be seen posterior to it (Fig. 13.51B).
¯Çü Stage 3 (extraretinal fibrovascular proliferation) extends
from the ridge into the vitreous. It is continuous with the
posterior aspect of the ridge, causing a ragged appearance
as the proliferation becomes more extensive (Fig. 13.51C).
The severity of stage 3 can be subdivided into mild, moderate and severe depending on the extent of extraretinal
fibrous tissue infiltrating the vitreous. The highest incidence of this stage is around the post-conceptual age of 35
weeks.
¯Çü Stage 4 (partial retinal detachment) is divided into extrafoveal
(stage 4A ÔÇô Fig. 13.51D) and foveal (stage 4B). ¯Çƒe detachment is generally concave and circumferentially orientated. In
progressive cases the ¯Ç£brous tissue continues to contract, and
the detachment increases in height and extends anteriorly and
posteriorly.
¯Çü Stage 5 refers to total retinal detachment.
¯Çü ÔÇÿPlusÔÇÖ disease signi¯Ç£es a tendency to progression and is characterized by dilatation and tortuosity of blood vessels (Fig.
13.51E) involving at least two quadrants of the posterior fundus. Other features include failure of the pupil to dilate and
vitreous haze. ÔÇÿPre-plusÔÇÖ disease is also described.
¯Çü Aggressive ROP (ÔÇÿrushÔÇÖ disease) describes a severe, rapidly
progressing form of ROP located in zones I or II. It is characterized by its posterior location, prominence of plus disease
and ill-de¯Ç£ned nature of the retinopathy. It is uncommon, but
if untreated usually progresses to stage 5, sometimes within a
few days.
¯Çü Extent of disease is de¯Ç£ned as 12 sectors using clock-hour
designations.

## Type

## Treatment guidelines at most centres have been revised based

on the Early Treatment of Retinopathy of Prematurity (ETROP)
clinical trial. ¯Çƒe concept of ÔÇÿthreshold diseaseÔÇÖ formerly taken as
the criterion for treatment has been superseded, with outcomes
improved by earlier intervention. ¯Çƒe outcome a¯ÇØer treatment varies depending on severity of disease; 30% of high-risk zone I ROP
will have an unfavourable visual outcome.
¯Çü Type 1. Treatment is now recommended within 72 hours for
type 1 disease.

{ Any ROP stage in zone I when accompanied by plus
disease.

{ Stage 3 to any extent within zone I.

{ Stage 2 or 3 in zone II, together with plus disease.
¯Çü Type 2 disease requires observation.

location.

{ Stage 1 or 2 in zone I without plus disease.

{ Stage 3 ROP within zone II without plus disease.

## Later phases

¯Çü Regression refers to disease involution and resolution. It may
be complete or incomplete. ¯Çƒe ¯Ç£rst sign is typically vascular
regression and tends to occur more rapidly a¯ÇØer anti-VEGF
treatment (1ÔÇô3 days) than a¯ÇØer laser photocoagulation (7ÔÇô14
days). Other clinical signs include resolution of intraretinal
haemorrhages, greater media clarity and better pupillary dilation. Regression of the ROP lesion is characterized by thinning
and whitening of neovascular tissue.
¯Çü Reactivation refers to recurrence of acute phase features. It
is seen more frequently a¯ÇØer anti-VEGF treatment than a¯ÇØer
spontaneous regression and very rarely occurs a¯ÇØer complete
laser photocoagulation.

## Screening

Formal criteria vary and risk factors and screening criteria developed in one country will not necessarily apply in another country.
In principle, babies born at or before 30ÔÇô32 weeksÔÇÖ gestational age,
or weighing 1500 g or less, should be screened for ROP. Severe illness in other premature babies may also prompt screening. ¯Çƒis
involves indirect ophthalmoscopy with a 28 D lens or a 2.2 panfunduscopic Volk lens and scleral depression, or a wide-¯Ç£eld retinal camera with careful oversight. Screening should begin 4ÔÇô7
weeks postnatally. Subsequent review is at 1- to 3-week intervals,
depending on the severity of the disease, continuing until retinal
vascularization reaches zone III. ¯Çƒe pupils in a premature infant
can be dilated with 0.5% cyclopentolate and 2.5% phenylephrine.

## Topical anaesthetic is instilled and a neonatal eyelid speculum

used. Monitoring, especially for apnoea, is prudent during and
a¯ÇØer examination. About 10% of babies screened require treatment. Refractive error, strabismus and amblyopia are more common in children with ROP and long-term monitoring is required.

## Techniques have evolved which allow small babies to be imaged

(Fig. 13.51F).

## Treatment

¯Çü Laser ablation of avascular peripheral retina (Fig. 13.52A and
B) has largely replaced cryotherapy because visual and anatomical outcomes are superior.
¯Çü Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents. ¯Çƒe BEAT-ROP (Bevacizumab Eliminates the Angiogenic ¯Çƒreat of Retinopathy of
Prematurity) study results have changed the approach to the
treatment of ROP. As a general principle, intravitreal antiVEGF treatment should be used as ¯Ç£rst line treatment for
zone I ROP, while laser therapy should be used for zone II
ROP. Bevacizumab appears to result in a lower rate of ROP
recurrence than ranibizumab. Allowing retinal development
to proceed normally without the destruction integral to laser
treatment is a potential advantage of anti-VEGF medication,
but there is mounting evidence that the disease can recur
following cessation of treatment. When compared to conventional laser treatment there is a greater chance of the eye
developing persistent peripheral vascularization with antiVEGF treatment, but a lower chance that the eye will develop
high myopia in time.
¯Çü Pars plana vitrectomy for tractional retinal detachment not
involving the macula (stage 4A) can be performed successfully
with respect to anatomical (90% success) and visual outcome.
¯Çƒe visual outcome in stages 4B (e.g. 60%) and 5 (e.g. 20%)
is typically disappointing even with successful anatomical
reattachment.

## Cicatricial disease

About 20% of infants with active ROP develop cicatricial complications, which range from innocuous to extremely severe. In
general, the more advanced or the more posterior the proliferative disease at the time of involution, the worse the cicatricial
sequelae. Findings range from moderate temporal vitreoretinal fibrosis and straightening of vascular arcades (Fig. 13.53A
and B) with ÔÇÿdraggingÔÇÖ of the macula and disc (Fig. 13.53C),
progressing to retrolental fibrovascular tissue that can lead to
falciform retinal fold formation (Fig. 13.53D) and to retinal
detachment (Fig. 13.53E), sometimes total (Fig. 13.53F) and
known as ÔÇÿretrolental fibroplasiaÔÇÖ (a term that has been used
synonymously with ROP in the past). Secondary angle-closure
glaucoma may develop due to progressive shallowing of the
anterior chamber caused by forward displacement of the irisÔÇô
lens diaphragm with anterior synechiae formation. Lensectomy
and anterior vitrectomy may be tried, but the results are generally poor.

RETINAL ARTERY MACROANEURYSM

A retinal artery macroaneurysm is a localized dilatation of a retinal arteriole and has a predilection for older hypertensive (75%)
women. Dyslipidaemia is also associated. ¯Çƒey are usually solitary
and 90% involve only one eye.

550 Retinopathy of Prematurity

(Courtesy of CK Patel.)

photocoagulation for type 1 disease. (Courtesy of S Chen.)

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Insidious impairment of vision due to leakage
involving the macula. Sudden visual loss due to haemorrhage
is less common.
¯Çü Fundus. A saccular arteriolar dilatation is typical, o¯ÇØen at a
bifurcation or an arteriovenous crossing on a temporal vascular
arcade. ¯Çƒe aneurysm may enlarge to several times the diameter
of the vessel. ¯Çƒere is associated retinal haemorrhage in 50%.
¯Çü Course

{ Chronic leakage. Persistent retinal oedema with exudate formation (Fig. 13.54A) is common and may a¯Ç×ect central vision.

{ Haemorrhage. Intra-, sub- or preretinal bleeding (Fig.
13.54B and C). ¯Çƒe prognosis for central visual function
in those with submacular haemorrhage is generally poor.

{ Spontaneous involution following thrombosis and ¯Ç£brosis
is very common and may precede or follow the development of leakage or haemorrhage.

of arteriolar occlusion), or both. Subthreshold laser may be
as e¯Ç×ective as standard photocoagulation. It may take several
months for oedema and exudate to fully absorb.
¯Çü Intravitreal bevacizumab closed 95% of macroaneurysms in a
case series, with resolution of macular oedema.
¯Çü Nd:YAG laser hyaloidotomy may be considered for a persistent premacular haemorrhage in order to disperse the blood
into the vitreous cavity (see Fig. 13.23), from where it may be
absorbed more quickly.
¯Çü Intravitreal gas injection with face-down positioning may
shi¯ÇØ subretinal haemorrhage away from the macula. Adjunctive intravitreal recombinant tissue plasminogen activator
(rtPA) may be used.
¯Çü Vitrectomy may be necessary for persistent vitreous
haemorrhage.

PRIMARY RETINAL TELANGIECTASIA

Retinal capillary telangiectasia is relatively common. Most cases
develop secondary to another retinal condition, typically involving
in¯Çøammation or vascular compromise; for example, DR and RVO.
Primary retinal telangiectasia comprises a group of rare, idiopathic,
congenital or acquired retinal vascular anomalies characterized by
dilatation and tortuosity of retinal blood vessels, multiple aneurysms, vascular leakage and the deposition of hard exudates. Retinal telangiectasis involves the capillary bed, although the arterioles
and venules may also be involved (Fig. 13.56).

## Idiopathic macular telangiectasia

¯Çƒis is described in Chapter 14

## Coats disease

## Introduction

## Coats disease is an idiopathic retinal telangiectasia that generally

occurs in early childhood. It is associated with intraretinal and

{ Other complications: epiretinal membrane, macular
neovascularization.
¯Çü FA. Uniform ¯Ç£lling of the macroaneurysm is typical (Fig. 13.54D),
with late leakage. Incomplete ¯Ç£lling is due to thrombosis.
¯Çü OCT can demonstrate the lesion itself, but its main use is to
document and monitor macular oedema and, less commonly,
subhyaloid haemorrhage.
¯Çü Vascular risk factors should be checked, especially BP and
serum lipids.

## Treatment

¯Çü Observation is indicated in eyes with good VA in which the
macula is not threatened and in those with mild retinal haemorrhage without signi¯Ç£cant oedema. In many cases macroaneurysms will spontaneously involute (Fig. 13.55A, C and D),
particularly following retinal or vitreous haemorrhage.
¯Çü Laser treatment (Fig. 13.55B) may be considered if oedema
or exudates threaten or involve the fovea, particularly if there
is documented visual deterioration. Burns may be applied
to the lesion itself, the surrounding area (to reduce the risk

subretinal exudation and frequently exudative retinal detachment,
without signs of vitreoretinal traction. About 75% of patients are
male and 95% have involvement of only one eye. Presentation is
most frequently in the ¯Ç£rst decade of life. Although it is not obviously inherited, a genetic predisposition may be involved as at least
some patients have a somatic mutation in the NDP gene, which is
also mutated in Norrie disease. It is now considered that Leber miliary aneurysms, previously regarded as a distinct condition, represent a generally milder form of the same disease, presenting later,
in a more localized pattern and carrying a better visual prognosis.
Di¯Ç×erential diagnosis from other causes of leukocoria in children,
particularly retinoblastoma, is important. Other retinal conditions
that may mimic Coats disease are familial exudative vitreoretinopathy, retinal haemangiomas in von HippelÔÇôLindau syndrome and
ROP. ¯Çƒe prognosis is variable and is dependent on the severity of
involvement at presentation. Younger children frequently have a
more aggressive clinical course.

{ Intra- and subretinal exudates (Fig. 13.57C), o¯ÇØen a¯Ç×ecting areas remote from the vascular abnormalities, particularly the macula. Progression to extensive exudative retinal
detachment (Fig. 13.57D) may occur.
¯Çü Complications include rubeosis iridis, glaucoma, uveitis,
cataract and phthisis bulbi.
¯Çü FA in mild cases shows early hyper¯Çøuorescence of telangiectasis and aneurysmal dilatations (Fig. 13.57E) and late staining
and leakage (Fig. 13.57F).

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Unilateral visual loss, strabismus or leukocoria
(Fig. 13.57A).
¯Çü Fundus

{ Telangiectasia and fusiform focal aneurysmal arteriolar
dilatations (Fig. 13.57B), often initially in the inferior
and temporal quadrants between the equator and ora
serrata.

## Treatment

¯Çü Observation in patients with mild, non-vision threatening
disease and in those with a comfortable eye with total retinal
detachment for whom there is no potential for restoration of
useful vision.
¯Çü Laser ablation of points of leakage should be considered if
progressive exudation is documented (Fig. 13.58A and B).
Multiple repeated treatments over an extended term are commonly required.
¯Çü Anti-VEGF therapy. Limited studies of anti-VEGF therapy
have been carried out, but initial results are promising, including as an adjunct to laser. Long-term safety in childhood
remains undetermined.
¯Çü Intravitreal triamcinolone (2ÔÇô4 mg) has been used with good
e¯Ç×ect in eyes with total exudative retinal detachment.
¯Çü Cryotherapy, with a double freezeÔÇôthaw method, in eyes with
extensive exudation or subtotal retinal detachment although
this may result in marked reaction with increased leakage.
¯Çƒerefore, laser photocoagulation is still the preferred option
if possible.
¯Çü Vitreoretinal surgery may be considered in eyes with signi¯Ç£cant tractional preretinal ¯Ç£brosis or total exudative detachment. ¯Çƒere is a poor visual prognosis but successful retinal
reattachment may prevent the development of neovascular
glaucoma.
¯Çü Enucleation may be required in painful eyes with neovascular
glaucoma.

EALES DISEASE

## Introduction

Eales disease is an idiopathic occlusive peripheral periphlebitis.
It is rare in White people but is an important cause of visual
morbidity in young South Asian males. It is characterized by
three overlapping stages ÔÇô inflammatory, occlusive and retinal
neovascular ÔÇô and is diagnosed principally by clinical examination. Recurrent vitreous haemorrhage may occur in the late
stages. The visual prognosis is good in the majority of cases.

## Tubercular protein hypersensitivity may be important in the

aetiology, but evidence is conflicting. Some patients may have
tubercular vasculitis.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Floaters or sudden visual reduction due to vitreous
haemorrhage.
¯Çü Systemic neurological features have been reported.
¯Çü Mild anterior uveitis is o¯ÇØen present.
¯Çü Fundus. ¯Çƒe condition is typically bilateral, though o¯ÇØen
asymmetrical.

{ Macular involvement with the same changes can occur but
is rare.
¯Çü Complications include tractional retinal detachment, macular
epiretinal membrane, neovascular glaucoma and cataract.
¯Çü FA will identify vasculitis and areas of non-perfusion
(Fig.13.59D). Wide-¯Ç£eld imaging is especially helpful.
¯Çü Investigation should be performed to rule out other causes of
vasculitis (e.g. sarcoidosis, tuberculosis) and peripheral retinal
neovascularization (e.g. haemoglobinopathies).

## Treatment

¯Çü Steroids. Periocular, systemic, topical and intravitreal steroids
seem to be helpful in the in¯Çøammatory stage.
¯Çü Antitubercular treatment. ¯Çƒis is strongly advocated by
some authorities but is controversial. It may be considered in
selected patients in combination with steroids, either to avoid
reactivation of prior tubercular infection (e.g. strongly positive skin test, QuantiFERON) or possibly when severe ocular
in¯Çøammatory signs are present.
¯Çü Scatter photocoagulation or cryotherapy of non-perfused
retina to reduce the neovascular stimulus.
¯Çü Intravitreal VEGF inhibitors are commonly used in the neovascular stage.
¯Çü Vitrectomy is helpful for persistent vitreous haemorrhage,
tractional detachment and macular epiretinal membrane.

RADIATION RETINOPATHY

Radiation retinopathy may develop following treatment of intraocular tumours by plaque therapy (brachytherapy) or external
beam irradiation of sinus, orbital or nasopharyngeal malignancies. It is characterized by delayed retinal microvascular changes
with endothelial cell loss, capillary occlusion and microaneurysm
formation. A¯Ç×ected patients may also develop cataract and keratopathy. ¯Çƒere is some evidence that it is more likely to occur in
genetically predisposed individuals. ¯Çƒe interval between exposure and disease is variable and unpredictable, although commonly between 6 months and 3 years. ¯Çƒere is a direct relationship
with radiation dose.
¯Çü Signs

{ Capillary occlusion with the development of telangiectasis
and microaneurysms.

{ Peripheral periphlebitis (Fig. 13.59A): sheathing, super¯Ç£cial retinal haemorrhages and sometimes cotton-wool
spots. Pigmented chorioretinal scars may be seen.

{ Branch retinal vein occlusion.

{ Peripheral capillary non-perfusion with microaneurysms,
tortuosity, vascular shunts and neovascularization (Fig.
13.59B and C) and recurrent vitreous haemorrhage (onethird of eyes) may develop at the junction of perfused
and non-perfused retina. Disc new vessels can sometimes
develop. ¯Çƒe vitreous haemorrhage tends to be limited and
to absorb over weeks, but can persist in some cases.

trauma, examine the posterior pole of both eyes for signs of
Purtscher retinopathy.

RETINOPATHY IN BLOOD DISORDERS

## Leukaemia

## Introduction

¯Çƒe leukaemias are malignancies of haematopoietic stem cells
involving abnormal proliferation of white blood cells. Acute leukaemia is characterized by the replacement of bone marrow with immature (blast) cells. Chronic leukaemia is associated, at least initially,
with well-di¯Ç×erentiated (mature) leukocytes and occurs almost
exclusively in adults. ¯Çƒe four major variants of leukaemia are:
¯Çü Acute lymphocytic (lymphoblastic) predominantly a¯Ç×ects
children, in whom there is around a 90% 5-year survival rate.
¯Çü Acute myeloid (myeloblastic) is most frequently seen in older
adults.
¯Çü Chronic lymphocytic has a very chronic course and many
patients die from unrelated disease.
¯Çü Chronic myelocytic has a progressive clinical course and
o¯ÇØen a less favourable prognosis.

## Ocular features

{ ¯Çƒe changes are best seen on FA (Fig. 13.60C and D).

{ Proliferative retinopathy (Fig. 13.60E).

{ Papillopathy (Fig. 13.60F). Radiation optic neuropathy
may occur but is less common as the nerve seems to be less
sensitive than retinal vessels.
¯Çü Treatment is frequently unsatisfactory. Options include laser,
steroids and intravitreal anti-VEGF agents.
¯Çü Prognosis depends on the severity of involvement. Poor prognostic features include papillopathy and proliferative retinopathy, which may result in vitreous haemorrhage and tractional
retinal detachment.

PURTSCHER RETINOPATHY

Purtscher retinopathy may follow severe trauma, especially chest
compressive, long-bone or head injury and involves occlusion and
ischaemia associated with microvascular damage. It is thought to
result from embolism and in some cases vascular occlusion by
other mechanisms such as complement-mediated white blood
cell aggregation. When it results from causes other than trauma
(e.g. fat or amniotic ¯Çøuid embolism, acute pancreatitis, preeclampsia, systemic vasculitides) it is sometimes referred to as
ÔÇÿPurtscher-likeÔÇÖ retinopathy. Presentation is with sudden bilateral
visual reduction, typically to 6/60 or less. Multiple unilateral or
bilateral super¯Ç£cial white retinal patches ÔÇô capillary bed infarcts
known as Purtscher ¯Çøecken (resembling large cotton-wool spots)
(Fig. 13.61A) are seen, o¯ÇØen associated with super¯Ç£cial peripapillary haemorrhages (Fig. 13.61B), typical cotton-wool spots and
disc swelling. Elevated complement 5a levels may be diagnostic
where there is no history of trauma. ¯Çƒe acute fundus changes
usually resolve within a few weeks, but only a small proportion
will regain normal vision. Treatment of the underlying cause is
not always possible.

VALSALVA RETINOPATHY

¯Çƒe Valsalva manoeuvre consists of forcible exhalation against a
closed glottis, thereby creating a sudden increase in intrathoracic
and intra-abdominal pressure (e.g. weight-li¯ÇØing, blowing up balloons). ¯Çƒe associated sudden rise in venous pressure may rupture
perifoveal capillaries leading to premacular haemorrhage of varying severity (Fig. 13.62A and B). Vitreous haemorrhage may also
occur. Treatment is with observation, or Nd:YAG laser membranotomy in some cases.

LIPAEMIA RETINALIS

Lipaemia retinalis is a rare condition characterized by creamywhite discoloration of retinal blood vessels (Fig. 13.63) in some
patients with hypertriglyceridaemia. In mild examples only
peripheral vessels are a¯Ç×ected, but in extreme cases the fundus
takes on a salmon colour. ¯Çƒe visualization of high levels of chylomicrons in blood vessels accounts for the fundal appearance.

## Ocular involvement is more commonly seen in the acute than the

chronic forms and virtually any ocular structure may be involved.
Primary leukaemic in¯Ç£ltration is fairly rare. Secondary changes
such as those associated with anaemia, thrombocytopenia, hyperviscosity and opportunistic infections are more common and
include intraocular bleeding, infection and vascular occlusion.
¯Çü Fundus

{ Retinal haemorrhages and cotton-wool spots are common
(Fig. 13.64A-D).

{ Roth spots are retinal haemorrhages with white centres
(Fig. 13.64E and F), the whitish element is thought to
be composed of coagulated ¯Ç£brin in most cases. ¯Çƒey
occur in acute leukaemia (Fig. 13.65) and in a range of
other conditions such as bacteraemia (classically subacute bacterial endocarditis), diabetes, hypertension and
anaemia.

{ Peripheral retinal neovascularization is an occasional feature of chronic myeloid leukaemia (Fig. 13.66A).

{ Retinal and choroidal in¯Ç£ltrates may occur, most commonly posterior to the equator (Fig. 13.66B) and may
masquerade as posterior uveitis. In chronic leukaemia
they may give rise to a ÔÇÿleopard skinÔÇÖ appearance (Fig.
13.66C).

{ Scattered haemorrhages may occur in patients with thrombocytopenia (Fig. 13.66D).

{ Optic nerve in¯Ç£ltration may cause swelling and visual loss.

## Anaemia

¯Çƒe anaemias are a group of disorders characterized by a decrease
in the number of circulating red blood cells or in the amount of
haemoglobin in each cell, or both. Retinal changes in anaemia are
usually innocuous and rarely of diagnostic importance.
¯Çü Retinopathy

{ Retinal venous tortuosity is related to the severity of anaemia but may occur in isolation.

## Hyperviscosity

¯Çƒe hyperviscosity states are a diverse group of rare disorders
characterized by increased blood viscosity due to polycythaemia
or abnormal plasma proteins.
¯Çü Polycythaemia is caused by the neoplastic proliferation
of erythrocytes with increased bone marrow activity and
hyperviscosity.

¯Çü Waldenstr├Âm macroglobulinaemia is a malignant lymphoproliferative disorder with monoclonal IgM production, which
most frequently a¯Ç×ects elderly men.
¯Çü Ocular features include retinal haemorrhages and venous
changes (Fig. 13.68A and B) and occasionally RVO and conjunctival telangiectasia.

566 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

¯Ç×e macula (Fig. 14.1A) is a round area at the posterior pole, lying
inside the temporal vascular arcades. It measures between 5 and 6 mm
in diameter and subserves the central 15ÔÇô20┬░ of the visual ¯ÇØeld. Histologically, it shows more than one layer of ganglion cells, in contrast
to the single ganglion cell layer of the peripheral retina. ¯Ç×e inner layers of the macula contain the yellow xanthophyll carotenoid pigments
lutein and zeaxanthin in far higher concentration than the peripheral
retina (hence the full name ÔÇÿmacula luteaÔÇÖ ÔÇô yellow plaque).
¯Çü ¯Çƒe fovea is a depression in the retinal surface at the centre of
the macula (Fig. 14.1BÔÇôD), with a diameter of 1.5 mm ÔÇô about
the same as the optic disc.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe foveola forms the central ¯Ç£oor of the fovea and has a
diameter of 0.35 mm (see Fig. 14.1B). It is the thinnest part
of the retina and is devoid of ganglion cells, consisting only
of a high density of cone photoreceptors and their nuclei (Fig.
14.2), together with M├╝ller cells.
¯Çü The umbo is a depression in the very centre of the foveola
(see Fig. 14.1B) which corresponds to the foveolar light

reflex (see Fig. 14.1A), loss of which may be an early sign
of damage.
¯Çü The foveal avascular zone (FAZ ÔÇô see Fig. 14.1B), a central
area containing no blood vessels but surrounded by a continuous network of capillaries, is located within the fovea
but extends beyond the foveola. The exact diameter varies
with age and in disease and its limits can be determined
with accuracy only by fluorescein angiography (average
0.6 mm).

## Retinal pigment epithelium

¯Çü Structure

{ ¯Ç×e retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) is composed of a
single layer of cells that are hexagonal in cross-section. ¯Ç×e
cells consist of an outer non-pigmented basal element containing the nucleus and an inner pigmented apical section
containing abundant melanosomes.

{ ¯Ç×e cell base is in contact with Bruch membrane and at
the cell apices multiple thread-like villous processes extend
between the outer segments of the photoreceptors.

A B

C D

(yellow circle), approximate extent of the foveal avascular zone (red circle), foveola (lilac circle), umbo (central white spot); ¯ÇØC¯Ç× OCT showing the foveal depression; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× three-dimensional
OCT.

{ At the posterior pole, particularly at the fovea, RPE cells
are taller and thinner, more regular in shape and contain
more numerous and larger melanosomes than in the
periphery.
¯Çü Function

{ RPE cells and intervening tight junctional complexes
(zonula occludentes) constitute the outer bloodÔÇôretinal
barrier, preventing extracellular ¯Ç£uid leaking into the subretinal space from the choriocapillaris and actively pumping ions and water out of the subretinal space.

{ Its integrity and that of Bruch membrane is important for
continued adhesion between the two, thought to be due to
a combination of osmotic and hydrostatic forces, possibly
with the aid of hemidesmosomal attachments.

{ Facilitation of photoreceptor turnover by the phagocytosis and lysosomal degradation of outer segments following
shedding.

{ Preservation of an optimal retinal milieu. Maintenance of
the outer bloodÔÇôretinal barrier is a key factor, as are the
inward transport of metabolites (mainly small molecules
such as amino acids and glucose) and the outward transport of metabolic waste products.

{ Storage, metabolism and transport of vitamin A in the
visual cycle.

¯Çü Function. ¯Ç×e RPE utilizes Bruch membrane as a route for the
transport of metabolic waste products out of the retinal environment. Changes in its structure are thought to be important
in the pathogenesis of many macular disorders ÔÇô for example,
intact Bruch membrane may be important in the suppression
of macular neovascularization (MNV).

CLINICAL EVALUATION OF MACULAR
DISEASE

## Symptoms

¯Çü Blurred vision and di¯Çøculty with close work is an early
symptom. Onset can be rapid in some conditions, such as
MNV.
¯Çü A positive scotoma, in which patients complain of something obstructing central vision, is a symptom of more
severe disease. This is in contrast to optic neuropathy,
which typically causes a missing area in the visual field
(negative scotoma).
¯Çü Metamorphopsia (distortion of perceived images) is a common symptom that does not occur in optic neuropathy.
¯Çü Micropsia (decrease in image size) is caused by spreading
apart of foveal cones and is uncommon.
¯Çü Macropsia (increase in image size) is due to crowding together
of foveal cones and is uncommon.
¯Çü Colour discrimination may be disturbed, but is generally less
evident than in even relatively mild optic neuropathy.
¯Çü Difficulties related to dark adaptation, such as poor
vision in dim light and persistence of after-images, may
occur.

TIP Metamorphopsia is a common early symptom of macular
disease and can be evaluated with an Amsler grid.

{ ¯Ç×e dense RPE pigment serves to absorb stray light.

## Bruch membrane

¯Çü Structure. Bruch membrane separates the RPE from the choriocapillaris and on electron microscopy consists of ¯ÇØve distinct elements:

{ ¯Ç×e basal lamina of the RPE.

{ An inner collagenous layer.

{ A thicker band of elastic ¯ÇØbres.

{ An outer collagenous layer.

{ ¯Ç×e basal lamina of the inner layer of the choriocapillaris.

INVESTIGATION OF MACULAR DISEASE

Fundus ¯Ç£uorescein angiography

## Introduction

Fluorescein angiography (FA) should be performed only if the
¯ÇØndings are likely to in¯Ç£uence management.
¯Çü Fluorescence is the property of certain molecules to emit
light of a longer wavelength when stimulated by light of a
shorter wavelength. ¯Ç×e excitation peak for ¯Ç£uorescein is
about 490 nm (in the blue part of the spectrum) ÔÇô the wavelength of maximal absorption of light energy by ¯Ç£uorescein.
Stimulated molecules will emit yellowÔÇôgreen light of about
530 nm (Fig. 14.3).
¯Çü Fluorescein (sodium ¯Ç£uorescein) is an orange water-soluble
dye that, when injected intravenously, remains largely intravascular (>70% bound to serum proteins). It is excreted in the
urine over 24ÔÇô36 hours.
¯Çü FA involves photographic surveillance of the passage of ¯Ç£uorescein through the retinal and choroidal circulations following intravenous injection.
¯Çü Outer bloodÔÇôretinal barrier. ¯Ç×e major choroidal vessels are
impermeable to both bound and free ¯Ç£uorescein. However,
the walls of the choriocapillaris contain fenestrations through
which unbound molecules escape into the extravascular
space, crossing Bruch membrane but on reaching the RPE are
blocked by intercellular complexes termed tight junctions or
zonula occludentes (Fig. 14.4).
¯Çü Inner bloodÔÇôretinal barrier is composed principally of the
tight junctions between retinal capillary endothelial cells,
across which neither bound nor free ¯Ç£uorescein can pass; the
basement membrane and pericytes play only a minor role in
this regard (Fig. 14.5A). Disruption of the inner bloodÔÇôretinal
barrier permits leakage of both bound and free ¯Ç£uorescein into
the extravascular space (Fig. 14.5B).
¯Çü Filters

{ Cobalt blue excitation ¯ÇØlter (Fig. 14.6). Incident white light
from the camera is ¯ÇØltered so that blue light enters the eye,

exciting the ¯Ç£uorescein molecules in the retinal and choroidal circulations.

{ YellowÔÇôgreen barrier ¯ÇØlter blocks any blue light re¯Ç£ected from
the eye, allowing only yellowÔÇôgreen emitted light to pass.
¯Çü Image capture in modern digital cameras uses a chargecoupled device (CCD). Digital imaging permits immediate
picture availability, easy storage and access, image manipulation and enhancement. Modern devices also typically require
a lower concentration of injected ¯Ç£uorescein to obtain highquality images, with a correspondingly substantially lower
incidence of adverse e¯ÇÜects.
