## Fluorescein in retinal vessels

¯Çü Contraindications

{ Fluorescein allergy is an absolute contraindication and
a history of a severe reaction to any allergen is a strong
relative contraindication. Preventative anti-allergy pretreatment may be helpful in some cases.

{ Other relative contraindications include renal failure (a
lower ¯Ç£uorescein dose is used), pregnancy, moderateÔÇô
severe asthma and signi¯ÇØcant cardiac disease.

{ Allergy to iodine-containing media or seafood is not a
contraindication to FA or to indocyanine green angiography (ICGA).

## Technique

Facilities must be in place to address possible adverse events. ¯Ç×is
includes adequate sta¯Çøng, a resuscitation trolley that includes
drugs for the treatment of anaphylaxis, a couch (or reclining chair)
and a receiver in case of vomiting. Signi¯ÇØcant nausea and vomiting are less common using the lower ¯Ç£uorescein concentrations
required by modern digital cameras.
¯Çü Adequate pharmacological mydriasis is important to obtain
high-quality images. Media opacity such as cataract may
reduce picture quality.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e procedure is explained and formal consent taken. It is
important to mention common and serious adverse e¯ÇÜects
(Table 14.1), particularly the invariable skin and urine staining.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e patient should be seated comfortably in front of the fundus camera and colour photographs, red-free (green incident
light, to enhance red detail) and auto¯Ç£uorescence images taken
as indicated.
¯Çü An intravenous cannula is inserted; a standard cannula is o¯ÇÖen
preferred rather than a less secure ÔÇÿbutter¯Ç£yÔÇÖ winged infusion
set. A¯ÇÖer cannulation, the line should be ¯Ç£ushed with normal
saline to check patency and exclude extravasation.
¯Çü Fluorescein, usually 5 ml of a 10% solution, is drawn up
into a syringe and injected over the course of 5ÔÇô10 seconds,

taking care not to rupture the cannulated vein (Fig. 14.7A
and B).
¯Çü Oral administration at a dose of 30 mg/kg is an alternative if
venous access cannot be obtained or is refused. A 5 ml vial
of 10% (100 mg/ml) sodium ¯Ç£uorescein contains 500 mg
and pictures should be taken over 20ÔÇô60 minutes following
ingestion.
¯Çü Images are taken at 1ÔÇô2 second intervals initially to capture
the critical early transit phases, beginning 5ÔÇô10 seconds a¯ÇÖer
injection, tapering frequency through subsequent phases.
¯Çü With monocular pathology, control pictures of the opposite
eye should be taken, usually a¯ÇÖer the initial transit phase has
been photographed in the index eye.
¯Çü If appropriate, images may be captured as late as 10ÔÇô20
minutes.
¯Çü Stereo images may be helpful to demonstrate elevation and
are usually taken by manually repositioning the camera sideways or by using a special device (a stereo separator) to adjust
the image. ¯Ç×ese are actually pseudo-stereo images as true
stereo requires simultaneous image capture from di¯ÇÜerent
angles.

## Angiographic phases

Fluorescein enters the eye through the ophthalmic artery, passing
into the choroidal circulation through the short posterior ciliary
arteries and into the retinal circulation through the central retinal
artery (Fig. 14.8). ¯Ç×e choroidal circulation ¯ÇØlls about 1 second
before the retinal. Precise details of the choroidal circulation are
not discernible, mainly because of rapid leakage of free ¯Ç£uorescein from the choriocapillaris. Melanin in the RPE cells also blocks
choroidal ¯Ç£uorescence. ¯Ç×e angiogram consists of the following
overlapping phases:
¯Çü ¯Çƒe choroidal (pre-arterial) phase typically occurs 9ÔÇô15 seconds a¯ÇÖer dye injection ÔÇô longer in patients with poor general
circulation ÔÇô and is characterized by patchy lobular ¯ÇØlling of
the choroid due to leakage of free ¯Ç£uorescein from the fenestrated choriocapillaris. A cilioretinal artery, if present, will
¯ÇØll at this time because it is derived from the posterior ciliary
circulation.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe arterial phase starts about a second a¯ÇÖer the onset of choroidal ¯Ç£uorescence and shows retinal arteriolar ¯ÇØlling and the
continuation of choroidal ¯ÇØlling (Fig. 14.9A).

¯Çü ¯Çƒe arteriovenous (capillary) phase shows complete ¯ÇØlling
of the arteries and capillaries with early laminar ¯Ç£ow in the
veins in which the dye appears to line the venous wall leaving
an axial hypo¯Ç£uorescent strip (Fig. 14.9B). ¯Ç×is phenomenon
re¯Ç£ects initial drainage from posterior pole capillaries ¯ÇØlling
the venous margins, as well as the small-vessel velocity pro¯ÇØle,
with faster plasma ¯Ç£ow adjacent to vessel walls where cellular
concentration is lower.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe venous phase. Laminar venous ¯Ç£ow (Fig. 14.9C) progresses to complete ¯ÇØlling (Fig. 14.9D), with late venous phase
featuring reducing arterial ¯Ç£uorescence. Maximal perifoveal
capillary ¯ÇØlling is reached at around 20ÔÇô25 seconds in patients
with normal cardiovascular function and the ¯ÇØrst pass of ¯Ç£uorescein circulation is generally completed by approximately 30
seconds.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe late (recirculation) phase demonstrates the e¯ÇÜects of
continuous recirculation, dilution and elimination of the
dye. With each succeeding wave, the intensity of ¯Ç£uorescence
becomes weaker although the disc shows staining. Fluorescein
is absent from the retinal vasculature a¯ÇÖer about 10 minutes.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe dark appearance of the fovea is caused by three factors
(Fig. 14.10A and B):

Causes of hyper¯Çƒuorescence

¯Çü Auto¯Ç×uorescent compounds, like lipofuscin in the retinal pigment epithelium, absorb blue light and emit yellowÔÇôgreen light
in a similar fashion to ¯Ç£uorescein, but weaker. Auto¯Ç£uorescence can be detected on standard fundus photography with
the excitation and barrier ¯ÇØlters both in place. Some modern

{ Absence of blood vessels in the FAZ.

{ Blockage of background choroidal ¯Ç£uorescence due to the
high density of xanthophyll at the fovea. Xanthophyll has
an important function in the macula: it acts as an antioxidant, protecting the photoreceptors from the potential
toxic e¯ÇÜects of light.

{ Blockage of background choroidal ¯Ç£uorescence by the RPE
cells at the fovea, which are larger and contain more melanin and lipofuscin than elsewhere in the retina.

retinal arteries; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× arteriovenous (capillary) phase showing complete arterial ¯ÇØlling and early
laminar venous ¯Ç×ow; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× early venous phase showing marked laminar venous ¯Ç×ow; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× midvenous phase showing almost complete venous ¯ÇØlling.

digital cameras have enhanced auto¯Ç£uorescence detection
capability, though imaging is most e¯ÇÜective with scanning
laser ophthalmoscopy. Auto¯Ç£uorescent lesions classically
include optic nerve head drusen (Fig. 14.11A) and astrocytic
hamartoma, but with increased availability of high-sensitivity
imaging, patterns associated with a wide range of posterior
segment pathology have been characterized, e.g. areas surrounding geographic atrophy, which show an accumulation
of lipofuscin in the RPE cells (lipofuscin is chemically a complex mixture of lipid, oxidized proteins and trace metals which
results from the oxidation of membrane lipids and proteins by
free radicals) (Fig. 14.11B).
¯Çü Pseudo¯Ç×uorescence (false ¯Ç£uorescence) refers to non¯Ç£uorescent re¯Ç£ected light visible prior to ¯Ç£uorescein injection.
¯Ç×is passes through the ¯ÇØlters due to the overlap of wavelengths passing through the excitation then the barrier ¯ÇØlters.
It is more evident when ¯ÇØlters are wearing out.
¯Çü Increased ¯Ç×uorescence may be caused by (a) enhanced visualization of normal ¯Ç£uorescein density, or (b) an increase in
¯Ç£uorescein content of tissues.
¯Çü A window defect is caused by atrophy or absence of the RPE
as in atrophic age-related macular degeneration (Fig. 14.12A),
a full-thickness macular hole, RPE tears and some drusen. ¯Ç×is
results in unmasking of normal background choroidal ¯Ç£uorescence, characterized by very early hyper¯Ç£uorescence that
increases in intensity and then fades without changing size or
shape (Fig. 14.12B and C).
¯Çü Pooling in an anatomical space occurs due to breakdown of
the outer bloodÔÇôretinal barrier (RPE tight junctions):

{ In the subretinal space, e.g. central serous chorioretinopathy (Fig. 14.13A). ¯Ç×is is characterized by early hyper¯Ç£uorescence, which, as the responsible leak tends to be only
small (Fig. 14.13B), slowly increases in intensity and area
(Fig. 14.13C), the maximum extent remaining relatively
well de¯ÇØned.

{ In the sub-RPE space, as in pigment epithelial detachment (Fig. 14.14A). ¯Ç×is is characterized by early

hyper¯Ç£uorescence (Fig. 14.14B) that increases in intensity
but not in size (Fig. 14.14C).
¯Çü Leakage of dye is characterized by fairly early hyper¯Ç£uorescence, increasing with time in both area and intensity. It occurs
as a result of breakdown of the inner bloodÔÇôretinal barrier due
to:

{ Vascular occlusion, which may involve the retinal arteries,
veins or capillaries (capillary drop-out ÔÇô Fig. 14.20A), or

{ Dysfunction or loss of existing vascular endothelial tight
junctions as in background diabetic retinopathy (DR),
retinal vein occlusion (RVO), cystoid macular oedema
(Fig. 14.15A) and disc swelling (Fig. 14.15B).

{ Primary absence of vascular endothelial tight junctions as
in MNV, proliferative diabetic retinopathy (Fig. 14.15C),
tumours and some vascular anomalies such as Coats
disease.
¯Çü Staining is a late phenomenon consisting of the prolonged
retention of dye in entities such as drusen, ¯ÇØbrous tissue,
exposed sclera and the normal optic disc and is seen in the
later phases of the angiogram, particularly a¯ÇÖer the dye has le¯ÇÖ
the choroidal and retinal circulations.

Causes of hypo¯Çƒuorescence

Reduction or absence of ¯Ç£uorescence may be due to: (a) optical
obstruction (masking or blockage) of normal ¯Ç£uorescein density
(Fig. 14.16) or (b) inadequate perfusion of tissue (¯ÇØlling defect).
¯Çü Masking of retinal ¯Ç×uorescence. Preretinal lesions such as
blood will block all ¯Ç£uorescence (Fig. 14.17AÔÇôC).
¯Çü Masking of background choroidal ¯Ç×uorescence allows persistence of ¯Ç£uorescence from super¯ÇØcial retinal vessels:

{ Deeper retinal lesions, e.g. intraretinal haemorrhages,
dense exudates.

{ Subretinal or sub-RPE lesions, e.g. blood (Fig. 14.18AÔÇôC).

{ Increased density of the RPE, e.g. congenital hypertrophy
(Fig. 14.19A and B).

{ Choroidal lesions, e.g. naevus (Fig. 14.19C).
¯Çü Filling defects may result from:

¯Çü Clinical ¯ÇØndings, including the patientÔÇÖs age and gender,
should be noted before assessing the images.
¯Çü Note whether images of right, le¯ÇÖ or both eyes have been taken.
¯Çü Comment on any colour and red-free images and on any preinjection demonstration of pseudo- or auto¯Ç£uorescence.
¯Çü Looking at the post-injection images, indicate whether the overall timing of ¯ÇØlling, especially arm-to-eye transit time, is normal.
¯Çü Brie¯Ç£y scan through the sequence of images in time order for
each eye in turn, initially concentrating on the eye with the
greatest number of shots as this is likely to be the one about
which there is greater concern. On the ¯ÇØrst review, look for
any characteristic major diagnostic, especially pathognomonic
features; for example, a lacy ¯ÇØlling pattern or a ÔÇÿsmokestackÔÇÖ
(see later).
¯Çü Go through the run for each eye in greater detail, noting the
evolution of any major features found on the ¯ÇØrst scan and
then providing a description of any other ¯ÇØndings using the
methodical consideration of the causes of hyper- and hypo¯Ç£uorescence set out above.

## Indocyanine green angiography

## Introduction

¯Çü Advantages over FA. Whilst FA is an excellent method of
studying the retinal circulation, it is of limited use in delineating the choroidal vasculature, due principally to masking by
the RPE. In contrast, the near-infrared light utilized in indocyanine green angiography (ICGA) penetrates ocular pigments
such as melanin and xanthophyll, as well as exudate and thin
layers of subretinal blood, making this technique eminently
suitable. An additional factor is that about 98% of ICG molecules bind to serum protein (mainly albumin); considerably
higher than the binding of ¯Ç£uorescein. As choriocapillaris

fenestrations are impermeable to larger protein molecules,
most ICG is retained within choroidal vessels. Infrared light is
also scattered less than visible light, making ICGA superior to
FA in eyes with media opacity. ICG stains the RPE in the late
stages of the angiogram.
¯Çü Image capture. ICG ¯Ç£uorescence is only 1/25th that of ¯Ç£uorescein so modern digital ICGA uses high-sensitivity videoangiographic image capture by means of an appropriately
adapted camera. Both the excitation (805 nm) and emission
(835 nm) ¯ÇØlters are set at infrared wavelengths (Fig. 14.21).
Alternatively, scanning laser ophthalmoscopy (SLO) systems
provide high contrast images, with less scattering of light
and fast image acquisition rates facilitating high-quality ICG
video.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe technique is similar to that of FA, but with an increased
emphasis on the acquisition of later images (up to about 45
minutes) than with FA. A dose of 25ÔÇô50 mg in 1ÔÇô2 ml water
for injection is used.
¯Çü Phases of ICGA: (a) early ÔÇô up to 60 seconds post-injection,
(b) early mid-phase ÔÇô 1ÔÇô3 minutes (Fig. 14.22A), (c) late midphase ÔÇô 3ÔÇô15 minutes, (Fig. 14.22B) and (d) late phase ÔÇô 15ÔÇô45
minutes.

Adverse e¯Ç×ects

ICGA is generally better tolerated than FA.
¯Çü Nausea, vomiting and urticaria are uncommon, but anaphylaxis probably occurs with approximately equal incidence to
FA.
¯Çü Serious reactions are exceptionally rare.
¯Çü ICGA is relatively contraindicated in liver disease (excretion is
hepatic) and as with FA in patients with a history of a severe
reaction to any allergen, moderate or severe asthma and signi¯ÇØcant cardiac disease. Its safety in pregnancy has not been
established.

## Indications

¯Çü Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV): ICGA is far superior to FA for the imaging of PCV (see Fig. 14.23).
¯Çü Exudative age-related macular degeneration (AMD). Conventional FA remains the primary method of assessment, but
ICGA can be a useful adjunct, particularly if PCV is suspected.

polyps (arrow) and leakage in polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy.

¯Çü Retinal angiomatous proliferation (Type 3 MNV). ICGA is
diagnostic in most cases, showing a hot spot in mid and or late
frames.
¯Çü Chronic central serous chorioretinopathy (CSR) in which it
is o¯ÇÖen di¯Çøcult to interpret areas of leakage on FA. However,
ICGA shows choroidal leakage and the presence of dilated
choroidal vessels. Previously unidenti¯ÇØed lesions elsewhere in
the fundus are also frequently visible using ICGA.
¯Çü Posterior uveitis. ICGA can provide useful information
beyond that available from FA in relation to diagnosis and the
extent of disease involvement (see Ch. 15).
¯Çü Choroidal tumours may be imaged e¯ÇÜectively, but ICGA is
inferior to clinical assessment for diagnosis.
¯Çü Breaks in Bruch membrane such as lacquer cracks and angioid streaks are more e¯ÇÜectively de¯ÇØned on ICGA than on FA.
¯Çü If FA is contraindicated.

## Optical coherence tomography

## Introduction

Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is a non-invasive,
non-contact imaging system providing high-resolution crosssectional images of the posterior segment. OCT is analogous
to B-scan ultrasonography but uses near-infrared light interferometry rather than sound waves, with images created by the
analysis of interference between re¯Ç£ected reference waves and
those re¯Ç£ected by tissue. Most instruments in current use employ
spectral/Fourier domain technology, in which the mechanical
movement required for image acquisition in older ÔÇÿtime domainÔÇÖ
machines has been eliminated and the information for each
point on the A-scan is collected simultaneously, speeding data
collection and improving resolution. Promising newer modalities include swept-source (SS) OCT that can acquire images at a
much higher rate and with extremely high retinal element resolution and better imaging depth. So-called ÔÇÿadaptive opticsÔÇÖ allows

correction of higher-order optical aberrations to improve resolution. Wide-¯ÇØeld, intraoperative, functional and Doppler (blood
¯Ç£ow measurement) OCT applications may all have clinical utility
in the future.
¯Ç×e diagnosis and monitoring of macular pathology has been
revolutionized by the advent of OCT imaging, e.g. AMD, diabetic
maculopathy, macular hole, epiretinal membrane and vitreomacular traction, CSR and retinal venous occlusion.
¯Ç×is technology allows a distinction to be made between retinal detachment and retinoschisis.

TIP OCT imaging is key to the diagnosis, monitoring and
treatment of macular disease.

## Normal appearance

## Fine retinal structures such as the external limiting membrane and

ganglion cell layer can be de¯ÇØned (Fig. 14.24). Detailed quantitative information on retinal thickness can be displayed numerically
(Fig. 14.25). ¯Ç×ree-dimensional images can be constructed and
di¯ÇÜerent retinal layers studied in relief. False-colour topographical
maps are rarely used these days.

OCT angiography

OCT angiography (OCTA) is a non-invasive diagnostic technique
that allows the blood ¯Ç£ow in the retina and choroid to be visualized without the need for an injection of contrast medium. ¯Ç×e
disadvantage of this technology is that the classic abnormalities of
traditional angiography (leakage, staining, pooling) are not shown.
¯Ç×e images are based on the detection of red blood cell movement
within the microvasculature of the back of the eye, using a series
of OCT B-scans. ¯Ç×ese scans are performed vertically in the same
retinal position. Di¯ÇÜerences between the scans generate detectable changing contrast as the red cells move through the vessels.
A two-dimensional horizontal map is then created of the microcirculation, within the various layers of the retina and choroid.
Importantly, it is the ¯Ç£ow that is visualized rather than the vessel
walls and ¯Ç£ow that is too slow or too fast may not be detected by
the technology.

## Applications

¯Çü Macular neovascular membrane:

{ Visualization of ¯Ç£ow in the outer retina.

{ Abnormal vasculature in areas featuring blood vessels (e.g.
choroid) (Fig. 14.26A and B).
¯Çü Non-exudative neovascular membrane. In dry AMD to diagnose a non-exudative neovascular membrane.
¯Çü Choroidal vessels. Visualization of abnormal choroidal vessels, particularly a¯ÇÖer treatment.
¯Çü Diabetic retinopathy:

{ Diagnosis of preretinal neovascularization and to di¯ÇÜerentiate intraretinal microvascular abnormalities (IRMA)
from new vessels.

{ Detection of microvascular changes without clinical
retinopathy.

{ To assess the microcirculation in patients with macular

TIP OCT angiography is a non-invasive imaging tool that
is useful for visualizing abnormal vessels in AMD, diabetic
retinopathy and other disorders.

Fundus auto¯Ç£uorescence

Imaging of fundus auto¯Ç£uorescence (FAF) using an enhanced
fundus camera or scanning laser ophthalmoscopy permits
visualization of accumulated lipofuscin in the retinal pigment
epithelium.
¯Çü Hyperauto¯Ç×uorescence. Many retinal diseases result in RPE
dysfunction and an accumulation of RPE lipofuscin, which
causes abnormal patterns of hyperauto¯Ç£uorescence on FAF
imaging. (a) It is particularly useful in the diagnosis and follow-up of patients with inherited retinal degeneration (see
Ch.15). (b) It can demonstrate more extensive macular disease
than is visible clinically (e.g. MEWDS, see Fig. 12.60C). (c) In
patients with geographic atrophy, FAF shows distinct areas
of hyperauto¯Ç£uorescence at the leading edges of lesions that
seems to precede retinal demise (see Fig. 14.11B).
¯Çü Hypoauto¯Ç×uorescence indicates areas of decreased lipofuscin, decreased RPE density or blockage of ¯Ç£uorescence (e.g.
RPE atrophy, ¯ÇØbrosis).
¯Çü Limitations of FAF include a susceptibility to artifact from
media opacities.

TIP Fundus auto¯Ç×uorescence has become particularly helpful
in the diagnosis and management of retinal diseases and the
clinical use is likely to expand in the future.

Wide¯Çƒ¯Çøeld imaging

Several wide-¯ÇØeld (also referred to as ultrawide-¯ÇØeld) highresolution imaging devices are now available (Fig. 14.27). ¯Ç×ese
are able to capture views of up to about 80% of the area of the retina
in a single image, o¯ÇÖen without dilating the pupil. Some have the
facility of imaging FAF and FA and can provide useful additional
information.

De¯ÇØnitions

¯Çü Posterior pole: retina within the arcades and slightly beyond.
¯Çü Mid-periphery: retina up to the posterior edge of the vortex
vein ampulla.
¯Çü Far periphery: retina anterior to the vortex vein ampulla.
¯Çü Wide-¯ÇØeld: single capture image centred on the fovea and capturing retina in all four quadrants posterior to and including
the vortex vein ampulla.
¯Çü Ultrawide-¯ÇØeld: single capture 200┬░ image of the retina
including the far periphery in all four quadrants.
¯Çü Pan-retina: single capture 360┬░ ora-to-ora view of the retina.

AGE-RELATED MACULAR
DEGENERATION

## Introduction

Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) is a degenerative disorder in which the structure and function of the macula deteriorates.
It is characterized by the presence of speci¯ÇØc clinical ¯ÇØndings,
including drusen and RPE changes, in the absence of another disorder. Later stages of the disease are associated with impairment of
vision secondary to neovascularization or atrophy.

Classi¯ÇØcation

¯Çü A consensus committee has provided a clinical classi¯ÇØcation
of AMD (Table 14.2) and has standardized the nomenclature
for reporting neovascular AMD.
¯Çü Main types. Conventionally, AMD has been divided as follows:

Table 14.2 Clinical Classi¯Çƒcation of Age-Related Macular
Degeneration (AMD)

Late AMD Neovascular AMD and/or

## Any geographic atrophy

Pigmentary abnormalities: any de¯Ç×nite hyper- or hypopigmentary
abnormalities associated with medium or large drusen but not due to other
known disease.
Drupelets: a newly proposed term for small drusen (<63¯ÇØ╬╝m).

## The size of drusen can be estimated by comparison with the approximately

125¯ÇØ╬╝m diameter of a retinal vein at the optic disc margin.

{ Dry (non-exudative, non-neovascular) AMD is the most
common form, comprising around 90% of diagnosed disease. Geographic atrophy (GA) is the advanced stage of
dry AMD. It has been suggested that the term ÔÇÿdry AMDÔÇÖ
should be used only to describe GA rather than the early
stages of AMD.

{ Wet (exudative, neovascular) AMD is much less common
than dry, but is associated with more rapid progression to
advanced sight loss. ¯Ç×e main clinical entities are: MNV,
pigment epithelial detachment (PED), retinal angiomatous
proliferation (RAP) and polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV).

TIP The term ÔÇÿchoroidal neovascularizationÔÇÖ (CNV) is no
longer appropriate because the neovascularization may start in
the outer retina. Macular neovascularization (MNV) should be
used instead.

## Epidemiology

¯Çü AMD is the most common cause of irreversible visual loss in
industrialized countries. In the USA, it is responsible for severe
sight loss (better eye worse than 6/60) in about 50% of Whites,
15% of Hispanics and 5% of Black people, with irreversible
visual loss. ¯Ç×e prevalence increases with age and symptoms
are rare in patients under 50 years of age.
¯Çü In the UK, signi¯ÇØcant visual impairment (binocularly 6/18 or
worse) from AMD a¯ÇÜects about 4% of the population aged
over 75 years and 14% of those over 90, with 1.6% of those
over 75 having binocular acuity of less than 6/60.

¯Çü Patients with late AMD in one eye, or even moderate vision
loss due to non-advanced AMD in one eye, have about a 50%
chance of developing advanced AMD in the fellow eye within
5 years.

## Risk factors

AMD is multifactorial in aetiology and is thought to involve a
complex interaction between polygenic, lifestyle and environmental factors.
¯Çü Age is the major risk factor.
¯Çü Race. Late AMD is more common in White individuals than
in other races.
¯Çü Heredity. Family history is important. ¯Ç×e risk of AMD is
up to three times greater if a ¯ÇØrst-degree relative has the disease. Variants in more than 50 genes have been implicated in
AMD including two major susceptibility genes: the complement factor H gene CFH (1q31), which helps to protect cells
from complement-mediated damage (mutation increases
risk seven times when homozygous), and the ARMS2 gene
(10q26), which encodes a protein that is a component of the
choroidal extracellular matrix (mutation increases risk eight
times when homozygous). Genes related to lipid metabolism
are also thought to be important. ¯Çƒere is no advantage in
undertaking genetic testing until studies con¯ÇØrm the value
of such testing for determining prognosis or the response
to treatment.
¯Çü Smoking roughly doubles the risk of AMD.
¯Çü Hypertension and other cardiovascular risk factors are likely
to be associated.
¯Çü Dietary factors. High fat intake and obesity may promote
AMD, with high antioxidant intake having a protective e¯ÇÜect
in some groups (see below).
¯Çü Aspirin. ¯Ç×ere is a weak, but statistically signi¯ÇØcant association. However, the bene¯ÇØts of aspirin usage (which include
preserving the duration and quality of life by decreasing the
risk of stroke and heart attack) outweigh the theoretical risk of
exacerbating wet AMD.
¯Çü Other factors such as cataract surgery, blue iris colour, high
sunlight exposure and female gender are suspected, but their
in¯Ç£uence remains less certain.

## Drusen

## Histopathology

Drusen (singular: druse) are extracellular deposits located at the
interface between the RPE and Bruch membrane (Fig. 14.28A).
¯Ç×ey are composed of a broad range of constituents derived from
immune-mediated and metabolic processes in the RPE. ¯Ç×eir precise role in the pathogenesis of AMD is unclear, but is positively
associated with the size of lesions and the presence or absence of
associated pigmentary abnormalities. Age-related drusen are rare
prior to the age of 40, but are common by the sixth decade. ¯Ç×e distribution is highly variable and they may be con¯ÇØned to the fovea,
may encircle it or form a band around the macular periphery. ¯Ç×ey
may also be seen in the peripheral and mid-peripheral fundus.

584 Age-Related Macular Degeneration

C D

E F

between the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and the inner collagenous layer of Bruch

showing an increase in number and size of drusen; ¯ÇØE¯Ç× drusen with associated pigmentary
abnormalities; ¯ÇØF¯Ç× red-free image of ¯ÇØE¯Ç×. (Courtesy of S Chen ÔÇô ¯Ç×gs C and D.)

## Clinical features

¯Ç×ere is a strong association between the size of drusen (Fig.
14.28B F) and the risk of developing late AMD over a 5-year
period.
¯Çü Small drusen (drupelets), sometimes termed ÔÇÿhardÔÇÖ drusen,
are typically well-de¯ÇØned whiteÔÇôyellow and by de¯ÇØnition
measure Ôëñ63 ╬╝m ÔÇô less than half the width of a retinal vein
at the optic disc margin ÔÇô in diameter. ¯Ç×eir presence as the
only ¯ÇØnding probably carries little increased risk of visual loss,
unless associated with pigmentary abnormalities.
¯Çü Intermediate drusen are fairly well de¯ÇØned yellowÔÇôwhite focal
deposits at the level of the RPE measuring between 63 ╬╝m and
125 ╬╝m. Without accompanying pigmentary abnormalities,
they carry only a small risk of progression to late AMD over 5
years, but this increases to over 10% if pigmentary abnormalities are present in both eyes.
¯Çü Large drusen are less well delineated yellowÔÇôwhite deep retinal lesions measuring over 125 ╬╝m in diameter. ¯Ç×e term ÔÇÿso¯ÇÖÔÇÖ
drusen is sometimes used synonymously. As they enlarge and
become more numerous (see Fig. 14.28C and D) they may
coalesce giving a localized elevation of the RPE, a ÔÇÿdrusenoid
RPE detachmentÔÇÖ ÔÇô see below. ¯Ç×e presence of large drusen in
both eyes is associated with a 13% risk of progression to late
AMD over 5 years, but with accompanying bilateral pigmentary abnormalities this rises to about 50%.
¯Çü Dystrophic calci¯ÇØcation may develop in all types of drusen.
¯Çü Pigmentary abnormalities. Hyper- and hypopigmentation
(see Fig. 14.28E and F) not due to other retinal disease is associated with a signi¯ÇØcantly higher likelihood of progression to
late AMD with visual loss.

TIP There is a strong association between the risk of
developing advanced AMD, drusen size and macular
pigmentary change.

OCT

Medium-sized and large drusen are seen as hyper-re¯Ç£ective irregular nodules beneath the RPE, located on or within Bruch membrane (see Fig. 14.29B).

## Fluorescein angiography

FA ¯ÇØndings depend on the state of the overlying RPE and on the
a¯Çønity of the drusen for ¯Ç£uorescein. Hyper¯Ç£uorescence can be
caused by a window defect due to atrophy of the overlying RPE,
or by late staining. Hypo¯Ç£uorescent drusen masking background
¯Ç£uorescence are hydrophobic, with a high lipid content and tend
not to stain.

Di¯Ç×erential diagnosis

A number of conditions feature lesions similar to age-related drusen and at least some may have a similar pathophysiological basis.
¯Çü Doyne honeycomb retinal dystrophy (malattia leventinese,
autosomal dominant radial drusen) is an uncommon condition in which fairly characteristic drusen (Fig. 14.29A) appear
during the second or third decades (see Ch. 15).

the skyÔÇÖ appearance. (Courtesy of S Chen ÔÇô ¯Ç×g. A; C Barry

- ¯Ç×gs B and C.)

¯Çü Cuticular drusen, also known as grouped early adult-onset
or basal laminar drusen (not to be confused with basal laminar deposit and basal linear deposit in AMD ÔÇô see dry AMD
below), tend to be seen in relatively young adults. ¯Ç×e lesions

586 Age-Related Macular Degeneration

consist of small (25ÔÇô75 ╬╝m) yellowish nodules (Fig. 14.29B)
that tend to cluster and increase in number with time and can
progress to serous PED. FA characteristically gives a ÔÇÿstars in
the skyÔÇÖ appearance (Fig. 14.29C). ¯Ç×e condition has been
linked to a variant of the CFH gene.
¯Çü Type 2 membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis is a
chronic renal disease that occurs in older children and adults.
A minority of patients develop bilateral di¯ÇÜuse drusen-like
lesions. ¯Ç×e CFH gene has again been implicated.

## Antioxidant supplementation

## Introduction

¯Ç×ere is substantial evidence, notably from the Age-Related Eye
Disease Study (AREDS, now known as AREDS1) and the follow-up
AREDS2, that taking high-dose antioxidant vitamins and minerals on
a regular basis can decrease the risk of the development of advanced
AMD in individuals with certain dry AMD features. ¯Ç×e recommendation was made in AREDS1 that individuals aged over 55 should
undergo examination for the following high-risk characteristics and if
one or more are present should consider antioxidant supplementation:
¯Çü Extensive intermediate- (ÔëÑ63ÔÇô125 ╬╝m) drusen.
¯Çü One or more large (ÔëÑ125 ╬╝m) drusen.
¯Çü GA in one or both eyes.
¯Çü Late AMD in one eye (greatest bene¯ÇØt in AREDS1).
In AREDS1 the reduction in risk of progression to advanced
AMD at 10 years was in the order of 25ÔÇô30% for those with the
more advanced of these signs at baseline who took supplements.
However, supplements did not discernibly reduce progression in
those with early or no AMD at baseline.

AREDS2

¯Ç×e regimen used in AREDS1 consisted of vitamin C, vitamin E, the
beta-carotene form of vitamin A and 80 mg daily of zinc (with copper to prevent zinc-induced anaemia). However, high zinc doses are
potentially associated with genitourinary tract problems and there
are data suggesting that 25 mg of zinc may be the maximal level that
is absorbed. Beta-carotene increases the incidence of lung cancer in
current and former smokers. AREDS2 looked at adjusting the betacarotene and zinc components and also whether additional or alternative supplements could enhance outcomes. AREDS2 found:
¯Çü ¯Ç×e carotenoids lutein and zeaxanthin are a safe alternative to
beta-carotene and are probably superior (possible 18% reduction in risk of advanced AMD above that conferred by the
AREDS1 regimen).
¯Çü Lutein and zeaxanthin supplementation added to the original
AREDS1 regimen was only associated with a statistically signi¯ÇØcantly reduced (26%) risk of AMD in patients in whom the
dietary intake of these was not already high (self-described and
blood testing). ¯Ç×ere was evidence that competition for absorption between di¯ÇÜerent carotenoids may have prevented the demonstration of superiority in other patients. ¯Ç×is group also showed
a one-third reduction in the likelihood of cataract surgery.
¯Çü Adding omega-3 fatty acids to the regimen did not seem to
enhance outcomes.

¯Çü Lowering the zinc dose did not lead to a statistically signi¯ÇØcant
prognostic worsening and is likely to be associated with a lower incidence of side-e¯ÇÜects such as gastrointestinal and urinary problems.
¯Çü Recommended daily supplementation based on AREDS2:

{ Vitamin E (400 IU).

{ Vitamin C (500 mg).

{ Lutein (10 mg).

{ Zeaxanthin (2 mg).

{ Zinc (25ÔÇô80 mg; the lower dose may be equally e¯ÇÜective).

{ Copper (2 mg; this may not be required with the lower zinc
dose).

## Other considerations

¯Çü A green leafy vegetable intake confers a lower risk of AMD
and for individuals with a strong family history of AMD and
those with early AMD who do not meet the AREDS criteria,
this may be a prudent lifestyle choice.
¯Çü Cessation of smoking should be advised.
¯Çü Protective measures against exposure to excessive sunlight
should be considered.
¯Çü Some authorities consider that evidence still supports the regular consumption of oily ¯ÇØsh.

TIP In a patient with dry AMD and high-risk characteristics,
the risk of developing severe AMD can be reduced with regular
anti-oxidant dietary supplementation.

Non¯Çƒexudative ¯ÇØdry, non¯Çƒneovascular¯Ç×
AMD

## Introduction

¯Ç×is is a common condition, with geographic atrophy threatening
central visual loss in up to 5 million people worldwide. Loss of
photoreceptors and RPE cells in the macula results in retinal atrophy, with the late stages referred to as geographic atrophy (GA).
Although the process of atrophy is slow, GA can eventually result
in signi¯ÇØcant loss of visual acuity. GA also remains an inevitable
long-term progressive outcome for many patients with wet AMD.
Lesion size is the main measure of disease progression and FAF
is the commonest imaging method that is used in clinical trials
to report lesion growth. Activation of complement factors C3 and
C5 plays an important role in the pathogenesis of dry AMD. Current research is aimed at manipulating the complement system by
inhibiting the e¯ÇÜects of these factors in the macular region.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms consist of gradual impairment of vision over months
or years. Both eyes are usually a¯ÇÜected, but o¯ÇÖen asymmetrically. Vision may ¯Ç£uctuate and is o¯ÇÖen better in bright light.
¯Çü Signs in approximately chronological order:

{ Numerous intermediateÔÇôlarge so¯ÇÖ drusen, which may
become con¯Ç£uent.

{ Focal hyper- and/or hypopigmentation of the RPE (Fig.
14.30A and B).

C D

E F

{ Enlargement of atrophic areas, within which larger choroidal vessels may become visible and pre-existing drusen disappear (GA ÔÇô Fig. 14.30E). Visual acuity may be
severely impaired if the fovea is involved. Rarely, MNV
may develop in an area of GA.

{ Visual cycle modulation by reducing the rate of vitamin A
processing using emixustat has not proved helpful in slowing the growth of atrophy.

{ Photocoagulation of drusen leads to a substantial reduction in their extent, but does not reduce the risk of progression to AMD.

expected performance. Intravitreal avacincaptad pegol (C5
inhibitor) or pegcetacoplan (C3 inhibitor) result in a reduction in geographic atrophy growth in the short term.

{ Drusen ÔÇô see above.

{ Loss of RPE and morphological alterations of the overlying retina of increasing severity are seen in GA, including increased hyper-re¯Ç£ectivity initially in the outer retinal
layers and eventual photoreceptor loss.

{ Outer retinal tubulations may be seen. ¯Ç×ese are thought
to consist of degenerating photoreceptors aggregated into
tubular structures that appear as roundish hypore¯Ç£ective
spaces (Fig. 14.30F), o¯ÇÖen around the margin of GA.

{ Sa¯ÇÜron (20 mg/day): short-term results suggest an
improvement in retinal function.
¯Çü Experimental surgery

{ Patients should be fully informed of the risks associated
with the surgery and the slender likelihood of long-term
central visual improvement.

{ Miniature intraocular telescope implantation may provide
bene¯ÇØt in selected cases.

{ RPE cells (derived from stem cells) implanted behind the
macula have resulted in improved vision in some patients.

{ Macular translocation surgery has a high complication
rate and has not been successful in improving vision.

{ Outer retinal corrugations. ¯Ç×is recently described phenomenon is an undulating hyper-re¯Ç£ective layer on OCT
thought to correspond to the histological ¯ÇØnding of basal
laminar deposit, a layer that accumulates between the RPE
and the RPE basement membrane (the inner layer of Bruch
membrane) in AMD. Basal linear deposit is a distinct ¯ÇØnding consisting of membranous debris laid down between
the RPE basement membrane and the inner collagenous
layer of Bruch membrane that may progress focally to form
drusen.
¯Çü Microperimetry is an accurate method of testing central
vision (see Fig. 1.26).
¯Çü FAF allows visualization of areas of atrophy and shows areas of
hyperauto¯Ç£uorescence in the junctional zone at higher risk of
progression to geographic atrophy (see Fig. 14.11B).
¯Çü FA of atrophic areas shows a window defect due to unmasking
of background choroidal ¯Ç£uorescence (see Fig. 14.12), if the
underlying choriocapillaris is still intact. Exposed sclera may
exhibit late staining.

{ Visual prostheses of various types are under investigation.

## Management

¯Çü Prophylaxis

{ Antioxidant supplementation if indicated.

{ Risk factors should be addressed, e.g. smoking, ocular sun
protection, cardiovascular, dietary.
¯Çü An Amsler grid should be provided for home use, with advice
to self-test on a regular basis and to seek professional advice
urgently in the event of any change in vision.
¯Çü Provision of low vision aids and visually impaired certi¯ÇØcation for individuals with signi¯ÇØcant visual loss, as this may
facilitate access to social and ¯ÇØnancial support.
¯Çü Potential new therapies

## Retinal pigment epithelial detachment

## Pathogenesis

Pigment epithelial detachment (PED) from the inner collagenous
layer of Bruch membrane is caused by disruption of the physiological forces maintaining adhesion. ¯Ç×e basic mechanism is thought
to be the reduction of hydraulic conductivity of a thickened and
dysfunctional Bruch membrane, thus impeding movement of ¯Ç£uid
from the RPE towards the choroid. Immune-mediated processes
may also be important.
Four types are recognised: serous, ¯ÇØbrovascular, drusenoid
and haemorrhagic (discussed below).

TIP The diagnosis of pigment epithelial detachment relies on
careful examination and OCT imaging of the macula.

Serous PED

¯Çü Symptoms. Patients can be asymptomatic, but usually present
with blurred central vision (sometimes induced hypermetropia) and metamorphopsia in one eye.
¯Çü Signs

{ An orange dome-shaped elevation with sharply delineated
edges, o¯ÇÖen with a pale margin of subretinal ¯Ç£uid (Fig.
14.31A). Multiple lesions may occur.

{ An associated pigment band may indicate chronicity.

{ ¯Ç×irteen di¯ÇÜerent complement inhibitors have been studied in clinical trials of dry AMD. However, many have been
ine¯ÇÜective despite the theoretical promise. Factors like the
timing of intervention, dosing schedule and selective permeability of these drugs may be responsible for the less than

{ Associated blood, lipid exudation, chorioretinal folds or
irregular subretinal ¯Ç£uid may indicate underlying MNV.

{ If no drusen are seen, PCV (see below) should be suspected.
¯Çü FA: a well-demarcated oval area of hyper¯Ç£uorescent pooling
(Fig. 14.31B) that increases in intensity but not in area with
time. An indentation (notch) may signify MNV.

A B

C D

FA at 10 minutes showing hyper¯Ç×uorescence; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× ICGA at 10 minutes showing hypo¯Ç×uorescence; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× OCT showing separation of the RPE from Bruch membrane. Subretinal hypo/
hyper-re¯Ç×ective material is present under the sensory retina.

¯Çü ICGA: an oval hypo¯Ç£uorescent area with a surrounding hyper¯Ç£uorescent ring (Fig. 14.31C). Occult MNV (focal hot spot or
di¯ÇÜuse plaque) is detected in over 90%.
¯Çü OCT shows separation of the RPE from Bruch membrane by
an optically empty area (Fig. 14.31D). MNV may be indicated
by a notch between the main elevation and a second small
mound.
¯Çü Natural course

{ Persistence with increasing atrophy and gradually worsening vision.

{ Patients aged over 60 have a worse prognosis (6/60 or less),
but speed of deterioration varies.

{ Resolution leaving GA with visual loss (spontaneous resolution with relative preservation of vision is more common
in younger patients).

{ RPE tear formation (see below) or haemorrhage from
MNV can occur, with sudden visual loss.

{ Up to a third of eyes develop clinical MNV within 2 years,
but the proportion is much higher angiographically.

¯Çü Management

{ Observation may be appropriate in clinically stable
patients without readily detectable MNV, especially those
younger than 60.

{ Intravitreal injection of anti-vascular endothelial growth
factor (VEGF) agents may stabilize or improve vision and
should be considered, particularly when there is associated
MNV. However, there is a 5ÔÇô20% risk of RPE tear.

{ Combining photodynamic therapy (PDT) with intravitreal anti-VEGF or intravitreal triamcinolone injection
(IVTA) can also be e¯ÇÜective, though the RPE tear risk
persists.

Fibrovascular PED

By de¯ÇØnition (Macular Photocoagulation Study classi¯ÇØcation),
¯ÇØbrovascular PED represents a form of ÔÇÿoccultÔÇÖ MNV (Type 1, see
below).

{ Signs. ¯Ç×e PED is much more irregular in outline and
elevation than serous PED.

¯Çü Natural course. ¯Ç×e outlook is usually better than other forms
of PED, with only gradual visual loss, though probably around
75% still progress to develop GA and 25% MNV by 10 years
from diagnosis. Long-term stability is common: at 3 years,
only about one-third will have GA or MNV.
¯Çü Management. Observation in most cases, with no evidence to
support the e¯Çøcacy of any intervention.

Haemorrhagic PED

Virtually every haemorrhagic PED has underlying MNV or polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV): the latter should always be
considered if no drusen are present.
¯Çü Symptoms. Sudden impairment of central vision.
¯Çü Signs

{ Elevated dark red dome-shaped lesion with a well-de¯ÇØned
outline (Fig. 14.33A and B).

Drusenoid PED

Drusenoid PED develops from con¯Ç£uent large so¯ÇÖ drusen and is
o¯ÇÖen bilateral.
¯Çü Signs. Shallow elevated pale areas with irregular scalloped
edges (Fig. 14.32A).
¯Çü FA. Early di¯ÇÜuse hypo¯Ç£uorescence with patchy relatively faint
early hyper¯Ç£uorescence, progressing to moderate irregular late
staining (Fig. 14.32B).
¯Çü ICGA. Hypo¯Ç£uorescence predominates.

{ Blood may break through into the subretinal space, assuming a more di¯ÇÜuse outline and a lighter red colour.
¯Çü FA. Dense masking of background ¯Ç£uorescence, but overlying

¯Çü Management of large haemorrhagic lesions is described below

PCV is discussed separately.

TIP Patients with a large pigment epithelial detachment
should be warned that there is a risk of inducing a pigment
epithelial tear with anti-VEGF injection.

## Retinal pigment epithelial tear

Tears may occur spontaneously, following laser (including PDT),
or a¯ÇÖer intravitreal injection.
¯Çü An RPE tear occurs at the junction of attached and detached
RPE. Two opposing forces play a role: traction from MNV
contraction and adhesive forces from the attached RPE. ¯Ç×e
tear appears on the opposite side of the neovascular membrane
and the contracted RPE monolayer comes to rest on the side
of the MNV.
¯Çü Risk factors

{ Older patients.

{ Long duration.

{ Large PED with a small ratio of MNV size to PED.

{ Wrinkles in the RPE on SD-OCT imaging.
¯Çü Symptoms. Sudden fall in vision with foveal involvement.
¯Çü Signs. A crescent-shaped pale area of RPE dehiscence is seen,
next to a darker area corresponding to the retracted and folded
¯Ç£ap (Fig. 14.34A).
¯Çü FA late phase shows hypo¯Ç£uorescence over the ¯Ç£ap due to
the thickened folded RPE, with adjacent hyper¯Ç£uorescence,
initially over the exposed choriocapillaris where the RPE is

absent and later due to scleral staining. ¯Ç×e two areas are o¯ÇÖen
separated by a sharply de¯ÇØned border (Fig. 14.34B).
¯Çü OCT. Loss of the normal dome shape of the RPE layer in the
PED, with hyper-re¯Ç£ectivity of the folded RPE (Fig. 14.34C
and D).
¯Çü ¯Çƒe prognosis for vision in patients with subfoveal tears is
poor. Good visual acuity (VA) is usually maintained if the
fovea is spared.

Macular neovascularization ¯ÇØMNV¯Ç×

## Introduction

Macular neovascularization (MNV) consists of a blood vessel complex that extends through Bruch membrane from the choriocapillaris into the sub-RPE (type 1) or subretinal (type 2) space. ¯Ç×e
neovascularization may also start in the outer retina (type 3), hence
the new term ÔÇÿmacular neovascularizationÔÇÖ (MNV) rather than ÔÇÿchoroidal neovascularizationÔÇÖ (CNV). ¯Ç×e aim of the new nomenclature
is to improve accuracy of communication. MNV occurs in many
di¯ÇÜerent disorders, usually when Bruch membrane and/or RPE
function has been compromised by a degenerative, in¯Ç£ammatory,
traumatic or neoplastic process. AMD is the most common causative association, followed by myopic degeneration. ¯Ç×e present
discussion relates to MNV arising de novo as the primary lesion in
neovascular AMD, but MNV may also develop secondary to retinal
angiomatous proliferation and polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy
(RAP and PCV ÔÇô see below), both of which are considered to be
variants of neovascular AMD. ¯Ç×e prognosis of untreated MNV is
poor, with ÔÇÿcounting ¯ÇØngersÔÇÖ vision a common outcome. An understanding of the pathogenesis has improved over recent years. ¯Ç×e
promotion and inhibition of blood vessel growth by cytokines is
important, particularly vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF),
which binds to endothelial cell receptors, promoting proliferation
and vascular leakage. ¯Ç×e inhibitory mediators pigment epitheliumderived factor (PEDF) and complement factor H (CFH) are also

592 Age-Related Macular Degeneration

RPE (arrow). (Courtesy of A Ambresin ÔÇô ¯Ç×gs C and D.)

thought to play key roles. Supplementary endothelial progenitor
cells are thought to be recruited from systemic reservoirs, enhancing growth of the new vessel complex.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Acute or subacute painless blurring of vision, usually
with metamorphopsia. Haemorrhage may give a positive scotoma.
¯Çü Signs

{ ¯Ç×e MNV itself may be identi¯ÇØable as a greyÔÇôgreen or
pinkish-yellow lesion (Fig. 14.35A).

{ Associated mediumÔÇôlarge drusen are a typical ¯ÇØnding in
the same or fellow eye.

{ Localized subretinal ¯Ç£uid, sometimes with cystoid macular oedema (CMO).

{ Intra- and subretinal lipid deposition, sometimes extensive
(Fig. 14.35B).

{ Haemorrhage (Fig. 14.35C) is common, e.g. subretinal,
preretinal/retrohyaloid, vitreous.

{ ¯Ç×ere may be an associated serous, ¯ÇØbrovascular drusenoid
or haemorrhagic PED.

{ Retinal and subretinal cicatrization (ÔÇÿdisciformÔÇÖ scar) in an
evolved or treated lesion (Fig. 14.35D).

## Fluorescein angiography

FA was previously used to diagnose MNV and to plan and monitor
the response to laser photocoagulation or PDT.
¯Çü Current indications include:

{ Diagnosis of MNV prior to committing to anti-VEGF
treatment. FA should usually be performed urgently on the
basis of clinical suspicion.

{ As an adjunct to diagnosis of an alternative form of neovascular AMD such as PCV and RAP.

{ Exceptionally, localization for extrafoveal photocoagulation, or guidance for PDT.
¯Çü Terminology used to describe MNV on FA is derived from the
Macular Photocoagulation Study:

{ Occult (70%). New term: Type 1 MNV. ¯Ç×e limits of the
neovascular membrane cannot be fully de¯ÇØned on FA (Fig.
14.36A D). Variants are ¯ÇØbrovascular PED (see above) and
ÔÇÿlate leakage of an undetermined sourceÔÇÖ (LLUS). Type 1

594 Age-Related Macular Degeneration

A B

C D

E

FA showing di¯Çøuse hyper¯Ç×uorescence but the limits of the membrane cannot be de¯ÇØned;
¯ÇØE¯Ç× OCT showing a PED with thickening of the overlying RPE and variable internal re¯Ç×ectivity
associated with adjacent subretinal ¯Ç×uid (arrow). (Courtesy of A Ambresin ÔÇô ¯Ç×g. E.)

MNV is commonly associated with AMD but can also
occur secondary to other conditions such as trauma and
pseudoxanthoma elasticum. It is present above Bruch
membrane, but below the RPE (Fig. 14.36E).

{ Classic (20%). New term: Type 2 MNV. Neovascular
membrane ¯ÇØlls with dye in a well-de¯ÇØned ÔÇÿlacyÔÇÖ pattern during early transit subsequently leaking into the
subretinal space over 1ÔÇô2 minutes, with late staining of
¯ÇØbrous tissue (Fig. 14.37A D). Most MNV is subfoveal;
extrafoveal being de¯ÇØned as ÔëÑ200 ╬╝m from the centre of
the foveal avascular zone on FA. It is present below the
neurosensory retina, but above the RPE (Fig. 14.37E).
Predominantly or minimally classic MNV is present
when the classic element is greater or less than 50% of the
total lesion respectively.

{ Retinal angiomatous proliferation (10%). New term:
Type 3 MNV. Neovascularisation originates from the
retinal circulation (typically the deep capillary plexus)
and grows towards the outer retina. Stippled hyper¯Ç£uorescence is seen over an area of elevated RPE that expands
to coalesce in the later stages of the angiogram (see Fig.
14.39C and D).

¯Çü Type 3 MNV: OCT ¯ÇØndings similar to type 1, with irregular
PEDs, subretinal ¯Ç£uid and intraretinal cysts. In some patients
dilated vascular elements are seen at the outer border of the
lesion (see Fig. 14.39E).

Type 3 MNV ¯ÇØretinal angiomatous
proliferation¯Ç×

## Introduction

¯Ç×e major component of the neovascular complex is initially
located within the retina. ¯Ç×e process may originate in the deep
retinal capillary plexus or within the choroid; in the latter case with
the early formation of a retinal-choroidal anastomosis (RCA). ¯Ç×e
disease is frequently bilateral and symmetrical and is probably
underdiagnosed. It may constitute 10ÔÇô20% of neovascular AMD
in White people.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Presentation is similar to that of MNV, but PED and exudate
are more frequent. Haemorrhages are also more common and
tend to be super¯ÇØcial and multiple.
¯Çü Stage 1: Intraretinal neovascularization (IRN). Dilated telangiectatic retinal vessels and small angiomatous lesions, typically accompanied by intra-, sub- and preretinal haemorrhage,
oedema and exudate (Fig. 14.39A).
¯Çü Stage 2: Subretinal neovascularization (SRN) extends into the
subretinal space associated with increasing oedema and exudate. A serous PED may be present.
¯Çü Stage 3: MNV. Perfusion is principally via the choroid, with
RCA formation. MNV is clearly evident clinically or angiographically; a disciform scar will o¯ÇÖen form.
¯Çü ICGA is diagnostic in most cases, showing a hot spot in mid
and/or late frames (Fig. 14.39B) and frequently a perfusing retinal arteriole and draining venule (ÔÇÿhairpin loopÔÇÖ when linked).
¯Çü FA is usually similar to occult or minimally classic MNV
(Fig. 14.39C and D), but may show focal intraretinal
hyper¯Ç£uorescence.
¯Çü OCT is commonly associated with marked intra- and subretinal ¯Ç£uid (Fig. 14.39E). Neovascularization can be seen as a
hyper-re¯Ç£ective area leading to a PED. Other features depend
on the stage.

## Treatment

Type 3 MNV is extremely sensitive to anti-VEGF treatment, especially in the early stages. Favourable outcomes in combination with
PDT have been reported. Limited success has been reported for
other modalities, including PDT alone and photocoagulation of
feeder vessels, though the latter may be used in resistant cases.

Haemorrhagic AMD

¯Çü ¯Çƒe visual prognosis for most eyes with extensive subretinal
or sub-RPE haemorrhage is poor.
¯Çü Clinical trials assessing surgical drainage of extensive subretinal or sub-RPE haemorrhage with MNV excision have

## Indocyanine green angiography

ICGA demonstrates MNV as a focal hyper¯Ç£uorescent ÔÇÿhot spotÔÇÖ
(Fig. 14.38) or ÔÇÿplaqueÔÇÖ. Bene¯ÇØts adjunctive to FA include:
¯Çü Increased sensitivity in the detection of MNV, for example if
low-density haemorrhage, ¯Ç£uid or pigment preclude adequate
FA visualization.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e distinction of MNV from other conditions that may have a
similar presentation, particularly PCV, RAP and CSR.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e delineation of occult MNV may still have utility for combined modality treatment and for patients who refuse intravitreal therapy.

## Optical coherence tomography

Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is critical in the diagnosis
and quantitative monitoring of the response to MNV treatment.
Typically, MNV is shown as a thickening and fragmentation of
the RPE and choriocapillaris. Subretinal and sub-RPE ¯Ç£uid, blood
and scarring are demonstrated. Outer retinal tubulations (see Fig.
14.30F) may be present, o¯ÇÖen in a branching pseudodendritic conformation. On OCT the classi¯ÇØcation is based on the location of
the pathology.
¯Çü Type 1 MNV: neovascularization is above Bruch membrane,
but below the retinal pigment epithelium and manifests as a
pigment epithelial detachment that is o¯ÇÖen multilobulated in
appearance. OCT shows an elevation of the RPE by material
with heterogenous re¯Ç£ectivity and vascular elements may be
seen (see Fig. 14.36E).
¯Çü Type 2 MNV: neovascularization is below the neurosensory
retina, but above the retinal pigment epithelium. It may be
associated with subretinal hyperre¯Ç£ective material and separation of the neurosensory retina from the RPE. Subretinal
¯Ç£uid, haemorrhage or intraretinal ¯Ç£uid may be present (see

not shown any signi¯ÇØcant improvement in prognosis and an
increased rate of rhegmatogenous retinal detachment. However, better results have been reported using intravitreal antiVEGF injection and liquefaction of blood by intravitreal (or
subretinal, requiring vitrectomy) recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (rtPA). In addition, pneumatic displacement
may be appropriate for large or thick haemorrhage.
¯Çü Anticoagulant treatment. If the patient takes a coumarin anticoagulant, liaison with the prescribing physician is worthwhile
to assess if this could be stopped as there is an association with
massive macular haemorrhage. Antiplatelet drugs do not usually require discontinuation, though aspirin may be associated
with a greater risk of MNV than other agents.
¯Çü Alternative pathology (e.g. PCV, macroaneurysm) that may
be associated with extensive haemorrhage should always be
considered.

Treatment with anti-VEGF agents

¯Çü Principles. Inhibitors of VEGF block its interaction with
receptors on the endothelial cell surface and so retard or

reverse vessel growth. ¯Ç×ey have become the predominant
means of treatment for MNV, dramatically improving the
visual prognosis. Genetic factors are associated with a variability in response so that visual outcomes can di¯ÇÜer from patient
to patient. Intravitreal injection is the standard method of
administration. Notable (but rare) risks include retinal detachment, damage to the lens, RPE tears and endophthalmitis.
Sustained elevation of intraocular pressure (IOP) and sterile
uveitis may occasionally occur. Anti-VEGF treatment is not
associated with arterial or venous thromboembolic events. All
available anti-VEGF agents seem to have potential for bene¯ÇØt
in a range of vascular eye diseases. As a general principle, the
better the visual acuity at the onset of treatment the better the
visual acuity at follow-up. ¯Ç×ere is some evidence that younger
age at treatment onset may confer a worse outcome. Treatment
should be initiated within 2ÔÇô3 weeks of symptom onset.
¯Çü Landmark studies. ¯Ç×e following large, randomized, doublemasked, sham-controlled trials provide the evidential basis
for anti-VEGF-treatment in individuals with neovascular agerelated macular degeneration.

hyper¯Ç×uorescence of the neovascular membrane and surrounding drusen; ¯ÇØE¯Ç× OCT showing

{ MARINA (Minimally Classic/Occult Trial of the AntiVEGF Antibody Ranibizumab in the treatment of Neovascular AMD) shows that ranibizumab treatment is more
likely to prevent visual loss than placebo in patients with
neovascular AMD; a bene¯ÇØt that is sustained for 2 years
regardless of gender, age, lesion size or type. ¯Ç×e study
also shows an improvement in angiographic and OCT
outcomes.

{ ANCHOR (Anti-VEGF Antibody Ranibizumab for the
Treatment of Predominantly Classic Choroidal Neovascularization in AMD) con¯ÇØrms that ranibizumab treatment
is more e¯ÇÜective than vertepor¯ÇØn PDT in respect of visual
acuity, with approximately one-third of subjects improving
by 15 letters.

{ PIER concludes that the outcomes are better if ranibizumab is injected monthly than if it is injected monthly for
3 months and then 3-monthly for 1 year.

{ HORIZON and SAILOR studies con¯ÇØrm that intravitreal
ranibizumab has a good safety pro¯ÇØle and is well tolerated.

{ PrONTO (Prospective OCT Imaging of Patients with Neovascular AMD Treated with Intraocular Ranibizumab)
shows that an OCT-guided retreatment strategy results in
similar visual acuity outcomes with fewer injections, than
a monthly schedule.

{ HARBOUR concludes that a 0.5 mg versus 2 mg monthly
versus ÔÇÿas-neededÔÇÖ injection of ranibizumab provides similar results in all treatment groups.

{ CATT (Comparison of Age-Related Macular Degeneration Treatment Trial) compares ranibizumab with bevacizumab intravitreal injections and shows no signi¯ÇØcant
di¯ÇÜerences in outcome.

{ IVAN (Alternative Treatments to Inhibit VEGF in AgeRelated Choroidal Neovascularization) is similar to CATT,
but uses a dosing schedule of monthly injections for 3
months then three consecutive monthly injections whenever retreatment is required. It shows equivalent visual
outcomes.

{ VIEW 1/2 (VEGF Trap-Eye: Investigation of E¯Çøcacy
and Safety in Wet AMD) shows that a¯Ç£ibercept (injected
bimonthly) is as e¯ÇÜective as ranibizumab (injected
monthly) in the treatment of neovascular AMD.

{ LUCERNE and TENAYA: shows visual bene¯ÇØts with faricimab given at up to 16-week intervals and demonstrates
the potential to extend the time between treatments with
sustained e¯Çøcacy.
¯Çü Indications. All MNV subtypes respond to anti-VEGF therapy, but bene¯ÇØt is only likely in the presence of active disease
(Fig. 14.40) (this treatment is of no bene¯ÇØt in patients with
a mature ¯ÇØbrotic disciform scar). Evidence for active MNV
includes ¯Ç£uid or haemorrhage, leakage on FA, an enlarging
MNV membrane, or deteriorating vision judged likely to be
due to MNV activity. An eye with almost any level of vision
may bene¯ÇØt, although better VA at the outset is associated with
a better ¯ÇØnal VA.
¯Çü A¯Ç×ibercept (Eylea) is a recombinant fusion protein that binds
to VEGF-A, VEGF-B and placental growth factor (PlGF). A¯ÇÖer

becoming commercially available, it was adopted rapidly into

dosing is required more frequently than every 2 months. ¯Ç×e
standard dose is 2 mg in 0.05 ml; an induction course of three
injections is given at monthly intervals.
¯Çü Ranibizumab (Lucentis). Ranibizumab is a humanized
monoclonal antibody fragment developed speci¯ÇØcally for use
in the eye. It is derived from the same parent mouse antibody
as bevacizumab and non-selectively binds and inhibits all isoforms of VEGF-A. ¯Ç×e usual dose is 0.5 mg in 0.05 ml. ¯Ç×ree
main treatment strategies are adopted in AMD:

{ Regular monthly injection is the regimen adopted in initial major trials. Overall, around 95% of patients maintain
vision regardless of lesion type and 35ÔÇô40% signi¯ÇØcantly
improve, most markedly during the ¯ÇØrst 3 months. ¯Ç×is
regimen o¯ÇÜers a marginally better visual outcome, but may
be associated with progression to geographic atrophy of

600 Pachychoroid Disease Spectrum

slightly greater severity than less intensive administration.
¯Ç×ere is a suggestion that systemic adverse events may,
counterintuitively, be less common with regular monthly
injections than discontinuous schedules.

anti-VEGF injection ÔÇô there are slight di¯ÇÜerences for intravitreal steroid administration.
¯Çü Patients can return to normal activity a¯ÇÖer 24 hours, but should
be warned to seek advice urgently should they experience any
deterioration in their vision or symptoms of in¯Ç£ammation.

TIP In patients with AMD secondary to MNV, the better the
visual acuity at the onset of anti-VEGF treatment the better the
¯ÇØnal visual acuity.

Treatment with photodynamic therapy (PDT)

Vertepor¯ÇØn is a light-activated compound preferentially taken up
by dividing cells including neovascular tissue. It is infused intravenously and then activated by diode laser to cause thrombosis.
¯Ç×e main indication is subfoveal predominantly classic MNV with
visual acuity of 6/60 or better. Severe adverse e¯ÇÜects are rare. PDT
should not be used in patients with porphyria. With the advent of
anti-VEGF treatment, PDT is now rarely used for MNV, but combination therapy (see below) and refusal of intravitreal treatment
remain indications. Reduced-intensity regimens have been used
with good e¯ÇÜect in CSR.

## Combination and other experimental therapies

Although anti-VEGF therapy has revolutionized the management
of MNV, further investigation is attempting to achieve outcomes
that are even better, particularly a reduction in the frequency of
intravitreal injections. To date, regimens that include the combination of PDT with anti-VEGF treatment have not been shown to be
superior to anti-VEGF treatment alone, but investigation is ongoing,
including parallel inhibition of anti-VEGF agents with inhibition of
other cytokines and with low-intensity macular radiotherapy. Other
avenues of potential therapeutic advantage are sustained-release
anti-VEGF systems and gene therapy utilizing adenoviral vectors to
facilitate the production of cytokines within the eye.

## Laser

¯Ç×ermal argon or diode laser ablation of MNV is now rarely used,
though may still be suitable for the treatment of small classic extrafoveal membranes well away from the macular centre and possibly
some cases of PCV and RAP.

PACHYCHOROID DISEASE SPECTRUM

¯Ç×is term refers to a group of macular disorders that share the
common features of a thick choroid, dilated and hyperpermeable choroidal vessels, attenuation of the choriocapillaris and
retinal pigment epithelial dysfunction. ¯Ç×ese changes may lead
to serous retinal detachment. Normal subfoveal choroidal thickness is approximately 300 ╬╝m and the median choroidal thickness
is about 260 ╬╝m (depending on the technique of measuring), but
varies considerably with age, gender and race. If a neovascular
membrane is present it tends to be a slow-growing type 1 MNV
producing ¯Ç£at, irregular RPE detachments. ¯Ç×ese disorders can be
investigated using OCT and ICG angiography. ¯Ç×e following variants are recognized:

{ ¯Ç×ree initial monthly injections followed by monthly
review with re-injection when deterioration occurs as
assessed by VA (e.g. loss of ¯ÇØve letters or more) and OCT
(e.g. retinal thickness increase of 100 ╬╝m or more).

{ ÔÇÿTreat and extendÔÇÖ entails administering three initial injections at monthly intervals and then gradually increasing
the period between injections until deterioration is evident. If possible, a tailored interval is determined for each
patient.

{ Minor intraocular in¯Ç£ammation may occur a¯ÇÖer the injection. ¯Ç×e endophthalmitis rate is about 1%.
¯Çü Bevacizumab (Avastin). In contrast to ranibizumab, bevacizumab is a complete antibody originally developed to target blood vessel growth in metastatic cancer deposits. Its use
for AMD and other indications is ÔÇÿo¯ÇÜ labelÔÇÖ. It is very much
cheaper than ranibizumab and a¯Ç£ibercept. Clinical trial results
suggest that it is approximately comparable to ranibizumab in
e¯Çøcacy and safety, though some studies have suggested that
the risk of serious systemic adverse events is marginally higher
with bevacizumab than ranibizumab. Treatment strategies in
AMD are similar to those used for ranibizumab. ¯Ç×e dose of
bevacizumab is usually 1.25 mg/0.05 ml.
¯Çü Brolucizumab (Beavu). ¯Ç×e drug is signi¯ÇØcantly smaller than
other anti-VEGF agents and has high binding a¯Çønity. It lasts
longer in the eye and thus permits less frequent dosing. ¯Ç×e
HAWK and HARRIER studies show non-inferiority to a¯Ç£ibercept with an improved dosing interval. Approximately half of
the patients on this agent can be maintained on a 3-monthly
injection schedule at 1 year.
¯Çü Faricimab (Vabysmo). ¯Ç×is is a new bispeci¯ÇØc antibody
that inhibits angiopoietin-2 and VEGF-A. ¯Ç×e TENAYA and
LUCERNE trials show non-inferiority to a¯Ç£ibercept at 2 years,
with dosing determined by visual acuity and central sub¯ÇØeld
macular thickness measured with OCT. ¯Ç×e drug is generally
well tolerated, but a small number develop intraocular in¯Ç£ammation. Almost 80% of patients can be managed on a 12-week
dosing schedule and 60% on a 16-week schedule. It may have
a signi¯ÇØcant impact on care by reducing the frequency of
injections.
¯Çü Pegaptanib (Macugen). Pegaptanib sodium was the ¯ÇØrst antiVEGF agent approved by regulatory authorities for ocular
treatment. ¯Ç×e results are similar to outcomes with PDT and
its use is now extremely limited.
¯Çü Biosimilars. ¯Ç×ese are medicines that are highly similar to
other biological medicines that are already in use, with no
clinical di¯ÇÜerences in terms of safety and e¯ÇÜectiveness. Two
ranibizumab biosimilars have received regulatory approval for
use: Byooviz and Ongavia/Ranivisio. At least 11 biosimilars
to ranibizumab, a¯Ç£ibercept and bevacizumab are in late-stage
development.
¯Çü Technique of intravitreal injection. Various protocols are in
use. A typical approach is set out in Table 14.3 and applies to

Table 14.3 Technique of Intravitreal Injection

ÔÇó¯Çü The procedure and its risks should be explained to the patient and appropriate consent obtained.
ÔÇó¯Çü The environment should be appropriate, e.g. a dedicated ÔÇÿclean roomÔÇÖ with adequate illumination.
ÔÇó¯Çü The indication and the eye to be treated should be checked and the eye marked.
ÔÇó¯Çü It should be con¯ÇØrmed that anterior and posterior segment examination (including IOP) has been carried out recently to
exclude contraindications.
ÔÇó¯Çü Con¯ÇØrmation should be obtained that a syringe of the drug to be injected is available.
ÔÇó¯Çü Bilateral injections are optimally administered at separate sessions to minimize risk, but if necessary di¯Çøerent
instruments and drug batches should be used.
ÔÇó¯Çü A surgical mask should be worn.
ÔÇó¯Çü Topical anaesthetic and mydriatic agents are instilled.
ÔÇó¯Çü Povidone-iodine 5% (chlorhexidine if allergic to iodine) is applied to the ocular surface and at least 3 minutes allowed
prior to injection.
ÔÇó¯Çü Subconjunctival lidocaine 1% or 2% may be used to supplement the topical agent. Some practitioners prefer lidocaine
gel, though concerns have been expressed regarding the possibility of microorganism retention within this.
ÔÇó¯Çü Hands are washed using a standard surgical procedure and sterile gloves donned.
ÔÇó¯Çü The periocular skin, eyelids and lashes are cleaned with 5ÔÇô10% povidone-iodine.
ÔÇó¯Çü As for other forms of intraocular surgery, a sterile periocular drape may be advisable.
ÔÇó¯Çü The sterile pouch containing a pre-prepared syringe is opened, or a sterile syringe is used to draw up the appropriate
volume of drug from a vial of ready-prepared drug. A needle (typically 30-gauge, 0.5 inch) on the syringe is primed to
expel any air.
ÔÇó¯Çü A sterile speculum is placed in the eye.
ÔÇó¯Çü The patient is instructed to look away from the injection site ÔÇô this is most commonly inferotemporal because of
ease of access, though any quadrant can be used; the 3 and 9 oÔÇÖclock positions are avoided because of the risk of
neurovascular damage.
ÔÇó¯Çü A gauge is used to identify an injection site 3.5ÔÇô4.0 mm posterior to the limbus (pars plana).
ÔÇó¯Çü Forceps can be used to stabilize the eye and, if wished, to apply anterior traction to the conjunctiva so that the
conjunctival hole does not overlie the scleral track.
ÔÇó¯Çü The needle is advanced perpendicularly through the sclera towards the centre of the eyeball and the required volume
of drug (usually 0.05 ml) is injected into the vitreous cavity. Some practitioners try to ÔÇÿstepÔÇÖ the needle track.
ÔÇó¯Çü The needle is removed and discarded.
ÔÇó¯Çü It has become usual practice not to use post-injection antibiotics as there is a suggestion that serial use may increase
the rate of infection by promoting bacterial resistance.
ÔÇó¯Çü Elevated IOP can occlude the central retinal artery and it is important routinely to ensure this remains perfused after
the procedure by checking the patientÔÇÖs vision (subjectively is adequate), directly visualizing the artery, or optimally
by checking the IOP (particularly in glaucoma patients). If occlusion occurs, urgent paracentesis should be carried out;
simply lying down may restore blood ¯Ç×ow.
ÔÇó¯Çü A clear plastic eye shield may be used until the local anaesthetic has worn o¯Çø and during sleep for the ¯ÇØrst night or two,
but practice varies.

¯Çü Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (see below).
¯Çü Central serous chorioretinopathy (see below).
¯Çü Pachychoroid neovasculopathy. ¯Ç×is is possibly an early stage
of polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy and features macular
neovascularization above focal areas of pachychoroid. ¯Ç×e
condition is treated with anti-VEGF injections combined with
full-dose PDT.
¯Çü Pachychoroid pigment epitheliopathy (PPE). ¯Ç×is condition
manifests with alteration of the retinal pigment epithelium,
usually without neurosensory detachment, although this may
occasionally occur. Patients present with normal visual acuity
and large choroidal vessels beneath elevations of RPE and subRPE drusen-like deposits.
¯Çü Peripapillary pachychoroid syndrome. ¯Ç×is rare condition
tends to a¯ÇÜect elderly males. It is characterised by peripapillary
choroidal thickening associated with intraretinal ¯Ç£uid and/or
subretinal ¯Ç£uid on the nasal side of the fovea and occasionally
optic disc oedema. ¯Ç×e condition is similar to chronic central
serous retinopathy. Macular neovascularization is not present.
Photodynamic therapy with vertepor¯ÇØn may be considered,
but is not always needed.

## Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy

## Introduction

Polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy (PCV, also called: pachychoroid
aneurysmal type 1 choroidal neovascularization) is characterized by
a branching vascular network of inner choroidal vessels with multiple terminal aneurysmal protuberances that appear to be the source
of bleeding and exudation. It is more common in patients of African and East Asian ethnic origin than in White patients and is more
common in women than men (5:1). ¯Ç×e disease is o¯ÇÖen bilateral
but tends to be asymmetrical. Risk factors include cigarette smoking, increased body mass index and increased levels of in¯Ç£ammatory
biomarkers (e.g. C-reactive protein). Overall, it is relatively common
and the presence of prominent haemorrhage should lead to the consideration of PCV, particularly if there is an absence of drusen and
the patient is relatively young and Asian or African.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Presentation is usually in late middle age with the sudden
onset of unilateral visual impairment.

602 Pachychoroid Disease Spectrum

{ Terminal swellings are frequently visible as reddish-orange

{ Multiple recurrent serosanguineous retinal and RPE
detachments.

{ Deterioration can be slow with intermittent bleeding and
leakage, resulting in macular damage and visual loss. Up to
50% may have a favourable outlook, with eventual spontaneous resolution of exudation and haemorrhage.
¯Çü FA shows an intense localized area of hyper¯Ç£uorescence (Fig.
14.41B).
¯Çü ICGA is the key investigation in PCV.

(Fig. 14.41C F).

## Central serous chorioretinopathy

## Overview

Central serous chorioretinopathy (CSR) is an idiopathic disorder
characterized by a localized serous detachment of the sensory retina at the macula secondary to leakage from the choriocapillaris
through one or more hyperpermeable RPE sites (see Fig. 14.13).
CSR has been classi¯ÇØed as one of the ÔÇÿpachychoroidÔÇÖ spectrum disorders; in eyes with unilateral CSR almost two-thirds of fellow eyes
have signs of pachychoroid pigment epitheliopathy. CSR typically
a¯ÇÜects one eye of a young or middle-aged White man. ¯Ç×e male to
female ratio is 3:1; females with CSR tend to be older. It presents in
two forms: acute (self-resolving within 3ÔÇô6 months) and chronic.
A SNP involving the complement factor H gene on chromosome
1 predisposes to the chronic form of CSR in White individuals.
¯Ç×e use of steroids in any form (including endogenous Cushing
syndrome) is signi¯ÇØcantly linked to this condition. Numerous
other risk factors and associations have been reported (including
Helicobacter pylori infection, renal dialysis, systemic hypertension,
psychological stress, pregnancy and sleep apnoea syndrome).

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. Unilateral blurring, metamorphopsia, micropsia
and mild dyschromatopsia.
¯Çü Signs

{ VA is typically 6/9ÔÇô6/18, but may improve with a lowstrength convex lens (correction of acquired hypermetropia from retinal elevation).

{ Round or oval detachment of the sensory retina at the
macula (Fig. 14.42A and see Fig. 14.13).

{ ¯Ç×e subretinal ¯Ç£uid may be clear (particularly in early
lesions) or turbid. Precipitates may be present on the posterior retinal surface.

{ One or more depigmented RPE foci (o¯ÇÖen small PEDs)
of variable size may be visible within the neurosensory
detachment. Small patches of RPE atrophy (Fig. 14.42B)
and hyperplasia elsewhere in the posterior pole may indicate the site of previous lesions and are typically seen easily
on FAF imaging (Fig. 14.42C).

{ Chronic lesions may be associated with substantial underlying atrophic change. Fluid can sometimes track downwards in a gravity-dependent fashion (gravitational tract),
best shown on FAF imaging (Fig. 14.42D) and can occasionally progress to bullous CSR (see below).

{ Choroidal thickening may be demonstrated.

## Treatment

¯Ç×e favourable prognosis without treatment in a signi¯ÇØcant proportion of cases should be borne in mind and asymptomatic
polyps may be observed.
¯Çü Combination treatment. ¯Ç×e results of combination therapy
of ranibizumab plus vertepor¯ÇØn PDT are superior to ranibizumab monotherapy in respect of best-corrected visual acuity
and complete polyp regression. Fewer injections are required
(EVEREST-II trial).
¯Çü Anti-VEGF agents are less e¯ÇÜective than when they are used
in typical MNV, but may suppress leakage and bleeding.
¯Çü PDT alone may lead to regression of polyps and result in an
extended treatment e¯ÇÜect. Early PDT provides a better outcome compared to delayed PDT. Consider PDT as a rescue
therapy in an eye with PCV that is refractory to a course of
anti-VEGF monotherapy.
¯Çü Laser photocoagulation of feeder vessels or polyps may
be e¯ÇÜective in selected cases, speci¯ÇØcally if the changes are
juxta-foveal.

TIP In a patient with AMD who has not responded to antiVEGF treatment after 3 months, undertake ICGA to check for
polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy.

{ Spontaneous resolution within 3ÔÇô6 months, with return to
near-normal or normal vision in around 80%. Recurrence
is seen in up to 50%.

{ Some patients (about 15%) follow a chronic course lasting
more than 12 months. Prolonged detachment is associated
with gradual photoreceptor and RPE degeneration and
permanently reduced vision. Multiple recurrent attacks
may also give a similar clinical picture.

{ ¯Ç×e optic disc should be examined to exclude a congenital
pit as the cause of a neurosensory detachment (see Ch. 19).
