## Investigation

¯Çü Amsler grid con¯ÇØrms metamorphopsia corresponding to the
neurosensory detachment.
¯Çü OCT shows an optically empty neurosensory elevation. Other
¯ÇØndings may include one or more smaller RPE detachments
(Fig. 14.42E), precipitates on the posterior surface of detached
retina and thickened choroid. Degenerative changes may be
seen in chronic or recurrent cases.
¯Çü FA shows an early hyper¯Ç£uorescent spot that gradually
enlarges (an ÔÇÿink blotÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 14.43A and B) or, less commonly,
forms a vertical column (ÔÇÿsmokestackÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 14.43C and D)

followed by di¯ÇÜusion throughout the detached area. An underlying PED may be demonstrated. Multiple focal leaks or diffuse areas of leakage can be evident, particularly in chronic or
recurrent disease.
¯Çü FAF shows a focal decrease in fundus auto¯Ç£uorescence at the
leakage site and at sites of old lesions (see Fig. 14.42C and D).
A gravitational tract is sometimes seen.
¯Çü ICGA. ¯Ç×e early phase may show dilated or compromised
choroidal vessels at the posterior pole and the mid-stage areas
of hyper¯Ç£uorescence due to choroidal hyperpermeability. Subclinical foci are commonly visible.

## Management

¯Çü Observation is appropriate in many cases. All treatment
modalities can be associated with RPE tear formation, which
can also occur spontaneously.
¯Çü Corticosteroid treatment should be discontinued, if possible,
particularly in chronic, recurrent or severe cases.
¯Çü Oral spironolactone (40 mg twice daily) may result in faster
resorption of subretinal ¯Ç£uid than no treatment in acute CSR.

606 Idiopathic Macular Telangiectasia

A B

¯ÇØA¯Ç× Prior to treatment; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× after successful treatment.

¯Çü Laser. Subthreshold (micropulse) diode laser to the RPE site of
leakage has shown good results (Fig. 14.44) in several studies
and is associated with signi¯ÇØcantly less retinal damage on OCT
than conventional photocoagulation.
¯Çü PDT with vertepor¯ÇØn at 30ÔÇô50% of the dose used for MNV
in conjunction with 50% light intensity typically leads to
complete resolution, including in severe chronic cases and is
associated with a considerably lower incidence of signi¯ÇØcant
choroidal ischaemia than higher-intensity regimens.
¯Çü Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents are commonly used in
conjunction with PDT. However, bevacizumab alone is
not helpful unless there is evidence of coexisting macular
neovascularization.
¯Çü Others. Case reports show bene¯ÇØt with a variety of agents,
including aspirin, beta-blockers and mifepristone, but controlled assessment is limited to date.

TIP All forms of corticosteroid treatment should be
discontinued if possible in patients with chronic or recurrent
central serous chorioretinopathy.

PERIPHERAL EXUDATIVE
HAEMORRHAGIC
CHORIORETINOPATHY

Peripheral exudative haemorrhagic chorioretinopathy (PEHCR) is
an uncommon disorder a¯ÇÜecting predominantly older women. It
typically manifests with peripheral retinal haemorrhage, exudation
and PED associated with MNV. It is bilateral in a few patients. ¯Ç×e
visual prognosis is o¯ÇÖen good, but is poor if the macula is involved.
¯Ç×e aetiology is unknown, though it may be a form of neovascular
AMD. Some cases share characteristics with polypoidal choroidal
vasculopathy. Treatment for sight-threatening disease generally
consists of intravitreal anti-VEGF injection.

IDIOPATHIC MACULAR
NEOVASCULARIZATION

Idiopathic MNV is an uncommon condition that a¯ÇÜects individuals under the age of 50 years and is usually unilateral. ¯Ç×e diagnosis is one of exclusion of other possible associations of MNV in
younger patients, such as angioid streaks, high myopia and chorioretinal in¯Ç£ammatory conditions (such as presumed ocular histoplasmosis). ¯Ç×e condition carries a better visual prognosis than
that associated with AMD and in some cases spontaneous resolution may occur. Treatment is typically with an anti-VEGF agent.

IDIOPATHIC MACULAR
TELANGIECTASIA

Idiopathic macular telangiectasia (IMT) is a condition of unknown
pathogenesis. It may be more common than previously believed
and can be confused with DR, prior RVO and other causes of
macular vascular changes. A family history is present in a small
proportion of cases.

Type 1: aneurysmal telangiectasia

¯Ç×is may be closely related to Coats disease, or more speci¯ÇØcally
the milder form of Coats previously known as Leber miliary aneurysms. It generally involves only one eye and both the peripheral
retina and macula can be a¯ÇÜected. Patients are typically middleaged males.
¯Çü Symptoms. MildÔÇômoderate blurring of vision in one eye.
¯Çü Signs

{ Chronic leakage and lipid deposition (Fig. 14.45A).

{ Telangiectasia and microaneurysms. Early signs may be
subtle and more readily detected on red-free photography.

{ Larger aneurysms form as the condition progresses.

{ Macular oedema, including cystoid changes.

C D

¯Çü OCT demonstrates retinal thickening, CMO and localized

¯Çü FA shows telangiectasia and multiple capillary, venular and
arteriolar aneurysms (Fig. 14.45A C) with late leakage and
CMO (Fig. 14.45D). ¯Ç×ere is minimal non-perfusion.
¯Çü Treatment is with laser to points and areas of leakage, but can
be technically di¯Çøcult depending on the proximity of changes
to the foveola. Intravitreal VEGF inhibitors may be e¯ÇÜective.

Type 2: perifoveal telangiectasia
¯ÇØMacTel¯Ç×

MacTel is primarily a neurodegenerative condition that is usually
bilateral, although asymmetry and even apparent unilaterality is
possible. MacTel is associated with changes in the inner and outer
retina, ultimately leading to photoreceptor atrophy, development
of retinal clumps and abnormal neovascular complexes. Degeneration of M├╝ller cells is thought to be an important pathogenic

mechanism. It is a genetically complex disease which has been
associated with reduced serum levels of serine. ¯Ç×e prevalence may
have been underestimated in the past and could be as high as 0.1%
in people over the age of 40 years (about 2 million worldwide).
¯Ç×e age of onset is most commonly in the 6th and 7th decades of
life, but much earlier cases have been described. Clinically relevant
manifestations are con¯ÇØned to the ÔÇÿMacTel areaÔÇÖ, an oval region
with a radius of 6 [o] horizontally and 5 [o] vertically centred on the
foveola, with larger areas only involved when a secondary neovascular membrane develops. Atrophy of the photoreceptor layer is
responsible for the visual loss in advanced cases.
¯Çü Symptoms. Blurring in one or both eyes, manifesting in the
early stages with di¯Çøculty in reading. Visual distortion may
be a feature.
¯Çü Signs

{ Early changes are subtle, manifesting as a lack of foveolar
re¯Ç£ex (Fig. 14.46A). OCT shows the pathological changes
at this stage (Fig. 14.46B).

Preliminary studies of an intravitreal implant (Renexus) which

Type 3: occlusive telangiectasia

¯Ç×is extremely rare condition presents in late middle age and carries
a poor visual prognosis. ¯Ç×e manifestations relate to capillary occlusion rather than telangiectasia (progressive occlusion of parafoveal
capillaries with marked aneurysmal dilatation of terminal capillaries). It is distinct from type 1 and type 2 macular telangiectasia.

CYSTOID MACULAR OEDEMA

## Introduction

Cystoid macular oedema (CMO) results from the accumulation of
¯Ç£uid in the outer plexiform and inner nuclear layers of the retina
with the formation of tiny cyst-like cavities (Fig. 14.47A). Fluid
may initially accumulate intracellularly in M├╝ller cells, with subsequent rupture. Coalescence of smaller cavities may occur over
time with subsequent progression to a foveal lamellar hole with
irreversible impairment of central vision. CMO is a non-speci¯ÇØc
manifestation of any type of macular oedema. Causes include:
¯Çü Ocular surgery and laser, e.g. phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation, panretinal
photocoagulation and miscellaneous other procedures.
¯Çü Retinal vascular disease, e.g. diabetic retinopathy, retinal vein
occlusion.
¯Çü In¯Ç×ammation, e.g. intermediate uveitis, severe or chronic
uveitis of any kind.
¯Çü Drug-induced, e.g. topical prostaglandin derivatives.
¯Çü Retinal dystrophies, e.g. retinitis pigmentosa.
¯Çü Conditions involving vitreomacular traction, e.g. epiretinal
membrane.
¯Çü MNV.
¯Çü Fundus tumours, e.g. retinal capillary haemangioma.
¯Çü Systemic disease, e.g. chronic renal failure.

## Diagnosis

{ Aneurysms are uncommon, but have been reported. Vascular leakage is mild; exudation with cystoid macular
oedema is not a feature of this disease.
¯Çü OCT is a key aid to the diagnosis. ¯Ç×e formation of hypore¯Ç£ective inner retinal cavities of variable size, morphologically distinct from those seen in CMO, are a characteristic ¯ÇØnding (see

disease is commonly seen underlying the fovea. ¯Ç×inning and disruption of the photoreceptor layers is also common, but is a late
sign. Pigment clumps are shown as intraretinal hyper-re¯Ç£ective
plaques with posterior shadowing. ¯Ç×ere is usually no overall
macular thickening, because outer retinal thinning is counteracted by inner retinal thickening. Hyper-re¯Ç£ective inner retinal
dots corresponding to telangiectatic vessels may be present.
¯Çü FAF changes occur early in the disease course and may precede clinically detectable signs. Heightened foveal auto¯Ç£uorescence is a common early ¯ÇØnding, which gradually increases in
extent (Fig. 14.46D).
¯Çü FA in early disease shows bilateral perifoveal telangiectasia
with early leakage from the abnormal vessels progressing to
di¯ÇÜuse leakage, though without CMO. ¯Ç×e cystoid spaces
identi¯ÇØable on OCT do not show hyper¯Ç£uorescence on FA. FA
is also used to con¯ÇØrm MNV (Fig. 14.46E and F).
¯Çü Macular pigment optical density (MPOD) imaging shows a
possibly pathognomonic pattern of oval reduction in density
corresponding to the late distribution of hyper¯Ç£uorescence on
FA. MPOD is preserved from 6┬░ outwards.
¯Çü Prognosis. Visual acuity generally does not deteriorate to less than
6/60, with an average acuity of 6/12. ¯Ç×e development of subretinal neovascularization is most o¯ÇÖen preceded by enlargement of
pigment clumps. Spread of the pigment increases the risk of developing poor vision and proliferative disease, particularly in females.
MNV in MacTel tends to carry a better prognosis than in AMD.
¯Çü Treatment. Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents can decrease leakage on FA in the non-proliferative stage but are not helpful

¯Çü Symptoms may include blurring, distortion and micropsia.
¯Çü Signs

{ Loss of the foveal depression, thickening of the retina
and multiple cystoid areas in the sensory retina (Fig.
14.47B), best seen with red-free light using a fundus
contact lens.

{ Optic disc swelling is sometimes present.

{ A lamellar hole may be visible.

{ Features of associated disease.
¯Çü Amsler chart demonstrates central blurring and distortion.
¯Çü FA. A petaloid pattern is seen due to dye accumulation in
microcystic spaces in the outer plexiform layer (Fig. 14.47C).
¯Çü OCT shows retinal thickening with cystic hypore¯Ç£ective
spaces and loss of the foveal depression (Fig. 14.47D). A lamellar hole may be demonstrated in advanced cases.

MICROCYSTIC MACULAR OEDEMA

Microcystic changes of the inner nuclear layer distinct from classic CMO can occur in eyes with optic neuritis and some other
forms of optic neuropathy. It is believed to be caused by retrograde degeneration of the inner retinal layers that manifests with
impaired ¯Ç£uid resorption.

VITREOMACULAR INTERFACE
DISORDERS

## Epiretinal membrane

## Introduction

An epiretinal membrane (ERM) is a transparent, avascular, ¯ÇØbrocellular structure that develops on or above the surface of the
retina. Proliferation of the cellular component and contraction of

the membrane leads to visual symptoms, primarily due to retinal
wrinkling, obstruction and localized elevation with or without
pseudocyst formation and CMO.
¯Çü Idiopathic

{ No apparent cause, as a relatively common ageing process.
¯Ç×e prevalence of early ERM (cellophane) is approximately
6.5% and advanced ERM (preretinal macular ¯ÇØbrosis),
approximately 2.5%.

{ Residual vitreous tissue remains on the retinal surface following cortical separation in around 50% of eyes, with subsequent
proliferation. ¯Ç×e predominant cellular constituent is glial
cells, probably derived from the indigenous posterior hyaloid
membrane (PHM) cell population (laminocytes). ERM development can occur at any stage of posterior vitreous detachment (PVD). It is now believed that the process of PVD from
initiation to completion o¯ÇÖen extends over the course of years.

{ About 10% are bilateral.

{ Tend to be milder than secondary ERMs.

¯Çü Secondary

{ Occur following retinal detachment surgery (most frequent cause of secondary ERM), retinal break, panretinal
photocoagulation, retinal cryotherapy, retinal vascular disease, in¯Ç£ammation and trauma.

{ Binocularity is dependent on whether both eyes are
a¯ÇÜected by the causative factors.

{ Cell type more varied, with prominent pigment cells ÔÇô
thought to be derived from the RPE.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Blurring and metamorphopsia. Mild cases are
o¯ÇÖen asymptomatic.
¯Çü Signs

{ VA is highly variable, depending on severity.

{ An irregular translucent sheen (cellophane maculopathy)
is present in early ERM, o¯ÇÖen best detected using green
(red-free) light (Fig. 14.48A).

{ As the membrane thickens and contracts it becomes more
obvious (macular pucker) and typically causes mild distortion of blood vessels (Fig. 14.48B).

{ Advanced ERM may give severe distortion of blood vessels, marked retinal wrinkling and striae and may obscure
underlying structures (Fig. 14.48C).

Full¯Çƒthickness macular hole

## Introduction

Full-thickness macular hole (FTMH) is a relatively common cause
of central visual loss, with a prevalence of approximately 3:1000.
¯Ç×e onset is most common in females aged 60ÔÇô70. Careful examination of the fellow eye is important as macular holes are bilateral
on presentation in about 30%. ¯Ç×e role of vitreomacular traction
(VMT) in the aetiology of macular hole has increasingly been
de¯ÇØned over recent years and is considered at greater length as a
separate topic later in this chapter. A new OCT-based classi¯ÇØcation has been published by the International Vitreomacular Traction Study (IVTS) Group and replaces the older Gass classi¯ÇØcation
of macular hole, which was based on careful clinical examination
and divided macular holes into four stages. Other causes of fullthickness macular hole include high myopia (which can lead to
macular retinal detachment) and blunt ocular trauma. Lesions that
may sometimes have a similar appearance include macular pseudohole and lamellar hole (see ÔÇÿVitreoretinal tractionÔÇÖ below). ¯Ç×e
clinical appearance of a macular hole is typical, but the di¯ÇÜerential diagnosis includes cystoid macular oedema, subfoveal drusen,
CSR and adult vitelliform macular dystrophy.

## Symptoms

Presentation of a full-thickness dehiscence may be with impairment of central vision in one eye, or as a relatively asymptomatic
deterioration, ¯ÇØrst noticed when the fellow eye is occluded or
at a routine sight test. Symptoms are absent or mild prior to the
development of a full-thickness lesion. Metamorphopsia may be
present.

Signs and OCT features

¯Çü Vitreomacular adhesion ÔÇô VMA: no distortion of the foveal
contour. ¯Ç×is term was proposed to denote the OCT ¯ÇØnding
of oblique foveal vitreoretinal traction before the appearance
of clinical changes.
¯Çü Vitreomacular traction ÔÇô VMT: distortion of foveal contour
in the absence of a hole. Clinically stage 1a: ÔÇÿImpendingÔÇÖ macular hole. Appears as ¯Ç£attening of the foveal depression with an
underlying yellow spot. Pathologically, the inner retinal layers
detach from the underlying photoreceptor layer, o¯ÇÖen with the
formation of a cyst-like schisis cavity. With loss of structural
support, the photoreceptor layer commonly undergoes centrifugal displacement. Clinically stage 1b: occult macular hole,
indicates foveal detachment and is characterised by a yellowish
ring (Fig. 14.49A).
¯Çü Small or medium FTMH with VMT: small is less than
250 ╬╝m or medium is 250ÔÇô400 ╬╝m measured at its narrowest
point with OCT. Clinically stage 2: small full-thickness hole
measuring less than 400 ╬╝m in diameter. ¯Ç×e defect may be
central, slightly eccentric or crescent-shaped (Fig. 14.49B). A
dehiscence is present in the inner retina with persistent vitreofoveolar adhesion. ¯Ç×e posterior hyaloid remains attached to
the fovea. Nearly all will progress to the next stage, with further visual decline.

{ Associated ¯ÇØndings may include macular pseudohole
(Fig. 14.48D), CMO, retinal telangiectasia and small
haemorrhages.
¯Çü Amsler grid testing typically shows distortion.
¯Çü OCT shows a highly re¯Ç£ective surface layer associated with
retinal thickening (Fig. 14.48E). Disruption of the innersegment/outer-segment junction may be associated with a
worse visual outcome following surgery. OCT is also useful to
exclude signi¯ÇØcant vitreomacular traction (see later).
¯Çü FA has been superseded by OCT for routine assessment
of ERM, but if undertaken it highlights vascular tortuosity and demonstrates leakage. It is sometimes indicated to
investigate the cause of an ERM, such as a prior retinal vein
occlusion.

## Treatment

¯Çü Observation if the membrane is mild and non-progressive.

## Spontaneous resolution of visual symptoms sometimes

occurs, typically due to complete separation of the ERM
from the retina. CMO or tractional detachment may require
fairly prompt surgery to minimize secondary degenerative
change.
¯Çü Surgical removal of the membrane via vitrectomy usually
improves or eliminates distortion (the main bene¯ÇØt), with an
improvement in visual acuity of at least two lines in around
75% or more; in about a quarter VA is unchanged and around
2% get worse. ¯Ç×e surgical complications are principally those
occurring a¯ÇÖer vitrectomy (see Ch. 16). Removal of the internal limiting membrane (ILM) in concert with ERM peeling
may be bene¯ÇØcial, but remains controversial. Visual improvement commonly does not occur for several months postoperatively. Recurrence is rare.

¯Çü Large FTMH with VMT: greater than 400 ╬╝m in diameter.
Clinically stage 3: large full-thickness macular hole measuring more than 400 ╬╝m associated with a red base in which
yellowÔÇôwhite dots may be seen (Fig. 14.49C). A surrounding
grey cu¯ÇÜ of subretinal ¯Ç£uid is usually present (Fig. 14.49D) and

an overlying retinal operculum (sometimes called a pseudooperculum) may be visible. Visual acuity is commonly reduced
to 6/60, but is occasionally better, particularly with eccentric
¯ÇØxation. An operculum (see Fig. 14.50C) consists primarily of

photoreceptor elements. By de¯ÇØnition, there is persistent parafoveal attachment of the vitreous cortex.
¯Çü Small, medium or large FTMH without VMT. Clinically
stage 4: full-thickness macular hole with complete posterior
vitreous detachment, suggested (but not con¯ÇØrmed) by the
presence of a Weiss ring. A signi¯ÇØcant proportion of idiopathic
macular holes have an associated ERM.
¯Çü Spontaneously resolved macular hole. Macular holes may
heal spontaneously, o¯ÇÖen resuming a near-normal or even
normal clinical and OCT appearance. A tiny outer retinal ÔÇô
o¯ÇÖen inner-segment/outer-segment junction ÔÇô or other subfoveolar defect may persist a¯ÇÖer spontaneous or surgical closure.
Solar retinopathy commonly gives a similar appearance, as
does scarring from several other conditions where the foveal
centre is a focus of damage.
¯Çü Macular microhole refers to a small (<150 ╬╝m) fullthickness foveal retinal defect and is synonymous with a
small full-thickness macular hole (stage 2 above). Symptoms
are often minimal and may consist of mild central blurring,
metamorphopsia (distortion) or disturbed reading vision.

## Visual acuity is typically impaired less than with a larger

FTMH and the abnormality may not be noticed immediately. The full-thickness defect typically heals within a few
weeks and by the time of initial examination only a welldemarcated red spot rather than a visibly full-thickness
defect may be present. At this stage OCT may show only a
small defect in the inner-segment/outer-segment layer (see

## Investigations

¯Çü Amsler grid testing will usually show non-speci¯ÇØc central distortion rather than a scotoma.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe WatzkeÔÇôAllen test is performed by projecting a narrow
slit beam over the centre of the hole vertically and horizontally, preferably using a fundus contact lens. A patient with a
macular hole will report that the beam is thinned or broken.

## Patients with other pathology usually see a distorted beam of

uniform thickness.
¯Çü OCT is extremely useful in diagnosis and staging (Fig. 14.50A
D) and can show the dynamic nature of the pathology.

the posterior hyaloid face; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× FTMH with intraretinal cystic spaces and overlying operculum

¯Çü FAF shows a markedly hyper¯Ç£uorescent foveolar spot in stages
3 and 4 and punctate ¯Ç£uorescence in stage 2.
¯Çü FA in a full-thickness hole shows an early well-de¯ÇØned window defect. In light of the e¯ÇÜectiveness of OCT in this condition there is no need to undertake ¯Ç£uorescein angiography.

TIP OCT is key to the con¯ÇØrmation of the diagnosis of macular
hole and for staging purposes.

## Treatment

¯Çü ¯Çƒe clinical stage and duration are the most important variables that in¯Ç£uence the management and prognosis.
¯Çü Observation. About 50% of stage 1 holes resolve following
spontaneous vitreo-foveolar separation, so these are managed
conservatively. About 10% of full-thickness holes also close
spontaneously, sometimes with marked visual improvement.

Spontaneous resolution is more common with smaller FTMH.
Treatment is not needed for spontaneously healed holes,
though vitrectomy with ERM peeling is sometimes indicated.

## Patients whose other eye is healthy should be warned that

the risk of the fellow eye becoming involved within 5 years is
around 10%. ¯Ç×e risk is less if there is already a PVD present.
¯Çü Pharmacological vitreolysis with ocriplasmin may be suitable
for small early-stage holes. It is discussed under ÔÇÿVitreomacular tractionÔÇÖ below.
¯Çü Intravitreal gas or air. Preliminary studies suggest that closure may follow an injection of a small bolus of gas into the
vitreous.
¯Çü Surgery may be considered in stage 2 or greater holes and
for some lamellar holes. Symptom duration before surgery is
strongly and consistently associated with poorer anatomical and
visual outcome a¯ÇÖer surgery. Better results are usually achieved
in patients with smaller holes present for under 6 months.

{ Operative treatment consists of vitrectomy, together with
(a) peeling of the ILM facilitated by vital dye staining, (b)
relief of vitreomacular traction by either induction of a
total PVD if not already present or removal of the perifoveal vitreous and (c) gas tamponade. ¯Ç×e reattachment
rate is improved by postoperative face-down positioning
(8 hours daily for 5 days). An extensive period of postoperative positioning is not required with modern techniques.

{ ¯Ç×e hole is closed in most cases and visual improvement
occurs over the course of months in 80ÔÇô90% of eyes, with
a ¯ÇØnal visual acuity of 6/12 or better in approximately
65%. Worsening of visual acuity occurs in up to 10% of
eyes. Mild residual abnormality on OCT such as a defect
in the inner-segment/outer-segment junction or other
disturbance adjacent to the RPE is common and may also
provide a diagnostic indicator of a spontaneously healed
macular hole or microhole.

{ Complications are the same as those occurring a¯ÇÖer vitrectomy (see Ch. 16).

## Vitreomacular traction

## Introduction

Physiological PVD (see Ch. 16) usually proceeds gradually over
an extended period to encompass complete separation from the
macula and optic nerve head: complete PVD. If adhesion of the gel
persists at the central macula, an anomalous PVD is present and
can precede a range of macular conditions.
¯Çü Vitreomacular adhesion (VMA) refers to residual attachment of
the vitreous within a 3 mm radius of the central macula in the
presence of perifoveal vitreous separation. In most cases it constitutes a stage in a dynamic process of PVD so may not incur
pathological sequelae. ¯Ç×ere is no distortion of the foveal contour
or any secondary retinal changes. Focal VMA involves an area of
attachment of Ôëñ1500 ╬╝m diameter, broad VMA >1500 ╬╝m.

¯Çü Vitreomacular traction (VMT) is de¯ÇØned as the presence of
retinal changes on OCT with evident perifoveal (within 3 mm)
PVD. Distortion of the foveal surface contour and/or other
structural retinal changes may be present. Focal (Fig. 14.51A)
and broad types are de¯ÇØned as for VMA. Concurrent VMT is
associated with other macular disease, e.g. AMD, RVO, DR.
Isolated VMT is not associated with other macular disease.
¯Çü Full-thickness macular hole (FTMH). A foveal lesion featuring interruption of all retinal layers from the internal limiting
membrane to the RPE (see above) (Fig. 14.51B). Other considerations include the status of the vitreous (with or without
VMT) and the presence of an identi¯ÇØable cause such as trauma.
¯Çü Lamellar macular hole. ¯Ç×is is a partial-thickness defect
of the inner retina at the fovea but maintenance of an intact
photoreceptor layer (see Fig. 14.51A). Its pathogenesis is
incompletely de¯ÇØned, but may develop from anomalous PVD,
sometimes following a foveal pseudocyst, or represent abortive
FTMH formation in some patients. Classically, lamellar hole
was described as a sequel to CMO.
¯Çü Macular pseudohole. ¯Ç×is lesion mimics the clinical appearance of a FTMH, but is caused by distortion of the perifoveal
retina into heaped edges by ERM, without any loss of retinal tissue and near-normal foveal thickness; there is a central defect in
the membrane. VMT may be present (see Fig. 14.48D).
¯Çü Epiretinal membrane (ERM). ERM is independent of the
IVTS classi¯ÇØcation, but most eyes with broad vitreomacular
traction have an associated ERM. Vitreous remnants on the
retinal surface following PVD provide a mechanism for the
development of idiopathic ERM.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms in VMT include decreased vision, metamorphopsia, photopsia and micropsia, but are usually milder in lamellar
holes and pseudoholes and absent in VMA.
¯Çü Signs in VMT may include retinal surface thickening, wrinkling and distortion, foveal pseudocyst, CMO, macular schisis

A B

operculum.

616 Degenerative Myopia

or detachment and capillary leakage. ¯Ç×e limit of the attached
gel may be visible as a whitish band or re¯Ç£ex. Visible changes
may be subtle. Both lamellar holes and pseudoholes can appear
as a discrete reddish oval or round foveal spot.
¯Çü OCT is the key investigation.

## Treatment

Treatment of FTMH and ERM is considered under separate topics
above.
¯Çü Observation. Spontaneous separation occurs in a proportion of patients with VMT and is more likely in milder cases.
Observation is appropriate in patients with VMA and in many
cases of VMT.
¯Çü Pharmacological vitreolysis. Intravitreal injection of ocriplasmin, a recombinant form of human plasmin, releases
VMT in over 25% of eyes and may close macular holes where
VMT is present (40% versus 10% with placebo). Results are
generally inferior to vitrectomy. Smaller areas of VMT and
smaller macular holes have better outcomes. ¯Ç×e presence of
ERM is a poorer prognostic indicator for ocriplasmin treatment. Di¯ÇÜuse retinal dysfunction of uncertain mechanism,
including substantially decreased acuity, has been reported as
a rare side e¯ÇÜect.
¯Çü Pars plana vitrectomy with peeling of the adherent area and
any associated ERM usually gives good results in VMT. ¯Ç×e
bene¯ÇØt of vitrectomy for lamellar and pseudoholes is less clearcut, but is probably greater if signi¯ÇØcant ERM is present.

DEGENERATIVE MYOPIA

## Introduction

## Myopia is the result of complex hereditary and environmental

factors. ¯Ç×ere is strong evidence for a causative association with
long-term intensive near visual activity, particularly reading
and using personal computers. A refractive error of more than
ÔêÆ6 dioptres constitutes a common de¯ÇØnition of high myopia, in
which axial length is usually greater than 26 mm. It a¯ÇÜects over
2% of adult Western European or American populations and may
be as high as 10% in East Asians. Pathological or degenerative
myopia is characterized by progressive anteroposterior elongation of the scleral envelope associated with a range of secondary ocular changes, principally thought to relate to mechanical
stretching of the involved tissues. It is a signi¯ÇØcant cause of legal
blindness, with maculopathy the most common cause of visual
loss.
Low-dose atropine (0.01%) instilled at bed-time can signi¯ÇØcantly slow the progression of myopia in children. Although there
is no consensus, a reasonable approach is to o¯ÇÜer this treatment
to children between the ages of 5 and 15 years whose myopia is
increasing at more than 1 dioptre per year.
¯Ç×ese children should also be encouraged to spend more time
outdoors in sunshine.
In children aged 7ÔÇô11 years with less than 5 dioptres of myopia, a high-add (+2.50D) centre-distance so¯ÇÖ multifocal contact lens can be considered. ¯Ç×e BLINK trial concludes that this

Table 14.4 Systemic Associations of High Myopia

## Down syndrome

## Stickler syndrome

## Marfan syndrome

## Prematurity

## Noonan syndrome

EhlersÔÇôDanlos syndrome
PierreÔÇôRobin syndrome

slows the rate of myopia progression and that the response is
dose-dependent

Table 14.4 lists systemic associations of high myopia.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü A pale tessellated (tigroid) appearance is due to di¯ÇÜuse attenuation of the RPE with visibility of large choroidal vessels (Fig.
14.52A).
¯Çü Focal chorioretinal atrophy is characterized by patchy visibility of choroidal vessels and o¯ÇÖen sclera (Fig. 14.52B).
¯Çü Anomalous optic nerve head. ¯Ç×is may appear unusually
small, large or anomalous with a ÔÇÿtiltedÔÇÖ conformation (Fig.
14.52C). Peripapillary chorioretinal atrophy is very common,
most commonly as a temporal crescent of thinned or absent
RPE.
¯Çü Acquired optic disc pit formation is not uncommon and is
thought to be due to expansion of the peripapillary region as
the eye enlarges over time.
¯Çü Lattice degeneration (see Ch. 16).
¯Çü Lacquer cracks are ruptures in the RPEÔÇôBruch membraneÔÇô
choriocapillaris complex characterized by ¯ÇØne irregular yellow
lines criss-crossing at the posterior pole (Fig. 14.52D) in around
5% of highly myopic eyes and can be complicated by MNV.
¯Çü Subretinal ÔÇÿcoinÔÇÖ haemorrhages (Fig. 14.52E) may develop
from lacquer cracks in the absence of MNV.
¯Çü Fuchs spot (Fig. 14.52F) is a raised, circular, pigmented lesion
at the macula developing a¯ÇÖer a subretinal haemorrhage has
absorbed.
¯Çü Staphyloma is a peripapillary or macular ectasia of the posterior sclera (Fig. 14.53A and B) due to focal thinning and
expansion present in about a third of eyes with pathological
myopia. Associations include macular hole formation and
ÔÇÿdome-shaped maculaÔÇÖ (see below).
¯Çü (Peripapillary) intrachoroidal cavitation, formerly described
as peripapillary detachment of pathological myopia (PDPM),
may occur adjacent to the nerve, commonly inferiorly (Fig.
14.53C). Clinically, it may be evident as a small yellowishorange peripapillary area typically inferior to the disc. It can
usually be identi¯ÇØed on OCT (Fig. 14.53D). Visual ¯ÇØeld defects
are common and frequently mimic glaucoma.
¯Çü Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RD) is much more
common in high myopia, the pathogenesis including increased
frequency of PVD, lattice degeneration, asymptomatic atrophic holes, myopic macular holes (see below) and occasionally
giant retinal tears.
¯Çü MNV

{ 10% of highly myopic eyes develop MNV.

A B

C D

E F

{ ¯Ç×e prognosis is better in younger patients with myopiarelated MNV than in AMD.

{ Anti-VEGF therapy is generally the treatment of choice.
¯Ç×e injection frequency may be less than that needed for
AMD, but RD risk is higher.

¯Çü Macular retinoschisis (foveoschisis) and macular retinal
detachment without macular hole formation may occur in
highly myopic eyes with posterior staphyloma, probably as
a result of vitreous traction. Some cases are associated with
intrachoroidal cavitation (see above). Retinoschisis may be

intrachoroidal cavitation (ICC) (arrow marks edge of cavitation); ¯ÇØD¯Ç× OCT appearance of ICC

mistaken clinically for CMO and is better characterized by
OCT than biomicroscopy.
¯Çü Macular hole may occur spontaneously or a¯ÇÖer relatively mild
trauma and is associated with the development of rhegmatogenous retinal detachment much more commonly than agerelated idiopathic macular hole. Myopic macular retinoschisis
and myopic macular hole may be part of the same pathological
process. Vitrectomy may be e¯ÇÜective for both, but the best surgical technique remains unde¯ÇØned.
¯Çü Peripapillary detachment is an innocuous yellowÔÇôorange
elevation of the RPE and sensory retina at the inferior
border of the myopic conus (anomalous optic nerve head
complex).
¯Çü Cataract. Posterior subcapsular or early onset nuclear sclerotic.
¯Çü Glaucoma. ¯Ç×ere is an increased prevalence of primary
open-angle glaucoma, pigmentary glaucoma and steroid
responsiveness.
¯Çü Amblyopia is uncommon but may develop when there is a signi¯ÇØcant di¯ÇÜerence in myopia between the two eyes.

¯Çü Dislocation of the lens (natural or arti¯ÇØcial) is a rare but wellrecognized risk.

ANGIOID STREAKS

## Introduction

Angioid streaks are crack-like dehiscences in brittle thickened and
calci¯ÇØed Bruch membrane, associated with atrophy of the overlying RPE. Approximately 50% of patients with angioid streaks have
a systemic association.
¯Çü Pseudoxanthoma elasticum (PXE), a hereditary disorder of
connective tissue in which there is progressive calci¯ÇØcation,
fragmentation and degeneration of elastic ¯ÇØbres in the skin,
eye and cardiovascular system, is by far the most common
association of angioid streaks (Gr├ÂnbladÔÇôStrandberg syndrome). ¯Ç×e disorder is caused by mutations in the ABCC6
gene. Patients develop a ÔÇÿplucked chickenÔÇÖ appearance of the
skin, most commonly on the neck, axillae and antecubital

fossae. Approximately 85% of patients develop ocular involvement of variable severity, usually a¯ÇÖer the second decade of
life.
¯Çü Paget disease is a chronic, progressive metabolic bone disease
characterized by excessive and disorganized resorption and
formation of bone. Angioid streaks occur in only about 2%.
It is thought that calcium binds to the elastin of Bruch membrane, imparting brittleness and fragility.
¯Çü Haemoglobinopathies occasionally associated with angioid streaks are numerous, including sickle-cell trait and disease and thalassaemia. In these, a brittle Bruch membrane is
thought to be due to iron deposition.
¯Çü Miscellaneous other associations have been reported. ¯Ç×ere
is probably no link between angioid streaks and EhlersÔÇô
Danlos syndrome.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Signs

{ Grey or dark red linear lesions with irregular serrated
edges that intercommunicate in a ring-like fashion around
the optic disc and radiate outwards from the peripapillary
area (Fig. 14.54A). ¯Ç×e streaks tend to increase in width
and extent slowly over time.

{ ÔÇÿPeau dÔÇÖorangeÔÇÖ (orange skin), also known as leopard skin,
mottled yellowish speckling (Fig. 14.54B) is common, particularly in cases associated with PXE.

{ Optic disc drusen are frequently (up to 25%) associated
(Fig. 14.54C and D).

{ Scleral depression is relatively contraindicated in these
eyes, due to the risk of further damage to Bruch membrane
leading to new angioid streaks or choroidal rupture.
¯Çü Complications. ¯Ç×ough angioid streaks are typically asymptomatic at ¯ÇØrst, visual impairment occurs eventually in more
than 70% of patients.

{ MNV is by far the most common cause of visual loss.

{ Choroidal rupture may occur following relatively trivial
trauma (Fig. 14.54E).

CHOROIDAL FOLDS

## Introduction

## Choroidal folds are parallel grooves or striae involving the inner

choroid, Bruch membrane, the RPE and sometimes the retina (chorioretinal folds). ¯Ç×ey are likely to develop in association with any
process that induces su¯Çøcient compressive stress within the choroid, Bruch membrane and retina. Primary mechanisms include
choroidal congestion and scleral compression, and occasionally
tissue contraction. Choroidal folds should be distinguished from
retinal folds, which have a di¯ÇÜerent pathogenesis (usually ERM).
Causes include:
¯Çü Idiopathic (ÔÇÿcongenitalÔÇÖ) folds may be present in healthy, o¯ÇÖen
hypermetropic, individuals in whom visual acuity is typically
una¯ÇÜected. ¯Ç×e folds are usually bilateral. A syndrome of idiopathic acquired hypermetropia with choroidal folds has been
described ÔÇô in these patients elevated intracranial pressure
should always be excluded even without evident papilloedema
(see next) although a constricted scleral canal causing optic
disc congestion has been proposed as an alternative mechanism in some patients.
¯Çü Papilloedema. Choroidal folds may occur in patients with
chronically elevated intracranial pressure, when they may
be associated with reduction of visual acuity that may be
permanent.
¯Çü Orbital disease such as retrobulbar tumours and thyroid
ophthalmopathy may cause choroidal folds associated with
impaired vision.
¯Çü Ocular disease such as pachychoroid disease, choroidal
tumours, in¯Ç£ammation such as posterior scleritis, scleral
buckling for retinal detachment and hypotony.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. ¯Ç×e e¯ÇÜect on vision is variable and dependent on
the cause. Many patients are asymptomatic.
¯Çü Signs

{ Parallel lines, grooves or striae typically located at the posterior pole. ¯Ç×e folds are usually horizontally orientated (Fig.
14.55), but in pachychoroid disease tend to be diagonal.

{ ¯Ç×e crest (elevated portion) of a fold is yellow and less pigmented as a result of stretching and thinning of the RPE
and the trough is darker due to compression of the RPE.

{ Foveal involvement by a streak.
¯Çü Red-free photography demonstrates the streaks.
¯Çü FA shows hyperfluorescent window defects due to RPE
atrophy overlying the streaks, associated with variable associated hypofluorescence corresponding to RPE hyperplasia.
FA is generally indicated only if MNV is suspected (Fig.
14.54F).
¯Çü FAF. Streaks are auto¯Ç£uorescent. ¯Ç×ey are o¯ÇÖen more extensive than clinically, which may con¯ÇØrm the diagnosis in subtle
cases. Peau dÔÇÖorange is shown.

## Treatment

Following systemic investigation where appropriate, usually via
referral to an appropriate physician, observation is the approach
in most cases. Patients should be warned against participating
in contact sports and advised to use protective spectacles when
necessary. MNV should usually be treated with intravitreal
anti-VEGF agents, but commonly recurs or develops at a new
site.

{ Clinical examination should be directed towards the exclusion of optic disc swelling, as well as other ocular or orbital
pathology.
¯Çü OCT allows di¯ÇÜerentiation between choroidal, chorioretinal
and retinal folds.
¯Çü FAF e¯ÇÜectively demonstrates the folds and may show associated atrophy. If this is marked the appearance can be mistaken
for angioid streaks.
¯Çü FA shows hyper¯Ç£uorescent crests as a result of increased background choroidal ¯Ç£uorescence showing through the stretched
and thinned RPE and hypo¯Ç£uorescent troughs due to blockage of choroidal ¯Ç£uorescence by the compressed and thickened
RPE.

the macula; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× angioid streaks and optic disc drusen; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× infrared image of patient in ¯ÇØC¯Ç×; ¯ÇØE¯Ç×

¯Çü Supplementary imaging. Ultrasound, computed tomography (CT) or magnetic resonance (MR) scanning of the orbits
or brain may be indicated. In elevated intracranial pressure
or acquired hypermetropia with choroidal folds, an enlarged
perineural space may be noted on B-scanning and MR of the
optic nerves.

HYPOTONY MACULOPATHY

## Introduction

Maculopathy may occur in eyes developing hypotony, de¯ÇØned
as IOP less than 6 mmHg. ¯Ç×e most common cause is excessive
drainage following glaucoma ¯ÇØltration surgery, particularly when
antimetabolites have been used at the time of surgery (see Ch.
11). Other causes include trauma (cyclodialysis cle¯ÇÖ, penetrating
injury), chronic uveitis (by directly impairing ciliary body function and by tractional ciliary body detachment due to cyclitic
membrane) and retinal detachment. Systemic causes of hypotony
(usually bilateral) include dehydration, hyperglycaemia in uncontrolled diabetes, uraemia and treatment with hyperosmotic agents
or carbonic anhydrase inhibitors. ¯Ç×e development of secondary
choroidal e¯ÇÜusion may act to perpetuate the hypotony. With time
the hypotonous process can itself lead to further damage, including sclerosis and atrophy of ciliary processes. Prolonged severe
hypotony may lead to phthisis bulbi and loss of the eye. Treatment
to restore normal IOP is directed according to cause.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü VA is variably a¯ÇÜected. Delayed normalization of IOP may
result in permanent visual impairment, though substantial
improvement has been reported following reversal of hypotony a¯ÇÖer several years.
¯Çü Fine retinal folds radiating outwards from the foveola, which
may also show CMO (Fig. 14.56).
¯Çü Chorioretinal folds may radiate outwards in branching fashion from the optic disc. ¯Ç×ese are due to scleral collapse with
resultant chorioretinal redundancy.

{ ¯Ç×e spot evolves to a sharply de¯ÇØned foveolar defect with
irregular borders (Fig. 14.57B), or a lamellar hole.
¯Çü OCT shows foveal thinning with a focal hypore¯Ç£ective area,
the depth of which correlates with the extent of visual acuity
loss but which generally includes the photoreceptor inner and
outer segments.
¯Çü Treatment is not available.
¯Çü Prognosis is good in most cases with improvement of visual
acuity to normal or near-normal levels within 6 months. In a
minority, signi¯ÇØcantly reduced vision persists.

¯Çü Miscellaneous. A variety of other features may be present,
related both to aetiology and secondary e¯ÇÜects of hypotony,
including a shallow anterior chamber, choroidal e¯ÇÜusion,
cataract, corneal decompensation, optic disc oedema, uveitis, wound leak or an unexpected ¯ÇØltering bleb adjacent to a
wound (e.g. following cataract surgery), cyclodialysis cle¯ÇÖ on
gonioscopy and retinal detachment.
¯Çü Ultrasound biomicroscopy may show a cyclitic membrane
or cyclodialysis cleft if there is clinical reason to suspect
this.
¯Çü OCT should be undertaken using horizontal and vertical rasters and will show chorioretinal folds.
¯Çü B-scan ultrasonography will demonstrate choroidal e¯ÇÜusions.
¯Çü A-scan ultrasonography or interferometry may show reduced
axial length.

SOLAR RETINOPATHY

¯Çü Pathogenesis. Retinal injury results from photochemical
e¯ÇÜects of solar radiation a¯ÇÖer directly or indirectly viewing the
sun (eclipse retinopathy).
¯Çü Presentation is within a few hours of exposure with impairment of central vision and a small central scotoma.
¯Çü Signs

{ VA is variable according to severity.

{ A small yellow or red foveolar spot (Fig. 14.57A) that fades
within a few weeks.

622 Dome-Shaped Macula

A B

C D

solar-induced foveolar defect; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× FAF showing focal laser injury; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× OCT of ¯ÇØC¯Ç× showing
intraretinal cavitation and disruption of the outer retina in the acute phase. (Courtesy of P
Charbel Issa ÔÇô ¯Ç×gs C and D.)

¯Çü Di¯Ç£erential diagnosis. Macula damage that is similar in

induced accidentally or by staring at a laser pointer (Fig.
14.57C and D).

TORPEDO MACULOPATHY

This is a rare condition of unknown cause, which is characterized by a single hypopigmented torpedo-shaped lesion
temporal to the fovea in asymptomatic young people (Fig.
14.58A). The average age at diagnosis is 8 years, although it
can be seen in infants. The visual acuity is not affected, but a
small scotoma may be found on visual field testing. It is typically unilateral and non-progressive, with a good long-term
prognosis. Fundus autofluorescence shows a hyperfluorescent
border surrounding a hypofluorescent area (Fig. 14.58B). OCT
shows attenuation of the outer retinal structures and conservation of the inner retinal layers (Fig. 14.58C). No treatment is
required.

FOCAL CHOROIDAL EXCAVATION

Focal choroidal excavation (FCE) is a relatively common condition in
which one or more areas of macular choroidal excavation are detected
in one or both eyes of a patient, typically middle-aged and possibly
more commonly Eastern Asian, without a history of ocular disease
known to produce choroidal thinning. Vision is variably a¯ÇÜected and
may be compromised by complications such as MNV, CSR and PCV.
Overlying pigment epithelial disturbance or small yellowish-white
deposits, sometimes vitelliform, are o¯ÇÖen seen clinically. On OCT,
in ÔÇÿconformingÔÇÖ FCE the overlying RPE and inner-segment/outersegment junction follows the outwards indentation of the excavation
(Fig. 14.59). By contrast, in ÔÇÿnon-conformingÔÇÖ FCE the photoreceptor
layers are disrupted and appear to be separated from the RPE.

DOME-SHAPED MACULA

This is a rare condition seen in myopic adults. Approximately
half are highly myopic and in half the condition involves both

A B

C

OCT showing localised outer retinal cavitation with loss of both the ellipsoid and interdigitation zones. (Courtesy of P Charbel Issa.)

eyes. Patients present with impaired vision and no other signs
of ocular disease. The cause is not known, but the condition
may represent a type of localized staphyloma. The diagnosis
is made on OCT analysis where a serous macular detachment
is often found. The key to the diagnosis is to undertake vertical and horizontal linear OCT scans, as the ridge may only
be visible in one median (Fig. 14.60). In approximately 25%
the subretinal fluid disappears spontaneously in time. There
is no treatment; in particular anti-VEGF injections have no
effect. The vision usually does not deteriorate despite the OCT
appearance.

LOW VISUAL AIDS

(Courtesy of P Charbel Issa.)

## Part of the effective management of patients with poor vision
as a consequence of bilateral macular disease is the use of

low visual aids. Skilled instruction on their use is key to
their successful implementation. All aids are based on simple

624 Charles Bonnet Syndrome (CBS)

A

B

the dome and subretinal ¯Ç×uid. (Courtesy of P Charbel Issa.)

magnification and good illumination. There are five basic types
of low visual aid:
¯Çü Non-optical devices (adaptive aids), such as large print books,
talking clocks and ¯Ç£uid level indicators.
¯Çü Tints and ¯ÇØlters. Grey lenses reduce light intensity and yellow
lenses improve contrast. An anti-re¯Ç£ective coating on spectacles can be used to reduce glare.
¯Çü Convex lens aids such as magni¯ÇØers are used for most patients.
Hand-held magni¯ÇØers are o¯ÇÖen helpful, particularly if the illumination is good (Fig. 14.61A and B). ¯Ç×e main advantage of
spectacle-mounted and dome magni¯ÇØers is that both hands are
free. A focussed image is always provided at a set distance, but
the amount of magni¯ÇØcation is limited and distortion at the
edges may occur.
¯Çü Telescopes. ¯Ç×ese can be focussed from distance to near, but
have the disadvantage of a small and shallow visual ¯ÇØeld.
¯Çü Electronic devices and portable video magni¯ÇØers such as
reading machines, image scanners and closed-circuit television. ¯Ç×ese options are expensive but allow signi¯ÇØcant

magni¯ÇØcation (from 1.5├ù to 45├ù), with adjustable font sizes
(Fig. 14.61C and D).

CHARLES BONNET SYNDROME (CBS)

¯Ç×is syndrome can occur in some people with poor sight and consists of three features: visual hallucinations, ocular pathology and
intact cognition.
¯Çü Demographic. CBS tends to happen in elderly people who
have lost most or all of their vision in both eyes and occurs in
up to 50% of all people with severe macular degeneration. It
may occur in young people and in those with other ocular disease (e.g. retinitis pigmentosa, glaucoma). ¯Ç×e symptoms are
not caused by mental illness, dementia or medication.
¯Çü Hallucinations. ¯Ç×ose a¯ÇÜected are usually aware that the hallucinations are not real. ¯Ç×e hallucinations are a consequence
of the visual association areas of the brain not receiving as
much information as usual (dea¯ÇÜerentation), leading to the
brain responding by ¯ÇØlling in the gaps with stored images.

A B

C D

video magni¯ÇØer; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× electronic magni¯ÇØer.

¯Çü Symptoms. ¯Ç×ere are two main types of hallucination associated with this syndrome: (a) simple repeated patterns taking
the form of grids, shapes or lines which can appear in vivid
colours (Fig. 14.62A), (b) complex images of people, objects or
landscapes (Fig. 14.62B). Based on their frequency, the hallucinations can be classi¯ÇØed as episodic, periodic or continuous.

¯Çü Management. ¯Ç×ere is no cure for the condition. ¯Ç×e key to
the management is to explain and to reassure the patient that
the hallucinations will become less pronounced in time. Preliminary research suggests that inhibitory transcranial direct
stimulation using a weak electrical current may reduce the frequency of symptoms.

628 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

## General

¯Çƒe inherited retinal degenerations (ÔÇÿretinal dystrophiesÔÇÖ) are a
group of disorders that commonly exert their major e¯Ç×ect on the
retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)ÔÇôphotoreceptor complex and
the choriocapillaris to cause a range of visual impairment. ¯Çƒe
most common example is retinitis pigmentosa. Hereditary retinal diseases are now the leading cause of blindness certi¯ÇØcation
in the working-age population (age 16ÔÇô64 years) in the UK. Some
inherited retinal degenerations manifest in early childhood while
others do not present until later in life. Isolated dystrophies have
features con¯ÇØned to the eye, whilst syndromic dystrophies are part
of a wider disease process that also a¯Ç×ects tissues elsewhere in the
body. In a patient with an inherited retinal degeneration, genetic
sequencing should be undertaken to determine the exact causative
gene, as mutations in a number of genes can cause the same clinical
phenotype (genetic heterogeneity) and di¯Ç×erent phenotypes can
be caused by the same gene (phenotypical heterogeneity). Mutations in more than 250 genes have been linked to inherited retinal
degenerations and a table of genes associated with these conditions
can be found online (https://sph.uth.edu/retnet/). Identi¯ÇØcation of
the causative gene allows the identi¯ÇØcation of patients who might
bene¯ÇØt from novel therapies, although this may only be possible
within a clinical trial setting.

TIP Mutations in di¯Ç×erent genes (genetic heterogeneity) can
cause the same clinical phenotype in an individual with an
inherited retinal degeneration.

## Anatomy

¯Çƒere are two types of retinal photoreceptor:
¯Çü ¯Çƒe rods are the most numerous (120 million) and are of the
densest concentration outside the macular region. ¯Çƒey are
most sensitive in dim illumination and are responsible for
night, motion sense and peripheral vision. If rod dysfunction
occurs earlier or is more severe than cone dysfunction, it will
result initially in poor night vision (nyctalopia). Rod and cone
loss results in loss of visual ¯ÇØeld.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe cones are far fewer in number (6 million) and are concentrated at the fovea. ¯Çƒere are three types of cones: L (long;
the commonest) responding to red wavelengths of light; M
(medium) responding to yellow-green wavelengths; and S
(short, only about 2% of the total) responding to blue wavelengths. ¯Çƒey are most sensitive in bright light and mediate day
vision, colour vision, central and ¯ÇØne vision. Cone dysfunction
therefore results in poor central vision, impairment of colour
vision (dyschromatopsia) and occasionally problems with day
vision (hemeralopia).
¯Çü Photoreceptor structure. Each photoreceptor consists of an
outer segment which is the light-sensitive portion of the cell
and an inner segment which is the manufacturing portion of
the cell, containing mitochondria and endoplasmic reticulum
(Fig. 15.1). ¯Çƒese segments are joined by the cilium. In each

RPE

epithelium.)

rod outer segment there are about 1000 separate discs, while in
the cones the discs are joined to a boundary membrane with an
aperture that allows the discs to be in contact with the extracellular space and therefore with each other. Photopigments
exist as transmembrane proteins within the discs. New discs
are formed in the region of the cilium and older discs are shed
from the tips of the outer segments and undergo phagocytosis
by the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE). For rods the discs are
shed in packets at the onset of morning light. ¯Çƒe process is
slower for cones and occurs at night.

## Inheritance

All inherited retinal degenerations are caused by genetic variants,
most of which are inherited and a few of which occur de novo. A
new mutation (allelic variant) can occur in an individual and can
subsequently be passed to future generations.
¯Çü Autosomal dominant (AD) conditions o¯Ç£en exhibit variable
expressivity and tend to have a later onset and milder course
than recessive disorders.
¯Çü Recessive conditions may be autosomal (AR) or X-linked.
¯Çƒey generally have an earlier onset and a more severe course
than AD conditions. In some cases, female carriers of X-linked
conditions show characteristic fundus ¯ÇØndings.
¯Çü X-linked dominant conditions are very rare and are usually
lethal in boys (e.g. Aicardi syndrome).
¯Çü Mitochondrial DNA is inherited solely via the maternal line.

## Inherited retinal degenerations associated with mitochondrial

DNA variants o¯Ç£en occur as part of a wider systemic disease.

## Heteroplasmy describes the presence of more than one type of

mitochondrial DNA within a cell. Because cells contain thousands of mitochondria, a low level of heteroplasmy is common
in normal individuals. ¯Çƒe presence and severity of an inherited retinal degeneration depends on the proportion of faulty
mitochondria. ¯Çƒe manifestations of mitochondrial-inherited
ocular disease usually do not appear before adulthood, as
many cell divisions are required for a cell to accumulate
enough mutant mitochondria to cause symptoms.

## Rod

## Cone

## Inner segment

¯Çü Digenic conditions are due to the combined e¯Ç×ect of mutations in two di¯Ç×erent genes.

TIP Mutations in the same gene can cause di¯Ç×erent
phenotypes (phenotypical heterogeneity) in an individual with
an inherited retinal degeneration.

Classi¯Çƒcation

As well as division by inheritance pattern, dystrophies can be
considered as generalized, in which the clinical e¯Ç×ects involve the
entire fundus (rod-cone or cone-rod, depending on which photoreceptor type is predominantly dysfunctional), or central (local,
macula) in which only the macula is a¯Ç×ected. ¯Çƒey can also be classi¯ÇØed according to the element that is the focus of the pathological
process (e.g. photoreceptors, RPE or choroid) and by whether they
are stationary (non-progressive) or progressive.

INVESTIGATION

A¯Ç£er taking a careful history (especially a detailed family history)
and undertaking a clinical examination, the main investigations that
are usually needed to make a diagnosis of inherited retinal degeneration are OCT and fundus auto¯Çøuorescence (FAF). Supplementary investigations include electrophysiological and genetic testing.

## Electroretinography

## Introduction

¯Çƒe electroretinogram (ERG) measures retinal electrical activity.
When stimulated by light of adequate intensity, ionic ¯Çøow ÔÇô principally sodium and potassium ÔÇô is induced in or out of cells such
that a potential is generated. ¯Çƒis retinal electrical activity can be
recorded with an electrode placed at or near the cornea and a reference electrode placed on the forehead. ¯Çƒe potential between the
two electrodes is then ampli¯ÇØed and displayed (Fig. 15.2). ¯Çƒe normal ERG is predominantly biphasic (Fig. 15.3A):
¯Çü ¯Çƒe a-wave is an initial fast corneal-negative de¯Çøection generated by the photoreceptors.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe b-wave is a subsequent slower positive large-amplitude
de¯Çøection. Although it is generated from M├╝ller and bipolar
cells, it is directly dependent on functional photoreceptors and
its magnitude makes it a convenient measure of photoreceptor
integrity. Its amplitude is measured from the a-wave trough to
the b-wave peak. ¯Çƒe b-wave is enhanced with dark adaptation
and increased light stimulus.
¯Çü Reduced ratio. As a general principle, a reduced ratio between
a- and b-waves indicates inner retinal dysfunction.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe c-wave is a third (negative) de¯Çøection generated by the
RPE and photoreceptors.
¯Çü Latency is the interval to the commencement of the a-wave
a¯Ç£er the stimulus is applied.
¯Çü Implicit time is the interval from the stimulus to the b-wave
peak.

## Electroretinography may be used for the diagnosis of a range of

di¯Ç×erent retinal disorders on the basis of characteristic patterns of

## Reference electrode

## Amplifier

## Active electrode

a-wave

b-wave

change, as well as monitoring of disease progress in dystrophies
and other conditions such as some forms of uveitis (e.g. birdshot
retinochoroiditis) and drug toxicity (e.g. hydroxychloroquine).

TIP High-quality retinal imaging techniques and speci¯ÇØc
genetic testing in a patient with an inherited retinal
degeneration is more commonly used than electroretinography.

Full-¯Çƒeld ERG

Guidelines for the preparation of a patient who requires an ERG
and the minimum set of responses that need to be obtained have
been published by the International Society for Clinical Electrophysiology of Vision (ISCEV-2022 update). A standard full-¯ÇØeld
ERG (Fig. 15.3B) consists of six recordings and is taken during
di¯Ç×use stimulation of the entire retinal area. It is used to assess
generalized retinal disorders, but may not detect localized pathology. ¯Çƒe ¯ÇØrst three recordings are elicited a¯Ç£er a minimum of 20
minutes of dark adaptation (scotopic) and the last three a¯Ç£er 10
minutes of adaptation to moderately bright di¯Ç×use illumination
(photopic). Several responses are usually averaged to improve the
signal-to-noise ratio. It may be di¯ÇÜcult to dark-adapt children for
30 minutes and therefore dim light (mesopic) conditions can be
utilized to evoke predominantly rod-mediated responses to lowintensity white or blue light stimuli.

{ Combined rod and cone responses are elicited with a very

¯Çü Photopic ERG

{ Cone responses are elicited with a single bright ¯Çøash,
resulting in an a- and a b-wave with subsequent small
oscillations.

{ Cone ¯Çøicker is used to isolate cones by using a ¯Çøickering
light stimulus at a frequency of 30 Hz to which rods cannot
respond. It provides a measure of the amplitude and implicit
time of the cone b-wave. Cone responses can be elicited
in normal eyes up to 50 Hz, a¯Ç£er which point individual
responses are no longer recordable (ÔÇÿcritical ¯Çøicker fusionÔÇÖ).

Multifocal ERG

Multifocal ERG is a method of producing topographical maps of
retinal function (Fig. 15.4). ¯Çƒe stimulus is scaled for variation in
photoreceptor density across the retina. At the fovea, where the
density of receptors is high, a lesser stimulus is employed than in
the periphery where receptor density is lower. As with conventional ERG, many types of measurements can be made. Both the
amplitude and timing of the troughs and peaks can be measured
and reported, and the information can be summarized in the form
of a three-dimensional plot which resembles the hill of vision.

Focal ERG

Focal (foveal) ERG can be used to assess macular disease, but is
rarely utilized.

Pattern ERG

A similar stimulus to that used in visual evoked potentials (see
Ch. 19), pattern reversal, is used to target ganglion cell function,

typically in order to detect subtle optic neuropathy and early glaucoma (see Fig. 11.25A and B).

Electro¯Çƒoculography

¯Çƒe electro-oculogram (EOG) measures the standing potential
between the electrically positive cornea and the electrically negative back of the eye (Fig. 15.5). It re¯Çøects the activity of the RPE and
the photoreceptors. ¯Çƒe ISCEV (www.iscev.org) has determined
standards for clinical EOG. In general, di¯Ç×use or widespread disease of the RPE is needed to signi¯ÇØcantly a¯Ç×ect the response. ¯Çƒe
EOG is always abnormal if there is widespread photoreceptor dysfunction. As there is much variation in EOG amplitude in normal
subjects, the result is calculated by dividing the maximal height
of the potential in the light (ÔÇÿlight peakÔÇÖ) by the minimal height of
the potential in the dark (ÔÇÿdark troughÔÇÖ). ¯Çƒis is expressed as a ratio
(Arden ratio) with a normal value that is typically more than 1.7.

## Dark trough

## Dark adaptometry

Dark adaptation (DA) is the phenomenon by which the visual system adapts to decreased illumination and evaluation of this is particularly useful in the investigation of nyctalopia. ¯Çƒe retina is exposed
to an intense light for a time su¯ÇÜcient to bleach 25% or more of the
rhodopsin in the retina. Following this, normal rods are insensitive
to light and cones respond only to very bright stimuli. Subsequent
recovery of light sensitivity can be monitored by placing the subject
in the dark and periodically presenting spots of light of varying intensity in the visual ¯ÇØeld and asking the subject if they are perceived. ¯Çƒe
threshold at which the subject just perceives a light is recorded, the
¯Çøashes repeated at regular intervals and the increased sensitivity of
the eye to light plotted: the dark adaptation curve (Fig. 15.6).
¯Çü ¯Çƒe cone branch of the curve represents the initial 5ÔÇô10
minutes of darkness during which cone sensitivity rapidly
improves. ¯Çƒe rod photoreceptors are also recovering, but
more slowly during this time.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe ÔÇÿrod-coneÔÇÖ break normally occurs a¯Ç£er 7ÔÇô10 minutes
when cones achieve their maximum sensitivity and the rods
become perceptibly more sensitive than cones.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe rod branch of the curve is slower and represents the continuation of improvement of rod sensitivity. A¯Ç£er 15ÔÇô30 minutes, the fully dark-adapted rods allow the subject to perceive
a spot of light over 100 times dimmer than would be possible
with cones alone. If the ¯Çøashes are focused onto the foveola
(where rods are absent), only a rapid segment corresponding
to cone adaptation is recorded.

## Genetic testing

¯Çƒis should be undertaken in association with a geneticist who has
access to a laboratory that can undertake the necessary tests.

¯Çü Single gene testing is used in patients with a family history of
a known genetic mutation, in order to con¯ÇØrm the molecular
diagnosis.
¯Çü Sequencing using targeted panels of all known inherited retinal
degeneration genes has been developed and yields high diagnostic rates. Once a genetic defect is identi¯ÇØed, Sanger single
gene sequencing can be undertaken to con¯ÇØrm the mutation.

TIP A monogenetic retinal degeneration can develop at
any age and although the genetic abnormality can often be
determined using modern techniques, a substantial number of
patients with an inherited retinal degeneration remain without a
genetic diagnosis.

GENERALIZED PHOTORECEPTOR
DYSTROPHIES

## Retinitis pigmentosa

## Introduction

¯Çü De¯Ç×nition. Retinitis pigmentosa (RP), or pigmented retinal
degeneration, denotes a clinically and genetically diverse group of
inherited di¯Ç×use retinal degenerative diseases initially predominantly a¯Ç×ecting the rod photoreceptors, with subsequent less
marked degeneration of cones and retinal pigment epithelium.
¯Çü Prevalence. It is the most common hereditary retinal degeneration, with a prevalence of 1:3000 to 1:5000. ¯Çƒe age of onset,
rate of progression, eventual visual loss and associated ocular
features are frequently related to the mode of inheritance.
However, the severity of disease at a given age can vary in different patients with the same gene defect.
¯Çü Inheritance. RP may occur as a sporadic disorder or can be
inherited in an AD, AR or X-linked pattern. Sporadic cases
are also AD, AR or X-linked, but they have no other known
a¯Ç×ected family members. ¯Çƒe reasons for this include: (a)
incomplete penetrance, (b) AR inheritance in small families,
(c) non-paternity, (d) death before onset of the disease.

632 Generalized Photoreceptor Dystrophies

¯Çü Genetic defect. Mutations in more than 100 gene loci are
known to cause non-syndromic RP. Genetic defects that cause
RP can be divided into the following: (a) those that a¯Ç×ect the
phototransduction cascade, (b) the retinoid cycle, (c) photoreceptor structure, (d) other biological functions of the photoreceptor and RPE. Despite advances in genetics, about half of
a¯Ç×ected individuals have no known molecular genetic abnormality. Most cases of AD-inherited RP are due to mutations of
the rhodopsin gene, which is important in the phototransduction cascade. X-linked is the least common inheritance pattern
and in 90% of cases is due to mutations in the RPGR gene,
which encodes a protein that is localized in the connecting
cilium of rod photoreceptors.
¯Çü Syndromic RP. In 20ÔÇô30% of cases, RP, o¯Ç£en atypical (see
below), is associated with a systemic disorder. ¯Çƒese conditions are usually of AR or mitochondrial inheritance.
¯Çü Leber congenital amaurosis. Around 5% of RP belongs to
the very early-onset severe type grouped together as Leber
congenital amaurosis (see separate topic).

## Diagnosis

¯Çƒe classic triad of ¯ÇØndings comprises bone-spicule retinal pigmentation, arteriolar attenuation and ÔÇÿwaxyÔÇÖ disc pallor. ¯Çƒere is
o¯Ç£en signi¯ÇØcant symmetry in the retinal appearance of the two
eyes. ¯Çƒere may be a family history of RP and a pedigree should
be prepared.
¯Çü Symptoms

{ Nyctalopia and dark adaptation di¯ÇÜculties are frequently
presenting symptoms, but peripheral visual problems may
be noticed.

{ Reduced central vision tends to be a later feature, but can
be involved earlier if a cataract develops.

{ Photopsia is not uncommon.
¯Çü Signs

{ Visual acuity may be normal. Contrast sensitivity may be
a¯Ç×ected at an earlier stage than visual acuity.

{ Bilateral mid-peripheral intraretinal perivascular ÔÇÿbonespiculeÔÇÖ pigmentary changes and RPE atrophy associated
with arteriolar narrowing (Fig. 15.7A and B).

{ ¯Çƒere is a gradual increase in density of the pigment with
anterior and posterior spread and a tessellated fundus
appearance develops due to unmasking of large choroidal
vessels (Fig. 15.7C).

{ Peripheral pigmentation may become severe, with marked
arteriolar narrowing and disc pallor (Fig. 15.7D).

{ ¯Çƒe macula may show atrophy, epiretinal membrane
(ERM) formation and cystoid macular oedema (CMO).

¯Çü RP sine pigmento is characterized by an absence or paucity of
pigment accumulation and is not a separate form of the disease.
Every patient with RP is initially ÔÇÿsine pigmentoÔÇÖ, as the bone
spicules only develop later, secondary to photoreceptor degeneration. Functional manifestations are similar to typical RP.
¯Çü Complications include posterior subcapsular cataract (common in all forms of RP), open-angle glaucoma (3%) and posterior vitreous detachment.
¯Çü Prognosis. AD has the best prognosis. X-linked is the most
severe form with reduction of central vision to 6/60 or less by
the ¯ÇØ¯Ç£h decade of life. AR is intermediate in severity
¯Çü Investigation. Investigation for infectious conditions that
mimic RP (e.g. syphilis), is sometimes warranted.

{ FAF shows an abnormal perimacular ring of
hyper-auto¯Çøuorescence (increased RPE lipofuscin
secondary to RPE dysfunction) and di¯Ç×use, patchy hypoauto¯Çøuorescence in the mid-periphery (Fig. 15.9A). Using
FAF as a stand-alone test, RP can be distinguished from a
normal fundus in 95% of cases.

{ OCT contributes to the analysis of macular involvement
in RP and has prognostic value. Macular oedema can be
detected in about 15% and macular atrophy in about 40%
of patients with RP.

{ Perimetry initially demonstrates small mid-peripheral
scotomas that gradually coalesce and may deteriorate to
leave a tiny island of residual central vision (Fig. 15.9B)
that may subsequently be extinguished. Microperimetry is
useful for central visual assessment.

{ Full-¯ÇØeld ERG is a sensitive diagnostic test, but is rarely
needed. In early disease it shows reduced scotopic rod
and combined responses (Fig. 15.10). Photopic responses
reduce with progression and eventually the ERG becomes
extinguished.

{ Dark adaptation is prolonged and measuring this using the
Dark Adaptation Test may be useful in equivocal early cases.

{ Genetic analysis may identify the particular mutation
responsible in an individual patient and facilitate genetic
counselling, including the risk of transmission to o¯Ç×spring.

TIP Identifying the precise mutation responsible for an
inherited retinal degeneration aids genetic counselling and can
facilitate enrolment in a clinical trial.

## Management

¯Çü Treatment. No speci¯ÇØc treatment is commercially available,
but gene therapy is showing promise in early trials. Luxturna
is an approved and commercially available gene therapy for
patients with RPE 65 mutations.
¯Çü Annual follow-up is recommended in order to detect treatable
vision-threatening complications, provide support and maintain contact in case of therapeutic innovation.
¯Çü Cataract surgery is o¯Ç£en bene¯ÇØcial.
¯Çü Low-vision aid provision, rehabilitation and social service
access when appropriate.
¯Çü Smoking should be avoided.

{ Myopia is common.

{ Optic disc drusen occur more frequently in patients with
RP.
¯Çü Female carriers of the X-linked form may have normal fundi
or show a golden-metallic (ÔÇÿtapetalÔÇÖ) re¯Çøex at the macula (Fig.
15.8A) and/or small peripheral patches of bone-spicule pigmentation. ¯Çƒe most striking feature of the carrier state is
centrifugal hyperauto¯Çøuorescent lines on FAF imaging (Fig.
15.8B).

and mottled hypoauto¯Çƒuorescence in the mid-periphery; (B) perimetry: visual ¯ÇØeld constriction in advanced retinitis pigmentosa.
