5ms/div

scotopic rod and combined responses.

¯Çü Light exposure. Some forms of RP may be modi¯ÇØed by reducing light exposure and therefore blocking short-wavelength
light below approximately 550nm might be bene¯ÇØcial. Indoor
amber spectacles blocking to 511ÔÇô527 nm may improve contrast sensitivity and reduce glare.
¯Çü CMO in RP may respond to oral acetazolamide and sometimes topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitors.
¯Çü Dietary supplementation. ¯Çƒe use of high-doses of vitamin
A has been explored, but there is no consensus on its potential bene¯ÇØcial e¯Ç×ect, which might be gene-dependent. Caution is advisable in light of potential adverse e¯Ç×ects. Several
other supplements and drugs (e.g. lutein, zeaxanthin, calciumchannel blockers) have shown potential bene¯ÇØts, but their e¯ÇÜcacy and safety in RP have not been fully ascertained.
¯Çü Retinotoxic medication should be avoided or used with caution. Examples include isotretinoin and other retinoids, phenothiazines, hydroxychloroquine, tamoxifen and vigabatrin.

TIP Therapeutic options that can be helpful in selected cases
of retinitis pigmentosa include phacoemulsi¯ÇØcation with IOL
implantation and medical treatment for macular oedema.

## Atypical retinitis pigmentosa

## Introduction

¯Çƒe term ÔÇÿatypical RPÔÇÖ has conventionally been used to group
together heterogeneous disorders clinically having features in
common with typical pigmentary retinal degeneration. ¯Çƒe precise
conditions included within this category vary between authors.

Atypical RP associated with a systemic disorder
(syndromic RP)

¯Çü Usher syndrome (AR, genetically heterogeneous) accounts
for about 5% of all cases of profound deafness in children and
about half of all cases of combined deafness and blindness.
¯Çƒere are three major types, ranging from type I (MYOA7
associated) (75%), which features profound congenital sensorineural deafness and severe RP with an extinguished ERG in
the ¯ÇØrst decade, to type III (2%), with progressive hearing loss,
vestibular dysfunction and relatively late-onset RP. Systemic
features are widely variable and can include premature ageing,
skeletal anomalies, mental handicap and early demise. ¯Çƒere
is o¯Ç£en a ÔÇÿsalt and pepperÔÇÖ pattern of retinal pigmentation
and optic atrophy. A study assessing the bene¯ÇØt of inserting
the MYOA7-associated gene is being undertaken in individuals with this genetic defect. ¯Çƒe severe deafness that typically
occurs in type 1 Usher syndrome can be treated with a cochlear
implant (Fig. 15.11A).
¯Çü BassenÔÇôKornzweig syndrome or abetalipoproteinaemia (AR)
is a condition in which fat and fat-soluble vitamin (A, D, E, K)
absorption is dysfunctional. ¯Çƒere is a failure to thrive in infancy,
with the development of severe spinocerebellar ataxia. A blood
¯ÇØlm shows ÔÇÿthornyÔÇÖ red cells (acanthocytosis ÔÇô Fig. 15.11B).

A B

D
C

deafness in type 1 Usher syndrome; (B) acanthocytosis in BassenÔÇôKornzweig syndrome; (C)
ichthyosis in adult Refsum disease; (D) polydactyly in BardetÔÇôBiedl syndrome.

¯Çƒe fundus exhibits scattered white dots followed by RP-like
changes developing towards the end of the ¯ÇØrst decade. Other
features include ptosis, ophthalmoplegia, strabismus and nystagmus. Vitamin supplementation and a low-fat diet should be
implemented.
¯Çü Refsum disease (AR) is rare and consists of genetically and
clinically distinct infantile and adult forms. Phytanic acid
accumulates throughout the body, with substantial and varied
skin (Fig. 15.11C), neurological and visceral features. Retinal changes may be similar to RP or take on a salt and pepper appearance and there may be other ocular features such as
cataract and optic atrophy A low phytanic acid diet can retard
progression.
¯Çü BardetÔÇôBiedl syndrome (most cases AR; genetically heterogenous) is rare and occurs as a consequence of mutations
in the genes responsible for proteins involved in non-motor
cilia function. It can encompass a range of systemic abnormalities including polydactyly (Fig. 15.11D), dysfunction of
the kidneys and mental handicap. Visual symptoms include

nyctalopia, visual ¯ÇØeld constriction and later, loss of visual
acuity. ¯Çƒere is typically a bullÔÇÖs-eye maculopathy due to conerod dystrophy and less frequently RP. Almost 80% have severe
changes by the age of 20 years.

## Sector retinitis pigmentosa

Sector (sectoral) RP (AD) is characterized by involvement of inferior quadrants only (Fig. 15.12). Progression is slow and many
cases are apparently stationary. Unilateral RP can also occur.

KearnsÔÇôSayre syndrome

Kearns-Sayre syndrome is rare and is most commonly due to single deletions in mitochondrial DNA. ¯Çƒe retinal disease is not an
atypical form of RP. Patients typically present within the ¯ÇØrst two
decades of life with chronic progressive external ophthalmoplegia,
bilateral ptosis (see Fig. 19.101A) and subsequently develop a salt
and pepper retinal appearance, most striking at the macula (see

may occur.

636 Generalized Photoreceptor Dystrophies

## Leber congenital amaurosis

Leber congenital amaurosis (AR, genetically heterogeneous) is a
severe inherited retinal degeneration a¯Ç×ecting rods and cones. It usually has a recessive inheritance pattern and is genetically heterogenous.
Variants in three of the at least 28 di¯Ç×erent genes known to be responsible for this disease are inherited in an AD manner. Mutation in the
RPE65 gene is one of the major causes. Some cases are association
with mental handicap, deafness, epilepsy, central nervous system and
renal anomalies, skeletal malformations and endocrine dysfunction.
¯Çü Presentation is with blindness at birth or early infancy,
associated with roving eye movements or nystagmus and
photoaversion.
¯Çü Signs are variable but may include:

{ Absent or diminished pupillary light re¯Çøexes.

{ ¯Çƒe fundi may be normal in early life apart from mild arteriolar narrowing.

{ Initially mild peripheral pigmentary retinopathy (Fig.

A B

C D

A Moore ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs A and B.)

{ In some sub-forms, severe macular pigmentation (Fig.
15.13B) or macular atrophy may occur (Fig. 15.13C).

{ Pigmentary retinopathy, optic atrophy and severe arteriolar narrowing in later childhood.

{ Oculodigital syndrome: constant rubbing of the eyes may
cause orbital fat atrophy with enophthalmos (Fig. 15.13D)
and subsequent keratoconus or keratoglobus.

{ Other associations include strabismus, hypermetropia or
myopia and cataract.

{ FAF shows hypoauto¯Çøuorescence in children with RPE 65
gene mutation.
¯Çü Treatment

{ Low visual aids, advice on schooling, rehabilitation and
access to social services.

{ A single subretinal injection of adeno-associated virus
(AAV)-mediated RPE65 gene therapy (Luxturna) has
shown remarkable results in some cases of LCA. Postoperative ocular in¯Çøammation, which occurs in about 50%
of cases, responds to immunosuppressive treatment. Some
other genetic causes are currently subject to clinical trials.

to the preservation of an organized photoreceptor layer. Variants
of this gene (AR inheritance) are associated with a wide and complex range of phenotypes, the commonest of which is Leber congenital amaurosis in infants (10% of all cases), retinitis pigmentosa
in teens and cone-rod dystrophies at any age. Distinctive features
include the presence of nummular pigmented deposits and small
yellow or white dots (Fig. 15.15A). FAF shows circumferential
hyperauto¯Çøuorescence in the macular region and alongside retinal arterioles. (Fig. 15.15B). OCT reveals preserved para-arteriolar
RPE and abnormally laminated, thickened retina (Fig. 15.15C).
¯Çƒere is no treatment, but CRB-1 gene therapy may o¯Ç×er help in
the future.

Cone and cone¯Çƒrod dystrophy

## Introduction

Progressive cone and cone-rod dystrophies are a clinically and
genetically heterogenous group of inherited retinal diseases characterized by cone photoreceptor degeneration, which may be followed
by rod photoreceptor loss. ¯Çƒe majority are AR, with some AD and
X-linked inheritance and mutations in at least 30 genes have been
implicated. As with other retinal dystrophies, the age of onset,
severity and rate of progression can vary substantially. A¯Ç×ected
individuals typically present with progressive loss of central vision,
disturbance of colour vision and photophobia. ¯Çƒe prognosis is
usually poor, with an eventual visual acuity of 6/60 or worse.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Gradual bilateral impairment of central and colour
vision, which may be accompanied by photophobia.
¯Çü Signs. ¯Çƒe features may evolve through the stages below.

## Pigmented paravenous chorioretinal
atrophy

Pigmented paravenous chorioretinal atrophy (predominantly AD)
is usually asymptomatic and non-progressive. ¯Çƒe ERG is normal. Paravenous bone-spicule pigmentation (Fig. 15.14) is seen,
together with sharply outlined zones of chorioretinal atrophy that
follow the course of the major retinal veins. Changes may also
encircle the optic disc. ¯Çƒe optic disc and vascular calibre are usually normal.

CRB-1 associated retinal dystrophy

CRB-1 encodes a transmembrane protein with the main role of
maintaining the zonula adherens junctions between photoreceptors, M├╝ller cells and the external limiting membrane and is key

{ ¯Çƒe macula may be virtually normal or show non-speci¯ÇØc
central pigmentary changes or atrophy.

{ Golden sheen may be seen in X-linked disease (Fig.
15.16A).

{ A bullÔÇÖs-eye maculopathy is classically described, but is not
universal. Other causes of a bullÔÇÖs-eye appearance are given
in Table 15.1

{ Progressive RPE atrophy at the macula with eventual
atrophy (Fig. 15.16B).
¯Çü Investigation

{ Colour vision: severe deuteronÔÇôtritan defect out of proportion to visual acuity in some patients.

{ OCT shows loss of the outer retinal layers in the macula
and foveal region (Fig. 15.17A). In advanced disease there
is complete atrophy of the macula.

{ FAF is o¯Ç£en the key diagnostic test, showing various annular patterns concentric with the fovea (Fig. 15.17B).

{ FA is never needed these days. However, if undertaken it
typically shows a round hyper¯Çøuorescent window defect
with a hypo¯Çøuorescent centre, resulting in a bullÔÇÖs-eye
appearance (Fig. 15.17C).

{ ERG: cone responses are subnormal, but rod responses are
preserved until late (Fig. 15.17D).

## Treatment

¯Çƒere is no speci¯ÇØc treatment for cone dystrophies. General measures (e.g. low visual aids and minimizing phototoxicity) as for
rod-cone dystrophies should be considered where applicable.

Stargardt disease/fundus
¯Ç×avimaculatus

## Introduction

Stargardt disease (juvenile macular dystrophy) and fundus flavimaculatus (FFM) are regarded as variants of the same disease
and together constitute the most common macular dystrophy
in adults and children. It is a common cause of central visual
loss in adults under the age of 50 years. The prevalence is 1 in
8000ÔÇô10 000 people. Three types are recognized: STGD1 (AR)
is the most common and is caused by mutations in the gene
ABCA4 (chromosome 1p), which affects the ability of the retina
to recycle the visual chromophore 11-cis-retinal. STGD3 (AD)
is caused by mutations in ELOVL4 and STGD4 (AD) is caused
by mutations in PROM1. Presentation is typically in childhood
or adolescence, but sometimes later. The prognosis is poor
and once the visual acuity drops below 6/12 it tends to worsen
rapidly.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Gradual impairment of central vision occurs that
may be out of proportion to examination ¯ÇØndings and manifests with di¯ÇÜculty when reading. A quarter of childhoodonset STGDT1 do not have any retinal lesions at presentation
and malingering may be suspected, but OCT imaging soon
clari¯ÇØes this misconception.
¯Çü Signs

{ ¯Çƒe macula may initially be normal or show non-speci¯ÇØc
mottling and ¯ÇØne ¯Çøecks (Fig. 15.18A), progressing to an
oval ÔÇÿsnail slimeÔÇÖ (Fig. 15.18B) or ÔÇÿbeaten-bronzeÔÇÖ appearance (Fig. 15.18C) and subsequently to atrophy (Fig.
15.18D) that may tend to a bullÔÇÖs-eye con¯ÇØguration. Rarely,
a small number of patients may develop macular neovascularization (MNV).

{ Numerous yellowÔÇôwhite round, oval or pisciform (¯ÇØshshaped) lesions at the level of the RPE. ¯Çƒese may be con¯ÇØned to the posterior pole (Fig. 15.18E) or extend to the
mid-periphery (Fig. 15.18F).

{ New lesions develop as older ones become ill-de¯ÇØned and
atrophic.

¯Çü Clinical trials have been initiated assessing the e¯Ç×ect of: (a)
gene therapy using a lentiviral vector for ABCA4, (b) stem cell
therapy, (c) pharmatherapy to reduce lipofuscin accumulation,
(d) metformin taken by mouth.

## Bietti crystalline corneoretinal
dystrophy

Bietti dystrophy (AR, CYP4V2 gene) is characterized by deposition of crystals in the retina and the super¯ÇØcial peripheral cornea.
It is a rare condition, but is more prevalent in Chinese and Japanese people than in other ethnic groups. ¯Çƒe CYP4V2 enzyme is
involved in fatty acid oxidation, but the speci¯ÇØc function is not
understood and it is not known how this leads to the speci¯ÇØc signs
found in this condition. ¯Çƒe rate of progression is variable. ¯Çƒere
is no treatment.
¯Çü Presentation. Young adults with slowly progressive visual loss
constitute the typical case.
¯Çü Signs

{ Super¯ÇØcial peripheral corneal crystals.

{ ¯Çƒe structure and function of the parapapillary retina is
spared, but can be involved in late-stage severe disease.
¯Çü Investigation

{ Visual ¯ÇØelds show central loss (Fig. 15.19A) and microperimetry can accurately document progression.

{ FA: ¯Çƒis has been superseded by OCT and FAF and is not
usually needed. ¯Çƒe classic feature is a ÔÇÿdark choroidÔÇÖ due
to masking of background choroidal ¯Çøuorescence by lipofuscin accumulation in the RPE. ¯Çƒe macula shows mixed
hyper- and hypo¯Çøuorescence. Fresh ¯Çøecks show early
hypo¯Çøuorescence due to blockage and late hyper¯Çøuorescence due to staining and old ¯Çøecks show RPE window
defects (Fig. 15.19B).

{ Indocyanine green angiography (ICGA) shows hypo¯Çøuorescent spots, o¯Ç£en more numerous than seen clinically.

{ FAF is key to the diagnosis in early cases and shows a
characteristic appearance with hyperauto¯Çøuorescent ¯Çøecks
(Fig. 15.19C and E) and macular hypoauto¯Çøuorescence.

{ OCT demonstrates outer retinal atrophy (Fig. 15.19D and F).

{ ERG: photopic is normal to subnormal, scotopic may be
normal.

{ EOG is commonly subnormal, especially in advanced
cases.

Familial benign ¯Ç×eck retina

Familial benign ¯Çøeck retina (benign ¯Çøecked retina syndrome) is a
very rare AR disorder. Individuals with the condition are asymptomatic, so the condition is usually discovered by chance. Numerous di¯Ç×usely distributed yellowÔÇôwhite polymorphous lesions spare
the fovea and extend to the far periphery (Fig. 15.21). ¯Çƒe ¯Çøecks
show auto¯Çøuorescence and are probably composed of lipofuscin.
¯Çƒe ERG is normal and the prognosis is excellent.

## Alport syndrome

Alport syndrome (predominantly X-linked) is caused by mutations
in several di¯Ç×erent genes, all of which encode particular forms of
type IV collagen, a major basement membrane component. It is
characterized by chronic renal failure and is o¯Ç£en associated with
high-tone sensorineural hearing loss by the age of 20. ¯Çƒere are
scattered yellowish punctate ¯Çøecks in the perimacular area, which

{ ¯Çƒere is gradual con¯Çøuence and expansion of the atrophic

TIP Stargardt disease is the most commonly inherited
macular dystrophy and is usually associated with mutations in
the ABCA4 gene.

## Treatment

¯Çü General measures should be considered as for RP. Protection
from excessive high-energy light exposure may be particularly
important.
¯Çü Avoid vitamin A supplementation as it may accelerate lipofuscin accumulation.

are o¯Ç£en subtle (Fig. 15.22A), and larger peripheral ¯Çøecks, some
of which may become con¯Çøuent, but rarely threaten vision (Fig.
15.22B). OCT is helpful to demonstrate the retinal changes and
typically shows temporal macular thinning (Fig. 15.22C). ¯Çƒe ERG
is normal and the prognosis for vision is excellent. Anterior lenticonus occurs in about 20% and posterior polymorphous corneal
dystrophy may occasionally be seen.

## Congenital stationary night blindness

## Introduction

Congenital stationary night blindness (CSNB) refers to a group
of disorders characterized by nyctalopia from birth, but nonprogressive retinal dysfunction. ¯Çƒe fundus appearance may be
normal or abnormal. A number of genetic mutations have been
reported that result in abnormal phototransduction and postphototransduction transmission.

644 Generalized Photoreceptor Dystrophies

A B

C

image showing retinal ¯Çƒecks and corresponding OCT showing temporal macular thinning
(arrow) and outer retinal disruption (arrowheads). (Courtesy of P Herrmann.)

## With a normal fundus appearance

CSNB with a normal fundus appearance is sometimes classi¯ÇØed
into type 1 (complete) and type 2 (incomplete) forms that are generally due to mutations in di¯Ç×erent genes. ¯Çƒe former is characterized by a complete absence of rod pathway function and essentially
normal cone function clinically and on ERG, the latter by impairment of both rod and cone function. Mutations in numerous genes
have been implicated, with X-linked, AD and AR inheritance patterns. ¯Çƒe AD form is usually associated with normal visual acuity,
but many AR and X-linked patients have poor vision with nystagmus and o¯Ç£en signi¯ÇØcant myopia.

## With an abnormal fundus appearance

¯Çü Oguchi disease (AR). ¯Çƒe fundus has an unusual goldenyellow colour in the light-adapted state (Fig. 15.23A), which
becomes normal a¯Ç£er prolonged dark adaptation (Mizuo or
MizuoÔÇôNakamura phenomenon ÔÇô Fig. 15.23B). Rod function
is absent a¯Ç£er 30 minutes of dark adaptation but recovers to a
near-normal level a¯Ç£er a long period of dark adaptation.

¯Çü Fundus albipunctatus is an AR or AD condition that may
be the same entity as retinitis punctata albescens. ¯Çƒe fundus
shows a multitude of subtle, tiny yellowÔÇôwhite spots at the posterior pole (Fig. 15.24A), sparing the fovea ÔÇô sometimes the
macula ÔÇô and extending to the periphery. In contrast to retinitis punctata albescens, the retinal blood vessels, optic disc,
peripheral ¯ÇØelds and visual acuity are believed to remain normal, though the natural history is not yet absolutely de¯ÇØned.
FA shows mottled hyper¯Çøuorescence, indicating depigmentation of the RPE. ¯Çƒe ERG is variably abnormal; both cones and
rods may be a¯Ç×ected.
¯Çü Retinitis punctata albescens (AR or AD) is rare and is characterized by scattered whitish-yellow spots, most numerous at
the equator, usually sparing the macula and associated with
arteriolar attenuation (Fig. 15.24B). ¯Çƒis form of ¯Çøecked retina may be a variant of fundus albipunctatus, as mutations in
RLBP1 (retinaldehyde-binding protein-1) have been identi¯ÇØed in both conditions. However, those identi¯ÇØed as having
retinitis punctata albescence tend to have a more serious and

A B

dark-adapted state. (Courtesy of C Mishra.)

A B

(Courtesy of C Barry.)

progressive disease, while those with fundus albipunctatus
tend to have a stable disease with night blindness being the
major symptom. ¯Çƒe relationship between these two conditions will become more transparent once the natural history
has been elucidated.

## Congenital achromatopsia

¯Çƒis is a group of congenital disorders in which colours cannot
be perceived and visual acuity is reduced, particularly in brightly
illuminated environments (hemeralopia). Mutations a¯Ç×ecting the
cone-speci¯ÇØc cyclic nucleotide gated (CNG) channel beta (B3)
and alpha (A3) subunits are responsible for about 50% and 25%
of complete achromatopsia, respectively. Animal models of the
disease have shown successful restoration of cone-mediated vision
following gene therapy for the CNGA3 and CNGB3 mutations.

Rod monochromatism (complete
achromatopsia)

In rod monochromatism (AR) visual acuity is poor, typically 6/60.
¯Çƒere is congenital nystagmus and photophobia. Colour vision is
totally absent, all colours appearing as shades of grey. ¯Çƒe macula
usually appears normal, but may be hypoplastic. ¯Çƒe photopic
(cone) ERG is abnormal and the scotopic may also be subnormal.

Blue cone monochromatism (incomplete
achromatopsia)

Blue cone monochromatism (X-linked) features only slightly subnormal acuity at 6/6ÔÇô6/9, but colour vision is completely absent.
Nystagmus and photophobia are not typical features. ¯Çƒere is a
normal macula. ¯Çƒe ERG is normal except for the absence of cone
responses to red and white light.

646 Macular Dystrophies

MACULAR DYSTROPHIES

## Best vitelliform macular dystrophy

## Introduction

Best vitelliform macular dystrophy (early- or juvenile-onset vitelliform macular dystrophy) is the second most common macular
dystrophy, a¯Ç£er Stargardt disease. It is due to allelic variation in
the bestrophin (BEST1) gene on chromosome 11q13. Bestrophin
is found on the plasma membrane of the RPE and functions as a
transmembrane ion channel. Inheritance is usually AD with variable penetrance and expressivity. Although there is signi¯ÇØcant
variability even in the same family, the prognosis is usually reasonably good until middle age, a¯Ç£er which visual acuity declines
in one or both eyes due to macular atrophy, scarring or MNV.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Signs. ¯Çƒere is gradual evolution through the following stages:

{ Pre-vitelliform is characterized by a subnormal EOG in
an asymptomatic infant or child with a normal fundus.

{ Vitelliform develops in infancy or early childhood and
does not usually impair vision. A round, sharply delineated (ÔÇÿsunny side up egg yolkÔÇÖ) macular lesion between 0.5
and 2 disc diameters in size develops within the RPE (Fig.
15.25A). ¯Çƒe size of the lesions and stage of development
in the two eyes may be asymmetrical and sometimes only
one eye is involved initially. Occasionally the condition
may be extramacular and multiple.

{ Pseudohypopyon may occur when part of the lesion
regresses (Fig. 15.25B), o¯Ç£en at puberty.

{ Vitelliruptive. ¯Çƒe lesion breaks up and visual acuity
drops (Fig. 15.25C).

{ Atrophic in which all pigment has disappeared leaving an
atrophic area of RPE.
¯Çü Investigation

{ FAF: the yellowish material is intensely hyperauto¯Çøuorescent. In the later atrophic stages hypoauto¯Çøuorescent areas
supervene.

{ FA shows corresponding hypo¯Çøuorescence due to masking
(Fig. 15.25D).

{ OCT shows material beneath, above and within the RPE
(Fig. 15.25E).

{ EOG is severely subnormal during all stages (Arden index
less than 1.5) and is also abnormal in carriers with clinically normal fundi.

may present with multifocal lesions. ¯Çƒe relationship between
multifocal vitelliform lesions, juvenile vitelliform (Best) dystrophy and adult-onset vitelliform macular dystrophy is incompletely de¯ÇØned, though some cases of each are associated with
mutations in the same genes.

Adult¯Çƒonset vitelliform macular
dystrophy (foveomacular vitelliform
dystrophy)

When symmetrical yellow deposits that resemble Best disease
develop in the macula of older individuals (usually over 40
years), the term ÔÇÿadult-onset vitelliform macular (foveomacular) dystrophyÔÇÖ is used. In contrast to juvenile Best disease, the
foveal lesions are o¯Ç£en smaller. ¯Çƒe lesions gradually increase
and then decrease in size over many years, leaving an area of
atrophic outer retina and RPE. ¯Çƒis process is accompanied by
a loss of visual acuity. ¯Çƒere is genetic heterogeneity including
mutations in the PRPH2, IMPG2 or the BEST1 gene. Vitelliform
macular lesions may also be observed in a wide range of other
conditions including acute idiopathic exudative polymorphous
vitelliform maculopathy and acute laser damage. ¯Çƒis appearance may occur secondary to basal laminar drusen, reticular
pseudodrusen and chronic vitreomacular traction. Adult-onset
vitelliform macular dystrophy may also be grouped in the
phenotypical spectrum of pattern dystrophy (see next). ¯Çƒere is
no treatment for the condition.
¯Çü Symptoms. O¯Ç£en the condition is discovered by chance, but
may present in late middle or old age with decreased central
vision. ¯Çƒe vision is o¯Ç£en reduced by one or more lines at
presentation and mild deterioration occurs subsequently. ¯Çƒe
prognosis varies from case to case.
¯Çü Signs. A round or oval slightly elevated yellowish subfoveal
deposit (Fig. 15.27A), generally smaller than the lesions of
Best disease, is seen in one or both eyes. ¯Çƒere may be central
pigmentation and numerous associated drusen are present in
some cases. ¯Çƒe material may persist, absorb, or break up and
disperse at a late stage, leaving atrophy of very variable severity.
Choroidal neovascularization sometimes supervenes.
¯Çü Investigation

648 Macular Dystrophies

## Pattern dystrophy of the retinal
pigment epithelium

Pattern (patterned) dystrophy of the RPE encompasses several
clinical appearances which are autosomal dominantly inherited.
¯Çƒis group is associated with the accumulation of lipofuscin in the
RPE and manifests with yellow, whitish, grey or pigmented deposits at the macula in a variety of appearances. ¯Çƒe most common
presenting symptom is reduced visual acuity or mild metamorphopsia in early adulthood or later, but many are asymptomatic.
¯Çƒe di¯Ç×erent clinical appearances are believed in many cases to
represent variable expression of a mutation in PRPH2, which
encodes peripherin-2, a protein involved in compaction of outer
segment discs of photoreceptors. Other genes and conditions can
give rise to a similar appearance and notably, a pattern dystrophylike appearance can occur in response to a range of pathogenic

A B

C D

vitelliform lesion in the inferior macula (arrowhead) and RPE atrophy (arrow); (D) vertical

increased choroidal backscatter, indicating RPE atrophy (arrow).

A B

pigment radiating from the foveola; (B) FA of the eye in (A) showing central and spoke-like
radiating hypo¯Çƒuorescence with surrounding hyper¯Çƒuorescence.

MIDD. ¯Çƒese dystrophies usually progress at a very slow rate and
the prognosis is generally good. ¯Çƒere is no treatment. Based on
the pigment distribution the pattern dystrophies have been classi¯ÇØed into ¯Ç×ve prominent patterns. Importantly, one pattern may
change into another in the same patient.
¯Çü Butter¯ÇØy-shaped pigment dystrophy: foveal yellow and
melanin pigmentation, commonly in a spoke-like or butter¯Çøy
wing-like conformation (Fig. 15.28A). FA shows central and
radiating hypo¯Çøuorescence with surrounding hyper¯Çøuorescence (Fig. 15.28B).
¯Çü Reticular dystrophy (Sj├Âgren): a network of pigmented lines
at the posterior pole. ¯Çƒis includes macroreticular (spidershaped) where pigment granules are initially seen at the fovea,
followed by reticular pigmentation that spreads to the periphery.
¯Çü Multifocal pattern dystrophy simulating fundus ¯ÇØavimaculatus: multiple, widely scattered, irregular yellow lesions which
may be similar to those seen in fundus ¯Çøavimaculatus.
¯Çü Adult-onset foveomacular vitelliform dystrophy ÔÇô see above.
¯Çü Fundus pulverulentus is extremely rare. Macular pigment
mottling develops.

North Carolina macular dystrophy

North Carolina macular dystrophy is a rare non-progressive condition. It was ¯ÇØrst described in families living in the mountains of
North Carolina and subsequently in many unrelated families in
other parts of the world. Inheritance is AD with complete penetrance, but highly variable expressivity. A third are asymptomatic,
a third have mild central visual loss and a third have moderate central visual loss. A few develop MNV with severe central visual loss.
Most a¯Ç×ected individuals have a mutation of the MCDR1 locus of
chromosome 6q, which results in dysregulation of the PRDM13
gene. Patients show a characteristic retinal phenotype and distribution of drusen that di¯Ç×ers from the drusen found in patients
with AMD. Despite the variability of macular phenotype, multimodal retinal imaging reveals consistent ¯ÇØndings.

¯Çü Grade 1 is characterized by yellowÔÇôwhite, drusen-like peripheral (Fig. 15.29A) and macular deposits that develop during
the ¯ÇØrst decade but may remain asymptomatic throughout life.
¯Çü Grade 2 is characterized by deep, con¯Çøuent macular deposits (Fig. 15.29B). ¯Çƒe long-term visual prognosis is guarded
because some patients develop neovascular maculopathy and
subretinal scarring.
¯Çü Grade 3 is characterized by atrophic macular lesions associated with variable impairment of visual acuity.
¯Çü FAF shows characteristic peripheral hyperauto¯Çøuorescent
streaks (Fig. 15.29C). ¯Çƒe atrophic macula shows hyperauto¯Çøuorescence in the early stages (Fig. 15.29D), followed by late
hypoauto¯Çøuorescence.
¯Çü OCT shows mild irregularity in the outer retina (Fig. 15.29E
and F).

## Familial dominant drusen

Familial dominant drusen (Doyne honeycomb choroiditis, malattia leventinese) is thought to represent an early-onset variant
of age-related macular degeneration. Inheritance is AD with variable expressivity. A mutation in the gene EFEMP1 (chromosome
2p16) is responsible, which results in increased lipid accumulation
in drusen. Signi¯ÇØcant di¯Ç×erences in severity and progression can
occur in the two eyes of the same patient and between patients
in the same family. Typically, asymptomatic yellowÔÇôwhite, elongated, radially orientated drusen centred on the macula develop in
the second decade (Fig. 15.30A). ¯Çƒe drusen may involve the disc
margin nasal to the disc. With age the lesions become increasingly
dense and acquire a honeycomb pattern (Fig. 15.30B). Initially the
visual symptoms are insidious and nyctalopia is not usually experienced. Central visual loss with acuity less than 6/60 may occur in
the seventh and eighth decades due to RPE degeneration, macular atrophy or occasionally MNV. Investigations include OCT and
FAF. ¯Çƒe ERG is normal, but the EOG is subnormal in those with
advanced disease.

## Sorsby fundus dystrophy

Sorsby fundus dystrophy (SFD) is a rare disorder that results in
bilateral visual loss, typically in late middle age. Inheritance is AD
with full penetrance but variable expressivity; allelic variation in
the gene TIMP3 (chromosome 22q) is responsible. ¯Çƒe predominant pathological feature is con¯Çøuent amorphous deposits found
between the basement membrane of the RPE and the inner layer
of Bruch membrane. Early presentation may be in the third decade
with nyctalopia, when con¯Çøuent yellowÔÇôwhite drusen-like deposits may be seen in the macula, along the arcades and nasal to the
disc (Fig. 15.31A), or in the ¯ÇØ¯Ç£h decade with sudden visual loss
due to exudative maculopathy secondary to MNV and subretinal
scarring (Fig. 15.31B). When early-onset drusen are associated
with MNV in young adulthood this diagnosis should be considered. Peripheral chorioretinal atrophy may occur by the seventh
decade and result in loss of ambulatory vision. OCT shows reticular pseudodrusen. ¯Çƒe ERG is initially normal but may be subnormal in later disease. Prolonged rod-mediated dark adaptation is a
sensitive test in early disease. Patients with MNV may respond to
anti-VEGF treatment.

## Concentric annular macular dystrophy

¯Çƒe prognosis is good in the majority of cases of (benign) concentric annular macular dystrophy, an AD disorder, although a
minority develop progressive loss of acuity and nyctalopia. Presentation is in adult life with mild impairment of central vision. A
paracentral ring scotoma is present on visual ¯ÇØeld testing.

## Central areolar choroidal dystrophy

Central areolar choroidal dystrophy, also termed central choroidal
sclerosis, is a genetically heterogeneous but typically AD condition
presenting in the third or fourth decades with gradual impairment

of central vision. Non-speci¯ÇØc foveal granularity progresses to
well-circumscribed RPE atrophy and loss of the choriocapillaris
(Fig. 15.32A) and subsequently slowly expanding geographic atrophy with prominence of large choroidal vessels (Fig. 15.32B). ¯Çƒere
is no treatment and the prognosis is for central vision is poor.

## Dominant cystoid macular oedema

Bilateral CMO (AD) commonly presents in adolescence with
gradual impairment of central vision. Treatment is ine¯Ç×ective and
macular atrophy inevitably ensues.

Sj├ÂgrenÔÇôLarsson syndrome

Sj├ÂgrenÔÇôLarsson syndrome (AR) is a neurocutaneous disorder
which occurs secondary to defective enzyme activity (fatty aldehyde dehydrogenase; ALDH3A2 encoded on chromosome 17p
with 11 distinct mutations). It is characterized by congenital ichthyosis, neurological problems and mild to moderate intellectual
disability. Presentation is with photophobia and poor vision, and
glistening yellowÔÇôwhite crystalline deposits develop at the macula
(Fig. 15.33), appearing during the ¯ÇØrst 2 years of life. Pigmentary
retinopathy (50%), cataract and colobomatous microphthalmos
may also occur.

## Maternally inherited diabetes and
deafness

Maternally inherited diabetes and deafness (MIDD) constitutes
around 1% of all cases of diabetes, with inheritance via mitochondrial DNA. Most patients develop progressive dystrophic
macular changes (Fig. 15.34A), but vision is not usually a¯Ç×ected.
¯Çƒe abnormal fundal appearance can be di¯ÇÜcult to see clinically, but FAF (Fig. 15.34B) and OCT (Fig. 15.34C) show typical
abnormalities.

## Choroideremia

(Courtesy of D Taylor and C S Hoyt, from Pediatric Ophthalmology and Strabismus, Elsevier Saunders 2005.)

Choroideremia is a progressive di¯Ç×use degeneration of the choroid,
RPE and photoreceptors. Inheritance is X-linked, so that males
are predominantly a¯Ç×ected. However, it is important to identify
female carriers, as 50% of their sons will develop choroideremia
and 50% of their daughters will be carriers. ¯Çƒe prognosis is very
poor. Although most patients retain some vision until the sixth
decade, severe visual loss occurs therea¯Ç£er. ¯Çƒe gene responsible
is CHM; a contiguous extended gene deletion can lead to deafness
and mental handicap.
¯Çü Symptoms. Nyctalopia, o¯Ç£en beginning in adolescence, is followed by reduced peripheral vision and some years later, by loss
of central vision. Clinically problematic disease occurs almost
exclusively in males, but females may rarely also develop severe
disease due to skewed inactivation of the X-chromosome.

## Gyrate atrophy

Gyrate atrophy (AR) is caused by a mutation in the OAT gene
(chromosome 10q) encoding the main ornithine degradation
enzyme, ornithine aminotransferase. De¯ÇØciency of the enzyme
leads to elevated ornithine levels in the plasma, urine, cerebrospinal ¯Çøuid and aqueous humour. ¯Çƒe visual prognosis is generally
poor, with legal blindness occurring around the age of 50.
¯Çü Symptoms. Myopia and nyctalopia in adolescence, with a subsequent gradual worsening of vision.

HEREDITARY VITREORETINOPATHIES

Juvenile X¯Çƒlinked retinoschisis

## Introduction

Juvenile retinoschisis is a rare congenital X-linked disease of the
retina caused by mutations in the RS1 gene (Xp22.13), which
encodes for retinoschisin. ¯Çƒis protein is secreted by photoreceptors and is involved in intercellular adhesion. ¯Çƒe condition
is characterized by bilateral maculopathy and can be associated
with peripheral retinoschisis. ¯Çƒe basic defect involves splitting
of the retinal nerve ¯ÇØbre layer from the rest of the sensory retina,
in contrast to acquired (age-related) retinoschisis in which splitting occurs at the outer plexiform layer. Vitreous haemorrhage
and retinal detachment are the main causes of signi¯ÇØcant visual
loss. ¯Çƒe visual acuity deteriorates during the ¯ÇØrst two decades as
a consequence of progressive maculopathy, but may then remain
reasonably stable until the ¯ÇØ¯Ç£h or sixth decades.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. ¯Çƒe presentation is usually in boys between the ages
of 5 and 10 years, who are experiencing di¯ÇÜculty with reading.
Less frequently, squint or nystagmus occurs in infancy associated with advanced peripheral retinoschisis and vitreous haemorrhage. Carrier females are asymptomatic and show no retinal
changes.
¯Çü Signs

{ ERG is subnormal in early disease and later becomes
extinguished.
¯Çü Treatment. ¯Çƒere is no cure for this condition. Cataract surgery may result in a transient improvement of vision. Reduction in ornithine levels with an arginine-restricted diet can
slow progression, but the highly restrictive nature of the diet
makes adherence to the diet di¯ÇÜcult. Vitamin B6 supplementation can reduce plasma ornithine in some patients.

## Progressive bifocal chorioretinal
atrophy

In progressive bifocal chorioretinal atrophy (AD), the implicated
gene region overlaps with that responsible for North Carolina
macular dystrophy but the two conditions are believed to be due
to di¯Ç×erent mutations. Dual foci of chorioretinal atrophy develop
temporal and nasal to the disc, with inevitable macular involvement
(Fig. 15.37). Nystagmus, myopia and retinal detachment may occur.

{ ¯Çƒe most common appearance is foveal schisis, appearing
as spoke-like striae radiating from the foveola, associated
with cystoid changes (Fig. 15.38A). Over time the striae
become less evident, leaving a blunted foveal re¯Çøex.

{ Whitish drusen-like dots and pigment variation may be
seen.

{ Peripheral schisis predominantly involves the inferotemporal quadrant. It does not extend but secondary

of the internal limiting membrane and the retinal nerve
¯ÇØbre layer, may develop oval defects (Fig. 15.38B) and

15.38D), vascular sheathing and pigmentary changes are
common and retinal ¯Çøecks and nasal dragging of retinal
vessels may be seen.

{ Complications include vitreous and intra-schisis haemorrhage, neovascularization, subretinal exudation and rarely
rhegmatogenous or tractional retinal detachment.
¯Çü Investigation

{ FAF shows variable macular abnormality, including spokelike patterns and central hypoauto¯Çøuorescence with surrounding hyperauto¯Çøuorescence.

{ OCT is useful for documenting maculopathy progression.
Cystic spaces in the inner nuclear and outer plexiform layers are commonly present (Fig. 15.38E), but the fovea may
simply appear disorganized.

## Deafness may be sensorineural or caused by recurrent otitis

media.
¯Çü Kniest dysplasia is usually caused by a defect in the type II
collagen gene COL2A1, which is also involved in Stickler syndrome type 1. Inheritance can be AD, but most cases represent
a de novo mutation. High myopia, vitreous degeneration, retinal detachment and ectopia lentis can occur. Systemic features
may include short stature, a round face and arthropathy.

## Diagnosis

¯Çƒe three characteristic ocular features are high myopia, vitreoretinal degeneration with an associated extremely high rate of retinal
detachment and cataract.
¯Çü Signs

## Treatment

¯Çü Topical or oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (e.g. dorzolamide eye drops three times daily) may reduce foveal schisis
and improve visual acuity in some patients.
¯Çü Vitrectomy may be required for vitreous haemorrhage or retinal detachment repair, but is technically challenging.
¯Çü Gene therapy is under investigation, with the aim of restoring normal function of the protein abnormality underlying
retinoschisis.

## Stickler syndrome

## Introduction

Stickler syndrome (hereditary arthro-ophthalmopathy) is a
genetically heterogeneous disorder of collagen connective tissue.
¯Çƒe condition a¯Ç×ects approximately 1 in 7500 to 9000 people. At
least eight clinically and genetically distinct sub-groups of Stickler syndrome are now recognized and it is possible to identify
the underlying genetic mutation in 95% of cases. Inheritance is
AD (STL1ÔÇôSTL3) or AR (STL4-STL8) with complete penetrance
but variable expressivity. Stickler syndrome is the most common
inherited cause of retinal detachment in children. Importantly,
there is a high risk of childhood blindness secondary to bilateral
retinal detachment in the three dominantly inherited sub-groups.
Stickler syndrome should be considered in any infant with cle¯Ç£
palate, deafness or joint hypermobility in association with myopia. In general, the prognosis has been poor, but is improving with
elevated standards of care.
¯Çü Stickler syndrome type I (STL1 ÔÇô membranous vitreous
type) is the most common form and is the result of mutations
in the COL2A1 gene, which encodes type 2 procollagen. ¯Çƒe
classic ocular and systemic features are present as originally
described by Stickler, though there is also a predominantly
ocular (non-syndromic) form of STL1.
¯Çü STL2 (beaded vitreous type) is caused by mutations in the
COL11A1 gene. Patients have congenital non-progressive high
myopia, sensorineural deafness and other features of Stickler
syndrome type 1.
¯Çü STL3 (non-ocular type) is due to mutations in the COL11A2
gene. A¯Ç×ected individuals have the typical systemic features,
but no ocular manifestations.
¯Çü STL4-STL8 (AR types) are extremely rare.
¯Çü Systemic features include mid-facial hypoplasia, joint hypermobility (Fig. 15.39A), Pierre-Robin-type features (micrognathia, cle¯Ç£ palate ÔÇô Fig. 15.39B and glossoptosis ÔÇô backward
displacement of the tongue), bi¯ÇØd uvula (Fig. 15.39C), mild

{ In STL1 patients exhibit an optically empty vitreous, a
retrolenticular membrane and circumferential equatorial
membranes that extend a short way into the vitreous cavity
(Fig. 15.39D and E).

{ In STL2 patients the vitreous has a ¯ÇØbrillary and beaded
appearance.

{ Radial lattice-like degeneration associated with RPE
hyperplasia, vascular sheathing and sclerosis.

{ Retinal detachment develops in approximately 50% in the
¯ÇØrst decade of life, o¯Ç£en as a result of multiple or giant
tears that may involve both eyes (Fig. 15.39F). ¯Çƒis should
not be confused with retinal dialysis.

{ Presenile cataract characterized by frequently nonprogressive peripheral cortical wedge-shaped, ¯Çøeck or
lamellar opacities is common.

{ Ectopia lentis can occur, but is uncommon.

{ Glaucoma (5ÔÇô10%) is associated with congenital angle
anomaly.

## Treatment

¯Çü Prophylactic 360┬░ retinal laser or cryotherapy reduces the incidence of retinal detachment. Regular screening with prophylactic treatment of retinal breaks is essential. Long-term review
of all patients is mandatory.
¯Çü Retinal detachment repair is challenging and vitrectomy is
generally indicated. Proliferative vitreoretinopathy is a particularly common complication and re-detachment may occur.
¯Çü Cataract in Stickler is o¯Ç£en visually inconsequential, particularly when early. If surgery is required, a careful preoperative
retinal evaluation with treatment of breaks should be performed. Vitreous loss and postoperative retinal detachment
are relatively common.
¯Çü Glaucoma treatment may be required. If early-onset and presumably related to angle anomaly, management is generally as
for congenital glaucoma (see Ch. 11).

TIP Prophylactic treatment is key to preserving vision in the
high-risk sub-groups of Stickler syndrome, as this reduces the
risk of visual loss secondary to retinal detachment.

## Wagner syndrome

Wagner syndrome (VCAN-related vitreoretinopathy) is a rare
condition having some features in common with Stickler syndrome, but no association with systemic abnormalities. Erosive

vitreoretinopathy is now known to be the same disorder. Inheritance is AD and mutations in the gene VCAN can be responsible.
Severity is variable, with up to 50% developing retinal detachment,
o¯Ç£en before the age of 15.

of the choriocapillaris. (Courtesy of E Messmer.)

## Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy

{ ¯Çƒe key abnormal ¯ÇØnding is an optically empty vitreous cavity (Fig. 15.40A) lacking structural elements and it is thought
this leads to reduced ÔÇÿsca¯Ç×oldingÔÇÖ support for the retina.

{ ¯Çƒe peripheral retinal vasculature is de¯ÇØcient.

{ Greyish-white avascular strands and membranes extend
into the vitreous cavity and there may be a circumferential
ridge-like condensation of the gel at or anterior to the retinal periphery (Fig. 15.40B).

{ Peripheral retinal changes including progressive chorioretinal atrophy (Fig. 15.40C) occur and nyctalopia is commonly troublesome. ¯Çƒe visual ¯ÇØelds gradually constrict.

Familial exudative vitreoretinopathy is a rare, slowly progressive condition characterized by failure of vascularization of the
temporal retinal periphery, similar to that seen in retinopathy of
prematurity. ¯Çƒe avascular peripheral retina leads to hypoxia and
stimulates neovascularization which can lead to vitreoretinal traction, subretinal exudation and haemorrhages, tractional retinal
detachment and foveal displacement. Inheritance is most commonly AD and rarely X-linked or AR, with high penetrance and
variable expressivity. To date, mutations in 10 genes have been
implicated which a¯Ç×ect normal retinal angiogenesis. ¯Çƒe clinical
appearance is o¯Ç£en asymmetrical and can vary signi¯ÇØcantly even
among a¯Ç×ected members of the same family. Presentation is in
childhood. ¯Çƒe prognosis is frequently poor, especially with early
aggressive onset.
¯Çü Signs

{ Cataract is common in younger adults and glaucoma can
develop.
¯Çü Investigation. FA shows non-perfusion due to choriocapillaris
loss (Fig. 15.40D). ¯Çƒe ERG may initially be normal, but later
shows a reduction of scotopic b-wave amplitudes and di¯Ç×use
cone-rod loss.
¯Çü Treatment. Retinal breaks and detachment are treated as they
occur, but extensive prophylaxis is avoided.

{ High myopia may be present.

{ Stage 1: peripheral avascularity. There is abrupt termination of retinal vessels at the temporal equator.

Enhanced S¯Çƒcone syndrome and
GoldmannÔÇôFavre syndrome

¯Çƒis is a rare inherited progressive retinal degeneration which has
a highly variable phenotype. ¯Çƒe human retina has three cone

{ Management depends on the clinical stage of the disease.

A B

foveal lamination with a thickened subfoveal ellipsoid zone (arrow). (Courtesy of S de Silva.)

photoreceptor types: short-wave sensitivity (S), middle-wave sensitivity (M) and long-wave sensitivity (L). Most inherited retinal dystrophies exhibit progressive attenuation of rods and all classes of cones.
However, enhanced S-cone syndrome is characterized by hyperfunction of S-cones and severe impairment of M- and L-cones, with
non-recordable rod function. GoldmannÔÇôFavre syndrome, which is
caused by mutations in the same gene, represents a severe variant.
¯Çü Inheritance is AR with variable expressivity. ¯Çƒe gene implicated is NR2E3, which encodes for a ligand-dependent transcription factor.
¯Çü Presentation is with nyctalopia in childhood and sometimes
hemeralopia (reduced vision in bright light). Colour vision is
decreased, but the tritan axis is preserved. Pigmentary changes
along the vascular arcades or mid-periphery may be associated in more advanced cases with round pigment clumps (Fig.
15.42A). Macular changes may include cystoid maculopathy
(without ¯Çøuorescein leakage) and schisis. Vitreous degeneration and peripheral retinoschisis can occur.
¯Çü OCT shows disrupted foveal lamination with a thickened subfoveal ellipsoid zone (Fig. 15.42B).
¯Çü ERG shows no rod response and the scotopic maximum
response and photopic ERG waveforms are similar.
¯Çü Prognosis is subject to considerable clinical variability; while
some patients maintain good visual acuity and show little progression, some patients develop extensive retinal degeneration and visual loss. About 30% have a visual acuity of 6/36 or
worse by late middle age.
¯Çü ¯Çƒere is no treatment other than supportive measures.

Snow¯Ç×ake vitreoretinal degeneration

¯Çƒis rare AD condition (gene KCNJ13) has some similarities to
Wagner syndrome. Retinal detachment is less common and the
prognosis is usually very good.
¯Çü Signs (Fig. 15.43)

{ Stage 1 shows extensive areas of ÔÇÿwhite without pressureÔÇÖ in
patients typically under the age of 15 years.

{ Stage 2 shows snow¯Çøake-like yellowÔÇôwhite crystalline
deposits in areas of ÔÇÿwhite with pressureÔÇÖ in patients aged
between 15 and 25.

{ Stage 3 manifests with vascular sheathing and pigmentation posterior to the area of snow¯Çøake degeneration in
patients aged between 25 and 50.

{ Stage 4 is characterized by increased pigmentation, gross
vascular attenuation, areas of chorioretinal atrophy and
less prominent snow¯Çøakes in patients over the age of 60.
¯Çƒe macula remains normal.

{ Other possible features include mild myopia, vitreous ¯ÇØbrillary degeneration and liquefaction, a waxy optic nerve head,
corneal guttae, retinal detachment and early-onset cataract.
¯Çü Investigation. ¯Çƒe ERG shows a low scotopic b-wave
amplitude.

662 Albinism

## Autosomal dominant neovascular

in¯Ç×ammatory vitreoretinopathy

A rare but interesting inherited (gene CAPN5) disorder, autosomal
dominant neovascular in¯Çøammatory vitreoretinopathy features panuveitis, o¯Ç£en of onset in early adulthood. ¯Çƒe initial symptom is typically
¯Çøoaters due to vitritis, with the development of peripheral vascular
closure with peripheral and then disc neovascularization, fundus pigmentation, epiretinal and subretinal ¯ÇØbrocellular membranes. Complications include vitreous haemorrhage, tractional retinal detachment,
CMO, cataract and neovascular glaucoma. ¯Çƒe ERG shows selective
loss of b-wave amplitude. ¯Çƒe prognosis can be poor. Peripheral retinal
photocoagulation and vitreous surgery may be required.

## Autosomal dominant
vitreoretinochoroidopathy

## Autosomal dominant vitreoretinochoroidopathy can be caused

by BEST1 mutations. Presentation is in adult life if symptomatic,
but frequently discovery is by chance. Vitreous cells and ¯ÇØbrillary
degeneration develop, with a non-progressive or very slowly progressive encircling band of pigmentary disturbance between the
ora serrata and equator, with a discrete posterior border. Within
the band there can be arteriolar attenuation, neovascularization,
punctate white opacities and later chorioretinal atrophy. Complications are uncommon, but can include CMO, vitreous haemorrhage and cataract. Microcornea and nanophthalmos have been
described in some patients. ¯Çƒe full-¯ÇØeld ERG is subnormal in
older patients only. ¯Çƒe prognosis is good.

ALBINISM

## Introduction

Albinism is a genetically determined, heterogeneous group of disorders of melanin synthesis in which either the eyes alone (ocular
albinism) or the eyes, skin and hair (oculocutaneous albinism)
may be a¯Ç×ected. ¯Çƒe latter may be either tyrosinase-positive or
tyrosinase-negative. ¯Çƒe di¯Ç×erent mutations are thought to act
through a common pathway involving reduced melanin synthesis in
the eye during development. Molecular genetics allows 7 subtypes
of non-syndromic oculocutaneous albinism (OCA) to be determined (OCA 1ÔÇô7). Patients with oculocutaneous albinism have an
increased risk of cutaneous basal cell and squamous cell carcinoma.

TIP Patients with oculocutaneous albinism are at increased
risk of cutaneous basal cell and squamous cell carcinoma and
should take precautions to avoid exposure to sunlight.

Tyrosinase¯Çƒnegative oculocutaneous
albinism (OCA type 1)

Individuals who have tyrosinase-negative (complete) albinism
are incapable of synthesizing any melanin and have white hair
and very pale skin (Fig. 15.44A) throughout life with a lack of

melanin pigment in all ocular structures. ¯Çƒe condition is caused
by mutations in TYR gene (chromosome 11q14), responsible for
the enzyme tyrosinase, usually with AR inheritance.
¯Çü Signs

{ VA is usually <6/60 due to foveal hypoplasia.

{ Nystagmus is typically pendular and horizontal. It usually increases in bright illumination and tends to lessen in
severity with age.

{ ¯Çƒe iris is diaphanous and translucent (Fig. 15.44B), giving
rise to a ÔÇÿpink-eyedÔÇÖ appearance (Fig. 15.44C).

{ ¯Çƒe fundus lacks pigment and shows conspicuously large
choroidal vessels. ¯Çƒere is also foveal hypoplasia with
absence of the foveal pit and poorly formed perimacular
vascular arcades (Fig. 15.44D).

{ ¯Çƒe optic chiasm has fewer uncrossed nerve ¯ÇØbres than
normal ÔÇô the majority of ¯ÇØbres from each eye cross to the
contralateral hemisphere. ¯Çƒis can be demonstrated with
visual evoked potentials.

{ Other features include high refractive errors of various
types, a positive angle kappa, squint and absent stereopsis.

Tyrosinase¯Çƒpositive oculocutaneous
albinism (OCA type 2)

Individuals who have tyrosinase-positive (incomplete) albinism
synthesize variable amounts of melanin. ¯Çƒeir hair may be white,
yellow or red and darkens with age. Skin is pale at birth but usually
darkens by 2 years of age (Fig. 15.45A). Inheritance is usually AR.
¯Çƒe condition is caused by hundreds of di¯Ç×erent mutations in the
OCA2 gene (chromosome 15q12), which plays a role in tyrosine
transport in melanosomes. ¯Çƒis is the commonest form of albinism in Africa and accounts for a third of cases worldwide.
¯Çü Ocular signs

{ VA is usually impaired due to foveal hypoplasia.

{ Iris may be blue or dark brown with variable translucency.

{ Fundus shows variable hypopigmentation (Fig. 15.45B).

Oculocutaneous albinism OCA
(types 3ÔÇô7)

OCT type 3 is seen especially in Black Africans. ¯Çƒe other subtypes are rare and have been reported in single families.

## Systemic associations

¯Çü ChediakÔÇôHigashi syndrome: a rare AR disorder arising from
a mutation of a lysosomal regulator protein. ¯Çƒis leads to failure of phagolysosome formation and recurrent pyogenic infections (especially Staphylococcus).
¯Çü HermanskyÔÇôPudlak syndrome: an extremely rare AR lysosomal storage disease with platelet dysfunction (easy bruising
and excessive bleeding). In some cases, pulmonary ¯ÇØbrosis and
granulomatous colitis with bleeding may occur.
¯Çü Waardenburg syndrome: an AD condition with a range of
systemic presentations including a white forelock, poliosis,
synophrys (ÔÇÿmonobrowÔÇÖ), deafness and sometimes limb and

neurological anomalies. Ocular features include lateral displacement of the medial canthi, hypochromic irides with

depigmentation.

Ocular albinism (OA)

In this variant, involvement is predominantly ocular, with normal skin and hair, although occasionally hypopigmented skin
macules may be seen. Two types are recognized: OA1 (Nettleship Falls syndrome) and OA2 (ForsiusÔÇôEriksson syndrome).
Inheritance is always X-linked. Female carriers are asymptomatic, but may show partial iris translucency, macular stippling
and mid-peripheral scattered areas of depigmentation and granularity (Fig. 15.47). A¯Ç×ected males have hypopigmented irides
and fundi.

CHERRY-RED SPOT AT THE MACULA

A cherry-red spot at the macula is a clinical sign seen in the context of thickening and loss of transparency of the retina at the
posterior pole. ¯Çƒe fovea is the thinnest part of the retina, allowing persistent transmission of the underlying vascular choroidal
hue when the surrounding retina becomes relatively opaque.
Causes include:
¯Çü Metabolic storage diseases. A group of rare inherited metabolic diseases, o¯Ç£en enzyme de¯ÇØciencies, leads to the pathological accumulation of lipid-based material in various tissues.

## With the passage of time the spot becomes less evident due

to retinal nerve ¯ÇØbre layer degeneration and consecutive optic
atrophy is seen. ¯Çƒey are almost exclusively AR. Some individual storage disorders are:

{ GM1 gangliosidosis (generalized): neurological features
are severe, with death typically by the age of 2 years. Subtle
corneal clouding can occur.

{ Mucolipidosis type I (sialidosis): a late-onset form features
myoclonus and seizures, but is compatible with a normal
life span. A more severe form causes severe neurodegeneration and death in early childhood. Corneal clouding and
optic atrophy may be seen.

{ GM2 gangliosidosis: this encompasses two conditions,
TayÔÇôSachs disease (Fig. 15.48) and Sandho¯Ç× disease. In

M Pennesi.)

Sachs disease.

both, there is progressive neurological deterioration, with
early blindness and death.

{ NiemannÔÇôPick disease: of the three main types (AÔÇôC),
only the ¯ÇØrst two feature a cherry-red spot. Abnormal ocular motility occurs in type C (chronic neuropathic).

{ Farber disease: systemically, aphonia, dermatitis, lymphadenopathy, psychomotor retardation, renal and cardiopulmonary disease may develop. Ocular features additional
to cherry-red spot development include pinguecula-like
conjunctival lesions and nodular corneal opacity.
¯Çü Central retinal and cilioretinal artery occlusion (see Ch. 13).
Retinal clouding results from oedema; the appearance is an
acute sign only.
¯Çü Retinotoxicity due to speci¯ÇØc agents including quinine, dapsone, gentamicin, carbon monoxide and methanol.
¯Çü Commotio retinae (see Ch. 22); oedema due to blunt trauma.

INTRODUCTION

## Anatomy of the peripheral retina

## Pars plana

¯Ç×e ciliary body starts 1 mm from the limbus and extends posteriorly for about 6 mm. ¯Ç×e anterior 2 mm consist of the pars
plicata, the remaining 4 mm, the ¯ÇØattened pars plana. In order not
to endanger either the lens or retina, the optimal location for a
pars plana surgical incision or intravitreal injection is 4 mm and
3.5 mm posterior to the limbus in phakic and pseudophakic eyes,
respectively. An incision through the mid-pars plana will usually
be located anterior to the vitreous base (see below).

## Ora serrata

¯Ç×e ora serrata (Fig. 16.1) is the junction between the retina and
ciliary body. In retinal detachment (RD), fusion of the sensory retina with the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and choroid limits
forward extension of subretinal ¯ÇØuid (SRF) at the ora. However,
there is no equivalent adhesion between the choroid and sclera and
choroidal detachments may progress anteriorly to involve the ciliary body (ciliochoroidal detachment).
¯Çü Dentate processes are tapering extensions of retina onto the
pars plana. ¯Ç×ey are more marked nasally than temporally and
display marked variation in contour.
¯Çü Oral bays are scalloped edges of pars plana epithelium between
dentate processes.
¯Çü Meridional folds (Fig. 16.2) are small radial folds of thickened
retinal tissue in line with dentate processes, most commonly

in the superonasal quadrant. A fold may occasionally exhibit a
small retinal hole at its apex. A meridional complex is a con¯Ç£guration in which a dentate process, usually with an associated
meridional fold, is aligned with a ciliary process.
¯Çü Enclosed oral bays (see Fig. 16.2) are small islands of pars
plana surrounded by retina as a result of the meeting of two
adjacent dentate processes. ¯Ç×ey should not be mistaken for
retinal holes.

## Vitreous base

¯Ç×e vitreous base (Fig. 16.3) is a 3ÔÇô4 mm wide zone straddling
the ora serrata, throughout which the cortical vitreous is strongly
attached. Following posterior vitreous detachment (PVD), the posterior hyaloid face remains attached at the vitreous base. Pre-existing
retinal holes within the attached vitreous base do not lead to RD.
Blunt trauma may cause an avulsion of the vitreous base, with tearing
of the non-pigmented epithelium of the pars plana along the baseÔÇÖs
anterior border and of the retina along the baseÔÇÖs posterior border.

## Innocuous peripheral retinal

degenerations (Fig. 16.4A)

## Peripheral retinal degenerations and other lesions carrying the

potential to lead to RD are described separately.
¯Çü Microcystoid (peripheral cystoid) degeneration consists of
tiny vesicles with indistinct boundaries on a greyish-white
background, making the retina appear thickened and less
transparent. ¯Ç×e degeneration starts adjacent to the ora serrata
and extends circumferentially and posteriorly with a smooth
undulating posterior border. Microcystoid degeneration is

## Vitreous
base

present in most adult eyes, increasing in extent with age. It is
not causally related to RD, though it may give rise to typical
degenerative retinoschisis.
¯Çü Paving stone degeneration is characterized by discrete yellowÔÇôwhite patches of focal chorioretinal atrophy that may have
pigmented margins (Fig. 16.4B and C). It is typically found
between the equator and the ora and is more common in the
inferior fundus. It is present to some extent in at least 25% of
normal eyes.
¯Çü Reticular (honeycomb) degeneration is an age-related change
consisting of a ¯Ç£ne network of perivascular pigmentation that
sometimes extends posterior to the equator (Fig. 16.4D).
¯Çü Peripheral drusen. Clustered or scattered small pale discrete
lesions (Fig. 16.4E) that may have hyperpigmented borders.
¯Ç×ey are similar to drusen at the posterior pole and usually
occur in the eyes of older individuals.
¯Çü Pars plana cyst. Clear-walled cysts, usually small, are derived
from non-pigmented ciliary epithelium. ¯Ç×ey are present in

5ÔÇô10% of eyes and are more common temporally. ¯Ç×ey do not
predispose to RD.

## Sites of vitreous adhesion

## Physiological

¯Ç×e peripheral cortical vitreous is loosely attached to the internal
limiting membrane (ILM) of the sensory retina. Sites of stronger
adhesion in the normal eye include:
¯Çü Vitreous base (very strong).
¯Çü Optic disc margins (fairly strong).
¯Çü Perifoveal (fairly weak).
¯Çü Peripheral blood vessels (usually weak).

## Pathological

## Abnormal adhesions may lead to retinal tear formation following

PVD, or to vitreomacular interface disease. Most are discussed in
detail later in this chapter.
¯Çü Lattice degeneration.
¯Çü Retinal pigment clumps.
¯Çü Cystic retinal tu¯Çøs.
¯Çü Vitreous base anomalies, such as extensions and posterior
islands.
¯Çü ÔÇÿWhite with pressureÔÇÖ and ÔÇÿwhite without pressureÔÇÖ.
¯Çü Zonular traction tu¯Çøs.
¯Çü Vitreomacular traction (see Ch. 14).
¯Çü Preretinal new vessels, e.g. proliferative diabetic retinopathy.

De¯Çƒnitions

¯Çü Retinal detachment (RD). RD refers to separation of the
neurosensory retina (NSR) from the RPE. ¯Ç×is results in the
accumulation of subretinal ¯ÇØuid (SRF) in the potential space
between the NSR and RPE.
¯Çü Rhegmatogenous (Greek rhegma ÔÇô break) RD requires a fullthickness defect in the sensory retina, which permits ¯ÇØuid
derived from synchytic (lique¯Ç£ed) vitreous to gain access to the
subretinal space. Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RRD),
as opposed to the presence merely of a cu¯ÇÜ of SRF surrounding
a retinal break, is said to be present when ¯ÇØuid extends further
than one optic disc diameter from the edge of the break.
¯Çü Tractional RD. ¯Ç×e NSR is pulled away from the RPE by contracting vitreoretinal membranes in the absence of a retinal
break.
¯Çü Exudative (serous, secondary) RD. SRF is derived from the
vessels of the NSR and/or choroid.
¯Çü Combined tractionalÔÇôrhegmatogenous RD results when a
retinal break is caused by traction from an adjacent area of
¯Ç£brovascular proliferation.
¯Çü Subclinical RD is generally used to refer to an asymptomatic
break surrounded by a relatively small amount of SRF, by de¯Ç£nition extending further than one disc diameter away from the
edge of the break but less than two disc diameters posterior to
the equator. It does not usually give rise to a subjective visual
¯Ç£eld defect. ¯Ç×e term is sometimes also used to describe an
asymptomatic RD of any extent.

paving stone; (D) honeycomb (reticular); (E) peripheral drusen.

## Ultrasonography

## Introduction

Ultrasonography (US) utilizes high frequency sound waves that
produce echoes as they strike the interface between acoustically
distinct structures. B-scan (two-dimensional) US is a key tool in
the diagnosis of RD in eyes with opaque media, particularly severe
vitreous haemorrhage.

## Technique

¯Çü ¯Ç×e examiner holds the US probe with the dominant hand
(Fig. 16.5A).
¯Çü Methylcellulose or an ophthalmic gel is placed on the tip of the
probe to act as a coupling agent.
¯Çü A cross-sectional view of the eye (Fig. 16.5B) is obtained and
the image can be viewed on a monitor.

¯Çü ¯Ç×e B-scan probe incorporates a marker for orientation that
correlates with a point on the display screen, usually to the
le¯Çø.
¯Çü Vertical scanning is performed with the marker on the probe
orientated superiorly (Fig. 16.6A).
¯Çü Horizontal scanning is performed with the marker orientated
towards the nose (Fig. 16.6B).
¯Çü ¯Ç×e eye is then examined with the patient looking straight
ahead, up, down, le¯Çø and right. For each position a vertical
and horizontal scan can be performed.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e examiner then moves the probe in the opposite direction
to the movement of the eye. For example, when examining the
right eye, the patient looks to the le¯Çø and probe is moved to
the patientÔÇÖs right, the nasal fundus anterior to the equator is
scanned and vice versa. Dynamic scanning is performed by
asking the patient to move the eye, whilst the probe position
is maintained.

A B

detachment. (Courtesy of P Terry.)

12

## Horizontal

**Vertical6**

pointing towards the brow; (B) horizontal scanning with the marker pointing towards the nose.

6

670 Peripheral Lesions Predisposing to Retinal Detachment

¯Çü Gain adjusts the ampli¯Ç£cation of the echo signal, similar to
volume control of a radio. Higher gain increases the sensitivity
of the instrument in displaying weak echoes such as vitreous
opacities. Lower gain only allows display of strong echoes such
as the retina and sclera, though improves resolution because it
narrows the beam.

PERIPHERAL LESIONS PREDISPOSING
TO RETINAL DETACHMENT

Patients with any predisposing lesion, or indeed any high-risk features for RD, should be educated about the nature of symptoms of
PVD and RD and the need to seek review urgently if these occur.

## Lattice degeneration

TIP Prophylactic treatment of lattice and snailtrack
degeneration is not required, as the lifetime risk of retinal
detachment secondary to these conditions is low.

## Snailtrack degeneration

## Snailtrack degeneration is characterized by sharply demarcated

bands of tightly packed ÔÇÿsnow¯ÇØakesÔÇÖ that give the peripheral retina
a white frost-like appearance (Fig. 16.9). It is viewed by some as a
precursor to lattice degeneration. Marked vitreous traction is seldom present so that U-tears rarely occur, although round holes are
relatively common. Prophylactic treatment is unnecessary.

## Cystic retinal tuft

A cystic retinal tu¯Çø (CRT), also known as a granular patch or retinal rosette, is a congenital abnormality consisting of a small, round
or oval, discrete elevated whitish lesion, typically in the equatorial
or peripheral retina, more commonly temporally. It may be associated with pigmentation at its base. It is comprised principally of
glial tissue. Strong vitreoretinal adhesion is commonly present and
both small round holes (Fig. 16.10) and horseshoe tears can occur.
It is an under-recognized lesion, though this may change with the
adoption of wide-¯Ç£eld imaging. CRT are present in up to 5% of the
population (bilateral in 20%) and may be the causative lesion in
5ÔÇô10% of eyes with RD, though the risk of RD in a given eye with
CRT is probably well under 1%.

## Degenerative retinoschisis

¯Çü Complications do not occur in most eyes with lattice
degeneration.

{ Tears may develop consequent to a PVD, when lattice is
sometimes visible on the ¯ÇØap of the tear.

{ Atrophic holes may rarely (2%) lead to RD. ¯Ç×e risk is
higher in young myopes. In these patients the RD may not
be preceded by acute symptoms of PVD (see below) and
SRF usually spreads slowly so that diagnosis may be delayed.
¯Çü Management. In an asymptomatic patient with lattice degeneration the areas of lattice do not need to be treated prophylactically, even if retinal holes are seen. However, the patient
should be advised of the symptoms of RD and written information should be provided. An associated asymptomatic
U-tear should be managed as discussed later in the chapter.

¯Çü Prevalence. Lattice degeneration is present in about 8% of the
population. It probably develops early in life, with a peak incidence during the second and third decades. It is found more
commonly in moderate myopes. ¯Ç×e lifetime risk of a RD in
an individual with lattice degeneration is approximately 1%.
However, lattice degeneration is present in about 40% of eyes
with RD. In these patients, detachment of the retina is caused
by premature PVD and tractional tears.
¯Çü Pathology. ¯Ç×ere is discontinuity of the ILM with variable
atrophy of the underlying NSR. ¯Ç×e vitreous overlying an area
of lattice is synchytic but the vitreous attachments around the
margins are exaggerated (Fig. 16.7).
¯Çü Signs. Lattice is most commonly bilateral, temporal and superior.

{ Spindle-shaped areas of retinal thinning, commonly
located between the equator and the posterior border of
the vitreous base.

{ Sclerosed vessels forming an arborizing network of white
lines is characteristic (Fig. 16.8A).

{ Some lesions may be associated with ÔÇÿsnow¯ÇØakesÔÇÖ, remnants of degenerate M├╝ller cells (Fig. 16.8B).

{ Associated hyperplasia of the RPE is common (Fig. 16.8C).

{ Small holes are common (Fig. 16.8D).

¯Çü Prevalence. Degenerative retinoschisis (RS) is present in about
5% of the population over the age of 20 years and is particularly prevalent in individuals who are hypermetropic.
¯Çü Pathology. RS is believed to develop from microcystoid
degeneration by a process of gradual coalescence of degenerative cavities (Fig. 16.11A), resulting in separation or splitting
of the NSR into inner and outer layers, with severing of neurones and complete loss of visual function in the a¯ÇÜected area.

## In typical retinoschisis the split occurs in the outer plexiform
layer and in the less common reticular retinoschisis at the level

of the nerve ¯Ç£bre layer.
¯Çü Symptoms

{ Photopsia and ¯ÇØoaters are absent because there is no

A B

C D

ÔÇÿsnow¯ÇØakesÔÇÖ; (C) lattice associated with pigmentary changes; (D) small holes within lattice
seen on scleral indentation. (Courtesy of NE Byer, from The Peripheral Retina in Pro¯Çƒle, A
Stereoscopic Atlas, Criterion Press, Torrance, CA, 1982 ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs B and D.)

{ It is rare for the patient to notice a visual ¯Ç£eld defect, even
with spread posterior to the equator.

{ Occasionally symptoms result from vitreous haemorrhage
or a progressive RD.
¯Çü Signs. RS is bilateral in up to 80%. Distinction between the
typical and reticular types is di¯ÇÖcult clinically, though the
inner layer is thinner and tends to be more elevated in the
latter. Di¯ÇÜerentiation is based principally on behaviour, with
complications much more common in the reticular form.

{ Early retinoschisis usually involves the extreme inferotemporal periphery of both fundi, appearing as an exaggeration of microcystoid degeneration with a smooth immobile
dome-shaped elevation of the retina (Fig. 16.11B). ¯Ç×is can
be shown with optical coherence tomography (OCT) (Fig.
16.11C) and is easily distinguished from a rhegmatogenous RD (Fig. 16.11D).

{ ¯Ç×e elevation is convex, smooth, thin and relatively immobile, unlike the opaque and corrugated appearance of a
rhegmatogenous RD (Fig. 16.12A).

{ ¯Ç×e thin inner leaf of the schisis cavity may be mistaken,
on cursory examination, for an atrophic longstanding
rhegmatogenous RD but demarcation lines and secondary
cysts in the inner leaf are absent.

{ ¯Ç×e lesion may progress circumferentially until it has
involved the entire periphery. ¯Ç×e typical form usually
remains anterior to the equator. ¯Ç×e reticular type is more
likely to spread posteriorly.

{ ¯Ç×e presence of a pigmented demarcation line is likely to
indicate the presence of associated RD.

{ ¯Ç×e surface of the inner layer may show ÔÇÿsnow¯ÇØakesÔÇÖ, whitish remnants of M├╝ller cell footplates, as well as sclerosis
of blood vessels and the schisis cavity may be bridged by
greyÔÇôwhite tissue strands.

{ Breaks may be present in one or both layers. Inner
layer breaks are small and round (Fig. 16.12B), whilst
the less common outer layer breaks are usually larger,
with rolled edges (Fig. 16.12C) and located behind the
equator.

672 Peripheral Lesions Predisposing to Retinal Detachment

A B

contain holes (arrow).

of NE Byer, from The Peripheral Retina in Pro¯Çƒle, A Stereoscopic Atlas, Criterion Press, Torrance, CA 1982.)

{ Microaneurysms and small telangiectatic vessels are common, particularly in the reticular type.

{ If a visual ¯Ç£eld defect is detectable, it is absolute, rather
than relative as in RD.
¯Çü Complications are uncommon and are thought to be much
more likely in the reticular form.

{ RD is rare; even in an eye with breaks in both layers the
incidence is only around 1%. ¯Ç×e detachment is almost
always asymptomatic, infrequently progressive and rarely
requires surgery.

{ Posterior extension of RS to involve the fovea is very rare
but can occur. Progression is generally slow.

{ Vitreous haemorrhage is rare.
¯Çü Management. ¯Ç×ough RD is rare, discussion of the symptoms is
prudent in all patients, especially those with double layer breaks.

{ A small peripheral RS discovered on incidental examination, especially if breaks are not present in both layers,
does not require routine review.

{ A large RS should be observed periodically, particularly if
breaks are present in both layers or it extends posterior to
the equator. Photography and visual ¯Ç£eld testing are useful, with OCT imaging when posterior extension is present. OCT is also useful for distinguishing between RS and
RD (see Fig. 16.11C and D).

{ Retinopexy or surgical repair may be indicated for relentless progression towards the fovea, when complication by
RD should be excluded.

{ Recurrent vitreous haemorrhage may necessitate
vitrectomy.

{ Progressive symptomatic RD should be addressed
promptly. More than one procedure may be necessary.
Scleral buckling may be adequate for smaller RD with
small outer layer breaks, but vitrectomy is generally indicated for more complex RD.

## Zonular traction tuft

¯Ç×is refers to a common (15%) phenomenon caused by an aberrant
zonular ¯Ç£bre extending posteriorly to be attached to the retina near
the ora serrata, and exerts traction on the retina at its base. It is typically located nasally. ¯Ç×e risk of retinal tear formation is around
2% and periodic long-term review is generally recommended.

## White with pressure and white without
pressure

¯Çü ÔÇÿWhite with pressureÔÇÖ (WWP) refers to retinal areas in
which a translucent whiteÔÇôgrey appearance can be induced
by scleral indentation (Fig. 16.13A). Each area has a fixed
configuration that does not change when indentation is
moved to an adjacent area. It may also be observed along
the posterior border of islands of lattice degeneration,
snailtrack degeneration and the outer layer of acquired retinoschisis. It is frequently seen in normal eyes and may be
associated with abnormally strong attachment of the vitreous gel, though may not indicate a higher risk of retinal
break formation.

A B

C D

M├╝ller cells; (B) circumferential microcystoid degeneration with progression to retinoschisis
supero- and inferotemporally; (C) OCT showing separation principally in the outer plexiform
layer; (D) OCT of rhegmatogenous retinal detachment in comparison. (Courtesy of J Harry
and G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

¯Çü ÔÇÿWhite without pressureÔÇÖ (WWOP) has the same appearance as WWP but is present without scleral indentation (Fig.
16.13B). WWOP corresponds to an area of fairly strong adhesion of condensed vitreous (Fig. 16.13C). On cursory examination a normal area of retina surrounded by WWOP may
be mistaken for a ¯ÇØat retinal hole. However, retinal breaks,
including giant tears, occasionally develop along the posterior
border of WWOP (see Fig. 16.13B). For this reason, if WWOP
is found in the fellow eye of a patient with a spontaneous giant
retinal tear, prophylactic therapy should be considered. Regular review should be considered for treated and untreated eyes,
though evidence for the bene¯Ç£t of this is limited.

## Myopic choroidal atrophy

Di¯ÇÜuse choroidal/chorioretinal atrophy in myopia is characterized
by di¯ÇÜuse or circumscribed (Fig. 16.14) choroidal depigmentation,
commonly associated with thinning of the overlying retina and
occurs typically in the posterior pole and equatorial area of highly

myopic eyes. Retinal holes developing in the atrophic retina may
occasionally lead to RD. Because of lack of contrast, small holes
may be very di¯ÇÖcult to visualize.

POSTERIOR VITREOUS DETACHMENT

## Introduction

PVD refers to separation of the cortical vitreous, along with the
delineating posterior hyaloid membrane (PHM), from the neurosensory retina posterior to the vitreous base. PVD occurs due to
vitreous gel liquefaction with age (synchysis) to form ¯ÇØuid-¯Ç£lled
cavities (Fig. 16.15A) and subsequently condensation (syneresis),
with access to the preretinal space allowed by a dehiscence in the
cortical gel and/or PHM. ¯Ç×e prevalence of PVD increases with
age; while less than 10% of individuals under 50 years have a PVD,
this rises to two-thirds of those over 70 years. It is usually a spontaneous event, but it can be induced by events such as cataract surgery, trauma, uveitis and panretinal photocoagulation. Perifoveal
