B; CL Schepens, ME Hartnett and T Hirose, from SchepensÔÇÖ
Retinal Detachment and Allied Diseases, ButterworthHeinemann 2000 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

¯Çü Signs

hyaloid detachment is followed by foveal separation, then detachment from the posterior retina as far as the equator, attachment
initially being retained at the optic disc. Subsequently complete
detachment of the cortical vitreous as far anteriorly as the vitreous
base takes place (Fig. 16.15B). ¯Ç×is process can occur in stages over
many months. With the exception of the vitreous base, physiological attachments to the retina and other structures are disengaged in
the course of a normal PVD. Overall, about 10% of all patients with
an acute symptomatic PVD will have a retinal tear. However, if a
vitreous haemorrhage is present, this rises to about 60%.

## Clinical features

{ ¯Ç×e detached PHM can o¯Çøen be seen clinically on slit lamp
examination as a crumpled translucent membrane in the
mid-vitreous cavity behind which the cavity is optically
clear (Fig. 16.16A).

{ Haemorrhage may be indicated by the presence of red
blood cells in the anterior vitreous or as (usually small)
focal intragel collections, or preretinally, when it sometimes forms a crescent shape bordering the limit of PHM
detachment. Its presence should prompt a careful search
for a retinal break (40ÔÇô90%), particularly with larger
amounts ÔÇô in such cases breaks tend to be posterior.

{ Pigment granules in the anterior vitreous on slit lamp
examination (the Shafer sign or ÔÇÿtobacco dustÔÇÖ ÔÇô see Fig.
16.21) are larger, darker and less re¯ÇØective than red blood
cells. ¯Ç×eir presence raises the possibility of a retinal break
(up to 95% sensitivity), with loss of continuity allowing
communication between the RPE and vitreous cavity.

{ Vitreous cells, if numerous, may signify the presence of a
break.

{ Retinal breaks (see below).
¯Çü Investigation. B-scan ultrasound (Fig. 16.16B) can demonstrate the extent of PVD. OCT can show separation of the posterior vitreous face and retina (Fig. 16.16C).

¯Çü Symptoms are usually, though not invariably, present.

{ Flashing lights (photopsia) in PVD is o¯Çøen described as a
lightning-like arc induced by eye or head movement and is
more noticeable in dim illumination. It is almost always seen in
the temporal periphery. ¯Ç×e mechanism is uncertain, but may
relate to traction on the optic disc and possibly at sites of vitreoretinal adhesion, including actual or potential retinal tears.

{ Floaters (myodesopsia) are mobile vitreous opacities most
evident against a bright pale background. ¯Ç×ey are o¯Çøen
described as spots, cobwebs or ¯ÇØies (muscae volitantes)
and are commonly present in individuals without a PVD,
especially myopes. A Weiss ring (Fig. 16.15C and D)
is the detached former attachment to the margin of the
optic disc and may be seen by the patient as a circle or other
large solitary lesion. Its presence does not necessarily indicate total PVD, nor does its absence con¯Ç£rm the absence
of PVD since it may be destroyed during the process of
separation. Floaters can also be due to vitreous blood.

{ Blurred vision. A di¯ÇÜuse haze may be due to dispersed
haemorrhage within the vitreous gel, with a variable
accompanying reduction in visual acuity (VA). Bleeding
can arise from a torn retinal blood vessel or from the site
of a retinal break. Blurring can also be caused by a visually
signi¯Ç£cant PHM or ¯ÇØoaters in the visual axis, which may
also cause impairment (usually slight) of acuity.

TIP A retinal break is usually present in a patient with a PVD
who has pigment granules (ÔÇÿtobacco dustÔÇÖ) in the vitreous on slit
lamp examination.

## Management

Patients with substantial acute symptoms of a PVD should be
examined as soon as possible, usually within 24ÔÇô48 hours, with
greater urgency in the presence of risk factors including myopia,
a past or family history of RD, high-risk syndromes such as Stickler, pseudophakia and symptoms such as a visual ¯Ç£eld defect,
reduced vision or very prominent ¯ÇØoaters. Enquiry should be
made about the presence of any condition predisposing to nonPVD vitreous haemorrhage, usually diabetes mellitus. If there is
only a single small ¯ÇØoater and no photopsia, evidence suggests
that the risk of retinal break in the symptomatic eye is insigni¯Ç£cantly higher than that of the asymptomatic fellow eye, so that
urgent assessment may not necessarily be required.
¯Çü Examination. ¯Ç×e anterior vitreous should be assessed for the
presence of blood and pigment. Careful retinal examination
including visualization of the ora serrata for 360┬░ should be performed and should generally include binocular indirect ophthalmoscopy or contact lens scleral indentation. ¯Ç×e asymptomatic
fellow eye should always be examined. If 10 or more vitreous cells
are present in a 1 mm slit lamp ¯Ç£eld the incidence of a retinal
break in a fellow asymptomatic eye has been reported as over 30%.
¯Çü Subsequent management. Recommendations for review vary
and the following is a general guide.

{ If there are no suspicious ¯Ç£ndings (e.g. vitreous blood) on
examination and no pre-existing risk factors as discussed

676 Retinal Breaks

A B

C D

biomicroscopy.

above then routine review may not be necessary. ¯Ç×e presence of features associated with higher risk should lead to
review a¯Çøer an interval of 1ÔÇô6 weeks depending on individual characteristics. Some authorities recommend further review in 6ÔÇô12 months.

{ Patients who present with multiple prominent ¯ÇØoaters or
hazy vision should be reviewed as this has been found to be
associated with a higher risk of retinal break.

{ Discharged patients should be given clear instructions
emphasizing the need to re-attend urgently in the event
of signi¯Ç£cant new symptoms. Optimally, written information reiterating the advice should be provided. Assurance
can be given that in most cases the ¯ÇØoaters will resolve and
become much less noticeable with time, though exceptionally vitrectomy is necessary.

{ If an area of the fundus cannot be viewed clearly due to
obscuration by blood, then weekly review is prudent.

{ Presentation with di¯ÇÜuse fundus-obscuring vitreous
haemorrhage (in the absence of a condition predisposing

to non-PVD vitreous haemorrhage) is associated with
a high risk of retinal break (60ÔÇô90%) and RD (40%). A
relative a¯ÇÜerent pupillary defect should be excluded and
B-scan ultrasonography performed regularly until resolution, in order to exclude an underlying detachment or
identi¯Ç£able break. A very low threshold for vitrectomy
should be adopted, particularly in the presence of other
risk factors, notably previous RD in the fellow eye.

{ ¯Ç×e management of retinal breaks is discussed below.

RETINAL BREAKS

## Introduction

## Retinal breaks develop in most cases as a result of traction at sites

of vitreoretinal adhesion and occur in up to about 1 in 5 eyes with
symptomatic PVD. In the presence of a break, retrohyaloid ¯ÇØuid
has access to the subretinal space. Asymptomatic retinal breaks of
some sort are present in about 8% of the general population.

A B

C

gel; (B) ultrasound B-scan; (C) OCT of posterior pole.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Timing. Breaks are usually present at or soon a¯Çøer the onset
of symptoms of PVD, although in a minority (up to 5%) tear
formation may be delayed by several weeks.
¯Çü Location. Tears associated with PVD are usually located in
the upper retina and are more commonly temporal than nasal.
Macular breaks related to PVD are rare, but when they occur
are usually round and in a myopic eye. ¯Ç×ey are aetiologically
distinct from age-related macular holes.
¯Çü Morphology. Retinal breaks may be ¯ÇØat or associated with a
surrounding cu¯ÇÜ of SRF. If ¯ÇØuid extends more than one disc
diameter from the edge of a break, a RD is said to be present.

{ U-tears (horseshoe) consist of a ¯ÇØap, its apex pulled anteriorly by the vitreous, the base remaining attached to the
retina (Fig. 16.17A).

{ Operculated tears in which the ¯ÇØap is completely torn away
from the retina by detached vitreous gel to leave a round
or oval break (Fig. 16.17B). ¯Ç×e separated retinal patch
is known as an operculum and can usually be seen suspended in the vitreous cavity in the region of the break,

which can be di¯ÇÖcult to delineate ÔÇô this may be aided by
the presence of preretinal blood at the site.

{ Retinal holes are round or oval, usually smaller than tears
and carry a lower risk of RD, which when it does occur is
most commonly a slowly progressive shallow RD in a young
female myope. A PVD is not necessarily present, but if vitreous separation has occurred an operculum may be visible in
the nearby vitreous cavity. Round holes may occur in lattice
degeneration. Round holes leading to RD may be distinct in
most cases from the round atrophic retinal holes (Fig. 16.7C)
that are a variant of paving stone degeneration and probably
carry a lower risk, though clinically distinction is not easy.

{ A dialysis is a circumferential tear along the ora serrata and
is usually a consequence of blunt ocular trauma. Importantly, the vitreous gel remains attached to the posterior
margin. It typically appears as a large very peripheral break
with a regular rolled edge (Fig. 16.17D). ¯Ç×e RD is o¯Çøen
slowly progressive in the absence of a PVD.

{ A giant retinal tear (Fig. 16.17E and F) is a variant of
U-tear, by de¯Ç£nition involving 90┬░ or more of the retinal
circumference. In contrast to dialysis, vitreous gel remains

678 Retinal Breaks

A B

C D

E F

(arrow) with subretinal ¯ÇØuid; (D) retinal dialysis (arrowheads); (E) giant retinal tear; (F)
vitreous attached to the anterior edge of a giant tear. (Courtesy of C Barry ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs D and E;
CL Schepens, ME Hartnett and T Hirose, from SchepensÔÇÖ Retinal Detachment and Allied
Diseases, Butterworth-Heinemann 2000 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. F.)

attached to the anterior margin of the break. It is most frequently located in the immediate post-oral retina or, less
commonly, at the equator.

## Management

¯Ç×e management of many categories of break remains imperfectly
de¯Ç£ned and approaches di¯ÇÜer between retinal subspecialists. ¯Ç×ere
has been a trend in recent years towards less aggressive prophylactic treatment of asymptomatic and operculated breaks, with
substitution by observation and patient education. Patients should
always be instructed about the symptoms of vitreous detachment
and RD, optimally supplemented by written information, and
should seek review urgently in the event of new visual symptoms.
¯Ç×e risk associated with prophylaxis is small, but includes new
break formation and epiretinal membrane formation. Severe complications are rare.
¯Çü Risk factors for progression to detachment

{ Miscellaneous factors include a history of RD in the fellow eye, prior cataract surgery (particularly if vitreous loss
occurred), myopia, a family history of RD and systemic
conditions such as Marfan, Stickler and EhlersÔÇôDanlos
syndromes. Evidence suggests that prophylactic treatment
should be considered for asymptomatic breaks, including
round operculated and atrophic holes prior to cataract surgery, laser capsulotomy and intravitreal injection, particularly when other risk factors are present.

{ Symptomatic breaks associated with an acute PVD are at
higher risk than asymptomatic breaks detected on routine
examination.

{ Size. Larger breaks carry a higher risk of progression.

{ Persistent vitreoretinal traction. An operculated tear, in
which the focus of vitreous traction has detached from
the break, is safer than a break, typically a U-tear, in which
traction persists. Round holes are rarely associated with
ongoing vitreoretinal traction. Apparent demonstration of
a complete PVD clinically or on ultrasonography, with no
residual attachment in the region of the break, is a favourable feature, though cannot be relied upon.

{ Shape. U-tears are at higher risk than round holes.

{ Location. Superior breaks are at higher risk of progression
to RD, probably due to the protective e¯ÇÜect of gravity upon
inferior breaks. With superotemporal tears, the macula is
threatened early in the event of RD. Equatorial breaks are
more likely to progress than oral breaks, as the latter are
usually located within the vitreous base.

{ Pigmentation around a retinal break indicates chronicity
and a degree of stability.

of 2ÔÇô4 weeks, then 1ÔÇô3 months, then 6ÔÇô12 months, then annually. ¯Ç×e presence of an intact bridging vessel overlying the
break may indicate ongoing vitreoretinal traction ÔÇô which may
also cause vitreous haemorrhage ÔÇô and treatment should be

¯Çü Asymptomatic U-tears. ¯Ç×e risk of progression to RD is low
at 5%, which is a similar rate to that in treated symptomatic
U-tears and observation as for operculated tears is generally
safe in the absence of factors indicating higher risk.
¯Çü Traumatic retinal breaks, including acute dialyses, should
always be treated.
¯Çü An asymptomatic dialysis can sometimes be followed without treatment. However, most cases are treated surgically if an
associated RD is present.
¯Çü Asymptomatic subclinical RD. Progression is not invariable in RD discovered incidentally, with about 10% becoming
symptomatic over 2ÔÇô3 years and a decision regarding intervention should be made on a case-by-case basis. For example,
many practitioners would prefer to treat rather than observe a
large superotemporal RD that extends posterior to the equator,
but may observe a small inferior longstanding RD. With any
option, fully informed patient consent and regular review is
vital. Surgery is generally indicated if progression occurs.
¯Çü Asymptomatic ¯Çƒat round holes do not require prophylactic
treatment, but some guidelines recommend review every 1ÔÇô2
years.

TIP A patient with a symptomatic retinal break associated
with an acute PVD is at risk of RD and needs rapid treatment.

## Treatment techniques

Retinal breaks without RD can be treated with laser (via a slit
lamp or binocular indirect ophthalmoscopy [BIO]) or cryotherapy. In most cases laser is the optimal technique as it is more
precise, causing less collateral retinal damage, with a lower risk
of epiretinal membrane formation. Adequate treatment of the
base of a very peripheral lesion may only be possible with BIO
or cryotherapy due to the requirement for indentation to visualize the area. Cryotherapy may be preferred for multiple contiguous tears or extensive lesions and in eyes with hazy media or
small pupils.
¯Çü Laser retinopexy. Using slit lamp delivery under topical
anaesthesia (occasionally regional or even general anaesthesia is required), typical settings are a duration of 0.1 second,
a spot size of 200ÔÇô300 ╬╝m with a three-mirror contact lens
or 100ÔÇô200 ╬╝m with a wide-¯Ç£eld lens and a starting power
of 200mW. ¯Ç×e power should be adjusted as appropriate to
obtain moderate blanching. With head-mounted BIO delivery, the spot size is estimated and adjusted by adjusting the
condensing lens (usually 20 D) position. ¯Ç×e lesion is surrounded with two to three rows of con¯ÇØuent burns (Fig.
16.18). With both forms of laser, care should be taken to
identify appropriate landmarks frequently to avoid inadvertent macular damage.

{ Aphakia, now rare, confers a higher risk.
¯Çü Acutely symptomatic U-tears. Up to 90% of these lead to RD.
Treatment (see below) reduces the risk to 5% so urgent intervention is needed.
¯Çü Operculated tears, particularly in a person who is asymptomatic, are believed to be at low risk of progression to RD and
can safely be observed in most cases. A recommended review
schedule (symptomatic or asymptomatic) is an initial interval

commonly required. For lesions behind the equator, a small

its rubber sleeve. ¯Ç×e instrument should initially be purged (e.g.

prophylactic cryotherapy to several retinal breaks.

temperature is set (typically ÔêÆ85┬░C). It is useful to check the
e¯ÇÜectiveness of the instrument by activating it in sterile water
for 10 seconds, when a 5-mm ice ball should form. Under BIO
visualization, the lesion is indented and the foot pedal depressed
until visible whitening of the retina is seen. It is critical not to
remove the tip from the treated area until thawing occurs (2ÔÇô3
seconds). Care should be taken to maintain orientation of the
probe whilst the tip is not visible and not to mistake indentation
by the sha¯Çø of the probe for that of the tip. ¯Ç×e lesion is surrounded by a single row of applications, in most cases achieved
by one or two applications to a tear. ¯Ç×e eye is usually padded

(Fig. 16.19). Review should usually take place a¯Çøer 1ÔÇô2 weeks.

RHEGMATOGENOUS RETINAL
DETACHMENT

## Introduction

## Pathogenesis

Rhegmatogenous RD a¯ÇÜects about 1 in 10000 of the population
each year, with both eyes eventually a¯ÇÜected in about 10%. In most
cases it is characterized by the presence of a retinal break in concert with vitreoretinal traction that allows accumulation of lique¯Ç£ed vitreous under the neurosensory retina, separating it from the
RPE. Even though a retinal break is present, a RD will almost never
occur if the vitreous is not at least partially lique¯Ç£ed and traction
is absent. Over 40% of RDs occur in myopic eyes and the higher
the refractive error the greater the risk. Vitreous degeneration and
PVD and predisposing lesions such as lattice and snailtrack degeneration are more common in myopia. Highly myopic eyes are also at
risk from RD due to small round holes in chorioretinal atrophy and
from macular holes. Vitreous loss during cataract surgery and laser
capsulotomy also carries a greater risk of RD in highly myopic eyes.

Identi¯Çƒcation of retinal breaks

¯Çü Distribution of breaks in eyes with RD is approximately as follows:
60% superotemporal quadrant, 15% superonasal, 15% inferotemporal and 10% inferonasal. ¯Ç×e upper temporal region should
therefore be examined in detail if a break cannot be detected initially. It should also be remembered that about 50% of eyes with
RD have more than one break, o¯Çøen within 90┬░ of each other.
¯Çü Con¯ÇØguration of SRF. SRF spread is governed by (a) gravity,
by (b) anatomical limits (ora serrata and optic nerve) and by
(c) the location of the primary retinal break. If the primary
break is located superiorly, the SRF ¯Ç£rst spreads inferiorly on
the same side of the fundus as the break and then superiorly
on the opposite side, so that the likely location of the primary
retinal break can be predicted.
¯Çü Modi¯ÇØed Linco¯Ç£ÔÇÖs rules:

{ A shallow inferior RD in which the SRF is slightly higher
on the temporal side points to a primary break located
inferiorly on that side (Fig. 16.20A).

{ A primary break located at 6 oÔÇÖclock will cause an inferior
RD with equal ¯ÇØuid levels (Fig. 16.20B).

{ In a bullous inferior RD, the primary break usually lies
above the horizontal meridian (Fig. 16.20C).

{ If the primary break is located in the upper nasal quadrant
the SRF will revolve around the optic disc and then rise on
the temporal side until it is level with the primary break
(Fig. 16.20D).

{ A subtotal RD with a superior wedge of attached retina
points to a primary break located in the periphery nearest
its highest border (Fig. 16.20E).

{ When the SRF crosses the vertical midline above, the primary break is near to 12 oÔÇÖclock, the lower edge of the RD
corresponding to the side of the break (Fig. 16.20F).

## Symptoms

¯Ç×e classic premonitory symptoms reported in about 60% of patients
with spontaneous rhegmatogenous RD are ¯ÇØashing lights and ¯ÇØoaters
associated with acute PVD. A¯Çøer a variable period of time a curtainlike relative peripheral visual ¯Ç£eld defect may ensue and can progress
to involve central vision. In some patients this may not be present
on waking in the morning, due to spontaneous absorption of SRF
while inactive overnight, only to reappear later in the day. A lower
¯Ç£eld defect is usually appreciated more quickly by the patient than an
upper defect. ¯Ç×e quadrant of the visual ¯Ç£eld in which the ¯Ç£eld defect
¯Ç£rst appears is useful in predicting the location of the primary retinal
break, which will be in the opposite quadrant. ¯Ç×e location of photopsia is of no value in predicting the site of the primary break. Loss
of central vision may be due to involvement of the fovea by SRF or,
infrequently, obstruction of the visual axis by a large bullous RD.

## Signs

## General

¯Çü Relative a¯Ç£erent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) is
present in an eye with an extensive RD.
¯Çü Intraocular pressure (IOP) is o¯Çøen lower by about 5 mmHg
compared with the normal eye. If the intraocular pressure is
extremely low, an associated choroidal detachment may be
present. It may be raised, characteristically in SchwartzÔÇôMatsuo
syndrome, in which RRD is associated with an apparent mild
anterior uveitis, o¯Çøen due to a dialysis secondary to previous
blunt trauma in a young man. ¯Ç×e aqueous cells are believed in
most cases to be displaced photoreceptor outer segments that
compromise trabecular out¯ÇØow. Both the aqueous ÔÇÿcellsÔÇÖ and
the elevated IOP typically resolve following repair of the RD.

D E F

682 Rhegmatogenous Retinal Detachment

¯Çü Iritis is common but usually mild and should be di¯ÇÜerentiated from SchwartzÔÇôMatsuo syndrome (above). Occasionally
it may be severe enough to cause posterior synechiae and the
underlying RD may be overlooked.
¯Çü Tobacco dustÔÇÖ consisting of pigment cells is commonly seen
in the anterior vitreous (Fig. 16.21). Substantial vitreous blood
or in¯ÇØammatory cells are also highly speci¯Ç£c.
¯Çü Retinal breaks (see Fig. 16.17) appear as discontinuities in the
retinal surface. ¯Ç×ey are usually red because of the colour contrast
between the sensory retina and underlying choroid. However, in
eyes with hypopigmented choroid (e.g. high myopia), the colour
contrast is decreased and small breaks may be overlooked.
¯Çü Retinal signs depend on the duration of RD and the presence or absence of proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR) as
described below.

## Fresh retinal detachment

¯Çü The RD has a convex configuration and a slightly opaque
and corrugated appearance as a result of retinal oedema
(Fig. 16.22AÔÇôC). There is loss of the underlying choroidal

¯Çü Macular pseudohole. Because of the thinness of the foveal

posterior pole is detached. ¯Ç×is should not be mistaken for a
true macular hole, which may give rise to RD in highly myopic

¯Çü B-scan ultrasonography shows good mobility of the retina
and vitreous.

## Longstanding retinal detachment

¯Çü Retinal thinning secondary to atrophy is a characteristic ¯Ç£nding and should not lead to a misdiagnosis of retinoschisis.

¯Çü Intraretinal cysts (Fig. 16.23AÔÇôC) may develop if the RD has
been present for about 1 year. ¯Ç×ese tend to disappear a¯Çøer
retinal reattachment.
¯Çü Subretinal demarcation lines (ÔÇÿhigh waterÔÇÖ or ÔÇÿtideÔÇÖ marks)
caused by proliferation of RPE cells at the junction of ¯ÇØat and
detached retina (Fig. 16.23D) are common, taking about 3
months to develop. Pigmentation tends to decrease over time.
Although representing sites of increased adhesion, they do not
invariably limit the spread of SRF.

## Proliferative vitreoretinopathy

Proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR) is caused by epiretinal and
subretinal membrane formation, contraction of which leads to
tangential retinal traction and ¯Ç£xed retinal fold formation (Fig.
16.24). Usually, PVR occurs following surgery for rhegmatogenous RD or penetrating injury, though it may also occur in eyes

684 Rhegmatogenous Retinal Detachment

A B

C D

to the equator of the retina; (C) early PVR with anteriorly retracted vitreous gel and equatorial
circumferential retinal folds; (D) advanced PVR with a funnel-like retinal detachment bridged
by dense vitreous membranes. (Courtesy of CL Schepens, ME Hartnett and T Hirose, from
SchepensÔÇÖ Retinal Detachment and Allied Diseases, Butterworth-Heinemann 2000.)

with rhegmatogenous RD that have not had previous retinal surgery. ¯Ç×e main features are retinal folds and rigidity so that retinal mobility induced by eye movements or scleral indentation is
decreased. Progression from one stage to the next is not inevitable.
¯Çü Grade A (minimal) PVR is characterized by di¯ÇÜuse vitreous haze
and tobacco dust. ¯Ç×ere may also be pigmented clumps on the inferior surface of the retina. Although these ¯Ç£ndings occur in many
eyes with RD, they are particularly severe in eyes with early PVR.
¯Çü Grade B (moderate) PVR is characterized by wrinkling of the
inner retinal surface (Fig. 16.25A), decreased mobility of vitreous
gel, rolled edges of retinal breaks, tortuosity of blood vessels and
retinal sti¯ÇÜness (Fig. 16.25B). ¯Ç×e epiretinal membranes responsible for these ¯Ç£ndings typically cannot be identi¯Ç£ed clinically.
¯Çü Grade C (marked) PVR is characterized by rigid full-thickness
retinal folds (o¯Çøen star-shaped) with heavy vitreous condensation and strands (Fig. 16.25C and D). It can be either anterior
(A) or posterior (P), the approximate dividing line being the
equator of the globe. ¯Ç×e severity of proliferation in each area

is expressed by the number of clock hours of retina involved
although proliferations need not be contiguous.
¯Çü Advanced disease shows gross reduction of retinal mobility
with retinal shortening and a characteristic funnel-like triangular conformation (see Fig. 16.24D).
¯Çü Management. PVR is usually managed by undertaking a pars
plana vitrectomy, with removal of the membranes. Peeling
the internal limiting membrane (ILM) at the time of surgery
in patients with advanced PVR improves the success rate of
surgery. It is sometimes necessary to perform a relaxing retinotomy to relieve traction. Per¯ÇØuorocarbon liquid is used to
stabilize the retina, which is then replaced with long-acting gas
or silicone oil (see later in this chapter).

Di¯Ç×erential diagnosis

¯Ç×e tractional and exudative forms of RD are described later in
the chapter.

## Degenerative retinoschisis

See above.

## Choroidal detachment

Causes of choroidal detachment (also known as ciliochoroidal or
choroidal e¯ÇÜusion) include hypotony, particularly following glaucoma drainage surgery (see Ch. 11), sulfonamide drugs such as
acetazolamide and topiramate, uveitis, posterior scleritis, choroidal tumours and a cyclodialysis cle¯Çø following trauma or surgery.
Occasionally it occurs secondary to RD. Idiopathic cases are generally labelled as uveal e¯ÇÜusion syndrome (see below).
¯Çü Symptoms. Photopsia and ¯ÇØoaters are absent because there is
no vitreoretinal traction. A visual ¯Ç£eld defect may be noticed if
the choroidal detachment is extensive.
¯Çü Signs

{ Low intraocular pressure is common as a result of the
cause and of concomitant detachment of the ciliary body.

{ ¯Ç×e elevations are brown, convex, smooth and relatively
immobile (Fig. 16.26A). Four lobes are typically present.
Temporal and nasal bullae tend to be most prominent.

{ Large ÔÇÿkissingÔÇÖ choroidal detachments may obscure the
view of the fundus.

urgency of intervention include the presence of a superior or
large break, from which SRF is likely to spread more rapidly and
advanced syneresis as is found in high myopia. Patients with dense
fresh vitreous haemorrhage in whom visualization of the fundus is
impossible should also be operated on as soon as possible if B-scan
ultrasonography shows an underlying RD. ¯Ç×e patient should not
eat or drink if urgent surgery is contemplated. Minimizing activity may be helpful and bed-rest with the head turned so that the
retinal break is in the most dependent position tends to reduce the
amount of SRF and therefore facilitates surgery.

TIP Urgent surgery is needed in a patient with an acute
progressive retinal detachment if the macula is threatened.
Even if the macula is o¯Ç× the visual results are better if the
surgery is done within 72 hours.

## Pneumatic retinopexy

Pneumatic retinopexy (Fig. 16.27) is an outpatient procedure in
which an intravitreal gas bubble together with cryotherapy or laser
are used to seal a retinal break and reattach the retina without scleral
buckling. ¯Ç×e most frequently used gases are sulfur hexa¯ÇØuoride
(SF6) and the longer-acting per¯ÇØuoropropane (C3F8) (Fig. 16.28).
It has the advantage of being a relatively quick, minimally invasive,
ÔÇÿo¯ÇÖce-basedÔÇÖ procedure. However, success rates are worse than those
achievable with conventional scleral buckling. ¯Ç×e procedure is usually reserved for treatment of uncomplicated RD with a small retinal
break or a cluster of breaks extending over an area of less than two
clock hours in the upper two-thirds of the peripheral retina.

## Principles of scleral buckling

Scleral buckling, sometimes referred to as conventional or external RD surgery as opposed to the internal approach of pars plana
vitrectomy (see below), is a surgical procedure in which material sutured onto the sclera (explant) creates an inward indentation (buckle ÔÇô Fig. 16.29). ¯Ç×e purpose is to close retinal breaks
by opposing the RPE to the sensory retina and to reduce dynamic
vitreoretinal traction at sites of local vitreoretinal adhesion. ¯Ç×is
technique should always be used for patients with a detachment of
the retina secondary to a post-traumatic dialysis.
¯Çü Explants are made from so¯Çø or hard silicone. ¯Ç×e entire break
should ideally be surrounded by about 2 mm of buckle. It is
also important for the buckle to involve the area of the vitreous
base anterior to the tear in order to prevent the possibility of
subsequent reopening of the tear and anterior leakage of SRF.
¯Ç×e dimensions of the retinal break can be assessed by comparing it with the diameter of the optic disc.
¯Çü Buckle con¯ÇØguration can be radial, segmental, circumferential or encircling, depending on the size, con¯Ç£guration and
number of breaks.
¯Çü Technique. ¯Ç×e conjunctiva is incised (peritomy) to facilitate access, following which retinal breaks are localized (Fig.
16.30A) and cryotherapy applied. An explant of appropriate
dimensions and orientation is then sutured to the sclera and
the position of the buckle checked in relation to the break (Fig.
16.30BÔÇôD).

{ ¯Ç×e elevations do not extend to the posterior pole because
they are limited by the vortex veins entering their scleral
canals (Fig. 16.26B). However, in contrast to RDs, they
extend anteriorly beyond the ora serrata.
¯Çü Treatment is directed at the cause. Drainage via partialthickness sclerectomies is occasionally required.

Uveal e¯Ç×usion syndrome

¯Ç×e uveal e¯ÇÜusion syndrome is a rare idiopathic, o¯Çøen bilateral,
condition that most frequently a¯ÇÜects middle-aged hypermetropic
men but can occur in association with nanophthalmos. ¯Ç×e cause
is thought to be impairment of normal ¯ÇØuid drainage from the
choroid via the sclera (which is sometimes of abnormal thickness
and composition) or vortex veins.
¯Çü Signs

{ In¯ÇØammation is absent or mild.

{ Ciliochoroidal detachment followed by exudative RD.

{ Following resolution, the RPE frequently shows a characteristic residual ÔÇÿleopard spotÔÇÖ mottling caused by
degenerative changes in the RPE associated with a high
concentration of protein in the SRF.
¯Çü Di¯Ç£erential diagnosis includes uveal e¯ÇÜusion secondary to
other causes (see above), choroidal haemorrhage and ring
melanoma of the anterior choroid.
¯Çü Treatment is usually with full-thickness sclerectomy, particularly in patients with nanophthalmos.

## Surgery

## Indications for urgent surgery

In general, an acutely symptomatic RD should be surgically
repaired as soon as possible, particularly if the macula is not
involved (see Fig. 16.22A). Other factors that may increase the

Drainage of subretinal ¯ÇØuid

Drainage of SRF via the sclera (e.g. the D-ACE: Drainage-AirCryotherapy-Explant) surgical technique (Fig. 16.31A) is advocated by many practitioners, citing more rapid retinal reattachment
in the presence of deep or longstanding viscous SRF. Others prefer
to avoid external drainage because of the potential complications
associated with the technique, such as retinal perforation or incarceration in the drainage site (Fig. 16.31B) and choroidal haemorrhage (Fig. 16.31C and D) and would rather perform a pars plana
vitrectomy as a primary option.

## Complications of scleral buckling

¯Çü Diplopia due to the mechanical e¯ÇÜect of the buckle is common. Early spontaneous resolution is typical, though intervention is sometimes necessary.
¯Çü Cystoid macular oedema occurs in up to 25%, but usually
responds to treatment. Other macular complications include
epiretinal membrane (around 15%), persistent subfoveal

¯ÇØuid and foveal structural disruption, usually in macula-o¯ÇÜ
detachments.
¯Çü Anterior segment ischaemia due to vascular compromise. ¯Ç×is is a particular risk with an encircling band
and with predisposing systemic conditions such as sickle
haemoglobinopathies.
¯Çü Buckle extrusion, intrusion or infection (Fig. 16.32).
Removal is usually required, with intensive antibiotic therapy
if needed.
¯Çü Elevated IOP. An early IOP spike usually resolves rapidly, but
occasionally can persist. Angle closure can occur.
¯Çü Choroidal detachment usually resolves spontaneously, presumably as scleral oedema settles and allows improved vortex
vein function.
¯Çü Surgical failure

for the presence of multiple breaks, particularly if the con¯Ç£guration of the RD does not correspond to the position of
the primary break.

can occasionally open old breaks and create new ones. Presentation is typically several weeks postoperatively with

{ Reopening of a retinal break in the absence of PVR can
occur as a result of inadequate cryotherapy or scleral buckling, or sometimes when buckle height decreases either
with time or following late surgical removal.

TIP The three commonest causes of failed retinal detachment
surgery are: proliferative vitreoretinopathy, failure to close all
breaks and the development of new breaks.

## Pars plana vitrectomy

PPV is discussed later in this chapter.

TRACTIONAL RETINAL DETACHMENT

¯Ç×e main causes of tractional RD are proliferative retinopathy
secondary to diabetes, retinopathy of prematurity and penetrating
posterior segment trauma (see Ch. 22).

## Pathogenesis of diabetic tractional retinal
detachment

Tractional RD is caused by progressive contraction of ¯Ç£brovascular
membranes over large areas of vitreoretinal adhesion. In contrast
to acute PVD in eyes with rhegmatogenous RD, PVD in diabetic
eyes is gradual and frequently incomplete. It is thought to be
caused by leakage of plasma constituents into the vitreous gel from
a ¯Ç£brovascular network adherent to the posterior vitreous surface.

## Owing to the strong adhesions of the cortical vitreous to areas of

¯Ç£brovascular proliferation, PVD is usually incomplete. In the rare
event of a subsequent complete PVD, the new blood vessels are
avulsed and RD does not develop. Vitreoretinal traction can be any
of the following:
¯Çü Tangential, caused by the contraction of epiretinal ¯Ç£brovascular membranes with puckering of the retina and distortion of
blood vessels.
¯Çü Anteroposterior, due to the contraction of ¯Ç£brovascular
membranes extending from the posterior retina, usually in
association with the major arcades, to the vitreous base anteriorly and/or bridging (trampoline).
¯Çü ¯Çøe result of contraction of ¯Ç£brovascular membranes stretching from one part of the retina to another or between vascular
arcades, tending to pull the two involved points together.

## Diagnosis

with acute PVD. A visual ¯Ç£eld defect usually progresses slowly
and may be stable for months or even years.

EXUDATIVE RETINAL DETACHMENT

## Pathogenesis

Exudative RD is characterized by the accumulation of SRF in the
absence of retinal breaks or traction. It may occur in a variety
of vascular, in¯ÇØammatory and neoplastic diseases involving the
retina, RPE and choroid in which ¯ÇØuid leaks outside the vessels
and accumulates under the retina. As long as the RPE is able to
compensate by pumping the leaking ¯ÇØuid into the choroidal circulation, RD does not occur. However, when the mechanism is overwhelmed or functions less than normally, ¯ÇØuid accumulates in the
subretinal space. Causes include:
¯Çü Choroidal tumours such as melanoma, haemangioma and
metastases. An intraocular tumour should be considered the
cause of an exudative RD until proved otherwise.
¯Çü In¯Çƒammation such as Harada disease and posterior
scleritis.
¯Çü Bullous central serous chorioretinopathy is a rare cause.
¯Çü Iatrogenic causes include RD surgery and panretinal
photocoagulation.
¯Çü Choroidal neovascularization, which may leak and give rise to
extensive subretinal accumulation of ¯ÇØuid at the posterior pole.
¯Çü Hypertensive choroidopathy, as may occur in toxaemia of
pregnancy, is a very rare cause.
¯Çü Idiopathic, such as uveal e¯ÇÜusion syndrome (see above).

TIP An exudative retinal detachment (which is often caused by
a choroidal tumour) is characterized by ÔÇÿshiftingÔÇÖ subretinal ¯ÇØuid.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Depending on the cause, both eyes may be
involved simultaneously.

{ ¯Ç×ere is no vitreoretinal traction, so photopsia is absent.

{ Floaters may be present if there is associated vitritis.

{ A visual ¯Ç£eld defect may develop suddenly and progress
rapidly.
¯Çü Signs

{ The RD has a convex configuration, as with a rhegmatogenous RD, but its surface is smooth and not
corrugated.

{ ¯Ç×e detached retina is very mobile and exhibits the phenomenon of ÔÇÿshi¯Çøing ¯ÇØuidÔÇÖ in which SRF detaches the
area of retina under which it accumulates (Fig. 16.35A
and B). For example, in the upright position the SRF
collects under the inferior retina, but on assuming the
supine position for several minutes, the inferior retina
¯ÇØattens and SRF shi¯Çøs posteriorly, detaching the superior
retina.

{ ¯Ç×e cause of the RD, such as a choroidal tumour (Fig.
16.36A), may be apparent when the fundus is examined
or on B-scan ultrasonography, or the patient may have an
associated systemic disease responsible for the RD (e.g.
Harada disease, toxaemia of pregnancy).

{ ÔÇÿLeopard spotsÔÇÖ consisting of scattered areas of subretinal
pigment clumping may be seen a¯Çøer the detachment has
¯ÇØattened (Fig. 16.36B).

## Treatment

Treatment depends on the cause. Some cases resolve spontaneously, whilst others are treated with systemic corticosteroids

PARS PLANA VITRECTOMY

## Introduction

## Instrumentation

¯Ç×e diameter of the sha¯Çø of vitrectomy instrumentation has conventionally been 0.9 mm (20-gauge), but smaller 23-gauge, 25-gauge and
even 27-gauge sutureless systems (transconjunctival microincision
vitrectomy ÔÇô MIVS) are increasingly becoming the standard of care,
o¯ÇÜering shorter operating time, less trauma and scarring, and faster
rehabilitation. Early concerns about an increased risk of postoperative
endophthalmitis do not seem to have been borne out and operative
success rates are comparable to larger-gauge instrumentation.

¯Çü Intraocular illumination is supplied by a ¯Ç£breoptic probe
(light pipe ÔÇô see Fig. 16.37A). Initially, reduced illumination
was a problem with the narrow calibre MIVS systems, because
of the limitations of halogen bulbs. Brighter sources consisting of xenon and potentially less phototoxic mercury vapour
sources have been introduced that match or exceed the levels of 20-gauge systems. ¯Ç×ese minimize retinal phototoxicity
by ¯Ç£ltering out higher-energy ultraviolet and blue light. Some
systems o¯ÇÜer adjustable lighting characteristics to increase the
contrast of particular structures. Wide-angle lighting and selfretaining ÔÇÿchandelierÔÇÖ sources are available ÔÇô the latter o¯ÇÜer the
advantages of freeing both of the surgeonÔÇÖs hands to carry out
true bimanual surgery (which can be particularly useful in
challenging cases) and of reducing phototoxicity (by increasing the working distance of the light probe from the retina).
Extremely small-gauge dual chandelier probes are available to
eliminate shadowing from the working ¯Ç£eld.

694 Pars Plana Vitrectomy

¯Çü Vitreous cutter (Fig. 16.37B) has an inner guillotine blade
that oscillates at very high speed (from 1500 up to 5000ÔÇô7500
cuts per minute [cpm] in the latest ÔÇÿultra-high speedÔÇÖ cutters),
cutting the vitreous gel into tiny pieces and simultaneously
removing it by suction into a collecting cassette. Faster cutting speeds translate into a lower level of traction exerted on
the vitreoretinal interface during surgery. Safer more e¯ÇÖcient
¯ÇØuidic control has been introduced in tandem.
¯Çü An infusion cannula is required in order to maintain the
vitreous cavity pressure and volume. ¯Ç×ese are generally selfretaining in small-gauge systems.
¯Çü Accessory instruments include scissors, forceps (Fig. 16.37C),
¯ÇØute needle and endodiathermy and endolaser delivery systems.
With the introduction of smaller-calibre cutters that are able to
manipulate ¯Ç£brovascular membranes more precisely and safely,
the requirement for additional instrumentation may decrease.
¯Çü Wide-angle viewing systems consist of an indirect lens
beneath the operating microscope and an incorporated series
of prisms to reinvert the image (Fig. 16.38). ¯Ç×e ¯Ç£eld of view
extends almost to the ora serrata. Higher magni¯Ç£cation smaller
¯Ç£eld lenses are available for macular surgery.

## Tamponading agents

¯Ç×ese achieve intraoperative retinal ¯ÇØattening in combination with
internal drainage of SRF and are commonly used postoperatively
for internal tamponade of retinal breaks.
¯Çü Expanding gases. Although air can be used, an expanding gas
is usually preferred in order to achieve prolonged tamponade.

## Postoperative positioning of the patient is used to maximize the

e¯ÇÜective vector and maintain surface tension around the break.

{ Sulfur hexa¯ÇØuoride (SF6) doubles its volume if used at a
100% concentration and lasts 10ÔÇô14 days.

{ Per¯ÇØuoroethane (C2F6) triples its volume at 100% and lasts
30ÔÇô35 days.

20ÔÇô30% for SF6 and 12ÔÇô16% for C3F8). Low-pressure environments, typically air travel and nitrous oxide anaesthesia,
must be avoided until gas absorption is complete, as these will
increase the intraocular gas pressure.

TIP If gas has been used at the time of RD repair, air travel
should be avoided because the gas bubble can expand with a
change in atmospheric pressure and cause an acute rise in IOP.

¯Çü Silicone oils have low speci¯Ç£c gravity ÔÇô they are lighter than
water and thus buoyant. ¯Ç×ey are commonly used for both
intraoperative retinal manipulation and prolonged postoperative intraocular tamponade and are particularly helpful in the
management of proliferative vitreoretinopathy. 1000 cs silicone is easier to inject and to remove whilst 5000 cs is more
viscous but may be less prone to emulsi¯Ç£cation.
¯Çü Heavy liquids (per¯ÇØuorocarbons) have high speci¯Ç£c gravity and
thus settle inferiorly in the vitreous cavity (Fig. 16.39). Per¯ÇØuoron-octane is most commonly used as it allows good visibility and
low viscosity. It acts as an intraoperative mechanical device and
can be used to ¯ÇØatten the posterior retina, open retinal folds
and improve visualization of additional membranes. Although
primarily developed for RD surgery, other indications include
haemostasis (by localizing bleeding), expression of lique¯Ç£ed
blood from behind the retina and anterior ¯ÇØoating of dislocated
lens fragments or intraocular lens (IOL). Cases of retinal toxicity and in¯ÇØammation have been reported.

## Indications

Although many simple rhegmatogenous RD can be treated successfully by scleral buckling techniques, PPV has greatly improved
the prognosis for more complex detachments. As techniques have
improved and surgeonsÔÇÖ familiarity and con¯Ç£dence has grown, the
threshold for vitrectomy surgery has fallen. Many vitreoretinal surgeons now feel that morbidity and success rates are better with vitrectomy than with scleral buckling for all pseudophakic and aphakic
RD and for those that would otherwise require external drainage of

{ Per¯ÇØuoropropane (C3F8) quadruples its volume at 100%
and lasts 55ÔÇô65 days.
¯Çü Gas concentration. Because the eye is usually le¯Çø almost
entirely gas-¯Ç£lled at the end of the procedure, most are used
at only an isovolumetric (non-expansile) concentration (e.g.

A B

(arrowheads) unrolled with heavy liquid (arrow). (Courtesy of C Barry ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

SRF. A recent survey of vitreoretinal trends in the USA reveals that
more than 80% of primary RD surgery is undertaken using a PPV
approach. ¯Ç×e guidelines below are therefore not absolute.

## Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment

¯Çü When retinal breaks cannot be visualized as a result of haemorrhage, vitreous debris, posterior capsular opacity, IOL edge e¯ÇÜects.
Vitrectomy is crucial to provide an adequate retinal view. Scleral
buckling carries a high risk of failure if any breaks are missed.
¯Çü When retinal breaks are unlikely to be closed by scleral
buckling, such as giant tears, large posterior breaks and in the
presence of PVR (Fig. 16.40AÔÇôC).

## Tractional retinal detachment

¯Çü Indications in diabetic RD

{ Tractional RD threatening or involving the macula (Fig.
16.40D). Vitrectomy is always combined with anti-VEGF
agents and/or internal panretinal photocoagulation to
prevent postoperative neovascularization that may cause
vitreous haemorrhage or rubeosis iridis. Extramacular
tractional RD may be observed without surgery because,
in many cases, it remains stationary for a long time provided proliferative retinopathy has been controlled.

## Technique

## Goals of surgery

¯Çü To separate the posterior hyaloid from the retinal surface.
¯Çü To remove the epiretinal tissue in order to release the central
and/or peripheral retinal traction.
¯Çü To close retinal breaks if present.

## Basic vitrectomy

¯Çü An infusion cannula is inserted (3.5 mm behind the limbus in
pseudophakic or aphakic eyes and 4 mm in phakic eyes) at the
level of the inferior border of the lateral rectus muscle. Limbal peritomy (conjunctival dissection) is required for conventional large-gauge systems, but is unnecessary in small-gauge
systems.
¯Çü Further sclerotomies are made at the 10 and 2 oÔÇÖclock positions, through which the vitreous cutter and ¯Ç£breoptic probe
are introduced (Fig. 16.41). ¯Ç×ese sclerotomies are self-sealing
with modern small-gauge systems, though wound leak occasionally occurs (Fig. 16.42).
¯Çü Excision. ¯Ç×e central vitreous gel and posterior hyaloid face
are excised.
¯Çü Two types of epithelial membrane are encountered:

{ Combined tractionalÔÇôrhegmatogenous RD should be
treated urgently, even if the macula is not involved, because
SRF is likely to spread quickly.
¯Çü Indications in penetrating trauma

{ Non-vascular membranes found in PVR and macular
pucker.

{ Prevention of tractional RD. Unlike diabetic retinopathy
where epiretinal membrane proliferation occurs mostly on
the posterior retina, ¯Ç£brocellular proliferation a¯Çøer penetrating trauma tends to develop on the pre-equatorial retina
and/or the ciliary body. Treatment is usually aimed at visual
rehabilitation and minimizing the tractional process.

{ Late tractional RD, which may be associated with an intraocular foreign body or retinal incarceration, occasionally
develops months a¯Çøer otherwise successful surgery.

{ Fibrovascular membranes found in patients with proliferative diabetic retinopathy.
¯Çü ¯Çøe surgical techniques employed involve delamination, segmentation and en-bloc dissection.
¯Çü Intraoperative tissue staining can be helpful. In particular
trypan blue can be used to stain an epiretinal membrane and
triamcinolone to stain vitreous.
¯Çü ¯Çøe above basic steps apply to all vitrectomy surgery. Subsequent steps depend on the speci¯Ç£c indication.
¯Çü Sutures. Transconjunctival small-gauge systems do not require
postoperative suturing.

## Proliferative vitreoretinopathy

¯Ç×e aims of surgery in PVR are to release both transvitreal traction by vitrectomy and tangential (surface) traction by membrane
dissection in order to restore retinal mobility and allow closure of
retinal breaks.
Localized ¯Ç£xed retinal folds may be freed by the removal of the
central plaque of epiretinal membrane. ¯Ç×is can usually be achieved
by engaging the tip of vertically cutting scissors or a pick-type
instrument in the edge of a valley between two adjacent folds (Fig.
16.43). ¯Ç×e membrane is either surgically dissected or simply peeled
from the surface of the retina. Small-gauge cutters may be used in
some cases to engage membranes directly and forceps can facilitate
this. A relieving retinotomy should be considered if the mobility of
the retina is believed to be insu¯ÇÖcient for sustained reattachment.

## Tractional retinal detachment

¯Ç×e goal of vitrectomy in tractional RDs is to release anteroposterior
and/or circumferential vitreoretinal traction. Because the membranes
are vascularized and the retina o¯Çøen friable, they cannot be simply
peeled from the surface of the retina as this would result in haemorrhage and tearing of the retina. ¯Ç×e three methods of removing ¯Ç£brovascular membranes in diabetic tractional RDs are the following:
¯Çü Delamination involves the horizontal cutting of individual
vascular pegs connecting a membrane to the surface of the
retina (Fig. 16.44A). ¯Ç×is allows the complete removal of ¯Ç£brovascular tissue from the retinal surface (Fig. 16.44B).
¯Çü Segmentation involves the vertical cutting of epiretinal membranes into small segments (Fig. 16.44C and D). It is used to
release circumferential vitreoretinal traction when delamination is di¯ÇÖcult or impossible.
¯Çü En-bloc dissection. ¯Ç×e key step is to separate posterior hyaloid from the retina. An incision is made in the epiretinal membrane using an illuminated pick. ¯Ç×e membrane is then peeled
away using an end-gripping membrane forceps (Fig. 16.45).

## Complications

¯Ç×e rate of complications has decreased signi¯Ç£cantly in recent
years because of technological advances and specialized training
in the ¯Ç£eld. Complications can nevertheless occur intraoperatively
and postoperatively.

## Intraoperative complications

¯Çü Posterior retinal breaks.
¯Çü Peripheral retinal breaks.
¯Çü Rarely, choroidal haemorrhage.

## Postoperative complications

¯Çü Retinal breaks and rhegmatogenous RD.
¯Çü Retinal fold (Fig. 16.46). ¯Ç×is is less likely to occur by placing
the patient face down immediately a¯Çøer surgery.
¯Çü In¯Çƒammation, particularly in patients with diabetes.
¯Çü Raised intraocular pressure.

{ Overexpansion of intraocular gas, usually when the concentration or volume of expansile gas is inadvertently too
great. Medical measures alone may be su¯ÇÖcient in some

cases, but if a substantial elevation of IOP occurs excess gas
can be removed at the slit lamp via the pars plana by using
a 30-gauge needle on a 1 ml syringe.

TIP Excessive concentrations of intraocular gas can result in
a signi¯Çƒcant rise in IOP. Excess gas can be removed at the slit
lamp by inserting a 30-gauge needle into the vitreous cavity via
the pars plana.

{ Silicone oil-associated glaucoma. Early glaucoma may
be caused by direct pupillary block by silicone oil (Fig.
16.47A). ¯Ç×is occurs particularly in an aphakic eye with
an intact iris diaphragm. In aphakic eyes this can be prevented by performing an inferior (Ando) iridectomy at
the time of surgery to allow free passage of aqueous to the
anterior chamber. Intraocular gas can also cause pupillary block. Late glaucoma is caused by emulsi¯Ç£ed silicone
in the anterior chamber (Fig. 16.47B) causing trabecular
obstruction and scarring. ¯Ç×e risk may be reduced by early
oil removal, though glaucoma can still occur. A long tube
seton may be required (Fig. 16.47C).

{ Other mechanisms include ghost cell, in¯ÇØammatory and
steroid-induced glaucoma (see Ch. 11). Angle closure can
also result from ciliochoroidal e¯ÇÜusion with anterior rotation of the lensÔÇôiris diaphragm, which may respond to
cycloplegia and steroids.
¯Çü Cataract

{ Gas-induced. A large or long-lasting intravitreal gas bubble typically gives rise to feathering of the posterior subcapsular lens, but this is usually transient.

{ Silicone-induced. Almost all phakic eyes with silicone oil
eventually develop cataract (Fig. 16.47D).

{ Delayed Following vitrectomy, substantial nuclear sclerosis commonly develops, especially if the patient is over 50
years of age.
¯Çü Band keratopathy is not uncommon with extended silicone
oil tamponade.

## Chapter

# Vitreous Opacities

701

702 KanskiÔÇÖs Clinical Ophthalmology

## Introduction

¯Çƒe vitreous is a transparent extracellular gel consisting of collagen, soluble proteins, hyaluronic acid and water. ¯Çƒe total vitreous
volume is approximately 4.0 ml. ¯Çƒe few cells normally present in
the gel are located predominantly in the cortex and include hyalocytes, astrocytes and glial cells. ¯Çƒe vitreous provides structural
support to the globe while allowing a clear and optically uniform
path to the retina. Once lique¯Ç×ed or surgically removed it does not
re-form. Vitreous opacities can be caused by a variety of pathological processes primarily involving other ocular sites. Apart from
vitreous haemorrhage, the conditions discussed below are those in
which the vitreous gel is the primary site of pathology.

## Muscae volitantes

Muscae volitantes (Latin for ÔÇÿhovering ¯ÇØiesÔÇÖ), commonly referred
to as ÔÇÿ¯ÇØoatersÔÇÖ, is an ubiquitous entoptic phenomenon of ¯ÇØy-, cobweb- or thread-like condensations that are best seen against a pale
background. It is thought to predominantly represent tiny embryological remnants in the vitreous gel. A sudden exacerbation can
occur due to vitreous haemorrhage or, more commonly, a change
in the conformation of the gel, such as a posterior vitreous detachment (see Fig. 16.15).

## Vitreous haemorrhage

## Vitreous haemorrhage is a common condition with many

causes (Table 17.1). Symptoms vary according to severity. Mild
haemorrhage (Fig. 17.1A) causes sudden onset floaters and diffuse blurring of vision, but may not affect visual acuity, whilst a
dense bleed may result in severe visual loss (Fig. 17.1B). B-scan
ultrasonography in non-clotted vitreous haemorrhage generally shows a uniform appearance and once cellular aggregates
develop, small particulate echoes become visible (Fig. 17.1C).

## Ultrasonography is critical in the evaluation of an eye with
dense vitreous haemorrhage to exclude an underlying retinal

tear or detachment (Fig. 17.1D). Treatment is dictated by severity and cause, but an increasingly low threshold is being adopted
for early vitrectomy (see Ch. 16) in cases of dense haemorrhage.

TIP Undertake ultrasound investigation in an eye with a
dense vitreous haemorrhage to exclude a retinal detachment or
choroidal melanoma.

## Terson syndrome

## Terson syndrome refers to the combination of intraocular and

subarachnoid haemorrhage secondary to aneurysmal rupture,
most commonly arising from the anterior communicating artery.
However, intraocular haemorrhage may also occur with subdural
haematoma and acute elevation of intracranial pressure from other
causes. ¯Çƒe haemorrhage is frequently bilateral and is typically
intraretinal and/or preretinal (Fig. 17.2), although occasionally
subhyaloid blood may break into the vitreous. It is probable that
intraocular bleeding is due to retinal venous stasis secondary to

Table 17.1 Causes of Vitreous Haemorrhage

ÔÇó¯Çü Acute posterior vitreous detachment associated either
with a retinal tear or avulsion of a peripheral vessel
ÔÇó¯Çü Proliferative retinopathy
ÔÇó¯Çü Diabetic
ÔÇó¯Çü Retinal vein occlusion
ÔÇó¯Çü Sickle cell disease
ÔÇó¯Çü Eales disease
ÔÇó¯Çü Vasculitis
ÔÇó¯Çü Miscellaneous retinal disorders
ÔÇó¯Çü Macroaneurysm
ÔÇó¯Çü Telangiectasia
ÔÇó¯Çü Capillary haemangioma
ÔÇó¯Çü Trauma
ÔÇó¯Çü Systemic
ÔÇó¯Çü Bleeding disorders
ÔÇó¯Çü Terson syndrome

increase in cavernous sinus pressure. Vitreous haemorrhage usually resolves spontaneously within a few months and the long-term
visual prognosis is good in most. Early vitrectomy may be considered in some cases.

## Asteroid hyalosis

Asteroid hyalosis is a common degenerative process in which calcium pyrophosphate particles collect within the vitreous gel. It
is seen clinically as numerous tiny round yellowÔÇôwhite opacities
of varying size and density (Fig. 17.3A and B). ¯Çƒese move with
the vitreous during eye movements but do not sediment inferiorly when the eye is immobile. Only one eye is a¯Ç£ected in 75%
of patients. It rarely causes visual problems and the majority of
patients are asymptomatic. An association with diabetes has been
suggested, but is unproven. ¯Çƒe prevalence of asteroid hyalosis
increases with age and a¯Ç£ects 3% of those aged 75ÔÇô86 years. It is
more common in men than in women. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) (Fig. 17.3C) and ultrasonography (Fig. 17.3D) show
high re¯ÇØectivity foci.

TIP Because asteroid hyalosis rarely causes visual symptoms,
dual pathology should be considered if the visual acuity is
reduced.

## Synchysis scintillans

Synchysis (synchisis) scintillans occurs as a consequence of chronic
vitreous haemorrhage, o¯Çøen in a blind eye. ¯Çƒe condition is usually discovered when frank haemorrhage is no longer present. ¯Çƒe
crystals are composed of cholesterol and are derived from plasma
cells or degraded products of erythrocytes and lie either freely or
engulfed within foreign body giant cells.
Numerous ¯ÇØat golden-brown refractile particles are seen (Fig.
17.4A), which tend to sediment inferiorly when the eye is immobile. Occasionally the anterior chamber may also be involved (Fig.
17.4B).

## Amyloidosis

## Amyloidosis is a localized or systemic condition in which there

is extracellular deposition of ¯Ç×brillary protein. Vitreous involvement typically occurs in familial amyloidosis, also characterized by polyneuropathy, prominent corneal nerves and pupillary
light-near dissociation. Vitreous opacities may be unilateral or
bilateral and are initially perivascular. Later they involve the
anterior gel and take on a characteristic sheet-like (ÔÇÿglass woolÔÇÖ)
appearance (Fig. 17.5A). ¯Çƒe opacities may become attached to
the posterior lens by thick footplates (Fig. 17.5B). Dense opaci¯Ç×cation resulting in signi¯Ç×cant visual impairment may require
vitrectomy.

## Vitreous cyst

Vitreous cysts can be congenital or acquired, acquired cysts being
caused by a range of pathology such as trauma and in¯ÇØammation.
Congenital cysts are pigmented or non-pigmented, the former
usually arising from the ciliary body pigment epithelium, the latter from remnants of the primary hyaloid vascular system. ¯Çƒey
are generally ¯Ç×xed ÔÇô non-pigmented cysts are typically attached
to the optic disc ÔÇô but can be found ¯ÇØoating freely in the posterior
(occasionally anterior) segment (Fig. 17.6). Treatment is seldom
required, but laser cystotomy or vitrectomy can be performed for
troublesome symptoms.

## Persistent fetal vasculature

In addition to non-pigmented vitreous cysts, remnants of the
hyaloid vessels can form a Bergmeister papilla, seen as a tu¯Çø
at the optic disc (Fig. 17.7), a Mittendorf dot on the posterior
lens surface and the more marked manifestations for which the
term persistent fetal vasculature is generally reserved, previously

706 KanskiÔÇÖs Clinical Ophthalmology

termed persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (see Ch. 20). A
congenital vitreous veil is a translucent, curtain-like structure
found in normal vitreous, which may be vascularized (Fig. 17.8).
It is extremely rare and is of unknown aetiology. Treatment is
not needed.

without manifest squint 722

708 Introduction

INTRODUCTION

De¯Çƒnitions

¯Çü ¯Çƒe visual axis passes from the fovea, through the nodal point
of the eye, to the point of ¯Ç×xation. In normal binocular single
vision (BSV) the visual axes of the two eyes intersect at the
point of ¯Ç×xation, the images being aligned by the fusion re¯ÇØex
and combined by binocular responsive cells in the visual cortex to give BSV.
¯Çü Orthophoria implies perfect ocular alignment in the absence
of any stimulus for fusion; this is uncommon.
¯Çü Heterophoria (ÔÇÿphoriaÔÇÖ) implies a tendency of the eyes to
deviate when fusion is blocked (latent squint).

{ Slight phoria is present in most normal individuals and is
overcome by the fusion re¯ÇØex. ¯Ç£e phoria can be either a
small inward imbalance (esophoria) or an outward imbalance (exophoria).

{ When fusion is insu¯Çøcient to control the imbalance, the
phoria is described as decompensating and is o¯ÇÜen associated with symptoms of binocular discomfort (asthenopia)
or double vision (diplopia).
¯Çü Heterotropia (ÔÇÿtropiaÔÇÖ) implies a manifest deviation in which
the visual axes do not intersect at the point of ¯Ç×xation.

{ ¯Ç£e images from the two eyes are misaligned so that either
double vision is present or, more commonly in children,
the image from the deviating eye is suppressed at cortical
level.

{ A childhood squint may occur because of failure of the
normal development of binocular fusion mechanisms or
as a result of oculomotor imbalance secondary to a di¯ÇÖerence in refraction between the two eyes (anisometropia).

{ Failure of fusion, for example secondary to poor vision in
one eye, may cause heterotropia in adulthood, or a squint
may develop because of weakness or mechanical restriction of the extraocular muscles, or damage to their nerve
supply.

{ Horizontal deviation of the eyes (latent or manifest) is the
most common form of strabismus.

{ Upward displacement of one eye relative to the other is
termed a hypertropia and a latent upward imbalance a
hyperphoria.

{ A large angle kappa may give the appearance of a squint
when none is present (pseudo-squint) and is seen most
commonly as a pseudoexotropia following displacement of
the macula in retinopathy of prematurity, where the angle
may signi¯Ç×cantly exceed +5┬░ (Fig. 18.1BÔÇôD).

## Anatomy of the extraocular muscles

## Principles

¯Ç£e lateral and medial orbital walls are at an angle of 45┬░ with each
other. ¯Ç£e orbital axis therefore forms an angle of 22.5┬░ with both
lateral and medial walls, though for the sake of simplicity this angle
is usually regarded as being 23┬░ (Fig. 18.2A). When the eye is looking straight ahead at a ¯Ç×xed point on the horizon with the head
erect (primary position of gaze), the visual axis forms an angle of
23┬░ with the orbital axis (Fig. 18.2B). ¯Ç£e actions of the extraocular
muscles depend on the position of the globe at the time of muscle
contraction (Fig. 18.2C and D).
¯Çü ¯Çƒe primary action of a muscle is its major e¯ÇÖect when the eye
is in the primary position.
¯Çü Subsidiary actions are the additional e¯ÇÖects, which depend on
the position of the eye.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe Listing plane is an imaginary coronal plane passing
through the centre of rotation of the globe. ¯Ç£e globe rotates
on the axes of Fick, which intersect in the Listing plane (Fig.
18.3).

{ ¯Ç£e globe rotates le¯ÇÜ and right on the vertical Z axis.

{ ¯Ç£e globe moves up and down on the horizontal X axis.

{ Torsional movements (wheel rotations) occur on the Y
(sagittal) axis which traverses the globe from front to back
(similar to the anatomical axis of the eye).

{ Downward displacement is termed a hypotropia and a
latent imbalance a hypophoria.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe anatomical axis is a line passing from the posterior pole
through the centre of the cornea. Because the fovea is usually
slightly temporal to the anatomical centre of the posterior pole
of the eye, the visual axis does not usually correspond to the
anatomical axis of the eye.
¯Çü Angle kappa is the angle, usually about 5┬░, subtended by the
visual and anatomical axes (Fig. 18.1).

{ ¯Ç£e angle is positive (normal) when the fovea is temporal to the centre of the posterior pole resulting in a nasal
displacement of the corneal re¯ÇØex and negative when the
converse applies.

{ Intorsion occurs when the superior limbus rotates nasally
and extorsion on temporal rotation.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe anatomy of the muscles is illustrated in Fig. 18.4A and B.

## Horizontal recti

When the eye is in the primary position, the horizontal recti are
purely horizontal movers on the vertical Z axis and have only primary actions.
¯Çü Medial rectus originates at the annulus of Zinn at the orbital
apex and inserts 5.5 mm behind the nasal limbus. Its sole
action in the primary position is adduction.
¯Çü Lateral rectus originates at the annulus of Zinn and inserts 6.9
mm behind the temporal limbus. Its sole action in the primary
position is abduction.

## Vertical recti

¯Ç£e vertical recti run in line with the orbital axis and are inserted
in front of the equator. ¯Ç£ey therefore form an angle of 23┬░ with the
visual axis (see Fig. 18.2C).
¯Çü Superior rectus originates from the upper part of the annulus
of Zinn and inserts 7.7 mm behind the superior limbus.

{ ¯Ç£e primary action is elevation (Fig. 18.5A); secondary
actions are adduction and intorsion.

18.5C).
¯Çü Inferior rectus originates at the lower part of the annulus of

{ ¯Ç£e primary action is depression; secondary actions are

{ When the globe is abducted 23┬░, the inferior rectus acts
purely as a depressor. As for superior rectus, this is the
optimal position of the globe for testing the function of the
inferior rectus muscle.

{ If the globe were adducted 67┬░, the inferior rectus could act
to extort the eye.

Spiral of Tillaux

¯Ç£e spiral of Tillaux (Fig. 18.6) is an imaginary line joining the
insertions of the four recti and is an important anatomical landmark when performing surgery. ¯Ç£e insertions are located progressively further away from the limbus in a spiral pattern. ¯Ç£e medial
rectus insertion is closest (5.5 mm), followed by the inferior rectus
(6.5 mm), lateral rectus (6.9 mm) and superior rectus (7.7 mm).

## Muscle pulleys

¯Çü ¯Ç£e four rectus muscles pass through condensations of connective tissue and smooth muscle just posterior to the equator.
¯Ç£ese condensations act as pulleys and minimize upward and
downward movements of the bellies of the medial and lateral
rectus muscles during upgaze and downgaze, and horizontal
movements of the superior and inferior rectus bellies in le¯ÇÜ
and right gaze.
¯Çü Pulleys are the e¯ÇÖective origins of the rectus muscles and play
an important role in the coordination of eye movements by
reducing the e¯ÇÖect of horizontal movements on vertical muscle actions and vice versa.
¯Çü Displacement of the pulleys is a cause of abnormalities of eye
movements such as ÔÇÿVÔÇÖ and ÔÇÿAÔÇÖ patterns (see Figs 18.69 and 18.70).

## Innervation

¯Çü Lateral rectus. Sixth cranial nerve (abducens nerve ÔÇô abducting muscle).
¯Çü Superior oblique. Fourth cranial nerve (trochlear nerve ÔÇô
muscle associated with the trochlea).
¯Çü Other muscles together with the levator muscle of the upper
lid and the ciliary and sphincter pupillae muscles are supplied
by the third (oculomotor) nerve.

## Ocular movements

## Ductions

Ductions are monocular movements around the axes of Fick. ¯Ç£ey
consist of adduction, abduction, elevation, depression, intorsion

and extorsion. ¯Ç£ey are tested by occluding the fellow eye and asking the patient to follow a target in each direction of gaze.

## Versions

Versions (Fig. 18.9, top) are binocular, simultaneous, conjugate
movements (conjugate ÔÇô in the same direction, so that the angle
between the eyes remains constant).
¯Çü Dextroversion and laevoversion (gaze right and gaze le¯ÇÜ), elevation (upgaze) and depression (downgaze). ¯Ç£ese four movements bring the globe into the secondary positions of gaze by
rotation around either the vertical (Z) or the horizontal (X)
axes of Fick.
¯Çü Dextroelevation and dextrodepression (gaze up and right;
gaze down and right) and laevoelevation and laevodepression (gaze up and le¯ÇÜ; gaze down and le¯ÇÜ). ¯Ç£ese four oblique
movements bring the eyes into the tertiary positions of gaze by
rotation around oblique axes lying in the Listing plane, equivalent to simultaneous movement about both the horizontal and
vertical axes.
¯Çü Torsional movements to maintain upright images occur on
tilting of the head; these are known as the righting re¯ÇØexes. On
head tilt to the right the superior limbi of the two eyes rotate
to the le¯ÇÜ, causing intorsion of the right globe and extorsion of
the le¯ÇÜ (laevocycloversion).

## Vergences

Vergences (Fig. 18.9, bottom) are binocular, simultaneous, disjugate movements (disjugate ÔÇô in opposite directions, so that
the angle between the eyes changes, also termed disjunctive).
Convergence is simultaneous adduction (inward turning) and
divergence is outwards movement from a convergent position.
Convergence may be voluntary or re¯ÇØex. Re¯ÇØex convergence has
four components:
¯Çü Tonic convergence, which implies inherent innervational tone
to the medial recti.

¯Çü Proximal convergence is induced by psychological awareness
of a near object.
¯Çü Fusional convergence is an optomotor re¯ÇØex that maintains
BSV by ensuring that similar images are projected onto corresponding retinal areas of each eye. It is initiated by bitemporal
retinal image disparity.
¯Çü Accommodative convergence is induced by the act of accommodation as part of the synkinetic-near re¯ÇØex.

{ Each dioptre of accommodation is accompanied by a
constant increment in accommodative convergence, giving the ÔÇÿaccommodative convergence to accommodationÔÇÖ
(AC/A) ratio.

{ ¯Ç£is is the amount of convergence in prism dioptres (╬ö)
per dioptre (D) change in accommodation.

{ ¯Ç£e normal value is 3ÔÇô5 ╬ö. ¯Ç£is means that 1 D of accommodation is associated with 3ÔÇô5 ╬ö of accommodative
convergence. Abnormalities of the AC/A ratio play an
important role in the aetiology of strabismus.

{ Changes in accommodation, convergence and pupil size,
which occur in concert with a change in the distance of
viewing, are known as the ÔÇÿnear triadÔÇÖ.

{ See fusional vergence and fusional amplitudes later in this
chapter.

## Positions of gaze

¯Çü ¯Çƒe Sherrington law of reciprocal innervation (Fig. 18.11)
states that increased innervation to an extraocular muscle (e.g.
right medial rectus) is accompanied by a reciprocal decrease
in innervation to its antagonist (e.g. right lateral rectus). ¯Ç£is
means that when the medial rectus contracts, the lateral rectus automatically relaxes and vice versa. ¯Ç£e Sherrington law
applies to both versions and vergences.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe Hering law of equal innervation states that during any
conjugate eye movement, equal and simultaneous innervation
¯ÇØows to the yoke muscles (Fig. 18.12).

¯Çü Six cardinal positions of gaze are identi¯Ç×ed in which one muscle in each eye is principally responsible for moving the eye
into that position as follows:

{ In the case of a paretic squint, the amount of innervation
to both eyes is symmetrical and always determined by the
¯Ç×xating eye, so that the angle of deviation will vary according to which eye is used for ¯Ç×xation.

{ For example, in the case of a le¯ÇÜ lateral rectus palsy, if
the right normal eye is used for ¯Ç×xation, there will be an
inward deviation of the le¯ÇÜ eye due to the unopposed
action of the antagonist of the paretic le¯ÇÜ lateral rectus
(le¯ÇÜ medial rectus). ¯Ç£e amount of misalignment of the
two eyes in this situation is called the primary deviation
(Fig. 18.13, le¯ÇÜ).

{ If the paretic le¯ÇÜ eye is now used for ¯Ç×xation, additional
innervation will ¯ÇØow to the le¯ÇÜ lateral rectus, in order to
establish this. However, according to the Hering law, an
equal amount of innervation will also ¯ÇØow to the right
medial rectus (yoke muscle). ¯Ç£is will result in an overaction of the right medial rectus and an excessive amount
of adduction of the right eye.

{ Dextroversion (right lateral rectus and le¯ÇÜ medial rectus).

{ Laevoversion (le¯ÇÜ lateral rectus and right medial rectus).

{ Dextroelevation (right superior rectus and le¯ÇÜ inferior
oblique).

{ Laevoelevation (le¯ÇÜ superior rectus and right inferior
oblique).

{ Dextrodepression (right inferior rectus and le¯ÇÜ superior
oblique).

{ ¯Ç£e amount of misalignment between the two eyes in this
situation is called the secondary deviation (Fig. 18.13,
right). In a paretic squint, the secondary deviation exceeds
the primary deviation.
¯Çü Muscle sequelae are the e¯ÇÖects of the interactions described
by these laws. ¯Ç£ey are of prime importance in diagnosing
ocular motility disorders and in particular in distinguishing
a recently acquired from a longstanding palsy (see ÔÇÿClinical
evaluationÔÇÖ). ¯Ç£e full pattern of changes takes a variable period
to develop:

{ Laevodepression (le¯ÇÜ inferior rectus and right superior
oblique).
¯Çü Nine diagnostic positions of gaze are those in which deviations are measured. ¯Ç£ey consist of the six cardinal positions,
the primary position, elevation and depression (Fig. 18.10).

## Laws of ocular motility

¯Çü AgonistÔÇôantagonist pairs are muscles of the same eye that
move the eye in opposite directions. ¯Ç£e agonist is the primary muscle moving the eye in a given direction. ¯Ç£e antagonist acts in the opposite direction to the agonist. For example,
the right lateral rectus is the antagonist to the right medial
rectus.
¯Çü Synergists are muscles of the same eye that move the eye in
the same direction. For example, the right superior rectus and
right inferior oblique act synergistically in elevation.
¯Çü Yoke muscles (contralateral synergists) are pairs of muscles,
one in each eye, that produce conjugate ocular movements.
For example, the yoke muscle of the le¯ÇÜ superior oblique is the
right inferior rectus.

{ Primary underaction (e.g. le¯ÇÜ superior oblique).

{ Secondary overaction of the contralateral synergist or yoke
muscle (right inferior rectus; Hering law).

{ Secondary overaction and later contracture of the unopposed ipsilateral antagonist (le¯ÇÜ inferior oblique; Sherrington law).

{ Secondary inhibition of the contralateral antagonist (right
superior rectus; Hering and Sherrington laws).

## Sensory considerations

## Basic aspects

¯Çü Normal BSV involves the simultaneous use of both eyes with
bifoveal ¯Ç×xation, so that each eye contributes to a common
single perception of the object of regard. ¯Ç£is represents the
highest form of binocular cooperation. Conditions necessary
for normal BSV are:

## Increased innervation to a given extraocular muscle is
accompanied by reciprocal decreased innervation to its
antagonist

## Equal innervation is supplied to yoke muscles to move both eyes
into the same direction of gaze

{ Normal routing of visual pathways with overlapping visual
¯Ç×elds.

{ Approximately equal image clarity and size for both eyes.

{ Binocularly driven neurones in the visual cortex.

{ Normal retinal (retinocortical) correspondence (NRC)
resulting in ÔÇÿcyclopeanÔÇÖ viewing.

{ Accurate neuromuscular development and coordination,
so that the visual axes are directed at, and maintain ¯Ç×xation
on, the object of regard.

{ BSV is based on NRC, which requires ¯Ç×rst an understanding of uniocular visual direction and projection.
¯Çü Visual direction is the projection of a given retinal element in
a speci¯Ç×c direction in subjective space.

{ ¯Ç£e principal visual direction is the direction in external
space interpreted as the line of sight. ¯Ç£is is normally the
visual direction of the fovea and is associated with a sense
of direct viewing.
