(sound eye fixating) (paretic eye fixating)

{ Secondary visual directions are the projecting directions of extrafoveal points with respect to the principal
direction of the fovea, associated with indirect (eccentric) viewing.
¯Çü Projection is the subjective interpretation of the position of
an object in space on the basis of stimulated retinal elements.

{ If a red object stimulates the right fovea (F) and a black
object that lies in the nasal ¯Ç×eld stimulates a temporal retinal element (T), the red object will be interpreted by the
brain as having originated from the straight-ahead position and the black object will be interpreted as having
originated in the nasal ¯Ç×eld (Fig. 18.14A). Similarly, nasal
retinal elements project into the temporal ¯Ç×eld, upper retinal elements into the lower ¯Ç×eld and vice versa.

{ With both eyes open, the red ¯Ç×xation object is now stimulating both foveae, which are corresponding retinal points.
¯Ç£e black object is now not only stimulating the temporal
retinal elements in the right eye but also the nasal elements
of the le¯ÇÜ eye. ¯Ç£e right eye therefore projects the object
into its nasal ¯Ç×eld and the le¯ÇÜ eye projects the object into
its temporal ¯Ç×eld.

{ ¯Ç£is retinotopic organization is re¯ÇØected back along the
visual pathways, each eye maintaining separate images
until the visual pathways converge onto binocularly
responsive neurones in the primary visual cortex.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe horopter is an imaginary plane in external space, relative
to both the observerÔÇÖs eyes for a given ¯Ç×xation target, all points
on which stimulate corresponding retinal elements and are
therefore seen singly and in the same plane (Fig. 18.14B). ¯Ç£is
plane passes through the intersection of the visual axes and
therefore includes the point of ¯Ç×xation in BSV.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe Panum fusional space (or volume) is a zone in front of
and behind the horopter in which objects stimulate slightly
non-corresponding retinal points (retinal disparity).

{ Points on the nasal retina of one eye have corresponding
points on the temporal retina of the other eye and vice
versa. For example, an object producing images on the
right nasal retina and the le¯ÇÜ temporal retina will be projected into the right side of visual space. ¯Ç£is is the basis of
normal retinal correspondence.

{ Because both of these retinal elements are corresponding
points, they will both project the object into the same position in space (the le¯ÇÜ side) and there will be no double vision.
¯Çü Retinomotor values

{ ¯Ç£e image of an object in the peripheral visual ¯Ç×eld falls on
an extrafoveal element. To establish ¯Ç×xation on this object
a saccadic version of accurate amplitude is required.

{ Each extrafoveal retinal element therefore has a retinomotor value proportional to its distance from the fovea,
which guides the amplitude of saccadic movements
required to ÔÇÿlook at itÔÇÖ.

{ Objects within the limits of the fusional space are seen singly and the disparity information is used to produce a perception of binocular depth (stereopsis). Objects in front of
and behind the Panum space appear double.

{ ¯Ç£is is the basis of physiological diplopia. ¯Ç£e Panum
space is shallow at ¯Ç×xation (6 seconds of arc) and deeper
towards the periphery (30ÔÇô40 seconds of arc at 15┬░ from
the fovea).

{ ¯Ç£e retinal areas stimulated by images falling within the
Panum fusional space are termed Panum fusional areas.

{ ¯Ç£erefore, objects on the horopter are seen singly and in
one plane. Objects in Panum fusional areas are seen singly
and stereoscopically. Objects outside Panum fusional areas
appear double.

{ Retinomotor value, zero at the fovea, increases progressively towards the retinal periphery.
¯Çü Corresponding points are areas on each retina that share the
same subjective visual direction (for example, the foveae share
the primary visual direction).

{ Physiological diplopia is usually accompanied by physiological suppression.
¯Çü BSV is characterized by the ability to fuse the images from the
two eyes and to perceive binocular depth:

716 Introduction

A

{ Motor fusion involves the maintenance of motor alignment
of the eyes to sustain bifoveal ¯Ç×xation. It is driven by retinal
image disparity, which stimulates fusional vergences.
¯Çü Fusional vergence involves disjugate eye movements to overcome retinal image disparity. Fusional convergence helps to
control an exophoria whereas fusional divergence helps to
control an esophoria. ¯Ç£e fusional vergence mechanism may
be decreased by fatigue or illness, converting a phoria to a
tropia. ¯Ç£e amplitude of fusional vergence mechanisms can
be improved by orthoptic exercises, particularly in the case of
near fusional convergence for the relief of convergence insuf¯Ç×ciency. Amplitudes can be measured with prisms or a synoptophore. Normal values are:

F T

{ Convergence: about 15ÔÇô20 ╬ö for distance and 25 ╬ö for near.

{ Divergence: about 6ÔÇô10 ╬ö for distance and 12ÔÇô14 ╬ö for
near.

{ Vertical: 2ÔÇô3 ╬ö

{ Cyclovergence: about 8┬░.
¯Çü Stereopsis is the perception of depth. It arises when objects
behind and in front of the point of ¯Ç×xation (but within Panum
fusional space) stimulate horizontally disparate retinal elements simultaneously. ¯Ç£e fusion of these disparate images
results in a single visual impression perceived in depth. A solid
object is seen stereoscopically (in three dimensions) because
each eye sees a slightly di¯ÇÖerent aspect of the object.
¯Çü Sensory perceptions. At the onset of a squint two sensory
perceptions arise based on the normal projection of the retinal areas stimulated; confusion and pathological diplopia may
result. ¯Ç£ese require simultaneous visual perception, that is,
the ability to perceive images from both eyes simultaneously.

## Young children readily suppress diplopia but it is persistent
and usually troublesome with strabismus in older children and

adults, when it arises a¯ÇÜer the sensitive period for binocularity
(see below).

element; T = temporal retinal element. (A) When a red object

is an imaginary plane in external space. All points on the

therefore seen singly and in the same plane.

{ Sensory fusion involves the integration by the visual areas
of the cerebral cortex of two similar images, one from each
eye, into one image. It may be central, which integrates the
image falling on the foveae, or peripheral, which integrates
parts of the image falling outside the foveae. It is possible to
maintain fusion with a central visual de¯Ç×cit in one eye, but
peripheral fusion is essential to BSV and may be a¯ÇÖected
in patients with advanced ¯Ç×eld changes in glaucoma and
pituitary lesions.

## Sensory adaptations to strabismus

¯Ç£e ocular sensory system in children has the ability to adapt to
anomalous states (confusion and diplopia) by two mechanisms:
suppression and abnormal retinal correspondence (ARC). ¯Ç£ese
occur because of the plasticity of the developing visual system in
children under the age of 6ÔÇô8 years. Occasional adults who develop
sudden-onset strabismus are able to ignore the second image a¯ÇÜer
a time and therefore do not complain of diplopia.
¯Çü Suppression involves active inhibition by the visual cortex of
the image from one eye when both eyes are open. Stimuli for
suppression include diplopia, confusion and a blurred image

{ Confusion is the simultaneous appreciation of two superimposed but dissimilar images caused by stimulation
of corresponding retinal points (usually the foveae) by
images of di¯ÇÖerent objects (Fig. 18.15).

{ Pathological diplopia is the simultaneous appreciation of
two images of the same object in di¯ÇÖerent positions and
results from images of the same object falling on noncorresponding retinal points. In esotropia the diplopia is
homonymous (uncrossed ÔÇô Fig. 18.16A), in exotropia the
diplopia is heteronymous (crossed ÔÇô Fig. 18.16B).

A Left Right

Left Right

from one eye resulting from astigmatism/anisometropia. Clinically, suppression may be:

{ Central or peripheral. In central suppression the image
from the fovea of the deviating eye is inhibited to avoid
confusion. Diplopia, on the other hand, is eradicated by
the process of peripheral suppression, in which the image
from the peripheral retina of the deviating eye is inhibited.

{ Monocular or alternating. Suppression is monocular when
the image from the dominant eye always predominates
over the image from the deviating (or more ametropic) eye,
so that the image from the latter is constantly suppressed.
¯Ç£is type of suppression leads to amblyopia. When suppression alternates (switches from one eye to the other),
amblyopia is less likely to develop.

{ Facultative or obligatory. Facultative suppression occurs
only when the eyes are misaligned. Obligatory suppression
is present at all times, irrespective of whether the eyes are
deviated or straight. Examples of facultative suppression
include intermittent exotropia and Duane syndrome.
¯Çü Abnormal (anomalous) retinal correspondence (ARC) is
a condition in which non-corresponding retinal elements
acquire a common subjective visual direction, i.e. fusion
occurs in the presence of a small-angle manifest squint; the
fovea of the ¯Ç×xating eye is paired with a non-foveal element
of the deviated eye. Binocular responses in ARC are never as
good as in normal bifoveal BSV. It represents a positive sensory
adaptation to strabismus (as opposed to negative adaptation by
suppression), which allows some anomalous binocular vision
in the presence of a heterotropia. It is most frequently encountered in small-angle esotropia (microtropia), but is less common in accommodative esotropia because of the variability of

the angle of deviation and in large-angle deviations because
the separation of the images is too great.
¯Çü Microtropia is discussed further later in this chapter (see Fig.
18.28)
¯Çü Consequences of strabismus

{ ¯Ç£e fovea of the squinting eye is suppressed to avoid
confusion.

{ Diplopia will occur, since corresponding retinal elements
receive di¯ÇÖerent images.

{ To avoid diplopia, the patient will develop either peripheral suppression of the squinting eye or ARC.

{ If constant unilateral suppression occurs this will subsequently lead to strabismic amblyopia.

B

Left Right

right esotropia with normal retinal correspondence; (B) heteronymous (crossed) diplopia in right exotropia with normal
retinal correspondence.

718 Amblyopia

## Motor adaptation to strabismus

## Motor adaptation involves the adoption of a compensatory head

posture (CHP) and occurs primarily in children with congenitally
abnormal eye movements who use the CHP to maintain BSV. In
these children loss of a CHP may indicate loss of binocular function and the need for surgical intervention. ¯Ç£ese patients may
present in adult life with symptoms of decompensation, o¯ÇÜen
unaware of their CHP. Acquired paretic strabismus in adults may
be consciously controlled by a CHP provided the deviation is neither too large nor too variable with gaze (incomitance). ¯Ç£e CHP
eliminates diplopia and helps to centralize the binocular visual
¯Ç×eld. ¯Ç£e patient will turn the head into the direction of the ¯Ç×eld of
action of the weak muscle, so that the eyes are then automatically
turned the opposite direction and as far as possible away from its
¯Ç×eld of action (i.e. the head will turn where the eye cannot).
¯Çü A face turn will be adopted to control a purely horizontal
deviation. For example, if the le¯ÇÜ lateral rectus is paralysed,
diplopia will occur in le¯ÇÜ gaze. ¯Ç£e face will be turned to the
le¯ÇÜ, which deviates the eyes to the right, away from the ¯Ç×eld
of action of the weak muscle and area of diplopia. A face turn
may also be adopted in a paresis of a vertically acting muscle
to avoid the side where the vertical deviation is greatest (e.g. in
a right superior oblique weakness the face is turned to the le¯ÇÜ).
¯Çü A head tilt is adopted to compensate for torsional and/or
vertical diplopia. In a right superior oblique weakness, the
right eye is relatively elevated and the head is tilted to the
le¯ÇÜ (Fig. 18.17), towards the hypotropic eye. ¯Ç£is reduces the
vertical separation of the diplopic images and permits fusion
to be regained. If there is a signi¯Ç×cant torsional component
preventing fusion, tilting the head in the same le¯ÇÜ direction
will reduce this by invoking the righting re¯ÇØexes (placing the
extorted right eye in a position that requires extorsion).
¯Çü Chin elevation or depression may be used to compensate for
weakness of an elevator or depressor muscle or to minimize
the horizontal deviation when an ÔÇÿAÔÇÖ or ÔÇÿVÔÇÖ pattern is present.

AMBLYOPIA

Classi¯Ç×cation

Amblyopia is the unilateral, or rarely bilateral, decrease in bestcorrected visual acuity (VA) caused by form vision deprivation
and/or abnormal binocular interaction, for which there is no identi¯Ç×able pathology of the eye or visual pathway.
¯Çü Strabismic amblyopia results from abnormal binocular interaction where there is continued monocular suppression of the
deviating eye.
¯Çü Anisometropic amblyopia is caused by a di¯ÇÖerence in refractive error between the eyes and may result from a di¯ÇÖerence of
as little as 1 dioptre. ¯Ç£e more ametropic eye receives a blurred
image, in a mild form of visual deprivation. It is frequently
associated with microstrabismus and may co-exist with strabismic amblyopia.
¯Çü Stimulus deprivation amblyopia results from vision deprivation. It may be unilateral or bilateral and is typically caused by

palsy.

opacities in the media (e.g. cataract) or ptosis that covers the
pupil.
¯Çü Bilateral ametropic amblyopia results from high symmetrical
refractive errors, usually hypermetropia.
¯Çü Meridional amblyopia results from image blur in one meridian. It can be unilateral or bilateral and is caused by uncorrected astigmatism (usually >1 D) persisting beyond the
period of emmetropization in early childhood.

## Diagnosis

In the absence of an organic lesion, a di¯ÇÖerence in best-corrected
VA of two Snellen lines or more (or >1 log unit) is indicative of
amblyopia. VA in amblyopia is usually better when reading single
letters than letters in a row. ¯Ç£is ÔÇÿcrowdingÔÇÖ phenomenon occurs
to a certain extent in normal individuals but is more marked in
amblyopes and must be taken into account when testing preverbal
children.

## Treatment

## It is essential to examine the fundi to diagnose any visible organic

disease prior to commencing treatment for amblyopia. Organic
disease and amblyopia may co-exist and a trial of patching may
still be indicated in the presence of organic disease. If acuity does
not respond to treatment, investigations such as electrophysiology
or imaging should be reconsidered. ¯Ç£e sensitive period during
which acuity of an amblyopic eye can be improved is usually up
to 7ÔÇô8 years in strabismic amblyopia and may be longer (into the
teens) for anisometropic amblyopia where good binocular function is present.
¯Çü Occlusion of the normal eye, to encourage use of the amblyopic eye, is the most e¯ÇÖective treatment. ¯Ç£e regimen, full-time
or part-time, depends on the age of the patient and the density
of amblyopia.

{ ¯Ç£e younger the patient, the more rapid the likely improvement but the greater the risk of inducing amblyopia in the
normal eye. It is therefore very important to monitor VA
regularly in both eyes during treatment.

{ ¯Ç£e better the VA at the start of occlusion, the shorter
the duration required, although there is wide variation
between patients.

{ If there has been no improvement after 6 months of
effective occlusion, further treatment is unlikely to be
fruitful.

{ Poor compliance is the single greatest barrier to improvement and must be monitored. Amblyopia treatment bene¯Ç×ts from time spent at the outset on communication of
the rationale and the di¯Çøculties involved.
¯Çü Penalization, in which vision in the normal eye is blurred
with atropine, is an alternative method. It may work best in
the treatment of mildÔÇômoderate amblyopia (6/24 or better),
especially when due to anisometropic hypermetropia. Patch
occlusion is likely to produce a quicker response than atropine, which has conventionally been reserved for use when
compliance with patch occlusion is poor. Penalization o¯ÇÖers
the particular advantage of being di¯Çøcult to thwart even if the
child objects. It also creates less of a psychosocial problem than
patching, especially in the school-going child. Weekend instillation may be adequate.

TIP Organic disease needs to be excluded before
commencing treatment for amblyopia.

CLINICAL EVALUATION

## History

¯Çü Variability is signi¯Ç×cant because intermittent strabismus indicates some degree of binocularity. An equally alternating deviation suggests symmetrical VA in both eyes.
¯Çü General health or developmental problems may be signi¯Ç×cant
(e.g. children with cerebral palsy have an increased incidence
of strabismus). In older patients, poor health and stress may
cause decompensation and in acquired paresis patients may
report associations or causal factors (trauma, neurological disease, diabetes etc.).
¯Çü Birth history, including period of gestation, birth weight and
any problems in utero, with delivery or in the neonatal period.
¯Çü Family history is important because strabismus is frequently
familial, although no de¯Ç×nitive inheritance pattern is recognized. It is also important to know what therapy was necessary
in other family members.
¯Çü Previous ocular history including refractive prescription and
compliance with spectacles or occlusion, previous surgery or
prisms is important to future treatment options and prognosis.

TIP In children, the later the onset of esotropia, the greater
the likelihood of an accommodative component to the
deviation.

## Visual acuity

## Testing in preverbal children

¯Çü Age of onset

{ ¯Ç£e earlier the onset, the more likely the need for surgical
correction.

{ ¯Ç£e later the onset, the greater the likelihood of an accommodative component (mostly arising between 18 and 36
months).

{ ¯Ç£e longer the duration of squint in early childhood the
greater the risk of amblyopia, unless ¯Ç×xation is freely alternating. Inspection of previous photographs may be useful
for the documentation of strabismus or CHP.
¯Çü Symptoms may indicate decompensation of a pre-existent
heterophoria or more signi¯Ç×cantly a recently acquired (usually
paretic) condition. In the former, the patient usually complains
of discomfort, blurring and possibly diplopia of indeterminate
onset and duration in comparison to the acquired condition
with the sudden onset of diplopia.

{ ¯Ç£e type of diplopia (horizontal, cyclovertical) should be
established, together with the direction of gaze in which it
predominates and whether any BSV is retained.

{ In adults it is very important to determine exactly what
problems the squint is causing as a basis for decisions
about treatment.

{ It is not unusual for patients to present with spurious
symptoms that mask embarrassment over a psychosocially
noticeable squint.

¯Ç£e evaluation can be separated into the qualitative assessment of
visual behaviour and the quantitative assessment of VA using preferential looking tests. Assessment of visual behaviour is achieved
as follows:
¯Çü Fixation and following may be assessed using bright attentiongrabbing targets (a face is o¯ÇÜen best). ¯Ç£is method indicates
whether the infant is visually alert and is of particular value in
a child suspected of being blind.
¯Çü Comparison between the behaviour of the two eyes may
reveal a unilateral preference. Occlusion of one eye, if
strongly objected to by the child, indicates poorer acuity in
the other eye. However, it is possible to have good visual
attention with each eye but unequal VA and all risk factors
for amblyopia must be considered in the interpretation of
results.
¯Çü Fixation behaviour can be used to establish unilateral preference if a manifest squint is present.

{ Fixation is promoted in the squinting eye by occluding
the dominant eye while the child ¯Ç×xates a target of interest
(preferably incorporating a light).

{ Fixation is then graded as central or non-central and steady
or unsteady (the corneal re¯ÇØection can be observed).

{ ¯Ç£e other eye is then uncovered and the ability to maintain
¯Ç×xation is observed.

{ If ¯Ç×xation immediately returns to the uncovered eye, then
VA is probably impaired.

{ If ¯Ç×xation is maintained through a blink, then VA is probably good.

720 Clinical Evaluation

{ If the patient alternates ¯Ç×xation, then the two eyes probably have equal vision.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe 10 ╬ö test is similar and can be used regardless of whether
a manifest squint is present. It involves the promotion of diplopia using a 10 ╬ö vertical prism. Alternation between the diplopic targets suggests equal VA.
¯Çü Rotation test is a gross qualitative test of the ability of an infant
to ¯Ç×xate with both eyes open. ¯Ç£e test is performed as follows:

{ ¯Ç£e examiner holds the child facing him or her and rotates
briskly through 360┬░.

{ If vision is normal, the eyes will deviate in the direction
of rotation under the in¯ÇØuence of the vestibulo-ocular
response. ¯Ç£e eyes ¯ÇØick back to the primary position to
produce a rotational nystagmus.

{ When rotation stops, nystagmus is brie¯ÇØy observed in the
opposite direction for 1ÔÇô2 seconds and should then cease
due to suppression of post-rotary nystagmus by ¯Ç×xation.

{ If vision is severely impaired, the post-rotation nystagmus
does not stop as quickly when rotation ceases because
the vestibulo-ocular response is not blocked by visual
feedback.
¯Çü Preferential looking tests can be used from early infancy and
are based on the fact that infants prefer to look at a pattern
rather than a homogeneous stimulus. ¯Ç£e infant is exposed
to a stimulus and the examiner observes the eyes for ¯Ç×xation movements, without themselves knowing the stimulus
position.

{ Tests in common use include the Teller and Keeler acuity
cards, which consist of black stripes (gratings) of varying
widths and Cardi¯ÇÖ acuity cards (Fig. 18.18), which consist
of familiar pictures with variable outline width.

{ Low-frequency (coarse) gratings or pictures with a wider
outline are seen more easily than high-frequency gratings
or thin outline pictures and an assessment of resolution
(not recognition) VA is made accordingly.

A

B

(A) Car with thin outline (6/7.5); (B) ¯Çƒsh with thick outline (6/15).

¯Çü At age 2 years most children will have su¯Çøcient language skills
to undertake a picture naming test such as the crowded Kay
pictures (Fig. 18.19A).
¯Çü At age 3 years most children will be able to undertake the
matching of letter optotypes as in the Keeler logMAR (Fig.
18.19B), Sonksen or Otago crowded tests. If a crowded letter
test proves too di¯Çøcult it is preferable to perform the crowded
Kay pictures than to use single optotype letters.
¯Çü Older children may continue with the crowded letter tests,
naming or matching them. LogMAR tests are in common
usage and are preferable to Snellen for all children at risk of
amblyopia.

## Tests for stereopsis

Stereopsis is measured in seconds of arc (1┬░ = 60 minutes of arc;
1 minute = 60 seconds); the lower the value the better the stereoacuity. It is useful to remember that normal spatial resolution (VA) is
1 minute and normal stereoacuity is 60 seconds (also 1 minute, but
conventionally expressed in seconds). Various tests, using di¯ÇÖering
principles, are employed to assess the stereoacuity. Random dot tests
(e.g. TNO, Frisby) provide the most de¯Ç×nitive evidence of high-grade
BSV. Where this is weak and/or based on ARC (see above), contourbased tests (e.g. Titmus) may provide more reliable information.

{ Since grating acuity o¯ÇÜen exceeds Snellen acuity in amblyopia, Teller cards may overestimate VA. ¯Ç£ese methods
may not be reliable if a proper forced-choice staircase protocol is not followed during testing and neither method
has high sensitivity to the presence of amblyopia. ¯Ç£e
results must be considered in combination with risk factors for amblyopia.
¯Çü Pattern visual evoked potentials (VEP) give a representation
of spatial acuity but are more commonly used in the diagnosis
of optic neuropathy.

## Testing in verbal children

¯Ç£e tests described below should be performed at 3ÔÇô4 metres from
the target, as it is easier to obtain compliance than at 6 metres,
with little or no clinical detriment. It is important to note that
amblyopia can only be accurately diagnosed using a crowded test
requiring target recognition and that logMAR tests (logarithm of
the minimal angle of resolution ÔÇô see Ch. 1) provide the best measure against which improvement with amblyopia therapy can be
assessed. ¯Ç£ese are readily available in formats suited to normal
children from 2 years onwards.

A

B

## Titmus

¯Ç£e Titmus test consists of a three-dimensional polarized vectograph comprising two plates in the form of a booklet viewed
through polarized spectacles. ¯Ç£e spectacles should be worn
before the plates are viewed. On the right is a large ¯ÇØy and on the
le¯ÇÜ is a series of nine squares (within each of which there are four
circles) and animals (Fig. 18.20). ¯Ç£e test should be performed at
a distance of 40 cm.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe ¯Ç×y is a test of gross stereopsis (3000 seconds) and is especially useful for young children. It should appear to stand out
from the page and the child is encouraged to pick up the tip of
one of its wings between ¯Ç×nger and thumb.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe animal component consists of three rows of stylized animals (400ÔÇô100 seconds), one of which will appear forward of
the plane of reference.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe circles comprise a graded series measuring 800ÔÇô40 seconds; one of a set of four circles should appear to stand out
from the plate surface.
¯Çü If the child has less than 800 seconds of stereopsis and prefers
¯Ç×xing with the right eye (le¯ÇÜ suppression), the bottom circle of
¯Ç×rst square of four circles is displaced to the le¯ÇÜ (and vice versa
if ¯Ç×xing with the le¯ÇÜ eye).

A

B

TNO

¯Ç£e TNO random dot test consists of seven plates of randomly
distributed paired red and green dots viewed with redÔÇôgreen spectacles and measures from 480 down to 15 seconds of arc at 40 cm.

## Within each plate the dots of one colour forming the target shape

(squares, crosses etc. ÔÇô Fig. 18.21) are displaced horizontally in
relation to paired dots of the other colour so that they have a different retinal disparity to those outside the target. Control shapes
are visible without the spectacles.

## Frisby

¯Ç£e Frisby stereotest consists of three transparent plastic plates of
varying thickness. On the surface of each plate are printed four
squares of small randomly distributed shapes (Fig. 18.22). One
of the squares contains a ÔÇÿhiddenÔÇÖ circle, in which the random
shapes are printed on the reverse of the plate. ¯Ç£e test does not
require special spectacles because disparity (600ÔÇô15 seconds) is
created by the thickness of the plate; the working distance must
be measured.

## Lang

¯Ç£e Lang stereotest does not require special spectacles. ¯Ç£e targets
(a star, moon, car and elephant) are seen alternately by each eye

722 Clinical Evaluation

A

B

with stereopsis. (A) Test target, (B) redÔÇôgreen spectacles.

through the built-in cylindrical lens elements. ¯Ç£e star is monocularly visible but provides a stereoscopic e¯ÇÖect to observers with
binocular vision. ¯Ç£e star helps to attract and maintain the interest of small children. Displacement of the dots creates disparity
(1200ÔÇô200 seconds) and the patient is asked to name or point to a
simple shape, such as a star, on the card (Fig. 18.23).

## Tests for binocular fusion in infants
without manifest squint

Base-out prism

¯Ç£is is a simple method for detecting fusion in children. ¯Ç£e test is
performed by placing a 20 ╬ö base-out prism in front of one eye (the
right eye in Fig. 18.24). ¯Ç£is displaces the retinal image temporally
with resultant diplopia.
¯Çü ¯Ç£ere will be a shi¯ÇÜ of the right eye to the le¯ÇÜ to resume ¯Ç×xation (right adduction) with a corresponding shi¯ÇÜ of the le¯ÇÜ eye
to the le¯ÇÜ (le¯ÇÜ abduction) in accordance with the Hering law
(Fig. 18.24B).
¯Çü ¯Ç£e le¯ÇÜ eye will then make a corrective re-¯Ç×xational saccade to
the right (le¯ÇÜ re-adduction) (Fig. 18.24C).
¯Çü On removal of the prism both eyes move to the right (Fig.
18.24D).

¯Çü ¯Ç£e le¯ÇÜ eye then makes an outward fusional movement (Fig.
18.24E).
¯Çü Most children with good BSV should be able to overcome a
20 ╬öprism from the age of 6 months. If not, weaker prisms
(16 ╬ö or 12 ╬ö) may be tried, but the response is then more dif¯Ç×cult to identify.

## Binocular convergence

## Simple convergence to an interesting target can be demonstrated

from 3 to 4 months. Both eyes should follow the approaching
target symmetrically ÔÇÿto the noseÔÇÖ. Over-convergence in the infant
may indicate an incipient esotropia. Divergence may be due
either to a tendency to a divergent deviation or simply lack of
interest.

## Tests for sensory anomalies

Worth four-dot test

¯Ç£is is a dissociation test that can be used with both distance and
near ¯Ç×xation and di¯ÇÖerentiates between BSV, ARC and suppression.

A

B

C

D

E

Results can only be interpreted if the presence or absence of a manifest squint is known at time of testing.
¯Çü Procedure

A

B

possible results (see text).

{ ¯Ç£e patient wears a green lens in front of the right eye,
which ¯Ç×lters out all colours except green and a red lens in
front of the le¯ÇÜ eye which will ¯Ç×lter out all colours except
red (Fig. 18.25A).

{ ¯Ç£e patient then views a box with four lights: one red, two
green and one white.
¯Çü Results (Fig. 18.25B)

{ If BSV is present, all four lights are seen.

{ If all four lights are seen in the presence of a manifest
deviation, harmonious ARC (see ÔÇÿSynoptophoreÔÇÖ below) is
present.

{ If two red lights are seen, right suppression is present.

{ If three green lights are seen, le¯ÇÜ suppression is present.

{ If two red and three green lights are seen, diplopia is
present.

{ If the green and red lights alternate, alternating suppression is present.

¯Çü Procedure. ¯Ç£e two lenses are placed at 45┬░ and 135┬░ in front
of each eye and the patient ¯Ç×xates on a focal light source (Fig.
18.26A). Each eye perceives an oblique line of light, perpendicular to that perceived by the fellow eye (Fig. 18.26B). Dissimilar images are thus presented to each eye under binocular
viewing conditions.
¯Çü Results (Fig. 18.26C) cannot be interpreted unless it is known
whether strabismus is present.

{ If the two streaks intersect at their centres in the form of
an oblique cross (an ÔÇÿXÔÇÖ), the patient has BSV if the eyes are
straight, or harmonious ARC in the presence of manifest
strabismus.

{ If the two lines are seen but they do not form a cross, diplopia is present.

{ If only one streak is seen, there is no simultaneous perception and suppression is present.

{ In theory, if a small gap is seen in one of the streaks, a central suppression scotoma (as found in microtropia) is present. In practice this is o¯ÇÜen di¯Çøcult to demonstrate and the
patient describes a cross. ¯Ç£e scotoma can be con¯Ç×rmed
with the 4 ╬ö prism test (see below).

## Bagolini striated glasses

¯Ç£is is a test for detecting BSV, ARC or suppression. Each lens has
¯Ç×ne striations that convert a point source of light into a line, as
with the Maddox rod (see below).

of a point of light through Bagolini lenses; (C) possible results.

TIP The 4¯ÇØ╬ö prism base-out test is useful to distinguish
bifoveal ¯Çƒxation from foveal suppression (central suppression
scotoma), which occurs during binocular viewing in patients
with mono¯Çƒxation syndrome.

4¯ÇØ╬ö prism test

¯Ç£is test distinguishes bifoveal ¯Ç×xation (normal BSV) from foveal
suppression (also known as a central suppression scotoma ÔÇô CSS)
in microtropia and employs the principle described in the 20 ╬ö test
(the Hering law and convergence) to overcome diplopia.
¯Çü With bifoveal ¯ÇØxation

{ ¯Ç£e prism is placed base-out (microtropia is commonly
esotropic not exotropic) in front of the right eye with
deviation of the image away from the fovea temporally,
followed by corrective movement of both eyes to the le¯ÇÜ
(Fig. 18.27A).

{ ¯Ç£e le¯ÇÜ eye then converges to fuse the images (Fig. 18.27B).
¯Çü In le¯Ç£ microtropia

{ ¯Ç£e patient ¯Ç×xates a distance target with both eyes open
and a 4 ╬ö prism is placed base-out in front of the eye with
suspected CSS (the le¯ÇÜ in Fig. 18.28).

{ ¯Ç£e image is moved temporally in the le¯ÇÜ eye but falls
within the CSS and no movement of either eye is observed
(Fig. 18.28A).

{ ¯Ç£e prism is then moved to the right eye which adducts to
maintain ¯Ç×xation. ¯Ç£e le¯ÇÜ eye similarly moves to the le¯ÇÜ
consistent with the Hering law of equal innervation, but
the second image falls within the CSS of the le¯ÇÜ eye and so
no subsequent re-¯Ç×xation movement is seen (Fig. 18.28B).

## Synoptophore

¯Ç£e synoptophore compensates for the angle of squint and allows
stimuli to be presented to both eyes simultaneously (Fig. 18.29A).

## It can thus be used to investigate the potential for binocular function in the presence of a manifest squint and is of particular value

in assessing young children (from age 3 years), who generally ¯Ç×nd
the test process enjoyable. It can also detect suppression and ARC.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e instrument consists of two cylindrical tubes with a mirrored right-angled bend and a +6.50 D lens in each eyepiece
(Fig. 18.29B, top). ¯Ç£is optically sets the testing distance as
equivalent to about 6 metres.
¯Çü Pictures are inserted in a slide carrier situated at the outer end
of each tube. ¯Ç£e two tubes are supported on columns that
enable the pictures to be moved in relation to each other and
any adjustments are indicated on a scale.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e synoptophore can measure horizontal, vertical and torsional misalignments simultaneously and is valuable in
determining surgical approach by assessing the di¯ÇÖerent contributions in the cardinal positions of gaze.

## Grades of binocular vision

## Binocular vision can be graded on the synoptophore as below

(Fig. 18.29B, bottom).
¯Çü First grade (simultaneous perception ÔÇô SP) is tested by introducing two dissimilar but not mutually antagonistic pictures,
such as a bird and a cage.

{ ¯Ç£e subject is then asked to put the bird into the cage by
moving the arm of the synoptophore.

{ If the two pictures cannot be seen simultaneously, then
suppression is present.

A

B

Left Right

Left Right

(B) fusional re-¯Çƒxation movement of the left eye.

{ Some retinal ÔÇÿrivalryÔÇÖ will occur although one picture is
smaller than the other, so that while the small one is seen
foveally, the larger one is seen parafoveally (and is thus
placed in front of the deviating eye).

{ Larger macular and paramacular slides are used if foveal
slides cannot be superimposed.
¯Çü Second grade (fusion). If simultaneous perception slides can
be superimposed then the test proceeds to the second grade,
which is the ability of the two eyes to produce a composite picture (sensory fusion) from two similar pictures, each of which
is incomplete in one small di¯ÇÖerent detail.

{ ¯Ç£e classic example is two rabbits, one lacking a tail and
the other lacking a bunch of ¯ÇØowers. If fusion is present,
one rabbit complete with tail and ¯ÇØowers will be seen.

{ ¯Ç£e range of fusion (motor fusion) is then tested by moving the arms of the synoptophore so that the eyes have to
converge and diverge in order to maintain fusion.

{ ¯Ç£e presence of simple fusion without any range is of little
value in everyday life.
¯Çü ¯Çƒird grade (stereopsis) is the ability to obtain an impression
of depth by the superimposition of two pictures of the same
object which have been taken from slightly di¯ÇÖerent angles.
¯Ç£e classic example is a bucket, which should be appreciated
in three dimensions.

## Detection of abnormal retinal correspondence

ARC is detected on the synoptophore as follows:

726 Clinical Evaluation

A

B

## Scotoma

## Scotoma

Left Right

Left Right

¯Çü ¯Ç£e subjective angle of deviation is that at which the SP slides
are superimposed. ¯Ç£e examiner determines the objective
angle of the deviation by presenting each fovea alternately with
a target by extinguishing one or other light and moving the
slide in front of the deviating eye until no movement of the
eyes is seen.
¯Çü If the subjective and objective angles coincide then retinal correspondence is normal.
¯Çü If the objective and subjective angles are di¯ÇÖerent, ARC is
present. ¯Ç£e di¯ÇÖerence in degrees between the subjective and
objective angles is the angle of anomaly. ARC is said to be harmonious when the objective angle equals the angle of anomaly
and inharmonious when it exceeds the angle of anomaly. It

is only in harmonious ARC that binocular responses can be
demonstrated. ¯Ç£e inharmonious form may represent a lesser
adaptation or an artefact of testing.

## Measurement of deviation

## Hirschberg test

¯Ç£e Hirschberg test gives a rough objective estimate of the angle
of a manifest strabismus and is especially useful in young or uncooperative patients or when ¯Ç×xation in the deviating eye is poor.
It is also useful in excluding pseudostrabismus. A pen torch is
shone into the eyes from armÔÇÖs length and the patient asked to

A B

¯Ç×xate the light. ¯Ç£e corneal re¯ÇØection of the light will be (more or
less) centred in the pupil of the ¯Ç×xating eye, but will be decentred
in a squinting eye, in the direction opposite to that of the deviation (Fig. 18.30). ¯Ç£e distance of the corneal light re¯ÇØection from
the centre of the pupil is noted; each millimetre of deviation is
approximately equal to 7┬░ (1┬░ Ôëê 2 prism dioptres). For example, if
the re¯ÇØex is situated at the temporal border of the pupil (assuming a pupillary diameter of 4 mm), the angle is about 15┬░; if it is
at the limbus, the angle is about 45┬░. As the pupil size varies, the
estimated angle varies.

Krimsky and prism re¯Ç£ection tests

Corneal re¯ÇØex assessment can be combined with prisms to give a
more accurate approximation of the angle in a manifest deviation.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe Krimsky test involves placement of prisms in front of the
¯Ç×xating eye until the corneal light re¯ÇØections are symmetrical
(Fig. 18.31A and B). ¯Ç£is test reduces the problem of parallax
and is more commonly used than the prism re¯ÇØection test.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe prism re¯Ç×ection test involves the placement of prisms in
front of the deviating eye until the corneal light re¯ÇØections are
symmetrical.

CoverÔÇôuncover test

¯Ç£e coverÔÇôuncover test consists of two parts:
¯Çü ¯Çƒe cover test detects heterotropia. It is helpful to begin the
near test using a light to observe the corneal re¯ÇØections and to
assess ¯Ç×xation in the deviating eye. It should then be repeated
for near using an accommodative target and for distance as
follows:

{ ¯Ç£e patient ¯Ç×xates on a straight-ahead target.

{ If a right deviation is suspected, the examiner covers the
¯Ç×xing le¯ÇÜ eye and notes any movement of the right eye to
take up ¯Ç×xation.

{ No movement indicates orthotropia (Fig. 18.32A) or le¯ÇÜ
exotropia (Fig. 18.32B).

{ Adduction of the right eye to take up ¯Ç×xation indicates right
exotropia and abduction, right esotropia (Fig. 18.32C).

A

B

C

right corneal re¯Ç£ex is near the inner border of the pupil
indicating an angle of about 10┬░; (B) the left corneal re¯Ç£ex
is near the temporal border of the pupil indicating an angle
of 15ÔÇô20┬░; (C) the left corneal re¯Ç£ex is near the limbus indicating an angle of close to 40 [o] . (Courtesy of ADN Murray.)

{ Downward movement indicates right hypertropia and
upward movement right hypotropia.

{ ¯Ç£e test is repeated on the opposite eye.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe uncover test detects heterophoria. It should be performed
both for near (using an accommodative target) and for distance as follows:

eye ¯Çƒxing, no movement indicates orthotropia or (B) left exotropia; (C) with the left eye ¯Çƒxing, abduction of the right eye
indicates esotropia.

{ ¯Ç£e patient ¯Ç×xates a straight-ahead distant target.

{ ¯Ç£e examiner covers the right eye and, a¯ÇÜer 2ÔÇô3 seconds,
removes the cover.

{ No movement indicates orthophoria (Fig. 18.33A). A keen
observer will frequently detect a very slight latent deviation in most normal individuals, as few individuals are
truly orthophoric, particularly on near ¯Ç×xation.

{ If the right eye had deviated while under cover, a re¯Ç×xation movement (recovery to BSV) is observed on being
uncovered.

{ Adduction (nasal recovery) of the right eye indicates exophoria (Fig. 18.33B) and abduction esophoria (Fig. 18.33C).

{ Upward or downward movement indicates a vertical phoria.

{ ¯Ç£e test is repeated for the opposite eye.

{ Most examiners perform the cover test and the uncover
test sequentially, hence the term coverÔÇôuncover test.

## Prism cover test

¯Ç£e prism cover test measures the angle of deviation and includes
the alternate cover test with prisms. It is performed with the patient
¯Ç×xing on a distant target in the primary position and in the eight
diagnostic positions of gaze. It is repeated in the primary position
with near ¯Ç×xation and in patients with intermittent exotropia, with
¯Ç×xation in the far distance. It is performed as follows:
¯Çü ¯Ç£e alternate cover test is ¯Ç×rst performed to establish the direction and approximate extent of deviation.
¯Çü Prisms of increasing strength are placed in front of one eye
with the base opposite the direction of the deviation (i.e. the
apex of the prism is pointed in the direction of the deviation). For example, in a convergent strabismus the prism is
held base-out and in a right hypertropia, base-down before
the right eye.

TIP When undertaking cover testing, the patient must ¯Çƒxate
on an accommodative target rather than on a light source.

## Alternate cover test

¯Ç£e alternate cover test induces dissociation to reveal the total
deviation when fusion is disrupted. It should be performed only
a¯ÇÜer the coverÔÇôuncover test.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e right eye is covered for several seconds.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e occluder is quickly shi¯ÇÜed to the opposite eye for 2 seconds, then back and forth several times. A¯ÇÜer the cover is
removed, the examiner notes the speed and smoothness of
recovery as the eyes return to their pre-dissociated state.
¯Çü A patient with a well-compensated heterophoria will have
straight eyes before and a¯ÇÜer the test has been performed
whereas a patient with poor control may decompensate to a
manifest deviation.

¯Çü ¯Ç£e alternate cover test is performed continuously as stronger prisms are introduced, typically using a prism bar consisting of a column of prisms of progressive strength (Fig. 18.34).
¯Ç£e amplitude of the re-¯Ç×xation movement should gradually
decrease as the strength of prism approaches the extent of
deviation.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e end-point is approached when no movement is seen. To
ensure the maximum angle is found, the prism strength can be
increased further until a movement is observed in the opposite
direction (the point of reversal) and then reduced again to ¯Ç×nd
the neutral value; the angle of deviation is then taken from the
strength of the prism.

## Maddox wing

¯Ç£e Maddox wing dissociates the eyes for near ¯Ç×xation (1/3 m)
and measures heterophoria. ¯Ç£e instrument is constructed in such
a way that the right eye sees only a white vertical arrow and a red
horizontal arrow, whereas the le¯ÇÜ eye sees only horizontal and vertical rows of numbers (Fig. 18.35).
¯Çü Horizontal deviation is measured by asking the patient to
which number the white arrow points.
¯Çü Vertical deviation is measured by asking the patient which
number intersects with the red arrow.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e amount of cyclophoria is determined by asking the patient
to move the red arrow so that it is parallel with the horizontal
row of numbers.

## Maddox rod

¯Ç£e Maddox rod consists of a series of fused cylindrical red or
white glass rods that convert the appearance of a white spot of light
into a red or white streak. ¯Ç£e optical properties of the rods cause
the streak of light to be at an angle of 90┬░ with the long axis of the
rods. When the glass rods are held horizontally, the streak will be
vertical and vice versa.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e rod is placed in front of the right eye (Fig. 18.36A). ¯Ç£is
dissociates the two eyes: the red streak seen by the right eye

A

B

cannot be fused with the unaltered white spot of light seen by
the le¯ÇÜ eye (Fig. 18.36B).
¯Çü ¯Ç£e amount of dissociation (Fig. 18.36C) is measured by the
superimposition of the two images using prisms. ¯Ç£e base of
the prism is placed in the position opposite to the direction of
the deviation.
¯Çü Both vertical and horizontal deviations can be measured in
this way but the test cannot di¯ÇÖerentiate a phoria from a tropia.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e double Maddox rod test is used to determine cyclodeviations. Traditionally, a red Maddox is placed before the right
eye and a white Maddox before the le¯ÇÜ. A Maddox rod is
placed in front of each eye in a trial frame or phoropter with
the lines arranged vertically so that the patient sees horizontal line images. ¯Ç£e patient or examiner rotates the axes of the
rods until the lines are perceived to be parallel. To facilitate
the patientÔÇÖs recognition of the two lines, it is o¯ÇÜen helpful to
dissociate the lines by placing a small prism base-up or basedown in front of one eye. ¯Ç£e degree of deviation and the
direction (incyclo- or excyclo-) can be determined by the angle
of rotation that causes the line images to appear horizontal and
parallel.

## Motility tests

## Ocular movements

## Examination of eye movements involves the assessment of smooth

pursuit movements followed by saccades.
¯Çü Versions towards the eight eccentric positions of gaze are tested
by asking the patient to follow a target, usually a pen or pen
torch (the latter o¯ÇÖers the advantage of corneal light re¯ÇØections
to aid assessment). Check versions and ductions (if required)
¯Ç×rst and then perform cover tests as discussed above. Versions
may also be elicited involuntarily in response to a noise or by the
dollÔÇÖs head manoeuvre in uncooperative patients.
¯Çü Ductions are assessed if reduced ocular motility is noted in
either or both eyes. A pen torch should be used with careful
attention to the position of the corneal re¯ÇØexes. ¯Ç£e fellow eye
is occluded and the patient asked to follow the torch into various
positions of gaze. Rotatory de¯Ç×ciencies are qualitatively assessed.
A simple numeric system may be employed using 0 to denote full
movement and ÔêÆ1 to ÔêÆ4 to denote increasing degrees of underaction (Fig. 18.37). Using the lateral rectus muscle for example, ÔêÆ4
would denote an inability to abduct the eye past the midline, ÔêÆ3
an inability to abduct the eye more than 22.5┬░ past the midline,
ÔêÆ2 inability to abduct the eye more than 45┬░ past the midline and
ÔêÆ1 inability to abduct the eye more than 67.5┬░ past the midline. In
the same way, overactions are assessed on a +1 to +4 basis. Both
are recorded pictorially. ¯Ç£ese measurements can be accurately
repeated with agreement between di¯ÇÖerent observers.

-1

-2

-3

-4

## Near point of convergence

¯Ç£e near point of convergence (NPC) is the nearest point on which
the eyes can maintain binocular ¯Ç×xation. It can be measured with the
RAF rule, which rests on the patientÔÇÖs cheeks (Fig. 18.38). A target
is slowly moved along the rule towards the patientÔÇÖs eyes until one
eye loses ¯Ç×xation and dri¯ÇÜs laterally (objective NPC). ¯Ç£e subjective
NPC is the point at which the patient reports diplopia. Normally, the
NPC should be nearer than 10 cm without undue e¯ÇÖort.

## Near point of accommodation

¯Ç£e near point of accommodation (NPA) is the nearest point on
which the eyes can maintain clear focus. It can also be measured
with the RAF rule. ¯Ç£e patient ¯Ç×xates a line of print, which is
then slowly moved towards the patient until it becomes blurred.
¯Ç£e distance at which this is ¯Ç×rst reported is read o¯ÇÖ the rule and
denotes the NPA. ¯Ç£e NPA recedes with age; when su¯Çøciently far
away to render reading di¯Çøcult without optical correction, presbyopia is present. At the age of 20 years the NPA is 8 cm and by the
age of 50 years it has receded to approximately 46 cm. ¯Ç£e amplitude of accommodation can also be assessed using concave lenses
in 0.5-dioptre steps whilst ¯Ç×xating the 6/6 Snellen line and reporting when the vision blurs.

## Fusional amplitudes

Fusional amplitudes measure the e¯Çøcacy of vergence movements.
¯Ç£ey may be tested with prisms bars or the synoptophore. An
increasingly strong prism is placed in front of one eye, which will
then abduct or adduct (depending on whether the prism is base-in
or base-out), in order to maintain bifoveal ¯Ç×xation. When a prism
greater than the fusional amplitude is reached, diplopia is reported
or one eye dri¯ÇÜs in the opposite direction, indicating the limit of
vergence ability. (For normal values, see fusional vergence above.)

## Postoperative diplopia test

¯Ç£is simple test is mandatory prior to strabismus surgery in all
non-binocular patients over 7ÔÇô8 years of age to assess the risk of
diplopia a¯ÇÜer surgery.

¯Çü Corrective prisms are placed in front of one eye (usually the
deviating eye) and the patient asked to ¯Ç×xate a straight-ahead
target with both eyes open. ¯Ç£e prisms are slowly increased
until the angle has been signi¯Ç×cantly overcorrected and the
patient reports if diplopia occurs.
¯Çü If suppression persists throughout there is little risk of diplopia
following surgery. However, in a consecutive exotropia of 35 ╬ö,
diplopia may be reported from 30 ╬ö and persist as the prism
correction mimics an esotropia.
¯Çü Diplopia may be intermittent or constant but in either case
constitutes an indication to perform a diagnostic botulinum
toxin test (see below).
¯Çü Diplopia is not restricted to patients with good VA in the deviating eye.
¯Çü Intractable diplopia is di¯Çøcult to treat.

## Field of binocular single vision

¯Ç£e ¯Ç×eld of BSV may be tested on Goldmann perimetry, tangent
screen or Hess chart and usually measures about 45┬░ to 50┬░ from
the ¯Ç×xation point, except where it is blocked by the nose. It represents the parts of the visual ¯Ç×eld of each eye that overlap and
where bifoveal fusion of the object of regard occurs (Fig. 18.39). In
patients with recent onset of an ocular motor paralysis or restrictive muscle defect, diplopia is usually the major symptom. ¯Ç£e test
is extremely valuable in providing an exact repeatable follow-up
measurement and is also useful in the preoperative and postoperative management of these patients.

Hess chart and Lees screen

A Hess chart is plotted to aid in the diagnosis and monitoring of a
patient with incomitant strabismus, such as an extraocular muscle
palsy (e.g. third, fourth or sixth nerve paresis) or a mechanical or
myopathic limitation (e.g. thyroid ophthalmopathy, blow-out fracture or myasthenia gravis). ¯Ç£e chart is commonly prepared using
either the Lees or Hess screen, which facilitate plotting of the dissociated ocular position as a measure of extraocular muscle action.
Information provided by the Hess chart should be regarded in the
context of other investigations such as the ¯Ç×eld of binocular single
vision. ¯Ç£e prism cover test is also very useful in the assessment of
incomitant squint.

## Hess screen

¯Ç£e Hess screen contains a tangent pattern displayed on a darkgrey background. Red lights that can be individually illuminated
by a control panel indicate the cardinal positions of gaze within
a central ¯Ç×eld (15┬░ from primary position) and a peripheral ¯Ç×eld
(30┬░); each square represents 5┬░ of ocular rotation. ¯Ç£e eyes are
dissociated by the use of reversible goggles incorporating a red
and a green lens, the red lens in front of the ¯Ç×xating eye and the
green lens the non-¯Ç×xating eye. Red points of lights are illuminated at selected positions on the screen. ¯Ç£e patient holds a
green pointer and is asked to superimpose a green light over each
red light in turn. In orthophoria the two lights should be more or
less superimposed in all positions of gaze. ¯Ç£e goggles are then

732 Clinical Evaluation

reversed and the procedure repeated. So¯ÇÜware is available that
facilitates the plotting of a Hess chart using a standard desktop
computer screen.

## Lees screen

¯Ç£is apparatus (Fig. 18.40) consists of two opalescent glass screens
at right-angles to each other, bisected by a two-sided plane mirror
that dissociates the eyes; each of the eyes can see only one of the
two screens. Each screen has a tangent pattern (two-dimensional
projection of a spherical surface) that is revealed only when the
screen is illuminated. ¯Ç£e patient is positioned facing the nonilluminated screen with his or her chin stabilized on a rest. Using
a pointer, the examiner indicates a target point on the illuminated
tangent pattern and the patient positions a pointer on the nonilluminated screen, at a position perceived to be superimposed on
the dot indicated by the examiner. ¯Ç£e non-illuminated screen is
brie¯ÇØy illuminated by the examiner using a footswitch to facilitate
recording of the dot indicated by the patient. When the procedure
has been completed for one eye, the patient is rotated through
90┬░ to face the previously illuminated screen and the procedure
repeated.

## Interpretation

rectus paresis of recent onset.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e smaller chart indicates the eye with the paretic muscle
(right eye).
¯Çü ¯Ç£e larger chart indicates the eye with the overacting yoke
muscle (le¯ÇÜ eye).

¯Çü The smaller chart will show its greatest restriction in the
main direction of action of the paretic muscle (right lateral
rectus).
¯Çü ¯Ç£e larger chart will show its greatest expansion in the main
direction of action of the yoke muscle (le¯ÇÜ medial rectus).
¯Çü ¯Ç£e degree of disparity between the plotted point and the template in any position of gaze gives an estimate of the angle of
deviation (each square = 5┬░).

inferior oblique; IR = inferior rectus; LR = lateral rectus; MR =
medial rectus; SO = superior oblique; SR = superior rectus.

## Changes with time

Progressive changes in the Hess chart with time are characteristic and useful both as a prognostic indicator and to guide
management.
¯Çü In right superior rectus palsy, the Hess chart will show underaction of the a¯ÇÖected muscle with an overaction of its yoke
muscle, the le¯ÇÜ inferior oblique (Fig. 18.42A). Because of the
great incomitance of the two charts, the diagnosis is straightforward. If the paretic muscle recovers its function, both charts
will revert to normal.
¯Çü Secondary contracture of the ipsilateral antagonist (right inferior
rectus) will manifest as an overaction which will lead to a secondary (inhibitional) palsy of the antagonist of the yoke muscle (le¯ÇÜ
superior oblique), which will show up on the chart as an underaction (Fig. 18.42B). ¯Ç£is could lead to the incorrect impression
that the le¯ÇÜ superior oblique is the primarily paretic muscle.
¯Çü With further passage of time, the two charts become progressively more concomitant, such that it may be impossible to
determine the initiating muscle weakness (Fig. 18.42C).

## Refraction and fundoscopy

Dilated fundoscopy is mandatory in the context of strabismus,
principally to exclude any underlying ocular pathology such as
macular scarring, optic disc hypoplasia or retinoblastoma as the
cause of the deviation. More commonly, strabismus is secondary to
refractive error; hypermetropia (hyperopia), astigmatism, anisometropia and myopia may all be associated.

## Cycloplegia

¯Ç£e most common refractive error to cause strabismus is hypermetropia. Accurate measurements of hypermetropia necessitate
e¯ÇÖective paralysis of the ciliary muscle (cycloplegia), in order to
neutralize the masking e¯ÇÖect of accommodation. In a young child
the risk of penalization amblyopia should be avoided by always
inducing cycloplegia in both eyes at one sitting, particularly if
atropine is used.
¯Çü Cyclopentolate (0.5% under 6 months and 1% subsequently).
One drop, repeated a¯ÇÜer 5 minutes, usually results in maximal cycloplegia within 30 minutes, with recovery of accommodation within 2ÔÇô3 hours and resolution of mydriasis within
24 hours. ¯Ç£e adequacy of cycloplegia can be determined by
comparing retinoscopy readings with the patient ¯Ç×xating for
distance and then for near (dynamic retinoscopy). Topical
anaesthesia with a well-tolerated agent such as proxymetacaine
prior to instillation of cyclopentolate is useful in preventing
ocular irritation and re¯ÇØex tearing, thus a¯ÇÖording better retention of the cyclopentolate in the conjunctival sac and e¯ÇÖective
cycloplegia.
¯Çü Atropine (0.5% under the age of 12 months and 1% subsequently) has a somewhat stronger cycloplegic e¯ÇÖect than cyclopentolate. In most cases this is clinically insigni¯Ç×cant, but may
be helpful in instances such as high hypermetropia or heavily
pigmented irides. As the onset of cycloplegia is slower, a parent
or carer may be supplied with topical atropine for instillation
at home twice daily over 1ÔÇô3 days prior to attendance (but not
on the day of examination) as either eye drops or ointment.
Drops are easier to instil, but there may be less risk of overdose
with ointment. ¯Ç£e atropine should be discontinued if there
are signs of systemic toxicity, such as ¯ÇØushing, fever or restlessness and immediate medical attention sought. ¯Ç£e visual
e¯ÇÖects may last for up to 2 weeks.

## Change of refraction with age in childhood

Because refraction changes with age, it is important to check
this in patients with strabismus at least every year and more frequently in younger children and if acuity is reduced. At birth
most babies are hypermetropic. A¯ÇÜer the age of 2 years there may
be an increase in hypermetropia and a decrease in astigmatism.

years, levelling o¯ÇÖ between the ages of 6 and 8 and subsequently
decreasing.

## When to prescribe

Most children are mildly hypermetropic (1ÔÇô3 D). ¯Ç£ere is some
evidence that fully correcting hypermetropia in a normal child
may reduce physiological emmetropization.
¯Çü Hypermetropia. In general, up to 4 D of hypermetropia
should not be corrected in a child without a squint unless they
are experiencing problems with near vision. With hypermetropia greater than this a two-thirds correction is usually given.
However, in the presence of esotropia, the full cycloplegic correction should be prescribed, even under the age of 2 years.
¯Çü Astigmatism. A cylinder of 1.50 D or more should probably
be prescribed, especially in anisometropia a¯ÇÜer the age of 18
months.
¯Çü Myopia. The necessity for correction depends on the age
of the child. Under the age of 2 years, ÔêÆ5.00 D or more of
myopia should be corrected; between the ages of 2 and 4
the amount is ÔêÆ3.00 D. Older children should have correction of even low myopia to allow clear distance vision.
Under-correction and bifocals may retard progression and
are under investigation.
¯Çü Anisometropia. A¯ÇÜer the age of 3 the full di¯ÇÖerence in refraction between the eyes should be prescribed if it is more than
1 D, with full hypermetropic correction in squint.

TIP In the presence of esotropia, the full cycloplegic
hypermetropic correction should be prescribed, even in
children under the age of 2 years.

PSEUDOSTRABISMUS

## Pseudostrabismus is the clinical impression of ocular deviation

when no squint is present.
¯Çü Epicanthic folds may simulate an esotropia (Fig. 18.47A).
¯Çü Abnormal interpupillary distance, if short may simulate an
esotropia and if wide an exotropia (Fig. 18.47B).
¯Çü Angle kappa is the angle between the visual and anatomical
(pupillary) axes (see Fig. 18.1).

centre of the posterior pole. ¯Ç£e eyes are therefore slightly
abducted to achieve bifoveal ¯Ç×xation and a light shone

the centre of the cornea in both eyes (Fig. 18.48A). ¯Ç£is is
termed a positive angle kappa.

18.48C).

HETEROPHORIA

Heterophoria may present clinically with associated visual symptoms when the fusional amplitudes are insu¯Çøcient to maintain
alignment, particularly at times of stress or poor health.
¯Çü Signs. Both esophoria and exophoria can be classi¯Ç×ed by the
distance at which the angle is greater: respectively, convergence
excess or weakness, divergence weakness or excess and mixed.
¯Çü Treatment

{ Orthoptic treatment is of most value in convergence weakness exophoria.

{ Any signi¯Ç×cant refractive error should be appropriately
corrected.

{ Symptom relief may otherwise be obtained using temporary stick-on Fresnel prisms and may be subsequently
incorporated into spectacles (maximum usually 10ÔÇô12 ╬ö,
split between the two eyes).

{ Surgery may occasionally be required for larger deviations.

VERGENCE ABNORMALITIES

Convergence insu¯Çøciency

Convergence insu¯Çøciency (CI) typically a¯ÇÖects individuals with
high near visual demand, such as students.
¯Çü Signs. Remote NPC independent of any heterophoria and
poor fusional convergence amplitudes.
¯Çü Treatment involves orthoptic exercises aimed at normalizing the near point and maximizing fusional amplitudes.
With good compliance, symptoms should be eliminated
within a few weeks but if persistent can be treated with
base-in prisms.
¯Çü Accommodative insu¯Çøciency (AI) is occasionally also present. It may be idiopathic (primary) or post-viral and typically
a¯ÇÖects school-age children. ¯Ç£e minimum reading correction
to give clear vision is prescribed but is o¯ÇÜen di¯Çøcult to discard.

Divergence insu¯Çøciency and divergence
paralysis

¯Çü Divergence insu¯Çøciency is recognized these days as being
part of a common condition called ÔÇÿsagging eye syndrome ÔÇô
SES,ÔÇÖ which occurs in elderly adults (see later in this chapter).
If symmetrical, age-related distance esotropia (ARDE) results
and if asymmetrical, unilateral hypotropia with excyclotropia
results (ÔÇÿcyclovertical strabismusÔÇÖ).

738 Esotropia

¯Çü Divergence paralysis is a rare condition typically associated
with underlying neurological disease such as intracranial
space-occupying lesions, cerebrovascular accidents and head
trauma. Presentation may be at any age and it may be di¯Çøcult
to distinguish from sixth nerve palsy. It is primarily a concomitant esodeviation with reduced or absent divergence fusional
amplitudes. However, it has none of the other clinical or radiological features of SES. It is di¯Çøcult to treat; prisms are the best
option.

Near re¯Ç£ex insu¯Çøciency

¯Çü Paresis of the near re¯ÇØex presents as dual convergence and
accommodation insu¯Çøciency. Mydriasis may be seen on
attempted near ¯Ç×xation. Treatment involves reading glasses,
base-in prisms and possibly botulinum toxin (orthoptic exercises have no e¯ÇÖect). It is di¯Çøcult to eradicate.
¯Çü Complete paralysis in which no convergence or accommodation can be initiated may be of functional origin, due to midbrain disease or a¯ÇÜer head trauma; recovery is possible.

Spasm of the near re¯Ç£ex

Spasm of the near re¯ÇØex is a functional condition a¯ÇÖecting patients
of all ages (mainly females). Diplopia, blurred vision and headaches are the presenting symptoms.
¯Çü Signs

{ Esotropia, pseudomyopia and miosis.

{ Spasm may be triggered when testing ocular movements
(Fig. 18.49A).

{ Observation of miosis is the key to the diagnosis (Fig. 18.49B).

ESOTROPIA

Esotropia (manifest convergent squint) may be concomitant or
incomitant. In a concomitant esotropia the variability of the angle
of deviation is within 5 ╬ö in di¯ÇÖerent horizontal gaze positions. In
an incomitant deviation the angle di¯ÇÖers in various positions of
gaze as a result of abnormal innervation or restriction. ¯Ç£is section
deals only with concomitant esotropia. A classi¯Ç×cation is shown in
Table 18.1. However, all squints are di¯ÇÖerent and not all ¯Ç×t neatly
into a classi¯Ç×cation. For example, a microtropia may occur with a
number of the other categories. It is more important to understand
the part played by binocular function, refractive error and accommodation in the pathophysiology of each individual squint and to
tailor treatment accordingly.

Early-onset esotropia

Up to the age of 4 months, infrequent episodes of convergence are
normal but therea¯ÇÜer ocular misalignment is abnormal. Earlyonset (essential infantile) esotropia is an idiopathic esotropia
developing within the ¯Ç×rst 6 months of life in an otherwise normal
infant with no signi¯Ç×cant refractive error and no limitation of ocular movements.

## Signs

¯Çü ¯Ç£e angle is usually fairly large (>30 ╬ö) and stable.
¯Çü Fixation in most infants is alternating in the primary position
(Fig. 18.50).
¯Çü ¯Ç£ere is cross-¯Ç×xating in side gaze, so that the child uses the
le¯ÇÜ eye in right gaze (Fig. 18.51A) and the right eye on le¯ÇÜ gaze
(Fig. 18.51B). Such cross-¯Ç×xation may give a false impression
of bilateral abduction de¯Ç×cits, as in bilateral sixth nerve palsy.
¯Çü Abduction can usually be demonstrated, either by the dollÔÇÖs
head manoeuvre or by rotating the child.

¯Çü Latent nystagmus (LN) is seen only when one eye is covered

means that the direction of the fast phase reverses according to

¯Çü Di¯ÇÜerential diagnosis includes bilateral congenital sixth

## Initial treatment

¯Çü Timing of surgery. A¯ÇÜer an initial period of variability, the
angle of esotropia becomes constant at about 4ÔÇô5 months
of age. Once any amblyopia and signi¯Ç×cant refractive error
have been appropriately corrected, the eyes should be surgically aligned within 4 months of the date of onset of constant
esotropia. Evidence is accumulating that surgical alignment
within that time frame may achieve a superior sensory outcome and improved binocular function.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe initial procedure can be either recession of both medial
rectus muscles or unilateral medial rectus muscle recession
with lateral rectus muscle resection. Angles larger than 50 ╬ö
will require recession of both medical rectus muscles as well as
resection of one lateral rectus muscle.
¯Çü Angles larger than 65 ╬ö may require recession of both medial
rectus muscles as well as resection of both lateral rectus muscles.
¯Çü Large recessions. Recessions of medial rectus muscles of
more than 6.5 mm have been abandoned as they lead to late
over-corrections.
¯Çü Signi¯ÇØcant inferior oblique overaction should be dealt with
surgically.
¯Çü An acceptable goal is alignment of the eyes to within 8 ╬ö,
associated with peripheral fusion and central suppression (Fig.
18.52). ¯Ç£is small-angle residual strabismus is o¯ÇÜen stable,
even though bifoveal fusion is not achieved.

## Subsequent treatment

¯Çü Under-correction may require further recession of the medial
recti, resection of one or both lateral recti or surgery to the
other eye, depending on the initial procedure.

Surgical treatment may be indicated for psychosocial reasons.
Options include superior rectus recession with or without posterior ¯Ç×xation sutures, resection or tuck of the inferior rectus
muscle and inferior oblique anterior transposition.
¯Çü Amblyopia subsequently develops in about 50% of cases as unilateral ¯Ç×xation preference commonly develops postoperatively.

TIP Inferior oblique overaction needs to be distinguished
from dissociated vertical deviation.

## Accommodative esotropia

Near vision involves both accommodation and convergence.

## Accommodation is the process by which the eye focuses on a near

target, by altering the curvature of the crystalline lens. Simultaneously, the eyes converge, in order to ¯Ç×xate bifoveally on the target.

## Both accommodation and convergence are quantitatively related

to the proximity of the target and have a fairly constant relationship to each other (AC/A ratio) as described previously. Abnormalities of the AC/A ratio are an important cause of certain types
of esotropia.

A

B

without glasses; (B) straight eyes for near and distance with
glasses.

## Refractive accommodative esotropia

In this type of accommodative esotropia, the AC/A ratio is normal
and esotropia is a physiological response to excessive hypermetropia, usually between +2.00 and +7.00 D. ¯Ç£e considerable degree
of accommodation required to focus clearly on even a distant target is accompanied by a proportionate amount of convergence,
which is beyond the patientÔÇÖs fusional divergence amplitude. It
cannot therefore be controlled and a manifest convergent squint
results. ¯Ç£e magnitude of the deviation varies little (usually <10 ╬ö)
between distance and near. ¯Ç£e deviation typically presents at the
age of 18 months to 3 years (range 6 months to 7 years).
¯Çü Fully accommodative esotropia is characterized by hypermetropia with esotropia when the refractive error is uncorrected
(Fig. 18.55A). ¯Ç£e deviation is eliminated and BSV is present
at all distances following optical correction of hypermetropia
(Fig. 18.55B). ¯Ç£is may occasionally be seen in a baby (Fig.
18.56), which illustrates the point that irrespective of age all
children with esotropia should undergo cycloplegic refraction.
¯Çü Partially accommodative esotropia is reduced but not
eliminated by full correction of hypermetropia (Fig. 18.57).

## Amblyopia is frequent as well as bilateral congenital superior

oblique weakness. Most cases show suppression of the squinting eye although ARC may occur, but of lower grade than in
microtropia.

Non-refractive accommodative esotropia

In this type of accommodative esotropia the AC/A ratio is high so
that a unit increase of accommodation is accompanied by a disproportionately large increase in convergence. ¯Ç£is occurs independently of refractive error, although hypermetropia frequently
coexists. Subtypes are set out below.
¯Çü Convergence excess

A

B

{ Esotropia for near, usually with suppression (Fig. 18.58B).

{ Straight eyes through bifocals (Fig. 18.58C).
¯Çü Hypoaccommodative convergence excess

{ High AC/A ratio due to decreased accommodation
(accommodation is weak, necessitating increased e¯ÇÖort,
which produces over-convergence).

{ Remote near point of accommodation.

{ Straight eyes with BSV for distance.

{ Esotropia for near, usually with suppression.

{ High AC/A ratio due to increased accommodative convergence (accommodation is normal, convergence is
increased).

¯Çü Correction of refractive error is the initial treatment.

{ Bilateral medial rectus recessions are performed in

pulley (ÔÇÿpulley ¯Ç×xationÔÇÖ) can also be used either as a ¯Ç×rst
procedure, or in the case of under-correction, following
bimedial recessions.

## Microtropia

Microtropia is a small angle (<10 ╬ö) squint. Binocular cooperation in microtropia is more substantial than in most manifest deviations and it may be considered more a description
of binocular status than a specific diagnosis. This condition
is also known as monofixation syndrome. It may be primary
or secondary, the latter representing the sequela to strabismus
surgery or other treatment (e.g. optical, such as fully accommodative esotropia controlled with glasses to a microtropia rather
than true bifoveal BSV) for a larger deviation and occasionally
to other pathology.
¯Çü Symptoms are rare unless there is an associated decompensating heterophoria.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e small manifest angle of deviation may not be readily
detectable on cover testing.
¯Çü ¯Ç£ere is a prominent association with anisometropia, commonly hypermetropia or hypermetropic astigmatism, with
amblyopia (typically mild) of the more ametropic eye.
¯Çü ¯Ç£ere is normal motor fusion as demonstrated by fusional
amplitudes.
¯Çü Anomalous retinal correspondence (ARC) is present, with
associated abnormal BSV. ¯Ç£e fovea of the ¯Ç×xating eye acquires
an anomalous common visual direction with an extrafoveal
location in the deviating eye. Under binocular conditions
extrafoveal ¯Ç×xation occurs in the deviating eye with a central
foveal suppression scotoma and peripheral fusion. However,
when the preferred eye is covered, ¯Ç×xation in the deviating eye

{ A¯ÇÜer the age of 8 years, refraction should be performed
without cycloplegia and the maximal amount of ÔÇÿplusÔÇÖ that
can be tolerated (manifest hypermetropia) prescribed.

{ For convergence excess esotropia bifocals may be prescribed to relieve accommodation (and thereby accommodative convergence), thus allowing the child to maintain
bifoveal ¯Ç×xation and ocular alignment at near (see Fig.
18.58C). ¯Ç£e minimum ÔÇÿaddÔÇÖ required to achieve this is
prescribed.

{ The most satisfactory form of bifocals is the executive
type in which the intersection crosses the lower border
of the pupil. The strength of the lower segment should
be gradually reduced and eliminated by the early teenage years.

{ Bifocals are also used in hypoaccommodative esotropia
where the AC/A ratio is not overly excessive and there is
a reasonable chance of discarding bifocal correction with
time.

{ At higher levels surgery is the better long-term option.
¯Ç£e ultimate prognosis for complete withdrawal of spectacles is related to the magnitude of the AC/A ratio and to
the degree of hypermetropia and associated astigmatism.
Spectacles may be needed only for close work.
¯Çü Surgery is aimed at restoring or improving BSV, or at improving the appearance of the squint and so the childÔÇÖs social

then returns to the fovea and the eye is aligned. Stereopsis is
usually present, but reduced.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e 4 ╬ö prism test, discussed earlier in the chapter, is useful in
assessment.

## Treatment

## Treatment involves correction of refractive error and occlusion for

amblyopia as indicated. ¯Ç£ere is evidence that aggressive treatment
sometimes leads to normalization. Most patients remain stable and
symptom-free.

## Other esotropias

Near esotropia (non-accommodative
convergence excess)

## Consecutive esotropia

weeks without further intervention.

## Sagging eye syndrome

¯Çü Introduction. ¯Ç£is is one of the commonest causes of acquired
strabismus in elderly non-myopic individuals and occurs in association with blepharoptosis and deepening of the superior sulcus.
¯Ç£e condition arises as a consequence of age-related degeneration of the ligaments which interconnect the extraocular muscles.
¯Ç£is results in bilateral inferior sagging of the lateral rectus pulley,
causing esotropia and/or cyclovertical malalignment.
¯Çü Symptoms. Initially the individual notices intermittent binocular
diplopia in the distance, which becomes permanent over a period
of several months. ¯Ç£e distance deviation is rarely more than 10╬ö
¯Çü Signs

Acute (late-onset) esotropia

¯Çü Presentation is at around 5ÔÇô6 years of age.
¯Çü Signs

{ Sudden onset of diplopia and esotropia.

{ Distance esotropia, with no near esotropia.

{ Normal ocular motility without signi¯Ç×cant refractive error.

{ Full horizontal ductions and clinically normal horizontal
saccadic eye velocities.

{ Underlying sixth nerve palsy must be excluded.
¯Çü Management

{ Limited supraduction of both eyes.

{ Because the onset of comitant esotropia in an older child
may indicate an underlying neurological disorder, it is
important to check the pupil re¯ÇØexes and exclude optic
disc changes, nystagmus and a sixth nerve palsy. Neuroradiological examination may be needed.
¯Çü Treatment is aimed at re-establishing BSV to prevent suppression using prisms, botulinum toxin or surgery.

Secondary (sensory) esotropia

Secondary esotropia is caused by a unilateral reduction in VA that
interferes with or abolishes fusion. Causes can include cataract,
optic atrophy or hypoplasia, macular scarring or retinoblastoma
(Fig. 18.59A and B). Fundus examination under mydriasis is therefore essential in all children with strabismus.

{ Blepharoptosis.

{ Deep superior sulcus.

{ If downward displacement of the lateral rectus pulley is
asymmetrical, unilateral hypotropia with excyclotropia
results (see: Cyclovertical strabismus).
¯Çü Treatment. Most patients can be treated conservatively by
using a prism incorporated in distance spectacles, as they do
not have diplopia when undertaking near tasks. If surgery is
needed, resection or plication of the lateral rectus or recession
of the medial rectus can be considered.

High myopia esotropia (ÔÇÿheavy eye syndromeÔÇÖ)

¯Çü Introduction. ¯Ç£is is a cause of acquired esotropia in adults secondary to high myopia (average: ÔêÆ18D; range ÔêÆ12D to ÔêÆ21D

744 Exotropia

and axial length 27ÔÇô35mm). ¯Ç£e condition occurs as a consequence of shi¯ÇÜing of the lateral rectus and superior rectus
muscles secondary to an elongated posterior portion of a
highly myopic eyeball. Imaging consistently demonstrates
inferior shi¯ÇÜ of the lateral rectus and nasal shi¯ÇÜ of the inferior
and superior recti causing the eye to shi¯ÇÜ supero-temporally
from the pulley array, converting the lateral rectus from abductor to infraductor.
¯Çü Symptoms. Patients present with diplopia associated with
limitation of abduction and elevation.
¯Çü Treatment. Surgery involves loop myopexy, uniting adjacent
borders of the lateral rectus and superior rectus with nonabsorbable sutures. ¯Ç£is condition responds poorly to lateral
rectus tightening or medial rectus recession alone.

## Cyclic esotropia

Cyclic esotropia is a very rare condition characterized by alternating manifest esotropia with suppression and BSV, each typically lasting 24 hours. ¯Ç£e condition may persist for months or
years and the patient may eventually develop a constant esotropia
requiring surgery.

EXOTROPIA

Constant (early-onset) exotropia

A

B

time; (B) left exotropia under conditions of visual inattention
or fatigue. (Courtesy of M Parulekar.)

¯Çü Distance excess exotropia, in which the angle of deviation is
greater for distance than near and increases further beyond 6
metres. Simulated and true forms are recognized.

¯Çü Presentation is o¯ÇÜen at birth.
¯Çü Signs

{ Normal refraction.

{ Large and constant angle.

{ DVD may be present.
¯Çü Neurological anomalies are frequently present, in contrast
with infantile esotropia.
¯Çü Treatment is mainly surgical and consists of lateral rectus
recession and medial rectus resection.
¯Çü Di¯ÇÜerential diagnosis is secondary exotropia, which may
conceal serious ocular pathology.

{ Simulated (formerly pseudo-divergence excess) is associated with a high AC/A ratio or with ÔÇÿtenacious proximal

than the near angle, but the deviation for near and distance
