## Intermittent exotropia

## Diagnosis

{ Presentation is o¯ÇÜen at around 2 years with exophoria,
which breaks down to exotropia under conditions of visual
inattention, bright light (resulting in re¯ÇØex closure of the
a¯ÇÖected eye), fatigue or ill health.

{ Signs. The eyes are straight with BSV at times (Fig.
18.60A) and manifest with suppression at other
times (Fig. 18.60B). Control of the squint varies with
the distance of fixation and other factors such as
concentration

TIP Children with intermittent exotropia tend to close an eye
when exposed to bright light.

{ True. ¯Ç£e angle for near remains signi¯Ç×cantly less than that
for distance with the above tests.
¯Çü Basic exotropia, in which control of the squint and the angle of
deviation are the same for distance and near ¯Ç×xation.
¯Çü Convergence insu¯Çøciency exotropia, in which the deviation
is greater for near ¯Ç×xation. It tends to occur in older children
and adults and may be associated with acquired myopia or
presbyopia.

## Treatment

¯Çü Spectacle correction in myopic patients may, in some cases,
control the deviation by stimulating accommodation and with
it, convergence. In some cases, over-minus prescription may
be useful.
¯Çü Part-time occlusion of the non-deviating eye may improve
control in some patients and orthoptic exercises may be helpful for near exotropia.
¯Çü Surgery. Patients with e¯ÇÖective and stable control of their
intermittent exotropia are o¯ÇÜen just observed. Surgery is
indicated if control is poor or is progressively deteriorating.

## Unilateral lateral rectus recession and medial rectus resection

are generally preferred except in true distance exotropia when

bilateral lateral rectus recessions are more usual. Similar results
are achieved with either approach. ¯Ç£e exodeviation is rarely
completely eliminated by surgery.

## Sensory exotropia

Secondary (sensory) exotropia is the result of monocular or binocular visual impairment by acquired lesions, such as cataract,
corneal scarring (Fig. 18.61A and B) or other media opacity.
Treatment consists of correction of the visual de¯Ç×cit, if possible,
followed by surgery if appropriate. A minority of patients develop
intractable diplopia due to loss of fusion, even when good VA is
restored to both eyes and the eyes are realigned.

## Consecutive exotropia

## Consecutive exotropia develops spontaneously in an amblyopic

eye, or more frequently following surgical correction of an esodeviation. Postoperative divergence occurring hours to months a¯ÇÜer
initial alignment is usually due to muscle slippage (see Fig. 18.75).
If it occurs years later, it is usually due to a stretched scar. Most
cases present in adult life with psychosocial concerns and can be
greatly helped by surgery. Careful evaluation of the risk of postoperative diplopia is required, although serious problems are uncommon. About 75% of patients are still well aligned 10 years a¯ÇÜer
surgery, although re-divergence may occur.

## Congenital cranial dysinnervation
disorders

Congenital cranial dysinnervation disorders (CCDD) or congenital innervation dysgenesis syndromes constitute a group of
disorders originally believed to be the result of congenital muscular ¯Ç×brosis, but now known to be the result of brainstem or
cranial nerve developmental disturbance, in most cases having
an identi¯Ç×able genetic basis. Systemic associations are recognized
for several.

## Duane retraction syndrome

In Duane retraction syndrome (DRS) there is failure of innervation of the lateral rectus by a hypoplastic sixth nerve nucleus, with
anomalous innervation of the lateral rectus by ¯Ç×bres from the third
nerve. ¯Ç£e condition is o¯ÇÜen bilateral. Up to half of patients have
associated systemic defects such as deafness, external ear abnormalities, speech disorder and skeletal abnormalities. Associated
mutations in several genes have been found. Approximately 10%
of cases are familial.

## Clinical features

¯Çü A face turn is typical and confers BSV with the face in the
turned position, thus avoiding amblyopia.
¯Çü Complete or partial restriction of abduction.
¯Çü Restricted adduction, usually partial.

A

B

opacity; (B) secondary to a dense cataract.

¯Çü Retraction of the globe on adduction as a result of cocontraction of the medial and lateral recti with resultant narrowing of the palpebral ¯Ç×ssure.
¯Çü An up-shoot or down-shoot in adduction may be present.

## In some cases this is produced by a tight lateral rectus muscle

slipping over or under the globe to produce an anomalous vertical movement.
¯Çü De¯ÇØciency of convergence in which the a¯ÇÖected eye remains
¯Ç×xed in the primary position while the una¯ÇÖected eye is
converging.

TIP A face turn, which is commonly found in Duane retraction
syndrome, confers binocular single vision with the face in the
turned position.

Classi¯Ç×cation (Huber)

¯Çü Type I, the most common, is characterized by (Fig. 18.62):

{ Limited or absent abduction.

{ Normal or mildly limited adduction.

{ In the primary position, straight or slight esotropia.
¯Çü Type II, the least common, is characterized by (Fig. 18.63):

{ Limited adduction.

{ Normal or mildly limited abduction.

{ In primary position, straight or slight exotropia.
¯Çü Type III is characterized by:

{ Limited adduction and abduction.

{ In the primary position, straight or slight esotropia.

{ In some cases, phenotypic variants have been allied to differing genotypes.

## Treatment

¯Ç£e majority of patients with Duane syndrome do not need any
surgical intervention.
¯Çü Most young children maintain BSV by using a CHP to compensate for their lateral rectus weakness and surgery is needed

eyes in the primary position; (B) extremely limited left abduction; (C) narrowing of the left palpebral ¯Çƒssure on adduction.

only if there is evidence of loss of binocular function. ¯Ç£is may
be indicated by failure to continue to use a CHP.
¯Çü In adults or children over the age of about 8 years surgery can
reduce a head posture that is psychosocially unacceptable or
causing neck discomfort. Surgery may also be necessary for
cosmetically unacceptable up-shoots, down-shoots or severe
globe retraction.
¯Çü Amblyopia, when present, is usually the result of anisometropia rather than strabismus.
¯Çü Depending on the clinical features, unilateral or bilateral
medial rectus and/or lateral rectus recession are the procedures of choice. In order to improve abduction, transposition of the superior and inferior rectus muscles (or just the
superior rectus muscle) to the lateral rectus muscle may be
required.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e lateral rectus of the involved side should not be resected, as
this increases retraction.

M├Âbius syndrome

M├Âbius syndrome is a rare, usually sporadic, condition, the basic
components of which are congenital non-progressive bilateral
sixth and seventh cranial nerve palsies that are believed to relate to
a developmental abnormality of the brainstem.
¯Çü Systemic features

{ Bilateral facial palsy, which is usually asymmetrical and
o¯ÇÜen incomplete, giving rise to an expressionless facial
appearance and problems with eyelid closure (Fig. 18.64A).

Congenital ¯Çƒbrosis of the extraocular
muscles

Congenital fibrosis of the extraocular muscles (CFEOM) is a
rare non-progressive, usually autosomal dominant, disorder
characterized by bilateral ptosis and restrictive external ophthalmoplegia. Numerous forms have been identified, of which
CFEOM1 is the most common and there is considerable genetic
heterogeneity. CFEOM1 is caused by mutations in the KIF21A
gene, which produces a protein used for intracellular transport
that plays a key role in some aspects of cranial nerve development and pathologically there is a hypoplastic superior division of the oculomotor nerve. Typically, vertical movements are
severely restricted with inability to elevate the eyes above the
horizontal plane.
In the primary position each eye is ¯Ç×xed below the horizontal by about 10┬░, with a corresponding compensatory chin elevation. ¯Ç£e degree of residual horizontal movement varies markedly.
Ptosis is common (Fig. 18.65).

{ ¯Ç£e ¯Ç×¯ÇÜh, eighth, tenth and twel¯ÇÜh (Fig. 18.64B) cranial
nerves may also be a¯ÇÖected.

{ Limb anomalies and mild mental handicap may be present.
¯Çü Ocular features

{ Bilateral sixth nerve palsy.

{ Horizontal gaze palsy (50%).

{ Occasionally, third and fourth nerve palsy and ptosis.

Strabismus ¯Çƒxus

## Strabismus fixus is a rare condition in which both eyes are fixed

by fibrous tightening of the medial recti (convergent strabismus
fixus ÔÇô Fig. 18.66A), or the lateral recti (divergent strabismus
fixus ÔÇô Fig. 18.66B). Congenital and acquired forms have been
described.

Other CCDD syndromes with
ophthalmic features

Marcus Gunn jaw-winking syndrome (see Ch. 2) is now believed
to be a CCDD, as are horizontal gaze palsy and progressive scoliosis, many cases of congenital ptosis, congenital fourth cranial
nerve palsy and congenital facial nerve palsy.

MONOCULAR ELEVATION DEFICIENCY

Monocular elevation de¯Ç×ciency (MED), formerly double elevator palsy, is a rare sporadic condition that in at least some cases
may be categorized as a CCDD. It is thought to manifest primarily
with a tight or contracted inferior rectus muscle or a hypoplastic
or ine¯ÇÖective superior rectus muscle. ¯Ç£ere is a profound inability
to elevate one eye across the horizontal plane, from abduction to
adduction (Fig. 18.67), with orthophoria in the primary position
in about one-third of cases. Chin elevation may be present. A baseup prism can be helpful.

BROWN SYNDROME

Brown syndrome is a condition involving mechanical restriction,
typically of the superior oblique tendon. It is usually congenital
but occasionally acquired. Recent evidence strongly suggests that
at least some congenital cases should be categorized as a CCDD.

All have limited elevation in adduction (Fig. 18.68A).

in adduction. (Courtesy of ADN Murray.)

procedure for congenital cases is lengthening of the superior

¯Çü Acquired; treatable aetiology should be addressed speci¯Ç×cally.
Depending on the cause, acquired cases may bene¯Ç×t from

TIP Congenital Brown syndrome does not usually require
treatment as long as binocular function is maintained with an
acceptable head posture.

ALPHABET PATTERNS

ÔÇÿVÔÇÖ or ÔÇÿAÔÇÖ patterns occur when the relative contributions of the superior rectus and inferior oblique to elevation, or of the inferior rectus and superior oblique to depression, are abnormal, resulting in
derangement of the balance of their horizontal vectors in up- and
downgaze. ¯Ç£ey can also be caused by anomalies in the position of
the rectus muscle pulleys. Assessment is by measuring horizontal
deviations in the primary position, upgaze and downgaze. ¯Ç£ey
can occur in both concomitant and incomitant deviations.

ÔÇÿVÔÇÖ pattern

A ÔÇÿVÔÇÖ pattern (Fig. 18.69) is said to be signi¯Ç×cant when the di¯ÇÖerence between upgaze and downgaze is ÔëÑ15 ╬ö

A

B

## Causes

¯Çü Inferior oblique overaction associated with fourth nerve
palsy.
¯Çü Superior oblique underaction with subsequent inferior oblique
overaction, seen in infantile esotropia as well as other childhood esotropias. ¯Ç£e eyes are o¯ÇÜen straight in upgaze with a
marked esodeviation in downgaze.
¯Çü Superior rectus underaction.
¯Çü Brown syndrome.
¯Çü Craniofacial anomalies featuring shallow orbits and downslanting palpebral ¯Ç×ssures.

## Treatment

## Treatment is by inferior oblique weakening or superior oblique

strengthening when oblique dysfunction is present. Without
oblique muscle dysfunction, treatment is as follows:
¯Çü ÔÇÿVÔÇÖ pattern esotropia (Fig. 18.69A) can be treated by bilateral
medial rectus recessions and downward transposition of the
tendons.

A

B

¯Çü ÔÇÿVÔÇÖ pattern exotropia (Fig. 18.69B) can be treated by bilateral lateral rectus recessions and upward transposition of the
tendons.

ÔÇÿAÔÇÖ pattern

An ÔÇÿAÔÇÖ pattern (Fig. 18.70) is considered signi¯Ç×cant if the di¯ÇÖerence
between upgaze and downgaze is ÔëÑ10 ╬ö. A particular complaint
may be di¯Çøculty with reading if the patient is binocular.

## Causes

¯Çü Primary superior oblique overaction is usually associated with
exodeviation in the primary position of gaze.
¯Çü Inferior oblique underaction/palsy with subsequent superior
oblique overaction.
¯Çü Inferior rectus underaction.

## Treatment

## Patients with oblique dysfunction are treated by superior oblique

posterior tenotomy, which will correct 20 ╬ö of ÔÇÿAÔÇÖ pattern. Total

750 Surgery

tenotomy will correct up to 50 ╬ö of ÔÇÿAÔÇÖ pattern. Treatment of cases
without oblique muscle dysfunction is as follows:
¯Çü ÔÇÿAÔÇÖ pattern esotropia (Fig. 18.70A) is treated by bilateral medial
rectus recessions and upward transposition of the tendons.
¯Çü ÔÇÿAÔÇÖ pattern exotropia (Fig. 18.70B) is treated by bilateral lateral
rectus recessions and downward transposition of the tendons.

SURGERY

¯Ç£e most common aims of surgery on the extraocular muscles are
to correct misalignment to improve appearance and, if possible,
to restore BSV. Surgery can also be used to reduce an abnormal
head posture and to expand or centralize a ¯Ç×eld of BSV. However,
the ¯Ç×rst step in the management of childhood strabismus involves
correction of any signi¯Ç×cant refractive error and/or treatment of
amblyopia. Once maximal visual potential is reached in both eyes,
any residual deviation can be treated surgically. ¯Ç£e three main
types of procedure are:
¯Çü Strengthening, to enhance the pull of a muscle.
¯Çü Weakening to decrease the e¯ÇÖective strength of action of a
muscle.
¯Çü Vector adjustment procedures that have the primary aim of
altering the direction of muscle action.

TIP Before surgical realignment is undertaken any signi¯Çƒcant
refractive error and/or underlying amblyopia must be corrected.

## Strengthening procedures

{ ¯Ç£e tendon is disinserted from the sclera and the amount
of recession is measured and marked on the sclera with
calipers (Fig. 18.73C).

{ ¯Ç£e detached end of the muscle is sutured to the sclera at
the measured distance behind its original insertion. Alternatively, a hang-back technique can be used (Fig. 18.73D).
¯Çü Inferior oblique recession

{ ¯Ç£e muscle belly is exposed through an inferotemporal
fornix incision.

{ A squint hook is passed behind the posterior border of the
muscle, which must be clearly visualized. Care is taken to
pick up the muscle without disrupting the Tenon capsule
and fat posterior to it.

{ An absorbable suture is passed through the anterior border
of the muscle at its insertion and tied.

{ ¯Ç£e muscle is disinserted, and the cut end sutured to the
sclera 3 mm posterior and temporal to the temporal edge
of the inferior rectus insertion.

¯Çü Resection shortens a muscle to enhance its e¯ÇÖective pull. It
is suitable only for a rectus muscle and involves the following
steps:

{ ¯Ç£e muscle is exposed and two absorbable sutures inserted
at a measured distance behind its insertion (Fig. 18.71AÔÇôE).

## Disinsertion

Disinsertion (or myectomy) involves detaching a muscle from
its insertion without reattachment. It is most commonly used to
weaken an overacting inferior oblique muscle, when the technique
is the same as for a recession except that the muscle is not sutured.
Very occasionally, disinsertion is performed on a severely contracted rectus muscle.

Posterior ¯Ç×xation suture

¯Ç£e principle of this (Faden) procedure is to suture the muscle belly
to the sclera posteriorly so as to decrease the pull of the muscle in
its ¯Ç×eld of action without a¯ÇÖecting the eye in the primary position.
¯Ç£e Faden procedure may be used on the medial rectus to reduce
convergence in a convergence excess esotropia and on the superior
rectus to treat DVD. When treating DVD, the superior rectus muscle
may also be recessed. ¯Ç£e belly of the muscle is then anchored to the
sclera with a non-absorbable suture about 12 mm behind its insertion. Alternatively, instead of a posterior ¯Ç×xation suture the muscle
belly can be sutured posteriorly to its pulley (ÔÇÿpulley ¯Ç×xationÔÇÖ), which
has the same e¯ÇÖect and avoids placement of the suture into the sclera.

## Transposition

## Transposition refers to the relocation of one or more extraocular

muscles to substitute for the action of an absent or severely de¯Ç×cient muscle. ¯Ç£e most common indication is severe lateral rectus
weakness due to acquired sixth cranial nerve palsy (see Fig. 19.79).
Other applications include CCDD (e.g. Duane syndrome), alphabet patterns and monocular elevation de¯Ç×cit. A variety of techniques involving recti and oblique muscles have been described.

## Adjustable sutures

## Indications

¯Ç£e results of strabismus surgery can be improved by the use
of adjustable suture techniques on the rectus muscles. While

{ ¯Ç£e muscle anterior to the sutures is excised (Fig. 18.71FÔÇô
H) and the cut end reattached to the original insertion.
¯Çü Plication (tucking) has a similar e¯ÇÖect to resection, but has
the advantage that it is less traumatic, does not sacri¯Ç×ce the
anterior ciliary vessels and can be easily reversed if needed. It
can be used to enhance the action of the rectus muscles and is
today frequently preferred to a resection. It can also be used
to tighten a lax superior oblique muscle in congenital fourth
nerve palsy (Fig. 18.72).
¯Çü Advancement of the muscle nearer to the limbus can be
used to enhance the action of a previously recessed rectus
muscle.

## Weakening procedures

## Recession

Recession slackens a muscle by moving it away from its insertion.
¯Çü Rectus muscle recession

{ ¯Ç£e muscle is exposed and two absorbable sutures are tied
through the outer quarters of the tendon (Fig. 18.73A and B).

and stretched with a Chavasse squint hook; (B) a single double-armed suture is placed with

many ophthalmologists use this technique in all adults, adjustable sutures are particularly indicated when a precise outcome
is essential and when the results with more conventional procedures are likely to be unpredictable. Examples include: (a)
restrictive strabismus (thyroid myopathy, blow-out fracture of
the ¯ÇØoor of the orbit), (b) previous trauma or surgery, (c) slipped
or lost muscles, (d) incomitant deviations (Duane syndrome,
M├Âbius syndrome, myasthenia gravis or paralytic strabismus),
(e) combined horizontal, vertical and torsional strabismus. ¯Ç£e
surgery can be done on any patient who can cooperate. Some
older children will tolerate postoperative adjustment under topical anaesthesia. If not, an option is to keep the child nil by mouth
in the recovery room and re-anaesthetize the child if necessary to
undertake the adjustment.

## Operative procedure

¯Çü ¯Ç£e muscle is exposed, sutures inserted and the tendon disinserted from the sclera as for a rectus muscle recession.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e two ends of the suture are passed, side by side, through the
stump of the insertion.

¯Çü A second suture is knotted and tied tightly around the muscle
suture anterior to its emergence from the stump: noose knot
(Fig. 18.74A).
¯Çü One end of the suture is cut short and the two ends tied
together to form a loop (Fig. 18.74B).
¯Çü ¯Ç£e conjunctiva is le¯ÇÜ open.

## Postoperative adjustment

¯Ç£is is performed under topical anaesthesia, usually a few hours
a¯ÇÜer surgery when the patient is fully awake.
¯Çü ¯Ç£e accuracy of alignment is assessed.
¯Çü If ocular alignment is satisfactory, the muscle suture is tied o¯ÇÖ
and its long ends cut short.
¯Çü If more recession is required, the noose knot is pulled anteriorly along the muscle suture, thereby providing additional
slack to the recessed muscle and enabling it to move posteriorly when the patient is asked to look towards the ¯Ç×eld of
action of the recessed muscle (Fig. 18.74C).
¯Çü If less recession is required, the muscle suture is pulled anteriorly and the knot tightened against the muscle stump (Fig.
18.74D).

¯Çü Operative

{ Lost or ÔÇÿslippedÔÇÖ muscle (especially the medial rectus

{ Globe perforation by a misplaced suture (especially in high

{ Opening of the posterior Tenon capsule leading to ¯Ç×brosis
involving fat and adjacent extraocular muscles (especially
when operating on the inferior oblique muscle).
¯Çü Postoperative

{ Over-correction and under-correction are common and

{ Anterior segment ischaemia is more likely to occur in

develop, is usually maximal at 1ÔÇô2 weeks following injection and
has generally worn o¯ÇÖ by 3 months. An extraocular muscle lengthens while it is paralyzed by botulinum and its antagonist contracts.
¯Ç£ese changes may produce long-term improvements in the alignment of the eyes. Results are best when there is fusion to stabilize
the alignment. Side e¯ÇÖects are uncommon, although about 16% of
adults and 25% of children may develop some degree of temporary
ptosis. ¯Ç£e following are the main indications:
¯Çü Postoperative small-angle residual strabismus (2ÔÇô8 weeks
following surgery). For example, a¯ÇÜer a recession for esotropia
that has resulted in a small under-correction, an injection can
be given into the medial rectus muscle. A¯ÇÜer the injection the
eye becomes divergent, with no adduction for 3 months. During this period that lateral rectus muscle shortens enough to
reduce the residual esotropia once the e¯ÇÖect of the toxin has
worn o¯ÇÖ.
¯Çü To correct infantile esotropia by injecting both medial recti,
so that the eyes become divergent. ¯Ç£e lateral rectus muscles
shorten and the angle of esotropia reduces and may even be
corrected. If not, the procedure can be repeated.
¯Çü Active thyroid ophthalmopathy, in¯ÇØamed or prephthisical
eye when surgery is inappropriate.
¯Çü To determine the risk of postoperative diplopia. For
example, in an adult with a consecutive left divergent squint
and left suppression, straightening the eyes may make suppression less effective resulting in diplopia. If postoperative
diplopia testing by correcting the angle with prisms is negative, then the risk of double vision after surgery is very low.
If testing is positive, then the left lateral rectus muscle can
be injected with toxin so that the eyes will either straighten
or converge and the risk of diplopia can be assessed over
several days while the eyes are straight. If diplopia does
occur, the patient is able to judge whether it is troublesome.
¯Çü To assess the potential for BSV in a patient with a constant
manifest squint by straightening the eyes temporarily. ¯Ç£e

BOTULINUM TOXIN
CHEMODENERVATION

## Temporary paralysis of an extraocular muscle can be induced by

an injection of botulinum toxin (BT) under topical anaesthesia and

A B

left sixth nerve palsy (see text).

deviation can then be corrected surgically if appropriate. A
small proportion of patients maintain BSV long-term a¯ÇÜer the
e¯ÇÖects of the toxin have worn o¯ÇÖ.
¯Çü In lateral rectus palsy botulinum toxin can be injected into
the ipsilateral medial rectus to give symptomatic relief during
recovery and to see whether there is any lateral rectus action
when there is medial rectus contracture (Fig. 18.76A). ¯Ç£e
temporary paralysis of the muscle causes relaxation so that the
horizontal forces on the globe are more balanced, thus allowing assessment of lateral rectus function (Fig. 18.76B). Medial
rectus recession or muscle transposition surgery may subsequently be required to achieve ocular alignment (Fig. 18.77AÔÇô
C). A similar approach can be used for fourth nerve palsy, with
injection into the ipsilateral inferior oblique or contralateral
inferior rectus.

NEUROIMAGING

## Computed tomography

## Physics

Computed tomography (CT) uses X-ray beams to obtain tissue
density values from which detailed cross-sectional images are
formed by a computer. Tissue density is represented by a grey scale,
white being maximum density (e.g. bone) and black being minimum density (e.g. air). Advanced CT scanners are able to acquire
thinner slices leading to improved spatial resolution, together with
faster examination times, without a proportionate increase in radiation dose. Images are acquired in an axial form and can be viewed
in any plane using computer reconstruction. ¯Çƒis multiplanar
information can be an advantage over magnetic resonance (MR)
with regard to anatomical detail. CT is widely available, easy to
perform, relatively inexpensive and quick, but unlike MR exposes
the patient to ionizing radiation.

## Contrast enhancement

Iodinated contrast material improves sensitivity and speci¯Ç×city but
is contraindicated in patients allergic to iodine and in those with
renal failure. Contrast is not indicated in acute haemorrhage, bony
injury or localization of foreign bodies because it may mask visualization of these high-density structures.

## Indications

¯Çü Orbital trauma, for the detection of bony lesions such as fractures (blood, herniation of extraocular muscles into the maxillary sinus (Fig. 19.1A) and surgical emphysema).
¯Çü Evaluation of the extraocular muscles in thyroid eye disease
(Fig. 19.1B). CT and MRI (see below) have complementary
advantages in the assessment of orbital disease.
¯Çü Bony involvement of orbital tumours is better assessed using
CT than MR (Fig. 19.1C).
¯Çü Orbital cellulitis for assessment of intraorbital extension and
subperiosteal abscess formation.

A B

C D

orbital ¯ÇØoor and soft tissue prolapse (arrow) into the maxillary sinus; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× axial image showing
bilateral enlargement of extraocular muscles; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× coronal image showing loss of the left superior bony orbit (arrow) secondary to a tumour; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× axial image showing an acute parenchymal
haematoma in the right temporal lobe. (Courtesy of N Sibtain ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. D.)

¯Çü Detection of intraorbital calci¯Çƒcation as in meningioma and
retinoblastoma.
¯Çü Detection of acute cerebral (Fig. 19.1D) or subarachnoid
haemorrhage, which is harder to visualize on MR within the
¯Ç×rst few hours of onset.
¯Çü When MR is contraindicated (e.g. ferrous foreign body).

## Magnetic resonance imaging

## Physics

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) depends on the rearrangement of positively charged hydrogen nuclei (protons) when a tissue is exposed to a short electromagnetic pulse. When the pulse
subsides, the nuclei return to their normal position, re-radiating
some of the absorbed energy. Sensitive receivers pick up this
electromagnetic echo. Unlike CT, it does not subject the patient

to ionizing radiation. ¯Çƒe signals are analyzed and displayed as a
cross-sectional image that may be axial, coronal or sagittal.

## Basic sequences

## Weighting refers to two methods of measuring the relaxation times

of the excited protons a¯ÇØer the magnetic ¯Ç×eld has been switched
o¯Ç£. Various body tissues have di¯Ç£erent relaxation times so that a
given tissue may be T1- or T2-weighted (i.e. best visualized on that
particular type of image). In practice, both types of scans are usually performed. It is easy to tell the di¯Ç£erence between CT and MR
images because bone appears white on CT but is not clearly demonstrated on MR.
¯Çü T1-weighted images are generally optimal for viewing normal
anatomy. Hypointense (dark) structures include vitreous (Fig.
19.2A) and cerebrospinal ¯Çøuid (CSF) (Fig. 19.2B). Hyperintense (bright) structures include fat, blood, contrast agents and
melanin.

A B

C D

hypointense (dark) and orbital fat is hyperintense (bright); ¯ÇØB¯Ç× T1-weighted midline sagittal
image through the brain in which the CSF is hypointense in the lateral and fourth ventricles
(arrows); ¯ÇØC¯Ç× T2-weighted axial image through the globe in which vitreous and orbital fat are
hyperintense; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× T2-weighted axial image through the brain in which the CSF in the lateral
ventricles is hyperintense

760 Neuroimaging

¯Çü T2-weighted images, in which water is shown as hyperintense,
are useful for viewing pathological changes because oedematous
tissue (e.g. in¯Çøammation) will display a brighter signal than
normal surrounding tissue. Vitreous (Fig. 19.2C) and CSF (Fig.
19.2D) are hyperintense as they have high water content. Blood
vessels appear black on T2 imaging unless they are occluded.

## Image enhancement

¯Çü Gadolinium contrast acquires magnetic moment when
placed in an electromagnetic ¯Ç×eld. Administered intravenously, it remains intravascular unless there is a breakdown of
the bloodÔÇôbrain barrier. It is only visualized on T1-weighted
images and enhancing lesions such as tumours and areas of
in¯Çøammation will appear bright. Ideally MR is performed both
before (Fig. 19.3A) and a¯ÇØer (Fig. 19.3B) administration of
gadolinium for most clinical indications. Special head or surface coils can also be used to improve spatial de¯Ç×nition of the
image. Adverse e¯Ç£ects with gadolinium are uncommon and
usually relatively innocuous.
¯Çü Fat-suppression techniques are useful for imaging the orbit
because the bright signal of orbital fat on conventional T1weighted imaging frequently obscures other orbital contents.
Fat suppression eliminates this bright signal and better delineates normal structures (optic nerve and extraocular muscles)
as well as tumours, in¯Çøammatory lesions and vascular malformations. ¯Çƒe two types of fat-suppression sequence used for
orbital imaging are:

{ T1 fat saturation used with gadolinium allows areas suspicious using other techniques to be enhanced, e.g. suppressing orbital fat signal to visualize optic nerve sheath or
perineural lesions (Fig. 19.3C).

¯Çü It cannot be used in patients with magnetic foreign objects
(e.g. cardiac pacemakers, intraocular foreign bodies and ferromagnetic aneurysm clips).
¯Çü Substantial patient cooperation is required, including remaining motionless. It is poorly tolerated by claustrophobic patients
as it involves lying in an enclosed space for many minutes.

Neuro-ophthalmic indications

MRI is the technique of choice for lesions of the intracranial visual
pathways.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e optic nerve is best visualized on coronal STIR images
in conjunction with coronal and axial T1 fat saturation postgadolinium images. Axial T1 images are useful for displaying
normal anatomy. MRI can detect lesions of the intraorbital
part of the optic nerve (e.g. neuritis, glioma) as well as intracranial extension of optic nerve tumours.
¯Çü Optic nerve sheath lesions (e.g. meningioma) are of similar
signal intensity to the nerve on T1- and T2-weighted images
but enhance avidly with gadolinium.
¯Çü Sellar masses (e.g. pituitary tumours) are best visualized by
T1-weighted contrast-enhanced studies. Coronal images optimally demonstrate the contents of the sella turcica as well as
the suprasellar and parasellar regions and are usually supplemented by sagittal images.
¯Çü Cavernous sinus pathology is best demonstrated on coronal
images; contrast may be required.
¯Çü Intracranial lesions of the visual pathways (e.g. in¯Çøammatory, demyelinating, neoplastic and vascular). MRI allows
further characterization of these lesions as well as better anatomical localization.

## Angiography

## Magnetic resonance angiography

Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) is a non-invasive method
of imaging the intra- and extracranial carotid and vertebrobasilar circulations (Fig. 19.4A) to demonstrate abnormalities such as
stenosis, dissection, occlusion, arteriovenous malformations and
aneurysms. ¯Çƒe motion sensitivity of MR is utilized to visualize
blood ¯Çøow within vessels and does not require contrast. However,
because of the reliance on active ¯Çøow, thrombosed aneurysms may
be missed and turbulent ¯Çøow may lead to di¯ÇÜculties in interpretation. ¯Çƒe technique has limited facility in the detection of very
small aneurysms. MRA adds about 10 minutes to the standard
MRI acquisition time.

## Magnetic resonance venography

Over recent years, increasing attention has been paid to intracranial venous system pathology, particularly dural sinus occlusion
and stenosis. Historically, the venous phase of conventional digital subtraction angiography (DSA) has been used for intracranial
venous assessment and still o¯Ç£ers high sensitivity and speci¯Ç×city.
However, both CT and MRI can be used to similar purpose and are
relatively non-invasive and low-risk compared to DSA. Technological advances have conferred greatly improved accuracy. Whilst

{ STIR (short T1 inversion recovery) is the optimal sequence
for detecting intrinsic lesions of the intraorbital optic
nerve (e.g. glioma- Fig. 19.3D; optic neuritis ÔÇô Fig. 19.3E).
STIR images have very low signal from fat but still have
high signal from water.
¯Çü FLAIR (¯Çøuid-attenuated inversion recovery) sequences suppress the bright CSF on T2-weighted images to allow better
visualization of adjacent pathological tissue such as periventricular plaques of demyelination (Fig. 19.3F).
¯Çü DWI/ADC (di¯Ç£usion-weighted imaging and apparent di¯Ç£usion coe¯ÇÜcient). DWI measures aberrance in expected Brownian motion of free water, and is useful in acute ischaemic
stroke to identify abnormalities at a very early stage ÔÇô within
minutes ÔÇô and in distinguishing ischaemic damage reversible
with treatment from irreversible damage such that intervention (e.g. with a thrombolytic agent) is unlikely to be of bene¯Ç×t.
¯Çü FIESTA and CISS (fast imaging employing steady-state acquisition and constructive interference in steady-state) are newer
high-resolution sequences. In neuro-ophthalmology, a particular application may be the investigation of cranial nerve palsy.

## Limitations

¯Çü Bone appears black and is not directly imaged.
¯Çü Recent haemorrhage is not detected, so MRI is inappropriate
in patients with suspected acute intracranial bleeding.

CTV (see below) is generally faster and o¯Ç£ers high spatial resolution, it entails a signi¯Ç×cant radiation dose and always requires
contrast for image acquisition. Magnetic resonance venography
(MRV) can be used with contrast or using non-contrast enhanced
techniques, among other indications making it suitable for patients
to whom contrast cannot be given. Substantial advances in MRV
pulse sequences now allow excellent visualization of the venous
system (Fig. 19.4B) and increasing reliance on MRV seems to
be evident. However, local resource availability, experience and
expertise are critical in determining the choice of technique.

## Computed tomographic angiography

Computed tomographic angiography (CTA) has been emerging
as the method of choice in the investigation of intracranial aneurysms (Fig. 19.4C). It enables acquisition of extremely thin slice
images of the brain following intravenous contrast. Images of the
vessels can be reconstructed in three dimensions and viewed from
any direction, aiding the approach to treatment. ¯Çƒe investigation

is safe and quick and does not carry the 1% risk of stroke associated with conventional catheter angiography.

## Computed tomographic venography

Computed tomographic venography (CTV) is a rapid highresolution technique in which patient motion artefact is of lesser
importance than with MRV. CTV is believed to be at least as sensitive as MRV in the diagnosis of cerebral venous thrombosis.
Both techniques may provide complementary ¯Ç×ndings in di¯ÇÜcult
diagnostic situations. However, contrast is always required, and a
signi¯Ç×cant radiation dose is delivered. Visualization of skull base
structures is limited by bony artefact relative to MRV. ¯Çƒe technique is similar to that of CTA.

## Conventional catheter angiography

Conventional intra-arterial catheter angiography is usually performed under local anaesthetic. A catheter is passed via the femoral artery into the internal carotid and vertebral arteries in the neck

may now be reserved for cases where CTA is equivocal or negative.

OPTIC NERVE

## Anatomy

Circulation (Fig. 19.5A)

¯Çü ¯Ç×e ophthalmic artery arises from the internal carotid artery
and runs inferior to the optic nerve in the optic canal.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e central retinal artery is the continuation of the ophthalmic artery a¯ÇØer it passes into the optic nerve 8ÔÇô12mm behind
the globe. ¯Çƒis supplies blood to the inner retina.

¯Çü Long posterior ciliary arteries. ¯Çƒe blood supply to the
optic nerve head arises from the circle of ZinnÔÇôHaller, which
receives blood from 4ÔÇô5 long posterior ciliary arteries, choroidal vessels and the pial arterial network.

General structure (Fig. 19.5B)

¯Çü A¯ÇØerent ¯Çƒbres. ¯Çƒe optic nerve carries approximately 1.2 million a¯Ç£erent nerve ¯Ç×bres, each of which originates in a retinal
ganglion cell. Most of these ¯Ç×bres synapse in the lateral geniculate body, although some reach other centres, notably the pretectal nuclei in the midbrain. Nearly one-third of the ¯Ç×bres
subserve the central 5┬░ of the visual ¯Ç×eld. Within the optic
nerve itself the nerve ¯Ç×bres are divided into about 600 bundles
by ¯Ç×brous septae derived from the pia mater.
¯Çü Surrounding layers (Fig. 19.5C)

{ ¯Çƒe outer sheath comprises the arachnoid mater and the
tougher dura mater, which is continuous with the sclera;
optic nerve fenestration involves incision of this outer

{ ¯Çƒe innermost layer is the delicate and vascular pia mater.

764 Optic Nerve

sheath. ¯Çƒe subarachnoid space is continuous with the
cerebral subarachnoid space and contains CSF.

## Anatomical subdivisions

¯Çƒe optic nerve is approximately 50 mm long from globe to chiasm. It can be subdivided into four segments (see Fig. 19.5A):
¯Çü Intraocular segment (optic nerve head) is the shortest, being
1 mm deep and approximately 1.5 mm in vertical diameter.
¯Çƒe ophthalmoscopically visible portion is called the optic disc
(Fig. 19.5D).
¯Çü Intraorbital segment is 25ÔÇô30 mm long and extends from the
globe to the optic foramen at the orbital apex. Its diameter is 3ÔÇô4
mm because of the addition of the myelin sheaths to the nerve
¯Ç×bres. At the orbital apex the nerve is surrounded by the tough
¯Ç×brous annulus of Zinn, from which originate the four rectus
muscles.
¯Çü Intracanalicular segment traverses the optic canal and measures about 6 mm. Unlike the intraorbital portion, it is ¯Ç×xed
to the canal, since the dura mater fuses with the periosteum.
¯Çü Intracranial segment joins the chiasm and varies in length
from 5 to 16 mm (average 10 mm). Long intracranial segments
are particularly vulnerable to damage by adjacent lesions such
as pituitary adenomas and aneurysms.

## Visual evoked potential

¯Çü Principle (Fig. 19.6). Visual (visually) evoked potential (VEP)
tests record electrical activity of the visual cortex created by
retinal stimulation. ¯Çƒe most common indications in ophthalmology are the monitoring of visual function in babies
and the investigation of optic neuropathy, particularly when
associated with demyelination. It can also be used to monitor
macular pathway function and to investigate functional (nonphysiological) visual loss.
¯Çü Technique. ¯Çƒe stimulus is either a ¯Çøash of light (¯Çøash VEP)
or a black-and-white checkerboard pattern on a screen that

periodically reverses polarity (pattern VEP). Several tests are
performed and the average potential is calculated.
¯Çü Interpretation. Latency (delay) and amplitude are assessed.
In optic neuropathy both parameters are a¯Ç£ected, with prolongation of latency and a decrease in amplitude. ¯Çƒreshold
VEP (by using di¯Ç£erent-sized check stimuli) can detect early
or subclinical dysfunction as smaller check size responses may
become abnormal earlier than responses to larger stimuli.

## Signs of optic nerve dysfunction

¯Çü Reduced visual acuity for distance and near is common, but
is non-speci¯Ç×c. Acuity may be preserved in some conditions.
¯Çü Relative a¯ÇØerent pupillary defect (see below).
¯Çü Dyschromatopsia is impairment of colour vision, which in the
context of optic nerve disease mainly a¯Ç£ects red and green. A
simple way of detecting a monocular colour vision defect is to
ask the patient to compare the colour of a red object using each
eye in turn or to undertake an Ishihara test.
¯Çü Diminished light brightness sensitivity, o¯ÇØen persisting a¯ÇØer
visual acuity returns to normal, for instance following the
acute stage of optic neuritis.
¯Çü Diminished contrast sensitivity (see Ch. 1).
¯Çü Visual ¯Çƒeld defects, which vary with the underlying pathology, include di¯Ç£use depression of the central visual ¯Ç×eld, central scotomas, centrocaecal scotomas, nerve ¯Ç×bre bundle and
altitudinal (Table 19.1).

TIP Loss of red-green colour vision is a common
manifestation of optic nerve disease and can be rapidly tested
with Ishihara plates.

cause

¯Çü In¯Ç£ammatory. Optic neuritis, including demyelinating, parainfectious, infectious and non-infectious and neuroretinitis.

## Optic atrophy

## Introduction

Optic atrophy refers to the late-stage changes that take place in
the optic nerve resulting from axonal degeneration in the pathway
between the retina and the lateral geniculate body, manifesting

with disturbance in visual function and in the appearance of the
optic nerve head.

## Primary optic atrophy

## Primary optic atrophy occurs without antecedent swelling of the

optic nerve head. It may be caused by lesions a¯Ç£ecting the visual
pathways at any point from the retrolaminar portion of the optic
nerve to the lateral geniculate body. Lesions anterior to the optic
chiasm result in unilateral optic atrophy, whereas those involving
the chiasm and optic tract will cause bilateral changes.
¯Çü Signs

{ Flat white disc with clearly delineated margins (Fig. 19.7A).

{ Reduction in the number of small blood vessels on the disc
surface.

{ Attenuation of peripapillary blood vessels and thinning of
the retinal nerve ¯Ç×bre layer (RNFL).

{ ¯Çƒe atrophy may be di¯Ç£use or sectoral depending on the
cause and level of the lesion. Temporal pallor of the optic

A B

C D

vessels; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× primary due to nutritional neuropathy ÔÇô note predominantly temporal pallor;
¯ÇØC¯Ç× secondary due to chronic papilloedema ÔÇô note Paton lines (arrows); ¯ÇØD¯Ç× consecutive due
to vasculitis.

766 Optic Nerve

nerve head may indicate atrophy of ¯Ç×bres of the papillomacular bundle and is classically seen following demyelinating optic neuritis. Band atrophy is a similar phenomenon
caused by involvement of the ¯Ç×bres entering the optic disc
nasally and temporally. It occurs in lesions of the optic chiasm or tract and gives nasal as well as temporal pallor.
¯Çü Important causes

{ Optic neuritis.

{ Compression by tumours and aneurysms.

{ Hereditary optic neuropathies.

{ Toxic and nutritional optic neuropathy, which may give
temporal pallor, particularly in early/milder cases when
the papillomacular ¯Ç×bres are preferentially a¯Ç£ected (Fig.
19.7B).

{ Trauma.

## Secondary optic atrophy

## Secondary optic atrophy is preceded by longstanding swelling of

the optic nerve head.
¯Çü Signs vary according to the cause and its course.

{ Slightly or moderately raised white or greyish disc with
poorly delineated margins due to gliosis (Fig. 19.7C).

{ Obscuration of the lamina cribrosa.

{ Reduction in the number of small blood vessels on the disc
surface.

posterior vitreous. Papillitis is the most common type of optic
neuritis in children, but can also a¯Ç£ect adults.
¯Çü Neuroretinitis is characterized by papillitis in association
with in¯Çøammation of the RNFL and a macular star ¯Ç×gure (see
below). It is the least common type and is only rarely a manifestation of demyelination.

## According to aetiology

¯Çü Demyelinating. ¯Çƒis is by far the most common cause.
¯Çü Parainfectious, following a viral infection or immunization.
¯Çü Infectious. ¯Çƒis may be sinus-related, or associated with conditions such as cat-scratch disease, syphilis, Lyme disease,
cryptococcal meningitis and herpes zoster.
¯Çü Non-infectious causes include sarcoidosis and systemic autoimmune diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus, polyarteritis nodosa and other vasculitides.

## Demyelinating optic neuritis

## Overview

Demyelination is a pathological process in which normally myelinated nerve ¯Ç×bres lose their insulating myelin layer. ¯Çƒe myelin is
phagocytosed by microglia and macrophages, subsequent to which
astrocytes lay down ¯Ç×brous tissue in plaques. Demyelinating disease disrupts nervous conduction within the white matter tracts of
the brain, brainstem and spinal cord.

## Demyelinating conditions that may involve the visual system

include the following:
¯Çü Isolated optic neuritis with no clinical evidence of generalized demyelination, although in a high proportion of cases this
subsequently develops.
¯Çü Multiple sclerosis (MS), by far the most common demyelinating disease (see below).
¯Çü Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), previously called Devic disease, is a rare autoimmune disease where
antibodies form against aquaporin 4 protein in the cell membranes of astrocytes.

{ Peripapillary circumferential retinochoroidal folds, especially temporal to the disc (Paton lines ÔÇô see Fig. 19.7C),
sheathing of arterioles and venous tortuosity may be
present.
¯Çü Causes include chronic papilloedema, anterior ischaemic
optic neuropathy and papillitis. Intraocular inflammatory
causes of marked disc swelling are sometimes considered
to cause secondary rather than consecutive atrophy (see
below).

## Consecutive optic atrophy

## Consecutive optic atrophy is caused by disease of the inner retina

or its blood supply. ¯Çƒe cause is usually obvious on fundus examination, e.g. extensive retinal photocoagulation, retinitis pigmentosa or prior central retinal artery occlusion. ¯Çƒe disc appears
waxy, with reasonably preserved architecture (Fig. 19.7D).

## Glaucomatous optic atrophy

Classi¯Ç£cation of optic neuritis

## According to ophthalmoscopic appearance

¯Çü Retrobulbar neuritis, in which the optic disc appears normal,
at least initially, because the optic nerve head is not involved. It
is the most common type in adults and is frequently associated
with multiple sclerosis (MS).
¯Çü Papillitis is characterized by hyperaemia and oedema of the

¯Çü Schilder disease, a very rare relentlessly progressive generalized disease with an onset prior to the age of 10 years and death
within 1ÔÇô2 years. Bilateral optic neuritis without subsequent

## Multiple sclerosis

MS is an idiopathic demyelinating disease involving central nervous system white matter. Infection with EpsteinÔÇôBarr virus (EBV)
signi¯Ç×cantly increases the chances of developing multiple sclerosis.
However, EBV in itself is not su¯ÇÜcient to trigger MS and other
unknown factors play a role. It is more common in women than
men.
¯Çü Presentation is typically in the thirdÔÇôfourth decades, generally with relapsing/remitting demyelination that may switch
later to an unremitting pattern and less commonly with progressive disease from the outset.

the corpus callosum; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× MRI showing transverse myelitis in NMOSD (Devic disease) (arrows).

## Clinical features of demyelinating optic neuritis

{ Usual age range 20ÔÇô50 years (mean around 30).

## Association between optic neuritis and multiple
sclerosis

¯Çü ¯Çƒe overall 15-year risk of developing MS following an acute
episode of optic neuritis is about 50%. With no lesions on MRI
the risk is 25%, but the risk is over 70% in patients with one or
more lesions on MRI. ¯Çƒe presence of MRI lesions is therefore
a very strong predictive factor.
¯Çü MS is not considered a hereditary disease, but the probability
of developing the disease is higher in closely related relatives.
¯Çƒere is an association between MS and HLA DR15 and DQ6.
¯Çü A substantially lower risk of developing MS when there are no
MRI lesions is conferred by the following factors, providing
critical support in deciding whether to commence immunomodulatory MS-prophylactic treatment following an optic
neuritis episode:

{ Di¯Ç£use depression of sensitivity in the entire central 30┬░ is
the most common.

{ Altitudinal/arcuate defects and focal central/centrocaecal
scotomas are also frequent.

{ Focal defects are frequently accompanied by an element of
superimposed generalized depression.
¯Çü Course. Vision worsens over several days to 3 weeks and then
begins to improve. Most recover within 4 weeks. Initial recovery is fairly rapid and then slower over 6ÔÇô12 months.

{ Male gender.

{ Absence of a viral syndrome preceding the optic neuritis.

{ Negative test for EpsteinÔÇôBarr virus.

{ Optic disc swelling, disc/peripapillary haemorrhages or
macular exudates.

A B

C D

altitudinal.

{ Vision reduced to no light perception.

{ Absence of periocular pain.
¯Çü Optic neuritis is the presenting feature of MS in up to 30%.
¯Çü Optic neuritis occurs at some point in 50% of patients with
established MS.

TIP In optic neuritis secondary to multiple sclerosis the vision
usually improves without treatment over a period of several
months.

## Prognosis

More than 90% of patients recover VA to 6/9 or better. Subtle
parameters of visual function, such as colour vision, may remain
abnormal. A mild relative a¯Ç£erent pupillary defect may persist.

## Temporal optic disc pallor or more marked optic atrophy may

ensue. About 10% develop chronic optic neuritis with slowly progressive or stepwise visual loss.

## Management following demyelinating optic
neuritis

Nearly 80ÔÇô85% of patients with MS experience a relapsing course.
If le¯ÇØ untreated most will develop neurological disability over time.

## Disease modifying therapies early in the course of active relapsing

MS can prevent relapses and reduce neurological disability. Some
of these medications are associated with potentially serious side
e¯Ç£ects and careful monitoring is required, preferably in a specialist
clinic.

## Indications for steroid treatment in optic
neuritis

¯Çü Starting treatment. Optic nerve axon preservation and ¯Ç×nal
visual acuity may be improved by starting intravenous steroid
therapy within 48 hours of the onset of symptoms.
¯Çü Speed of recovery. When the VA is worse than 6/12 within the
¯Ç×rst week of onset, treatment may speed up recovery by 2ÔÇô3
weeks and delay the onset of clinical MS over the short term.
¯Çƒis may be relevant in the patients with poor vision in the
fellow eye, but the limited bene¯Ç×t must be balanced against the
risks of high-dose steroids.
¯Çü Steroid regimen. Intravenous methylprednisolone sodium
succinate 1 g daily (250mg six-hourly) for 3 days, followed by
oral prednisolone (1 mg/kg daily) for 11 days, subsequently
tapered over 3 days.
¯Çü Oral prednisolone may increase the risk of recurrence of optic
neuritis if used without prior intravenous steroid.

Disease modifying therapies for MS

¯Çü Interferon beta (IFN├ƒ) is used in the early stages, but can
cause transient ¯Çøu-like symptoms. Four interferon drugs are
currently approved to treat MS and vary in their administration routes and frequency of injection: subcutaneous IFN├ƒ-1a
and -1b, intramuscular IFN├ƒ-1a and long-acting subcutaneous
peginterferon beta-1a (given every 2 weeks). ¯Çƒe long-term
safety pro¯Ç×le and e¯ÇÜcacy makes IFN├ƒ an important ¯Ç×rst-line
therapeutic option.
¯Çü Glatiramer acetate (subcutaneous injection) is used to reduce
the frequency of relapses of MS and possibly decreases disability progression. An injection site reaction occurs in about onethird. ¯Çƒe method of action is not known, but it may possibly act
as a decoy, diverting the autoimmune response against myelin.
¯Çü Monoclonal antibody treatment is showing promise in the
treatment of MS, but use of these agents has been associated
with systemic side e¯Ç£ects, notably opportunistic infections
and abnormal liver function. ¯Çƒe following medication can
be considered in di¯ÇÜcult cases: natalizumab, ocrelizumab and
alemtuzumab.

¯Çü Natalizumab is used for highly active relapsingÔÇôremitting MS
in patients who have not responded favourably to treatment
with interferon beta or glatiramer acetate and reduces relapses
by about two-thirds, slowing disability by half.
¯Çü Ocrelizumab is helpful for highly active disease and primary
progressive disease, but may rarely cause progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (which can be fatal).
¯Çü Alemtuzumab may be the most cost-e¯Ç£ective of these medications because of the favourable dosing strategy.

Approach to treatment of MS

¯Çü A multidisciplinary approach is important in these patients.
¯Çü First attack suggestive of MS: intravenous corticosteroids,
interferon beta or glatiramer acetate.
¯Çü Relapsing MS: (a) inactive disease: regular clinical monitoring; (b) active and highly active disease: disease modifying
therapy (alemtuzumab, cladribine, ¯Ç×ngolimod, natalizumab,
ocrelizumab, mitoxantrone).
¯Çü Progressive MS: ocrelizumab.

## Parainfectious optic neuritis

## Optic neuritis may be associated with viral infections such

as measles, mumps, chickenpox, rubella, whooping cough
and glandular fever and may also occur following immunization. Children are affected much more frequently than
adults. Presentation is usually 1ÔÇô3 weeks after a viral infection, with acute severe visual loss generally involving both
eyes. Bilateral papillitis is the rule. Occasionally there may be
a neuroretinitis or the discs may be normal. The prognosis for
spontaneous visual recovery is very good and treatment is not
required in the majority of patients. However, when visual loss
is severe and bilateral or involves an only seeing eye, intravenous steroids should be considered, with antiviral cover where
appropriate.

## Infectious optic neuritis

¯Çü Sinus-related optic neuritis is uncommon and is sometimes
characterized by recurrent attacks of unilateral visual loss
associated with severe headache and spheno-ethmoidal
sinusitis. Possible mechanisms include direct spread of
infection, occlusive vasculitis and mucocoele. Treatment
is with systemic antibiotics and, if appropriate, surgical
drainage.
¯Çü Cat-scratch fever (benign lymphoreticulosis) is usually caused
by Bartonella henselae inoculated by a cat scratch or bite (see
below and also Ch. 12). Numerous ophthalmological features
have been described, notably neuroretinitis.
¯Çü Syphilis may cause acute papillitis or neuroretinitis during the
primary or secondary stages (see Ch. 12).
¯Çü Lyme disease (borreliosis) is a spirochaetal infection caused
by Borrelia burgdorferi transmitted by a tick bite (see Ch. 12).
It may cause neuroretinitis and occasionally acute retrobulbar neuritis, which may be associated with other neurological
manifestations and can mimic MS.

770 Optic Nerve

¯Çü Cryptococcal meningitis in patients with acquired immunode¯Ç×ciency syndrome (AIDS) may be associated with acute
optic neuritis, which may be bilateral (see Ch. 12).
¯Çü Varicella zoster virus may cause papillitis by spread from contiguous retinitis (i.e. acute retinal necrosis, progressive retinal
necrosis ÔÇô see Ch. 12) or associated with herpes zoster ophthalmicus. Primary optic neuritis is uncommon but may occur
in immunocompromised patients, some of whom may subsequently develop viral retinitis.

TIP Bilateral severe visual loss secondary to optic neuritis
may occur in children 1ÔÇô3 weeks after a viral infection, but the
prognosis for visual recovery is good.

Non¯Çƒinfectious optic neuritis

## Sarcoidosis

Optic neuritis a¯Ç£ects 1ÔÇô5% of patients with neurosarcoidosis. It
may occasionally be the presenting feature of sarcoidosis, but usually develops during the course of established systemic disease.
¯Çƒe optic nerve head may exhibit a lumpy appearance suggestive
of granulomatous in¯Ç×ltration and there may be associated vitritis
(Fig. 19.11). ¯Çƒe response to steroid therapy is o¯ÇØen rapid, though
vision may decline if treatment is tapered or stopped prematurely
and some patients require long-term low-dose therapy. Methotrexate may also be used as an adjunct to steroids or as monotherapy in
steroid-intolerant patients.

## Autoimmune

Autoimmune optic nerve involvement may take the form of retrobulbar neuritis or anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (see below).

## Some patients may also experience slowly progressive visual loss

suggestive of compression. Treatment is with systemic steroids and
other immunosuppressants.

## Neuroretinitis

## Introduction

## Neuroretinitis refers to the combination of optic neuritis and

signs of retinal, usually macular, inflammation. Cat-scratch
fever is responsible for 60% of cases. About 25% of cases are
idiopathic (Leber idiopathic stellate neuroretinitis). Other
notable causes include syphilis, Lyme disease, mumps and
leptospirosis.

## Diagnosis

star.

¯Çü Symptoms. Painless unilateral visual impairment, usually
gradually worsening over about a week.
¯Çü Signs

{ VA is impaired to a variable degree.

{ Signs of optic nerve dysfunction are usually mild or absent,
as visual loss is largely due to macular involvement.

{ Papillitis associated with peripapillary and macular
oedema.

{ A macular star (Fig. 19.12) typically appears as disc swelling settles. ¯Çƒe macular star resolves with a return to normal or near-normal VA over 6ÔÇô12 months.

{ Venous engorgement and splinter haemorrhages may be
present in severe cases.

{ Fellow eye involvement occasionally develops.
¯Çü Optical coherence tomography (OCT) demonstrates suband intraretinal ¯Çøuid to a variable extent.
¯Çü Fluorescein angiography (FA) shows di¯Ç£use leakage from
super¯Ç×cial disc vessels.
¯Çü Blood tests may include serology for Bartonella and other
causes according to clinical suspicion (see also Ch. 12).

## Treatment

¯Çƒis is speci¯Ç×c to the cause and o¯ÇØen consists of antibiotics. Recurrent idiopathic cases may require treatment with steroids and/or
other immunosuppressants.

Non¯Çƒarteritic anterior ischaemic optic
neuropathy

## Introduction

Non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (NAION) is
caused by occlusion of the short posterior ciliary arteries resulting
in partial or total infarction of the optic nerve head. Predispositions include structural crowding of the optic nerve head so that
the physiological cup is either very small or absent, hypertension
(very common), diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidaemia, collagen vascular disease, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, hyperhomocysteinaemia, sudden hypotensive events, cataract surgery and
sleep apnoea syndrome. ¯Çƒis condition may occasionally follow air
travel or exposure to high altitude. Patients are usually over the age
of 50, but are typically younger than those who develop arteritic
ION (see below).

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms

{ Sudden painless monocular visual loss, which is frequently
discovered on awakening, suggesting that nocturnal hypotension may play a role.
¯Çü Signs

{ VA is normal or only slightly reduced in about 30%. ¯Çƒe
remainder have moderate to severe impairment.

{ Visual ¯Ç×eld defects are typically inferior altitudinal but
central, paracentral, quadrantic and arcuate defects may
also be seen.

{ Dyschromatopsia is usually proportional to the level of
visual impairment, in contrast to optic neuritis in which
colour vision may be severely impaired when VA is reasonably good.

{ Di¯Ç£use or sectoral hyperaemic disc swelling, o¯ÇØen associated
with a few peripapillary splinter haemorrhages (Fig. 19.13).

showing optic disc swelling.

it does not appear to reduce the risk of involvement of the
fellow eye.

## Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic
neuropathy

Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AAION) is caused
by giant cell arteritis (GCA). About 50% of patients with GCA have
polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) at diagnosis, while around 20% of
PMR patients will develop GCA. PMR is characterized by pain and
sti¯Ç£ness in proximal muscle groups, typically the shoulders and
biceps, that is worse on waking. Symptoms can be severe but generally respond dramatically to a lowÔÇômedium dose (initially 15ÔÇô20
mg daily) of oral prednisolone. ¯Çƒe causative relationship between
GCA and PMR remains uncertain.

## Diagnosis of giant cell arteritis

GCA is a granulomatous necrotizing arteritis (Fig. 19.14A) with
a predilection for large and medium-size arteries, particularly the
major aortic branches and the super¯Ç×cial temporal (STA), ophthalmic, posterior ciliary and proximal vertebral arteries. ¯Çƒe severity and extent of involvement are associated with the quantity of
elastic tissue in the media and adventitia and intracranial arteries
are usually spared as they possess little elastic tissue. Smoking, low
body mass index and early menopause may be independent risk
factors. Patients are usually elderly (average 70 years) and the condition is extremely rare under the age of 50. Women are a¯Ç£ected
four times more commonly than men. ¯Çƒe diagnosis of both
GCA and PMR is mainly made on the basis of clinical ¯Ç×ndings.
¯Çƒe American College of Rheumatology criteria may be helpful
in diagnostic decision-making (Table 19.2). ¯Çƒe classi¯Ç×cation was
updated in 2022, to incorporate recent developments (like the halo
sign on temporal artery ultrasound).
¯Çü Symptoms

{ Scalp tenderness, ¯Ç×rst noticed when combing the hair, is
common.

{ Disc swelling gradually resolves and pallor ensues 3ÔÇô6
weeks a¯ÇØer onset.
¯Çü Investigation should include assessment of the blood pressure and determination of the fasting lipid pro¯Ç×le and blood
glucose. It is also very important to exclude occult giant cell
arteritis (see below). Atypical features may prompt special
investigations, such as neuroimaging.
¯Çü Prognosis. Improvement in vision is common although recurrence occurs in about 6%. About 50% of eyes achieve 6/9 or
better, though 25% will only reach 6/60 or worse.
¯Çü Fellow eye. Involvement of the fellow eye occurs in about 10%
of patients a¯ÇØer 2 years and 15% a¯ÇØer 5 years.

## Treatment

¯Çü ¯Çƒere is no de¯Ç×nitive treatment.
¯Çü Optic nerve fenestration has not been shown to be of bene¯Ç×t.
¯Çü Some authorities advocate short-term systemic steroid
treatment.
¯Çü Any underlying systemic predispositions should be treated.
¯Çü Although aspirin is e¯Ç£ective in reducing systemic vascular
events and is frequently prescribed in patients with NAION,

or temporal areas or be more generalized.

{ Jaw claudication (cramp-like pain on chewing), caused

{ AAION (see below).

A negative TAB should not prevent ongoing treatment in

initial biopsy.

{ Colour Doppler and duplex ultrasonography shows a

hypoechoic halo is not pathognomonic of GCA, as atherosclerosis and other in¯Çøammatory arterial conditions
can cause a false positive halo. Doppler imaging is a useful aid to locating the artery for biopsy when it cannot be
palpated.

{ Extracranial large vessel imaging. Aortic imaging with
ultrasonography, MRA or positron emission tomography
(PET) scanning may be used to exclude aortic aneurysm
or dissection due to aortitis. Serial long-term imaging is
increasingly being advocated to exclude these potentially
life-threatening complications, the risk of which has now
been demonstrated to be substantially increased in and following GCA.

{ Shoulder joint (axillary) imaging. Recent work suggests
that particular in¯Çøammatory features in the shoulder
joints and axilla on MRI and ultrasonography may be useful diagnostically in PMR.

{ Scalp gangrene may occur in very severe cases.

{ Rare complications include dissecting aneurysms, aortic
incompetence, myocardial infarction, renal failure and
brainstem stroke.
¯Çü Investigations

{ Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is o¯ÇØen very high,
with a level of >60 mm/hr, although in approximately 20%
of patients it is normal, even lowÔÇônormal.

{ C-reactive protein (CRP) is raised.

{ Full blood count: elevated platelets and normocytic normochromic anaemia are commonly present. Platelet level
is a stronger predictor for GCA than ESR or CRP.

{ Liver function tests are abnormal in one-third.

{ Autoantibodies are normal.

{ Temporal artery biopsy (TAB) should be performed if
GCA is suspected. Steroid treatment (see below) should
never be withheld pending biopsy, which should ideally
be performed within 3 days of commencing steroids.

## Systemic steroid administration of duration greater than

7ÔÇô10 days may suppress histological evidence of active
arteritis although this is not invariable. In patients with
ocular involvement, it is advisable to take the biopsy
from the ipsilateral side. ¯Çƒe ideal location is the temple
because it lessens the risk of major nerve damage. At least
2.5 cm of artery should be collected and serial sections

Treatment of giant cell arteritis without AAION

Treatment in the absence of visual symptoms is with oral prednisolone. An initial dose of 1 mg/kg daily is typical, the subsequent duration of treatment being governed by the response of
symptoms and the level of the ESR or CRP. Symptoms may recur
without a corresponding rise in ESR or CRP and vice versa. Most
patients need treatment for 1ÔÇô2 years, although some may require
inde¯Ç×nite maintenance therapy. Rapid tapering should generally
not occur, with added caution when the dose is reduced to below
about 10 mg a day. CRP may play an important role in monitoring
disease activity, as the level seems to fall more rapidly than the ESR
in response to treatment.

## Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic
neuropathy

Arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AAION) a¯Ç£ects
30ÔÇô50% of untreated patients with GCA, of whom one-third
develop involvement of the fellow eye, usually within a week of the
¯Ç×rst. Posterior ischaemic optic neuropathy is much less common.
¯Çü Symptoms

{ Sudden, profound unilateral visual loss not uncommonly
preceded by transient visual obscurations (amaurosis
fugax) and sometimes by double vision.
