## Other manifestations

¯Çü Cilioretinal artery occlusion may occasionally be combined
with AAION (Fig. 19.14F).
¯Çü Central retinal artery occlusion is o¯ÇØen combined with
occlusion of a posterior ciliary artery ÔÇô with resultant choroidal hypoperfusion ÔÇô as the two can arise from the ophthalmic
artery by a common trunk.
¯Çü Ocular ischaemic syndrome due to involvement of the ophthalmic artery is rare.
¯Çü ÔÇÿPseudo-Foster Kennedy syndromeÔÇÖ is a consequence of anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy, in which active disease in one
eye (Fig. 19.15A) is associated with contralateral atrophy in
the other eye secondary to a previous episode (Fig. 19.15B). It
should be distinguished from true Foster Kennedy syndrome,
which is uncommon and is classically caused by a frontal
space-occupying lesion, characterized by ipsilateral compressive optic atrophy and contralateral papilloedema secondary
to raised intracranial pressure.

## Posterior ischaemic optic neuropathy

Posterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (PION) is much less
common than the anterior variety. It is caused by ischaemia of the
retrolaminar portion of the optic nerve supplied by the surrounding pial capillary plexus, which in turn is supplied by pial branches
of the ophthalmic artery. Only a small number of capillaries actually penetrate the nerve and extend to its central portion among
the pial septae. ¯Çƒe diagnosis of PION should be made only a¯ÇØer
other causes of retrobulbar optic neuropathy have been excluded,
such as compression or in¯Çøammation. Initially, the optic disc
appears normal, but pallor develops over weeks.
¯Çü Operative (perioperative) PION develops following a variety
of surgical procedures, most notably involving the heart and
the spine. It occurs in about 0.02% of these procedures. ¯Çƒe
major risk factors appear to be anaemia and intraoperative
hypovolaemic hypotension. Bilateral involvement is common
and the visual prognosis is poor. Prompt blood transfusion and
treatment of facial/orbital swelling may be of bene¯Ç×t.
¯Çü Arteritic PION is associated with GCA and carries a poor
visual prognosis.
¯Çü Non-arteritic PION is associated with the same systemic risk
factors as NAION, but is not associated with a crowded optic

{ Over 1ÔÇô2 months, the swelling gradually resolves and
severe optic atrophy ensues.
¯Çü Prognosis is very poor. Visual loss is usually permanent,
although, very rarely, prompt administration of systemic
steroids may be associated with partial recovery.

TIP Immediate systemic steroid treatment should be
prescribed for a patient with ischaemic optic neuropathy
secondary to temporal arteritis, to reduce the risk of visual loss
in the fellow eye.

¯Çü Treatment is aimed at preventing blindness of the fellow eye,
as visual loss in the index eye is unlikely to improve even
with immediate treatment. ¯Çƒe second eye may still become
involved in 25% despite early steroid administration. ¯Çƒe regimen is as follows:

{ Intravenous methylprednisolone, 500 mg to 1 g/day for 3
days followed by oral prednisolone 1ÔÇô2 mg/kg daily. A¯ÇØer
3 more days the oral dose is reduced to 50ÔÇô60 mg (not less
than 0.75 mg/kg) for 4 weeks or until symptom resolution and ESR/CRP normalization. A typical subsequent
regimen consists of reducing the daily dose by 10 mg/day
every 2 weeks until 20 mg/day is reached, with tapering
a¯ÇØerwards titrated against ESR/CRP and symptoms, e.g. a
2.5 mg reduction every 2ÔÇô4 weeks to 10 mg then a 1 mg
reduction every 1ÔÇô2 months.

{ Enteric-coated prednisolone may be appropriate, particularly in patients with a history of peptic ulceration.

{ Steroid treatment should be accompanied by bone and
gastrointestinal protection, e.g. a weekly bisphosphonate,
calcium/vitamin D supplementation and a proton pump
inhibitor.

{ Monitoring should be performed by a physician with
appropriate training and should look particularly for
steroid-related complications. A full blood count, ESR/
CRP, urea and electrolytes, random glucose and blood
pressure should be checked at each visit. Every 1ÔÇô2 years, a
chest X-ray or more sophisticated imaging should be performed to exclude an aortic aneurysm and bone mineral
density should be assessed.

{ Antiplatelet therapy, e.g. aspirin 600 mg stat then 100
mg/day should be commenced as this has been shown to
reduce the risk of visual loss and stroke.

A B

eye showing subtle optic atrophy.

disc. ¯Çƒe visual prognosis is similar to NAION. Some practitioners prescribe a short course of high-dose systemic steroid
in early cases.

## Diabetic papillopathy

¯Çƒe cause of diabetic papillopathy is unknown, but it possibly represents a mild form of non-arteritic ischaemic optic neuropathy.
Younger patients (under 50 years) are more likely to be a¯Ç£ected
and usually present with slight visual symptoms. ¯Çƒe optic disc
shows either hyperaemic oedema or marked telangiectasia of the
inner surface microvasculature, which can be mistaken for neovascularization (see Fig. 13.24). Diabetic retinopathy is usually present, with macular oedema in 25% and retinal neovascularization in
about 10%. It should be di¯Ç£erentiated from NAION, which has a
poor prognosis. ¯Çƒere is no treatment, but the disc oedema slowly
resolves over a period of 2ÔÇô10 months resulting in a ¯Ç×nal visual
acuity of 6/12 or better in 80% of cases, subject to the e¯Ç£ect of coexisting diabetic retinopathy.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e criteria for diagnosis include:

the ages of 15 and 35 years, although in atypical cases the condition may a¯Ç£ect females and present at any age between 10 and 60
years. ¯Çƒe diagnosis of LHON should therefore be considered in
any patient with bilateral optic neuropathy, irrespective of age.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Typically, acute or subacute severe painless unilateral (50%) loss of central vision occurs. In initially unilateral
cases, the fellow eye becomes similarly a¯Ç£ected within weeks
or months.
¯Çü Signs during the acute stage are o¯ÇØen subtle and easily overlooked and in some patients the disc may be entirely normal.

{ In typical cases there is disc hyperaemia with obscuration
of the disc margins (Fig. 19.16A).

{ Dilated capillaries on the disc surface, which may extend
onto adjacent retina (telangiectatic microangiopathy ÔÇô Fig.
19.16B).

{ Swelling of the peripapillary nerve ¯Ç×bre layer
(pseudo-oedema).

{ Colour vision is likely to be subnormal.

{ ¯Çƒere is o¯ÇØen a relative a¯Ç£erent pupillary defect.

{ ¯Çƒe presence of diabetes (type 1 in 70%, type 2 in 30%).

{ Dilatation and tortuosity of posterior pole vasculature.

{ Optic disc oedema (unilateral in about 60%).

{ Mild optic nerve dysfunction.

{ Absence of ocular in¯Çøammation or raised intracranial
pressure.

{ Subsequently, the vessels and pseudo-oedema regress and
severe optic atrophy supervenes (Fig. 19.16C), with nerve
¯Ç×bre layer dropout most pronounced in the papillomacular bundle.

{ Telangiectatic microangiopathy may be present in asymptomatic female relatives.

## Leber hereditary optic neuropathy

## Introduction

Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON) is a rare ganglion cell
degeneration that typically a¯Ç£ects the papillomacular bundle. ¯Çƒe
condition is caused by maternally inherited mitochondrial DNA
point mutations, most frequently (50ÔÇô90%) at nucleotide position
11778 (G to A) in the MT-ND4 gene; primarily a¯Ç£ecting respiratory chain complex genes. It is the commonest inherited mitochondrial disorder. ¯Çƒe condition typically a¯Ç£ects males between

{ Surprisingly, the pupillary light reactions may remain
fairly brisk.
¯Çü LHON plus refers to rare variants with additional manifestations such as neuromuscular dysfunction.
¯Çü Prognosis is poor, although some visual recovery may
occur in a minority of cases even years later. Most patients
suffer permanent bilateral visual loss with a final VA of 6/60
or less. The prognosis is better if the age of onset is less
than 20 years and especially if the age of onset is less than

776 Optic Nerve

A

B

C

showing hyperaemic disc swelling with blurring of margins;
¯ÇØB¯Ç× marked telangiectatic microangiopathy; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× late ÔÇô atrophic
appearance.

TIP Undertake genetic testing for Leber hereditary optic
neuropathy in any patient who presents with bilateral optic
neuropathy of unknown cause.

10 years. The 11778 mutation carries the worst prognosis
and the 14484 mutation has the best chance of spontaneous
visual recovery.
¯Çü Investigations

{ OCT. Patients show peripapillary retinal thinning. Unaffected carriers frequently show variable thickening of the
temporal RNFL, perhaps due to compensatory mitochondrial accumulation.

{ Visual ¯Ç×eld defects usually consist of central or centrocaecal scotomas, with preserved peripheral vision.

{ FA shows no leakage from the disc or microangiopathic
vessels.

{ Genetic testing to look for the three common causative
mutations.

## Treatment

¯Çü Apart from symptomatic measures such as low vision aids,
treatment is generally ine¯Ç£ective.
¯Çü Mitochondrial ÔÇÿcocktailsÔÇÖ have been tried, which include various vitamins, co-factors (CoQ10), creatine and alpha-lipoic
acid, with anecdotal success. Subspecialist prescription of
one or more of these may be worthwhile, particularly in early
disease or prior to second eye involvement.
¯Çü Counterintuitively, the cyanocobalamin (but not the hydroxocobalamin) form of vitamin B12 has been reported to worsen
outcomes.
¯Çü Smoking and excessive alcohol consumption should be
discouraged.
¯Çü Idebenone (900 mg/day) appears to be neuroprotective in
this condition and may have a role to play in some patients.
¯Çü Intravitreal gene therapy is showing promising results; an
adeno-associated virus vector expressing normal ND4 complementary DNA is used in patients with the 11778 mutation.

## Miscellaneous hereditary optic

neuropathies ¯ÇØatrophies¯Ç×

¯Çƒis heterogeneous group of rare disorders are characterized primarily by bilateral optic atrophy. ¯Çƒere is no e¯Ç£ective treatment,
though the measures described above for LHON may be tried.

Dominant optic atrophy (Kjer type optic
atrophy, optic atrophy type 1)

¯Çü Inheritance is autosomal dominant (AD). ¯Çƒis is the most common hereditary optic neuropathy, with an incidence of around
1:50000. It is frequently due to a mutation in the OPA1 gene
on chromosome 3, which causes mitochondrial dysfunction,
but other genes can be responsible and X-linked and autosomal
recessive (AR) forms have been reported. ¯Çƒere is usually high
penetrance but variable expressivity in the dominant forms.
¯Çü Presentation is typically, though not always, in childhood with
insidious visual loss. ¯Çƒere is usually a family history, but the
course may be variable even within the same family.
¯Çü Optic atrophy may be subtle and temporal (Fig. 19.17A and B) or
di¯Ç£use (Fig. 19.17C and D). ¯Çƒere may be enlargement of the cup.

A B

C D

di¯Ç×use pallor.

¯Çü Prognosis is variable (¯Ç×nal VA 6/12ÔÇô6/60) with considerable
di¯Ç£erences within and between families. Very slow progression over decades is typical.
¯Çü Systemic abnormalities. Twenty per cent develop sensorineural hearing loss. Other features are less common.

## Behr syndrome

¯Çü Inheritance is AR; heterozygotes may have mild features.
¯Çü Presentation is in early childhood with reduced vision.
¯Çü Optic atrophy is di¯Ç£use.
¯Çü Prognosis is variable, with moderate to severe visual loss and
nystagmus.
¯Çü Systemic abnormalities include spastic gait, ataxia and mental
handicap.

## Wolfram syndrome

Wolfram syndrome is also referred to as DIDMOAD (diabetes
insipidus, diabetes mellitus, optic atrophy and deafness).
¯Çü Inheritance. Three genetic forms are recognized, caused
by a variety of mutations in WFS1, which gives Wolfram
syndrome 1, CISD2 ÔÇô Wolfram syndrome 2 ÔÇô and probably

a form caused by a mitochondrial DNA mutation, with
inheritance being AR, AD or via the maternal mitochondrial line.
¯Çü Presentation is usually between the ages of 5 and 21 years.
Diabetes mellitus is typically the ¯Ç×rst manifestation, followed
by visual problems.
¯Çü Optic atrophy is di¯Ç£use and severe and may be associated with
disc cupping.
¯Çü Prognosis is typically poor (¯Ç×nal VA is <6/60).
¯Çü Systemic abnormalities (apart from DIDMOAD) are highly
variable, presumably in part due to genetic heterogeneity
and may include anosmia, ataxia, seizures, mental handicap,
short stature, endocrine abnormalities and elevated CSF protein. Life expectancy is usually substantially reduced.

## Nutritional optic neuropathy

## Introduction

Nutritional optic neuropathy (tobacco-alcohol amblyopia) is
an uncommon but underdiagnosed acquired optic neuropathy.

778 Optic Nerve

The mechanism is thought to be deficient mitochondrial function, similar to that of the heritable optic neuropathies, and
like these the papillomacular bundle is preferentially affected.
In Western developed countries the condition typically affects
individuals with high alcohol and tobacco consumption. Most
patients have neglected their diet and the features are most
likely to be due principally to deficiency in the B-complex vitamins, particularly cyanocobalamin (B12) and thiamine (B1), but
also riboflavin (B2), niacin (B3) and pyridoxine (B6). Copper,
folic acid and protein deficiency may also be important and
direct toxic effects of alcohol and tobacco may also be significant. A strict vegan diet can also be causative, as can dietary
deficiency in elderly patients. A similar clinical picture may be
seen due to toxicity from medication or environmental (e.g.
occupational) toxins. In under-resourced geographic regions,
nutritional aetiological factors due to inadequate dietary intake
predominate and nutritional optic neuropathy can be epidemic.

## Pernicious anaemia sufferers may develop the condition due to

reduced vitamin B12 absorption.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms

{ ¯Çƒe insidious onset of painless bilateral central blurring
associated with abnormal colour vision.

{ Enquiry should be made about potentially causative medication (see Ch. 21), environmental toxin exposure and a
vegan diet.

{ A family history of optic neuropathy should be excluded.

{ Peripheral neurological symptoms (sensory loss, gait disturbance) raise the suspicion of a peripheral neuropathy.
¯Çü Signs

{ VA is very variably a¯Ç£ected.

{ ¯Çƒe discs at presentation are normal in most cases, but
some patients show subtle pallor, o¯ÇØen temporal, splintershaped haemorrhages on or around the disc, or minimal
oedema.

{ ¯Çƒe pupil reactions are o¯ÇØen normal, but may respond
weakly in more severe cases.

¯Çü Prognosis is good in early cases provided patients comply with
treatment although visual recovery may be slow. Colour perception returns more slowly than measured acuity. In advanced
cases there is likely to be a substantial permanent de¯Ç×cit, but
it is highly unusual for complete loss of useful vision to occur,
with preservation of peripheral ¯Ç×eld usual even in quite severe
cases.
¯Çü Blood tests. B12 (cobalamin) and folate (serum and red cell)
and possibly other vitamin levels such as B1 (thiamine) and B2,
a full blood count and ¯Ç×lm (macrocytic anaemia) and serum
protein levels. Screening for speci¯Ç×c toxins or for LHON (see
above) mutations may be indicated. A high serum methylmalonate and/or homocysteine level may be an indicator of
potentially functional B12 de¯Ç×ciency, even in the presence of a
lowÔÇônormal B12.
¯Çü VEP. ¯Çƒe P100 amplitude is markedly reduced but with normal or near-normal latency.
¯Çü MRI is commonly considered to rule out intracranial pathology, particularly when an atypical clinical picture is present
(e.g. marked asymmetry, disc pallor but good vision).

## Treatment

Consideration should be given to co-management with a general
physician or neurologist. Compliance with treatment and review is
commonly poor in tobacco-alcohol amblyopia.
¯Çü Dietary revision with formal nutritional advice, incorporating
increased fruit and leafy green vegetable intake.
¯Çü Abstention from alcohol and tobacco is a priority, but is not
always achievable.
¯Çü Vitamins. A daily multivitamin preparation, plus thiamine
(100 mg twice daily) and folate (1 mg daily). In someone who
is both folate and B12 de¯Ç×cient it is prudent to correct the
B12 de¯Ç×ciency ¯Ç×rst to avoid precipitating subacute combined
degeneration of the cord.
¯Çü Intramuscular hydroxocobalamin (vitamin B12) injections.
¯Çƒe use of injected B12 has become less popular in recent
years, as evidence shows that even in malabsorption states
oral treatment may be e¯Ç£ective. Hydroxocobalamin injections improve vision in tobacco-alcohol amblyopia, perhaps
by reversing cyanide toxicity and should be considered in
severe or unresponsive cases. Regimens range from 1 mg
weekly for 8 weeks to monthly for several months. Injections
every 3 months are typically continued for life.
¯Çü Exposure to the identi¯Çƒed agent should be discontinued
immediately in cases due to medication or environmental
toxicity.

## Papilloedema

## Introduction

## Papilloedema is swelling of the optic nerve head secondary to

raised intracranial pressure (ICP). ÔÇÿDisc swellingÔÇÖ and ÔÇÿdisc oedemaÔÇÖ
are non-speci¯Ç×c terms that include papilloedema but also a disc
swollen from other causes. All patients with papilloedema should
be suspected of harbouring an intracranial mass. Not all patients

{ Other ocular and systemic features of nutritional de¯Ç×ciency should be sought (and investigations carried out
accordingly), e.g. Korsako¯Ç£ syndrome, Wernicke disease
and pernicious anaemia. Xerophthalmia and beriberi
should be considered in developing nations.
¯Çü Colour vision testing. A reduction in colour vision is disproportionate to the reduction in acuity. Red desaturation is likely
to be present. Ishihara chart testing is a simple, moderately
quantitative test that is widely available.
¯Çü Visual ¯Çƒeld defects are bilateral, relatively symmetrical, centrocaecal scotomas. ¯Çƒe margins of the defects are di¯ÇÜcult to
de¯Ç×ne with a white target but are more substantive with a red
target.
¯Çü OCT may show peripapillary RNFL thickening and will help
to exclude macular pathology.
¯Çü Fundus auto¯Ç£uorescence (FAF) is useful to exclude macula
changes secondary to a cone or cone-rod dystrophy, that may
present with colour de¯Ç×ciency and a central scotoma.

Diagnosis of raised ICP

¯Çü Headaches, which characteristically occur early in the
morning and may wake the patient from sleep, although
less commonly they can occur at any time of day. The pain
may be generalized or localized and may intensify with
head movement, bending or coughing. They tend to get
progressively worse over time. Very rarely, headache may
be absent.
¯Çü Nausea, o¯ÇØen episodic and with associated projectile vomiting
may occur as an isolated feature or may precede the onset of
headaches.
¯Çü Deterioration of consciousness as severity increases, initially
with drowsiness and somnolence. A dramatic deterioration in
the level of consciousness may be indicative of brainstem distortion and requires immediate attention.
¯Çü Visual symptoms are commonly absent in mild or early raised
ICP.

and D), but not if normal or if increased ONSD is due to

Obese young adult women are the most commonly a¯Ç£ected
group. Various medications including the contraceptive pill have
been implicated (strictly ÔÇÿsecondary intracranial hypertensionÔÇÖ
if a cause is identi¯Ç×ed), as well as a range of conditions such as
systemic lupus erythematosus, Lyme disease and sleep apnoea
syndrome.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms and signs are similar to those of papilloedema,
with headache in over 90%. Pulsatile tinnitus may be experienced, and cranial nerve palsies and occasionally other
symptoms may occur. ¯Çƒe long-term visual prognosis is usually good, but up to a quarter will have a degree of permanent
impairment.
¯Çü Investigation is as for papilloedema: ONSD is increased
and MRI may show slit-like ventricles and flattening of
the pituitary gland (ÔÇÿempty sellaÔÇÖ sign). MRV is usually carried out to exclude cerebral venous sinus thrombosis or

## Idiopathic intracranial hypertension

## Introduction

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension, previously known as benign
intracranial hypertension or pseudotumour cerebri, is characterized by elevated ICP that by de¯Ç×nition has no identi¯Ç×able cause.

782 Optic Nerve

A B

C D

stenosis. Additional investigation for an occult cause should
be considered, especially in patients who do not fit the usual
profile.

## Treatment

¯Çü Weight loss, including via bariatric surgery, can be e¯Ç£ective
and formal dietary intervention is strongly recommended.
¯Çü Medical. Acetazolamide is the drug of ¯Ç×rst choice, although
furosemide can also be considered. ¯Çƒe use of steroids is controversial, but a short course is sometimes used in severe papilloedema. Intravenous mannitol is usually reserved for acute
exacerbations.
¯Çü Lumbar puncture. ¯Çƒis may be used for acute exacerbations,
but usually has a short-lived e¯Ç£ect.
¯Çü Surgical. In unresponsive cases optic nerve fenestration can be
helpful. Other options include lumboperitoneal shunting and
transverse dural sinus stenting, but these options have a high
complication rate.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e ophthalmologistÔÇÖs role is usually con¯Ç×ned to diagnosis
and the monitoring of visual function with VA, colour vision
and ¯Ç×elds, and optic nerve appearance/photography.

A tilted optic disc is a common anomaly, usually bilateral,
describing an apparently oblique entry of the optic nerve into
the globe. It is strongly associated with myopia and astigmatism.
Tilting is de¯Ç×ned as being present when the ratio of the longest
diameter of the disc to the shortest (ovality index) is greater than
1.3 (Fig. 19.23A), but this will not include many subjectively
apparently tilted discs. A major implication of tilting is di¯ÇÜculty
in excluding superimposed glaucomatous damage, as the temporal, particularly the inferotemporal, neuroretinal rim is frequently very thin.
¯Çü Signs. Small, oval or D-shaped disc: the axis is most frequently
directed inferonasally, but may be horizontal or nearly vertical.

{ ¯Çƒe disc margin is indistinct where retinal nerve ¯Ç×bres are
elevated.

{ Peripapillary chorioretinal thinning may be marked and
situs inversus may be present. ¯Çƒe temporal vessels deviate
nasally before turning temporally (see Fig. 19.23A).

## Torsional disc

A torsional (torsioned) disc is said to be present when its long axis
is inclined at more than 15┬░ from the vertical meridian, a line at 90┬░
to a horizontal line connecting the foveola to the centre of the optic
disc (Fig. 19.23B). It is o¯ÇØen present in association with tilting and
is also associated with myopia.

## Optic disc drusen

Disc drusen are deposits, usually calci¯Ç×ed, within the substance
of the optic nerve head that are present in up to 2% of the population, o¯ÇØen bilaterally (75%). ¯Çƒey are usually sporadic but, in some
cases, have AD inheritance. ¯Çƒey are thought to consist of focal
collections of axonal metabolic products.
¯Çü Symptoms are usually absent, but some patients may experience episodic blurring, possibly due to transient ischaemia
related to a crowding e¯Ç£ect.
¯Çü Buried drusen. Particularly in childhood, drusen may be
obscured beneath the disc surface (Fig. 19.25A and B) and is a
common cause of pseudopapilloedema. A lumpy disc appearance, an absent cup and anomalous vascular patterns including
proximal branching, an increased number of major vessels and
tortuosity suggest drusen, as well as a lack of papilloedematous
features such as hyperaemia and vessel obscuration. Visibility
of drusen typically increases in the early teens.
¯Çü Exposed drusen. Drusen at or close to the disc surface appear
as whitish pearl-like lesions of a range of sizes (Fig. 19.25C and
D). ¯Çƒey tend to enlarge slowly over many years.
¯Çü Retinal nerve ¯Çƒbre layer thinning is common.
¯Çü Visual ¯Çƒeld defects of a range of con¯Ç×gurations are present
in up to 75% and are o¯ÇØen progressive, though a substantial
impact on sight is rare. No de¯Ç×nitive treatment is recognized,
though reducing IOP and other speculative measures may be
tried in selected cases.

{ FA does not usually show hyper¯Çøuorescence of the detachment, which is demonstrated well on FAF and OCT.
¯Çü Management. Long-term follow-up is unnecessary. However,
patients with this condition should be warned to return if their

## Optic disc coloboma

¯Çƒe embryonic ¯Ç×ssure of the developing eye is located inferiorly
and slightly nasally and extends from the optic nerve to the margin of the pupil. A coloboma is a defect in one or more ocular
structures due to the ¯Ç×ssureÔÇÖs incomplete closure. ¯Çƒe defect may
be unilateral or bilateral and usually occurs sporadically in otherwise normal individuals (Fig. 19.26), though numerous systemic
associations have been described, e.g. chromosomal abnormalities,
CHARGE syndrome, Goldenhar syndrome and central nervous
system abnormalities. An AD variety is caused by a mutation in
the gene PAX6
¯Çü Signs

{ VA is o¯ÇØen decreased; amblyopia and refractive error may
be present.

{ A disc pit is sometimes associated (Fig. 19.27A). Pit-like
excavations are sometimes seen with a disc coloboma.

{ A disc coloboma may be associated with, or even continuous with, a large chorioretinal coloboma (Fig. 19.27B).

{ A focal, glistening white, bowl-shaped excavation,
decentred inferiorly so that the inferior neuroretinal
rim is thin or absent and normal disc tissue is confined

## Morning glory anomaly

Morning glory anomaly is a rare, usually unilateral and sporadic
condition that has a spectrum of severity. Bilateral cases, which are
rarer still, may be hereditary. As in coloboma, a PAX6 gene mutation can be responsible. Systemic associations are uncommon, but
the most important is frontonasal dysplasia, characterized by midfacial anomalies, a basal encephalocele and other midline brain
malformations, including pituitary insu¯ÇÜciency (Fig. 19.28A).
¯Çü Signs

{ VA may be normal or impaired to a variable extent.

in a radial pattern like the spokes of a wheel. ¯Çƒey are
increased in number and it may be di¯ÇÜcult to distinguish
arteries from veins.
¯Çü Complications include serous retinal detachment (30%) and
choroidal neovascularization.

¯Çƒe hypoplastic optic nerve, unilateral or bilateral, carries a diminished number of nerve ¯Ç×bres. Its frequency seems to be increasing,
though it remains relatively rare. Young maternal age, primiparity
and maternal exposure to various agents have been linked. It may
occur as an isolated anomaly in an otherwise normal eye, but systemic associations are common (see below).
¯Çü Symptoms

{ ¯Çƒe double-ring sign is characteristic and consists of a

19.29A). A thin outer pigmented line may also be seen.
¯Çü Ocular associations include astigmatism, amblyopia, ¯Ç×eld
defects, foveal hypoplasia, aniridia and microphthalmos.
¯Çü Systemic associations. Optic disc hypoplasia is associated
with a wide variety of developmental midline brain defects;
pituitary and hypothalamic de¯Ç×cits are common. Historically, the most frequent association has been considered to be
ÔÇÿsepto-optic dysplasiaÔÇÖ (de Morsier syndrome) ÔÇô bilateral optic
nerve hypoplasia, absent septum pellucidum, corpus callosum
agenesis (Fig. 19.29BÔÇôD) and hypopituitarism ÔÇô but it is now
believed that this complex does not exist as a speci¯Ç×c entity.
¯Çü Investigation. All a¯Ç£ected children should be screened for
systemic associations, especially hormonal de¯Ç×ciencies and
developmental delay.

## Aicardi syndrome

## Aicardi syndrome consists of a range of central nervous system and

other malformations in association with multiple bilateral depigmented chorioretinal lacunae clustered around a hypoplastic, colobomatous or pigmented optic disc (Fig. 19.31). Other ocular features
can include cataract and coloboma. Inheritance is X-linked dominant; the condition is lethal in utero for males. Death usually occurs
within the ¯Ç×rst few years of life in a¯Ç£ected girls.

## Acquired vascular abnormalities

¯Çü Disc collaterals are veno-venular shunts that develop within
the existing vascular network to bypass an area of closure and
are most frequently associated with retinal vein occlusion. ¯Çƒe
collaterals appear as distended ¯Çøat vessels that start and end on
the disc (Fig. 19.33A).
¯Çü Optociliary shunts are usually unilateral anastomoses
between the retinal and choroidal circulations, consisting of
enlarged pre-existing peripapillary capillaries. ¯Çƒe vessels
appear as large tortuous channels that disappear at the disc
margin (Fig. 19.33B). ¯Çƒe most common association is an
optic nerve sheath meningioma. Other causes are discussed in

¯Çü Disc new vessels manifest as ¯Ç×ne, lace-like vessels that may
extend onto adjacent retina. ¯Çƒe vessels may be ¯Çøat or elevated
above the disc and may be associated with gliosis (Fig. 19.33C).

## Miscellaneous anomalies

¯Çü Peripapillary staphyloma is a non-hereditary, usually unilateral condition in which a relatively normal disc sits at the base
of a deep excavation whose walls, as well as the surrounding
choroid and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), show atrophic
changes (Fig. 19.34A). VA is markedly reduced and local retinal detachment may be present. Unlike other excavated optic
disc anomalies, it is rarely associated with other congenital
defects or systemic diseases.
¯Çü Papillorenal (renal-coloboma) syndrome is an AD genetic
condition caused in most cases by a mutation in PAX2 and
characterized by renal and optic disc dysplasia. ¯Çƒe discs do
not exhibit true hypoplasia, but a failure of angiogenesis. ¯Çƒey
are normal in size with central excavation and replacement
of the central retinal vasculature by cilioretinal vessels (Fig.
19.34B).
¯Çü Optic disc dysplasia is a descriptive term for a markedly
deformed disc that does not conform to any recognizable
category (Fig. 19.34C).

¯Çü Megalopapilla is a typically bilateral condition in which both
the horizontal and vertical disc diameters are 2.1 mm or more,
or the disc area is greater than 2.5 mm [2] . Although the cup-todisc ratio is greater than normal, the cup should retain its normal con¯Ç×guration with no evidence of focal neuroretinal rim
loss. Although OCT may show peripapillary RNFL thinning,
macular ganglion cell complex imaging is typically normal.
¯Çü Optic nerve aplasia is an extremely rare condition in which
the optic disc is absent or rudimentary and retinal vessels are
absent or few in number and abnormal. ¯Çƒere may be retinal
pigmentary disturbance, especially at the site where the optic
disc might have been. Other ocular and systemic developmental defects may be present.

## Anatomy

Light re¯Çƒex

¯Çƒe light re¯Çøex is mediated by the retinal photoreceptors and subserved by four neurones (Fig. 19.35).
¯Çü First (sensory) connects each retina with both pretectal nuclei
in the midbrain at the level of the superior colliculi. Impulses
originating from the nasal retina are conducted by ¯Ç×bres that
decussate in the chiasm and pass up the opposite optic tract
to terminate in the contralateral pretectal nucleus. Impulses
originating in the temporal retina are conducted by uncrossed
¯Ç×bres (ipsilateral optic tract) that terminate in the ipsilateral
pretectal nucleus.
¯Çü Second (internuncial) connects each pretectal nucleus to both
EdingerÔÇôWestphal nuclei. ¯Çƒus, a uniocular light stimulus
evokes bilateral and symmetrical pupillary constriction. Damage to internuncial neurones is responsible for lightÔÇônear dissociation in neurosyphilis and pinealomas.
¯Çü ¯Ç×ird (preganglionic motor) connects the EdingerÔÇôWestphal
nucleus to the ciliary ganglion. ¯Çƒe parasympathetic ¯Ç×bres
pass through the oculomotor nerve, enter its inferior division

A

B

C

vessels.

and reach the ciliary ganglion via the nerve to the inferior
oblique muscle.
¯Çü Fourth (postganglionic motor) leaves the ciliary ganglion and
passes in the short ciliary nerves to innervate the sphincter

A

B

C

dysplasia. (Courtesy of D Taylor and CS Hoyt, from Pediatric
Ophthalmology and Strabismus, Elsevier 2005 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

pupillae. ¯Çƒe ciliary ganglion is located within the muscle
cone, just behind the globe. It should be noted that, although
the ciliary ganglion serves as a conduit for other nerve ¯Ç×bres,
only the parasympathetic ¯Ç×bres synapse there.

pupil but subtler. ¯Çƒus, the pupils respond weakly to stimulation of
the diseased eye and briskly to that of the normal eye. ¯Çƒe di¯Ç£erence between the pupillary reactions of the two eyes is highlighted
by the ÔÇÿswinging ¯Çøashlight testÔÇÖ in which a light source is alternatively switched from one eye to the other and back, thus stimulating each eye in rapid succession. A le¯ÇØ relative pupillary defect is
characterized by the following:
¯Çü When the normal eye is stimulated, both pupils constrict
(Fig. 19.36A).
¯Çü When the light is swung to the diseased eye, the stimulus delivered to the constriction mechanism is reduced and both pupils
dilate instead of constricting (Fig. 19.36B).
¯Çü When the normal eye is again stimulated, both pupils constrict
once more (Fig. 19.36C).
¯Çü When the diseased eye is stimulated, both pupils dilate
(Fig. 19.36D).
It should be remembered that in a¯Ç£erent (sensory) lesions,
the pupils are equal in size. Anisocoria (asymmetrical pupil
diameter) implies disease of the e¯Ç£erent (motor) nerve or the iris
itself.

TIP In a¯Ç×erent (sensory) lesions of the visual pathway the
pupils are equal in size. Anisocoria (inequality of the pupillary
size) indicates an abnormality of the e¯Ç×erent (motor) pathway.

Horner syndrome ¯ÇØoculosympathetic
palsy¯Ç×

## Anatomy

¯Çƒe sympathetic supply involves three neurones (Fig. 19.37):
¯Çü First (central) starts in the posterior hypothalamus and
descends, uncrossed, down the brainstem to terminate in the
ciliospinal centre of Budge, in the intermediolateral horn of
the spinal cord, located between C8 and T2.
¯Çü Second (preganglionic) passes from the ciliospinal centre to
the superior cervical ganglion in the neck. During its long
course, it is closely related to the apical pleura where it may
be damaged by bronchogenic carcinoma (Pancoast tumour) or
during surgery on the neck.
¯Çü ¯Ç×ird (postganglionic) ascends along the internal carotid
artery to enter the cavernous sinus where it joins the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve. ¯Çƒe sympathetic ¯Ç×bres
reach the ciliary body and the dilator pupillae muscle via the
nasociliary nerve and the long ciliary nerves.

## Causes

¯Çƒe causes of Horner syndrome are shown in Table 19.4. Most
commonly, isolated (without additional neurological features)
postganglionic Horner syndrome is microvascular in aetiology,
but this cannot be assumed.

## Presentation

¯Çƒe majority of cases are unilateral. Causes of bilateral involvement
include cervical spine injuries and autonomic diabetic neuropathy.

Near re¯Çƒex

¯Çƒe near re¯Çøex, a synkinesis rather than a true re¯Çøex, is activated
when gaze is changed from a distant to a near target (see also Ch.
18). It comprises accommodation, convergence and miosis. Vision
is not a prerequisite and there is no clinical condition in which
the light re¯Çøex is present but the near response absent. Although
the ¯Ç×nal pathways for the near and light re¯Çøexes are identical (i.e.
third nerve, ciliary ganglion, short ciliary nerves), the centre for
the near re¯Çøex is ill-de¯Ç×ned. ¯Çƒere are probably two supranuclear
in¯Çøuences: the frontal and occipital lobes. ¯Çƒe midbrain centre for
the near re¯Çøex is probably located more ventrally than the pretectal
nucleus and this may explain why compressive lesions such as pinealomas, preferentially involving the dorsal internuncial neurones
involved in the light re¯Çøex, spare the near re¯Çøex ¯Ç×bres until later.

A¯Çøerent pupillary defect

Absolute a¯ÇØerent pupillary defect

An absolute a¯Ç£erent pupillary defect (amaurotic pupil) is caused by
a complete optic nerve lesion and is characterized by the following:
¯Çü ¯Çƒe involved eye is completely blind (i.e. no light perception).
¯Çü Both pupils are equal in size.
¯Çü When the a¯Ç£ected eye is stimulated by light neither pupil
reacts.
¯Çü When the normal eye is stimulated both pupils react normally.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe near re¯Çøex is normal in both eyes.

Relative a¯ÇØerent pupillary defect

A relative pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) is caused by an
incomplete optic nerve lesion or severe retinal disease, but never
by a dense cataract. ¯Çƒe clinical features are those of an amaurotic

A

B

C

D

pupils constrict; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× when the light is swung to the diseased
left eye, both pupils dilate; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× when the normal eye is again
stimulated, both pupils constrict once more; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× when the
diseased eye is stimulated, both pupils dilate.

Painful Horner syndrome, especially of acute onset, should raise
the possibility of carotid dissection (Fig. 19.38).
¯Çü Mild ptosis (usually 1ÔÇô2 mm) as a result of weakness of M├╝ller
muscle and miosis due to the unopposed action of the sphincter pupillae with resultant anisocoria (Fig. 19.39A and B).
¯Çü A key examination ¯Ç×nding is that anisocoria is accentuated in
dim light, since in contrast to a normal fellow pupil the Horner
pupil will dilate only very slowly. ¯Çƒe dark-induced anisocoria
diminishes with time spent in the dark environment.
¯Çü Pupillary constriction to light and near stimuli is normal.

supply.

¯Çü Slight elevation of the inferior eyelid (inferior ptosis) as a result

¯Çü Reduced ipsilateral sweating, but because the sudomotor ¯Ç×bres
supplying the skin of the face run along the external carotid

¯Çü ¯Çƒe cranial nerves and peripheral nervous system (e.g. T1 ÔÇô
grip strength), as well as the neck and regional lymph nodes
should be examined, if necessary by a neurologist.

adrenaline may be used to di¯Ç£erentiate a preganglionic (abnormal ¯Ç×rst- or second-order neurone) from a postganglionic lesion
(abnormal third-order neurone), though as the distinction may

## Treatment

Any identi¯Ç×ed cause should be addressed as appropriate. ¯Çƒe ptosis of Horner syndrome is mild but surgery can be considered at
the patientÔÇÖs discretion. Apraclonidine may be helpful as a temporizing measure.

## Adie pupil

## Introduction

An Adie pupil (tonic pupil, Adie syndrome) is caused by denervation of the postganglionic parasympathetic supply to the sphincter pupillae and the ciliary muscle and may follow a viral illness.
It is occasionally inherited in an AD pattern. Sites of dysfunction are presumed to be the ciliary ganglion and in HolmesÔÇôAdie
syndrome, the dorsal root ganglion involved in re¯Çøex pathways.
It typically a¯Ç£ects young women and presents in one eye in 80%,
though involvement of the second eye typically develops within
months or years.

## Diagnosis

{ Large, regular (irregularity sometimes reported) pupil
(Fig. 19.41A).

¯Çü Symptoms. Patients may notice anisocoria, or may have blurring for near due to impaired accommodation.
¯Çü Signs

{ Explanation: In postganglionic Horner syndrome the dilator pupillae muscle develops denervation hypersensitivity
to adrenergic neurotransmitters due to its dysfunctional
local motor nerve.
¯Çü Hydroxyamphetamine 1%. Two drops are instilled into each
eye. It is slightly more sensitive than phenylephrine testing.

{ ¯Çƒe direct light re¯Çøex is absent or sluggish (Fig. 19.41B).

{ Result: A normal or preganglionic Horner pupil will dilate,
but a postganglionic Horner will not.

{ On slit lamp examination, vermiform movements of the
pupillary border are typically seen.

{ Constriction is also absent or sluggish in response to light
stimulation of the fellow eye (consensual light re¯Çøex ÔÇô Fig.
19.41C).

{ ¯Çƒe pupil responds slowly to near, following which redilatation is also slow.

{ Accommodation may manifest similar tonicity, with
slowed and impaired focusing for near and prolonged refocusing in the distance.

{ Explanation: Hydroxyamphetamine potentiates the release
of noradrenaline from functioning postganglionic nerve
endings. In a lesion of the third-order neurone (postganglionic) there is no release of noradrenaline from the dysfunctional nerve.
¯Çü Adrenaline 0.1% has an action similar to that of phenylephrine.

## Investigation

Specialist neurological or neuro-ophthalmological assessment
should be sought for a con¯Ç×rmed Horner syndrome. An acute presentation should be regarded as an emergency. In contrast, if the
features have been present for more than a year and there are no
other localizing signs then the likely diagnostic yield from further
investigation is very low. ¯Çƒe mainstay of investigation is imaging. CT or MR angiography examining the region from the aortic
arch to the circle of Willis will facilitate exclusion of neck (including carotid), apical lung, thyroid and skull base lesions. MR may
be utilized if greater so¯ÇØ tissue de¯Ç×nition is required, such as to
exclude a brainstem stroke. Plain X-rays and carotid ultrasound
imaging have limited utility.

{ In longstanding cases the pupil may become small (ÔÇÿlittle
old AdieÔÇÖ).
¯Çü Associations include diminished lower limb deep tendon
re¯Çøexes (HolmesÔÇôAdie syndrome ÔÇô Fig. 19.41D) and other
features of autonomic nerve dysfunction such as excessive
sweating (Ross syndrome), orthostatic hypotension and occasionally bowel obstruction or urinary retention. Typical Adietype pupils are o¯ÇØen present in generalized dysautonomia,
with a wide range of causes.
¯Çü Pharmacological testing. Instillation of 0.1ÔÇô0.125% pilocarpine into both eyes leads to constriction of the abnormal
pupil due to denervation hypersensitivity, with the normal
pupil una¯Ç£ected. Some diabetic patients may also show this
response and very occasionally both pupils constrict in normal
individuals.

## Treatment

Treatment is not usually required, but reading glasses may be
used to address near vision di¯ÇÜculties and sunglasses or lowconcentration pilocarpine drops can be used for cosmetic reasons
and to reduce photophobia. ¯Çƒoracic sympathectomy can be considered for excessive sweating.

## Miscellaneous pupillary abnormalities

¯Çü Physiological anisocoria (Fig. 19.42). Anisocoria of around
1 mm is present in about 20% of the normal population. ¯Çƒe
asymmetry persists to the same proportion under di¯Ç£ering
levels of illumination. Exceptionally, apraclonidine or cocaine
testing may be needed to exclude Horner syndrome.
¯Çü Pharmacological mydriasis (Fig. 19.43). Dilatation of one
or both pupils due to instillation of a mydriatic agent can be
inadvertent (e.g. use of eye drops prescribed for someone else,
rubbing the eye a¯ÇØer touching a car sickness scopolamine skin

patch) or deliberate for the purpose of malingering. ¯Çƒe pupil
does not constrict in bright light or on accommodation and
there is no response to any concentration of pilocarpine. ¯Çƒere
are no other neurological features.
¯Çü Argyll Robertson pupils (Fig. 19.44) are caused by neurosyphilis and have been attributed to a dorsal midbrain lesion

¯Çü Benign episodic unilateral mydriasis (BEUM). This

a migrainous phenomenon or a range of heterogeneous
aetiologies. Mydriasis tends to last for minutes or hours
before resolving completely. There may be mild associated

CHIASM

## Anatomy

## Pituitary gland

¯Çƒe sella turcica (Turkish saddle) is a deep saddle-shaped depression in the superior surface of the body of the sphenoid bone in
which the pituitary gland lies (Fig. 19.47). ¯Çƒe roof of the sella
is formed by a fold of dura mater, the diaphragma sellae, which
stretches from the anterior to the posterior clinoids. ¯Çƒe optic
nerves and chiasm lie above the diaphragma sellae. Posteriorly, the
chiasm is continuous with the optic tracts and forms the anterior
wall of the third ventricle. A visual ¯Ç×eld defect in a patient with
a pituitary tumour therefore generally indicates suprasellar extension. Tumours less than 10 mm in diameter (microadenomas) tend
to remain con¯Ç×ned to the sella, whereas those larger than 10 mm

not. (Courtesy of JJ Kanski, from Signs in Ophthalmology:
Causes and Di¯Ç×erential Diagnosis, Mosby 2010.)

(macroadenomas) o¯ÇØen extend outside the sella. Causes of chiasmal disease are listed in Table 19.6

TIP A visual ¯Çƒeld defect in a patient with a pituitary tumour
usually indicates suprasellar extension.

## Parachiasmal vascular structures

¯Çü Venous. The cavernous sinuses lie lateral to the sella so that
horizontally extending pituitary tumours affect the cavernous sinus and may damage the intracavernous parts of the
third, fourth and sixth cranial nerves. Conversely, aneurysms arising from the intracavernous part of the internal
carotid artery may erode into the sella and mimic pituitary
tumours.

## Physiology

## Pituitary hormones

¯Çƒe lobules of the anterior part of the pituitary gland are composed of six cell types. Five of these secrete hormones and the sixth
(follicular cell) has no secretory function. Pituitary adenomas of
mixed-cell type are common; any of the six cell types may proliferate to produce an adenoma. ¯Çƒe current classi¯Ç×cation of pituitary
tumours is discussed further below.
¯Çü ¯Ç×e anterior pituitary lobe is under the control of various
inhibiting and promoting factors synthesized in the hypothalamus, passing to the pituitary through the hypothalamusÔÇô
hypophyseal portal system:

{ Human growth hormone (HGH or GH) or somatotropin.

{ Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH).

{ Luteinizing hormone (LH).

{ Prolactin (PRL).

{ ¯Çƒyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH).

{ Adrenocorticotrophic hormone (ACTH), which regulates
blood cortisol levels.

## Ophthalmic features of large adenomas

Patients with a large pituitary lesion may ¯Ç×rst present with vague
visual symptoms. A low threshold should be adopted for visual
¯Ç×eld assessment in patients with unexplained unilateral central
visual impairment and chronic headache.

TIP A visual ¯Çƒeld test should be undertaken as the initial
step in a patient with unexplained unilateral central visual
impairment, in order to exclude compressive optic neuropathy.

## Investigation

¯Çü MRI with gadolinium contrast (Fig. 19.54) utilizing multiple planes and thin sections demonstrates the relationship
between a mass lesion and the chiasm and is usually the preferred imaging modality. Adenomas are typically hypointense
on T1 and hyperintense on T2 images.
¯Çü CT will demonstrate enlargement or erosion of the sella.
¯Çü Endocrinological evaluation is complex, particularly as combined hormonal over- and under-secretion may be present and
is usually undertaken by an endocrinologist.

Prolactin-secreting (lactotrophic) adenoma

Lactotrophic adenoma or prolactinoma (formerly known as chromophobe adenoma, though lesions can be weakly acidophilic) is
the most common pituitary adenoma. Patients are typically young
to middle-aged adults. In women excessive prolactin secretion
leads to the infertilityÔÇôamenorrhoeaÔÇôgalactorrhoea syndrome and
in men may cause hypogonadism, impotence, sterility, decreased
libido and occasionally gynaecomastia and galactorrhoea. Up to
95% remain as microadenomas, though those that do not may
present initially with visual features.

## Corticotrophic adenoma

A corticotrophic adenoma (a variant of basophil adenoma)
secretes ACTH and causes Cushing disease (Cushing syndrome
refers to the clinical picture of increased blood cortisol from any
cause), which may include central obesity and moon face, cutaneous striae, pigmentation and other features such as hypertension.

## Somatotrophic adenoma

Somatotrophic (acidophil) tumours secrete excessive GH, causing
acromegaly in adults (once bone elongation has been completed)
and gigantism in children. Presentation is in middle age with

802 Chiasm

(dark blue hatched area).

pituitary fossa and compressing the optic chiasm. ¯ÇØA¯Ç× Sagittal and ¯ÇØB¯Ç× coronal images.

features including enlargement of the head, hands, feet and tongue,
coarseness of features with prominent supraorbital ridges and
nasolabial folds (Fig. 19.55), enlargement of the jaw with dental
malocclusion and hirsutism in females. ¯Çƒere are many complications, including diabetes mellitus, hypertension, cardiomyopathy
and carpal tunnel syndrome.

## Treatment of pituitary adenomas

¯Çü Observation may be appropriate for incidentally discovered
and clinically silent tumours.
¯Çü Medical therapy is usually the initial step and consists of the
reduction in tumour size and secretion using agents such as
dopamine agonists (e.g. cabergoline and the older bromocriptine) and somatostatin analogues such as octreotide, with supplementary hormonal correction as appropriate.
¯Çü Surgery consists of tumour debulking rather than complete excision and is usually carried out endoscopically via a

trans-sphenoidal approach through a gum incision behind
the upper lip. Indications include the failure or intolerance of
medical management and sometimes decompression for acute
visual loss (see below). Visual ¯Ç×eld improvement is fastest in
the earliest weeks and months following surgery.
¯Çü Radiotherapy is rarely employed due to the risk of complications. Newer techniques include intensity-modulated radiation therapy and stereotactic radiosurgery.
¯Çü Monitoring. Long-term ophthalmological review is required,
with serial assessment of visual function.

## Pituitary apoplexy

Pituitary apoplexy (PA) is caused by acute haemorrhage into or
infarction of the pituitary gland and is usually associated with a
previously undiagnosed adenoma. Sheehan syndrome is infarction of the pituitary usually associated with childbirth and is

A B

generally regarded as a form of PA. PA typically manifests with the
sudden onset of a severe headache, nausea and vomiting, sometimes with meningism and occasionally reduced consciousness or
stroke. ¯Çƒere is o¯ÇØen reduced VA and/or a bitemporal hemianopia
depending on the anatomical e¯Ç£ects of the lesion. Double vision
due to compromise of the adjacent ocular motor nerves is common. Acute hormonal insu¯ÇÜciency can lead to life-threatening
complications such as an Addisonian crisis. Investigations include
MR, urgent visual ¯Ç×eld testing and hormonal assessment. Acute
medical management, including hormone administration and surgical decompression, may be necessary.

## Craniopharyngioma

Craniopharyngioma is a slow-growing tumour arising from vestigial remnants of the Rathke pouch along the pituitary stalk.
A¯Ç£ected children frequently present with dwar¯Ç×sm, delayed sexual
development and obesity due to interference with hypothalamic
function. Adults usually present with visual impairment.
Visual ¯Ç×eld defects are complex and may be due to involvement of the optic nerves, chiasm or tracts.
¯Çƒe initial defect frequently involves both inferotemporal
¯Ç×elds because the tumour compresses the chiasm from above and
behind, damaging the upper nasal ¯Ç×bres (see Fig. 19.52). MRI
shows a solid tumour that appears isointense on T1 images (Fig.
19.56). Cystic components appear hyperintense on T1 images.
Treatment is mainly surgical, but recurrences are common.

## Meningioma

Intracranial meningiomas typically a¯Ç£ect middle-aged women.
Visual ¯Ç×eld defects and clinical signs depend on the location of the
tumour (Fig. 19.57).
¯Çü Tuberculum sellae meningiomas o¯ÇØen produce a junctional
scotoma (see above and Fig. 19.57) due to their location.
¯Çü Sphenoidal ridge tumours compress the optic nerve early
if the tumour is located medially and late if the lateral aspect
of the sphenoid bone and middle cranial fossa are involved

supra-sellar region of a patient with a large craniopharyngioma. The optic chiasm is compressed to the extent that it
cannot be clearly distinguished on the surface of the tumour.

(Fig. 19.58A). A classic ¯Ç×nding in the latter is fullness in the
temporal fossa due to hyperostosis (Fig. 19.58B).
¯Çü Olfactory groove meningioma may cause loss of the sense of
smell, as well as optic nerve compression.
¯Çü Treatment usually consists of surgery, but radiotherapy is
sometimes required. Observation alone may be adequate.

RETROCHIASMAL PATHWAYS

## Optic tracts

## Overview

## Retrochiasmal pathology results in partial or total binocular visual

¯Ç×eld defects involving contralateral visual space. ¯Çƒis hemianopia
(hemianopsia) involving the same side of the ¯Ç×eld in both eyes is
homonymous, in contradistinction to the bitemporal hemianopia
seen in chiasmal compression, which produces heteronymous loss,
with opposite sides of the visual ¯Ç×eld a¯Ç£ected in each eye.

804 Retrochiasmal Pathways

## Congruity

A homonymous hemianopia may be incomplete or complete. In
the context of incomplete hemianopia, congruity refers to how
closely the extent and pattern of ¯Ç×eld loss in one eye matches that
of the other. Almost identical ¯Ç×eld defects in either eye are therefore highly congruous, while mismatching right and le¯ÇØ visual
¯Ç×eld defects are incongruous. Hemianopia secondary to pathology in the anterior retrochiasmal visual pathways is characteristically incongruous, while that due to pathology further back
(e.g. the posterior optic radiations) manifests a higher degree of
congruity.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Homonymous hemianopia

{ ¯Çƒe causes of optic tract disease are similar to those a¯Ç£ecting the chiasm but the tract is particularly vulnerable when
the chiasm is pre¯Ç×xed (see above).
¯Çü Wernicke hemianopic pupil

{ ¯Çƒe optic tracts arise at the posterior aspect of the chiasm,
diverge and extend posteriorly around the cerebral peduncles, to terminate in the lateral geniculate bodies.

{ Each optic tract contains crossed ¯Ç×bres from the contralateral nasal hemiretina and uncrossed ¯Ç×bres from the
ipsilateral temporal hemiretina.

{ Nerve ¯Ç×bres originating from corresponding retinal elements are, however, not closely aligned.

{ Homonymous hemianopia caused by optic tract lesions is
therefore characteristically incongruous.

{ Lesions of the lateral geniculate body also produce asymmetrical hemianopic defects.

LE RE

## Junctional scotoma

## Tuberculum sellae meningioma

{ ¯Çƒe optic tracts contain both visual and pupillomotor
¯Ç×bres. ¯Çƒe visual ¯Ç×bres terminate in the lateral geniculate
body but the pupillary ¯Ç×bres leave the optic tract anterior
to the lateral geniculate body, projecting through the brachium of the superior colliculus to terminate in the pretectal nuclei.

{ An optic tract lesion may therefore give rise to an a¯Ç£erent
pupillary conduction defect.

{ Characteristically, the pupillary light re¯Çøex will be normal
when the una¯Ç£ected hemiretina is stimulated and absent
when the involved hemiretina is stimulated (i.e. light is
shone from the hemianopic side).

¯Çü Contralateral pyramidal signs may occur when an optic tract

## Optic radiations

## Anatomy

¯Çƒe optic radiations extend from the lateral geniculate body to
the striate cortex, which is located on the medial aspect of the
occipital lobe, above and below the calcarine ¯Ç×ssure (Fig. 19.59).
As the radiations pass posteriorly, ¯Ç×bres from corresponding
retinal elements lie progressively closer together. For this reason,
incomplete hemianopia caused by posterior radiation lesions are
more congruous than those involving the anterior radiations.
Because these ¯Ç×bres are third-order neurones that originate in
the lateral geniculate body, lesions of the optic radiations do not
produce optic atrophy. ¯Çƒe optic radiations and visual cortex
have a dual blood supply from the middle and posterior cerebral
arteries.

## Temporal radiations

¯Çü Visual ¯Çƒeld defect consists of a contralateral superior homonymous quadrantanopia (ÔÇÿpie in the skyÔÇÖ), because the inferior
¯Ç×bres of the optic radiations, which subserve the upper visual
¯Ç×elds, ¯Ç×rst sweep antero-inferiorly (Meyer loop) into the temporal lobe around the anterior tip of the temporal horn of the
lateral ventricle (see ÔÇÿaÔÇÖ in Fig. 19.59).
¯Çü Associated features are likely to include contralateral hemisensory disturbance and hemiparesis, because the temporal
radiations pass very close to the sensory and motor ¯Ç×bres of
the internal capsule before passing posteriorly and re-joining
the superior ¯Ç×bres. Other features of temporal lobe disease
include paroxysmal olfactory and gustatory hallucinations
(uncinate ¯Ç×ts), formed visual hallucinations and seizures,
with receptive dysphasia if the dominant hemisphere is
involved.

a. Temporal radiation lesion

## Lateral ventricle

b. Anterior parietal
radiation lesion

c. Main radiation lesion

## Temporal horn of
lateral ventricle

## Anterior parietal radiations

¯Çü Visual ¯Çƒeld defect consists of a contralateral inferior homonymous quadrantanopia (ÔÇÿpie on the ¯ÇøoorÔÇÖ) because the superior ¯Ç×bres of the radiations, which subserve the inferior visual
¯Ç×elds, proceed directly posteriorly through the parietal lobe to
the occipital cortex. However, a lesion involving only the anterior parietal part of the radiations is rare. Hemianopia resulting from a parietal lobe lesion tends to be relatively congruous
(see ÔÇÿbÔÇÖ in Fig. 19.59).
¯Çü Associated features of dominant parietal lobe disease include
acalculia, agraphia, le¯ÇØÔÇôright disorientation and ¯Ç×nger agnosia. Non-dominant lobe lesions may cause dressing and constitutional apraxia and spatial neglect.

## Main radiations

Deep in the parietal lobe, the optic radiations lie just external to
the trigone and the occipital horn of the lateral ventricle. Lesions in
this area usually cause a complete homonymous hemianopia (see
ÔÇÿcÔÇÖ in Fig. 19.59).
¯Çü Optokinetic nystagmus (OKN), elicited with a rotating
striped optokinetic drum, may be useful in localizing the cause
of an isolated homonymous hemianopia.

{ Physiological OKN involves smooth pursuit of a target,
followed momentarily by a saccade in the opposite direction to ¯Ç×xate on the next target.

{ If a homonymous hemianopia is due to a lesion in the parietal lobe, the smooth pursuit pathways towards the side of
the lesion are likely to be a¯Ç£ected, making this component
of OKN defective. OKN will therefore be asymmetrical:
erratic when the drum is rotated towards the side of the
lesion, but regular when the drum is rotated away from the
side of the lesion.

{ If the lesion is in the occipital lobe, the smooth pursuit
pathways are intact and OKN will be symmetrical ÔÇô this is
the Cogan dictum, which also states that the parietal lobe
lesion is more likely to be a tumour and the occipital lesion
an infarction.

d. Anterior visual
cortex lesion

806 Cerebral Visual Impairment (CVI)

## Occipital cortex

## Clinical features

¯Çü Visual ¯Çƒeld defects

{ In the striate cortex the peripheral visual ¯Ç×elds are represented anteriorly. ¯Çƒis part of the occipital lobe is supplied
by a branch of the posterior cerebral artery.

{ Central macular vision is represented posteriorly just
lateral to the tip of the calcarine cortex, an area supplied
mainly by a branch of the middle cerebral artery. Occlusion of the posterior cerebral artery will therefore tend
to produce a macular-sparing congruous homonymous
hemianopia (see ÔÇÿdÔÇÖ in Fig. 19.59).

{ Damage to the tip of the occipital cortex, as might occur
from a head injury, tends to give rise to congruous, homonymous, macular defects (see ÔÇÿeÔÇÖ in Fig. 19.59), although
macular sparing may sometimes occur with vascular
lesions of the occipital lobe.

## Balint syndrome

## Balint syndrome refers to the combination of simultanagnosia

(inability to discern the overall impression of an image whilst
distinguishing individual components), optic ataxia (defective
visually-directed reaching) and ocular apraxia (impairment of
voluntary saccades). It is typically due to parieto-occipital disease,
with a range of causes reported.

## Posterior reversible encephalopathy
syndrome

Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) or reversible posterior leukoencephalopathy syndrome refers to a clinical
presentation thought to result from vascular endothelial dysfunction seen in patients with malignant hypertension or eclampsia,
treatment with some drugs (e.g. tacrolimus, ciclosporin) and rarely
other associations such as autoimmune disease. Cortical visual
de¯Ç×cits can occasionally be the presenting feature, others including headache and seizures.

CEREBRAL VISUAL IMPAIRMENT (CVI)

## Introduction

ÔÇÿCerebral visual impairmentÔÇÖ is the term used to describe visual
disability which cannot be attributed to a disorder of the eyes and
anterior visual pathways. It is a consequence of abnormality in the
parts of the brain that process visual information. CVI is the commonest cause of visual disability in children living in industrialised
countries and a¯Ç£ects up to 3% of mainstream school children. It
can be a di¯ÇÜcult diagnosis to make in subtle cases where the visual
acuity is normal, particularly as brain imaging may reveal no signi¯Ç×cant abnormality. CVI can limit social interaction, mobility
and learning.

## Causes

¯Çü Children: especially premature birth and its cerebral complications (hypoxia and ischaemia), neonatal hypoglycemia, head
injury, infections (meningitis and encephalitis), hydrocephalus, developmental brain defects.
¯Çü Adults: head injury, stroke, dementia.

## Anatomical basis and outcomes of visual brain
injury

¯Çü ¯Ç×e posterior parietal lobes are connected to the occipital
lobes by the superior longitudinal fasciculus. ¯Çƒis functional
pathway is called the ÔÇÿdorsal streamÔÇÖ, which creates a subconscious real-time dynamic three-dimensional reproduction
of our surroundings. ¯Çƒis is mapped to body position and
allows accurate visually guided movement and visual search.
Balint syndrome (when severe) and dorsal stream dysfunction
(when less profound) results from bilateral posterior parietal
pathology.
¯Çü Bilateral middle temporal lobe pathology slightly anterior to
the occipital lobes causes di¯ÇÜculty in seeing moving targets
(dyskinetopsia).

{ ¯Çƒe front of the calcarine cortex subserves the temporal
extremity of the visual ¯Ç×eld of the contralateral eye, the
area of visual space that extends beyond the ¯Ç×eld of binocular single vision and is perceived monocularly. A lesion in
this area may therefore give rise to a monocular temporal
¯Ç×eld defect in the contralateral eye, known as a temporal
crescent.
¯Çü Associated features of visual cortex disease (cortical blindness) include formed visual hallucinations, denial of blindness
(Anton syndrome) and the Riddoch phenomenon (only moving visual targets perceived).

## Causes

¯Çü Stroke in the territory of the posterior cerebral artery is
responsible for over 90% of homonymous hemianopia with no
other neurological de¯Ç×cit.
¯Çü Other causes include trauma, tumours and rarely migraine.
A range of uncommon in¯Çøammatory (including autoimmune
and infective), degenerative and toxic disorders can also be
causative, but will usually manifest with more widespread
neurological features. Imaging and lumbar puncture are typically key to diagnosis. Mimicking by retinal disease such as
acute zonal occult outer retinopathy (AZOOR) may occur.

## Benson syndrome

Benson syndrome (posterior cortical atrophy) is a rare condition
o¯ÇØen referred to as a visual variant of Alzheimer disease. Patients
may have normal VA, anterior and posterior segment examination, but commonly abnormal colour vision, homonymous ¯Ç×eld
defects, visual agnosia, alexia and acalculia. ¯Çƒe diagnosis is
commonly missed. Other degenerative conditions o¯ÇØen exhibiting early occipital cortical involvement include Alzheimer and
CreutzfeldtÔÇôJakob disease; presentation in the latter can be with
acute visual symptoms only. Electroretinography may be abnormal. In immunocompromised states, including drug-induced,
progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy can present with isolated occipital features.

¯Çü Infarction of both occipital lobes that spares the middle
temporal lobes can conversely result in either an ability to see
moving objects but not static targets, or result in the Riddoch
phenomenon, which occurs when a person who is otherwise
blind can perceive moving objects. Retained ability to recognize emotion conveyed by facial expression despite cortical
blindness is called ÔÇÿe¯Ç£ective blindsightÔÇÖ.

## Symptoms

¯Çü Di¯Çøculty with visual complexity: di¯ÇÜculty in seeing an
object when other stimuli are present (e.g. sounds) or where
there is a visually complex environment.
¯Çü Visual ¯Çƒeld preference. ¯Çƒe child prefers to look at objects in
a particular visual ¯Ç×eld.
¯Çü Absence of visually guided reach: impairment of a childÔÇÖs
ability to look at an object while reaching for it.
¯Çü Visual latency. ¯Çƒe visual responses are slow and o¯ÇØen delayed.
¯Çü Preference for familiar objects and rich, highly saturated
colours like red and yellow.
¯Çü Staring. ¯Çƒese children may have a need to look at rapidly
moving objects (e.g. ¯Ç×xating on a rapidly moving fan) and
non-purposeful gaze (e.g. tendency to stare at a light source).
¯Çü Atypical visual re¯Ç£exes. For example, delayed or absent protective blink response.

## Management

¯Çü Key issues. Early intervention, educational support and visual
rehabilitation are central to helping these individuals develop
and learn.
¯Çü Identify the speci¯Çƒc visual di¯Çøculty. Take a structured history from the patient/child and a family member.
¯Çü Provide an explanation for the origin of the symptoms.
¯Çü Devise strategies to compensate for the impairment, preferably by using a trained multi-disciplinary team.

OCULAR MOTOR NERVES

## Third nerve

## Nuclear complex

¯Çƒe nuclear complex of the third (oculomotor) nerve is situated
in the midbrain at the level of the superior colliculus, ventral to
the Sylvian aqueduct (Fig. 19.60). It is composed of the following
paired and unpaired sub nuclei:
¯Çü Levator subnucleus is an unpaired caudal midline structure
that innervates both levator muscles. Lesions con¯Ç×ned to this
area will therefore give rise to bilateral ptosis.
¯Çü Superior rectus subnuclei are paired: each innervates the
respective contralateral superior rectus. A nuclear third nerve
palsy will therefore spare the ipsilateral and a¯Ç£ect the contralateral, superior rectus.
¯Çü Medial rectus, inferior rectus and inferior oblique subnuclei are paired and innervate their corresponding ipsilateral
muscles. Lesions con¯Ç×ned to the nuclear complex are relatively
uncommon. ¯Çƒe most frequent causes are vascular disease,
primary tumours and metastases. Involvement of the paired
medial rectus subnuclei cause a wall-eyed bilateral internuclear ophthalmoplegia (WEBINO), characterized by exotropia
with defective convergence and adduction. Lesions involving
the entire nucleus are o¯ÇØen associated with involvement of the
adjacent and caudal fourth nerve nucleus.

## Fasciculus

¯Çƒe fasciculus consists of e¯Ç£erent ¯Ç×bres that pass from the third
nerve nucleus through the red nucleus and the medial aspect of
the cerebral peduncle (see Fig. 19.60). ¯Çƒey then emerge from the
midbrain and pass into the interpeduncular space. ¯Çƒe causes of
nuclear and fascicular lesions are similar, except that demyelination may a¯Ç£ect the fasciculus.

808 Ocular Motor Nerves

¯Çü Benedikt syndrome involves the fasciculus as it passes through
the red nucleus and is characterized by ipsilateral third nerve
palsy and contralateral extrapyramidal signs such as hemi
tremor.
¯Çü Weber syndrome involves the fasciculus as it passes through
the cerebral peduncle and is characterized by ipsilateral third
nerve palsy and a contralateral hemiparesis.
¯Çü Nothnagel syndrome involves the fasciculus and the superior
cerebellar peduncle and is characterized by ipsilateral third
nerve palsy and cerebellar ataxia.
¯Çü Claude syndrome is a combination of Benedikt and Nothnagel
syndromes.

## Basilar

¯Çƒe basilar part starts as a series of ÔÇÿrootletsÔÇÖ that leave the midbrain
on the medial aspect of the cerebral peduncle, before coalescing
to form the main trunk. ¯Çƒe nerve then passes between the posterior cerebral and superior cerebellar arteries, running lateral to
and parallel with the posterior communicating artery (Fig. 19.61).

## As the nerve traverses the base of the skull along its subarachnoid

course unaccompanied by any other cranial nerve, an isolated
third nerve palsy is commonly basilar. ¯Çƒe following are important causes:
¯Çü Aneurysm of the posterior communicating artery at its junction with the internal carotid artery (Fig. 19.62), typically
presents acutely as a pupil-involving painful third nerve palsy.
In a recent population-based study, compression from an
aneurysm only accounted for 6% of acute third nerve palsies.
Approximately two-thirds had pupillary involvement.
¯Çü Head trauma, resulting in extradural or subdural haematoma,
may cause a tentorial pressure cone with downward herniation of the temporal lobe. ¯Çƒis compresses the third nerve as it
passes over the tentorial edge (Fig. 19.63), initially causing irritative miosis followed by mydriasis and complete third nerve
palsy.

## Intracavernous

¯Çƒe third nerve then enters the cavernous sinus by piercing the
dura just lateral to the posterior clinoid process. Within the sinus,
the third nerve runs in the lateral wall above the fourth nerve (Fig.
19.64). In the anterior part of the cavernous sinus, the nerve divides
into superior and inferior branches that enter the orbit through the
superior orbital ¯Ç×ssure within the annulus of Zinn. ¯Çƒe following
are potential causes of intracavernous third nerve palsy:
¯Çü Diabetes.
¯Çü Pituitary apoplexy (see above).
¯Çü Miscellaneous pathology such as aneurysm, meningioma,
carotid-cavernous ¯Ç×stula and granulomatous in¯Çøammation
(TolosaÔÇôHunt syndrome). Because of its close proximity to
other cranial nerves, intracavernous third nerve lesions are
likely to be associated with involvement of the fourth and sixth
nerves and the ¯Ç×rst division of the ¯Ç×¯ÇØh nerve.

## Intraorbital

¯Çü Superior division innervates the levator and superior rectus
muscles.

## Lesions of the inferior division are characterized by limited

adduction and depression, together with a dilated pupil. Both
superior and inferior division palsies are commonly traumatic or vascular.

Pupillomotor ¯Ç×bres

Between the brainstem and the cavernous sinus, the pupillomotor
parasympathetic ¯Ç×bres are located super¯Ç×cially in the superomedial
part of the third nerve (Fig. 19.65). ¯Çƒey derive their blood supply
from the pial blood vessels, whereas the main interior trunk of the
nerve is supplied by the vasa nervorum. Involvement or sparing of
the pupil is important, as it frequently di¯Ç£erentiates a ÔÇÿsurgicalÔÇÖ from
a ÔÇÿmedicalÔÇÖ lesion:

¯Çü ÔÇÿSurgicalÔÇÖ lesions such as aneurysms, trauma and uncal herniation characteristically involve the pupil by compressing
the pial blood vessels and the super¯Ç×cially located pupillary
¯Ç×bres.
¯Çü ÔÇÿMedicalÔÇÖ lesions such as occur in hypertension and diabetes
usually spare the pupil (Fig. 19.66). ¯Çƒis is because the microangiopathy associated with medical lesions involves the vasa
nervorum, causing ischaemia of the main trunk of the nerve,
leaving the super¯Ç×cial pupillary ¯Ç×bres intact.
¯Çü ¯Ç×ese principles are not infallible as pupillary involvement
may be seen in some microangiopathic palsies, while pupillary sparing does not invariably exclude aneurysm or other
compressive lesion. Pupillary involvement may develop a
few days a¯ÇØer the onset of diplopia as an aneurysm expands.
Exceptionally, pupillary involvement may be the only sign of
third nerve palsy (basal meningitis, uncal herniation). Like
other features of third nerve palsy, pupillary involvement
may be complete or partial, so mild pupillary signs may be
clinically signi¯Ç×cant.

TIP Medical conditions (hypertension, diabetes) tend to
cause a pupil-sparing third nerve palsy, but this principle is not
infallible.

## Signs

Partial involvement will give milder degrees of ophthalmoplegia.
¯Çƒe other cranial nerves and peripheral nervous system should
always be examined.
¯Çü Profound ptosis due to weakness of the levator muscle (Fig.
19.67A).
¯Çü Abduction and depression in the primary position (ÔÇÿdown
and outÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 19.67B) due to unopposed action of the lateral rectus and superior oblique muscles. ¯Çƒe intact superior
oblique muscle also causes intorsion of the eye at rest, which
increases on attempted downgaze.
¯Çü Limited adduction due to medial rectus weakness (Fig.
19.66C and Fig. 19.67C).
¯Çü Normal abduction as the lateral rectus is intact (Fig. 19.66D).
¯Çü Limited elevation due to weakness of the superior rectus and
inferior oblique (Fig. 19.66E and Fig. 19.67D).
¯Çü Limited depression due to weakness of the inferior rectus
(Fig. 19.66F).
¯Çü Dilated pupil and defective accommodation due to parasympathetic palsy (Fig. 19.67).

## Aberrant regeneration

Aberrant regeneration may follow acute traumatic and compressive, but not vascular, third nerve palsies. ¯Çƒis is because the endoneural nerve sheaths, which may be breached in traumatic and
compressive lesions, remain intact with vascular lesions. Bizarre
defects in ocular motility, such as elevation of the upper eyelid on
attempted adduction or depression (the pseudo-Graefe or pseudovon Graefe phenomenon), are caused by misdirected regenerating axons that re-innervate the incorrect extraocular muscle (Fig.
19.68). ¯Çƒe pupil may also be involved.

810 Ocular Motor Nerves

in the primary position; ¯ÇØC¯Ç× limitation of adduction; ¯ÇØD¯Ç× normal abduction; ¯ÇØE¯Ç× limitation of elevation; ¯ÇØF¯Ç× slight limitation of depression.

## Causes of isolated third nerve palsy

Clinical evaluation and imaging facilitate identi¯Ç×cation of a cause
in most cases.
¯Çü Microvascular disease associated with systemic risk factors such as hypertension and diabetes are the most common cause of third nerve palsy, accounting for about 40%
of cases. Marked periorbital pain is often associated, which
is therefore not helpful in distinguishing an aneurysmal
cause. In diabetes-related paresis, motility disturbance is
typically profound, but there is usually (80%) relative or
complete pupil sparing and when pupillary involvement
does occur some degree of reaction to light is almost always
preserved. As a general rule, where there is a complete third
nerve palsy and a normally responsive pupil the cause is significantly more likely to be microvascular than secondary
to an aneurysm.
¯Çü Aneurysm of the posterior communicating artery at its junction with the internal carotid is a very important cause of isolated third nerve palsy with involvement of the pupil. Pain is
o¯ÇØen, though not invariably, present and the extent of pupillary involvement characteristically exceeds the severity of
motility dysfunction. An aneurysm of the internal carotid
within the cavernous sinus tends to also involve other cranial
nerves (see above).
¯Çü Trauma, both direct and secondary to subdural haematoma
with uncal herniation. However, the development of third

nerve palsy following relatively trivial head trauma should
alert the clinician to the possibility of an underlying aneurysm
or tumour.
¯Çü Miscellaneous uncommon causes include tumour, in¯Çøammatory disease such as syphilis, Lyme disease and sarcoidosis, GCA and vasculitis associated with collagen vascular
disorders.
¯Çü Episodic. Brief episodes of third nerve dysfunction with spontaneous recovery may be idiopathic or occur with migraine,
compression, ischaemia and alterations in intracranial pressure. Myasthenia gravis may mimic intermittent pupil-sparing
third nerve paresis.

## Investigation

¯Çü Vascular risk factor assessment similar to that for retinal arterial disease (see Ch. 13). Supplementary investigation may be
required if a rarer aetiology such as infection (e.g. syphilis,
Lyme disease) or vasculitis (including GCA) is suspected. In
some cases, a lumbar puncture may be needed.
¯Çü CT angiography should be performed with great urgency if
the clinical features are suspicious of an expanding aneurysm,
especially if there is marked pupillary involvement with milder
motility dysfunction and only partial ptosis.
¯Çü MRI brain and orbits with venography, including speci¯Ç×c
exclusion of a brainstem stroke or tumour, cavernous sinus or
orbital apex lesion.
¯Çü Conventional cerebral angiography is occasionally indicated.

A B

C D

elevation.

TIP Urgent CT angiography is needed in a patient who
presents with an acute third nerve palsy that involves the pupil,
to exclude an expanding intracranial aneurysm.

## Treatment

A

B

¯ÇØA¯Ç× Lid elevation with reduced adduction on attempted right
gaze; ¯ÇØB¯Ç× lid elevation with depression of the eye (pseudo
von Graefe sign). (Courtesy of ADN Murray.)

¯Çü Observation is usually appropriate in presumed microvascular
cases; the majority will resolve over weeks or months.
Temporary (e.g. Fresnel stick-on) prisms may be useful if the
angle of deviation is small, but uniocular occlusion may be
necessary to avoid diplopia if the ptosis component is partial
or recovering. Botulinum toxin injection into the uninvolved
lateral rectus muscle is sometimes used to prevent its contracture when recovery time is prolonged.
¯Çü Surgical treatment of the ocular motility element and ptosis should be contemplated only a¯ÇØer spontaneous improvement has ceased, usually not earlier than 6ÔÇô12 months from
onset. Numerous innovative surgical techniques have been
described.

812 Ocular Motor Nerves

## Fourth nerve

## Anatomy

¯Çƒe fourth (trochlear) cranial nerve (Fig. 19.69) supplies only the
superior oblique muscle.
¯Çü Key features

{ It is a very long and slender nerve, increasing its
vulnerability.

{ It is the only cranial nerve to emerge from the dorsal aspect
of the brain.

{ It is the only decussated (crossed) cranial nerve besides the
optic nerve, innervating the superior oblique muscle contralateral to its nucleus.
