BENIGN EPIBULBAR TUMOURS

## Conjunctival naevus

## Introduction

A conjunctival naevus is the most common melanocytic conjunctival tumour. ¯Çƒe overall risk of malignant transformation is less
than 1%. Treatment by excision is usually for cosmesis, but sometimes for irritation or a suspicion of malignancy. ¯Çƒe histological
appearance is similar to that of a cutaneous naevus, but as there is
no conjunctival dermis, subepithelial and stromal replace dermal
in the nomenclature:
¯Çü Compound naevus is characterized by the presence of naevus cells at the epithelialÔÇôsubepithelial junction and within the
subepithelial stroma, o¯Ç×en with epithelial inclusions such as
cysts and goblet cells (Fig. 20.1A).
¯Çü Subepithelial lesions remain localized.
¯Çü Junctional naevus is uncommon and consists of nests of naevus cells at the epithelialÔÇôsubepithelial junction (Fig. 20.1B).

## Clinical features

¯Çü Symptoms. ¯Çƒe lesion is initially noticed in the ¯ÇØrst or second
decade.
¯Çü Signs

{ A solitary slightly or moderately elevated pigmented or
partially pigmented lesion of variable size, most frequently
juxtalimbal. Over half contain small cysts (Fig. 20.1C and
D).

Large lesions may cause irritation, interfere with lid closure or
encroach onto the cornea.

## Treatment

Small lesions may resolve spontaneously. Large lesions are treated
by excision, sometimes with cryotherapy to the base and the surrounding area. Options for recurrences include subconjunctival
interferon alfa, carbon dioxide laser vaporization, topical mitomycin C and oral cimetidine.

## Limbal dermoid

A limbal dermoid is a choristoma (a mass of histologically normal
tissue in an abnormal location) consisting of a mass of collagenous
tissue containing dermal elements, which is covered by strati¯ÇØed
squamous epithelium (Fig. 20.3A). Presentation is in early childhood, with a smooth, yellowish, so¯Ç× subconjunctival mass commonly located at the inferotemporal limbus, o¯Ç×en with protruding
hair (Fig. 20.3B). Lesions are occasionally very large and may virtually encircle the limbus (Fig. 20.3C). Treatment is indicated for
cosmesis, chronic irritation, dellen formation and amblyopia from
astigmatism or involvement of the visual axis. A small dermoid
can undergo simple excision, but lamellar keratosclerectomy may
be required for large lesions.

## Systemic associations

¯Çü Goldenhar syndrome (oculoauriculovertebral spectrum ÔÇô

{ A naevus is mobile over the underlying sclera.

{ ¯Çƒe extent of pigmentation is variable. Absence of pigmentation is relatively common (Fig. 20.1E).

{ ¯Çƒe plica, fornix and caruncle (Fig. 20.1F) are uncommon
locations.

{ Systemic features include hypoplasia of the malar, maxillary and mandibular regions, macrostomia and microtia,
preauricular and facial skin tags, hemivertebrae (usually
cervical), mental handicap, cardiac, renal and central nervous system (CNS) anomalies.

{ A naevus may become in¯Ç£amed, especially in children
and adolescents, and this may be mistaken for malignant
change.
¯Çü Signs of potential malignancy

{ An unusual site such as the palpebral or forniceal
conjunctiva.

{ Ocular features, apart from a dermoid, include upper
lid notching or coloboma, microphthalmos and disc
coloboma.
¯Çü Treacher Collins syndrome (see Ch. 2).
¯Çü Linear naevus sebaceous of Jadassohn.

{ Systemic features include warty or scaly cutaneous
lesions, infantile spasms, CNS anomalies and developmental delay.

{ Ocular features, apart from a dermoid, include ptosis,
cloudy cornea, lid colobomas, fundus colobomas and
microphthalmos.

{ Prominent feeder vessels.

{ Sudden growth or increase in pigmentation.

{ Development a¯Ç×er the second decade.

## Conjunctival papilloma

Conjunctival papillomas are strongly associated with human papillomavirus infection, especially types 6 and 11. Histopathology
reveals a ¯ÇØbrovascular core covered by an irregular proliferation of
non-keratinized strati¯ÇØed squamous epithelium containing goblet
cells (Fig. 20.2A).

## Clinical features

Lesions are sessile (wide base and ¯Ç£attish pro¯ÇØle ÔÇô Fig. 20.2B and
C) or pedunculated (frond-like ÔÇô Fig. 20.2D) and are frequently
located in the juxtalimbal area, fornix or the caruncle. ¯Çƒey are
usually solitary, but may be multiple.

## Dermolipoma

A dermolipoma is similar in composition to a solid dermoid but
also contains fatty tissue. Presentation tends to be in adult life as
a so¯Ç× yellowish subconjunctival mass near the outer canthus (Fig.
20.4A). ¯Çƒe surface is usually keratinized and may exhibit hairs.

## Occasionally the lesion may extend into the orbit or anteriorly

towards the limbus. Treatment is generally avoided because of
the possibility of complications arising from the surgery, such as
scarring, ptosis, dry eye and ocular motility problems. In selected
cases, debulking the anterior portion may improve cosmesis with

A B

C D

E F

naevus histology ÔÇô see text; (C) pigmented naevus; (D) cystic pigmented naevus; (E) partially pigmented naevus; (F) caruncular location. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from
Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs A and B.)

lower risk. It is critical to distinguish a dermolipoma from a prominent lacrimal gland lobe and from orbital fat prolapse (Fig. 20.4B).
Myxoma and lymphoma can also present in a similar fashion (Fig.
20.4C and D).

## Pyogenic granuloma

A pyogenic granuloma is a fibrovascular proliferative response
to a conjunctival insult such as surgery or trauma, or occurs

846 Benign Epibulbar Tumours

A B

C D

papilloma with feeder vessels; (D) pedunculated papilloma.

in association with a chalazion or foreign body incarceration.

## Histology shows granulation tissue with both acute and chronic
inflammatory cells and a proliferation of small blood vessels

(the term ÔÇÿpyogenic granulomaÔÇÖ is a misnomer as the lesion is
neither pyogenic nor granulomatous). Presentation is typically
a few weeks after surgery for chalazion, strabismus or enucleation, with a rapidly growing dark pink fleshy conjunctival mass
(Fig. 20.5A). Treatment with topical steroids is often successful,
but resistant cases require excision. The differential diagnosis
includes suture granuloma (Fig. 20.5B), which can often be
large and mistaken for a malignant lesion and Tenon capsule
granuloma or cyst.

## Miscellaneous benign epibulbar
tumours

¯Çü Epibulbar telangiectasia (Fig. 20.6A) may be associated with
SturgeÔÇôWeber syndrome.
¯Çü Reactive pseudo-epitheliomatous hyperplasia is a rapidly
growing white juxtalimbal hyperkeratotic nodule (Fig. 20.6B)
that develops secondary to irritation.

¯Çü Melanocytoma is a rare congenital lesion. It manifests as a
slowly enlarging black lump (Fig. 20.6C) that cannot be moved
freely over the globe.
¯Çü Epibulbar angioma is a rare late consequence of radiation
treatment (Fig. 20.6D).

## Benign melanosis

Benign conjunctival epithelial melanosis (conjunctival hypermelanosis) is a normal variant, more common in dark-skinned individuals (over 90% occur in Black people and 5% occur in White
individuals) due to the presence of excess melanin within basal
layer conjunctival epithelial melanocytes. Melanocyte numbers are
normal, that is, there is no melanocytic hyperplasia. It may have a
protective e¯Çøect against neoplasia. Benign melanosis appears during the ¯ÇØrst few years of life and becomes static by early adulthood.
Both eyes are a¯Çøected but involvement may be asymmetrical.
Areas of ¯Ç£at, patchy, brownish pigmentation may be seen throughout the conjunctiva but are o¯Ç×en concentrated at the limbus (Fig.
20.7A) and around perforating branches of vessels or nerves as
they enter the sclera (Fig. 20.7B). ¯Çƒe pigmented epithelium moves

C D

Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

cysts are present.

MALIGNANT AND PREMALIGNANT
EPIBULBAR TUMOURS

Primary acquired melanosis/conjunctival melanocytic intraepithelial
neoplasia

## Introduction

Approximately 75% of conjunctival melanomas (see below) arise
in areas of melanocytic hyperplasia. Recent trends in the description of pigmented conjunctival lesions have led to controversy
relating to terminology. Some classi¯ÇØcations have historically
used the term primary acquired melanosis (PAM) to encompass
both benign epithelial melanosis (see above) and melanocytosis/melanocytic hyperplasia (with and without atypia), whilst
others have restricted its use to the latter category. To avoid

this ambiguity, the term conjunctival melanocytic intraepithelial neoplasia (C-MIN) may be preferred for lesions exhibiting
proliferation of melanocytes, with PAM reserved for clinical
description before a histological diagnosis has been established.
¯Çƒe di¯Çøerential diagnosis includes conjunctival naevus, benign
melanosis, congenital ocular melanocytosis (see below), secondary pigmentation in Addison disease and pigmented squamous
cell carcinoma.
¯Çü PAM without cellular atypia or with mild atypia is a benign
intraepithelial proliferation of epithelial melanocytes (Fig.
20.8A) with little or no risk of malignant transformation.
¯Çü PAM with severe atypia can be regarded as melanoma in
situ that has a substantial chance of progression to invasive
melanoma over several years. ¯Çƒe risk is higher the greater
the extent of the lesion when measured in clock hours. Severe
atypia is present in a minority.
¯Çü C-MIN is graded from 0 to 10 according to degree of atypia
and spread, 0 corresponding to an absence of any melanocyte
proliferation or atypia (i.e. melanosis only) and 5 to conjunctival melanoma in situ.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Treatment

¯Çü Symptoms. A pigmented area is noticed on the surface of one
or both (10%) eyes at a median age of 56 years, usually in a
White individual.
¯Çü Signs

{ Uni- or multifocal ¯Ç£at areas of irregular golden-brown to
dark chocolate-coloured epithelial pigmentation, typically
involving the limbus and interpalpebral region (Fig. 20.8B
and C). PAM sine pigmento has been reported.

{ Because any part of the conjunctiva may be a¯Çøected, it is
important to evert the eyelids (Fig. 20.8D). C-MIN may
also extend onto the cornea.

{ Observation of small (less than one clock hour) lesions
may be appropriate, but some specialists advocate excision
biopsy of every lesion.

{ Excision biopsy is generally preferred to incisional biopsy
if possible (see under conjunctival melanoma below for
surgical approach).

{ Double freezeÔÇôthaw cryotherapy may be administered to
the excision site and/or following histological con¯ÇØrmation of C-MIN postoperative topical mitomycin C, e.g.

performed.

{ Long-term follow-up is mandatory in all cases.

epithelium, followed by topical mitomycin C.

## Conjunctival melanoma

Conjunctival melanoma is an uncommon but serious condition,
accounting for about 12% of conjunctival tumours and 2% of all
ocular malignancies. Around 75% arise from an area of PAM,
about 25% from a pre-existing junctional or compound naevus and rarely, de novo. Presentation is o¯Ç×en in the sixth decade,
though patients with the rare dysplastic naevus syndrome develop
multiple melanomas at a considerably younger age. ¯Çƒe di¯Çøerential
diagnosis includes naevus, ciliary body melanoma with extraocular
extension, melanocytoma and pigmented conjunctival squamous
carcinoma. ¯Çƒe overall mortality is up to 19% at 5 years and 30%
at 10 years. Metastasis occurs in 20ÔÇô30%, particularly to regional
lymph nodes, lung, brain and liver. Factors associated with a poor
prognosis include caruncular, forniceal or lid margin location and
tumour thickness of 2 mm or more.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Histology shows melanomatous cellular atypia with invasion
of the subepithelial stroma (Fig. 20.9A).
¯Çü Appearance. A black or grey vascularized nodule that may
be ¯ÇØxed to the episclera. ¯Çƒe limbus is a common site, but
a melanoma may arise anywhere in the conjunctiva (Fig.
20.9BÔÇôD). A conjunctival melanoma may extend into the
eyelid (Fig. 20.9E and F). Association with PAM/C-MIN is
very common.
¯Çü Amelanotic tumours may give rise to diagnostic di¯ÇÜculty.
¯Çü B-scan ultrasonography may be helpful in characterization of
the lesion.
¯Çü Systemic screening consists of regular general examination,
liver function testing and ultrasound, chest X-ray and possibly
whole-body positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) imaging where available.
¯Çü Sentinel lymph node biopsy may be helpful in staging, though
its place has not yet been fully de¯ÇØned.

## Treatment

¯Çü Circumscribed lesions are treated by excision with a wide
margin. As tumour seeding can occur during excision, contact with the tumour itself should be avoided and fresh instruments used to close the conjunctival defect.
¯Çü Adjunctive radiotherapy is routinely administered by some
authorities, even if histology suggests that excision is complete.
Cryotherapy to the bed and surrounding tissue is an alternative. Proton beam radiotherapy can be applied if the caruncle
or fornix is involved.
¯Çü Di¯Çøuse melanoma associated with extensive PAM/C-MIN is
treated by excision of localized nodules with mitomycin C or
cryotherapy to the di¯Çøuse component.
¯Çü Orbital recurrences are treated by local resection and radiotherapy. Exenteration may not improve survival and is therefore reserved for patients with extensive and aggressive disease
when the eye cannot be preserved.

¯Çü ¯Çƒe drug vemurafenib improves survival in patients with
metastatic disease when the BRAF V600E mutation is present
(50% of primary and metastatic conjunctival melanomas).
¯Çü Multifocal disease, non-limbal tumour location, tumour margin involvement and lack of adjunctive treatment are associated with a greater likelihood of recurrence.

## Ocular surface squamous neoplasia

## Introduction

Ocular surface squamous neoplasia (OSSN) describes a spectrum of slowly progressive, benign, premalignant and malignant
epithelial lesions of the conjunctiva and cornea. Older adults are
usually a¯Çøected unless a predisposing systemic condition is present. Squamous carcinoma accounts for about 14% of conjunctival
tumours. ¯Çƒere is a male predominance in Europe and the USA,
but in sub-Saharan Africa it is more common in women, particularly if they are HIV-positive. Risk factors include ultraviolet light
exposure, a pale complexion, ciclosporin, smoking, petroleum
product exposure, acquired immunode¯ÇØciency syndrome (AIDS)
and xeroderma pigmentosum. Human papillomavirus infection
(especially type 16) has been implicated in some cases. Metastatic
disease is rare.

## Diagnosis

{ Squamous cell carcinoma where there is invasion of the
underlying stroma (Fig. 20.10B).
¯Çü Symptoms. A visible mass in one eye, sometimes accompanied by conjunctivitis-type symptoms.
¯Çü Signs are variable, and clinical correlation with histological
severity is unreliable. Most tend to develop within the interpalpebral ¯ÇØssure, particularly at the limbus, although any part
of the conjunctiva or cornea may be involved. ¯Çƒe lesion may
appear ¯Ç£eshy, gelatinous, leukoplakic or papillomatous, super¯ÇØcial or feeder vessels may be prominent, or the appearance
may be avascular (Fig. 20.10CÔÇôF). Intraocular extension is
uncommon. In patients who are heavy smokers the tumour
tends to be large and in the inferior fornix.
¯Çü Investigations include ultrasonic biomicroscopy (UBM) or
anterior segment optical coherence tomography (OCT) to estimate the depth of invasion, exfoliative cytology and impression cytology.

## Treatment

¯Çü Excision with 2ÔÇô4 mm margins and assessment for completeness of clearance has been the conventional standard approach
o¯Ç×en with intraoperative frozen section. It is important not
to cut below Bowman membrane as recurrent disease can

¯Çü Histology shows a spectrum of abnormality. ¯Çƒe ¯ÇØrst two are
sometimes termed conjunctivalÔÇôcorneal intraepithelial neoplasia (CCIN):

{ Conjunctival epithelial dysplasia, where dysplastic cells are
con¯ÇØned to the basal epithelial layers.

{ Carcinoma in situ, where dysplastic cells involve the full
thickness of the epithelium (Fig. 20.10A).

potentially penetrate the corneal stroma. Complete histological excision may be associated with recurrence. Adjunctive
measures reduce the rate of reappearance and include cryotherapy, brachytherapy or topical chemotherapy. Subconjunctival interferon injections can also be employed.

avoid the scarring and stem cell damage associated with extensive excision, (b) reduce tumour size prior to excision, or (c)
treat recurrence.

keratopathy. Recurrence rates are less than 10%.

{ Topical interferon alfa-2b requires refrigeration and

of patients. Subconjunctival interferon is available for
patients with a superiorly located tumour. In individuals
whose immune system is suppressed, a once-daily instillation of interferon drops should be used lifelong to prevent
recurrence.

{ Alternatively, Mitomycin C can also be used, but it is more
likely to be associated with ocular side e¯Çøects than other
options.

## Lymphoproliferative lesions

## Introduction

## The most common conjunctival lymphoproliferative lesion is

reactive lymphoid hyperplasia, a proliferation of both B and T
cells with germinal follicle formation. Conjunctival lymphoma
may arise de novo, by extension from orbital lymphoma or
associated with systemic lymphoma at diagnosis (up to 30%).
Most conjunctival lymphomas are of B-cell origin, arising from
mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) and tending to be
indolent. Lymphoma accounts for about 7% of all conjunctival
tumours.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Histology shows reactive lymphoid hyperplasia with a germinal lymphoid follicle consisting of immature lymphoid cells at
the centre and mature cells at the periphery (Fig. 20.11A).
¯Çü Symptoms. Painless swelling, redness or irritation (which is
o¯Ç×en bilateral when systemic disease is more likely). Other
possible symptoms include ptosis and diplopia.
¯Çü Signs. A slowly growing salmon-pink or ¯Ç£esh-coloured mobile
in¯ÇØltrate is seen on the epibulbar surface (Fig. 20.11B) or in
the fornices (Fig. 20.11C and see Fig.20.4D). Rarely, a di¯Çøuse
lesion (Fig. 20.11D) may mimic chronic conjunctivitis.
¯Çü Biopsy is taken to con¯ÇØrm diagnosis. ¯Çƒe uninvolved eye
should also be biopsied (inferior fornix).
¯Çü Investigation for systemic involvement.

## Treatment

¯Çü Systemic disease is treated if needed.
¯Çü External beam radiotherapy.
¯Çü Other options include chemotherapy, excision of small lesions
with adjuvant treatment, cryotherapy and intralesional injections of interferon alfa-2b or rituximab.
¯Çü Prognosis is dependent on the histological features. Patients
with mantle cell lymphoma have a very poor chance of 5-year
survival.

## Primary amyloidosis

## Amyloidosis is a heterogeneous group of diseases characterized by extracellular amyloid deposits in different organs

including the orbit and periocular tissues. The most common form of local amyloidosis is caused by the deposition of
monoclonal immunoglobulin light chains by a benign B-cell
or plasma-cell clone. It is a rare cause of an orbital mass and
can mimic lymphoma (Fig. 20.12). It presents with a wide
spectrum of clinical findings, including ptosis and epiphora.
Approximately 25% have periocular discomfort. The usual age
of presentation is about 60 years and two-thirds are unilateral.
Only 5% have systemic involvement. The diagnosis is made
histologically using Congo red staining, which shows green
birefringence. The usual treatment is surgical excision of the
mass and observation.

## Kaposi sarcoma

## Kaposi sarcoma is a slowly growing tumour that is typically

found in individuals with AIDS, but occasionally in the elderly
and when there is long-term immunosuppression. A vascular bright red or purplish plaque or nodule is seen (Fig. 20.13),
sometimes resembling (or associated with) conjunctival haemorrhage. Histology reveals a proliferation of spindle-shaped cells,
vascular channels and in¯Ç£ammatory cells. If treatment is necessary, systemic AIDS therapy should be optimized, with local
radiotherapy, excision and local or systemic chemotherapy as
additional options.

IRIS TUMOURS

## Iris naevus

## An iris naevus consists of a proliferation of melanocytes in the

super¯ÇØcial iris stroma and appears as a circumscribed solitary ¯Ç£at
or variably elevated pigmented lesion (Fig. 20.14). ¯Çƒe normal iris
architecture is disrupted. A di¯Çøuse naevus characteristically occurs
in congenital ocular melanocytosis and in iris naevus (CoganÔÇô
Reese) syndrome (see Ch. 11). ¯Çƒe malignant transformation rate
is up to 8% over 15 years. Risk factors include young age (under
40), inferior location, bleeding from the lesion, di¯Çøuse iris involvement, feathery margins and ectropion uveae. Observation should
be lifelong and involve documentation by slit lamp examination
and photography.

Iris freckle (ephelis)

Iris freckles are super¯ÇØcial lesions that are smaller than a naevus,
with no elevation or distortion (Fig. 20.15A) and show increased
melanocyte pigmentation with a normal number of cells.

Brush¯Çƒeld spots

¯Çƒese are small whitish peripheral iris speckles arranged in a concentric ring (Fig. 20.15B), occurring particularly in Down syndrome though also as a normal ¯ÇØnding in blue eyes. ¯Çƒey consist
of a mildly hyperplastic focus surrounded by a ring of hypoplasia
and have no malignant potential (see also Ch. 10).

## Lisch nodules

¯Çƒese are small well-de¯ÇØned nodules (Fig. 20.15C and D) found
in both eyes of virtually all patients with neuro¯ÇØbromatosis type
1 (see Ch. 19).

## Iris melanoma

## Introduction

About 8% of uveal melanomas arise in the iris. ¯Çƒe prognosis is
comparatively good ÔÇô only about 5% of patients develop metastasis within 10 years of treatment. Conditions associated with or
predisposing to uveal melanomas include fair skin and lighter iris
colour, numerous and/or atypical (dysplastic) cutaneous naevi, iris
or choroidal naevus, congenital ocular and oculodermal melanocytosis (naevus of Ota) and uveal melanocytoma. Chronic sunlight
exposure and arc welding are environmental risk factors. Presentation is typically in middle age, a decade earlier than ciliary body
and choroidal melanoma.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Histology usually shows di¯Çøusely in¯ÇØltrating spindle cells (see
below) of low-grade malignancy (Fig. 20.16A). A minority contain an epithelioid cell component and can be more aggressive.

A B

C D

¯Çü Symptoms. Enlargement of a pre-existing naevus is typical,
noticed either by the patient or at a routine eye examination.

## Signs indicative of malignant transformation include growth

and the development of prominent blood vessels.
¯Çü Signs

{ A pigmented (Fig. 20.16B and C) nodule at least 3 mm in
diameter and 1 mm thick, typically located in the inferior half
of the iris and o¯Ç×en associated with surface blood vessels.

{ Pupillary distortion, ectropion uveae (Fig. 20.16D) and
occasionally localized cataract may be seen, although these
can also occur with naevi.

{ A non-pigmented melanoma may occasionally be seen
(Fig. 20.16E).

{ Growth is usually slow, with extension across the iris surface.

{ ¯Çƒe angle and anterior ciliary body may be in¯ÇØltrated, but
extrascleral extension is rare (Fig. 20.16F).

## Treatment

¯Çü Sector iridectomy for small tumours and iridocyclectomy for
tumours invading the angle.
¯Çü Radiotherapy with a radioactive plaque (brachytherapy) or
external irradiation with a proton beam.
¯Çü Enucleation may be required for di¯Çøusely growing tumours if
radiotherapy is not possible.
¯Çü Monitoring for recurrence and metastasis.

## Metastatic tumours

Metastasis to the iris (Fig. 20.17) is rare and is characterized by
one or more fast-growing white, pink or yellow masses that may be
associated with anterior uveitis and occasionally hyphaema. Breast
cancer, lung cancer and melanoma of the skin are among the most
common primary types.

## Miscellaneous iris tumours

¯Çü Juvenile xanthogranuloma is a rare idiopathic granulomatous disease of early childhood. Iris involvement is

{ Complications include hyphaema, cataract and glaucoma.
¯Çü Ultrasound biomicroscopy is used to rule out ciliary body
involvement.
¯Çü Fine-needle aspiration biopsy may be employed prior to
major surgical intervention.
¯Çü Systemic investigation should be carried out.

cellular nests; (B) typical iris melanoma; (C) nodular melanoma at pupil margin; (D) extensive

A B

characterized by a localized or di¯Çøuse yellow lesion (Fig.
20.18A) that may be associated with spontaneous hyphaema
or, less commonly, anterior uveitis and glaucoma. It can also
involve the skin (Fig. 20.18B and C), muscle, stomach, salivary glands and other organs.
¯Çü Leiomyoma is an extremely rare benign tumour arising from
smooth muscle. ¯Çƒe appearance is similar to that of an amelanotic melanoma (Fig. 20.18D).
¯Çü Melanocytoma is a darkly pigmented nodular mass with
a mossy, granular surface, most frequently occupying the
peripheral iris (Fig. 20.18E). It may undergo spontaneous
necrosis resulting in seeding of the iris stroma and chamber
angle, with elevation of intraocular pressure.
¯Çü Vascular tumours of various types have been described, the
most common being racemose haemangioma (arteriovenous
communication), typically seen as a large ectatic vessel and/or
bunch of grapes (Fig. 20.18F).

IRIS CYSTS

Iris cysts are uncommon. Anterior segment OCT and ultrasound
biomicroscopy are especially useful to di¯Çøerentiate some lesions
from ciliary body tumours.
¯Çü Primary epithelial cysts

{ Cysts arise from the iris or iridociliary pigment epithelium.

{ ¯Çƒree-quarters are in the peripheral iris, where they appear
as a smooth, dome-shaped bulging (Fig. 20.19A and B),
best seen on gonioscopy (Fig. 20.19C). Mid-zone lesions
also appear as a bulge in the iris visible a¯Ç×er dilatation. At
the pupillary margin (least common) cysts appear darkly
pigmented and o¯Ç×en elongated.

{ ¯Çƒey may rarely dislodge to ¯Ç£oat freely in the anterior
chamber or vitreous.

{ Most are asymptomatic and innocuous. Rarely, large cysts
may obstruct vision and focal photocoagulation to the cyst
wall may be needed.

¯Çü Primary stromal cysts may be congenital (more aggressive)
or acquired.

CILIARY BODY TUMOURS

## Ciliary body melanoma

## Introduction

Ciliary body melanomas comprise around 12% of all uveal melanomas. Risk factors are the same as iris melanoma. Presentation is usually in the sixth decade with visual symptoms, although occasionally
discovery is incidental. ¯Çƒe diagnosis may be delayed as the lesion
is easily missed. Distinction is principally from an iridociliary cyst.
Uveal e¯Çøusion syndrome may cause diagnostic di¯ÇÜculty that can
be resolved by imaging. Other ciliary body tumours are extremely
rare and include melanocytoma, medulloepithelioma, metastases,
adenocarcinoma, adenoma, neurolemmoma and leiomyoma.

{ Solitary unilateral structure with a smooth translucent
anterior wall lying on or within the iris (Fig. 20.19D). Contained debris may be visible.

{ May remain stable for many years before enlarging and can
sometimes rupture.

{ Most congenital cysts require treatment by aspiration or
surgical excision. Ethanol-induced sclerosis may avoid the
need for excision.
¯Çü Secondary cysts

{ Traumatic cysts (Fig. 20.20A) occur following deposition of
epithelial cells from the conjunctiva or cornea onto the iris
a¯Ç×er penetrating or surgical trauma. ¯Çƒey frequently enlarge,
leading to corneal oedema, anterior uveitis and glaucoma.

{ Extended use of long-acting miotics may be associated
with usually bilateral small, multiple cysts located along
the pupillary border (Fig. 20.20B).

{ ¯Çƒeir development can be prevented by the concomitant
use of topical phenylephrine 2.5%.

{ Parasitic cysts are extremely rare.

TIP Investigate any asymptomatic middle-aged individual with
a prominent episcleral (sentinel) vessel, as this can be a subtle
sign of ciliary body melanoma.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Signs

{ A large tumour may be visualized with pupillary dilatation
(Fig. 20.21A).

{ Overlying prominent episcleral (sentinel) vessels (Fig.
20.21B).

{ Erosion through the iris root may mimic iris melanoma,
extraocular extension through scleral vessels a conjunctival melanoma (Fig. 20.21C).

{ Displacement of the lens (Fig. 20.21D) may cause astigmatism, subluxation or cataract.
¯Çü Investigations

{ ¯Çƒree-mirror contact lens examination and binocular
indirect ophthalmoscopy.

{ Biopsy involving excisional, incisional or ¯ÇØne-needle aspiration techniques may be helpful in selected cases. Genetic
pro¯ÇØling may help to determine the likelihood of metastasis (see choroidal melanoma).

{ Investigation for systemic involvement, particularly hepatic.

## Treatment

¯Çü Iridocyclectomy or sclerouvectomy for small or mediumsized tumours involving no more than one-third of the angle.
Complications include retinal detachment, vitreous haemorrhage, cataract, lens subluxation, hypotony and incomplete
resection.
¯Çü Radiotherapy by brachytherapy or proton beam irradiation.
¯Çü Enucleation may be necessary for large tumours.
¯Çü Systemic treatment is indicated when metastatic disease is
evident.

## Medulloepithelioma

Medulloepithelioma (previously known as diktyoma) is a rare
embryonal neoplasm that arises from the inner layer of the optic

{ Gonioscopy to detect angle invasion.

{ Ultrasonic biomicroscopy.

A B

extension from ciliary body tumour (arrow). (Courtesy of M Parulekar ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

cup and can be benign or malignant. Presentation is usually in
the ¯ÇØrst decade with visual loss, pain, photophobia, leukocoria, or
proptosis in advanced cases. A white, pink, yellow or brown ciliary body, retrolental or anterior chamber mass is seen (Fig. 20.22).
Enucleation may be required.

TUMOURS OF THE CHOROID

## Choroidal naevus

Choroidal naevi are present in 5ÔÇô10% of White individuals, but
are rare in darker-skinned races. Growth occurs mainly during
the pre-pubertal years and is extremely rare in adulthood. For this
reason, detectable growth should raise a suspicion of malignancy.
¯Çƒe lifetime risk of malignant transformation is low. Histologically,
the tumour is composed of a proliferation of spindle cell melanocytes within the choroid (Fig. 20.23A). It is vital to identify features
suggesting a greater likelihood of malignancy. As with cutaneous
melanoma, early treatment is associated with a substantially lower
risk of metastasis. An extended period without any change provides no guarantee that progression will not occur in the future, as
sudden enlargement has been documented following many years
of stability.

TIP Choroidal naevi are common and the risk of malignant
transformation is low. However, a change in the size and
appearance in an adult should raise a suspicion of malignancy.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Most are asymptomatic and are detected on routine examination.
¯Çü Signs

{ A depigmented halo is very common (Fig. 20.23E).

{ Amelanotic lesions can occur.
¯Çü Features suspicious of early melanoma

{ Symptoms such as photopsia and blurred vision (6/15 or
worse).

{ ¯Çƒe presence of overlying orange pigment (lipofuscin ÔÇô

{ ¯Çƒe presence of associated subretinal ¯Ç£uid.

{ ¯Çƒickness greater than 2 mm and diameter over 5 mm.

{ Margin within 3 mm of the optic disc.

{ Absence of drusen and a halo.

{ Acoustic hollowness on ultrasonography.
¯Çü Investigations

{ Photographic documentation (Fig. 20.24A).

{ Fundus auto¯Ç£uorescence (FAF) (Fig. 20.24B) can demonstrate subtle orange pigment that may not be easily discernible clinically. A naevus may have patchy increased
auto¯Ç£uorescence, but the appearance of intense di¯Çøuse or
even con¯Ç£uent hyperauto¯Ç£uorescence is a useful diagnostic indicator of melanoma.

{ OCT (Fig. 20.24C) is useful for measurement of lesion
thickness as well as the detection of subtle associated
fluid (a high-risk factor). Secondary retinal changes
tend to be more marked overlying a melanoma than a
naevus.

{ Fluorescein angiography (FA) is not helpful in distinguishing a melanoma from a naevus.

{ Ultrasonography (US) shows a localized ¯Ç£at or slightly
elevated lesion with high internal acoustic re¯Ç£ectivity; a
hollow acoustic appearance is a risk factor for progression.
Lesion thickness should be measured.

{ Usually post-equatorial, oval or circular, brown to slategrey lesion with indistinct feathery margins (Fig. 20.23B
and C).

TIP In a pigmented choroidal lesion, clinical features
suggestive of melanoma include: visual change, overlying
orange pigment, subretinal ¯ÇØuid, thickness >2 mm, diameter
>5 mm, absence of drusen and absence of a halo.

{ Overlying drusen are typical (Fig. 20.23CÔÇôE).

## Treatment

¯Çü Baseline fundus photography and ultrasonography or OCT,
with inde¯ÇØnite regular review.
¯Çü Review interval is determined by level of suspicion:

{ No suspicious features: 6 months until stability established
(e.g. 1 year), then annual review.

for iris melanoma (above). Presentation peaks at around the age of
60 years. Most cases occur sporadically. Choroidal melanoma has
distinct molecular features that di¯Çøer from those found in cutaneous melanoma. Histopathology reveals spindle (Fig. 20.25A)
and epithelioid (Fig. 20.25B) cell types; the former being arranged
in bundles and having a better prognosis. Epithelioid cells are
larger and more pleomorphic, with more frequent mitotic ¯ÇØgures.

## Tumour composition is commonly exclusively spindle cell or a

mixture of spindle and epithelioid. Lesions may penetrate Bruch
membrane and the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) with herniation into the subretinal space, classically assuming the shape of a
collar stud (Fig. 20.25C). Scleral channel and vortex vein invasion
can lead to orbital spread. Metastasis is commonly to the liver,
bone and lung, but only about 1ÔÇô2% of patients have detectable
metastases at the time of presentation. Mortality is up to 50% at
10 years. Adverse prognostic factors include particular histological
features (e.g. large numbers of epithelioid cells, mitotic activity),
larger tumour dimensions, tumour genetic characteristics (e.g.
somatic mutations in the tumour suppressor gene BAP1, present in
nearly 50% of uveal melanomas, imply a greater chance of metastasis), extrascleral extension and anterior location.

{ One or two suspicious features: 4ÔÇô6 months.

{ ¯Çƒree or more suspicious features: consider referral for
ocular oncology subspecialist assessment.
¯Çü Growth. If growth is documented or highly suspicious features
are present, the lesion should generally be regarded as a melanoma and managed accordingly.

## Choroidal melanoma

## Choroidal melanoma is the most common primary intraocular

malignancy in adults and accounts for 80% of all uveal melanomas,
but it is still relatively uncommon. ¯Çƒese tumours lack the most
typical cutaneous melanoma-associated mutations and are instead
characterized by a di¯Çøerent set of genes. Predisposing factors are as

866 Tumours of the Choroid

A

B

C

   - tightly arranged fusiform cells with indistinct cell membranes and slender or plump oval nuclei; (B) epithelioid cells

   - large pleomorphic cells with distinct cell membranes, large
vesicular nuclei with prominent nucleoli, and abundant cytoplasm; (C) penetration of Bruch membrane in a ÔÇÿcollar studÔÇÖ
fashion. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from Clinical
Ophthalmic Pathology, Butterworth-Heinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs
AÔÇôC.)

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms are o¯Ç×en absent, with a tumour detected by chance
on routine fundus examination. A range of visual disturbance
can occur depending on tumour characteristics.
¯Çü Signs

{ A solitary elevated subretinal grey-brown (Fig. 20.26A) or
rarely amelanotic (Fig. 20.26B) dome-shaped mass. Diffuse in¯ÇØltration is uncommon.

{ About 60% are located within 3 mm of the optic disc (Fig.
20.26C) or fovea.

{ Clumps of overlying orange pigment are common (Fig.
20.26D).

{ If the tumour breaks through Bruch membrane it acquires
a ÔÇÿcollar studÔÇÖ appearance (see Fig. 20.25C and Fig. 20.28C).

{ Associated haemorrhage and subretinal ¯Ç£uid (Fig. 20.26E) are
common. ¯Çƒe latter may result in exudative retinal detachment (Fig. 20.26F), which can mask the underlying lesion.

{ Other signs can include sentinel vessels (Fig. 20.27), choroidal folds, in¯Ç£ammation, rubeosis iridis, secondary
glaucoma and cataract.

Di¯Çƒerential diagnosis

¯Çü Pigmented lesions

{ A choroidal naevus usually exhibits numerous surface
drusen, without serous retinal detachment and little if any
orange pigment.

{ Melanocytoma is deeply pigmented and usually located at
the optic disc.

{ Congenital hypertrophy of the RPE is ¯Ç£at, is o¯Ç×en greyÔÇô
black and has a well-de¯ÇØned margin with lacunae.

{ Haemorrhage in the subretinal or suprachoroidal space,
for example from choroidal neovascularization or retinal
artery macroaneurysm.

{ Metastatic cutaneous melanoma has a smooth surface, a
light brown colour, indistinct margins, extensive retinal
detachment and o¯Ç×en a past history of malignancy.
¯Çü Non-pigmented lesions

{ Circumscribed choroidal haemangioma is typically posterior, pink, dome-shaped and has a smooth surface.

{ Metastasis is o¯Ç×en associated with exudative retinal
detachment.

{ Solitary choroidal granuloma, e.g. sarcoidosis, tuberculosis.

{ Posterior scleritis, which can present with a large elevated
lesion. In contrast to melanoma, pain is a common feature.

{ Large elevated choroidal neovascular lesion, which can
be eccentrically located, usually in the temporal preequatorial region, is typically associated with exudate and
fresh haemorrhage, both of which rarely accompany a
melanoma.

{ Prominent vortex vein ampulla is characterized by a small,
smooth, brown, dome-shaped lesion, which disappears
with pressure on the eye.

## Investigation

Examination is su¯ÇÜcient for diagnosis in the majority of cases.

868 Tumours of the Choroid

A B

(Courtesy of C Barry.)

¯Çü FA is of limited diagnostic value because there is no pathognomonic pattern. ¯Çƒe most common ¯ÇØndings are an intrinsic
tumour (ÔÇÿdualÔÇÖ) circulation (Fig. 20.28A), mottled ¯Ç£uorescence
during the arteriovenous phase and late di¯Çøuse leakage and
staining. FA may, however, be useful in the di¯Çøerential diagnosis of simulating lesions.
¯Çü Ultrasound is used to measure lesion dimensions and to
detect tumours through opaque media and exudative retinal
detachment and may also demonstrate extraocular extension.
¯Çƒe characteristic ¯ÇØndings are internal homogeneity with low
to medium re¯Ç£ectivity, choroidal excavation (Fig. 20.28B) and
orbital shadowing. A basal acoustically quiet zone referred to
as ÔÇÿacoustic hollowingÔÇÖ is typical and is due to the greater tissue
homogeneity in this region. A ÔÇÿcollar studÔÇÖ con¯ÇØguration (Fig.
20.28C) is almost pathognomonic when present.
¯Çü FAF. Intense di¯Çøuse or con¯Ç£uent hyperauto¯Ç£uorescence, if
present, is a useful diagnostic indicator of melanoma.
¯Çü OCT measures dimensions and may demonstrate associated
subretinal ¯Ç£uid, o¯Ç×en before clinically apparent. Secondary
retinal changes are o¯Ç×en evident overlying the lesion.
¯Çü Indocyanine green angiography (ICGA) usually shows
hypo¯Ç£uorescence throughout the study and provides more
information than FA about the extent of the tumour, due to
lower interference from the RPE.
¯Çü Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (Fig. 20.28D) is useful to
demonstrate extraocular extension and may be of some help in
di¯Çøerential diagnosis.
¯Çü Biopsy is useful when the diagnosis cannot be established by
less invasive methods. It may be performed either with a ¯ÇØne
needle or using the 25-gauge vitrectomy system, the latter providing a larger sample.
¯Çü Immunocytochemistry for PAX8 expression can help distinguish intraocular tumours with overlapping features.
Melanoma and melanocytoma are PAX8-negative whereas
pigmented adenocarcinomas are PAX8-positive.
¯Çü Genetic tumour analysis is becoming increasingly important in management, particularly with regard to prognosis, as

metastasis occurs almost exclusively with certain genetic pro¯ÇØles. Simple chromosomal changes like the loss of one copy
of chromosome 3 (M3) and changes in chromosome 8 (i8q)
strongly correlate with poor outcome, especially when they
occur together in the same tumour.
¯Çü Prognostic indicators combine anatomic ¯ÇØndings with
genetic and histopathological data. ¯Çƒe Liverpool Uveal Melanoma Prognosticator (which is an online resource) is useful to
determine prognosis for life.
¯Çü Reduced surveillance can be adopted for patients where the
melanoma genetic pro¯ÇØle reveals a low risk of metastasis.
¯Çü Systemic investigation is directed principally towards
detecting metastatic spread, though it may also be used to
search for a primary tumour elsewhere if choroidal metastasis is likely. Liver function testing and ultrasonography
are mainstays. Chest radiography rarely shows lung secondaries in the absence of liver disease. ¯Çƒe comparative value
of whole-body PET/CT imaging is not fully de¯ÇØned. It has
greater sensitivity for detecting metastatic disease, particularly extrahepatic lesions, but involves a substantial ionizing
radiation dose.

## Treatment

## Treatment is performed to avoid the development of a painful and

unsightly eye whilst conserving as much useful vision as possible.
¯Çƒe extent to which ocular treatment in¯Ç£uences survival is not yet
de¯ÇØned, though there is some evidence that the risk of metastasis
is lower with smaller earlier tumours. Management is individualized based on the characteristics of the particular tumour and the
patient (e.g. general health, age, preferences, state of fellow eye).
¯Çƒe size of the tumour (apical height and largest basal diameter)
determines the treatment strategy.
¯Çü Brachytherapy (episcleral plaque radiotherapy) may be used
for tumours less than 20 mm in basal diameter and up to 10
mm thick in which there is a reasonable chance of salvaging
vision. Survival is similar to that following enucleation. A
plaque is sutured to the sclera (Fig. 20.29A) for several days

A B

C D

(C) B-scan of a ÔÇÿcollar studÔÇÖ tumour; (D) T2-weighted axial MRI showing a choroidal melanoma
without extraocular spread. (Courtesy of B Damato ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs A and B; G. Kontos ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

according to dosage requirement. Regression begins about
1ÔÇô2 months a¯Ç×er treatment and continues over several years,
leaving a ¯Ç£at or dome-shaped pigmented scar (Fig. 20.29B).
Complications include cataract, papillopathy (with or without
disc neovascularization) and radiation retinopathy. Release of
cytokines by the irradiated tumour can also cause retinopathy
and other complications (ÔÇÿtoxic tumour syndromeÔÇÖ) that may
need to be treated speci¯ÇØcally (e.g. endo/exoresection, intravitreal steroid/anti-VEGF).
¯Çü External beam radiotherapy. Fractionated irradiation with
charged particles such as protons achieves a high dose in the
tumour with relative sparing of adjacent tissues and is used for
tumours unsuitable for brachytherapy either because of large
size or posterior location. Targeting is aided by suturing radioopaque tantalum markers to the sclera. Regression is slower
than with brachytherapy, but survival is comparable. Intraocular complications are similar, but extraocular complications
such as loss of lashes, eyelid depigmentation and keratitis may
be seen.

¯Çü Stereotactic radiotherapy uses multiple collimated beams
from di¯Çøerent directions, either concurrently or sequentially,
so that only the tumour receives a high dose of radiation. It is
relatively e¯Çøective, though there may be a comparatively high
complication rate.
¯Çü Transpupillary thermotherapy (TTT) uses an infrared laser
beam to induce tumour cell death by hyperthermia rather
than coagulation. Indications include the treatment of a small
tumour when radiotherapy is considered inappropriate. It can
be used as an adjunct to radiotherapy, particularly for visionthreatening exudation. Tumour response is gradual, the lesion
¯ÇØrst becoming darker and ¯Ç£atter and eventually disappearing
to leave bare sclera (Fig. 20.30). Complications include retinal
traction, retinal tear formation with rhegmatogenous detachment, vascular occlusion and neovascularization. Local recurrence is common, especially if the tumour is thick, amelanotic
or involves the disc margin.
¯Çü Trans-scleral choroidectomy. ¯Çƒis is a technically di¯ÇÜcult
procedure that may be used for carefully selected tumours that

870 Tumours of the Choroid

A B

treatment. (Courtesy of S Chen ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

¯Çü ¯Çƒere is no standard treatment for metastatic disease.
Tebentafusc, an immunotherapy drug, can be considered in
some patients with metastatic choroidal melanoma and can
result in a modest increase in overall survival time. Immune
checkpoint inhibitors that are e¯Çøective for cutaneous melanoma are not e¯Ç×ective against uveal melanoma.
¯Çü Systemic chemotherapy has not been shown to be of bene¯ÇØt
in cases where there is no evidence of metastatic spread.

## Circumscribed choroidal
haemangioma

lesion in the macular region.

are too thick for radiotherapy but less than about 16 mm in
diameter. Complications include retinal detachment, hypotony, wound dehiscence and local tumour recurrence.
¯Çü Light-activated AU-011, a novel treatment for small melanomas (2ÔÇô3.4 mm), is showing promise in early trials. A solution
of nano-particles derived from the human papilloma virus
attached to an infrared ¯Ç£uorescent dye is injected into the vitreous cavity. ¯Çƒese particles bind to the melanoma cells. When
irradiated with infrared light the dye becomes activated and
selectively causes melanoma cell necrosis.
¯Çü Enucleation. Indications include large tumour size, optic disc
invasion, extensive involvement of the ciliary body or angle,
irreversible loss of useful vision and poor motivation to keep
the eye. It is essential to perform ophthalmoscopy a¯Ç×er surgical
draping to ensure that the correct eye is removed. Manipulation of the eye should be kept to a minimum. Orbital recurrence is rare if there is no extraocular tumour spread or if any
such extension is completely excised.

Circumscribed choroidal haemangioma (CCH) consists of a mass
of varying-sized vascular channels within the choroid. It is not
associated with systemic disease. It may be dormant throughout
life or may give rise to symptoms, usually in early adulthood, as
a result of exudative retinal detachment. Slow enlargement can
occur over many years.

TIP A choroidal haemangioma can cause an exudative retinal
detachment in a young adult.

{ Subretinal ¯Ç£uid is usually present in symptomatic cases.

{ Complications include surface ¯ÇØbrous metaplasia (Fig.
20.31C), cystoid retinal degeneration, RPE degeneration
and subretinal ¯ÇØbrosis.
¯Çü OCT. ¯Çƒe overlying retina may be normal or show sub- and
intraretinal ¯Ç£uid, retinoschisis and atrophy.
¯Çü FA reveals early spotty hyper¯Ç£uorescence (see Fig. 20.31B) and
late di¯Çøuse but intense hyper¯Ç£uorescence.

¯Çü FAF. ¯Çƒe lesion itself shows little or no intrinsic auto¯Ç£uorescence. Associated overlying orange pigment and (fresh)

¯Çü Ultrasound shows an acoustically solid lesion (high internal
re¯Ç£ectivity) with a well-de¯ÇØned anterior surface (Fig. 20.31D).
¯Çü MRI. ¯Çƒe tumour is iso- or hyperintense to the vitreous in T1weighted images and isointense in T2-weighted images, with
marked enhancement by gadolinium.

## Treatment

¯Çƒe following may be used if vision is threatened. ¯Çƒe superiority
of any particular approach has not been established.
¯Çü Photodynamic therapy (PDT) using the same parameters
as for macular neovascularization. Treatment may need to be
repeated a¯Ç×er a few months if subretinal ¯Ç£uid persists.

¯Çü TTT for lesions not involving the macula.
¯Çü Photocoagulation using a conventional thermal laser or
micropulse treatment may be e¯Çøective.
¯Çü Radiotherapy may be used for resistant lesions.
¯Çü Intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy typically reduces serous retinal detachment and can be used in combination with other
modalities.
¯Çü Oral propranolol may be of bene¯ÇØt, particularly for associated exudative retinal detachment, but does not seem to consistently reduce tumour size.

Di¯Ç×use choroidal haemangioma

Di¯Çøuse choroidal haemangioma usually a¯Çøects over half of the
choroid and enlarges very slowly. It occurs almost exclusively in
patients with SturgeÔÇôWeber syndrome ipsilateral to the naevus
¯Ç£ammeus (see Ch. 2). ¯Çƒe fundus has a di¯Çøuse deep red colour
that is most marked at the posterior pole (Fig. 20.32A). Localized
areas of thickening, simulating a circumscribed haemangioma,

872 Tumours of the Choroid

A B

B-scan showing di¯Ç×use choroidal thickening. (Courtesy of B Damato ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

may be present within the larger lesion. B-scan ultrasonography
shows di¯Çøuse choroidal thickening (Fig. 20.32B). Complications
include secondary retinal cystoid degeneration and exudative
retinal detachment. Neovascular glaucoma can ensue if exudative
detachment is not treated. Treatment of vision-threatening cases
involves PDT or low-dose radiotherapy.

## Optic disc melanocytoma

Melanocytoma (magnocellular naevus) is a rare, distinctive,
unilateral, heavily pigmented congenital hamartoma seen most
frequently in the optic nerve head (Fig. 20.33A) but may arise
anywhere in the uvea. About half involve one quadrant of the
optic disc. Histology shows large deeply pigmented polyhedral or
spindle cells with small nuclei. In contrast to choroidal melanoma,
melanocytoma is more common in dark-skinned individuals. It is
discovered at an average age of 50 years and is more likely to a¯Çøect
women than men. Most cases have no symptoms and the condition is detected on routine examination. ¯Çƒe tumour is generally
stationary and treatment is not required except in the very rare
event of malignant transformation. Other complications include
spontaneous tumour necrosis, optic nerve compression and retinal
vein obstruction.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü A dark brown or black, ¯Ç£at or slightly elevated lesion with
feathery edges that may extend over the edge of the disc to
involve the adjacent choroid in about half of patients (Fig.
20.33B).
¯Çü Occasionally a large tumour occupies most of the disc surface
and may lead to pigment dispersion into the vitreous.
¯Çü A relative a¯Çøerent pupillary defect may be present, even if
visual acuity (VA) is good.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe visual ¯ÇØeld is abnormal in 90% of patients. ¯Çƒe defect corresponds to a localised absence of the capillary network within
the melanocytoma that can be shown with OCTA.

¯Çü OCT is useful to document the size and has a characteristic
appearance (Fig. 20.33C).
¯Çü FA shows persistent dense hypo¯Ç£uorescence due to masking.

## Choroidal osteoma

Choroidal osteoma is a rare benign ossifying tumour that o¯Ç×en
a¯Çøects young adults and has a strong female preponderance. Both
eyes are a¯Çøected in about 10ÔÇô20%. Histology shows mature cancellous bone, with overlying RPE atrophy. Presentation is in the
secondÔÇôthird decades. Masquerading lesions include osseous
metaplasia in association with a choroidal haemangioma and
sclerochoroidal calci¯ÇØcation, the latter characterized by multiple
geographical yellowÔÇôwhite fundus lesions that usually involve
both eyes of an older adult.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Gradual visual impairment if the macula is
involved by the tumour itself, or occasionally more rapid
deterioration secondary to macular neovascularization.
¯Çü Signs

{ Initially orange, maturing lesions appear yellowÔÇôwhite.
¯Çƒey are ¯Ç£at or minimally elevated with well-de¯ÇØned, scalloped margins near the disc or at the posterior pole (Fig.

{ Slow growth may occur over several years (Fig. 20.34C and
D).

{ Spontaneous resorption and decalci¯ÇØcation may rarely

hypoauto¯Ç£uorescence in decalci¯ÇØed regions.

Sclerochoroidal calci¯Çƒcation

¯Çƒis is a rare condition that is usually diagnosed coincidently in
asymptomatic elderly White people. ¯Çƒe condition is commonly
misdiagnosed and the visual prognosis is good as the macula is
usually not involved. ¯Çƒe placoid lesions, which measure about
3.5mm in diameter, are ¯Ç£at or minimally raised, are yellowish in
appearance and are found in both eyes in about 50% of patients.

¯Çƒe calcium deposits start in the sclera and expand into the choroid. ¯Çƒe diagnosis is made clinically and can be con¯ÇØrmed with
OCT (Fig. 20.36). Most cases are idiopathic, but patients with
calcium-phosphorus metabolic disorders are at higher risk. Parathyroid adenoma, Bartter syndrome and Gitelman syndrome
should be excluded because of the systemic complications associated with these conditions.

## Metastatic tumours

¯Çƒe choroid is by far the most common (90%) site for uveal
metastases. ¯Çƒe most frequent primary sites are the breast and
bronchus. A choroidal secondary may be the initial presentation
of a bronchial carcinoma, whereas a past history of breast cancer
is the rule in patients with secondary spread. Other less common primary sites include the gastrointestinal tract, kidney and
skin (melanoma). In patients with known systemic cancer 2ÔÇô4%
demonstrate ocular metastasis. Survival is poor, with a median life
expectancy of 8ÔÇô12 months and a 3-year survival of about one in
three, depending on the primary tumour.

TIP Choroidal metastasis may occur secondary to bronchus
or breast carcinoma and indicates a poor prognosis and short
life expectancy.

unknown.

## Treatment

¯Çü Observation, if the patient is asymptomatic or receiving systemic chemotherapy, which may also be bene¯ÇØcial for choroidal metastases.
¯Çü Radiotherapy, either external beam or brachytherapy, typically for solitary lesions.
¯Çü TTT and PDT are useful for small tumours with minimal
subretinal ¯Ç£uid.
¯Çü Anti-VEGF treatment may lead to regression of metastases in
some cases, as well as addressing secondary phenomena such
as subretinal ¯Ç£uid accumulation. It is probably more e¯Çøective
in small early lesions.

NEURAL RETINAL TUMOURS

## Retinoblastoma

## Introduction

Retinoblastoma is rare, occurring in up to 1:18000 live births, but
is the most common primary intraocular malignancy of childhood and accounts for about 3% of all childhood cancers. A¯Ç×er
uveal melanoma, it is the second most common malignant intraocular tumour. Survival rates are over 95% in specialized centres,
with high rates of globe salvage and preservation of vision in many
eyes, but are much lower in the developing world. ¯Çƒe psychological impact on parents of a child with retinoblastoma is high,
with many su¯Çøering from depression, anxiety and stress. Tumours
are composed of small basophilic cells (retinoblasts) with large
hyperchromatic nuclei and scanty cytoplasm. Many retinoblastomas are undi¯Çøerentiated but varying degrees of di¯Çøerentiation
are characterized by the formation of structures known as rosettes

(FlexnerÔÇôWintersteiner, HomerÔÇôWright and ¯Ç£eurettes ÔÇô Fig.
20.38A). Growth (Fig. 20.38B) may be endophytic (into the vitreous) with seeding of tumour cells throughout the eye, or exophytic
(into the subretinal space) leading to retinal detachment, or mixed,
or the retina may be di¯Çøusely in¯ÇØltrated. Optic nerve invasion (Fig.
20.38C) may occur, with spread of tumour along the subarachnoid
space to the brain. Metastatic spread is to regional nodes, lung,
brain and bone.

TIP Retinoblastoma needs to be excluded in a young child
with leukocoria or a squint.

## Genetics

¯Çƒe genetics of retinoblastoma are o¯Ç×en highlighted as a paradigm
illustrating the genetic basis of cancer. ¯Çƒe tumour suppressor
gene in which mutations predisposing to retinoblastoma occur
is RB1; over 900 di¯Çøerent mutations have been reported to date.
¯Çƒe size of a gene deletion tends to correlate with aggressive retinoblastoma behaviour. Mutations in RB1 or associated genes in a
common pathway are also disrupted in many sporadic tumours.
Modi¯ÇØer genes for retinoblastoma have also been identi¯ÇØed and
may constitute therapeutic targets.
¯Çü Heritable (hereditary, germline) retinoblastoma accounts
for 40%. In heritable retinoblastoma one of the pair of alleles
of RB1 is mutated in all the cells in the body. When a further mutagenic event (ÔÇÿsecond hitÔÇÖ according to the ÔÇÿtwo-hitÔÇÖ
hypothesis proposed by Knudson) a¯Çøects the second allele,
the cell may then undergo malignant transformation. Because
of the presence of the mutation in all cells, a large majority
of these children develop bilateral and multifocal tumours.

## Heritable retinoblastoma patients also have a predisposition to

non-ocular cancers such as pinealoblastoma (ÔÇÿtrilateral retinoblastomaÔÇÖ, which occurs in up to 10%, usually before the age
of 5), osteosarcoma, so¯Ç× tissue sarcoma and melanoma; each
of these tends to occur in a particular age group. ¯Çƒe risk of a
second malignancy is about 6% but this increases ¯ÇØve-fold if
external beam irradiation has been used to treat the original
tumour, the second tumour tending to arise within the irradiated ¯ÇØeld.

tumour showing abundant FlexnerÔÇôWintersteiner rosettes;

optic nerve demonstrating an area of tumour in¯Çƒltration.

¯Çü Non-heritable (non-hereditary, somatic) retinoblastoma. ¯Çƒe
tumour is unilateral, not transmissible and does not predispose the patient to second non-ocular cancers. If a patient has
a solitary retinoblastoma and no positive family history, this is
almost certainly (but not conclusively) non-heritable so that
the risk in each sibling and the patientÔÇÖs o¯Çøspring is about 1%.

## Ninety per cent of children with unilateral retinoblastoma will

have the non-hereditary form.
¯Çü Screening of at-risk family members. Germline mutations are
autosomal dominant and so transmitted to 50%, but because of
incomplete penetrance only 40% of o¯Çøspring will be a¯Çøected.
Detection of mutations in RB1 has approached 95% in recent
years. Once identi¯ÇØed in a particular child, the same mutation
can be sought in siblings, its presence con¯ÇØrming their highrisk status. Siblings at risk of retinoblastoma should be screened
by prenatal ultrasonography, by ophthalmoscopy soon a¯Ç×er
birth and then regularly until the age of 5ÔÇô7 years. Early diagnosis correlates with a higher chance of preserving vision, salvaging the eye and preserving life. With e¯Çøective screening of
babies with RB1 pathogenic variants who develop retinoblastoma, a ¯ÇØnal visual acuity of 6/6 to 6/12 can be achieved in up to
90%. If a child has heritable retinoblastoma, the risk to siblings
is 2% if the parents are healthy and 40% if a parent is a¯Çøected.
It is important that all family members, including the parents,
are examined for the presence of retinoblastoma-associated eye
lesions (retinomas, calci¯ÇØed retinal scars, phthisis).

TIP Heritable retinoblastoma patients are predisposed to
non-ocular tumours (pinealoblastoma, osteosarcoma, soft tissue
sarcoma and melanoma).

International classi¯Ç×cation of retinoblastoma

¯Çü Group A: Small intraretinal tumours (<3 mm) away from
foveola and disc.
¯Çü Group B: Tumours >3 mm, macular or juxtapapillary location,
or with subretinal ¯Ç£uid.
¯Çü Group C: Tumour with focal subretinal or vitreous seeding
within 3 mm of tumour.
¯Çü Group D: Tumour with di¯Çøuse subretinal or vitreous seeding

  - 3 mm from tumour.
¯Çü Group E: Extensive retinoblastoma occupying >50% of the
globe with or without neovascular glaucoma, haemorrhage,
extension of tumour to optic nerve or anterior chamber.

## Clinical features

¯Çü Presentation is within the ¯ÇØrst year of life in bilateral cases
and around 2 years of age if the tumour is unilateral. Careful
enquiry about a family history of ocular tumours is critical.

{ Leukocoria (white pupillary re¯Ç£ex) is the commonest presentation (60%) (Fig. 20.39A) and may be ¯ÇØrst noticed in
family photographs.

{ Strabismus is the second most common (20%). Fundus
examination is therefore mandatory in all cases of childhood squint.

{ Cycloplegic refraction.
¯Çü Ultrasound is used mainly to assess tumour size. It also
detects calcification (Fig. 20.40E) within the tumour and is
helpful in the exclusion of simulating lesions such as Coats
disease.
¯Çü Wide-¯Ç£eld photography (portable if necessary) is useful
for both surveying and documentation and o¯Çøers particular
advantages in the management of retinoblastoma.
¯Çü CT also detects calci¯ÇØcation (Fig. 20.40F) but entails a signi¯ÇØcant dose of radiation so is avoided by many practitioners.
Plain X-rays may be used to detect calci¯ÇØcation in resourcepoor regions.

nerve evaluation, detection of extraocular extension and
pinealoblastoma and to aid di¯Çøerentiation from simulating
conditions.
¯Çü Systemic assessment includes physical examination and MRI
scans of the orbit and skull as a minimum in high-risk cases.

## If these indicate the presence of metastatic disease then bone

scans, bone marrow aspiration and lumbar puncture are also
performed.
¯Çü Genetic studies on tumour tissue and blood samples from the
patient and relatives.

## Investigation

¯Çü Red re¯ÇØex testing with a direct ophthalmoscope is a simple
screening test for leukocoria that is easily employed in the
community.
¯Çü Examination under anaesthesia includes the following:

## Treatment

A collaborative approach is required, involving the ophthalmologist, paediatric oncologist, ocular pathologist, geneticist, allied
health professionals and parents. Treatment is highly individualized. ¯Çƒe prognosis has improved signi¯ÇØcantly in recent years.
¯Çü Chemotherapy is the mainstay of treatment in most cases and
may be used in conjunction with local treatments (focal consolidation ÔÇô see below).

{ Intravenous treatment: carboplatin, etoposide and vincristine (CEV) are given in three to six cycles according
to the grade of retinoblastoma. Single- (carboplatin alone)
or dual-agent therapy has also given favourable results in
some circumstances, such as bridging therapy to allow
deferral of more aggressive measures.

{ Selective ophthalmic artery infusion: this is a promising
new therapeutic option with high rates of globe salvage.
¯Çƒe treatment o¯Çøers signi¯ÇØcantly better results than intravenous treatment in eyes classi¯ÇØed as group D. In this
procedure a small-calibre cannula is introduced via the
femoral artery into the opening of the ophthalmic artery.

minutes and is carried into the branches of the arteries that
supply blood to the uvea and retina. Chorioretinal ischaemia and visual loss have been reported, but the overall
safety pro¯ÇØle is better than IV multi-drug chemotherapy.

{ Intravitreal melphalan seems to be e¯Çøective for vitreous seeding, though it carries a small risk of extraocular
dissemination.

{ Systemic spread, especially CNS involvement, involves
aggressive treatment with high-dose chemotherapy and
autologous stem cell rescue.
¯Çü Review. Careful review at frequent intervals is generally
required following treatment, in order to detect recurrence
or the development of a new tumour, particularly in heritable
disease.

¯Çü Extraocular extension

{ Adjuvant chemotherapy consisting of a 6-month course of
CEV is given subsequent to enucleation at some centres if
there is retrolaminar or massive choroidal spread.

{ External beam radiotherapy is indicated when there is
tumour extension to the cut end of the optic nerve at enucleation, or extension through the sclera.

{ Chemo-reduction may be followed by focal treatment with
cryotherapy or TTT to consolidate tumour control.
¯Çü TTT achieves focal consolidation following chemotherapy, or
is sometimes used as an isolated treatment. Focal techniques
such as TTT and cryotherapy exert both a direct e¯Çøect and
probably increase susceptibility to the e¯Çøects of chemotherapy.
¯Çü Cryotherapy using a triple freezeÔÇôthaw technique is useful for
pre-equatorial tumours without either deep invasion or vitreous seeding.
¯Çü Brachytherapy using a radioactive plaque can be utilized for
an anterior tumour if there is no vitreous seeding and in other
circumstances such as resistance to chemotherapy (Fig. 20.41).
¯Çü External beam radiotherapy should be avoided, particularly
in patients with heritable retinoblastoma, because of the risk
of inducing a second malignancy. At present this option is
used only for eyes with residual or relapsed retinoblastoma
following previous intravenous or ophthalmic artery infusion.
Adverse e¯Çøects include cataract, radiation neuropathy, radiation retinopathy and hypoplasia of the bony orbit.
¯Çü Enucleation is generally indicated if there is neovascular glaucoma, anterior chamber in¯ÇØltration, optic nerve invasion or if
a tumour occupies more than half the vitreous volume (group
E). It is also considered if chemo-reduction fails and is useful
for di¯Çøuse retinoblastoma because of a poor visual prognosis
and a high risk of recurrence with other modalities. Enucleation should be performed with minimal manipulation and it
is imperative to excise a section of optic nerve to at least
10 mm.

TIP External beam radiotherapy should be avoided if possible
in children with heritable retinoblastoma because of the risk of
inducing a late second malignancy in the radiation ¯Çƒeld.

{ ¯Çƒe size and density of the retrolental ¯ÇØbrovascular tissue

{ Complications include cataract and angle-closure glaucoma.

¯Çü Coats disease is almost always unilateral, more common
in boys and tends to present later than retinoblastoma (see
Ch. 13).
¯Çü Retinopathy of prematurity, if advanced, may cause retinal
detachment and leukocoria. Diagnosis is usually straightforward because of the history of prematurity and low birthweight (see Ch. 13).
¯Çü Toxocariasis. Chronic Toxocara endophthalmitis (see Ch.
12) may cause a cyclitic membrane and a white pupil. A
granuloma at the posterior pole may resemble an endophytic
retinoblastoma.
¯Çü Uveitis may mimic the di¯Çøuse in¯ÇØltrating type of retinoblastoma seen in older children. Conversely, retinoblastoma may
be mistaken for uveitis, endophthalmitis or orbital cellulitis.
¯Çü Vitreoretinal dysplasia is caused by faulty di¯Çøerentiation of
the retina and vitreous that results in a detached dysplastic retina forming a retrolental mass with leukocoria (Fig. 20.43A and
B). Other features include microphthalmos, shallow anterior
chamber and elongated ciliary processes. Dysplasia may occur
in isolation or in association with systemic abnormalities:

{ Norrie disease is an X-linked recessive disorder in which
a¯Çøected males are blind at birth or early infancy. It is
caused by mutations in the NDP gene. Systemic features
include cochlear deafness and mental retardation.

{ Incontinentia pigmenti is an X-linked dominant condition
that is lethal in utero for boys. Mutations have been found
in the NEMO gene. It is characterized by a vesiculobullous rash on the trunk and extremities (Fig. 20.43C) that
with time is replaced by linear pigmentation (Fig. 20.43D).
Other features include malformation of teeth, hair, nails,
bones and CNS.

{ WalkerÔÇôWarburg syndrome is an autosomal recessive condition characterized by absence of cortical gyri
and cerebellar malformations that may be associated
with hydrocephalus and encephalocele. Neonatal death
is common and survivors su¯Çøer severe developmental
delay. Apart from vitreoretinal dysplasia other ocular

features include Peters anomaly, cataract, uveal coloboma,
microphthalmos and optic nerve hypoplasia.
¯Çü Other tumours

{ Retinoma (retinocytoma) is a variant of retinoblastoma
that generally exhibits benign behaviour but has a genetic
pro¯ÇØle indicating premalignancy ÔÇô rarely, a retinoma can
undergo late transformation into a rapidly growing retinoblastoma. It manifests as a smooth whitish dome-shaped
lesion, which typically involutes spontaneously to a calci¯ÇØed mass associated with RPE alteration and chorioretinal
atrophy (Fig. 20.44).

{ Retinal astrocytoma, which may be multifocal and bilateral (see below).

## Retinal astrocytoma

## Introduction

Astrocytoma (astrocytic hamartoma) of the retina and optic
nerve head is rare. It frequently does not threaten vision or
require treatment. Most are endophytic, protruding into the
vitreous, but exophytic subretinal tumours can occur. An astrocytoma may be encountered as an incidental solitary lesion
in normal individuals but is most frequently seen in tuberous
sclerosis (see below) and occasionally in association with neurofibromatosis type 1 and retinitis pigmentosa. About 50% of
patients with tuberous sclerosis have one or more astrocytomas,
which may be bilateral.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Most tumours are asymptomatic and detected on
screening for tuberous sclerosis. Isolated lesions, which are not
associated with known systemic disease, are usually found at a
routine eye examination.
¯Çü Signs. A yellowish or white semi-transparent plaque, nodule
or mulberry-like lesion, o¯Ç×en semi-transparent in its periphery and calci¯ÇØed centrally (Fig. 20.45A).

A B

C D

vesicular-bullous rash in incontinentia pigmenti; (D) linear cutaneous pigmentation in an older
child. (Courtesy of J Harry and G Misson, from Clinical Ophthalmic Pathology, ButterworthHeinemann 2001 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A.)

¯Çü OCT shows a granular hyper-re¯Ç£ective appearance of the retinal layers with relative sparing of underlying RPE.
¯Çü FAF ¯ÇØndings depend on the individual lesionÔÇÖs characteristics.
Hyperauto¯Ç£uorescence is seen in seven calci¯ÇØed areas (Fig.
20.45D), with hypoauto¯Ç£uorescence elsewhere.
¯Çü FA shows a prominent super¯ÇØcial vascular network within the
tumour in the arterial phase followed by late leakage and staining.
¯Çü Systemic assessment. With an isolated astrocytoma discovered
incidentally in an older patient, the likelihood of the presence
of forme fruste tuberous sclerosis is low. However, the option of
genetic testing and systemic investigation may be o¯Çøered.

## Tuberous sclerosis

¯Çü Lesions can be large (Fig. 20.45B) and/or have a cystic component (Fig. 20.45C). Growth with vision-threatening complications can occur.

Tuberous sclerosis (Bourneville disease) is an autosomal dominant
phacomatosis characterized by the development of hamartomas in
multiple organ systems from all primary germ layers. ¯Çƒe mutation
is in the TSC1 gene on chromosome 9q34 coding for hamartin, or
in the TSC2 gene on chromosome 16p13 coding for tuberin. ¯Çƒese
proteins act as suppressors of tumour growth. ¯Çƒe classic triad of
epilepsy, mental retardation and adenoma sebaceum is present in

884 Retinal Vascular Tumours

variant; (D) red-free image of lesion infero-temporal to the disc.

only a minority of patients, but is diagnostic. About 60% of cases
are sporadic and 40% are autosomal dominant.
¯Çü Cutaneous signs

¯Çü Visceral tumours

{ Renal angiomyolipomas and cysts.

{ Adenoma sebaceum, consisting of ¯ÇØbroangiomatous red
papules with a butter¯Ç£y distribution around the nose and
cheeks (Fig. 20.46A), is universal.

{ Ash leaf spots are hypopigmented macules on the trunk,
limbs and scalp (Fig. 20.46B). In infants with sparse skin
pigmentation, they are best detected using ultraviolet light,
under which they ¯Ç£uoresce (Wood lamp).

{ Shagreen patches consist of di¯Çøuse thickening over the
lumbar region.

{ Pulmonary lymphangiomatosis.
¯Çü Ocular features apart from fundus astrocytomas include

{ Cardiac rhabdomyomas.

nodules and giant cell astrocytic hamartomas.

RETINAL VASCULAR TUMOURS

## Capillary haemangioma

## Introduction

Retinal capillary haemangioma is a rare sight-threatening tumour
that may occasionally occur in isolation, although about 50% of
patients with solitary lesions and virtually all patients with multiple lesions have von HippelÔÇôLindau disease (VHL ÔÇô see below).
¯Çƒe prevalence of retinal tumours in VHL (o¯Ç×en referred to as
haemangioblastomas) is approximately 60%. Vascular endothelial
growth factor (VEGF) is important in the development of retinal

{ Learning di¯ÇÜculties.

{ Seizures.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. Tumours may be detected by screening of those at
risk or because of symptoms due to macular exudates or retinal
detachment.
¯Çü Signs

{ Early tumours appear as small red oval or round lesions
located between an arteriole and venule (Fig. 20.47A).

{ A well-established tumour is seen as a round orange-red
mass, usually located in the superior or inferior temporal
periphery with dilatation and tortuosity of the supplying
artery and draining vein extending from the optic disc
(Fig. 20.47B).

A

B

C

ash leaf spot (arrow); (C) subungual hamartoma.

lesions, which are composed of capillary-like vascular channels
between large foamy cells. ¯Çƒe median age at haemangioma diagnosis in patients with VHL is earlier (25 years) than in those without VHL (31 years).

{ A juxtapapillary site is common (Fig. 20.47C and D).
Lesions are o¯Ç×en on the temporal side of the disc, so that
leakage can rapidly threaten the fovea.
¯Çü Complications

{ Vitreous haemorrhage, secondary glaucoma and phthisis
bulbi.
¯Çü FA shows early hyper¯Ç£uorescence and late leakage (Fig.
20.48).

## Treatment

¯Çü Observation is advised for asymptomatic juxtapapillary
haemangiomas without exudation, because these may
remain inactive for many years and because of the high
risk of iatrogenic visual loss. Early peripheral lesions are
not usually left untreated because they are relatively easy
to ablate.
¯Çü Laser photocoagulation of small lesions. A¯Ç×er closing the
feeder vessels, the tumour is treated with low-energy, longduration burns. Multiple sessions may be needed.
¯Çü Cryotherapy for larger peripheral lesions, especially those
with exudative retinal detachment. Vigorous treatment of a
large lesion may cause extensive but usually temporary exudative retinal detachment.
¯Çü Brachytherapy for lesions too large for cryotherapy.
¯Çü Vitreoretinal surgery may be required for non-absorbing
vitreous haemorrhage, epiretinal ¯ÇØbrosis or tractional retinal
detachment. If appropriate, the tumour may be destroyed by
endolaser photocoagulation or surgical removal.
¯Çü Other modalities include photodynamic therapy (PDT),
which avoids damage to adjacent tissues and anti-VEGF
agents. ¯Çƒese may be particularly appropriate for juxtapapillary tumours, which are otherwise virtually untreatable without visual loss.

Von HippelÔÇôLindau disease

Inherited VHL is autosomal dominant; about 20% are sporadic.
¯Çƒe disease shows almost complete penetrance by 60 years of age.
It is caused by a mutation in the VHL tumour suppressor gene on
chromosome 3 which results in increased expression of tumourinducing molecules.

{ Leakage with exudate formation and/or haemorrhage.

{ Fibrotic bands, which can progress to tractional or rhegmatogenous retinal detachment.
