## Cavernous haemangioma

## Introduction

## Cavernous haemangioma of the retina and optic nerve head is a

rare unilateral congenital hamartoma. It is usually sporadic but
occasionally can be inherited as autosomal dominant with incomplete penetrance in combination with lesions of the skin and CNS.
Mutations in several di¯Çøerent genes have been implicated. Histopathology shows multiple thin-walled dilated channels with surface gliosis.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms may occur secondary to vitreous haemorrhage.
More frequently the lesions are detected by chance.
¯Çü Signs

¯Çü Clinical features

{ Retinal haemangioblastoma is one of the ¯ÇØrst manifestations of the disease with an age of onset of about 25 years,
although about 5% can present under the age of 10.

{ CNS haemangioblastoma involving the cerebellum (Fig.
20.49), spinal cord, medulla or pons a¯Çøects about 25% of
patients with retinal tumours.

{ Clusters of saccular aneurysms resembling a ÔÇÿbunch of grapesÔÇÖ
(Fig. 20.50AÔÇôC), with associated greyish ¯ÇØbrous tissue.

{ Because of sluggish ¯Ç£ow of blood, the red cells may sediment and separate from plasma, giving rise to ÔÇÿmenisciÔÇÖ or
¯Ç£uid levels within the lesion.

{ A lesion occasionally involves the optic nerve head.

{ Phaeochromocytoma.

{ Renal carcinoma and pancreatic islet cell carcinoma.

{ Cysts of testes, kidneys, ovaries, lungs, liver and pancreas.

{ Polycythaemia, which may be the` result of factors released
by a cerebellar or renal tumour.

{ Endolymphatic sac tumours develop in the inner ear in
10%, with consequent hearing and balance di¯ÇÜculties.
¯Çü Screening is vital as it is impossible to predict which patients
with retinal haemangiomas will harbour systemic lesions. Relatives should also be screened because of the dominant inheritance pattern of the disease. ¯Çƒe following screening protocol
should be regularly performed in patients with established
VHL and relatives at risk.

{ Annual physical examination, retinal examination from
age 2 to 5 years (6-monthly from ages 10 to 30 years),
renal ultrasonography from age 15 years, 24-hour urine
collection for estimation of vanillylmandelic acid and
catecholamine levels from age 2 to 5 years to detect
phaeochromocytoma.

{ Audiometry should be performed if there are any hearing
or balance problems.

{ Two-yearly abdominal and brain MRI scans from the age
of 15. If CNS lesions are symptom-free, treatment may not
be required.

{ It is thought safe to discontinue screening at around age 60
years if no abnormality has been identi¯ÇØed.

{ Genetic testing is indicated in all patients with suspected
VHL and in ¯ÇØrst- and second-degree relatives. With

{ Haemorrhage and epiretinal membrane formation can
occur.
¯Çü OCT demonstrates the aneurysmal vessels (Fig. 20.50D).
¯Çü FA highlights the sedimentation of erythrocytes and shows
delayed ¯ÇØlling in the venous phase and lack of leakage.

## Treatment

Rarely, vitrectomy may be necessary for non-absorbing vitreous
haemorrhage, but photocoagulation should be avoided as it may
precipitate haemorrhage and enlargement of the tumour.

## Congenital retinal arteriovenous

communication (racemose
haemangioma)

A congenital arteriovenous communication, the more severe
forms of which are o¯Ç×en termed racemose haemangioma, is a rare
sporadic congenital malformation, with unilateral involvement in
single or multiple sites of the same eye, most commonly temporally. Occasionally reported complications include haemorrhage,
exudation and vascular occlusion, though the vision is commonly
una¯Çøected and the condition discovered at a routine examination.
A visual ¯ÇØeld defect may be present. Some patients may harbour
ipsilateral brain, facial bone and skin lesions (Wyburn-Mason syndrome), particularly those with more severe retinal changes.
¯Çü Group 1 consists of an anastomosis with the interposition of
an abnormal capillary or arteriolar plexus. A congenital retinal

888 Primary Intraocular Lymphoma

A B

C D

nerve; (C) larger lesion showing ¯Çƒbrosis; (D) OCT of lesion in (C) demonstrating aneurysms.

macrovessel is o¯Ç×en present (Fig. 20.51A). ¯Çƒis is an aberrant blood vessel, o¯Ç×en larger than normal and usually a vein,
located in the posterior pole; it may cross the fovea and horizontal raphe. Areas of capillary non-perfusion and foveal cysts
may be seen. ¯Çƒe anomaly is non-progressive and is usually
associated with good vision.
¯Çü Group 2. Direct arteriovenous communication, without an
intervening capillary bed. ¯Çƒe intervening channels may be
intermediate or large in size (Fig. 20.51B).
¯Çü Group 3. Many large-calibre tortuous anastomosing blood vessels that are o¯Ç×en more numerous than normal, with venules
and arterioles having a similar appearance. ¯Çƒe changes extend
from and are most evident in the region of the optic disc. With
time the dilatation and tortuosity become more marked and
sclerosis may be seen (Fig. 20.51C) but no leakage is seen on
FA (Fig. 20.51D).

## Vasoproliferative tumour

## Retinal vasoproliferative tumour is a rare gliovascular lesion

that can be primary (80%) or secondary to conditions such as

intermediate uveitis, ocular trauma and retinitis pigmentosa. Secondary lesions may be multiple and occasionally bilateral depending on the underlying aetiology. Histology shows glial cells and a
network of ¯ÇØne capillaries with some larger dilated vessels. Presentation is usually in the third to ¯ÇØ¯Ç×h decades, with blurring of vision
due to macular exudation. A reddish-yellow globular gliovascular
mass (Fig. 20.52A) is seen, most frequently in the inferotemporal
periphery; retinal vessels may be seen entering the lesion posteriorly. Although the tumour is benign it can cause profound visual
loss. Complications include subretinal exudation (Fig. 20.52B),
exudative retinal detachment, macular oedema and ¯ÇØbrosis and
haemorrhage. Treatment with cryotherapy or brachytherapy
induces regression of the tumour and exudation, but the visual
prognosis is poor if there is maculopathy.

Lymphoma is a group of conditions characterized by neoplastic proliferation of cells of the immune system typi¯ÇØed by

890 Primary Intraocular Lymphoma

lymphadenopathy, constitutional symptoms and, occasionally,
CNS involvement. ¯Çƒe main classi¯ÇØcation and ocular manifestations are as follows:
¯Çü Hodgkin disease may cause anterior uveitis, vitritis and multifocal fundus lesions resembling chorioretinitis.
¯Çü Non-Hodgkin lymphoma can manifest with conjunctival
involvement, orbital involvement, Mikulicz syndrome and
uveal in¯ÇØltration.
¯Çü CNS B-cell lymphoma may be associated with intermediate
uveitis and sub-RPE in¯ÇØltrates.
¯Çü Primary vitreoretinal lymphoma (PVRL) represents a subset
of primary central nervous system lymphoma (PCNSL), a variant of extranodal non-Hodgkin lymphoma. ¯Çƒe PCNSL cells
are large, pleomorphic B lymphocytes with large multilobular
nuclei, prominent nucleoli and scanty cytoplasm. ¯Çƒe tumour
arises from within the brain, spinal cord and leptomeninges,
is aggressive and has a poor prognosis. About 20% of patients
with PCNSL have ocular manifestations, which can precede
or follow neurological involvement. Most patients with PVRL

{ Occasionally coalescence of sub-RPE deposits may completely encircle the fundus.

{ Other features include retinal vasculitis, vascular occlusion, exudative retinal detachment and optic atrophy.

subsequently develop CNS symptoms, with a mean delay of 29
months. Most of those a¯Çøected are older adults.
¯Çü Primary uveal lymphoma is a rare entity, usually of B-cell origin, that tends to follow an extended relatively benign course
and will not be considered further in this section.

## Ocular features

¯Çü Symptoms. Unilateral ¯Ç£oaters, blurred vision, red eye or
photophobia. ¯Çƒe symptoms frequently become bilateral a¯Ç×er
a variable interval.
¯Çü Signs of PVRL

{ Mild anterior uveitis with cells, ¯Ç£are and keratic
precipitates.

{ Vitritis may impede visualization of the fundus.

{ Large, o¯Ç×en multifocal, subretinal in¯ÇØltrates (Fig. 20.53).

A B

C D

phase; (C) extensive subretinal in¯Çƒltration with overlying mottling; (D) multifocal subretinal
in¯Çƒltrates without mottling. (Courtesy of C Barry ÔÇô ¯Çƒgs A and B.)

{ Absence of cystoid macular oedema (CMO) is an important diagnostic indicator, since this is almost always present in true in¯Ç£ammatory vitritis.

## Neurological features

¯Çü Symptoms. An intracranial mass may cause headache, nausea,
personality change, focal de¯ÇØcit or seizures. Leptomeningeal
disease may cause neuropathy and spinal cord involvement
may cause bilateral motor and sensory de¯ÇØcits.
¯Çü Clinical neurological examination may demonstrate abnormalities such as cranial nerve palsies, hemiparesis and ataxia.

## Investigation

¯Çü OCT will con¯ÇØrm an absence of CMO in most cases, aiding
di¯Çøerentiation from vitritis and may demonstrate subretinal
in¯ÇØltrative lesions.
¯Çü FA shows blockage with a granular characteristic, due to the
presence of sub-RPE accumulation of lymphomatous cells
(ÔÇÿleopard skin spotsÔÇÖ).
¯Çü Ultrasound may show vitreous debris, elevated subretinal
lesions, retinal detachment and thickening of the optic nerve.
¯Çü Cytology of vitreous samples or subretinal nodules.
¯Çü Immunohistochemistry based on cell-surface markers allows
identi¯ÇØcation of the lymphocytic proliferation, which is of a
B-cell type in most patients.
¯Çü MRI of head and spine with gadolinium contrast may detect
one or more intracranial tumours, di¯Çøuse meningeal or periventricular lesions and/or localized intradural spinal masses.
¯Çü Lumbar puncture may demonstrate malignant cells in the
CSF in a minority of patients with abnormal MR imaging, but
a positive result avoids the need for brain or eye biopsy.

## Treatment

Treatment of both the eye and the brain is o¯Ç×en indicated. Radiotherapy and chemotherapy are the mainstays, but an optimal
algorithm has not been established. Treatment limited to the eye
may not improve survival, but local treatment may be preferable
if there is monocular disease and no evidence of involvement
elsewhere.
¯Çü Radiotherapy has long been the ¯ÇØrst-line treatment for PVRL,
but recurrence is common and complications such as radiation
retinopathy and cataract can occur. It now tends to be reserved
for some cases of bilateral disease but may be associated with
lower recurrence rates than chemotherapy alone. It is better
tolerated by younger patients.
¯Çü Intravitreal methotrexate is useful for recurrent disease, but
close monitoring is needed to detect ocular complications and
any recurrence.
¯Çü Systemic chemotherapy with a variety of agents such as methotrexate can prolong survival in patients with CNS disease.
¯Çƒis can be given in combination with whole brain irradiation but neurotoxicity is a problem. A variety of methods have
been developed to overcome the bloodÔÇôbrain barrier. Systemic
treatment is usually e¯Çøective for ocular disease and this is
preferred to ocular radiotherapy in some centres because in
addition to avoiding radiation-induced complications it may

improve survival. Monotherapy for primary intraocular lymphoma (PIOL) with ifosfamide or trofosfamide has also been
successful.
¯Çü Biologic agents involving speci¯ÇØc anti-B cell monoclonal
antibodies (e.g. rituximab), may represent a useful alternative,
but are generally given intravitreally because of poor penetration through the bloodÔÇôbrain barrier.

TUMOURS OF THE RETINAL PIGMENT
EPITHELIUM

Congenital hypertrophy of the RPE

## Introduction

## Congenital hypertrophy of the retinal pigment epithelium

(CHRPE) is the term used to encompass three entities with distinct features and implications: solitary CHRPE, grouped CHRPE
and atypical CHRPE.

Solitary (unifocal) CHRPE

¯Çü A ¯Ç£at or minimally elevated dark-grey or black (Fig. 20.54A),
round, oval or larger scalloped-edged lesion with well-de¯ÇØned
margins, usually located near the equator or in the peripheral
fundus.
¯Çü A depigmented halo located just inside the margin (Fig.
20.54B) and depigmented lacunae (Fig. 20.54C) are common,
particularly as a lesion matures. Some lesions may become virtually totally depigmented.
¯Çü Median diameter is 4ÔÇô5 mm but lesions can be much smaller
or very large, especially in the far periphery (Fig. 20.54D)
and may cause diagnostic di¯ÇÜculty unless the possibility of
CHRPE is borne in mind.
¯Çü A posterior pole location is uncommon (2%).
¯Çü Histopathology shows densely packed RPE cells replete with
large melanosomes ÔÇô a combination of cellular hyperplasia and
hypertrophy.
¯Çü Although formerly regarded as following a consistently stable
course, a slight enlargement of the involved area over time is
very common and the development of a presumed adenomatous or adenocarcinomatous nodule arising within the lesion
(see below) occurs in around 2%. Long-term review is therefore now advised.
¯Çü Solitary CHRPE does not have an association with increased
gastrointestinal malignancy risk.

Grouped (multifocal) CHRPE

¯Çü Multiple lesions, much smaller than those of solitary CHRPE
and without haloes or lacunae, orientated in a pattern simulating animal footprints (ÔÇÿbear tracksÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 20.55A), o¯Ç×en con¯ÇØned to one sector or quadrant of the fundus with the smaller
spots located more centrally.
¯Çü ¯Çƒere are typically several groups.
¯Çü Rarely the lesions may be depigmented (ÔÇÿpolar bear tracksÔÇÖ ÔÇô

¯Çü Only one eye is involved in almost all cases.

Atypical congenital hypertrophy of the RPE

¯Çü Multiple oval, spindle, comma- or ¯ÇØshtail-shaped lesions of
very variable size associated with irregularly hypopigmented
margins and perilesional areas (Fig. 20.56).
¯Çü Both eyes are involved.
¯Çü ¯Çƒe lesions have a haphazard, not sectoral, distribution and
may be pigmented, depigmented or heterogeneous.
¯Çü To emphasize the distinction with the CHRPE variants that
are not associated with gastrointestinal malignancy, the alternative term ÔÇÿretinal pigment epithelial hamartomas associated
with familial adenomatous polyposisÔÇÖ (RPEH-FAP) has been
proposed.
¯Çü Systemic associations

{ Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is an autosomal
dominant condition characterized by adenomatous polyps
throughout the rectum and colon, which usually start to
develop in adolescence. It results from a germline mutation
in the gene APC; mutations falling within a certain codon
range in this gene are associated with the presence of related
CHRPE. Patients undergo regular colonoscopy and polyp
excision. If untreated, virtually all patients with FAP develop
carcinoma of the colorectal region by the age of 50 years. As
a result of the dominant inheritance pattern, genetic testing
and intensive survey of at-risk family members is imperative. Between 70% and 80% of patients with FAP have atypical CHRPE lesions, which are present at birth.

{ Gardner syndrome is characterized by FAP, osteomas of the
skull, mandible and long bones, and cutaneous so¯Ç× tissue
tumours such as epidermoid cysts, lipomas and ¯ÇØbromas.

{ Turcot syndrome is an autosomal dominant or autosomal
recessive condition characterized by FAP and tumours of
the CNS, particularly medulloblastoma and glioma.

## Combined hamartoma of the retina and

RPE

## Introduction

Combined hamartoma of the retina and RPE is a rare lesion that
is probably congenital. It may be more common in males. It typically occurs sporadically in normal individuals but sometimes in
association with a systemic disorder, particularly neuro¯ÇØbromatosis type 2, which should be considered particularly in children
or if bilateral lesions are present. Histopathology shows thickening of the RPE and sensory retina with prominent glial and
vascular tissue.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Symptoms. ¯Çƒe most common presentation is with decreased
vision or strabismus in early childhood, though symptom
onset in late childhood or early adulthood is also frequent.
¯Çü VA is 6/60 or worse in 40ÔÇô50%, usually secondary to macular
involvement.
¯Çü Fundus

{ Slightly elevated deep grey or brown ÔÇô sometimes orange,
yellow or green ÔÇô pigmented lesion that blends into the
adjacent RPE at its margins (Fig. 20.57A).

{ Super¯ÇØcial whitish epiretinal membrane (ERM) formation
with retinal wrinkling. Occasionally vitreoretinal interface
changes are marked, sometimes with focal tractional retinal detachment.

{ Tortuosity and prominence of overlying retinal vessels,
with evidence of feeder and draining vessels.

{ ¯Çƒe lesion is usually located at the posterior pole, o¯Ç×en
in a juxtapapillary or macular site; peripheral lesions
are uncommon. Dragging of the disc or macula may be
present.

{ Uncommon ¯ÇØndings include secondary macular oedema,
exudation (Fig. 20.57B), choroidal and preretinal neovascularization, retinoschisis and retinal detachment.

894 Tumours of the Retinal Pigment Epithelium

A B

C D

E

and RPE disorganization, associated with a saw-toothed appearance of the retinal surface
secondary to an epiretinal membrane.

¯Çü FA shows early hyper¯Ç£uorescence of the vascular abnormalities and blockage by pigment; the late phase may show leakage
(Fig. 20.57C and D).
¯Çü OCT demonstrates localized full-thickness retinal and RPE
disorganization (Fig. 20.57E). ERM formation has variously
been reported as intrinsic to or distinct from the main lesion.

## Treatment

¯Çü Monitoring for complications.
¯Çü Amblyopia therapy is important in young children.
¯Çü Speci¯Ç£c treatment for macular neovascularization (MNV)
and leakage where indicated.
¯Çü Vitrectomy for ERM has provided bene¯ÇØt in some cases.

## Congenital simple hamartoma of the

RPE

Congenital simple hamartoma of the RPE is a rare entity, usually incidentally diagnosed in asymptomatic children and young
adults. It is typically a small (1.5 mm or less) jet-black nodular
lesion, with well-de¯ÇØned margins, that appears to involve the full
thickness of the retina and RPE and to protrude into the vitreous
cavity (Fig. 20.58A). A feeding artery and draining vein are generally seen. ¯Çƒe lesion is typically located immediately adjacent to
the foveola. VA is usually normal, but is occasionally impaired as a
result of central foveal involvement or traction.

## Adenoma and adenocarcinoma of the

RPE

Adenoma of the RPE is an oval pigmented lesion that most
commonly arises in the peripheral fundus, sometimes from
an area of solitary CHRPE. Associated vitreous in¯Ç£ammatory cells and retinal exudation are frequently present. Associated prominent feeding and draining vessels may develop. ¯Çƒe

behaviour of an adenocarcinoma is thought to be similar to that
of an adenoma. Most lesions are simply observed in the absence
of vision-threatening complications, as metastasis has not been
reported. Fine-needle biopsy is sometimes required for diagnostic
clari¯ÇØcation.

Hyperplasia and migration of the RPE
simulating uveal melanoma

Rare cases have been reported of massively proliferating RPE cells
simulating a uveal melanoma, including by extraocular extension.
True malignant tumours of the RPE are extraordinarily rare.

PARANEOPLASTIC SYNDROMES

## Paraneoplastic retinopathies are rare diseases that might be missed

or misdiagnosed by the unwary observer. Many of the patients
present with visual symptoms before the primary malignancy is
diagnosed. It is therefore important for clinicians to be familiar
with these syndromes in order to detect the underlying malignancy as early as possible.

Bilateral di¯Ç×use uveal melanocytic
proliferation

Bilateral di¯Çøuse uveal melanocytic proliferation (BDUMP) is a very
rare paraneoplastic syndrome occurring usually in patients with
systemic, o¯Ç×en occult, malignancy. It is characterized by the proliferation of benign melanocytes in the outer choroid o¯Ç×en manifesting with multiple naevus-like choroidal lesions (Fig. 20.58B), but
a variety of other anterior and posterior ocular segment features
have been reported, including red-grey subretinal patches, rapidonset cataract, episcleral and conjunctival lesions. Vision can be
severely diminished. ¯Çƒe mechanism is uncertain, but a circulating
factor may be responsible in many cases. Detection of an occult

A B

epithelium; (B) naevus-like lesions in di¯Ç×use uveal melanocytic proliferation. (Courtesy of
A Leys.)

896 Paraneoplastic Syndromes

primary malignancy might enable early treatment to enhance
survival. Treatment of BDUMP itself is generally unrewarding ÔÇô
improvement with plasmapheresis has been reported ÔÇô although
successful treatment of the underlying primary tumour may be
followed by regression of the BDUMP, but without improvement
in vision.

Cancer-associated retinopathy

Cancer-associated retinopathy (CAR) is most frequently associated with small cell bronchial carcinoma. Visual symptoms precede
the diagnosis of malignancy in half the cases, typically by several
months but sometimes several years. Subacute visual loss occurs
over weeks to months, associated with photopsia. Colour impairment, glare, photosensitivity and central scotoma are attributed to
cone dysfunction. Night blindness, impaired dark adaptation, ring
scotoma and peripheral ¯ÇØeld loss occur due to rod dysfunction.
¯Çƒe fundus o¯Ç×en appears normal on presentation, but attenuated
arterioles, optic disc pallor and mild RPE changes develop as the

disease progresses. ¯Çƒe electroretinogram (ERG) is abnormal at an
early stage under both photopic and scotopic conditions. As with
BDUMP, CAR should prompt a thorough search for an underlying
malignancy, though this is not always found and the condition is
then regarded as an autoimmune retinopathy.

Melanoma-associated retinopathy

¯Çƒe presentation of melanoma-associated retinopathy (MAR)
di¯Çøers from CAR in that the visual symptoms usually arise a¯Ç×er,
rather than prior to, the diagnosis of cutaneous melanoma. ¯Çƒere
may be concurrent vitiligo. Autoantibodies from MAR sera react
against bipolar cells in human retina. Clinical and electrophysiological data also implicate bipolar cells as the target in MAR.
Generally, symptoms and signs are less marked than in CAR. ¯Çƒe
ERG shows marked reduction of dark-adapted and light-adapted
b-wave and preservation of a-wave (normal photoreceptor function), characteristic of bipolar cell dysfunction. ¯Çƒe prognosis for
vision is usually good.

EYELIDS

¯Çü Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are
used for the treatment of many solid tumours. ¯Çƒere is a dosedependent risk of side e¯Ç×ects. Trichomegaly is a common ¯ÇØnding, while blepharitis and dry eye syndrome are less common.
Cicatricial ectropion is rare.
¯Çü Imatinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor and is used for the
treatment of chronic myeloid leukaemia and gastrointestinal
stromal tumours. Up to two-thirds of patients develop periorbital oedema, usually 5ÔÇô8 weeks a¯Ç£er starting treatment.
¯Çƒis is a mild problem and does not require discontinuation of
medication. Ptosis has also been reported.
¯Çü Dasatinib is used for acute lymphoblastic leukemia and rarely
can cause eyelash poliosis, which is reversible on stopping the
medication.
¯Çü Ipilimumab is used for the treatment of metastatic malignant melanoma. Symptoms and signs similar to thyroid eye
disease have been reported. ¯Çƒis usually responds to systemic
steroids.
¯Çü Bortezomib is a proteasome inhibitor which is used to treat
multiple myeloma and predisposes to recurrent chalazia.
¯Çü Isotretinoids are used in the treatment of acne and can cause
blepharoconjunctivitis, xerosis, corneal opacities, night blindness and pseudotumour. ¯Çƒese side e¯Ç×ects are reversible on
stopping the medication.

CORNEA

Vortex keratopathy (cornea verticillata)

## Clinical features

In approximately chronological order:
¯Çü Fine golden-brown opacities form an irregular horizontal line
in the lower corneal epithelium of both eyes, similar to that of
the common age-related HudsonÔÇôSt├ñhli iron line.
¯Çü Several irregular branching horizontal lines form a pattern
resembling the whiskers of a cat.
¯Çü With an increasing number of branches a whorled pattern
develops, centred on a point below the pupil and swirling outwards, usually sparing the limbus.
¯Çü Associated pigmented clumps and iron deposition have been
described.

## Causes

{ Amiodarone can also cause anterior subcapsular lens
deposits and optic neuropathy (see below).
¯Çü Antimalarials

{ Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine are quinolones
used in the treatment of certain autoimmune connective
tissue diseases and in the prophylaxis and treatment of
malaria.

{ In contrast to chloroquine retinopathy (see below), keratopathy bears no relationship to dosage or duration of
treatment. ¯Çƒe changes are usually reversible on cessation of therapy and sometimes clear despite continued
administration.
¯Çü Others. Numerous other drugs have been reported as occasional causes of vortex keratopathy.
¯Çü Fabry disease.

¯Çü Amiodarone

{ Amiodarone is a cardiac antiarrhythmic agent.

{ Virtually all patients develop keratopathy (Fig. 21.1A),
o¯Ç£en soon a¯Ç£er commencement. In general, the higher the
dose and the longer the duration of use, the more substantial the corneal changes.

{ Vision is minimally impaired in around 5%, with loss of
a single line of Snellen acuity, mild blurring and haloes.
¯Çƒis is rarely su¯Çøcient to recommend discontinuation of the drug. ¯Çƒe keratopathy reverses (slowly) on
discontinuation.

## Chlorpromazine

Chlorpromazine is used as a sedative and to treat psychotic mental illness. Some patients on long-term therapy may develop subtle
di¯Ç×use yellowish-brown granular deposits in the corneal endothelium, Descemet membrane and deep stroma (Fig. 21.1B) within
the palpebral ¯ÇØssure area. Anterior lens capsule deposits and retinopathy may also occur (see below).

## Argyrosis

## Argyrosis is a discoloration of ocular tissues secondary to silver

deposits and may be iatrogenic or from occupational exposure.
Keratopathy is characterized by greyish-brown granular deposits
in Descemet membrane (Fig. 21.1C). ¯Çƒe conjunctiva may also be
a¯Ç×ected.

## Chrysiasis

Chrysotherapy is the therapeutic administration of gold, usually in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis. Chrysiasis is the
deposition of gold in living tissue and typically occurs only
after prolonged administration. Virtually all patients who have
received a total dose of gold compound exceeding 1500 mg
develop corneal deposits, characterized by dust-like or glittering purple granules scattered throughout the epithelium and
stroma, concentrated in the deep layers and the periphery (Fig.
21.1D). The findings are innocuous and are not an indication
for cessation of therapy. In some cases, the deposits clear after
stopping treatment whilst in others they may persist. Other
toxic effects of gold are innocuous lens deposits and, occasionally, marginal keratitis.

## Amantadine

Amantadine is an oral agent used in the treatment of Parkinson
disease and related conditions. Some patients develop di¯Ç×use white
punctate opacities that may be associated with epithelial oedema,
1ÔÇô2 weeks a¯Ç£er commencement of the drug, which resolve with
discontinuation.

A

C

## Dupilumab

B

D

the deep corneal stroma and endothelium (arrow). (Courtesy of L Zografos ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. C.)

## Dupilumab is used for eczema and atopic dermatitis and can cause

conjunctivitis, refractory herpetic keratitis and uveitis.

CILIARY EFFUSION

## Topiramate

## Topiramate is an anticonvulsant and is also used in the treatment

of migraine. It can cause acute angle closure with associated myopia secondary to ciliochoroidal e¯Ç×usion. A similar phenomenon
has been reported with other drugs, especially other sulfonamides
(including acetazolamide) and bupropion.
¯Çü Presentation is usually within a month of starting treatment,
with blurred vision and sometimes haloes, ocular pain and
redness.
¯Çü Signs include shallowing of the anterior chamber and raised
intraocular pressure.
¯Çü Treatment consists of reducing the intraocular pressure and
stopping the drug.

¯Çü Prognosis is usually good provided the complication is
recognized.

TIP Topiramate is used in the treatment of migraine and may
result in acute angle closure secondary to swelling of the ciliary
body.

LENS

## Steroids

Systemic and topical steroids can lead to cataract formation. ¯Çƒe
resultant opacities are initially posterior subcapsular (Fig. 21.2A),
with subsequent anterior subcapsular involvement. Individual vulnerability seems to vary; children may be more susceptible than
adults. ¯Çƒe relationship between dose and duration and cataract
formation is unclear, though higher doses and length of treatment
are associated with greater risk. Early opacities may regress if steroids are discontinued, though sometimes progression may occur
despite withdrawal.

900 Uveitis

TIP Excessive use of topical steroids can result in a posterior
subcapsular cataract and/or secondary glaucoma.

## Other drugs

¯Çü Chlorpromazine may cause the deposition of innocuous, ¯ÇØne,
stellate, yellowish-brown granules on the anterior lens capsule
within the pupillary area (Fig. 21.2B) in 50% of patients who
have received a cumulative dose of 1000 g. ¯Çƒe deposits persist
despite discontinuation.
¯Çü Gold causes innocuous anterior capsular deposits in about
50% of patients on treatment for longer than 3 years.
¯Çü Allopurinol, used in the treatment of gout, increases the risk
of cataract formation in elderly patients with a high cumulative
dose or extended treatment duration.

UVEITIS

## Rifabutin

## Rifabutin is used mainly in the management and prophylaxis

of mycobacterial infections. It may cause acute anterior uveitis (AAU), typically associated with a hypopyon. ¯Çƒe associated
vitritis may be mistaken for endophthalmitis. Concomitant use
of drugs such as clarithromycin and ¯ÇÜuconazole that inhibit the
metabolism of rifabutin will increase the risk of uveitis. Treatment
involves dose reduction or withdrawal of the drug.

## Cidofovir

Cidofovir is used in the management of cytomegalovirus (CMV)
retinitis in acquired immunode¯ÇØciency syndrome (AIDS). AAU
with few cells but marked ¯ÇØbrinous exudate may develop following
repeated intravenous infusions. Vitritis is common and hypopyon
may occur with long-term administration. Treatment with topical

steroids and mydriatics is usually successful, obviating the need to
discontinue therapy.

## Bisphosphonates

## Bisphosphonates are a class of drugs that retard absorption of

bone, commonly in osteoporosis but also in several other conditions. ¯Çƒey activate a sub-group of T cells and it is thought that
uveitis (usually anterior) and scleritis sometimes seen following
administration is related to this. In¯ÇÜammation typically occurs
within 2 days of commencement of a bisphosphonate, or earlier
a¯Ç£er intravenous administration.

## Sulfonamides

Uveitis has been reported associated with sulfonamide treatment,
but is rare. Sulfonamides can also precipitate ciliary e¯Ç×usion with
myopia and angle closure (see above) and are a well-documented
cause of StevensÔÇôJohnson syndrome.

## Fluoroquinolones

Systemic administration of ¯ÇÜuoroquinolone antibiotics, particularly moxi¯ÇÜoxacin, has been associated with acute extensive
anterior segment pigment dispersion (see also bilateral acute iris
transillumination (BAIT) and bilateral acute depigmentation of
the iris (BADI) in Ch. 11). It is not clear whether in¯ÇÜammation is
the primary mechanism, which may be phototoxicity due to sensitization by the drug in predisposed individuals.

## Tumour necrosis factor inhibitors

Although these drugs (e.g. etanercept, in¯ÇÜiximab, adalimumab)
have been adopted for the treatment of ocular in¯ÇÜammation, paradoxically uveitis has also been documented as an adverse e¯Ç×ect.
¯Çƒe induction of sarcoidosis has also been reported.

A B

due to chlorpromazine.

RETINA

## Diagnosis

## Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine

## Introduction

Chloroquine was used to prevent and treat malaria in the past,
but is less o¯Ç£en prescribed in recent times because of resistance
to the medication. Hydroxychloroquine is used to treat long-term
in¯ÇÜammatory disorders of the joints and skin. ¯Çƒese medications
are concentrated in melanin-containing structures of the eye, such
as the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and choroid. Hydroxychloroquine is generally safe and cost e¯Ç×ective but can cause permanent loss of vision because of retinal toxicity, particularly if
the recommended dose is exceeded. Retinal adverse e¯Ç×ects may
be more common than previously believed and progression can
occur despite discontinuation of the drug. Signi¯ÇØcant damage can
occur without signs on fundus examination and optical coherence
tomography (OCT) scanning of the macula has therefore emerged
as a key part of screening. Renal impairment and the use of tamoxifen increases the risk of toxicity. Obesity may lead to miscalculation of the safe dose, as hydroxychloroquine is not stored in fat.
Vortex keratopathy may occur (see above).
¯Çü Chloroquine can result in retinal toxicity. ¯Çƒe risk of toxicity increases signi¯ÇØcantly when the cumulative dose exceeds
300 g. Individual susceptibility seems highly variable.
¯Çü Hydroxychloroquine is much safer than chloroquine, particularly if the dose is kept to less than 5 mg/kg real weight/
day. ¯Çƒe risk of developing retinal toxicity has been reported
to be 7.5% for an individual taking the medication for more
than 5 years. Between 20% and 50% of people taking hydroxychloroquine for more than 20 years will have some signs of
retinopathy. ¯Çƒe risk increases with a cumulative dose over
1000 g, equating to a standard twice-daily dose of 200 mg for
around 7 years.

A B

to detect toxicity at this stage prior to irreversible damage.

## Screening

¯Çü Patient education: the importance of regular screening should
be emphasized.
¯Çü Baseline assessment before or soon a¯Ç£er commencement of
treatment is advisable, including documentation of functional
visual parameters together with an OCT scan of the macula.
¯Çƒis serves as a basis for future comparison and also helps
to exclude pre-existing maculopathy as a potential relative
contraindication.
¯Çü Annual review. In the absence of special risk factors, routine
annual review with ancillary testing (central visual ¯ÇØelds, FAF
and SD-OCT) should begin 5 years a¯Ç£er starting treatment.
Review one year a¯Ç£er starting treatment: (a) in children, (b)
those on chloroquine treatment or (c) if there are supplementary risk factors such as age-related maculopathy, substantial
renal impairment, or tamoxifen use.
¯Çü Discontinuation of the drug should be discussed with the
patientÔÇÖs rheumatologist if toxicity is suspected.

¯Çü ¯Çƒioridazine is used to treat schizophrenia and related psychoses. ¯Çƒe normal daily dose is 150ÔÇô600 mg. Doses that
exceed 800 mg/day for just a few weeks may be su¯Çøcient to
cause reduced VA and impairment of dark adaptation. Progressive retinal toxicity can occur.

¯Çü Chlorpromazine. ¯Çƒe normal daily dose is 75ÔÇô300 mg. Retinal toxicity is a risk only if substantially larger doses are used
over a prolonged period and is characterized by non-speci¯ÇØc

¯Çü Digitalis (digoxin) is used as second-line treatment for atrial
¯ÇØbrillation and symptomatic heart failure. It has a narrow
therapeutic margin and toxicity can result in digestive, neurological and visual disturbance. A spectrum of visual abnormality may occur, including decreased visual acuity, scotomas,
photophobia and dyschromatopsia, which is most commonly
found on formal colour testing. Xanthopsia (yellow vision),
cyanopsia (blue vision) and chloropsia (green vision) have
all been reported. Electroretinogram abnormalities include
delayed implicit time and reduced amplitude of the B-wave.

Drug-induced crystalline maculopathies

grey lesions in the outer retina and RPE. Visual impairment is

A rare side e¯Ç×ect is optic neuritis, reversible on cessation of

¯Çü Canthaxanthin is a carotenoid used, o¯Ç£en in quite high doses,
to simulate sun tanning. Over prolonged periods it may cause
the deposition of innocuous glistening yellow inner retinal
deposits in a doughnut conformation at the posterior poles
(Fig. 21.6B). ¯Çƒe deposition is slowly reversible. Because of

¯Çü Methoxy¯Ç×urane is an inhalant general anaesthetic. It is

secondary hyperoxalosis. Ocular involvement is characterized
by calcium oxalate crystals scattered throughout the retina
associated with mild visual impairment and subsequently by
RPE hyperplasia at the posterior pole (Fig. 21.6C).
¯Çü Nitrofurantoin is an antibiotic used principally in the treatment of urinary tract infections. Long-term use may result in
slight visual impairment associated with super¯ÇØcial and deep
glistening intraretinal deposits distributed in a circinate pattern throughout the posterior pole.

## Other drugs causing retinopathy

¯Çü Interferon alfa is used in a range of conditions, including
hepatitis C and numerous malignant tumours. Retinopathy occurs in some patients, particularly those on high-dose
therapy, and is characterized by cotton-wool spots and retinal
haemorrhages (Fig. 21.7A). FA shows areas of focal capillary
non-perfusion. Changes usually resolve spontaneously with
cessation of therapy and in the majority of patients the visual
prognosis is good. Less common ocular side e¯Ç×ects include
cystoid macular oedema, extraocular muscle paresis, optic disc
oedema and retinal vein occlusion.
¯Çü Desferrioxamine (also known as deferoxamine) is a chelating
agent used to manage chronic iron overload, generally to prevent haemosiderosis in patients with conditions requiring regular transfusion. It is most commonly administered via slow
subcutaneous infusion. Patients present with rapid visual loss.

## Initially the fundi may be normal or show only mild macular

greying, but within several weeks mottled pigmentary changes
develop (Fig. 21.8A), associated with abnormal electrodiagnostic ¯ÇØndings. FA shows widespread punctate hyper¯ÇÜuorescence (Fig. 21.8B).
¯Çü Nicotinic acid is a cholesterol-lowering agent. A minority of
patients develop retinopathy when doses greater than 1.5 g
daily are used, in the form of a cystoid maculopathy suggestive

¯Çü Pentosan polysulfate is the only medication that can be
used in the treatment of interstitial cystitis. A characteristic progressive symmetrical maculopathy may occur with
long-term use (usually more than 7 years) and high cumulative exposure. Prolonged dark adaptation, nyctalopia and
blurred vision are initial symptoms, followed by visual loss
secondary to changes in the macular region. There is no

¯Çü Alkyl nitrites (ÔÇÿpoppersÔÇÖ) are recreational drugs used before
sexual activity and can rarely cause a toxic maculopathy. ¯Çƒe
patients are usually young and present with bilateral loss of
visual acuity and photopsia. Clinically a small yellow spot can
be seen in the fovea, which is easily seen using auto¯ÇÜuorescence
imaging (Fig. 21.9A). OCT shows a selective defect in the foveal
inner-segment ellipsoid band (Fig. 21.9B). ¯Çƒere is no treatment, but spontaneous recovery of visual function can occur.

OPTIC NERVE

## Ethambutol

Ethambutol is used in the treatment of tuberculosis, generally in a
multidrug regimen. Its overall safety pro¯ÇØle is favourable, but acute
or chronic optic neuritis can occur. Toxicity is dose- and durationdependent, with an incidence as high as 18% at a daily dose over 35

A B

(Courtesy of R Smith.)

A

B

showing yellow spot in the fovea (arrow); (B) OCT showing
defect within the photoreceptor layer.

mg/kg daily. It is rare (<1%) with a standard daily dose of 15 mg/kg
daily. Toxicity typically occurs between 3 and 6 months of starting
treatment, though it has been reported a¯Ç£er only a few days. Renal
dysfunction may confer a higher risk of toxicity as ethambutol is
excreted via the kidneys.
¯Çü Symptoms may be absent, but typically include painless bilateral blurring, usually central though sometimes paracentral or
peripheral. Impairment of colour vision may be noticed.

¯Çü Signs include minimal to severe reduction in VA, normal or
slightly swollen optic discs with splinter-shaped haemorrhages
and normal or sluggish pupils. RedÔÇôgreen dyschromatopsia is the
most common objective abnormality of colour vision, but subtle
(undetectable on Ishihara testing) blueÔÇôyellow defects may be an
early ¯ÇØnding. Loss of contrast sensitivity is an early sign.
¯Çü Visual ¯ÇØeld defects can be central or peripheral.
¯Çü Prognosis is good following cessation of treatment, although
recovery can take time. A minority sustain permanent visual
impairment, with optic atrophy.
¯Çü Screening. Baseline VA and Ishihara testing are prudent prior
to starting ethambutol and the patient should be advised of
the necessity of reporting any visual disturbance. Repeat testing should be performed every 6 months in patients on the
standard dose. Ethambutol should be stopped immediately if
toxicity develops, with consideration also given to discontinuation of isoniazid if it is being used synchronously (see below).

TIP To reduce the risk of toxic optic neuropathy, the dose of
ethambutol should not exceed 15 mg/kg daily.

## Isoniazid

Isoniazid may very rarely cause toxic optic neuropathy. ¯Çƒe risk is
higher when given in combination with ethambutol.

## Amiodarone

Optic neuropathy, probably secondary to demyelination, a¯Ç×ects
1ÔÇô2% of patients on long-term amiodarone treatment. It is almost
certainly not dose-related. Distinction from non-arteritic anterior
ischaemic optic neuropathy (NAION), which also a¯Ç×ects patients
with systemic vascular disease, may be di¯Çøcult and it has been
suggested that NAION is more common in patients on amiodarone. Di¯Ç×erentiation is clinically important as it is key to a decision

906 Visual Cortex

about whether to discontinue the drug. ¯Çƒe presence of a crowded
optic disc, speed of onset, bilateral involvement, duration of disc
swelling and features of systemic amiodarone toxicity may be helpful in this regard.
¯Çü Presentation is with sudden or insidious unilateral or bilateral
visual impairment, a¯Ç£er a mean period of 6ÔÇô9 months taking
the drug. About one-third of patients are asymptomatic.
¯Çü Signs in a majority are unilateral or bilateral optic disc swelling
that may persist for a few months a¯Ç£er medication is stopped.
Corneal features are discussed above.
¯Çü Investigation

{ Cranial imaging may be indicated when bilateral disc
swelling is present.

{ Visual ¯ÇØeld defects are of varied con¯ÇØguration and severity
and may be reversible or permanent.

{ Prognosis is variable; cessation of the drug usually
improves vision, but 20% may deteriorate further. Final
VA of worse than 6/60 occurs in around 20% of eyes.

{ Screening has been suggested by some authors, but
no consensus exists as to its bene¯ÇØt. Patients should be
warned of the risk and cautioned to report visual symptoms immediately.

## Vigabatrin

Bilateral concentric, predominantly nasal visual ¯ÇØeld constriction occurs in many patients taking the anti-epileptic drug
vigabatrin due to photoreceptor and ganglion cell damage. It is
uncommon at a cumulative total dose of <1 kg, but very common
at >3 kg. A maximum daily dose of 3 g is recommended in adults.
Vigabatrin should be avoided in patients with pre-existing ¯ÇØeld
defects and should not be prescribed in conjunction with other
potentially retinotoxic drugs. ¯Çƒe risk is higher in males than
females.
¯Çü Presentation is usually months or years a¯Ç£er starting treatment with bilateral concentric or binasal visual ¯ÇØeld defects.
Blurring and symptoms related to the defects may occur, but

there may be no symptoms, with normal VA unless scotomas
are at or close to ¯ÇØxation.
¯Çü Signs may be absent but optic atrophy may be present. Other
subtle signs can include peripheral atrophy, arteriolar narrowing, abnormal macular re¯ÇÜexes and surface wrinkling.
OCT can detect peripapillary retinal nerve ¯ÇØbre layer atrophy,
including prior to the development of ¯ÇØeld defects.
¯Çü Prognosis. Changes persist if treatment is stopped, but sometimes do not progress if continued.
¯Çü Screening. A baseline visual assessment, including visual
¯ÇØelds and OCT, is recommended prior to starting treatment
and therea¯Ç£er every 3 months. In young children and others
unable to perform ¯ÇØeld or OCT testing, the optimal monitoring modality has yet to be determined.

## Methotrexate

## Methotrexate is a very rare cause of acute or gradual visual loss

due to optic neuropathy. Reduced VA, dyschromatopsia and visual
¯ÇØeld loss occur and could be linked to folic acid metabolism, levels
of which should be checked. Vitamin B12 and folate supplementation may be helpful.

Sildena¯Çƒl

Non-arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy is a rare side
e¯Ç×ect. Cyanopsia is more common, but clears spontaneously.
A¯Ç×ected individuals report intensely blue-coloured vision, di¯Çøculty distinguishing redÔÇôgreen colours and photophobia.

VISUAL CORTEX

## Bevacizumab

## Bevacizumab is used for colon and rectal tumours and may rarely

result in corticular blindness, which is not fully reversible on stopping the medication.

EYELID TRAUMA

## Periocular haematoma

A ÔÇÿblack eyeÔÇÖ, consisting of a haematoma (focal collection of blood)
and/or periocular ecchymosis (di¯Çƒuse bruising) and oedema (Fig.
22.1A), is the most common blunt injury to the eyelid or forehead
and is generally innocuous. It is, however, critical to exclude the
following more serious conditions:
¯Çü Trauma to the globe or orbit. It is easier to examine the
globe before the lids become oedematous. Once swelling is
established, gentle sustained pressure to open the lids will
often displace tissue fluid sufficiently to allow visualization
of the anterior segment. It is critical not to apply any force
on the globe itself until its integrity has been confirmed.
Urgent imaging such as computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) or bedside ultrasonography (taking meticulous care to avoid applying pressure on
the globe) should be considered if there is suspicion of an
underlying injury to the eyeball and adequate clinical visualization is not possible.
¯Çü Orbital roof fracture, especially if the black eye is associated
with a subconjunctival haemorrhage without a visible posterior limit (which would indicate the anterior extension of a
posterior bleeding point) (Fig. 22.1B).
¯Çü Basal skull fracture, which may give rise to characteristic
bilateral ring haematomas (ÔÇÿpanda eyesÔÇÖ ÔÇô Fig. 22.1C).

TIP Bilateral periocular haematoma (ÔÇÿpandaÔÇÖ eyes) can be a
sign of a skull-base fracture.

## Laceration

The presence of a lid laceration, however insignificant, mandates careful exploration of the wound and examination of the
globe and adnexal structures. Any lid defect should be repaired
by direct closure whenever possible, even under tension, since
this affords the best functional and cosmetic result (Fig. 22.2A
and B).
¯Çü Super¯Çƒcial lacerations parallel to the lid margin without gaping can be sutured with 6-0 black silk or nylon. ¯Ç×e sutures are
removed a¯ÇØer 5ÔÇô6 days.
¯Çü Infection is always a risk, even from a small laceration (Fig. 22.2C).
¯Çü Lid margin lacerations invariably gape without careful closure
and to prevent notching must be sutured with optimal alignment (see Fig. 2.57C):

the meibomian gland ori¯Ç£ces, about 2 mm from the wound
edges and 2 mm deep and le¯ÇØ untied.

which are tied anteriorly.

{ ¯Ç×e lid margin silk suture is then tied so that the cut edges
slightly pucker the wound. ¯Ç×e ends are le¯ÇØ fairly long, e.g.
2 cm.

¯Çü Canalicular lacerations should be repaired within 24 hours

(Crawford tube), which is threaded down the lacrimal system
and tied in the nose, following which the laceration is sutured.
Alternatively, repair of a single canaliculus can be performed
using a monocanalicular stent (e.g. Mini Monoka ÔÇô Fig. 22.3B)
and, if necessary, suturing its footplate to the lid using 8-0
material. ¯Ç×e tubing is le¯ÇØ in situ for 3ÔÇô6 months.
¯Çü Tetanus status. Ensure that the patientÔÇÖs tetanus immunization
status is satisfactory a¯ÇØer any injury. Without prior immunization, 250 units of human tetanus immunoglobulin are given
intramuscularly (IM). If previously immunized but a booster
has not been administered within the last 10 years, subcutaneous tetanus toxoid should be given.

the so¯ÇØ tissues of the lid or orbit (Fig. 22.4A and C), a CT scan
should be undertaken (Fig. 22.4B and D).

TIP There is always a risk of orbital cellulitis after a
penetrating lid laceration, particularly if there is a retained
foreign body.

ORBITAL TRAUMA

Orbital ¯Çƒoor fracture

## Introduction

A blow-out fracture of the orbital ¯ÇÜoor is typically caused by a sudden increase in the orbital pressure from an impacting object that
is greater in diameter than the orbital aperture (about 5 cm), such
as a ¯Ç£st or tennis ball, so that the eyeball itself is displaced and
transmits rather than absorbs the impact (Fig. 22.5A). Since the
bones of the lateral wall and the roof are usually able to withstand
such trauma, the fracture most frequently involves the ¯ÇÜoor of the
orbit along the thin bone covering the infraorbital canal. Occasionally, the medial orbital wall may also be fractured. Fractures
of the orbital rim and adjacent facial bones require appropriately
tailored management. Clinical features vary with the severity of
trauma and the interval between injury and examination. Care
should be taken to ensure that a full evaluation for head and systemic injury has been performed and any necessary interspecialty
referrals initiated.

## Diagnosis

¯Çü Visual function, especially acuity, should be recorded and
monitored.
¯Çü Periocular signs include variable ecchymosis, oedema (Fig.
22.5B) and occasionally subcutaneous emphysema (a crackling
sensation on palpation due to air in the subcutaneous tissues).
¯Çü Infraorbital nerve anaesthesia involving the lower lid, cheek,
side of nose, upper lip, upper teeth and gums is common as the
fracture frequently involves the infraorbital canal.
¯Çü Diplopia may be caused by one of the following mechanisms:

{ Haemorrhage and oedema in the orbit may cause tightening of the septa connecting the inferior rectus and inferior
oblique muscles to the periorbita, thus restricting movement of the globe. Ocular motility usually improves as the
haemorrhage and oedema resolve.

{ Diplopia typically occurs in both upgaze (Fig. 22.6A
and C) and downgaze secondary to mechanical entrapment within the fracture of the inferior rectus or inferior
oblique muscle or adjacent connective tissue and fat (Fig.
22.6D).

A B

periocular ecchymosis, oedema and subconjunctival haemorrhage.

A

B

C

D

eye blow-out fracture of the left orbit in a child; (D) coronal CT scan showing entrapment of the left inferior rectus
muscle.

{ Forced duction and the di¯Çƒerential intraocular pressure
test (increasing IOP as a restricted muscle presses on the
globe) are positive. Diplopia may subsequently improve
if it is mainly due to entrapment of oedematous connective tissue and fat, but usually persists if there is signi¯Ç£cant
involvement of the muscles themselves.

{ Direct injury to an extraocular muscle, associated with
a negative forced duction test. ¯Ç×e muscle ¯Ç£bres usually
regenerate and normal function o¯ÇØen returns within about
2 months.
¯Çü Enophthalmos (Fig. 22.6B) may be present if the fracture
is severe, although it tends to manifest only after a few days
as initial oedema resolves. Late enophthalmos is uncommon, even in large fractures. Six months after a large
orbital floor fracture only 20% will have enophthalmos of
more than 2 mm.
¯Çü Ocular damage (e.g. hyphaema, angle recession, retinal dialysis) should be excluded by careful examination of the globe,
although this is relatively uncommon in association with a
blow-out fracture.
¯Çü Hess chart testing (Fig. 22.7) to map eye movements is useful
in assessment and monitoring.
¯Çü CT with coronal sections (Fig. 22.8 and see Fig. 22.6D) aids in
evaluation of the extent of a fracture and determination of the
nature of maxillary antral so¯ÇØ tissue densities, which may represent prolapsed orbital fat, extraocular muscles, haematoma
or unrelated antral polyps.

## Treatment

¯Çü Initial treatment generally consists of observation, with the
prescription of oral antibiotics. Ice packs and nasal decongestants may be helpful. ¯Ç×e patient should be instructed not
to blow his or her nose, because of the possibility of forcing
infected sinus contents into the orbit. Systemic steroids are
occasionally required for severe orbital oedema, particularly if
this is compromising the optic nerve.

912 Orbital Trauma

(superior rectus and inferior oblique) and restriction on downgaze (inferior rectus). There is
also secondary overaction of the right eye.

showing a defect in the orbital ¯Çƒoor and the ÔÇÿtear dropÔÇÖ sign
due to soft tissue prolapse into the maxillary antrum (arrow).

¯Çü Subsequent treatment is aimed at the prevention of permanent vertical diplopia and/or cosmetically unacceptable
enophthalmos.

{ Small cracks without herniation do not require treatment
as the risk of permanent complications is small.

{ Fractures involving up to one-half of the orbital ¯ÇÜoor, with
little or no herniation, no signi¯Ç£cant enophthalmos and
improving diplopia, also do not require treatment.

{ Fractures involving more than one-half of the orbital ¯ÇÜoor
may develop enophthalmos if le¯ÇØ untreated.

{ Fractures with entrapment of orbital contents, enophthalmos of greater than 2 mm and/or persistent and signi¯Ç£cant
diplopia in the primary position should be repaired within
2 weeks. However, it has recently been reported that in
patients with enophthalmos of less than 2 mm there is no
di¯Çƒerence in outcome between those undergoing surgery
at 2 weeks and those undergoing surgery a¯ÇØer 6 months.
In patients with diplopia and tissue incarceration but without muscle entrapment, improvement o¯ÇØen occurs without surgery. If the diplopia persists, delayed surgery is still
likely to achieve resolution of diplopia except in extreme
upgaze.
¯Çü ÔÇÿWhite-eyedÔÇÖ fracture is a subgroup for which urgent repair
is required to avoid permanent neuromuscular damage. ¯Ç×e
scenario is generally seen in patients less than 18 years of age,
typically with little visible external so¯ÇØ tissue injury and usually a¯Çƒects the orbital ¯ÇÜoor (see Fig. 22.6C). It involves the
acute incarceration of herniated tissue in a ÔÇÿtrap-doorÔÇÖ e¯Çƒect
and occurs because of the greater elasticity of bone in younger
people (see Fig. 22.6D). Patients may experience acute nausea, vomiting, headache and persistent activation of the oculocardiac re¯ÇÜex can occur. CT features may be subtle.
¯Çü Surgical repair is performed via a transconjunctival or subciliary incision or via the maxillary sinus, with elevation of
the periosteum from the orbital ¯ÇÜoor, freeing of trapped
orbital contents and repair of the bony defect with a synthetic
implant.

## Roof fracture

## Introduction

Roof fractures are rarely encountered by ophthalmologists. Isolated fractures, caused by falling on a sharp object or sometimes a
relatively minor blow to the brow or forehead, are most common
in children and o¯ÇØen do not require treatment. Fractures due to
major trauma, with associated displacement of the orbital rim or
signi¯Ç£cant disturbance of other craniofacial bones, typically a¯Çƒect
adults.

## Diagnosis

A haematoma of the upper eyelid is typical, together with periocular ecchymosis. ¯Ç×ese o¯ÇØen develop over the course of a few
hours and may progressively spread to the side opposite the fracture. Other features of orbital wall fracture as discussed above may
be present. Large fractures may be associated with pulsation of the
globe due to transmission of cerebrospinal ¯ÇÜuid (CSF) pressure,
best detected with applanation tonometry.

## Treatment

Small fractures may not require treatment, but it is important to
exclude a CSF leak, which carries a risk of meningitis. Sizeable
bony defects with downward displacement of fragments usually
warrant reconstructive surgery. General management is similar to
that of an orbital ¯ÇÜoor fracture (see above).

Blow-out medial wall fracture

Medial wall orbital fractures are usually associated with ¯ÇÜoor fractures. It is uncommon to ¯Ç£nd an isolated fracture of the medial
wall. Signs include periorbital ecchymosis and frequently subcutaneous emphysema, which typically develops on blowing the nose.

## Defective ocular motility involving abduction and adduction is

present if the medial rectus muscle is entrapped. CT will demonstrate the fracture. Treatment involves release of incarcerated tissue and repair of the bony defect (Fig. 22.9).

## Lateral wall fracture

Acute lateral wall fractures (see Fig. 22.11D) are rarely encountered by ophthalmologists. Because the lateral wall of the orbit is
more solid than the other walls, a fracture is usually associated
with extensive facial damage.

## Orbital haemorrhage

## Introduction

Orbital (retrobulbar) haemorrhage is important chie¯ÇÜy due to the
associated risk of acute orbital compartment syndrome with compressive optic neuropathy and can lead to irreversible blindness of
the a¯Çƒected eye in severe cases. It can occur without or in association with an orbital bony injury. Iatrogenic orbital haemorrhage
is not uncommon, typically resulting from a peri- or retrobulbar

A

B

(A) Defective left abduction; (B) CT coronal view showing
fractures of the medial wall (arrow) and ¯Çƒoor (arrowhead).
(Courtesy of A Pearson.)

local anaesthetic block performed to facilitate intraocular surgery.
Rare causes include bleeding from vascular anomalies and occasionally spontaneous haemorrhage due to poor clotting.

## Diagnosis

Proptosis, eyelid oedema and ecchymosis, haemorrhagic chemosis, ocular motility dysfunction, decreased visual acuity, elevated
intraocular pressure, optic disc swelling and a relative a¯Çƒerent
pupillary defect are among the possible signs.

## Treatment

## Treatment should be started immediately if progressive visual

deterioration occurs. Canthotomy alone is rarely adequate.
¯Çü Canthotomy. A¯ÇØer clamping the incision site for 60 seconds,
scissors are used to make a 1ÔÇô2 cm horizontal full-thickness
incision under local anaesthesia (e.g. 1ÔÇô2 ml lidocaine 1ÔÇô2%
with adrenaline) at the angle of the lateral canthus (Fig.
22.10A).
¯Çü Cantholysis. Following canthotomy, the lower lid is retracted
downwards and the inferior crus of the lateral canthal tendon
is cut (Fig. 22.10B) using blunt-tipped scissors, directed inferiorly and inserted adjacent and parallel to the lateral orbital
rim between conjunctiva and skin and angled away from the
eyeball. Blood is gently encouraged to drain. If necessary, the
superior limb of the tendon can also be cut, but this carries a
substantial risk of damage to adnexal structures.

TRAUMA TO THE GLOBE

## Introduction

## Terminology

¯Çü Closed injury is commonly due to blunt trauma. ¯Ç×e corneoscleral wall of the globe is intact.
¯Çü Open injury involves a full-thickness wound of the corneoscleral envelope.

914 Trauma to the Globe

A B

(arrow showing anterior orbital rim); (B) disinsertion of inferior crus of the lateral canthal tendon.

¯Çü Contusion is a closed injury resulting from blunt trauma.
Damage may occur at or distant to the site of impact.
¯Çü Rupture is a full-thickness wound caused by blunt trauma. ¯Ç×e
globe gives way at its weakest point, which may not be at the
site of impact.
¯Çü Laceration is a full-thickness defect in the eye wall produced
by a tearing injury, usually as the result of a direct impact.
¯Çü Lamellar laceration is a partial-thickness laceration.
¯Çü Incised injury is caused by a sharp object such as glass or a
knife.
¯Çü Penetrating injury refers to a single full-thickness wound,
usually caused by a sharp object, without an exit wound. A
penetrating injury may be associated with intraocular retention of a foreign body.
¯Çü Perforation consists of two full-thickness wounds, one entry
and one exit, usually caused by a missile.

## Investigation

¯Çü Plain radiographs may be taken when the presence of a foreign body is suspected (Fig. 22.11A). A metallic foreign body
which is within the eye will move up and down if the radiographs are taken in upgaze and downgaze.
¯Çü Ultrasonography may be useful in the detection of intraocular foreign bodies, globe rupture, suprachoroidal haemorrhage
and retinal detachment. It should be performed as gently as
possible if there is a possibility of an open globe injury, taking
great care not to apply pressure on the globe.
¯Çü CT is superior to plain radiography in the detection and localization of intraocular foreign bodies (Figs 21.11BÔÇôD). It is also
of value in determining the integrity of intracranial, facial and
intraocular structures (see Fig. 22.11C).
¯Çü MRI is more accurate than CT in the detection and assessment
of injuries of the globe itself, such as an occult posterior rupture, though not for bony injury. However, MRI should not be
performed if a ferrous metallic foreign body is suspected.
¯Çü Electrodiagnostic tests may be useful in assessing the integrity of the optic nerve and retina, particularly if some time has

passed since the original injury and there is the suspicion of a
retained intraocular foreign body (IOFB).

TIP MRI scanning should not be undertaken if a ferrous
metallic foreign body is suspected in a patient with a
penetrating injury.

## Blunt trauma

¯Ç×e most common causes of blunt trauma are sporting injuries
and assault. Severe blunt trauma to the globe results in anteroposterior compression with simultaneous expansion in the equatorial plane (Fig. 22.12) o¯ÇØen associated with a transient but severe
increase in IOP. Although the impact is primarily absorbed by the
lensÔÇôiris diaphragm and the vitreous base, damage can also occur
to the posterior pole. ¯Ç×e extent of ocular damage depends on the
severity of trauma. ¯Ç×e prognosis varies, but is usually determined
by the extent of retinal injury.

## Cornea

¯Çü Corneal abrasion involves a breach of the epithelium (Fig.
22.13A) and stains with ¯ÇÜuorescein (Fig. 22.13B). It is a painful condition, exacerbated by ocular movement. If located
over the pupillary area, vision may be signi¯Ç£cantly impaired.
Details of treatment are discussed under ÔÇÿRecurrent corneal
epithelial erosionÔÇÖ in Chapter 7
¯Çü Acute corneal oedema may develop following blunt trauma,
secondary to focal or di¯Çƒuse dysfunction of the endothelium
and is sometimes seen underlying a large abrasion. It is commonly associated with folds in Descemet membrane and stromal thickening, but usually clears spontaneously.
¯Çü Tears in Descemet membrane are usually vertical (Fig.
22.13C) and most commonly arise as the result of birth trauma.

## Hyphaema

Hyphaema (haemorrhage in the anterior chamber) is a common
complication of blunt ocular injury. ¯Ç×e source of bleeding is

typically the iris root or ciliary body face. Characteristically, the
blood settles inferiorly with a resultant ÔÇÿ¯ÇÜuid levelÔÇÖ (Fig. 22.14A),
except when the hyphaema is total (Fig. 22.14B). Uncontrolled
high IOP can result in ischaemic optic neuropathy and staining of
the cornea (Fig. 22.14C) (see Ch. 11).

## Anterior uvea

¯Çü Pupil. ¯Ç×e iris may momentarily be compressed against the
anterior surface of the lens by severe anteroposterior force,
with resultant imprinting of pigment from the pupillary margin. Transient miosis accompanies the compression, evidenced
by the pattern of pigment corresponding to the size of the
constricted pupil (Vossius ring ÔÇô Fig. 22.15A). Damage to the
iris sphincter may result in traumatic mydriasis, which can be
temporary or permanent. ¯Ç×e pupil reacts sluggishly or not at
all to both light and accommodation. Radial tears in the pupillary margin are common (Fig. 22.15B).
¯Çü Iridodialysis is a dehiscence of the iris from the ciliary body
at its root. ¯Ç×e pupil is typically D-shaped and the dialysis is

full hyphaema an occult posterior scleral rupture should be

seen as a dark biconvex area near the limbus (Fig. 22.15C).
Retroillumination shows the extent of the injury (Fig.
22.15D). An iridodialysis may be asymptomatic if it is
covered by the upper lid. However, monocular diplopia
and glare sometimes ensue if the dehiscence is exposed
in the palpebral aperture. Traumatic aniridia (360┬░ iridodialysis) is rare. In a pseudophakic eye, the detached iris
may be ejected through the cataract surgical incision (Fig.
22.16A). The symptoms of glare can be reduced and the
cosmetic appearance can be improved by inserting a prosthetic iris (Fig. 22.16B).
¯Çü Ciliary body (see below).

## Intraocular pressure

It is important for IOP to be monitored carefully, particularly in
the early period following trauma (see Ch. 11). Elevation of IOP
can occur for a variety of reasons, including hyphaema and in¯ÇÜammation. In the presence of hypotony, it is important to exclude an
occult open injury. Tears extending into the face of the ciliary body
(angle recession) are associated with a 6ÔÇô9% risk of late glaucoma
(Fig. 22.17).

## Lens

¯Çü Cataract formation is a common sequel to blunt trauma. Postulated mechanisms include direct damage to the lens ¯Ç£bres themselves and minute ruptures in the lens capsule with an in¯ÇÜux of
aqueous humour, hydration of lens ¯Ç£bres and consequent opaci¯Ç£cation. A ring-shaped anterior subcapsular opacity may underlie a
Vossius ring. Commonly opaci¯Ç£cation occurs in the posterior subcapsular cortex along the posterior sutures, resulting in a ¯ÇÜowershaped (ÔÇÿrosetteÔÇÖ) opacity (Fig. 22.18A) that may subsequently
disappear, remain stationary or progress to maturity (Fig. 22.18B).
Cataract surgery may be necessary for visually signi¯Ç£cant opacity.
¯Çü Subluxation of the lens may occur, secondary to tearing of
the suspensory ligament. A subluxated lens tends to deviate
towards the meridian of intact zonules. ¯Ç×e anterior chamber
may deepen over the area of zonular dehiscence if the lens
rotates posteriorly. ¯Ç×e edge of a subluxated lens may be visible under mydriasis (see Fig. 22.18B) and trembling of the iris
(iridodonesis) or lens (phakodonesis) may be seen on ocular
movement. Subluxation of magnitude su¯Çøcient to render the
pupil partly aphakic may result in uniocular diplopia. Lenticular astigmatism due to tilting may occur.

918 Trauma to the Globe

A B

implant and ciliary processes; (B) prosthetic iris. (Courtesy of C Barry.)

is asymmetry of anterior chamber depth. ¯Ç×e anterior chamber
of an a¯Çƒected eye is classically deep, with posterior rotation of
the irisÔÇôlens diaphragm and IOP in the a¯Çƒected eye is low. ¯Ç×e
rupture is o¯ÇØen found slightly behind the insertion of the rectus
muscles where the sclera is thinnest. Gentle B-scan ultrasonography can demonstrate a posterior rupture, but CT or MRI may be
necessary. ¯Ç×e principles of repair of a rupture in the sclera are
described later.

## Vitreous haemorrhage

Vitreous haemorrhage may occur, commonly in association with
posterior vitreous detachment. Pigment cells (ÔÇÿtobacco dustÔÇÖ) can
be seen ¯ÇÜoating in the anterior vitreous and though not necessarily associated with a retinal break, should always prompt careful
retinal assessment.

(arrowheads indicate the extent of the recession).

¯Çü Dislocation due to 360┬░ rupture of the zonular ¯Ç£bres is rare.
¯Ç×e lens may dislocate into the vitreous (Fig. 22.18C), or
less commonly, into the anterior chamber (Fig. 22.18D). An
underlying predisposing condition such as pseudoexfoliation
should considered.

## Globe rupture

Rupture of the globe may result from severe blunt trauma. ¯Ç×e
prognosis is poor if the initial visual level is light perception
or worse. ¯Ç×e rupture is usually anterior, in the vicinity of the
Schlemm canal, with prolapse of structures such as the lens,
iris, ciliary body and vitreous (Fig. 22.19A and B). An anterior
rupture may be masked by extensive subconjunctival haemorrhage. Rupture at the site of a surgical wound (e.g. cataract, keratoplasty, vitrectomy) commonly follows substantial blunt force.

## An occult posterior rupture can be associated with little visible

damage to the anterior segment, but should be suspected if there

## Commotio retinae

## Commotio retinae is caused by concussion of the sensory retina
resulting in cloudy swelling that gives the involved area a grey

appearance (Fig. 22.20A and B). It most frequently a¯Çƒects the temporal fundus. If the macula is involved, a ÔÇÿcherry-red spotÔÇÖ may be
seen at the fovea. Severe involvement may be associated with intraretinal haemorrhage that can involve the macula. ¯Ç×e prognosis in
mild cases is good, with spontaneous resolution in around 6 weeks.
Severe commotio may result in progressive pigmentary degeneration (Fig. 22.20C) and macular hole formation.

## Chorioretinitis sclopetaria

¯Ç×is term describes a closed globe injury that follows a highvelocity projectile passing into the orbit close to the outside of the
globe. Deformation from the sudden force causes the chorioretinal
layers to split and retract leaving the intact sclera exposed (Fig.
21.20D). Complications include vitreous haemorrhage and, occasionally, retinal detachment. ¯Ç×e prognosis depends on the cause
of the injury (low muzzle energy has a worse prognosis than a high
muzzle injury), the location of damage and whether the macula is

A B

C D
