involved. Most patients experience improvement of vision a¯ÇØer the
injury, but only 15% achieve 6/6 vision.

## Choroidal rupture

Choroidal rupture involves the choroid, Bruch membrane and
retinal pigment epithelium (RPE). It may be direct or indirect. Direct ruptures are located anteriorly at the site of impact
and run parallel with the ora serrata. Indirect ruptures occur
opposite the site of impact. ¯Ç×e visual prognosis is poor if the
fovea is involved (Fig. 22.21A). A fresh rupture may be partially
obscured by subretinal haemorrhage (Fig. 22.21B), which may
break through the internal limiting membrane with resultant
subhyaloid or vitreous haemorrhage. Weeks to months later,
on absorption of the blood, a white crescentic vertical streak
of exposed underlying sclera concentric with the optic disc
becomes visible (Fig. 22.21C and D). An uncommon late complication is the development of a choroidal neovascular membrane in the region of the rupture.

## Retinal breaks and detachment

Trauma is responsible for about 10% of all cases of retinal detachment (RD) and is the most common cause in children, particularly
boys. A variety of breaks may develop in traumatized eyes either at
the time of impact or subsequently.
¯Çü A retinal dialysis (Fig. 22.22A) is a break occurring at the ora
serrata, caused by traction from the relatively inelastic vitreous gel along the posterior aspect of the vitreous base. ¯Ç×e tear
may be associated with avulsion of the vitreous base, giving
rise to an overhanging ÔÇÿbucket-handleÔÇÖ appearance comprising a strip of ciliary epithelium, ora serrata and the immediate
post-oral retina into which basal vitreous gel remains inserted.
A traumatic dialysis occurs most frequently in the superonasal and inferotemporal quadrants. Although they occur at the
time of injury, they do not inevitably result in RD. In cases
where detachment occurs, subretinal ¯ÇÜuid commonly does not
develop until several months later and progression is typically
slow.

A B

C D

¯Çü Equatorial breaks are less frequent and are due to direct retinal disruption at the point of scleral impact.
¯Çü RD secondary to a giant tear may occasionally be seen (Fig.
22.22B).
¯Çü A macular hole may occur either at the time of injury or following resolution of commotio retinae (Fig. 22.22C).
¯Çü Choroidal detachment. ¯Ç×is can occur in a so¯ÇØ eye a¯ÇØer a
severe injury and needs to be distinguished from a retinal
detachment (Fig. 22.22D).

## Traumatic optic neuropathy

Traumatic optic neuropathy follows ocular, orbital or head
trauma and presents with sudden visual loss that cannot be
explained by other ocular pathology. It occurs in up to 5% of
facial fractures.
¯Çü Classi¯Çƒcation

{ Direct, due to blunt or sharp optic nerve damage from
agents such as displaced bony fragments, a projectile, or
local haematoma.

{ Indirect, in which force is transmitted secondarily to the
nerve without apparent direct disruption due to impacts
upon the eye, orbit or other cranial structures.
¯Çü Mechanisms include contusion, deformation, compression or
transection of the nerve, intraneural haemorrhage, shearing
(acceleration of the nerve at the optic canal where it is tethered to the dural sheath, thought to rupture the microvascular
supply), secondary vasospasm, oedema and transmission of a
shock wave through the orbit.
¯Çü Presentation. ¯Ç×ough major head injury is not unusual, associated trauma may be deceptively minor. Indirect neuropathy
is considerably more common than direct. Vision is o¯ÇØen very
poor from the outset, with only perception of light in around
50%. Typically, the only objective ¯Ç£nding is an a¯Çƒerent pupillary defect. ¯Ç×e optic nerve head and fundus are initially normal, with pallor developing over subsequent days and weeks
(Fig. 22.23). It is important to exclude reversible causes of traumatic visual loss such as compressive orbital haemorrhage (see
above).

922 Trauma to the Globe

A B

C D

haemorrhage associated with extensive choroidal detachment.

optic canal.

¯Çü Investigation. Assessment should be individualized. Some
clinicians request CT, MR or both for all cases, others limit
imaging to patients with observed visual decline. CT is more
e¯Çƒective in the demonstration of bony abnormalities such as
optic canal fracture, but MRI is superior for so¯ÇØ tissue changes
(e.g. haematoma). With either modality, very thin sections are
recommended.
¯Çü Treatment. Spontaneous visual improvement occurs in up to
about half of patients with an indirect injury. However, if there
is initially no light perception the prognosis is poor. Several
treatment options have been advocated but no clear bene¯Ç£t has
been shown and all carry signi¯Ç£cant risks.

{ Steroids (intravenous methylprednisolone) should be
considered for otherwise healthy patients with severe
visual loss or in those with delayed visual loss. If used,
these should be started within the ¯Ç£rst 8 hours, but the
optimal regimen has not been determined and their

A B

stage showing a cavity where the optic nerve head (arrow) has retracted from the dural
sheath. (Courtesy of J Donald M Gass, from Stereoscopic Atlas of Macular Diseases, Mosby
1997 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

use remains controversial. In a trial of high-dose corticosteroid treatment of patients with acute brain injury
(CRASH study) patients receiving steroids were at higher
risk of death.

{ Optic nerve decompression (e.g. endonasal, transethmoidal) may be considered if there is progressive visual
deterioration despite steroids. Compression of the nerve by
bony fragment or haematoma may also be an indication.
However, optic canal fracture is a poor prognostic indicator and there is no evidence that surgery improves the
outlook, whilst carrying a signi¯Ç£cant risk of complications.

## Optic nerve avulsion

## Optic nerve avulsion is rare and typically occurs when an object

intrudes between the globe and the orbital wall, displacing the
eye. Postulated mechanisms include sudden extreme rotation or
anterior displacement of the globe. Avulsion may be isolated or
occur in association with other ocular or orbital injuries. Fundus
examination shows a striking cavity where the optic nerve head
has retracted from its dural sheath (Fig. 22.24A and B). ¯Ç×ere is no
treatment and the visual prognosis depends on whether avulsion
is partial or complete.

## Abusive head trauma

Abusive head trauma (ÔÇÿshaken babyÔÇÖ syndrome) is a form of physical abuse occurring typically in children under the age of 2 years.
Mortality is more than 25% and it is responsible for up to 50% of
deaths from child abuse. It is caused principally by violent shaking,
o¯ÇØen in association with impact injury to the head. ¯Ç×ese children
should be examined in conjunction with a specialist paediatrician
whenever characteristic ophthalmic features are identi¯Ç£ed. ¯Ç×e
pattern of injury results from rotational acceleration and deceleration of the head, in contrast to the linear forces generated by a fall.
Direct trauma is not the main mechanism of brain damage. Brainstem traction injury causes apnoea and the consequent hypoxia
leads to raised intracranial pressure and ischaemia.

TIP A small child with characteristic ophthalmic features
of abusive head trauma (especially unexplained retinal
haemorrhages) should be examined in conjunction with a
specialist paediatrician.

¯Çü Presentation is frequently with irritability, lethargy and vomiting, which may be initially misdiagnosed as gastroenteritis or
other infection because the history of injury is withheld.
¯Çü Systemic features may include signs of impact head injury,
ranging from skull fractures to so¯ÇØ tissue bruises. Subdural
and subarachnoid haemorrhage is common and many survivors su¯Çƒer substantial neurological handicap. Multiple rib and
long bone fractures may be present. In some cases, examination ¯Ç£ndings are limited to the ocular features.
¯Çü Ocular features

{ Periocular bruising and subconjunctival haemorrhages
(Fig. 22.25A).

{ Retinal haemorrhages, bilateral or unilateral (20%), are the
most common feature. ¯Ç×e haemorrhages typically involve
multiple layers and may also be pre- or subretinal (Fig.
22.25B). ¯Ç×ey are most obvious in the posterior pole, but
o¯ÇØen extend to the periphery.

{ Poor visual responses and a¯Çƒerent pupillary defects.

{ Visual loss occurs in about 20% of cases, largely as a result
of cerebral damage.

## Penetrating trauma

## Introduction

## Penetrating injuries are three times more common in males than

females and typically occur in a younger age group (50% aged
15ÔÇô34). ¯Ç×e most frequent causes are assault, domestic/occupational accidents and sport. Serious eye trauma can be prevented by
the appropriate use of protective eyewear. ¯Ç×e extent of the injury is
determined by the size of the object, its speed at the time of impact

924 Trauma to the Globe

A B

(B) fundus haemorrhages involving di¯ÇØerent levels. (Courtesy of G Kontos ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. A; R Bates ÔÇô
¯Çƒg. B.)

and its composition. Sharp objects such as knives cause wellde¯Ç£ned cuts in the globe. However, the extent of damage caused
by ¯ÇÜying foreign bodies is determined by their kinetic energy. For
example, an air gun pellet is large and although relatively slowmoving has a high kinetic energy and can thus cause considerable
ocular damage. Of paramount importance is the risk of infection
that may follow any penetrating injury. Endophthalmitis or panophthalmitis, o¯ÇØen more severe than the initial injury, may ensue
with loss of the eye. Risk factors include delay in primary repair,
ruptured lens capsule and a dirty wound. Prophylactic intravitreal antibiotics (see Ch. 10) should be considered; vancomycin is
a common choice. As with eyelid trauma, tetanus status should be
ascertained. An eye with an open injury should be covered by a
protective eye shield.

TIP Most penetrating eye injuries need urgent surgical repair,
usually under general anaesthesia.

## Corneal

## Peaking of the pupil and shallowing of the anterior chamber are

key signs, though full-thickness corneal penetration may be present without these signs. ¯Ç×e technique of primary repair depends
on the extent of the wound and associated complications such as
iris incarceration, ¯ÇÜat anterior chamber and damage to intraocular
contents.
¯Çü Small shelving wounds with a formed anterior chamber may
not always require suturing as they can heal spontaneously or
with the aid of a so¯ÇØ bandage contact lens (Fig. 22.26A).
¯Çü Medium-sized wounds should be sutured without delay, especially if the anterior chamber is shallow or ¯ÇÜat. 10-0 nylon
is used, with shorter stitches near the visual axis opposing
perpendicular edges ¯Ç£rst and apical portions of wounds last.
A postoperative bandage contact lens may be applied. ¯Ç×e
corneoscleral junction should be sutured with 9-0 nylon.

¯Çü With iris involvement. If possible, the iris should be carefully
repositioned. However, excision of the prolapsed portion may
be required, particularly if it appears necrotic or if there is a
risk of contamination by foreign material (Fig. 22.26B).
¯Çü With lens damage (Fig. 22.26C) Wounds are treated by ¯Ç£rst
suturing the laceration then removing the lens by phacoemulsi¯Ç£cation or with a vitreous cutter. Primary implantation of
an intraocular lens is frequently associated with a favourable
visual outcome and a low rate of postoperative complications.
¯Çü Late scarring. If the injury involves the visual axis (Fig.
22.26D), penetrating keratoplasty may be needed.

## Scleral

## Signs of an occult scleral wound are described in the section above

discussing globe injury in blunt trauma.
¯Çü Anterior scleral lacerations have a better prognosis than those posterior to the ora serrata. A foreign body may be found in the wound
(Fig. 22.27A). An anterior scleral wound may be associated with
serious complications such as iridociliary prolapse (Fig. 22.27B
and C) and vitreous incarceration (Fig. 22.27D). ¯Ç×e latter, unless
appropriately managed, may result in subsequent ¯Ç£brous proliferation along the plane of incarcerated vitreous, with the development
of tractional RD. Viable uveal tissue should be reposited and prolapsed vitreous cut ¯ÇÜush with the wound, with subsequent vitreoretinal assessment. 8-0 nylon or 7-0 absorbable material such as
polyglactin should be used for scleral suturing in this setting.
¯Çü Posterior scleral lacerations are frequently associated with
retinal damage. Primary repair of the sclera to restore globe
integrity should be the initial priority.

## Prognosis

¯Ç×is is dependent on the severity of the injury and the complications that arise secondary to the injury. In particular, the prognosis
is in¯ÇÜuenced by: (a) the initial visual acuity, (b) the presence of a
relative pupillary defect, (c) the presence of an intraocular foreign

A B

C D

metallic foreign body is removed the anterior chamber may shallow and a single corneal
suture may then need to be inserted; (B) severe corneal slicing injury with extensive iris prolapse (arrow); (C) associated with lens damage, showing linear cut in the lens and prolapsed
lens contents (arrow); (D) late scarring within the visual axis.

body, (d) the presence of infection and (e) the presence of a retinal
injury/detachment.

## Retinal detachment

Traumatic tractional RD following a penetrating injury may result
from vitreous incarceration in the wound. Subsequent ¯Ç£broblastic
proliferation is exacerbated by the presence of blood in the vitreous gel. Contraction of the resultant epiretinal ¯Ç£brosis can progress
to cause an anterior tractional RD. A retinal break may develop
several weeks later, leading to a more rapidly progressing rhegmatogenous detachment.

TIP To prevent extrusion of the intraocular contents when
repairing a penetrating injury, it is important not to exert any
pressure on the eye.

Enucleation/evisceration

## Primary enucleation or evisceration should be considered for

extremely severe injuries, especially when it is impossible to
repair the sclera and where there is no prospect of retention of
vision (see Fig. 4.49). This usually reduces the rehabilitation
time and allows a rapid return to work. Secondary enucleation
or evisceration may be considered following primary repair
if the eye is blind and irreversibly damaged, particularly if it
is also unsightly and uncomfortable. In both instances, the
surgery is safe with a low complication and infection rate.
Based on anecdotal evidence, it has been recommended that
if enucleation or evisceration is to be performed it should
take place within 10ÔÇô14 days of the original injury in order to
prevent the rare complication of sympathetic ophthalmia (see
Ch. 12).

TIP In a patient with a small lid wound who has been working
with tools, always lift the lids to check for a small penetrating
injury in the sclera.

Super¯Ç×cial foreign body

## Subtarsal

A small foreign body, such as a particle of steel, coal or sand,
o¯ÇØen impacts on the corneal or conjunctival surface. ¯Ç×is may be
washed along the tear ¯Ç£lm into the lacrimal drainage system or
adhere to the superior tarsal conjunctiva (Fig. 22.28A) and abrade
the cornea with every blink, when a pathognomonic pattern of
linear corneal abrasions may be seen (Fig. 22.28B). Occasionally
a barbed foreign body, such as an insect (Fig. 22.28C) or plant
material, will become deeply embedded, with resultant substantial
discomfort.

TIP In a patient with a linear corneal abrasion, search for a
small foreign body on the tarsal surface.

## Corneal

¯Çü Clinical features. Marked ocular grittiness is characteristic.
Magni¯Ç£cation is o¯ÇØen required (Fig. 22.28D). Leukocytic in¯Ç£ltration is typically seen around the embedded foreign body
and ferrous particles in situ for even a few hours cause rust
staining of the bed of the abrasion. Mild secondary uveitis may
occur, with associated irritative miosis and photophobia.
¯Çü Management

{ A high index of suspicion should be maintained for the presence of an IOFB. Posteriorsegment examination and if necessary plain X-ray imaging can be used to help to exclude this.

{ A slit lamp is preferred to determine the position and
depth of the foreign body and to guide removal using a
sterile hypodermic needle (o¯ÇØen 25-gauge).

{ A residual ÔÇÿrust ringÔÇÖ is easiest to remove with a sterile burr.

{ Antibiotic ointment is instilled, subsequent duration of use
depending on severity.

{ A cycloplegic and topical non-steroidal anti-in¯ÇÜammatory
can be prescribed if required to promote comfort.

{ If a corneal foreign body is not removed, there is a signi¯Ç£cant risk of secondary infection and corneal ulceration.

A B

C D

abrasion stained with ¯Çƒuorescein; (C) insect retained in the inner canthus; (D) recently embedded corneal foreign body.

Any discharge, in¯Ç£ltrate, or signi¯Ç£cant uveitis should raise
suspicion of secondary bacterial infection, with subsequent management as for bacterial keratitis. Metallic particles seem to be associated with a lower risk of infection
than organic and stone foreign bodies.

## Intraocular foreign body

## Introduction

An IOFB may traumatize the eye mechanically, introduce infection
or exert other toxic e¯Çƒects on the intraocular structures. It may
lodge in any of the structures it encounters, thus may be located
anywhere in the anterior (Fig. 22.29A and B) or posterior segments
(Fig. 22.29C and D). Notable mechanical e¯Çƒects include cataract
formation secondary to capsular injury, vitreous liquefaction and
retinal haemorrhages and tears. Stones and organic foreign bodies
are associated with a high rate of infection. Intravitreal antibiotic
prophylaxis is generally recommended. Many substances are inert,
including glass, plastics, gold and silver. However, iron and copper may undergo dissociation and result in siderosis and chalcosis
respectively (see below).

## Diagnosis

¯Çü History. A careful history may be key to determining the origin and nature of the foreign material.
¯Çü Examination should pay special attention to possible sites of
entry or exit. Topical ¯ÇÜuorescein may be helpful to identify
an entry wound. Projection from wounds may allow logical
deduction of the location of a foreign body. Gonioscopy and
fundoscopy must be considered, taking care to minimize pressure on the eye. Associated signs such as lid laceration and
anterior segment damage should be noted.
¯Çü CT with axial and coronal cuts is used to detect and localize a metallic IOFB, providing cross-sectional images with a
sensitivity and speci¯Ç£city superior to plain radiography and
ultrasonography.
¯Çü MRI is contraindicated in the context of a metallic (speci¯Ç£cally
ferrous) IOFB.

## Treatment

¯Çü Magnetic removal of ferrous foreign bodies involves the creation of a sclerotomy adjacent to the foreign body, with application of a magnet followed by cryotherapy to the retinal break.

928 Trauma to the Globe

A B

C D

vitreous (arrow) with retinal detachment; (D) retinal impaction, with localized in¯Çƒammation
suggestive of early infection.

¯Çü Forceps removal may be used for non-magnetic foreign bodies and magnetic foreign bodies that cannot be
safely removed with a magnet. It involves pars plana vitrectomy and removal of the foreign body with forceps
either through the pars plana or limbus depending on the
circumstances.
¯Çü Prophylaxis against infection (see below).

TIP A missed intraocular foreign body with a ferrous
component can result in late visual loss secondary to siderosis.

## Siderosis

Steel is the most common foreign body constituent and is typically projected into the eye by hammering or power tool use. A
ferrous IOFB undergoes dissociation with the consequent deposition of iron in the intraocular epithelial structures, notably the
lens epithelium, iris and ciliary body epithelium and the sensory
retina. It exerts a toxic e¯Çƒect on cellular enzyme systems, with
resultant cell death. Signs include anterior capsular cataract,

consisting of radially distributed iron deposits on the anterior
lens capsule (Fig. 22.30A), reddish-brown staining of the iris
that may give rise to heterochromia iridis and pigmentary retinopathy followed by atrophy of the retina and RPE (Fig. 22.30B),
potentially leading to profound visual loss. Trabecular damage
can cause glaucoma. Electroretinography shows progressive
attenuation of the b-wave over time.

## Chalcosis

¯Ç×e ocular reaction to an IOFB with a high copper content
involves a violent endophthalmitis-like picture, o¯ÇØen with progression to phthisis bulbi. On the other hand, an alloy with a
relatively low copper content such as brass or bronze, results in
chalcosis. Electrolytically dissociated copper becomes deposited
intraocularly, resulting in a picture similar to that seen in Wilson
disease. ¯Ç×us, a KayserÔÇôFleischer ring develops and an anterior
ÔÇÿsun¯ÇÜowerÔÇÖ cataract. Retinal deposition results in golden plaques
visible ophthalmoscopically. Since copper is less retinotoxic than
iron, degenerative retinopathy does not develop and visual function may be preserved.

A B

with an impacted ferrous foreign body. (Courtesy of J Donald M Gass, from Stereoscopic
Atlas of Macular Diseases, Mosby 1997 ÔÇô ¯Çƒg. B.)

## Bacterial endophthalmitis

Endophthalmitis develops in about 1 in 10 cases of penetrating
trauma with retained foreign body.
¯Çü Risk factors include delay in primary repair, retained
IOFB and the position and extent of wounds. Clinical signs
are the same as acute postoperative endophthalmitis (see
Ch. 10).
¯Çü Pathogens. Staphylococcus spp. and Bacillus spp. are isolated
from about 90% of culture-positive cases.
¯Çü Management

{ Prophylactic antibiotics (e.g. cipro¯ÇÜoxacin 750 mg twice
daily or moxi¯ÇÜoxacin 400 mg once daily) can be given
for open globe injuries, together with topical antibiotic,
steroid and cycloplegia.

{ Prompt removal of a retained IOFB.

{ Prophylactic intravitreal antibiotics, especially for highrisk cases (e.g. agricultural injuries).

{ Culture of a removed IOFB.

{ Treatment for established cases is the same as for acute
postoperative bacterial endophthalmitis (see Ch. 10).

remainder at home. Chemical injury is the second commonest cause of work-based eye injuries at 12%, behind ÔÇÿforeign
bodyÔÇÖ injuries at 43%. Alkali burns are twice as common as
acid burns, since alkalis are more widely used both at home
and in industry. The severity of a chemical injury is related
to the properties of the chemical, the area of affected ocular
surface, duration of exposure (including retention of particulate chemical on the surface of the globe or under the upper
lid) and related effects such as thermal damage. Alkalis tend to
penetrate more deeply than acids as the latter coagulate surface
proteins, forming a protective barrier. The most commonly
involved alkalis are ammonia, sodium hydroxide and lime.

## Ammonia and sodium hydroxide characteristically produce

severe damage because of rapid penetration. Hydrofluoric acid
used in glass etching and cleaning also tends to rapidly penetrate the ocular tissues, whilst sulphuric acid may be complicated by thermal effects and high velocity impacts associated
with car battery explosion.

## Pathophysiology

¯Çü Damage by severe chemical injuries tends to progress as below:

CHEMICAL INJURIES

## Introduction

A chemical eye injury should be considered an acute emergency
that requires urgent treatment to reduce the risk of permanent
visual loss. Early and appropriate treatment is fundamental to the
successful management.

## Aetiology

## Chemical injuries range in severity from trivial to potentially

blinding. The majority are accidental, but a few are due to
assault. Two-thirds of accidental burns occur at work and the

{ Necrosis of the conjunctival and corneal epithelium with
disruption and occlusion of the limbal vasculature. Loss of
limbal stem cells may lead to conjunctivalization and vascularization of the corneal surface, or persistent corneal
epithelial defects with sterile corneal ulceration and perforation. Longer-term e¯Çƒects include ocular surface wetting disorders, symblepharon formation and cicatricial entropion.

{ Deeper penetration causes the breakdown and precipitation of glycosaminoglycans and stromal corneal
opaci¯Ç£cation.

{ Anterior chamber penetration results in iris and lens damage.

{ Ciliary epithelial damage impairs secretion of ascorbate,
which is required for collagen production and corneal repair.

{ Hypotony and phthisis bulbi may ensue in severe cases.

930 Chemical Injuries

¯Çü Healing

{ ¯Ç×e epithelium heals by migration of epithelial cells originating from limbal stem cells.

{ Damaged stromal collagen is phagocytosed by keratocytes
and new collagen is synthesized.

## Management

## Emergency treatment

A chemical burn requires urgent treatment by the ¯Ç£rst person
who sees the a¯Çƒected person. Immediate treatment is as follows:
¯Çü Copious irrigation is crucial to minimize duration of contact
with the chemical and to normalize the pH in the conjunctival sac as soon as possible. ¯Ç×e speed and e¯Çøcacy of irrigation
is the most important prognostic factor following chemical
injury. Topical anaesthetic should be instilled prior to irrigation, as this dramatically improves comfort and facilitates
cooperation. A lid speculum may be helpful. Tap water should
be used if necessary to avoid any delay, but a sterile balanced
bu¯Çƒered solution, such as normal saline or Ringer lactate,
should be used to irrigate the eye for 15ÔÇô30 minutes or until
the measured pH is neutral.
¯Çü Double-eversion of the upper eyelid should be performed so
that any retained particulate matter trapped in the fornices is
identi¯Ç£ed and removed.
¯Çü Debridement of necrotic areas of corneal epithelium should
be performed at the slit lamp to promote re-epithelialization
and remove associated chemical residue.
¯Çü Admission to hospital is usually required for severe injuries
(grade 3 and 4 ÔÇô see below) in order to ensure adequate instillation of eye drops in the early stages.

TIP The initial management of a chemical injury to the eye
is immediate copious ocular irrigation. If available, instillation
of a topical anaesthetic improves comfort and facilitates
cooperation.

## Grading of severity

## Acute chemical injuries are graded in order to plan subsequent

treatment and to provide an indication of the prognosis. Currently,
there are two classi¯Ç£cations used for grading chemical injuries:
the Roper-Hall classi¯Ç£cation (see below) and the Dua classi¯Ç£cation. In the Roper-Hall classi¯Ç£cation, grading is performed on the
basis of corneal clarity and severity of limbal ischaemia. ¯Ç×e latter is
assessed by observing the patency of the deep and super¯Ç£cial vessels
at the limbus. ¯Ç×e Dua classi¯Ç£cation is more detailed and is based on
the number of clock hours of a¯Çƒected limbus and the percentage of
conjunctival involvement. ¯Ç×is provides a better prognostic predictor than the Roper-Hall classi¯Ç£cation, particularly for grade 4 cases.
¯Çü Grade 1 (Fig. 22.31A) is characterized by a clear cornea
(epithelial damage only) and no limbal ischaemia (excellent
prognosis).
¯Çü Grade 2 (Fig. 22.31 B) shows a hazy cornea but with visible iris
detail and less than one-third of the limbus being ischaemic
(good prognosis).

¯Çü Grade 3 (Fig. 22.31C) manifests total loss of corneal epithelium, stromal haze obscuring iris detail and between onethird and one-half limbal ischaemia (guarded prognosis).
¯Çü Grade 4 (Fig. 22.31D) manifests with an opaque cornea and more
than half of the limbus showing ischaemia (poor prognosis).

## Other features that should be noted at the initial assessment are

the extent of corneal and conjunctival epithelial loss, iris changes,
the status of the lens and the IOP.

## Medical treatment

Most mild injuries (grade 1 and 2) are treated with topical antibiotic ointment for about a week, with topical steroids and cycloplegics if necessary. ¯Ç×e main aims of treatment of more severe
burns are to reduce in¯ÇÜammation, promote epithelial regeneration and prevent corneal ulceration. For moderateÔÇôsevere injuries, preservative-free drops should be used.
¯Çü Steroids reduce in¯ÇÜammation and neutrophil in¯Ç£ltration
and address anterior uveitis. However, they also impair stromal healing by reducing collagen synthesis and inhibiting
¯Ç£broblast migration. For this reason, topical steroids may be
used initially (usually 4ÔÇô8 times daily, strength depending on
injury severity) but must be tailed o¯Çƒ a¯ÇØer 7ÔÇô10 days when
sterile corneal ulceration is most likely to occur. Steroids
may be replaced by topical non-steroidal anti-in¯ÇÜammatory
drugs, which do not a¯Çƒect keratocyte function.
¯Çü Cycloplegia to improve comfort (e.g. atropine 1% twice daily).
¯Çü Preservative-free topical antibiotic drops are used for prophylaxis of bacterial infection (e.g. chloramphenicol four
times daily).
¯Çü Ascorbic acid improves wound healing by promoting the
synthesis of mature collagen by corneal ¯Ç£broblasts. Topical
sodium ascorbate 10% can be given 2-hourly in addition to a
systemic dose of 1ÔÇô2 g vitamin C (L-ascorbic acid) four times
daily (not in patients with renal disease).
¯Çü Citric acid is a powerful inhibitor of neutrophil activity and
reduces the intensity of the in¯ÇÜammatory response. Chelation
of extracellular calcium by citrate also appears to inhibit collagenase. For grade 3 and 4, topical sodium citrate 10% should be
instilled 2-hourly for about 10 days and may also be given orally
(2 g four times daily). ¯Ç×e aim is to eliminate the second wave of
phagocytes, which normally occurs about 7 days a¯ÇØer the injury.
Ascorbate and citrate can be tapered as the epithelium heals.
¯Çü Tetracyclines are e¯Çƒective collagenase inhibitors and also
inhibit neutrophil activity and reduce ulceration. ¯Ç×ey should
be considered if there is signi¯Ç£cant corneal melting and can
be administered both topically (tetracycline ointment four
times daily) and systemically (doxycycline 100 mg twice daily
tapering to once daily). Acetylcysteine 10% six times daily is
an alternative anticollagenase agent given topically.
¯Çü Symblepharon formation should be prevented as necessary by
lysis of developing adhesions with a sterile glass rod or damp
cotton bud.
¯Çü IOP should be monitored, with treatment if necessary. Oral
acetazolamide is recommended to avoid adding further to the
ocular surface burden.
¯Çü Periocular skin injury may require a dermatology
opinion.

A B

C D

(B) grade 2 ÔÇô corneal haze but visible iris detail; (C) grade 3 ÔÇô corneal haze obscuring iris
details; (D) grade 4 ÔÇô marked conjunctival ischaemia and an opaque cornea.

## Surgery

¯Çü Early surgery may be necessary to promote revascularization of
the limbus, restore the limbal cell population and re-establish the
fornices. One or more of the following procedures may be used:

{ Advancement of Tenon capsule with suturing to the limbus is aimed at re-establishing limbal vascularity to help to
prevent the development of corneal ulceration.

{ Limbal stem cell transplantation from the patientÔÇÖs other
eye (autogra¯ÇØ) or from a donor (allogra¯ÇØ) is aimed at
restoring normal corneal epithelium.

{ Amniotic membrane gra¯ÇØing to promote epithelialization
and suppression of ¯Ç£brosis.

{ Gluing or keratoplasty may be needed for actual or
impending perforation.
¯Çü Late surgery may involve:

{ Division of conjunctival bands (Fig. 22.32A) and symblepharon (Fig. 22.32B).

{ Correction of eyelid deformities such as cicatricial entropion (see Fig. 2.55B).

{ Keratoplasty for corneal scarring (Fig. 22.32C) should
be delayed for at least 6 months and preferably longer, to
allow maximal resolution of in¯ÇÜammation.

{ In a very severely damaged eye (Fig. 22.32D) a keratoprosthesis may be required (see Fig. 8.11).

THERMAL BURNS

¯Ç×ermal injuries range in severity from trivial to potentially
blinding. Most involve the eyelids and face, but the surface of the
cornea may be burnt resulting in severe corneal scarring (Fig.
22.33A and B). Most mild injuries are treated with topical antibiotic drops for about a week with topical steroids and cycloplegics.

## Plastic surgery to the lids may be required if there is cicatrization

and abnormal lid position. Corneal scarring will usually require
keratoplasty.

{ Conjunctival or other mucous membrane gra¯ÇØing.

932 Thermal Burns

A B

C D

(C) corneal scarring with pannus; (D) extensive cicatrization.
